contents:
Este archivo existe únicamente para responder a la URL /search con la plantilla de diseño search relacionada.
No se muestra ningún contenido aquí, todo el contenido se basa en la plantilla layouts/page/search.html
Establecer una prioridad muy baja en el mapa del sitio le dirá a los motores de búsqueda que éste no es un contenido importante.
Esta implementación utiliza Fusejs, jquery y mark.js
Configuración inicial La búsqueda depende del tipo de contenido de salida adicional de JSON en config.toml
``` [outputs] home = [“HTML”, “JSON”] ```
Búsqueda de archivos adicionales Para buscar campos adicionales definidos en el front matter, debes añadirlo en 2 lugares.
Editar layouts/_default/index.JSON Esto expone los valores en /index.json: por ejemplo, para agregar categories ``` … “contents”:{{ .Content | plainify | jsonify }} {{ if .Params.tags }}, “tags”:{{ .Params.tags | jsonify }}{{end}}, “categories” : {{ .Params.categories | jsonify }}, … ```
Editar las opciones de fuse.js para buscar static/js/search.js ``` keys: [ “title”, “contents”, “tags”, “categories” ] ```

contents:
This file exists solely to respond to /search URL with the related search layout template.
No content shown here is rendered, all content is based in the template layouts/page/search.html
Setting a very low sitemap priority will tell search engines this is not important content.
This implementation uses Fusejs, jquery and mark.js
Initial setup Search depends on additional output content type of JSON in config.toml ``` [outputs] home = [“HTML”, “JSON”] ```
Searching additional fileds To search additional fields defined in front matter, you must add it in 2 places.
Edit layouts/_default/index.JSON This exposes the values in /index.json i.e. add category ``` … “contents”:{{ .Content | plainify | jsonify }} {{ if .Params.tags }}, “tags”:{{ .Params.tags | jsonify }}{{end}}, “categories” : {{ .Params.categories | jsonify }}, … ```
Edit fuse.js options to Search static/js/search.js ``` keys: [ “title”, “contents”, “tags”, “categories” ] ```

Edit layouts/_default/index.JSON This exposes the values in /index.json i.e. add category ``` … “contents”:{{ .Content | plainify | jsonify }} {{ if .Params.tags }}, “tags”:{{ .Params.tags | jsonify }}{{end}}, “categories” : {{ .Params.categories | jsonify }}, … ```
Edit fuse.js options to Search static/js/search.js ``` keys: [ “title”, “contents”, “tags”, “categories” ] ```

contents:
Lesson Content In the verbal section of the GMAT, between 9 and 13 of the 36 questions will be Sentence Correction questions.
In this course, we will cover all of the concepts, strategies, and skills that you’ll need to correctly answer every Sentence Correction question you see on test day.
Sentence Structure Subject-Verb Agreement Pronouns and Antecedents Modifiers Verb Tense, Mood, and Voice Parallelism Logical Comparisons Let’s get started on mastering Sentence Correction by discussing the basic layout of Sentence Correction questions.
summary:

In this course, we will cover all of the concepts, strategies, and skills that you’ll need to correctly answer every Sentence Correction question you see on test day.
Sentence Structure Subject-Verb Agreement Pronouns and Antecedents Modifiers Verb Tense, Mood, and Voice Parallelism Logical Comparisons Let’s get started on mastering Sentence Correction by discussing the basic layout of Sentence Correction questions.
title: 1.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content Each Sentence Correction question presents us with a sentence, and all or part of the sentence is underlined.
The first answer choice, answer choice (A), offers phrasing that is a repeat of the underlined portion of the original sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Sentence Correction questions, the first answer choice offers phrasing that is a repeat of underlined portion of the original sentence. Each of the other four choices presents an alternative way of wording the underlined portion of the sentence.
Our task in answering a Sentence Correction question is to select the answer choice that offers wording that produces the best version of the sentence.
Generally, the best version of the sentence will have the following characteristics:
It will be grammatically correct. It will effectively convey a meaning that makes sense. 💡MUST KNOW:
Our task in answering a Sentence Correction question is to select the answer choice that offers wording that produces the best version of the sentence.
To see how Sentence Correction questions work, let’s consider some examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 1 Public confidence in the promises made by politicians are at an all-time low, yet turnout at the polls are higher than ever. 1. [ ] (A) the promises made by politicians are at an all-time low, yet turnout at the polls are higher > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) politicians who make promises are at an all-time low, yet it has higher turnout at the polls > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) promises made by politicians is at an all-time low, yet turnout at the polls is higher 4. [ ] (D) the promises politicians make are at an all-time low, yet its turnout at the polls is higher > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) promises politicians make is at an all-time low, yet with turnout at the polls, it is higher > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium While the five answer choices in this question are somewhat similar to each other, there are key differences between the choices. By carefully reading the sentence version produced with each choice, we can see that only one of the sentence versions both is grammatically correct and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(A) Public confidence in the promises made by politicians are at an all-time low, yet turnout at the polls are higher than ever.
The version created with this choice is not grammatically correct because the singular subject “public confidence” is paired with the plural verb “are,” and the singular subject “turnout” is paired with the plural verb “are.”
“Public confidence … are” and “turnout … are” are not correct.
What would be correct is “Public confidence … is” and “turnout … is.”
If you have a hard time seeing this issue, one powerful strategy you can use is to cross off the phrases between the nouns and verbs:
Public confidence in the promises made by politicians are
turnout at the polls are
By crossing off the intervening phrases, we are able to more clearly see which nouns are supposed to pair with which verbs, and then assess whether the pairing is grammatical. (B) Public confidence in politicians who make promises are at an all-time low, yet it has higher turnout at the polls than ever.
The version created with this choice is not grammatically correct because the singular subject “public confidence” is paired with the plural verb “are.”
If you have a hard time seeing this issue, one powerful strategy you can use is to cross off the phrase between the noun and verb:
Public confidence in politicians who make promises are
Additionally, the singular pronoun “it” could only possibly refer to either the singular noun “public confidence” or the singular noun “all-time low”; all of the other nouns in the sentence are plural and thus cannot be paired with a singular pronoun. After all, neither “public confidence has higher turnout at the polls” nor “all-time low has higher turnout at the polls” makes any sense. People, not a quality such as “confidence” or an amount such as “low,” go to the polls. YOUR CHOICE(C) Public confidence in promises made by politicians is at an all-time low, yet turnout at the polls is higher than ever.
In this version, “Public confidence … is” and “turnout … is” are grammatically correct.
Notice also that, when we read this version, there is no question in our minds regarding what the sentence is meant to convey. Because the singular nouns are properly paired with singular verbs, we can clearly see which “action” belongs to which thing in the sentence. We see that “public confidence” is the thing that “is at an all-time low,” and that “turnout” is the thing that “is higher than ever.” Since this version is grammatically correct and effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense, it is the correct answer. (D) Public confidence in the promises politicians make are at an all-time low, yet its turnout at the polls is higher than ever.
This version is not grammatically correct because the singular subject “public confidence” is paired with the plural verb “are.”
If you have a hard time seeing this issue, one powerful strategy you can use is to cross off the phrase between the noun and verb:
Public confidence in the promises politicians make are
Additionally, the singular “its” could only possibly refer to the singular “public confidence,” but saying that the “turnout” of “public confidence” is higher makes no sense. People, not a quality they have such as “confidence,” go to the polls. (E) Public confidence in promises politicians make is at an all-time low, yet with turnout at the polls, it is higher than ever.
In this version, the pronoun “it” in “it is higher than ever,” could refer only to either “public confidence” or “turnout at the polls.” So, this version conveys either of the following:
with turnout at the polls, public confidence is higher than ever
with turnout at the polls, turnout is higher than ever
Notice that it doesn’t make sense to say that public confidence is higher than ever with turnout at the polls. After all, public confidence is not “with” turnout.
Also, it certainly doesn’t make sense to say that turnout is higher than ever with turnout at the polls.
Thus, the meaning of the version created with this choice does not make sense.
Key Concept: Check to be sure that singular nouns are paired with singular verbs and that plural nouns are paired with plural verbs. Key Concept: When checking for subject-verb agreement, cross off any prepositional phrases that appear between the subject and finite verb.
Key Concept: Be sure that the noun to which a pronoun refers makes sense as the person or thing performing the action associated with that pronoun.
Key Concept: Don’t select answer choices that produce sentence versions that convey illogical meanings.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 2 Research has shown that bananas in a container with kiwis causes the kiwis to ripen quickly. 1. [ ] (A) that bananas in a container with kiwis causes the kiwis > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) that, if bananas are in a container with kiwis, they cause them > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) bananas and kiwis in a container, causing them > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) that bananas in a container with kiwis cause the kiwis 5. [ ] (E) that, when bananas are in a container and cause kiwis > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium We can see that, while the five answer choices in this question are somewhat similar to each other, there are key differences between the choices. By carefully reading the sentence version produced via the use of each choice, we can see that only one of the sentence versions both is grammatically correct and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(A) Research has shown that bananas in a container with kiwis causes the kiwis to ripen quickly.
This version is not grammatically correct because the plural subject “bananas” is paired with the singular verb “causes.” “Bananas … causes” is not correct.
What would be correct is “bananas … cause.”
(B) Research has shown that, if bananas are in a container with kiwis, they cause them to ripen quickly.
This version has no grammatical errors. However, if we look carefully, we can see that the meaning conveyed by this version is not clear.
Notice that the sentence is about bananas and kiwis. OK. Now, notice that the sentence says, “they cause them to ripen quickly.”
The issue this version has is that whether “they” and “them” refers to “bananas” or “kiwis” is not clear. Is the sentence meant to convey that bananas cause kiwis to ripen quickly or that kiwis cause bananas to ripen quickly? The sentence does not make clear which is which.
So, this version is incorrect because it does not effectively convey a clear meaning.
(C) Research has shown bananas and kiwis in a container, causing them to ripen quickly.
This version is grammatically correct, but what it conveys does not make sense. Notice that this version says, “Research has shown bananas and kiwis in a container.”
So, essentially this version says, “Research has shown bananas and kiwis.”
Would research show bananas and kiwis? Of course not.
Research doesn’t show fruit. It would show something about fruit, but it would not show bananas and kiwis themselves. So, what this version conveys is nonsensical.
A version that conveys a nonsensical meaning is not correct.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Research has shown that bananas in a container with kiwis cause the kiwis to ripen quickly.
The version created via the use of this choice is grammatically correct and conveys a meaning that makes complete sense.
“bananas … cause” is grammatically correct.
Notice also that the meaning conveyed is clear. When we read this version, there’s no question in our minds regarding what the sentence is meant to convey.
Since this version is grammatically correct and effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense, it is the correct answer.
(E) Research has shown that, when bananas are in a container and cause kiwis to ripen quickly.
Notice what we get if we put this answer choice together with the non-underlined portions:
Research has shown that, when bananas are in a container and cause kiwis to ripen quickly.
All we have is the beginning of a sentence. When we read this version, we are left wondering, “When bananas are in a container and cause kiwis to ripen quickly, what?”
So, this version does not express a complete thought. It’s just part of a sentence, not an entire sentence.
Since this version is not a complete sentence, this version is structurally flawed and is not correct.
Correct Answer: (D) Don’t worry if you didn’t catch all the issues in the above examples.
Let’s now discuss what Sentence Correction Questions test.
summary:
Lesson Content Each Sentence Correction question presents us with a sentence, and all or part of the sentence is underlined.
The first answer choice, answer choice (A), offers phrasing that is a repeat of the underlined portion of the original sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Sentence Correction questions, the first answer choice offers phrasing that is a repeat of underlined portion of the original sentence. Each of the other four choices presents an alternative way of wording the underlined portion of the sentence.
title: 1.2 The Basic Layout of Sentence Correction Questions

contents:
Lesson Content It would be easy to get the impression that Sentence Correction questions test only grammar knowledge, and the truth is that grammar errors do appear frequently in Sentence Correction questions.
However, while grammar knowledge may serve as a good foundation for correctly answering Sentence Correction questions, such knowledge is neither sufficient to get us to the correct answer to many questions nor what Sentence Correction really tests.
💡MUST KNOW:
Grammar knowledge may serve as a good foundation for correctly answering Sentence Correction questions, but grammar knowledge is neither sufficient to get us to the correct answer to many Sentence Correction questions nor what Sentence Correction really tests.
So, if Sentence Correction is not a test of grammar knowledge, what does Sentence Correction test?
What Sentence Correction really tests is our skill in noticing issues.
Consider the following example.
A study of the feeding behaviors of bats indicate that a bat can consume twenty mosquitos in a minute.
If we look closely, we can see that the singular noun “study” is paired with the plural verb “indicate” to form “a study … indicate,” which is incorrect.
A study of the feeding behaviors of bats indicate that a bat can consume twenty mosquitos in a minute.
Notice that “study” is not next to “indicate,” as it might be in a test of basic grammar knowledge.
So, if this sentence were in a Sentence Correction question, the question would test our skill in noticing that “study” and “indicate” are incorrectly paired even though the fact that they are incorrectly paired is obscured by the presence of “of the feeding behaviors of bats” between “study” and “indicate.”
Here’s another example.
White-tailed deer, found in Canada, the United States, Mexico, and Central and South America, they are the smallest members of the North American deer family.
The sentence above may look OK at first, but there’s an issue that we can notice: the subject of the sentence is repeated unnecessarily.
White-tailed deer, found in Canada, the United States, Mexico, and Central and South America, they are the smallest members of the North American deer family.
The sentence begins with a subject, “white tailed deer.” Then, “they,” which refers to “white tailed deer,” also serves as the subject of the sentence.
Of course, it’s likely that we could study English grammar fairly extensively and never run into a discussion of unnecessarily repeated subjects, since it’s fairly obvious that repeating a subject for no reason does not make sense.
The following example has another type of issue.
Many crops have been damaged by the ongoing flooding, and they are raising food prices nationwide.
The above sentence is grammatically correct, but it has a significant issue: because the only plural noun in the sentence to which “they” could refer is “crops,” the sentence seems to convey that the crops themselves are raising prices.
GMAT Sentence Correction constantly tests our skill in noticing things by presenting us with sentences that may seem to be well constructed but are grammatically flawed or convey illogical or nonsensical meanings.
💡MUST KNOW:
GMAT Sentence Correction constantly tests our skill in noticing things by presenting us with sentences that may seem to be well constructed but are grammatically flawed or convey illogical or nonsensical meanings.
So, what does all of the above mean to us? It means that training to master GMAT Sentence correction must involve not only reviewing sentence construction basics and grammar but also learning to notice all kinds of issues in the construction of sentences and in the meanings conveyed by sentences.
That GMAT Sentence Correction would test our skill in noticing issues totally makes sense, because the GMAT is an entrance exam, not for grammar school, but for business school, where noticing the logic of and issues in business strategies, accounting methods, and other aspects of businesses is key to success.
💡MUST KNOW:
Training to master GMAT Sentence Correction must involve not only reviewing sentence construction basics and grammar but also learning to notice issues in the construction of sentences and in the meanings conveyed by sentences.
In the chapters to come, we’ll discuss numerous types of issues that can appear in Sentence Correction questions.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is well written or has a significant issue. For a well-constructed sentence that conveys a meaning that makes sense, select Correct. For a sentence with a significant issue select Incorrect. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 To often be left by power outages without electricity or the ability to pump significant quantities of water, the people in the area stock up on supplies. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 The time that a Formula E car takes to accelerate from 0 to 100 kilometers per hour is faster than the time a Formula 1 car takes to do so. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 3 By the time Election Day arrives, both candidates, having done their utmost to win, will be thoroughly exhausted. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 While setting the world record for speed achieved by a sailing yacht by sailing at close to 100 kilometers per hour, the yacht Hydroptere was constructed in shipyards in France. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 5 The tennis shoes that Adele borrowed recently were taken from her car by a thief. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 6 The author said in 2020 the price of her books would need to double. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 7 The Treaty of Versailles set forth the terms of peace between the Allies and Germany and held Germany responsible for the destruction of World War I in Europe. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
This sentence might seem OK at first. However, by carefully considering what it’s actually conveying, we can see that it conveys the illogical meaning that people stock up on supplies in order “to often be left … without electricity or the ability to pump significant quantities of water.” One possible version that makes sense is:
Because they are often left by power outages without electricity or the ability to pump significant quantities of water, the people in the area stock up on supplies.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
While we may be able to tell what this sentence is meant to convey, if we consider this sentence carefully, we can see that it conveys the illogical meaning that one amount of time is faster than another amount of time. An amount of time would not be faster than another amount of time. What the sentence should convey is that the Formula E car takes less time to accelerate than the Formula 1 car takes. One possible correct version of the sentence is:
A Formula E car takes less time to accelerate from 0 to 100 kilometers per hour than a Formula 1 car takes to do so.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
The sentence is well constructed and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
The construction of the sentence is grammatically correct, but the sentence still has a significant issue: the way in which it’s worded results in its conveying that events happened in a way that does not make sense, as the sentence conveys that Hydroptere set the world record for speed achieved by a sailing yacht while it was being constructed in shipyards. Of course, those two events could not have occurred at the same time.
One version of the sentence that makes sense is:
The yacht Hydroptere, which was constructed in shipyards in France, set the world record for speed achieved by a sailing yacht by sailing at close to 100 kilometers per hour.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
Did Adele recently borrow the tennis shoes or did the thief recently take them? Because of the placement of the adverb “recently” between the verbs “borrowed” and “were taken,” we can’t be sure. To say that differently, consider how we might read this sentence. Some of you read it as:
The tennis shoes that Adele borrowed recently were taken from her car by a thief.
However, some of you read it as:
The tennis shoes that Adele borrowed recently were taken from her car by a thief.
In other words, the meaning conveyed by the sentence is ambiguous. So, the sentence can’t be correct.
Either of the following sentences would be correct. Notice that each conveys something different:
The tennis shoes that Adele recently borrowed were taken from her car by a thief.
or
The tennis shoes that Adele borrowed were recently taken from her car by a thief.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
Notice the placement of the phrase “in 2020.” What happened when? Did the author make the statement in 2020, or was the statement made about something that would occur by 2020? In other words, some of you read the sentence as:
The author said in 2020 the price of her books would need to double.
And some of you read it as:
The author said in 2020 the price of her books would need to double.
In other words, the sentence conveys an ambiguous meaning, so it can’t be correct.
Look at the following corrected sentences, each of which clearly conveys one meaning:
The author said that the price of her books would need to double in 2020.
The above version makes clear that the doubling must occur in 2020.
In 2020, the author said that the price of her books would need to double.
The above version makes clear that the author made the statement in 2020.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 7 Explanation:
Destruction of World War I makes no sense, as it seems to convey that the war itself was destroyed. How can a World War be destroyed? Europe could be destroyed, but a World War could not. Notice that, although there are no errors of grammar in the sentence, it is illogical and thus incorrect.
A corrected sentence might read:
The Treaty of Versailles set forth the terms of peace between the Allies and Germany and held Germany responsible for the destruction of Europe caused by World War I.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now begin discussing some important strategies we can use in answering Sentence Correction questions.
summary:
Lesson Content It would be easy to get the impression that Sentence Correction questions test only grammar knowledge, and the truth is that grammar errors do appear frequently in Sentence Correction questions.
However, while grammar knowledge may serve as a good foundation for correctly answering Sentence Correction questions, such knowledge is neither sufficient to get us to the correct answer to many questions nor what Sentence Correction really tests.
💡MUST KNOW:
title: 1.3 GMAT Sentence Correction Questions Primarily Test Skill in Noticing Issues.

contents:
Lesson Content To consistently succeed in identifying issues in the sentence versions in Sentence Correction questions, we have to read the sentences in their entirety, or holistically.
💡MUST KNOW:
To consistently succeed in identifying issues in the sentence versions in Sentence Correction questions, we have to read the sentences in their entirety, or holistically. If we consider the sentences only one part at a time, we can easily fool ourselves into thinking an incorrect version is correct.
For example, we could confirm that the subject and main verb of a sentence work logically with each other, that the adjectives in the sentence work logically with what they modify, and that there are no issues with the pronouns in the sentence and decide that the sentence is correct.
So, you can see that, since Sentence Correction versions can have a variety of issues involving not just grammar but also structure and meaning conveyed, we have to consider the sentences as sentences, not just as collections of words and phrases that might have errors.
Consider the following example.
(By the way, for best results, take your time in answering these example questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 3 Ostriches are able to run fast for very long distances, but they are able to escape predators such as hyenas that normally catch prey by exhausting the prey. 1. [ ] (A) Ostriches are able to run fast for very long distances, but they are > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Because ostriches are able to run fast for very long distances, it is > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) To be able to run fast for very long distances, ostriches are > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) An ostrich, able to run fast for very long distances, are > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Since it is able to run fast for very long distances, the ostrich is View Solution Question Level: medium Some of the incorrect versions of the sentence in this question are clearly grammatically flawed. However, other incorrect versions are grammatically perfect and are incorrect only because they convey illogical meanings. So, checking only for grammar errors is not sufficient for arriving at the correct answer. Determining which choice is the correct answer requires reading each version in its entirety to see whether what it conveys makes sense.
(A) Ostriches are able to run fast for very long distances, but they are able to escape predators such as hyenas that normally catch prey by exhausting the prey.
This version is grammatically perfect. The plural subject “ostriches” agrees with the plural verb “are,” the plural pronoun “they” logically refers to the plural “ostriches,” and the rest of the sentence is free of grammar errors.
Notice, however, that this version conveys a nonsensical meaning.
The word “but” is used to communicate that two ideas connected by “but” contrast with each other, but in this case, the two ideas connected by “but” would never contrast with each other.
The two ideas connected by “but” are “Ostriches are able to run fast for very long distances” and “they are able to escape.” Of course, being able to run fast and being able to escape don’t contrast with each other. Instead, the fact that ostriches are able to run fast explains why they are able to escape. So, saying that ostriches are able to run fast “but” they are able to escape does not make sense.
(B) Because ostriches are able to run fast for very long distances, it is able to escape predators such as hyenas that normally catch prey by exhausting the prey.
This version is clearly grammatically flawed. It starts off mentioning the plural “ostriches,” but then says, “it is able to escape.” The singular pronoun “it” cannot refer to the plural “ostriches.” So, “it” has nothing to refer to.
(C) To be able to run fast for very long distances, ostriches are able to escape predators such as hyenas that normally catch prey by exhausting the prey.
This version is grammatically correct. However, by reading this version in its entirety, we can see that it conveys a meaning that does not make sense.
Since the word “to” indicates purpose, in the context of a sentence like this one, “to be able” means “for the purpose of being able.”
So, this version conveys the meaning “for the purpose of being able to run fast for very long distances, ostriches are able to escape.” Of course, that meaning does not make sense. Ostriches would not be able to escape in order to run fast for long distances. Rather, they would be able to run fast for long distances in order to be able to escape. So, this version conveys a meaning that is basically the reverse of what would make sense.
(D) An ostrich, able to run fast for very long distances, are able to escape predators such as hyenas that normally catch prey by exhausting the prey.
This version is clearly grammatically flawed because the singular subject, “an ostrich,” does not agree with the plural verb “are.” We can more easily see the error by crossing off the wording between the subject and the verb.
An ostrich, able to run fast for very long distances, are able to escape
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Since it is able to run fast for very long distances, the ostrich is able to escape predators such as hyenas that normally catch prey by exhausting the prey.
This version is grammatically perfect. The singular subjects “it” and “ostrich” agree with the singular verb “is,” the singular pronoun “it” logically refers to the singular “ostrich,” and the rest of the sentence is free of grammar errors.
Furthermore, the meaning conveyed by this version makes sense.
So, we can see that this choice is the correct answer.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss how to approach answering Sentence Correction questions.
summary:
Lesson Content To consistently succeed in identifying issues in the sentence versions in Sentence Correction questions, we have to read the sentences in their entirety, or holistically.
💡MUST KNOW:
To consistently succeed in identifying issues in the sentence versions in Sentence Correction questions, we have to read the sentences in their entirety, or holistically. If we consider the sentences only one part at a time, we can easily fool ourselves into thinking an incorrect version is correct.
title: 1.4 When Answering Sentence Correction Questions, Read the Sentence Versions Holistically.

contents:
Lesson Content When answering a Sentence Correction question, begin by reading the entire original sentence to identify whether there are any clear errors of grammar or structure present.
If the original version seems to be grammatically correct, then we keep choice (A) as a contender and move on to the other choices.
💡MUST KNOW:
When we are answering a Sentence Correction question, if the original version seems to be grammatically correct, then we keep choice (A) as a contender and move on to the other choices. However, if the underlined portion of the original sentence is clearly incorrect, we eliminate choice (A).
For example, if the original version has a clear error in subject-verb agreement, a clear error in verb tense, or any other clear error, we can confidently eliminate (A).
We then read the other four versions vertically for that same error.
💡MUST KNOW:
If that exact grammatical error is present in any other versions, we eliminate those answer choices in which that same error is repeated.
Now, and we will talk more about this soon, we MUST BE SURE that we are eliminating ONLY the EXACT same grammatical error.
For example, let’s say that the original sentence uses the plural pronoun “they” with a singular noun.
The reason is that one (or more) of the other choices may have adjusted the underlined words so that now “they” works correctly in the sentences.
Instead of matching words only, we must read each version carefully, seeking to determine whether, in each case, the use of the word “they” really is an error.
Now, often, and even more often than not in harder questions, any errors that we find in the original sentence won’t appear in any of the other four versions.
Our next move is scanning the rest of the versions for other grammar errors.
Having scanned the five sentence versions for clear grammar errors and eliminated any in which we found grammar errors, we may have eliminated all versions but one, in which case we have found the correct answer to the question.
💡MUST KNOW:
Having scanned the five sentence versions for clear grammar errors and eliminated any in which we found grammar errors, we may have eliminated all versions but one, in which case we have found the correct answer to the question.
However, in many Sentence Correction questions, two or more sentence versions will be grammatically correct, and so, even after we have eliminated any versions in which we have spotted grammar errors, we may be left with multiple choices to choose among.
In the chapters that follow, we will discuss ways of determining whether a sentence conveys a meaning that makes sense.
💡MUST KNOW:
In many cases, our final step in answering a Sentence Correction question will be to eliminate versions that don’t convey meanings that make sense and find a version that does convey a meaning that makes sense.
Let’s now apply this approach to answering a Sentence Correction question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 4 The whale shark is the largest shark of all, but they are, to a human, less dangerous than the white shark by eating only small fish, shrimp, and plankton. 1. [ ] (A) The whale shark is the largest shark of all, but they are, to a human, less dangerous than the white shark by eating > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Whale sharks are the largest of all sharks but are, to a human, less dangerous than white sharks since whale sharks eat 3. [ ] (C) The whale shark is the largest shark of all, but they are, to a human, less dangerous than a white shark, eating > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) The whale shark, the largest of all sharks, are, to a human, less dangerous than the white shark because of eating > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Whale sharks are the largest of all sharks, but they are, to a human, less dangerous than white sharks by eating > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium (A) The whale shark is the largest shark of all, but they are, to a human, less dangerous than the white shark by eating only small fish, shrimp, and plankton.
Notice that this version starts off talking about “the whale shark,” which is singular, but then jumps to using the plural pronoun “they.” As a result, since “they” cannot refer to the singular “the whale shark,” “they” has nothing to refer to.
So, we can eliminate this choice because it has a clear grammar error, since a plural pronoun needs a plural noun to refer to.
If we scan the other versions, we can see that choice (C) has the same pronoun issue: “they” has nothing to refer to.
Notice also that, while choice (E) also uses the pronoun “they,” choice (E) does not have a pronoun issue, because (E) uses the plural “whale sharks.” So, “they” in (E) correctly refers to “whale sharks.”
CORRECT ANSWER(B) Whale sharks are the largest of all sharks but are, to a human, less dangerous than white sharks since whale sharks eat only small fish, shrimp, and plankton.
This choice is grammatically correct and uses the conjunction “since” to effectively convey a meaning that makes sense.
(C) The whale shark is the largest shark of all, but they are, to a human, less dangerous than a white shark, eating only small fish, shrimp, and plankton.
Like the version created via the use of choice (A), this version starts off talking about “the whale shark,” which is singular, but then jumps to using the plural pronoun “they.” As a result, since “they” cannot refer to the singular “the whale shark,” “they” has nothing to refer to.
So, we can eliminate this choice because it has a clear grammar error, since a plural pronoun needs a plural noun to refer to.
(D) The whale shark, the largest of all sharks, are, to a human, less dangerous than the white shark because of eating only small fish, shrimp, and plankton.
In this version, the singular subject “the whale shark” does not agree with the plural verb “are,” as we can more easily see by crossing off what appears between “the whale shark” and “are.”
The whale shark, the largest of all sharks, are, to a human, less dangerous
We can confidently eliminate this choice because it has a clear grammar error.
(E) Whale sharks are the largest of all sharks, but they are, to a human, less dangerous than white sharks by eating only small fish, shrimp, and plankton.
This choice may be more challenging to eliminate than (A), (C), or (D), because, whereas those choices have grammar errors, this version is grammatically correct. So, we have to use meaning to choose between this choice and the correct answer, (B).
The way to eliminate this choice is to see that this version says that whale sharks “are … less dangerous than white sharks by eating only small fish, shrimp, and plankton.” Notice that saying that whale sharks are less dangerous “by eating” certain things does not really make sense. Whale sharks would not make themselves less dangerous by eating.
Choice (B), on the other hand, uses “since” rather than “by” to convey a meaning that makes sense.
Correct Answer: (B)
summary:
Lesson Content When answering a Sentence Correction question, begin by reading the entire original sentence to identify whether there are any clear errors of grammar or structure present.
If the original version seems to be grammatically correct, then we keep choice (A) as a contender and move on to the other choices.
💡MUST KNOW:
When we are answering a Sentence Correction question, if the original version seems to be grammatically correct, then we keep choice (A) as a contender and move on to the other choices.
title: 1.5 How To Approach Sentence Correction Questions

contents:
Lesson Content When reading written communications (outside of Sentence Correction), we’re lenient with the writer and make efforts to understand the writer’s intended message, even if the writing has errors in grammar, word choice, style, or meaning.
In other words, we give the writer the benefit of the doubt, saying “I know what the writer meant to say,” even if the writer didn’t exactly say what was meant.
💡MUST KNOW:
Normally, when we read, our goal is to comprehend the intended meaning, whether it be of a book, text message, or email, regardless of the writing style or quality.
To give the writer the benefit of the doubt, we often:
• decipher the meaning of a sentence even when the wording makes it ambiguous
• determine the writer’s message even when it’s not explicitly stated
• use context and other hints to understand writing even when it’s unclear
So, why are we discussing these things we often do when reading?
We’re discussing them because we MUST NOT do any of these things when answering GMAT Sentence Correction questions.
Here’s why.
💡MUST KNOW:
The goal of Sentence Correction questions is to test WHETHER WE CAN PICK UP ON FLAWS IN WRITING.
To pick up on flaws in writing, we must be hypercritical of the writing.
💡MUST KNOW:
To pick up on flaws in writing, we must be hypercritical of the writing. We must not give the writer the benefit of the doubt. We must not say “I know what the writer meant.”
In Sentence Correction, when a sentence says something unclear, we call it out.
In Sentence Correction questions, we’re seeking out flaws in the sentences!
In other words, we’re not seeking to understand what the sentences would mean if they were well written.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Sentence Correction questions, we’re not seeking to understand what the sentences would mean if they were well-written. We’re seeking to understand what they do mean AS WRITTEN.
So, when reading sentence versions in Sentence Correction questions, we don’t read them the way we would read most other things.
Rather, we read the sentences literally.
💡MUST KNOW:
When reading sentence versions in Sentence Correction questions, we don’t read them the way we would read most other things. Rather, we read them EXACTLY AS WRITTEN.
For example, if someone wrote the following in an email, you would know exactly what the person meant.
The waiter brought us bubbling glasses full of champagne.
We all know what is meant by this sentence.
bubbling glasses
Since when can glasses be bubbling? Glasses don’t bubble.
A corrected sentence could be:
The waiter brought us glasses full of bubbling champagne.
Ah.
What if you saw the following in a news article?
To increase the tourists who visit it, the town has restored some historic buildings.
We know what this sentence is attempting to convey, that to cause more tourists to visit it, the town has restored some buildings.
A correct version could be:
To increase the number of tourists who visit it, the town has restored some historic buildings.
Now, the sentence makes clear that the town restored the buildings to “increase the number of tourists,” rather than to increase the tourists themselves.
What about the following?
By going there during the off season, Puerto Rico was fun for us.
If someone sent that sentence as a text message, you would know that it means that some people had fun in Puerto Rico by going there during the offseason.
However, if we read the sentence literally, we see that “By going there …, Puerto Rico was fun,” conveys that Puerto Rico was fun by going to Puerto Rico, as if Puerto Rico could go to itself.
Of course, that meaning doesn’t make sense.
A correct version is:
By going to Puerto Rico during the offseason, we had fun.
Now the sentence says that we had fun by going to Puerto Rico.
OK, so, what’s the key takeaway here? The key takeaway is that, to master Sentence Correction, we must learn to read sentences in a new way.
💡MUST KNOW:
To master Sentence Correction, we must learn to read sentences in a new way. So, we must practice reading sentences literally to see what they convey as written.
In fact, we can sum up our job with regard to meaning in Sentence Correction as follows:
• Determine what meaning a sentence version conveys when read literally.
• Decide whether that meaning makes sense.
💡MUST KNOW:
Our job with regard to meaning in Sentence Correction is to:
So, as you go through this course, when answering the Sentence Correction practice questions, be sure to practice reading sentences literally, determining what they convey, and deciding whether their meanings make sense.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice to strengthen our skill in reading sentences in this way.
Targeted Practice For each sentence, select Incorrect if it conveys a meaning that does not make sense or Correct if it conveys a meaning that makes sense. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 In the tree was a hungry nest of baby birds waiting for their mother to return. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 Leaping high into the air and doing flips, the show put on by the performers was amazing. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 3 Although the painting was unlike anything the viewers had seen previously, they immediately understood the artist’s message. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 With bears that live in the area, the campers stored their food in bear-proof containers. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 5 By working efficiently, the project was completed by the team ahead of schedule. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 6 The new building is a similarity to some of the other buildings in the financial district. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 7 Having read the book, the people watching the movie knew how it would end. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 8 In 763 BCE, when an insurrection occurred in Assyria, the moon blocked the light of the sun to make people believe that the two events were connected. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 9 Because the students interested in religion had decreased, the school stopped offering classes in that subject. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
Notice that, by saying, “a hungry nest,” this sentence conveys that the nest, rather than the baby birds, was hungry. Since a nest would not be hungry, the meaning conveyed by this sentence doesn’t make sense.
A correct version is:
In the tree was a nest of hungry baby birds waiting for their mother to return.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
Notice that this sentence says, “leaping high into the air and doing flips, the show.” Thus, it conveys that “the show” was “leaping high into the air and doing flips.” Since a show would not have been “leaping” or “doing flips,” that meaning is illogical.
We’ll discuss this type of error more in the Modifiers chapter of the course.
A correct version is:
Leaping high into the air and doing flips, the performers put on an amazing show.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
This sentence effectively communicates a logical meaning about the painting and the fact that the viewers understood the artist’s message.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
Did the bears help the campers store the food? Probably not. So, this sentence conveys an illogical meaning since it doesn’t make sense that the campers were “with bears that live in the area” when the campers stored their food.
In Sentence Correction, we have to be careful when we see the word “with” in a sentence. A sentence that uses “with” may communicate a meaning that involves two things being “with” each other when it’s not logical that they would be “with” each other.
Here’s a correct version:
Because bears live in the area, the campers stored their food in bear-proof containers.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
Notice that this sentence says, “By working efficiently, the project was completed.” Thus, it conveys that the project was completed by working efficiently. Since a project would not have worked efficiently, that meaning doesn’t make sense.
A correct version is:
By working efficiently, the team completed the project ahead of schedule.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
Would a building be “a similarity”? No. A similarity is a way things are alike, not something with walls and floors. So, since this sentence says that the new building is a similarity, its meaning is nonsensical.
A correct version is:
The new building is similar to some of the other buildings in the financial district.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 7 Explanation:
The meaning conveyed by this sentence, that the people watching the movie had read the book and therefore knew how the movie would end, makes sense.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 8 Explanation:
This sentence may sound correct. However, if we read it carefully, we see that it says that “the moon blocked the light of the sun to make people believe.”
Notice that, by using the wording “to make people believe,” the sentence conveys that the moon blocked light for the purpose of making people believe that the two events were connected. In other words, the sentence suggests that the moon intentionally blocked the light to cause people to believe something.
Since the moon would not have intentionally blocked the light for the purpose of making people believe something, the meaning of this sentence is nonsensical.
You’ll see more sentences with this type of error as you study Sentence Correction.
A correct sentence is:
In 763 BCE, because when an insurrection occurred in Assyria, the moon blocked the light of the sun, people believed that the two events were connected.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 9 Explanation:
This sentence says that the students interested in religion “had decreased.” Since the students themselves would not have “decreased,” i.e., become smaller, that meaning doesn’t make sense.
What makes sense is that the number of students interested in religion had decreased.
A correct sentence is:
Because the number of students interested in religion had decreased, the school stopped offering classes in that subject.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 5 In contrast with other novels of the time, Jane Austen’s novels focused on everyday life and ordinary people. 1. [x] (A) In contrast with other novels of the time, Jane Austen’s novels focused 2. [ ] (B) As a contrast with other novels of the time, Jane Austen focused her novels > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) With other novels of the time that were contrasts, Jane Austen focused her novels > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) When it was contrasted with other novels of the time, Jane Austen’s novels were focused > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) To be contrasted with other novels of the time, the novels of Jane Austen focused > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: N/A Most of the sentence versions in this question are grammatically correct. So, to determine which choice is the correct answer, we have to consider the meaning conveyed by each version and whether that meaning makes sense.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) In contrast with other novels of the time, Jane Austen’s novels focused on everyday life and ordinary people.
“In contrast with” has a meaning similar to “unlike.” So, this version conveys the logical meaning that, unlike other novels of the time, Jane Austen’s novels focused on everyday life and ordinary people.
Since this version has no grammatical errors and conveys a meaning that makes sense, it’s correct.
(B) As a contrast with other novels of the time, Jane Austen focused her novels on everyday life and ordinary people.
Notice that this version says, “As a contrast with other novels …, Jane Austen focused.” Thus, it conveys that Jane Austen was focused “as a contrast.” In other words, it suggests that Jane Austen herself was “a contrast with other novels.”
Since a person would not a be “a contrast,” much less “a contrast with other novels,” the meaning conveyed by this version doesn’t make sense.
(C) With other novels of the time that were contrasts, Jane Austen focused her novels on everyday life and ordinary people.
Notice that this version says, “With other novels of the time that were contrasts, Jane Austen focused.” We see that it conveys that Jane Austen was “with other novels of the time” when she “focused.”
Since it doesn’t make sense that Jane Austen was “with other novels” or that she focused with other novels, the meaning of this version doesn’t make sense.
(D) When it was contrasted with other novels of the time, Jane Austen’s novels were focused on everyday life and ordinary people.
This version is blatantly incorrect since the pronoun “it” has nothing to refer to. After all, “it” is singular, while “Jane Austen’s novels” is plural.
(E) To be contrasted with other novels of the time, the novels of Jane Austen focused on everyday life and ordinary people.
Notice that this version says, “To be contrasted …, the novels of Jane Austen focused.”
Reading the sentence literally, we see that, by using “to,” it communicates that the novels focused on everyday life and ordinary people so that they would be contrasted, as if novels would intentionally focus on certain things for the purpose of being “contrasted.”
Since novels would not intentionally do anything “to be contrasted,” this meaning is nonsensical.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 6 Some wellness coaches claim that by doing meditation and following a vegan diet, the aging of people has been reversed in its process. 1. [ ] (A) by doing meditation and following a vegan diet, the aging of people has been reversed in its process > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) doing meditation and following a vegan diet has reversed people who were in the aging process > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) through doing meditation and following a vegan diet, people have reversed the process of aging 4. [ ] (D) doing meditation and following a vegan diet has been the reverse of people in the process of aging > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) through doing meditation and following a vegan diet, people have been reversed in their aging processes > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: N/A We can tell what the sentence in this question is meant to say by reading any of the versions, but only one version actually conveys that meaning. All the others convey meanings that don’t make sense.
(A) Some wellness coaches claim that by doing meditation and following a vegan diet, the aging of people has been reversed in its process .
Notice that this version says, “by doing meditation and following a vegan diet, the aging of people has been reversed.”
Reading the sentence literally, we see that it conveys that aging has been reversed “by doing meditation and following a vegan diet,” as if aging reversed itself by doing those things.
Since aging would not do those things, the meaning of this sentence doesn’t make sense.
(B) Some wellness coaches claim that doing meditation and following a vegan diet has reversed people who were in the aging process .
Notice that this version says that “doing meditation and following a vegan diet has reversed people.” Since meditation and diet would not reverse people, that meaning doesn’t make sense.
What might make sense is that doing meditation and following a vegan diet have reversed the aging process, but the sentence doesn’t convey that meaning.
Also, notice that the singular verb “has” does not agree with the subject “doing meditation and following a vegan diet.” That subject is a list two items and therefore is plural, not singular.
So, this version is incorrect because it both conveys a nonsensical meaning and has a grammatical error.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Some wellness coaches claim that through doing meditation and following a vegan diet, people have reversed the process of aging .
This sentence conveys that people have reversed the process of aging through taking the actions of doing meditation and following a vegan diet.
Since that meaning makes sense and this version has no grammatical errors, this version is correct.
(D) Some wellness coaches claim that doing meditation and following a vegan diet has been the reverse of people in the process of aging .
Notice that this version says, “doing meditation and following a vegan diet has been the reverse of people.”
The idea that “doing meditation and following a vegan diet,” which are actions, have been “the reverse,” i.e., the opposite, “of people” is nonsensical.
Also, notice that the singular verb “has” does not agree with the subject “doing meditation and following a vegan diet.” That subject is a list two items and therefore is plural, not singular.
So, this version is incorrect because it both conveys a nonsensical meaning and has a grammatical error.
(E) Some wellness coaches claim that through doing meditation and following a vegan diet, people have been reversed in their aging processes .
Notice that this version says, “by doing …, people have been reversed in their aging processes.”
The idea that “people have been reversed” by doing meditation and following a vegan diet is nonsensical. What would have been reversed is people’s aging processes, not the people themselves.
So, the meaning conveyed by this version doesn’t make sense.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss the importance of the non-underlined portions of the sentences in Sentence Correction questions.
summary:
Lesson Content When reading written communications (outside of Sentence Correction), we’re lenient with the writer and make efforts to understand the writer’s intended message, even if the writing has errors in grammar, word choice, style, or meaning.
In other words, we give the writer the benefit of the doubt, saying “I know what the writer meant to say,” even if the writer didn’t exactly say what was meant.
💡MUST KNOW:
title: 1.6 Do Not Give Sentence Correction Questions the Benefit of the Doubt: Read the Sentences Literally

contents:
Lesson Content Don’t for a second think that our job is to evaluate only the portion of the sentence that appears in the answer choices.
In fact, often when a person falls into a Sentence Correction trap, she does so because she failed to carefully consider the non-underlined portion of the sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering a Sentence Correction question, we must carefully consider both the underlined and the non-underlined portions of a sentence.
Consider the following examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 7 Howler monkeys, which can be known for their loud howls and can be considered divine patrons of the artisans by the ancient Mayans. 1. [ ] (A) can be known for their loud howls and can be considered > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) are known for their loud howls, were considered 3. [ ] (C) are known for their loud howls and were considered > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) have been known for their loud howls and for their considerable > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) are known for loud howls that are considerable > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy All of the answer choices in this question, with the possible exception of choice (B), look pretty reasonable when they are read alone, without the non-underlined portions of the sentence. So, we might be tempted to choose a choice other than (B) if we were to consider the choices without combining them with the non-underlined portions.
However, the versions that result when the choices other than (B) are combined with the non-underlined portions are not even sentences. Also, the meanings conveyed by some of the versions created via the use of the choices other than (B) are ridiculous.
So, as it turns out, choice (B), the choice that sounds the least right when read on its own, is the correct answer.
(A) Howler monkeys, which can be known for their loud howls and can be considered divine patrons of the artisans by the ancient Mayans.
Notice what this version is actually composed of.
It begins with a noun, “howler monkeys.” Then comes “which can be known for their loud howls and can be considered divine patrons,” which tells us something about howler monkeys but does not tell us what howler monkeys do.
So, the version created via the use of this choice is just a noun and some information about that noun that don’t add up to a complete sentence. In other words, this version lacks an independent clause, a structure we will discuss extensively in the next chapter, and without an independent clause we can’t have a sentence (you will soon be a professional with this concept).
CORRECT ANSWER(B) Howler monkeys, which are known for their loud howls, were considered divine patrons of the artisans by the ancient Mayans.
This choice sounds choppy and, since it includes two verbs, “are” and “were,” without a conjunction, such as “and,” between them, it may seem to be incorrect. Notice however, that, when we combine this choice with the non-underlined portions, we get a sentence that makes complete sense.
Howler monkeys, which are known for their loud howls, were considered divine patrons of the artisans by the ancient Mayans.
(C) Howler monkeys, which are known for their loud howls and were considered divine patrons of the artisans by the ancient Mayans.
This choice flows well and may appear to be correct, because it has a pattern to it, as “are known” nicely matches “were considered.”
However, when we combine this choice with the non-underlined portions, we get the following.
Howler monkeys, which are known for their loud howls and were considered divine patrons of the artisans by the ancient Mayans.
It begins with a noun, “howler monkeys.” Then comes “which are known for their loud howls and were considered divine patrons,” which tells us something about howler monkeys but does not tell us what howler monkeys do.
So, the version created via the use of this choice is just a noun and some information about that noun that don’t add up to a complete sentence. In other words, this version lacks an independent clause, a structure we will discuss extensively in the next chapter, and without an independent clause we can’t have a sentence (you will soon be a professional with this concept).
(D) Howler monkeys, which have been known for their loud howls and for their considerable divine patrons of the artisans by the ancient Mayans.
This choice flows well and may appear to be correct, because it has a pattern to it, as “for their loud howls” nicely matches “for their considerable.”
However, when we combine this choice with the non-underlined portions, we get the following:
Howler monkeys, which have been known for their loud howls and for their considerable divine patrons of the artisans by the ancient Mayans.
We can see that this version is just a noun, “howler monkeys,” followed by some information about that noun, “which are known for their loud howls and for ….” So, the version created via the use of this choice is not a complete sentence. In other words, this version lacks an independent clause, a structure we will discuss extensively in the next chapter, and without an independent clause we can’t have a sentence (you will soon be a professional with this concept).
Also, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that howler monkeys have considerable divine patrons of the artisans.
(E) Howler monkeys, which are known for loud howls that are considerable divine patrons of the artisans by the ancient Mayans.
This choice flows well and may appear to be correct, because “that are considerable” appears to be the beginning of something that would logically provide more information about the “loud howls.” However, look at what we get when we combine this choice with the non-underlined portions:
Howler monkeys, which are known for their loud howls that are considerable divine patrons of the artisans by the ancient Mayans.
The resulting structure conveys the comically nonsensical meaning that the monkeys’ howls are considerable divine patrons of the artisans.
Also, we can see that this version is just a noun, “howler monkeys,” followed by some information about that noun, “which are known for their loud howls that ….” So, the version created via the use of this choice is not a complete sentence. In other words, this version lacks an independent clause, a structure we will discuss extensively in the next chapter, and without an independent clause we can’t have a sentence (you will soon be a professional with this concept).
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 8 A new line for trains that achieve speeds in excess of 150 miles per hour are expected to be completed within the next two years. 1. [ ] (A) trains that achieve speeds in excess of 150 miles per hour are expected > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) trains, which achieve speeds that go faster than 150 miles per hour, are expected > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) trains that achieve speeds in excess of 150 miles per hour is expected 4. [ ] (D) trains that achieve a speed that is in excess of 150 miles per hour and is expected > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) trains achieving a speed that goes faster than 150 miles per hour expects > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy Notice that, in the underlined portion of the sentence, we have the plural nouns “trains.” If we are not careful, the presence of this plural noun will lead us to believe that the sentence requires the plural verb “are” in “are expecting.” However, when we read the entire sentence, paying just as much attention to the non-underlined portion as to the underlined portion, we see that it is the “new line” that IS expected to be completed within the next two years.
Notice how easy it would be to not read the non-underlined portion of the sentence and, as a result, conclude that the sentence requires the plural verb “are.” Don’t do this. Be sure to read the entire sentence holistically.
Figuring out what the sentence is actually about will help us to find the right answer to this question. By reading carefully, we can see that the sentence is about a new train line that is expected to be completed within the next two years. Armed with this information, we can analyze the answer choices to determine which is the best.
(A) A new line for trains that achieve speeds in excess of 150 miles per hour are expected to be completed within the next two years.
If we were to consider this choice on its own, we might get the incorrect impression that this choice is the correct answer, as it could appear to convey in a grammatically correct manner that trains are expected.
However, once we consider this choice in the context of the entire sentence, we can see that it is not correct. Trains are not expected but, rather, a line is expected to be completed. So, when we combine this choice with the non-underlined portion of the sentence, we get “A new line … are expected …,” which is not grammatically correct, because the singular “a new line” does not agree with the plural “are expected.”
(B) A new line for trains, which achieve speeds that go faster than 150 miles per hour, are expected to be completed within the next two years.
If we were to consider this choice on its own, we might get the incorrect impression that this choice is the correct answer, as it could appear to convey in a grammatically correct manner that trains are expected.
However, once we consider this choice in the context of the entire sentence, we can see that it is not correct. Trains are not expected but, rather, a line is expected to be completed. So, when we combine this choice with the non-underlined portion of the sentence, we get “A new line … are expected …,” which is not grammatically correct, because the singular “a new line” does not agree with the plural “are expected.”
Notice also that “speeds that go faster than 150 miles per hour” conveys a nonsensical meaning. Trains might go faster than 150 miles per hour, but speeds would not “go faster.”
CORRECT ANSWER(C) A new line for trains that achieve speeds in excess of 150 miles per hour is expected to be completed within the next two years.
Considered on its own, this choice could appear to be grammatically flawed, as we might get the impression that the noun “trains” is meant to be paired with “is expected,” as in “trains … is expected,” which would be incorrect because “trains,” a plural noun, would not agree with the singular “is expected.”
However, once we consider this choice in the context of the entire sentence, we can see that what the sentence is actually conveying is that a new line is expected to be completed. So, the version created via the use of this choice is “A new line … is expected …,” which is grammatically correct and conveys a logical meaning.
(D) A new line for trains that achieve a speed that is in excess of 150 miles per hour and is expected to be completed within the next two years.
Considering this choice on its own, we might get the impression that it is correct, as it seems to clearly and correctly outline characteristics of a speed. However, when we combine this choice with the non-underlined portion, we can see that the resulting structure does not express a complete thought and, thus, is not a complete sentence. The structure is merely a noun, “a new line,” followed by a lot of wording that says more about that noun.
A new line for trains that achieve … and is …
(E) A new line for trains achieving a speed that goes faster than 150 miles per hour expects to be completed within the next two years.
Even without considering this choice in the context of the entire sentence, we can see that this choice is very unlikely to be the correct answer, because it conveys the nonsensical meaning that a speed goes faster than 150 miles per hour. A train might go faster than 150 miles per hour, but a speed itself would not “go faster.”
To confirm that this choice is incorrect, we can consider it in the context of the entire sentence. When we do so, we see that the sentence created via the use of this choice not only conveys the nonsensical meaning that a speed “goes faster” but also conveys the nonsensical meaning that the line itself “expects to be completed.” Someone might expect a train line to be completed, and a train line might be expected to be completed, but a train line would not expect itself to be completed.
Correct Answer: (C)
summary:
Lesson Content Don’t for a second think that our job is to evaluate only the portion of the sentence that appears in the answer choices.
In fact, often when a person falls into a Sentence Correction trap, she does so because she failed to carefully consider the non-underlined portion of the sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering a Sentence Correction question, we must carefully consider both the underlined and the non-underlined portions of a sentence.
title: 1.7 The Non-Underlined Portion of the Sentence is just as Important as the Answer Choices.

contents:
Lesson Content At some point, you may have heard of the idea of the Sentence Correction answer choices having “splits.” The idea of a split is simple.
💡MUST KNOW:
We have a split when some of the answer choices utilize one structure while the other answer choices utilize a different, dueling structure.
For example, maybe some choices use “their” while others use “its.” Maybe some choices use “because” while others use “due to.” Now, in some, but not all, circumstances, the splits can be helpful for us since they can clue us into what is being tested.
For example, in the example that follows, notice that we have a split.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 9 After analyzing the data, if the fog would persist was uncertain so the meteorologist did what she felt was most conservative, she alerted her viewers to be prepared for rain. 1. [ ] (A) if the fog would persist was uncertain so the meteorologist did what she felt was most conservative, she alerted > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) there was uncertainty regarding if the fog was going to persist, and so the meteorologist did what she felt was most conservative; alerting > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) the uncertainty about if the fog was going to persist caused the meteorologist to do what she felt was most conservative; the alerting of > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) the meteorologist was uncertain whether the fog would persist, so she did what she felt was most conservative; she alerted 5. [ ] (E) the meteorologist was uncertain whether or not the fog would persist so she did what she felt was most conservative, alerting > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium This question has a 3-2 split. Three choices include “if” while two choices include “whether.” By noticing this split, we can direct our attention to one of the key decision points that we’ll use in choosing among the choices.
“Whether” is used when a person is deciding between alternatives or when we make a statement involving uncertainty about two possible alternatives. So, we use “whether” in statements like “Bianca was wondering whether to x,” or “It was unclear whether x or y.”
On the other hand, “if” is used only for statements of conditionality. In other words, we use if in situations such as “If x, then y.”
The words “whether” and “if” are not interchangeable.
For example:
Correct: I don’t know whether I will go skiing this weekend. (uncertainty)
Incorrect: I don’t know if I will go skiing this weekend.
Correct: If you don’t turn in your homework, you will earn a low grade. (conditionality)
Incorrect: Whether you don’t turn in your homework, you will earn a low grade.
Also, the expression “whether or not” is redundant. What is correct is to say simply, “whether.”
(A) After analyzing the data, if the fog would persist was uncertain so the meteorologist did what she felt was most conservative, she alerted her viewers to be prepared for rain.
The first issue we see in this choice is that it uses “if” in expressing what the meteorologist was uncertain about. While people often use “if” in this way, the GMAT does not consider using “if” in this way correct. The GMAT reserves “if” for statements of conditionality. In scanning the answer choices, we can see the (B) and (C) also make the same mistake.
Also, notice what this version says at the beginning: “After analyzing the data, if the fog would persist was uncertain.” For reasons that we’ll discuss later in the course, that part of the sentence conveys that “if the fog would persist” was analyzing the data, a nonsensical meaning. After “after analyzing the data,” we need a noun that names something capable of analyzing data. Of course, “if the fog would persist” does not have that capability.
Also, just for those of you who may have noticed, yes, this version is a run-on sentence and, thus, has a major error of a type that we will study extensively in the next chapter.
(B) After analyzing the data, there was uncertainty regarding if the fog was going to persist, and so the meteorologist did what she felt was most conservative; alerting her viewers to be prepared for rain.
The first issue we see in this choice is that it uses “if” in expressing what the meteorologist was uncertain about. While people often use “if” in this way, the GMAT does not consider using “if” in this way correct. The GMAT reserves “if” for statements of conditionality.
Also, notice what this version says at the beginning: “After analyzing the data, there was uncertainty regarding if the fog was going to persist.” For reasons that we’ll discuss later in the course, that part of the sentence conveys that “there” was analyzing the data, a nonsensical meaning. After “after analyzing the data” we need a noun that names something capable of analyzing data. Of course, the word “there” does not have that capability.
(C) After analyzing the data, the uncertainty about if the fog was going to persist caused the meteorologist to do what she felt was most conservative; the alerting of her viewers to be prepared for rain.
The first issue we see in this choice is that it uses “if” in expressing what the meteorologist was uncertain about. While people often use “if” in this way, the GMAT does not consider using “if” in this way correct. The GMAT reserves “if” for statements of conditionality.
Also, notice what this version says at the beginning: “After analyzing the data, the uncertainty about if the fog was going to persist.” For reasons that we’ll discuss later in the course, that part of the sentence conveys that “the uncertainty” was analyzing the data, a nonsensical meaning. After “after analyzing the data” we need a noun that names something capable of analyzing data. Of course, “the uncertainty” does not have that capability.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) After analyzing the data, the meteorologist was uncertain whether the fog would persist, so she did what she felt was most conservative; she alerted her viewers to be prepared for rain.
This version correctly uses “whether” in making a statement about the uncertainty of the meteorologist (regarding whether the fog would persist).
Furthermore, notice that this version logically places the noun “meteorologist” directly after “After analyzing the data” and, thus, conveys the logical meaning that it was the meteorologist who was analyzing the data.
(E) After analyzing the data, the meteorologist was uncertain whether or not the fog would persist so she did what she felt was most conservative, alerting her viewers to be prepared for rain.
This version uses the unidiomatic expression “whether or not.” The correct expression is “whether.”
Also, just for those of you who may have noticed, yes, this version is a run-on sentence and, thus, has a major error of a type that we will study extensively in the next chapter.
Correct Answer: (D) Ok, great.
However, sometimes the splits are present to distract us from the real issues in the sentences and don’t actually affect the choices.
For example, consider the following sentences:
The people creating the new marketing strategy is meeting with the product design team next week.
The people who are creating the new marketing strategy are meeting with the product design team next week.
The people creating the new marketing strategy, they are meeting with the product design team next week.
