Notice the split?
Two sentences use “the people creating” while one sentence uses “the people who are creating.” Guess what? This difference is immaterial.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 10 Surprising to many is that, while Manhattan still has a high population density, the number of people living there is lower than it was in 1910. 1. [x] (A) Manhattan still has a high population density, the number of people living there is lower than it was in 2. [ ] (B) Manhattan still has a high population density, the people living there are lower than they were in > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Manhattan’s population density is still high, the people living there is a lower number than > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Manhattan’s population density is still high, the people living there have been lowered from what they were in > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Manhattan still has a high population density, the people living there are lower than the number were in > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy Three of the choices in this question begin with “Manhattan still has a high population density,” and the other two choices begin with “Manhattan’s population density is still high.” So, this question has a 3-2 split.
However, both “Manhattan still has a high population density” and “Manhattan’s population density is still high” are correct, and they mean essentially the same thing. Thus, the 3-2 split in this question does not highlight decision points that we can use to answer the question and is just a distraction.
So, to arrive at the correct answer, we have to find other differences between the choices, differences that do serve as decision points.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) Surprising to many is that, while Manhattan still has a high population density, the number of people living there is lower than it was in 1910.
This version is grammatically correct and effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(B) Surprising to many is that, while Manhattan still has a high population density, the people living there are lower than they were in 1910.
This version is grammatically correct. Notice what it says though. It says that the people living in Manhattan are “lower than they were in 1910.”
Of course, that statement does not make sense. The point to be made is not that the people themselves are somehow lower than they were in 1910; the point to be made is that the number of people living in Manhattan is lower than it was in 1910, but that’s not what this version says.
(C) Surprising to many is that, while Manhattan’s population density is still high, the people living there is a lower number than 1910.
This version has an error in grammar. The plural subject “people” does not agree with the singular verb “is.”
Furthermore, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that people “is” a number.
(D) Surprising to many is that, while Manhattan’s population density is still high, the people living there have been lowered from what they were in 1910.
This version is grammatically correct. Notice what it says though. It says that the people living in Manhattan “have been lowered from what they were in 1910.”
So, this version seems to convey that some outside force somehow “lowered” the people living in Manhattan “from what they were.” Since, of course, nothing has lowered the people living in Manhattan, and since lowering people from “what they were” does not make sense at all, this version is nonsensical.
(E) Surprising to many is that, while Manhattan still has a high population density, the people living there are lower than the number were in 1910.
This version has an error in grammar. The singular subject “the number” does not agree with the plural verb “were.”
Furthermore, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that people are lower than a number.
Correct Answer: (A) Finally, in some cases, there are no splits.
Consider the following example sentences:
The people creating the new marketing strategy is meeting with the product design team next week.
The people who are creating the new marketing strategy are meeting with the product design team next week.
Meeting with the product design team next week were the people creating the new marketing strategy.
Notice that each of these sentences is fairly unique.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 11 In the early 1900s, in order to travel from place to place, freight trains would be hopped onto by tramps even though entering freight cars without authorization is now extremely difficult. 1. [ ] (A) freight trains would be hopped onto by tramps even though entering freight cars without authorization is now extremely difficult > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) tramps would hop onto freight trains, though it is now extremely difficult to enter freight cars without authorization 3. [ ] (C) hopping onto freight trains would be done though it is now extremely difficult to enter freight cars without authorization by tramps > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) tramps, hopping onto freight trains, though entering freight cars without authorization being extremely difficult now > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) there were tramps, and they hopped onto freight trains even though now it is extremely difficult to enter freight cars without authorization > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy Since each choice in this question is unique, this question has no splits that we can use to identify decision points. So, in order to find the correct answer to this question, we have to carefully analyze each version on its own to determine whether it is grammatically correct and effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(A) In the early 1900s, in order to travel from place to place, freight trains would be hopped onto by tramps even though entering freight cars without authorization is now extremely difficult .
This version is grammatically correct. Notice what it conveys though. For reasons that will be discussed in a later chapter, since “in order to travel from place to place” is directly followed by “freight trains would be hopped onto by tramps,” this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that trains traveled by being hopped onto by tramps.
As if that meaning were not ridiculous enough, this version goes on to say that, in the early 1900s, “trains would be hopped onto by tramps even though entering freight cars without authorization is now extremely difficult.”
Since “even though” means “despite the fact that,” this version conveys the illogical meaning that, in the early 1900s, trains were hopped onto by tramps despite the fact that entering freight cars is difficult now, as if the fact that entering freight cars is difficult now would have somehow affected tramps hopping onto freight trains in the past.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) In the early 1900s, in order to travel from place to place, tramps would hop onto freight trains, though it is now extremely difficult to enter freight cars without authorization .
This version is grammatically correct.
Furthermore, since “in order to travel from place to place” is followed by “tramps would hop onto freight trains,” this version conveys the logical meaning that tramps traveled by hopping onto trains.
Since this version is grammatically correct and conveys a meaning that makes sense, this choice is the correct answer.
(C) In the early 1900s, in order to travel from place to place, hopping onto freight trains would be done though it is now extremely difficult to enter freight cars without authorization by tramps .
This version is grammatically correct. Notice what it conveys though. For reasons that will be discussed in a later chapter, since “in order to travel from place to place” is directly followed by “hopping onto freight trains would be done,” this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that hopping onto freight trains traveled.
As if that meaning were not ridiculous enough, since “by tramps” does not come right after “hopping” and instead appears all the way at the end of this version, right after “authorization,” this version seems to convey that entering a freight car now requires authorization by tramps.
(D) In the early 1900s, in order to travel from place to place, tramps, hopping onto freight trains, though entering freight cars without authorization being extremely difficult now .
Notice what we get when we combine this answer choice with the non-underlined portion.
in order to travel from place to place, tramps, hopping onto freight trains,
We can see that that thought is not complete: there’s no verb that says what “tramps, hopping onto freight trains” do.
So, this version can be eliminated because it is not a complete sentence.
(E) In the early 1900s, in order to travel from place to place, there were tramps, and they hopped onto freight trains even though now it is extremely difficult to enter freight cars without authorization .
This version is grammatically correct. Notice what it says though: “in order to travel from place to place, there were tramps.” What exactly that statement means is not clear. Who or what was traveling? What did tramps have to do with traveling? Read literally, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that tramps existed so that someone or something unidentified could travel.
As if that meaning were not ridiculous enough, this version goes on to say that, in the early 1900s, tramps “hopped onto freight trains even though now it is extremely difficult to enter freight cars.”
Since “even though” means “despite the fact that,” this version conveys the illogical meaning that, in the early 1900s, tramps hopped onto trains despite the fact that entering freight cars is difficult now, as if the fact that entering freight cars is difficult now would have somehow affected tramps hopping onto freight trains in the past.
Correct Answer: (B) We have seen that sometimes splits can be useful to us, sometimes splits are present to distract us, and sometimes there are no splits in the answer choices.
When there are splits, there’s a good possibility that the splits are associated with issues in the sentence versions.
At the same time, regardless of whether there are splits in a question, we must carefully evaluate each sentence version holistically to determine which choice is the best.
Let’s now discuss using strong decision points in choosing among Sentence Correction answer choices.
summary:
Lesson Content At some point, you may have heard of the idea of the Sentence Correction answer choices having “splits.” The idea of a split is simple.
💡MUST KNOW:
We have a split when some of the answer choices utilize one structure while the other answer choices utilize a different, dueling structure.
For example, maybe some choices use “their” while others use “its.” Maybe some choices use “because” while others use “due to.
title: 1.8 Splits in Sentence Correction Questions

contents:
Lesson Content In most incorrect answer choices in Sentence Correction questions, there are multiple flaws.
💡MUST KNOW:
In most incorrect answer choices in Sentence Correction questions, there are multiple flaws. So, if we are not certain that one possible flaw is actually a flaw, we can generally find a flaw that we are more sure about.
So, if we are having trouble deciding whether one characteristic of a sentence choice renders that choice incorrect, rather than spend time wondering about that one characteristic, we can look for a stronger or clearer reason to eliminate that choice.
Let’s see how this process can work by considering an example:
Researchers studying the spider monkey have noticed them to live in matriarchal troops.
When we read that sentence, we see that it includes the expression “noticed them to live.” Does that expression make sense? Is it idiomatically correct? It is incorrect because “noticed X to Y” is not an expression used in English, but if we were to see the above sentence in a Sentence Correction question, we might not be sure whether the presence of “noticed them to live” in the sentence makes the sentence incorrect.
So, let’s look for a stronger decision point.
There is another flaw in the sentence.
OK, so now, because we see that the use of “them” is clearly incorrect, we have a strong decision point, and therefore we would be able to confidently decide that the above example sentence is incorrect if it were a sentence version in a Sentence Correction question.
Consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 12 Researchers evolution is being studied by whom have found that development of new traits do not always result in increased fitness of a species. 1. [ ] (A) Researchers evolution is being studied by whom have found that development of new traits do not always result > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Researchers who study evolution and find that development of a new trait does not always result > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) In studying evolution, researchers have found that development of a new trait does not always result 4. [ ] (D) Researchers who study evolution has found development of new traits not always resulting > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Studying evolution, development of new traits has been found not always to result by researchers > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy In some cases, we can easily tell that characteristics of sentence versions in this question constitute flaws that make the versions incorrect. In other cases, however, whether characteristics constitute flaws is less clear. In a case in which we aren’t sure whether a characteristic constitutes a flaw, it makes sense to look for another characteristic that we can tell for sure constitutes a flaw.
(A) Researchers evolution is being studied by whom have found that development of new traits do not always result in increased fitness of a species.
When we read this version, the wording “Researchers evolution is being studied by whom” jumps out at us as convoluted, but is it incorrect? That wording means “researchers who study evolution.” So, it seems possible that that wording is just an alternative way of saying, “researchers who study evolution” and that the presence of that wording in this version does not make this version incorrect.
So, let’s look for a clearer reason to eliminate this choice.
As we continue reading this version, we see that the singular subject “development” and the plural verb “do” do not agree. We can more easily see that they do not agree by crossing off what appears between “development” and “do”:
development of new traits do not always result in increased fitness
By finding this subject-verb agreement error, we have found a clear reason to eliminate this choice.
(B) Researchers who study evolution and find that development of a new trait does not always result in increased fitness of a species.
Notice that this version says, “Researchers who study evolution and find …” without ever completing the thought. It leaves the reader wondering, “Researchers who study and find do what?”
In other words, in this version, there’s no verb to go with the subject “researchers,” and thus this version is not a complete sentence.
The fact that this version is not a complete sentence is a clear reason to eliminate this choice.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) In studying evolution, researchers have found that development of a new trait does not always result in increased fitness of a species.
The plural subject “researchers” agrees with the plural verb “have,” the singular subject “development” agrees with the singular verb “does,” and the rest of this version is grammatically correct.
Also, this version effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
So, this version is the correct answer.
(D) Researchers who study evolution has found development of new traits not always resulting in increased fitness of a species.
Toward the end of this version, we see “found development of new traits not always resulting in increased fitness of a species.” Is “found development of new traits not always resulting” correct or incorrect? It certainly sounds incorrect, but whether it’s incorrect may not be entirely clear to us.
So, let’s look for a clearer reason to eliminate this choice.
Toward the beginning of this version, we see “Researchers who study evolution has found.” Notice that the plural subject “researchers” does not agree with the singular verb “has found.”
By finding this subject-verb agreement error, we have found a clear reason to eliminate this choice.
(E) Studying evolution, development of new traits has been found not always to result by researchers in increased fitness of a species.
This version has some clear errors.
Notice what it says at the beginning: “Studying evolution, development of new traits has been found ….” For reasons that we’ll discuss later in the course, that part conveys that “development of new traits” has been studying evolution, a nonsensical meaning.
Further along, this version says, “development of new traits has been found not always to result by researchers.” So, this version seems to say that development of new traits somehow results “by researchers,” which is nonsense.
So, this version is clearly incorrect because it conveys nonsensical meanings.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss arriving at the correct answer by eliminating incorrect choices.
summary:
Lesson Content In most incorrect answer choices in Sentence Correction questions, there are multiple flaws.
💡MUST KNOW:
In most incorrect answer choices in Sentence Correction questions, there are multiple flaws. So, if we are not certain that one possible flaw is actually a flaw, we can generally find a flaw that we are more sure about.
So, if we are having trouble deciding whether one characteristic of a sentence choice renders that choice incorrect, rather than spend time wondering about that one characteristic, we can look for a stronger or clearer reason to eliminate that choice.
title: 1.9 Looking for Strong Decision Points When Choosing Among Sentence Correction Answer Choices

contents:
Lesson Content There are many ways in which sentences that appear in Sentence Correction questions can be correctly written.
💡MUST KNOW:
Sometimes, even often, we won’t be used to recognizing the ways in which sentences that appear in Sentence Correction questions can be correctly written because the sentence structures are different from what we’re used to.
Also, the correct versions of sentences in Sentence Correction questions are not always particularly well written.
So, because the correct versions sometimes use unfamiliar structures or are not particularly well written, if, in answering a Sentence Correction question, we only seek to find the correct version, we may not recognize it and, therefore, get the question incorrect.
Thus, instead of seeking the correct sentence directly, we will use process of elimination to remove the four versions that are most clearly flawed.
💡MUST KNOW:
When solving Sentence Correction questions, instead of seeking the correct version directly, use process of elimination to remove the four versions that are the most clearly flawed. If we can confidently remove four answers, the one remaining must be correct.
For example, if a version has an error in subject verb agreement, it’s gone.
Also, it’s important to know that we don’t need to find every flaw in a sentence for us to eliminate it.
💡MUST KNOW:
When we are answering Sentence Correction questions, just one flaw in a sentence version is all it takes for us to have grounds to confidently eliminate that version. Eliminate wrong answers as quickly as possible by finding the easiest to find errors in each incorrect sentence.
Along these lines, we can learn a lot from the way that Sherlock Holmes, the fictional brilliant private detective, went about solving puzzles.
“Once you have eliminated the impossible, whatever remains, however improbable, must be the truth.”
This advice is helpful in answering Sentence Correction questions because, even if we can’t see why the correct answer is correct, if we can eliminate four sentences that we know to contain errors, then we can confidently choose the correct answer.
Consider the following examples:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 13 According to numerous peer-reviewed studies, retractions of scientific work does little to amend public misconceptions that result from the initial publication of erroneous information. 1. [ ] (A) does little to amend public misconceptions that result > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) do little to amend public misconceptions resulting 3. [ ] (C) doing little to amend public misconceptions that result > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) that do little to amend public misconceptions results > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) that do little to amend public misconceptions resulting > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy The key to correctly answering this question is to find at least one clear flaw in each of four of the five answer choices. Remember, just one flaw is all it takes for us to have grounds to confidently eliminate a choice. If we are able to find at least one solid reason why an answer choice is incorrect, and do that for four choices, then we know that the remaining choice must be the correct answer, even if that answer may “sound” a bit odd.
(A) According to numerous peer-reviewed studies, retractions of scientific work does little to amend public misconceptions that result from the initial publication of erroneous information.
The plural subject of this version, “retractions,” does not agree with the singular verb “does.” Notice that the subject of this version is not the singular “work.” We can see the error more easily by crossing off what appears between the subject and the verb:
retractions of scientific work does
As we can see, “retractions … does” is not grammatical. Rather, “retractions … do” would be correct.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) According to numerous peer-reviewed studies, retractions of scientific work do little to amend public misconceptions resulting from the initial publication of erroneous information.
Notice that the version created with this choice correctly pairs the plural subject “retractions” with the plural verb “do.”
If you were reluctant to select this choice because you thought “resulting” does not “sound” as good as “that result,” keep in mind that correct SC sentences may not always sound the way you might initially think to write them. The truth is that “public misconceptions resulting from the initial publication …” is perfectly grammatical and logical.
Since the version created with this choice contains no grammatical errors and conveys a logical meaning, this choice is correct.
(C) According to numerous peer-reviewed studies, retractions of scientific work doing little to amend public misconceptions that result from the initial publication of erroneous information.
There is no main verb in the version created with this choice (i.e., no verb that names the action of the sentence subject). Thus, the sentence created is not complete. We can see the error more easily by crossing off what appears between the subject and the verb that it would pair with:
“retractions of scientific work doing little to amend public misconceptions that result” … do/are what?
“Doing” alone cannot serve as the main verb of a sentence. In this context, “doing” merely provides further description of the type of “retractions” being discussed, “retractions … doing little ….”
“To amend” is an infinitive verb, and thus cannot be the main verb in the sentence. “Result” provides further description of the “public misconceptions”; they are “public misconceptions that result from ….”
Since the version created with this choice does not contain a main verb to pair with the subject “retractions,” and thus is not a complete sentence, this choice is incorrect.
If you were unable to identify that the sentence created with this choice is incomplete, rest assured, we will cover in detail how to identify the main verb of a sentence in the next chapter.
(D) According to numerous peer-reviewed studies, retractions of scientific work that do little to amend public misconceptions results from the initial publication of erroneous information.
The plural subject of this version, “retractions,” does not agree with the singular verb “results.” We can see the error more easily by crossing off what appears between the subject and the verb:
retractions of scientific work that do little to amend public misconceptions results
As we can see, “retractions … results” is not grammatical. Rather, “retractions … result” would be correct.
(E) According to numerous peer-reviewed studies, retractions of scientific work that do little to amend public misconceptions resulting from the initial publication of erroneous information.
The sentence created with this choice is incomplete. Notice that the subject “retractions” is not followed by a main verb.
To better see the error, we can cross off the phrase beginning with “of”:
“retractions of scientific work that do little to amend public misconceptions resulting from the initial publication of erroneous information.” … do/are what?
Having crossed off the phrase that follows “retractions,” we can see that there is nothing in the sentence for “retractions” to do or be. Notice that the part of the sentence containing verbs, “that does little to amend public misconceptions resulting from …” further describes “scientific work.”
Without a main verb to pair with the sentence subject, the sentence does not convey a complete thought, and this choice is incorrect.
If you were unable to identify that the sentence created with this choice is incomplete, rest assured, we will cover in detail how to identify the main verb of a sentence in the next chapter.
Key Concept: Every sentence must have at least one subject and at least one main verb.
Key Concept: Check to be sure that singular nouns are paired with singular verbs and that plural nouns are paired with plural verbs.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss why it’s important to be confident in our elimination of incorrect choices.
summary:
Lesson Content There are many ways in which sentences that appear in Sentence Correction questions can be correctly written.
💡MUST KNOW:
Sometimes, even often, we won’t be used to recognizing the ways in which sentences that appear in Sentence Correction questions can be correctly written because the sentence structures are different from what we’re used to.
Also, the correct versions of sentences in Sentence Correction questions are not always particularly well written.
title: 1.10 Seek Out Four Clearly Incorrect Answers, and Then Choose the One Remaining.

contents:
Lesson Content We must eliminate answer choices because we see why they are incorrect, not because we can’t see why they are correct.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Sentence Correction questions, we must eliminate choices because we see why they are incorrect, not because we can’t see why they are correct.
This brings up an important point.
💡MUST KNOW:
As you move through the Target Test Prep Course, you must be able to articulate why each of the four incorrect answer choices is incorrect. Don’t move on from incorrect choices until you fully understand why each is incorrect.
Let’s now discuss what to do if we are stuck between two final answer choices.
summary:
Lesson Content We must eliminate answer choices because we see why they are incorrect, not because we can’t see why they are correct.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Sentence Correction questions, we must eliminate choices because we see why they are incorrect, not because we can’t see why they are correct.
This brings up an important point.
💡MUST KNOW:
As you move through the Target Test Prep Course, you must be able to articulate why each of the four incorrect answer choices is incorrect.
title: 1.10.1 We Must Be Confident in Our Elimination of Incorrect Choices.

contents:
Lesson Content Often, we’ll have correctly eliminated three of the five answer choices and be left with two.
We’re seeking to determine what makes them different because both sentences can’t be correct, so there must be at least one key point of difference between them that will render one more effective (correct) than the other.
Does one use the singular “is” while the other uses the plural verb “are?” Does one use the pronoun “their” while the other uses the singular “it?” Does one use a simple past tense verb, such as “walked,” while the other uses a verb in the past perfect tense, such as “had walked.” Seeking out the differences between the sentences will allow us to more precisely see what the issue in the incorrect version is.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Sentence Correction questions, if we’re debating between two answers, seek to pinpoint the essential ways in which the sentences differ. There must be at least one key point of difference between them that will render one more effective (correct) than the other.
Of course, as you now know, the sentences could have false splits that serve to distract us from the real issues present in the sentences, so we can’t assume that all differences between the sentences matter, but we need to at least begin by considering how the two sentences are different.
Now, what happens if we can’t find something wrong with one of the two remaining sentences? If we’ve carefully and confidently evaluated the sentences and cannot find an error, we need to consider that it may be the case that, although both sentences are grammatically correct, one says something illogical.
If we can’t find an error of logic (and there are no errors of grammar), we must consider that we’ve improperly evaluated the other three sentences and return to them to reevaluate their correctness.
Consider the following Examples:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 14 An outdoor recess period is now rare in schools, so much so that physical activity has been integrated into class assignments by some teachers as a substitute for them. 1. [ ] (A) An outdoor recess period is now rare in schools, so much so that physical activity has been integrated into class assignments by some teachers as a substitute for them. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) An outdoor recess period, now rare in schools, so much that some teachers integrated physical activity into class assignments to substitute for it. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Because they are now so rare in schools, class assignments have physical activity integrated into them as some teachers’ substitute for outdoor recess periods. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) An outdoor recess period is now so rare in schools that some teachers have integrated physical activity into class assignments as a substitute for it. 5. [ ] (E) Because they are now so rare in schools, some teachers have integrated physical activity into class assignments to substitute for an outdoor recess period. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy Some of the choices in this question are relatively easy to eliminate. For instance, choice (B) is clearly incorrect because it’s not a complete sentence, and choice (E) has a clear meaning error, in that it seems to communicate that “some teachers” are “now so rare in many states.”
However, some other choices are more difficult to eliminate. So, once we have eliminated all but two or three choices, we have to carefully compare the differences between the remaining choices to determine which is correct.
(A) An outdoor recess period is now rare in schools, so much so that physical activity has been integrated into class assignments by some teachers as a substitute for them. To what does the pronoun “them” at the end of the sentence refer? There is no logical antecedent of “them” in the sentence, and thus this choice can’t be correct.
Given what the sentence says, it must be that what teachers have been using physical activity integrated into class assignments as a substitute for is “an outdoor recess period.” However, the plural “them” cannot refer to the singular “an outdoor recess period.” We need the singular “it” to refer to “an outdoor recess period.”
In fact, none of the plural nouns in the sentence (schools, class assignments, teachers) makes sense as the antecedent of “them” in this context.
(B) An outdoor recess period, now rare in schools, so much that some teachers integrated physical activity into class assignments to substitute for it. This choice corrects the pronoun error we saw in (A), using the singular “it” to logically refer to “an outdoor recess period.” However, we must be careful when evaluating SC answer choices not to simply look for a correction of an error we saw in another choice. We must evaluate the entire sentence on its own merits. If we do so, we see that the sentence created with this choice is incomplete. Notice that there is no main verb in the sentence to indicate what “an outdoor recess period” is/does at a particular point in time. In other words, whereas choice (A) included the verb “is” after “an outdoor recess period,” this choice replaces “is” with a comma, so there is no main verb to go with the sentence subject.
Additionally, the use of the phrase “so much that” does not make sense in this context. Ask yourself, “so much” what? There is nothing in the sentence that there could logically be “so much” of. It does not make sense to say that an outdoor recess period is rare in schools “so much.” The idiom “so much so” would make more sense for discussing the fact that recess is “rare.”
(C) Because they are now so rare in schools, class assignments have physical activity integrated into them as some teachers’ substitute for outdoor recess periods. In this choice, the plural pronoun “they” seems to refer to the plural noun “class assignments,” conveying an illogical meaning:
Because [class assignments] are now so rare in schools, class assignments have physical activity integrated into them as some teachers’ substitute for outdoor recess periods.
Notice that it doesn’t make sense that class assignments would have physical activity integrated into them as a substitute for outdoor recess periods because class assignments “are now so rare.” After all, if class assignments are so rare, physical activity integrated into them wouldn’t serve as much of a substitute for recess. Right?
A logical meaning would be that “outdoor recess periods” are what are “now so rare.” However, because “class assignments” is the first plural noun to follow the introductory phrase, and because “outdoor recess periods” is far away from “they” at the end of the sentence, the way the sentence is constructed indicates that “they” refers (illogically) to “class assignments.”
CORRECT ANSWER(D) An outdoor recess period is now so rare in schools that some teachers have integrated physical activity into class assignments as a substitute for it. This choice is the only one that conveys a clear, logical, and complete thought, with no ambiguous or illogical pronouns or other grammatical errors. Notice that the main verb “is” properly pairs with the singular subject “an outdoor recess period,” so the sentence is grammatically correct.
Additionally, the singular pronoun “it” clearly and logically refers to the singular “an outdoor recess period.”
(E) Because they are now so rare in schools, some teachers have integrated physical activity into class assignments to substitute for an outdoor recess period. Notice that, since “teachers” is the first plural noun following the introductory phrase “Because they are now so rare in schools,” the pronoun “they” seems to refer to “some teachers,” conveying an illogical meaning.
It doesn’t make sense that “some teachers” are “rare in schools in many states.”
Also, it doesn’t make sense that, because some teachers are rare, those teachers would integrate physical activity into class assignments to substitute for an outdoor recess period. Why would one thing be the reason for the other? Those two facts have no logical connection to each other.
A logical meaning would be that an outdoor recess period is what is “now so rare. However, the plural pronoun “they” cannot correctly refer to the singular “an outdoor recess period.” Key Concept: Every sentence must have at least one subject and at least one finite verb.
Key Concept: Be sure that a pronoun clearly refers to only one antecedent in the sentence, not multiple possible antecedents. Key Concept: Be sure that the noun to which a pronoun refers makes sense as the person or thing performing the action associated with that pronoun.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 15 The harvesting of apples and peaches were disrupted this year by stormy weather, and as a result, much of it ended up rotting on the ground. 1. [ ] (A) were disrupted this year by stormy weather, and as a result, much of it > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) was disrupted this year by stormy weather, and resultingly, many of them 3. [ ] (C) was disrupted this year by stormy weather, with the result that much of it > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) were disrupted this year by stormy weather, with the result that much of that > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) was disrupted this year by stormy weather and resulted in much, which > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard Two of the answer choices in this question, the two that use the plural “were,” are relatively easy to eliminate, because we can see that “were” does not agree with the singular subject “harvesting.”
However, once we have eliminated those choices, choosing among the rest becomes a bit more challenging, because (B) uses the strange sounding “resultingly” but may be correct, (C) sounds OK, and (E) is grammatically correct but may not convey a logical meaning.
So, to arrive at the correct answer, we have to carefully analyze the differences between the choices to determine which is actually the best one.
(A) The harvesting of apples and peaches were disrupted this year by stormy weather, and as a result, much of it ended up rotting on the ground.
The plural verb “were disrupted” does not agree with the subject, which is not the plural “apples and peaches” but the singular “harvesting.” Crossing off what appears between “harvesting” and “were disrupted” makes seeing the issue easier.
The harvesting of apples and peaches were disrupted
Also, notice that the pronoun “it” has nothing that it would logically refer to. “It” would not refer to “harvesting,” because “harvesting” would not have “ended up rotting on the ground,” and “it” would not refer to “apples” or “peaches,” because “it” is singular, while “apples” and “peaches” are plural.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) The harvesting of apples and peaches was disrupted this year by stormy weather, and resultingly, many of them ended up rotting on the ground.
This version “sounds” a little strange, because the adverb “resultingly” is not one that we see or hear used often.
Nevertheless, since “resultingly” means “consequently,” it makes sense to say “and resultingly, many of them ended up rotting.”
If we weren’t sure whether we should choose this choice over (C) or (E), we could compare the choices to see how they differ and decide which is the best.
We could compare this choice with (C) and see that (C) uses “it,” while this choice uses “them,” and see that “it” has nothing to refer to, whereas the plural “them” logically refers to the plural “apples and peaches.”
We could compare this choice with (E) and see that, whereas what “much” in (E) means is not clear, this version conveys a meaning that makes sense.
So, since this version is grammatically correct and effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense, it is the correct answer.
(C) The harvesting of apples and peaches was disrupted this year by stormy weather, with the result that much of it ended up rotting on the ground.
This version correctly uses the verb “was” with the singular subject “harvesting.” So, it does not have the subject-verb agreement error that (A) and (D) have.
However, the pronoun “it” has nothing that it would logically refer to. “It” would not refer to “harvesting,” because “harvesting” would not have “ended up rotting on the ground,” and “it” would not refer to “apples” or “peaches,” because “it” is singular, while “apples” and “peaches” are plural.
(D) The harvesting of apples and peaches were disrupted this year by stormy weather, with the result that much of that ended up rotting on the ground.
The plural verb “were disrupted” does not agree with the subject, which is not the plural “apples and peaches” but the singular “harvesting.” Crossing off what appears between “harvesting” and “were disrupted” makes seeing the issue easier.
The harvesting of apples and peaches were disrupted
Also, notice that the pronoun “that” has nothing that it would logically refer to. “That” would not refer to “harvesting,” because “harvesting” would not have “ended up rotting on the ground,” and “that” would not refer to “apples” or “peaches,” because “that” is singular, while “apples” and “peaches” are plural.
(E) The harvesting of apples and peaches was disrupted this year by stormy weather and resulted in much, which ended up rotting on the ground.
This choice is not quite as easy to eliminate as (A), (C), or (D), because, unlike those three choices, this choice is grammatically correct.
Nevertheless, we can use meaning to eliminate this choice.
Notice what this choice says: “The harvesting … was disrupted … and resulted in much, which ended up rotting on the ground.
The harvesting resulted in “much,” and “much” ended up rotting on the ground? “Much” what?
Since the sentence does not indicate what “much” is, and since it’s unclear what harvesting could possibly have resulted in that would have ended up rotting on the ground, this version does not effectively convey a meaning that makes sense.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now talk about how to use a scratch pad in eliminating choices.
summary:
Lesson Content Often, we’ll have correctly eliminated three of the five answer choices and be left with two.
We’re seeking to determine what makes them different because both sentences can’t be correct, so there must be at least one key point of difference between them that will render one more effective (correct) than the other.
Does one use the singular “is” while the other uses the plural verb “are?” Does one use the pronoun “their” while the other uses the singular “it?
title: 1.10.2 What to Do If Stuck Between Two Final Answer Choices

contents:
Lesson Content Many people believe the false notion that the original, or choice (A), version of the sentence always conveys the meaning intended by the author.
Let’s be clear about this: there is no such thing as “the intended meaning” in Sentence Correction questions.
💡MUST KNOW:
There is no such thing as “the intended meaning” in Sentence Correction questions; there is nothing special about the meaning expressed by the version created via the use of choice (A). We won’t know the exact meaning intended by the author until we find the correct answer, which might be (A) or might be any of the other four answers.
Let’s clarify this idea a bit.
💡MUST KNOW:
Any answer choice can be correct as long as its sentence is free from grammar errors and makes logical sense.
In other words, if the original sentence is incorrect, we don’t carry with us what we think the meaning of the correct sentence should be given what the original sentence says.
Consider the following simple mini-example:
Bob, the man who lives two houses down from us, walks his dog wearing a Speedo.
(A) Bob, the man who lives two houses down from us, walks his dog wearing a Speedo.
(B) While wearing a speedo, Bob, the man who lives two houses down from us, walks his dog.
Notice that the original sentence is wrong because it illogically conveys that the dog is wearing a Speedo.
OK, so we know that answer choice (A) is wrong.
However, these people would be making a mistake.
What was the author’s intended meaning? Well, because answer choice (B) is correct, we see that her meaning was to convey that the man wears the Speedo.
The point to see here is that it is perfectly OK for an answer choice to change the meaning from the meaning conveyed by the original sentence.
Don’t fall into the trap of thinking that the original sentence has a monopoly on the intended meaning of the sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Sentence Correction questions, it is perfectly OK for the correct answer to change the meaning from the meaning conveyed by the original sentence. Our job is to choose the most effective sentence, even if the meaning of that most effective sentence deviates from the meaning of the original sentence.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 16 Before leaves fall from trees, they recycle the nutrients contained in autumn. 1. [ ] (A) Before leaves fall from trees, they recycle the nutrients contained > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) A leaf falling after recycling by the tree of the nutrients contained > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) A leaf falls after the nutrients they contain is recycled by the tree > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Leaves falling after recycling of the nutrients contained in them by trees > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Trees recycle the nutrients contained in leaves before the leaves fall View Solution Question Level: easy The original version of the sentence in this question conveys a nonsensical meaning. “They recycle the nutrients contained in autumn” suggests that nutrients could be contained in autumn, a season of the year.
Since the meaning conveyed by the original version is nonsensical, there is no way the correct version of the sentence will convey the meaning the original version conveys, because the correct version has to convey a meaning that makes sense.
So, in order to arrive at the correct answer, rather than look for a version that conveys the meaning conveyed by the original version, we have to look for a version that effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(A) Before leaves fall from trees, they recycle the nutrients contained in autumn.
Notice that the pronoun “they” in this version could refer to “leaves,” to “trees,” or to both “leaves” and “trees.” Since what “they” is meant to refer to is not clear, the meaning conveyed by this version is ambiguous. Do leaves, trees, or both leaves and trees recycle nutrients? This version does not clearly indicate which of them recycle nutrients.
Also, by saying, “nutrients contained in autumn” this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that nutrients are contained in autumn, a season of the year.
(B) A leaf falling after recycling by the tree of the nutrients contained in autumn.
This version describes a leaf without ever completing the thought. Notice that it says, “a leaf” and then continues by saying more about the leaf, “falling after recycling …,” but leaves the reader wondering “A leaf falling after recycling does what?”
So, this version has no verb to tell us what the leaf does and, thus, is not a complete sentence.
Also, by saying, “nutrients contained in autumn,” this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that nutrients are contained in autumn, a season of the year.
(C) A leaf falls after the nutrients they contain is recycled by the tree in autumn.
The pronoun “they” in this version has nothing to refer to. It seems to refer to “leaves,” but “leaves” does not appear in the sentence. Only the singular “a leaf” appears. Since, “they” is plural, “they” cannot refer to the singular “a leaf.” A plural pronoun has to have a plural noun to refer to.
Also, the plural subject “nutrients” does not agree with the singular verb “is recycled.” So, “nutrients they contain is recycled” is incorrect.
(D) Leaves falling after recycling of the nutrients contained in them by trees in autumn.
This version describes leaves without ever completing the thought. Notice that it says, “leaves” and then continues by saying more about leaves, “falling after recycling …,” but leaves the reader wondering “Leaves falling after recycling do what?”
So, this version has no verb to tell us what the leaves do and, thus, is not a complete sentence.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Trees recycle the nutrients contained in leaves before the leaves fall in autumn.
This version is grammatically correct and effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense. So, even though this version does not convey the meaning conveyed by the original version – the original version conveys that nutrients are contained in autumn, whereas this version conveys that leaves fall in autumn – this choice is the correct answer.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss why we should not eliminate an answer just because it sounds awkward.
summary:
Lesson Content Many people believe the false notion that the original, or choice (A), version of the sentence always conveys the meaning intended by the author.
Let’s be clear about this: there is no such thing as “the intended meaning” in Sentence Correction questions.
💡MUST KNOW:
There is no such thing as “the intended meaning” in Sentence Correction questions; there is nothing special about the meaning expressed by the version created via the use of choice (A).
title: 1.12 Don’t Eliminate an Answer Choice Because “It Changes the Meaning.”

contents:
Lesson Content Incorrect choices in Sentence Correction questions are incorrect because of clear errors in meaning, logic, or grammar.
💡MUST KNOW:
Often, when a person does not see what’s wrong with an option in a Sentence Correction question, he labels the version as “awkward.”
We’ve been teaching the GMAT for over 15 years, and to this day, we still haven’t seen anyone clearly articulate what the word “awkward” means about an incorrect sentence version.
Let’s be clear.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Sentence Correction questions, incorrect sentences are only very rarely incorrect because they are “awkward.” In general, sentence versions are incorrect for clear, definable reasons; they are incorrect because of errors in grammar, structure, or meaning. Claiming that a sentence is incorrect because it is awkward generally indicates that we have failed to see why it is incorrect.
Thus, as you proceed through this course, you’ll notice that we always provide at least one (and usually more than one) clear reason why a sentence is wrong.
We want you to hold yourself to the same high standard.
We’ll explore this concept much more in the chapters to come.
summary:
Lesson Content Incorrect choices in Sentence Correction questions are incorrect because of clear errors in meaning, logic, or grammar.
💡MUST KNOW:
Often, when a person does not see what’s wrong with an option in a Sentence Correction question, he labels the version as “awkward.”
We’ve been teaching the GMAT for over 15 years, and to this day, we still haven’t seen anyone clearly articulate what the word “awkward” means about an incorrect sentence version.
title: 1.13 Don’t Eliminate an Answer Choice Because it Sounds “Awkward.”

contents:
Lesson Content Many people approach sentence correction questions by using their “ears.” In other words, they choose answers based upon what “sounds” right to them or what feels right to them.
💡MUST KNOW:
Approaching sentence correction questions by using their “ears” may help some people get some easy- and medium-level questions correct some of the time, but it certainly will not help most people get most of the questions – especially the harder questions – correct most of the time.
There are a number of reasons why using our “ears” is ineffective.
First, many of us, even those of us who are good writers, commit a litany of errors of logic, grammar, and style when we write.
💡MUST KNOW:
Many of us, even those of us who are good writers, commit a litany of errors of logic, grammar, and style when we write. Therefore, if we use “what we do often” as a proxy in seeking correct answers to Sentence Correction questions, we’re bound to get questions incorrect with high frequency.
Second, often the correct answer to a Sentence Correction question sounds very wrong to us because we are not used to the sophisticated, “ivory tower” writing used in many Sentence Correction questions.
Third, sometimes a sentence has no grammar errors whatsoever, yet the sentence is incorrect because it says something illogical.
💡MUST KNOW:
Our “ears” cannot help us find errors in logic in Sentence Correction questions.
So, we must resist the urge to use our “ears” when solving Sentence Correction questions.
💡MUST KNOW:
We must resist the urge to use our “ear” when solving Sentence Correction questions. We must instead actively engage our critical thinking skills to correctly answer Sentence Correction questions.
Consider the following examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 17 The ability of dragonflies to consume the equivalent of their own body weight each day from pesky insects make them a welcome sight in a garden. 1. [ ] (A) to consume the equivalent of their own body weight each day from pesky insects make them > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) to each day consume the equivalent of their own body weight in pesky insects makes them 3. [ ] (C) about consuming the equivalent of their own body weight in pesky insects each day makes it > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) to consume the equivalent of its own body weight in pesky insects each day and makes it > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) each day consumes the equivalent of its own body weight in pesky insects, making it > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy In evaluating the answer choices, we must remember not to rely on our “ear” to determine which choices are incorrect and which choice is correct. Rather, we must identify clear, specific errors in grammar or illogical meanings in incorrect answer choices. If a choice contains no grammar or meaning errors, even if it does not “sound” how we might expect it to, then that choice will be correct.
(A) The ability of dragonflies to consume the equivalent of their own body weight each day from pesky insects make them a welcome sight in a garden.
If we read this choice casually, it might “sound” OK, but actually, it has multiple errors that we can notice by reading it carefully.
Notice that the subject of the sentence is the singular noun “the ability,” not the plural “dragonflies.” Thus, the plural verb “make” is incorrect, since it does not agree with the singular “ability.” We can see the error more easily by crossing off what appears between the subject and the verb:
The ability of dragonflies to consume the equivalent of their own body weight each day from pesky insects make
“The ability … make” is not grammatical. “The ability … makes” would be correct.
Notice also that it does not quite make sense to say that dragonflies “consume the equivalent of their own body weight each day from pesky insects,” as if the insects have the dragonflies’ body weight to give to the dragonflies. Rather, it makes sense to say that dragonflies “consume the equivalent of their own body weight” in insects, meaning that the quantity of insects a dragonfly consumes is equivalent to the amount the dragonfly weighs.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) The ability of dragonflies to each day consume the equivalent of their own body weight in pesky insects makes them a welcome sight in a garden.
The phrasing “ to each day consume the equivalent” may seem somewhat awkward, but we must remember not to rely on our “ear” to find correct answers and eliminate incorrect choices. “ To each day consume the equivalent” is perfectly grammatical and conveys a logical meaning. So, that phrasing is not a reason to eliminate this choice.
In fact, this choice is the only one that does not contain any errors in grammar or convey any illogical meanings. Notice that the singular verb “makes” properly pairs with the singular subject “the ability.” Furthermore, the plural pronouns “their” and “them” properly refer to the plural noun “dragonflies.”
The sentence is grammatical and the meaning it conveys is logical. So, this choice is correct.
(C) The ability of dragonflies about consuming the equivalent of their own body weight in pesky insects each day makes it a welcome sight in a garden.
The pronoun “it” does not have a logical antecedent. If “it” refers to “the ability of dragonflies,” then the sentence is saying that “the ability of dragonflies … makes the ability of dragonflies a welcome sight,” a nonsensical statement.
It also does not make sense for “it” to refer to “the equivalent of their own body weight,” “body weight,” or “each day.”
There is no singular noun in the sentence to which “it” could logically refer, and thus the version created with this choice does not convey a meaning that makes sense.
Notice also that it does not make sense to say “the ability … about consuming.” People and things can have an ability to do something, but it is not correct to say that they have an ability “about” doing something.
(D) The ability of dragonflies to consume the equivalent of its own body weight in pesky insects each day and makes it a welcome sight in a garden.
The singular pronouns “its” and “it” do not have a logical antecedent.
Given the placement of “its” in the sentence, there is only one singular noun to which these pronouns could possibly refer: “the ability.” Of course, it does not make sense to say “The ability of dragonflies to consume the equivalent of the ability’s own body weight ….” That statement is completely nonsensical.
Notice also that the sentence is not even complete. We get a second action of “the ability” toward the end of the sentence, “and makes it a welcome sight in a garden,” but where is the first action? In other words, where is the verb that would complete the first half of “and makes”? That verb is missing from the sentence.
“To consume” is an infinitive verb, and thus it cannot be a main verb in the sentence. In other words, “to consume” does not name an action or state of being at a specific point in time. So, it does not serve to tell us what “the ability” does or is in addition to “and makes.”
So, the sentence created with this choice does not convey a complete thought or a clear and logical meaning.
(E) The ability of dragonflies each day consumes the equivalent of its own body weight in pesky insects, making it a welcome sight in a garden.
This choice is a good reminder that we must always read an SC answer choice in the context of the full sentence. If we do so, we see the following:
The ability of dragonflies each day consumes the equivalent of its own body weight in pesky insects, making it a welcome sight in a garden.
Notice that the subject “the ability” pairs with the verb “consumes.” Of course, the meaning conveyed by this version is completely nonsensical. It does not make sense to say that an ability consumes its own body weight.
Always read SC answer choices in the context of the full sentence!
Key Concept: Every sentence must have at least one subject and at least one finite verb.
Key Concept: Confirm that singular subjects are paired with singular verbs and that plural subjects are paired with plural verbs. Key Concept: Be sure that the noun to which a pronoun refers makes sense as the person or thing performing the action associated with that pronoun.
Key Concept: If a choice contains no grammar or meaning errors, even if it does not “sound” how we might expect it to, then that choice will be correct.
Key Concept: Be sure to carefully read both the underlined and the non-underlined portions of a sentence.
Key Concept: Don’t select answer choices that produce sentence versions that convey illogical meanings.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 18 That hidden chambers were found by a stonemason while the house of worship was being renovated has not been revealed to the public. 1. [x] (A) That hidden chambers were found by a stonemason while the house of worship was being renovated has not been 2. [ ] (B) Renovating the house of worship, hidden chambers found by a stonemason was not > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) A stonemason found hidden chambers renovating the house of worship but not being > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Hidden chambers were found while renovating the house of worship by a stonemason but has not been > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Renovating the house of worship, hidden chambers being found by a stonemason but not > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard Since the correct answer to this question uses a structure that most people don’t often use or see used, getting this question correct is a bit tricky. However, if we carefully assess the grammar and logic of each version, we’ll find clear flaws that we can use to eliminate the incorrect choices and, thus, arrive at the correct answer.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) That hidden chambers were found by a stonemason while the house of worship was being renovated has not been revealed to the public.
This choice may “sound” incorrect at first, because of the way it’s constructed. So, if we use our “ears,” we may quickly decide that this choice is not correct.
The subject of this version is the noun clause “that hidden chambers were found by a stonemason while the house of worship was being renovated.” (Noun clauses are discussed in the next chapter.)
That noun clause may not “sound” like a sentence subject, but it basically means the same thing as “the fact that hidden chambers were found …,” and we can see that “the fact that hidden chambers were found” would be something that “has not been revealed to the public.”
So, this version is grammatically correct and conveys a meaning that makes sense: the fact that hidden chambers were found has not been revealed to the public.
(B) Renovating the house of worship, hidden chambers found by a stonemason was not revealed to the public.
This version has a clear grammar error. The plural subject “hidden chambers” does not agree with the singular verb “was,” as we can more easily see by crossing off what appears between “hidden chambers” and “was.”
hidden chambers found by a stonemason was not revealed
Also, notice what this version says at the beginning: “Renovating the house of worship, hidden chambers found by a stonemason was.” For reasons that we’ll discuss later in the course, that part of the sentence conveys that hidden chambers were renovating the house of worship, a nonsensical meaning.
(C) A stonemason found hidden chambers renovating the house of worship but not being revealed to the public.
This choice is tricky because it’s grammatically correct and “sounds” OK, even though it’s not correct.
To eliminate this choice, we have to notice what it says, “A stonemason found hidden chambers renovating the house of worship but not being revealed.” That conveys the nonsensical meaning that hidden chambers were renovating the house of worship.
We can eliminate this choice because it conveys a nonsensical meaning.
(D) Hidden chambers were found while renovating the house of worship by a stonemason but has not been revealed to the public.
This version has a clear grammar error. The plural subject “hidden chambers” does not agree with the verb “has,” as we can more easily see by crossing off what appears between “hidden chambers” and “has.”
Hidden chambers were found while renovating the house of worship by a stonemason but has not been
Also, notice what this version says at the beginning: “Hidden chambers were found while renovating the house of worship.” For reasons that we’ll discuss later in the course, that part of the sentence conveys that hidden chambers were renovating the house of worship, a nonsensical meaning.
(E) Renovating the house of worship, hidden chambers being found by a stonemason but not revealed to the public.
This version may appear OK at first. However, if we look at it carefully, we can notice that it says, “hidden chambers being found … but not revealed” without ever completing the thought with a verb that says what the hidden chambers do. It leaves the reader wondering, “Hidden chambers being found … but not revealed do what?”
So, this choice can be eliminated because this version is not a complete sentence.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss being careful when later versions of a sentence fix errors that appear in earlier versions.
summary:
Lesson Content Many people approach sentence correction questions by using their “ears.” In other words, they choose answers based upon what “sounds” right to them or what feels right to them.
💡MUST KNOW:
Approaching sentence correction questions by using their “ears” may help some people get some easy- and medium-level questions correct some of the time, but it certainly will not help most people get most of the questions – especially the harder questions – correct most of the time.
title: 1.14 Do Not Seek to Determine What’s Correct or Incorrect Using Your “Ear.”

contents:
Lesson Content Imagine that, in a question, the original sentence incorrectly uses the plural verb “were” with the singular subject “the company.” Most of you, even at this point, know that a singular subject must be paired with a singular verb.
Now, what happens if choice (B) fixes this issue? In other words, what if the sentence produced by answer choice (B) correctly uses the singular noun “the company” with the singular verb “was.” In that case we choose answer (B) as correct, right?
Not necessarily.
So, as always, we must read each sentence version in its entirety.
💡MUST KNOW:
When we are answering a Sentence Correction question, the fact that a sentence produced by a later answer choice fixes an error found in the original sentence, or any earlier sentence, does not allow us, on that basis alone, to choose the new sentence as correct.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 19 The twenty pages of the document contains a description of the room in which the possessions of the governor were found when the archeologists unearthed the long-buried city. 1. [ ] (A) The twenty pages of the document contains a description of the room in which the possessions of the governor were > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) A twenty-page document containing a description of the room in which the possessions of the governor were > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) The twenty-page document contains a description of the room from which the possessions of the governor was > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) The twenty-page document contains a description of the room in which the possessions of the governor were 5. [ ] (E) A twenty-page document contains a description of the room from which the possessions of the governor was > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium The original version of the sentence in this question has a subject-verb agreement error. The plural subject “the twenty pages of the document” does not agree with the singular verb “contains.”
To arrive at the correct answer to the question, we have to be careful not to get too excited and choose the first choice we see that fixes this subject-verb agreement error, because the first choice that fixes this error, choice (C), has new errors.
(A) The twenty pages of the document contains a description of the room in which the possessions of the governor were found when the archeologists unearthed the long-buried city.
The plural subject “the twenty pages” does not agree with the singular verb “contains,” as we can more easily see by crossing off what appears between “the twenty pages” and “contains.”
The twenty pages of the document contains
(B) A twenty-page document containing a description of the room in which the possessions of the governor were found when the archeologists unearthed the long-buried city.
This version describes the document without ever completing the thought. Notice that it says, “a twenty-page document” and then continues by saying more about the document, “containing a description of the room …,” but leaves the reader wondering “A twenty-page document containing a description of the room does what?”
So, this version has no verb to tell us what the document does and, thus, is not a complete sentence.
(C) The twenty-page document contains a description of the room from which the possessions of the governor was found when the archeologists unearthed the long-buried city.
This version fixes the subject-verb agreement error seen in (A) by changing “the twenty pages of the document” to “the twenty-page document.” The singular subject “the twenty-page document” agrees with the singular verb “contains.”
However, we have to be careful not to choose this choice just because it fixes an error that exists in a previous version, because this version has new errors.
For one thing, we can see that this version misuses “from” in “the room from which the possessions of the governor were found.” The possessions would have been found “in” a room, not “from” a room.
Also, the plural subject “possessions” does not agree with the singular verb “was found,” as we can more easily see by crossing off what appears between “possessions” and “was found.”
the possessions of the governor was found
CORRECT ANSWER(D) The twenty-page document contains a description of the room in which the possessions of the governor were found when the archeologists unearthed the long-buried city.
This version is grammatically correct from beginning to end and effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(E) A twenty-page document contains a description of the room from which the possessions of the governor was found when the archeologists unearthed the long-buried city.
This version fixes the lack of a verb seen in (B), by using “contains” rather than “containing.” By using “contains,” this version, unlike the version created via the use of (B), tells us what the document does: it “contains.”
However, we have to be careful not to choose this choice just because it fixes an error that exists in a previous version, because this version has new errors.
For one thing, we can see that this version misuses “from” in “the room from which the possessions of the governor were found.” The possessions would have been found “in” a room, not “from” a room.
Also, the plural subject “possessions” does not agree with the singular verb “was found,” as we can more easily see by crossing off what appears between “possessions” and “was found.”
the possessions of the governor was found
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss not falling into choosing choice (E).
summary:
Lesson Content Imagine that, in a question, the original sentence incorrectly uses the plural verb “were” with the singular subject “the company.” Most of you, even at this point, know that a singular subject must be paired with a singular verb.
Now, what happens if choice (B) fixes this issue? In other words, what if the sentence produced by answer choice (B) correctly uses the singular noun “the company” with the singular verb “was.
title: 1.15 Be Careful with Later Versions That Fix Earlier Errors.

contents:
Lesson Content Often, in answering Sentence Correction questions, people fall into choosing choice (E), the last answer choice, without a clear reason to choose choice (E).
As you can imagine, falling into choosing choice (E) in this way often results in a person’s getting a question incorrect.
To be clear, answer choice (E) will be correct just as frequently as the other four answer choices.
However, don’t choose (E) just because you haven’t decided that another choice is correct, and (E) is the only one left.
💡MUST KNOW:
Although answer choice (E) will be correct just as frequently as the other four answer choices, you should not choose answer choice (E) as correct simply because you do not see why any of the other answer options are correct.
So, when you get to choice (E), if you haven’t found clear reasons why choice (E) or other choices are incorrect or correct, then the move is to go back through the choices and find clear reasons for choosing one choice over the others.
Consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 20 Policymakers in government has largely ignored the dire predictions the latest climate change assessment make, both because of the current global economic downturn and corporate influence. 1. [ ] (A) Policymakers in government has largely ignored the dire predictions the latest climate change assessment make, because of both the current global economic downturn and > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) The dire predictions the latest climate change assessment make have largely been ignored by government policymakers, both because of the current global economic downturn and > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) The dire predictions made in the latest climate change assessment have largely been ignored by government policymakers, both because of the current global economic downturn and because of 4. [ ] (D) Policymakers in government is largely ignoring the dire predictions the latest climate change assessment make, both as a result of the current global economic downturn as well as of > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) The dire predictions made in the latest climate change assessment has largely been ignored by government policymakers, both because of the current global economic downturn and also > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy In evaluating the answer choices in this Sentence Correction question, we must be sure to find specific reasons why one choice is better than the others, rather than simply select choice (E) because nothing else seems correct. If we get to choice (E) without identifying a sentence version that’s clearly correct, we may have to go through all the choices again.
Be careful not to just fall into choosing choice (E)!
(A) Policymakers in government has largely ignored the dire predictions the latest climate change assessment make, because of both the current global economic downturn and corporate influence.
The first flaw we see in this version is that the plural subject “policymakers” does not agree with the singular verb “has.” A plural subject must be paired with a plural verb.
Continuing through the sentence, we can see also that the singular subject “climate change assessment” is incorrectly paired with the plural verb “make.” After all, the thing making “the dire predictions” is the “latest climate change assessment.” So, the wording should instead be “the latest climate change assessment makes.”
(B) The dire predictions the latest climate change assessment make have largely been ignored by government policymakers, both because of the current global economic downturn and corporate influence.
If you spotted the second subject-verb agreement error in choice (A), you probably noticed that this choice commits the same error: the singular noun “latest climate change assessment” does not properly pair with the plural verb “make.” The correct pairing would be “the latest climate change assessment makes.”
Notice also the “both … and” construction in this version of the sentence. The elements of this construction should be parallel, meaning that, since “both” is followed by “because of,” “and” should also be followed by “because of.” So, since “and” is followed by only the phrase “corporate influence,” the “both … and” construction is not correctly parallel.
If you didn’t spot the error in parallelism, don’t worry; you’ll get plenty of practice with this skill in the Parallelism chapter.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) The dire predictions made in the latest climate change assessment have largely been ignored by government policymakers, both because of the current global economic downturn and because of corporate influence.
This version is the only one that does not contain any subject-verb agreement errors.
Also, the “both … and” construction in this version is correctly parallel since “both” and “and” are both followed by “because of.”
Notice also that the plural noun “dire predictions” is correctly paired with the plural verb “have.”
The sentence is grammatically correct and its meaning is logical.
(D) Policymakers in government is largely ignoring the dire predictions the latest climate change assessment make, both as a result of the current global economic downturn as well as of corporate influence.
The first flaw we see in this version is that the plural subject “policymakers” does not agree with the singular verb “is.” A plural subject must be paired with a plural verb.
Also we must be careful to pinpoint which noun in the sentence pairs with the plural verb “make.” The thing making “the dire predictions” is the singular “latest climate change assessment.” Thus, since the singular “assessment” cannot pair with the plural verb “make,” this choice is incorrect. The correct pairing would be “the latest climate change assessment makes.”
This choice also contains the illogical phrase “both … as well as.” The correct wording is “both … and.”
(E) The dire predictions made in the latest climate change assessment has largely been ignored by government policymakers, both because of the current global economic downturn and also corporate influence.
If you have not decided on another choice, you may be particularly tempted by this choice because it changes the incorrect “make” we saw in other choices to the correct “made.”
However, this choice commits a different subject-verb agreement error, incorrectly pairing the plural noun “dire predictions” with the singular verb “has.” The “predictions” are the thing ignored by policymakers, not the singular “assessment.”
If you had trouble seeing the subject-verb agreement error, it is helpful to cross off the phrase separating the noun from the main verb:
The dire predictions made in the latest climate change assessment has … been ignored
Now, we can clearly see that “dire predictions … has” is not grammatical. “Dire predictions … have” would be correct.
Additionally, this choice contains a redundancy: the phrase “both … and also” is incorrect, because “and” and “also” have the same meaning in that context. The correct wording is simply “both … and.”
Be careful not to fall into selecting choice (E) just because you’re having trouble finding a choice that seems correct. You must evaluate each answer choice on its own merits.
