Key Concept: Confirm that singular subjects are paired with singular verbs and that plural subjects are paired with plural verbs. Key Concept: Make sure that the two halves of correlative constructions such as “both X and Y,” “not only X but also Y,” and “not X but Y” are parallel in structure.
Key Concept: The second half of the idiom “both X and Y” cannot use “also” or “as well as” in the place of or in addition to “and.”
Key Concept: When checking for subject-verb agreement, cross off any noun modifiers that come between the subject and finite verb.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 21 Although it is clear that koan meditation is becoming more widely used, it is also clear that meditation of that form is not likely as becoming the most popular with people in general. 1. [ ] (A) it is also clear that meditation of that form is not likely as becoming > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) what is clear also is that that form of meditation is unlikely to become 3. [ ] (C) it is clear also that meditation of that form is unlikely for becoming > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) also clear is that form of meditation is not likely in becoming > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) though it is also clear that meditation of that form is unlikely to become > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium As we go through the choices in this question, choices (A), (B), (C), and (D) may all seem strange and incorrect to us. So, since choice (E) “sounds” OK, when we get to choice (E), we may be tempted to quickly choose it without much analysis.
Choice (E) has a major error, however. So, to get this question correct, we have to be careful not to “fall into choosing choice (E).”
(A) Although it is clear that koan meditation is becoming more widely used, it is also clear that meditation of that form is not likely as becoming the most popular with people in general.
We can see that this version uses the wording “not likely as becoming,” which is not correct English and does not convey a meaning that makes sense.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) Although it is clear that koan meditation is becoming more widely used, what is clear also is that that form of meditation is unlikely to become the most popular with people in general.
Because this version uses “what is,” and because “that that” may seem odd, we may initially be reluctant to choose this choice.
However, using “what is” the way this version uses it is correct.
Also, the first “that” in “that that” is part of “what is clear is that.” Then, the second “that” is used to indicate that the sentence is continuing to discuss “that form of meditation.” So, using “that” twice in a row makes sense.
So, this version is grammatically correct and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(C) Although it is clear that koan meditation is becoming more widely used, it is clear also that meditation of that form is unlikely for becoming the most popular with people in general.
We can see that this version uses the wording “unlikely for becoming,” which is not correct English and does not convey a meaning that makes sense.
(D) Although it is clear that koan meditation is becoming more widely used, also clear is that form of meditation is not likely in becoming the most popular with people in general.
We can see that this version uses the wording “not likely in becoming,” which is not correct English and does not convey a meaning that makes sense.
(E) Although it is clear that koan meditation is becoming more widely used, though it is also clear that meditation of that form is unlikely to become the most popular with people in general.
This version does not “sound” strange or incorrect the way all the other versions do, but this version has a clear error. So, we have to be careful not to fall into choosing this choice (E) just because it seems OK when we first read it.
The problem with this version is that it uses both “although” and “though” even though they mean essentially the same thing and serve the same purpose in the sentence.
We can more easily see that using both “although” and “though” is incorrect by simplifying the sentence.
Although it is clear …, though it is also clear ….
In order for the sentence to be correct, we need to use one or the other as in:
Although it is clear …, it is also clear ….
or
It is clear …, though it is also clear ….
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss how much time to allocate to answering Sentence Correction questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Often, in answering Sentence Correction questions, people fall into choosing choice (E), the last answer choice, without a clear reason to choose choice (E).
As you can imagine, falling into choosing choice (E) in this way often results in a person’s getting a question incorrect.
To be clear, answer choice (E) will be correct just as frequently as the other four answer choices.
However, don’t choose (E) just because you haven’t decided that another choice is correct, and (E) is the only one left.
title: 1.16 Be Careful of Falling Into Answer Choice (E).

contents:
Lesson Content On test day, each Sentence Correction question will take you, on average, about 1.5 to 1.75 minutes to complete.
💡MUST KNOW:
On test day, each Sentence Correction question will take you about 1.5 to 1.75 minutes each to complete. Keep in mind that this time is an average; some will be solved faster, and some will require more time.
That said, answering Sentence Correction questions when you are taking the GMAT and practicing answering Sentence Correction questions when you are preparing to take the GMAT are two different activities.
💡MUST KNOW:
As you are working through the learning phase of the TTP Sentence Correction course, you should work through the chapter examples and the chapter test questions untimed.
This information is very important.
No matter what you have heard from anyone else about how to practice Sentence Correction, do Sentence Correction practice questions untimed, focus on understanding and accuracy, and let speed come with skill. Here’s why.
If you can’t solve a question within the recommended time frame, then you don’t yet have the skills you need to solve that question in that amount of time.
To make matters worse, blindly marking a question just to answer it within a two- or three-minute time frame and then getting it wrong can kick your self-confidence down.
So, go slowly and methodically.
You won’t learn to succeed in Sentence Correction by getting questions incorrect in under two minutes each.
Your study plan will begin to nudge you when you should begin practicing these questions under time pressure.
Let’s now discuss leveraging the predictability of Sentence Correction questions.
summary:
Lesson Content On test day, each Sentence Correction question will take you, on average, about 1.5 to 1.75 minutes to complete.
💡MUST KNOW:
On test day, each Sentence Correction question will take you about 1.5 to 1.75 minutes each to complete. Keep in mind that this time is an average; some will be solved faster, and some will require more time.
That said, answering Sentence Correction questions when you are taking the GMAT and practicing answering Sentence Correction questions when you are preparing to take the GMAT are two different activities.
title: 1.17 Timing and Sentence Correction Questions

contents:
Lesson Content As you proceed, it will be helpful to keep in mind the fact that the errors that appear in Sentence Correction questions are limited to a handful of major topics.
(1) Subject-Verb Agreement
(2) Pronouns and Antecedents
(3) Modifiers
(4) Verb Tense, Mood, and Voice
(5) Parallelism
(6) Logical Comparisons
(7) Sentence Structure
(8) Idioms
(9) Logic and Meaning
Notice that, at a high level, there are not too many different types of errors that appear.
Let’s get to work!!!! Next up is the chapter on Sentence Structure.

title: 1.18 We Can Leverage the Predictability of Sentence Correction Questions.

contents:
Lesson Content At the core of Sentence Correction is proper sentence structure, making this chapter one of the more important chapters in this course.
Although the information presented here is foundational, almost all students will find either that they never learned this material or that much of it has been long forgotten.
💡MUST KNOW:
Although the information presented here is foundational, almost all students will find either that they never learned this material or that much of it has been long forgotten.
In either case, without a mastery of the information presented here, you may find that most of the chapters that follow will be less than fully effective as learning tools.
So, take your time to learn this material well.
Let’s begin by discussing phrases and clauses.
summary:
Lesson Content At the core of Sentence Correction is proper sentence structure, making this chapter one of the more important chapters in this course.
Although the information presented here is foundational, almost all students will find either that they never learned this material or that much of it has been long forgotten.
💡MUST KNOW:
Although the information presented here is foundational, almost all students will find either that they never learned this material or that much of it has been long forgotten.
title: 2.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content In language, we put words together to form two elementary types of structures: phrases and clauses.
A phrase is simply a collection of words that cannot stand alone as a sentence because there is no subject-verb relationship.
💡MUST KNOW:
A phrase is a collection of words without a subject-verb relationship. A phrase can never stand alone as a sentence.
The following are examples of phrases:
Notice that none of these phrases can stand alone as a sentence.
Incorrect: For the national good.
Notice also that none of the phrases has a sense of a subject performing an action.
To form any sentence in English one must have a clause.
In order for a string of words to qualify as a clause, it must have a subject-verb relationship.
For example:
Tom ate his lunch.
Vivek is the happiest guy in town.
that the fog rolled in
Because the volcano was spurting
Although you love eating pistachios
whichever path you choose
Notice that, in each of the above strings of words, there is a subject doing something or being something.
This subject-verb relationship is a good start.
Tom to eat his lunch
Because the volcano spurting
that the fog rolling in
Although you eating pistachios
Vivek to be the happiest guy in town
Likely you can immediately tell that the above strings of words are not clauses.
None of the above utterances have a verb that indicates when the actions they mention took place.
When did any of the actions mentioned by these collections of words occur? We don’t know, right? For example, when did Tom “to eat” his lunch? Past? Present? Future? Who knows! There is not a verb that answers the question “When did the action occur?”
So, the reason why the above collections of words are not clauses is that they don’t have verbs that indicate when actions took place.
We have a name for a verb form that answers that question in a clause: “finite verb.”
A finite verb is a verb that answers the question “When did the action occur?”
💡MUST KNOW:
A finite verb is a verb that answers the question “When did the action occur?”
Looking back, notice that each of those five phrases above lacks a finite verb.
Tom ate his lunch.
Tom had eaten his lunch.
Because the volcano is spurting
Because the volcano was spurting
that the fog will roll in
that the fog can roll in
Although you would eat pistachios
Although you do eat pistachios
So then, simply put, a clause is not just any set of words that contains both a noun and a verb but rather a set of words that contains a subject and, specifically, a finite verb.
clause = [subject] + [finite verb]
The clause may contain many other things, but it must have those two elements.
💡MUST KNOW:
A clause has both a subject and a finite verb. A phrase lacks a subject, a finite verb, or both.
In order to determine whether a group of words is a phrase or a clause, check for the presence of a subject and a finite verb.
💡MUST KNOW:
In order to determine whether a group of words is a phrase or a clause, check for a subject and a finite verb. If both are present, we have a clause. If either is missing, we have a phrase.
Consider the following set of words:
the weather is pleasant
Notice that we have a subject “weather” and a finite verb “is,” a verb that indicates that the event mentioned is occurring in the present.
Consider the following examples of clauses.
because you loved me
Sara is at the beach
although you are going skiing next weekend
that the cats had been jumping up and down on the bed
Estella wears a bright pink scarf
Because the snow has freshly fallen
At this point, you know that a clause contains a subject and a finite verb.
the boy to run down the hill
all the pickles eaten by the children
the students looking to better their futures
It’s important to see that the verb forms in these examples are not sufficient for creating clauses.
Why are we belaboring this point about the finite verb? Because most, if not all students, when asked what constitutes a clause (or a sentence for that matter) will answer “a subject and a verb.” However, it’s crucial to understand that it’s not just any verb that makes a clause (or a sentence); it’s a finite verb.
My parents gave me a car for graduation.
I will finish my homework tomorrow.
Esterhazy was drinking from your cup!
Sonia would have been leading if not for her sloppy habits.
That approach is sure to work.
I have eaten in the cafeteria.
Zala had contradicted everything.
They are eating all the avocados.
They were eating all the avocados.
The entire team would have gone.
I can explain myself.
Algernon must be older than I.
We now understand the difference between a phrase and a clause.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following strings of words is a phrase or a clause. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 ducks waddling across the peninsula 1. [x] (A) Phrase 2. [ ] (B) Clause > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 because of Pilar’s retirement 1. [x] (A) Phrase 2. [ ] (B) Clause > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 whereas some planets have multiple moons 1. [ ] (A) Phrase > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Clause ## Targeted Practice: 4 the delegation chosen by the council 1. [x] (A) Phrase 2. [ ] (B) Clause > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 after a faster chip was developed 1. [ ] (A) Phrase > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Clause ## Targeted Practice: 6 over twenty-two years after the release of the first version of the now well-known author’s popular trilogy 1. [x] (A) Phrase 2. [ ] (B) Clause > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 7 that stress management counseling can be more effective than medication in ameliorating symptoms 1. [ ] (A) Phrase > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Clause ## Targeted Practice: 8 as they go 1. [ ] (A) Phrase > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Clause Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
Since “waddling” does not indicate when the ducks were waddling, this string of words does not include a finite verb. So, it is a phrase.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
“Retirement” is a noun, not a verb. So, this string of words includes no verb and, thus, must be a phrase, not a clause.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
This string of words includes a subject, “some planets,” that has a subject-verb relationship with a finite verb, “have.” So, it is a clause.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
Since “chosen” is not a finite verb, this collection of words is a phrase.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
This collection of words includes a subject, “a faster chip,” and a finite verb, “was.” So, it’s a clause.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
Don’t be fooled by the length of this string of words. While it is fairly long, it does not include a finite verb and, therefore, is a phrase.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 7 Explanation:
This string of words includes a subject, “stress management counseling,” and a finite verb, “can.” Therefore, it is a clause.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 8 Explanation:
This string of words includes a subject, “they,” and a finite verb, “go.” Therefore, it is a clause.
Correct Answer: (B) In English there are different types of clauses, independent clauses and dependent clauses.

A phrase is simply a collection of words that cannot stand alone as a sentence because there is no subject-verb relationship.
💡MUST KNOW:
A phrase is a collection of words without a subject-verb relationship. A phrase can never stand alone as a sentence.
The following are examples of phrases:
Notice that none of these phrases can stand alone as a sentence.
title: 2.2 The Differences Between Phrases and Clauses

contents:
Lesson Content There are two major hallmarks of an independent clause:
· An independent clause contains both a subject and a finite verb.
· An independent clause can stand alone as a sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
An independent clause contains a subject and a finite verb and can stand alone as a sentence.
Why are independent clauses important? Well, every English sentence must have at least one independent clause, and the correct version of a sentence in a Sentence Correction question always contains at least one independent clause.
💡MUST KNOW:
Every English sentence must have at least one independent clause, and the correct version of a sentence in a Sentence Correction question always contains at least one independent clause.
The following are examples of independent clauses, with their subjects and finite verbs bolded.
I respect you.
I love you.
The crowd laughed.
Jeff and Sara were walking the dog.
Bears hibernate during the winter.
The dog and the cat are eating their dinner.
Neither Mashid nor her sisters are at the beach.
Eating bananas in the sun is my favorite pastime.
To arrive at the restaurant before ten would have been close to a miracle.
Notice that each of these independent clauses contains a subject and a finite verb.
At this point, let’s introduce an important term: “main verb.” A main verb is the finite verb found in an independent clause.
💡MUST KNOW:
In any independent clause, the finite verb in that clause is called a main verb.
For example:
The leopard slept in the tree.
The building will sway in the wind.
My computer runs quickly.
The stores were crowded with shoppers.
Morning fog is beautiful.
In the clauses above, “slept,” “will,” “runs,” “were,” and “is” are the main verbs.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following structures contains an independent clause. Select Yes if the structure contains an independent clause or No if it does not. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 Before the twentieth century, no one had ever been known to run a mile in less than 4 minutes. 1. [x] (A) Yes 2. [ ] (B) No > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 Despite possessing a dominant defense far surpassing their defense of the previous year, the 1986 Chicago Bears failing to create a football dynasty or to return to the Super Bowl. 1. [ ] (A) Yes > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) No ## Targeted Practice: 3 Despite possessing a dominant defense far surpassing their defense of the previous year, the 1986 Chicago Bears failed to create a football dynasty or to return to the Super Bowl. 1. [x] (A) Yes 2. [ ] (B) No > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 Carlos Fuentes, perhaps Mexico’s greatest novelist, was appointed ambassador to France by his government in 1975. 1. [x] (A) Yes 2. [ ] (B) No > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 In Newtonian physics, the sequence of physical phenomena is completely determined by its past and by the determinations of all positions and momenta at any one moment. 1. [x] (A) Yes 2. [ ] (B) No > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 6 The United States remaining a nation characterized by a spirit of capitalism despite the spread of socialism to much of the world. 1. [ ] (A) Yes > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) No ## Targeted Practice: 7 Like other forms of apparatus meant to retain a large number of decisions, Mr. Williams’ repeater working on the scanning principle. 1. [ ] (A) Yes > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) No ## Targeted Practice: 8 Before Ronald Reagan, no one being so foolhardy as to predict an actor’s making it all the way to the White House. 1. [ ] (A) Yes > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) No Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
“No one had ever been known to run a mile in less than 4 minutes” is an independent clause with the subject “no one” and the finite verb “had.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
Since “possessing,” “surpassing,” “failing,” “to create,” and “to return” are not finite verbs, this string of words does not include a finite verb and, thus, does not contain an independent clause.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
“The 1986 Chicago Bears failed to create a football dynasty or to return to the Super Bowl” is an independent clause with the subject “the 1986 Chicago Bears” and the finite verb “failed.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
This string of words is an independent clause with the subject “Carlos Fuentes” and the finite verb “was.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
“The sequence of physical phenomena is completely determined by its past and by the determinations of all positions and momenta at any one moment” is an independent clause with the subject “the sequence of physical phenomena” and the finite verb “is.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
Since neither “remaining” nor “characterized” is a finite verb, this string of words does not include a main verb or an independent clause.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 7 Explanation:
Notice that “working” is not a finite verb. So, this string of words does not include a clause.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 8 Explanation:
Since “being,” “to predict,” and “making” are not finite verbs, this string of words does not contain a finite verb or an independent clause.
Correct Answer: (B) There is one more required characteristic of an independent clause: there must be no word that removes the clause’s independence.
Let’s now discuss dependent clauses.
summary:
Lesson Content There are two major hallmarks of an independent clause:
· An independent clause contains both a subject and a finite verb.
· An independent clause can stand alone as a sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
An independent clause contains a subject and a finite verb and can stand alone as a sentence.
Why are independent clauses important? Well, every English sentence must have at least one independent clause, and the correct version of a sentence in a Sentence Correction question always contains at least one independent clause.
title: 2.3 Independent Clauses

contents:
Lesson Content Clauses that cannot constitute sentences on their own are, quite logically, called dependent clauses.
💡MUST KNOW:
Clauses that cannot constitute sentences on their own are called dependent clauses. These clauses fulfill specific functions within sentences and serve to enhance or modify the meaning expressed by an independent clause.
There are three distinct types of dependent clauses:
(1) Subordinate clauses
(2) Relative clauses
(3) Noun clauses
Let’s continue by discussing subordinate clauses first.
summary:
Lesson Content Clauses that cannot constitute sentences on their own are, quite logically, called dependent clauses.
💡MUST KNOW:
Clauses that cannot constitute sentences on their own are called dependent clauses. These clauses fulfill specific functions within sentences and serve to enhance or modify the meaning expressed by an independent clause.
There are three distinct types of dependent clauses:
(1) Subordinate clauses
(2) Relative clauses
(3) Noun clauses
Let’s continue by discussing subordinate clauses first.
title: 2.4 Dependent Clauses

contents:
Lesson Content Consider one of our last examples of an independent clause:
The crowd laughed.
The clause has a subject and a finite verb - all that is needed, in this instance, for a complete sentence.
Incorrect: Because the crowd laughed.
Although you may not know exactly what is missing here, it’s almost certainly clear to you that we don’t have a sentence.
“Because” is a member of a class of words called subordinating conjunctions, words that can take away the independence of a clause when placed at the head of the clause.
💡MUST KNOW:
When a subordinating conjunction is placed at the head of a clause, that clause becomes a subordinate clause, a type of dependent clause.
The following are examples of subordinate clauses headed by subordinating conjunctions:
since John was at the beach
although Scott is typing fast
whereas Douglas may complain about the strict rules
because you and I are best friends
unless she arrives at the airport by midnight
once the mountain lion puts its cubs to bed
if you want to become a pilot
Two things are important to notice.
Now, we need to be able to recognize subordinate conjunctions when we see them.
after, before, until, once, while, although, whereas, because, if, unless, though, even though, as much as, just as, whenever
💡MUST KNOW:
If a clause begins with a subordinating conjunction, it is a subordinate clause.
We can visualize the structure of a subordinate clause as follows:
subordinate clause = [subordinating conjunction] + [subject] + [finite verb]
💡MUST KNOW:
subordinate clause = [subordinating conjunction] + [subject] + [finite verb]
Let’s get some practice recognizing subordinate clauses.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following structures contains a subordinate clause. Select Yes if the structure contains a subordinate clause or No if it does not. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 Despite skepticism, Vermont’s new policies passed the overwhelmingly Democratic legislature. 1. [ ] (A) Yes > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) No ## Targeted Practice: 2 Although scientists dream of universal flu vaccines and therapies to throttle incipient pandemics, such wide-ranging preventive measures still elude development. 1. [x] (A) Yes 2. [ ] (B) No > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 Better times began after his penance brought peace to his household. 1. [x] (A) Yes 2. [ ] (B) No > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 Some subjects, having experienced paranormal episodes, found themselves unable to sleep more than four hours at a stretch. 1. [ ] (A) Yes > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) No ## Targeted Practice: 5 Stonehenge is one of the Seven Wonders of the World and has been an attention drawer for ages. 1. [ ] (A) Yes > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) No ## Targeted Practice: 6 Whenever people experiencing the issue adjust their habits and lifestyle choices, their health improves. 1. [x] (A) Yes 2. [ ] (B) No > Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
This sentence includes only “despite skepticism,” which is not a clause, and an independent clause with the subject “Vermont’s new policies” and the main verb “passed.” So, it does not include a subordinate clause.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
This sentence includes a subordinate clause that begins with “although” and has the subject “scientists” and the finite verb “dream.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
This sentence includes an independent clause, “better times began,” and a subordinate clause that begins with “after” and has the subject “his penance” and the finite verb “brought.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
This sentence includes only one clause, an independent clause with the subject “some subjects” and the main verb “found.”
Notice that “having experienced paranormal episodes” has no subject or finite verb and, thus, is not a clause.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
This sentence is composed of one independent clause with the subject “Stonehenge” and verbs “is” and “has been.” So, it does not include a subordinate clause.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
This sentence includes a subordinate clause that begins with “whenever” and has the subject “people experiencing the issue” and the finite verb “adjust.”
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss sentences.
summary:
Lesson Content Consider one of our last examples of an independent clause:
The crowd laughed.
The clause has a subject and a finite verb - all that is needed, in this instance, for a complete sentence.
Incorrect: Because the crowd laughed.
Although you may not know exactly what is missing here, it’s almost certainly clear to you that we don’t have a sentence.
“Because” is a member of a class of words called subordinating conjunctions, words that can take away the independence of a clause when placed at the head of the clause.
title: 2.5 The Subordinate Clause

contents:
Lesson Content In the simplest sense, a sentence is a collection of words that contains at least one independent clause.
Let’s continue by talking about subjects.
Most of you are familiar with the concept of the subject of a sentence.
For example:
Whales are highly intelligent animals.
The blue ball was rolling down the hill.
Doug will ask Martha to come to his house for dinner.
In the sentences above, “whales,” “ball,” and “Doug” are the subjects performing the actions.
Incorrect: are highly intelligent animals.
Incorrect: was rolling down the hill.
Incorrect: will ask Martha to come to his house for dinner.
💡MUST KNOW:
The subject of a sentence is the person or thing performing the action in the sentence. Without at least one subject, there can be no sentence.
In addition to needing at least one subject, because every sentence must contain at least one independent clause, every sentence must contain at least one main verb.
Whales are highly intelligent animals.
The blue ball was rolling down the hill.
Doug will ask Martha to come to his house for dinner.
In the sentences above, “are,” “was,” and “will” are the main verbs.
Without at least one subject and at least one main verb, we don’t have a sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
Every English sentence must contain at least one main verb.
Notice that, if we remove the main verbs from the sentences above, we no longer have sentences:
Incorrect: Whales highly intelligent animals.
Incorrect: The blue ball rolling down the hill.
Incorrect: Doug ask Martha to come to his house for dinner.
Some of you are questioning the second example, saying, “Wait a minute – ‘rolling’ is a verb.”
Yes, technically, “rolling” is a verb (it’s a present participle, and we will talk more about present participles later), but present participles like “rolling” can’t serve as the main verb in a sentence.
In fact, we don’t need to have ever heard of the term present participle to see that “rolling” can’t be a main verb because it does not tell us when the action occurred; it is not a finite verb.
When we don’t have at least one subject and at least one main verb in a sentence, we have a sentence fragment.
Any answer choice that produces a sentence fragment will not be a correct answer on the GMAT.
💡MUST KNOW:
When we lack either a subject or a main verb in a sentence, we have a sentence fragment. Any answer choice that produces a sentence fragment will not be a correct answer on the GMAT.
This concept seems simple enough, but if we’re not careful, we can run into trouble in answering Sentence Correction questions.
Consider the following:
Incorrect: A love story serving as a source of deep insights into human relations.
Notice that this sentence fragment almost seems to be a sentence, but when we look closely, we see that the structure lacks a main verb.
Notice that the following versions, however, are sentences, because they do contain main verbs:
A love story serving as a source of deep insights into human relations is being written by Martha.
The finite verb “is” tells us that the action is occurring now and serves as the main verb in the sentence.
A love story serving as a source of deep insights into human relations was being written by Martha.
The finite verb “was” tells us that the action occurred in the past, and serves as the main verb in the sentence.
A love story serving as a source of deep insights into human relations will be written by Martha.
The finite verb “will” tells us that the action will occur in the future and serves as the main verb in the sentence.
Let’s look at a couple more sentence fragments:
Incorrect: Parents driving to the grocery store with plenty of gas in the tank.
Imagine that someone spoke those words to you.
We appear to have the verb “driving” and the subject “parents.” However, let’s try to answer our question, “When did the action take place?” In the past? In the present? In the future?
In taking a closer look at the sentence fragment, we see that the present participle “driving” does not provide enough information to answer the question “When?”
If the action were currently taking place, a complete sentence would convey that “Parents are driving to the grocery store ….” If the action had already occurred, the sentence would tell us “Parents were driving” or perhaps “Parents drove.” We don’t get such information from the participle “driving,” and thus we have a fragment, rather than a complete sentence.
Here’s another fragment:
Incorrect: With donations to the climate-change charity from numerous benefactors at the gala, including local businesses, national corporations, community organizations, and private families.
The fragment above provides a lot of information, saying so much that we may think we have a complete sentence.
So, when answering Sentence Correction questions, we must be sure to choose answers that produce versions with subjects and main verbs.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following is a complete sentence or a sentence fragment, selecting Correct for a sentence or Incorrect for a fragment. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 The perpetrator escaping through the window before the arrival of the police. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 Better known as the three-term mayor of New York City who switched parties – from Democrat to Republican to Independent – at the start of and throughout his political career and successfully campaigned to extend the city’s term limits. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 3 The weeks-long teachers’ strike, which had previously received little to no coverage beyond the small city’s local news outlets, garnered national attention when it sparked walkouts and picketing in neighboring states. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 With his support decimated after the allegations of corruption surfaced and with his campaign coffers virtually exhausted, the candidate, although not admitting guilt, announcing an end to his run for office. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 5 The study linking the drug to a twofold increase in heart attacks and other adverse cardiac events in patients aged 60 and older has yet to be replicated with those patients with a history of heart problems excluded, leading researchers to call into question the study’s broad cohorts and overall methodology. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 6 After yet another delay, the passengers, exhausted and sweaty in the summer heat, becoming more concerned. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
There is no main verb that goes with the subject “the perpetrator,” and thus this structure is a fragment. If you have trouble seeing the issue, ask yourself, “When did the action occur?” There is no way to tell.
The fragment would become a sentence if we were to change “escaping” to the finite verb “escaped.”
The perpetrator escaped through the window before the arrival of the police.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
This structure is a fragment without a subject or a main verb. It’s just a long description of someone that tells us neither who that person is nor what the person did.
With the addition of a subject and a verb, the fragment becomes a sentence.
He was better known as the three-term mayor of New York City who switched parties – from Democrat to Republican to Independent – at the start of and throughout his political career and successfully campaigned to extend the city’s term limits.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
This is a complete sentence with a subject, “teachers’ strike,” and main verb, “garnered.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
This structure is a fragment because the subject, “the candidate,” does not have a main verb. The participle “announcing” cannot on its own serve as a main verb.
By changing “announcing” to “announced,” we can make the fragment into a complete sentence.
With his support decimated after the allegations of corruption surfaced and with his campaign coffers virtually exhausted, the candidate, although not admitting guilt, announced an end to his run for office.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
This is a complete sentence with a subject, “the study,” and a main verb, “has.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
This string does not include an independent clause. There is no main verb that goes with what appear to be the sentence’s subject, “the passengers,” since “becoming” is not a finite verb.
A complete sentence is:
After yet another delay, the passengers, exhausted and sweaty in the summer heat, became more concerned.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s do some Concept Mastery:
Let’s now discuss the major types of sentences we’ll encounter in Sentence Correction questions.
summary:
Lesson Content In the simplest sense, a sentence is a collection of words that contains at least one independent clause.
Let’s continue by talking about subjects.
Most of you are familiar with the concept of the subject of a sentence.
For example:
Whales are highly intelligent animals.
The blue ball was rolling down the hill.
Doug will ask Martha to come to his house for dinner.
In the sentences above, “whales,” “ball,” and “Doug” are the subjects performing the actions.
title: 2.6 What is a Sentence

contents:
Lesson Content When talking about the structure of sentences, we divide sentences into four types:
(1) Simple Sentences
(2) Compound Sentences
(3) Complex Sentences
(4) Compound-Complex Sentences
We’ll carefully discuss each of these sentence types, beginning with the simple sentence.
summary:
Lesson Content When talking about the structure of sentences, we divide sentences into four types:
(1) Simple Sentences
(2) Compound Sentences
(3) Complex Sentences
(4) Compound-Complex Sentences
We’ll carefully discuss each of these sentence types, beginning with the simple sentence.
title: 2.7 Major Sentence Types Based on Structure

contents:
Lesson Content A simple sentence consists of a single independent clause followed by a period.
💡MUST KNOW:
A simple sentence consists of a single independent clause followed by a period.
The following are examples of simple sentences:
I do.
You will.
I was at the beach.
John and Jeff were reading books.
Spending time with animals is one of the best ways to enjoy your days.
We’ll discuss simple sentences in more detail as we progress, but for now it’s important to understand that the simple sentence is the simplest collection of words that can constitute an English sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
The simple sentence is the simplest collection of words that can constitute an English sentence.
Let’s next discuss compound sentences.
summary:
Lesson Content A simple sentence consists of a single independent clause followed by a period.
💡MUST KNOW:
A simple sentence consists of a single independent clause followed by a period.
The following are examples of simple sentences:
I do.
You will.
I was at the beach.
John and Jeff were reading books.
Spending time with animals is one of the best ways to enjoy your days.
We’ll discuss simple sentences in more detail as we progress, but for now it’s important to understand that the simple sentence is the simplest collection of words that can constitute an English sentence.
title: 2.8 The Simple Sentence

contents:
Lesson Content A compound sentence is composed of at least two independent clauses.
💡MUST KNOW:
A compound sentence is composed of at least two independent clauses.
We must connect these independent clauses in specific ways.
One way is to use what’s referred to as a coordinating conjunction.
There are seven coordinating conjunctions:
for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so
We can use the mnemonic device “FANBOYS” to help us distinguish these conjunctions from other types of transition words.
For And Nor But Or Yet So
💡MUST KNOW:
The coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS): For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So.
When independent clauses are connected by a coordinating conjunction, a comma must precede the conjunction.
Consider the following example sentences:
I ran, and I fell.
She jumped on the bed, and she screamed at the top of her lungs.
I went to the store, yet I bought nothing.
John went hiking, but Sara went to the beach.
I wrote her the letter, but I chose not to mail it.
Blaze and Molly will go to the park, or they’ll take a nap instead.
The walrus and the seal were eating lunch, and they were also taking in some sun.
Notice that, in each of these example sentences, two independent clauses are connected by a comma and a coordinating conjunction.
So, we have an important rule for creating compound sentences: a compound sentence can be created by connecting two independent clauses using a comma and a coordinating conjunction.
In fact, we can create a structural formula for the compound sentence:
Compound Sentence = [independent clause] + [,] + [coordinating conjunction] + [independent clause] + [.]
💡MUST KNOW:
Compound Sentence = [independent clause] + [,] + [coordinating conjunction] + [independent clause] + [.]
It is important to note that the comma is not optional.
At some point, the test writers will likely tempt us to accept invalid ways of forming a compound sentence.
Consider two simple independent clauses: “I ran” and “I fell.” Would the following be correct?
Incorrect: I ran I fell.
No – of course not.
💡MUST KNOW:
Linking two independent clauses with no intermediary word or punctuation is always incorrect.
Now, our ear won’t help us in this situation because we can’t hear a period or its absence.
The following are all run-on sentences:
Incorrect: I went to the gym I love to work out.
Incorrect: She was on a long flight she is tired now.
Incorrect: The dog jumped over the fence he has so much energy.
Incorrect: I went to the movies I ate a large bucket of popcorn.
What about the following? Would the following be correct?
Incorrect: I ran, I fell.
No – this sentence also has a major error.
Incorrect: I went swimming, the water felt amazing.
Incorrect: Peter runs track, he’s a great athlete.
Incorrect: Johanna loves eating mangoes, she can eat more than anyone I know.
💡MUST KNOW:
Linking two independent clauses with only a comma is always incorrect. The error is known as a “comma splice.”
What about the following sentence? Is it correct?
Incorrect: I ran and I fell.
Although this sentence includes the coordinating conjunction “and,” this sentence is also incorrectly written.
💡MUST KNOW:
Linking two independent clauses with only a coordinating conjunction is always incorrect in a GMAT Sentence Correction question.
Each of the incorrect versions below is a run-on sentence:
Incorrect: I went to the gym and I lifted weights.
Correct: I went to the gym, and I lifted weights.
Incorrect: She was on a long flight yesterday so she is taking the day off today.
Correct: She was on a long flight yesterday, so she is taking the day off today.
Incorrect: I was not hungry but I ate a large bucket of popcorn.
Correct: I was not hungry, but I ate a large bucket of popcorn.
As sentences grow more complicated (like the ones you’ll likely encounter in Sentence Correction questions), this small, but important, flaw becomes easy to miss.
Consider the following example sentence:
Incorrect: Responding to critics, the committee refused to admit any culpability in the devastation caused by cost overruns and it specifically diverted blame to the change in bureaucratic oversight.
Can you spot the error? Let’s break down the sentence:
Independent clause: “the committee refused to admit any culpability in the devastation caused by cost overruns”
Independent clause: “it specifically diverted blame to the change in bureaucratic oversight.”
The grammatical error is the lack of a comma before the coordinating conjunction “and.” This point is not a minor one.
Responding to critics, the committee refused to admit any culpability in the devastation caused by cost overruns, and it specifically diverted blame to the change in bureaucratic oversight
Sentence correction questions may also attempt to trick us into choosing as correct sentences that seem well-written but actually violate the rules we have discussed.
Incorrect: I ran, consequently, I fell.
This sentence seems ok, but it’s a comma splice.
Correct: I ran, and, consequently, I fell.
So, we can see that the absence of a comma before a coordinating conjunction forbids having an independent clause follow.
Make sure you fully understand the following structures:
The majority of voters showed their displeasure, yet _____________.
The majority of voters showed their displeasure, yet they still failed to cast dissenting votes.
The majority of voters showed their displeasure yet _____________. (This forbids the filling of the blank with an independent clause because there is no comma before the coordinating conjunction “yet.”)
The majority of voters showed their displeasure yet still failed to cast dissenting votes.
Your ear cannot help you in these contexts.
💡MUST KNOW:
The absence of a comma before a coordinating conjunction forbids having an independent clause follow because the result would be a run-on sentence.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 On the evening of April 18, 1775, Paul Revere was instructed to ride to Lexington, Massachusetts to warn Samuel Adams and John Hancock that British troops were marching to arrest them. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 "Ashes to Ashes," the No. 4 song on the UK Singles Chart in its first week of release, rising to No. 1 a week later, becoming Bowie's fastest-selling single up to that point. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 3 Steganography involves the concealment of information, sometimes within computer files, and electronic communications may include steganographic coding inside a transport layer, such as a document file, image file, program, or protocol. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 The Kray Twins were English gangsters, they were the foremost perpetrators of organized crime in the East End of London during the 1950s and 60s. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
This sentence is correct as written. It includes one independent clause with subject “Paul Revere” and main verb “was instructed,” and commas are used correctly.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
This is a sentence fragment. Though it contains two verbs, “rising” and “becoming,” these verbs are not finite verbs; they don’t tell us when the action occurred. A corrected sentence is:
“Ashes to Ashes,” the No. 4 song on the UK Singles Chart in its first week of release, rose to No. 1 a week later, becoming Bowie’s fastest-selling single up to that point.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
Despite its length, this is a complete sentence and correct as written. Notice that two independent clauses are connect by a comma and the coordinating conjunction “and.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
This is a run-on sentence because it uses only a comma to connect two independent clauses. In other words, it is a comma splice. A corrected version is:
The Kray Twins were English gangsters, and they were the foremost perpetrators of organized crime in the East End of London during the 1950s and 60s.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now consider a Sentence Correction example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 1 The career of James Marshall Hendrix, the singer, songwriter, and guitarist known to most as Jimi Hendrix, lasting only four years in mainstream American culture, but he is regarded as one of the greatest instrumentalists in Rock and Roll history. 1. [ ] (A) The career of James Marshall Hendrix, the singer, songwriter, and guitarist known to most as Jimi Hendrix, lasting only four years in mainstream American culture, but he is regarded > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) James Marshall Hendrix’s career, the singer, songwriter, and guitarist known to most as Jimi Hendrix, lasting only four years in mainstream American culture but regarded > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) James Marshall Hendrix, the singer, songwriter, and guitarist known to most as Jimi Hendrix, had a career lasting only four years in mainstream American culture, but it is regarded > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) James Marshall Hendrix’s career, the singer, songwriter, and guitarist known to most as Jimi Hendrix, lasted only four years in mainstream American culture but is regarded > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) James Marshall Hendrix, the singer, songwriter, and guitarist known to most as Jimi Hendrix, had a career that lasted only four years in mainstream American culture, but he is regarded View Solution Question Level: easy Some of the versions created via the use of the choices in this question are blatantly incorrect because they lack main verbs. Others include main verbs but do not convey meanings that make sense.
(A) The career of James Marshall Hendrix, the singer, songwriter, and guitarist known to most as Jimi Hendrix, lasting only four years in mainstream American culture, but he is regarded as one of the greatest instrumentalists in Rock and Roll history.
This version incorrectly uses a comma and the conjunction “but” to connect a long phrase to a clause.
Notice that “lasting” is not a finite verb, and thus there is no finite verb before “but.” So, everything that appears before “but” is just a long phrase.
We can see the issue more easily by simplifying the sentence:
The career of James Marshall Hendrix … lasting only four years …, but he is regarded ….
(B) James Marshall Hendrix’s career, the singer, songwriter, and guitarist known to most as Jimi Hendrix, lasting only four years in mainstream American culture but regarded as one of the greatest instrumentalists in Rock and Roll history.
Since “lasting” and “regarded” are not finite verbs, this version has no main verb.
Also, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that Hendrix’s career, rather than Hendrix himself, was a singer, songwriter, and guitarist, and that his career was regarded as one of the greatest instrumentalists.
(C) James Marshall Hendrix, the singer, songwriter, and guitarist known to most as Jimi Hendrix, had a career lasting only four years in mainstream American culture, but it is regarded as one of the greatest instrumentalists in Rock and Roll history.
This version has finite verbs “had” and “is.” However, by reading this choice along with the non-underlined portion, we can see that this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that Hendrix’s career, rather than Hendrix himself, is regarded as one of the greatest instrumentalists.
(D) James Marshall Hendrix’s career, the singer, songwriter, and guitarist known to most as Jimi Hendrix, lasted only four years in mainstream American culture but is regarded as one of the greatest instrumentalists in Rock and Roll history.
This version has finite verbs “lasted” and “is.” However, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that Hendrix’s career, rather than Hendrix himself, was a singer, songwriter, and guitarist, and that his career is regarded as one of the greatest instrumentalists.
YOUR CHOICE(E) James Marshall Hendrix, the singer, songwriter, and guitarist known to most as Jimi Hendrix, had a career that lasted only four years in mainstream American culture, but he is regarded as one of the greatest instrumentalists in Rock and Roll history.
This version correctly uses a comma and the conjunction “but” to connect two independent clauses.
The first clause has the subject “James Marshall Hendrix” and the main verb “had.” The second clause has the subject “he” and the main verb “is.”
This version is grammatically correct and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss how to use a semicolon to connect two independent clauses.
