💡MUST KNOW:
A compound sentence is composed of at least two independent clauses.
We must connect these independent clauses in specific ways.
One way is to use what’s referred to as a coordinating conjunction.
There are seven coordinating conjunctions:
for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so
We can use the mnemonic device “FANBOYS” to help us distinguish these conjunctions from other types of transition words.
title: 2.9 The Compound Sentence

contents:
Lesson Content We have just discussed that one way to connect two independent clauses is by using a comma and a coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).
Consider the following example:
IC IC
My breakfast was cold; the door had been left open.
The semicolon here acts in the same way as a comma and a coordinating conjunction would.
When a semicolon is used as a substitute for a comma and coordinating conjunction, an independent clause must be found on each side of the semicolon.
💡MUST KNOW:
When a semicolon is used as a substitute for a comma and coordinating conjunction, an independent clause must be found on each side of the semicolon.
Let’s look at a few more example sentences:
The company’s year-end financials were just released; the company earned more this year than it earned last year.
I have a boxing match tomorrow; I can’t go dancing with you tonight.
The GMAT is a game of determination; the highest scores are often earned by those willing to prepare the most thoroughly.
Notice that, in each of the example sentences above, there are independent clauses on both sides of the semicolon.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 The drop spindle is an ancient tool for spinning fiber into yarn or thread, it has been around for a very long time and has changed little since its first invention. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 The Central Park Five were falsely convicted; there was no actual evidence that tied them to the event. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 The Oregon Trail computer game debuted in Don Rawitsch's classroom on December 3, 1971, despite bugs, the game was immediately popular. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 4 Improving nutrition and reducing stress prompted changes in the behavior of genes; among them some genes believed to be tumor suppressors. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
In this case, two independent clauses and joined with a comma. So, we have a comma splice. One way to make the structure a correct sentence is to connect the two clauses with a semicolon rather than a comma. A correct version is:
The drop spindle is an ancient tool for spinning fiber into yarn or thread; it has been around for a very long time and has changed little since its first invention.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
This sentence correctly uses a semicolon to connect two independent clauses.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
This structure uses a comma to connect two independent clauses and, thus, is a comma splice. One option to fix it is to place a semicolon before the word “despite” to connect the two independent clauses.
The Oregon Trail computer game debuted in Don Rawitsch’s classroom on December 3, 1971; despite bugs, the game was immediately popular.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
A semicolon is incorrectly used to connect a clause to a phrase. While what precedes the semicolon is an independent clause, what follows the semicolon includes no main verb. The sentence could be fixed through the addition of a main verb to what follows the semicolon.
Improving nutrition and reducing stress prompted changes in the behavior of genes; among them were some genes believed to be tumor suppressors.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now turn our attention to the colon.
summary:
Lesson Content We have just discussed that one way to connect two independent clauses is by using a comma and a coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).
Consider the following example:
IC IC
My breakfast was cold; the door had been left open.
The semicolon here acts in the same way as a comma and a coordinating conjunction would.
When a semicolon is used as a substitute for a comma and coordinating conjunction, an independent clause must be found on each side of the semicolon.
title: 2.10 A Semicolon Can be Used to Connect Two Independent Clauses

contents:
Lesson Content We’ll encounter the colon from time to time in Sentence Correction questions.
Whenever we have a colon, what precedes the colon must be able to stand on its own as a complete sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
Whenever we have a colon, what precedes the colon must be able to stand on its own as a complete sentence.
For example:
The board of directors made its final decision: the CEO will be replaced immediately.
Aditya had a productive day: he completed his studying and walked the dogs.
My dad tells me the same thing every morning before school: “Don’t talk to strangers.”
Breakfast provided the guests with some lovely choices: English muffins, fresh fruit salad, and skillet potatoes.
Here is a simple rule for being happy: find gratitude in your life.
Notice that, in each of those examples, an independent clause precedes the colon.
Now, the colon may be followed by an independent clause, but it need not be; it is also grammatically correct for the colon to be followed by an example or series of examples.
For example:
I love everything about strawberries: the taste, the texture, and the smell.
Please tell us one thing: how many people will attend.
Last night they served a delicious dinner: kelp noodles with red sauce.
Students who earn top GMAT scores usually do three things: study with TTP, work hard, and persist until they succeed.
They had a great day: beaches, boating, and a balloon ride.
💡MUST KNOW:
A colon must be preceded by an independent clause. It may be followed by an independent clause, but it need not be. The colon can also be followed by an example or series of examples.
Watch out for a common mistake made with the colon.
Incorrect: My morning routine included: pushups and sit ups.
Of course, “My morning routine included” is not a complete thought and could not stand on its own as a sentence.
We could correctly say the following:
My morning routine included some exercises: pushups and sit ups.
Notice that now what comes before the colon expresses a complete thought.
This sentence is also incorrectly using the colon:
Incorrect: My favorite activity was: tumbleweed chasing.
Similarly, in this context, the verb “was” needs to be followed by either a noun or an adjective.
In general, it’s important to understand that a colon cannot be used if the sentence preceding the colon is not complete.
💡MUST KNOW:
A colon cannot be used if the sentence preceding the colon is not complete.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 In order to address the issue of violence in the school, the administration used: conflict resolution classes, hall monitors, and counseling. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 The committee defining their goals for the year: to balance the budget, to support entrepreneurship, and to address environmental issues. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 3 Sometimes survival in a challenging situation depends on one key thing: determination. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 The implications of the findings are clear: dogs are descendants of wolves, not jackals. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 Bananas contain a variety of nutrients; potassium, magnesium, vitamin B6, and vitamin C. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
What precedes the colon is not a complete thought. “The administration used” has to be followed by a noun that names what the administration used, but in this case, the nouns that name what the administration used are incorrectly separated from “used” by a colon.
A correct version would omit the colon.
In order to address the issue of violence in the school, the administration used conflict resolution classes, hall monitors, and counseling.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
What precedes the colon could not stand on its own as a sentence. Since “defining” is not a finite verb, what precedes the clause does not include a finite verb and, thus, includes no independent clause. To correct the sentence, we could change “defining” to the finite verb “defined.”
The committee defined their goals for the year: to balance the budget, to support entrepreneurship, and to address environmental issues.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
A complete sentence precedes the colon.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
A complete clause precedes the colon, and a complete clause that provides further information about “the implications of the findings” follows the colon.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
Notice that this sentence incorrectly uses a semicolon rather than a colon to connect a clause and a list. We could correct the sentence by replacing the semicolon with a colon.
Bananas contain a variety of nutrients: potassium, magnesium, vitamin B6, and vitamin C.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
So far, our sentences have been composed of independent clauses.
When a subordinate clause is included in a sentence, we call that sentence a complex sentence.
summary:
Lesson Content We’ll encounter the colon from time to time in Sentence Correction questions.
Whenever we have a colon, what precedes the colon must be able to stand on its own as a complete sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
Whenever we have a colon, what precedes the colon must be able to stand on its own as a complete sentence.
For example:
The board of directors made its final decision: the CEO will be replaced immediately.
title: 2.11 Correct Use of a Colon in a Sentence

contents:
Lesson Content A complex sentence is composed of one independent clause and at least one subordinate clause (SC).
💡MUST KNOW:
A complex sentence is composed of one independent clause and at least one subordinate clause.
The following are examples of complex sentences:
SC IC
Because Lucy loves wine, I brought her a pinot noir from Burgundy.
IC SC
I brought Lucy a pinot noir from Burgundy because she loves wine.
SC IC
Although Sebastian loved the flying dog, he loved the princess more.
Notice that each of these sentences contains at least one subordinate clause and an independent clause.
It is important to understand that a subordinate clause actually plays a specific modifying role in a sentence.
I left yesterday.
In this sentence, it should be clear that “yesterday” tells us when the action “left” took place.
I left after eating.
I left after we finished eating.
Notice that the subordinate clause in the above version is an adverb clause; it expresses when “I left.” In other words, this subordinate clause functions as an adverb by telling us when the action took place.
The natural place for an adverb to appear is somewhere after the verb.
I hit a home run on the third pitch.
I jumped up and down while you were trying to study. However, in English we have the flexibility to move things around; we just have to let the reader know when something is out of its normal place.
Consider the following sentences:
On the third pitch, I hit a home run.
I, on the third pitch, hit a home run.
Notice that a complex sentence can take three different forms.
I jostled you while you were sleeping.
While you were sleeping, I jostled you.
I, while you were sleeping, jostled you.
It’s important to understand that all three of these sentences are perfectly grammatical.
💡MUST KNOW:
When the subordinate clause is out of place in a sentence, a comma or commas are required to indicate the departure from the expected sentence arrangement.
As we have seen, when a subordinate clause precedes an independent clause, the subordinate clause should be followed by a comma.
Consider the following sentences:
SC IC
Because I love the ocean, I spent the summer in Portofino. (mandatory comma)
Notice that, when the subordinate clause comes first, the comma between the subordinate clause and the independent clause is mandatory.
However, both of the following sentences are correct:
I spent the summer in Portofino, because I love the ocean.
I spent the summer in Portofino because I love the ocean.
We see that, when a subordinate clause comes after an independent clause, the comma between the two clauses is optional.
💡MUST KNOW:
When a subordinate precedes an independent clause, the subordinate clause must be followed by a comma. When a subordinate clause comes after an independent clause, the comma between the two clauses is optional.
The test-writers can use complex sentences to create false splits.
Because I wanted to work out, I went to the gym.
I went to the gym, because I wanted to work out.
I went to the gym because I wanted to work out.
Actually, as you know from what we just learned, all of these sentences are correct! Remember when a subordinate clause begins a complex sentence, it must be followed by a comma.
Be careful to avoid the following incorrect variation:
Incorrect: Although I was quite hungry, so I chose something more nutritious.
Here, a coordinating conjunction is incorrectly joining a subordinate clause to an independent clause.
Notice that the conjunction “although” already connects the two clauses.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now discuss the compound-complex sentence.
summary:
Lesson Content A complex sentence is composed of one independent clause and at least one subordinate clause (SC).
💡MUST KNOW:
A complex sentence is composed of one independent clause and at least one subordinate clause.
The following are examples of complex sentences:
SC IC
Because Lucy loves wine, I brought her a pinot noir from Burgundy.
IC SC
I brought Lucy a pinot noir from Burgundy because she loves wine.
title: 2.12 Complex Sentences

contents:
Lesson Content A compound-complex sentence is a sentence with more than one independent clause and at least one subordinate clause.For example:
SC IC IC
Because we went to the beach, I ran in the soft sand, so I could build my endurance.
This example features one subordinate clause followed by two independent clauses, making this a compound-complex sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
A compound-complex sentence is a sentence with more than one independent clause and at least one subordinate clause.
Let’s look at a few more examples:
SC IC IC
While you were at the beach, I ran a few errands, and I bought groceries for dinner.
This example features one subordinate clause followed by two independent clauses and, thus, is a compound-complex sentence.
When we join two independent clauses within a compound-complex sentence, the same rules apply as do in a compound sentence: we must link the independent clauses either with a comma and a coordinating conjunction or with a semicolon.
SC IC IC
Although I was quite hungry, I decided to wait until I got home to eat, but all the apples had been eaten.
Note that, in this example, the opening subordinate clause is followed by a comma, which alerts the reader that the independent clause is starting.
💡MUST KNOW:
When joining two independent clauses within a compound-complex sentence, we must link the independent clauses either with a comma and a coordinating conjunction or with a semicolon.
Let’s consider another compound-complex sentence.
SC IC IC
Because Sara loves to read, she often spends her afternoons at the bookstore, or she curls up in her favorite reading chair at home.
Note that this example begins with a subordinate clause followed by a comma.
Whenever a subordinating conjunction immediately follows a coordinating conjunction, there must be at least three clauses in the sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
Whenever a subordinating conjunction immediately follows a coordinating conjunction, there must be at least three clauses in the sentence.
Consider the following example:
I wanted to go scuba diving, but because I was tired from our long run, I read a book instead.
Notice that we have a subordinating conjunction “because” immediately after a coordinating conjunction “but”.
Since the subordinate clause “because I was tired from our long run” correctly modifies the second independent clause, “I read a book instead,” it is properly placed and properly punctuated, with a comma before the coordinating conjunction and a comma at the end of the subordinate clause.
Be on the lookout for incorrectly constructed sentences.
Incorrect: I wanted to go scuba diving, but because I was tired from our long run.
This sentence incorrectly drops the third clause, which would be modified by the subordinate clause.
💡MUST KNOW:
When a subordinating conjunction follows a coordinating conjunction in a compound-complex sentence, make sure all necessary clauses appear in the sentence.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 My lab partner has been contributing less and less to our assignments and although I would prefer to avoid confrontation, I think I should talk to him before the situation worsens any further. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 Harry’s girlfriend used to spend every Friday night with him, but ever since she started her new job she has been canceling their Friday plans and working late instead. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 3 Voter registration has surged in the months leading up to the election, with previously underrepresented demographics such as younger voters achieving record numbers, yet even if this trend translates to increased turnout at the polls. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 4 Amid the recent spate of data breaches, both companies and consumers are scrambling to safeguard personal and financial information stored online, but before the latest Facebook hack affected an estimated 30 million users the public gave little consideration to the dangers of the “social login” feature. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 5 Dress codes at schools from Massachusetts to Texas were found to disproportionately impact students from certain ethnic groups, even those as young as five years old, and because students are routinely sent home for the day for dress code violations, some parents have resorted to taking legal action to ensure that their children are not unfairly excluded from classrooms and school sports teams. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
The sentence is missing a necessary comma after “assignments.” A comma is required to set off the clause that begins with “and although.” Note that the sentence correctly includes a comma after “confrontation” to set off the final clause beginning with “I think.” The correct sentence is:
My lab partner has been contributing less and less to our assignments, and although I would prefer to avoid confrontation, I think I should talk to him before the situation worsens any further.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
A comma correctly precedes “but ever since.” However, we are missing the comma that should separate the second and third clauses. The correct sentence is:
Harry’s girlfriend used to spend every Friday night with him, but ever since she started her new job, she has been canceling their Friday plans and working late instead.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
The sentence is correctly punctuated but incomplete. What may happen even if the trend translates to increased turnout? The coordinating conjunction/subordinating conjunction pair “yet even if” signals a two-part proposition, but the sentence provides only the first half of that statement. A possible correction is:
Voter registration has surged in the months leading up to the election, with previously underrepresented demographics such as younger voters achieving record numbers, yet even if this trend translates to increased turnout at the polls, voter participation still may be too low to significantly change the makeup of Congress.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
The sentence correctly places a comma before the coordinating conjunction/subordinating conjunction pair “but before,” but it is missing a closing comma to set off the clause.
The correct sentence is:
Amid the recent spate of data breaches, both companies and consumers are scrambling to safeguard personal and financial information stored online, but before the latest Facebook hack affected an estimated 30 million users, the public gave little consideration to the dangers of the “social login” feature.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
The clause beginning with the coordinating conjunction/subordinating conjunction pair “and because” is correctly set off from the rest of the sentence by a comma before “and” and a comma after “violations.”
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now turn our attention to the relative clause, which is another important type of dependent clause.
summary:
Lesson Content A compound-complex sentence is a sentence with more than one independent clause and at least one subordinate clause.For example:
SC IC IC
Because we went to the beach, I ran in the soft sand, so I could build my endurance.
This example features one subordinate clause followed by two independent clauses, making this a compound-complex sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
A compound-complex sentence is a sentence with more than one independent clause and at least one subordinate clause.
title: 2.13 Compound-Complex Sentences

contents:
Lesson Content Earlier in the chapter, we said that there are three types of dependent clauses: subordinate clauses, relative clauses, and noun clauses.
Relative clauses begin with relative pronouns.
that, which, who, whom, whose, where, when
💡MUST KNOW:
Memorize the relative pronouns:
A relative clause is a type of dependent clause, and as such, a relative clause is unable to stand alone as a sentence.
Relative clauses always appear embedded within other clauses.
💡MUST KNOW:
Relative clauses always appear embedded within other clauses. They are unable to stand alone as sentences.
Like a subordinate clause, a relative clause begins with a word that takes away the clause’s independence: a relative pronoun.
Consider the following sentence:
The baboon had an angry look about it.
Of course, the above simple sentence is composed of an independent clause containing a subject, “baboon,” a finite verb, “had,” and no word to take away the clause’s independence.
At the same time, an English sentence is like a modular couch: another section can always be added.
The baboon that grasped its baby had an angry look about it.
Having more to say about the baboon, we have added a relative clause.
Notice that the relative clause begins with a relative pronoun, “that.”
As we have discussed, every clause must have its own subject (and a finite verb, which answers the question “When did the action occur?”).
My boat, which has an electric motor, is faster than most boats with gasoline engines.
The subject of the relative clause is “which.” The finite verb is “has.”
The man who solved the problem is smiling happily.
The subject of the relative clause is “who.” The finite verb is “solved.”
The star whose supply of hydrogen is running out will soon collapse.
The subject of the relative clause is “supply of hydrogen.” The finite verb is “is.”
💡MUST KNOW:
Every relative clause must have a subject and a finite verb.
In some relative clauses, the relative pronoun is the subject of the relative clause.
When the Relative Pronoun is the Subject of the Relative Clause
Let’s again consider our example:
The baboon that grasped its baby had an angry look about it.
In this example, the relative pronoun “that,” like a personal pronoun, stands for a noun already mentioned, “the baboon.”
The relative clause “that grasped its baby” contains a finite verb in the past tense: “grasped.”
The finite verb has a subject, but the grammatical subject of the relative clause is not “baboon.” The boundaries of the relative clause are “that” and “baby.” So, the subject must be found within those boundaries.
When the relative pronoun is the subject of the relative clause, it may not be omitted from the clause.
💡MUST KNOW:
When the relative pronoun is the subject of the relative clause, it may not be omitted from the clause.
Notice how we create an ungrammatical, and illogical, structure if we remove the relative pronoun “that”:
Incorrect: The baboon grasped its baby had an angry look about it.
Baboon is the subject of the independent clause.
Let’s consider another example.
John lives on a beach that becomes populated with sea turtles between May and November.
Incorrect: John lives on a beach becomes populated with sea turtles between May and November.
Have a look at each of the following sentences in which the relative clauses are bolded.
I lost the sapphire that was given to me.
“That” is the subject of the relative clause; “was” is the finite verb
The man who lives next to me plays the guitar.
“Who” is the subject of the relative clause; “lives” is the finite verb.
Target Test Prep, which provides awesome GMAT prep, helped me to score 770.
“Which” is the subject of the relative clause; “provides” is the finite verb.
When the Relative Pronoun is Not the Subject of the Relative Clause
In other cases, the relative pronoun is not the subject of the relative clause.
When the relative pronoun is not the subject of the clause, the relative pronoun can be dropped.
Consider the following example:
The baboon that Irving was feeding had an angry look about it.
Notice that, in this case, the relative pronoun “that” is not the subject of the relative clause; “Irving” is.
The baboon Irving was feeding had an angry look about it.
The baboon that Irving was feeding had an angry look about it.
If the GMAT were to present us with two sentence versions like the above two, the difference between the relative clauses would be only something to distract us from seeing the real issues elsewhere in the sentence versions.
💡MUST KNOW:
When a relative pronoun is not the subject of the relative clause, it can be omitted from the sentence.
Which of the following two sentences is correct?
Valerio is at the coffee shop that he mentioned yesterday.
Valerio is at the coffee shop he mentioned yesterday.
Both sentences are correct as written.
Notice that both of the following sentences are correct because “that” is not the subject of the relative clause.
These are the books that the librarian has marked to be donated to the local school.
These are the books the librarian has marked to be donated to the local school.
So then the structure of a relative clause is simple:
relative clause = [relative pronoun acting as the subject] + [finite verb]
or
relative clause = [relative pronoun not acting as the subject] + [subject] + [finite verb]
💡MUST KNOW:
relative clause = [relative pronoun acting as the subject] + [finite verb]
Let’s do some Targeted Practice
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 A branch of Buddhism involves an emphasis on critical thinking is Theravada Buddhism. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 The single-engine monoplane Charles Lindbergh flew across the Atlantic Ocean is currently on display at the Smithsonian Institution’s National Air and Space Museum. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 Blaise Compaore organized the coup, resulted in a change of regime in Burkina Faso in 1987. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 4 Even though the people whom the tourists saw were not actually members of the royal family, the group enjoyed the outing immensely. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 Having repaired the beehives bears had damaged, the farmer pondered how to prevent such damage going forward. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
This sentence has two separate main verbs, “involves” and “is,” but only one subject. What’s wrong with the sentence is that the word “that” has been omitted from the relative clause “that involves an emphasis on critical thinking.” Since “that” would be the subject of the relative clause, the relative clause lacks a subject.
A corrected version of the sentence is:
A branch of Buddhism that involves an emphasis on critical thinking is Theravada Buddhism.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
Since the subject of the relative clause “Charles Lindbergh flew across the Atlantic Ocean” is “Charles Lindbergh,” the sentence is correctly constructed even though the relative pronoun “that” is omitted from the relative clause.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
We can see that something is missing from this sentence. The verb “resulted” has no subject. What’s wrong with the sentence is that the word “which” has been omitted from the relative clause “which resulted in a change of regime in Burkina Faso in 1987.” Since “which” would be the subject of the relative clause, the relative clause lacks a subject.
A corrected version of the sentence is:
Blaise Compaore organized the coup, which resulted in a change of regime in Burkina Faso in 1987.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
The relative clause “whom the tourists saw” is correctly constructed, and the sentence is otherwise grammatically correct.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
Since the subject of the relative clause “bears had damaged” is “bears,” the sentence is correctly constructed even though the relative pronoun “that” is omitted from the relative clause.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now discuss how the GMAT uses relative clauses to create sentence fragments.

Lesson Content Earlier in the chapter, we said that there are three types of dependent clauses: subordinate clauses, relative clauses, and noun clauses.
Relative clauses begin with relative pronouns.
that, which, who, whom, whose, where, when
💡MUST KNOW:
Memorize the relative pronouns:
A relative clause is a type of dependent clause, and as such, a relative clause is unable to stand alone as a sentence.
Relative clauses always appear embedded within other clauses.
title: 2.14 The Relative Clause: Another Type of Dependent Clause

contents:
Lesson Content Remember that a relative clause is a dependent clause, and therefore it cannot stand alone as a sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
A relative clause is a dependent clause, and therefore it cannot stand alone as a sentence. In other words, a relative clause must be combined with an independent clause if we are to have a sentence.
However, in Sentence Correction questions, we’ll often encounter sentence versions that are just relative clauses attached to nouns.
Consider the following well-constructed sentence.
Correct: Max Planck is considered one of the most important figures in modern physics.
Notice what happens when we add a relative pronoun immediately after the subject:
Incorrect: Max Planck, who is considered one of the most important figures in modern physics.
We now have a fragment, because there is no longer an independent clause or a main verb.
In fact, the longer and the more complex the fragment, the more apt we are to accept it as a sentence:
Incorrect: Max Planck, who is considered one of the most important figures in modern physics because of his work in the field of quantum theory, which earned him the Nobel Prize in physics in 1918.
If we’re not looking carefully, we may accept that fragment as a sentence.
One simple way to turn that fragment into a sentence is to remove the relative pronoun “who.”
Max Planck is considered one of the most important figures in modern physics because of his work in the field of quantum theory, which earned him the Nobel Prize in physics in 1918.
Now we have a sentence.
Incorrect: Max Planck, who is considered one of the most important figures in modern physics because of his work in the field of quantum theory, which earned him the Nobel Prize in physics in 1918.
Notice how easy it now is to see that that structure lacks an independent clause.
💡MUST KNOW:
A good strategy that we can use to determine whether we actually have a sentence (or just a fragment) is to cross off any relative clauses.
In the previous example, we used the relative pronoun “who,” but any of the relative pronouns can be used to create fragments.
Incorrect: The polymerase chain reaction, which allows scientists to quickly make tens of millions of copies of a specific sample of DNA and has aided in the discovery of many new diagnostic tests, vaccines, and medical therapeutics.
Notice that all we have is a noun, “polymerase chain reaction,” followed a long relative clause, “which … therapeutics.” The structure therefore lacks a main verb; there is no independent clause.
Incorrect: The polymerase chain reaction, which allows scientists to quickly make tens of millions of copies of a specific sample of DNA and has aided in the discovery of many new diagnostic tests, vaccines, and medical therapeutics.
We could fix the fragment by removing the relative pronoun (“which”):
The polymerase chain reaction allows scientists to quickly make tens of millions of copies of a specific sample of DNA and has aided in the discovery of many new diagnostic tests, vaccines, and medical therapeutics.
Let’s look at another example sentence:
Incorrect: Gettysburg, where, between July 1, 1863 and July 3, 1863, the Union army under the command of General George Meade engaged the Confederate army under the command of General Robert E.
Notice that the noun “Gettysburg” is followed by a long relative clause, “where … North.” The structure therefore lacks a main verb; there is no independent clause.
Incorrect: Gettysburg, where, between July 1, 1863 and July 3, 1863, the Union army under the command of General George Meade engaged the Confederate army under the command of General Robert E. Lee, stopping the second, and most ambitious, push by the Confederate army into the North.
We could fix the fragment by adding a finite verb:
Gettysburg is where, between July 1, 1863 and July 3, 1863, the Union army under the command of General George Meade engaged the Confederate army under the command of General Robert E.
Let’s consider one more example sentence:
Incorrect: Found in the latest model, the autopilot feature that autonomously performs almost all of the tasks that a driver would need to perform and that uses forward-looking radar and eight cameras, in addition to 360-degree sonar, which detects obstacles in an eight-meter radius around the car.
Notice that this structure lacks a main verb.
Incorrect: Found in the latest model, the autopilot feature that autonomously performs almost all of the tasks that a driver would need to perform and that uses forward-looking radar and eight cameras, in addition to 360-degree sonar, which detects obstacles in an eight-meter radius around the car.
We could fix the fragment by adding a finite verb:
Found in the latest model, the autopilot is a feature that autonomously performs almost all of the tasks that a driver would need to perform and that uses forward-looking radar and eight cameras, in addition to 360-degree sonar, which detects obstacles in an eight-meter radius around the car.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice For each of the following structures, choose “Fragment” if the structure is a fragment or “Sentence” if the structure is a sentence. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 Gary Kasparov, who began dominating the chess world at the age of 22, and who began training at the age of 10 at the chess school of Mikhail Botvinnik. 1. [x] (A) Fragment 2. [ ] (B) Sentence > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 Located in Schwangau, Germany, the Neuschwanstein Castle, which is a 19th-century romantic eclecticism palace that was commissioned by King Ludwig II in 1868. 1. [x] (A) Fragment 2. [ ] (B) Sentence > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 Guadalupe Island, an island off the Pacific Coast of Mexico, where about 366 unique great white sharks have been identified, and where the water visibility is among some of the best in the world, allowing for amazing scuba diving. 1. [x] (A) Fragment 2. [ ] (B) Sentence > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 Originally bred in Japan for hunting, the Shiba Inu, whose physical characteristics make it well-adapted for living in mountainous regions, and whose high intelligence makes it a natural problem solver, makes a great pet. 1. [ ] (A) Fragment > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Sentence ## Targeted Practice: 5 The Apache tribal chief Mangas Coloradas, who was otherwise known by the alias “Red Sleeve,” and who led a series of heroic retaliatory raids against the Mexican army in the 1830s after it took the scalps of Apache women and children. 1. [x] (A) Fragment 2. [ ] (B) Sentence > Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
Let’s cross off the relative clause:
Gary Kasparov, who began dominating the chess world at the age of 22, and who began training at the age of 10 at the chess school of Mikhail Botvinnik.
We see that the structure is composed of only a noun, “Gary Kasparov,” and a relative clause, “who … Mikhail Botvinnik.” Thus, there is no independent clause or main verb.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
Let’s cross off the relative clause:
Located in Schwangau, Germany, the Neuschwanstein Castle, which is a 19th-century romantic eclecticism palace that was commissioned by King Ludwig II in 1868.
We see that the structure lacks a main verb; there is no independent clause. Remember that a sentence’s main verb can never be located in a relative clause, because a relative clause is a dependent clause.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
Let’s cross off the relative clause:
Guadalupe Island, an island off the Pacific Coast of Mexico, where about 366 unique great white sharks have been identified, and where the water visibility is among some of the best in the world, allowing for amazing scuba diving.
We see that the structure lacks a main verb; there is no independent clause. Remember that a sentence’s main verb can never be located in a relative clause, because a relative clause is a dependent clause.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
Let’s cross off the relative clause:
Originally bred in Japan for hunting, the Shiba Inu, whose physical characteristics make it well-adapted for living in mountainous regions, and whose high intelligence makes it a natural problem solver, makes a great pet.
Without the relative clause, we have:
Originally bred in Japan for hunting, the Shiba Inu makes a great pet.
We can see that we have a complete sentence that includes an independent clause with the subject “Shiba Inu” and the main verb “makes.”
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
Let’s cross off the relative clause:
The Apache tribal chief Mangas Coloradas, who was otherwise known by the alias “Red Sleeve,” and who led a series of heroic retaliatory raids against the Mexican army in the 1830s after it took the scalps of Apache women and children.
We see that the structure is composed of only a noun phrase, “the Apache tribal chief Mangas Coloradas,” and a relative clause, “who … children.” Thus, there is no independent clause or main verb.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now turn our attention to noun clauses.
summary:
Lesson Content Remember that a relative clause is a dependent clause, and therefore it cannot stand alone as a sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
A relative clause is a dependent clause, and therefore it cannot stand alone as a sentence. In other words, a relative clause must be combined with an independent clause if we are to have a sentence.
However, in Sentence Correction questions, we’ll often encounter sentence versions that are just relative clauses attached to nouns.
title: 2.15 Relative Clauses are Often Used to Create Sentence Fragments.

contents:
Lesson Content A noun clause is a clause that plays the role of a noun.
Many noun clauses begin with the following words:
that, which, how, who, whom, where, whether, what, why
For example, in each of the following sentences the noun clauses are bolded:
Marty feels that spiritual development has no limits.
That holiday leave had been cancelled depressed the entire company.
The crowd was unhappy with what happened next.
Why she left in the middle of the night was a mystery.
Whether a vaccine will be developed is yet to be determined.
💡MUST KNOW:
Many noun clauses begin with the words “that,” “which,” “how,” “who,” “whom,” “where,” “whether,” “what,” and “why.”
Like nouns, noun clauses can perform a variety of roles in sentences.
summary:
Lesson Content A noun clause is a clause that plays the role of a noun.
Many noun clauses begin with the following words:
that, which, how, who, whom, where, whether, what, why
For example, in each of the following sentences the noun clauses are bolded:
Marty feels that spiritual development has no limits.
That holiday leave had been cancelled depressed the entire company.
The crowd was unhappy with what happened next.
title: 2.16 Noun Clauses

contents:
Lesson Content Many people believe that the subject of a sentence must be a noun.
That Napoleon’s ruse would fail in its intended objective had not occurred to the French generals.
This is a perfectly acceptable sentence.
Notice that each of the following sentences has a noun clause serving as the subject:
That she had the nerve to attend the party is shocking.
What few believed could exist was eventually created.
Whichever movie you choose is fine by me.
That I would be chosen the winner came as a surprise to all.
Whoever made this course is a real genius.
Whether we can go to Lanai is debatable.
That sales have increased this quarter is good news.
What Jeff said is confusing.
Why he had gone to Madrid was what everyone wanted to know.
Where we met is none of your business.
💡MUST KNOW:
A noun clause can fulfill the role of the subject of a sentence.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether a noun clause is the subject of each sentence. If a noun or noun phrase is the subject, select Noun. If a noun clause is the subject, select Noun clause. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now discuss noun clauses as direct objects.
summary:
Lesson Content Many people believe that the subject of a sentence must be a noun.
That Napoleon’s ruse would fail in its intended objective had not occurred to the French generals.
This is a perfectly acceptable sentence.
Notice that each of the following sentences has a noun clause serving as the subject:
That she had the nerve to attend the party is shocking.
What few believed could exist was eventually created.
title: 2.17 A Noun Clause Can Serve as the Subject of a Sentence.

contents:
Lesson Content We already know that a noun clause can serve as the subject of a sentence.
For example:
Dayo picked apples.
The noun “apples” is the direct object.
For example:
The deer ate the green grass.
The noun phrase “the green grass” is the direct object.
Direct objects don’t have to be single nouns though.
I knew why the deer was eating my flowers. I felt that it was time to go swimming. Rin Tin Tin wondered whether the grass was greener on the other side. The court mandated that deer be allowed to swim and eat green grass.
Penelope has yet to determine how green grass tastes.
Notice that, in noun clauses, a word such as “that,” “which,” or “where” that introduces the clause does not refer to a noun.
💡MUST KNOW:
In a noun clause, a word such as “that,” “which,” or “where” that introduces the clause does not relate the clause back to a noun as the relative pronouns in relative clauses do.
In noun clauses that serve as direct objects, the word “that” is often dropped:
I knew that dinner would be served soon.
I knew dinner would be served soon.
The above two sentences are equally acceptable, and in any Sentence Correction question with such a difference between sentence versions, the difference can be ignored.
💡MUST KNOW:
In noun clauses that serve as direct objects, the word “that” is often dropped.
Finally, let’s discuss a noun clause as the object of a preposition.
summary:
Lesson Content We already know that a noun clause can serve as the subject of a sentence.
For example:
Dayo picked apples.
The noun “apples” is the direct object.
For example:
The deer ate the green grass.
The noun phrase “the green grass” is the direct object.
Direct objects don’t have to be single nouns though.
I knew why the deer was eating my flowers. I felt that it was time to go swimming.
title: 2.18 A Noun Clause Can Serve as a Direct Object

contents:
Lesson Content A noun clause can also serve as the object of a preposition.
Incorrect: I was unhappy about.
Incorrect: The bill was limited by.
Those sets of words do not express complete thoughts.
For example, a preposition can be followed by a single noun:
I was unhappy about the weather.
I was unhappy about the oranges.
I was unhappy about market conditions.
The bill was limited by greed.
The bill was limited by time.
The bill was limited by money.
However, a preposition can also be followed by a noun clause.
I was unhappy about what the auditors found.
I was unhappy about how the matter was handled.
I was unhappy about what was going on in El Salvador.
The bill was limited by what the super PACs were willing to accept.
The bill was limited by how it had been written.
The bill was limited by what was available to spend.
Notice that, in each of these example sentences, we have a noun clause acting as the object of a preposition.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice For each sentence, determine whether the noun clause in the sentence is a direct object or the object of a preposition. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 People have found that engaging in even minimal exercise can be beneficial. 1. [x] (A) Direct Object 2. [ ] (B) Object of a Preposition > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 The accountant explained why accelerated depreciation was the better option. 1. [x] (A) Direct Object 2. [ ] (B) Object of a Preposition > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 Everyone was amazed by what the children had achieved. 1. [ ] (A) Direct Object > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Object of a Preposition ## Targeted Practice: 4 Though nobody says so openly, everyone knows what Hector did last summer. 1. [x] (A) Direct Object 2. [ ] (B) Object of a Preposition > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 An outline of how the events unfolded would be helpful. 1. [ ] (A) Direct Object > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Object of a Preposition ## Targeted Practice: 6 The committee asked for information on why the costs were so high. 1. [ ] (A) Direct Object > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Object of a Preposition Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
The noun clause “that engaging in even minimal exercise can be beneficial” receives the action of the verb “found.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
The noun clause “why accelerated depreciation was the better option” receives the action of the verb “explained.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
The noun clause “what the children had achieved” is the object of the preposition “by.”
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
The noun clause “what Hector did last summer” receives the action of the verb “knows.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
The noun clause “how the events unfolded” is the object of the preposition “of.”
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
The noun clause “why the costs were so high” is the object of the preposition “on.”
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss how to distinguish between a relative clause and a noun clause.
summary:
Lesson Content A noun clause can also serve as the object of a preposition.
Incorrect: I was unhappy about.
Incorrect: The bill was limited by.
Those sets of words do not express complete thoughts.
For example, a preposition can be followed by a single noun:
I was unhappy about the weather.
I was unhappy about the oranges.
I was unhappy about market conditions.
The bill was limited by greed.
The bill was limited by time.
title: 2.19 Noun Clause as Object of a Preposition

contents:
Lesson Content The proper use of relative clauses and noun clauses is important in GMAT Sentence Correction.
Since relative clauses and noun clauses look similar, when we see a clause that begins with a word such as “that,” “which,” “who,” or “where,” we need to decide whether it is a relative clause or a noun clause.
It will help to remember that a relative clause must follow the noun that the clause is modifying.
Consider the sentence you read a moment ago:
It will help to remember that a relative clause must follow the noun that the clause is modifying.
The underlined clause begins with “that” and is not following a noun.
A clause that is not following a noun cannot be a relative clause.
You should also be able to recognize the clause underlined above, which again begins with “that.” This clause is following a noun, the word “clause.” The meaning of the sentence indicates that the relative pronoun “that” stands for the word “clause” and relates the information in the clause to “clause.” Therefore, this underlined clause must be a relative clause.
Figuring out which of the two types of clause you have does not have to be complicated.
A clause that follows a noun and modifies that noun is a relative clause.
A clause that acts as a noun is a noun clause.
💡MUST KNOW:
It is important to recognize and to distinguish between relative clauses and noun clauses.
Let’s consider some more examples:
The elephants that live in the area generally avoid humans.
The clause “that live in the area” follows the noun “elephants” and modifies “elephants.” So, it is a relative clause.
Drawings of the old train station, which was located on South Street, can be seen in the lobby of Town Hall.
The clause “which was located on South Street” follows the noun “station” and modifies “station.” So, it is a relative clause.
That Shaniqua had won the election surprised nobody.
The clause “that Shaniqua had won the election” does not follow a noun and serves as the subject of the sentence.
Eddie sat there and wondered which came first, the chicken or the egg.
The clause “which came first” does not follow a noun and serves as the direct object of “wondered.” So, it must be a noun clause.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice For each of the following sentences determine whether the dependent clause in the sentence is a relative clause or a noun clause. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 The travel company said that it was impossible to predict a timeframe for a recovery. 1. [ ] (A) Relative Clause > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Noun Clause ## Targeted Practice: 2 The families of the captured paratroopers protested against what had become a very unpopular war. 1. [ ] (A) Relative Clause > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Noun Clause ## Targeted Practice: 3 Cameras that use film have certainly declined in popularity. 1. [x] (A) Relative Clause 2. [ ] (B) Noun Clause > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 The majority of back pain is the result of muscle and ligament strain or weakness, which can often be prevented through development of core strength and proper posture. 1. [x] (A) Relative Clause 2. [ ] (B) Noun Clause > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 What has been referred to as the Silver Era began in the late 1800s. 1. [ ] (A) Relative Clause > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Noun Clause Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
The dependent clause in this sentence is “that it was impossible to predict a timeframe for a recovery.” The clause follows the verb “said” and serves as the direct object of “said.” Therefore, it is a noun clause.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
The dependent clause in this sentence is “what had become a very unpopular war.” Notice that the dependent clause follows a preposition, “against,” and serves as the object of that preposition. Therefore, it must be a noun clause.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
The dependent clause in this sentence is “that use film.” The clause follows the noun “cameras” and describes “cameras.” Therefore, it is a relative clause.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
The dependent clause in this sentence is “which can often be prevented by developing core strength and proper posture.” The clause follows the noun phrase “muscle and ligament strain or weakness” and describes “muscle and ligament strain or weakness.” Therefore, it is a relative clause.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
The dependent clause in this sentence is “what has been referred to as the Silver Era.” The clause does not follow a noun and serves as the subject of the sentence. Therefore, it is a noun clause.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss sentence structure involving items in a series.
summary:
Lesson Content The proper use of relative clauses and noun clauses is important in GMAT Sentence Correction.
Since relative clauses and noun clauses look similar, when we see a clause that begins with a word such as “that,” “which,” “who,” or “where,” we need to decide whether it is a relative clause or a noun clause.
It will help to remember that a relative clause must follow the noun that the clause is modifying.
title: 2.20 Distinguishing Between Relative Clauses and Noun Clauses

contents:
Lesson Content When three or more items appear in a series, a comma and a coordinating conjunction must appear before the last item in the series.
For breakfast, I had cereal, toast, and juice.
Notice that, in this sentence, a comma and the coordinating conjunction “and” precede the final noun, “juice.” The comma just before the coordinating conjunction is referred to as the “Oxford Comma.” It must be present in such a list on the GMAT.
In each of the following sentences, notice how a comma precedes the coordinating conjunction prior to the final item in the list.
The politician bribed the directors, Tom, and Nancy.
Paul loves hot peppers, peanut butter, cocoa, and bananas.
The team loves to meditate, play ping pong, and swim.
The GMAT rewards hard work, determination, and organization.
Having an MBA helps people to earn a higher salary, become promoted more quickly, and garner respect from their colleagues.
The following sentences, therefore, are incorrect because they include lists of three items but lack the “Oxford” comma:
Incorrect: Planes have engines, wings and wheels.
Incorrect: Justine ordered avocados, beets and kale.
Incorrect: Baseball, soccer and polo are Rafi’s favorite sports.
💡MUST KNOW:
When three or more items appear in a series, a comma and a coordinating conjunction must appear before the last item in the series.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice For each of the following sentences, determine whether it is correctly or incorrectly constructed. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 The major staple crops of Nigeria are sorghum, cassava, yam, maize, rice and millet. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 Many comics involving Donald Duck, Scrooge McDuck or Donald’s children were drawn by cartoonist Carl Barks. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 3 To warm up before a concert, a pianist may: play scales, play arpeggios, or do Hanon exercises. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 4 Taking chromium supplements, reducing weight, and exercising regularly are all known to result in reductions in blood sugar levels. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 By combining rice and beans, chickpeas and sesame seeds, one can achieve amino acid balance. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
This sentence is missing the comma before the coordinating conjunction “and” that precedes the last item in the list, “millet.” A correct version is:
The major staple crops of Nigeria are cassava, yam, maize, sorghum, rice, and millet.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
This sentence is missing the comma before the coordinating conjunction “or” that precedes the last item in the list, “Donald’s children.” A correct version is:
Many comics involving Donald Duck, Scrooge McDuck, or Donald’s children were drawn by cartoonist Carl Barks.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
While the list in this sentence is correctly constructed, the sentence is incorrectly constructed, as the part of the sentence that comes before the colon could not stand on its own as a sentence. We can correct the sentence by simply removing the colon.
To warm up before a concert, a pianist may play scales, play arpeggios, or do Hanon exercises.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
The sentence correctly uses a comma and the coordinating conjunction “and” before the last item in the list, “exercising regularly,” and it is grammatically correct overall.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
We can see that the list in this sentence is incorrectly constructed because there is no comma before the coordinating conjunction “and” that appears before the last item “sesame seeds.”
At the same time, because “and” appears twice, the list appears to be composed of two items, “rice and beans” and “chickpeas and sesame seeds.” So, one way to correct the list is to replace the comma between those two items with the conjunction “or.”
By combining rice and beans or chickpeas and sesame seeds, one can achieve amino acid balance.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now discuss how we can use a semicolon to clarify lists.
summary:
Lesson Content When three or more items appear in a series, a comma and a coordinating conjunction must appear before the last item in the series.
For breakfast, I had cereal, toast, and juice.
Notice that, in this sentence, a comma and the coordinating conjunction “and” precede the final noun, “juice.” The comma just before the coordinating conjunction is referred to as the “Oxford Comma.” It must be present in such a list on the GMAT.
title: 2.21 Items in a Series

contents:
Lesson Content As we know, a semicolon can be used between two independent clauses.
Consider the following example:
When Monica is in Europe, she’ll visit London, England; Paris, France; Rome, Italy; and Vienna, Austria.
Notice that the sentence uses semicolons to more clearly separate items in a list that could be confusing if commas were used instead.
Imagine the sentence without the semicolons:
Incorrect: When Monica is in Europe, she’ll visit London, England, Paris, France, Rome, Italy, and Vienna, Austria.
Yikes! What’s going on there? Is Monica going to visit London, England or both London and England? Is she going to visit Paris, France or both Paris and France?
We can see that what the list is meant to convey is much clearer when semicolons are used to separate the items than when only commas are used.
💡MUST KNOW:
Semicolons can be used to separate items in a list when using commas would make the list confusing.
Let’s look at another example:
At the dinner will be Fidel Castro, the Prime Minister of Cuba; John F.
We can see that use of semicolons keeps the list from being confusing.
Imagine the sentence without the semicolons:
Incorrect: At the dinner will be Fidel Castro, the Prime Minister of Cuba, John F.
Again, yikes! What’s going on there?
Let’s look at one more example:
I need to purchase a dry-erase board, a notebook, and a calculator to study for math; an iPad for English; and a leather-bound journal for history.
Notice how semicolons are used to make the list clear when the list would be unclear without the semicolons:
Incorrect: I need to purchase a dry-erase board, a notebook, and a calculator to study for math, an iPad for English, and a leather-bound journal for history.
Who knows what the sentence is trying to say.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice For each sentence, determine whether the list needs semicolons for clarity or does not need semicolons. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 On the sculptor’s table were a hammer, a useful tool for shaping metal; a chisel, a tool that can be used to chip stone; and several files and rasps, which are used for emphasizing shapes and smoothing surfaces. 1. [x] (A) Needs Semicolons 2. [ ] (B) Does Not Need Semicolons > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 Once the living room; kitchen; and master bedroom were decorated, the house felt like home. 1. [ ] (A) Needs Semicolons > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Does Not Need Semicolons ## Targeted Practice: 3 The spider monkey, a New World monkey; the vervet monkey, an Old World monkey; and the capuchin monkey, another New World monkey, have somewhat similar diets. 1. [x] (A) Needs Semicolons 2. [ ] (B) Does Not Need Semicolons > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 In addition to improving their diets, the subjects also engaged in walking, working out, or cycling, alone or in groups; doing an hour of daily stress-reducing yoga-type stretching, breathing, and meditation; and group support sessions. 1. [x] (A) Needs Semicolons 2. [ ] (B) Does Not Need Semicolons > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 The panelists, among them an opera singer; a ballet dancer; two orchestral wind instrument players; and a jazz drummer, all felt that the platform would help them achieve their career aims. 1. [ ] (A) Needs semicolons > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Does not need semicolons Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
We can see that, if this sentence did not use semicolons in the list, it would be unclear that “a hammer, a useful tool for shaping metal” is one element, rather than two elements, “a hammer” and “useful tool for shaping metal. Similarly, it would be unclear that “a chisel, a tool that can be used to chip stone” is one element, rather than two elements, “a chisel” and “a tool that can be used to chip stone.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
That “living room,” “kitchen,” and “master bedroom” are the three elements of the list would be clear if the sentence used commas rather than semicolons.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
We can see that, if this sentence did not use semicolons in the list, it would be somewhat unclear that “the spider monkey, a New World monkey” is one element, rather than two elements, “the spider monkey” and “a New World monkey. Similarly, it would be somewhat unclear that “the vervet monkey, an Old World monkey” is one element, rather than two elements.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
If this sentence did not use semicolons between list items, it would be difficult to determine where one item ends and the next one begins.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
That “an opera singer,” “a ballet dancer,” “two orchestral wind instrument players,” and “a jazz drummer” are the four elements of the list would be clear if the sentence used commas rather than semicolons.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
summary:
Lesson Content As we know, a semicolon can be used between two independent clauses.
Consider the following example:
When Monica is in Europe, she’ll visit London, England; Paris, France; Rome, Italy; and Vienna, Austria.
Notice that the sentence uses semicolons to more clearly separate items in a list that could be confusing if commas were used instead.
Imagine the sentence without the semicolons:
Incorrect: When Monica is in Europe, she’ll visit London, England, Paris, France, Rome, Italy, and Vienna, Austria.
title: 2.22 Using Semicolons to Clarify Lists

contents:
Lesson Content At this point, we already know that any English sentence must contain at least one subject and at least one finite verb serving as the main verb of the sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
Any English sentence must contain at least one subject and at least one finite verb serving as the main verb of the sentence.
We also know that finite verbs are special in the sense that they allow us to determine when in time a subject performs an action.
💡MUST KNOW:
Finite verbs are special in the sense that they allow us to determine when in time a subject performs an action.
In addition, we know that the finite verb found in an independent clause is referred to as a main verb.
💡MUST KNOW:
The finite verb found in an independent clause is referred to as a main verb.
Finally, we know that one of the tricks of the GMAT is to present us with collections of words lacking either a subject or a main verb to see whether we’ll accept these collections of words as sentences.
We’re going to begin this chapter with a discussion of how a subject and its finite verb in any clause must agree in number.
summary:
Lesson Content At this point, we already know that any English sentence must contain at least one subject and at least one finite verb serving as the main verb of the sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
Any English sentence must contain at least one subject and at least one finite verb serving as the main verb of the sentence.
We also know that finite verbs are special in the sense that they allow us to determine when in time a subject performs an action.
title: 3.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content In addition to the rule that each sentence must have at least one subject and at least one main verb, when a sentence has more than one clause, each of the clauses must contain a subject that agrees in number with the finite verb in that clause.
In other words, if the subject of a clause is singular, the subject’s finite verb in that clause must be singular, and if the subject of a clause is plural, the subject’s finite verb in that clause must be plural.
There are no exceptions to this rule.
💡MUST KNOW:
In any clause in a sentence, the subject of that clause must agree in number with that subject’s finite verb in that clause.
Why is this information important? Because sentences can be, and almost always are, composed of multiple clauses.
Because she has a lot of energy, Estella loves walking with her owner, but she prefers to walk off-leash.
Notice that in each clause, the subject and subject’s finite verb agree in number.
Clause 1: Because she has a lot of energy
Clause 2: Estella loves walking with her owner
Clause 3: she prefers to walk off-leash
So, in a correct version of a sentence in a Sentence Correction question, if the subject of a clause is singular, the subject’s finite verb is singular, and, if the subject is plural, the subject’s finite verb is plural.
💡MUST KNOW:
In a correct version of a sentence in a Sentence Correction question, if the subject of a clause is singular, the subject’s finite verb is singular, and, if the subject is plural, the subject’s finite verb is plural.
For example:
Since predators are prevalent in the rain forest, birds fly north to have young.
In this example, there is an independent clause in which the subject, “birds,” and the main verb, “fly,” agree.
For example:
Although the current president was unpopular among younger voters, he was reelected.
In this example, there is an independent clause in which the subject, “he,” and the main verb, “was,” agree.
For example:
Molly and Blaze were swimming at the beach, and Estella and Frisky were sleeping on their beds.
In this sentence we have two independent clauses.
So, to make sure that we are choosing the correct answer to a Sentence Correction question, we have to notice whether all subjects and verbs in the version created via using that choice agree.
Let’s now discuss how to find the subject and finite verb in a clause.
summary:
Lesson Content In addition to the rule that each sentence must have at least one subject and at least one main verb, when a sentence has more than one clause, each of the clauses must contain a subject that agrees in number with the finite verb in that clause.
In other words, if the subject of a clause is singular, the subject’s finite verb in that clause must be singular, and if the subject of a clause is plural, the subject’s finite verb in that clause must be plural.
title: 3.2 A Subject and Its Finite Verb in Any Clause Must Agree in Number

contents:
Lesson Content In order to ensure that the subject and finite verb of a clause agree, we must first identify these two parts of the clause.
💡MUST KNOW:
The subject of a clause is the noun that is doing or being what the main verb expresses.
In order to determine which noun is doing or being what the finite verb expresses, and is thus the subject, first identify the action word in the clause, which is the main verb.
💡MUST KNOW:
To determine which noun is doing or being what the finite verb expresses, and is thus the subject, first identify the action word in the clause, which is the main verb.
Let’s put our strategy to use to identify the subject in the following underlined clause:
If the boy wearing the green shirt wins the spelling bee, his parents will be excited.
The action in the underlined clause is that someone or something wins.
For regular verbs, the rule is that we place an s at the end of a singular verb, and we drop the s at the end of a plural verb.
Since the subject of the clause, “boy,” is singular, the verb must also be singular, meaning it must end in s.
Let’s look at what happens when we make the subject plural:
If the boys wearing the green shirts win the spelling bee, their parents will be excited.
Now, the subject of the clause is “boys.” Since the subject is plural, it must take a plural verb.
💡MUST KNOW:
For regular verbs, the rule is that we place an s at the end of a singular verb, and we drop the s at the end of a plural verb.
Here’s an example in which the subject of the clause is the noun that is being, rather than doing, what the main verb expresses:
As the wind picked up, the sailboats in the harbor on the south side of the island were rocked by waves.
The main verb of the underlined independent clause is “were rocked.” To find the subject of the clause, we need to find the noun that names what was rocked, which is “sailboats.”
Remember, for a sentence to be correct, the subject and verb of each clause found in the sentence must agree.
💡MUST KNOW:
For a sentence to be correct, the subject and verb of each clause found in the sentence must agree.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the underlined clauses is correctly written. Pay special attention to the subject performing the action and the finite verb, which answers the question “When did the action occur?” --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions In many sentences and clauses, subjects and their verbs are very close together.
Incorrect: The kindergarten teacher had the children gather snowflakes to demonstrate that, like people, no two snowflakes is similar enough to justify labeling them exact replicas.
Notice that the noun “snowflakes” is plural, but the finite verb “is” is singular.
The kindergarten teacher had the children gather snowflakes to demonstrate that, like people, no two snowflakes are similar enough to justify labeling them exact replicas.
However, although fundamentally this concept of subject-verb agreement is simple, there are many sentence constructions that can appear in Sentence Correction questions that make noticing subject-verb agreement issues more challenging.
Let’s begin discussing these ways by looking at sentences in which prepositional phrases are placed between subjects and their verbs.
summary:
Lesson Content In order to ensure that the subject and finite verb of a clause agree, we must first identify these two parts of the clause.
💡MUST KNOW:
The subject of a clause is the noun that is doing or being what the main verb expresses.
In order to determine which noun is doing or being what the finite verb expresses, and is thus the subject, first identify the action word in the clause, which is the main verb.
title: 3.3 Identifying the Subject and Finite Verb in a Clause

contents:
Lesson Content Our ears have been trained to pair verbs with the nouns immediately preceding them, so we may make a mistake when a prepositional phrase separates a subject and its verb.
💡MUST KNOW:
Our ears have been trained to pair verbs with the nouns immediately preceding them, so we may make a mistake when a prepositional phrase separates a subject and its verb.
As a quick refresher, remember that a prepositional phrase is a type of modifying phrase that begins with a preposition, such as “by,” “for,” or “in.”
The hippopotamus is swimming in the lake.
The preposition “in” begins the prepositional phrase “in the lake,” which tells us where the hippo is swimming.
The president is eating lunch with the vice president.
The preposition “with” begins the prepositional phrase “with the vice president,” which tells us with whom the president is eating lunch.
In December, I always spend time walking along the river bank. The preposition “in” begins the prepositional phrase “in December,” which tells us when the action in the sentence occurs.
During the summer months, I partake in the sports of volleyball, paddle boarding, and surfing. The preposition “during” begins the prepositional phrase “during the summer months,” which tells us when the action in the sentence occurs.
The bear was running to the woods, where his favorite blueberry bushes are located.
The preposition “to” begins the prepositional phrase “to the woods,” which tells us to where the bear was running.
Ok – back to subject-verb agreement.
The dispute about whether to get rid of the old books (has/have) the family in an uproar.
Notice the prepositional phrase “about whether to get rid of the old books.”
In this sentence, the noun that directly precedes the verb (has/have) is the plural “books.” Since our ears have been trained to pair verbs with the nouns immediately preceding them, it may sound correct to say:
Incorrect: The dispute about whether to get rid of the old books have the family in an uproar.
However, the books are not performing the action in this sentence; what is causing the uproar is “the dispute.” If we ignore the prepositional phrase that separates the subject from the finite verb, we can see that there is a subject-verb agreement problem when we use the plural verb “have”:
Incorrect: The dispute about whether to get rid of the old books have the family in an uproar.
The singular subject “dispute” does not agree with the plural finite verb “have.” In order for the subject and verb to agree, we must use the singular verb “has,” as demonstrated below:
The dispute about whether to get rid of the old books has the family in an uproar.
Although we’ll see a few important exceptions shortly, a very good general strategy for checking for subject-verb agreement is to ignore a prepositional phrase found between a subject and its finite verb.
💡MUST KNOW:
In checking for subject-verb agreement, a very good general strategy is to ignore a prepositional phrase that comes between a subject and its finite verb. We’ll discuss a few important exceptions a bit later in the chapter.
Let’s look at another example sentence:
The once beautiful house with the black shutters and the grey, sloping roof made of marble and numerous composites (has/have) been neglected for some time, but the right buyer could return the house to its previous splendor.
Because a list of nouns ending with the plural noun “composites” immediately precedes the finite verb (has/have), our ear may tell us that the correct sentence is:
Incorrect: The once beautiful house with the black shutters and the grey, sloping roof made of marble and numerous composites have been neglected for some time, but the right buyer could return the house to its previous splendor.
However, the intention of the sentence is not to say that shutters and composites have been neglected.
Incorrect: The once beautiful house with the black shutters and the grey, sloping roof made of marble and numerous composites have been neglected for some time, but the right buyer could return the house to its previous splendor.
To be grammatically correct, the sentence must read:
The once beautiful house with the black shutters and the grey, sloping roof made of marble and numerous composites has been neglected for some time, but the right buyer could return the house to its previous splendor.
Students often ask, “What if the subject of a clause is found within a prepositional phrase and we cross off that prepositional phrase?” The good news is that, with the exception of some special situations that we’ll discuss later on, the subject of a clause will not be found in a prepositional phrase.
💡MUST KNOW:
