YOUR CHOICE(A) Used for mining copper ores located at shallow depths is open pit mining, which is less expensive than underground mining.
This version correctly pairs the singular verb “is … used” with the singular subject “open pit mining.”
The sentence contains no grammatical errors and effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(B) Used for mining copper ores located at shallow depths are open pit mining, this is less expensive than underground mining.
This version incorrectly pairs the plural verb “are … used” with the singular subject “open pit mining.”
Notice that the sentence is inverted: “Used for mining copper ores located at shallow depths” describes the subject “open pit mining,” which appears after the main verb rather than before the main verb.
Thus, we need the main verb to agree, not with the plural noun “depths,” but with the singular “open pit mining,” which follows the verb.
Notice also that this version connects two clauses, “used … are opening pit mining” and “this is less expensive,” using only a comma. So, this version is an improperly constructed comma splice.
(C) Used for mining copper ores located at shallow depths, open pit mining, which is less expensive than underground mining.
This version does not contain a main verb and, thus, does not contain an independent clause. Therefore, this version is not a complete sentence.
Notice that, in this version, “used” in “Used for mining copper located at shallow depths” is not a finite verb. Rather the phrase “used for mining copper …” describes “open pit mining,” as we can more easily see by considering this simplified version:
“Used for mining …, open pit mining ….”
Additionally, “is” in “which is less expensive …” is part of a relative clause describing “open pit mining,” and thus “is” is not a main verb.
(D) Used for mining copper ores located at shallow depths are open pit mining, which is less expensive than underground mining.
This version incorrectly pairs the plural verb “are” with the singular subject “open pit mining.”
Notice that the sentence is inverted: “Used for mining copper ores located at shallow depths” describes the subject “open pit mining,” which appears after the main verb rather than before the main verb.
Thus, we need the main verb to agree, not with the plural noun “depths,” which immediately precedes the main verb, but with the singular “open pit mining,” which follows the verb.
(E) Used for mining copper ores located at shallow depths, open pit mining that is less expensive than underground mining.
This version does not contain a main verb and, thus, does not contain an independent clause. Therefore, this version is not a complete sentence.
Notice that, in this version, “used” in “Used for mining copper located at shallow depths” is not a finite verb. Rather the phrase “used for mining copper …” describes “open pit mining,” as we can more easily see by considering this simplified version:
“Used for mining …, open pit mining ….”
Additionally, “is” in “that is less expensive …” is part of a relative clause describing “open pit mining,” and thus “is” is not a main verb.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 10 So dangerous for ships navigating the waters off the Outer Banks of North Carolina, the area that became known as the Graveyard of the Atlantic. 1. [ ] (A) navigating the waters off the Outer Banks of North Carolina, the area that > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) was navigating the waters off the Outer Banks of North Carolina that the area 3. [ ] (C) were navigating the waters off the Outer Banks of North Carolina, and so, the area > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) that were navigating the waters off the Outer Banks of North Carolina that the area > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) that navigate the waters off the Outer Banks of North Carolina, an area that > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard Correctly answering this question takes noticing that the correct version is an inverted sentence and that the subject of the sentence is the singular “navigating.”
Since the subject is the singular “navigating, the main verb must also be singular.
(A) So dangerous for ships navigating the waters off the Outer Banks of North Carolina, the area that became known as the Graveyard of the Atlantic.
This version has no main verb. “Navigating” is not a finite verb, and “became” appears within the relative clause “that became known ….” So, this version is not a complete sentence.
YOUR CHOICE(B) So dangerous for ships was navigating the waters off the Outer Banks of North Carolina that the area became known as the Graveyard of the Atlantic.
In this version, we have the finite verb “was.”
The subject could not be “so dangerous for ships,” because it does not make sense to say that “so dangerous for ships” was navigating. In other words, “so dangerous for ships” would not navigate waters.
There is another possible subject for “was,” however. That possible subject is “navigating the waters,” which comes after, rather than before, “was.”
With “navigating the waters” as the subject, the sentence conveys that navigating the waters off the Outer Banks was so dangerous for ships that the area became known as the Graveyard of the Atlantic. So, the use of this choice results in the creation of a well-constructed inverted sentence, in which “navigating the waters” is the subject and “was” is the main verb.
(C) So dangerous for ships were navigating the waters off the Outer Banks of North Carolina, and so, the area became known as the Graveyard of the Atlantic.
The use of “were” may seem to be correct, because “were” follows “ships.” However, as discussed in the explanation for choice (B), the verb does not go with “ships” but rather with the singular “navigating.” So, we need the singular “was” rather than the plural “were.”
Also, this version is not logical. With “so dangerous” we need “that,” as in “navigating was so dangerous that …,” but this version combines “so dangerous” with “and so,” to create the illogical “So dangerous were navigating the waters …, and so, the area became known as the graveyard.”
(D) So dangerous for ships that were navigating the waters off the Outer Banks of North Carolina that the area became known as the Graveyard of the Atlantic.
This version has no subject and no main verb. In other words, this version does not say that something was so challenging and dangerous that it became known as the Graveyard of the Atlantic. It includes only a phrase that begins with “so challenging and dangerous that …” without including a subject and a main verb.
The only finite verbs included in this version are “were” and “became.” Since “were” appears within the relative clause, “that were navigating the waters …,” and since “became” appears within the subordinate clause “that the area became known,” neither “were” nor “became” can be the main verb of a sentence.
(E) So dangerous for ships that navigate the waters off the Outer Banks of North Carolina, an area that became known as the Graveyard of the Atlantic.
This version is merely a long phrase that begins with “so dangerous for ships that.” It has neither a subject nor a main verb.
The only finite verbs included in this version are “navigate” and “became.” Since “navigate” appears within the relative clause, “that navigate the waters …,” and since “became” appears within the relative clause “that became known,” neither “navigate” nor “became” can be the main verb of a sentence.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss collective nouns.
summary:
Lesson Content Leave it to the GMAT test-makers to turn sentences inside out.
💡MUST KNOW:
In an inverted sentence, the main verb precedes the subject.
Let’s look at some examples of inverted sentences.
The first version of the sentence below is the version that most of us would write; we tend to place the subject before its finite verb.
Coyotes,medium-sized, dog-like animals, are found in many suburban areas.
Found in many suburban areas are coyotes, medium-sized, dog-like animals.
title: 3.10 Inverted Sentences

contents:
Lesson Content Collective nouns are nouns that name groups of people or things.
💡MUST KNOW:
Collective nouns are nouns that refer to groups of people or things. When collective nouns appear as subjects on GMAT Sentence Correction questions, they are almost always considered singular.
Consider the following sentence:
The team is at the lake this weekend, for a workshop on conflict resolution skills.
Notice that the collective noun “team” is singular since in this context it refers to a single collection of people.
Thus, we need the singular verb “is.”
It would be incorrect to use the plural verb “are.”
Incorrect: The team are at the lake this weekend, for a workshop on conflict resolution skills.
Again, the correct verb is “is” because nowhere in the sentence is there evidence that the sentence is referring to any individual members of the team.
Sometimes, however, a collective noun will require a plural verb, although this situation is less likely.
Notice the difference between the above example sentence and the following sentence:
After the conflict resolution workshop had ended, the team were practicing speaking articulately to each other and using emotional intelligence to diffuse problems among members of the team.
Notice that the above sentence is about the individual members of the team.
Only individual people could be “practicing speaking articulately to each other and using emotional intelligence to diffuse problems among members of the team.” The team itself as a single entity would not have been “practicing speaking articulately to each other.” So, in this case, “the team” refers to a set of individuals and is considered a plural subject.
Let’s consider some more examples:
The crew sometimes argue among themselves over who should handle each of the tasks.
The crew is scheduled to arrive in the afternoon.
In each of the sentences above, the subject is the same, “crew.” The context indicates whether to use a singular or plural verb.
In the case of the first sentence, it is clear that a crew would not argue with itself.
In the second sentence, the “crew” acts as a single unit, as the entire crew is scheduled to arrive in the afternoon.
💡MUST KNOW:
When the context of a sentence makes it clear that the members of a group are not acting as a single unit but rather as individuals, a plural verb is required.
Let’s look at a couple of additional examples:
The board needs a quorum before it holds a vote on amendments to the bylaws.
The panel always starts off with an orderly discussion but quickly devolves into shouting and name-calling.
In the first example, the sentence is focused on the collective requirements and actions of the board.
Similarly, in the second example, we are concerned not with the individual statements of members of the panel, but with the conduct of the panel as a whole.
Collective Nouns Followed by Prepositional Phrases
Another way in which collective nouns can appear is followed by prepositional phrases.
pack of wolves
group of protesters
family of bears
Subject-verb agreement in sentence with structures of this type works similarly to subject-verb agreement in sentences with collective nouns alone.
If the focus of the sentence is on the group as a whole rather than on the members of the group, the verb should be singular, as it is in the following example:
The flock of geese flying overhead was the largest that I have ever seen.
In the above example, the focus of the sentence is on the size of the entire flock of geese.
Here’s another.
The crowd of people was dispersed by the manager of the hotel.
In the example above, the manager did not disperse individuals.
💡MUST KNOW:
When the context of a sentence containing a subject made up of a collective noun followed by a prepositional phrase makes it clear that the group is acting as a single unit, a singular verb is required.
On the other hand, if the focus of the sentence is on the individual members within the group, then the verb should be plural.
A group of dogs were chasing each other in the park earlier.
In the above example, it is not the group that was chasing but, rather, the dogs that were chasing each other.
Here’s another example in which a plural verb is required.
Only a couple of the experts are aware that they have been chosen to restore the artwork.
In the sentence above, it is not the couple that is aware but rather the experts who are aware.
💡MUST KNOW:
When the context of a sentence containing a collective noun followed by a prepositional phrase makes clear that the members of the group are not acting as a single unit but rather as individuals, a plural verb is required.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice For each of the following sentences, choose the correct verb. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now try a Sentence Correction example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 11 As the divers in the cage were lowering into the water, a horde of sharks that were coming at them from all directions, some eyeing the cage from a distance, others swimming right up to the cage and poking at it with their noses. 1. [ ] (A) were lowering into the water, a horde of sharks that were > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) lowered into the water with a horde of sharks that was > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) were lowered into the water, a horde of sharks were 4. [ ] (D) was lowering into the water with a horde of sharks > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) was lowered into the water, a horde of sharks was > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium This sentence contains two noun + prepositional phrase structures: “divers in the cage” and “horde of sharks.”
The main verb of the first clause of the sentence has to agree with the plural “divers,” as “cage” is not the subject of the clause. “Divers” is the subject.
In the correct version of the sentence, the version created via the use of choice (C), “horde of sharks” is the subject of the main clause. The collective noun, “horde,” could be considered singular or considered plural depending on the context. In this case, “horde” must be considered plural, since individual sharks, rather than the horde as a unit, would be “coming at them from all directions, some eyeing the cage from a distance, others swimming right up to the cage and poking at it with their noses.”
(A) As the divers in the cage were lowering into the water, a horde of sharks that were coming at them from all directions, some eyeing the cage from a distance, others swimming right up to the cage and poking at it with their noses.
In this version, the plural “were” is correctly used with the plural subject “divers” and “that were” is correctly used with “sharks.”
However, this version has no independent clause. It starts with a dependent clause, “as the divers …,” and then has a second part that has only a subject, “horde of sharks” and no main verb. Notice that everything that follows “horde of sharks” is a long relative clause, “that were coming ….” The main verb of a sentence cannot be contained in a relative clause.
Also, the expression “the divers … were lowering into the water” does not really make sense. “Lowering” is an action that someone has to perform on something, but the sentence does not say that the divers lowered something. It’s says only “the divers … were lowering.” What would be correct is “the divers … were lowered into the water,” “the divers … were lowering themselves into the water,” or “the divers … were descending into the water.”
(B) As the divers in the cage lowered into the water with a horde of sharks that was coming at them from all directions, some eyeing the cage from a distance, others swimming right up to the cage and poking at it with their noses.
This version contains only a dependent clause, “As the divers in the cage lowered into the water,” and a very long prepositional phrase that begins with “with” and continues to the end of this version. So, this version has no independent clause and, thus, is not a complete sentence.
Also, the expression “the divers … lowered into the water” does not really make sense. “Lowered” is an action that someone has to perform on something, but the sentence does not say that the divers lowered something. It’s says only “the divers … lowered.” What would be correct is “the divers … were lowered into the water,” “the divers … lowered themselves into the water,” or “the divers … descended into the water.”
Also, since the sentence conveys that the sharks were coming from all directions and acting in various ways, “a horde of sharks that was” is incorrect. The point is not that the horde was coming from all directions. The sharks were coming from all directions. So, the sentence is focused on the individual sharks within the horde, rather than on the horde as a unit. Thus, “horde of sharks” is considered plural, and “that,” which has nothing singular to refer to, must also be considered plural. So, rather than use “was,” the sentence has to use the plural “were,” as in “a horde of sharks that were coming at them from all directions.”
YOUR CHOICE(C) As the divers in the cage were lowered into the water, a horde of sharks were coming at them from all directions, some eyeing the cage from a distance, others swimming right up to the cage and poking at it with their noses.
In the first clause, the plural “divers” and the plural “were” agree.
Also, the sentence says that the sharks were coming from all directions and acting in various ways. So, the point is not that the horde was coming from all directions. The sharks were coming from all directions. So, the sentence is focused on the individual sharks within the horde, rather than on the horde as a unit. Thus, “horde of sharks” is considered a plural subject, and it makes sense to use the plural “were,” in “a horde of sharks were coming at them from all directions.”
(D) As the divers in the cage was lowering into the water with a horde of sharks coming at them from all directions, some eyeing the cage from a distance, others swimming right up to the cage and poking at it with their noses.
In this version, the singular verb “was” does not agree with the plural subject “divers.” “In the cage” is a prepositional phrase providing information about the divers, and “cage” is not the subject of the sentence.
Also, this version has no independent clause. It is made up of only the dependent clause “As the divers in the cage was lowering …” and a very long prepositional phrase that begins with “with” and continues to the end of this version.
(E) As the divers in the cage was lowered into the water, a horde of sharks was coming at them from all directions, some eyeing the cage from a distance, others swimming right up to the cage and poking at it with their noses.
In the first clause of this version, “was” does not agree with the plural subject, “divers.”
Also, since the sentence says that the sharks were coming from all directions and acting in various ways, “a horde of sharks was” is incorrect. The point is not that the horde was coming from all directions. The sharks as individuals were coming from all directions. So, in this case, the collective noun “horde” is considered a plural subject and, thus, requires the plural “were,” as in “a horde of sharks were coming at them from all directions.”
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss the collective nouns “majority” and “minority”.
summary:
Lesson Content Collective nouns are nouns that name groups of people or things.
💡MUST KNOW:
Collective nouns are nouns that refer to groups of people or things. When collective nouns appear as subjects on GMAT Sentence Correction questions, they are almost always considered singular.
Consider the following sentence:
The team is at the lake this weekend, for a workshop on conflict resolution skills.
Notice that the collective noun “team” is singular since in this context it refers to a single collection of people.
title: 3.11 Collective Nouns

contents:
Lesson Content Like other collective nouns that we have discussed, the collective nouns “majority” and “minority” can be considered singular or plural depending on the contexts in which they appear.
In the following sentences, “majority” and “minority” are plural:
A majority of the students are already applying for jobs.
In today’s world, in which most people have some knowledge of geography, only a small minority believe Earth to be flat.
Logic dictates that the first sentence is conveying that it is the individual students who are applying for jobs, not the majority itself that is applying for jobs.
Incorrect: A majority of the students is already applying for jobs.
So, in this case, we need the plural “are” to go with “A majority of the students.”
Similarly, in the second sentence, the minority itself is not what believes Earth to be flat.
In the following sentences, “majority” and “minority” are singular:
A two-thirds majority of the ministers was required for the passage of a new amendment.
A large enough minority is sufficient for blocking passage of a new amendment.
In the two examples above, the sentences are not focused on the ministers but rather on the size of the majority as a whole and size of the minority as a whole.
💡MUST KNOW:
Like other collective nouns that we have discussed, the collective nouns “majority” and “minority” can be considered singular or plural depending on the contexts in which they appear. Also, like other collective nouns, “majority,” and “minority” can be used alone or combined with prepositional phrases.
Now, let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice For each of the following sentences, choose the correct verb. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Now, let’s apply our “majority” and “minority” subject-verb agreement skills to finding the best answer to the following Sentence Correction question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 12 Even if it is only a small minority of people who has extreme political beliefs, when they are vocal, they can gain significant political power. 1. [ ] (A) if it is only a small minority of people who has extreme political beliefs, when > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) if there is only a small minority of people who has extreme political beliefs, if > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) if only a small minority of people have extreme political beliefs, when 4. [ ] (D) when being only a small minority of people that have extreme political beliefs, if > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) when only a small minority of people has extreme political beliefs, when > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) Even if it is only a small minority of people who has extreme political beliefs, when they are vocal, they can gain significant political power.
Logically, it is the people who make up the “small minority,” not the minority itself, who have “beliefs.” Thus, we don’t need the singular verb “has” to pair with the singular “minority.” Rather, we need the plural “have” to pair with the plural “people.”
Notice also that the phrasing “Even if it is only a small minority of people …” is unnecessarily wordy, and thus the meaning conveyed is somewhat muddled. A clearer, more straightforward phrasing would be “Even if only a small minority of people ….” (B) Even if there is only a small minority of people who has extreme political beliefs, if they are vocal, they can gain significant political power.
Logically, it is the people who make up the “small minority,” not the minority itself, who have “beliefs.” Thus, we don’t need the singular verb “has” to pair with the singular “minority.” Rather, we need the plural “have” to pair with the plural “people.”
YOUR CHOICE(C) Even if only a small minority of people have extreme political beliefs, when they are vocal, they can gain significant political power.
This choice correctly pairs the plural verb “have” with the plural noun “people,” logically indicating that the people themselves, not the “minority,” have “beliefs.”
This choice also does not include any of the unnecessary wordiness or unclear meanings we see in some other choices. The sentence meaning is clear and logical, and the sentence contains no grammatical errors.
(D) Even when being only a small minority of people that have extreme political beliefs, if they are vocal, they can gain significant political power.
This choice correctly pairs the plural verb “have” with the plural noun “people,” logically indicating that the people themselves, not the “minority,” have “beliefs.” However, it is never correct to refer to people using the pronoun “that.” We need the pronoun “who” to refer to “people.”
Additionally, the phrasing “when being only a small minority of people” conveys an unclear and illogical meaning, seeming to indicate that people are actively making some kind of personal choice to either be or not be a small minority at certain times. Clearly, such a meaning is nonsensical. (E) Even when only a small minority of people has extreme political beliefs, when they are vocal, they can gain significant political power.
This choice may be attractive because it is so succinct and because, whereas choice (C) — the actually correct choice — uses “if” and “when,” this choice uses “when” in both the first and second half of the sentence. However, in reality, there is nothing illogical or incorrect about using “if” and “when,” as opposed to repeating “when,” in this context. More importantly, this choice incorrectly uses the singular verb “has,” illogically indicating that the “minority,” not the “people,” is what has “beliefs.” Of course, the people themselves have “beliefs,” and so we need the plural verb “have” to pair with the plural noun “people.”
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 13 Realizing that the undecided voters, becoming a smaller group as Election Day approached, with a majority of the votes was required for winning, the candidates campaigned intensely. 1. [ ] (A) that the undecided voters, becoming a smaller group as Election Day approached, with a majority of the votes was required > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) that the group of undecided voters were becoming smaller as Election Day approached and that a majority of the votes were required > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) that the group of undecided voters was becoming smaller as Election Day approached and that a majority of the votes was required 4. [ ] (D) that, as a group, the undecided voters were becoming smaller as Election Day approached and that the majority of the votes were a requirement > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) that the undecided voters was becoming a smaller group as Election Day approached and that a majority of the votes were required > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium The sentence mentions two collective entities, “the group of undecided voters” and “a majority of the votes.”
The sentence says that “the group of undecided voters” was becoming smaller. Since it does not make sense that the undecided voters themselves were becoming smaller, it must be that it is the group as a whole that was becoming smaller. So, the correct verb to go with “the group of undecided voters” is the singular “was,” as in “the group … was becoming smaller.”
Regarding “a majority of the votes,” the point of the sentence is not that certain individual votes were required for winning. The point is that winning requires a particular thing, “a majority of the votes.” So, “a majority of the votes” is best understood to be a singular subject that goes with the singular verb “was.”
(A) Realizing that the undecided voters, becoming a smaller group as Election Day approached, with a majority of the votes was required for winning, the candidates campaigned intensely.
This awkwardly constructed version says that “the undecided voters … with a majority of the votes was required for winning.” What that statement means is not entirely clear. Are the undecided voters without a majority of the votes not required for winning?
Also, “voters” does not agree with the singular “was.”
Also, it does not quite make sense to describe “the undecided voters” as “becoming a smaller group.” A group itself would become a smaller group. The voters in the group would not become a smaller group.
(B) Realizing that the group of undecided voters were becoming smaller as Election Day approached and that a majority of the votes were required for winning, the candidates campaigned intensely.
In this version, “the group of undecided voters” is paired with the plural verb “were becoming.” Since “were becoming” does not agree with the singular “group,” “were becoming” must go with the plural “voters.” Thus, version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the undecided voters themselves were becoming smaller.
Also, “a majority of the votes were required for winning” does not clearly convey that a majority of the votes was required for winning. Rather, the use of “were” indicates that the focus is on the individual votes themselves. So, that clause conveys the nonsensical meaning that most of the votes were required for winning, but some votes were not, as if there were two kinds of votes, required ones and not required ones.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Realizing that the group of undecided voters was becoming smaller as Election Day approached and that a majority of the votes was required for winning, the candidates campaigned intensely.
By pairing “the group of undecided voters” with the singular “was” this version indicates that the group of undecided voters as a whole was becoming smaller, a meaning that makes sense.
Also, “a majority of the votes was required for winning” clearly conveys that, in order to win, a candidate had to have a majority of the votes.
(D) Realizing that, as a group, the undecided voters were becoming smaller as Election Day approached and that the majority of the votes were a requirement for winning, the candidates campaigned intensely.
By saying, “as a group, the undecided voters were becoming smaller,” this version conveys the nonsensical meaning, that, as a group, the undecided voters themselves were becoming smaller people.
Also, “the majority of the votes would be a requirement for winning” is awkward and not quite logical. It seems to convey that some votes, the majority of them, are “a requirement” while other votes are not “a requirement.”
(E) Realizing that the undecided voters was becoming a smaller group as Election Day approached and that a majority of the votes were required for winning, the candidates campaigned intensely.
In this version, the plural “undecided voters” is incorrectly paired with the singular “was.”
Also, in saying, “the undecided voters was becoming a smaller group,” this version does not clearly convey that the group was becoming smaller. The statement illogically focuses on what the individual voters were becoming, as if voters themselves could become a smaller group.
Also, “a majority of the votes were required for winning” does not clearly convey that a majority of the votes was required for winning. Rather, because the use of “were” indicates that the focus is on the individual votes themselves, that clause conveys the somewhat nonsensical meaning that most of the votes were required, but some votes were not, as if there were two kinds of votes, required ones and not required ones.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now briefly discuss a strategy to use in a case in which whether a collective noun should be considered singular or considered plural is not clear.
summary:
Lesson Content Like other collective nouns that we have discussed, the collective nouns “majority” and “minority” can be considered singular or plural depending on the contexts in which they appear.
In the following sentences, “majority” and “minority” are plural:
A majority of the students are already applying for jobs.
In today’s world, in which most people have some knowledge of geography, only a small minority believe Earth to be flat.
title: 3.12 The Collective Nouns “Majority” and “Minority”

contents:
Lesson Content Of course, since a collective noun subject can be correctly paired with a singular verb or plural verb, we could see a Sentence Correction question in which it is not entirely clear whether a collective noun would most logically be considered a singular subject or plural subject.
In such a case, our best move may be to choose an answer choice by looking for differences between choices other than agreement between that collective noun and its associated verb.
Let’s use this method to choose among the answer choices in the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 14 Since a group of high school students was expected to attend the introductory economics class the next day, the professor, along with his assistants, was racing to put together a lesson that would both fit the curriculum and capture the interest of novices. 1. [x] (A) Since a group of high school students was expected to attend the introductory economics class the next day, the professor, along with his assistants, was racing 2. [ ] (B) Since a group of high school students were expected to attend the introductory economics class the next day, the professor, along with his assistants, were racing > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Since a group of high school students were expected to attend the introductory economics class the next day, the professor and his assistants was in a race > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) With a group of high school students, who was expected to attend the introductory economics class the next day, the professor, along with his assistants, were in a race > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) A group of high school students expecting to attend the introductory economics class the next day; the professor and his assistants were in a race > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium Even though we have discussed that the GMAT leans toward using singular verbs with collective nouns, we also know that, when the focus of a clause is on the individuals in a group, the verb used with a collective noun should be plural. So, in seeing this question, we might wonder whether the “group of high school students” should be considered one group that will be attending the class, in which case the singular “was expected” would be preferred, or the sentence is focused on the individuals in the group, each of whom will be attending the class, in which case using the plural “were expected” would make more sense.
Fortunately, the question does not force us to make that decision, because there are other differences among the choices that we can use to find the correct answer. So, we can put aside the question of whether the “group of students” is best considered a singular or plural subject and base our choice on things we are more confident about.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Since a group of high school students was expected to attend the introductory economics class the next day, the professor, along with his assistants, was racing to put together a lesson that would both fit the curriculum and capture the interest of novices.
We may not be entirely sure whether “group of students” should be considered a singular subject or a plural subject. So, we may not be certain that “was expected” is correct.
However, we know that, even though the additive phrase, “along with his assistants,” follows “professor,” a singular subject like “professor” remains singular even if it is followed by an additive phrase. So, “was racing” agrees with “professor,” making this choice the only one in which there is no flaw in the clause about the professor and his assistants.
(B) Since a group of high school students were expected to attend the introductory economics class the next day, the professor, along with his assistants, were racing to put together a lesson that would both fit the curriculum and capture the interest of novices.
We may not be entirely sure whether “group of students” should be considered a singular subject or a plural subject. So, we may not be certain that “were expected” is incorrect.
However, we know that, even though the additive phrase, “along with his assistants,” follows “professor,” a singular subject like “professor” remains singular even if it is followed by an additive phrase. Thus, “were racing” disagrees with the subject of the main clause, “professor,” making this choice incorrect.
(C) Since a group of high school students were expected to attend the introductory economics class the next day, the professor and his assistants was in a race to put together a lesson that would both fit the curriculum and capture the interest of novices.
We may not be entirely sure whether “group of students” should be considered a singular subject or a plural subject. So, we may not be certain that “were expected” is incorrect. However, the singular “was” does not agree with the compound subject, “the professor and his assistants.”
Also, it does not really make sense to say that the professor and his assistants “were in a race to put together a lesson.”
(D) With a group of high school students, who was expected to attend the introductory economics class the next day, the professor, along with his assistants, were in a race to put together a lesson that would both fit the curriculum and capture the interest of novices.
The pronoun “who” can refer only to people, and not to things. So, “who” would not refer to “group” but rather to “students,” which is plural. So, we need “who were” and, thus, “who was” is incorrect.
Also, we know that, even though the additive phrase, “along with his assistants,” follows “professor,” a singular subject like “professor” remains singular, even if it is followed by an additive phrase. Thus, in the main clause, “were” disagrees with the subject, “professor.”
Also, this version nonsensically conveys that the professor and his assistants were in a race with a group of high school students.
(E) A group of high school students expecting to attend the introductory economics class the next day; the professor and his assistants were in a race to put together a lesson that would both fit the curriculum and capture the interest of novices.
When a semicolon connects two parts of a sentence that express ideas, both of the parts of the sentence that are connected by the semicolon must be complete clauses. In this case, however, the part that precedes the semicolon has no main verb. Rather, it consists only of the noun “group” along with multiple modifiers. So, the part that precedes the semicolon is not a complete clause.
Also, this version conveys the illogical meaning that the professor and his assistants were in a race to put together a lesson.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss subjects with certain preceding elements.
summary:
Lesson Content Of course, since a collective noun subject can be correctly paired with a singular verb or plural verb, we could see a Sentence Correction question in which it is not entirely clear whether a collective noun would most logically be considered a singular subject or plural subject.
In such a case, our best move may be to choose an answer choice by looking for differences between choices other than agreement between that collective noun and its associated verb.
title: 3.13 Collective Nouns that are not Clearly Singular or Plural in Sentence Correction Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Some preceding elements in a subject can require that the subject take a singular verb even if the subject seems to be plural.
For instance, a subject composed of a noun preceded by “every,” “each,” or “many a” always takes a singular verb.
Consider the following examples:
The guys at the prom look amazing.
Every guy at the prom looks amazing.
In the first sentence, the subject, “guys,” is plural and therefore requires a plural verb.
However, when the subject begins with “every,” as is the case in the second sentence, the subject is understood to be singular.
💡MUST KNOW:
When a subject begins with the word(s) “every,” “each,” “many a,” or “many an,” the subject always takes a singular verb.
Let’s look at another example.
Each of us (prefers/prefer) a different style of cooking, and so, it has been difficult to reach a consensus about the menu.
Although the pronoun “us” seems to signal the need for a plural verb, in this case, “us” is part of an expression that begins with “each.” So, the singular verb “prefers” is correct.
Each of us prefers a different style of cooking, and so, it has been difficult to reach a consensus about the menu.
Let’s look at one more example.
Clarisse’s inbox is filled with more than a thousand emails, and many an email (has/have) gone unanswered.
The sentence above is made up of two independent clauses, the second of which begins with the phrase “many an.” Since any subject that begins with “many an” is singular, we know that the verb that follows must be singular, despite the fact that we can tell that “many an email” is, in a sense, referring to multiple emails.
Clarisse’s inbox is filled with more than a thousand emails, and many an email has gone unanswered.
Note that we would still require the singular “has” following “many an” even if the preceding clause were a dependent clause.
Though there are more than a thousand emails in Clarisse’s inbox, many an email has gone unanswered.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Select the correct verb form for each subject. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now answer a Sentence Correction question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 15 Multiverse theory posits that there are a multitude, or even an infinite number, of universes in existence, each with its own laws of physics and potential for intelligent life. 1. [x] (A) multitude, or even an infinite number, of universes in existence, each with its 2. [ ] (B) multitude as well as an even infinite number of universes in existence and that every one of them have their > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) multitude, along with even an infinite number, of universes in existence, each has its > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) multitude, or even an infinite number, of universes in existence, so that each have their > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) multitude, and even an infinite number, of universes in existence, every one of which have its > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy YOUR CHOICE(A) Multiverse theory posits that there are a multitude, or even an infinite number, of universes in existence, each with its own laws of physics and potential for intelligent life.
The version created with this choice conveys a logical meaning and is grammatically correct.
Notice that the singular “each,” which in this context conveys the meaning “each universe,” is correctly paired with the singular “its.” This version also correctly uses “or” to separate the two possibilities that multiverse theory posits: that there are either a “multitude” of universes or an “infinite number” of them, not both. Thus, this version avoids conveying the illogical meaning conveyed by some others.
(B) Multiverse theory posits that there are a multitude as well as an even infinite number of universes in existence and that every one of them have their own laws of physics and potential for intelligent life.
Since the plural pronoun “them” is preceded by the phrase “every one of,” the verb and pronoun following them must be in singular form. In other words, the subject becomes the singular “every one,” not the plural “them.”
So, the plural “have their” is incorrect. “Every one of them has its own laws …” would be correct.
Additionally, it is illogical to say that “there are a multitude as well as an even infinite number of universes,” because if there are an infinite number of universes, then by definition, there are a multitude.
(C) Multiverse theory posits that there are a multitude, along with even an infinite number, of universes in existence, each has its own laws of physics and potential for intelligent life.
The version created by this choice contains a comma splice, meaning that two independent clauses are joined by only a comma. Notice that the following two clauses are joined by nothing more than a comma:
Multiverse theory posits that there are a multitude, along with even an infinite number, of universes in existence,
each has its own laws of physics and potential for intelligent life.
Those are two complete sentences, right? In other words, each of those expresses a complete thought and can stand on its own. We cannot join two complete sentences with a comma alone. Rather, we need to use a semicolon to join them or place a conjunction such as “and” after the comma. Comma splices can be difficult to spot if we don’t read an answer choice in the context of the full sentence. So, we must always make sure to consider the full sentence created, not just the answer choice in isolation.
Additionally, it is illogical to say that “there are a multitude, along with even an infinite number, of universes,” because if there are an infinite number of universes, then by definition, there are a multitude.
(D) Multiverse theory posits that there are a multitude, or even an infinite number, of universes in existence, so that each have their own laws of physics and potential for intelligent life.
We must pair “each” with a singular verb and pronoun. Thus, the plural “have their” is incorrect.
Notice that the implied meaning of “each” in this context is “each universe,” which is singular. It is not grammatical to say that “each [universe] have their own laws.” We must say that each “has its” own laws.
There is also an illogical meaning conveyed by the version created with this choice. It does not quite make sense to say that multiverse theory posits that there are a multitude of universes “so that” the universes have certain qualities. The nonsensical meaning conveyed is that the theory posits something for the purpose of giving universes certain qualities. The theory either accurately or inaccurately describes universes, but a theory cannot actually affect the nature of universes.
(E) Multiverse theory posits that there are a multitude, and even an infinite number, of universes in existence, every one of which have its own laws of physics and potential for intelligent life.
The phrase “every one of” should be paired with the singular verb “has,” not the plural “have.” So, “every one of which have its own laws” is incorrect.
Additionally, it is illogical to say that “there are a multitude, and even an infinite number, of universes,” because if there are an infinite number of universes, then by definition, there are a multitude. So, the sentence should say that there are either a “multitude” or “an infinite number,” not both.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 16 Believed by many leaders is that, in order for our nation to achieve financial solvency, each of our millions of citizens has to seek financial solvency, because a nation can be only as solid as its citizens. 1. [x] (A) Believed by many leaders is that, in order for our nation to achieve financial solvency, each of our millions of citizens has 2. [ ] (B) So that our nation achieves financial solvency, many leaders believe that all of our millions of citizens has > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) It is believed by many leaders that, for our nation to achieve financial solvency, each of our millions of citizens who has > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Many leaders believe that, in order for our nation to achieve financial solvency, every one of our millions of citizens have > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) So that our nation will achieve financial solvency, many leaders believe that each of our millions of citizens has > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard Correctly answering this question requires noticing subject-verb agreement issues and paying careful attention to the meanings conveyed by the basically grammatically correct choices. The correct answer is written to seem a little off. So, if we’re not careful, we’ll be tempted to choose one of the more correct sounding incorrect choices.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Believed by many leaders is that, in order for our nation to achieve financial solvency, each of our millions of citizens has to seek financial solvency, because a nation can be only as solid as its citizens.
This choice employs an inverted sentence structure to obscure the fact that it is the correct answer to this question. Notice, the subject of the sentence is expressed by the noun clause that follows “is.” So, we could reverse the sentence to make it the following:
That, in order for our nation to achieve financial solvency, each of our millions of citizens has to seek financial solvency, because a nation can be only as solid as its citizens is believed by many leaders.
Also, this version correctly uses the singular verb “has” with the singular subject “each of our millions of citizens.”
(B) So that our nation achieves financial solvency, many leaders believe that all of our millions of citizens has to seek financial solvency, because a nation can be only as solid as its citizens.
This choice can be eliminated because the version created via the use of this choice does not make sense, since it does not make sense to say that “many leaders believe” something in order for the nation to achieve financial solvency.
Also, this version incorrectly uses a singular verb, “has,” with subject “all of our millions of citizens,” which does not start with “each” or “every” but, rather, with “all,” and so this subject is plural, not singular.
(C) It is believed by many leaders that, for our nation to achieve financial solvency, each of our millions of citizens who has to seek financial solvency, because a nation can be only as solid as its citizens.
While this version starts off correctly, when we combine this answer choice with the non-underlined portion, we see that what may at first appear to be a noun clause beginning with “that” actually has no main verb. As a result, we have the following illogical structure: “that … each of our millions of citizens who has to seek financial solvency.” We can see even more clearly why this structure does not work if we read it along with the rest of the sentence.
It is believed by many leaders that … each of our millions of citizens who has to achieve financial solvency ….
(D) Many leaders believe that, in order for our nation to achieve financial solvency, every one of our millions of citizens have to seek financial solvency, because a nation can be only as solid as its citizens.
This version is correct except for one word, the plural “have,” which does not agree with the singular subject “every one of our millions of citizens.”
(E) So that our nation will achieve financial solvency, many leaders believe that each of our millions of citizens has to seek financial solvency, because a nation can be only as solid as its citizens.
All subjects and verbs in this version agree, and this version is basically grammatically correct.
However, this version expresses an illogical meaning, that many leaders believe something so that the nation will achieve financial solvency. It doesn’t make sense that leaders would believe something so that the nation will achieve financial solvency. We have to be careful to notice such meaning issues, so that we don’t choose an illogical choice like this one over one like the inversely structured, somewhat odd sounding choice (A).
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss pronoun-verb agreement.
summary:
Lesson Content Some preceding elements in a subject can require that the subject take a singular verb even if the subject seems to be plural.
For instance, a subject composed of a noun preceded by “every,” “each,” or “many a” always takes a singular verb.
Consider the following examples:
The guys at the prom look amazing.
Every guy at the prom looks amazing.
In the first sentence, the subject, “guys,” is plural and therefore requires a plural verb.
title: 3.14 Subjects with Certain Preceding Elements

contents:
Lesson Content A pronoun is a word that takes the place of a noun.
Robert said that he will be able to attend the party.
In this sentence, the pronoun “he” refers to the antecedent “Robert.” Notice that, by using the pronoun, we don’t have to repeat the noun “Robert.”
The zebra and the hippo decided that they were tired, so they looked for a comfortable place to sleep.
In this sentence, the pronoun “they” refers to the antecedents “the zebra and the hippo.” Notice that, by using the pronoun, we don’t have to repeat the nouns “zebra and hippo.”
Pronouns follow the same rules of subject-verb agreement that nouns follow.
💡MUST KNOW:
Pronouns follow the same rules of subject-verb agreement that nouns follow. If the subject of a clause is a pronoun rather than a noun, it still must agree in number with its verb.
We’ll cover pronouns in greater detail in the Pronouns chapter, but for now, we’ll discuss two types of pronouns that must agree with their verbs: relative pronouns and indefinite pronouns.
Let’s discuss relative pronouns and subject-verb agreement.
summary:
Lesson Content A pronoun is a word that takes the place of a noun.
Robert said that he will be able to attend the party.
In this sentence, the pronoun “he” refers to the antecedent “Robert.” Notice that, by using the pronoun, we don’t have to repeat the noun “Robert.”
The zebra and the hippo decided that they were tired, so they looked for a comfortable place to sleep.
In this sentence, the pronoun “they” refers to the antecedents “the zebra and the hippo.
title: 3.15 Pronoun-Verb Agreement

contents:
Lesson Content Relative pronouns, such as “which,” “that,” and “who,” can be singular or plural depending on their referents.
The dresser that is on sale is still expensive.
The curtains that are on sale are beautiful.
As demonstrated in the sentences above, the relative pronoun “that” can take a singular or plural verb depending on whether the noun to which “that” refers is singular or plural.
💡MUST KNOW:
When a relative pronoun refers to a singular noun, it too is singular and takes a singular verb. On the other hand, when a relative pronoun refers to a plural noun, it is likewise plural and requires a plural verb.
Let’s examine some more examples:
Jurors are sometimes sympathetic toward a defendant who claims to have committed a “crime of passion.”
In the sentence above, the relative pronoun “who” refers to the singular “defendant” and, thus, requires the singular verb “claims.”
The proposed upgrades to the community center, which include the addition of a basketball court and a reading room, will be voted on at the community board meeting later this month.
The subject of the sentence above is “the proposed upgrades,” and the relative clause beginning with “which” further describes those upgrades.
The only restaurant within two miles of here that serves burritos is Coyote Flaco.
The relative pronoun “that” refers to the singular “restaurant,” and so, we use the singular verb “serves.”
Let’s do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Select the correct singular or plural verb. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Now, let’s use our relative pronoun subject-verb agreement skills to answer a tricky sentence correction question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 17 Ironically, the plan to build carbon-neutral factories that has the capacity to manufacture thousands of emissions-free trucks annually are being opposed by locals citing negative environmental impacts. 1. [ ] (A) factories that has the capacity to annually manufacture thousands of emissions-free trucks are being opposed by locals citing > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) factories, which has the capacity to manufacture thousands of emissions-free trucks annually, is opposed by locals who cite > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) factories that have the capacity to annually manufacture thousands of emissions-free trucks are opposed by locals citing > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) factories that have the capacity to manufacture thousands of emissions-free trucks annually and are opposed by locals who cite > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) factories that have the capacity to manufacture annually thousands of emissions-free trucks is opposed by locals citing View Solution Question Level: easy (A) Ironically, the plan to build carbon-neutral factories that has the capacity to annually manufacture thousands of emissions-free trucks are being opposed by locals citing negative environmental impacts.
Notice that the relative pronoun “that” in this choice refers to the plural noun phrase “carbon-neutral factories.” Thus, the singular verb “has” is incorrect, since a singular verb does not properly pair with a plural noun. “Carbon-neutral factories that have …” would be correct.
Also, the plural verb “are” does not agree with the singular subject “the plan.”
If you have a hard time seeing this issue, one powerful strategy you can use is to cross off everything between the noun and verb.
Ironically, the plan to build carbon-neutral factories that has the capacity to manufacture thousands of emissions-free trucks annually are being opposed
(B) Ironically, the plan to build carbon-neutral factories, which has the capacity to manufacture thousands of emissions-free trucks annually, is opposed by locals who cite negative environmental impacts.
Notice that the relative pronoun “which” in this choice refers to the plural noun phrase “carbon-neutral factories.” Thus, the singular verb “has” is incorrect, since a singular verb does not properly pair with a plural noun. “Carbon-neutral factories, which have …” would be correct.
(C) Ironically, the plan to build carbon-neutral factories that have the capacity to annually manufacture thousands of emissions-free trucks are opposed by locals citing negative environmental impacts.
In this version, the plural verb “are” does not agree with the singular subject “the plan.”
If you have a hard time seeing this issue, one powerful strategy you can use is to cross off everything between the noun and verb.
Ironically, the plan to build carbon-neutral factories that have the capacity to annually manufacture thousands of emissions-free trucks are opposed
(D) Ironically, the plan to build carbon-neutral factories that have the capacity to manufacture thousands of emissions-free trucks annually and are opposed by locals who cite negative environmental impacts.
This version is composed of only the adverb “ironically;” a noun phrase, “the plan to build carbon-neutral factories;” and a long relative clause that begins with “that” and runs all the way to the period. So, even though this version doesn’t have any subject-verb agreement issues, it’s incorrect because it has no independent clause and thus isn’t a complete sentence.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Ironically, the plan to build carbon-neutral factories that have the capacity to manufacture annually thousands of emissions-free trucks is opposed by locals citing negative environmental impacts.
In this version, the verb “have” works logically with the noun phrase that precedes “that,” the plural “carbon-neutral factories.”
Also, the singular verb “is” agrees with the singular subject “the plan,” as we can more easily see by crossing off what appears between the subject and the verb.
Ironically, the plan to build carbon-neutral factories that have the capacity to manufacture annually thousands of emissions-free trucks is opposed
This choice is the only one that produces a grammatically correct complete sentence.
Key Concept: Confirm that singular subjects are paired with singular verbs and that plural subjects are paired with plural verbs. Key Concept: When a relative pronoun refers to a singular noun, the pronoun too is singular and takes a singular verb. When a relative pronoun refers to a plural noun, the pronoun is likewise plural and takes a plural verb.
Key Concept: When checking for subject-verb agreement, cross off any noun modifiers that come between the subject and finite verb.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 18 The startup bike company’s first model, which, though not well known as some other bikes, were less expensive, quickly became popular with cyclists worldwide. 1. [ ] (A) well known as some other bikes, were less expensive, quickly became > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) as well known as some other bikes, were less expensive, and they quickly became > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) as well known as some other bikes, was less expensive, quickly became 4. [ ] (D) well known as some other bike, was less expensive and quickly became > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) well known, as were some other bikes, was less expensive, quickly becoming > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard In every version of the sentence, there is a relative clause beginning with “which” that modifies “model.” Since “model” is singular, “which” must be singular and, therefore, has to be paired with the singular “was,” rather than the plural “were.”
Once we have eliminated the choices in which “which” is incorrectly paired with “were,” we have to find other decision points in order to choose the correct answer from the remaining choices.
(A) The startup bike company’s first model, which, though not well known as some other bikes, were less expensive, quickly became popular with cyclists worldwide.
Since “which” refers to “model,” “which” must be singular. However, in this version, “which” is incorrectly paired with the plural “were.” To see this pairing more clearly, we could cross off what comes between “which” and “were.”
The startup bike company’s first model, which, though not well known as some other bikes, were less expensive
Also, “though not well known as some other bikes” is missing “as” before “well known.” As a result, it conveys that the first model was not well known to be some other bikes.
(B) The startup bike company’s first model, which, though not as well known as some other bikes, were less expensive, and they quickly became popular with cyclists worldwide.
Since “which” refers to “model,” “which” must be singular, but, in this version, “which” is incorrectly paired with the plural “were.” To see this pairing more clearly, we could cross off the what comes between “which” and “were.”
The startup bike company’s first model, which, though not as well known as some other bikes, were less expensive
Also, the inclusion of “and they” in this version renders the sentence illogically constructed, as we can more clearly see if we leave out the relative clause that begins with “which.”
The startup bike company’s first model … and they quickly became popular with cyclists worldwide.
Notice also that the pronoun “they” does not agree in number with what it seems to refer to, “model.”
YOUR CHOICE(C) The startup bike company’s first model, which, though not as well known as some other bikes, was less expensive, quickly became popular with cyclists worldwide.
This choice may seem incorrect because the ending section, “was less expensive, quickly became,” appears to be illogical.
However, by carefully evaluating this choice in the context of the non-underlined portions of the sentence, we can see that it’s actually the correct answer.
First, since “which” refers to “model,” “which” must be singular. So, pairing “which” with “was” in the relative clause is correct, as we can more easily see by crossing off what appears between “which” and “was.”
The startup bike company’s first model, which, though not as well known as some other bikes, was less expensive
Next, we can confirm that the rest of this version works by crossing off the relative clause.
The startup bike company’s first model, which, though not as well known as some other bikes, was less expensive, quickly became popular with cyclists worldwide.
We can see that the main clause of this version is “The startup bike company’s first model … quickly became popular with cyclists worldwide,” which is logical.
So, this version is grammatically correct and effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(D) The startup bike company’s first model, which, though not well known as some other bike, was less expensive and quickly became popular with cyclists worldwide.
This choice is a trap written to look correct even though it has some significant flaws.
Since “which” refers to “model,” “which” must be singular. So, pairing “which” with “was” is correct.
However, “well known as some other bike” is missing “as” before “well known.” Thus, as written, “not well known as some other bike” conveys that the company’s first model was not well known to be some other bike.
Also, the addition of “and” before “quickly became” turns everything that follows “model” into a relative clause that modifies “model,” as we can more easily see by crossing off the relative clause.
The startup bike company’s first model, which, though not well known as some other bike, was less expensive and quickly become popular with cyclists worldwide.
So this version has no main verb or independent clause and is not a complete sentence.
(E) The startup bike company’s first model, which, though not well known, as were some other bikes, was less expensive, quickly becoming popular with cyclists worldwide.
Since “which” refers to “model,” “which” must be singular. So, pairing “which” with “was” is correct. So, though somewhat awkward, the modifier beginning with “which” works in this version.
However, the use of “quickly becoming” rather than “quickly became” makes everything that follows “model” into a relative clause that modifies “model,” as we can more easily see by crossing off the relative clause.
The startup bike company’s first model, which, though not well known, as were some other companies’ bikes, was less expensive, quickly becoming popular with cyclists worldwide.
Thus, this version has no main verb or independent clause and is not a complete sentence.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s continue to the next topic.
summary:
Lesson Content Relative pronouns, such as “which,” “that,” and “who,” can be singular or plural depending on their referents.
The dresser that is on sale is still expensive.
The curtains that are on sale are beautiful.
As demonstrated in the sentences above, the relative pronoun “that” can take a singular or plural verb depending on whether the noun to which “that” refers is singular or plural.
💡MUST KNOW:
When a relative pronoun refers to a singular noun, it too is singular and takes a singular verb.
title: 3.16 Relative Pronouns

contents:
Lesson Content An indefinite pronoun is exactly what it sounds like, a pronoun that doesn’t refer to any one thing in particular.
💡MUST KNOW:
An indefinite pronoun is a pronoun that doesn’t refer to any one thing in particular.
There are many indefinite pronouns, and subject-verb agreement rules vary depending on which one is used.
Let’s first discuss singular indefinite pronouns.
summary:
Lesson Content An indefinite pronoun is exactly what it sounds like, a pronoun that doesn’t refer to any one thing in particular.
💡MUST KNOW:
An indefinite pronoun is a pronoun that doesn’t refer to any one thing in particular.
There are many indefinite pronouns, and subject-verb agreement rules vary depending on which one is used.
Let’s first discuss singular indefinite pronouns.
title: 3.17 Indefinite Pronouns

contents:
Lesson Content Some indefinite pronouns are always singular and, therefore, always require a singular verb.
These pronouns are:
anybody, anyone, anything, each, either1, everybody, everyone, everything, neither1, nobody, no one, nothing, one, somebody, someone, something, whatever, whoever2
Consider the following examples in which singular indefinite pronouns are paired with singular verbs:
After the concert, everyone was ready to go home.
If someone fixes up this house, it will be lovely.
Once Andrea addresses whatever has been bothering her, she will feel better.
Whoever wins the election will inherit quite a mess.
Be sure to commit this list of singular indefinite pronouns to memory.
💡MUST KNOW:
Singular indefinite pronouns always take singular verbs. These pronouns are:
1Note that when “either” is used in an “either … or” construction, we must follow the rules concerning “either … or.” The same applies to “neither” in a “neither … nor” construction.
💡MUST KNOW:
When “either” is used in an “either … or” construction, we must follow the rules concerning “either … or.” The same applies to “neither” in a “neither … nor” construction. In these cases, we follow the rule that the verb in a clause or sentence containing a compound subject joined by the conjunctions “or,” “nor,” “either … or,” or “neither … nor” must agree with the subject noun to which it is closest.
2Note that, when “whoever” precedes a plural noun, as in, for example, “whoever the owners,” the plural noun may be the subject of the sentence and take a plural verb, as in:
Whoever the owners are, they are doing a great job of preserving the wetlands on the property.
Whoever the officers of the company are, they are letting the earnings per share slide.
In Sentence Correction questions, singular indefinite pronouns often will be followed by prepositional phrases.
Consider the following example:
Neither of the girls (likes/like) doing chores on the weekends.
The intervening prepositional phrase “of the girls” may tempt us to choose the plural verb “like,” because our ears have a tendency to determine whether we need a singular or a plural verb by considering the noun closest to that verb.
However, “like” is incorrect in this sentence, because the indefinite pronoun “neither” functions as the subject of the sentence and is always singular.
Neither of the girls likes doing chores on the weekends.
Let’s look at another example:
Nothing about the meetings (makes/make) the board feel confident that the executives will produce a balanced budget for the upcoming fiscal year.
It may sound natural to your ear to follow the prepositional phrase “about the meetings” with the plural verb “make.” However, because the phrase is preceded by the singular indefinite pronoun “nothing,” we must use the singular verb “makes.”
Nothing about the meetings makes the board feel confident that the executives will produce a balanced budget for the upcoming fiscal year.
Let’s try another example:
Everything about his actions (suggests/suggest) that he isn’t concerned with other people’s opinions of him.
While “his actions” would certainly “suggest,” “Everything” “suggests.” Thus, the correct sentence is:
Everything about his actions suggests that he isn’t concerned with other people’s opinions of him.
Let’s try a different type of example:
No one participating in these early morning gym classes (has/have) completely woken up at the beginning, but the adrenaline starts to kick in after about fifteen minutes of exercise.
Here we have an example of a sentence in which the indefinite pronoun is immediately followed by a participle, “participating ,” rather than a preposition.
No one participating in these early morning gym classes has completely woken up at the beginning, but the adrenaline starts to kick in after about fifteen minutes of exercise.
Let’s look at another example.
Although we advertised the job opening on a variety of employment and social networking sites, nobody among the numerous applicants (qualifies/qualify) to lead such a large team.
Words like “numerous” and “applicants” may tempt you to choose the plural form of the verb that follows them, but “nobody,” even “among numerous applicants,” remains singular, and the verb that goes with “nobody” must be singular as well.
Although we advertised the job opening on a variety of employment and social networking sites, nobody among the numerous applicants qualifies to lead such a large team.
Let’s try a slight variation on the above example:
Although we advertised the job opening on a variety of employment and social networking sites, not one of the numerous applicants (qualifies/qualify) to lead such a large team.
Would we still use a singular verb with “not one”? The resulting structure may sound a bit strange to the ear, but the answer is yes.
Although we advertised the job opening on a variety of employment and social networking sites, not one of the numerous applicants qualifies to lead such a large team.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Select the correct verb form for each subject. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Now, notice how singular indefinite pronouns work in this Sentence Correction question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 19 While most researchers subscribe to one of two main schools of thought on the formation of Earth’s moon, neither of them completely explaining all aspects of the composition of the two bodies. 1. [ ] (A) them completely explaining > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) them complete their explanations of > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) them completely explain > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) the two completely explains 5. [ ] (E) the two explain completely > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy The subject of the second clause of the sentence begins with the singular indefinite pronoun “neither.” So, to agree with the subject, the main verb of that clause will have to be singular.
(A) While most researchers subscribe to one of two main schools of thought on the formation of Earth’s moon, neither of them completely explaining all aspects of the composition of the two bodies.
This version does not include an independent clause.
Notice that “explaining” in “neither of them completely explaining” is not a finite verb and, therefore, cannot on its own serve as a main verb.
Thus, this version includes only a subordinate clause, “while most researchers subscribe …,” and a phrase “neither of them completely explaining ….”
Without an independent clause, this version cannot be a complete sentence.
(B) While most researchers subscribe to one of two main schools of thought on the formation of Earth’s moon, neither of them complete their explanations of all aspects of the composition of the two bodies.
The subject of the second clause, “neither of them” is singular and requires a singular verb. Therefore, the plural “complete” does not agree with the subject.
Also, this version conveys that the schools of thought did not complete their explanations, a meaning that is not completely logical.
(C) While most researchers subscribe to one of two main schools of thought on the formation of Earth’s moon, neither of them completely explain all aspects of the composition of the two bodies.
The subject of the second clause, “neither of them” is singular and requires a singular verb. So, the plural “explain” does not agree with the subject.
(Notice that, if we’re not careful, we could incorrectly perceive the presence of “them” as indicating that we need the plural verb “explain.” In answering a Sentence Correction question, we have to remember to consider the non-underlined portion of the sentence, which, in this case, contains the singular subject “neither.”)
YOUR CHOICE(D) While most researchers subscribe to one of two main schools of thought on the formation of Earth’s moon, neither of the two completely explains all aspects of the composition of the two bodies.
The subject of the second clause, “neither of the two” is singular and requires a singular verb. So, the singular “explains” agrees with the subject, and this version concisely conveys a logical meaning.
(Notice that this choice is created to look incorrect on its own, without the non-underlined portions of the sentence, as, if one perceives “two” to be the subject of a clause and “explains” to be the verb, then the subject and verb appear to disagree.)
(E) While most researchers subscribe to one of two main schools of thought on the formation of Earth’s moon, neither of the two explain completely all aspects of the composition of the two bodies.
The subject of the second clause, “neither of the two” is singular and requires a singular verb. So, the plural “explain” does not agree with the subject.
(Notice that this choice is a trap choice created to make “two” seem to be a subject that agrees with the plural verb “explain.”)
Also, the placement of “completely” just before “all” makes the meaning conveyed less clear and may make the wording seem to be redundant, as one could perceive the sentence to be conveying that neither of the two schools of thought explains “completely all” aspects.
Correct Answer: (D) You now know a lot about singular indefinite pronouns.
summary:
Lesson Content Some indefinite pronouns are always singular and, therefore, always require a singular verb.
These pronouns are:
anybody, anyone, anything, each, either1, everybody, everyone, everything, neither1, nobody, no one, nothing, one, somebody, someone, something, whatever, whoever2
Consider the following examples in which singular indefinite pronouns are paired with singular verbs:
After the concert, everyone was ready to go home.
If someone fixes up this house, it will be lovely.
title: 3.18 Singular Indefinite Pronouns

contents:
Lesson Content Some indefinite pronouns are always plural and therefore always take a plural verb.
These indefinite pronouns include the following: “both,” “few,” “many,” and “several.”
Be sure to memorize these plural indefinite pronouns.
💡MUST KNOW:
A plural indefinite pronoun always takes a plural verb. These pronouns include the following: “both,” “few,” “many,” and “several.”
In most cases, it is intuitive to pair a plural verb with a plural indefinite pronoun.
Consider the following examples:
Both of my sisters like to watch cartoons on the weekends.
Several of the students prefer to read silently as opposed to aloud.
