Few of the companies in the community provide annual donations for the fundraiser.
Many of the animals up for adoption are abandoned pets.
Targeted Practice For each of the following sentences, select the correct verb form for each indefinite pronoun. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Now, let’s apply our knowledge of plural indefinite pronouns as we consider the choices in the following Sentence Correction question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 20 Since many of the deer in the southeastern section of the state has had young, officials at the wildlife department have confidence in the area that it is sufficiently populated with deer. 1. [ ] (A) Since many of the deer in the southeastern section of the state has had young, officials at the wildlife department have confidence in the area that it > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) With many of the deer in the southeastern section of the state that have had young, officials at the wildlife department have confidence that the area > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Because of many of the deer in the southeastern section of the state that has had young, officials at the wildlife department, confident that the area > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Many of the deer in the southeastern section of the state has had young; therefore, officials at the wildlife department have confidence in the area that > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) As many of the deer in the southeastern section of the state have had young, officials at the wildlife department are confident that that area View Solution Question Level: easy The sentence is composed of multiple clauses.
In most versions, the subject of one of the clauses is “many of the deer.” Since “many” is always plural, “many of the deer” has to be paired with a plural verb.
Since the question has multiple answer choices that are grammatically correct, in order to arrive at the correct answer, we have to first eliminate the choices that are clearly grammatically incorrect and then choose, out of the remaining choices, the choice that expresses a meaning that makes sense.
(A) Since many of the deer in the southeastern section of the state has had young, officials at the wildlife department have confidence in the area that it is sufficiently populated with deer.
In the first clause of the sentence, the singular main verb, “has had,” does not agree with the plural subject, “many of the deer.”
Also, while “officials … have confidence in the area that it is sufficiently populated” may not blatantly violate any rules of grammar, it is awkward and expresses a somewhat muddled meaning. It would make sense to say that officials “are confident that the area is sufficiently populated.” However, this version mashes together the idea that “officials … have confidence in the area” and the idea that officials are confident that “it is sufficiently populated” in a way that doesn’t make sense.
(B) With many of the deer in the southeastern section of the state that have had young, officials at the wildlife department have confidence that the area is sufficiently populated with deer.
In this version, the plural subject of the first clause, “many of the deer,” agrees with the main verb of that clause, “have had,” and this version is basically grammatically correct.
However, this version expresses the nonsensical meaning that the officials have confidence “with” many of the deer that have had young,” as if officials are somehow “with” many of the deer in having confidence that the area is sufficiently populated. We could say that this version seems to convey that both the officials and the deer have confidence.
Notice also that the wording “officials … have confidence that the area” is somewhat idiomatically incorrect. The ideal phrasing to use in this context is “are confident that.”
(C) Because of many of the deer in the southeastern section of the state that has had young, officials at the wildlife department, confident that the area is sufficiently populated with deer.
This version starts off illogically, as, rather than express that the officials are confident because many deer have had young, it appears to convey that “Because of many of the deer” the officials are confident, providing no clear logical connection between the existence of the deer and the confidence of the officials.
Also, the singular “that has” could be perceived as referring to and modifying the singular “state.” However, since logic dictates that it is the deer, rather than the state, that have had young, “that has had young” must modify “many of the deer,” which is plural, and, therefore, doesn’t agree with “that has had young.”
Also, this version has no main verb.
(D) Many of the deer in the southeastern section of the state has had young; therefore, officials at the wildlife department have confidence in the area that is sufficiently populated with deer.
In the first clause, the plural subject, “many of the deer,” does not agree with the singular main verb, “has had.”
Also, when this answer choice is combined with the non-underlined portion, the second clause, “officials have confidence in the area that is sufficiently populated with deer,” does not work logically with the first clause, since which area is “the area” is not made clear. Is “the area” the southwestern portion of the state?
Also, the idea that officials “have confidence in” the area is not fully logical, as “to have confidence in” generally means to somehow trust someone or something. While it’s possible that officials could somehow “have confidence in” an area, how that idea would apply in this context is not clear.
YOUR CHOICE(E) As many of the deer in the southeastern section of the state have had young, officials at the wildlife department are confident that that area is sufficiently populated with deer.
While the use of “as” in this context is not as good as the use of “since” or “because,” the use of “as” is acceptable. In fact, “as” is used in this correct version to make us question whether this version is correct.
Also, the use of the odd sounding “that that,” although correct, is meant to make this version seem possibly incorrect.
All the same, this version is correct.
The plural subject “many of the deer” agrees with the plural “have had,” and this version effectively expresses a meaning that makes sense.
Correct Answer: (E) Some indefinite pronouns are sometimes singular and sometimes plural.
summary:
Lesson Content Some indefinite pronouns are always plural and therefore always take a plural verb.
These indefinite pronouns include the following: “both,” “few,” “many,” and “several.”
Be sure to memorize these plural indefinite pronouns.
💡MUST KNOW:
A plural indefinite pronoun always takes a plural verb. These pronouns include the following: “both,” “few,” “many,” and “several.”
In most cases, it is intuitive to pair a plural verb with a plural indefinite pronoun.
title: 3.19 Plural Indefinite Pronouns

contents:
Lesson Content Some indefinite pronouns can be singular or plural depending on their referents.
Such indefinite pronouns include “some,” “any,” “none,” “all,” “more,” and “most.”
You can remember this list by using the acronym SANAMM, which consists of the first letters of the pronouns in the list, “Some,” “Any,” “None,” “All,” “More,” and “Most.” These indefinite pronouns are often referred to as the SANAMM pronouns.
The rules of usage of these pronouns are simple:
When a SANAMM pronoun refers to a singular noun, it takes a singular verb. When a SANAMM pronoun refers to a plural noun, it takes a plural verb. Let’s consider some example sentences:
All of the class was present yesterday.
All of the students want the teacher to extend the deadline.
Both of the above sentences are correct.
In the second sentence, the same indefinite pronoun, “all,” refers to the plural noun “students.” Therefore, “all” takes the plural verb “want.”
💡MUST KNOW:
The SANAMM indefinite pronouns, “some,” “any,” “none,” “all,” “more,” and “most,” can be singular or plural depending on their referents.
In the section titled “Placing a Prepositional Phrase Between the Subject and the Verb,” you learned the following:
Although we’ll see a few important exceptions, a good general strategy when checking for subject-verb agreement is to ignore prepositional phrases found between a subject and its verb.
Notice that, when one of the indefinite pronouns “some,” “any,” “none,” “all,” “more,” or “most” appears at the beginning of a subject, we can’t ignore a prepositional phrase that comes between the pronoun and the clause’s main verb.
💡MUST KNOW:
When a subject begins with one of the indefinite pronouns “some,” “any,” “none,” “all,” “more,” or “most,” we can’t ignore the prepositional phrase that comes between the subject and the verb.
For example:
Some of the birds were already sleeping in their nests.
Some of the water is still in the lake.
Notice that in the first sentence, the plural “were” is correct, since the subject is “some of the birds.” In other words, since we have the plural noun “birds” in the prepositional phrase following “some,” we need a plural verb.
In the second sentence, notice that we have “some of the water,” and since the noun “water” is singular, we need the singular verb “is.”
Now consider the following two sentences:
Most of the time that they had for working on their projects was instead spent socializing with one another.
Most of the projects that the class had neglected while socializing were turned in late.
Unlike in the sentences in the previous example, in these sentences, the noun and verb are not next to each other.
In the first example, the plural noun “projects” is followed by the singular verb “was,” which wording may sound strange.
To more easily see the relationship between the subject and verb, let’s cross off the relative clause:
Most of the time that they had for working on their projects was instead spent socializing with one another.
Notice that, with the relative clause crossed off, we can more clearly see that the indefinite pronoun “most” goes with the noun “time,” and that, since “time” is a singular noun, we need the singular verb “was.”
In the second sentence, on the other hand, the subject, “most of the projects,” requires the plural verb “were,” since the noun “projects” is plural.
Most of the projects that the class had neglected while socializing were turned in late.
Let’s look at a final sentence:
None of the members of the organization care about securing increased funding for school sports, because funding for arts programs has yet to return to previous levels.
Although the indefinite pronoun “none” is followed by two prepositional phrases, “of the members” and “of the organization,” for the purposes of subject-verb agreement, we are concerned only with the noun that is closest to the indefinite pronoun, the noun to which the indefinite pronoun refers.
None of the organization’s members care about securing increased funding for school sports, because funding for arts programs has yet to return to previous levels.
The meaning is unchanged, but we can see that the verb “care” clearly goes with “members” and not with “organization’s.” Remember that we must read Sentence Correction questions carefully and holistically, paying close attention to what’s said.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Select the correct verb form for each pronoun given its referent. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Now, let’s apply our ever more sophisticated subject-verb agreement skills to finding the correct answer to a Sentence Correction question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 21 Because of the extreme difference between the pressure deep underwater and the pressure at high elevation, the leader of the expeditions are ensuring that none of the people who dove this morning goes mountain climbing this afternoon. 1. [ ] (A) expeditions are ensuring that none of the people who dove this morning goes > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) expeditions is ensuring that none of the people who dove this morning go 3. [ ] (C) expeditions, who is ensuring that none of the people who dove this morning go > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) expeditions, ensuring that none of the people diving this morning and going > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) expeditions is ensuring that none of the people dove this morning while going > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy The underlined portion of the sentence contains finite verbs of multiple clauses.
One of those clauses is the main clause of the sentence, which, in the correct version, is the following:
“the leader of the expeditions is ensuring that …”
The subject of the above clause is the singular “leader,” which requires the singular verb “is.”
Another clause is the noun clause that is the object of the first clause, which in the correct version is:
that none of the people who dove this morning go mountain climbing …
The subject of the above clause is “none of the people.” “None” can be plural or singular, depending on what it refers to. In this case, “none” refers to the plural “people.” So, the main verb of the clause must also be plural. So, the plural “go” is correct.
(A) Because of the extreme difference between the pressure deep underwater and the pressure at high elevation, the leader of the expeditions are ensuring that none of the people who dove this morning goes mountain climbing this afternoon.
This answer is a trap, because it is created to look as if “expeditions” is the subject and “are” is the main verb of a clause. The truth is that the subject of the clause is the singular “leader,” and so “are” is incorrect.
Also, the subject of the noun clause “that none of the people who dove this morning goes …” is “none of the people,” which requires a plural verb. Therefore, the singular verb “goes” is incorrect.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Because of the extreme difference between the pressure deep underwater and the pressure at high elevation, the leader of the expeditions is ensuring that none of the people who dove this morning go mountain climbing this afternoon.
In this version, the singular subject “leader” is correctly paired with the singular verb “is,” and the plural subject “none of the people” is correctly paired with the plural “go.”
(C) Because of the extreme difference between the pressure deep underwater and the pressure at high elevation, the leader of the expeditions, who is ensuring that none of the people who dove this morning go mountain climbing this afternoon.
This version does not include a main verb. It begins with a phrase, “Because of the extreme difference …,” which is followed by the noun phrase “the leader of the expedition” and then a relative clause, “who is ensuring ….” So, this version does not include an independent clause and, thus, is not a complete sentence.
(D) Because of the extreme difference between the pressure deep underwater and the pressure at high elevation, the leader of the expeditions, ensuring that none of the people diving this morning and going mountain climbing this afternoon.
This version does not include a main verb. It begins with a phrase, “Because of the extreme difference …,” which is followed by the noun phrase “the leader of the expedition” and then another phrase, “ensuring ….” In other words, since “ensuring” cannot on its own serve as a main verb, this version has no main verb and, thus, is not a complete sentence.
(E) Because of the extreme difference between the pressure deep underwater and the pressure at high elevation, the leader of the expeditions is ensuring that none of the people dove this morning while going mountain climbing this afternoon.
This version is grammatically correct. The singular subject “leader” is correctly paired with the singular verb “is,” and the plural subject “none of the people” is correctly paired with the plural “dove.”
However, this version conveys a nonsensical meaning, as it does not make sense that the leader of the expeditions would ensure that people didn’t dive in the morning while they were going mountain climbing in the afternoon. That people would dive while mountain climbing does not seem to make sense, and that people would engage in activities in the morning and in the afternoon simultaneously does not make sense at all.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss subject-verb agreement involving numbers, fractions, and percentages.
summary:
Lesson Content Some indefinite pronouns can be singular or plural depending on their referents.
Such indefinite pronouns include “some,” “any,” “none,” “all,” “more,” and “most.”
You can remember this list by using the acronym SANAMM, which consists of the first letters of the pronouns in the list, “Some,” “Any,” “None,” “All,” “More,” and “Most.” These indefinite pronouns are often referred to as the SANAMM pronouns.
The rules of usage of these pronouns are simple:
title: 3.20 Other Indefinite Pronouns – Sometimes Singular, Sometimes Plural

contents:
Lesson Content Let’s begin our discussion of subject-verb agreement involving numbers, fractions, percentages by discussing subjects that include the word “number.”
In a sentence subject, the word “number” can be preceded by either “the” or “a.”
When, in a subject, “number” is preceded by “the,” the sentence is about the number itself.
The number of students who apply for summer internships has been increasing.
An official count revealed that the number of people who attended the show was 3521.
In each of the examples above, “the number of” is used, because each sentence is about a particular number.
In contrast, when, in a subject, “number” is preceded by “a,” the sentence is about a number of things, and so the subject is plural, as are the subjects in the following examples.
A number of students have applied for summer internships at the Rainforest Alliance.
We noticed that a small number of the people seeking admission were using counterfeit tickets.
In each of the examples above, “number” is preceded by “a.” So, in these examples “number” acts like a collective noun that is followed by “of” + a plural noun, which is a plural subject.
Notice that the above two sentences are not focused on the numbers themselves.
Now, let’s consider a more challenging example:
The number of convicted felons who return to prison after completing their sentences, whether re-incarcerated only months after being released or several years later, has not declined in decades despite increasingly harsh sentencing practices and the implementation of mandatory minimums.
In this rather long sentence, a long relative clause separates the phrase “the number of convicted felons” from the singular verb that goes with it, “has.” Notice that, although we use the plural verb “return” with the plural noun “felons” within the relative clause “who return to prison …,” we still use the singular verb “has” with the singular subject “the number of convicted felons.”
Of course, crossing off the prepositional phrase “of convicted felons” and the relative clause can help us see the correct subject-verb agreement:
The number of convicted felons who return to prison after completing their sentences, whether re-incarcerated only months after being released or several years later, has not declined in decades despite increasingly harsh sentencing practices and the implementation of mandatory minimums.
Let’s consider a final example:
A number of simple, cost-effective upgrades to the building, including the installation of new windows and the use of compact fluorescent lighting in large common spaces as well as individual offices, all but ensure that the company’s energy costs will be lower this year.
Once again, we have a series of phrases that separate the subject, “a number of … upgrades,” from the plural verb that goes with it, “ensure.” In considering subject-verb agreement in a sentence like this, it helps to first identify any dependent phrases that can be ignored.
A number of simple, cost-effective upgrades to the building, including the installation of new windows and the use of compact fluorescent lighting in large common spaces as well as individual offices, all but ensure that the company’s energy costs will be lower this year.
💡MUST KNOW:
Subjects that begin with “the number of” take singular verbs. Subjects that begin with “a number of” take plural verbs.
Let’s now discuss subject-verb agreement involving the word “percentage.”
summary:
Lesson Content Let’s begin our discussion of subject-verb agreement involving numbers, fractions, percentages by discussing subjects that include the word “number.”
In a sentence subject, the word “number” can be preceded by either “the” or “a.”
When, in a subject, “number” is preceded by “the,” the sentence is about the number itself.
The number of students who apply for summer internships has been increasing.
An official count revealed that the number of people who attended the show was 3521.
title: 3.21 Subject-Verb Agreement Involving Numbers, Fractions, and Percentages

contents:
Lesson Content Like “number,” “percentage,” in a sentence subject, can be preceded by either “the” or “a.”
When, in a subject, “percentage” is preceded by “the,” the sentence is about the percentage itself, and so the subject is singular, as are the subjects in the following examples.
The percentage of people in America who do note vote is astounding.
Most experts believed that the percentage of cars represented by the electric car category was going to increase rapidly.
In each of the examples above, “the percentage of” is used, because each sentence is conveying something about a percentage, which is, of course, a singular subject.
Note that the presence of “the percentage of” does not affect a verb contained within a relative clause modifying a noun in the subject.
💡MUST KNOW:
A subject that begins with “the percentage of” takes a singular verb.
In contrast, when, in a subject, “percentage” is preceded by “a,” the sentence is focused not on the percentage itself but rather on what the percentage is a percentage of.
Consider the following examples:
A percentage of the water is not contaminated.
A percentage of the lakes are relatively unpolluted.
The first sentence is about “a percentage of the water.” Since “water” is singular, the subject, “a percentage of the water,” is paired with the singular “is.”
The second sentence is about “a percentage of the lakes.” Since “lakes” is plural, the subject is paired with the plural “are.”
💡MUST KNOW:
A subject that begins with “a percentage of” takes a verb that agrees in number with what the percentage is a percentage of.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice In each case, determine whether the verb should be singular or plural. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
OK, now let’s apply our skills to answering a Sentence Correction question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 22 While the number of milkweed plants available to monarch butterflies have decreased with increased herbicide use, an increase in the percentage of gardeners surveyed who indicate that they are planting native milkweed to help with addressing the issue. 1. [ ] (A) butterflies have decreased with increased herbicide use, an increase in the percentage of gardeners surveyed who indicate > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) butterflies has been decreased from increased herbicide use, an increase in the percentage of gardeners surveyed indicates > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) butterflies has been decreasing by increasing herbicide use, as an increasing percentage, the gardeners surveyed indicate > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) butterflies has decreased with increased herbicide use, an increasing percentage of gardeners who are surveyed indicate 5. [ ] (E) butterflies have decreased by increased herbicide use, the increasing percentage of gardeners surveyed indicate > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium The correct version of the sentence consists of a subordinate clause, “while the number … has …,” and an independent clause, “an increasing percentage of gardeners … indicate ….”
The subject of the subordinate clause is “the number of milkweed plants.” Since “the number” is always singular, in the correct version, the main verb of the subordinate clause is the singular “has decreased.”
In the correct version, the subject of the independent clause is “an increasing percentage of gardeners.” Since this subject begins with “an … percentage,” what the percentage is a percentage of determines whether the subject is singular or plural. Since the percentage is a percentage of gardeners, which is plural, the use of the plural verb “indicate” is correct.
Meanwhile, since there are in this question multiple answer choices that are basically grammatically correct, once we determine which choices have subjects and verbs that agree, we have to use meaning to find our way to the one correct answer.
(A) While the number of milkweed plants available to monarch butterflies have decreased with increased herbicide use, an increase in the percentage of gardeners surveyed who indicate that they are planting native milkweed to help with addressing the issue.
The singular subject “the number of milkweed plants” is incorrectly paired with the plural verb “have.”
Also, this version has no independent clause. It begins with a subordinate clause, “while the number … have …,” and then what follows is simply a phrase that begins with “an increase” and includes a relative clause, “who indicate …,” but does not include a main verb or independent clause.
(B) While the number of milkweed plants available to monarch butterflies has been decreased from increased herbicide use, an increase in the percentage of gardeners surveyed indicates that they are planting native milkweed to help with addressing the issue.
While the singular subject “the number of milkweed plants” is correctly paired with the singular verb “has,” the wording “has been decreased from increased herbicide use” conveys an unclear and not quite logical meaning.
How exactly would a “number” have “been decreased from increased herbicide use”? Since “from” means, basically, “starting at,” if anything, this version seems to convey the nonsensical meaning that “the number” started at “increased herbicide use” and “has been decreased from” there.
The sentence might almost make sense if “by” were used instead of “from,” as in, “the number … has been decreased by increased herbicide use,” but, as written, this version does not convey anything logical.
Also, the second clause conveys the illogical meaning that an increase in the percentage of gardeners surveyed (as in, more gardeners are surveyed than were surveyed previously), indicates that they are planting native milkweed to help with addressing the issue. How would the fact that the percentage of gardeners who are surveyed has increased indicate that gardeners are planting native milkweed? It wouldn’t.
(C) While the number of milkweed plants available to monarch butterflies has been decreasing by increasing herbicide use, as an increasing percentage, the gardeners surveyed indicate that they are planting native milkweed to help with addressing the issue.
The singular subject “the number of milkweed plants” is correctly paired with the plural verb “has been decreasing,” and this version is otherwise grammatically correct. However, we have to be careful not to be tricked into choosing this choice by the grammatical correctness of this version.
Notice that “the number of milkweed plants … has been decreasing by increasing herbicide use” conveys the nonsensical idea that the number of milkweed plants has been decreasing itself by increasing herbicide use.
Also, while “the gardeners” is correctly paired with the plural verb “indicate,” “as an increasing percentage, the gardeners surveyed” suggests the nonsensical meaning that all of the gardeners surveyed are “an increasing percentage.” How would gardeners surveyed be an increasing percentage? They wouldn’t, and even if they would, the sentence doesn’t say what the gardeners surveyed are an increasing percentage of.
So, this version is an illogical mess.
YOUR CHOICE(D) While the number of milkweed plants available to monarch butterflies has decreased with increased herbicide use, an increasing percentage of gardeners who are surveyed indicate that they are planting native milkweed to help with addressing the issue.
The singular subject “the number of milkweed plants” is correctly paired with the singular verb “has,” the plural subject “an increasing percentage of gardeners” is correctly paired with the plural verb “indicate,” and the sentence is otherwise grammatically correct and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(E) While the number of milkweed plants available to monarch butterflies have decreased by increased herbicide use, the increasing percentage of gardeners surveyed indicate that they are planting native milkweed to help with addressing the issue.
The singular subject “the number of milkweed plants” is incorrectly paired with the plural verb “have.”
Also, by using the preposition “by,” the sentence seems to suggest the nonsensical idea that the number of milkweed plants has decreased itself “by” increased herbicide use.
Finally, the singular “the increasing percentage” is incorrectly paired with the plural “indicate.”
Correct Answer: (D)
summary:
Lesson Content Like “number,” “percentage,” in a sentence subject, can be preceded by either “the” or “a.”
When, in a subject, “percentage” is preceded by “the,” the sentence is about the percentage itself, and so the subject is singular, as are the subjects in the following examples.
The percentage of people in America who do note vote is astounding.
Most experts believed that the percentage of cars represented by the electric car category was going to increase rapidly.
title: 3.22 Subject-Verb Agreement Involving the Word “Percentage”

contents:
Lesson Content We have discussed that subjects that begin with the SANAMM pronouns can be singular or plural depending on the nouns to which the pronouns refer.
Consider the following examples:
Half of the class is going on a field trip.
One-third of the students have shown some interest in geology.
In the first sentence, the fraction “half” is part of a single entity, “the class.” Therefore, the subject, “half of the class,” takes a singular verb, “is.”
In the second sentence also, we have a fraction, “one-third,” but the fraction is a fraction of a plural noun, “students.” Therefore, the subject, “one-third of the students,” must take a plural verb, “have.”
Notice that, in these situations involving fractions, we cannot ignore the prepositional phrase that comes after the fraction in the subject.
💡MUST KNOW:
A subject that begins with a fraction such as “half” or “one-fourth” can take a singular or plural verb depending on what the fraction is a fraction of. When a subject begins with a fraction, we cannot ignore a prepositional phrase that comes between the fraction and the main verb.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Choose the correct singular or plural verb. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Now, let’s answer a Sentence Correction question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 23 Whereas not even one-hundredth of the worldwide fleet of vehicles on the roads is made up of electric vehicles, in Norway, by 2018, one-tenth of the cars on the road were electric. 1. [x] (A) Whereas not even one-hundredth of the worldwide fleet of vehicles on the roads is made up of electric vehicles, in Norway, by 2018, one-tenth of the cars on the road were 2. [ ] (B) While not even one-hundredth of the worldwide fleet of road vehicles makes up electric vehicles, in Norway, as of 2018, one-tenth of the cars on the road were once > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Since not even one-hundredth of the worldwide fleet of vehicles on the roads is made up of electric vehicles, in Norway, by 2018, one-tenth of the cars on the road had been > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Though not even one-hundredth of the worldwide fleet of road vehicles are made up of electric vehicles, in Norway, as of 2018, one-tenth of the cars on the road was > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) With not even one-hundredth of the worldwide fleet of road vehicles making up electric vehicles, in Norway, since 2018, one-tenth of the cars on the road has been > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium Every version of the sentence in this question includes two fractions that function as the subjects of clauses.
The first fraction is “one-hundredth of the worldwide fleet of vehicles on the roads.” Since the fraction “one hundredth” is a fraction of the singular “fleet,” this fraction takes a singular verb. Notice, this fraction is not a fraction of the vehicles on the roads. “Vehicles on the roads” is a part of a prepositional phrase, “of the vehicles on the roads,” that modifies “fleet.”
The second fraction is “one-tenth of the cars on the road.” Since the fraction “one-tenth” is a fraction of the plural “cars,” this fraction takes a plural verb.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) Whereas not even one-hundredth of the worldwide fleet of vehicles on the roads is made up of electric vehicles, in Norway, by 2018, one-tenth of the cars on the road were electric.
The singular “is” agrees with the singular “one-hundredth of the worldwide fleet,” the plural “were” agrees with the plural “one-tenth of the cars on the road,” and this version conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(B) While not even one-hundredth of the worldwide fleet of road vehicles makes up electric vehicles, in Norway, as of 2018, one-tenth of the cars on the road were once electric.
While the singular “makes” agrees with the singular “one-hundredth of the worldwide fleet,” and the plural “were” agrees with the plural “one-tenth of the cars on the road,” the meaning conveyed by this version does not make sense.
First this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that one-hundredth of the fleet of vehicles makes up electric cars, as if electric cars are made of a fleet of vehicles.
Then, by using the wording “were once,” this version conveys the illogical idea that one-tenth of the cars on the road in Norway “were once” electric, implying that the same cars are no longer electric.
(C) Since not even one-hundredth of the worldwide fleet of vehicles on the roads is made up of electric vehicles, in Norway, by 2018, one-tenth of the cars on the road had been electric.
This version is basically grammatically correct but conveys a meaning that does not make sense.
First the conjunction “since” indicates that what’s expressed by the second clause is the effect of what’s expressed in the first clause, but it does not make sense that one-tenth of the cars on the roads in Norway would be electric “since” not even one-hundredth of the worldwide fleet of vehicles is made up of electric vehicles.
Also, this version expresses that the cars in Norway “had been” electric, as if the cars were electric and then changed to not electric.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Though not even one-hundredth of the worldwide fleet of road vehicles are made up of electric vehicles, in Norway, as of 2018, one-tenth of the cars on the road was electric.
The plural “are” does not agree with the singular “one-hundredth of the fleet.”
Also, the singular “was” does not agree with the plural “one-tenth of the cars.”
(E) With not even one-hundredth of the worldwide fleet of road vehicles making up electric vehicles, in Norway, since 2018, one-tenth of the cars on the road has been electric.
The singular “has been” does not agree with the plural “one-tenth of the cars.”
Also, the beginning of this sentence conveys a meaning that does not make sense, that the fleet of vehicles “make up” electric vehicles, as if electric vehicles are made of a fleet of vehicles.
Also, the use of “with” at the beginning of the sentence does not make sense, as the sentence mentions nothing that is “with” anything else.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss subject-verb agreement and the word “percent.”
summary:
Lesson Content We have discussed that subjects that begin with the SANAMM pronouns can be singular or plural depending on the nouns to which the pronouns refer.
Consider the following examples:
Half of the class is going on a field trip.
One-third of the students have shown some interest in geology.
In the first sentence, the fraction “half” is part of a single entity, “the class.” Therefore, the subject, “half of the class,” takes a singular verb, “is.
title: 3.23 Subject-Verb Agreement Involving Fractions

contents:
Lesson Content The rules we follow in situations in which portions are described via the use of the word “percent” are similar to the ones we follow in situations in which subjects involve fractions.
Consider the following examples:
Ninety percent of the board was present.
Fifty percent of the board members are in favor of the acquisition.
Notice that the first sentence is about “ninety percent” of “the board.” Since “the board” is singular, “ninety percent of the board” agrees with the singular verb “was.”
The second sentence, on the other hand, is about “fifty percent” of “the board members.” Since “board members” is plural, we need the plural verb “are.”
💡MUST KNOW:
In a situation involving a subject that is a portion described via the use of the word “percent,” whether the subject is singular or plural depends on whether what the portion is a portion of is singular or plural.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Determine whether the singular verb or the plural verb agrees with the subject in each clause. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s take these concepts for a spin in a Sentence Correction question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 24 While close to 80 percent of the survey respondents indicate that they take calls while driving, only about half of the respondents believe that their doing so does not negatively impact their driving performance. 1. [x] (A) While close to 80 percent of the survey respondents indicate that they take calls while driving, only about half of the respondents believe that their 2. [ ] (B) Close to 80 percent of respondents to the survey indicates taking calls while driving, though only about half believes that > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) With close to 80 percent of the survey respondents who indicate that they take calls while driving, only about half of the respondents believe that > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Although close to 80 percent of the respondents to the survey indicate taking calls while driving, only about half of them believes that their > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Though there are close to 80 percent of the respondents to the survey who indicate having taken calls while driving, only about half believes > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium In the various versions of the sentence, two portions are mentioned, “80 percent of those responding to the survey” and “half of the respondents.” While the wording changes somewhat from version to version, in all versions, both portions are portions of plural entities, “those responding” and “respondents.” So, both portions are considered plural subjects.
YOUR CHOICE(A) While close to 80 percent of the survey respondents indicate that they take calls while driving, only about half of the respondents believe that their doing so does not negatively impact their driving performance.
The plural subject “80 percent of those responding” is correctly paired with the plural “indicate,” and the plural “half of the respondents” is correctly paired with the plural “believe,” and the sentence conveys a meaning that makes sense.
Notice that the possibly unfamiliar wording “their doing so” is used to make this correct answer seem a little off.
(B) Close to 80 percent of respondents to the survey indicates taking calls while driving, though only about half believes that doing so does not negatively impact their driving performance.
The plural subject “80 percent of respondents” is incorrectly paired with the singular “indicates.”
Also, in the second clause, the singular “believes” does not agree with “half,” even though “half” may seem to be singular, because “half” is understood to mean “half of the respondents,” which is plural.
(C) With close to 80 percent of the survey respondents who indicate that they take calls while driving, only about half of the respondents believe that doing so does not negatively impact their driving performance.
While this version is basically grammatically correct, it does not convey a logical meaning, as it conveys that half of the respondents believe that taking calls while driving does not negatively affect their driving performance “with” 80 percent of the respondents, and it does not make sense that half of the respondents are “with” 80 percent of the respondents.
In order not to be fooled into choosing a choice like this one, we have to be careful to go beyond checking for grammar issues to considering the entire version of the sentence and the meaning that it conveys.
(D) Although close to 80 percent of the respondents to the survey indicate taking calls while driving, only about half of them believes that their doing so does not negatively impact their driving performance.
The plural “half of the them” is incorrectly paired with the singular “believes.”
Also, “indicate taking calls while driving” does not convey a clear meaning. Are the respondents indicating that they are “taking calls while driving,” that others are “taking calls while driving”? What exactly do they indicate about taking calls while driving?
(E) Though there are close to 80 percent of the respondents to the survey who indicate having taken calls while driving, only about half believes doing so does not negatively impact their driving performance.
The plural “indicate” agrees with what the relative pronoun “who” refers to, the plural “respondents.”
However, the first clause, “there are close to 80 percent of the respondents who indicate …,” conveys the illogical meaning that close to 80 percent of the respondents who indicate having taken calls while driving simply exist. In other words, “there are close to 80 percent of the respondents” means, basically, that 80 percent of the respondents exist.
Also, in the second clause, the singular “believes” does not agree with “half,” even though “half” may seem to be singular, because “half” is understood to mean “half of the respondents,” which is plural.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss some unusual singular and plural verb forms.
summary:
Lesson Content The rules we follow in situations in which portions are described via the use of the word “percent” are similar to the ones we follow in situations in which subjects involve fractions.
Consider the following examples:
Ninety percent of the board was present.
Fifty percent of the board members are in favor of the acquisition.
Notice that the first sentence is about “ninety percent” of “the board.” Since “the board” is singular, “ninety percent of the board” agrees with the singular verb “was.
title: 3.24 Subject-Verb Agreement and the Word “Percent”

contents:
Lesson Content In English, nouns that end in -s are often plural.
1 Datum/Data: While “datum” and “data” are, strictly speaking, the singular and plural of “datum,” usage has evolved, and currently, “data” can be used to convey a singular meaning or a plural meaning.
2 Medium/Media: When “media” refers to the press, it is often considered a collective noun that takes a singular verb (e.g., “The media is out in full force”).
Let’s look at some examples of these words in sentences:
An alumnus receives 10 percent off of all costs of admission to university events.
The alumni receive 10 percent off of all costs of admission to university events.
The criterion for the competition is too strict.
The criteria for the competition are too strict.
The syllabus is very detailed.
The syllabi are very detailed.
Furthermore, there are some nouns that appear to be plural, but are actually singular.
Mathematics is my worst subject.
Good news makes me smile.
Diabetes is on the rise in the United States.
The mumps is a disease that affects only humans.
Note that some of these nouns that appear to be plural are fields of study that end in –ics, such as “politics,” “economics,” “ethics,” “statistics,” “linguistics,” and the aforementioned “mathematics.” When used to denote a branch of study, these nouns are always singular.
His politics get in the way of his personal relationships.
There aren’t any statistics to bolster the claim.
The ethics of eating meat are a primary concern for many vegetarians and vegans.
The economics of restaurant ownership make success a one-in-a-million shot.
💡MUST KNOW:
Commit to memory these unusual singular and plural forms.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Select the correct singular or plural verb forms. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Now, let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Now, let’s apply our knowledge of unusual singular and plural forms as we look for the correct answer to the following question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 25 For many years, few considered the possible disease that occurs when cell nuclei is not functioning well, because people thought nuclei was so important for cell function that cells without properly functioning nuclei would simply die. 1. [ ] (A) the possible disease that occurs when cell nuclei is not functioning well, because people thought nuclei was > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) the possibility that diseases occur when cell nuclei are not functioning well, because people thought nuclei to be 3. [ ] (C) the disease that occurs, possibly when cell nuclei are not functioning well, because of people thinking that nuclei were > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) diseases possibly occurring when cell nuclei was not functioning well, because people thinking about nuclei were > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) diseases that possibly occur when cell nuclei is not functioning well, because of people who were thinking that nuclei is > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium A key element of correctly answering this question is understanding that “nuclei” is not a singular noun. We don’t necessarily have to choose a choice on the basis of whether a verb agrees with “nuclei,” but if we were to mistakenly think that “nuclei” is a singular noun, then we would rule out the correct answer, in which “nuclei” is the subject of and is paired with a plural verb in a clause.
Meanwhile, some of the incorrect choices in this question have subject-verb agreement errors, and all of the incorrect choices convey illogical or nonsensical meanings.
(A) For many years, few considered the possible disease that occurs when cell nuclei is not functioning well, because people thought nuclei was so important for cell function that cells without properly functioning nuclei would simply die.
Since “nuclei” is plural, neither “nuclei is” nor “nuclei was” is correct, since “is” and “was” are singular.
Also, this version does not convey a clear, logical meaning. For one thing, what “few considered the possible disease that occurs” means is not clear, as it does not clearly convey in what sense or in what context people were not considering the disease.
Also, “the possible disease that occurs” is not logical. If the disease occurs, then it is clearly possible, so saying that the disease is a possible disease that occurs does not make sense.
So, this version is written to sound as if it says something but actually doesn’t convey much of anything.
YOUR CHOICE(B) For many years, few considered the possibility that diseases occur when cell nuclei are not functioning well, because people thought nuclei to be so important for cell function that cells without properly functioning nuclei would simply die.
In this version, the plural “nuclei” is correctly paired with the plural verb “are.”
Also “few considered the possibility that” makes sense, and this version correctly uses the idiomatic expression “thought … to be” to clearly convey a logical meaning.
(C) For many years, few considered the disease that occurs, possibly when cell nuclei are not functioning well, because of people thinking that nuclei were so important for cell function that cells without properly functioning nuclei would simply die.
In this version, the plural “nuclei” is correctly paired with the plural “are” and “were.”
However, this version conveys a nonsensical meaning, that few people considered the disease that occurs because of people thinking that nuclei were important.
(D) For many years, few considered diseases possibly occurring when cell nuclei was not functioning well, because people thinking about nuclei were so important for cell function that cells without properly functioning nuclei would simply die.
In this version, the plural “nuclei” is incorrectly paired with the singular “was.”
Also, this sentence does not convey a logical meaning, the most blatant meaning issue being the fact that the sentence conveys that people thinking about nuclei were so important for cell function that cells without properly functioning nuclei would simply die.
(E) For many years, few considered diseases that possibly occur when cell nuclei is not functioning well, because of people who were thinking that nuclei is so important for cell function that cells without properly functioning nuclei would simply die.
In this version, the plural “nuclei” is twice incorrectly paired with the singular “is.”
Also, because this version uses the wording “because of people who were thinking that nuclei is so important,” this version is not quite logical, as it does not make sense that few people considered something because of people who were thinking something. The point to be made is that few people considered something about certain diseases because people in general were thinking something else about those diseases.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss some nontraditional subjects.
summary:
Lesson Content In English, nouns that end in -s are often plural.
1 Datum/Data: While “datum” and “data” are, strictly speaking, the singular and plural of “datum,” usage has evolved, and currently, “data” can be used to convey a singular meaning or a plural meaning.
2 Medium/Media: When “media” refers to the press, it is often considered a collective noun that takes a singular verb (e.g., “The media is out in full force”).
title: 3.25 Unusual Singular and Plural Forms

contents:
Lesson Content In some cases, the subjects of sentences aren’t simple nouns.
Let’s take a look at some examples:
To catch trains is difficult at certain times of the day.
Swimming is good exercise.
What I want is for you to be healthy.
In the first sentence, the subject is “to catch trains,” which is an infinitive.
In the second sentence, the subject is “Swimming,” a gerund, which is a verb acting as a noun and ending in -ing.
In the third sentence, the noun clause “what I want” acts as the subject.
Note that, in all of these sentences, the nontraditional subjects take singular verbs.
💡MUST KNOW:
Subjects in the forms of infinitives, gerunds, and noun phrases/clauses always take singular verbs.
Let’s look at a few more examples:
Riding in a car without a seatbelt on increases the likelihood of suffering injuries during a crash.
The nontraditional subject is the gerund “riding,” which takes the singular verb “increases.”
Failing one test is not the end of the world.
The subject is the gerund “failing,” which takes the singular “is.”
Where I spend my afternoons is none of your business.
The subject is “Where I spend my afternoons,” a noun clause, which takes the singular “is.”
To know you is to love you.
The subject is the infinitive “To know you,” which takes the singular verb “is.”
That you have two dogs and four cats is not a problem for me.
The noun clause “that you have two dogs and four cats” is the subject and takes the singular verb “is.”
That the Gentle Barn had gained the respect of animal lovers across the United States was no surprise.
The noun clause “that the Gentle Barn had gained the respect of animal lovers across the United States” is the subject of the sentence and takes the singular verb “was.”
Whether the Holy Grail actually exists has yet to be determined.
The noun clause “whether the Holy Grail actually exists” is the subject and takes the singular verb “has.”
To train for ultra-marathons during the winter months is to spend many hours running alone in the cold, but the rewards can be sweet.
The infinitive “to train for ultra-marathons during the winter months” is the subject and requires the singular verb “is.”
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Select the correct verb form for each nontraditional subject. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Now, here’s a Sentence Correction question with a nontraditional subject.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 26 While foraging for plant-based foods such as berries and kelp is not the primary means by which polar bears feed themselves, consuming these foods, which, though not calorie rich, contain key nutrients, is beneficial to the bears’ health. 1. [x] (A) While foraging for plant-based foods such as berries and kelp is not the primary means by which polar bears feed themselves, consuming these foods, which, though not calorie rich, contain key nutrients, is 2. [ ] (B) With foraging for plant-based foods such as berries and kelp not being primarily a means by which polar bears feed themselves, consuming these foods, though not calorie rich, containing key nutrients > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Although foraging for plant-based foods such as berries and kelp are not the primary means by which polar bears feed themselves, consuming these foods, which, though not calorie rich, contain key nutrients > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Even though foraging for plant-based foods such as berries and kelp is not primarily the means by which polar bears feed themselves, consuming these foods, though not calorie rich, contains key nutrients that are > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Though foraging for plant based-foods such as berries and kelp are not a primary means by which polar bears feed themselves, consuming these foods, which, though not calorie rich, provide key nutrients, are > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium In all of the versions of the sentence in this question, there are multiple clauses.
The subject of the first clause in most versions is “foraging,” which is a nontraditional subject that requires a singular verb. We have to be careful not to be confused by the presence of the prepositional phrase “for plant-based foods such as berries and kelp” into thinking that the verb that goes with “foraging” should be plural.
Some of the other clauses that appear are the following:
the bears’ consuming these foods … is
by which polar bears feed themselves
bears consuming these foods … are
which, though not calorie rich, contain …”
To answer this question correctly, we have to notice subject-verb agreement issues in any of the clauses.
We also have to pay careful attention to meaning conveyed.
YOUR CHOICE(A) While foraging for plant-based foods such as berries and kelp is not the primary means by which polar bears feed themselves, consuming these foods, which, though not calorie rich, contain key nutrients, is beneficial to the bears’ health.
The verb in the first clause, “is,” agrees with the nontraditional subject “foraging for plant-based foods such as berries and kelp.”
Also, the verb in the main clause, “is,” agrees with the subject, “consuming these foods.”
So, this version is grammatically correct, and it effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(B) With foraging for plant-based foods such as berries and kelp not being primarily a means by which polar bears feed themselves, consuming these foods, though not calorie rich, containing key nutrients beneficial to the bears’ health.
In this version, “not being primarily a means” conveys an illogical meaning. The point to be made is not that foraging for plant-based foods is not primarily a means for polar bears to feed themselves but, rather, primarily something else. The point is that foraging for plant-based foods is not the primary means by which polar bears feed themselves.
Meanwhile, since the only finite verb in this version, “feed,” is within the relative clause “by which polar bears feed themselves,” this version has no main verb and, thus, no independent clause. So, this version is not a complete sentence.
(C) Although foraging for plant-based foods such as berries and kelp are not the primary means by which polar bears feed themselves, consuming these foods, which, though not calorie rich, contain key nutrients beneficial to the bears’ health.
In this version, the plural “are” is incorrectly used with “foraging.”
Notice also that this version includes only a subordinate clause, “although foraging for plant-based foods such as berries and kelp are not the primary means by which polar bears feed themselves,” a phrase, “consuming these foods,” and a relative clause, “which, though not calorie rich, contain key nutrients beneficial to the bears’ health.” So, this version does not include an independent clause and, thus, is not a complete sentence.
(D) Even though foraging for plant-based foods such as berries and kelp is not primarily the means by which polar bears feed themselves, consuming these foods, though not calorie rich, contains key nutrients that are beneficial to the bears’ health.
This version is grammatically correct but does not convey a meaning that makes sense.
In this version, the verb in the first clause, “is,” agrees with “foraging.”
However, “is not primarily the means” conveys an illogical meaning. The point to be made is not that foraging for plant-based foods is not primarily the means for polar bears to feed themselves but, rather, primarily something else. The point is that foraging for plant-based foods is not the primary means by which polar bears feed themselves.
Furthermore, “consuming these foods, though not calorie rich, contains key nutrients” conveys the nonsensical meaning that “consuming these foods” is not calorie rich but contains key nutrients. Of course, the foods themselves, not consuming the foods, would be not calorie rich but contain nutrients.
(E) Though foraging for plant based-foods such as berries and kelp are not a primary means by which polar bears feed themselves, consuming these foods, which, though not calorie rich, provide key nutrients, are beneficial to the bears’ health.
In the first clause of this version, the plural “are” does not agree with the singular subject “foraging for plant-based foods.”
Also, in the main clause, the plural verb “are” does not agree with the singular subject “consuming.”
Correct Answer: (A)
summary:
Lesson Content In some cases, the subjects of sentences aren’t simple nouns.
Let’s take a look at some examples:
To catch trains is difficult at certain times of the day.
Swimming is good exercise.
What I want is for you to be healthy.
In the first sentence, the subject is “to catch trains,” which is an infinitive.
In the second sentence, the subject is “Swimming,” a gerund, which is a verb acting as a noun and ending in -ing.
title: 3.26 Nontraditional Subjects

contents:
Lesson Content As we already know, a pronoun is a word that takes the place of a noun.
💡MUST KNOW:
A pronoun is a word that takes the place of a noun. We use pronouns so that we don’t have to repeat nouns in sentences.
For example:
Sara is a skilled artist, and she consistently produces intriguing work.
The pronoun “she” takes the place of the noun “Sara.” We refer, then, to “Sara” as the antecedent (or the referent) of the pronoun “she.” Having the pronoun “she” available allows us not to have to repeat the noun “Sara.”
So, pronouns give us flexibility in writing.
However, errors in pronoun usage are common, and such errors significantly erode the clarity of sentences, and flawed pronoun usage is common in Sentence Correction questions.
As this chapter unfolds, we’ll examine a number of pronoun usage issues that commonly appear in Sentence Correction questions.
summary:
Lesson Content As we already know, a pronoun is a word that takes the place of a noun.
💡MUST KNOW:
A pronoun is a word that takes the place of a noun. We use pronouns so that we don’t have to repeat nouns in sentences.
For example:
Sara is a skilled artist, and she consistently produces intriguing work.
The pronoun “she” takes the place of the noun “Sara.” We refer, then, to “Sara” as the antecedent (or the referent) of the pronoun “she.
title: 4.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content As we now know, an antecedent, or referent, is the noun to which a pronoun refers.
💡MUST KNOW:
An antecedent, or referent, is the noun to which a pronoun refers. A pronoun can refer to a noun that comes either before or after it in a sentence.
Of course, in order for a sentence to effectively convey a meaning, it must be clear which noun is the antecedent of each pronoun in the sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
In order for a sentence to effectively convey a meaning, it must be clear which noun is the antecedent of each pronoun in the sentence.
Let’s look at a sentence featuring a pronoun with multiple possible antecedents:
Incorrect: In a fit of anger, Jane poured the water from the vase and threw it.
To check for any errors involving a pronoun, we first identify the pronoun and then identify any possible nouns (antecedents) to which the pronoun could refer.
“It” is the only pronoun in the sentence.
To clear up this issue, we could eliminate the pronoun or one of the possible antecedents.
In a fit of anger, Jane poured the water from the vase and threw the vase.
Replacing the pronoun “it” with its intended antecedent, “the vase,” eliminates any confusion as to what Jane threw.
Alternatively, we could rephrase the sentence and, thus, clear up the pronoun-antecedent issue without using repetitious language:
In a fit of anger, Jane poured the water from and threw the vase.
💡MUST KNOW:
To check for pronoun-antecedent errors, when there is a pronoun in a sentence, identify its possible antecedents.
Let’s look at another example sentence:
Incorrect: Maria texted Marisol many times while she was in Los Angeles, .
Let’s identify the possible referents of the pronoun “she.” There are two possible referents: “Maria” and “Marisol.” Thus, the sentence does not clearly convey who was in Los Angeles.
A correct version of the sentence could read:
While Maria was in Los Angeles, she texted Marisol many times.
Now the sentence makes clear who was in Los Angeles.
You may be worried about the repetition of nouns in sentences.
💡MUST KNOW:
If repetition of a noun is required to clarify the meaning of a sentence, the repetition is not considered redundancy on the GMAT.
Let’s look at some more challenging examples.
Incorrect: The collapse of the country’s economy has resulted in a tightening of the military’s grip on key resources such as gold and oil, although the extraction and export of it has slowed to a trickle.
We might guess, given the wording “slowed to a trickle,” that the pronoun “it” refers to “oil.” However, the truth is that “it” could refer to “gold” or “oil,” because the extraction and export of either gold or oil could “slow to a trickle.” So, we must eliminate the problem of multiple possible antecedents, possibly by repeating the word “oil.”
The collapse of the county’s economy has resulted in a tightening of the military’s grip on key resources such as gold and oil, although the extraction and export of oil has slowed to a trickle.
Now the sentence makes clear that the extraction and export of oil, not gold, is what has slowed.
Here’s another one:
Incorrect: The foundation was set up to fund expeditions for researching the lifestyles of gorillas, as they have been becoming rather rare.
Are “expeditions” becoming rather rare, or are “gorillas”? While we might be tempted to decide that “they” refers to “gorillas,” in a Sentence Correction question, a version like this sentence would be considered incorrect, since which noun “they” refers to isn’t one-hundred percent clear.
A possible correction is:
The foundation was set up to fund expeditions for researching the lifestyles of gorillas, as such expeditions have been becoming rather rare.
By replacing the pronoun with a noun, we have created a sentence that conveys a clear meaning.
Let’s consider one more:
Incorrect: Recently, anthropologists found people of a nomadic tribe living in an area that had been considered uninhabitable by many of them.
In this example, the pronoun “them” could conceivably refer to either of two plural nouns, “anthropologists” or “people.” Since it’s not clear which noun is the antecedent, the GMAT would prefer another version of the sentence.
A possible preferred version is:
Recently, anthropologists found people of a nomadic tribe living in an area that had been widely considered uninhabitable.
In this version, there is no pronoun-antecedent issue because the pronoun “them” is not even used.
Notice that the two versions in this last example don’t convey the exact same meaning, and that what makes the preferred version preferred is simply that it effectively conveys a clear meaning.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 While many of the plane’s passengers felt that they had received the wrong meals because of errors made by flight attendants, the airline didn’t receive any complaints about them. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 Paris is a beautiful city, and London has stunning architecture; I can’t wait to visit it! 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 3 This year, a record number of women are vying for the governorship, and they are making a strong showing in the primaries. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 After the president met with the ambassador, he decided to rewrite his speech. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 5 While reports from people involved in the relief effort are being discussed in detail in many news publications, the information that they have been providing has been insufficient for making a determination regarding how well the crisis is being managed. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 6 There will be sunshine for the entire afternoon, so let’s make the most of it by having a picnic in the park. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
The pronoun “them” has three possible antecedents: “meals,” “errors,” and “flight attendants.” Thus, this sentence does not effectively convey a clear meaning. A corrected version might read:
While many of the plane’s passengers felt that they had gotten the wrong meals because of errors made by flight attendants, the airline didn’t receive any complaints about the errors.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
The pronoun “it” has two possible antecedents: “Paris” and “London.” From the context, a reader might decide that “it” refers to London. Really though, whether “it” refers to “Paris” or “London” is not clear. A correct version is:
While Paris is a beautiful city, I can’t wait to visit London, which has stunning architecture.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
The pronoun “they” has only one possible antecedent: the plural noun “women.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
The two pronouns “he” and “his” have two possible antecedents: “president” and “ambassador.” Thus, the sentence does not clearly indicate who decided to rewrite whose speech.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
The pronoun “they” could logically refer to “reports,” “people,” or “news publications.” One version that the GMAT would prefer is the following, which makes clear that the information has been provided by the people involved in the relief efforts:
While reports from people involved in the relief effort are being discussed in detail in many news publications, the information provided by these individuals has been insufficient for making a determination regarding how well the crisis is being managed.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
The pronoun “it” could refer to either “sunshine” or “afternoon.”
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Now, let’s consider some Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 1 Some state governors have suggested that federal regulators are using excessive enforcement to punish anyone not in favor of certain policies. 1. [x] (A) that federal regulators are using excessive enforcement to punish anyone not in favor of certain 2. [ ] (B) that federal regulators are using excessive enforcement to punish, because they are not in favor of certain of their > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) that federal regulators use excessive enforcement as punishment for anyone not in favor of certain of their > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) federal regulators, using excessive enforcement as punishment for not being in favor of certain > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) that federal regulators are using excessive enforcement, thus punishing them for not being in favor of certain > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy In this question, the lack of clear relationships between the pronouns “they,” “them,” and “their” and their antecedents results in sentence versions that don’t convey clear meanings. It’s not clear whose policies the policies are or who is punishing whom.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Some state governors have suggested that federal regulators are using excessive enforcement to punish anyone not in favor of certain policies.
This version clearly conveys that what the governors have suggested is that the regulators are punishing anyone not in favor of certain policies.
(B) Some state governors have suggested that federal regulators are using excessive enforcement to punish, because they are not in favor of certain of their policies.
This version starts off OK, but, since it’s not clear whether “they” and “their” refer to “governors” or “regulators,” the sentence does not clearly convey who is not in favor of the policies or whose policies they are.
(C) Some state governors have suggested that federal regulators use excessive enforcement as punishment for anyone not in favor of certain of their policies.
This version starts off unclearly, as it’s not quite clear whether the governors are suggesting that the regulators are using enforcement as punishment or that the regulators should use enforcement as punishment. Then, since “their” could refer to either “governors” or “regulators,” the meaning conveyed by this version becomes even more unclear.
(D) Some state governors have suggested federal regulators, using excessive enforcement as punishment for not being in favor of certain policies.
This choice might look OK in isolation. However, when we combine this choice with the non-underlined portions, we see that this version conveys the illogical meaning that the governors have suggested federal regulators, whatever that even means.
Furthermore, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that, when they have “suggested federal regulators,” the governors have been using excessive enforcement as punishment.
So, even though this version contains no pronouns, it’s definitely not the correct answer.
(E) Some state governors have suggested that federal regulators are using excessive enforcement, thus punishing them for not being in favor of certain policies.
Since the antecedent of “them” is not clear, this version could convey either that the governors are punishing the regulators or that the regulators are punishing the governors.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 2 The heat shedding design of the “cool roof” of this house makes it better suited for warmer climates than for cooler ones. 1. [ ] (A) The heat shedding design of the “cool roof” of this house makes it > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) This house, with its “cool roof,” designed to shed heat, making it > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) On this house is a “cool roof,” which is designed to shed heat, and it is > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Since the roof of this house is a “cool roof,” designed to shed heat, > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Because it has a “cool roof,” designed to shed heat, this house is View Solution Question Level: easy In the original version of the sentence, the pronoun “it” could refer to either “roof” or “house.” The correct version more clearly indicates which of these two things is meant to be described as better suited for warmer climates than for cooler ones.
(A) The heat shedding design of the “cool roof” of this house makes it better suited for warmer climates than for cooler ones.
The meaning conveyed by this version is unclear, as the pronoun “it” could refer to either “roof” or “house.”
(B) This house, with its “cool roof,” designed to shed heat, making it better suited for warmer climates than for cooler ones.
The meaning of this version is rather muddled, and the wording is awkward. It is not clear whether “it” refers to “house” or “roof,” so what makes what better suited for warmer climates than cooler ones is not clear.
Also, this version contains no main verb. Without a main verb, there can be no independent clause, and we can’t have a sentence without an independent clause.
(C) On this house is a “cool roof,” which is designed to shed heat, and it is better suited for warmer climates than for cooler ones.
The meaning conveyed by this version is unclear, as the pronoun “it” could refer to either “roof” or “house.”
(D) Since the roof of this house is a “cool roof,” designed to shed heat, better suited for warmer climates than for cooler ones.
Since this version contains no pronouns, it cannot have any pronoun related issues. However, it also has no independent clause and, thus, is not a complete sentence.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Because it has a “cool roof,” designed to shed heat, this house is better suited for warmer climates than for cooler ones.
In this version, “it” clearly refers to “house,” as the sentence mentions nothing else that would have a “cool roof,” and this version is otherwise correct and effectively conveys a clear meaning.
Correct Answer: (E) To complete our discussion of pronouns that have multiple possible referents, let’s address a concept that sometimes creates confusion: meaning can indicate the referent of a pronoun.
summary:
Lesson Content As we now know, an antecedent, or referent, is the noun to which a pronoun refers.
💡MUST KNOW:
An antecedent, or referent, is the noun to which a pronoun refers. A pronoun can refer to a noun that comes either before or after it in a sentence.
Of course, in order for a sentence to effectively convey a meaning, it must be clear which noun is the antecedent of each pronoun in the sentence.
title: 4.2 Pronoun Issue One: Multiple Possible Antecedents

contents:
Lesson Content Sometimes, even when there are multiple nouns to which a pronoun in a sentence could conceivably refer, which noun is the referent of the pronoun may be clear because meaning indicates which noun the pronoun refers to.
💡MUST KNOW:
Sometimes, even when there are multiple nouns to which a pronoun in a sentence could conceivably refer, which noun is the referent of the pronoun may be clear because meaning indicates which noun the pronoun refers to.
Consider the following example:
After the antique clock that had been sitting on the shelf collecting dust was cleaned and adjusted, it worked well, telling the time perfectly and chiming every half hour.
This sentence includes the pronoun “it,” which could conceivably refer to “clock,” “shelf,” or “dust.” However, is the meaning of the above sentence ambiguous? Let’s consider the sentence logically.
Neither a shelf nor dust would tell the time or chime every half hour.
Here’s another example:
Since the musicians have gotten great reviews and have been invited to perform at multiple festivals, they are quite happy with the progress that they have made.
The pronoun “they” appears in the above example, and there are in the sentence three nouns, “musicians,” “reviews,” and “festivals,” to which “they” could conceivably refer.
So, before we decide that a pronoun lacks a clear antecedent, we must take care to holistically evaluate the sentence, checking to determine whether the logic and meaning of the sentence make clear to which noun the pronoun refers.
💡MUST KNOW:
Before we decide that a pronoun lacks a clear antecedent, we must take care to holistically evaluate the sentence, checking to determine whether the logic and meaning of the sentence make clear to which noun the pronoun refers.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 After the children packed their backpacks and put on their coats, they set out on a ten-mile hike to the campsite. 1.
