[x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 The auto enthusiasts were excited to take the restored car out on the road and see how well it would drive. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 Although the hungry hikers wanted to eat beans, avocados, and nachos at their favorite restaurant, it was closed, so they instead settled on sharing a hummus platter at one that was open. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 Because it is the fastest ever produced, the TechX SUV has earned numerous awards from the Sports Car and Drivers Association. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 Numerous pots were found in some rooms of the palaces of the sultans who ruled over the people of the Ottoman Empire, and it is believed that they stored rosewater in them. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 6 While they were faded and had holes in them, the jeans that Ally and her sister purchased were just what they wanted. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
Even though the plural pronoun “they” could refer to “children,” “backpacks,” or “coats,” since the likelihood that the author of this sentence meant to convey that backpacks or coats set off on a hike is approximately zero, it’s clear that “they” refers to children, and thus this sentence has no pronoun reference ambiguity issues.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
Although the pronoun “it” could in theory refer to either “car” or “road,” since it’s abundantly clear that the auto enthusiasts were not wondering how well the road would drive, in this sentence, “it” would logically refer only to “car.” So, this sentence has no pronoun reference ambiguity issues.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
Although, the pronouns “their” and “they” could in theory refer to “beans,” “avocados,” “nachos,” or “hikers,” it is abundantly clear that the only noun in the sentence with the ability to have a favorite restaurant or share a hummus platter is “hikers.” Thus, “their” and “they” must refer to hikers.
Also, although the pronoun “one” could in theory refer to “restaurant” or “a hummus platter,” the sentence makes it abundantly clear that “one” refers to the noun “restaurant.” After all, it’s illogical to say, “sharing a hummus platter at a hummus platter that was open.” So, this sentence has no pronoun reference ambiguity issues.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
Although the pronoun “it” could refer to either the “TechX Suv” or the “Sports Car and Drivers Association,”it is abundantly clear that “it” refers to the TechX Suv because an association would not be “the fastest ever produced.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
While it seems likely that “they” refers to “sultans” and that “them” refers to “pots,” “they” could also refer to “people,” and “them” could also refer to “rooms” or “palaces.” So, this sentence does not effectively convey a clear meaning.
A correct version is:
Numerous pots were found in some rooms of the palace of the sultans who ruled over the people of the Ottoman Empire, and it is believed that the sultans stored rosewater in those pots.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
The pronouns “they” and “them” at the beginning of the sentence must refer to “the jeans,” since it’s the jeans, and not Ally and her sister, that would have been faded and had holes in them. In addition, the “they” at the end of the sentence clearly refers to “Ally and her sister” since “jeans” would not have “wanted” jeans.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s move on to the next type of pronoun issue: the missing antecedent.
summary:
Lesson Content Sometimes, even when there are multiple nouns to which a pronoun in a sentence could conceivably refer, which noun is the referent of the pronoun may be clear because meaning indicates which noun the pronoun refers to.
💡MUST KNOW:
Sometimes, even when there are multiple nouns to which a pronoun in a sentence could conceivably refer, which noun is the referent of the pronoun may be clear because meaning indicates which noun the pronoun refers to.
title: 4.2.1 Meaning Can Indicate the Referent of a Pronoun.

contents:
Lesson Content Whereas some sentences will feature a pronoun with multiple possible antecedents, as we saw in the previous section, others will lack an antecedent altogether.
Of course, as we know, each pronoun in a sentence must have one clear antecedent.
💡MUST KNOW:
We may encounter sentences that feature a pronoun but lack an antecedent for that pronoun. Answer choices offering such sentences will be incorrect.
Consider the following example:
Incorrect: The orchard’s production is high, but selling them is easy.
In this sentence, there is no noun for the pronoun “them” to replace.
The sentence could be made more effective via the addition of an antecedent:
The orchard’s production of apples is high, but selling them is easy.
Including the noun “apples” provides an antecedent for “them” and results in a sentence that conveys a clear meaning.
Alternatively, we could improve the sentence by replacing the pronoun with a noun:
The orchard’s production is high, but selling the apples is easy.
Let’s look at another example sentence:
Incorrect: Unlike some other schools in the area, the public high school actively encourages its students to seriously pursue extracurricular activities, even requiring students to choose it at the beginning of each semester.
There are two pronouns in the sentence above: “its” and “it.” The first pronoun, “its,” refers to “high school,” but the pronoun “it” has no noun that it replaces.
Unlike some other schools in the area, the public high school actively encourages its students to seriously pursue extracurricular activities, even requiring students to choose an activity at the beginning of each semester.
We have all heard of the infamous “they.” The infamous “they” do all kinds of things.
In each of these cases, who exactly “they” are is not made clear.
Consider the following example:
Incorrect: After they discovered gene expression, great progress was made in developing understanding of how to prevent and solve health issues.
While one could make the case that perhaps “they” actually does refer to some specific group of people, the truth is that, in this context, “they” doesn’t seem to refer to anyone in particular.
After the discovery of gene expression, great progress was made in developing understanding of how to prevent and solve health issues.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences would likely be considered an incorrect version or a correct version in a Sentence Correction question. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 Watching the cricket game was an enjoyable experience, as was cheering for them to win. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 Before they invented the electric starter, a driver would start a car’s motor by using a hand crank. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 3 The animals that the students saw during the trip through the rainforest were amazing, but it took longer than they had expected. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 Previously, the animals of only a few species were known to be able to count objects, but now it is substantial. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 5 Years of people’s smoking left Grand Central Terminal’s ceiling covered in dark residue, its magnificent color and designs obscured like that, until the terminal was restored in the 1990s. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 6 The mayor wants the contractors installing the new bike paths to finish before the start of the festival, but there is no practical way for them to do this. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
The sentence mentions no people to which “them” could refer. The GMAT would prefer the following version:
Watching the players in the cricket game was an enjoyable experience, as was cheering for them to win.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
The sentence does not include a referent for “they,” and likely there is not any actual group of people to whom “they” truly refers. A preferred version would be:
Prior to the invention of the electric starter, a driver would start a car’s motor by using a hand crank.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
In this sentence, “it” logically refers to “trip,” and “they” logically refers to “students.”
Notice also that, since it’s clear that it wasn’t the rainforest that “took longer,” there is no ambiguity regarding what “it” refers to. “It” must refer to “trip,” not “rainforest.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
There is no referent for “it.” The GMAT would prefer a version like the following:
Previously, animals of only a few species were known to be able to count objects, but now the number of species whose members are known to exhibit this skill is substantial.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
The pronoun “that” has no referent, as the sentence mentions nothing that the obscured color and designs were like. A corrected version of the sentence is: Years of people’s smoking left Grand Central Terminal’s ceiling covered in dark residue, its magnificent color and designs obscured, until the terminal was restored in the 1990s.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
There is no clear referent for the pronoun “this.” So, while “do this” is often used in common speech, the GMAT would probably prefer a different version. Honestly, Sentence Correction question writers differ in their opinions of what is acceptable. So, we can’t be 100 percent sure that “do this” will never appear in a correct answer. However, probably the GMAT would prefer the following version, in which “this” is replaced with “so.”
The mayor wants the contractors installing the new bike paths to finish before the start of the festival, but there is no practical way for them to do so.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s do some Concept Mastery:
Now, let’s practice noticing pronouns without logical antecedents in Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 3 Skeptics contend that, though it often has a mundane cause, seemingly supernatural phenomena are easily explained in scientific terms. 1. [ ] (A) that, though it often has a mundane cause, seemingly supernatural phenomena are > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) that any phenomenon that is seemingly supernatural often have mundane causes and are > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) that seemingly supernatural phenomena often have mundane causes that can be 4. [ ] (D) that it often has a mundane cause if it is seemingly supernatural phenomena, as those are > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) that they often have mundane causes, but a seemingly supernatural phenomenon is > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) Skeptics contend that, though it often has a mundane cause, seemingly supernatural phenomena are easily explained in scientific terms.
The singular pronoun “it” has no antecedent in the version created with this choice. To what does “it” refer in the sentence? There is no singular noun in the sentence to which “it” could possibly refer. The two contenders, “supernatural phenomena” and “skeptics,” are both plural nouns (and we would never refer to “skeptics,” who are people, with the pronoun “it”).
Since this version has a pronoun that is missing an antecedent, this choice cannot possibly be correct. Notice also that the use of the contrast word “though” does not make sense in this context because there is no contrast between having a mundane cause and being easily explained in scientific terms. Those two things are perfectly in line with each other. (B) Skeptics contend that any phenomenon that is seemingly supernatural often have mundane causes and are easily explained in scientific terms.
The singular noun “phenomenon” is incorrectly paired with the plural verbs “have” and “are” in the version created with this choice.
To more clearly see this error, we can cross off the relative clause that appears between the noun and verbs:
Skeptics contend that any phenomenon that is seemingly supernatural often have mundane causes and are easily explained
“Phenomenon have … and are” is not grammatically correct. “Phenomenon has … as is” would be correct.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Skeptics contend that seemingly supernatural phenomena often have mundane causes that can be easily explained in scientific terms.
This choice is the only one that creates a sentence version that is grammatically correct and conveys a clear meaning. Notice that this version eliminates the mismatched pronoun issues we see in some of the other choices. Furthermore, this version correctly pairs the plural noun “phenomena” with the plural verb “have.”
(D) Skeptics contend that it often has a mundane cause if it is seemingly supernatural phenomena, as those are easily explained in scientific terms.
The singular pronoun “it” and singular verbs “has” and “is” do not properly pair with the plural noun “phenomena.” We need the plural pronoun “they” and the plural verbs “have” and “are” to pair with “phenomena.” Notice that there is no singular antecedent in the sentence to which “it” and the associated verbs could refer.
Notice that, if you weren’t sure whether “phenomena” is singular or plural, you could still eliminate this choice because, if “phenomena” is plural, then “it,” “has,” and “is” do not agree with “phenomena,” and if “phenomena” were singular, then “those” would not agree with it.
Additionally, even with the correct pronouns and verbs to refer to “phenomena,” the sentence would convey a circular and illogical meaning. It does not make sense to say that seemingly supernatural phenomena often have a mundane cause if they are seemingly supernatural phenomena. The phenomena either are or aren’t “seemingly supernatural.” (E) Skeptics contend that they often have mundane causes, but a seemingly supernatural phenomenon is easily explained in scientific terms.
The plural pronoun “they” does not have a logical antecedent in the sentence. “They” cannot refer to the singular “phenomenon.” We would need the plural “phenomena” to refer to “they.”
If we were to believe that “they” is supposed to refer to the plural noun “skeptics,” the sentence would be saying that “skeptics often have mundane causes.” However, while it could make sense to say that skepticism or skeptical beliefs “have mundane causes,” it doesn’t make sense to say that people are caused by something, mundane or otherwise.
Furthermore, the conjunction “but” is used to connect contrasting statements. However, neither meaning of “they” would create a statement that contrasts with the fact that “a seemingly supernatural phenomenon is easily explained in scientific terms.” So, the use of “but” does not make sense in this context.
With respect to both grammar and meaning, there is no way to make heads or tails of the version created with this choice.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 4 While the shares have been valued at all-time highs in recent weeks, they expect it to take a dip before reaching a new peak around the holidays. 1. [ ] (A) they expect it > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) it is expected > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) they expect the stock > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) investors expect it > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) the stock is expected View Solution Question Level: medium (A) While the shares have been valued at all-time highs in recent weeks, they expect it to take a dip before reaching a new peak around the holidays.
The pronoun “they” has no clear and logical antecedent. Given the context “they expect it,” “they” conveys a nonsensical meaning if it refers to the plural noun “shares,” “highs,” or “weeks.”
Even if we assume that “they” refers to “the infamous ‘they’” – some general “they” outside the sentence – we are still left with another problem: the antecedent of the pronoun “it” is missing from the sentence. Notice that the singular pronoun “it” cannot possibly refer to the plural noun “shares.” Logically, the “it” that would “take a dip” would be the stock whose “shares have been valued at all-time highs in recent weeks,” but the singular noun “stock” (or another appropriate singular noun) does not appear anywhere in the sentence.
Since the GMAT does not prefer sentences that use “the infamous ‘they’,” and since the pronoun “it” has no antecedent, this choice cannot be correct.
(B) While the shares have been valued at all-time highs in recent weeks, it is expected to take a dip before reaching a new peak around the holidays.
The antecedent of the pronoun “it” is missing from the sentence. Logically, the “it” that would “take a dip” would be the stock whose “shares have been valued at all-time highs in recent weeks,” but the singular noun “stock” (or another appropriate singular noun) does not appear anywhere in the sentence.
Since the sentence contains a pronoun that is missing an antecedent, this choice is not correct.
(C) While the shares have been valued at all-time highs in recent weeks, they expect the stock to take a dip before reaching a new peak around the holidays.
The pronoun “they” has no clear and logical antecedent. Given the context “they expect the stock,” “they” conveys a nonsensical meaning if it refers to the plural noun “shares,” “highs,” or “weeks.”
Even if we assume that “they” refers to “the infamous ‘they’” – some general “they” outside the sentence – we know that the GMAT does not prefer sentences that use “the infamous ‘they’.” Thus, we should look for a version of the sentence that conveys a clearer meaning.
YOUR CHOICE(D) While the shares have been valued at all-time highs in recent weeks, investors expect it to take a dip before reaching a new peak around the holidays.
The antecedent of the pronoun “it” is missing from the sentence. Notice that the singular pronoun “it” cannot possibly refer to the plural noun “shares.” Logically, the “it” that would “take a dip” would be the stock whose “shares have been valued at all-time highs in recent weeks,” but the singular noun “stock” (or another appropriate singular noun) does not appear anywhere in the sentence.
Since the sentence contains a pronoun that is missing an antecedent, this choice is not correct.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) While the shares have been valued at all-time highs in recent weeks, the stock is expected to take a dip before reaching a new peak around the holidays.
This version is the only one that eliminates pronouns with unclear or no antecedents and conveys a clear, logical meaning.
Instead of using the pronoun “it,” which has no singular noun to replace in the sentence, this version uses the noun “the stock.”
Notice that it does not really matter who is expecting the stock to take a dip, only that “the stock is expected to take a dip.” Thus, this version avoids the problem that other versions have of using “they” with no clear and logical antecedent.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 5 Numerous anecdotal reports indicate that squirrels that would attempt to enter an attic by chewing through eaves can be prevented from doing so via use of a humane repellent. 1. [x] (A) that squirrels that would attempt to enter an attic by chewing through eaves can be prevented from doing so via use of a humane repellent 2. [ ] (B) that, when squirrels attempt to enter an attic by chewing through eaves, it can be prevented through the use of a humane repellent > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) that, if squirrels attempted to enter an attic by chewing through eaves, the use of a humane repellent can prevent them from doing that > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) that squirrels that attempt to enter an attic by chewing through eaves can be prevented by using a humane repellent > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) that, while squirrels may attempt to enter an attic by chewing through eaves, this can be prevented if a humane repellent is used > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard YOUR CHOICE(A) Numerous anecdotal reports indicate that squirrels that would attempt to enter an attic by chewing through eaves can be prevented from doing so via use of a humane repellent .
This version conveys the logical meaning that, via the use of a humane repellant, squirrels that would attempt to enter an attic can be prevented from attempting to enter that attic.
Notice that the use of “doing so” allows the sentence to avoid using a pronoun such as “that” in an expression such as “doing that.”
Since there is no pronoun in this version, there is no need for there to be a referent for a pronoun.
(B) Numerous anecdotal reports indicate that, when squirrels attempt to enter an attic by chewing through eaves, it can be prevented through the use of a humane repellent .
“When squirrels attempt to enter …” is a description of a point in time “when” something happens. So, this sentence conveys the illogical meaning that, at a point in time, “it” can be prevented. Since, obviously, a point in time would not be prevented, “it” has no logical antecedent. Notice that nothing that would be prevented is directly mentioned before “it.”
(C) Numerous anecdotal reports indicate that, if squirrels attempted to enter an attic by chewing through eaves, the use of a humane repellent can prevent them from doing that .
This version conveys the illogical meaning that, if an event, “squirrels attempted to enter,” occurred in the past, the use of repellent can “prevent them from doing that,” as if, had squirrels not attempted to enter, the use of repellent would not “prevent them from doing that” in the future.
Also, since something that squirrels would do in the future is not directly mentioned, the sentence includes no logical referent for “that” in “prevent them from doing that.”
(D) Numerous anecdotal reports indicate that squirrels that attempt to enter an attic by chewing through eaves can be prevented by using a humane repellent .
This version conveys the illogical meaning that squirrels themselves can be prevented. It’s the actions of squirrels, such as squirrels’ attempts to enter an attic, that can be prevented, not squirrels.
Also, the meaning conveyed by “can be prevented by using a humane repellent” does not quite make sense. Something would be “prevented by a repellent” or “prevented through the use of a repellent,” but it wouldn’t be “prevented by using a repellent.” “Using X” conveys how someone does something, not what does it.
(E) Numerous anecdotal reports indicate that, while squirrels may attempt to enter an attic by chewing through eaves, this can be prevented if a humane repellent is used .
There is no antecedent for the pronoun “this.” Notice that the sentence does not directly mention something that “can be prevented.” “While squirrels may attempt X” is not something that would be prevented.
Also, “this can be prevented if a humane repellent is used” does not clearly convey that the repellent would prevent the occurrence in question. Rather, it conveys that the occurrence can be prevented “if a humane repellent is used,” as if something else would prevent the occurrence if the repellent were used. This meaning is not quite logical.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 6 The company reported that it had received many inquiries about the new pea protein product, but few of them followed up their inquiries by making purchases. 1. [ ] (A) but few of them followed up their inquiries by > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) but few of those inquiries by people were followed up by > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) but, of those, few were followed up by people 4. [ ] (D) but few of them followed it up by > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) but few people who inquired followed it up by > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard (A) The company reported that it had received many inquiries about the new pea protein product, but few of them followed up their inquiries by making purchases.
The pronoun “them” has no logical antecedent. The only plural noun to which “them” could conceivably refer is “inquiries,” and inquiries would not have “followed up their inquiries.”
We can tell that “them” is supposed to refer to the people who made the inquiries, but the people who made the inquiries are not mentioned.
(B) The company reported that it had received many inquiries about the new pea protein product, but few of those inquiries by people were followed up by making purchases.
While this choice itself may appear to be correct, when we combine this choice with the non-underlined portion “making purchases,” we see that this version conveys a nonsensical meaning.
“Few of those inquiries … were followed up by making purchases” suggests that inquiries would be followed up by making purchases, as if somehow, inquiries have the ability to make purchases, and by making purchases, inquiries would be followed up.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) The company reported that it had received many inquiries about the new pea protein product, but, of those, few were followed up by people making purchases.
Both “those” and “few” logically refer to “inquiries,” and this version is grammatically correct and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(D) The company reported that it had received many inquiries about the new pea protein product, but few of them followed it up by making purchases.
The pronoun “them” has no logical antecedent. The only plural noun to which “them” could conceivably refer is “inquiries,” and inquiries would not have “followed … up by making purchases.” We can tell that “them” is supposed to refer to the people who made the inquiries, but the people who made the inquiries are not mentioned.
Also, this version includes no logical referent for the pronoun “it.” The sentence says only, “few of them inquired” and does not mention an “inquiry” or something else that few of them followed up and that “it” would refer to. Since it does not make sense that someone would have followed up “the company” or “the new pea protein product,” “it” doesn’t logically refer to either of those singular nouns.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The company reported that it had received many inquiries about the new pea protein product, but few people who inquired followed it up by making purchases.
This version includes no logical referent for the pronoun “it.” The sentence says only that people “inquired” and does not mention an “inquiry” or something else that people would follow up and that “it” would refer to. Since people would not follow up “the company” or “the new pea protein product,” “it” doesn’t logically refer to either of those singular nouns.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss recognizing that a sentence is flawed because a pronoun has an adjective as its antecedent.
summary:
Lesson Content Whereas some sentences will feature a pronoun with multiple possible antecedents, as we saw in the previous section, others will lack an antecedent altogether.
Of course, as we know, each pronoun in a sentence must have one clear antecedent.
💡MUST KNOW:
We may encounter sentences that feature a pronoun but lack an antecedent for that pronoun. Answer choices offering such sentences will be incorrect.
Consider the following example:
title: 4.3 Pronoun Issue Two: A Pronoun’s Antecedent Is Missing from the Sentence

contents:
Lesson Content Pronouns cannot take adjectives as their antecedents.
💡MUST KNOW:
An adjective can’t be the antecedent of a pronoun.
Sometimes in a sentence, a word that could serve as the antecedent of a pronoun is present but acting, not as a noun, but as an adjective and, therefore, cannot function logically as the antecedent of a pronoun in that sentence.
Consider the following example:
Incorrect: The number of grams of Colombian gold sold annually has increased each year since 2005, a fact demonstrating buyer confidence in the quality of the gold produced there.
The pronoun in this sentence is “there.” Does the pronoun have an antecedent? If we don’t read carefully enough, it may appear that “Colombian” is the antecedent.
The following version of the sentence conveys a clear meaning:
The number of grams of gold from Colombia sold annually has increased each year since 2005, a fact demonstrating buyer confidence in the quality of the gold produced there.
Now the pronoun “there” has the antecedent “Colombia.”
Consider the following sentence:
Incorrect: I wish the bread section were at the front of the store, so that I could purchase it more conveniently.
Clearly, the intended antecedent of the pronoun “it” is “bread.” In other words, the speaker used the pronoun “it” to avoid repeating “bread.” However, the speaker made a small yet crucial error.
So then, is the word “section” the antecedent of the pronoun “it?” No, the word “section” cannot be the antecedent because shoppers do not purchase sections of the grocery store (particularly not conveniently).
I wish the bread section were at the front of the store, so that I could purchase bread more conveniently.
Alternatively, we could remove the noun that the hidden antecedent is modifying so that the antecedent is no longer hidden:
I wish the bread section were at the front of the store, so that I could purchase it more conveniently.
Removing the noun “section” from the sentence allows “bread” to function as a noun rather than as an adjective, making “bread” the clear and logical antecedent of the pronoun “it.”
Let’s look at some more examples and seek to pinpoint the errors in logic and meaning that reveal faulty antecedents because pronouns are attempting to take adjectives as their antecedents.
Incorrect: Jason was nervous about the chemistry exam because it was not his best subject.
A chemistry exam is not a subject in school.
Jason was nervous about the chemistry exam because chemistry was not his best subject.
Here’s another:
Incorrect: When Lena visits New York City, she always attends a Broadway show, even though it’s a touristy section of Manhattan.
Is it logical to call a Broadway show a “section of Manhattan?” The touristy section to which “it” is supposed to refer is Broadway itself.
In the following version, the problem is solved:
When Lena visits New York City, she always attends a Broadway show, even though Broadway is a touristy section of Manhattan.
Now Broadway, not a show, is correctly identified as the section of Manhattan.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 Not far from our house is a vegetable stand, and they are priced quite reasonably there. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 While the pilot was excited to see the collection of classic planes, he was reluctant to fly in any of them. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 While doing farm work, David Elliott, of Australia, found fossilized dinosaur bones, and he could tell that it had been a big one. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 4 The freight train that derailed was made up mostly of coal cars, and tons of it was spilled all over the area near the tracks. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
While “they” must refer to a noun, not only is “vegetable” not plural, but also “vegetable” modifies “stand” and, therefore, is not a noun and cannot be the antecedent of “they.” One possible correct version is:
Not far from our house is a vegetable stand, and the vegetables are priced quite reasonably there.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
Although “planes” is part of a prepositional phrase “of classic planes,” which modifies “collection,” since “planes” functions as a noun, it can serve as the referent of the pronoun “them.” Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
While we can tell that what had been a big one was a dinosaur, the sentence is not effective, as there is no clear referent for “it,” and thus the sentence does not clearly indicate what had been a big one. A better version is:
While doing farm work, David Elliott, of Australia, found the fossilized bones of a dinosaur, and he could tell that it had been a big one.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
Since “coal” serves as an adjective modifying “cars,” rather than as a noun, the sentence conveys that tons of freight train was spilled all over the area near the tracks, a meaning that does not make sense. What makes more sense is that tons of coal was spilled all over the area near the tracks. So, a better version is:
The freight train that derailed was made up mostly of cars carrying coal, and tons of it was spilled all over the area near the tracks.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now answer some sentence correction questions in which some of the pronoun issues that we have discussed so far appear.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 7 Since the restaurant’s investment in a state-of-the-art juice press, they have been selling it much more, alone and as ingredients in healthy drinks. 1. [ ] (A) Since the restaurant’s investment in a state-of-the-art juice press, they have been selling it > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) The restaurant, having invested in a state-of-the-art juice press, it has been selling them > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Since the restaurant invested in a state-of-the-art juice press, it has been selling juices 4. [ ] (D) With the restaurant’s investment in a state-of-the-art juice press, they sell > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Having invested in a state-of-the-art juice press, the restaurant sells it > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy While all versions of the sentence mention a juice press, a juice press is not juice. So, when versions of the sentence use pronouns to say that the restaurant is “selling it” or “selling them,” the pronouns “it” or “them” seem either to illogically refer to the juice press or to have no referents. The correct version of the sentence uses nouns and a pronoun effectively, clearly conveying a logical meaning.
(A) Since the restaurant’s investment in a state-of-the-art juice press, they have been selling it much more, alone and as ingredients in healthy drinks.
The pronoun “they” has no clear referent. While one might reasonably assume that “they” refers to people who own or work at the restaurant, the sentence is not effective, because it leaves the reader wondering who exactly “they” are.
Also, the pronoun “it” has no clear referent. It could refer to “juice press,” but that people involved with the restaurant would be selling the juice press does not make sense. Alternatively, “it” could be juice, but “juice” does not appear as a noun in the sentence, only as an adjective modifying “press.”
Meanwhile, there is also an issue with agreement in number, as the sentence seems to be saying that “it,” which is singular, is being used as “ingredients,” which is plural.
(B) The restaurant, having invested in a state-of-the-art juice press, it has been selling them much more, alone and as ingredients in healthy drinks.
This sentence is awkwardly constructed, in that the role of subject is filled twice, first by the noun “restaurant,” and then by the pronoun “it.”
Also, the pronoun “them” has no antecedent, as the word “juice” in “juice press” is not serving as a noun, but rather as an adjective. So, while the reader may be able to deduce that “them” refers to juices, the sentence does not clearly indicate that it does.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Since the restaurant invested in a state-of-the-art juice press, it has been selling juices much more, alone and as ingredients in healthy drinks.
While conceivably “it” could refer to “juice press,” the truth is that meaning clearly dictates that “it” refers to “restaurant.” So, “it” has a clear referent.
Also, by using the noun “juices,” this version, unlike the versions that use pronouns in that spot, clearly conveys what the restaurant is selling.
Thus, this version clearly and concisely conveys its meaning.
(D) With the restaurant’s investment in a state-of-the-art juice press, they sell much more, alone and as ingredients in healthy drinks.
This version starts off with a meaning issue, as the use of “with” results in this version’s conveying a vague and somewhat illogical meaning. In what sense exactly are “they” “with” the investment in the juice press?
Also, “they” has no clear referent. While one might reasonably assume that “they” refers to people who own or work at the restaurant, the sentence is not effective, because it leaves the reader wondering who exactly “they” are.
Also, notice that, when this choice is combined with the non-underlined portion, the wording that results is “they sell much more, alone and as ingredients ….” What “they” sell much more of is not made clear, and the sentence could be interpreted as conveying that “they” sell, alone or as ingredients, in other words, that whoever is selling acts as ingredients.
(E) Having invested in a state-of-the-art juice press, the restaurant sells it much more, alone and as ingredients in healthy drinks.
While this version is concise, since juice itself is not mentioned, the apparent referent of “it” is “juice press,” and thus, this version seems to convey the illogical meaning that the restaurant sells the juice press much more.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 8 Driving in a sandstorm can result in harm to a car’s engine, as, while airborne, it can clog the car’s air filter or even enter the fuel line. 1. [ ] (A) as, while airborne, it > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) while it is airborne, since sand > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) as sand, while airborne, 4. [ ] (D) as, while airborne, some > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) since sand, while airborne, some > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy In the original version, there is no logical antecedent for the pronoun “it,” as the word “sand” is, in this case, part of the word “sandstorm,” and a sandstorm itself would not be airborne and, thus, clog an air filter or enter a fuel line. What makes more sense is that sand, airborne during the storm, would clog the air filter or enter the fuel line, and the correct version of the sentence clearly conveys that it is sand that does so.
(A) Driving in a sandstorm can result in harm to a car’s engine, as, while airborne, it can clog the car’s air filter or even enter the fuel line.
Since a sandstorm would not be airborne and, thus, clog an air filter or enter a fuel line, there is no logical antecedent for “it” in this version.
(B) Driving in a sandstorm can result in harm to a car’s engine, while it is airborne, since sand can clog the car’s air filter or even enter the fuel line.
In this version, “while it is airborne” seems to modify “engine,” resulting in the sentence’s conveying the nonsensical meaning that driving in a sandstorm can result in harm to a car’s engine, while that engine is airborne, since sand can clog the car’s air filter or even enter the fuel line.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Driving in a sandstorm can result in harm to a car’s engine, as sand, while airborne, can clog the car’s air filter or even enter the fuel line.
The use of “sand,” rather than the pronoun “it,” results in this version’s clearly conveying that sand, while airborne, can clog a car’s air filter or enter the fuel line.
(D) Driving in a sandstorm can result in harm to a car’s engine, as, while airborne, some can clog the car’s air filter or even enter the fuel line.
While we may be able to discern that the pronoun “some” refers to “sand,” this version does not provide a logical referent for “some.” Thus, this version does not clearly convey what it is that, while airborne, can clog a car’s air filter or enter the fuel line.
(E) Driving in a sandstorm can result in harm to a car’s engine, since sand, while airborne, some can clog the car’s air filter or even enter the fuel line.
While this version clearly conveys that it is sand that, while airborne, can clog a car’s air filter or enter the fuel line, this version includes an unnecessary pronoun, “some,” which needlessly fills the role of the subject of the second clause of the sentence, which role is already filled by the noun “sand.”
Correct Answer: (C) Now, there is one important exception to this rule about pronouns not referring to adjectives.
summary:
Lesson Content Pronouns cannot take adjectives as their antecedents.
💡MUST KNOW:
An adjective can’t be the antecedent of a pronoun.
Sometimes in a sentence, a word that could serve as the antecedent of a pronoun is present but acting, not as a noun, but as an adjective and, therefore, cannot function logically as the antecedent of a pronoun in that sentence.
Consider the following example:
Incorrect: The number of grams of Colombian gold sold annually has increased each year since 2005, a fact demonstrating buyer confidence in the quality of the gold produced there.
title: 4.4 Pronoun Issue Three: A Pronoun has an Adjective as its Antecedent.

contents:
Lesson Content Unlike many other authorities on English grammar, which do not consider it acceptable for a noun in the possessive form to be the antecedent of a pronoun, the GMAT does consider it acceptable for a noun in the possessive form to be the antecedent of a pronoun.
💡MUST KNOW:
The GMAT considers it acceptable for a noun in the possessive form to be the antecedent of a pronoun.
Let’s unpack this idea.
Nouns in the possessive form are also adjectives.
The boss’s dog is running fast.
The fish’s eggs will hatch soon.
Jim Morrison’s music is delightful.
California’s climate is beautiful.
Sara’s car is very clean.
For example, which dog? “The boss’s dog.” For example, which climate? “California’s climate.” For example, which car? “Sara’s car.”
Although these nouns in the possessive form are essentially adjectives, the GMAT would consider it acceptable for pronouns to have these nouns as antecedents.
The boss’s dog is running fast, and his cat is taking a nap.
The fish’s eggs will hatch soon; it will then begin to swim upstream with its babies.
Jim Morrison’s music is delightful, but his poetry is ethereal.
California’s climate is beautiful; it is a wonderful place to live.
Sara’s car is very clean because she spent all day washing it.
So, what does this information mean to us? What this information means to us is that, if in answering a Sentence Correction question, we see a choice that has a pronoun referring to a noun in the possessive form, we should not eliminate that choice just because it has a pronoun referring to a noun in the possessive form.
Many students come to TTP under the illusion that it is not acceptable for a noun in the possessive form to be the antecedent of a pronoun.
Let’s now discuss another type of pronoun error: pronoun-antecedent disagreement.
summary:
Lesson Content Unlike many other authorities on English grammar, which do not consider it acceptable for a noun in the possessive form to be the antecedent of a pronoun, the GMAT does consider it acceptable for a noun in the possessive form to be the antecedent of a pronoun.
💡MUST KNOW:
The GMAT considers it acceptable for a noun in the possessive form to be the antecedent of a pronoun.
title: 4.4.1 It’s Acceptable to Have a Possessive as the Antecedent of a Pronoun.

contents:
Lesson Content Just as subjects must agree in number with their verbs, pronouns and antecedents must agree.
💡MUST KNOW:
A pronoun must agree in number with its antecedent. A singular pronoun must refer to a singular antecedent, and a plural pronoun must refer to a plural antecedent.
To ensure pronoun-antecedent agreement, when there is a pronoun in a sentence, identify its antecedent and check for agreement.
Incorrect: The prices at the department store are set high, and it is marked down to normal levels on sale days.
We see the singular pronoun “it.” Once we find the pronoun, the next step is to look for the antecedent to ensure that it agrees in number with the pronoun.
The prices at the department store are set high, and they are marked down to normal levels on sale days.
Of course, in a GMAT question, the agreement problem might be solved in a less obvious way, such as through eliminating the pronoun entirely:
The prices at the department store are set high and then marked down to normal levels on sale days.
Let’s consider another example:
Incorrect: Because the city is well managed, their tax revenue often exceeds their expenses.
We see the plural possessive pronoun “their.” The antecedent is “city,” a singular noun, so the pronoun and its antecedent do not agree.
Because the city is well managed, its tax revenue often exceeds its expenses.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 Yesterday, the winds were strong, and it blew Monica’s hat off her head and across the field. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 If the people of this country better managed their lives, it would not be as deeply in debt. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 While families can often be quite supportive, sometimes it is not very understanding. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 4 Even if she or he is well fed, well clothed, and well sheltered, women and men repressed by a dictatorial regime will be dissatisfied. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 5 Although the first known example of a bicycle was built in 1817, their use did not become widespread until nearly fifty years later. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 6 Unlike the claws of members of the canid family, the claws of a lion are retractable, an adaptation that ensures that their claws are protected when not in use. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
We see that the sentence uses the singular pronoun “it,” but looking back for an antecedent we see only the plural noun “winds.” So, the pronoun and its antecedent don’t agree in number. Here’s a correct version:
Yesterday, the wind was strong, and it blew Monica’s hat off her head and across the field.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
This sentence is a little tricky because it jumps from “people” and “their lives” to “it” being “deeply in debt.” All the same, a country could be deeply in debt because of issues related to how the people in the country live. So, the sentence correctly uses the singular pronoun “it” to refer to the singular “country” and effectively expresses a logical meaning.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
We see that the sentence uses the singular pronoun “it,” but looking back for an antecedent we see only the plural noun “families.” So, the pronoun and its antecedent don’t agree in number. A correct version is:
While families can often be quite supportive, sometimes they are not very understanding.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
As we read the sentence, we first see the singular pronouns “she” and “he” and later come to the plural nouns “women” and “men.” While we can tell what the sentence is meant to express, the sentence is flawed because the singular “she” and “he” do not agree with the plural “women” and “men.”
One way to correct the sentence is to remove the pronouns entirely:
Even if well fed, well clothed, and well sheltered, women and men repressed by a dictatorial regime will be dissatisfied.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
We can tell that the plural pronoun “their” refers to bicycles. However, the plural noun “bicycles” does not appear in the sentence. Rather, what we see when we look back for a referent is the singular “bicycle.” So, “their” does not agree with its referent. We could correct the sentence by replacing “their” with the noun “bicycles”:
Although the first known example of a bicycle was built in 1817, the use of bicycles did not become widespread until nearly fifty years later.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
Toward the end of the sentence, we see the pronoun “their.”
Looking back, we see no plural noun to which “their” logically refers. Only the singular noun “lion” is used in describing an animal with retractable claws that would be “protected.” So, “their” does not agree with its antecedent.
We could fix the sentence by using the plural “lions”:
Unlike those of the members of the canid family, the claws of lions are retractable, an adaptation that ensures that their claws are protected when not in use.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s answer a Sentence Correction example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 9 When the company, releasing three new models, their profits did not grow as much as had been anticipated. 1. [ ] (A) When the company, releasing three new models, their profits did not grow > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) The company’s releasing three new models had profit growth that was not > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Although the company has released three new models, their profits have not grown > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) The company, though releasing three new models, its profits did not grow > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Though the company had released three new models, its profits did not grow View Solution Question Level: easy A company is a single entity. So, unless a sentence somehow indicates that “company” refers to the people at the company, it makes the most sense to consider “company” singular and to refer to it with the singular pronoun “it.”
In this case in particular, the sentence is mentioning the profits of the company as a whole. So, clearly, the use of the singular possessive pronoun “its” with “profits” makes the most sense.
(A) When the company, releasing three new models, their profits did not grow as much as had been anticipated.
Since three new models would not have profits that would grow when the company released the models, the profits mentioned would be the company’s profits. Thus, the plural “their” incorrectly refers to the singular “company.”
Also, the first part of the sentence does not make sense, as “when the company” should begin a clause, but there is no verb present to complete such a clause. “Releasing,” a participle, cannot on its own serve as the main verb of a clause and is instead part of the modifier “releasing three new models.”
(B) The company’s releasing three new models had profit growth that was not as much as had been anticipated.
While this version is concise and grammatically correct, it conveys a nonsensical meaning, that the releasing of three new models had profit growth. Releasing three new models might result in profit growth, but releasing three new models would not itself have profit growth.
(C) Although the company has released three new models, their profits have not grown as much as had been anticipated.
Since three new models would not have profits that would grow when the company released the models, the profits mentioned would be the company’s profits. Thus, the plural “their” incorrectly refers to the singular “company.”
Notice that the use of the singular “has released” with “company” provides some confirmation that “company” is considered singular in this sentence. “Company” must be singular or plural. So, either “has” or “their” must be incorrect.
(D) The company, though releasing three new models, its profits did not grow as much as had been anticipated.
While this version correctly uses the singular “its” with the singular antecedent “company,” the sentence is illogically constructed. Notice that the subject of the sentence is “it’s profits” and that “the company” is simply tacked onto the beginning of the sentence and not connected to the sentence in any logical way.
Also, the modifier “though releasing three new models,” which is in the present tense, does not clearly convey that the company released the new models before experiencing lower than expected profit growth. So, the sentence does not effectively indicate the sequence of events.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Though the company had released three new models, its profits did not grow as much as had been anticipated.
In this version, the singular “its” agrees with its antecedent, “company.”
Also, this version clearly indicates the sequence of events and the relationship between the release of the new models and the lower than anticipated profit growth.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss pronouns that refer to “each” or “every” must be singular..
summary:
Lesson Content Just as subjects must agree in number with their verbs, pronouns and antecedents must agree.
💡MUST KNOW:
A pronoun must agree in number with its antecedent. A singular pronoun must refer to a singular antecedent, and a plural pronoun must refer to a plural antecedent.
To ensure pronoun-antecedent agreement, when there is a pronoun in a sentence, identify its antecedent and check for agreement.
Incorrect: The prices at the department store are set high, and it is marked down to normal levels on sale days.
title: 4.5 Pronoun Issue Four: A Pronoun Doesn’t Agree in Number with its Antecedent.

contents:
Lesson Content Pronouns that refer to “each” or “every” must be singular, regardless of whether “each” or “every” appears at the beginning of a phrase that contains a plural noun or that describes a group.
💡MUST KNOW:
Pronouns that correctly refer to “each” or “every” are always singular, regardless of whether “each” or “every” begins a phrase that contains a plural noun or that describes a group.
Consider the following sentences:
Incorrect: Each of the theories were examined, and then they were categorized according to degree of plausibility.
Correct: Each of the theories was examined, and then it was categorized according to degree of plausibility.
Incorrect: Every one of the chemicals discussed in the report are widely used even though they are highly toxic.
Correct: Every one of the chemicals discussed in the report is widely used even though it is highly toxic.
Incorrect: Every ecologist affiliated with the organization should attend the meeting and provide an update on the progress their team has made.
Correct: Every ecologist affiliated with the organization should attend the meeting and provide an update on the progress her team has made.
Notice that, although these sentences contain plural nouns, such as “theories” or “chemicals,” or mention groups, the pronouns used are singular.
Here are some more examples of sentences that contain phrases that begin with “each” or “every” and contain singular pronouns that refer to “each” or “every”:
They will photograph each of the houses before they paint it.
Each poet may submit up to five of his poems.
They mentioned that every one of the animals that they had observed rears its young in a unique way.
Every cyclist on the team was tested for drug use regardless of how she had placed.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Choose the pronoun that agrees with its antecedent. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now discuss pronoun-antecedent agreement when the antecedent is an indefinite pronoun.

💡MUST KNOW:
Pronouns that correctly refer to “each” or “every” are always singular, regardless of whether “each” or “every” begins a phrase that contains a plural noun or that describes a group.
Consider the following sentences:
title: 4.5.1 Pronouns that Refer to “Each” or “Every” Must Be Singular.

contents:
Lesson Content As we already know, indefinite pronouns are pronouns that do not refer to a specific person, place, or thing.
Singular Indefinite Pronouns Plural Indefinite Pronouns
Other Indefinite Pronouns – Sometimes Singular, Sometimes Plural
Some of the most common pronoun-antecedent errors on the GMAT include two pronouns – an indefinite pronoun and a possessive pronoun.
Take the following example:
Incorrect: Everyone has their own problems.
In this sentence, we have two pronouns: the indefinite pronoun “everyone” and the possessive pronoun “their.” In this case, the indefinite pronoun “everyone” acts as the antecedent for the possessive pronoun “their.”
Since English-language speakers often use possessive pronouns incorrectly in speech, this sentence may sound correct to you.
💡MUST KNOW:
The indefinite pronoun “everyone” is always singular. That it is singular makes sense, as “everyone” is really just “every” and “one” combined, and pronouns that correctly refer to phrases that begin with “every” are always singular. Other common singular indefinite pronouns are “anyone,” “someone,” “no one,” and “nobody.”
Thus, we have an error in pronoun-antecedent agreement.
Everyone has his own problems.
Everyone has her own problems.
Everyone has his or her own problems.
Note that, in the sentences above, we must also deal with the issue of gender agreement.
💡MUST KNOW:
Possessive pronouns and indefinite pronouns that appear together in a sentence must agree in number.
Let’s look at some other examples.
Incorrect: Anyone can make a difference in their community when working alone, but effecting change is often easier when one is working as part of a like-minded collective.
The possessive pronoun “their” does not agree with its singular antecedent, the indefinite pronoun “anyone.” We must use a singular possessive pronoun.
The following is a correct version of the sentence:
Anyone can make a difference in her or his community when working alone, but effecting change is often easier when one is working as part of a like-minded collective.
Here’s another:
Incorrect: No one who genuinely cares about earning a high grade in this class would wait until the last week of the semester to start writing their term paper, particularly since the term paper is weighted even more heavily than the final exam.
“No one” is a singular antecedent, which requires singular pronouns to refer to it.
The following is a correct version of the sentence:
No one who genuinely cares about earning a high grade in this class would wait until the last week of the semester to start writing her term paper, particularly since the term paper is weighted even more heavily than the final exam.
Let’s look at one more:
Incorrect: Throughout much of the country, the immigration debate is dominated by claims that current law allows everyone to cross the border unimpeded and that their motives for entering the country are usually nefarious.
At first glance, the sentence above appears to make sense, but upon closer reading, we see that the plural possessive pronoun “their” has no antecedent, since the indefinite pronoun “everyone” is singular.
The following is a correct version of the sentence:
Throughout much of the country, the immigration debate is dominated by claims that current law allows everyone to cross the border unimpeded and that people’s motives for entering the country are usually nefarious.
In this case, it makes sense to replace “their” with the possessive noun “people’s” in order to clarify whose motives we’re talking about and avoid the pronoun-antecedent disagreement with “everyone.”
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Choose the pronoun that agrees with its antecedent. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Remember, a pronoun and its antecedent must agree in number.
Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now answer a Sentence Correction question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 10 Parkour differs from freerunning in that parkour involves simply taking an efficient line over, under, or through obstacles, whereas people freerunning add flips and other stylistic moves. 1. [x] (A) Parkour differs from freerunning in that parkour involves simply taking an efficient line over, under, or through obstacles, whereas people freerunning add 2. [ ] (B) What makes parkour and freerunning different is that, in doing parkour, people simply take an efficient line over, under, or through obstacles, while, in freerunning, it adds > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Parkour is different from freerunning in that, when they do parkour, they simply take an efficient line over, under, or through obstacles, unlike freerunning, when they add > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Parkour and freerunning differ because everyone doing parkour simply take an efficient line over, under, or through obstacles, but not when they are freerunning, adding > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Whereas, in parkour, it means taking an efficient line over, under, or through obstacles, in freerunning, it is different, because of adding > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy This sentence contrasts two different activities, parkour and freerunning. In order for the sentence to be effective, it has to convey the difference between the two activities in a clear, organized manner, and any pronoun-referent relationships must be clear.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Parkour differs from freerunning in that parkour involves simply taking an efficient line over, under, or through obstacles, whereas people freerunning add flips and other stylistic moves.
The way in which this version is constructed – one clause begins with “parkour involves,” and the next begins with “people freerunning add” – may cause this version to sound a little off.
However, since the subjects of two clauses that work together to present a contrast do not have to match, this sentence is actually well constructed and conveys its meaning effectively.
(B) What makes parkour and freerunning different is that, in doing parkour, people simply take an efficient line over, under, or through obstacles, while, in freerunning, it adds flips and other stylistic moves.
This sentence starts off with a meaning issue, as “what makes parkour and freerunning different” does not clearly convey that a contrast between the two is being made. One could interpret that wording as conveying that the rest of the sentence will convey how the two activities are different from something else.
Also, the use of the pronoun “it” is flawed. Since it does not make sense that “it” would refer to any of the singular nouns in the sentence, “it” must refer to “people.” Of course, this reference is flawed because “it” does not agree in number with “people.”
(C) Parkour is different from freerunning in that, when they do parkour, they simply take an efficient line over, under, or through obstacles, unlike freerunning, when they add flips and other stylistic moves.
The pronoun “they” lacks a referent, as it does not clearly refer to any particular group of people.
Also, the contrast made is faulty, because the sentence attempts to compare “when they do parkour” with freerunning itself.
Also, what the wording “when they add flips and other stylistic moves” is meant to convey is not clear, since that wording does not clearly indicate that flips and other stylistic moves are characteristics of freerunning.
(D) Parkour and freerunning differ because everyone doing parkour simply take an efficient line over, under, or through obstacles, but not when they are freerunning, adding flips and other stylistic moves.
This version begins with a meaning issue, as “parkour and freerunning differ because” seems to convey that what follows will convey why they differ rather than how they differ.
Also, the singular indefinite pronoun “everyone” does not agree with the plural “take.”
Also, “they” does not agree with its antecedent, “everyone.”
Also, the purpose of the phrase “adding flips …” is not clear.
(E) Whereas, in parkour, it means taking an efficient line over, under, or through obstacles, in freerunning, it is different, because of adding flips and other stylistic moves.
Both times the pronoun “it” appears in the sentence there is no clear referent.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now make sure that we’ll notice if a sentence contains a pronoun that is completely unneeded.
summary:
Lesson Content As we already know, indefinite pronouns are pronouns that do not refer to a specific person, place, or thing.
Singular Indefinite Pronouns Plural Indefinite Pronouns
Other Indefinite Pronouns – Sometimes Singular, Sometimes Plural
Some of the most common pronoun-antecedent errors on the GMAT include two pronouns – an indefinite pronoun and a possessive pronoun.
Take the following example:
Incorrect: Everyone has their own problems.
In this sentence, we have two pronouns: the indefinite pronoun “everyone” and the possessive pronoun “their.
title: 4.5.2 Pronoun-Antecedent Agreement when the Antecedent is an Indefinite Pronoun

contents:
Lesson Content Some sentences contain unnecessary pronouns.
💡MUST KNOW:
If a sentence contains a noun and a pronoun that play the same role or two pronouns that play the same role, then the sentence contains a useless word and, thus, is illogically constructed.
Consider the following example:
Incorrect: Rich Roll, once an unhealthy corporate lawyer, he decided to change his life at age 40.
While the above sentence may sound basically fine, if we consider it carefully, we can see that the noun “Rich Roll” and the pronoun “he” both fill the role of the subject of the sentence.
Incorrect: Rich Roll, once an unhealthy corporate lawyer, he decided to change his life at age 40.
Once the phrase is removed, it becomes obvious that the pronoun “he” is unnecessary.
Rich Roll, once an unhealthy corporate lawyer, decided to change his life at age 40.
or
Once an unhealthy corporate lawyer, Rich Roll decided to change his life at age 40.
Let’s consider another example:
Incorrect: The San Francisco garter snake, which is a subspecies of the common garter snake and is considered one of the most colorful snakes inhabiting Earth today, it has been designated an endangered species since 1967.
Once again, we have a sentence in which the subject, “the San Francisco Garter snake,” is unnecessarily repeated by a pronoun, “it.” We can make the repetition more obvious by crossing off the long relative clause that follows “snake”:
Incorrect: The San Francisco garter snake, which is a subspecies of the common garter snake and is considered one of the most colorful snakes inhabiting Earth today, it has been designated an endangered species since 1967.
The fact that the clause that appears between “The San Francisco garter snake” and “it” is quite long does not change the fact that there is no need to repeat the subject.
The San Francisco garter snake, which is a subspecies of the common garter snake and is considered one of the most colorful snakes inhabiting Earth today, has been designated an endangered species since 1967.
💡MUST KNOW:
Sentence Correction question writers may add unnecessary pronouns to incorrect answer choices to test test-takers’ attention to detail.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 Tennis champion Boris Becker, who thrilled fans with his flamboyant playing, he struggled after his early success and was declared bankrupt in 2017. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 The lead scientist, along with her research team, they found evidence indicating that commonly held beliefs about the progression of the illness were incorrect. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 3 Even after people brought the Wright brothers’ feats to the attention of the Daily News, one of the newspapers serving Dayton, Ohio, it still did not cover the story. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 Questions regarding the rigor of biomedical research arose when Amgen, a major pharmaceutical firm, having based investment decisions on research results, it discovered that many of the results were not reproducible. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
The pronoun “he” is unnecessary, as the role of subject is filled by the noun “Boris Becker.” A correct version is:
Tennis champion Boris Becker, who thrilled fans with his flamboyant playing, struggled after his early success and was declared bankrupt in 2017.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
The pronoun “they” is unnecessary, as the sentence already has a subject, “the lead scientist.” A correct version is:
The lead scientist, along with her research team, found evidence indicating that commonly held beliefs about the progression of the illness were incorrect.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
While the pronoun “it” refers to the noun “the Daily News,” the pronoun and the noun do not fill the same role in the sentence. The pronoun “it” is the subject of a clause, while “the Daily News” is part of a modifier in a different clause. So, the use of both the noun and the pronoun makes sense.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
The pronoun “it” serves as the subject of a clause, a role already filled by the noun “Amgen.” A correct version is:
Questions regarding the rigor of biomedical research arose when Amgen, a major pharmaceutical firm, having based investment decisions on research results, discovered that many of the results were not reproducible.
Correct Answer: (B) Now, let’s do some Concept Mastery:
Now, let’s see how this unnecessary pronoun issue can work in Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 11 Atheists contend that atheism, which is often depicted as anti-religious, it is simply non-religious. 1. [ ] (A) anti-religious, it > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) anti-religious, 3. [ ] (C) anti-religious, but it > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) anti-religious, that it > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) anti-religious but > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) Atheists contend that atheism, which is often depicted as anti-religious, it is simply non-religious.
The pronoun “it” in “it is simply non-religious” is redundant. To better see this error, we can eliminate the relative clause that separates the first part of the independent clause from the second part:
Atheists contend that atheism, which is often depicted as anti-religious, it is simply non-religious.
Notice that the pronoun “it” refers to the noun “atheism.” So, the meaning conveyed is “Atheists contend that atheism atheism is simply non-religious.” Thus, the pronoun “it” is unnecessary in this context.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Atheists contend that atheism, which is often depicted as anti-religious, is simply non-religious.
This is the only choice that creates a complete, grammatical sentence with no redundant pronouns. To confirm that the sentence is complete and that it makes sense, we can eliminate the relative clause that breaks up the independent clause:
Atheists contend that atheism, which is often depicted as anti-religious, is simply non-religious.
“Atheists contend that atheism is simply non-religious” is a logical and complete independent clause. The relative pronoun “which” properly modifies the noun “atheism.”
The sentence meaning makes sense and the sentence contains no redundancies.
(C) Atheists contend that atheism, which is often depicted as anti-religious, but it is simply non-religious.
This version contains an incomplete clause.
Since “but it is simply non-religious” begins a new thought, we are left asking the question, “Atheists contend that atheism what?”
To better see this error, we can eliminate the relative clause that separates the first part of the sentence from the final clause:
Atheists contend that atheism, which is often depicted as anti-religious, but it is simply non-religious.
“Atheists contend that atheism” is not a complete thought. Thus, this choice is incorrect.
(D) Atheists contend that atheism, which is often depicted as anti-religious, that it is simply non-religious.
The pronouns “that” and “it” in “that it is simply non-religious” are redundant. To better see this error, we can eliminate the relative clause that separates the first part of the independent clause from the second part:
Atheists contend that atheism, which is often depicted as anti-religious, that it is simply non-religious.
“Atheists contend that atheism that it is …” does not make sense. Notice that, since the pronoun “it” refers to the noun “atheism,” the meaning conveyed is, “Atheists contend that atheism that atheism is ….”
(E) Atheists contend that atheism, which is often depicted as anti-religious but is simply non-religious.
This version contains an incomplete clause.
Since everything from “which” to the end of this version is one long relative clause, we are left asking the question, “Atheists contend that atheism what?”
To better see this error, we can eliminate the relative clause:
Atheists contend that atheism, which is often depicted as anti-religious but is simply non-religious.
“Atheists contend that atheism” is not a complete thought. Thus, this choice is incorrect.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 12 The analysts on the call noticed the CEO, when pressed for more information on the inventory growth, that he steered the conversation toward a related, but less controversial, topic. 1. [ ] (A) The analysts on the call noticed the CEO, when pressed for more information on the inventory growth, that he steered > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) The CEO, the analysts on the call noticed, when pressed for more information on the inventory growth, steering > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) The analysts on the call noticed that the CEO, when pressed for more information on the inventory growth, he steered > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) As the analysts on the call noticed, the CEO, pressed for more information on the inventory growth, steered 5. [ ] (E) The CEO, when pressed for more information on the inventory growth, and the analysts on the call noticed this, he steered > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy In all of the versions of the sentence, “the CEO” serves as either the subject of a modifying clause or the subject of an independent clause, and in some versions, the same role is also played by the pronoun “he.” Thus, in each of some versions there are two words that simultaneous fill the role of the subject, and therefore those versions are poorly constructed.
(A) The analysts on the call noticed the CEO, when pressed for more information on the inventory growth, that he steered the conversation toward a related, but less controversial, topic.
This version uses “that” illogically. “That” should either be directly after “noticed” or not be present at all.
Also, in this version, two words, “CEO” and “he,” fill the role of subject, a structural issue that makes the sentence wordy and awkward.
(B) The CEO, the analysts on the call noticed, when pressed for more information on the inventory growth, steering the conversation toward a related, but less controversial, topic.
Since the participle “steering” cannot on its own serve as the main verb of a sentence, this version contains no main verb and, therefore, is not a complete sentence.
(C) The analysts on the call noticed that the CEO, when pressed for more information on the inventory growth, he steered the conversation toward a related, but less controversial, topic.
This version is poorly constructed, in that it uses both “the CEO” and the pronoun “he” as the subject of a clause. In other words, this version would be fine if the pronoun “he” were removed, but, as it is, it’s redundant.
YOUR CHOICE(D) As the analysts on the call noticed, the CEO, pressed for more information on the inventory growth, steered the conversation toward a related, but less controversial, topic.
This version clearly and effectively conveys that, as the analysts noticed, the CEO steered the conversation toward a related topic.
(E) The CEO, when pressed for more information on the inventory growth, and the analysts on the call noticed this, he steered the conversation toward a related, but less controversial, topic.
The version is illogically constructed in that, two words, “CEO” and “he,” simultaneously fill the role of the subject. Also, the overall structure, with the clause “the analysts on the call noticed this” dropped into the middle of the sentence, is awkward.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss illogical antecedents.
summary:
Lesson Content Some sentences contain unnecessary pronouns.
💡MUST KNOW:
If a sentence contains a noun and a pronoun that play the same role or two pronouns that play the same role, then the sentence contains a useless word and, thus, is illogically constructed.
Consider the following example:
Incorrect: Rich Roll, once an unhealthy corporate lawyer, he decided to change his life at age 40.
While the above sentence may sound basically fine, if we consider it carefully, we can see that the noun “Rich Roll” and the pronoun “he” both fill the role of the subject of the sentence.
