title: 4.6 Pronoun Issue Five: A Sentence Contains an Unneeded Pronoun.

contents:
Lesson Content Some pronoun-antecedent errors on the GMAT involve pronouns and antecedents that don’t work logically with each other.
Incorrect: The term “social media” is often used to describe platforms such as Facebook and Twitter, but it is actually any form of internet communication, including blogs, wikis, and more.
In this sentence, the pronoun “it” refers to the antecedent ‘the term “social media,”’ not “social media” itself.
The term “social media” is often used to describe platforms such as Facebook and Twitter, but it actually refers to any form of internet communication, including blogs, wikis, and more.
Another way to fix the sentence is to replace the pronoun with the noun to which it is intended to refer, as in the sentence below:
The term “social media” is often used to describe platforms such as Facebook and Twitter, but social media is actually any form of internet communication, including blogs, wikis, and more.
💡MUST KNOW:
When the word “term” is used in reference to the name of something, make sure that any pronouns meant to refer to that thing refer to the thing itself and not to the word “term.”
Let’s look at a couple of additional examples.
Incorrect: The label “socialism” has long been avoided by many, but recent news stories have made it a more mainstream ideology.
Presumably, the pronoun “it” is supposed to refer to socialism, the newly mainstream ideology.
The label “socialism” has long been avoided by many, but recent news stories have made socialism a more mainstream ideology.
Simply replacing the pronoun with the noun “socialism” makes clear what the ideology is.
Here is another:
Incorrect: The moniker “Never Trumpers” was popularized during the 2016 United States presidential election, though they have dwindled in number since that time. The pronoun “they” presumably should refer to the Never Trumpers, but since the sentence mentions the moniker and not the Never Trumpers themselves, the use of “they” is not logical.
The moniker “Never Trumpers” was popularized during the 2016 United States presidential election, though the Never Trumpers have dwindled in number since that time.
The sentence now makes sense.
💡MUST KNOW:
When a word such as “term,” “label,” or “name” introduces a noun in quotes, be on the lookout for a pronoun-antecedent error in the sentence.
Let’s look at a pronoun-antecedent error that may be less obvious.
Incorrect: People often say the brand name Coke when they order it in a restaurant, but what they actually are asking for is any cola.
What exactly are the people ordering? Certainly not “the brand name Coke.” They are ordering a soda, not a brand name.
People often say the brand name Coke when they order cola in a restaurant, but what they actually are asking for is any cola.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each sentence contains a logical pronoun-antecedent pair and thus is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Note that it is not necessary to eliminate every use of the pronoun; we must simply make sure that the pronoun is preceded by the noun that it is supposed to replace.
Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Now, let’s practice noticing pronouns with illogical antecedents in Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 13 When it began training in 1980, SEAL Team Six was so named for the purpose of misleading Soviet intelligence officials into thinking that six SEAL teams existed when, in fact, there were only two. 1. [x] (A) SEAL Team Six was so named for the purpose of misleading 2. [ ] (B) the name “SEAL Team Six” was used to mislead > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) the name “SEAL Team Six” used for the purpose of misleading > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) the purpose of using the name “SEAL Team Six” was to mislead > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) SEAL Team Six were named that way to mislead > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard In evaluating the answer choices, we must be sure to consider the full sentence created, not just the underlined portion. Notice that, in this case, the underlined portion of the sentence begins immediately after the introductory phrase “When it began its training in 1980.” So, the noun that immediately follows that phrase (and thus begins the underlined portion) must be something that could logically be the “it” that “began its training in 1980.”
YOUR CHOICE(A) When it began training in 1980, SEAL Team Six was so named for the purpose of misleading Soviet intelligence officials into thinking that six SEAL teams existed when, in fact, there were only two.
Although the phrasing of the sentence created with this choice may seem somewhat unnatural at first, this choice is in fact the only one with which a grammatically correct, complete, and logical sentence is created.
The singular pronoun “it” in “When it began training in 1980” logically refers to the singular “SEAL Team Six,” and the singular subject “SEAL Team Six” is properly paired with the singular verb “was,” which is the main verb of the sentence.
(B) When it began training in 1980, the name “SEAL Team Six” was used to mislead Soviet intelligence officials into thinking that six SEAL teams existed when, in fact, there were only two.
While it makes sense to say that “the name ‘SEAL Team Six’ was used to mislead Soviet intelligence officials …,” we must remember to read the answer choice in the context of the full sentence. When we do so, we see that the sentence created with this choice is illogical.
Notice that “the name ‘SEAL Team Six’” is the only singular noun or noun phrase in the sentence. Thus, “it” in “When it began training in 1980,” can refer only to “the name ‘SEAL Team Six.’” Since the idea that the name “began training” is nonsensical, “the name ‘SEAL Team Six’” is an illogical antecedent of “it.”
(C) When it began training in 1980, the name “SEAL Team Six” used for the purpose of misleading Soviet intelligence officials into thinking that six SEAL teams existed when, in fact, there were only two.
In this version, “it” in “When it began training in 1980,” seems to refer to “the name ‘SEAL Team Six.’” However, since the idea that the name “began training” is nonsensical, “the name ‘SEAL Team Six’” is an illogical antecedent of “it.”
Additionally, notice that the version created with this choice is not a complete sentence. “Used” is not a finite verb. After all, “used” doesn’t name an action done by “the name ‘Seal Team Six.’” Rather, “used” is a participle that describes “the name ‘Seal Team Six.’” So, this version has no main verb and, thus, no independent clause.
(D) When it began training in 1980, the purpose of using the name “SEAL Team Six” was to mislead Soviet intelligence officials into thinking that six SEAL teams existed when, in fact, there were only two.
While it makes sense to say that “the purpose of using the name ‘SEAL Team Six’ was to mislead Soviet intelligence officials …,” we must remember to read the answer choice in the context of the full sentence. In context, this choice produces a nonsensical sentence.
The statement “When it began training in 1980, the purpose …” conveys that the purpose “began training,” a nonsensical meaning.
(E) When it began training in 1980, SEAL Team Six were named that way to mislead Soviet intelligence officials into thinking that six SEAL teams existed when, in fact, there were only two.
Notice that, in “When it began training in 1980, ‘SEAL Team Six’,” the singular pronoun “it” logically refers to the singular “SEAL Team Six.” However, it does not make sense to then also pair the singular “SEAL Team Six” with the plural verb “were,” as this choice does. After all, if “SEAL Team Six” is singular and is correctly referred to by the singular “it,” then “SEAL Team Six” cannot in the same sentence also be plural and agree with the plural “were.”
Additionally, the wording “‘SEAL Team Six were named that way” does not convey an entirely clear meaning. Named what “way”? There is no particular “way” described in the sentence.
Key Concept: Be sure that the noun to which a pronoun refers makes sense as the person or thing performing the action associated with that pronoun. Key Concept: Every sentence must have at least one subject and at least one finite verb.
Key Concept: Confirm that singular subjects are paired with singular verbs and that plural subjects are paired with plural verbs.
Key Concept: If a choice contains no grammar or meaning errors, even if it does not “sound” how we might expect it to, then that choice will be correct.
Key Concept: Don’t select answer choices that produce sentence versions that convey illogical meanings.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 14 While Aaron had aspired to play the game of football professionally, he quit it upon discovering how much practice was required for learning how to kick it well enough. 1. [ ] (A) While Aaron had aspired to play the game of football professionally, he quit it upon discovering how much practice was required for learning how to kick it > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Aaron, aspiring to play the game of football professionally, quitting when he discovered how much practice was required for learning how to kick > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Though Aaron had aspired to play the game of football professionally, but he quit upon discovering how much practice was required for learning how to kick the ball > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Although Aaron had aspired to play the game of football professionally, he quit that when he discovered how much practice was required for learning how to kick it > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Aaron, aspiring to play the game of football professionally, quit upon his discovering how much practice was required for learning how to kick View Solution Question Level: medium When the pronouns “it” and “that” are used in the second clauses of some versions of the sentence, the pronouns do not have logical antecedents. So, while a reader could figure out the intended meanings of those versions, those versions do not express a meaning effectively. The correct version, which does not include either “it” or “that,” effectively conveys a logical meaning.
(A) While Aaron had aspired to play the game of football professionally, he quit it upon discovering how much practice was required for learning how to kick it well enough.
In this version, the pronoun “it” is used twice.
The first “it” seems to refer to “the game of football.” While this reference is perhaps not ideal, since the sentence does not make clear that Aaron ever played the game of football, if we go with the idea that the first “it” refers to “the game of football,” the use of the first “it” basically makes sense.
However, since the football itself was never mentioned and the game of football was, the “it” at the end of the sentence also seems to refer to “the game of football.” As a result, the sentence seems to convey the nonsensical meaning that practice was required for learning to kick the game of football.
(B) Aaron, aspiring to play the game of football professionally, quitting when he discovered how much practice was required for learning how to kick well enough.
This version is composed of the noun “Aaron” and two phrases, “aspiring to play …” and “quitting when he discovered …” Therefore, this version is not a complete sentence.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Though Aaron had aspired to play the game of football professionally, but he quit upon discovering how much practice was required for learning how to kick the ball well enough.
This version uses the “the ball” rather than “it” to clearly convey that it was learning to kick the ball that would take practice, and this version has no pronoun-referent issues. However, because the sentence opens with the conjunction “though,” the conjunction “but” is redundant, and the structure of this version is therefore illogical.
(D) Although Aaron had aspired to play the game of football professionally, he quit that when he discovered how much practice was required for learning how to kick it well enough.
The pronoun “that” has no clear antecedent, as it is not clear what Aaron quit.
Also, since the football itself was not mentioned, “it” seems to refer to “the game of football,” and, as a result, the sentence seems to convey that practice was required for learning to kick the game of football.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Aaron, aspiring to play the game of football professionally, quit upon his discovering how much practice was required for learning how to kick well enough.
While this version does not make entirely clear what Aaron quit or what he had to learn to kick, because this version does not use the pronoun “it” or “that,” this version is not characterized by the types of pronoun-referent issues seen in the other versions and conveys a meaning that basically makes sense.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 15 The name “pie” is used for various types of pastries, and it is served sometimes as a main course dish and sometimes as a dessert. 1. [ ] (A) and it is served sometimes as a main course dish and sometimes as a dessert > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) sometimes they are served as main course dishes and sometimes as desserts > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) sometimes served as main course dishes and sometimes as desserts 4. [ ] (D) and is sometimes served as a main course dish or dessert > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) and they sometimes serve it as a main course dish and sometimes as a dessert > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard A name cannot logically be “served” as a dish, since a name is a word. So, even though the singular pronoun “it” agrees with the singular “name,” the original version conveys an illogical meaning, that the name “pie” is served. The correct version uses a structure such that the meaning conveyed is that pastries, rather than the name, are served.
(A) The name “pie” is used for various types of pastries, and it is served sometimes as a main course dish and sometimes as a dessert .
Since the singular pronoun “it” can only refer to a singular noun, “it” seems to refer to ‘the name “pie,”’ and thus this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the name “pie” is served as a main course dish or dessert.
(B) The name “pie” is used for various types of pastries, sometimes they are served as main course dishes and sometimes as desserts .
While this version does a fairly good job of expressing a logical meaning, since there is only a comma and no conjunction or semicolon connecting the two clauses that make up this version, the structure of this version is not that of a proper sentence but, rather, a comma splice.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The name “pie” is used for various types of pastries, sometimes served as main course dishes and sometimes as desserts .
In this version, the phrase “sometimes served as main course dishes and sometimes as desserts” correctly modifies “pastries,” and the meaning conveyed is logical, as pastries, unlike the name “pie,” would be served as main course dishes or desserts.
(D) The name “pie” is used for various types of pastries, and is sometimes served as a main course dish or dessert .
While this version does not refer to ‘the name “pie”’ by using a pronoun, the meaning that this version conveys is nonsensical, since the only possible subject of “is … served” is ‘the name “pie,”’ and so, this version conveys that the name “pie” is served as a main course dish or dessert.
(E) The name “pie” is used for various types of pastries, and they sometimes serve it as a main course dish and sometimes as a dessert .
The pronoun “they” has no logical antecedent and does not refer to anyone in particular. Therefore, the use of “they” in this version is not ideal and probably would not be preferred by the GMAT.
Also, since the singular pronoun “it” can only refer to a singular noun, “it” seems to refer to ‘the name “pie,”’ and, thus, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that “they” serve the name “pie” as a main course dish or dessert.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 16 While the term “bourse” may refer generally to any non-U.S. 1. [ ] (A) it also is the Paris stock exchange specifically > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) but also it is specifically the Paris stock exchange > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) that could be the Paris stock exchange specifically > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) it may refer specifically to the Paris stock exchange 5. [ ] (E) the term also may be the Paris stock exchange specifically > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: hard (A) While the term “bourse” may refer generally to any non-U.S. stock exchange, it also is the Paris stock exchange specifically .
Since “it” refers to “the term ‘bourse’” rather than “bourse” itself, this version contains an illogical antecedent.
It does not make sense to say “it also is the Paris stock exchange specifically,” because the meaning conveyed is, “the term ‘bourse’ also is the Paris stock exchange specifically.” Of course, saying that a term “is” a stock exchange makes no sense.
(B) While the term “bourse” may refer generally to any non-U.S. stock exchange, but also it is specifically the Paris stock exchange .
Since “it” refers to “the term ‘bourse’” rather than “bourse” itself, this version contains an illogical antecedent.
It does not make sense to say, “but also it is specifically the Paris stock exchange,” because the meaning conveyed is, “but also the term ‘bourse’ is specifically the Paris stock exchange.” Of course, saying that a term “is” a stock exchange makes no sense.
Additionally, starting the second clause with the conjunction “but” is redundant because the first clause starts with the conjunction “while.” In other words, in this version, “but” and “while” perform the same function of connecting the two clauses in a way that expresses the contrast between the two ways the term “bourse” is used.
(C) While the term “bourse” may refer generally to any non-U.S. stock exchange, that could be the Paris stock exchange specifically .
Although it isn’t entirely clear to what “that” refers, even if we assume that “that” refers to the noun “the term ‘bourse’,” the sentence still conveys an illogical meaning because “the term” cannot logically “be” the Paris stock exchange.
Since the sentence meaning is both unclear and illogical, this choice is not correct.
YOUR CHOICE(D) While the term “bourse” may refer generally to any non-U.S. stock exchange, it may refer specifically to the Paris stock exchange .
This choice is the only one that contains a pronoun with a logical antecedent. As in the previous choices, “it” refers to “the term ‘bourse’.” However, in this case, “the term” itself is not illogically called a stock exchange. Rather, this choice states that “it [the term] may refer specifically to the Paris stock exchange,” a meaning that makes sense.
Since this is the only choice that does not contain an illogical antecedent or convey an illogical meaning, this is the correct choice.
(E) While the term “bourse” may refer generally to any non-U.S. stock exchange, the term also may be the Paris stock exchange specifically .
Don’t be fooled by the repetition of the noun “the term.” Even without the pronoun/antecedent error we see in other versions of the sentence, this version still conveys an illogical meaning because “the term” cannot logically “be” the Paris stock exchange.
So, although this version does not contain a pronoun with an illogical antecedent, it does not correct the mistake of illogically calling “the term” itself a stock exchange.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss a key GMAT Sentence Correction convention: in GMAT Sentence Correction, generally, a pronoun will not refer to a clause in a correct answer.
summary:
Lesson Content Some pronoun-antecedent errors on the GMAT involve pronouns and antecedents that don’t work logically with each other.
Incorrect: The term “social media” is often used to describe platforms such as Facebook and Twitter, but it is actually any form of internet communication, including blogs, wikis, and more.
In this sentence, the pronoun “it” refers to the antecedent ‘the term “social media,”’ not “social media” itself.
The term “social media” is often used to describe platforms such as Facebook and Twitter, but it actually refers to any form of internet communication, including blogs, wikis, and more.
title: 4.7 Pronoun Issue Six: The Sentence Contains Illogical Antecedents.

contents:
Lesson Content Although, in everyday speech, people often use pronouns to refer to clauses, on the GMAT, constructions that involve pronouns referring to clauses are not correct.
💡MUST KNOW:
On the GMAT, versions in which pronouns refer to entire clauses are not correct.
Consider the following sentence:
Incorrect: My dog remained lost for days, which caused me to lose sleep until it was found.
As currently structured, the relative pronoun “which” appears to refer to the entire clause “My dog was lost for days.” We don’t use “which” in this manner.
The GMAT would therefore prefer one of the following:
My dog remained lost for days, causing me to lose sleep until it was found.
I lost sleep until my dog, which remained lost for days, was found.
In the first preferred version, we replaced the relative clause beginning with the pronoun “which” with a phrase beginning with the present participle “causing.” This phrase appropriately modifies the entire clause.
In the second corrected version, we reordered the sentence so that the pronoun “which” would properly refer to the noun that precedes it, rather than to an entire clause.
Here’s another example:
Incorrect: The job was less difficult than Shane had expected it to be, but that didn’t make up for the lack of pay.
The pronoun “that” appears to refer to the entire clause “The job was less difficult than Shane had expected it to be.” We don’t use the pronoun “that” in this manner.
The job was less difficult than Shane had expected it to be, but the lack of difficulty didn’t make up for the lack of pay.
The job’s being less difficult than Shane had expected it to be didn’t make up for the lack of pay.
The easiest correction is to simply replace the pronoun with a noun, or, in this case, a noun phrase, as is done to create the first correct version of the sentence.
OK, here’s another:
Incorrect: The road was severely damaged, and it made driving on it almost impossible.
The first “it” in the second part of the sentence could refer to “the road,” but that reference is not logical, and thus, “it” seems to refer to the sentence’s entire first clause, “The road was severely damaged.” Given the ambiguity of the meaning conveyed and the fact that the pronoun seems to refer to a clause, the GMAT would prefer a version like one of the following:
The road was severely damaged, so driving on it was almost impossible.
Driving on the road was almost impossible because it was severely damaged.
In both of the above sentences, we eliminated the pronoun that referred to a clause.
*Caveat Emptor: There are a few official GMAT questions that do in fact use pronouns to refer to entire clauses.
So, what would happen in the very unlikely event that, in taking the GMAT, you ruled out four answer choices because they are clearly wrong, and you’re now left with one answer that has no other errors of grammar or logic yet uses a pronoun that refers to an entire clause? Well, you would choose that answer as correct and move on, never for a moment losing any sleep over it.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is not preferred or preferred. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery:
Let’s now answer some Sentence Correction questions involving the concept of pronouns incorrectly referring to clauses.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 17 The social-network game GreenSpace allows users to send their avatars into outer space to clear away pieces of trash that humans have littered onto other planets, which promotes environmentalism. 1. [ ] (A) which promotes > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) and that promotes > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) promoting 4. [ ] (D) in which it promotes > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) and this is promoting > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) The social-network game GreenSpace allows users to send their avatars into outer space to clear away pieces of trash that humans have littered onto other planets, which promotes environmentalism.
Remember, in GMAT Sentence Correction, a pronoun generally will not refer to an entire clause in a correct answer. In this case, the relative pronoun “which” seems to be attempting to refer to the entire independent clause that precedes it.
Notice that “which” cannot possibly modify the noun that directly precedes it, “planets,” because the plural “planets” does not agree with the singular “promotes” and furthermore because planets cannot possibly promote environmentalism.
Since “which” is attempting to modify an entire clause instead of one specific noun, let’s look for a better answer choice.
(B) The social-network game GreenSpace allows users to send their avatars into outer space to clear away pieces of trash that humans have littered onto other planets, and that promotes environmentalism.
Remember, in GMAT Sentence Correction, a pronoun generally will not refer to an entire clause in a correct answer. Although the meaning of the relative pronoun “that” in this context is not completely clear, “that” seems to be attempting to refer to the entire independent clause that precedes it.
Since “that” is attempting to modify an entire clause, let’s look for a better answer choice.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The social-network game GreenSpace allows users to send their avatars into outer space to clear away pieces of trash that humans have littered onto other planets, promoting environmentalism.
Unlike the other versions, this version does not include a pronoun whose antecedent is ambiguous or a pronoun that attempts to refer to an entire clause. Instead, this version properly modifies the entire preceding clause with the phrase “promoting environmentalism.”
The meaning conveyed is clear and logical, and the sentence is grammatically correct.
(D) The social-network game GreenSpace allows users to send their avatars into outer space to clear away pieces of trash that humans have littered onto other planets, in which it promotes environmentalism.
The phrase “in which it promotes environmentalism” illogically modifies the noun “planets,” conveying a nonsensical meaning. “In which” is too far removed from “the social-network game GreenSpace” to properly modify that noun phrase.
Additionally, it is unclear to what the pronoun “it” refers. Logically, the game is what “promotes environmentalism,” but if the intended meaning is “in which the social-network game GreenSpace promotes environmentalism,” we are still left asking to what “in which” refers. In other words, in what does the game promote environmentalism?
Since this version does not convey a clear and logical meaning, this answer choice is not correct.
(E) The social-network game GreenSpace allows users to send their avatars into outer space to clear away pieces of trash that humans have littered onto other planets, and this is promoting environmentalism.
Remember, in GMAT Sentence Correction, a pronoun generally will not refer to an entire clause in a correct answer. Although the meaning of the pronoun “this” in this context is not completely clear, “this” seems to be attempting to refer to the entire independent clause that precedes it.
Since “this” is attempting to modify an entire clause, we should search for a better answer choice.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 18 In 2015, the Alabama state legislature worked to eliminate many laws considered outdated and obsolete, because of which Alabama’s law against bear wrestling was repealed, which caused some to wonder whether bear wrestling was once again legal in Alabama. 1. [ ] (A) because of which Alabama’s law against bear wrestling was repealed, which caused > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) which resulted in the repeal of Alabama’s law against bear wrestling, and it caused > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) and it resulted in Alabama’s law against bear wrestling that was repealed, this, in turn, causing > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) and, because of this, Alabama’s law against bear wrestling was repealed and caused > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) and, as a result, Alabama’s law against bear wrestling was repealed, an outcome causing View Solution Question Level: easy This sentence is meant to convey that Alabama’s law against bear wrestling was repealed as a result of the state legislature’s working to eliminate laws considered outdated and obsolete, and that this repeal caused some people to wonder whether bear wrestling was once again legal in Alabama.
However, in incorrect versions of the sentence, illogical use of pronouns results in the versions’ being awkward and ineffective or conveying nonsensical meanings.
(A) In 2015, the Alabama state legislature worked to eliminate many laws considered outdated and obsolete, because of which Alabama’s law against bear wrestling was repealed, which caused some to wonder whether bear wrestling was once again legal in Alabama.
In this version, the first pronoun “which” could conceivably, but illogically, refer to “laws,” in which case the sentence would convey the illogical meaning that, because of laws, the law against bear was repealed.
However, what seems more likely is that “which” refers to the entire first clause of the sentence. Read either way, the sentence is awkward, does not clearly convey a meaning, and employs a pronoun to refer to a clause, a structure not preferred by the GMAT.
Also, “which” appears a second time, once again seeming to refer to a clause.
(B) In 2015, the Alabama state legislature worked to eliminate many laws considered outdated and obsolete, which resulted in the repeal of Alabama’s law against bear wrestling, and it caused some to wonder whether bear wrestling was once again legal in Alabama.
In this version, the pronoun “which” seems to refer to the entire previous clause, a structure not preferred by the GMAT. Meanwhile, “which” could instead refer to “laws.” So, the GMAT probably would not prefer this use of “which.”
Also, what the pronoun “it” refers to is not clear, as “it” could conceivably refer to “the repeal,” “law,” the sentence’s first clause, or the sentence’s second clause.
(C) In 2015, the Alabama state legislature worked to eliminate many laws considered outdated and obsolete, and it resulted in Alabama’s law against bear wrestling that was repealed, this, in turn, causing some to wonder whether bear wrestling was once again legal in Alabama.
The pronoun “it” could conceivably refer to “legislature” or the first clause of the sentence. Read either way the sentence awkward and ineffective.
Further, the meaning conveyed is that “it,” whatever “it” is, somehow resulted in the law against bear wrestling, which meaning is nonsensical.
Finally, what “this” refers to is not clear. Conceivably, “this” refers to “law,” in which case, this version nonsensically conveys that the law against bear wrestling caused some to wonder whether bear wrestling was legal.
(D) In 2015, the Alabama state legislature worked to eliminate many laws considered outdated and obsolete, and, because of this, Alabama’s law against bear wrestling was repealed and caused some to wonder whether bear wrestling was once again legal in Alabama.
The pronoun “this” seems to refer to the first clause of the sentence, a structure not preferred by the GMAT.
Also, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the law caused people to wonder whether bear wrestling was once again legal.
YOUR CHOICE(E) In 2015, the Alabama state legislature worked to eliminate many laws considered outdated and obsolete, and, as a result, Alabama’s law against bear wrestling was repealed, an outcome causing some to wonder whether bear wrestling was once again legal in Alabama.
This version closes with an abstract appositive, “an outcome causing ….” This uncommon structure is included in this version for the purpose of making this version sound a little off in order to obscure the fact that this choice is the correct answer.
All the same, this version has no pronoun use issues and effectively conveys a logical meaning.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 19 Though at least one reviewer has characterized the Mexican food served at Johnny Tacos as inauthentic, this doesn’t deter the many who patronize the cantina, excited to have an opportunity to experience Mexican food in Indonesia. 1. [ ] (A) Though at least one reviewer has characterized the Mexican food served at Johnny Tacos as inauthentic, this doesn’t deter > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) That at least one reviewer has characterized the Mexican food served at Johnny Tacos as inauthentic doesn’t deter 3. [ ] (C) At least one reviewer has characterized the Mexican food served at Johnny Tacos as inauthentic, the existence of which doesn’t deter > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) At least one reviewer has characterized as inauthentic the Mexican food served at Johnny Tacos, which hasn’t been a deterrent to > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) While at least one reviewer has characterized the Mexican food served at Johnny Tacos as inauthentic, seeing that has not deterred > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium In the original version, the pronoun “this” incorrectly refers to the clause that precedes it. Similar errors appear in all of the other versions except for one.
(A) Though at least one reviewer has characterized the Mexican food served at Johnny Tacos as inauthentic, this doesn’t deter the many who patronize the cantina, excited to have an opportunity to experience Mexican food in Indonesia.
Since this version uses “this” to refer to an idea expressed in the preceding clause, the GMAT would probably prefer a different version with a different kind of structure.
YOUR CHOICE(B) That at least one reviewer has characterized the Mexican food served at Johnny Tacos as inauthentic doesn’t deter the many who patronize the cantina, excited to have an opportunity to experience Mexican food in Indonesia.
This version uses a noun clause as its subject, a structure that, while uncommon, effectively conveys the meaning to be conveyed, and so, the GMAT would probably prefer this very clear version over the any of the versions that use pronouns to refer to ideas or clauses.
(C) At least one reviewer has characterized the Mexican food served at Johnny Tacos as inauthentic, the existence of which doesn’t deter the many who patronize the cantina, excited to have an opportunity to experience Mexican food in Indonesia.
The pronoun “which” may seem to refer to a review, but since no review is mentioned in this version, this version mentions nothing such that the “existence” of that thing would deter people from patronizing the cantina. So, there is no clear antecedent of the pronoun “which.”
(D) At least one reviewer has characterized as inauthentic the Mexican food served at Johnny Tacos, which hasn’t been a deterrent to the many who patronize the cantina, excited to have an opportunity to experience Mexican food in Indonesia.
“Which” seems to refer to the entire preceding clause, because “the Mexican food served at Johnny Tacos” would not be something that “hasn’t been a deterrent to the many who patronize the cantina. So, this version is not ideally constructed and probably would not be preferred by the GMAT.
(E) While at least one reviewer has characterized the Mexican food served at Johnny Tacos as inauthentic, seeing that has not deterred the many who patronize the cantina, excited to have an opportunity to experience Mexican food in Indonesia.
Since the sentence mentions nothing that people would “see,” there is no clear referent for “that.” So, the GMAT would prefer a version that more effectively conveys a logical meaning, a version like the version created via the use of (B).
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now continue to the next topic.
summary:
Lesson Content Although, in everyday speech, people often use pronouns to refer to clauses, on the GMAT, constructions that involve pronouns referring to clauses are not correct.
💡MUST KNOW:
On the GMAT, versions in which pronouns refer to entire clauses are not correct.
Consider the following sentence:
Incorrect: My dog remained lost for days, which caused me to lose sleep until it was found.
As currently structured, the relative pronoun “which” appears to refer to the entire clause “My dog was lost for days.
title: 4.8 Pronoun Issue Seven: A Pronoun is Referring to a Clause.

contents:
Lesson Content A pronoun’s case is its categorization into one of three types: subject, object, and possessive.
To see how pronouns of these types function in sentences, consider the following examples:
I invited you to our Halloween party.
“I” is the subject pronoun.
Everybody is hoping that the star player stays on his current team.
“Everybody” is the subject pronoun because it is doing the action of hoping.
You should give your mother the flowers that they picked.
“You” is a subject pronoun.
The SEAL Team refused to leave any of its members behind.
“Its” is a possessive pronoun.
We asked him where their keys were.
“We” is a subject pronoun.
💡MUST KNOW:
If the pronoun is doing the action, a subject pronoun must be used. If the pronoun is being acted upon or is the object of a preposition, an object pronoun must be used. If the pronoun is indicating ownership, a possessive pronoun must be used.
Some pronoun errors in the Sentence Correction portion of the GMAT involve the use of a pronoun of an incorrect case.
Let’s discuss pronoun case errors in compound subject and compound objects.
summary:
Lesson Content A pronoun’s case is its categorization into one of three types: subject, object, and possessive.
To see how pronouns of these types function in sentences, consider the following examples:
I invited you to our Halloween party.
“I” is the subject pronoun.
Everybody is hoping that the star player stays on his current team.
“Everybody” is the subject pronoun because it is doing the action of hoping.
You should give your mother the flowers that they picked.
title: 4.9 Pronoun Issue Eight: The Sentence Uses an Incorrect Pronoun Case.

contents:
Lesson Content Sometimes we see a pronoun as part of a compound subject or compound object.
Jack and I went snorkeling in Maui.
Would you like to eat dinner with Sara and me?
If neither he nor the director attends the event, we’ll use stock photographs of him and the other cast members.
Of course, in a Sentence Correction question, a pronoun in a compound subject or compound object could be of the wrong case.
💡MUST KNOW:
Using an object pronoun when the pronoun is actually acting as the subject of the sentence or clause is an error in pronoun case.
Consider the following example:
Incorrect: On the new chef’s first day, the restaurant owner and him spent hours talking about the menu.
Since “him” is part of the compound subject (doing the action) in the sentence, the pronoun should be the subject pronoun “he,” not the object pronoun “him”:
On the new chef’s first day, the restaurant owner and he spent hours talking about the menu.
In such situations, determining the correct pronoun case can be confusing.
Marty and (I/me) went to the beach.
To get the pronoun case correct, we drop the other part of the compound subject (the noun “Marty”).
I went to the beach.
Thus, the sentence must be:
Marty and I went to the beach.
OK, that sentence was easy.
Sara gave the gifts to Bita and (I/me).
It’s tempting to think that the following is correct:
Incorrect: Sara gave the gifts to Bita and I.
However, if we apply our rule and drop the other part of the compound, “Bita,” we see that the result does not make sense:
Incorrect: Sara gave the gifts to I.
Thus, the correct sentence must be:
Sara gave the gifts to Bita and me.
💡MUST KNOW:
When we have a pronoun as part of a compound subject or a compound object, we can drop the part of the compound subject or compound object that is not the pronoun, and then determine how the sentence would need to read with just the pronoun present.
Let’s consider another example:
The investigators assured the CEO that the team being sent would include the director and (they/them).
To determine which of the two is the correct pronoun, let’s isolate (they/them) by removing “the director and”:
The investigators assured the CEO that the team being sent would include the director and (they/them).
Now we can more easily tell that the action “include” is being done to the individuals referred to by the pronoun, and that we need the object pronoun “them.”
The investigators assured the CEO that the team being sent would include the director and them.
English speakers commonly use incorrect pronouns in compound subjects or compound objects.
The head of the legal department will brief my assistant and me tomorrow about any liability the company may face, but between you and me, I think we’re in the clear.
In the sentence above, you may be tempted to use “I” instead of “me,” as you’ve probably heard such constructions many times in spoken English.
Let’s look at another example:
They and their detractors have been battling in the court of public opinion for weeks, though neither side’s story has gained much credibility.
In the sentence above, “they and their detractors” are the ones “battling,” and so the subject pronoun “they” is correct, however unnatural it may sound.
They have been battling in the court of public opinion for weeks, though neither side’s story has gained much credibility.
Let’s look at one more example.
Campaigning in this district means knocking on every door, shaking every hand, and listening to every concern, but neither we nor our supporters are willing to give up.
Looking at the sentence above, you may be tempted to use the pronoun “us” instead of “we.” Indeed, “us” is correct in the construction “neither of us” because, in that context, “us” is the object of the preposition “of.” However, in our current example, “neither we nor our supporters” forms a compound subject that performs the action of not being willing to give up.
Campaigning in this district means knocking on every door, shaking every hand, and listening to every concern, but we are not willing to give up.
Although “neither we nor our supporters” may sound strange, by isolating the pronoun, we can clearly see that “we,” not “us,” is correct.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Choose the correct pronoun for each of the following sentences. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery:
Let’s now answer a sentence correction question by applying what we have been discussing.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 20 Political pollsters lost credibility when they wrongly predicted the outcome of the presidential election, and the debate between them and their critics about the merits of polling is yet unresolved. 1. [x] (A) the debate between them and their critics about the merits of polling is yet unresolved 2. [ ] (B) them and their critics have yet to resolve the debate about the merits of polling > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) they still have not resolved the debate between they and their critics about polling merits > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) between they and their critics, the debate about polling merits has not been resolved yet > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) the debate about the merits of polling between them and their critics are still unresolved > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy YOUR CHOICE(A) Political pollsters lost credibility when they wrongly predicted the outcome of the presidential election, and the debate between them and their critics about the merits of polling is yet unresolved .
This choice correctly uses the object pronoun “them” in the compound object “them and their critics,” which is the object of the preposition “between.”
The sentence does not contain any grammatical errors and conveys a clear and logical meaning.
(B) Political pollsters lost credibility when they wrongly predicted the outcome of the presidential election, and them and their critics have yet to resolve the debate about the merits of polling .
This choice mistakenly uses the object pronoun “them” where the subject pronoun “they” is needed.
“Them and their critics” are the people performing the action “have yet to resolve.” Since “them and their critics” are performing the action rather than having the action done to them, “they and their critics” would be correct.
We can more easily see this error in pronoun case by crossing off the second part of the compound subject:
“them and their critics have yet to resolve the debate …”
“Them … have yet to resolve” is not grammatically correct. “They have yet to resolve” would be correct.
(C) Political pollsters lost credibility when they wrongly predicted the outcome of the presidential election, and they still have not resolved the debate between they and their critics about polling merits .
The subject pronoun “they” in “they and their critics” is incorrect because “they” is the object of the preposition “between.” Thus, we need the object pronoun “them,” as in “the debate between them and their critics.”
(D) Political pollsters lost credibility when they wrongly predicted the outcome of the presidential election, and between they and their critics, the debate about polling merits has not been resolved yet .
The subject pronoun “they” in “they and their critics” is incorrect because “they” is the object of the preposition “between.” We need the object pronoun “them,” as in “between them and their critics.”
(E) Political pollsters lost credibility when they wrongly predicted the outcome of the presidential election, and the debate about the merits of polling between them and their critics are still unresolved .
This choice has a subject-verb agreement error. Notice that the thing that is “still unresolved” is “the debate,” a singular noun. Thus, the plural verb “are” is incorrect. To better see this error, we can cross of the preposition phrase between the subject and verb:
“the debate about the merits of polling between them and their critics are still unresolved”
“The debate … are” is not grammatically correct. “The debate … is” would be correct.
Notice also that, given the overall context of the sentence, the phrasing “merits of polling between them and their critics” conveys a somewhat illogical meaning. Logically, the polling is not what is between pollsters and critics; the debate is what is between the pollsters and critics. After all, the sentence discusses predicting the outcome of a presidential election. That prediction would not be based on polling that was just between pollsters and their critics. So, the placement of the phrase “between them and their critics” is off.
Key Concept: If a pronoun is doing the action, a subject pronoun must be used. If a pronoun is being acted upon or is the object of a preposition, an object pronoun must be used.
Key Concept: When checking for proper pronoun usage in a compound subject or a compound object, cross off the part of the compound that is not the pronoun and see how the sentence reads with just the pronoun present.
Key Concept: Confirm that singular subjects are paired with singular verbs and that plural subjects are paired with plural verbs. Key Concept: When checking for subject-verb agreement, cross off any prepositional phrases that appear between the subject and finite verb. election. That prediction would not be based on polling that was just between pollsters and their critics. So, the placement of the phrase “between them and their critics” is off. Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 21 After botanist Ariel Novoplansky exposed plants to simulated drought, he discovered them and neighboring plants were communicating with each other through their roots. 1. [ ] (A) them and neighboring plants were > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) them and neighboring plants as > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) them and neighboring plants as they were > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) they and neighboring plants to be > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) that neighboring plants and they were View Solution Question Level: medium Correctly answering this question requires determining what makes sense in terms of what the botanist would have discovered and also understanding the correct use of the subject pronoun “they” and the object pronoun “them.” While some of the incorrect choices may seem possibly correct, the correct answer is so clearly correct that we can confidently choose it over any other.
(A) After botanist Ariel Novoplansky exposed plants to simulated drought, he discovered them and neighboring plants were communicating with each other through their roots.
This version incorrectly uses the object pronoun “them” as the subject of “were.”
While it may seem that “them” is correct since “them” seems to be the object of “he discovered,” what he discovered was not the plants themselves. He discovered the plants were communicating.
(B) After botanist Ariel Novoplansky exposed plants to simulated drought, he discovered them and neighboring plants as communicating with each other through their roots.
This version sounds incorrect and is incorrect, since in English there is no idiom “discovered … as” as in “discovered the plants as communicating.”
(C) After botanist Ariel Novoplansky exposed plants to simulated drought, he discovered them and neighboring plants as they were communicating with each other through their roots.
This version could be tempting, as it is grammatically correct, since “them” correctly functions as the object of “discovered.” However, the meaning conveyed is nonsensical, as the botanist would not have discovered the plants after the botanist exposed the plants to simulated drought.
(D) After botanist Ariel Novoplansky exposed plants to simulated drought, he discovered they and neighboring plants to be communicating with each other through their roots.
This version could be tempting if one perceives the subject pronoun “they” to be correctly serving as the subject of “to be communicating.” Let’s test the version though, by eliminating the non-pronoun part of the compound structure and seeing how the sentence reads.
botanist Ariel Novoplansky … discovered they to be communicating
Probably, even to someone who doesn’t know exactly how the idiom “discovered … to be” works, that version would sound incorrect, and it is in fact idiomatically incorrect.
YOUR CHOICE(E) After botanist Ariel Novoplansky exposed plants to simulated drought, he discovered that neighboring plants and they were communicating with each other through their roots.
This version correctly uses the subject pronoun “they” as part of the compound subject of “were communicating” and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s continue by discussing the use of the pronouns “who”/“whoever” and “whom”/“whomever.”
summary:
Lesson Content Sometimes we see a pronoun as part of a compound subject or compound object.
Jack and I went snorkeling in Maui.
Would you like to eat dinner with Sara and me?
If neither he nor the director attends the event, we’ll use stock photographs of him and the other cast members.
Of course, in a Sentence Correction question, a pronoun in a compound subject or compound object could be of the wrong case.
title: 4.9.1 Errors in Pronoun Case in Compound Subjects and Compound Objects

contents:
Lesson Content In the list at the beginning of this section, we saw that “who” and “whoever” are subject pronouns and that “whom” and “whomever” are object pronouns.
While which of the pronouns are which case is not difficult to remember, which of them should be used in a particular situation is not always obvious, and of course, GMAT question writers can use “who”/“whoever” or “whom”/“whomever” in creating tricky Sentence Correction answer choices.
In a sentence like the following example, it’s fairly obvious that “who” should be used, because the person being referred to is clearly acting:
That the department head would resign soon was obvious, but who would serve as the new head was not.
Using the subject pronoun “who” makes sense because the person referred to by “who” is someone who would take the action “serve.”
Similarly, that “whom” should be used in the following sentence is clear, because the person being referred to is clearly the receiver of an action:
We have to know whom to call if the computer malfunctions.
Using the object pronoun “whom” makes sense, because the person referred to by “whom” is the receiver of the action; the person is the person to call.
The following strategy can help us to choose between “who” and “whom” or between “whoever” and “whomever.”
Strategy: One way to determine whether to use “who”/“whoever” or “whom”/“whomever” is to replace the pronoun with “he” or “him.” If “he” is correct, then “who” or “whoever” is the correct pronoun to use.
Let’s use this strategy to determine which pronoun is correct in the following sentence.
Each of the experts (who/whom) had analyzed the results was given an opportunity to speak.
When we replace (who/whom) with “he,” we get “he had analyzed.” When we replace (who/whom) with “him,” we get “him had analyzed.” Obviously, “he” is the correct choice, and therefore, “who” is the correct pronoun to use in the sentence.
Each of the experts who had analyzed the results was given an opportunity to speak.
OK, so far, we have seen “who” and “whom” used in situations such that whether a subject or object pronoun should be used is pretty clear.
Nobody saw (who/whom) pulled the alarm.
Notice the issue in this case.
We have “Nobody saw (who/whom).” So, (who/whom) seems to be the object of “nobody saw.” Thus, we might expect the correct pronoun to be “whom,” as in “Nobody saw whom.”
At the same time, we have “(who/whom) pulled the alarm.” So, (who/whom) is the subject of “pulled the alarm.” Now, the correct pronoun appears to be “who,” as in “who pulled the alarm.”
So, should “whom” be used because the pronoun refers to the person nobody saw, or should “who” be used because the pronoun refers to the person who took the action “pulled the alarm”?
In cases like this one, the correct pronoun to use is subject pronoun “who.”
Even though, in a way, the person referred to is being acted upon, it is more important to make clear that the person took the action.
Nobody saw who pulled the alarm.
If this rule seems difficult to keep straight, just remember that, whenever we have to choose between “who”/“whoever” and “whom”/“whomever,” if the pronoun serves as a subject, the correct pronoun to use is the subject pronoun “who”/“whoever.”
💡MUST KNOW:
Whenever we have to choose between “who”/“whoever” and “whom”/“whomever,” if the pronoun serves as a subject, the correct pronoun to use is the subject pronoun “who”/“whoever.”
Let’s consider another example:
When they walked into the bank to rob it, they hadn’t considered (who/whom) might be inside.
Once again, “whom” could seem to be the correct pronoun to use, because the pronoun seems to be the object of “considered.”
Notice, however, that what is really being considered is the entire concept “who would be inside” and that the pronoun “(who/whom)” is a subject, taking the action “might be inside.” So, the correct pronoun to use in this case is “who.”
When they walked into the bank to rob it, they hadn’t considered who might be inside.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice In each case, determine which pronoun should be used. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now apply what we have been discussing to answering some Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 22 After the Swedish crown jewels were stolen, they speculated that whomever had taken them had done so on behalf of a specialist collector. 1. [ ] (A) they speculated that whomever had taken them had > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) there was speculation that whoever had taken them had 3. [ ] (C) the speculation was about whomever had taken them having > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) the speculation about them was whoever had taken them having > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) speculation about the jewels was that whoever had taken them, having > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy Answering this question correctly primarily requires noticing whether the subject pronoun “whoever” or the object pronoun “whomever” is being used correctly.
Once we have eliminated the choices that use the wrong pronouns, we can use structure and meaning issues to choose among the remaining choices.
(A) After the Swedish crown jewels were stolen, they speculated that whomever had taken them had done so on behalf of a specialist collector.
The pronoun “they” has no clear referent, as the only plural noun in the sentence, “jewels” would not speculate and no particular group of people who speculated is mentioned or otherwise defined.
Also, the object pronoun “whomever” is misused as the subject of the verb “had taken.”
YOUR CHOICE(B) After the Swedish crown jewels were stolen, there was speculation that whoever had taken them had done so on behalf of a specialist collector.
This version correctly uses the subject pronoun “whoever” as the subject of the verb “had taken” and concisely conveys a logical meaning.
(C) After the Swedish crown jewels were stolen, the speculation was about whomever had taken them having done so on behalf of a specialist collector.
The wording “the speculation” tends to indicate that the sentence is about some particular speculation, while in reality what the sentence seems to be about is speculation in general about the motive for taking the jewels. So, that wording is not optimal.
Also, the object pronoun “whomever” is misused as the subject of the verb “had taken.”
Also, beyond including a pronoun error, the wording “the speculation was about whomever had taken them having done so on behalf of a specialist collector” is convoluted and does not convey a meaning that makes complete sense.
(D) After the Swedish crown jewels were stolen, the speculation about them was whoever had taken them having done so on behalf of a specialist collector.
While “whoever” is correctly used as the subject of “had taken,” this version does not effectively convey what the speculation was.
By simplifying the sentence by removing “about them,” we get “the speculation … was whoever had taken them having done so.” We can clearly see that there is an issue. The issue is that this version does not provide a clause to express what the speculation was, a clause such as “that whoever had taken them had done so ….” Rather, this version is structured so as to convey the nonsensical meaning that “the speculation” was “whoever.”
(E) After the Swedish crown jewels were stolen, speculation about the jewels was that whoever had taken them, having done so on behalf of a specialist collector.
This version could appear to be correct because “whoever” is correctly used as the subject of “had taken.”
However, this version conveys the ridiculous meaning that speculation about the jewels was that “whoever had taken them.”
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now ensure that we won’t be fooled into eliminating a correct answer just because it uses a possessive pronoun with a gerund.
summary:
Lesson Content In the list at the beginning of this section, we saw that “who” and “whoever” are subject pronouns and that “whom” and “whomever” are object pronouns.
While which of the pronouns are which case is not difficult to remember, which of them should be used in a particular situation is not always obvious, and of course, GMAT question writers can use “who”/“whoever” or “whom”/“whomever” in creating tricky Sentence Correction answer choices.
title: 4.9.2 “Who”/“Whoever” and “Whom”/“Whomever”

contents:
Lesson Content A gerund is a form of verb that ends in “-ing” and is used as a noun.
Skateboarding is popular worldwide.
Many people enjoy public speaking.
Often, in sentences, gerunds are preceded by possessive pronouns.
Consider the following example:
During the meeting with the citizens, the mayor seemed to be surprised by their questioning the validity of the data.
While you might be tempted to believe that the correct wording is “surprised by them questioning,” the sentence is actually correct as written, with the possessive pronoun “their” preceding the gerund “questioning.”
To see why using a possessive pronoun before a gerund makes sense, consider what’s being conveyed by the sentence in the example above.
So, placing a possessive pronoun before a gerund makes sense.
The president’s advisors were concerned about his worrying about popularity poll results.
The salespeople were sure that the manager would approve of their having offered the client a special deal.
The head of research told the team members that he appreciated their being honest with him about the flaws in the model.
💡MUST KNOW:
Be careful not to eliminate a choice that includes a possessive pronoun-gerund combination just because it “sounds funny.”
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each sentence is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 The manager was glad to see that the band members were already warming up, but the head of security was concerned by their being on site so early. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 The inexperienced young teacher was determined to succeed, but his yelling at the students was causing more problems than it was solving. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 The trader was having a tough week, and him hiding the growing losses from his colleagues was not helping matters. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 4 The members of the pop duo said that their taking acting classes had been very helpful. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 Elisa looked around for her father and eventually found him swimming in the pond. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 6 While the group’s songs were terrific, them dancing was even better. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
The possessive pronoun “their” is correctly used to indicate who possesses the gerund “being,” because, in this case, what the head of security was concerned about was “their being on site so early.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
The possessive pronoun “his” is correctly used to indicate that the yelling is the young teacher’s yelling.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
It does not make sense to use the object pronoun “him” in this context. After all, we would never say “him … was not helping matters.”
We need to use the possessive pronoun “his” with the gerund “hiding” because what was not helping matters was “his hiding the growing losses.”
A correct version is:
The trader was having a tough week, and his hiding the growing losses from his colleagues was not helping matters.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
The possessive pronoun “their” is correctly used with the gerund “taking” to express whose taking acting classes the sentence is about.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
Notice that, in this case, Elisa found her father, not his swimming. In other words, she found “him.” So, using the object pronoun “him” makes sense.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
It does not make sense to use the object pronoun “them” in this context. After all, we would never say “them … was even better.”
We need to use the possessive pronoun “their” with the gerund “dancing” because what was even better was “their dancing.”
A correct version is:
While the group’s songs were terrific, their dancing was even better.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 23 One thing that sets American Millennials apart from members of other generations is their having redefined the American dream. 1. [x] (A) their having redefined 2. [ ] (B) redefining > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) because they have redefined > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) to have redefined > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) that of redefining > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy A path to correctly answering this question is noticing that the sentence is conveying that a “thing that sets American Millennials apart from other generations” is something. So, the use of the correct answer will result in a version that effectively conveys what that something is.
YOUR CHOICE(A) One thing that sets American Millennials apart from members of other generations is their having redefined the American dream.
Since “having redefined” is a gerund and, thus, serves as a noun, this version is effective, in that it conveys that the “thing” that sets Millennials apart is Millennials’ having redefined the American dream.
Also, notice that logic dictates that “their” refers to “American Millennials,” and not to “members of other generations,” because it is Millennials’ having redefined the American dream that would set Millennials apart from members of other generations.
So, this version effectively conveys a clear meaning.
(B) One thing that sets American Millennials apart from members of other generations is redefining the American dream.
This version could be tempting, because this choice is the most concise one. However, the brevity of this choice comes at the expense of clarity, as what this version is meant to convey is not entirely clear. Are Millennials redefining the American dream? The version does not clearly say so and does not make clear what redefining the American dream has to do with Millennials.
(C) One thing that sets American Millennials apart from members of other generations is because they have redefined the American dream.
This use of this choice results in an illogically constructed sentence. The resulting sentence is essentially “One thing … is because they have redefined the American dream.” Read literally, the sentence basically conveys that “one thing” exists (i.e., is) because the Millennials have redefined the American dream. Clearly, that meaning is not the point to be made.
(D) One thing that sets American Millennials apart from members of other generations is to have redefined the American dream.
While this version almost seems to work, its construction is not quite logical, and it does not convey a clear meaning.
The most obvious issue is that this version does not clearly connect redefining the American dream to the Millennials, as “to have redefined the American dream” does not indicate that the Millennials are the ones who have redefined the American dream.
Also, even if we assume that the Millennials are somehow connected to “to have redefined the American dream,” the sentence does not convey whether the Millennials have redefined the American dream or will redefine the American dream, or what exactly the relationship is between redefining the American dream and the Millennials.
(E) One thing that sets American Millennials apart from members of other generations is that of redefining the American dream.
This version is awkward and does not convey a clear meaning.
The pronoun “that” is essentially an unnecessary repetition of “thing,” as we can see in this simplified version: “One thing is that of redefining ….” Essentially, this version is saying “One thing is the thing of redefining ….”
Also, this version does not make clear that it is the Millennials who are redefining the American dream.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss demonstrative pronouns and demonstrative adjectives.
summary:
Lesson Content A gerund is a form of verb that ends in “-ing” and is used as a noun.
Skateboarding is popular worldwide.
Many people enjoy public speaking.
Often, in sentences, gerunds are preceded by possessive pronouns.
Consider the following example:
During the meeting with the citizens, the mayor seemed to be surprised by their questioning the validity of the data.
While you might be tempted to believe that the correct wording is “surprised by them questioning,” the sentence is actually correct as written, with the possessive pronoun “their” preceding the gerund “questioning.
title: 4.9.3 Possessive Pronouns and Gerunds

contents:
Lesson Content A demonstrative pronoun is a pronoun that points out or “demonstrates” the noun in the sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
A demonstrative pronoun takes the place of a noun and always stands alone. The major demonstrative pronouns are “this,” “that,” “these,” and “those.”
Consider the following example:
The tires on most sports cars are wider than those on most mid-size sedans.
In the sentence above, “those” is a demonstrative pronoun.
Now, demonstrative pronouns often work differently from how personal pronouns work.
The students in the room rejoiced because class had been canceled, and they began making plans to go to the beach.
The personal pronoun “they” refers to “the students in the room.”
Once the restored plane reached 180 knots, it gracefully lifted off the runway.
The personal pronoun “it” refers to “the restored plane.”
On the other hand, demonstrative pronouns often do not refer directly to their antecedents:
The speed of today’s cars well exceeds that of cars of the 1950s.
In the sentence above, the demonstrative pronoun “that” replaces the antecedent “speed.” However, the speed referred to by “that” is not the same as the speed that is mentioned earlier in the sentence.
While repeating “the speed” to make the comparison would not be grammatically incorrect, such repetition isn’t necessary because we can use a demonstrative pronoun.
Let’s consider some more example sentences:
The range of the new model is greater than that of the previous model.
The books in my library are older than those in your library.
The GDP of the United States is larger than that of Monaco.
In these three examples, “that” and “those” are used as demonstrative pronouns.
The words “this,” “that,” “these,” and “those” can also function as demonstrative adjectives.
Consider the usage of “that” in the following sentence:
That book is long.
In this case, “that” does not stand alone as a pronoun.
💡MUST KNOW:
A demonstrative adjective is always immediately followed by a noun because adjectives modify nouns.
It’s important to be able to tell when “this,” “that,” “these,” or “those” is being used as a demonstrative pronoun and when it is being used as a demonstrative adjective.
Let’s look at some examples.
This bakery has a better selection of breads, but that bakery has more exotic pastries.
The pastries at the bakery on the corner are more exotic than those at the bakery across the street.
In the first example, we are distinguishing between two bakeries: this bakery and that bakery.
In the second example, the focus shifts, and we’re comparing a particular item at each of the bakeries, pastries.
Here’s another pair:
Even with all of the recent improvements in CGI technology, this film still does not look realistic.
Superhero films of today rely on CGI much more than those of the early 2000s did.
In the first sentence, a demonstrative adjective tells us which film does not look realistic (this film).
Use of Demonstrative Pronouns Where Personal Pronouns Would Make More Sense
As we have discussed, demonstrative pronouns not only stand in for nouns but also point out or “demonstrate.” Personal pronouns, on the other hand, simply stand in for nouns.
Consider the following example:
Incorrect: While pandas may seem cuddly or endearing, these have exceedingly strong bites and large dexterous claws.
Since, in this example, pandas are not compared to other animals or pointed out in some way, it does not make sense to use the demonstrative pronoun “these,” and it would make more sense to simply use the personal pronoun “they,” as in:
While pandas may seem cuddly or endearing, they have exceedingly strong bites and large dexterous claws.
Here’s another example in which the use of a demonstrative pronoun doesn’t make sense:
While the schooner America was considered a fast sailing yacht in its time, that would be no match for the high-tech sailboats that compete for the America’s cup today.
In this example, the use of the pronoun “that” doesn’t make sense, as conveying the sentence’s intended meaning does not require the use of a pronoun that points out the schooner America.
While the schooner America was considered a fast sailing yacht in its time, it would be no match for the high-tech sailboats that compete for the America’s cup today.
💡MUST KNOW:
Sentence Correction questions may include choices that are incorrect because they use demonstrative pronouns in situations in which using personal pronouns would make more sense.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Determine whether each sentence is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now consider some Sentence Correction questions in which demonstrative pronouns are used inappropriately.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 24 Although Prince Edward Island is the smallest of the Canadian provinces, there is a robust agricultural industry in which they produce one quarter of Canada’s potatoes. 1. [ ] (A) there is a robust agricultural industry in which they produce > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) the agricultural industry of that is robust, producing > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) that has a robust agricultural industry that produces > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) its agricultural industry is robust, producing 5. [ ] (E) this has a robust agricultural industry, which produces > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) Although Prince Edward Island is the smallest of the Canadian provinces, there is a robust agricultural industry in which they produce one quarter of Canada’s potatoes.
The pronoun “they” does not have a logical antecedent. The only plural noun to which “they” could refer is “provinces,” but from the context “Although Prince Edward Island is the smallest of the Canadian provinces,” we can see that the logical meaning is that Prince Edward Island in particular, not all of the provinces, produces one quarter of Canada’s potatoes. Of course, the plural pronoun “they” cannot refer to the singular noun “Prince Edward Island.”
Additionally, “there is a robust agricultural industry” conveys a vague meaning because “there is” simply conveys that “a robust agricultural industry” exists in general but does not link that industry to Prince Edward Island specifically.
(B) Although Prince Edward Island is the smallest of the Canadian provinces, the agricultural industry of that is robust, producing one quarter of Canada’s potatoes.
It’s unclear to what “that” refers in “the agricultural industry of that is robust.” This version is a good example of a sentence in which the use of a personal pronoun, rather than a demonstrative pronoun, would clarify the meaning.
Since no comparison is expressed by the sentence, the use of the demonstrative pronoun does not make sense. In other words, although we may assume that “that” refers to “Prince Edward Island,” in this context, “that” could be anything. In this case, the personal pronoun “it” would directly refer to the singular noun “Prince Edward Island,” conveying a clear and logical meaning.
(C) Although Prince Edward Island is the smallest of the Canadian provinces, that has a robust agricultural industry that produces one quarter of Canada’s potatoes.
It’s unclear to what “that” refers in “that has a robust agricultural industry.” This version is a good example of a sentence in which the use of a personal pronoun, rather than a demonstrative pronoun, would clarify the meaning.
Since no comparison is expressed by the sentence, the use of the demonstrative pronoun does not make sense. In other words, although we may assume that “that” refers to “Prince Edward Island,” in this context, “that” could be anything. In this case, the personal pronoun “it” would directly refer to the singular noun “Prince Edward Island,” conveying a clear and logical meaning.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Although Prince Edward Island is the smallest of the Canadian provinces, its agricultural industry is robust, producing one quarter of Canada’s potatoes.
This version uses the possessive personal pronoun “its” to directly refer to the singular noun “Prince Edward Island,” conveying a clear and logical meaning.
In fact, this is the only version that uses a personal pronoun to refer directly to the subject “Prince Edward Island.”
Notice that there is no other singular noun to which “its” could possibly refer.
The meaning is unambiguous, and the sentence contains no grammatical errors.
