(E) Although Prince Edward Island is the smallest of the Canadian provinces, this has a robust agricultural industry, which produces one quarter of Canada’s potatoes.
It’s unclear to what “this” refers in “this has a robust agricultural industry.” Notice that the sentence does not use “this” as a demonstrative adjective to specify that “this island has a robust agricultural industry.” Doing so would convey a clearer meaning.
As the sentence stands, although we may assume that “this” refers to “Prince Edward Island,” in fact “this” could be anything because “this” is not pointing out a specific noun in this context.
Notice that this choice is essentially the same grammatically as the incorrect choice (C). In other words, “that has” and “this has” are no different in this context; they are two versions of the same error. [Notice also that “industry that produces” in choice (C) and “industry, which produces” here also are simply two (in this case, valid) ways to say the same thing, that the industry produces the potatoes.]
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 25 In 2000, 52 Oscar award statuettes that were supposed to be delivered to a location in Los Angeles but were instead thrown into a dumpster, where they were found by a man looking for some packing boxes. 1. [ ] (A) statuettes that were supposed to be delivered to a location in Los Angeles but were instead thrown > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) statuettes, these were supposed to be delivered to a location in Los Angeles but were instead thrown > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) statuettes supposed to be delivered to a location in Los Angeles were instead thrown 4. [ ] (D) statuettes were supposed to be delivered to a location in Los Angeles; instead they threw these > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) statuettes, they were supposed to deliver them to a location in Los Angeles, but they instead threw them > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium Eliminating most of the incorrect choices in this question requires noticing pronoun use issues. These issues include the use of demonstrative pronouns where personal pronouns would make more sense and the presence of pronouns that have no clear antecedents.
(A) In 2000, 52 Oscar award statuettes that were supposed to be delivered to a location in Los Angeles but were instead thrown into a dumpster, where they were found by a man looking for some packing boxes.
Since, in this version, everything that follows the noun “statuettes” is merely a long relative clause that modifies “statuettes,” this version has no main verb and, thus, is not a complete sentence.
(B) In 2000, 52 Oscar award statuettes, these were supposed to be delivered to a location in Los Angeles but were instead thrown into a dumpster, where they were found by a man looking for some packing boxes.
This version starts off awkwardly as the role of subject is filled twice, first by the noun phrase “52 Oscar statuettes,” and then by the pronoun “these.”
Another issue that this version has is that the demonstrative pronoun “these” is used in a situation in which the use of a demonstrative pronoun does not make sense, as the sentence is not set up to point out any particular set of Oscar statuettes.
YOUR CHOICE(C) In 2000, 52 Oscar award statuettes supposed to be delivered to a location in Los Angeles were instead thrown into a dumpster, where they were found by a man looking for some packing boxes.
In this well structured version, the subject, “52 Oscar award statuettes supposed to be delivered to a location in Los Angeles” is followed by the main verb, “were thrown.”
The resulting sentence is grammatically sound and clearly conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(D) In 2000, 52 Oscar award statuettes were supposed to be delivered to a location in Los Angeles; instead they threw these into a dumpster, where they were found by a man looking for some packing boxes.
One issue that this version has is the use of “they” with no clear referent, as the sentence does not seem to be referring to any particular group of people with its use of “they,” and such use of “they” does not convey a clear meaning and is not preferred by the GMAT.
Also, “these” is a demonstrative pronoun, but it does not make sense to use such a pronoun in this situation, since the sentence is not set up to point out any particular items.
(E) In 2000, 52 Oscar award statuettes, they were supposed to deliver them to a location in Los Angeles, but they instead threw them into a dumpster, where they were found by a man looking for some packing boxes.
This version starts off awkwardly, as the phrase “52 Oscar statuettes” stands alone with no verb and is not well connected to the rest of the sentence.
Also, the pronoun “they,” which appears twice in this version, has no clear antecedent and does not appear to refer to any particular group of people. Such use of “they” does not convey a clear meaning and is not preferred by the GMAT.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 26 While the arms of the Tyrannosaurus rex seem puny and useless, there is reason to believe the Tyrannosaurus, using these and, thus, grasping prey. 1. [ ] (A) the Tyrannosaurus, using these and, thus, grasping prey > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) the Tyrannosaurus to have used them to grasp prey 3. [ ] (C) that the Tyrannosaurus could have used those and grasped prey > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) in the Tyrannosaurus that would have used those to grasp prey > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) in the Tyrannosaurus using them and grasping prey > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard The original version has multiple issues, among them that the demonstrative pronoun “these” is used in a situation in which the use of a personal pronoun would make more sense, as the sentence is not set up to point out any particular set of arms.
The correct version uses a personal pronoun, rather than a demonstrative pronoun, and concisely conveys a clear meaning.
(A) While the arms of the Tyrannosaurus rex seem puny and useless, there is reason to believe the Tyrannosaurus, using these and, thus, grasping prey .
In this version the demonstrative pronoun “these” is used inappropriately, as the sentence is not set up to point out any particular set of arms.
Also, this version uses “to believe” in a way that does not make sense, seeming to convey that there is reason to believe something that the Tyrannosaurus has claimed, rather than that there is reason to believe something about the Tyrannosaurus.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) While the arms of the Tyrannosaurus rex seem puny and useless, there is reason to believe the Tyrannosaurus to have used them to grasp prey .
This version appropriately uses the personal pronoun “them” to refer to the arms of the Tyrannosaurus and clearly conveys that there is reason to believe the Tyrannosaurus to have used its arms in a certain way.
Note that the idiom “believe X to Y” is used in this choice for the purpose of making this correct answer “sound funny” and, thus, seem to be incorrect.
YOUR CHOICE(C) While the arms of the Tyrannosaurus rex seem puny and useless, there is reason to believe that the Tyrannosaurus could have used those and grasped prey .
In this version, the demonstrative pronoun “those” is used inappropriately, as the sentence is not set up to point out any particular set of arms.
Also, in this version, the use of the arms and the grasping of prey are illogically separated. The point to be made is not that the Tyrannosaurus used its arms and grasped prey, but rather, that the Tyrannosaurus used its arms to grasp prey.
(D) While the arms of the Tyrannosaurus rex seem puny and useless, there is reason to believe in the Tyrannosaurus that would have used those to grasp prey .
In this version the demonstrative pronoun “those” is used inappropriately, as the sentence is not set up to point out any particular set of arms.
Also, this version uses “to believe in” in a way that does not make sense. The point to be made is not that there is reason to believe in the Tyrannosaurus, but rather that there is reason to believe something about the Tyrannosaurus.
(E) While the arms of the Tyrannosaurus rex seem puny and useless, there is reason to believe in the Tyrannosaurus using them and grasping prey .
This version uses “to believe in” in a way that does not make sense. The point to be made is not that there is reason to believe in the Tyrannosaurus, but rather that there is reason to believe something about the Tyrannosaurus.
Overall, this version conveys a nonsensical meaning, that there is a reason to believe in a certain Tyrannosaurus that is using its arms and grasping prey.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now make sure that we won’t be confused by what’s called “the expletive ‘it.’”
summary:
Lesson Content A demonstrative pronoun is a pronoun that points out or “demonstrates” the noun in the sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
A demonstrative pronoun takes the place of a noun and always stands alone. The major demonstrative pronouns are “this,” “that,” “these,” and “those.”
Consider the following example:
The tires on most sports cars are wider than those on most mid-size sedans.
In the sentence above, “those” is a demonstrative pronoun.
title: 4.10 Demonstrative Pronouns and Demonstrative Adjectives

contents:
Lesson Content As we look out for pronouns without logical referents, we have to be careful not to be confused by what is called “the expletive ‘it.’”
Consider the following examples:
It is apparent that Milan is a skilled writer.
Because he had been training, it was easy for Michael to swim across the lake.
In each of the above examples, the pronoun “it” has no referent and thus may seem to be incorrectly used.
However, we have heard and read sentences like the above examples.
When the pronoun “it” is used as it is in the above sentences, it is called “the expletive ‘it.’”
An expletive “it” in a sentence does not refer to any noun and is considered a “placeholder subject” that does not express any meaning.
💡MUST KNOW:
An expletive “it” in a sentence does not refer to any noun and is considered a “placeholder subject” that does not express any meaning.
To better understand how an expletive “it” works, let’s consider our first example again:
It is apparent that Milan is a skilled writer.
In that sentence, “it” does not express any meaning.
How can we tell? We can tell by seeing that, if we replace “it” with “that Milan is a skilled writer,” the sentence works and conveys the same meaning:
That Milan is a skilled writer is apparent.
The above sentence works and conveys the same meaning that the version that uses “it” conveys.
We can do the same with the second example:
Because he had been training, it was easy for Michael to swim across the lake.
Because he had been training, for Michael to swim across the lake was easy.
Once again, when we replace “it” with something that appears later in the sentence, the sentence works and conveys the same meaning that the version that uses “it” conveys.
So, we see that an expletive “it” can stand in for something that appears later in a sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
An expletive “it” often stands in for something that appears later in a sentence.
Now, notice that, in the sentence “That Milan is a skilled writer is apparent,” the noun clause “that Milan is a skilled writer” is the subject.
In other words, what is apparent? That Milan is a skilled writer is apparent.
So then, what we have done by using an expletive “it” in “It is apparent that Milan is a skilled writer,” is delayed the subject.
In fact, a subject that is delayed in this way is called a “delayed subject.”
So, an expletive “it” often appears in combination with a delayed subject.
💡MUST KNOW:
An expletive “it” often appears in combination with a delayed subject.
OK, great.
In the past, people commonly burned leaves, but it is rare now.
In this example, like in the previous examples, we see the pronoun “it.” At the same time, unlike in the previous examples, in this example, “it” is not used correctly.
In this example, “it” seems to refer to “burning leaves,” but burning leaves is not directly mentioned in the sentence.
Now, likely you are wondering, “But wait a minute.
Yes, we did.
A pronoun that has no logical referent is not correctly used unless that pronoun is used as an expletive.
💡MUST KNOW:
A pronoun that has no logical referent is not correctly used unless that pronoun is used as an expletive.
So, we can see that a key aspect of deciding whether “it” is being used correctly is checking to see whether “it” is being used as an expletive.
We can check to see whether “it” is being used as an expletive by looking for a delayed subject because, in most cases, where there is an expletive “it,” there is also a delayed subject.
Let’s consider our example again:
In the past, people commonly burned leaves, but it is rare now.
Notice that there is no delayed subject that we can logically substitute for “it.” If, for instance, we replace “it” with “rare now,” we get the following nonsensical sentence:
In the past, people commonly burned leaves, but rare now is.
So, in this case, we have the pronoun “it” without a referent, and since there is no delayed subject, we have no reason to believe that “it” is an expletive.
Now, let’s consider another example.
The young violinist realized that it is fun to learn challenging new pieces.
In this example, see the pronoun “it,” and looking through the sentence, we see that the pronoun “it” has no referent.
In this case, there is a delayed subject: “to learn challenging new pieces.”
We can tell because, when we replace “it” with “to learn challenging new pieces,” the sentence works and conveys the same meaning:
The young violinist realized that to learn challenging new pieces is fun.
So, since we have found a delayed subject, we now know that “it” is not used incorrectly in this case.
OK, now what about this one?
The senators were hoping to achieve great things, but it did not work out.
Once again, we see the pronoun “it” without a referent, since there’s no singular noun to which “it” would logically refer.
Looking over the sentence, we see that there is no delayed subject that we could logically substitute for “it.” If, for instance, we put “work out” where “it” is, we get the following nonsensical sentence:
The senators were hoping to achieve great things, but work out did not.
So, in this case, there’s no delayed subject.
Thus, in this case, “it” does not have a referent, and also there is no reason to believe that “it” is an expletive.
Now, there are some exceptions in which “it” is used as an expletive without a delayed subject, such as the following:
It is raining.
It is two o’clock.
However, since these exceptions are familiar expressions, we don’t have to worry about being confused by them.
So, to confirm that the pronoun “it” without a referent is not being used correctly, we can check for the presence of a delayed subject.
💡MUST KNOW:
To confirm that the pronoun “it” without a referent is not being used correctly, we can check for the presence of a delayed subject. If there is a delayed subject that we can logically substitute for “it,” “it” is an expletive. If there is no delayed subject that we can logically substitute for “it,” we can be confident that “it” is not correctly used.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now practice applying what we have discussed about the expletive “it” in answering Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 27 According to researchers, it is common for generally upbeat people to feel depressed during the winter months. 1. [x] (A) it is common for generally upbeat people to feel depressed during the winter months 2. [ ] (B) that generally upbeat people feel depressed are common during the winter months > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) people who are generally upbeat commonly feel it during the winter months, in which they are depressed > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) during the winter months, generally upbeat people feel it commonly as being depressed > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) feeling depressed during the winter months are common for generally upbeat people > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy YOUR CHOICE(A) According to researchers, it is common for generally upbeat people to feel depressed during the winter months .
This version correctly uses the expletive “it” in “it is common for generally upbeat people to feel depressed during the winter months.” In this case, “it” stands in for the subject “for generally upbeat people to feel depressed during the winter months.”
Notice that if we substitute the delayed subject for “it,” the meaning makes sense:
for generally upbeat people to feel depressed during the winter months is common
This is the only choice that creates a sentence that both is grammatically correct and conveys a clear meaning.
(B) According to researchers, that generally upbeat people feel depressed are common during the winter months .
This version contains a subject-verb agreement error. The subject “that generally upbeat people feel depressed” is singular, and thus the plural verb “are” is incorrect.
(C) According to researchers, people who are generally upbeat commonly feel it during the winter months, in which they are depressed .
Notice that this version does not use an expletive “it”; rather, it contains a pronoun that has no antecedent.
The sentence does not mention anything that people who are generally upbeat commonly feel during the winter months.
Also, there is no delayed subject that could be substituted for “it.”
(D) According to researchers, during the winter months, generally upbeat people feel it commonly as being depressed .
Notice that this version does not use an expletive “it”; rather, it contains a pronoun without any logical antecedent.
The sentence does not mention anything that people who are generally upbeat commonly feel during the winter months.
Also, there is no delayed subject that could be substituted for “it.”
So, what “it” refers to is unclear.
(E) According to researchers, feeling depressed during the winter months are common for generally upbeat people .
This version contains a subject-verb agreement error. The subject “feeling depressed” is singular, and thus the plural verb “are” is incorrect.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 28 The results of sleep research indicate that, if you can fall asleep readily at any time of day, it probably means that you are sleep deprived. 1. [ ] (A) it probably means that > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) it is probable that it means that > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) that probably means > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) it is probable that 5. [ ] (E) what that probably means is that > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: hard There is no way for a dependent clause starting with “if” to serve as the antecedent of a pronoun, because, even if we were to go with the idea that a fact can be the antecedent of a pronoun, a dependent clause that begins with “if” does not state a fact. It expresses a condition upon which the validity of a fact depends.
So, the pronoun “it” or “that” cannot refer to “if you can fall asleep readily at any time of day.”
Therefore, there is nothing for “it,” in some choices, or “that,” in other choices, to refer to.
Thus, only in the correct version, in which there is a delayed subject, is “it” correctly used.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The results of sleep research indicate that, if you can fall asleep readily at any time of day, it probably means that you are sleep deprived.
There is no referent for “it” in this version, since the sentence mentions nothing that would mean that you are sleep deprived.
Also, “it” is not an expletive because “that you are sleep deprived” is not a delayed subject that we can logically substitute for the second “it.” If we do the substitution, we get the following incomplete thought:
The results of sleep research indicate that … that you are sleep deprived probably means.
That you are sleep deprived probably means what?
(B) The results of sleep research indicate that, if you can fall asleep readily at any time of day, it is probable that it means that you are sleep deprived.
The first “it” in this version serves as an expletive “it” with the delayed subject “that it means that you are probably sleep deprived.”
However, the second “it” has no referent since the sentence mentions nothing that would mean that you are sleep deprived, and also, the second “it” is not an expletive because “that you are sleep deprived” is not a delayed subject that we can logically substitute for the second “it.” If we do the substitution, we get the following incomplete thought:
The results of sleep research indicate that … it is probable that that you are sleep deprived means.
That you are sleep deprived means what?
(C) The results of sleep research indicate that, if you can fall asleep readily at any time of day, that probably means you are sleep deprived.
There is no referent for “that” in this version, since the sentence mentions nothing that would mean you are sleep deprived.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) The results of sleep research indicate that, if you can fall asleep readily at any time of day, it is probable that you are sleep deprived.
In this version, “it” serves as an expletive “it” with the delayed subject “that you are sleep deprived.” We can confirm that this version is correct by replacing “it” with the delayed subject and seeing that the sentence still works:
The results of sleep research indicate that, if you can fall asleep readily at any time of day, that you are sleep deprived is probable.
(E) The results of sleep research indicate that, if you can fall asleep readily at any time of day, what that probably means is that you are sleep deprived.
There is no referent for the first “that” in this version, since the sentence mentions nothing that would mean that you are sleep deprived.
Correct Answer: (D)
summary:
Lesson Content As we look out for pronouns without logical referents, we have to be careful not to be confused by what is called “the expletive ‘it.’”
Consider the following examples:
It is apparent that Milan is a skilled writer.
Because he had been training, it was easy for Michael to swim across the lake.
In each of the above examples, the pronoun “it” has no referent and thus may seem to be incorrectly used.
title: 4.11 The Expletive “It” and Delayed Subjects

contents:
Lesson Content A modifier is a word, phrase, or clause that modifies, or changes, another word or expression.
💡MUST KNOW:
A modifier is a word, phrase, or clause that modifies, or changes, another word or expression.
As you already know, in answering SC questions, the evaluation of a sentence’s meaning and logic is just as important as the evaluation of a sentence’s grammar and style.
The GMAT uses SC questions involving modifiers to test our skill in determining what makes sense and what doesn’t.
Does the sentence make sense if read literally?
Is the meaning conveyed by the sentence logical?
Does the sentence say what the author intended it to say?
Is the meaning of the sentence clear, or must we guess what the author intended?
In this chapter, we’ll comprehensively cover common types of modifiers, discuss how to recognize them, and discuss strategies for determining whether the ways in which modifiers are used in sentences make sense and are effective.
The major concepts we’ll cover include:
• Adjectives and Adverbs
• Misplaced Modifiers
• Misplaced Introductory Modifiers
• Dangling Modifiers
• The Possessive Modification Trap
• Squinting Modifiers
• Prepositional Phrases
• Modifiers of Time
• Relative Clauses
• Appositives
• Participles and Participial Phrases
• Absolute Phrases
Before we get into detail, let’s take a summary look at types of modifiers you’ll likely encounter in Sentence Correction questions.
Adjectives:
The blue plane was sitting on the runway.
“Blue” modifies the noun “plane,” providing more detail about it.
The hungry bear was delighted to find the camper’s food.
“Hungry” modifies the noun “bear,” providing more detail about it.
Adverbs:
The little boy jumped up and down excitedly.
“Excitedly” modifies the verb “jumped,” describing how the boy jumped.
You must carefully evaluate your options.
“Carefully” modifies the verb “evaluate,” telling us how we should evaluate.
Prepositional Phrases:
The town of Manchester is beautiful.
“Of Manchester” modifies the noun “town,” telling us which town.
He took the Ace of diamonds from the deck of cards.
“Of diamonds” modifies the noun “ace,” telling us which ace, and “from the deck of cards” modifies the verb “took,” telling us from where he took the ace of diamonds.
Relative Clauses:
The boat that was used in the race is for sale.
“That was used in the race” modifies the noun “boat,” telling us which boat is for sale.
The Shiba Inu, which is the oldest breed of dog, is so cool!
“Which is the oldest breed of dog” modifies the noun “Shiba Inu,” telling us more about it.
Present Participles:
The crying baby needs a nap.
“Crying” modifies the noun “baby,” telling us about what the baby is doing.
The giggling girl was happy to see her friend.
“Giggling” modifies the noun “girl,” telling us which girl.
Past Participles:
The tired runner took a nap.
“Tired” modifies the noun “runner,” telling us which runner.
Battered by the rain, the tent began to leak.
“Battered by rain” modifies the noun “tent,” telling us what happened to the tent.
Appositives:
The fish, a piranha, swam near my feet.
“A piranha” modifies the noun “fish,” telling us what type of fish.
Scott’s dog Blaze has red fur.
“Blaze” modifies the noun “Scott’s dog,” telling us more about Scott’s dog.
Absolute Phrases:
Its sensitive antennae moving back and forth, the ant could smell the sweet chocolate chips.
“Its sensitive antennae moving back and forth” modifies “the ant” and also tells us more about the entire clause that follows the modifier.
Her soufflé rising on the table and almost ready to be served, the chef polished the dessert plate.
“Her soufflé rising on the table and almost ready to be served” modifies “the chef” and tells us more about the entire clause that follows the modifier.
Don’t worry if any of these types of modifiers seem new to you; we’ll cover them all.
Let’s begin by discussing the two most basic types of modifiers, adjectives and adverbs.
summary:
Lesson Content A modifier is a word, phrase, or clause that modifies, or changes, another word or expression.
💡MUST KNOW:
A modifier is a word, phrase, or clause that modifies, or changes, another word or expression.
As you already know, in answering SC questions, the evaluation of a sentence’s meaning and logic is just as important as the evaluation of a sentence’s grammar and style.
The GMAT uses SC questions involving modifiers to test our skill in determining what makes sense and what doesn’t.
title: 5.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content Adjectives are modifiers that can modify only nouns or pronouns, while adverbs are modifiers that can modify verbs, adjectives, and other adverbs.
💡MUST KNOW:
Adjectives can modify only nouns or pronouns. Adverbs can modify verbs, adjectives, and other adverbs.
Here are some examples of common adjectives: hard, bright, pretty, thoughtless, spurious, enthusiastic, yellow, tentative, sonorous, new, good.
The new book is now in stores.
She made a thoughtless comment.
The pretty horse is sleeping.
Here are some examples of common adverbs: occasionally, often, seldom, always, never, clearly, famously, judiciously, slowly, well.
We run occasionally.
The car approached them slowly.
He spoke convincingly.
The most common use of an adverb is to modify a verb.
As you may have noticed from the adverb examples above, many, but not all, adverbs end with -ly.
💡MUST KNOW:
A word ending in –ly typically will not modify a noun, because most –ly words are adverbs.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether the underlined word is being used as an adjective or an adverb. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions There are a few issues involving adjectives and adverbs that commonly appear in Sentence Correction questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Adjectives are modifiers that can modify only nouns or pronouns, while adverbs are modifiers that can modify verbs, adjectives, and other adverbs.
💡MUST KNOW:
Adjectives can modify only nouns or pronouns. Adverbs can modify verbs, adjectives, and other adverbs.
Here are some examples of common adjectives: hard, bright, pretty, thoughtless, spurious, enthusiastic, yellow, tentative, sonorous, new, good.
The new book is now in stores.
She made a thoughtless comment.
title: 5.2 Adjectives and Adverbs

contents:
Lesson Content One way in which adjectives and adverbs can be used to create issues in Sentence Correction questions is via using an adverb when an adjective is required or vice versa.
How do we know whether we need an adjective or an adverb in a sentence? The context will always tell us whether an adjective or adverb is correct.
💡MUST KNOW:
The meaning of a sentence will enable us to determine whether an adverb or an adjective should be used at a given point in a sentence.
Consider the following example:
Incorrect: The professor was a supposedly expert in illuminated manuscripts.
We can tell that, in the above sentence, “supposedly” is misused, because “supposedly” is an adverb, but it is being used to modify a noun, “expert.” One way to fix the sentence is to replace the adverb “supposedly” with the adjective “supposed.”
The professor was a supposed expert in illuminated manuscripts.
Here’s a less obviously incorrect example:
Incorrect: Some people believe that true equality cannot be reached when people of different genders are educated and housed separate.
In this example, the adjective “separate” is misused to modify the verbs “educated” and “housed.” We need an adverb instead.
Some people believe that true equality cannot be reached when people of different genders are educated and housed separately.
Noticing the misuse of the adverb and the misuse of the adjective in the last two examples was fairly straightforward, as the misused modifiers, “supposedly” and “separate” were directly adjacent to the words that they were modifying.
Incorrect: The reporters noticed that the prime minister’s attendance at the briefings had become less regularly.
Is the adverb “regularly” meant to modify the verb “become?” By considering the meaning of the sentence we can tell that “regularly” must be meant to modify something other than “become,” because saying that something “had become less regularly” doesn’t make sense.
By considering the sentence as a whole, we can tell that “regularly” must be meant to describe “attendance,” but since “attendance” is a noun, describing it by using the adverb “regularly” is incorrect.
The reporters noticed that the prime minister’s attendance at the briefings had become less regular.
Of course, in a Sentence Correction question, such an issue might be solved in a less simple way, such as by changing the sentence so that the use of the adverb makes sense:
The reporters noticed that the prime minister had been attending the briefings less regularly.
Since, in this version, the adverb “regularly” modifies the verb “had been attending,” the adverb is correctly used.
💡MUST KNOW:
Be sure that a sentence does not use an adverb when an adjective is required or vice versa.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Determine whether each sentence is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Now, let’s answer a Sentence Correction question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 1 The playing of some heavy metal guitarists is rather skillfully, but, ironically, so loudly that a listener may have difficulty appreciating it. 1. [ ] (A) The playing of some heavy metal guitarists is rather skillfully, but, ironically, so loudly > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Some guitarists, when playing heavy metal, do so rather skillfully but, ironically, so loudly 3. [ ] (C) When playing, heavy metal guitarists are, in some cases, rather skillful, but, ironically, it is so loudly > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) In the cases of some heavy metal guitarists, when they play it, it is rather skillful, but, ironically, so loud > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Some guitarists who play heavy metal are rather skillful but, ironically, so loudly > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium The adverb “loudly” should not be used to modify nouns, such as “playing” or “guitarists.” So, to answer this question correctly, we have to notice whether “loudly” modifies a noun or modifies a verb when “loudly” appears in an answer choice.
(A) The playing of some heavy metal guitarists is rather skillfully, but, ironically, so loudly that a listener may have difficulty appreciating it.
This choice could be tempting because it is structured in an organized way that includes the use of parallel modifiers “skillfully” and “loudly.”
However, since “skillfully” and “loudly” are adverbs, using them to modify the gerund “playing,” which is essentially a noun, is incorrect.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Some guitarists, when playing heavy metal, do so rather skillfully but, ironically, so loudly that a listener may have difficulty appreciating it.
In this version, the adverbs “skillfully” and “loudly” are correctly used to modify the verb “do.”
(C) When playing, heavy metal guitarists are, in some cases, rather skillful, but, ironically, it is so loudly that a listener may have difficulty appreciating it.
Using the adjective “skillful” to modify “guitarists” makes sense.
However, the pronoun “it” has no clear referent.
Also, the adverb “loudly” is incorrectly used to modify the pronoun “it.”
(D) In the cases of some heavy metal guitarists, when they play it, it is rather skillful, but, ironically, so loud that a listener may have difficulty appreciating it.
In this version, “it” may refer to “heavy metal.” However, since “heavy metal” appears only as a modifier modifying “guitarists” and not as a noun, there is no referent for “it.”
Also, while using the adjectives “skillful” and “loud” to modify “it” could make sense, since we don’t know what “it” is, we don’t know what the sentence is describing as skillful and loud, and, certainly, the music, heavy metal, would not be skillful. So, this version does not make sense.
(E) Some guitarists who play heavy metal are rather skillful but, ironically, so loudly that a listener may have difficulty appreciating it.
In this version, the adjective “skillful” is correctly used to modify “guitarists.”
However, while using “loudly” to modify “play” would make sense, the sentence is structured in a way such that “loudly” does not modify “play” but, rather, incorrectly modifies “guitarists.”
Correct Answer: (B) So, we have seen one way in which adjectives and adverbs are used to create issues in Sentence Correction questions, using an adjective where an adverb is needed or vice versa.
summary:
Lesson Content One way in which adjectives and adverbs can be used to create issues in Sentence Correction questions is via using an adverb when an adjective is required or vice versa.
How do we know whether we need an adjective or an adverb in a sentence? The context will always tell us whether an adjective or adverb is correct.
💡MUST KNOW:
The meaning of a sentence will enable us to determine whether an adverb or an adjective should be used at a given point in a sentence.
title: 5.2.1 Using an Adjective where an Adverb is Required or an Adverb where an Adjective is Required

contents:
Lesson Content As the name implies, a misplaced modifier is a modifier that is in the wrong place in a sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
The misplacement of modifiers results in errors in meaning, and misplaced modifiers are frequently used in incorrect answer choices in Sentence Correction questions.
Misplaced modifier questions can be particularly challenging because even native English speakers often misplace modifiers in their speech and writing.
Let’s see how misplacement can affect the meaning of a sentence by considering the effects of misplacing adjectives.
summary:
Lesson Content As the name implies, a misplaced modifier is a modifier that is in the wrong place in a sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
The misplacement of modifiers results in errors in meaning, and misplaced modifiers are frequently used in incorrect answer choices in Sentence Correction questions.
Misplaced modifier questions can be particularly challenging because even native English speakers often misplace modifiers in their speech and writing.
Let’s see how misplacement can affect the meaning of a sentence by considering the effects of misplacing adjectives.
title: 5.3 Misplaced Modifiers

contents:
Lesson Content When an adjective is incorrectly separated from the noun it modifies, the meaning of the sentence can be distorted.
Incorrect: On the tables at the event were nutritious trays of fruits and vegetables.
Hmm – those nutritious trays sure sound delicious, don’t they? Better have a good dentist on call, right? Of course, the adjective “nutritious” is misplaced.
On the tables at the event were trays of nutritious fruits and vegetables.
Let’s look at another example sentence:
Incorrect: The farmer showed up at my house with a fresh crate full of zucchini.
A “fresh crate,” huh? Had it grown on a crate vine? The adjective “fresh” is misplaced.
The farmer showed up at my house with a crate full of fresh zucchini.
Let’s look at another example sentence:
Incorrect: Because the fighter was trying to lose two more pounds before the big fight, his trainer purchased low-calorie bottles of sports drinks for him to drink.
Low-calorie bottles? Those sound very delicious, right? Of course, the adjective “low-calorie” is misplaced; the drinks are low-calorie; a bottle wouldn’t be.
Because the fighter was trying to lose two more pounds before the big fight, his trainer purchased bottles of low-calorie sports drinks for him to drink.
To do well in Sentence Correction, we must pay close attention to the meaning and placement of every word in a sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
Read sentences carefully to ensure that adjectives modify the correct nouns. Logic and meaning will indicate where the adjective should be placed.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. Be on the lookout for misplaced adjectives. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Now, let’s consider a Sentence Correction example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 2 Workers, tearing down the Cressoni Theater, they discovered a gold collection of ancient coins that had engravings about emperors such as Valentinian III and Libio Severo and indicated that the coins were minted before 474 CE. 1. [ ] (A) Workers, tearing down the Cressoni Theater, they discovered a gold collection of ancient coins that had engravings about emperors such as Valentinian III and Libio Severo and > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) When workers were tearing down the Cressoni Theater, they discovered a collection of ancient gold coins that had engravings about emperors such as Valentinian III and Libio Severo, the presence of which 3. [ ] (C) Workers tearing down the Cressoni Theater discovered a gold collection of ancient coins, which had engravings about emperors such as Valentinian III and Libio Severo, and they > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Workers, who, in tearing down the Cressoni Theater and discovering a collection of ancient gold coins that had engravings about emperors such as Valentinian III and Libio Severo, > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) When workers were tearing down the Cressoni Theater and discovering an ancient gold collection of coins that had engravings about emperors such as Valentinian III and Libio Severo, they > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy Using the adjective “gold” to modify “coins” makes sense, but it does not make sense to use “gold” to modify “collection.” So, most of the incorrect choices in this question can be identified via our noticing that they include either “gold collection of ancient coins” or “ancient gold collection of coins,” neither of which makes sense. To confirm that those choices are incorrect and to eliminate the last remaining incorrect choice, we can notice other errors in structure and meaning.
(A) Workers, tearing down the Cressoni Theater, they discovered a gold collection of ancient coins that had engravings about emperors such as Valentinian III and Libio Severo and indicated that the coins were minted before 474 CE.
The adjective “gold” is illogically used to modify “collection,” rather than to modify “coins.”
Also, the pronoun “they” unnecessarily fills the role of subject, as “workers” has already filled that role.
Also, it is not clear whether the sentence is meant to convey that the workers indicated that the coins were minted before 474 CE or that the coins themselves indicated that the coins were minted before 474 CE, neither of which meanings is completely logical anyway.
YOUR CHOICE(B) When workers were tearing down the Cressoni Theater, they discovered a collection of ancient gold coins that had engravings about emperors such as Valentinian III and Libio Severo, the presence of which indicated that the coins were minted before 474 CE.
“Gold” logically modifies “coins,” and the sentence is well structured and, thus, effectively conveys that the workers found the coins and that the presence of the engravings indicated that the coins were minted before 474 CE.
(C) Workers tearing down the Cressoni Theater discovered a gold collection of ancient coins, which had engravings about emperors such as Valentinian III and Libio Severo, and they indicated that the coins were minted before 474 CE.
The adjective “gold” is illogically used to modify “collection,” rather than to modify “coins.”
Also, what “they” in “they indicated” refers to is not clear. It most logically would refer to the “engravings” but could, conceivably, refer to “workers” or even to “coins.”
(D) Workers, who, in tearing down the Cressoni Theater and discovering a collection of ancient gold coins that had engravings about emperors such as Valentinian III and Libio Severo, indicated that the coins were minted before 474 CE.
In this version, “gold” logically modifies “coins.”
However, this version is not a complete sentence. We can see more clearly that it is a fragment by removing the long phrase “in tearing down the Cressoni Theater … Libio Severo.” By doing so, we get the following, which is clearly just the noun “workers” followed by a relative clause that begins with “who.”
Workers, who … indicated that they were minted before 474 CE.
(E) When workers were tearing down the Cressoni Theater and discovering an ancient gold collection of coins that had engravings about emperors such as Valentinian III and Libio Severo, they indicated that the coins were minted before 474 CE.
In this version, “ancient gold” is illogically used to modify “collection” rather than “coins.”
Also, “when workers were tearing down the Cressoni Theater and discovering” conveys the somewhat muddled meaning that the workers were tearing down the theater and discovering coins at the same time, possibly continuously.
Also, since “they” in “they indicated” seems to refer to workers, the sentence conveys the not quite logical meaning that, when the workers were tearing down the theater and discovering the collection, the workers indicated that the coins were minted before 474 CE.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss misplaced adverbs.
summary:
Lesson Content When an adjective is incorrectly separated from the noun it modifies, the meaning of the sentence can be distorted.
Incorrect: On the tables at the event were nutritious trays of fruits and vegetables.
Hmm – those nutritious trays sure sound delicious, don’t they? Better have a good dentist on call, right? Of course, the adjective “nutritious” is misplaced.
On the tables at the event were trays of nutritious fruits and vegetables.
title: 5.3.1 Misplaced Adjectives

contents:
Lesson Content When we evaluate a sentence, we must take care to ensure that any adverbs that the sentence contains are placed logically since, like the incorrect placement of an adjective, the incorrect placement of an adverb can significantly distort the meaning of a sentence.
Although we could use any adverbs as examples, for this discussion, let’s focus on some adverbs that are called limiting words, since these words frequently appear in misplaced modifier questions.
To see how placement of a limiting word can affect meaning, let’s consider some sentences that contain the commonly used adverb “only”:
I ate only fruits and vegetables for two weeks.
I ate fruits and vegetables for only two weeks.
I only ate fruits and vegetables for two weeks.
Each of the sentences is grammatically correct.
In the first sentence, “only” is placed directly before “fruits and vegetables.” So, the sentence conveys that, for two weeks, I ate no foods other than fruits and vegetables.
In the second sentence, “only” is placed just before “two weeks.” So, the sentence conveys that the period of time during which I ate fruits and vegetables was only two weeks.
Since, in the third sentence, “only” is placed directly before “ate,” what the sentence conveys is not clear, and could be rather illogical.
Consider another example:
Erica can only drink water.
Only Erica can drink water.
Erica can drink water only.
Each says something different.
The second says that Erica is the one person who can drink water; no other person can drink it.
The third says that Erica can drink water, but she cannot drink anything else.
As you can see, changes in the placement of the adverb “only” completely changed the meaning of the sentences, although no words were added or removed.
While one could make the case that all the sentences above about Erica may make sense, in a Sentence Correction question, only one choice is correct.
On the GMAT, we can often choose the choice that conveys a logical meaning by keeping in mind one simple idea: Generally, the best placement of a limiting adverb is immediately before the word or phrase that it is meant to modify.
💡MUST KNOW:
Generally, the best placement of a limiting adverb is immediately before the word or phrase that it is meant to modify.
For example, let’s say we want to communicate that our golf team practices on Monday, but not on other days.
(1) The team only practices on Mondays.
(2) The team practices only on Mondays.
Because in the first sentence, “only” is placed directly before “practice,” the sentence communicates the idea that, although the team may meet (and possibly practice) on other days of the week, on Mondays the team does nothing but practice (instead of eating ice cream together and then practicing, or instead of sleeping or drinking water, for example).
If we keep in mind the idea that, generally, the best placement of a limiting adverb (in this case “only”), is directly before the word or phrase that it modifies, choosing the sentence that conveys the meaning that we wanted to convey is an easy task.
The team practices only on Mondays.
Now look, you must interject some of your own critical analysis when applying this idea.
The team practices on Mondays only.
Since the meaning is clear, the placement of “only” directly after “Monday” is also correct.
Of course, a Sentence Correction question will not start off by providing a meaning that the correct answer has to convey.
Let’s look at some more examples.
I almost paid $1,500 for a round-trip ticket from Los Angeles to Istanbul.
I paid almost $1,500 for a round-trip ticket from Los Angeles to Istanbul.
Do these sentences say the same thing? Of course not.
Consider the following two versions of a sentence.
Unlike fish of many other types, white sharks only breed once every two years.
Unlike fish of many other types, white sharks breed only once every two years.
The first sentence says something illogical; it says that the only thing white sharks do is breed once every two years.
Let’s look at another example.
The missing documents were finally found by the computer expert who comes in weekly hidden in the external drive.
The missing documents were, by the computer expert who comes in weekly, finally found hidden in the external drive.
The first sentence is comical, right? The expert comes in hidden in the external drive? Can you picture that scene? The second sentence, of course, expresses the meaning that the author intended; the missing documents were found hidden in the external drive.
When we answer a Sentence Correction question, we must carefully consider every word in the sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
When we answer a Sentence Correction question, we must carefully consider every word in the sentence. If we don’t read carefully and critically, we’ll miss small but important details, such as the effects of the placement of adjectives and adverbs.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice From each of the following pairs, select the sentence that conveys what is outlined in the scenario. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Now, let’s see how adverb misplacement can work in a Sentence Correction question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 3 Workers digging in New Zealand discovered an ancient waka, which is a Maori word for “canoe,” that partially had been completed and then buried. 1. [ ] (A) waka, which is a Maori word for “canoe,” that partially had been completed and then buried > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) waka that was the word that the Maori used for a canoe, which had partially been completed when they buried it > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) waka being a Maori canoe, which partially was completed and then buried > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) waka, a Maori version of a canoe, that had been partially completed before being buried 5. [ ] (E) waka that was what the Maori called a canoe, which had been completed partially before its burial > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy The adverb “partially” appears in all of the versions of the sentence. However, in all versions but one, “partially” is not well placed. The point that the sentence is meant to make is that the waka was partially completed. In order for the sentence to convey this meaning, “partially” has to directly precede “completed.” When “partially” is placed directly before a word other than “completed,” such as “was” or “had,” the meaning conveyed becomes illogical or ambiguous.
(A) Workers digging in New Zealand discovered an ancient waka, which is a Maori word for “canoe,” that partially had been completed and then buried .
The sentence is about a waka, which is a type of canoe, not about a word. So, the use of the modifier “which is a Maori word for canoe,” which indicates that a waka is a word, does not make sense. A word would not be partially completed and then buried.
Also, the placement of “partially” directly before “had” does not make sense. The point to be made is not that the waka partially had but did not completely have something. The point is that the waka had been partially completed.
(B) Workers digging in New Zealand discovered an ancient waka that was the word that the Maori used for a canoe, which had partially been completed when they buried it .
The sentence is about a waka, which is a type of canoe, not about a word. So, the use of the modifier “that was the word that the Maori used for a canoe,” which indicates that a waka is a word, does not make sense. A word would not be partially completed and then buried.
Also, the placement of “partially” directly before “been” does not quite make sense. The point to be made is that the waka had been partially completed. So, optimally, “partially” would directly precede “completed” rather than precede “been.”
Also, the pronoun “they” has no logical referent, as “workers” is the only plural noun to which “they” could refer, but it does not make sense that the workers would have discovered a canoe that had been partially completed when the workers buried it.
(C) Workers digging in New Zealand discovered an ancient waka being a Maori canoe, which partially was completed and then buried .
The wording “waka being a Maori canoe” conveys the nonsensical meaning that the waka was actively choosing to be a Maori canoe.
Also, the adverb “partially” is misplaced. The point is not that the waka “partially was” completed and buried. The point to be made is that the waka was partially completed. So, “partially” should directly precede “completed” rather than precede “was.”
YOUR CHOICE(D) Workers digging in New Zealand discovered an ancient waka, a Maori version of a canoe, that had been partially completed before being buried .
In this version, the modifier “a Maori version of a canoe” logically modifies “waka.”
Also, “partially” is placed just before “completed,” so the sentence conveys the logical meaning that the waka had been partially completed.
(E) Workers digging in New Zealand discovered an ancient waka that was what the Maori called a canoe, which had been completed partially before its burial .
This version is illogical because a waka is not “what the Maori called a canoe;” a waka is a Maori canoe.
Also, the placement of “partially” results in a sentence that does not convey a clear meaning. As written, the sentence could convey either that the waka was completed partially or that the waka was completed and that its completion was “partially before” its burial.
Also, the expression “its burial” tends to convey that something was buried in a ceremonial way rather than simply buried, a meaning that does not make sense in this context.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s continue by discussing an important rule that we can use to identify modifier and meaning issues: noun modifiers must be placed as closely as possible to what they modify.
summary:
Lesson Content When we evaluate a sentence, we must take care to ensure that any adverbs that the sentence contains are placed logically since, like the incorrect placement of an adjective, the incorrect placement of an adverb can significantly distort the meaning of a sentence.
Although we could use any adverbs as examples, for this discussion, let’s focus on some adverbs that are called limiting words, since these words frequently appear in misplaced modifier questions.
title: 5.3.2 Misplaced Adverbs

contents:
Lesson Content As you’ve begun to see, the positions of words, phrases, and clauses matter greatly to the meaning of a sentence, and small changes in the placement of words, phrases, and clauses can translate into dramatic changes in the meaning of a sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
A noun modifier must be placed as closely as possible to what it modifies.
Consider the following examples in which the modifiers are in bold and the nouns being modified are underlined.
Correct: The man who was wearing the red scarf is upstairs. Incorrect: The man is upstairs who was wearing the red scarf.
The clause “who was wearing the red scarf” modifies the noun “man.” So, the clause should be placed as closely as possible to the noun “man.”
Correct: The house that is adjacent to mine is quite large.
Incorrect: The house is quite large that is adjacent to mine.
We see that “that is adjacent to mine” is meant to modify the noun “house.” So, the modifier must be placed as closely as possible to the noun “house.” It makes no sense to place the modifier next to the adjective “large.” The second sentence conveys that “large” is “adjacent to mine.” Of course, such a meaning is illogical.
Correct: The Boeing 747, which is very fast, is an amazing plane and can fly for up to 20 hours.
Incorrect: The Boeing 747 is an amazing plane and can fly for up to 20 hours, which is very fast.
The modifier “which is very fast” is meant to modify the noun “Boeing 747,” so, we place this noun modifier right next to “Boeing 747,” the noun it modifies.
Correct: Barack Obama, the 44th president of the United States, had been a community organizer. Incorrect: Barack Obama had been a community organizer, the 44th president of the United States.
The noun modifier “the 44th president of the United States” is meant to modify the noun “Barack Obama.” So, we put the modifier directly after this noun.
At this point, don’t worry if you were not able to recognize all of the structures in bold as modifiers.
As the chapter continues to unfold, we’ll have opportunities to practice applying this concept.
summary:
Lesson Content As you’ve begun to see, the positions of words, phrases, and clauses matter greatly to the meaning of a sentence, and small changes in the placement of words, phrases, and clauses can translate into dramatic changes in the meaning of a sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
A noun modifier must be placed as closely as possible to what it modifies.
Consider the following examples in which the modifiers are in bold and the nouns being modified are underlined.
title: 5.3.3 A Noun Modifier Must Be Placed as Closely as Possible to What It Modifies.

contents:
Lesson Content A prepositional phrase is a modifier composed of a preposition, such as “in,” “under,” “by,” or “for,” followed by a noun, and possibly some modifiers that modify that noun.
in boxes
under rocks
by the lake
for illiterate individuals
Prepositional phrases can serve as adjectives or adverbs.
When prepositional phrases serve as adjectives, they modify nouns, as in the following examples:
On the shelf was chocolate in boxes.
The prepositional phrase “in boxes” modifies the noun “chocolate.”
Most of the houses by the lake were built decades ago.
The prepositional phrase “by the lake” modifies the noun “houses.”
When prepositional phrases serve as adverbs, they generally modify verbs.
Some species of insects live under rocks.
The prepositional phrase “under rocks” modifies “live” and indicates where the insects live.
The hikers took a break at noon.
The prepositional phrase “at noon” modifies “took” and indicates when the hikers took a break.
In the waves, some dolphins were playing.
The prepositional phrase “in the waves” modifies “were playing” and indicates where the dolphins were playing.
Writers can mistakenly position a prepositional phrase, rendering a sentence illogical.
💡MUST KNOW:
A modifying prepositional phrase must always be as close as possible to the word that it modifies.
When a modifying prepositional phrase is misplaced, odd – and sometimes humorous – things occur.
Incorrect: A man was walking a dog in a Speedo.
Unless the dog was wearing a Speedo, the prepositional phrase “in a Speedo” is a misplaced modifier; it should be modifying the noun “man.”
The sentence could be fixed in various ways that eliminate the misplaced modifier, including:
A man in a Speedo was walking a dog.
Notice that, in the revised version of the sentence, the prepositional phrase “in a Speedo” is close to the noun that it modifies, “man.”
Spot the problem in the following sentence:
Incorrect: The craftsman worked to develop a guitar that would allow a player to perfectly express himself when he played a song for twenty years.
Playing a song for twenty years? Humorous, right? The prepositional phrase “for twenty years” is misplaced, because it should be modifying “worked.” A corrected sentence could read:
The craftsman worked for twenty years to develop a guitar that would allow a player to perfectly express himself when he played a song.
The following is also correct, because a prepositional phrase that modifies a finite verb in a clause can be placed before the clause:
For twenty years, the craftsman worked to develop a guitar that would allow a player to perfectly express himself when he played a song.
Let’s try another example.
Incorrect: Every Saturday afternoon, the men who lived in the senior home on Kimball Avenue played cards in the park under a tree.
Pay close attention when you encounter a sentence that contains more than one prepositional phrase, because the order of the phrases matters.
Every Saturday afternoon, the men who lived in the senior home on Kimball Avenue played cards under a tree in the park.
Let’s look at one more.
Incorrect: Justine still refuses to create an online profile, even though several of her friends met people whom they ended up marrying on dating websites.
Unless Justine’s friends were live streaming their weddings on dating websites, the above sentence contains a misplaced prepositional phrase.
Justine still refuses to create an online profile, even though several of her friends ended up marrying people whom they met on dating websites.
Notice we must rearrange the sentence a bit more in this case to ensure that it clearly conveys that the couples met on dating websites and did not marry on dating websites.
Incorrect: Justine still refuses to create an online profile, even though several of her friends met people on dating websites whom they ended up marrying.
Although the above sentence places the prepositional phrase fairly close to the verb, “met,” that the phrase is meant to modify, the sentence does not clearly express the idea that the people met on dating websites.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Sentence Correction questions, be on the lookout for prepositional phrases that are improperly placed and, thus, modify the wrong things. Such sentences will depart from the meanings that their authors were seeking to express.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is incorrect or correct. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 The sommelier, wearing the colorful pin given only to master sommeliers, served samples of rare French wines to the people attending the event in fine crystal stemware. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 The famous driver, dressed in red and holding his black, shiny helmet at his side, served the children cookies cut in the shape of the car that he had used to win multiple championship races. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 Jonathan, a man of style and grace, stopped to help a family of ducks that seemed lost on the way to work. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 4 On the way to school, Samantha, the girl who won the talent show last year and who donates time to the animal shelter, stopped to rescue a puppy. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 We bantered about the soccer game in the stands. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 6 In the oven, Jacques checked the roasted vegetables. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 7 We read about the lost dog that was rescued on Facebook. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 8 Some people standing on the platform said that they saw a man boarding the train that left for Prague at 9:02 with a large, blue suitcase. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 9 The cake was frosted by the assistant pastry chef with pink icing. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 10 The paleontologist discusses newfound information on the many types of dinosaurs that lived during the Jurassic period in his new book. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
This sentence contains a misplaced modifier. As written, “served samples of rare French wines to the people attending the event in fine crystal stemware” makes it appear that the people or, perhaps, the event was in fine crystal stemware. Of course, we want the sentence to convey that the samples of wines were served in fine crystal stemware. The following correctly written sentence does so.
The sommelier, wearing the colorful pin given only to master sommeliers, served to the people attending the event samples of rare French wines in fine crystal stemware.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
The sentence contains three prepositional phrases, “in red, “at his side,” and “in the shape of the car….” All three are correctly placed, with the result that the sentence effectively conveys a logical meaning.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
This sentence contains a misplaced modifier, “on the way to work,” and therefore conveys that the ducks were lost on their way to work. Of course, we want the sentence to convey that Jonathan stopped on his way to work. The following sentence does just that.
On the way to work, Jonathan, a man of style and grace, stopped to help a family of ducks that seemed lost.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
Remember, a prepositional phrase that modifies a finite verb in a clause can be placed before the clause.
So, “on the way to school” is correctly placed to modify the finite verb “stopped.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
Was the soccer game happening in the stands? Probably not, right? So, the prepositional phrase “in the stands” is misplaced. The people were bantering in the stands, so we need the prepositional phrase to be placed in a way such that that meaning is the one conveyed. A corrected sentence is:
In the stands, we bantered about the soccer game.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
Was Jacques in the oven? We hope not. The prepositional phrase “in the oven” is misplaced. It should be modifying the vegetables, not Jacques. A corrected sentence is:
Jacques checked the roasted vegetables in the oven.
or
Jacques checked the roasted vegetables, which were in the oven.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 7 Explanation:
Can anyone explain how a lost dog could be rescued on Facebook? This sentence is illogical, right? The author meant to say that people read on Facebook that the dog was rescued. A corrected sentence could read:
On Facebook, we read about the lost dog that was rescued.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 8 Explanation:
Can you imagine the train leaving with a large, blue suitcase? That sight would be interesting, right? The prepositional phrase “with a large, blue suitcase” is misplaced, since it should be modifying “man” not “train.” A corrected sentence is:
Some people standing on the platform said that they saw a man with a large, blue suitcase boarding the train that left for Prague at 9:02.
Now, some of you will be tempted to claim that the sentence now says that a “blue suitcase is boarding the train.” However, as we will discuss later, a noun and its modifiers can make a new, unique noun. So, “a man with a large, blue suitcase” is the noun, and that man was “boarding the train.”
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 9 Explanation:
Can you imagine an assistant pastry chef covered with pink icing? The prepositional phrase “with pink icing” is misplaced. The phrase should be modifying the cake, not the assistant pastry chef. An effective sentence is:
The assistant pastry chef frosted the cake with pink icing.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 10 Explanation:
Can you picture the dinosaurs living in the Jurassic period in the new book? That meaning is not the meaning that the author intended, right? The prepositional phrase “in his new book” is improperly modifying “lived during the Jurassic period.” An effective sentence could read:
In his new book, the paleontologist discusses newfound information on the many types of dinosaurs that lived during the Jurassic period.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s practice applying what we just learned in answering Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 4 It was demonstrated by a study of genes conducted at a university that deactivation of genes associated with development of illness results from adoption of a healthy lifestyle. 1. [x] (A) It was demonstrated by a study of genes conducted at a university that deactivation of genes associated with development of illness results from adoption of 2. [ ] (B) A study of genes conducted at a university demonstrating deactivation of genes associated with the development of illness resulting from adopting > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) When conducted as a study, genes at a university demonstrated resulting deactivation of genes associated with development of illness from adoption of > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) At a university, genes associated with development of illness in a conducted study demonstrated that deactivation of genes results from adopting > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) A study conducted of genes at a university demonstrated resulting deactivation of genes associated with development of illness from adoption of > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium We can eliminate most of the incorrect choices in this question through noticing issues with the placement of prepositional phrases.
YOUR CHOICE(A) It was demonstrated by a study of genes conducted at a university that deactivation of genes associated with development of illness results from adoption of a healthy lifestyle.
It’s a little tricky to tell that this choice is the correct answer, because this version uses an expletive “it” and delayed subject construction “it was demonstrated … that.”
All the same, by reading carefully, we can see that this version has no modifier issues: “a study of genes conducted at a university” makes sense, and “deactivation of genes … results from adoption of a healthy lifestyle” makes sense.
So, this version has no errors and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(B) A study of genes conducted at a university demonstrating deactivation of genes associated with the development of illness resulting from adopting a healthy lifestyle.
This version has no main verb and, thus, is not a complete sentence.
(C) When conducted as a study, genes at a university demonstrated resulting deactivation of genes associated with development of illness from adoption of a healthy lifestyle.
As a result of the placement of the prepositional phrase “at a university” directly after “genes,” this version suggests the illogical idea that genes were “at a university.”
Similarly, as a result of the placement of the prepositional phrase “from adoption of a healthy lifestyle” directly after “development of illness,” this version suggests the illogical idea that development of illness is “from adoption of a healthy lifestyle.”
Notice also that “when conducted as a study, genes … demonstrated” suggests the nonsensical idea that genes demonstrated something when genes were “conducted as a study.” A study of genes would be “conducted.” Genes themselves would not be “conducted as a study.”
(D) At a university, genes associated with development of illness in a conducted study demonstrated that deactivation of genes results from adopting a healthy lifestyle.
Because the prepositional phrase “at a university” is placed before the clause “genes … demonstrated …,” this version conveys the illogical idea that genes demonstrated something at a university.
Also, since the prepositional phrase “in a conducted study” is placed directly after “development of illness,” this version seems to suggest the illogical idea that illness would develop in a conducted study. The GMAT would prefer a version that more clearly conveys a logical meaning when read literally.
(E) A study conducted of genes at a university demonstrated resulting deactivation of genes associated with development of illness from adoption of a healthy lifestyle.
As a result of the placement of the prepositional phrase “of genes” after “a study conducted,” this version does not effectively convey a logical meaning. After all, there is no way for a study to be “conducted of genes.” A study of genes could be conducted, but this version does not effectively convey the meaning that a study of genes was conducted.
Also, as a result of the placement of the prepositional phrase “from adoption of a healthy lifestyle” directly after “development of illness,” this version suggests the illogical idea that development of illness is “from adoption of a healthy lifestyle.”
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 5 Sea and air travel are much safer now than they were in the past because computers used to make accurate predictions based in part on detailed information on how storms are formed by weather forecasters. 1. [ ] (A) because computers used to make accurate predictions based in part on detailed information on how storms are formed by weather forecasters > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) due to the use of computers that accurately make predictions based in part on detailed information on the formation of storms by weather forecasters > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) because weather forecasters’ use of computers makes predictions that are accurately based in part on detailed information on storm formation > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) due to computers used to accurately make predictions based in part on detailed information on the formation of storms by weather forecasters > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) because of weather forecasters’ using computers to make accurate predictions based in part on detailed information on how storms are formed View Solution Question Level: hard In order to correctly answer this question, we have to notice placement of modifiers and meanings conveyed.
For instance, the placement of the prepositional phrase “by weather forecasters” after “storms are formed” or after “the formation of storms” results in sentences that convey the nonsensical meaning that storms are formed by weather forecasters.
(A) Sea and air travel are much safer now than they were in the past because computers used to make accurate predictions based in part on detailed information on how storms are formed by weather forecasters .
Rather than say “because computers are used to,” this version says merely “computers used to.” Therefore, it conveys the illogical meaning that sea and air travel are safer now than they were in the past because computers used to (meaning did in the past but no longer do), make accurate predictions.
Also, since the prepositional phrase “by weather forecasters” directly follows “storms are formed,” this version seems to convey that storms are formed by weather forecasters.
(B) Sea and air travel are much safer now than they were in the past due to the use of computers that accurately make predictions based in part on detailed information on the formation of storms by weather forecasters .
The expression “due to” is incorrectly used. On the GMAT, and in most formal writing, “due to” is correctly used to begin a phrase that modifies a noun. In this case, “due to” incorrectly begins a phrase that modifies a verb, “are.”
Also, the adverb “accurately” is misused. The point to be made is not that computers “accurately make” predictions. The act of making is not one that can be accurate or inaccurate. It is what is made, in this case “predictions,” that would be accurate or inaccurate.
Also, since the prepositional phrase “by weather forecasters” directly follows “the formation of storms,” this version seems to convey that storms are formed by weather forecasters.
(C) Sea and air travel are much safer now than they were in the past because weather forecasters’ use of computers makes predictions that are accurately based in part on detailed information on storm formation .
This version suggests the illogical idea that the forecasters’ use of computers makes predictions. The forecasters themselves use computers to make predictions. Their “use of computers” does not make predictions.
Also, the adverb “accurately” is misused. The point to be made is that the predictions are accurate, not that the predictions are accurately based on information. One would not accurately base a prediction on information.
(D) Sea and air travel are much safer now than they were in the past due to computers used to accurately make predictions based in part on detailed information on the formation of storms by weather forecasters .
The expression “due to” is incorrectly used. On the GMAT, and in most formal writing, “due to” is correctly used to begin a phrase that modifies a noun. In this case, “due to” incorrectly begins a phrase that modifies a verb, “are.”
Also, the adverb “accurately” is misused. The point to be made is not that computers “accurately make” predictions. The act of making is not one that can be accurate or inaccurate. It is what is made, in this case “predictions” that would be accurate or inaccurate.
Also, since the prepositional phrase “by weather forecasters” directly follows “the formation of storms,” this version seems to convey that storms are formed by weather forecasters.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Sea and air travel are much safer now than they were in the past because of weather forecasters’ using computers to make accurate predictions based in part on detailed information on how storms are formed .
This version conveys the logical meaning that sea and air travel are safer because of weather forecasters’ using computer to make accurate predictions.
The use of the possessive “forecasters’” with the gerund “using” is correct, but may not sound correct to many test-takers. In fact, the structure, possessive + gerund, is included in this choice for the specific purpose of making this correct answer sound incorrect.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss prepositional phrases of time.
summary:
