Lesson Content A prepositional phrase is a modifier composed of a preposition, such as “in,” “under,” “by,” or “for,” followed by a noun, and possibly some modifiers that modify that noun.
in boxes
under rocks
by the lake
for illiterate individuals
Prepositional phrases can serve as adjectives or adverbs.
When prepositional phrases serve as adjectives, they modify nouns, as in the following examples:
On the shelf was chocolate in boxes.
title: 5.3.4 Misplaced Prepositional Phrases

contents:
Lesson Content Prepositional phrases of time include structures such as “by 1920,” “after the Cold War ended,” and “in 2018.” When we encounter such phrases, we must take extra care to ensure that their placement does not result in ambiguity, confusion, or an illogical meaning.
Incorrect: The board of directors said in 2020 the price of the company’s product would need to double.
This sentence is confusing at best.
The board of directors said the price of the company’s product would need to double in 2020. The above sentence makes clear that the doubling must occur in 2020.
In 2020, the board of directors said the price of the company’s product would need to double.
The above sentence makes clear that the board made that statement in 2020.
💡MUST KNOW:
When we encounter prepositional phrases of time, we must take extra care to ensure that their placement does not result in ambiguity, confusion, or an illogical meaning.
Let’s look at another example:
Incorrect: The CEO reported in 2050 her company would be selling self-flying cars.
What happened in 2050? Did the CEO report in 2050, or was her report that in 2050 her company would be selling self-flying cars? Consider the following more effective versions:
The CEO reported that her company would be selling self-flying cars in 2050.
In 2050, the CEO reported that her company would be selling self-flying cars.
Notice that both sentences make clear what happened when, although they convey different things.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each sentence is correct, clearly conveying a logical meaning, or incorrect because it conveys an illogical meaning or its meaning is not clear. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 The division manager said on Monday the team would meet to discuss the action plan. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 In January, Thomas said that the CEO would visit the production plant in the spring. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 According to the report, without a radical shift in the global economy that will eliminate greenhouse gas emissions as soon as 2040 deadly heat waves, droughts, famine, and coastal flooding will affect millions of people worldwide. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 4 The companies announced on Tuesday the $70 billion merger would be made official, though industry analysts say that the unprecedented consolidation barely manages to skirt antitrust laws. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
Did the manager make a statement on Monday, or did she make a statement about what would happen on Monday? Two correct versions are:
On Monday, the division manager said that the team would meet to discuss the action plan.
The division manager said that the team would meet to discuss the action plan on Monday.
The first communicates that the manager made a comment on Monday (that the team would meet in the future). The second makes clear that the team would be meeting on Monday
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
This sentence makes clear that the statement was made in January and that the event mentioned by Thomas would occur in the spring.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
Is the report telling us that, in order for catastrophic events to be avoided, a radical shift must occur “as soon as 2040,” or is it telling us that catastrophic events will occur “as soon as 2040” without a radical shift? The sentence’s meaning is unclear. Two more effective versions are:
According to the report, without a radical shift in the global economy that will eliminate greenhouse gas emissions, deadly heat waves, droughts, famine, and coastal flooding will affect millions of people worldwide as soon as 2040.
The above sentence makes clear that “deadly heat waves, droughts, famine, and coastal flooding will affect millions of people worldwide as soon as 2040.”
or
According to the report, without a radical shift in the global economy that will eliminate greenhouse gas emissions by 2040, deadly heat waves, droughts, famine, and coastal flooding will affect millions of people worldwide.
This sentence makes clear that we need a radical shift in the global economy that will eliminate greenhouse gas emissions by 2040. Also notice that the use of “by” makes more sense than the use of “as soon as” in this context. After all, “as soon as” does not convey that the shift must occur by a particular time.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
Did the announcement occur on Tuesday or will the merger be made official on Tuesday? We can’t be sure from the sentence as written. A possible correction is:
On Tuesday, the companies announced that the $70 billion merger would be made official, though industry analysts say that the unprecedented consolidation barely manages to skirt antitrust laws.
The above sentence makes clear that the announcement was on Tuesday.
or
The companies announced that the $70 billion merger would be made official on Tuesday, though industry analysts say that the unprecedented consolidation barely manages to skirt antitrust laws.
The above sentence communicates that the merger will be made official on Tuesday.
Correct Answer: (B) Consider the following Sentence Correction example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 6 Secretary of State George Marshall proposed his four-year, multi-billion-dollar plan to rebuild Europe following the destruction of World War II in 1947, and his namesake Marshall Plan, which was meant to both aid economic redevelopment and stave off communism, was enacted the following year, marking what many historians consider the beginning of the Cold War. 1. [ ] (A) Secretary of State George Marshall proposed his four-year, multi-billion-dollar plan to rebuild Europe following the destruction of World War II in 1947, and his namesake Marshall Plan, which was meant > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) In 1947, Secretary of State George Marshall proposed his four-year, multi-billion-dollar plan to rebuild Europe following the destruction coming from World War II, and his namesake Marshall Plan, which was meaning > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Secretary of State George Marshall proposed his four-year, multi-billion-dollar plan to rebuild Europe following its destruction of World War II, and, in 1947, his namesake Marshall Plan, which was meant > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Proposed by secretary of State George Marshall was his four-year, multi-billion-dollar plan to rebuild Europe in 1947, following the destruction by World War II, his namesake Marshall Plan meaning > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) In 1947, Secretary of State George Marshall proposed his four-year, multi-billion-dollar plan to rebuild Europe following its destruction during World War II, and his namesake Marshall Plan, which was meant View Solution Question Level: easy The placement of the prepositional phrase of time “in 1947” has to result in a sentence that conveys a clear and logical meaning. In some versions “in 1947” is placed in ways such that the versions convey illogical or ambiguous meanings.
(A) Secretary of State George Marshall proposed his four-year, multi-billion-dollar plan to rebuild Europe following the destruction of World War II in 1947, and his namesake Marshall Plan, which was meant to both aid economic redevelopment and stave off communism, was enacted the following year, marking what many historians consider the beginning of the Cold War.
First, we see that the prepositional phrase of time “in 1947” is placed a way such that the sentence’s meaning is confusing. Did Marshall propose his plan in 1947, or was the destruction in 1947? As the sentence is written, we can’t tell, right?
Also, “destruction of World War II” makes no sense, as it seems to convey that the war itself was destroyed. How can a World War be destroyed? A building or a road could be destroyed, but a World War could not.
(B) In 1947, Secretary of State George Marshall proposed his four-year, multi-billion-dollar plan to rebuild Europe following the destruction coming from World War II, and his namesake Marshall Plan, which was meaning to both aid economic redevelopment and stave off communism, was enacted the following year, marking what many historians consider the beginning of the Cold War.
We see a much better placement of “in 1947.” Placed at the beginning of the sentence, the modifier makes clear that Marshall proposed his plan in 1947.
However, “destruction coming from World War II” is illogical. As written, it’s almost as if World War II has sent something in the mail.
Also, saying that the plan was “meaning to” is not quite logical. A plan might be meant to do something, but “meaning to” conveys that the plan itself was actively planning to do something.
(C) Secretary of State George Marshall proposed his four-year, multi-billion-dollar plan to rebuild Europe following its destruction of World War II, and, in 1947, his namesake Marshall Plan, which was meant to both aid economic redevelopment and stave off communism, was enacted the following year, marking what many historians consider the beginning of the Cold War.
“Its destruction of World War II” makes no sense, as it seems to convey that the war itself was destroyed. How can a World War be destroyed? A building or a road could be destroyed, but a World War could not.
Also, if we look carefully at what follows the phrase “in 1947,” we see that “in 1947” is placed in a way such that the sentence conveys the illogical meaning that, in 1947, the plan was enacted the following year. The plan must have been enacted either in 1947 or the following year, not both.
(D) Proposed by secretary of State George Marshall was his four-year, multi-billion-dollar plan to rebuild Europe in 1947, following the destruction by World War II, his namesake Marshall Plan meaning to both aid economic redevelopment and stave off communism, was enacted the following year, marking what many historians consider the beginning of the Cold War.
Because of the placement of “in 1947,” this version conveys that the four-year plan was to rebuild Europe in 1947. Since 1947 is not four years, this meaning is not logical.
Also, “the destruction by World War II” does not quite make sense, as “the destruction” does not make clear that Europe was destroyed.
Also, saying that the plan was “meaning to” is not quite logical. A plan might be meant to do something, but “meaning to” conveys that the plan itself was actively planning to do something.
YOUR CHOICE(E) In 1947, Secretary of State George Marshall proposed his four-year, multi-billion-dollar plan to rebuild Europe following its destruction during World War II, and his namesake Marshall Plan, which was meant to both aid economic redevelopment and stave off communism, was enacted the following year, marking what many historians consider the beginning of the Cold War.
In this version, the phrase “in 1947” is placed in a way such that the phrase clearly indicates that Marshall proposed the plan in 1947.
Also, “plan to rebuild Europe following its destruction during World War II” conveys a clear and logical meaning.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s continue by discussing how to identify misplaced introductory modifiers.
summary:
Lesson Content Prepositional phrases of time include structures such as “by 1920,” “after the Cold War ended,” and “in 2018.” When we encounter such phrases, we must take extra care to ensure that their placement does not result in ambiguity, confusion, or an illogical meaning.
Incorrect: The board of directors said in 2020 the price of the company’s product would need to double.
This sentence is confusing at best.
The board of directors said the price of the company’s product would need to double in 2020.
title: 5.3.5 Pay Special Attention to Prepositional Phrases of Time

contents:
Lesson Content We often encounter sentences that open with introductory (opening) modifiers.
Consider the following example:
Built to last, the lighthouse has weathered many storms.
We can see that the sentence opens with an introductory noun modifier, “built to last.” The noun that the introductory modifier is meant to modify, “the lighthouse,” appears right after the introductory modifier.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an introductory modifier is a noun modifier, the noun that it modifies must appear right after the introductory modifier.
If the noun that the opening modifier is meant to modify doesn’t appear right after the introductory modifier, then the introductory modifier is misplaced.
💡MUST KNOW:
If the noun that the opening modifier is meant to modify doesn’t appear right after the introductory modifier, then the introductory modifier is misplaced.
Consider the following two sentences:
Incorrect: Tired and hungry, the aroma from the campsite grill cheered the hikers as they climbed the last hill.
Correct: Tired and hungry, the hikers were cheered by the aroma from the campsite grill as they climbed the last hill.
The opening modifier, “tired and hungry,” is a noun modifier.
However, in the first sentence, the noun “the aroma” appears right after “tired and hungry,” and thus this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the aroma was “tired and hungry.” In other words, “tired and hungry” is a misplaced introductory modifier.
In the second sentence, on the other hand, the noun that the modifier is supposed to describe, “the hikers,” does appear right after the modifier.
Of course, there are other ways to correctly phrase the sentence, and any of these variations could show up on the GMAT as correct answers.
As they climbed the last hill, the tired and hungry hikers were cheered by the aroma from the campsite grill.
The hikers, tired and hungry, were cheered by the aroma from the campsite grill as they climbed the last hill.
Both of these sentences are correct because the noun modifier “tired and hungry” is placed close to and clearly modifies a noun that it would logically modify, “hikers.”
Let’s consider another.
Incorrect: An award-winning photograph, the flat look of Tugo Cheng’s colorful image of the Tianshan Mountains was achieved through taking the photograph when the sunlight was not strong.
This sentence contains a misplaced introductory modifier.
By moving the nouns around, we get the following version, which makes sense:
An award-winning photograph, Tugo Cheng’s colorful image of the Tianshan Mountains has a flat look achieved through taking the photograph when the sunlight was not strong.
Notice that, now, the opening modifier “an award-winning photograph,” logically modifies the noun “Tugo Cheng’s colorful image.”
A structure that often confuses test-takers is the use of an expletive “it” just after an introductory modifying phrase.
Consider the following sentence:
Incorrect: Running fast along the shore, it was clear that the Shiba Inu was having a great time.
At this point, you know that that sentence is incorrectly written.
We could rewrite the sentence as:
Running fast along the shore, the Shiba Inu was clearly having a great time.
We see that the phrase “running fast along the shore” now properly modifies the noun “Shiba Inu.”
Here’s another example in which an introductory modifier illogically modifies an expletive “it:”
Incorrect: Having beaten the returns of the S&P 500 over the past ten years, it is likely that the Gemini Fund will soon close to new investors.
Look at the opening modifying phrase “Having beaten the returns of the S&P 500 over the past ten years.” What is that phrase intended to modify? Certainly not “it,” which does not have as its referent “the Gemini Fund.” A correct version is:
Having beaten the returns of the S&P 500 over the past ten years, the Gemini Fund will likely soon close to new investors.
Notice that, now, the opening phrase properly modifies “the Gemini Fund.”
💡MUST KNOW:
When an introductory noun modifier is followed by a clause that has “it” as its subject, it may be that the modifier does not logically modify “it.”
Be vigilant for misplaced introductory modifiers!
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now answer a Sentence Correction question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 7 Now best known as the date of Julius Caesar’s assassination, the Ides of March was, in ancient Rome, traditionally a day of feasting, revelry, and rituals honoring various deities. 1. [x] (A) the Ides of March was, in ancient Rome, traditionally a day of feasting, revelry, and rituals honoring various deities 2. [ ] (B) ancient Rome traditionally feasted, reveled, and honored various deities on the Ides of March > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) traditionally, ancient Rome had a day of feasting, revelry, and rituals in honor of their various deities on the Ides of March > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) there was traditionally feasting, revelry, and rituals that honored various deities on the Ides of March in ancient Rome > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) the tradition on the Ides of March was a day of feasting, revelry, and rituals in which they honored various deities in ancient Rome > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy In evaluating the answer choices in a Sentence Correction question, we must be sure to consider the full sentence created, not just the underlined portion. Notice that in this case, the nonunderlined portion of the sentence is the introductory modifier “Now best known as the date of Julius Caesar’s assassination,” which modifies whatever noun immediately follows it. So, the noun that immediately follows that phrase (and thus begins the underlined portion) must be something that could logically be “best known as the date of Julius Caesar’s assassination.”
YOUR CHOICE(A) Now best known as the date of Julius Caesar’s assassination, the Ides of March was, in ancient Rome, traditionally a day of feasting, revelry, and rituals honoring various deities .
In the sentence created with this choice, the introductory phrase “Now best known as the date of Julius Caesar’s assassination” logically modifies the noun phrase “the Ides of March.”
Thus, this version of the sentence effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(B) Now best known as the date of Julius Caesar’s assassination, ancient Rome traditionally feasted, reveled, and honored various deities on the Ides of March .
It does not make sense to say that “ancient Rome” is “now best known as the date of Julius Caesar’s assassination,” as the sentence created with this choice does.
Remember, the introductory phrase in the sentence modifies the noun that immediately follows it. In this case that noun is “ancient Rome,” which is a place, not a “date.” (C) Now best known as the date of Julius Caesar’s assassination, traditionally, ancient Rome had a day of feasting, revelry, and rituals in honor of their various deities on the Ides of March .
It does not make sense to say that “ancient Rome” is “now best known as the date of Julius Caesar’s assassination,” as the sentence created with this choice does.
Remember, the introductory phrase in the sentence modifies the noun that immediately follows it. In this case that noun is “ancient Rome,” which is a place, not a “date.” The placement of the adverb “traditionally” between the introductory phrase and the noun does not change the fact that the phrase still (illogically) modifies that noun.
Additionally, the pronoun “their” is missing an antecedent in the sentence. Notice that the logical antecedent, “Romans,” is not mentioned anywhere in the sentence. Only “ancient Rome,” a place (and a singular noun, which cannot be paired with the plural “their”), is mentioned.
(D) Now best known as the date of Julius Caesar’s assassination, there was traditionally feasting, revelry, and rituals that honored various deities on the Ides of March in ancient Rome .
What is “now best known as the date of Julius Caesar’s assassination”? The statement “there was traditionally feasting” is not a “date” and thus is not logically modified by the introductory phrase.
Logically, the “date” mentioned in the introductory phrase is “the Ides of March.” However, “the Ides of March” appears too far from the introductory phrase to be properly modified by it. “The Ides of March” should appear immediately after the introductory modifier in order for a logical meaning to be conveyed. (E) Now best known as the date of Julius Caesar’s assassination, the tradition on the Ides of March was a day of feasting, revelry, and rituals in which they honored various deities in ancient Rome .
It does not make sense to say that “the tradition” is “now best known as the date of Julius Caesar’s assassination,” as the sentence created with this choice does. Remember, the introductory phrase in the sentence modifies the noun that immediately follows it. In this case that noun is “the tradition,” which is not a “date.” The logical “date” is “the Ides of March,” but that noun phrase is part of a prepositional phrase modifying “the tradition” and thus is not properly modified by the introductory phrase. “The Ides of March” should appear immediately after the introductory modifier in order for a logical meaning to be conveyed. Additionally, the pronoun “they” is missing an antecedent. Notice that the logical antecedent, “Romans,” is not mentioned anywhere in the sentence. Only “ancient Rome,” a place (and a singular noun, which cannot be paired with the plural “they”), is mentioned.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 8 Many in number, a key source of energy for generation of electrical power in Central America is the area’s volcanoes, which once merely posed as threats but now benefit the people who live around them. 1. [ ] (A) Many in number, a key source of energy for generation of electrical power in Central America is the area’s volcanoes, which once merely posed as threats but now benefit > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Key sources of energy for the generation of electrical power in Central America, the area’s many volcanoes no longer merely pose threats to but now benefit 3. [ ] (C) Being key sources of energy for electrical power generation in Central America, the mere threats posed by the area’s many volcanoes are now benefits to > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) As key sources of energy for electrical power generation in Central America, the area’s many volcanoes were merely posing as threats, but now they benefit > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Key sources of energy for generation of electrical power in the area, Central America has many volcanoes, which once merely posed threateningly but now benefit > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard We can eliminate most of the answer choices in this question by noticing that they include opening modifiers that don’t logically modify the nouns that follow the modifiers.
(A) Many in number, a key source of energy for generation of electrical power in Central America is the area’s volcanoes, which once merely posed as threats but now benefit the people who live around them.
This sentence contains a misplaced introductory modifier. The opening modifier, “many in number,” does not logically modify “a key source,” which is the noun that follows the modifier.
Also, “to pose as” means “to pretend to be.” So, in saying, “volcanoes, which once merely posed as threats,” the sentence conveys the nonsensical meaning that the volcanoes pretended to be threats.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) Key sources of energy for the generation of electrical power in Central America, the area’s many volcanoes no longer merely pose threats to but now benefit the people who live around them.
The opening modifier, “key sources of energy for the generation of electrical power in Central America,” logically modifies “the area’s many volcanoes,” which is the noun that follows the modifier.
The wording of the end of the choice, “no longer merely pose threats to but now benefit,” is written to sound awkward and seem wrong. However, actually, it works well with the non-underlined portion, as we can see by combining the two to create “no longer merely pose threats to but now benefit the people,” which is a shorter way of saying, “no longer merely pose threats to the people but now benefit the people.”
(C) Being key sources of energy for electrical power generation in Central America, the mere threats posed by the area’s many volcanoes are now benefits to the people who live around them.
This sentence contains a misplaced introductory modifier. The opening modifier, “being key sources of energy …,” does not make sense as a modifier of the noun phrase that follows the modifier, “mere threats.”
Partly as a result of this modifier misplacement, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that “mere threats” are “being sources of energy” and “are now benefits.”
YOUR CHOICE(D) As key sources of energy for electrical power generation in Central America, the area’s many volcanoes were merely posing as threats, but now they benefit the people who live around them.
The opening modifier, “as key sources of energy for generation of electrical power in Central America,” works logically with the noun that follows the modifier, “the area’s many volcanoes.” However, the meaning conveyed by this version is nonsensical.
“To pose as” means “to pretend to be.” So, in saying, “As key sources of energy …, the area’s many volcanoes were merely posing as threats,” the sentence conveys the nonsensical meaning that, as key sources of energy, the volcanoes were pretending to be threats.
(E) Key sources of energy for generation of electrical power in the area, Central America has many volcanoes, which once merely posed threateningly but now benefit the people who live around them.
This sentence contains a misplaced introductory modifier. The opening modifier, “key sources of energy” is illogically modifying the noun “Central America.”
Also, “to pose threateningly” means “to choose to stand in a way that is threatening.” So, in saying, “volcanoes, which once merely posed threateningly,” the sentence conveys the nonsensical meaning that volcanoes chose to stand in ways that were threatening.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss dangling modifiers.
summary:
Lesson Content We often encounter sentences that open with introductory (opening) modifiers.
Consider the following example:
Built to last, the lighthouse has weathered many storms.
We can see that the sentence opens with an introductory noun modifier, “built to last.” The noun that the introductory modifier is meant to modify, “the lighthouse,” appears right after the introductory modifier.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an introductory modifier is a noun modifier, the noun that it modifies must appear right after the introductory modifier.
title: 5.3.6 Misplaced Introductory Modifiers

contents:
Lesson Content Dangling modifiers are similar to misplaced modifiers, except in that, in a case of a dangling modifier, the noun that the modifier is supposed to describe is completely absent from the sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
In a case of a dangling modifier, the noun that the modifier is supposed to describe is completely absent from the sentence. Such a sentence is always incorrect.
Dangling modifiers are common in Sentence Correction questions.
Take, for example, the following sentence:
Incorrect: Having completed their final exams, parties all over campus began.
Since the noun “parties” follows the comma that separates the introductory phrase (which also happens to be a noun modifier), from the main clause, the sentence conveys that it was “parties” that had completed their final exams.
The only other noun in the clause that the modifier could modify is “campus,” another thing that would not complete an exam.
Although the sentence leads us to believe that students completed the final exams, the noun “students” is completely missing from the sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
Dangling modifiers result in sentences with ridiculous meanings. Such sentences can never be correct.
The easiest way to correct the dangling modifier is to simply place the noun that should be modified after the comma separating the introductory phrase from the main clause:
Having completed their final exams, the students partied all over campus.
Note that the remainder of the sentence likely will need to be changed for the sentence to make sense.
💡MUST KNOW:
A noun being modified in a sentence must not be absent from the sentence.
There are other ways in which a sentence containing a dangling modifier can be revised.
Parties began all over campus as the students completed their final exams.
All over campus, students joined parties after completing their final exams.
After the students completed their final exams, parties all over campus began.
Each of these sentences expresses essentially the same meaning in a slightly different way.
Let’s look at another example.
Incorrect: Not realizing that they would have to hike for miles, their boots had been left at home.
In this clearly incorrect sentence, the dangling modifier indicates that the boots were unaware that they would have hike for miles.
To fix the sentence, we can use our strategy, placing the noun that should be modified after the comma separating the introductory phrase from the main clause.
Not realizing that they would have to hike for miles, the anthropologists had left their boots at home.
Consider a final example sentence:
Incorrect: Using the newest underwater sonography techniques, the missing pirate ship was finally found.
Notice that, although this sentence seems to be correct as written, there is a subtle yet important flaw: There is no noun present that could be reasonably portrayed as “using the newest underwater sonography techniques.” So, the logic of this sentence is off.
Using the newest underwater sonography techniques, the archeologist finally found the missing pirate ship.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 Searching hastily through the notes, the correct answer was found. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 Having been to Six Flags, the family members agreed that they enjoy Magic Mountain more. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 Chomping on the tasty food as if it had not eaten in months, the dog salivated as it savored each morsel. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 With a reluctant smile, the money was returned to the proper owner. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 5 With a cranky look, the secretary handed the file to the client. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 6 Using the new diagnostic technique, the engineer was able to find the microscopic crack in the plane’s wing quickly. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 7 Always seeking ways to improve, Jonathan’s work is commendable. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 8 Having eaten at the Inn of the Seventh Ray, an amazing eatery just northwest of Los Angeles serving a wide assortment of culinary delights, the food at Tanavoli’s Kitchen, another amazing eatery close to Los Angeles, is better. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 9 Having maintained a strong connection to her Polish roots despite becoming a naturalized French citizen, the two-time Nobel Prize-winning chemist and physicist Marie Curie, née Skłodowska, named the first element that she discovered, polonium, after her country of origin, to which she returned as a visitor throughout her life. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 10 Determined to win the respect of his colleagues, who viewed his prominent position within the company as undeserved, his ideas as risky, and his management style as inexperienced, the company’s earnings more than doubled in the year after the brash, young upstart's promotion, and many of the his peers who had doubted him most began to call him a visionary. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
The sentence contains a dangling modifier. Who was searching hastily through the notes? The noun is missing from the sentence. We could correct this sentence by rewriting it as:
Searching hastily through the notes, Lorraine found the correct answer.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
“Having been to Six Flags” correctly modifies the subject of the sentence, “the family members.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
“The dog” is “chomping.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
This sentence contains a dangling modifier. Who gave the reluctant smile? Notice that there is not any appropriate noun in the sentence. We could correctly rewrite this sentence as:
With a reluctant smile, the woman who had been driving the bus returned the money to the proper owner.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
The secretary gave the cranky look.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
The engineer is using the new diagnostic technique.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 7 Explanation:
This sentence contains a dangling modifier. Jonathan’s work cannot be “always seeking ways to improve.” The noun “Jonathan” is missing from the sentence. As you know from a previous section, “Jonathan’s work” cannot stand in for “Jonathan” himself. The sentence could be correctly rewritten as:
Always seeking ways to improve, Jonathan does commendable work.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 8 Explanation:
This sentence contains a dangling modifier. Who ate at the Inn of the Seventh Ray? There is no noun present that could be reasonably portrayed as performing this action. The sentence could be correctly rewritten as: Having eaten at the Inn of the Seventh Ray, an amazing eatery just northwest of Los Angeles serving a wide assortment of culinary delights, I think that the food at Tanavoli’s Kitchen, another amazing eatery close to Los Angeles, is better.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 9 Explanation:
“Having maintained a strong connection” properly modifies the noun phrase/subject “the two-time Nobel Prize-winning chemist and physicist Marie Curie.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 10 Explanation:
Who is “determined to win the respect of his colleagues”? This phrase should modify “the young upstart,” but the brash young upstart is far from the modifier, and only in the possessive form, “upstart’s,” rather than in the form of a standalone noun that could be modified by “determined to win ….” A correct version is:
Determined to win the respect of his colleagues, who viewed his prominent position within the company as undeserved, his ideas as risky, and his management style as inexperienced, the brash, young upstart more than doubled the company’s earnings in the year after his promotion, and many of his peers who had doubted him most began to call him a visionary.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Consider the following Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 9 Constructing physical locations, an increasing amount of online shopping is moving offline. 1. [ ] (A) an increasing amount of online shopping is moving offline > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) online shopping is increasingly moving offline > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) the number of online shops moving offline is increasing > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) an increasing amount of offline shopping that was online has moved > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) shops that were online have increasingly moved offline View Solution Question Level: easy (A) Constructing physical locations, an increasing amount of online shopping is moving offline .
“Online shopping” is an inanimate thing that cannot possibly be “constructing physical locations.” So, it does not make sense to say “Constructing physical locations, an increasing amount of online shopping ….”
In fact, there is nothing mentioned in the sentence that could possibly be “constructing physical locations.” Thus, the opening modifier is a dangling modifier; there is no noun in the sentence that the modifier could logically modify. (B) Constructing physical locations, online shopping is increasingly moving offline .
“Online shopping” is an inanimate thing that cannot possibly be “constructing physical locations.” So, it does not make sense to say “Constructing physical locations, online shopping ….”
In fact, there is nothing mentioned in the sentence that could possibly be “constructing physical locations.” Thus, the opening modifier is a dangling modifier; there is no noun in the sentence that the modifier could logically modify.
(C) Constructing physical locations, the number of online shops moving offline is increasing .
A “number” cannot possibly be “constructing physical locations.” So, it does not make sense to say “Constructing physical locations, the number of online shops ….” The opening modifier is misplaced.
It would make sense to say that the shops themselves, not “the number,” are “constructing physical locations.”
(D) Constructing physical locations, an increasing amount of offline shopping that was online has moved .
“Offline shopping” is an inanimate thing that cannot possibly be “constructing physical locations.” So, it does not make sense to say “Constructing physical locations, an increasing amount of offline shopping ….”
In fact, there is nothing mentioned in the sentence that could carry out that action. Thus, the opening modifier is a dangling modifier.
Notice also that the meaning conveyed by this choice is muddled and not quite logical. It does not make sense to say “offline shopping that was only online,” because shopping that was only online would not simultaneously be “offline shopping.” Logically, there can be “offline shopping” and there can be “shopping that was only online,” but if the shopping is one of those, it is by definition not the other.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Constructing physical locations, shops that were online have increasingly moved offline .
In this choice, the opening modifier “constructing physical locations” logically modifies the noun “shops.”
Furthermore, this choice produces a clear and logical timeline of events, making clear that the shops “were online only” previously, but they “have increasingly moved offline” since that time.
So, this version effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
Key Concept: Check to make sure that the noun a modifier is supposed to describe is present in the sentence.
Key Concept: If the noun that an introductory noun modifier is meant to modify doesn’t appear right after the modifier, then the modifier is misplaced.
Key Concept: Don’t select answer choices that produce sentence versions that convey illogical meanings.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 10 Having been alarmed by widespread declines in equities markets, gold’s price, which is often used as a safe-haven investment during times of economic trouble, has risen dramatically in recent months. 1. [ ] (A) Having been alarmed by widespread declines in equities markets, gold’s price, which is often used > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Because of alarm resulting from widespread declines in equities markets, the price of gold, which is often used 3. [ ] (C) Since being alarmed by widespread declines in equities markets, the price of gold, often used > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) As a result of alarm by widespread declines in equities markets, gold’s price, used often > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Alarmed by widespread declines in equities markets, the price of gold, often used > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard Correctly answering this question requires noticing that the subject of each version is either “gold’s price” or “the price of gold” and that, therefore, any noun modifier that opens a version has to logically modify one of those subjects.
Also, the version created via the use of choice (E), though not logical, “sounds correct.” So, having read through choices (A), (B), (C), and (D), some of which “sound funny,” we could easily be tempted to incorrectly choose (E).
(A) Having been alarmed by widespread declines in equities markets, gold’s price, which is often used as a safe-haven investment during times of economic trouble, has risen dramatically in recent months.
The opening modifier “having been alarmed by” can logically modify only something that can have been alarmed. However, the noun “gold’s price,” which must be the target of the opening modifier, would not be alarmed, and there is in the sentence no suitable target for the modifier. Thus, the modifier is dangling.
Also, the use of the modifier “which is often used as a safe-haven investment …” to modify “gold’s price” results in the sentence’s conveying the illogical meaning that the price of gold, rather than gold itself, is used as a safe-haven investment.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Because of alarm resulting from widespread declines in equities markets, the price of gold, which is often used as a safe-haven investment during times of economic trouble, has risen dramatically in recent months.
Rather than open with a noun modifier before its main clause, this sentence opens with a prepositional phrase, “because of alarm …,” that explains why the price of gold has risen.
So, this version effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(C) Since being alarmed by widespread declines in equities markets, the price of gold, often used as a safe-haven investment during times of economic trouble, has risen dramatically in recent months.
The opening modifier “Since being alarmed by …” can logically modify only something that can be alarmed. However, the noun “the price of gold,” which must be the target of the opening modifier, would not be alarmed, and there is in the sentence no suitable target for the modifier. Thus, the opening modifier is dangling.
(D) As a result of alarm by widespread declines in equities markets, gold’s price, used often as a safe-haven investment during times of economic trouble, has risen dramatically in recent months.
While the expression “alarmed by” is idiomatically correct, the wording “As a result of alarm by widespread declines” is illogical, as “by” must work with a verb in this type of context. For example, “as a result of alarm caused by widespread declines” would be correct.
Also, the use of the modifier “often used as a safe-haven investment …” to modify “gold’s price” results in the sentence’s conveying the illogical meaning that the price of gold, rather than gold itself, is used as a safe-haven investment.
(E) Alarmed by widespread declines in equities markets, the price of gold, often used as a safe-haven investment during times of economic trouble, has risen dramatically in recent months.
This choice could be tempting, as the version created via the use of this choice is concise and “sounds right.” However, the opening modifier “Alarmed by …” can logically modify only something that can be alarmed, and the noun “the price of gold,” which must be the target of the opening modifier, would not be alarmed, and there is in the sentence no suitable target for the modifier. Thus, the opening modifier is dangling.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 11 Inspection of goods during production is often recommended, because, when inspected only after production, an entire order of finished goods can be found to be flawed. 1. [x] (A) when inspected 2. [ ] (B) when inspecting > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) when they inspect > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) inspecting them > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) by being inspected > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard To answer this question correctly, we have to notice that the subject of the final clause of the sentence is “an entire order of finished goods.” So, a noun modifier that precedes the final clause has to work effectively with that subject. Noun modifiers that cannot modify that subject may be left dangling.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Inspection of goods during production is often recommended, because, when inspected only after production, an entire order of finished goods can be found to be flawed.
The modifier created via the use of this choice, “when inspected only after production,” logically modifies the noun that follows the modifier, “an entire order of finished goods,” and the resulting sentence conveys a logical meaning.
(B) Inspection of goods during production is often recommended, because, when inspecting only after production, an entire order of finished goods can be found to be flawed.
The modifier created via the use of this choice is meant to modify whoever is inspecting goods, as it says “when inspecting only after production,” as if to indicate that the clause to follow will be about someone who is inspecting after production. However, no people are mentioned. So, the modifier created via the use of this choice is a dangling modifier.
(C) Inspection of goods during production is often recommended, because, when they inspect only after production, an entire order of finished goods can be found to be flawed.
The pronoun “they” has no logical referent, as the only plural noun in the sentence is “goods,” and goods would not inspect. So, the sentence mentions nobody whom the dependent clause “when they inspect only after production” would be about.
(D) Inspection of goods during production is often recommended, because, inspecting them only after production, an entire order of finished goods can be found to be flawed.
We can tell that the modifier created via the use of this choice is meant to modify someone who is inspecting after production; the modifier indicates that the clause that follows will be about someone “inspecting them after production.” However, no people are mentioned in the clause. So, the modifier is dangling.
(E) Inspection of goods during production is often recommended, because, by being inspected only after production, an entire order of finished goods can be found to be flawed.
The version created via the use of this choice conveys the nonsensical meaning that, “by being inspected after production,” the goods themselves somehow cause an entire order to be found to be flawed.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss a common trap that involves possessives and modification.
summary:
Lesson Content Dangling modifiers are similar to misplaced modifiers, except in that, in a case of a dangling modifier, the noun that the modifier is supposed to describe is completely absent from the sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
In a case of a dangling modifier, the noun that the modifier is supposed to describe is completely absent from the sentence. Such a sentence is always incorrect.
Dangling modifiers are common in Sentence Correction questions.
title: 5.4 Dangling Modifiers

contents:
Lesson Content Nouns in possessive form often don’t seem much different from ordinary nouns, but the two types of nouns do not work in the same way in sentences.
Consider the following sentence:
Incorrect: Awarded the Nobel Prize for his work in chemistry, Linus Pauling’s research was wide-ranging and hard-hitting, and had a positive impact on many future generations of chemists.
The possessive “Linus Pauling’s” is not the same as the standalone noun “Linus Pauling.” Rather, “Linus Pauling’s” is a possessive noun, which, in this sentence, modifies “research.” “Linus Pauling’s research” cannot be “awarded the Nobel Prize for his work in chemistry.” It’s Linus Pauling himself who was awarded the Nobel Prize for his work.
Let’s look at another example sentence:
Incorrect: Eating the delicious guacamole after having been on a restricted diet for two months, Robert’s face showed nothing but enjoyment.
We can see that the modifying phrase “Eating the delicious guacamole after having been on a restricted diet for two months” cannot logically modify “Robert’s face.” After all, “Robert’s face” is not capable of eating guacamole and was not on a restricted diet for two months.
Be careful when you encounter possessives after modifying phrases.
💡MUST KNOW:
Be on the lookout for a modifying phrase followed by a possessive noun. In such a case, the modification and the resulting sentence may be illogical.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Consider the following Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 12 Having only climbed halfway up the mountain, the heavy snow stalled the hikers’ progress, obscured the trail, and covered landmarks normally used for navigating. 1. [ ] (A) Having only climbed halfway up the mountain, the heavy snow stalled the hikers’ progress, obscured the trail, and covered > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Only having climbed halfway up the mountain, the hikers’ progress was stalled by the heavy snow that obscured the trail and covered > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Having climbed only halfway up the mountain, the progress of the hikers was stalled by the heavy snow, which obscured the trail to cover > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Only having climbed halfway up the mountain, the hikers found their progress stalled by the heavy snow that obscured the trail from covering > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Having climbed only halfway up the mountain, the hikers found their progress stalled by the heavy snow, which obscured the trail and covered View Solution Question Level: easy (A) Having only climbed halfway up the mountain, the heavy snow stalled the hikers’ progress, obscured the trail, and covered landmarks normally used for navigating.
The adverb “only” in “having only climbed” is misplaced. Because “only” is placed just before “climbed,” the sentence conveys the somewhat illogical meaning that the hikers had “only climbed” and had not done anything else. “Only” should instead be placed just before “halfway,” so that the sentence conveys the logical meaning that the hikers had climbed “only halfway.”
Also, since the opening modifier, “having only climbed …” modifies the noun that follows it, “the heavy snow,” this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the heavy snow, rather than the hikers, was climbing up the mountain.
(B) Only having climbed halfway up the mountain, the hikers’ progress was stalled by the heavy snow that obscured the trail and covered landmarks normally used for navigating.
The adverb “only” is misplaced. “Only having climbed” conveys the somewhat illogical meaning that the hikers had only climbed and had not done anything else. What would be more logical is that the hikers found their progress stalled, “having climbed only halfway.”
Furthermore, the opening modifier “only having climbed halfway up the mountain” illogically modifies “the hikers’ progress.” The progress wasn’t climbing. The hikers were, but the noun “hikers” does not appear in the sentence. Only the possessive form “hikers’” is used, and hikers’ would not climb the mountain. “Hikers’” serves only to modify “progress.”
(C) Having climbed only halfway up the mountain, the progress of the hikers was stalled by the heavy snow, which obscured the trail to cover landmarks normally used for navigating.
In this version, the opening modifier “having climbed only halfway up the mountain” is placed so as to illogically modify “the progress of the hikers.” The progress of the hikers wasn’t climbing; the hikers were. So, the meaning conveyed by this version does not make sense.
Notice also that, “to” in “which obscured the trail to cover landmarks” seems to indicate purpose. As a result, “which obscured the trail to cover landmarks” conveys the illogical meaning that the snow obscured the trail for the purpose of covering landmarks, as if the snow chose to obscure the trail for that purpose.
(D) Only having climbed halfway up the mountain, the hikers found their progress stalled by the heavy snow that obscured the trail from covering landmarks normally used for navigating.
The adverb “only” is misplaced. “Only having climbed” conveys the somewhat illogical meaning that the hikers had only climbed and had not done anything else. What would be more logical is that the hikers found their progress stalled, “having climbed only halfway.”
Also “heavy snow that obscured the trail from covering” does not make sense. The snow would not have obscured the trail from covering landmarks. The preposition “from” is not used in expressing how something was achieved. It would have obscured the trail by covering landmarks. “By” is used in expressing how something was achieved.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Having climbed only halfway up the mountain, the hikers found their progress stalled by the heavy snow, which obscured the trail and covered landmarks normally used for navigating.
The opening modifier, “having climbed only halfway up the mountain,” logically modifies the subject, “the hikers,” and the rest of the modifiers in this version are placed in ways such that the sentence effectively conveys a clear, logical meaning.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 13 Happy consuming many foods, including fruits, vegetables, and twigs, the Black Rat’s desire to be close to human populations and its ability to harbor a wide range of infectious bacteria in its blood that includes Yersinia pestis, which was the pathogen responsible for causing the Black Plague, make the Black Rat a highly effective disease vector. 1. [ ] (A) Happy consuming many foods, including fruits, vegetables, and twigs, the Black Rat’s desire to be close to human populations and its ability to harbor a wide range of infectious bacteria in its blood that includes Yersinia pestis, which was the pathogen responsible for causing the Black Plague, make > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Happy consuming many foods, such as fruits, vegetables, and twigs, the Black Rat’s desire to be close to human populations and its ability to harbor in its blood a wide-range of infectious bacteria, such as Yersinia pestis, the pathogen responsible for causing the Black Plague, makes > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Happy consuming many foods, such as fruits, vegetables, and twigs, the Black Rat has a desire to be close to human populations, which, along with its ability to harbor in its blood a wide-range of infectious bacteria, such as Yersinia pestis, the Black Plague pathogen, makes 4. [ ] (D) The Black Rat’s desire to be close to human populations and happy consuming many foods, including fruits, vegetables, and twigs, and its ability to harbor in its blood a wide-range of infectious bacteria, such as Yersinia pestis, the pathogen responsible for causing the Black Plague, makes > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) The Black Rat is happy consuming many foods, including fruits, vegetables, and twigs, and therefore desires to be in close proximity to humans and its ability to harbor in its blood a wide-range of infectious bacteria, such as Yersinia pestis, the pathogen responsible for causing the Black Plague, to make > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy Correctly answering this question takes noticing that the opening modifier “happy consuming many foods” would logically modify “Black Rat” but would not logically modify “Black Rat’s desire,” which is the subject of some of the choices. A test-taker who does not notice that “happy consuming many foods” does not logically modify the subjects in some choices could be tempted into choosing a choice that sounds well-constructed but does not convey a logical meaning.
(A) Happy consuming many foods, including fruits, vegetables, and twigs, the Black Rat’s desire to be close to human populations and its ability to harbor a wide range of infectious bacteria in its blood that includes Yersinia pestis, which was the pathogen responsible for causing the Black Plague, make the Black Rat a highly effective disease vector.
The opening modifier, “happy consuming many foods,” does not logically modify the subject of the sentence, “the Black Rat’s desire … and its ability ….” While the Black Rat would be “happy consuming many foods,” “the Black Rat’s desire … and its ability” would not be happy or consume foods.
Also, the use of the modifier “that includes Yersinia pestis” after “blood” results in the sentence’s conveying the illogical meaning that some of the Black Rat’s blood includes Yersinia pestis. Blood would not “include” bacteria.
(B) Happy consuming many foods, such as fruits, vegetables, and twigs, the Black Rat’s desire to be close to human populations and its ability to harbor in its blood a wide-range of infectious bacteria, such as Yersinia pestis, the pathogen responsible for causing the Black Plague, makes the Black Rat a highly effective disease vector.
The opening modifier, “happy consuming many foods,” does not logically modify the subject of the sentence, “the Black Rat’s desire … and its ability ….” While the black rat would be “happy consuming many foods,” “the Black Rat’s desire … and its ability” would not be happy or consume foods.
Also, the singular main verb, “makes,” does not agree in number with the compound subject, “the Black Rat’s desire … and its ability …,” as we can more easily see by crossing off the modifiers that appear between the subject and the main verb:
the Black Rat’s desire to be close to human populations and its ability to harbor in its blood a wide-range of infectious bacteria, such as Yersinia pestis, the pathogen responsible for causing the Black Plague, makes
YOUR CHOICE(C) Happy consuming many foods, such as fruits, vegetables, and twigs, the Black Rat has a desire to be close to human populations, which, along with its ability to harbor in its blood a wide-range of infectious bacteria, such as Yersinia pestis, the Black Plague pathogen, makes the Black Rat a highly effective disease vector.
In this version, the opening modifier, “happy consuming many foods,” logically modifies the subject of the sentence, “the Black Rat.”
Also, the singular verb “makes” in the relative clause “which, along with its ability …” agrees with the referent of the pronoun “which,” which referent is “desire to be close to human populations.” We might be tempted to believe that “which” refers to the plural “populations,” but, in that case, the sentence would not convey a meaning that makes sense, and the verb “makes” would not agree in number with the referent of “which.” So, we can tell that “which” must refer to the entire noun phrase “desire to be close to human populations.”
Notice that, as discussed in the chapter on subject-verb agreement, the additive phrase “along with its ability …” is not part of the subject of the relative clause in which it appears, and, therefore, its presence does not make the subject of that clause plural.
(D) The Black Rat’s desire to be close to human populations and happy consuming many foods, including fruits, vegetables, and twigs, and its ability to harbor in its blood a wide-range of infectious bacteria, such as Yersinia pestis, the pathogen responsible for causing the Black Plague, makes the Black Rat a highly effective disease vector.
In saying, “The Black Rat’s desire to be close to human populations and happy consuming many foods,” this version suggests the illogical idea that the Black Rat has a desire “to be happy … consuming many foods …,” as we can more easily see by crossing off some of the wording:
The Black Rat’s desire to be close to human populations and happy consuming many foods
Also, the singular main verb, “makes,” does not agree with the compound subject of the sentence, “the Black Rat’s desire … and … its ability …,” as we can more easily see by crossing off the modifiers that appear between the subject and the main verb:
The Black Rat’s desire to be close to human populations and happy consuming many foods, including fruits, vegetables, and twigs, and its ability to harbor in its blood a wide-range of infectious bacteria, such as Yersinia pestis, the pathogen responsible for causing the Black Plague, makes
(E) The Black Rat is happy consuming many foods, including fruits, vegetables, and twigs, and therefore desires to be in close proximity to humans and its ability to harbor in its blood a wide-range of infectious bacteria, such as Yersinia pestis, the pathogen responsible for causing the Black Plague, to make the Black Rat a highly effective disease vector.
If read quickly, this version might seem to be OK. However, by reading it carefully we can see that “The Black Rat is happy … and … desires to be in close proximity to humans and its ability to harbor bacteria” conveys the illogical meaning that the Black Rat “desires to be … in close proximity to its ability to harbor bacteria.” Of course, the Black Rat would not desire to be in proximity to an ability, since the idea of being in close proximity to an ability is nonsensical.
Also, let’s cross of some modifiers to understand what “to make the Black Rat a highly effective disease vector” does:
The Black Rat is happy consuming many foods, including fruits, vegetables, and twigs, and therefore desires to be in close proximity to humans and its ability to harbor in its blood a wide-range of infectious bacteria, such as Yersinia pestis, the pathogen responsible for causing the Black Plague, to make the Black Rat a highly effective disease vector.
We can see that this version conveys the following:
The Black Rat is happy consuming many foods … and therefore desires to be in close proximity to humans and its ability to harbor in its blood a wide-range of infectious bacteria, … to make the Black Rat a highly effective disease vector.
So, this version conveys that the Black Rat is happy consuming foods and desires to be in close proximity to humans in order to make the Black Rat a disease vector. Of course, that meaning is ridiculous.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 14 Supposedly killed by a sheriff in 1881, Billy the Kid may have remained alive, actually, his death faked so that he could live without fear of arrest. 1. [x] (A) Billy the Kid may have remained alive, actually, his death faked so that he could live without fear of arrest 2. [ ] (B) it may be that Billy the Kid’s death was faked so that he could live without fear of arrest, and actually remained alive > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Billy the Kid’s death may have been faked so that he could live without fear of arrest, and actually remained alive > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) it may be that, in order to live without fear of arrest, Billy the Kid’s death was faked, and actually remained alive > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) it may be that Billy the Kid actually remained alive, his death faked so that he could live without fear of arrest > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium Correctly answering this question takes keeping the non-underlined opening modifier “supposedly killed by a sheriff …” in mind while going through the choices, and noticing that the opening modifier does not logically modify the pronoun “it” or “Billy the kid’s death.”
One way in which we could end up incorrectly answering this question is by getting caught up in the flaws in choices (B), (C), and (D) and, thus, forgetting to keep the opening modifier in mind when considering (E) and, thus, choosing incorrect answer (E), which looks correct in isolation but does not work with the opening modifier.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) Supposedly killed by a sheriff in 1881, Billy the Kid may have remained alive, actually, his death faked so that he could live without fear of arrest .
The opening modifier, “supposedly killed by a sheriff,” logically modifies the subject of the main clause, “Billy the Kid,” and this version effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(B) Supposedly killed by a sheriff in 1881, it may be that Billy the Kid’s death was faked so that he could live without fear of arrest, and actually remained alive .
The opening modifier, “Supposedly killed by a sheriff in 1881,” has to modify the noun or pronoun that follows it. So, since the pronoun “it” does not refer to anything that would have been killed, this version does not make sense.
Also, the structure of this version is flawed, and, as a result, this version conveys the somewhat illogical meaning that Billy the Kid’s death was faked so that he actually remained alive.
(C) Supposedly killed by a sheriff in 1881, Billy the Kid’s death may have been faked so that he could live without fear of arrest, and actually remained alive .
The opening modifier, “Supposedly killed by a sheriff in 1881,” has to modify the noun that follows it. So, since “Billy the Kid’s death” would not have been killed, this version does not make sense.
Also, the structure of this version is flawed, and, as a result, this version conveys the illogical meaning that Billy the Kid’s death was faked so that he actually remained alive.
(D) Supposedly killed by a sheriff in 1881, it may be that, in order to live without fear of arrest, Billy the Kid’s death was faked, and actually remained alive .
The opening modifier “Supposedly killed by a sheriff in 1881” has to modify the noun or pronoun that follows it. So, since the pronoun “it,” does not refer to anything that would have been killed, this version does not make sense.
Also, the structure of this version is flawed, and, as a result, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that Billy the Kid’s death actually remained alive.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Supposedly killed by a sheriff in 1881, it may be that Billy the Kid actually remained alive, his death faked so that he could live without fear of arrest .
This choice could be tempting, as, unlike choices (B), (C), and (D), this choice looks correct in isolation. However, when this choice is combined with the non-underlined portion, it becomes apparent that the choice is not correct.
The opening modifier, “Supposedly killed by a sheriff in 1881,” has to modify the noun or pronoun that follows it. So, since the pronoun “it,” does not refer to anything that would have been killed, this version does not make sense.
Correct Answer: (A) Now, let’s discuss squinting modifiers.
summary:
Lesson Content Nouns in possessive form often don’t seem much different from ordinary nouns, but the two types of nouns do not work in the same way in sentences.
Consider the following sentence:
Incorrect: Awarded the Nobel Prize for his work in chemistry, Linus Pauling’s research was wide-ranging and hard-hitting, and had a positive impact on many future generations of chemists.
The possessive “Linus Pauling’s” is not the same as the standalone noun “Linus Pauling.
title: 5.5 The Possessive Modification Trap

contents:
Lesson Content When a modifier is placed between two words or expressions, either of which it could modify, the conveyed meaning can be ambiguous.
Incorrect: People who train consistently achieve score increases.
Notice that the adverb “consistently” is a squinting modifier because it is placed between two verbs, “train” and “achieve,” either of which it could be modifying.
For the sentence to have one clear meaning, the modifier must be placed differently, so that it becomes clear which word is being modified.
For example:
People who consistently train achieve score increases.
or
People who train achieve score increases consistently.
Notice that, in each sentence, “consistently” now clearly modifies one word.
💡MUST KNOW:
For a sentence to be effective, it must be clear what any modifiers in the sentence modify. Be on the lookout for squinting modifiers, which are modifiers between two words or phrases, either of which the modifier could modify.
Let’s look at another example:
Incorrect: The library books that Jack borrowed recently were taken from his locker by a bully.
Did Jack recently borrow the books or did the bully recently take them? Because of the placement of the adverb “recently” between the verbs “borrowed” and “were taken,” we can’t be sure.
The library books that Jack recently borrowed were taken from his locker by a bully.
or
The library books that Jack borrowed were recently taken from his locker by a bully.
Let’s look at one more example.
Incorrect: Listening to music often lifted Clarissa’s spirits. In this case, the squinting modifier “often” appears between a noun, “music,” and a verb, “lifted,” rather than between two verbs.
Clarissa lifted her spirits by listening to music often.
Often, listening to music lifted Clarissa’s spirits.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Now, let’s consider a Sentence Correction example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 15 Aware that cyclists who ride uphill quickly build strength and stamina, the team members choose to train in the mountainous region in the western part of the country. 1. [ ] (A) that cyclists who ride uphill quickly build strength and stamina, the team members choose > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) that cyclists who ride uphill build strength and stamina quickly, the team members’ choice is > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) of cyclists who are riding up hills building strength and stamina quickly, the team members choose > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) that cyclists who ride uphill build strength and stamina quickly, the team members choose 5. [ ] (E) of cyclists riding up hills that quickly build strength and stamina, the choice of the team members is > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy In the original version, “quickly” is a squinting modifier; as placed it could modify either “ride uphill” or “build.” So, the meaning conveyed by the original version is ambiguous. In the correct version, “quickly” clearly modifies one verb.
(A) Aware that cyclists who ride uphill quickly build strength and stamina, the team members choose to train in the mountainous region in the western part of the country.
The adverb “quickly” could be meant to modify either “ride uphill” or “build.”
(B) Aware that cyclists who ride uphill build strength and stamina quickly, the team members’ choice is to train in the mountainous region in the western part of the country.
“Quickly” is placed so as to clearly modify “build.”
However, the placement of “the team members’ choice” directly after the opening modifier, “aware that cyclists … build …,” results in a meaning issue, because “team members’” is not the subject of the sentence but, rather, a noun in the possessive form that modifies “choice.” As a result, the conveyed meaning is that the choice is “aware that cyclists … build ….” A choice would not be aware of something.
(C) Aware of cyclists who are riding up hills building strength and stamina quickly, the team members choose to train in the mountainous region in the western part of the country.
This version conveys the illogical meaning that the team members train in the mountainous region because they are aware of cyclists who are riding up hills.
Also, this version nonsensically conveys that the hills are “building strength and stamina.”
YOUR CHOICE(D) Aware that cyclists who ride uphill build strength and stamina quickly, the team members choose to train in the mountainous region in the western part of the country.
“Quickly” is placed so as to clearly modify “build,” and the rest of the sentence is well constructed and conveys a logical meaning.
(E) Aware of cyclists riding up hills that quickly build strength and stamina, the choice of the team members is to train in the mountainous region in the western part of the country.
This version conveys the illogical meaning that the team members train in the mountainous region because they are aware of cyclists riding up hills.
Also, the wording “hills that build strength and stamina” conveys the illogical meaning that the hills themselves build strength and stamina.
Also, the placement of the opening modifier “aware of …” before the subject, “the choice,” results in the sentence’s conveying the illogical meaning that the choice is aware.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss relative clauses.
summary:
Lesson Content When a modifier is placed between two words or expressions, either of which it could modify, the conveyed meaning can be ambiguous.
Incorrect: People who train consistently achieve score increases.
Notice that the adverb “consistently” is a squinting modifier because it is placed between two verbs, “train” and “achieve,” either of which it could be modifying.
For the sentence to have one clear meaning, the modifier must be placed differently, so that it becomes clear which word is being modified.
title: 5.6 Squinting Modifiers
