title: 5.7 Relative Clauses

contents:
Lesson Content The relative pronouns “that” and “which” refer to nouns that represent things other than people.
💡MUST KNOW:
The relative pronouns “that” and “which” refer to nouns that represent things other than people. In other words, never use “that” or “which” to refer to people.
Because both “that” and “which” refer to objects or ideas (but never to people), they are easily confused.
All the same, in Sentence Correction questions, the following common convention has been followed.
So, what are restrictive and nonrestrictive relative clauses?
A restrictive relative clause somehow restricts the meaning of the noun that the restrictive relative clause modifies.
A nonrestrictive relative clause, on the other hand, does not restrict the meaning of the noun that it modifies.
In Sentence Correction questions, if a relative clause is restrictive, the correct pronoun to use is “that.” If a clause is nonrestrictive, the correct pronoun to use is “which.”
💡MUST KNOW:
In Sentence Correction questions, the words “that” and “which” are not interchangeable. The relative pronoun “that” introduces restrictive relative clauses, whereas the relative pronoun “which” introduces nonrestrictive relative clauses.
Let’s talk a little more about what it means to be restrictive or nonrestrictive.
Juice that is frozen lasts for months.
In this sentence, the word “that” begins the relative clause “that is frozen,” which is restrictive.
For contrast, consider this sentence:
Mount Everest, which is the tallest mountain on Earth, has lured adventure seekers since the early 1900s.
This sentence includes the nonrestrictive relative clause “which is the tallest mountain on Earth.” Why do we use a nonrestrictive clause that begins with the relative pronoun “which” in this sentence? Well, Mount Everest is the only Mount Everest.
There is a simple strategy that we can employ to determine whether a relative clause should be restrictive and begin with the restrictive relative pronoun “that” or be nonrestrictive and begin with the nonrestrictive relative pronoun “which:”
To determine whether the underlined clause is restrictive or nonrestrictive, eliminate it from the sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
To determine whether an underlined clause is restrictive or nonrestrictive, eliminate it from the sentence. If it can be eliminated without the core meaning of the sentence changing, it is nonrestrictive and will use the pronoun “which.”
Consider the following sentence:
Science textbooks (that/which) mention creationism have been banned from public schools.
Let’s cross out the relative clause – that is, the clause that begins with the relative pronoun – and read the sentence again.
Science textbooks (that/which) mention creationism have been banned from public schools.
Now we ask: does the sentence mean the same thing without the relative clause? Clearly, the answer is no.
Science textbooks that mention creationism have been banned from public schools.
Let’s look at another example:
John’s best work pants (that/which) got filthy while he was doing yard work have to be washed before Monday.
Using the clause elimination strategy, we can reread the sentence without the relative clause:
John’s best work pants (that/which) got filthy while he was doing yard work have to be washed before Monday.
After reading the sentence without the relative clause, ask yourself whether the meaning of the sentence is still intact.
In other words, there is no need to categorize (restrict) John’s best work pants into a subset of pants to preserve the sentence’s meaning.
John’s best work pants, which got filthy while he was doing yard work, have to be washed before Monday.
There’s something else to notice about the clause in the sentence above.
💡MUST KNOW:
Nonrestrictive relative clauses must always be set off from the rest of sentences by commas.
Consider the following example sentences, which contain nonrestrictive modifiers.
The new phone model, which utilizes two cameras to take photos, is available in an array of colors.
The Vegan Joint, which serves up healthy plant-based meals, has very tasty food.
Last week, Tasha ate at Crossroads Kitchen, which has an amazing wine program.
Notice that the nonrestrictive relative clauses are always preceded by commas and followed by commas or periods.
Note, however, that, while nonrestrictive relative clauses are always set off by commas, it is not the case that the word “which” must always be preceded by a comma.
Claudia does not know which apple [which one] she will eat.
Furthermore, when “which” is immediately preceded by a preposition, as in “in which,” “by which,” “through which,” “on which,” “from which,” and “to which,” no comma precedes “which.”
Consider the following example sentences:
The car in which we were riding was black.
The fog through which we traveled was thick.
Maria does not know which dress she will wear to the gala.
💡MUST KNOW:
When the word “which” is preceded by a preposition or is used to mean “which one,” no comma preceding “which” is necessary.
In contrast, restrictive relative clauses are not set off from the rest of sentences by commas, as we can see in the following example:
The turtle that made the nest was huge.
However, there are situations in which a comma will correctly precede the word “that.”
Consider the following sentence:
The items, a chair and a mirror, that were delivered yesterday are both in the front room.
Notice that there is a comma before “that” not because a comma is required prior to a restrictive relative clause, but because “a chair and a mirror” are modifying “the items,” telling us what items are being discussed.
The car was moving so fast, streaking by, that we had difficulty seeing the driver.
Notice that the comma before “that” belongs to the modifier “streaking by.” In other words, the comma is a consequence of the sentence structure that precedes “that.” This sentence is correct as written.
She said, in closing, that she considered the matter resolved.
Again, notice that the comma before “that” is there merely as a consequence of the sentence structure preceding the word “that.” This sentence is correctly written.
The message here is that, if we analyze a sentence non-holistically, incorrectly thinking that a comma should never precede “that” and focusing simply on whether a comma precedes “that,” we might make an incorrect decision.
💡MUST KNOW:
Knowing the rules is an essential part of success in Sentence Correction. However, we must also analyze sentences holistically, carefully evaluating the use of each word, structure, and piece of punctuation as it fits into the entire sentence.
Now, let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether the underlined clause should be restrictive and use the restrictive relative pronoun “that” or should be nonrestrictive and use the nonrestrictive relative pronoun “which.” --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s practice applying what we just discussed in answering Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 16 Many first time visitors to New York City, that is known for having its aggressive culture, are surprised and find the locals to be friendly and helpful. 1. [ ] (A) City, that is known for having its aggressive culture, are surprised and find > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) City, which is known for having an aggressive culture, are surprised upon finding 3. [ ] (C) City, known for having an aggressive culture, are surprised, finding > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) City, which is known for their aggressive culture, are surprised by the finding that > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) City that is known for having an aggressive culture are surprised when they find > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium Finding the correct answer to this question takes noticing how restrictive and nonrestrictive relative clauses are used and noticing the effects that structure and word choice have on meaning.
(A) Many first time visitors to New York City, that is known for having its aggressive culture, are surprised and find the locals to be friendly and helpful.
The relative clause “that is known for having its aggressive culture” is set off from the rest of the sentence, and therefore it must be nonrestrictive. However, it begins with the relative pronoun “that,” which the GMAT uses to begin restrictive relative clauses.
Also, the use of the pronoun “its” results in the sentence’s conveying a somewhat illogical meaning. Clearly, the culture of New York City is the city’s culture. Saying that it is known for having its culture does not make sense in this context.
Also, the wording “surprised and find” illogically separates the visitors’ being surprised and their finding the locals to be friendly and helpful. The point is that they are surprised because of what they find, but this wording does not convey that there is a connection between the two aspects of their experiences.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Many first time visitors to New York City, which is known for having an aggressive culture, are surprised upon finding the locals to be friendly and helpful.
This version effectively uses a nonrestrictive relative clause “which is known for having an aggressive culture” to show the contrast between what the city is known for and the experience of first time visitors. The use of the pronoun “which” is in line with the GMAT’s stance on the correct pronoun to use to begin a nonrestrictive relative clause, and the wording “surprised upon finding” appropriately connects the two aspects of the experience, indicating that being surprised is a result of finding.
(C) Many first time visitors to New York City, known for having an aggressive culture, are surprised, finding the locals to be friendly and helpful.
Whether the phrase “known for having an aggressive culture” is meant to modify “visitors” or “New York City” is not clear.
Also, the wording “are surprised, finding” does not effectively connect being surprised and finding. As a result, the sentence conveys that the visitors are surprised, without indicating what surprises them, and then adds that they find the locals to be friendly and helpful without clearly connecting the act of finding to the rest of what the sentence describes.
(D) Many first time visitors to New York City, which is known for their aggressive culture, are surprised by the finding that the locals to be friendly and helpful.
The use of the pronoun “their” does not make sense. Since “which” could only refer to “New York City,” this version seems to convey the illogical meaning that New York City is known for the aggressive culture of first time visitors to the city. Alternatively, “their” could be perceived as referring to the residents of New York City. However, only the city, and not the residents, is mentioned. So, the idea that “their” refers to the residents is not supported by the wording of the sentence.
Also, the wording “surprised by the finding that” is not an ideal way to express that the visitors are surprised by what they find. “Surprised by the finding that” could convey that the visitors are surprised by someone else’s finding about New York City locals.
(E) Many first time visitors to New York City that is known for having an aggressive culture are surprised when they find the locals to be friendly and helpful.
Because there are no commas setting it off from the rest of the sentence, and because it begins with “that,” the relative clause “that is known for having an aggressive culture” is clearly restrictive. The use of a restrictive modifier in this context does not make sense, as there is only one New York City, and therefore restricting the noun “New York City” does not make sense.
Also, the wording “surprised when they find” is not ideal, as it does not clearly express that the surprise is the result of finding, but rather expresses only that the two experiences, being surprised and finding, occur simultaneously.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 17 Spotting a double rainbow in the sky is widely regarded as a rare occurrence, although weather conditions, which the twin reflections result from​, are actually quite common. 1. [ ] (A) although weather conditions, which the twin reflections result from, > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) because the twin reflections, which are the result of weather conditions, > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) though the twin reflections are the result of weather conditions that 4. [ ] (D) although weather conditions, which result in the twin reflections, > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) since the weather conditions that result in the twin reflections > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard Notice that, in order to make sense, the sentence must make clear that the weather conditions mentioned are particular weather conditions: those that result in a double rainbow. In order for the sentence to specify which weather conditions it is about, the sentence has to use a restrictive, rather than a nonrestrictive, modifier.
(A) Spotting a double rainbow in the sky is widely regarded as a rare occurrence, although weather conditions, which the twin reflections result from, are actually quite common.
What weather conditions are actually quite common? Clearly it does not make sense to say that all weather conditions are actually quite common, since weather conditions are a constant feature of our atmosphere. As long as Earth exists, there will be “weather conditions.” So, saying, “weather conditions … are actually quite common,” is pointless.
Thus, it does not make sense to use the nonrestrictive clause “which the twin reflections result from” to modify “weather conditions.” The sentence needs to specify which weather conditions it is mentioning, making clear they are the specific weather conditions that result in a double rainbow, as in, “although weather conditions that the twin reflections result from are actually quite common.” So, a restrictive relative clause should be used instead.
(B) Spotting a double rainbow in the sky is widely regarded as a rare occurrence, because the twin reflections, which are the result of weather conditions, are actually quite common.
It’s true that twin reflections are the result of weather conditions, so the nonrestrictive clause “which are the result of weather conditions” is not incorrect.
However, does it make sense to say that spotting a double rainbow is considered rare because double rainbows are common? Of course not. So, what this version says does not make sense.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Spotting a double rainbow in the sky is widely regarded as a rare occurrence, though the twin reflections are the result of weather conditions that are actually quite common.
This version uses a restrictive relative clause, which is not set off by commas and begins with “that,” to make clear that the weather conditions mentioned are specific weather conditions. The sentence meaning is clear and logical.
(D) Spotting a double rainbow in the sky is widely regarded as a rare occurrence, although weather conditions, which result in the twin reflections, are actually quite common.
What weather conditions are actually quite common? Clearly it does not make sense to say that all weather conditions are actually quite common, since weather conditions are a constant feature of our atmosphere. As long as Earth exists, there will be “weather conditions.” So, saying, “weather conditions … are actually quite common,” is pointless.
Thus, it does not make sense to use the nonrestrictive clause “which result in the twin reflections” to modify “weather conditions.” The sentence needs to specify which weather conditions it is mentioning, making clear that they are the specific weather conditions that result in a double rainbow, as in, “although weather conditions that result in the twin reflections are actually quite common.” So, a restrictive relative clause should be used instead.
(E) Spotting a double rainbow in the sky is widely regarded as a rare occurrence, since the weather conditions that result in the twin reflections are actually quite common.
This version correctly uses a restrictive relative clause to specify which weather conditions we’re concerned with – those that result in twin reflections – but the use of “since” does not make sense. Would spotting a double rainbow be considered rare because the conditions that result in it are common? Of course not. So, what this version says does not make sense.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss the proper placement of relative clauses, including those that begin with “that” and “which,” in sentences.
summary:
Lesson Content The relative pronouns “that” and “which” refer to nouns that represent things other than people.
💡MUST KNOW:
The relative pronouns “that” and “which” refer to nouns that represent things other than people. In other words, never use “that” or “which” to refer to people.
Because both “that” and “which” refer to objects or ideas (but never to people), they are easily confused.
All the same, in Sentence Correction questions, the following common convention has been followed.
title: 5.7.1 “That” versus “Which”

contents:
Lesson Content Like faulty placement of adjectives and adverbs, misplacement of relative clauses can result in the creation of illogical sentences.
Incorrect: The salesman sold the car to the woman that had 15,000 miles.
Notice that the restrictive relative clause “that had 15,000 miles” is incorrectly modifying “woman.” Of course, it’s not the woman who had 15,000 miles; it’s the car she purchased.
The salesman sold to the woman the car that had 15,000 miles.
Now the relative clause correctly modifies the noun “car.”
Let’s look at another example.
Incorrect: The teenage girl turned her nose up at the gift given to her by her parents for her birthday, which was very expensive.
We must determine what “was very expensive.” Although teenage girls can require expensive things, the girls themselves cannot be expensive, nor can parents, birthdays, or noses (this sentence is clearly not about plastic surgery).
Although very expensive, the gift given to the teenage girl on her birthday by her parents provoked her to turn up her nose.
The teenage girl turned up her nose at the very expensive gift given to her on her birthday by her parents.
On her birthday, the teenage girl’s parents gave her a very expensive gift, which caused her to turn up her nose.
Each of these sentences is effective and avoids the confusion caused by the misplaced modifier in the original sentence.
So then, any time we encounter a relative clause in a Sentence Correction question, we must ensure that its placement makes sense.
Let’s look at another example:
Incorrect: The plane is still sitting on the runway that was scheduled to depart two hours ago.
Notice that the relative clause “that was scheduled to depart two hours ago” is a misplaced modifier.
The plane that was scheduled to depart two hours ago is still sitting on the runway.
Notice that now the relative clause properly modifies “plane.”
Here’s another:
Incorrect: Scientists have found that the Great Pyramid of Giza focuses electromagnetic energy, which is an ancient structure that remains largely intact today.
What is an ancient structure that remains largely intact today? Certainly not “electromagnetic energy.” So, by considering the meaning conveyed by the sentence, we can tell that the relative clause is misplaced; it should be modifying “the Great Pyramid of Giza.” A corrected version is:
Scientists have found that the Great Pyramid of Giza, which is an ancient structure that remains largely intact today, focuses electromagnetic energy.
Now the relative clause is properly modifying the correct noun, “Great Pyramid of Giza.”
So, we can use a rule that will help us: with a few exceptions that we’ll discuss in the coming sections, relative clauses, including those that begin with “that” or “which,” must come immediately after the nouns that they modify.
💡MUST KNOW:
With a few exceptions that we’ll discuss in the coming sections, relative clauses, including those that begin with “that” or “which,” must come immediately after the nouns that they modify.
Let’s look at a few more examples:
Avocados that are ripe do not last long.
Notice that the relative clause “that are ripe” modifies the noun “avocados.” Thus, the proper placement of “that are ripe” is right after the noun it modifies, “avocados.”
The Boeing 777, which is a very large commercial airliner, is an engineering marvel.
Notice that the relative clause “which is a very large commercial airliner” modifies the noun “Boeing 777.” Thus, the proper placement of “which is a very large commercial airliner” is right after the noun that it modifies, Boeing 777.
Relative clauses that begin with pronouns other than “that” or “which” follow a similar pattern.
The woman on the plane is going to Boston who has a cat with her.
Notice that the relative clause “who has a cat with her” is misplaced and, thus, seems to be illogically modifying the noun “Boston.” Of course, it should be modifying the noun “woman.” Clearer versions are:
The woman on the plane has a cat with her and is going to Boston.
The woman who has a cat with her on the plane is going to Boston.
On the plane to Boston is a woman who has a cat.
The woman on the plane has a cat with her, and she is going to Boston.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is incorrect or correct. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 The woman baked an apple pie, using her mother’s recipe, which tasted delicious. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 The drink was served by the waiter, which had a cherry garnish. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 3 Many of the films on Thadie’s must-see list were showing at the new multiplex, which was near her house. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 The Gettysburg Address is among the most famous speeches in history, which was delivered by President Abraham Lincoln in 1863. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 5 Wild animals that are raised in captivity, even those under the care of well-trained professionals, tend to have shorter life spans than animals in their natural habitats. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 6 New York City’s famed hotel The Pierre has long been a favorite of celebrities, which is located across the street from Central Park. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 7 Cars are more popular with drivers that get better gas mileage because they save drivers money over the lives of the cars. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 8 The grunge rock movement, which emerged in Seattle in the mid-1980s and reached its peak in popularity in the early 1990s, was in part a response to the flamboyant, pop-influenced “hair bands” dominating American music at that time. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
The noun that should be modified is “pie” because it is the pie, not the recipe, that tasted delicious. A corrected version is:
Using her mother’s recipe, the woman baked an apple pie, which tasted delicious.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
The noun that should be modified is “drink.” The drink would have the cherry garnish, not the waiter. A corrected version is:
The drink, which had a cherry garnish, was served by the waiter.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
The noun that should be modified is “multiplex,” and thus the sentence is correct as written.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
Since the “Address” and not “history” was delivered by the President, the relative clause “which was delivered …” should appear directly after “Gettysburg Address,” not after “history.” A corrected version could read:
The Gettysburg Address, which was delivered by President Abraham Lincoln in 1863, is among the most famous speeches in history.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
The relative clause “that are raised in captivity” correctly appears after the noun that it modifies, “animals.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
The relative clause “which is located …” must be placed after “The Pierre,” not after “favorite” or “celebrities.” A corrected version is:
New York City’s famed hotel The Pierre, which is located across the street from Central Park, has long been a favorite of celebrities.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 7 Explanation:
As written, the sentence is very awkward and could be read as conveying the nonsensical meaning that the drivers get better gas mileage because they save drivers money. The relative clause “that get better gas mileage” must be moved, so that it immediately follows the noun that it modifies, “cars.”
Cars that get better gas mileage are more popular with drivers because they save drivers money over the lives of the cars.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 8 Explanation:
The long relative clause beginning with “which” correctly follows “grunge rock movement” and is set off by commas.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Consider the following Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 18 Berries and plants that grow in the area surrounding the defunct Chernobyl nuclear facility contain radioactive substances in extraordinarily high concentrations and are therefore unsafe to eat, but animals living there eat what grows there and often experience severe illnesses as a result. 1. [x] (A) plants that grow in the area surrounding the defunct Chernobyl nuclear facility contain radioactive substances in extraordinarily high concentrations and are 2. [ ] (B) plants growing in the area surrounding the defunct Chernobyl nuclear facility containing radioactive substances in extraordinarily high concentrations and > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) plants grow in the area that surrounds the defunct Chernobyl nuclear facility, contains radioactive substances in extraordinarily high concentrations, and is > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) plants, which grow in the area surrounding the defunct Chernobyl nuclear facility, contain radioactive substances in extraordinary high concentrations and are > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) plants grow in the area surrounding the defunct Chernobyl nuclear facility, which contains radioactive substances in extraordinary high concentrations and is > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy Some answer choices in this question could appear to be correct if we read them without considering the non-underlined portions of the sentence. So, correctly answering this question takes both noticing the results of the ways in which modifiers are used and carefully considering the choices in combination with the non-underlined portions.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Berries and plants that grow in the area surrounding the defunct Chernobyl nuclear facility contain radioactive substances in extraordinarily high concentrations and are therefore unsafe to eat, but animals living there eat what grows there and often experience severe illnesses as a result.
Notice that, in this version, the restrictive modifier “that grow in the area …” indicates that the sentence is about a specific set of berries and plants, the berries and plants that grow in the area surrounding the defunct Chernobyl nuclear facility.
As a result, what this version conveys about those berries and plants and about the nuclear facility makes sense.
(B) Berries and plants growing in the area surrounding the defunct Chernobyl nuclear facility containing radioactive substances in extraordinarily high concentrations and therefore unsafe to eat, but animals living there eat what grows there and often experience severe illnesses as a result.
This choice could be tempting if read alone without the non-underlined portions, since it contains three participles that match each other: “growing,” “surrounding,” and “containing.”
However, closer examination reveals that the structure of this version is flawed.
Since the second part of the sentence is a clause connected to the first part by the conjunction “but,” the first part should also be a clause. However, the first part contains no main verb, as none of the verb forms in the first part, “growing,” “surrounding,” and “containing,” can, on their own, serve as main verbs, and, therefore, the first part of the sentence is not a clause.
So, this version is composed of a phrase illogically connected to a clause by the conjunction “but,” as we can more easily see by simplifying the sentence:
Berries and plants growing in the area … and therefore unsafe to eat, but animals living there eat what grows there …
(C) Berries and plants grow in the area that surrounds the defunct Chernobyl nuclear facility, contains radioactive substances in extraordinarily high concentrations, and is therefore unsafe to eat, but animals living there eat what grows there and often experience severe illnesses as a result.
In this version, the modifier “that surrounds … contains … and is therefore unsafe to eat” is placed directly after and, therefore, seems to modify “area.” So, this version conveys the illogical meaning that the area is unsafe to eat.
Alternatively, “contains … and is therefore unsafe to eat” could be taken as meaning that the berries and plants contain radioactive substances in high concentrations and are therefore unsafe to eat, but in that case, the singular verbs “contains” and “is” would not agree with the plural subject “berries and plants.”
(D) Berries and plants, which grow in the area surrounding the defunct Chernobyl nuclear facility, contain radioactive substances in extraordinary high concentrations and are therefore unsafe to eat, but animals living there eat what grows there and often experience severe illnesses as a result.
Because the relative clause that follows “berries and plants” in this version is a non-restrictive modifier, the version is not about a specific set of berries and plants but rather about berries and plants in general. So, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that all berries and plants, which grow in the area surrounding the defunct nuclear facility (as if all berries and plants grow in that area and nowhere else), contain radioactive substances in high concentrations and are therefore unsafe to eat.
(E) Berries and plants grow in the area surrounding the defunct Chernobyl nuclear facility, which contains radioactive substances in extraordinary high concentrations and is therefore unsafe to eat, but animals living there eat what grows there and often experience severe illnesses as a result.
In this version, the modifier “which contains … and is therefore unsafe to eat” is placed directly after and, therefore, modifies “facility,” and thus this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the nuclear facility is unsafe to eat.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 19 The graduate students were eager to see the presentation by the archeologist, an expert on pottery made by ancient Mayan people who arrived the day before. 1. [ ] (A) an expert on pottery made by ancient Mayan people who arrived the day before > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) who was an expert on pottery made by ancient Mayan people and who arrived the day before 3. [ ] (C) an expert who arrived the day before on pottery made by ancient Mayan people > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) who was an expert on pottery made by ancient Mayan people, who arrived the day before > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) who arrived the day before being an expert on pottery made by ancient Mayan people > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium Depending on where the relative clause “who arrived the day before” is placed, the sentence may either make sense or convey a nonsensical meaning.
(A) The graduate students were eager to see the presentation by the archeologist, an expert on pottery made by ancient Mayan people who arrived the day before .
The relative clause “who arrived the day before” is placed directly after and seems to modify “ancient Mayan people.” Thus, this version seems to convey the nonsensical meaning that ancient Mayan people arrived the day before.
Admittedly, we could decipher that, since ancient Mayan people would not have arrived the day before, it must have been the expert who arrived the day before. All the same, the GMAT would not prefer this version, considering it ineffective, because it so strongly seems to convey the nonsensical meaning that the ancient Mayan people arrived the day before and is, therefore, not well constructed.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The graduate students were eager to see the presentation by the archeologist, who was an expert on pottery made by ancient Mayan people and who arrived the day before .
Both “who was an expert on pottery …” and “who arrived the day before” are placed in ways such that they modify something that they would logically modify, “archeologist,” and such that the sentence conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(C) The graduate students were eager to see the presentation by the archeologist, an expert who arrived the day before on pottery made by ancient Mayan people .
“Who arrived the day before” is placed so as to clearly and logically modify “expert.” However, because “who arrived the day before” is placed just before “on pottery,” this version conveys that the expert had arrived on pottery.
(D) The graduate students were eager to see the presentation by the archeologist, who was an expert on pottery made by ancient Mayan people, who arrived the day before .
The relative clause “who arrived the day before” is placed directly after and seems to modify “ancient Mayan people.” So, this version seems to convey the nonsensical meaning that ancient Mayan people arrived the day before.
(E) The graduate students were eager to see the presentation by the archeologist, who arrived the day before being an expert on pottery made by ancient Mayan people .
“Who arrived the day before” is placed so as to clearly and logically modify “archeologist.” However, because “who arrived the day before” is placed just before “being an expert,” this version conveys an ambiguous meaning that seems to be that the archeologist arrived on the day before the archeologist was being an expert on pottery, a meaning that does not make sense.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss the situations in which relative clauses can modify their antecedents remotely.
summary:
Lesson Content Like faulty placement of adjectives and adverbs, misplacement of relative clauses can result in the creation of illogical sentences.
Incorrect: The salesman sold the car to the woman that had 15,000 miles.
Notice that the restrictive relative clause “that had 15,000 miles” is incorrectly modifying “woman.” Of course, it’s not the woman who had 15,000 miles; it’s the car she purchased.
The salesman sold to the woman the car that had 15,000 miles.
title: 5.7.2 The Proper Placement of Relative Clauses

contents:
Lesson Content In most cases in which we see a relative clause in a correct answer to a Sentence Correction question, that clause will directly follow the noun that the clause modifies.
💡MUST KNOW:
There are structures that are considered acceptable or correct in which a relative clause is separated from the noun that it modifies. In such a structure, the relative clause is called a “remote relative clause.”
There are two ways in which a relative clause may be separated from the noun that it modifies in a correct answer.
A relative clause may be separated from the noun that it modifies by another modifier.
A relative clause may be separated from the noun that it modifies by another modifier, such as a prepositional phrase or an appositive.
Consider the following example:
The town on the island, which is a sleepy seaside village, attracts many tourists each summer.
Is the relative clause “which is a sleepy seaside village” properly placed? The answer is yes, it is.
Students often wonder whether “which is a sleepy seaside village” should modify “town” or “island.” The answer is that, essentially, “town on the island” is one large noun, and the relative clause “which is a sleepy seaside village” is modifying the entire noun.
Let’s look at that sentence again:
The town on the island, which is a sleepy seaside village, attracts many tourists each summer.
Notice that the prepositional phrase, “on the island” acts as a restrictive modifier.
Let’s examine this idea further.
The tree grows taller every year.
The tree by the river grows taller every year.
The tree by the river that hangs over the bank grows taller every year.
The tree by the river that hangs over the bank below the ford grows taller every year.
Notice that each new sentence contains a greater number of modifiers describing the noun “tree.”
Let’s look at another sentence:
Marty has a fruit stand in his town’s farmers market that he uses not only to sell fruits and vegetables but also to spread his wisdom of logic and reasoning.
Presented with a sentence like this one, students often wonder whether the restrictive relative clause “that he uses to not only sell fruits and vegetables but also to spread his wisdom of logic and reasoning” should be next to “fruit stand” instead of next to “market.” The answer is that this sentence is correct as written.
Notice that the prepositional phrase “in his town’s farmers market” is essential to our understanding of what fruit stand is being discussed.
Thus, even though the modifier “that he uses to sell …” is not placed directly after the noun “stand,” the placement of the modifier makes sense.
Let’s consider another example:
Employees organized the walkout to show support for their colleagues in corporate offices abroad who had been denied an annual pay increase in favor of record bonuses for top executives.
The relative clause beginning with “who had been denied …” correctly modifies “colleagues in corporate offices abroad.”
💡MUST KNOW:
A relative clause may be separated from the noun that it modifies by another modifier, such as a prepositional phrase or an appositive.
Now, you might be wondering how we decide which noun a relative clause is meant to modify if the clause is placed after a structure that contains multiple nouns, such as “town on the island” or “tree by the river.” The answer is that the relative clause is understood to modify the shortest logical target.
Let’s explore this concept by considering an example:
The most beautiful house in the valley, which was built last summer, is not overly large.
The relative clause “which was built last summer” is placed after “the most beautiful house in the valley, which contains two nouns, “house” and “valley.” To determine what the clause is meant to modify, we can look for the shortest logical target.
The shortest possible target of the clause is “valley,” but “valley” is not a logical target of the clause, because a valley would not be “built last summer.”
The next target to consider is “house in the valley.” We can see that “house in the valley” is a logical target of the clause, because a house in the valley would be “built last summer.” Thus, “which was built last summer” is understood to modify “house in the valley.”
OK, what about this example?
The most beautiful house in the valley, which runs between two mountains, is not overly large.
In this case, the relative clause “which runs between two mountains” logically modifies “valley.” So, in this case we don’t have to check any further to find the target of the clause, because “valley” is the shortest logical target of the modifier and, thus, must be what it modifies.
Let’s consider one more example:
The F22 Raptor, a modern military fighter jet, has two engines with movable parts that direct their exhaust, each of which produces 35,000 pounds of thrust.
“Each of which produces 35,000 pounds of thrust” is a relative clause.
The shortest possible target is “exhaust,” but exhaust would not produce 35,000 pounds of thrust, nor would the next shortest possible target, “movable parts that direct their exhaust.”
The shortest logical target of the relative clause turns out to be “two engines with movable parts that direct their exhaust,” since it is logical that each of two engines “produces 35,000 pounds of thrust.” So, we can tell that the clause modifies “two engines with movable parts that direct their exhaust.”
💡MUST KNOW:
A relative clause placed after a structure that contains multiple nouns is understood to modify the shortest logical target.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 The popular route along the Mount Whitney Trail is roughly 22 miles long and peaks at more than 4,400 meters above sea level, which takes 12 to 18 hours of trekking in extreme conditions to complete. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 The Bechdel test, a measure of the representation of women in movies that was developed by Alison Bechdel, stipulates that a film must feature at least two named female characters who talk to each other about a topic unrelated to a man. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 Although Colorado boasts the largest concentration of major summits, the mountains of Alaska are preferred by many hiking enthusiasts, which is best known as the site of Denali. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 4 Investors are reluctant to take positions even at current price levels, 52-week lows for stocks in key sectors, which, analysts caution, may not represent the bottom of the downward trend that has gripped the market for the past two months. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 Albums for long term photo storage, which must be constructed of materials that are free from acid, lignin, and PVC in order to be labeled “archival,” are significantly more expensive than standard albums but worth the expense for storing treasured photos taken before the advent of digital photography. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 6 The commonly held belief that walking 10,000 steps per day has many health benefits, which is a habit that many Americans find difficult to maintain, was popularized in the 1960s by a Japanese company marketing pedometers and was not initially based on scientific research. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 7 Tomorrow, three analysts will arrive who will take over for a week while the current team takes a break. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 8 A new type of fiber is being developed in the company’s main laboratory, which is expected to be used as a strengthening component in a variety of products. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 9 A conference is being planned that is expected to include presentations by many noted experts in the field. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 10 In Iceland, geothermal energy is used in winter to heat streets and sidewalks, which is abundant and can be harnessed at low cost. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 11 In a remote area of the Pacific Ocean, a new volcanic island is forming that seems likely to merge with a small island that already exists in the area. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
What takes 12 to 18 hours of trekking to complete? Sea level? No, it’s the popular route that takes 12 to 18 hours of trekking to complete. While a relative clause can be separated from what it modifies by a restrictive modifier such as a prepositional phrase, in this case the relative clause “which takes 12 to 18 hours …” is separated from what it modifies, “the popular route,” by multiple nouns, verbs and modifiers. So, in this case, the modifier is not just remote; it’s misplaced. An effective sentence is:
The popular route along the Mount Whitney Trail, which takes twelve to eighteen hours of trekking in extreme conditions to complete, is roughly 22 miles long and peaks at more than 4,400 meters above sea level.
Note that, even though the relative clause modifies “route,” the placement of the clause after the prepositional phrase “along the Mount Whitney Trail” is fine, as the clause modifies what is essentially one long noun, “the popular route along the Mount Whitney Trail.”
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
Even though the relative clause “that was developed by Alison Bechdel” is separated from the noun that it modifies, “measure,” by two prepositional phrases, since “a measure of the representation of women in movies” is essentially all one long noun phrase, the placement of the relative clause directly after that noun phrase makes sense and results in a sentence that conveys a clear and logical meaning.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
Does it make sense that “hiking enthusiasts” would be “best known as the site of Denali?” No. In this sentence, the relative clause “which is best known as the site of Denali” is not just remote; it’s misplaced. Clearly, this clause is meant to modify “Alaska,” not “enthusiasts.” So, to fix the sentence, we have to move the clause. An effective version of the sentence is:
Although Colorado boasts the largest concentration of major summits, the mountains of Alaska, which is best known as the site of Denali, are preferred by many hiking enthusiasts.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
Since the relative clause “which, analysts caution, may not represent …” is separated from the noun it modifies, “price levels,” only by an appositive describing the price levels, “52-weeks lows for stocks in key sectors,” the sentence is grammatically correct and effectively conveys a logical meaning.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
The relative clause “which must be constructed …” must be meant to modify “albums.” All the same, the placement of the clause after the prepositional phrase “for long term photo storage” is correct, as it’s clear that the clause does not modify “storage,” but rather, modifies the entire noun phrase, “albums for long term photo storage.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
The relative clause, “which is a habit that many Americans find difficult to maintain” must be meant to modify “walking 10,000 steps a day” not “health benefits.” So, since the clause is separated from what it is meant to modify, not just by another modifier, but by a main verb, “has,” and a noun, “health benefits,” the clause is not just remote; it’s misplaced. A more effective sentence is:
The commonly held belief that walking 10,000 steps per day, which is a habit that many Americans find difficult to maintain, has many health benefits was popularized in the 1960s by a Japanese company marketing pedometers and was not initially based on scientific research.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 7 Explanation:
While the relative clause “who will take over … break” is separated from the noun that it modifies, “analysts,” by the sentence’s main verb, the meaning of the sentence is clear. So, while the construction of this sentence may not be ideal, it would be considered acceptable by the GMAT.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 8 Explanation:
A reader may be able to figure out that the relative clause “which is expected … products” is meant to modify “fiber.” However, the clause is separated from what it is meant to modify not only by the sentence’s main verb but also by the noun phrase “company’s main laboratory,” and the clause is placed as if it were meant to modify “laboratory” rather than “fiber.” In order for the sentence to work, the modifier has to be moved. An effective version is:
A new type of fiber, which is expected to be used as a strengthening component in a variety of products, is being developed in the company’s main laboratory.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 9 Explanation:
While the relative clause “that is expected to include … field” is separated from the noun that it modifies, “conference,” by the sentence’s main verb, “is being planned,” the meaning of the sentence is clear.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 10 Explanation:
In this case, what makes sense is that the sentence is meant to convey that geothermal energy, rather than streets and sidewalks, is abundant and can be harnessed at low cost. However, the relative clause “which is abundant and can be harnessed at low cost” is separated from “geothermal energy” not only by the sentence’s main verb, but also by a string of modifiers, “in winter to heat streets and sidewalks.” So, the relative clause is not just remote; it’s misplaced. In order for the sentence to convey its meaning effectively, the relative clause has to be closer to what it modifies, as in:
In Iceland, geothermal energy, which is abundant and can be harnessed at low cost, is used during the winter to heat the streets and sidewalks of towns.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 11 Explanation:
While the relative clause “that seems likely to merge …” is separated from the noun that it modifies, “island,” by the sentence’s main verb, “is forming,” the meaning of the sentence is clear.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss the second way in which a relative clause may be separated from the noun that it modifies.
A relative clause may be separated from the noun that it modifies by the main verb of the sentence.
We have seen that a relative clause can be separated from the noun it modifies by another modifier.
In some cases, relative clauses that modify the subjects of sentences are rather long.
Even as we speak, solar panels that will generate power much more efficiently than any panels in use today are being developed.
We can see that the subject “solar panels” and the main verb “are being developed” are quite far apart because they are separated by the long relative clause “that will generate power much more efficiently than any panels in use today.” As a result, when we read that sentence, after we see the subject, we have to read for quite a while before arriving at the main verb.
To avoid separating a sentence’s subject and main verb that way, we can write the sentence differently, putting the main verb of the sentence directly after the subject and before the relative clause that modifies the subject.
Even as we speak, solar panels are being developed that will generate power much more efficiently than any panels in use today.
Now, the subject and main verb are not separated by the long relative clause.
Of course, to the untrained eye, the relative clause may seem to be misplaced because a verb separates the clause from the noun it modifies.
That said, such a structure won’t work in every sentence.
Of course, the main verb in the example, “are being developed,” is a verb that indicates that a development is occurring.
Let’s look at another example:
An expert is coming who will help the design team determine the best way to use cutting edge technology in some of our new products.
The subject is “expert.” The main verb is “is coming.” Notice the long relative clause “who will help the design team to determine the best way to use cutting edge technology in some of our new products.” Can you image how the sentence would read if that long relative clause were placed between the subject and main verb? Wouldn’t such a sentence be challenging to read? Take a look:
An expert who will help the design team determine the best way to use cutting edge technology in some of our new products is coming.
We wait a long time to get to the action of the sentence, right? Notice that the original version of the sentence avoids having such an issue by placing the subject close to the main verb.
So – what’s the takeaway?
Certain types of main verbs that indicate arrival, positioning, and coming into being can exist between a relative clause and the noun that it modifies in cases in which there is unlikely to be any confusion and the author would like to avoid putting the main verb after a long intervening relative clause.
💡MUST KNOW:
Certain types of main verbs that indicate arrival, positioning, and coming into being can exist between a relative clause and the noun that it modifies in cases in which there is unlikely to be any confusion and the author would like to avoid putting the main verb after a long intervening relative clause.

Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 7 Explanation:

Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 8 Explanation:
A reader may be able to figure out that the relative clause “which is expected … products” is meant to modify “fiber.” However, the clause is separated from what it is meant to modify not only by the sentence’s main verb but also by the noun phrase “company’s main laboratory,” and the clause is placed as if it were meant to modify “laboratory” rather than “fiber.” In order for the sentence to work, the modifier has to be moved. An effective version is:

Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 10 Explanation:
In this case, what makes sense is that the sentence is meant to convey that geothermal energy, rather than streets and sidewalks, is abundant and can be harnessed at low cost. However, the relative clause “which is abundant and can be harnessed at low cost” is separated from “geothermal energy” not only by the sentence’s main verb, but also by a string of modifiers, “in winter to heat streets and sidewalks.” So, the relative clause is not just remote; it’s misplaced. Correct Answer: (A) Let’s practice applying what we just learned in answering Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 20 A holistic method for solving cancer is being developed at the university’s wellness clinic, which has few negative side effects but, at this stage, has only been working for a fraction of the people who have used it. 1. [ ] (A) A holistic method for solving cancer is being developed at the university’s wellness clinic, which has few negative side effects but, at this stage, has only been working for > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Under development at the university’s wellness clinic is a holistic method for solving cancer that has few negative side effects, which, at this stage, are working for only > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) At the university’s wellness clinic, a holistic method for solving cancer is being developed that has few negative side effects but, at this stage, has been working for only 4. [ ] (D) While in development at the university’s wellness clinic, a holistic method for solving cancer at this stage has few negative side effects, which has been working for only > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Being developed at the university’s wellness clinic, a holistic method for solving cancer that has few negative side effects but that, at this stage, only works for > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy The answer choices in this question include the relative clauses “which has few negative side effects,” “that has few negative side effects,” and “which is/are working ….” Arriving at the correct answer requires noticing whether the relative clauses clearly modify things that they would logically modify.
(A) A holistic method for solving cancer is being developed at the university’s wellness clinic, which has few negative side effects but, at this stage, has only been working for a fraction of the people who have used it.
In this version, because the relative clause “which has few negative side effects” is placed after “wellness clinic,” the clause seems to illogically modify “wellness clinic.” Also, to confirm that this choice is not the correct answer, we can notice the placement of “only” before “been.” The point to be made is not that the method has only been working. The point to be made is that it has been working for only a small fraction of the people who use it.
(B) Under development at the university’s wellness clinic is a holistic method for solving cancer that has few negative side effects, which, at this stage, are working for only a fraction of the people who have used it.
This version works up to the first comma. After the first comma, the modifier “which, at this stage, are working …” seems to illogically modify “side effects.” Since side effects would not be “working for a fraction of the people,” that modification does not make sense.
YOUR CHOICE(C) At the university’s wellness clinic, a holistic method for solving cancer is being developed that has few negative side effects but, at this stage, has been working for only a fraction of the people who have used it.
While the relative clause “that has few negative side effects” is separated from what it modifies, “method for solving cancer,” by the main verb of the sentence, the GMAT would find this sentence acceptable, since what the modifier is meant to modify is clear, and since placing the main verb after the modifier would result in the main verb’s being placed far from the subject.
Also, the modifier itself is well structured and, thus, clearly conveys that the method has few negative side effects but has been working for only a small fraction of the people who have used it.
(D) While in development at the university’s wellness clinic, a holistic method for solving cancer at this stage has few negative side effects, which has been working for only a fraction of the people who have used it.
The beginning of this version conveys the somewhat illogical meaning that the method has few negative side effects while in development. So, when it is not in development it has many negative side effects?
Also, what “at this stage” is meant to modify is not clear. Is “at this stage” meant to indicate that the method is at a stage, or to modify “cancer?” If “at this stage” is meant to modify “cancer,” a further issue that exists is that the sentence does not mention any particular stage of cancer. So, what “this stage” would refer to is not clear.
Also, the relative clause “which has been working …” has no clear target. It would make sense for this clause to modify “method,” but “method” is far removed from this clause.
(E) Being developed at the university’s wellness clinic, a holistic method for solving cancer that has few negative side effects but that, at this stage, only works for a fraction of the people who have used it.
This version would make sense if it had a main verb. However, it does not have a main verb and, therefore, is not a complete sentence.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 21 The experience of chronic stress generally results in elevation of levels of stress hormones, which, in turn, results in reduced immune function and increased weight gain. 1. [x] (A) elevation of levels of stress hormones, which, in turn, results 2. [ ] (B) levels of stress hormones that are elevated that, in turn, results > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) elevating levels of stress hormones and, in turn, result > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) levels of stress hormones that elevate and that, in turn, result > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) an elevated level of stress hormones, which, in turn, result > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard Correctly answering this question requires noticing meaning issues and issues involving subject-verb agreement.
It also requires noticing that, in the correct answer, the modifier “which … results” refers not to the noun that directly precedes it, “levels,” but rather to the noun phrase “elevation of levels of stress hormones.”
YOUR CHOICE(A) The experience of chronic stress generally results in elevation of levels of stress hormones, which, in turn, results in reduced immune function and increased weight gain.
The relative clause “which, in turn, results …” clearly and logically modifies the entire preceding noun phrase, “elevation of levels of stress hormones.”
Notice also that the singular “elevation” agrees with the singular verb “results” in the modifier.
Thus, this version is grammatically correct and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(B) The experience of chronic stress generally results in levels of stress hormones that are elevated that, in turn, results in reduced immune function and increased weight gain.
The relative clause “that, in turn, results …” has to modify one of the preceding nouns, “levels” and “stress hormones.” Since neither noun is singular, the singular “results” in the modifier cannot agree with any noun that the clause could modify. So, there is a subject-verb agreement issue.
(C) The experience of chronic stress generally results in elevating levels of stress hormones and, in turn, result in reduced immune function and increased weight gain.
The expression “elevating levels” is nonsensical. The levels would be elevated; levels would not themselves elevate.
Also, since the core of the sentence is “The experience results … and result …,” there is a subject-verb agreement issue, as the singular “experience” does not agree with the plural “result.”
Also, the experience, which is just one thing, would not result and, in turn, result. There has to be a second thing that would result “in turn.”
(D) The experience of chronic stress generally results in levels of stress hormones that elevate and that, in turn, result in reduced immune function and increased weight gain.
This version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the experience of chronic stress results in “levels of stress hormones that elevate.” In other words, this version does not convey that the experience of chronic stress causes levels of stress hormones to elevate. Rather it conveys that the experience of chronic stress results in levels of stress hormones – not high or low levels but levels in general, as if no levels of stress hormones would exist without stress – and that the levels then elevate themselves, as if levels can elevate themselves.
(E) The experience of chronic stress generally results in an elevated level of stress hormones, which, in turn, result in reduced immune function and increased weight gain.
This version expresses the illogical idea that stress hormones result in reduced immune function and increased weight gain.
That idea is illogical because tangible things, such as stress hormones, cannot “result in” something. Only a process or event, such as elevation of stress hormones, can result in something.
So, stress hormones may cause reduced immune function and increased weight gain, but stress hormones would not result in those effects.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss the proper use of the relative pronouns “that,” “who,” “whom,” and “which.”
summary:
Lesson Content In most cases in which we see a relative clause in a correct answer to a Sentence Correction question, that clause will directly follow the noun that the clause modifies.
💡MUST KNOW:
There are structures that are considered acceptable or correct in which a relative clause is separated from the noun that it modifies. In such a structure, the relative clause is called a “remote relative clause.”
There are two ways in which a relative clause may be separated from the noun that it modifies in a correct answer.
title: 5.7.3 When Relative Clauses Can Modify Their Antecedents “Remotely”

contents:
Lesson Content As we discussed in a previous section, the relative pronoun “that” can be correctly used to introduce a restrictive relative clause.
In formal writing, the relative pronoun “that” should refer only to objects or ideas, never people.
For example:
The woman who lives next door to me is a pianist.
A woman is a “who.”
Incorrect: The man that plays the violin also has a very good voice.
A man cannot be a “that.”
The person with whom I was speaking is the writer who wrote and produced the movie that we watched last weekend.
“Who” and “whom” are used to refer to people.
The idea that most excited him turned out to have the worst consequences
Since an “idea” is a thing, it is referred to by “that.”
Incorrect: The person that was speaking is very smart.
A person cannot be a “that.”
Archery, which is far more fun than I had expected, has failed to maintain its popularity.
“Which” refers to things.
Incorrect: The girl with which I was speaking was quite funny.
“Which” cannot describe people.
The girl with whom I was speaking was quite funny.
💡MUST KNOW:
The relative pronoun “that” should be used to refer only to objects or ideas, not people. Similarly, the relative pronoun “which” cannot be used to refer to people. The relative pronouns “who” and “whom” are used to refer to human antecedents.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether the underlined word(s) should be “that” or “who.” --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now discuss “who” and “whom”.
summary:
Lesson Content As we discussed in a previous section, the relative pronoun “that” can be correctly used to introduce a restrictive relative clause.
In formal writing, the relative pronoun “that” should refer only to objects or ideas, never people.
For example:
The woman who lives next door to me is a pianist.
A woman is a “who.”
Incorrect: The man that plays the violin also has a very good voice.
title: 5.7.4 “That,” “Who,” “Whom,” and “Which”

contents:
Lesson Content We should also be clear about the correct usage of “who” and “whom.”
As discussed in the Pronouns chapter, the pronouns “who” and “whom” should not be used interchangeably, even though both refer to people, not objects.
A subject pronoun performs an action, whereas an object pronoun is acted upon.
💡MUST KNOW:
The subject pronoun “who” performs the action in a clause, whereas the object pronoun “whom” is acted upon.
For example:
People who love other people are the happiest people in the world.
“Who” refers to people, and in this case, people are doing the action, loving, and are therefore the subject of the clause.
Arnold’s sister, whom we all have met, graduated a year early.
Here, Arnold’s sister is not performing the action mentioned in the relative clause “whom we all have met.” She is the direct object of the verb “have met.”
💡MUST KNOW:
The relative pronouns “who” and “whom” refer to human antecedents. In a relative clause, “who” serves as a subject; “whom” is used as an object.
Consider the following example:
I didn’t see the person with (who/whom) you were talking.
The person taking the action described in the modifier “with (who/whom) you were talking” is “you.” That is, “you” did the talking.
I didn’t see the person with whom you were talking.
Let’s look at another example:
He found the attendant (who/whom) has my car keys.
In this case, “attendant” is the object of “found.” However, “who/whom” is not the object of “found.” If we isolate the relative clause that modifies “attendant,” we get “(who/whom) has my car keys.” It becomes clear that we need the subject pronoun “who,” because, within the relative clause, having the keys is the “action” and “who/whom” represents the subject taking that action.
He found the attendant who has my car keys.
A strategy that can help with determining whether we need “who” or “whom” is to replace “who” or “whom” with another subject or object pronoun that is easier to use.
he has my car keys
We wouldn’t say:
Incorrect: him has my car keys
Since “he” is a subject pronoun, we need the subject pronoun “who.”
It’s helpful to be able to recognize some common subject pronouns and object pronouns.
Subject Pronouns: I, you, he, she, it, we, they, who
Object Pronouns: me, you, him, her, it, us, them, whom
💡MUST KNOW:
Commit the following two lists to memory.
Another thing to understand about these relative pronouns is that, when people make up unified groups such as armies, teams, companies, and committees, the relative pronoun “that” or “which” is generally used to refer to these groups.
For example, consider the noun “team.” Although a team of people is, obviously, composed of people, generally we would not say “a team who ….” Instead, we’d say “a team that ….”
The reason is that an entity made up of people such as a team, group, or class, is considered a thing when the context treats such an entity as a single unit.
💡MUST KNOW:
Entities made up of people such as teams, groups, or classes, are considered things when the context treats such an entity as a single unit and, in such cases, are referred to via the use of the relative pronouns “that” or “which.”
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice For each of the following sentences, choose the correct words from the underlined sets. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Consider the following Sentence Correction example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 22 Althea Gibson, who won the French Open in 1956, became the first African-American, winning a Grand Slam tennis title. 1. [ ] (A) Gibson, who won the French Open in 1956, became the first African-American, winning > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Gibson that won the French Open in 1956 to become the first African-American winner of > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Gibson, who won the French Open in 1956, becoming the first African-American to have won > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Gibson won the French Open in 1956, becoming the first African-American whom won > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Gibson, winning the French Open in 1956, became the first African-American to win View Solution Question Level: medium Correctly answering this question requires both noticing whether relative pronouns are correctly used and carefully considering the meanings conveyed by the versions.
(A) Althea Gibson, who won the French Open in 1956, became the first African-American, winning a Grand Slam tennis title.
While this version correctly uses the subject pronoun “who” as the subject of the clause “who won the French Open in 1956,” it conveys an illogical meaning: that Althea Gibson became the first African-American, and that in the process of doing so, she won a Grand Slam tennis title.
(B) Althea Gibson that won the French Open in 1956 to become the first African-American winner of a Grand Slam tennis title.
In this version, “that” is incorrectly used to refer to a person, “Althea Gibson.”
Also, this version is composed of only a noun, “Althea Gibson,” and a long modifier. Thus, this version has no main verb.
(C) Althea Gibson, who won the French Open in 1956, becoming the first African-American to have won a Grand Slam tennis title.
While this version correctly uses the subject pronoun “who” as the subject of the clause “who won the French Open in 1956,” this version is composed of only a noun, “Althea Gibson,” and a long modifier “who won ….” Thus, this version has no main verb.
(D) Althea Gibson won the French Open in 1956, becoming the first African-American whom won a Grand Slam tennis title.
In this version, the object pronoun “whom” incorrectly serves as the subject of the clause, “whom won a Grand Slam tennis title.”
YOUR CHOICE(E) Althea Gibson, winning the French Open in 1956, became the first African-American to win a Grand Slam tennis title.
This version clearly and correctly conveys that, in winning the French Open in 1956, Althea Gibson became the first African-American to win a Grand Slam tennis title.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss the relative pronoun “whose.”
summary:
Lesson Content We should also be clear about the correct usage of “who” and “whom.”
As discussed in the Pronouns chapter, the pronouns “who” and “whom” should not be used interchangeably, even though both refer to people, not objects.
A subject pronoun performs an action, whereas an object pronoun is acted upon.
💡MUST KNOW:
The subject pronoun “who” performs the action in a clause, whereas the object pronoun “whom” is acted upon.
title: 5.7.5 “Who” and “Whom”

contents:
Lesson Content The relative pronoun “whose” can refer to things or people.
The car whose fender I dented belongs to the police chief.
My mother, whose bracelet I borrowed, has always been willing to share.
The dog whose paws are big also has a loving heart.
💡MUST KNOW:
The relative pronoun “whose” can refer to things or people. “Whose” is the English language’s only possessive relative pronoun.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Choose the correct words from the underlined pairs. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Consider the following Sentence Correction example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 23 The contestant with the total number of points that are the highest wins the Icarus Cup, a competition in which each person competing flies on his or her own power. 1. [ ] (A) with the total number of points that are the highest > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) whose total number of points is highest 3. [ ] (C) the total number of points of which is highest > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) that has the highest total number of points > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) having a total number of points being the highest > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy In this question, all of the answer choices modify “contestant.” The sentence indicates that people compete in the Icarus Cup. Thus, we can presume that “the contestant” is a human. Therefore, some answer choices can be eliminated on the basis of the fact that the relative pronouns “which” and “that” cannot be correctly used to refer to a human contestant.” The other answer choices can be evaluated on the basis of the degree to which the meanings that they convey are logical.
(A) The contestant with the total number of points that are the highest wins the Icarus Cup, a competition in which each person competing flies on his or her own power.
While this version may sound correct, a careful reading reveals that it is illogical, as it conveys that the winner is selected on the basis of how high the points themselves are rather than on the basis of how high the total number of points is. Points would not be high. The number of points would be.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The contestant whose total number of points is highest wins the Icarus Cup, a competition in which each person competing flies on his or her own power.
This version correctly uses the possessive relative pronoun “whose” to refer to “contestant” and conveys a logical meaning.
(C) The contestant the total number of points of which is highest wins the Icarus Cup, a competition in which each person competing flies on his or her own power.
This version is a bit awkward, but it might be OK if it did not incorrectly use “which” to refer to the noun “contestant,” which represents a human.
(D) The contestant that has the highest total number of points wins the Icarus Cup, a competition in which each person competing flies on his or her own power.
This version incorrectly uses “that” to refer to the noun “contestant,” which represents a human.
(E) The contestant having a total number of points being the highest wins the Icarus Cup, a competition in which each person competing flies on his or her own power.
The use of the word “being” in this version results in the sentence’s conveying a somewhat illogical meaning, that the points or, possibly, the total number of points is actively “being the highest.” In other words, the version conveys that the contestant with the points that are “being the highest” wins.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now move on to our next major topic: appositives.
