Lesson Content The relative pronoun “whose” can refer to things or people.
The car whose fender I dented belongs to the police chief.
My mother, whose bracelet I borrowed, has always been willing to share.
The dog whose paws are big also has a loving heart.
💡MUST KNOW:
The relative pronoun “whose” can refer to things or people. “Whose” is the English language’s only possessive relative pronoun.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
title: 5.7.6 Whose

contents:
Lesson Content An appositive is an element of a sentence that modifies or further describes another element of a sentence by renaming it.
On the GMAT, appositives generally appear in the forms of nouns, pronouns, noun phrases, or noun clauses that are used to modify other nouns, pronouns, noun phrases, or noun clauses.
💡MUST KNOW:
Appositives generally appear on the GMAT in the form of nouns, pronouns, noun phrases, or noun clauses that modify other nouns, pronouns, noun phrases, or noun clauses.
Here are some examples.
Nisha’s house, a bungalow, has solar panels on the roof.
The noun phrase “a bungalow” is an appositive modifying “house.”
My sister Lucia is working to preserve the rainforest.
The noun “sister” is renamed by the proper noun “Lucia.” So, “Lucia” is an appositive that further identifies “sister.”
An amazing example of Renaissance art, the statue dominated the exhibit.
The noun phrase “an amazing example of Renaissance art” is an appositive that modifies “statue.”
The ship that Robert designed, the Shenandoah, was built in 1964.
“The Shenandoah” is an appositive that modifies the noun phrase “the ship that Robert designed.”
King’s conclusion, that nonviolent methods would be effective, proved to be correct.
The noun clause “that nonviolent methods would be effective” is an appositive that modifies “King’s conclusion.”
During the meeting, Laura, the most knowledgeable person in the room, presented several exceptional points in favor of ratifying the new construction proposal.
“The most knowledgeable person in the room” is an appositive phrase that further describes “Laura.” The appositive tells us that Laura was the most knowledgeable person in the room.
Avocados, nutritious fruits packed with beneficial fats, are good complements for grapefruit or fresh peaches.
The noun phrase “nutritious fruits packed with beneficial fats” further describes the noun “avocados.” The appositive tells us that avocados are nutritious fruits packed with beneficial fats.
Sometimes students have difficulty determining whether a noun modifier meets the definition of an appositive.
💡MUST KNOW:
If, when we replace a noun in a sentence with its modifier, the sentence retains its core meaning, the noun modifier is an appositive.
Let’s put this strategy to use in the following example:
My niece Kristin is having a birthday party next Saturday.
First, we must identify the noun being modified in the sentence.
Kristin is having a birthday party next Saturday.
Now, ask yourself whether the sentence retains its meaning.
Appositives are often more than one word.
Next Saturday, Kristin is having a party, an event bound to be fit for a queen, in celebration of her birthday.
The technique for identifying the noun modifier as an appositive is the same, despite the modifier’s length.
Next Saturday, Kristin is having an event bound to be fit for a queen in celebration of her birthday.
Therefore, the noun modifier is indeed an appositive.
💡MUST KNOW:
Replace the noun being modified with its modifier to identify an appositive.
Let’s now discuss the placement of appositives.
summary:
Lesson Content An appositive is an element of a sentence that modifies or further describes another element of a sentence by renaming it.
On the GMAT, appositives generally appear in the forms of nouns, pronouns, noun phrases, or noun clauses that are used to modify other nouns, pronouns, noun phrases, or noun clauses.
💡MUST KNOW:
Appositives generally appear on the GMAT in the form of nouns, pronouns, noun phrases, or noun clauses that modify other nouns, pronouns, noun phrases, or noun clauses.
title: 5.8 Appositives

contents:
Lesson Content Appositives usually, though not always, appear beside what they modify.
Incorrect: My brother, running through the park, Eduardo saw a rare California condor.
The separation of “Eduardo” from what it modifies, “my brother,” results in an awkward, illogical sentence.
Running through the park, my brother Eduardo saw a rare California condor.
Let’s look at another example:
Incorrect: A brown dog, jumping up and down, Brownie was excited to see its owners.
The separation of the “brown dog” and what it modifies, “Brownie,” results in an awkward, illogical sentence.
Jumping up and down, Brownie, a brown dog, was excited to see its owners.
Notice that, now, the appositive “a brown dog” clearly modifies “Brownie.”
💡MUST KNOW:
An appositive will usually, but not always, appear directly beside what it modifies.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Consider the following Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 24 In 1901, the first working version of the radio was built by Marconi, an invention originally developed by Tesla. 1. [ ] (A) In 1901, the first working version of the radio was built by Marconi, an invention originally developed by Tesla. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) The first working version of the radio, being an invention originally developed by Tesla, was built by Marconi in 1901. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Marconi built the first working version of the radio in 1901, which was an invention originally developed by Tesla. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) In 1901, Marconi built the first working version of the radio, an invention originally developed by Tesla. 5. [ ] (E) In 1901, the first working version of the radio, built by Marconi, had been an invention originally developed by Tesla. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy Correctly answering this question takes noticing that only one version makes sense. The placement of modifiers in and the wording of other versions results in sentences that convey illogical meanings.
(A) In 1901, the first working version of the radio was built by Marconi, an invention originally developed by Tesla. The placement of the appositive “an invention originally developed by Tesla” directly after “Marconi” results in the sentence’s seeming to convey the illogical meaning that Marconi was an invention developed by Tesla.
(B) The first working version of the radio, being an invention originally developed by Tesla, was built by Marconi in 1901. This version illogically conveys that the first working version of the radio was being an invention developed by Tesla. The radio is an invention developed by Tesla, but not the first working version of the radio, and, also, saying that either the “first working version of the radio” or simply “the radio” was “being an invention” does not really make sense.
(C) Marconi built the first working version of the radio in 1901, which was an invention originally developed by Tesla. Notice that “radio in 1901” is not a noun phrase that could be modified by “which was an invention …” since “in 1901” does not modify “radio” but, rather, modifies “built,” expressing when Marconi built. Thus, “which was an invention …” is illogically separated from what it’s meant to modify, “radio,” by “in 1901.”
As a result, “which was an invention …” seems to modify “1901,” rather than “the radio.”
So, this version seems to convey the nonsensical meaning that 1901 was an invention developed by Tesla.
YOUR CHOICE(D) In 1901, Marconi built the first working version of the radio, an invention originally developed by Tesla. The appositive “an invention originally developed by Tesla” is placed so as to modify “the radio,” resulting in a sentence that effectively conveys a logical meaning.
(E) In 1901, the first working version of the radio, built by Marconi, had been an invention originally developed by Tesla. This version conveys a somewhat illogical meaning: that, in 1901 the first working version of the radio built by Marconi had been an invention developed by Tesla.
For one thing, it would make much more sense to say that the radio was developed by Tesla and that the first working version was built by Marconi.
Also, even if we were to decide that it makes sense to say that the first working version was an invention developed by Tesla, saying that the radio had been an invention developed by Tesla in 1901 does not make sense, since it does not make sense to restrict the time when it was an invention developed by Tesla to 1901. Once it had been developed by Tesla, it was an invention developed by Tesla from then on, not just in 1901.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 25 Alan Blumlein is best known for his stereophonic recording research, a method that encodes the relative positions of the sound sources recorded. 1. [ ] (A) Alan Blumlein is best known for his stereophonic recording research, > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Alan Blumlein, best known for his research into stereophonic recording, which is > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Alan Blumlein is best known for his research into stereophonic recording, 4. [ ] (D) Alan Blumlein’s research into stereophonic recording is how he is best known, > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Alan Blumlein, best known for his research into stereophonic recording, is > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium Correctly answering this question takes finding a version in which the combination of the non-underlined portion with an answer choice results in a logical sentence.
(A) Alan Blumlein is best known for his stereophonic recording research, a method that encodes the relative positions of the sound sources recorded.
In this version, the non-underlined portion is used as an appositive. This appositive, “a method …” is placed directly after and, therefore, seems to modify “research.” Since “research” is not a method that encodes the relative positions of sounds, this version does not make sense.
(B) Alan Blumlein, best known for his research into stereophonic recording, which is a method that encodes the relative positions of the sound sources recorded.
This version is composed of only a noun, “Alan Blumlein,” and a long modifier, “best known ….” Thus, this version has no main verb and, thus, is not a complete sentence.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Alan Blumlein is best known for his research into stereophonic recording, a method that encodes the relative positions of the sound sources recorded.
In this version, the non-underlined portion is used as an appositive. This appositive, “a method …” is placed directly after and, therefore, seems to modify “stereophonic recording.” Since, stereophonic recording is a type of recording, even if we are not sure what exactly stereophonic recording is, it seems reasonable to describe stereophonic recording as a method that does something in particular.
(D) Alan Blumlein’s research into stereophonic recording is how he is best known, a method that encodes the relative positions of the sound sources recorded.
In this version, the non-underlined portion is used as an appositive. This appositive, “a method …,” is placed directly after and, therefore, seems to modify “how he is best known.” Since “how he is best known” is not a method that encodes the relative positions of sounds, this version does not make sense.
(E) Alan Blumlein, best known for his research into stereophonic recording, is a method that encodes the relative positions of the sound sources recorded.
This version conveys the nonsensical meaning that Alan Blumlein is a method.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss abstract appositives.
summary:
Lesson Content Appositives usually, though not always, appear beside what they modify.
Incorrect: My brother, running through the park, Eduardo saw a rare California condor.
The separation of “Eduardo” from what it modifies, “my brother,” results in an awkward, illogical sentence.
Running through the park, my brother Eduardo saw a rare California condor.
Let’s look at another example:
Incorrect: A brown dog, jumping up and down, Brownie was excited to see its owners.
title: 5.8.1 Appositives Almost Always Appear Beside What They Modify.

contents:
Lesson Content So far, the appositives that we have discussed have renamed and modified specific words or phrases.
Consider the following example:
Earth’s population of humans is expected to increase substantially over the coming decades, a development that could have significant negative effects on quality of life on the planet.
The phrase “a development that could have significant negative effects on quality of life” is an abstract appositive.
Let’s consider another example:
After the judge announced her decision, the governor spent much of the rest of the day ranting on social media, a spectacle that shocked not only his supporters but also his detractors.
Like the phrase in the previous example, the phrase “a spectacle that shocked not only his supporters but also his detractors” does not rename and modify any specific word or phrase that appears before it.
💡MUST KNOW:
GMAT Sentence Correction questions often contain structures called “abstract appositives,” which, rather than rename and modify specific words or phrases, rename and modify entire ideas presented in clauses.
Let’s now discuss a way in which we may see abstract appositives used in Sentence Correction questions.
summary:
Lesson Content So far, the appositives that we have discussed have renamed and modified specific words or phrases.
Consider the following example:
Earth’s population of humans is expected to increase substantially over the coming decades, a development that could have significant negative effects on quality of life on the planet.
The phrase “a development that could have significant negative effects on quality of life” is an abstract appositive.
Let’s consider another example:
title: 5.8.2 Abstract Appositives

contents:
Lesson Content Choosing the correct answer to a Sentence Correction question generally involves choosing the choice that most effectively conveys a meaning.
Consider the following pair of examples:
In 1957, Chien-Shiung Wu was passed over by those in charge of awarding of the Nobel Prize in Physics, which many people found outrageous.
In 1957, Chien-Shiung Wu was passed over by those in charge of awarding of the Nobel Prize in Physics, a decision that many people found outrageous.
Notice the difference between the effectiveness of the first version, which employs the relative clause “which many people found outrageous,” and the effectiveness of the second version, which employs the abstract appositive “a decision that many people found outrageous.”
In the first, what the relative clause “which many people found outrageous” is meant to modify is not entirely clear.
The second version, on the other hand, uses the abstract appositive “a decision that many people found outrageous” to rename and modify the entire idea presented in the preceding clause and, thus, to clearly convey that it was the decision to pass over Chien-Shiung Wu that many people found outrageous.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Sentence Correction questions, abstract appositives may appear in sentence versions that are more effective than versions created via the use of other structures.
Of course, not every sentence that includes an abstract appositive is effective.
Targeted Practice From each of the following pairs of sentences, choose the one that is more effective. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now apply what we have learned about abstract appositives to answering some Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 26 The legislators voted to repeal the unpopular law, a decision cheered by many. 1. [x] (A) law, 2. [ ] (B) law that was > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) law and were > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) law, which was > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) law, being > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy All of the versions in this question are basically grammatically correct. So, correctly answering this question takes noticing which version employs modifiers in a way such that the version effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The legislators voted to repeal the unpopular law, a decision cheered by many.
The abstract appositive “a decision cheered by many” renames and logically modifies what is presented in the preceding clause, the legislators’ voting to repeal the law. Since a law would not be a decision cheered by many, what the closing appositive modifies is clear.
(B) The legislators voted to repeal the unpopular law that was a decision cheered by many.
The relative clause “that was a decision cheered by many” seems to modify “unpopular law.” Thus, this version conveys the illogical meaning that the unpopular law itself was a decision cheered by many.
(C) The legislators voted to repeal the unpopular law and were a decision cheered by many.
By considering this choice along with the non-underlined portion of the sentence, we see that this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the legislators were a decision cheered by many.
(D) The legislators voted to repeal the unpopular law, which was a decision cheered by many.
The pronoun “which” could refer to the entire preceding clause, but “which” could also refer to “law.” So, the meaning of this version is somewhat ambiguous. Also, in GMAT Sentence Correction, using a relative pronoun to refer to a clause is not preferred. So, the combination of ambiguity and a pronoun’s possibly referring to a clause makes this choice clearly inferior to choice (A), which uses an abstract appositive to concisely convey a clear meaning.
(E) The legislators voted to repeal the unpopular law, being a decision cheered by many.
The use of the closing participial phrase “being a decision cheered by many” results in the sentence’s conveying the nonsensical meaning that, as the legislators voted to repeal the law, the legislators were being a decision.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 27 The results of experiments performed by Sir Frederick William Herschel indicated that there was a form of light not visible to the human eye, which was a development that led to the discovery of infrared. 1. [ ] (A) the human eye, which was a development > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) human eyes, which were developments > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) the human eye, and these were developments > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) human eyes, a development 5. [ ] (E) the human eye and that it was a development > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy Most versions of the sentence attempt to use pronouns to refer to either parts of the main clause or the entire main clause, and none of these versions effectively convey logical meanings. Only the version that uses an abstract appositive to modify the entire main clause effectively conveys a logical meaning.
(A) The results of experiments performed by Sir Frederick William Herschel indicated that there was a form of light not visible to the human eye, which was a development that led to the discovery of infrared.
The relative pronoun “which” could refer to the entire preceding clause, a pronoun-referent relationship not preferred by the GMAT. Alternatively, “which” could refer to “a form of light” or to “the human eye.” So, the meaning of this version is somewhat ambiguous.
So, the combination of ambiguity and a pronoun’s possibly referring to a clause makes this choice clearly inferior to choice (D), which uses an abstract appositive to concisely convey a clear meaning.
(B) The results of experiments performed by Sir Frederick William Herschel indicated that there was a form of light not visible to human eyes, which were developments that led to the discovery of infrared.
The modifier “which were developments …” seems to modify “human eyes.” Since it does not make sense that “human eyes” would be the developments leading to the discovery of infrared, this version does not make sense.
(C) The results of experiments performed by Sir Frederick William Herschel indicated that there was a form of light not visible to the human eye, and these were developments that led to the discovery of infrared.
Whether “these” refers to “results” or “experiments” is not clear.
Further, regardless of which of those nouns “these” refers to, the sentence does not make sense, as it does not make sense that the “results” or the “experiments” would be the developments leading to the discovery of infrared.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The results of experiments performed by Sir Frederick William Herschel indicated that there was a form of light not visible to human eyes, a development that led to the discovery of infrared.
In this version, the abstract appositive “a development leading to the discovery of infrared” modifies the entire preceding clause, and the resulting sentence clearly conveys that the results’ indicating that there was a form of light not visible to human eyes was a development leading to the discovery of infrared.
Notice, this correct answer uses “human eyes” rather than the more conventional “the human eye.” While using “the human eye” would probably be better than using “human eyes,” the use of “human eyes” is acceptable and serves the purpose of obscuring the fact that this choice is the correct answer, partly because there seems to be an agreement issue, since “human eyes” is plural, while “a development” is singular.
(E) The results of experiments performed by Sir Frederick William Herschel indicated that there was a form of light not visible to the human eye and that it was a development that led to the discovery of infrared.
The pronoun “it” could refer to “form of light” or to “the human eye,” but neither would be a development leading to the discovery of infrared.
Correct Answer: (D) Now that we’ve covered appositives, let’s move on to our next concepts: participles and participial phrases.
summary:
Lesson Content Choosing the correct answer to a Sentence Correction question generally involves choosing the choice that most effectively conveys a meaning.
Consider the following pair of examples:
In 1957, Chien-Shiung Wu was passed over by those in charge of awarding of the Nobel Prize in Physics, which many people found outrageous.
In 1957, Chien-Shiung Wu was passed over by those in charge of awarding of the Nobel Prize in Physics, a decision that many people found outrageous.
title: 5.8.3 How Abstract Appositives May Be Used in Sentence Correction Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Sentences containing participles or participial phrases appear quite often in Sentence Correction questions.
Let’s begin by discussing present participles and past participles.
Present Participles and Past Participles
A participle is a verb form that functions as a modifier.
Present Participle Examples: “jumping,” “running,” “singing,” and “setting”
Past Participle Examples: “ruined,” “laminated,” “spoken,” and “swollen”
💡MUST KNOW:
A participle is a verb form that functions as a modifier. Participles come in two varieties: present participles, which always end in -ing, and past participles, which usually end in –ed or -en.
Let’s discuss participles functioning as adjectives.
Participles Functioning as Adjectives
One way a participle can be used in a sentence is as an adjective – simply modifying a noun.
In the following examples, present participles are modifying nouns:
“jumping crickets,” “singing men,” “setting sun,” “running dog”
In the following examples, past participles are modifying nouns:
“ruined castle,” “laminated copy,” “spoken word,” “swollen foot”
As in the above examples, generally, participles are associated with or modify nouns.
💡MUST KNOW:
The noun associated with a participle can be either (1) the agent doing the action of the participle or (2) the receiver of the effect of the participle.
The agent of a participle is similar to the subject of a sentence.
A receiver is similar to an object in a sentence, in that the receiver is the thing being affected.
Each of the following examples includes two participles, one followed by an agent and the other by a receiver.
Within minutes, the swimming sailors had righted the capsized boat.
The first participle, “swimming,” is followed by its agent, “sailors.” The second participle, “capsized,” is followed by its receiver, “boat.”
The completed work of art was admired by the smiling patrons.
The first participle, “completed,” is followed by its receiver, “work of art.” The second participle, “smiling,” is followed by its agent, “patrons.”
Let’s now discuss the fact that a participle can never on its own be the main verb in a clause.

Let’s begin by discussing present participles and past participles.
Present Participles and Past Participles
A participle is a verb form that functions as a modifier.
Present Participle Examples: “jumping,” “running,” “singing,” and “setting”
Past Participle Examples: “ruined,” “laminated,” “spoken,” and “swollen”
💡MUST KNOW:
A participle is a verb form that functions as a modifier. Participles come in two varieties: present participles, which always end in -ing, and past participles, which usually end in –ed or -en.
title: 5.9 Participles and Participial Phrases

contents:
Lesson Content A participle can never on its own function as the main verb in a clause.
💡MUST KNOW:
A participle can never on its own function as the main verb (finite verb) in a clause. So, a sentence in which the only verb form is a participle will not be complete.
Because participles look like verbs, sometimes a participle can be perceived to be the main verb in a sentence when, in fact, no main verb (finite verb) actually exists.
Incorrect: The man, swimming in a sea of uncertainty without any good options.
Upon careful inspection, we see that the above construction is missing a finite verb; the construction is not a sentence.
The man, swimming in a sea of uncertainty without any good options, cried.
Now that we have added a finite verb, the sentence is complete.
💡MUST KNOW:
In considering a sentence version, identify the subject and finite verb to ensure that the sentence is complete.
Let’s look at another example sentence:
The members of the team, disheartened by the loss, hung their heads.
We have two verb forms: “disheartened” and “hung.” However, only one is functioning as a finite verb, while the other is acting as a modifier.
In this case, the subject of the sentence is “members” and the action performed is “hung.” The other verb form in the sentence, “disheartened,” is a past participle that describes the members.
Incorrect: The members of the team, disheartened by the loss.
Of course, one way to correct these types of sentences is to insert a helping verb between the noun and the participle.
The members of the team were disheartened by the loss.
The man was swimming in a sea of uncertainty without any good options.
Another fix is to change a participle to a past tense or present tense verb.
Incorrect: The man, swimming in a sea of uncertainty without any good options.
Correct: The man swam in a sea of uncertainty without any good options.
Correct: The man swims in a sea of uncertainty without any good options.
By changing the participle to a past tense verb or a present tense verb, we can correct the sentence fragment.
💡MUST KNOW:
A sentence can be rendered complete through the insertion of a helping verb before a participle or through changing a participle to a present or past tense verb.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now continue to the next topic.

💡MUST KNOW:
A participle can never on its own function as the main verb (finite verb) in a clause. So, a sentence in which the only verb form is a participle will not be complete.
Because participles look like verbs, sometimes a participle can be perceived to be the main verb in a sentence when, in fact, no main verb (finite verb) actually exists.
title: 5.9.1 A Participle on its Own Can Never Be the Main Verb in a Clause

contents:
Lesson Content In Sentence Correction questions, participles often appear in participial phrases, which are phrases based on, and usually beginning with, participles.
Here are some examples of, present participial phrases (phrases based on present participles):
spinning incessantly
jumping in the grass
successfully navigating the channel
The following phrases are past participial phrases (phrases based on past participles):
ruined by vandals
lost in the fog
quickly forgotten by many
Let’s now discuss how participial phrases are used in sentences.
summary:
Lesson Content In Sentence Correction questions, participles often appear in participial phrases, which are phrases based on, and usually beginning with, participles.
Here are some examples of, present participial phrases (phrases based on present participles):
spinning incessantly
jumping in the grass
successfully navigating the channel
The following phrases are past participial phrases (phrases based on past participles):
ruined by vandals
lost in the fog
quickly forgotten by many
Let’s now discuss how participial phrases are used in sentences.
title: 5.9.2 Participial Phrases

contents:
Lesson Content For the purposes of learning what we need to know in order to correctly answer GMAT Sentence Correction questions, we can put most present participial phrases, which are phrases based on present participles, or “–ing” verbs, into three general categories:
(1) Present participial phrases used to restrictively modify specific nouns.
(2) Present participial phrases that appear at the beginning of or in the middle of clauses and modify multiple aspects of the clauses, are nonrestrictive, and can be treated as nonrestrictive noun modifiers for the purpose of placement.
(3) Present participial phrases that appear at the end of clauses and modify entire clauses, in which cases the subjects of the clauses being modified are usually, but not always, the agents of the participles.
💡MUST KNOW:
We can put most present participial phrases into three general categories:
GMAT Sentence Correction question writers often create flawed sentences by using present participial phrases in illogical ways.
Let’s discuss present participial phrases used to restrictively modify specific nouns.
summary:
Lesson Content For the purposes of learning what we need to know in order to correctly answer GMAT Sentence Correction questions, we can put most present participial phrases, which are phrases based on present participles, or “–ing” verbs, into three general categories:
(1) Present participial phrases used to restrictively modify specific nouns.
(2) Present participial phrases that appear at the beginning of or in the middle of clauses and modify multiple aspects of the clauses, are nonrestrictive, and can be treated as nonrestrictive noun modifiers for the purpose of placement.
title: 5.9.3 How Present Participial Phrases are Used in Sentences

contents:
Lesson Content A present participial phrase can be used to restrictively modify a specific noun.
For example:
The cat licking its paws is considering taking a nap.
Notice that the present participial phrase “licking its paws” serves to restrictively modify the noun “cat,” making clear to us that the sentence is not speaking of all cats; it’s speaking of a specific cat – the one licking its paws.
When a present participial phrase is used to restrictively modify a specific noun, the present participial phrase is placed adjacent to – usually after – the noun that it modifies, without a comma between the phrase and the noun:
The woman sprinting in the soft sand has strong legs.
Of course, this sentence is not about all women; it’s about the one woman who is sprinting in the soft sand.
Let’s consider another sentence:
The fish swimming in the stream made the water ripple.
The present participial phrase “swimming in the stream” modifies the noun “fish.” We can tell that the phrase is meant to modify the noun that immediately precedes it by noticing that there is no comma between “swimming in the stream” and the noun that it modifies, “fish.” The noun “fish” is restricted by the modifier “swimming in the stream.” The modifier defines which fish the sentence is about.
Let’s examine one more:
The performers were given detailed instructions by the woman directing the production.
The present participial phrase “directing the production” modifies the noun “woman.” We can tell that the phrase is meant to modify the noun that immediately precedes it by noticing that there is no comma between “directing the production” and the noun that precedes it, “woman.” The participial phrase “directing the production” indicates that the woman mentioned in the sentence is a specific woman, the woman directing the production.
💡MUST KNOW:
We can tell that a present participial phrase modifies the noun that immediately precedes it by noticing that there is no comma between the phrase and the immediately preceding noun.
Now, incorrect sentences often contain present participial phrases that are misplaced and, thus, convey illogical meanings.
The doctor carefully examined the patient’s knee wearing a long white coat.
The fact that the present participial phrase “wearing a long white coat” is not separated by a comma from the noun that precedes it, “knee,” indicates that the phrase modifies “knee.” Thus, the sentence conveys the illogical meaning that the patient’s knee was wearing a long white coat.
The doctor wearing a long white coat carefully examined the patient’s knee.
We see that the present participial phrase “wearing a long white coat” is now correctly modifying the noun “doctor.”
Let’s examine another sentence:
The engineers found cracks in the airplane’s wings inspecting the fleet.
The fact that the present participial phrase “inspecting the fleet” is not separated by a comma from the noun that precedes it, “wings,” indicates that the phrase modifies “wings.” Thus, the sentence conveys the illogical meaning that the airplane’s wings were inspecting the fleet.
A version that conveys a logical meaning is:
The engineers inspecting the fleet found cracks in the airplane’s wings.
We see that the present participial phrase “inspecting the fleet” is now correctly modifying the noun “engineers.”
Let’s do some Targeted Practice to lock in this concept.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each sentence is incorrect or correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 The flowers growing in the valley are violets. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 The student discovered a new species of frog doing research for his thesis. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 3 Archeologists found some previously undiscovered Mayan ruins using the latest equipment. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 4 Their eyes wide with wonder, the children watched the shooting stars streaking through the night sky. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 Over the years, people have solved a variety of complex math problems competing in the International Mathematical Olympiad. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 6 If none of the people running for office have good management skills, then regardless of who wins, the country’s problems will not be solved. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 7 A golden eagle was spotted by one of the mountain climbers circling in the sky as they made their way to the summit. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 8 The heavy rain filled the streams and rivers falling through the night, and, by morning, they had begun to overflow their banks. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 9 The people waiting for the train were entertained by the teenagers skateboarding in the park. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
The present participial phrase “growing in the valley” modifies “flowers” and is restrictive, because it indicates which flowers are being discussed. The sentence is not about the flowers growing on the hillside or those growing by the lake. It’s about the flowers growing in the valley.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
The fact that the participial phrase “doing research for his thesis” is not separated by a comma from the noun, “frog,” that precedes it indicates that the phrase modifies the immediately preceding noun. Thus, the sentence conveys a nonsensical meaning, that the frog was doing research for his thesis. A more logical version is:
The student doing research for his thesis discovered a new species of frog.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
The fact that the participial phrase “using the latest equipment” is not separated by a comma from the noun that precedes it, “ruins,” indicates that the phrase modifies “ruins.” Thus, the sentence conveys the illogical meaning that “ruins” were using the latest equipment. A version that conveys a logical meaning is:
Archeologists using the latest equipment found some previously undiscovered Mayan ruins.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
Using the participial phrase “streaking through the sky” to modify “shooting stars” makes sense.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
The fact that the participial phrase “competing in the International Mathematical Olympiad” is not separated by a comma from the noun that precedes it, “problems,” indicates that the phrase modifies “problems.” Thus, the sentence conveys the illogical meaning that math problems were competing in the International Mathematical Olympiad. A version that conveys a logical meaning is:
Over the years, people competing in the International Mathematical Olympiad have solved a variety of complex math problems.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
Using the participial phrase “running for office” to modify “people” makes sense.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 7 Explanation:
The fact that the participial phrase “circling in the sky” is not separated by a comma from the noun phrase that precedes it, “mountain climbers,” indicates that the phrase modifies “mountain climbers.” Thus, the sentence conveys the illogical meaning that mountain climbers were circling in the sky as they made their way to the summit. In order for the sentence to make sense, “circling in the sky” has to be placed directly after what it’s meant to modify, “golden eagle,” as in:
A golden eagle circling in the sky was spotted by one of the mountain climbers as they made their way to the summit.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 8 Explanation:
The fact that the participial phrase “falling through the night” is not separated by a comma from the nouns that precede it, “streams and rivers,” indicates that the phrase modifies “streams and rivers.” Thus, the sentence conveys the illogical meaning that streams and rivers were falling through the night. In order for the sentence to make sense, “falling through the night” has to be placed directly after what it’s meant to modify, “rain,” as in:
The heavy rain falling through the night filled the streams and rivers, and, by morning, they had begun to overflow their banks.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 9 Explanation:
The participial phrase “waiting for the train” modifies “people” and specifies which people were entertained. Further along in the sentence, the participial phrase “skateboarding in the park” modifies “teenagers” and specifies which teenagers entertained the people waiting for the train.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Now, let’s consider some Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 28 Arriving at Ellis Island during the early twentieth century, the percentage of the immigrants denied entry into the United States was small. 1. [ ] (A) Arriving at Ellis Island during the early twentieth century, the percentage of immigrants denied entry into the United States was small. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) During the early twentieth century, only a small percentage of the immigrants were denied entry into the United States arriving at Ellis Island. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) During the early twentieth century arriving at Ellis Island, only a small percentage of the immigrants were denied entry into the United States. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Arriving at Ellis Island, the United States denied entry to only a small percentage of the immigrants during the early twentieth century. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) During the early twentieth century, only a small percentage of the immigrants arriving at Ellis Island were denied entry into the United States. View Solution Question Level: easy The key to correctly answering this question is noticing the effects of the placement of the participial phrase “arriving at Ellis Island.” In all versions but one, the phrase is misplaced.
(A) Arriving at Ellis Island during the early twentieth century, the percentage of immigrants denied entry into the United States was small. In this version, “arriving at Ellis Island” may seem to logically modify “immigrants.” However, this version is not exactly about immigrants. It’s about a percentage, “the percentage of immigrants denied entry.” So, what the phrase “arriving at Ellis Island” is really modifying is “the percentage of immigrants denied entry.” So, this version conveys the illogical meaning that “the percentage” was arriving at Ellis Island.
(B) During the early twentieth century, only a small percentage of the immigrants were denied entry into the United States arriving at Ellis Island. The phrase “arriving at Ellis Island” is placed directly after “the United States” and is not separated from “the United States” by a comma. So, we can tell that the phrase modifies “the United States.” Thus, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the United States was arriving at Ellis Island.
(C) During the early twentieth century arriving at Ellis Island, only a small percentage of the immigrants were denied entry into the United States. The phrase “arriving at Ellis Island” is placed directly after “early twentieth century” and is not separated from “century” by a comma. So, we can tell that the phrase modifies “early twentieth century.” Thus, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the twentieth century was arriving at Ellis Island.
(D) Arriving at Ellis Island, the United States denied entry to only a small percentage of the immigrants during the early twentieth century. In this version, the opening modifier “arriving at Ellis Island” is placed so as to modify “the United States.” Thus, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the United States was arriving at Ellis Island.
YOUR CHOICE(E) During the early twentieth century, only a small percentage of the immigrants arriving at Ellis Island were denied entry into the United States. In this version, the phrase “arriving at Ellis Island” is placed directly after “immigrants” and is not separated from “immigrants” by a comma. Thus, the phrase must modify “immigrants,” and therefore this version clearly indicates that it is describing the experience of immigrants arriving at Ellis Island.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 29 Over time, treasure hunters have discovered gold and other precious metals that is located deep below the ocean’s surface using underwater sonar techniques. 1. [ ] (A) have discovered gold and other precious metals that is located deep below the ocean’s surface using underwater sonar techniques > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) have discovered located deep below the surface of the ocean gold and other precious metals using underwater sonar techniques > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) using underwater sonar techniques and discovering gold and other precious metals located deep below the ocean’s surface > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) using underwater sonar techniques have discovered in locations deep beneath the surface of the ocean gold and other precious metals 5. [ ] (E) using underwater sonar techniques to discover gold and other precious metal located deep below the ocean’s surface > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy Correctly answering this question requires noticing how the placement of participial phrases affects the meanings conveyed by the versions of the sentence. Remember that, when there is no comma separating a noun from a present participial phrase that follows the noun, that participial phrase directly modifies that noun.
(A) Over time, treasure hunters have discovered gold and other precious metals that is located deep below the ocean’s surface using underwater sonar techniques .
The singular verb “is” in the relative clause “that is located deep below the ocean’s surface” does not agree in number with “gold and other precious metals,” which is the plural noun that the relative clause modifies. To be correct, the relative clause would need the plural verb “are” as in “gold and other precious metals that are located.”
Furthermore, there is no comma separating “ocean’s surface” and “using underwater sonar techniques.” So, the present participial phrase “using underwater sonar techniques” modifies “ocean’s surface.” As a result, this version conveys the illogical meaning that the ocean’s surface was using underwater sonar techniques.
(B) Over time, treasure hunters have discovered located deep below the surface of the ocean gold and other precious metals using underwater sonar techniques .
There is no comma separating “gold and other precious metals” and “using underwater sonar techniques.” So, the present participial phrase “using underwater sonar techniques” modifies “gold and other precious metals.” As a result, this version conveys the illogical meaning that the gold and other precious metals were using underwater sonar techniques.
(C) Over time, treasure hunters using underwater sonar techniques and discovering gold and other precious metals located deep below the ocean’s surface .
This version does not contain a main verb and, thus, is not a sentence.
The participles “using” and “discovering” are part of a present participial phrase restrictively modifying the noun “treasure hunters.” In other words, “using underwater sonar techniques and discovering gold and other precious metals located deep below the ocean’s surface” is one long restrictive modifier telling us which treasure hunters this version is about. The main verb of a sentence will never be found within a phrase modifying a noun.
If you have trouble seeing that this version is not a sentence, add in an action:
Over time, treasure hunters using underwater sonar techniques and discovering gold and other precious metals located deep below the ocean’s surface ate pizza.
Notice that we now have a sentence, the subject of which is “treasure hunters” and the main verb of which is “ate.”
YOUR CHOICE(D) Over time, treasure hunters using underwater sonar techniques have discovered in locations deep beneath the surface of the ocean gold and other precious metals .
Because there is no comma separating “treasure hunters” and “using underwater sonar techniques,” the participial phrase “using underwater sonar techniques” correctly modifies “treasure hunters.”
The sentence is grammatically correct and effectively conveys a logical meaning.
(E) Over time, treasure hunters using underwater sonar techniques to discover gold and other precious metal located deep below the ocean’s surface .
This version does not contain a main verb and, thus, is not a sentence.
The participle “using” is part of a present participial phrase restrictively modifying the noun “treasure hunters.” In other words, “using underwater sonar techniques to discover gold and other precious metal located deep below the ocean’s surface” is one long restrictive modifier telling us which treasure hunters this version is about. The main verb of a sentence will never be found within a phrase modifying a noun.
If you have trouble seeing that this version is not a sentence, add in an action:
Over time, treasure hunters using underwater sonar techniques to discover gold and other precious metal located deep below the ocean’s surface played ping pong.
Notice that we now have a sentence, the subject of which is “treasure hunters” and the main verb of which is “played.”
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 30 Rhinoceros horn purveyors prey on ignorant and sometimes desperate consumers selling them this virtually useless material without special powers for exorbitant prices. 1. [ ] (A) purveyors prey on ignorant and sometimes desperate consumers selling them > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) purveyors preying on ignorant and sometimes desperate consumers sell 3. [ ] (C) purveyors, preying on ignorant and sometimes desperate consumers to sell > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) purveyors prey on ignorant and sometimes desperate consumers selling > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) purveyors, preying on ignorant and sometimes desperate consumers selling > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard A present participial phrase not separated from the preceding noun by a comma modifies the preceding noun.
Choosing the correct answer to this question requires noticing the meanings that result from participial phrases’ modifying nouns that directly precede the phrases and determining which meaning makes sense.
(A) Rhinoceros horn purveyors prey on ignorant and sometimes desperate consumers selling them this virtually useless material without special powers for exorbitant prices.
Since “selling” follows “consumers” and is not separated from “consumers” by a comma, the present participial phrase “selling them this virtually useless material without special powers for exorbitant prices” modifies “consumers.”
Thus, this version conveys the illogical meaning that purveyors prey on consumers who are selling virtually useless material for exorbitant prices. Of course, purveyors, not consumers, would be selling the virtually useless material.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Rhinoceros horn purveyors preying on ignorant and sometimes desperate consumers sell this virtually useless material without special powers for exorbitant prices.
In this version, the participial phrase “preying on ignorant and sometimes desperate consumers” clearly modifies “purveyors.”
As a result, this version conveys the logical meaning that rhinoceros horn purveyors sell virtually useless material without special powers.
(C) Rhinoceros horn purveyors, preying on ignorant and sometimes desperate consumers to sell this virtually useless material without special powers for exorbitant prices.
This version includes only a noun phrase, “rhinoceros horn purveyors,” followed by a participial phrase, “preying ….”
Since the participle “preying” cannot on its own serve as a main verb, this version contains no main verb and is not a complete sentence.
(D) Rhinoceros horn purveyors prey on ignorant and sometimes desperate consumers selling this virtually useless material without special powers for exorbitant prices.
Since “selling” follows “consumers” and is not separated from “consumers” by a comma, the present participial phrase “selling this virtually useless material without special powers for exorbitant prices” modifies “consumers.”
Thus, this version conveys the illogical meaning that purveyors prey on consumers who are selling virtually useless material for exorbitant prices. Of course, purveyors, not consumers, would be selling the virtually useless material.
Notice, this version is almost identical to the version created via the use of (A). The difference between the two choices – “them” is included in (A) but not in (D) – constitutes an insignificant split meant to confuse people analyzing the question.
(E) Rhinoceros horn purveyors, preying on ignorant and sometimes desperate consumers selling this virtually useless material without special powers for exorbitant prices.
This version includes only a noun phrase, “rhinoceros horn purveyors,” followed by a participial phrase, “preying ….” Thus, since the participle “preying” cannot on its own serve as the main verb of a sentence, this version contains no main verb and is not a complete sentence.
Also, since “selling” follows “consumers” and is not separated from “consumers” by a comma, the present participial phrase “selling this virtually useless material without special powers for exorbitant prices” modifies “consumers.”
Thus, this version conveys the illogical meaning that the purveyors prey on consumers who are selling virtually useless material for exorbitant prices. Of course, purveyors, not consumers, would be selling the virtually useless material.
Notice, this choice makes choice (D), which is similar to this choice, seem correct by comparison. We have to be careful not to be fooled into choosing an illogical choice by the fact that a similar choice is more clearly incorrect.
Correct Answer: (B) Now, let’s move on to the second category of present participial phrases.
summary:
Lesson Content A present participial phrase can be used to restrictively modify a specific noun.
For example:
The cat licking its paws is considering taking a nap.
Notice that the present participial phrase “licking its paws” serves to restrictively modify the noun “cat,” making clear to us that the sentence is not speaking of all cats; it’s speaking of a specific cat – the one licking its paws.
When a present participial phrase is used to restrictively modify a specific noun, the present participial phrase is placed adjacent to – usually after – the noun that it modifies, without a comma between the phrase and the noun:
title: 5.9.4 Present Participial Phrases that Restrictively Modify Specific Nouns

contents:
Lesson Content A present participial phrase that appears at the beginning or in the middle of a clause and is set off from the rest of the clause by a comma or commas can be treated as a nonrestrictive noun modifier.
Sailing with the wind, the clipper ship quickly reached its destination.
The present participial phrase “sailing with the wind” modifies the noun “clipper ship.” It also explains how the clipper ship reached its destination.
All the same, for the purpose of evaluating their placement, we can treat phrases like this one as noun modifiers.
The same phrase could be placed in the middle of the clause instead.
The clipper ship, sailing with the wind, quickly reached its destination.
Notice that, in both of the above examples, the participial phrase “sailing with the wind” is set off from the rest of the sentence by a comma or commas.
The clipper ship, sailing with the wind, quickly reached its destination.
Let’s revisit the “fish swimming in the stream” example, adding commas around the participial phrase this time.
The fish, swimming in the stream, made the water ripple.
Unlike in the earlier version of this example, in which there were no commas around the phrase “swimming in the stream,” in this version, the phrase is set off by commas.
Now, rather than tell us which fish the sentence is about, the phrase tells us what the fish were doing as they made the water ripple.
The same phrase would work also at the beginning of the sentence.
Swimming in the stream, the fish made the water ripple.
Let’s consider another example of this type of use of a present participial phrase.
Hissing loudly, two cats faced off in the darkness.
Two cats, hissing loudly, faced off in the darkness.
We can tell that the participial phrase, “hissing loudly” is nonrestrictive, because commas separate it from the rest of the sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
For the purpose of evaluating placement, a present participial phrase that appears at the beginning or in the middle of a clause and is set off from the rest of the clause by a comma or commas can be treated as a nonrestrictive noun modifier.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice From each of the following pairs, select the sentence that conveys what is outlined in the scenario. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Misplaced Modifiers Involving Non-Restrictive Present Participial Phrases A present participial phrase that appears at the beginning or in the middle of a clause and is set off from the rest of the clause by a comma or commas will often be misplaced and, thus, modify the wrong noun.
Let’s look at an example:
Incorrect: Crying loudly for the entire overnight flight, the other passengers were extremely annoyed by the infant, cute as she was in her pink pajamas.
Notice that the present participial phrase “crying loudly for the entire overnight flight” is misplaced and illogically modifies the noun “the other passengers.” As a result, this sentence conveys the illogical meaning that it was the “other passengers” who were crying loudly.
In order for the sentence to convey a logical meaning, “the infant” must appear at the beginning of the clause, right after the introductory phrase “crying loudly …,” as in:
Crying loudly for the entire overnight flight, the infant, cute as she was in her pink pajamas, was extremely annoying to the other passengers.
Let’s consider another example:
Incorrect: Wafting through the air, some bears were attracted by the aroma of the cooking food.
We can see that the present participial phrase “wafting through the air” illogically modifies the noun “some bears.” Of course, “some bears” were not wafting through the air.
Wafting through the air, the aroma of the cooking food attracted some bears.
💡MUST KNOW:
Be on the lookout for an introductory phrase that begins with a present participle; we may have a misplaced modifier question on our hands. The noun that follows an introductory phrase that begins with a present participle must be the noun that that phrase is meant to modify.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some concept mastery.
Consider the following Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 31 Practicing daily, the aspiring singer’s skills were able to be improved and, thus, won a spot in the major singing competition. 1. [ ] (A) Practicing daily, the aspiring singer’s skills were able to be improved and, thus, won > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Practicing daily, the aspiring singer, being able to improve his skills, thus winning > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Through daily practice, the aspiring singer’s skills were able to be improved and, thus, won > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) The aspiring singer, practicing daily, was able to improve his skills and, thus, won 5. [ ] (E) The aspiring singer was able, by improving his skills practicing daily, to thus win > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy Arriving at the correct answer to this question takes noticing whether present participial phrases are placed so as to modify nouns that they would logically modify.
(A) Practicing daily, the aspiring singer’s skills were able to be improved and, thus, won a spot in the major singing competition.
Since the opening present participial phrase “practicing daily” is followed by “the aspiring singer’s skills,” “practicing daily” illogically modifies “skills.” Of course, the singer’s skills would not have been “practicing daily.”
Also, “were able to” means “had the knowledge, skill, or ability to achieve something.” So, in saying, “the aspiring singer’s skills were able to be improved,” this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the singer’s skills had the knowledge, skill, or ability to achieve being improved. Of course, the singer’s skills would not have these attributes. The singer himself would.
Finally, notice that the sentence says, “the singer’s skills were able … and, thus, won a spot,” conveying the nonsensical meaning that the singer’s skills, rather than the singer himself, won a spot in the competition.
(B) Practicing daily, the aspiring singer, being able to improve his skills, thus winning a spot in the major singing competition.
Since the participles “practicing,” “being,” and “winning” cannot serve as main verbs of a sentence, this version has no main verb and is not a complete sentence.
(C) Through daily practice, the aspiring singer’s skills were able to be improved and, thus, won a spot in the major singing competition.
“Were able to” means “had the knowledge, skill, or ability to achieve something.” So, in saying, “the aspiring singer’s skills were able to be improved,” this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the singer’s skills had the knowledge, skill, or ability to achieve being improved. Of course, the singer’s skills would not have these attributes. The singer himself would.
Also, notice that the sentence says, “the singer’s skills were able … and, thus, won a spot,” conveying the nonsensical meaning that the singer’s skills, rather than the singer himself, won a spot in the competition.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The aspiring singer, practicing daily, was able to improve his skills and, thus, won a spot in the major singing competition.
In this version, the present participial phrase “practicing daily” is placed just after “aspiring singer” and clearly modifies “aspiring singer.” Of course, this placement make sense since an aspiring singer would practice daily.
This version is grammatically correct and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(E) The aspiring singer was able, by improving his skills practicing daily, to thus win a spot in the major singing competition.
In this version, the present participial phrase “practicing daily” is placed just after “skills” and is not separated from “skills” by a comma. Therefore, the phrase seems to modify “skills.” Of course, skills would not practice daily.
Also, “by” and “thus” have similar functions in a sentence. So, using “thus” with “by” is redundant and does not make sense. The sentence should use one or the other as in:
The aspiring singer was able, by improving his skills practicing daily, to thus win a spot in the major singing competition.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 32 Vegan athletes excel in sports from bodybuilding to sprinting that avoid experiencing the health issues attributable to consuming meat and animal products. 1. [ ] (A) Vegan athletes excel in sports from bodybuilding to sprinting that avoid experiencing the health issues attributable to consuming meat and animal products. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) In sports from bodybuilding to sprinting, vegan athletes excel while avoiding experiencing the health issues attributable to consuming meat and animal products. 3. [ ] (C) While avoiding experiencing the health issues attributable to consuming meat and animal products, a vegan diet allows athletes to excel in sports from bodybuilding to sprinting. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Bodybuilding and sprinting, a vegan diet allows athletes to excel in sports while avoiding experiencing the health issues attributable to consuming meat and animal products. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Avoiding experiencing the health issues attributable to consuming meat and animal products, the diets of vegan athletes allow them to excel in sports from bodybuilding to sprinting. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium There are many misplaced modifiers in the answer choices in this question. So, correctly answering it takes finding a choice in which modifiers are placed in ways such that the resulting sentence conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(A) Vegan athletes excel in sports from bodybuilding to sprinting that avoid experiencing the health issues attributable to consuming meat and animal products. The placement of the modifier “that avoid experiencing …” causes it to illogically modify “sports from bodybuilding to sprinting.” The resulting sentence conveys the nonsensical meaning that sports from bodybuilding to sprinting avoid experiencing health issues.
YOUR CHOICE(B) In sports from bodybuilding to sprinting, vegan athletes excel while avoiding experiencing the health issues attributable to consuming meat and animal products. The opening prepositional phrase, “in sports from bodybuilding to sprinting,” is logically followed by “vegan athletes excel,” and the remainder of the sentence makes sense.
(C) While avoiding experiencing the health issues attributable to consuming meat and animal products, a vegan diet allows athletes to excel in sports from bodybuilding to sprinting. The opening phrase, “While avoiding experiencing health issues …,” illogically modifies “a vegan diet allows.” A vegan diet would not avoid experiencing health issues.
(D) Bodybuilding and sprinting, a vegan diet allows athletes to excel in sports while avoiding experiencing the health issues attributable to consuming meat and animal products. Since the opening participial modifier, “bodybuilding and sprinting,” must modify the subject of the clause that follows the modifier, “a vegan diet,” this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that a vegan diet is bodybuilding and sprinting.
(E) Avoiding experiencing the health issues attributable to consuming meat and animal products, the diets of vegan athletes allow them to excel in sports from bodybuilding to sprinting. Since the opening participial modifier, “avoiding experiencing the health issues …,” must modify the subject of the clause that follows the modifier “the diets of vegan athletes,” this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that vegan diets are avoiding experiencing health issues.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 33 Having demonstrated that people can change gene expression by meditating, it is the head researcher’s opinion that the study will likely have a significant impact on the field of epigenetics. 1. [ ] (A) Having demonstrated that people can change gene expression by meditating, it is the head researcher’s opinion that the study will likely have a significant impact on the field of epigenetics. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) The head researcher’s opinion is that, since the study has demonstrated that people can change gene expression by meditating, its having a significant impact on the field of epigenetics is likely. 3. [ ] (C) Having demonstrated that people can change gene expression by meditating, there is a likelihood that the study will have a significant impact on the field of epigenetics in the head researcher’s opinion. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) The head researcher’s opinion, having demonstrated that people can change gene expression by meditating, is that it is likely that the study will have a significant impact on the field of epigenetics. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) The study, having demonstrated that people can change gene expression by meditating, has a likelihood that will have a significant impact on the field of epigenetics in the opinion of the head researcher. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard Correctly answering this question requires paying careful attention to introductory participial phrases and what those phrases seem to be modifying in order to eliminate choices in which the phrases are so placed as to modify things that they do not logically modify. At the same time, since not all incorrect choices include misplaced participial phrases, we have to be careful not to become so focused on participial phrase placement that we miss other issues.
(A) Having demonstrated that people can change gene expression by meditating, it is the head researcher’s opinion that the study will likely have a significant impact on the field of epigenetics. The sentence begins with the present participial phrase “Having demonstrated that people can change gene expression by meditating.” The noun or pronoun that follows this opening modifier must be a noun that is capable of “having demonstrated something.” We see that the pronoun “it” directly follows the opening modifier, but the pronoun “it” is not a noun or pronoun that is capable of “having demonstrated something.” Therefore, we have a misplaced introductory modifier, and this sentence is incorrect; the participial phrase is misplaced.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) The head researcher’s opinion is that, since the study has demonstrated that people can change gene expression by meditating, its having a significant impact on the field of epigenetics is likely. This choice is a bit tricky because, unlike many of the other choices, it uses a dependent clause, since the study has demonstrated …,” rather than a participial phrase. So, if we are focused on modifier placement issues, we might mistakenly perceive this choice as another one similar to (A), in which an introductory modifier illogically modifies “it.” However, if we read the sentence carefully, we see that it is well constructed and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
Notice that the last clause in this version is written to “sound funny,” and therefore seem incorrect, as a result of the last clause’s having as its subject the gerund phrase “its having a significant impact on the field of epigenetics.”
(C) Having demonstrated that people can change gene expression by meditating, there is a likelihood that the study will have a significant impact on the field of epigenetics in the head researcher’s opinion. The participial phrase “having demonstrated …” is meant to modify the clause that follows it. However, the clause uses a delayed subject, and so, what appears right after the participial is the expletive “there.” Since “there” certainly would not have demonstrated something, it does not make sense for the “having demonstrated …” to modify “there.” So, it’s clear that the opening participial phrase is misplaced.
Also, “having demonstrated …, there is a likelihood that the study will have a significant impact” is illogical in another way as well. The sentence does not say that it is likely that the study will have a significant impact. Rather it conveys that, since something demonstrated that people can affect gene expression by meditating, a likelihood that the study will have a significant impact exists. Expressing this idea is absurd since, under all conditions, a likelihood that the study will have a significant impact exists. That likelihood may be anything from 0 percent to 100 percent, but it will always exist. So, expressing that it will exist under certain conditions, “having demonstrated,” does not make sense.
Finally, because the prepositional phrase “in the head researcher’s opinion” appears directly after “the field of epigenetics,” the prepositional phrase seems to modify “the field of epigenetics.” As a result, this version suggests the nonsensical idea that the field of epigenetics is located in the head researcher’s opinion.
(D) The head researcher’s opinion, having demonstrated that people can change gene expression by meditating, is that it is likely that the study will have a significant impact on the field of epigenetics. Because “having demonstrated that people can change gene expression by meditating” directly follows “the head researcher’s opinion,” this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the head researcher’s opinion has demonstrated that people can change gene expression by meditating. The head researcher’s opinion might be that the study has demonstrated that people can change gene expression by meditating, but the opinion itself would have not demonstrated that.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The study, having demonstrated that people can change gene expression by meditating, has a likelihood that will have a significant impact on the field of epigenetics in the opinion of the head researcher. In this version, the participial phrase “having demonstrated …” is placed so as to logically modify “the study.” However, even though that modifier placement is logical, we have to be careful not to choose this version, because what this version expresses does not make sense.
This version does not say that the study is likely to have a significant impact. Instead, it conveys the nonsensical meaning that the study “has a likelihood that will have a significant impact.” Of course, “a likelihood” would not “have a significant impact on the field of epigenetics.”
Furthermore, because the prepositional phrase “in the opinion of the head researcher” appears directly after “the field of epigenetics,” the prepositional phrase seems to modify “the field of epigenetics.” As a result, this version suggests the nonsensical idea that the field of epigenetics is located in the opinion of the head researcher.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s move on to the third category of present participial phrases.
summary:
Lesson Content A present participial phrase that appears at the beginning or in the middle of a clause and is set off from the rest of the clause by a comma or commas can be treated as a nonrestrictive noun modifier.
Sailing with the wind, the clipper ship quickly reached its destination.
The present participial phrase “sailing with the wind” modifies the noun “clipper ship.” It also explains how the clipper ship reached its destination.
title: 5.9.5 Present Participial Phrases that Appear at the Beginning or in the Middle of Clauses, are Nonrestrictive, and Can be Treated as Noun Modifiers for the Purpose of Placement

contents:
Lesson Content When a present participial phrase comes at the end of a clause and is set off from that clause by a comma, the participial phrase does not have to modify the noun closest to it.
For example:
The horses ran across the field, kicking up dirt with their hooves.
Notice that the present participial phrase “kicking up dirt with their hooves” is separated from the clause by a comma.
Rather the phrase modifies the entire clause that precedes it, and the subject of the clause, “horses,” is the agent of the participle, “kicking,” that begins the phrase.
So, the sentence conveys that the horses ran and that, as the horses ran, they were kicking up dirt.
Let’s consider another example:
Bees fly from flower to flower, making a soft buzzing sound.
We can see that the present participial phrase “making a soft buzzing sound” is set off from the clause that precedes it by a comma.
Also, we can see that the subject of the clause, “bees,” is the agent of the participle “making.”
Thus, the sentence conveys that bees fly from flower to flower and that, as the bees fly from flower to flower, they make a soft buzzing sound.
💡MUST KNOW:
A present participial phrase can be used to modify an entire preceding clause, in which case the phrase is set off from the preceding clause by a comma and normally has as its agent the subject of the clause that it modifies.
In the above examples, the participle in the closing participial phrase has as its agent the subject of the preceding clause, and normally, closing present participial phrases work in that way.
One exception occurs in sentences that use “including.” Often, when a closing participial phrase begins with “including,” “including” does not take the subject of the preceding clause as its agent.
Ringo Starr sang some songs that are still widely played today, including “Yellow Submarine.”
The meaning expressed by this example is not that Ringo Starr included “Yellow Submarine” as he sang.
A second exception occurs when the closing participial phrase modifies an actor-action pair that appears between the subject and the closing participial phrase.
The video was of a man picking apples, singing as he worked.
In this case, we see that an actor, “a man,” engaging in an action, “picking apples,” appears between the subject of the sentence and the closing participial phrase “singing as he worked.”
Of course, the man picking apples, not the video, would have been the one “singing as he worked.”
So, we can tell that the closing participial phrase “singing as he worked” modifies the actor-action pair “a man” and “picking apples,” and that “a man,” rather than “the video,” is the agent of “singing.”
💡MUST KNOW:
Normally, the agent of the participle in a closing participial phrase is the subject of the preceding clause. However, there are some exceptions.
OK, now that we have covered the exceptions, let’s continue discussing what closing present participial phrases that modify entire clauses normally do.
Closing present participial phrases often describe:
• the results of the actions mentioned in the preceding clauses.
• the causes of the events described in the preceding clauses.
• events that are related to the events described in the preceding clause and were occurring while the events described in the preceding clause were occurring.
Here’s an example in which a closing present participial phrase describes the results of the actions mentioned in the preceding clause:
The strong winds blew apart structures and pushed ocean water into the town, turning the buildings on Main Street into piles of debris.
In the above example, the participial phrase “turning the buildings on Main Street into piles of debris” doesn’t modify “town.” It modifies the entire clause that precedes it, describing the results of the actions described by the clause.
