Here’s an example in which a closing present participial phrase describes the cause of the events described by the preceding clause.
The dolphins suddenly changed course, having sensed our presence in the water.
In this example, the participial phrase “having sensed our presence in the water” has as its agent the subject of the clause, “dolphins,” and explains why the dolphins changed course.
In the next example, a closing present participial phrase describes events that are related to the events described in the preceding clause and were occurring while the events described in the preceding clause were occurring.
The reporter ran across the field, carrying a camera in one hand and holding her phone to her ear with the other.
In this example, the present participial phrase “carrying a camera … and holding her phone to her ear …” has as its agent the subject of the clause, “the reporter,” and describes what the reporter was doing as she ran across the field.
💡MUST KNOW:
Present participial phrases that modify entire clauses often describe the results of the actions mentioned in the preceding clauses, the causes of the events described in the preceding clauses, or events that were occurring while the events described in the preceding clause were occurring.
A Present Participial Phrase Located at the End of a Clause Can be Misplaced. A small change to sentence structure, the removal of a comma, can render a present participial phrase at the end of a clause misplaced.
Consider the following sentence:
Incorrect: The horses ran across the field kicking up dirt with their hooves.
Notice that without the comma between “field” and “kicking,” the present participial phrase is now misplaced; it’s modifying the noun “field,” conveying an illogical meaning.
The horses ran across the field, kicking up dirt with their hooves.
Notice that, in the correct version, the present participial phrase is separated from the clause by a comma.
Let’s examine another sentence:
Incorrect: The strong winds blew apart structures and pushed ocean water into the town turning the buildings on Main Street into piles of debris.
Notice that without the comma between “town” and “turning,” the present participial phrase is now misplaced; it’s modifying the noun “town,” conveying an illogical meaning.
As we saw above, the following is correct:
The strong winds blew apart structures and pushed ocean water into the town, turning the buildings on Main Street into piles of debris.
Notice that, in the correct version, the present participial phrase is separated from the clause by a comma.
Let’s examine another sentence:
Incorrect: The dolphins suddenly changed course having sensed our presence in the water.
Notice that without the comma between “course” and “having,” the present participial phrase is now misplaced; it’s modifying the noun “course,” conveying an illogical meaning.
As we saw above, the following is correct:
The dolphins suddenly changed course, having sensed our presence in the water.
Notice that, in the correct version, the present participial phrase is separated from the clause by a comma.
💡MUST KNOW:
A small change to sentence structure, the removal of a comma, can render a present participial phrase at the end a clause misplaced. Be on the lookout for misplaced modifiers.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice From each of the following pairs, select the sentence that conveys what is outlined in the scenario. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 Scenario: The coach shouted as the coach directed the players. 1. [x] (A) The coach directed the players, shouting as they practiced the plays. 2. [ ] (B) The coach directed the players shouting as they practiced the plays. > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 Scenario: The campers chased the coyotes, and the coyotes were running while carrying camping supplies. 1. [x] (A) The campers chased the coyotes running as fast as they could while carrying camping supplies. 2. [ ] (B) The campers chased the coyotes, running as fast as they could while carrying camping supplies. > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 Scenario: An usher ruined the play by yelling. 1. [ ] (A) An usher started yelling at the rowdy people in the back row ruining the play for everyone else. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) An usher started yelling at the rowdy people in the back row, ruining the play for everyone else. ## Targeted Practice: 4 Scenario: The head of research argued with the executives, and the executives were claiming that data had been falsified. 1. [x] (A) The head of research argued with the executives claiming that the data had been falsified. 2. [ ] (B) The head of research argued with the executives, claiming that the data had been falsified. > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 Scenario: The photographer crept and sought to avoid detection. 1. [ ] (A) The photographer crept closer to the deer seeking to avoid detection by hiding among the bushes. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) The photographer crept closer to the deer, seeking to avoid detection by hiding among the bushes. Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
Both sentences include the present participial phrase “shouting as they practiced the plays.”
However, only in (a) is the phrase separated from the rest of the clause by a comma. That comma indicates that “shouting as they practiced the plays” does not modify “players” but, rather, modifies the entire clause and has the subject of the clause, “the coach,” as its agent. So, (a) conveys that the coach shouted.
In (b), there is no comma between “players” and “shouting as they practiced the plays.” Thus, in (b), the phrase modifies the noun that immediately precedes it, “players.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
The fact that, in (a), there is no comma between the phrase “running as fast as they could …” and “coyotes” indicates that the phrase modifies “coyotes.”
In (b), there is a comma between “coyotes” and “running …,” indicating that the phrase modifies not “coyotes” but rather the entire clause, and has “campers” as its agent. Thus, (b) conveys that the campers, not the coyotes were running while carrying camping supplies.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
In sentence (b), the comma before the closing present participial phrase “ruining the play for everyone else” indicates that the phrase does not modify “rowdy people in the back row” but, rather, modifies the entire clause and has “usher” as its agent. Thus, sentence (b) conveys that the usher ruined the play.
In sentence (a), there is no comma between “rowdy people in the back row” and “ruining.” Thus, “ruining” modifies “the rowdy people in the back row,” and sentence (a) conveys that the rowdy people in the back row were ruining the play.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
Since, in (a), there is no comma between “executives” and the present participial phrase “claiming that the data had been falsified,” the phrase modifies “executives.” Since, in (b), a comma precedes “claiming that the data had been falsified,” the phrase modifies the entire preceding clause, and the subject of the clause, “the head of research,” is the agent of “claiming.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
In (b), a comma precedes the present participial phrase “seeking to avoid detection ….” So, the phrase modifies the preceding clause, and the subject of the clause, “the photographer,” is the agent of “seeking.”
In (a), on the other hand, there is no comma between “deer” and the present participial phrase “seeking to avoid detection ….” So, in (a), the phrase modifies “deer.”
Correct Answer: (B) Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 34 According to the research, a particular meditation technique termed “open monitoring,” it strengthens a neural signal associated with recognition of errors potentially improving the ability of people to detect and respond to mistakes. 1. [ ] (A) monitoring,” it strengthens a neural signal associated with recognition of errors potentially improving the ability of people > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) monitoring” strengthens a neural signal associated with error recognition, which is potentially improvement in people’s ability > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) monitoring” strengthens a neural signal associated with error recognition, this has the potential to improve the ability of people > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) monitoring,” strengthening a neural signal associated with recognition of errors, potentially improving people’s ability > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) monitoring” strengthens a neural signal associated with error recognition, potentially improving people’s ability View Solution Question Level: easy (A) According to the research, a particular meditation technique termed “open monitoring,” it strengthens a neural signal associated with recognition of errors potentially improving the ability of people to detect and respond to mistakes.
Because there is no comma between the noun “errors” and the present participial phrase “potentially improving the ability of people,” the phrase appears to modify “errors.” Thus, this version seems to convey the illogical meaning that “errors” are “potentially improving the ability of people to detect and respond to mistakes.”
Of course, it does not make sense that the “errors” are “potentially improving the ability of people” to detect mistakes. A logical meaning would be that “open monitoring” improves people’s ability (by strengthening the neural signal). Thus, there should be a comma separating the closing participial phrase from the preceding clause, so that the phrase modifies the subject of the clause, “open monitoring,” not “errors.”
Additionally, the pronoun “it” is an unnecessary repetition of the subject of the sentence. In this context, “it” refers to the noun phrase “a particular meditation technique termed ‘open monitoring.’” Thus, this choice creates a sentence that repeats the subject twice in a row, saying essentially, “‘open monitoring,’ ‘open monitoring’ strengthens.”
(B) According to the research, a particular meditation technique termed “open monitoring” strengthens a neural signal associated with error recognition, which is potentially improvement in people’s ability to detect and respond to mistakes.
In this version, there is nothing to which the pronoun “which” can logically refer. After all, nothing that precedes “which” can logically be described as “improvement in people’s ability to detect and respond to mistakes.”
While “open monitoring” may cause improvement in people’s ability to detect and respond to mistakes by strengthening a neural signal, the fact that “open monitoring” strengthens a neural signal is not itself “improvement in people’s ability to detect and respond to mistakes.” Also, neither “a neural signal” nor “error recognition” can logically be described as “improvement in people’s ability.”
(C) According to the research, a particular meditation technique termed “open monitoring” strengthens a neural signal associated with error recognition, this has the potential to improve the ability of people to detect and respond to mistakes.
This choice incorrectly joins two independent clauses with a comma alone. “A particular meditation technique termed “open monitoring” strengthens a neural signal associated with error recognition” is an independent clause; it stands alone as a complete thought.
Likewise, “this has the potential to improve the ability of people to detect and respond to mistakes” is an independent clause.
We need either a semicolon or a comma and coordinating conjunction (such as “and”) to join two independent clauses.
Additionally, what the pronoun “this” refers to is not clear, but “this” seems to illogically refer to the preceding clause. As a result, the sentence seems to communicate the illogical meaning that the fact that “opening monitoring” strengthens a neural signal has the potential to improve the ability of people to detect and respond to mistakes. A fact would not improve people’s ability to do something.
(D) According to the research, a particular meditation technique termed “open monitoring,” strengthening a neural signal associated with recognition of errors, potentially improving people’s ability to detect and respond to mistakes.
This choice creates an incomplete sentence. Notice that neither “strengthening” nor “improving” is a finite verb, and thus neither can act as the main verb of the sentence. Likewise, “to detect and respond” are infinitive verbs (the second “to” before “respond” is implied), and thus they cannot be main verbs.
Without a main verb in the sentence, what we have is nothing more than a very long description of the meditation technique, not a complete sentence. YOUR CHOICE(E) According to the research, a particular meditation technique termed “open monitoring” strengthens a neural signal associated with error recognition, potentially improving people’s ability to detect and respond to mistakes.
In this choice, the present participial phrase “potentially improving people’s ability” is separated from the preceding clause by a comma. Thus, the participial phrase modifies “open monitoring,” which logically is the thing that “strengthens a neural signal” and is therefore “potentially improving people’s ability.”
The sentence created makes sense and contains no grammatical errors.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 35 The stylish car does not have a windscreen, but a recessed instrument panel acts as an aerodynamic windbreaker for the driver, sitting in the cockpit. 1. [ ] (A) does not have a windscreen, but a recessed instrument panel acts as an aerodynamic windbreaker for the driver, > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) has not a windscreen but a recessed instrument panel acting as an aerodynamic windbreaker for the driver while > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) has not a windscreen but a recessed instrument panel that acts as an aerodynamic windbreaker for the driver 4. [ ] (D) does not have a windscreen, but a recessed instrument panel acts as an aerodynamic windbreaker with the driver > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) does not have a windscreen, but a recessed instrument panel acts as if there is an aerodynamic windbreaker as the driver is > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard In this question, the correct version is structured in an unfamiliar way, so the correct version sounds incorrect. So, correctly answering this question requires carefully considering how participial phrases are used in the choices and discerning the meanings conveyed by the various versions. Without careful consideration of meaning, one might be tempted to choose a choice the use of which results in a version that sounds basically correct but does not convey a truly logical meaning.
(A) The stylish car does not have a windscreen, but a recessed instrument panel acts as an aerodynamic windbreaker for the driver, sitting in the cockpit.
In this version, the comma before the closing participial phrase “sitting in the cockpit” indicates that the phrase modifies the entire preceding clause and that the phrase has the subject of the clause, “recessed instrument panel,” as its agent. Thus, this version conveys the illogical meaning that the instrument panel sits in the cockpit.
(B) The stylish car has not a windscreen but a recessed instrument panel acting as an aerodynamic windbreaker for the driver while sitting in the cockpit.
In this version, the phrase “while sitting in the cockpit” is not a noun modifier, and it does not modify “driver.” Rather, the phrase works adverbially to modify “acting,” indicating that the instrument panel is acting while sitting in the cockpit. Thus, this version suggests the illogical idea that the instrument panel is sitting in the cockpit.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The stylish car has not a windscreen but a recessed instrument panel that acts as an aerodynamic windbreaker for the driver sitting in the cockpit.
In this version, the fact that there is no comma between “driver” and “sitting in the cockpit” indicates that “sitting in the cockpit” modifies “driver.” So, while this version may sound a little strange, it clearly and concisely conveys a logical meaning.
Also, this version is better than any of the others because of the following.
Unlike the versions created via the use of (A), (D), and (E), this version clearly conveys that the car has the instrument panel. The versions created via the use of (A), (D), and (E) convey that “a recessed instrument panel acts” but do not clearly indicate that the instrument panel is part of the car.
Also, “that acts …” in this version conveys that the instrument panel acts as a windbreaker for the driver more effectively than does “acting as …” in (B). The phrase in (B) seems to convey that the instrument panel is always acting as a windbreaker, even when the car is not moving, while the phrase in (A) simply conveys that the instrument panel acts as a windbreaker, presumably when the car is moving.
(D) The stylish car does not have a windscreen, but a recessed instrument panel acts as an aerodynamic windbreaker with the driver sitting in the cockpit.
This choice could be tempting, as the version created via the use of this choice starts off well. However, the wording “with the driver sitting in the cockpit” conveys a meaning that does not make sense, that the instrument panel is somehow with the driver sitting in the cockpit.
(E) The stylish car does not have a windscreen, but a recessed instrument panel acts as if there is an aerodynamic windbreaker as the driver is sitting in the cockpit.
This choice could be tempting, as the version created via the use of this choice starts off well. However, the wording “acts as if there is an aerodynamic windbreaker” conveys the nonsensical meaning that the instrument panel is actively doing something along the lines of pretending that there is a windbreaker.
Also, the wording “as the driver is sitting in the cockpit” conveys an ambiguous meaning, which could be that the instrument panel acts as a windbreaker while the driver is in the act of seating himself or herself.
Correct Answer: (C) Now, let’s take our discussion of present participial phrases to a higher level of sophistication by going beyond the effects of placement to the overall logic and coherence of sentences.
summary:
Lesson Content When a present participial phrase comes at the end of a clause and is set off from that clause by a comma, the participial phrase does not have to modify the noun closest to it.
For example:
The horses ran across the field, kicking up dirt with their hooves.
Notice that the present participial phrase “kicking up dirt with their hooves” is separated from the clause by a comma.
title: 5.9.6 Present Participial Phrases that Appear at the Ends of Clauses and Modify Entire Clauses

contents:
Lesson Content Present participial phrases always indicate simultaneity.
Consider the following sentence:
Using fresh ingredients, the chef prepared the meal.
Notice that these two things happen at the same time.
However, notice what happens in the following two sentences:
Incorrect: Using fresh ingredients, the chef looked for a recipe for the meal.
Incorrect: Using fresh ingredients, the chef served the meal on white china.
Neither of these sentences is logical because both of them use present participial phrases to describe events that would not possibly have occurred simultaneously with the events described by the main clauses.
In the case of the first sentence, what the participial phrase describes, using fresh ingredients, would have occurred after what the main clause describes, looking for a recipe.
In the case of the second sentence, what the participial phrase describes, using fresh ingredients, would have occurred before what the main clause describes, serving the meal.
💡MUST KNOW:
Participial phrases always indicate simultaneity; the event described by a participle phrase must occur at the same time as the event described by the clause that the phrase modifies.
Consider the following example:
Incorrect: Working hard for hours, the crew took a break.
At first, this example might seem to make sense, as it makes sense that a crew would work hard and take a break.
However, since the present participial phrase “working hard for hours” indicates simultaneity, what the above sentence actually conveys is that the crew worked and took a break at the same time.
Of course, those two events could not possibly have occurred at the same time.
After working hard for hours, the crew took a break.
The crew took a break after working hard for hours.
In general, when a writer utilizes a present participial phrase in discussing events that do not occur at the same time, he or she creates an illogical sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
When a writer utilizes a present participial phrase in discussing events that do not occur at the same time, he or she creates an illogical sentence.
Here’s another example.
Incorrect: The lively raccoon washed the food, eating it quickly.
This sentence may seem fine at first.
Notice, however, that the participial phrase “eating it quickly” doesn’t work with the rest of the sentence.
Both of the following sentences are potential corrections:
The lively raccoon washed the food and then ate it quickly.
Having washed the food, the lively raccoon ate it quickly.
Now, sometimes when a sentence uses a present participial phrase to modify a clause, the events discussed are simultaneous, but using the participial phrase to modify the clause still results in an illogical sentence.
Consider the following example.
Incorrect: The famous actress took a break from filming, knocking a sacred rock from where it had been placed and almost killing a sound technician.
Notice that the action described by the main clause and the action described by the present participial phrase could occur at the same time (she took a break and knocked the rock from where it had been placed).
However, the sentence as a whole is illogical.
In order for the sentence to be logical, it would have to be structured in a different way such as:
While the famous actress was taking a break from filming, she knocked a sacred rock from where it had been placed and almost killed a sound technician.
The above version conveys that the two events “taking a break” and “knocked a sacred rock” occurred at the same time without connecting them in an illogical way.
What we’re seeing is that it’s not enough for a present participial phrase to logically modify a noun that appears in a sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
It’s not enough for a present participial phrase to logically modify a noun that appears in a sentence. In order for a sentence to make sense, any participial phrase that appears in that sentence has to work logically with the rest of the sentence.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 The cat was sitting on the bed, purring loudly. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 Agreeing with Erica that a day at the beach is a day better spent than a day at work, Marty decided to go to the beach on Sunday. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 The black bear, suddenly running away to avoid the wrath of another bear, foraged for food. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 4 The ambassador spoke passionately to the crowd at the convention, leaving through a side door without saying “goodbye” to anyone. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 5 The car crashed into the house, resulting in significant damage. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 6 Erupting, on average, once every two years, Mount Etna has already erupted twice this year. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 7 During a stay on Tahiti, the painter Paul Gauguin, using people on the island as subject matter, created some of his finest paintings. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 8 The building housing the university’s rare book collection has various book preservation related design features, dominating the space in the quadrangle in which it is located. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 9 Entrepreneur Jirka Rysavy, spending most of his adult life in the United States, left his native Czechoslovakia in 1984. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
The present participial phrase “purring loudly” works logically with the clause that precedes it, describing something that was occurring while the cat was sitting on the bed.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
Marty’s agreeing with Erica and his deciding to go to the beach occurred simultaneously and are logically connected. So, the present participial phrase “agreeing with Erica …” logically modifies the clause that follows it.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
As written, the sentence suggests that a black bear was running away and foraged for food at the same time. Of course, that meaning is not logical. What would make more sense is that the black bear was foraging for food and then suddenly ran away, as is conveyed by:
The black bear was foraging for food and then suddenly ran away to avoid the wrath of another bear.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
This sentence does not make sense. The ambassador would not speak and leave simultaneously. A more logical sentence is:
The ambassador spoke passionately to the crowd at the convention and then left through a side door without saying “goodbye” to anyone.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
The participial phrase “resulting in significant damage” logically modifies the entire preceding clause, describing the results of the event described in the clause, although there is no clear agent of the participle “resulting.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
The participial phrase “erupting, on average, once every two years,” does not work logically with the rest of the sentence. As written, the sentence seems to convey that volcano has erupted once every two years simultaneously with its erupting twice this year. These events would not occur simultaneously. A more logical sentence is:
Although, on average, Mount Etna erupts once every two years, it has already erupted twice this year.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 7 Explanation:
The events “using people” and “created … paintings” would have occurred simultaneously and are logically connected. So, this sentence is logically constructed and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 8 Explanation:
The sentence does not make sense, because closing participial phrase, “dominating the space in the quadrangle in which it is located,” is almost completely unrelated to what is described by the clause: the building’s having “various book preservation related design features.”
In order to be to logical, the sentence has to be structured differently, as is the following version:
The building housing the university’s rare book collection has various book preservation related design features and dominates the space in the quadrangle in which it is located.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 9 Explanation:
The event “spending most of his adult life in the United States” and the event “left his native Czechoslovakia in 1984” clearly did not occur simultaneously. So, the use of the present participial phrase “spending most of his adult life in the United States” to modify the clause does not make sense.
In order for the sentence to make sense, it has to be reworded so that it connects the events described in a more logical manner, as in:
Entrepreneur Jirka Rysavy has spent most of his adult life in the United States, having left his native Czechoslovakia in 1984.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Now, consider the following Sentence Correction examples, being careful to notice that some versions, while grammatically correct, convey illogical meanings.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 36 Former Cuban president Fidel Castro masterminded the creation of the famous Cohiba cigar, suddenly quitting smoking for health reasons, at age 59. 1. [ ] (A) Castro masterminded the creation of the famous Cohiba cigar, suddenly quitting smoking for health reasons, at age 59 > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Castro, masterminding the creation of the famous Cohiba cigar, and then suddenly, at age 59, quit smoking for health reasons > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Castro, who had masterminded the creation of the famous Cohiba cigar, suddenly quit smoking, at age 59, for health reasons 4. [ ] (D) Castro, who, having masterminded the creation of the famous Cohiba cigar, suddenly quit smoking, at age 59, for health reasons > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Castro suddenly quit smoking for health reasons, at age 59, masterminding the creation of the famous Cohiba cigar > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy Each version of this sentence contains a present participial phrase, a relative clause, or both a present participial phrase and a relative clause.
Since the verbs contained in participial phrases and relative clauses cannot serve as the main verbs of sentences, two versions are missing main verbs.
Choosing the correct answer from the three remaining choices requires noticing that, while the three remaining versions are complete sentences, only one of them conveys a logical meaning.
(A) Former Cuban president Fidel Castro masterminded the creation of the famous Cohiba cigar, suddenly quitting smoking for health reasons, at age 59 .
This version is a complete sentence. However, the meaning conveyed by this version does not make sense.
The placement of the present participial phrase “suddenly quitting smoking …” indicates that the phrase modifies the entire preceding clause. Thus, since the events “masterminded the creation of the famous Cohiba cigar” and “suddenly quitting smoking” likely did not happen simultaneously and are not logically connected, the sentence does not make sense.
(B) Former Cuban president Fidel Castro, masterminding the creation of the famous Cohiba cigar, and then suddenly, at age 59, quit smoking for health reasons .
In this version, the participial modifier “masterminding the creation” is illogically connected by “, and” with the finite verb “quit.” We can more easily see this issue if we simplify the sentence.
Fidel Castro, masterminding the … cigar …, and then … quit smoking.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Former Cuban president Fidel Castro, who had masterminded the creation of the famous Cohiba cigar, suddenly quit smoking, at age 59, for health reasons .
In this version, the modifier, “who had masterminded the creation of the famous Cohiba cigar,” adds information to the sentence in a logical way.
So, the sentence effectively conveys that Castro suddenly quit smoking at age 59 and provides some additional information, that Castro had masterminded the creation of the famous Cohiba cigar.
(D) Former Cuban president Fidel Castro, who, having masterminded the creation of the famous Cohiba cigar, suddenly quit smoking, at age 59, for health reasons .
The two verb forms in this version, “having masterminded” and “quit,” are parts of modifiers. “Having masterminded” modifies “who,” and “quit” is part of the modifier “who quit ….” Thus, this version is composed of only the noun phrase, “former Cuban president Fidel Castro,” and a long modifier, “who, having masterminded …” So, this version has no main verb and, therefore, is not a complete sentence.
(E) Former Cuban president Fidel Castro suddenly quit smoking for health reasons, at age 59, masterminding the creation of the famous Cohiba cigar .
While this version is a complete sentence, the meaning conveyed by this version does not make sense.
The placement of the present participial phrase, “masterminding the creation of the famous Cohiba cigar,” indicates that the phrase modifies the entire preceding clause. Thus, since the events “suddenly quit smoking” and “masterminding the creation of the famous Cohiba cigar” likely did not happen simultaneously and are not logically connected, the sentence does not make sense.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 37 A 7,000-watt solar energy system now costs around $15,000, being a price that many can afford. 1. [ ] (A) $15,000, being a price that many can afford > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) $15,000, a price affordable for many 3. [ ] (C) $15,000 and is a price affordable for many > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) $15,000, which is at a price that many can afford > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) $15,000, making it an affordable price for many > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium While the sentence seems to be meant to convey that $15,000 is a price that many can afford, only one version conveys that meaning. So, finding the correct answer to this question takes noticing how the use of modifiers, along with other wording choices, affects the meanings conveyed by the various versions.
(A) A 7,000-watt solar energy system now costs around $15,000, being a price that many can afford .
The present participial phrase “being a price …” appears after the clause and is separated from the preceding clause by a comma. So, we can tell that the phrase modifies the entire preceding clause rather than the noun that immediately precedes the phrase. Thus, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that a 7000-watt solar energy system is being a price that many can afford.
YOUR CHOICE(B) A 7,000-watt solar energy system now costs around $15,000, a price affordable for many .
This version uses an appositive, “a price affordable for many,” to modify the noun that precedes it, “$15,000,” and thus conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(C) A 7,000-watt solar energy system now costs around $15,000 and is a price affordable for many .
By combining this choice with the non-underlined portion, we get a version that conveys the nonsensical meaning that a solar energy system costs $15,000 and that the solar energy system itself is a price affordable for many.
(D) A 7,000-watt solar energy system now costs around $15,000, which is at a price that many can afford .
This version conveys the nonsensical meaning that $15,000 is at a price, as opposed to a more logical meaning, such as that $15,000 is a price.
(E) A 7,000-watt solar energy system now costs around $15,000, making it an affordable price for many .
Since the present participial phrase “making it an affordable price for many” appears after the clause and is separated from the preceding clause by a comma, we can tell that the phrase modifies the entire preceding clause. Thus, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that, by costing $15,000, a 7000-watt solar energy system makes $15,000 an affordable price for many.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss past participial phrases, starting with past participial phrases used to restrictively modify nouns.
summary:
Lesson Content Present participial phrases always indicate simultaneity.
Consider the following sentence:
Using fresh ingredients, the chef prepared the meal.
Notice that these two things happen at the same time.
However, notice what happens in the following two sentences:
Incorrect: Using fresh ingredients, the chef looked for a recipe for the meal.
Incorrect: Using fresh ingredients, the chef served the meal on white china.
Neither of these sentences is logical because both of them use present participial phrases to describe events that would not possibly have occurred simultaneously with the events described by the main clauses.
title: 5.9.7 The Meaning Conveyed by a Present Participial Phrase Must Fit with the Rest of the Sentence in which the Phrase Appears

contents:
Lesson Content Past participial phrases, phrases based on past participles (usually “-ed” or “-en” verbs), can be used to restrictively modify nouns.
In these situations, the past participial phrase is placed directly before or after the noun that it modifies and is not separated from the noun by commas.
💡MUST KNOW:
Past participial phrases can be used to restrictively modify nouns. In these situations, the past participial phrase is placed directly before or after the nouns that it modifies and is not separated from the noun by commas.
Consider the following example:
Pumpkins harvested in the fall can last until the following summer if stored in certain conditions.
In the above example, the past participial phrase “harvested in the fall” restrictively modifies the noun “pumpkins.” We can tell that the modifier is restrictive by noticing that there are no commas setting “harvested in the fall” off from the rest of the sentence.
Because this modifier is restrictive, it tells us which specific pumpkins this sentence is about, pumpkins harvested in the fall.
Let’s consider another example:
After the storm, the newspaper published photographs of buildings damaged by floodwaters.
In the above example, the past participial phrase “damaged by floodwaters” restrictively modifies “buildings” and, thus, indicates that the newspaper didn’t publish photos of just any buildings; it published photographs of buildings damaged by floodwaters.
Here’s another example:
The company hired a firm known for devising outrageous publicity stunts in a bid to quickly become a household name.
In this example, the sentence employs the restrictive past participial phrase “known for devising …” to convey that the company hired a specific type of firm, one known for devising outrageous publicity stunts.
Let’s look at one more:
The damaged beyond recognition automobile once belonged to a famous actor.
In this example, the sentence utilizes the restrictive past participial phrase “damaged beyond recognition” to indicate exactly which automobile is mentioned.
Let’s now discuss past participial phrases used to nonrestrictively modify nouns.
summary:
Lesson Content Past participial phrases, phrases based on past participles (usually “-ed” or “-en” verbs), can be used to restrictively modify nouns.
In these situations, the past participial phrase is placed directly before or after the noun that it modifies and is not separated from the noun by commas.
💡MUST KNOW:
Past participial phrases can be used to restrictively modify nouns. In these situations, the past participial phrase is placed directly before or after the nouns that it modifies and is not separated from the noun by commas.
title: 5.9.8 Past Participial Phrases Used to Restrictively Modify Nouns

contents:
Lesson Content A past participial phrase can be used to nonrestrictively modify a noun.
💡MUST KNOW:
A past participial phrase can be used to nonrestrictively modify a noun. In such a case, the phrase may appear directly before or directly after the noun that it modifies and is separated from the noun and other sentence elements by commas.
Consider the following example:
At the top of the hill stood a small house, painted white.
The past participial phrase “painted white” nonrestrictively modifies the noun “house.” We can tell that the modifier is nonrestrictive by noticing that there is a comma setting “painted white” off from the rest of the sentence.
Since a nonrestrictive past participial phrase can appear before or after the noun that it modifies, we could also say:
Painted white, a small house stood at the top of the hill.
Here’s another example:
Considered the best small team in the field, the husband-wife duo regularly produces groundbreaking work.
In this example, we can tell that the past participial phrase “considered the best …” is used nonrestrictively, because it is set off from the rest of the sentence by a comma.
Notice that we could also say:
The husband-wife duo, considered the best small team in the field, regularly produces groundbreaking work.
Consider one more example:
Covered in mud, the cyclists skillfully navigated the challenging terrain.
The cyclists, covered in mud, skillfully navigated the challenging terrain.
Notice that both sentences are correct because a nonrestrictive past participial phrase can appear before or after the noun that it modifies.
Let’s now discuss how Sentence Correction question writers can create issues involving past participial phrases.
summary:
Lesson Content A past participial phrase can be used to nonrestrictively modify a noun.
💡MUST KNOW:
A past participial phrase can be used to nonrestrictively modify a noun. In such a case, the phrase may appear directly before or directly after the noun that it modifies and is separated from the noun and other sentence elements by commas.
Consider the following example:
At the top of the hill stood a small house, painted white.
title: 5.9.9 Past Participial Phrases Used to Nonrestrictively Modify Nouns

contents:
Lesson Content Sentence Correction question creators can create issues in sentences by misplacing past participial phrases.
Incorrect: Awarded the Nobel Prize for his work in chemistry, research done by Linus Pauling was wide-ranging and hard-hitting.
Notice the opening past participial phrase, “awarded the Nobel Prize for his work in chemistry.” What follows this phrase must be the noun that it is meant to modify: Linus Pauling.
Awarded the Nobel Prize for his work in chemistry, Linus Pauling conducted research that was wide-ranging and hard-hitting.
We can see that the opening phrase “awarded the Nobel Prize for his work in chemistry” is now properly modifying “Linus Pauling.” The noun that follows an introductory phrase that begins with a past participle must be the noun that that phrase intends to modify.
Let’s look at another example involving a past participle.
Incorrect: Acclaimed for his outlandish performance pieces and multimedia displays, the upcoming exhibition by this artist is sure to attract many visitors.
The upcoming exhibition is not “acclaimed for his outlandish performance pieces …” It is the artist who is acclaimed.
Acclaimed for his outlandish performance pieces and multimedia displays, this artist is sure to attract many visitors to his upcoming exhibition.
Note that we had to rearrange the clause following the introductory phrase quite a bit to ensure that the correct noun was modified.
Here’s another example:
Incorrect: Known for its lovely architecture, Pedro de Heredia founded the city of Cartagena in 1533.
In this sentence, the past participial phrase “known for its lovely architecture” is placed close to and seems to modify “Pedro de Heredia.” Of course, this modification does not make sense.
The city of Cartagena, known for its lovely architecture, was founded by Pedro de Heredia in 1533.
Let’s look at one more example.
Incorrect: Heralded as a pivotal breakthrough in cancer research, the lead scientist presented the group’s findings at an annual convention attended by top researchers from around the globe.
The findings, not the scientist, are being “heralded as a pivotal breakthrough.” A correct sentence might read:
Heralded as a pivotal breakthrough in cancer research, the group’s findings were presented by the lead scientist at an annual convention attended by top researchers from around the globe.
💡MUST KNOW:
Be on the lookout for an introductory phrase that begins with a past participle; we may have a misplaced modifier question on our hands. The noun that follows an introductory phrase that begins with a past participle must be the noun that that phrase is meant to modify.
Of course, these issues with past participle placement are not limited to the beginning of a sentence.
Incorrect: A new wind farm is under construction in the valley, expected to provide enough power for a small city.
Since the past participial phrase “expected to provide …” directly follows “valley,” the phrase seems to modify “valley.” Of course, this modification does not make sense, as it’s not the valley that is expected to provide power; it’s the wind farm.
A new wind farm, expected to provide enough power for a small city, is under construction in the valley.
Now the past participial phrase “expected to provide …” is placed in a way such that the phrase modifies something that it would logically modify, “wind farm.”
💡MUST KNOW:
One way in which Sentence Correction question writers can create issues involving past participial phrases is by misplacing the phrases, so that the phrases modify the wrong nouns.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each sentence is incorrect or correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Consider the following Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 38 Lava balloons, bubbles surrounded by crusts of lava, their gas content allowing them to float on the surface of the ocean. 1. [ ] (A) Lava balloons, bubbles surrounded by crusts of lava, their gas content allowing them to float on the surface of the ocean. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Because of their gas content, lava balloons are bubbles allowed to float on the ocean’s surface surrounded by crusts of lava. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) The gas content of lava balloons, bubbles surrounded by crusts of lava, allows them to float on the surface of the ocean. 4. [ ] (D) When lava balloons are allowed to float on the ocean’s surface because of their gas content, it is because they are bubbles surrounded by crusts of lava. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Surrounded by a crust of lava, the gas content of lava balloons is what allows these bubbles to float on the surface of the ocean. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium The choices in this question all convey different meanings, only one of which makes sense. Some versions are illogical partly because the past participial phrase “surrounded by a crust of lava” is placed in ways such that phrase modifies nouns that it does not logically modify.
(A) Lava balloons, bubbles surrounded by crusts of lava, their gas content allowing them to float on the surface of the ocean. Since the verb forms “surrounded” and “allowing” are participles rather than finite verbs, this version has no main verb and, thus, is not a complete sentence.
(B) Because of their gas content, lava balloons are bubbles allowed to float on the ocean’s surface surrounded by crusts of lava. The placement of the past participial phrase “surrounded by crusts of lava” directly after “ocean’s surface” results in this sentence’s seeming to convey that the ocean’s surface is surrounded by crusts of lava.
Also, the overall meaning of this version is nonsensical, as the version seems to convey that lava balloons are bubbles that are allowed to float, as if they have been given permission, because of their gas content.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The gas content of lava balloons, bubbles surrounded by crusts of lava, allows them to float on the surface of the ocean. This version uses the appositive “bubbles surrounded by crusts of lava” to effectively describe lava balloons and concisely conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(D) When lava balloons are allowed to float on the ocean’s surface because of their gas content, it is because they are bubbles surrounded by crusts of lava. The overall meaning of this version is nonsensical, as it seems to suggest that lava balloons are only sometimes “allowed to float,” as if they are given permission because they have gas content, and that they are allowed to float because they are bubbles surrounded by crusts of lava. So, this version does not present a logical explanation of the floating of the bubbles.
Also, there is no clear referent for the pronoun “it,” as the pronoun “it” would not logically refer to an entire clause, and there is in the sentence no noun that “it” would logically represent.
(E) Surrounded by a crust of lava, the gas content of lava balloons is what allows these bubbles to float on the surface of the ocean. Since “surrounded by a crust of lava” directly precedes “the gas content,” this version conveys the illogical meaning that the gas content, in other words the percentage of the composition of or the amount of gas contained in lava balloons, is surrounded by a crust of lava.
Further, this version conveys the illogical meaning that the gas content of all lava balloons is surrounded by a single crust of lava.
Also, the wording “the gas content of lava balloons is what allows these bubbles” does not clearly indicate that the lava balloons are the bubbles.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 39 Created by artist Jacques Majorelle situated on two and a half acres in the center of Marrakesh, the sights seen by visitors to the Majorelle Garden includes many cacti and a cubist villa painted a stunning blue. 1. [ ] (A) Created by artist Jacques Majorelle situated on two and a half acres in the center of Marrakesh, the sights seen by visitors to the Majorelle Garden includes > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) When visitors to the Majorelle Garden see sights, created by artist Jacques Majorelle and situated on two and a half acres in the center of Marrakesh, it includes > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Created by artist Jacques Majorelle, visitors to the Majorelle garden situated on two and a half acres in the center of Marrakesh see sights to include > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Seen by visitors to the Majorelle garden, created by artist Jacques Majorelle and situated on two and a half acres in the center of Marrakesh, are sights including 5. [ ] (E) The sights seen by visitors to the Majorelle Garden that was created by artist Jacques Majorelle situated on two and a half acres in the center of Marrakesh including > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium To answer this question correctly, we have to notice the placement of past participial phrases in order to find a version that conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(A) Created by artist Jacques Majorelle situated on two and a half acres in the center of Marrakesh, the sights seen by visitors to the Majorelle Garden includes many cacti and a cubist villa painted a stunning blue.
Notice that the past participial phrase “situated on two and a half acres in the center of Marrakesh” is placed directly after “artist Jacques Majorelle” without a comma between the two and is followed by a comma. As a result, the phrase seems to illogically modify the artist, who would not be “situated on two and a half acres.”
Also, it does not even quite make sense to say that “sights” are “situated on two and a half acres.” So, that phrase is clearly misplaced.
Meanwhile, since the subject of the main clause is the plural “sights,” not the singular “the Majorelle Garden,” the clause’s verb, the singular “includes,” does not agree with the subject.
(B) When visitors to the Majorelle Garden see sights, created by artist Jacques Majorelle and situated on two and a half acres in the center of Marrakesh, it includes many cacti and a cubist villa painted a stunning blue.
The first issue we come to as we read this version is that “sights” is modified by the phrase “created by artist Jacques Majorelle and situated on two and a half acres in the center of Marrakesh.” While it would make sense to describe a garden as created by artist Jacques Majorelle and situated on two and a half acres, it does not really make sense to describe sights in that way. This issue is not a clear, strong decision point, but it does make this version less than ideal.
The issue that makes this choice clearly incorrect is that the use of “it” is problematic.
Since sights would include many cacti and a cubist villa, the singular “it”’ seems to incorrectly refer to the plural “sights.”
On the other hand, we could decide that “it” must refer to the singular “the Majorelle Garden.” However, in that case, we have a meaning issue, because in that case, the sentence conveys the nonsensical meaning that the garden includes cacti and a villa only “when visitors to the Majorelle Garden see sights,” as if the cacti and villa cease to exist when the visitors don’t see sights.
(C) Created by artist Jacques Majorelle, visitors to the Majorelle garden situated on two and a half acres in the center of Marrakesh see sights to include many cacti and a cubist villa painted a stunning blue.
Since what follows the opening past participial phrase “created by artist Jacques Majorelle” is “visitors,” the opening phrase is misplaced, since visitors to the garden would not have been created by Jacques Majorelle.
Also, “sights to include many cacti and a cubist villa” expresses that the sights will include those things in the future but do not currently include them. As a result, “visitors … see sights to include many cacti and a cubist villa” conveys the illogical meaning that the visitors see sights that will include many cacti and a villa but do not include them when the visitors see them.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Seen by visitors to the Majorelle garden, created by artist Jacques Majorelle and situated on two and a half acres in the center of Marrakesh, are sights including many cacti and a cubist villa painted a stunning blue.
This version is a little tricky, because it is an inverted sentence in which the main verb, “are … seen,” precedes the subject, “sights.”
All the same, this version is well constructed, with the past participial phrase “created by artist Jacques Majorelle and situated on two and a half acres …” correctly placed to logically modify “the Majorelle Garden.”
This version is grammatically correct and effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(E) The sights seen by visitors to the Majorelle Garden that was created by artist Jacques Majorelle situated on two and a half acres in the center of Marrakesh including many cacti and a cubist villa painted a stunning blue.
The use of the restrictive relative clause “that was created by artist Jacques Majorelle …” to modify “the Majorelle Garden” is questionable because, presumably, there is only one “Majorelle Garden.” So, using a restrictive modifier to define which Majorelle Garden the sentence is about doesn’t seem to make sense. This issue is not a strong decision point, because it’s possible that there is more than one Majorelle Garden, but it does make this version suspect, especially in the context of the other versions, which tend to indicate that there is only one Majorelle Garden.
Next, we see that “situated on two and a half acres …” directly follows “Jacques Majorelle” and seems to illogically modify “Jacques Majorelle.” While it’s arguable that “situated on two and a half acres …” is meant to modify the entire preceding phrase, “the Majorelle Garden that was created by artist Jacques Majorelle,” the placement of “situated on two and a half acres …” is certainly not ideal.
Finally, what takes this choice out of contention for sure is that, since “including” is not a finite verb,” this version includes no main verb and, thus, is not a complete sentence.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss another way in which Sentence Correction question writers can create issues involving past participial phrases.
summary:
Lesson Content Sentence Correction question creators can create issues in sentences by misplacing past participial phrases.
Incorrect: Awarded the Nobel Prize for his work in chemistry, research done by Linus Pauling was wide-ranging and hard-hitting.
Notice the opening past participial phrase, “awarded the Nobel Prize for his work in chemistry.” What follows this phrase must be the noun that it is meant to modify: Linus Pauling.
Awarded the Nobel Prize for his work in chemistry, Linus Pauling conducted research that was wide-ranging and hard-hitting.
title: 5.9.10 Issues Involving Past Participial Phrases

contents:
Lesson Content A second way in which Sentence Correction question writers can create issues involving past participial phrases is to use a restrictive or nonrestrictive past participial phrase where using a past participial phrase of the other type, nonrestrictive or restrictive, would make more sense.
Incorrect: Only a tiny percentage of students pursue degrees in physics considered one of the most challenging college subjects.
Since there is no comma between “physics” and the past participial phrase “considered one of the …,” the use of the phrase restricts “physics” to one particular type of physics, the physics considered one of the most challenging college subjects.
Only a tiny percentage of students pursue degrees in physics, considered one of the most challenging college subjects.
Let’s consider another example:
Incorrect: The original Eiffel Tower built in 1887 was constructed along with other unique structures for an exposition.
In this example, “built in 1887” restrictively modifies “the original Eiffel Tower,” and thus specifies that the sentence is about the tower built in 1887.
The original Eiffel Tower, built in 1887, was constructed along with other unique structures for an exposition.
Let’s consider one more:
Incorrect: Most animals, found in the deepest regions of the ocean, have slow metabolisms that allow them to survive on small quantities of food.
In this case, the phrase “found in the deepest regions of the ocean” is set off from the rest of the sentence by commas and, therefore, is nonrestrictive.
Most animals found in the deepest regions of the ocean have slow metabolisms that allow them to survive on small quantities of food.
Now, the sentence makes sense because is about only one category of animals, those found in the deepest regions of the ocean.
Of course, the GMAT generally would not merely add or remove commas to differentiate correct answers from incorrect choices.
💡MUST KNOW:
A second way in which Sentence Correction question writers can create issues involving past participial phrases is to use a restrictive or nonrestrictive past participial phrase where using a past participial phrase of the other type, nonrestrictive or restrictive, would make more sense.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each sentence is incorrect or correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 Children treated respectfully tend to grow up to be respectful. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 Chocolate, made from ruby cocoa beans, was first introduced in 2017 and is pink in color. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 3 The expert confirmed that the car found in the barn was a classic roadster. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 Ranked fourth in terms of the number of Nobel Prizes per capita is the country of Switzerland known for its beautiful countryside. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 5 Gravity discovered by Sir Isaac Newton was once believed to be necessary for plant growth. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 6 Most automobiles, designed to work both on roads and in the water, are not ideal vehicles for use in either environment. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
It makes sense that the topic of this sentence is a specific group of children, those treated respectfully. So, the restrictive, no commas use of the past participial phrase “treated respectfully” makes sense.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
This sentence conveys that chocolate was first introduced in 2017 and is pink, a meaning that is clearly nonsensical to anyone who is familiar with chocolate. The reason the sentence conveys this meaning is that the past participial phrase “made from ruby cocoa beans” is set off from the rest of the sentence by commas and, therefore, is nonrestrictive. So, the phrase does not specify a type of chocolate. Rather, it adds more information about all chocolate.
The sentence can be fixed via the removal of the commas, which makes the modifier restrictive and results in the sentence’s conveying that a specific type of chocolate, “chocolate made from ruby cocoa beans,” was first introduced in 2017 and is pink in color.
Chocolate made from ruby cocoa beans was first introduced in 2017 and is pink in color.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
Since the past participial phrase “found in the barn” directly follows “car” and is not set off from the rest of the sentence by commas, we can tell that it specifies which car the sentence is about. Specifying that the car is a particular car, the one found in the barn, makes sense.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
Since there is only one country of Switzerland, it does not make sense to use a restrictive modifier such as “known for its beautiful countryside” to further specify which country the sentence is about. So, in order for this sentence to make sense, there has to be a comma between “Switzerland” and “known for its beautiful countryside,” as in:
Ranked fourth in terms of the number of Nobel Prizes per capita is the country of Switzerland, known for its beautiful countryside.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
Since the past participial phrase “discovered by Sir Isaac Newton” is not set off from the rest of the sentence by commas, it restrictively modifies “gravity.” Thus, the sentence conveys the illogical meaning that gravity in a particular category, the gravity discovered by Sir Isaac Newton, was once believed to be necessary for plant growth, as if other gravity, gravity not discovered by him, would not have worked.
In order for the sentence to make sense, the phrase “discovered by Sir Isaac Newton” has to be set off by commas, so that it is a nonrestrictive modifier. With the addition of those commas, the sentence is not about gravity in a particular category but rather about gravity in general.
Gravity, discovered by Sir Isaac Newton, was once believed to be necessary for plant growth.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
The fact that the past participial phrase “designed to work both on roads and in the water” is set off from the rest of the sentence by commas indicates that it does not put what it modifies, “most automobiles,” into a subcategory. Thus, this sentence conveys the illogical meaning that most automobiles are designed to work both on roads and in the water and are not ideal vehicles for use in either environment.
By removing the commas, we can make the modifier restrictive. The resulting sentence is about only cars in a particular category, those designed to work both on roads and in the water, and this version makes sense.
Most automobiles designed to work both on roads and in the water are not ideal vehicles for use in either environment.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Consider the following Sentence Correction example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 40 Publicized scientific studies, retracted because of methodological errors or conflicts of interest, can continue to strongly influence public perception of an issue even after they disavow their research. 1. [ ] (A) studies, retracted because of methodological errors or conflicts of interest, can continue to strongly influence public perception of an issue even after they disavow their research > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) studies retracted because of methodological errors or conflicts of interest can continue to strongly influence public perception of an issue even after the research is disavowed 3. [ ] (C) studies retracted from methodological errors or conflicts of interest can continue to strongly influence public perception of an issue even after they have disavowed the research > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) studies, retracted as a result of methodological errors or conflicts of interest, can continue to strongly influence public perception of an issue even after researchers disavow it > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) studies that are retracted, resulting from methodological errors or conflicts of interest, can continue to strongly influence public perception of an issue even after being disavowed by researchers > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) Publicized scientific studies, retracted because of methodological errors or conflicts of interest, can continue to strongly influence public perception of an issue even after they disavow their research .
The nonrestrictive past participial phrase in this choice, “retracted because of methodological errors or conflicts of interest,” conveys the illogical meaning that all “publicized scientific studies” are “retracted because ….”
Of course, common sense tells us that many (if not most) publicized scientific studies are never retracted, neither for the reasons mentioned in the sentence nor for any other reason.
In this context, it would make sense to use a restrictive past participial phrase to modify the noun phrase “publicized scientific studies,” rather than use a nonrestrictive past participial phrase. In other words, the past participial phrase should not be set off by commas. A restrictive past participial phrase would logically specify that a certain category of “publicized scientific studies” is being discussed, those publicized studies that are retracted (as opposed to publicized studies in general).
Notice also that the plural pronouns “they” and “their” are missing an antecedent in the sentence. These pronouns can’t logically refer to “studies,” “errors,” or “conflicts” since none of those things would “disavow” (deny support for) their research. Presumably, these pronouns are supposed to refer to the researchers who worked on the studies, as those would be the people who “disavow their research” in this context. However, “researchers” or any similarly logical antecedent does not actually appear anywhere in the sentence. YOUR CHOICE(B) Publicized scientific studies retracted because of methodological errors or conflicts of interest can continue to strongly influence public perception of an issue even after the research is disavowed .
This choice logically uses a restrictive past participial phrase to specify that a certain category of “publicized scientific studies” is being discussed, those publicized studies that are “retracted because of methodological errors or conflicts of interest” (as opposed to publicized studies in general).
Thus, this version conveys a clear and logical meaning. Furthermore, this version contains no grammatical errors.
(C) Publicized scientific studies retracted from methodological errors or conflicts of interest can continue to strongly influence public perception of an issue even after they have disavowed the research .
The plural pronoun “they” is missing an antecedent in the sentence. “They” can’t logically refer to “studies,” “errors,” or “conflicts” since none of those things would “disavow” (deny support for) their research. Presumably, “they” is supposed to refer to the researchers who worked on the studies, as those would be the people who “have disavowed the research” (by retracting their studies). However, “researchers” or any similarly logical antecedent does not actually appear anywhere in the sentence. Additionally, it does not make sense to say “publicized scientific studies retracted from methodological errors or conflicts of interest,” as if the errors and conflicts had possession of the studies. Such a meaning is nonsensical.
(D) Publicized scientific studies, retracted as a result of methodological errors or conflicts of interest, can continue to strongly influence public perception of an issue even after researchers disavow it .
The nonrestrictive past participial phrase in this choice, “retracted as a result of methodological errors or conflicts of interest,” conveys the illogical meaning that all “publicized scientific studies” are “retracted as a result of ….”
Of course, common sense tells us that many (if not most) publicized scientific studies are never retracted, neither for the reasons mentioned in the sentence nor for any other reason.
In this context, it would make sense to use a restrictive past participial phrase to modify the noun phrase “publicized scientific studies,” rather than use a nonrestrictive past participial phrase. In other words, the past participial phrase should not be set off by commas. A restrictive past participial phrase would logically specify that a certain category of “publicized scientific studies” is being discussed, those publicized studies that are retracted (as opposed to publicized studies in general).
Also, it’s unclear to what the singular pronoun “it” in “after researchers disavow it” is supposed to refer. Do researchers disavow “an issue”? “Public perception”? Given the overall context that the studies are retracted, it would seem most logical that the researchers disavow the studies. But of course, the singular pronoun “it” cannot refer to the plural noun “studies.” So, there is no clear and logical antecedent of “it” in the sentence.
(E) Publicized scientific studies that are retracted, resulting from methodological errors or conflicts of interest, can continue to strongly influence public perception of an issue even after being disavowed by researchers .
In this version, the present participial phrase “resulting from methodological errors or conflicts of interest” modifies “publicized scientific studies that are retracted.” As a result, this version communicates the illogical meaning that “publicized scientific studies” themselves are “resulting from methodological errors or conflicts of interest.”
Logically, retractions are what are “resulting from methodological errors or conflicts.” However, the noun “retractions” does not appear in this version, which instead uses the verb “retracted.”
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 41 Because the atmospheric pressure on Mars is relatively low, liquid water, considered by many scientists a component essential to the development of life forms, both complex ones and simple ones, is not found on or near the surface of Mars in substantial quantities. 1. [x] (A) water, considered by many scientists a component essential to the development of life forms, both complex ones and simple ones, is 2. [ ] (B) water is considered by many scientists a component essential for the development of both complex and simple life forms but > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) water, which many scientists consider an essential component to the development of life forms that are both complex and simple, is > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) water many scientists consider to be a component essential to the development of life forms, both complex and simple, is > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) water considered by many scientists a component essential for the development of life forms, which are both complex and simple, is > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard Since the topic of the sentence is liquid water in general, not a specific type or category of liquid water, it makes sense to modify “liquid water” via the use of a nonrestrictive modifier.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Because the atmospheric pressure on Mars is relatively low, liquid water, considered by many scientists a component essential to the development of life forms, both complex ones and simple ones, is not found on or near the surface of Mars in substantial quantities.
In this version “liquid water” is modified via the use of a nonrestrictive past participial phrase, “considered by many …,” which provides additional information about liquid water without illogically restricting the water mentioned to a certain type.
Also, while the wording “life forms, both complex ones and simple ones” may seem wordy, the use of that wording results in the sentence’s clearly conveying that liquid water is considered essential to the development of multiple types of life forms.
(Notice, the use of “to” in some choices and “for” in other choices constitutes an insignificant split. Either of the prepositions is acceptable in the context in which they are used in this sentence.)
(B) Because the atmospheric pressure on Mars is relatively low, liquid water is considered by many scientists a component essential for the development of both complex and simple life forms but not found on or near the surface of Mars in substantial quantities.
This version conveys the illogical meaning that water is considered a component essential for the development of life forms because the atmospheric pressure on Mars is relatively low.
Also, it’s not clear whether the sentence is meant to convey that liquid water is considered a component but is considered not found or to convey that liquid water is considered a component but is not found.
(C) Because the atmospheric pressure on Mars is relatively low, liquid water, which many scientists consider an essential component to the development of life forms that are both complex and simple, is not found on or near the surface of Mars in substantial quantities.
The use of a nonrestrictive modifier beginning with “which” to modify “liquid water” makes sense and makes this version “sound right.” However, the wording within the modifier is not optimal.
Because, in this version, “component” is between “essential” and “to the development,” this version does not effectively convey that water is a “component essential to the development of life forms.” Rather, this version conveys that water is an “essential component” with making clear what the component is essential to.
Instead, this version conveys that water is an essential component “to the development of life forms,” as if to say that, according to the development of life forms, water is an essential component, as if the development of life forms has an opinion on the matter.
Also, the use of the wording “life forms that are both complex and simple” results in the sentence’s seeming to convey that water is essential to the development of certain types of life forms, life forms that are simultaneously complex and simple.
(D) Because the atmospheric pressure on Mars is relatively low, liquid water many scientists consider to be a component essential to the development of life forms, both complex and simple, is not found on or near the surface of Mars in substantial quantities.
Since there are no commas around the modifier “many scientists consider …,” the modifier is restrictive and illogically restricts the liquid water discussed in the sentence to a certain type, liquid water that many scientists consider a component essential to the development of life forms.
Also, “consider to be” is unidiomatic and does not make sense: the idiomatic version uses “consider” without “to be,” and, statements without “to be,” such as “they consider liquid water an essential component” make more sense than statements with “to be,” such as “they consider water to be an essential component.”
Also, what the modifier “both complex and simple” is meant to convey is not clear. Is it meant to convey that life forms are both complex and simple simultaneously? Is it meant to convey that water is necessary for the development life forms of two types, complex ones and simple ones?
(E) Because the atmospheric pressure on Mars is relatively low, liquid water considered by many scientists a component essential for the development of life forms, which are both complex and simple, is not found on or near the surface of Mars in substantial quantities.
While this version may seem correct, since there are no commas around the modifier “considered by many scientists …,” the modifier illogically restricts the liquid water discussed to only the liquid water considered by many scientists essential for the development of life forms, as if there are multiple types of liquid water, and as if “water considered by many scientists essential for the development of life forms” is a specific type of water.
Also, the wording “life forms, which are both complex and simple” does not convey a clear meaning, and seems to convey, for no reason clearly connected to what the rest of the sentence says, that life forms are simultaneously complex and simple.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss absolute phrases.
summary:
Lesson Content A second way in which Sentence Correction question writers can create issues involving past participial phrases is to use a restrictive or nonrestrictive past participial phrase where using a past participial phrase of the other type, nonrestrictive or restrictive, would make more sense.
Incorrect: Only a tiny percentage of students pursue degrees in physics considered one of the most challenging college subjects.
Since there is no comma between “physics” and the past participial phrase “considered one of the …,” the use of the phrase restricts “physics” to one particular type of physics, the physics considered one of the most challenging college subjects.
title: 5.9.11 Use of a Restrictive or Nonrestrictive Past Participial Phrase where using a Past Participial Phrase of the Other Type, Nonrestrictive or Restrictive, Would Make More Sense

contents:
Lesson Content An absolute phrase is a phrase that:
• consists of only a noun and its modifiers, and
• modifies an entire clause.
To remember that absolute phrases modify entire clauses, people sometimes say, “Absolute phrases modify absolutely everything.”
The following sentence contains an absolute phrase.
His work completed, John headed home for the night.
In this example, “his work completed” is an absolute phrase.
Here’s another:
Jennifer sat waiting in the dentist chair, her arms and legs crossed.
