The absolute phrase “her arms and legs crossed” contains the nouns “arms and legs,” along with modifiers “her” and “crossed.” The phrase modifies the entire clause “Jennifer sat waiting in the dentist chair,” describing how Jennifer sat in the dentist chair.
Here’s another:
The staff members were tired and stressed out, the tense negotiations having dragged on for days.
The absolute phrase “the tense negotiations having dragged on for days” contains the noun “negotiations,” along with modifiers “tense” and “having dragged on for days.” The phrase modifies the entire clause “the staff members were tired and stressed out,” providing information explaining why the staff members were tired and stressed out.
Let’s now discuss two common ways in which absolute phrases are used.
Two Ways in Which Absolute Phrases Are Used
Absolute phrases are used in two ways:
• To add descriptive information to a clause.
• To add explanatory information to a clause.
In each of the following two examples, an absolute phrase adds descriptive information.
The boat sat anchored, its sails billowing in the wind.
Absolute Phrase: “its sails billowing in the wind”
Effect of Phrase: Adds information describing how the boat was as it sat anchored.
The chorus, their voices resonant and mellifluous, brought the house down with their rendition of the work.
Absolute Phrase: “their voices resonant and mellifluous”
Effect of Phrase: Describes how the voices of the chorus sounded as the chorus brought the house down.
In each of the following examples, an absolute phrase adds explanatory information.
The weather being sunny and beautiful, we decided to walk rather than drive.
Absolute Phrase: “the weather being sunny and beautiful”
Effect of Phrase: Explains why we decided to walk rather than drive.
Our ravenous appetites satiated, we asked the waiter to pack the rest of the five-course meal to go.
Absolute Phrase: “our ravenous appetites satiated”
Effect of Phrase: Explains why we asked the waiter to pack the rest of the meal.
His flight canceled, Jack was forced to take a red-eye that had a two-hour layover in Phoenix.
Absolute Phrase: “his flight canceled”
Effect of Phrase: Explains why Jack was forced to take a different flight.
💡MUST KNOW:
An absolute phrase is a phrase composed of a noun and its modifiers that modifies an entire clause, adding information that is descriptive or explanatory.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether the information added by the underlined absolute phrase is primarily descriptive or primarily explanatory. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 Their tent having been destroyed by the bear, the campers had to scramble to find shelter from the oncoming storm. 1. [ ] (A) Descriptive > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Explanatory ## Targeted Practice: 2 Her face aglow with excitement, my sister announced that she had gotten the job. 1. [x] (A) Descriptive 2. [ ] (B) Explanatory > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 The restaurant was busy every night, its menu having been well received by the target clientele. 1. [ ] (A) Descriptive > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Explanatory ## Targeted Practice: 4 The shrubs around the house looked much better, the recent rains having soaked them and the soil in which they grow with fresh water. 1. [ ] (A) Descriptive > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Explanatory ## Targeted Practice: 5 The sun rose over the mountain, its rays gradually illuminating the beauty of the valley. 1. [x] (A) Descriptive 2. [ ] (B) Explanatory > Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
The absolute phrase “their tent having been destroyed by the bear” adds information explaining why the campers had to scramble to find shelter.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
The absolute phrase “her face aglow with excitement” adds information describing how “my sister” was as she announced her news.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
The absolute phrase “its menu having been well received by the target clientele” adds information explaining why the restaurant was busy.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
The absolute phrase “the recent rains having soaked them and the soil in which they grow with plenty of fresh water” adds information explaining why the shrubs looked better.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
The absolute phrase “its rays gradually illuminating the beauty of the valley” adds information describing the rising of the sun.
Correct Answer: (A) Now that we have gone over how absolute phrases work, let’s discuss how they can appear in Sentence Correction questions.
summary:
Lesson Content An absolute phrase is a phrase that:
• consists of only a noun and its modifiers, and
• modifies an entire clause.
To remember that absolute phrases modify entire clauses, people sometimes say, “Absolute phrases modify absolutely everything.”
The following sentence contains an absolute phrase.
His work completed, John headed home for the night.
In this example, “his work completed” is an absolute phrase.
Here’s another:
Jennifer sat waiting in the dentist chair, her arms and legs crossed.
title: 5.10 Absolute Phrases

contents:
Lesson Content In some cases, absolute phrases are used in ways that make correct answers clearly better than the other options.
Let’s begin by discussing how an absolute phrase can make an answer choice correct by being the best means of expressing a thought.
Consider the following two sentence versions.
Incorrect: Having spirits, which had been lifted by the joyful music, the crowd sang along with the band.
Their spirits having been lifted by the joyful music, the crowd sang along with the band.
Notice that, since “which had been lifted by the joyful music” is a nonessential modifier, the first version says, essentially, “Having spirits, … the crowd sang along with the band.” So, the first version does not effectively express the cause-and-effect connection between the crowd’s spirits’ having been lifted by the music and the crowd’s singing along with the band.
On the other hand, the second version uses an explanatory absolute phrase “their spirits having been lifted by the joyful music” to concisely and effectively express that the crowd sang along with the band because their spirits had been lifted by the joyful music.
So, the version that uses an absolute phrase is the better version.
Here’s another pair in which the version that uses an absolute phrase is more effective:
Incorrect: Increases in global temperatures are leaving polar bears hungry, along with the system via which they feed themselves having been disrupted.
Increases in global temperatures are leaving polar bears hungry, the system via which they feed themselves having been disrupted.
Notice the difference between the effectiveness of the first version and the effectiveness of the second version.
In the first, the wording “hungry, along with the system via which they feed themselves having been disrupted” describes two aspects of what polar bears are experiencing, but connecting the two via the use of the expression “along with” does not really make sense.
The second version, on the other hand, employs the absolute phrase “the system via which they feed themselves having been disrupted” to concisely convey that the disruption of their system of feeding themselves is the cause of the bears’ being hungry.
OK, now that we have seen some ways in which the use of absolute phrases can make sentences effective, let’s look at some examples of flawed use of absolute phrases.
Absolute Phrase Where a Clause Should Be:
Incorrect: The members of the committee having arrived at a decision, the chairperson declaring the end of the meeting.
This example is essentially made up of two absolute phrases, “the members of the committee having arrived at a decision” and “the chairperson declaring the end of the meeting.” Of course, this structure doesn’t constitute a sentence, as it lacks an independent clause.
The members of the committee having arrived at a decision, the chairperson declared the end of the meeting.
Absolute Phrase with an Illogical Relationship with the Clause that it Modifies:
Incorrect: Its foot caught in a trap, the environmentalist freed the bear.
We can tell from the use of the pronoun “its,” and from a common-sense interpretation of the sentence, that the foot is the bear’s.
Also, notice the following.
Incorrect: The environmentalist freed the bear, its foot caught in a trap.
In order for this sentence to make sense, it has to have a different kind of modifier.
The environmentalist freed the bear, whose foot had been caught in a trap.
Now the sentence makes sense.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Identify the absolute phrase to determine whether each sentence is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 The introductory remarks finally having been concluded, the featured speaker took the stage. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 The deadline nearing, and Everett working through the weekend to complete his application, a pot of coffee by his side. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 3 Although they already were full from the roasted corn, sweet potatoes, and green beans, the children gobbled up the apple crisp, their enjoyment was obvious. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 4 The mandatory recount underway, voters waited eagerly to learn the result of the close Senate race, which had been rife with acrimony from its start. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 Floor swept, bunks made, and soldiers standing at attention, the unit ready for the morning inspection by the commanding officer. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 6 Noah was completely unprepared for the tax audit, while his start-up’s financial records having been in disarray ever since his partner quit. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 7 Its bustling factories shuttered and its grand old mansions crumbling and condemned, the recession made Josh’s hometown look like a different universe, and the dwindling of the local workforce meant that any economic recovery would be slow. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 8 Troubled overseas markets and contentious trade relations with China stifling their recovery, so prices of commodities such as copper, oil, and gas have yet to bounce back fully from last month’s downturn, though traders are, for the moment, no longer in a selling frenzy. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
We see at the beginning of the sentence an absolute phrase composed of one noun, “remarks,” and some modifiers that modify “remarks,” and the absolute phrase logically modifies the clause that follows it.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
We must have a main verb somewhere in the sentence; neither “working” nor “to complete” qualifies as such. “The deadline nearing” is an absolute phrase, which, of course, does not contain the main verb. Notice that the conclusion of the sentence, “a pot of coffee by his side,” is also an absolute phrase. A complete sentence is:
The deadline nearing, Everett worked through the weekend to complete his application, a pot of coffee by his side.
Notice that the absolute phrase “the deadline nearing” explains why Everett worked through the weekend.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
We cannot join the two independent clauses “the children gobbled up the apple crisp” and “their enjoyment was obvious” with only a comma.
We can correct the sentence by changing the second independent clause to an absolute phrase (by removing the main verb from that clause).
Although they already were full from the roasted corn, sweet potatoes, and green beans, the children gobbled up the apple crisp, their enjoyment obvious.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
The absolute phrase “the mandatory recount underway” correctly modifies and adds descriptive information to the independent clause “voters waited eagerly to learn the result of the close Senate race.”
Also, the relative clause “which had been rife…” clearly refers to the noun that directly precedes it, “the close Senate race.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
The sentence is missing an independent clause. As currently written, what we have is merely two phrases. We cannot have a complete sentence made up of only phrases. A complete sentence is:
Floor swept, bunks made, and soldiers standing at attention, the unit was ready for the morning inspection by the commanding officer.
Notice that the absolute phrase “Floor swept, bunks made, and soldiers standing at attention” adds descriptive information.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
The subordinating conjunction “while” should not introduce the phrase “his start-up’s financial records having been in disarray ….” We can make the sentence logical by removing the conjunction, making the phrase an absolute phrase, as in:
Noah was completely unprepared for the tax audit, his start-up’s financial records having been in disarray ever since his partner quit.
Notice that the absolute phrase “his start-up’s financial records having been in disarray ever since his partner quit” provides explanatory information.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 7 Explanation:
“Its bustling factories shuttered…” is an absolute phrase that would logically describe “Josh’s hometown.” However, since the subject of the sentence is “the recession,” the opening modifier, while modifying the entire clause, focuses on “the recession.” Thus, the sentence conveys that the factories and mansions belong to the recession. Rephrasing the sentence will fix this modification issue. A more effective sentence is:
Its bustling factories shuttered and its grand old mansions crumbling and condemned, Josh’s hometown looked like a different universe after the recession, and the dwindling of the local workforce meant that any economic recovery would be slow.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 8 Explanation:
The phrase “troubled overseas markets … stifling their recovery” should not be connected to the independent clause that follows it by the conjunction “so.” In this type of context “so” should connect two clauses, and in no situation would “so” correctly connect a phrase and a clause. A correct version of the sentence uses an absolute phrase connected to a clause by a comma only.
Troubled overseas markets and contentious trade relations with China stifling their recovery, prices of commodities such as copper, oil, and gas have yet to bounce back fully from last month’s downturn, though traders are, for the moment, no longer in a selling frenzy.
Notice that the absolute phrase “Troubled overseas markets and contentious trade relations with China stifling their recovery” provides explanatory information.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Now, let’s answer some Sentence Correction questions that involve absolute phrases.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 42 The old mall sat empty and abandoned, as well as its stores that were casualties of internet shopping. 1. [ ] (A) as well as its stores that were > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) and with its stores that were > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) and its stores > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) its stores 5. [ ] (E) having as its stores > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium In every version, the part of the sentence that follows the comma adds information to what the main clause has stated. However, in only one case, the case in which an absolute phrase is used, does the information that follows the comma logically complement what has already been stated.
(A) The old mall sat empty and abandoned, as well as its stores that were casualties of internet shopping.
While this version may seem to make sense, what it says does not really make sense. If we simplify the sentence, we can see that it says essentially the following:
The mall sat empty and abandoned, as well as its stores.
The above sentence does not clearly convey that the stores were empty and abandoned or convey much of anything. The part about the stores is merely tacked onto the first part with no clear purpose and, thus, does not convey any particular meaning.
(B) The old mall sat empty and abandoned, and with its stores that were casualties of internet shopping.
This version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the mall sat empty and abandoned and sat with its stores.
(C) The old mall sat empty and abandoned, and its stores casualties of internet shopping.
This version almost seems to work. However, what follows the conjunction “and” has to match what comes before “and.” In this case, a clause precedes “and” but the second part of the sentence is not a clause as it has no main verb, and there is nothing before “and” that could be logically paired with “its stores casualties of internet shopping.” As a result, the structure works before the comma, but what comes after the comma does not work and is not clearly connected logically to what precedes the comma, as we can see by combining the answer choice with the non-underlined portions.
The old mall sat empty and abandoned, and its stores casualties of internet shopping.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The old mall sat empty and abandoned, its stores casualties of internet shopping.
In this version, the absolute phrase “its stores casualties of internet shopping” clearly and concisely modifies the first part of the sentence, providing information that explains why the mall is empty and abandoned.
(E) The old mall sat empty and abandoned, having as its stores casualties of internet shopping.
While this version is grammatically correct, what it conveys, that the mall had casualties of internet shopping as its stores, is, while perhaps poetic, not the type of clear meaning that is normally conveyed by the correct answer to a Sentence Correction question.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 43 Having feet that were pounding the track, the runners ran by where the coach stood watching. 1. [ ] (A) Having feet that were pounding the track, the runners ran by where the coach stood watching. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) The runners ran by where the coach stood watching along with their feet pounding the track. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) The runners ran by where the coach stood watching, their feet pounding the track. 4. [ ] (D) Having their feet pounding the track, the coach watched the runners run by where she stood. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) The coach stood watching the runners, their feet pounding the track running by. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium The main clause of this original version of this sentence expresses that the runners ran by where the coach stood watching. While the opening modifier “having feet that were pounding the track” adds descriptive information, what it conveys is illogically focused on the fact that the runners had feet. The best version of the sentence uses a more concise modifier that more effectively describes what occurred.
(A) Having feet that were pounding the track, the runners ran by where the coach stood watching. The opening modifier illogically focuses on the fact that the runners had feet, as if the reason why they ran by is that they had feet.
(B) The runners ran by where the coach stood watching along with their feet pounding the track. This version conveys an illogical meaning, that the coach was watching along with the runners’ feet.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The runners ran by where the coach stood watching, their feet pounding the track. In this version, the closing absolute phrase “their feet pounding the track” concisely and effectively adds information describing an aspect of how the runners were when they ran by.
(D) Having their feet pounding the track, the coach watched the runners run by where she stood. The opening modifier “having their feet pounding the track” is placed in a way such that the modifier modifies “coach,” and thus, this version conveys the illogical meaning that the coach had the runners’ feet pounding the track.
(E) The coach stood watching the runners, their feet pounding the track running by. In this version, the absolute phrase “their feet pounding the track running by” nonsensically conveys either that the runners’ feet were running by or that the track was running by.
Correct Answer: (C)
summary:
Lesson Content In some cases, absolute phrases are used in ways that make correct answers clearly better than the other options.
Let’s begin by discussing how an absolute phrase can make an answer choice correct by being the best means of expressing a thought.
Consider the following two sentence versions.
Incorrect: Having spirits, which had been lifted by the joyful music, the crowd sang along with the band.
Their spirits having been lifted by the joyful music, the crowd sang along with the band.
title: 5.10.1 How Absolute Phrases Can Appear in Sentence Correction Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Getting Sentence Correction questions correct often requires considering verb tense, mood, or voice.
However, as is the case with other aspects of sentence construction, too often people attempt to use their “ear” to answer questions involving these concepts.
In the following sections, we’ll cover various verb tense, mood, and voice related concepts and strategies.
Let’s begin by discussing the present tense.
summary:
Lesson Content Getting Sentence Correction questions correct often requires considering verb tense, mood, or voice.
However, as is the case with other aspects of sentence construction, too often people attempt to use their “ear” to answer questions involving these concepts.
In the following sections, we’ll cover various verb tense, mood, and voice related concepts and strategies.
Let’s begin by discussing the present tense.
title: 6.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content Despite its name, the present tense is not most commonly used in describing events that are presently occurring.
Consider the examples below:
The red heart is a universal symbol of love.
Sara loves going to the beach.
The dogs enjoy running in the woods.
Pigs don’t like too much direct sunlight on their sensitive skin.
Brazilians celebrate Carnaval every year.
Rome is the capital city of Italy.
Eating pizza is not healthy.
Flossing your teeth is a fun practice.
Notice that, in these example sentences, the present tense verbs are used for conveying facts that are true across times.
💡MUST KNOW:
We typically use the present tense to describe things that happen routinely or to discuss facts and universal truths that do not change over time.
Also, we often see present tense verbs used to describe policies, laws, rules, and regulations that were enacted at some point in the past but still have their effects in the present.
For example:
Originally written in 1791, the 1st Amendment to the United States Constitution, which makes it illegal for Congress to pass any law that infringes upon citizen’s rights to freedom of speech, is often a topic of debate these days.
Notice that the verbs “makes,” and “infringes” are in the present tense because, although the 1st amendment was written in 1791, its effects are still in place today.
💡MUST KNOW:
Present tense verbs are often used to describe policies, laws, rules, and regulations that were enacted at some point in the past but still have their effects in the present.
Let’s now discuss the simple past tense.
summary:
Lesson Content Despite its name, the present tense is not most commonly used in describing events that are presently occurring.
Consider the examples below:
The red heart is a universal symbol of love.
Sara loves going to the beach.
The dogs enjoy running in the woods.
Pigs don’t like too much direct sunlight on their sensitive skin.
Brazilians celebrate Carnaval every year.
Rome is the capital city of Italy.
Eating pizza is not healthy.
title: 6.2 The Present Tense

contents:
Lesson Content When a simple past tense verb is used, we’re describing an event that occurred – and ended – in the past.
💡MUST KNOW:
The simple past tense is used to describe events that occurred – and ended – in the past.
The simple past tense is most commonly formed by placing the ending –ed after the stem of a regular verb.
He finished his paper just before the deadline.
We walked the dog before sunrise.
Last year, attendance at the festival exceeded expectations.
In 1929, the stock market crashed.
Earlier this morning, Ernie the mule jumped into the swimming pool.
In each of these examples, notice that the action began – and ended – in the past.
💡MUST KNOW:
In the cases of most verbs, the simple past tense form is created by adding –ed to the stem of the verb.
Let’s now look at the future tense.
summary:
Lesson Content When a simple past tense verb is used, we’re describing an event that occurred – and ended – in the past.
💡MUST KNOW:
The simple past tense is used to describe events that occurred – and ended – in the past.
The simple past tense is most commonly formed by placing the ending –ed after the stem of a regular verb.
He finished his paper just before the deadline.
title: 6.3 The Simple Past Tense

contents:
Lesson Content The future tense is used for discussing events that have not yet occurred but will occur in the future.
The little boy will go to kindergarten next year.
John and Rebecca will be at the movies tonight.
Blaze and Molly will eat their dinner a bit later.
The rabbit will rest for a few minutes next to the brook.
The sun will burn out one day.
Notice that each of these examples describes an event that will occur in the future.
💡MUST KNOW:
The future tense is used when discussing events that have not yet occurred but will occur in the future. The future tense is formed by preceding the base verb with the helping verb “will.”
Note that, to describe an event in the future, we can also use the following construction:
a form of “to be” + “going to” + the base verb
A form of “to be” + “going to” + the base verb is generally used when future events are planned or seem likely to occur.
I am going to go to the beach later today.
Sara is going to start taking French lessons.
Because oil producing countries have agreed to increase output, commodity prices are going to decline.
Since the government has kept interest rates too low for too long, inflation is going to begin eroding the purchasing power of citizens.
💡MUST KNOW:
The construction a form of “to be” + “going to” + the base verb can be used to create future tense expressions. The future tense expressed in this way is generally used in sentences about future events that are planned or that seem likely to occur.
Let’s now discuss how to decide whether the use of a tense in a sentence version makes sense.
summary:
Lesson Content The future tense is used for discussing events that have not yet occurred but will occur in the future.
The little boy will go to kindergarten next year.
John and Rebecca will be at the movies tonight.
Blaze and Molly will eat their dinner a bit later.
The rabbit will rest for a few minutes next to the brook.
The sun will burn out one day.
Notice that each of these examples describes an event that will occur in the future.
title: 6.4 The Future Tense

contents:
Lesson Content Sentence Correction questions involving verb tense issues – even difficult ones – become easy once we realize that each of the sentence versions in these questions will include well-placed clues indicating exactly which tense should be used.
💡MUST KNOW:
Correctly answering verb, tense, mood questions is more about solving a puzzle than it is about being a grammarian.
Will we need some grammar knowledge? We will.
💡MUST KNOW:
In each of the sentence versions in Sentence Correction questions involving verb tense issues, the test-maker will include well-placed clues indicating exactly which tense must be used. To choose the version with the most logical use of tense, we must notice the clues provided.
Let’s discuss some specific strategies that we can employ.
summary:
Lesson Content Sentence Correction questions involving verb tense issues – even difficult ones – become easy once we realize that each of the sentence versions in these questions will include well-placed clues indicating exactly which tense should be used.
💡MUST KNOW:
Correctly answering verb, tense, mood questions is more about solving a puzzle than it is about being a grammarian.
Will we need some grammar knowledge? We will.
💡MUST KNOW:
title: 6.5 Context Clues in Sentences Tell Us Which Tense Must be Used

contents:
Lesson Content When faced with an SC question involving verb tense, one powerful strategy that we can utilize is to use verb context clues in the sentence versions to eliminate those with verb tenses that simply can’t work together.
For example, consider the following three sentences.
Incorrect: Each time I traveled to Colombia, I visit Medellín and Cartagena.
Incorrect: Each time will travel to Colombia, I visit Medellín and Cartagena.
Correct: Each time I travel to Colombia, I visit Medellín and Cartagena.
The verb clue here is the present tense verb “visit.” Remember that present tense verbs can describe not only actions that are occurring in the current moment, but also actions that occur regularly or are not subject to the passage of time.
The first sentence version does not make sense because the past tense verb “traveled” describes an action that has ended in the past.
The second sentence version does not make sense either because the future tense verb “will travel” describes an action that has not yet happened.
The third sentence version makes sense because the verbs “travel” and “visit” are both in the simple present and describe actions that happen with regularity.
Let’s look at another version of the sentence:
Incorrect: Each time I will travel to Colombia, I visited Medellín and Cartagena.
Incorrect: Each time I travel to Colombia, I visited Medellín and Cartagena.
Correct: Each time I traveled to Colombia, I visited Medellín and Cartagena.
The verb clue now is the past tense “visited,” and, thus, we can tell that the sentence is about actions that, although habitual, began and ended in the past.
The first sentence version does not make sense because the future tense verb “will travel” describes an action that has not yet happened.
The second sentence version does not make sense either because the present tense verb “travel” conveys that I regularly go to Colombia.
The third sentence version makes sense because it uses the past tense “traveled” and the past tense “visited.” Both of the actions ended in the past, so there is no confliction between the verb tenses.
Let’s examine one more example sentence:
Incorrect: I traveled to Colombia every summer, and I will visit Medellín and Cartagena during those trips.
Correct: I will travel to Colombia every summer, and I will visit Medellín and Cartagena during those trips.
The future tense “will visit” is our clue, and it indicates that the sentence is about an action that will occur in the future.
The first sentence version does not make sense because the past tense verb “traveled” describes an action that occurred in the past.
The second sentence version makes sense because both “will travel” and “will visit” are in the future tense.
So, we can see that using verb context clues in sentences is a very smart way to eliminate sentence versions that can’t possibly be correct.
Let’s now discuss another VTM strategy.
summary:
Lesson Content When faced with an SC question involving verb tense, one powerful strategy that we can utilize is to use verb context clues in the sentence versions to eliminate those with verb tenses that simply can’t work together.
For example, consider the following three sentences.
Incorrect: Each time I traveled to Colombia, I visit Medellín and Cartagena.
Incorrect: Each time will travel to Colombia, I visit Medellín and Cartagena.
title: 6.5.1 VTM Strategy One: Use Verb Context Clues to Eliminate Versions with Tenses that Can’t Work Together

contents:
Lesson Content In addition to verb context clues, GMAT questions will provide us with all sorts of other helpful clues that will allow us to rule out sentence versions with verb tenses that can’t possibly work.
Consider the following sentence versions:
Incorrect: In the past, Rome’s senate experiences high levels of corruption.
Incorrect: In the past, Rome’s senate will experience high levels of corruption.
Correct: In the past, Rome’s senate experienced high levels of corruption.
Notice that by saying, “in the past,” the sentence provides us with a valuable clue that signals that the action occurred in the past.
We also eliminate the first sentence version immediately because the present tense “experiences” conveys that the action occurs regularly, but an action “in the past” can’t occur regularly.
We can also eliminate the second sentence version immediately because the future tense “will experience” conveys that the action did not yet occur, but an action “in the past” has already begun and ended.
The third sentence version works well because the past tense “experienced” makes sense given that the action occurred “in the past.”
Notice that the simple context clue, “in the past” was all we needed to see to get to the correct version.
Let’s try another:
Incorrect: Regardless of when the current CEO decides to retire, the board found a replacement quickly, so that the company will not face the same vulnerabilities that it faced after the last CEO resigned.
Incorrect: Regardless of when the current CEO decides to retire, the board finds a replacement quickly, so that the company will not face the same vulnerabilities that it faced after the last CEO resigned.
Correct: Regardless of when the current CEO decides to retire, the board will find a replacement quickly, so that the company will not face the same vulnerabilities that it faced after the last CEO resigned.
We must look for keywords and other context clues to determine which tense logically fits the timeline of events.
We can eliminate version one because if the CEO has not yet decided to retire, how could the board have found a replacement?
We can also eliminate version two because the present tense “finds” illogically conveys that the board habitually “finds” a new CEO.
Sentence version three works well because the future tense “will find” make sense considering that the CEO has not yet decided to retire.
As we can see, by paying attention to context clues, we can assess the timeline and choose correctly among the past, present, and future tenses, even when two or more of those tenses are already contained in a sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
In considering a sentence containing a combination of past, present, and future tenses, use keywords, key phrases, and other context clues to arrive at a logical timeline of events.
As the chapter progresses, we’ll discuss how to find the correct verb tense in more complicated scenarios, but for now, it’s important that we can effectively choose between the simple past, present, and future tenses.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether present tense, past tense, or future tense should be used. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Consider the following Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 1 As many feel that elephants used in its acts are the circus engaging in cruelty to animals, the fall in ticket sales was dramatic, with the circus eventually shutting down. 1. [ ] (A) As many feel that elephants used in its acts are the circus engaging in cruelty to animals, the fall in ticket sales was dramatic, with the circus eventually shutting down. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) With many feeling that the use of elephants in the circus’s acts engaged in cruelty to animals, ticket sales fell dramatically and eventually shut the circus down. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Once the use of elephants in its acts had many feeling that the circus will engage in cruelty to animals, ticket sales fell dramatically, eventually shutting down the circus. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Since many felt that, by using elephants in its acts, the circus was engaging in cruelty to animals, ticket sales fell dramatically, and the circus eventually shut down. 5. [ ] (E) Because many felt that, by using elephants in its acts, the circus is engaging in cruelty to animals, ticket sales fell dramatically, with the circus eventually shut down. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy The correct version of the sentence will use tenses in ways such that all of the events mentioned are presented in a logical timeline, and the correct version will be otherwise logical and grammatically correct.
(A) As many feel that elephants used in its acts are the circus engaging in cruelty to animals, the fall in ticket sales was dramatic, with the circus eventually shutting down. Context clues, such as the use of the past tense “was” and the fact that the circus has shut down, indicate that these events occurred in the past. Therefore, the use of the present tense “feel” and “are engaging” in discussing actions engaged in by the circus does not make sense.
Also, this version conveys the illogical meaning that people feel that elephants are the circus engaging in cruelty to animals.
(B) With many feeling that the use of elephants in the circus’s acts engaged in cruelty to animals, ticket sales fell dramatically and eventually shut the circus down. Since it makes sense that all the events mentioned would have occurred in the past, this version’s use of the past tense in all clauses makes sense.
However, this version conveys the illogical meaning that the “use of elephants” itself “engaged in cruelty to animals.” The use of elephants may constitute or involve cruelty to animals, but would not “engage in” cruelty to animals.
Also, this version conveys the illogical meaning that “ticket sales … shut the circus down.”
(C) Once the use of elephants in its acts had many feeling that the circus will engage in cruelty to animals, ticket sales fell dramatically, eventually shutting down the circus. Context clues, such as the use of the past tense “fell” and the fact that the circus has been shut down, indicate that these events occurred in the past. Therefore, the use of the future tense “will engage” in discussing an action engaged in by the circus does not make sense.
Also, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that, by falling, ticket sales shut down the circus.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Since many felt that, by using elephants in its acts, the circus was engaging in cruelty to animals, ticket sales fell dramatically, and the circus eventually shut down. Since it makes sense that all the events mentioned would have occurred in the past, this version’s use of the past tense in all clauses makes sense.
Also, all aspects of the cause-and-effect scenario presented in this version make sense.
(E) Because many felt that, by using elephants in its acts, the circus is engaging in cruelty to animals, ticket sales fell dramatically, with the circus eventually shut down. Context clues, such as the use of the past tense “felt” and “fell” and the fact that the circus has shut down, indicate that these events occurred in the past. Therefore, the use of the present tense “is engaging” in discussing actions engaged in by the circus does not make sense.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 2 Previously, Pittsburgh was known for having a sky so polluted with smoke that the sun was not visible, though currently, the city, having spent the last several decades reinventing itself, is considered one of America’s most livable cities. 1. [x] (A) Previously, Pittsburgh was known for having a sky so polluted with smoke that the sun was not visible, though currently, the city, having spent the last several decades reinventing itself, is 2. [ ] (B) Unlike previously, when Pittsburgh is known for having a sky so polluted with smoke that the sun was not visible, currently, the city, which has spent the last several decades reinventing itself, was > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Previously, Pittsburgh is known for having a sky so polluted with smoke that the sun is not visible; however, currently, the city spent the last several decades reinventing itself and is > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Whereas, previously, Pittsburgh was known for having a sky so polluted with smoke that the sun was not visible, currently, the city that has spent the last several decades reinventing itself was > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Although previously, Pittsburgh, known for having a sky so polluted with smoke that the sun was not visible, currently, the city, spending the last several decades reinventing itself, is > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy In this question, the answer choices contain adverb context clues “previously,” which indicates that events discussed occurred in the past, and “currently,” which indicates that events discussed are occurring in the present.
So, the tenses of the main verbs of the clauses that follow these adverbs must fit these adverbs, and other verb forms in the sentence must then work with the main verbs of the clauses to express a coherent a logical timeline.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Previously, Pittsburgh was known for having a sky so polluted with smoke that the sun was not visible, though currently, the city, having spent the last several decades reinventing itself, is considered one of America’s most livable cities.
In this version, the past tense “Pittsburgh was known” and “the sun was not visible” work with the adverb “previously,” and the present tense “the city … is considered” works with the adverb “currently.”
Also, the present participial phrase “having spent the last several decades reinventing itself” works logically with “the city … is considered.”
(B) Unlike previously, when Pittsburgh is known for having a sky so polluted with smoke that the sun was not visible, currently, the city, which has spent the last several decades reinventing itself, was considered one of America’s most livable cities.
The present tense “Pittsburgh is known” does not work with the adverb “previously,” which indicates that the events discussed occurred, not in the present, but in the past.
Also, the past tense “the city … was” does not work with “currently,” since “currently” indicates that the events discussed are occurring in the present.
(C) Previously, Pittsburgh is known for having a sky so polluted with smoke that the sun is not visible; however, currently, the city spent the last several decades reinventing itself and is considered one of America’s most livable cities.
The present tense “Pittsburgh is known” and “the sun is” do not work with the adverb “previously,” which indicates that the events discussed occurred, not in the present, but in the past.
Also, the past tense “the city spent” does not work with the adverb “currently,” as “currently” indicates that the events discussed are occurring in the present.
(D) Whereas, previously, Pittsburgh was known for having a sky so polluted with smoke that the sun was not visible, currently, the city that has spent the last several decades reinventing itself was considered one of America’s most livable cities.
The past tense “Pittsburgh was known” and “the sun was” work with the adverb “previously.”
However, the past tense “the city was” does not work with the adverb “currently,” since “currently” indicates that the events discussed are occurring in the present.
Also, the restrictive modifier “that has spent the last several decades reinventing itself” specifies a city without indicating that that city is Pittsburgh, and as a result, the sentence illogically seems to be about two different cities, Pittsburgh and “the city that has spent the last several decades reinventing itself.”
(E) Although previously, Pittsburgh, known for having a sky so polluted with smoke that the sun was not visible, currently, the city, spending the last several decades reinventing itself, is considered one of America’s most livable cities.
The structure of this version is flawed, as we can see by removing the nonessential modifier “known for having … visible.” When we do so, we get the following:
Although previously, Pittsburgh, currently, the city, spending the last several decades reinventing itself, is considered …
This structure has two subjects, “Pittsburgh” and “the city” for one verb “is considered.” It also illogically employs two adverbs, “previously” and “currently,” to open one clause.
Also, the present participle “spending” does not work logically with “the last several decades,” since the last several decades are in the past rather than in the present. “Having spent” is required instead.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now move on to the perfect tenses: present perfect, past perfect, and future perfect.
summary:
Lesson Content In addition to verb context clues, GMAT questions will provide us with all sorts of other helpful clues that will allow us to rule out sentence versions with verb tenses that can’t possibly work.
Consider the following sentence versions:
Incorrect: In the past, Rome’s senate experiences high levels of corruption.
Incorrect: In the past, Rome’s senate will experience high levels of corruption.
Correct: In the past, Rome’s senate experienced high levels of corruption.
title: 6.5.2 VTM Strategy Two: Use Other Context Clues to Eliminate Versions with Tenses that Can’t Work

contents:
Lesson Content When an event began in the past and continues into the present, we use the present perfect tense.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an action began in the past and continues into the present, we use the present perfect tense
The present perfect tense is formed by combining “has” or “have” with the past participle of the main verb.
I have lived here for five years.
The above sentence conveys that the event “lived” began in the past but is still happening in the present moment.
Since 1904, the Explorers’ Club has served as a meeting point and unifying force for explorers and scientists worldwide.
The use of the present perfect “has served” indicates that the action, which began in 1904, is still occurring today.
She has won the gold medal in the school spelling bee twice.
Notice that the present perfect “has won” is used in describing the accomplishment of winning the school spelling bee.
He has been to Istanbul six times.
In the sentence above, the present perfect tense is used to describe past actions that have implications in the present moment.
She has wrecked the car again.
In this final example, the present perfect tense is used to emphasize that, although the wreck happened in the past, it has implications in the present moment.
💡MUST KNOW:
Present perfect tense = “have” or “has” + past participle.
Just as we saw with the simple past, present, and future tenses, commonly used context clues in a sentence will tell us if we must use (or can’t use) the present perfect tense.
💡MUST KNOW:
Context clues in a sentence will tell us if we must use (or can’t use) the present perfect tense.
Let’s talk about these now.
summary:
Lesson Content When an event began in the past and continues into the present, we use the present perfect tense.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an action began in the past and continues into the present, we use the present perfect tense
The present perfect tense is formed by combining “has” or “have” with the past participle of the main verb.
I have lived here for five years.
The above sentence conveys that the event “lived” began in the past but is still happening in the present moment.
title: 6.6 Present Perfect Tense

contents:
Lesson Content On the GMAT, when “since” is used to convey that a subject has engaged in an action from the past to the present, the present perfect tense must be used.
💡MUST KNOW:
When “since” is used to convey that a subject has engaged in an action from the past to the present, the present perfect tense must be used.
Consider the examples below.
She has worked with a vocal coach ever since her early years as a singer.
The subject is no longer in her early years as a singer.
Since the day she met Bernard, she has been happier than ever.
The sentence above describes the subject’s current mood, which began on a day in the past.
So, how do we put this knowledge into action on test day? We eliminate any sentence version that uses “since” to convey that a subject has engaged in an action from the past to the present YET does not use the present perfect tense.
Key Strategy: Eliminate any sentence version that uses “since” to convey that a subject has engaged in an action from the past to the present YET does not use the present perfect tense.
Let’s continue practicing using context clues to eliminate sentences that can’t be correct:
Incorrect: Since going to that seminar last week, he was much more motivated to improve his health.
Incorrect: Since going to that seminar last week, he will be much more motivated to improve his health.
Correct: Since going to that seminar last week, he has been much more motivated to improve his health.
In sentence version one, the past tense “was” does not make sense because “was” describes an action that ended in the past, but the sentence is attempting to describe something that continues in the present.
Similarly, the second sentence version is broken because the future tense “will be” describes an action that has not yet started, but the sentence is attempting to describe something that continues in the present.
The third sentence version works well because “since” indicates that the subject began to be motivated in the past and continues to be motivated in the present.
Let’s continue to practice eliminating sentence versions with verb tenses that can’t be correct:
Incorrect: The food served at the restaurant became much more interesting since the head chef returned from her research trip to Asia.
Incorrect: The food served at the restaurant will become much more interesting since the head chef returned from her research trip to Asia.
Correct: The food served at the restaurant has become much more interesting since the head chef returned from her research trip to Asia.
Sentence version one uses “since” to attempt to tell us that the food began to become more interesting at the point in time when the chef returned and that this development continues into the present, but it illogically also uses the past tense “became,” which conveys the food’s getting more interesting has already ended.
Sentence version two is also illogical because the use of the future tense “will become” describes something that has not yet happened.
Sentence version three works well because “has become” works logically with “since” to convey that the food began to become more interesting at the point in time when the chef returned and that this development continues into the present.
Targeted Practice Determined whether each sentence is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery:
Let’s practice applying what we just learned in answering Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 3 Since he was nine years old, Hank is dreaming of meeting his favorite basketball player, so now that he is finally going to, it is no surprise that he is nervous. 1. [ ] (A) Since he was nine years old, Hank is dreaming > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Since nine years old, Hank dreamed > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Since he was nine years old, Hank had dreamed > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Since nine years old, Hank is dreaming > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Since he was nine years old, Hank has dreamed View Solution Question Level: easy Remember, when “since” is used to convey that a subject has engaged in an action “from the past point in time when” and continues to engage in that action in the present, it makes sense to use the present perfect tense.
(A) Since he was nine years old, Hank is dreaming of meeting his favorite basketball player, so now that he is finally going to, it is no surprise that he is nervous.
This version illogically uses the present continuous “is dreaming” in describing an action that Hank has engaged in “since he was nine years old.”
Notice that the keyword “since” and the past tense verb “was” tell us that the action “dreaming” started in the past. On the other hand, the present continuous “is dreaming” tells us that the action is currently ongoing; it does not make any connection to the past starting point when “he was nine years old.”
(B) Since nine years old, Hank dreamed of meeting his favorite basketball player, so now that he is finally going to, it is no surprise that he is nervous.
“Since” means “from the past point in time when,” but “nine years old” is not a past point in time. “Nine years old” is a modifier used to modify a noun in an expression such as “he was nine years old.” So, “since nine years old” does not make sense.
Additionally, the simple past tense “dreamed” does not capture the logical meaning that Hank’s dreaming began at a past point and continues to the present, which meaning would be in line with “now that he is finally going to, it is no surprise that he is nervous.” “Dreamed” communicates that Hank dreamed of meeting his favorite basketball player in the past but no longer does.
(C) Since he was nine years old, Hank had dreamed of meeting his favorite basketball player, so now that he is finally going to, it is no surprise that he is nervous.
The use of the past perfect tense “had dreamed” does not make sense in this context.
The past perfect “had dreamed” indicates that, at some point in the past, Hank dreamed of meeting his favorite basketball player and that he no longer dreams of meeting his favorite basketball player.
Of course, if he no longer dreams of meeting his favorite basketball player, then the sentence is illogical. After all, the sentence says that “it is no surprise that he is nervous,” but the fact that Hank had dreamed of meeting his favorite basketball player but no longer does would not be a reason for him to be nervous “now that he is finally going to” meet him.
(D) Since nine years old, Hank is dreaming of meeting his favorite basketball player, so now that he is finally going to, it is no surprise that he is nervous.
“Since” means “from the past point in time when,” but “nine years old” is not a past point in time. “Nine years old” is a modifier used to modify a noun in an expression such as “he was nine years old.” So, “since nine years old” does not make sense.
Additionally, this version illogically uses the present continuous “is dreaming” in describing an action that Hank has engaged in “since nine years old.”
Notice that “since nine years old” seems meant to indicate that the action “dreaming” started in the past. However, present continuous tense tells us that the action is currently ongoing; it does not make any connection to any past starting point.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Since he was nine years old, Hank has dreamed of meeting his favorite basketball player, so now that he is finally going to, it is no surprise that he is nervous.
“Since he was nine years old” makes sense. “Since” means “from the past point in time when,” and when “he was nine years old” is a point in time. So, “since” and “he was nine years old” work logically together.
Also, this version correctly uses the present perfect tense “has dreamed” to indicate that an action began in the past, “since he was nine years old,” and continues in the present, which meaning is in line with “now that he is finally going to, it is no surprise that he is nervous.”
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 4 Since the early weeks of 1970, conspiracy theorists and alien enthusiasts are debating the authenticity of audio recordings released by NASA, which supposedly show that astronauts on the far side of the moon, the side of the moon beyond radio contact with Earth, in May 1969, listening to unexplained music, potentially of extra-terrestrial origin. 1. [ ] (A) enthusiasts are debating the authenticity of audio recordings released by NASA, which supposedly show that > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) enthusiasts have debated the authenticity of audio recordings released by NASA, which supposedly show 3. [ ] (C) enthusiasts, debating the authenticity of audio recordings released by NASA, supposedly have shown > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) enthusiasts debated the authenticity of audio recordings released by NASA and supposedly showed > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) enthusiasts have debated the authenticity of audio recordings released by NASA, supposedly showing > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium (A) Since the early weeks of 1970, conspiracy theorists and alien enthusiasts are debating the authenticity of audio recordings released by NASA, which supposedly show that astronauts on the far side of the moon, the side of the moon beyond radio contact with Earth, in May 1969, listening to unexplained music, potentially of extra-terrestrial origin.
The use of the present continuous “are debating” does not make sense in this context because, while the action “debating” may be ongoing in the present, the sentence indicates that the debating began at a point in time in the past, “the early weeks of 1970.” The present continuous, which indicates that an event is ongoing in the present, does not match a timeframe that began in the past.
Also, the presence of “that” renders the end of the sentence illogical. “Recordings …, which … show that” should be followed by a clause describing what the recordings show, but, in this case, only a noun, “astronauts,” and modifiers that modify “astronauts” follow “show that.” So, basically the sentence says “recordings show that astronauts” without a verb that expresses what the astronauts did. “Listening,” a participle, cannot on its own serve as a main verb and simply modifies “astronauts.”
YOUR CHOICE(B) Since the early weeks of 1970, conspiracy theorists and alien enthusiasts have debated the authenticity of audio recordings released by NASA, which supposedly show astronauts on the far side of the moon, the side of the moon beyond radio contact with Earth, in May 1969, listening to unexplained music, potentially of extra-terrestrial origin.
Because it makes sense that the conspiracy theorists and alien enthusiasts would have debated the authenticity of the recordings from the early weeks of 1970 to the present, the use of the present perfect “have debated” makes sense.
Also, since it makes sense that the recordings would still exist in the present and still show astronauts on the far side of the moon listening to music, the use of the present tense “show” makes sense.
Finally, “which supposedly show …” correctly modifies the phrase “audio recordings released by NASA.”
(C) Since the early weeks of 1970, conspiracy theorists and alien enthusiasts, debating the authenticity of audio recordings released by NASA, supposedly have shown astronauts on the far side of the moon, the side of the moon beyond radio contact with Earth, in May 1969, listening to unexplained music, potentially of extra-terrestrial origin.
While this version is grammatically correct. However, it expresses a meaning that does not make sense, as we can see by combining this answer choice with the non-underlined portion of the sentence:
Since the early weeks of 1970, conspiracy theorists and alien enthusiasts, debating the authenticity of audio recordings released by NASA, supposedly have shown astronauts on the far side of the moon … in May 1969
Notice that this version conveys the meaning that, in the process of debating the authenticity of audio recordings, conspiracy theorists and alien enthusiasts have shown astronauts on the far side of the moon in May 1969. People would not show astronauts by debating, and would not, by debating since the early weeks of 1970, show astronauts on the far side of the moon in May 1969.
(D) Since the early weeks of 1970, conspiracy theorists and alien enthusiasts debated the authenticity of audio recordings released by NASA and supposedly showed astronauts on the far side of the moon, the side of the moon beyond radio contact with Earth, in May 1969, listening to unexplained music, potentially of extra-terrestrial origin.
The use of the simple past “debated” with “since the early weeks of 1970” is not correct. “Since the early weeks of 1970” sets the beginning of a timeframe that began in 1970 and extents to the present. On the other hand, “debated” conveys that the debating went on only in the past, not up to the present time. So, the simple past tense “debated” does not work logically with “since the early weeks of 1970.”
Also, the sentence conveys the illogical meaning that, since the early weeks of 1970, the conspiracy theorists and alien enthusiasts showed astronauts on the dark side of the moon in 1969.
(E) Since the early weeks of 1970, conspiracy theorists and alien enthusiasts have debated the authenticity of audio recordings released by NASA, supposedly showing astronauts on the far side of the moon, the side of the moon beyond radio contact with Earth, in May 1969, listening to unexplained music, potentially of extra-terrestrial origin.
The participial phrase “supposedly showing …” at the end of the sentence set off from the rest of the sentence by a comma would most logically take the subject of the sentence, “conspiracy theorists and alien enthusiasts,” as its agent. Therefore, the sentence expresses the nonsensical meaning that, by debating the authenticity of audio recordings since the early weeks of 1970, conspiracy theorists and alien enthusiasts have shown astronauts on the far side of the moon in 1969.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 5 According to scientists at NASA’s Goddard Institute for Space Studies, Earth’s average global temperature, as a critical measure of Earth’s ability to sustain the existence of its many species, has increased by roughly 1.4 degrees Fahrenheit since about 1900; nearly two-thirds of that increase have occurred since 1975. 1. [ ] (A) as a critical measure of Earth’s ability to sustain the existence of its many species, has increased by roughly 1.4 degrees Fahrenheit since about 1900; nearly two-thirds of that increase have occurred > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) being a critical measure of Earth’s ability to sustain the existence of its many species, increased by roughly 1.4 degrees Fahrenheit since about 1900, nearly two-thirds of which occurred > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) which is as critical as a measure of Earth’s ability to sustain the existence of its many species, increased by roughly 1.4 degrees Fahrenheit since about 1900; nearly two-thirds of the increase occurred > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) a critical measure of Earth’s ability to sustain the existence of its many species, has increased by roughly 1.4 degrees Fahrenheit since about 1900, nearly two-thirds of the increase having occurred 5. [ ] (E) which is critical when measuring Earth’s ability to sustain the existence of its many species, increased by roughly 1.4 degrees Fahrenheit since about 1900, with nearly two-thirds of that increase occurring > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium (A) According to scientists at NASA’s Goddard Institute for Space Studies, Earth’s average global temperature, as a critical measure of Earth’s ability to sustain the existence of its many species, has increased by roughly 1.4 degrees Fahrenheit since about 1900; nearly two-thirds of that increase have occurred since 1975.
While “two-thirds” may seem plural, since what “two-thirds” refers to is “two-thirds” of the increase, which is singular, “two-thirds” should be paired with a singular verb, not the plural “have.” When a fraction of something is a subject, whether that fraction should be paired with a singular verb or a plural verb depends on what the fraction is a fraction of. If it’s a fraction of something singular, then it needs a singular verb, which, in this case, would be “has.”
A more difficult error to see it that the use of the expression “as a critical measure …” does not make sense. By using that expression, the sentence suggests that the increasing of the average global temperature has been somehow tied to or conditioned upon the temperature’s being a critical measure. What makes sense is that the temperature has increased and that, as a separate matter, the temperature is a critical measure. Saying that the temperature has increased “as a critical measure” suggests that the temperature’s being a critical measure has a significance that it does not have.
(B) According to scientists at NASA’s Goddard Institute for Space Studies, Earth’s average global temperature, being a critical measure of Earth’s ability to sustain the existence of its many species, increased by roughly 1.4 degrees Fahrenheit since about 1900, nearly two-thirds of which occurred since 1975.
The pronoun, “which” has no logical referent, since the sentence does not mention the temperature increase in the form of a noun, and therefore there is nothing in the sentence to which “which” could refer that would have “occurred since 1975.”
The use of the simple past “increased” and “occurred” is not correct. “Since about 1900” and “since 1975” indicate that the events occurred over periods of time that began in the past and extend to the present, whereas “increased” and “occurred” convey that the events occurred only in the past, not up to the present. So, “increased” and “occurred” do not work logically with “since about 1900” and “since 1975.”
Finally, by using the participial phrase “being a critical measure …,” the sentence conveys the illogical meaning that, as a result of “being a critical measure,” the average global temperature increased.
(C) According to scientists at NASA’s Goddard Institute for Space Studies, Earth’s average global temperature, which is as critical as a measure of Earth’s ability to sustain the existence of its many species, increased by roughly 1.4 degrees Fahrenheit since about 1900; nearly two-thirds of the increase occurred since 1975.
By saying,“The average global temperature is as critical as a measure of Earth’s ability …,” this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the average global temperate is “as critical as,” i.e., as important as, “a measure of Earth’s ability to sustain the existence of its many species.” Since the average global temperature is itself “a measure of Earth’s ability to sustain the existence of its many species,” this version conveys, essentially, that the average global temperature is as critical as itself.
Also, the use of the simple past “increased” and “occurred” is not correct. “Since about 1900” and “since 1975” indicate that the events occurred over periods of time that began in the past and extend to the present, whereas “increased” and “occurred” convey that the events occurred only in the past, not up to the present. So, “increased” and “occurred” do not work logically with “since about 1900” and “since 1975.”
YOUR CHOICE(D) According to scientists at NASA’s Goddard Institute for Space Studies, Earth’s average global temperature, a critical measure of Earth’s ability to sustain the existence of its many species, has increased by roughly 1.4 degrees Fahrenheit since about 1900, nearly two-thirds of the increase having occurred since 1975.
This version uses the appositive “a critical measure of Earth’s ability …” to logically describe “the average global temperature” as “a critical measure ….”
Also, it uses the present perfect “has increased” with “since about 1900” and uses “having occurred” with “since 1975” to effectively indicate that the events described have been occurring over time since the points in time mentioned.
(E) According to scientists at NASA’s Goddard Institute for Space Studies, Earth’s average global temperature, which is critical when measuring Earth’s ability to sustain the existence of its many species, increased by roughly 1.4 degrees Fahrenheit since about 1900, with nearly two-thirds of that increase occurring since 1975.
This choice could be tempting because “which is critical …” sounds right. Notice, however, that the meaning conveyed is illogical. The average global temperature may be an important, or “critical,” measure. However, the average global temperature “is critical when measuring” conveys the meaning that, when the average global temperature is measuring, it is critical, suggesting that, when it is not measuring, it is not critical.
Also, the use of the simple past “increased” is not correct. “Since about 1900” indicates that the event occurred over a period of time that began in the past and extends to the present, whereas “increased” conveys that the event occurred only in the past, not up to the present. So, “increased” does not work logically with “since about 1900.”
Correct Answer: (D)
summary:
Lesson Content On the GMAT, when “since” is used to convey that a subject has engaged in an action from the past to the present, the present perfect tense must be used.
💡MUST KNOW:
When “since” is used to convey that a subject has engaged in an action from the past to the present, the present perfect tense must be used.
Consider the examples below.
She has worked with a vocal coach ever since her early years as a singer.
title: 6.6.1 When We Must Use the Present Perfect Tense

contents:
Lesson Content When a sentence describes an event that has clearly ended in the past, the use of the present perfect is not correct.
💡MUST KNOW:
When a sentence describes an event that clearly ended in the past, the present perfect is not correct.
The reason is that, as we know, the present perfect tense describes an action that began in the past and continues to be happening in the present or continues to be affecting the present, so if the action ended in the past, using the present perfect is illogical.
💡MUST KNOW:
If an action clearly ended in the past, using the present perfect is illogical.
How do we put this knowledge into action on test day? When we encounter a sentence version that describes an event that clearly ended in the past but also uses the present perfect tense to describe that action, eliminate that sentence version!
Key Strategy: When a sentence version that describes an event that clearly ended in the past but also uses the present perfect tense to describe that action, eliminate that sentence version!
Let’s practice with some example sentences:
Incorrect: Last year, the board has met six times.
Correct: Last year, the board met six times.
Sentence version one does not work because the present perfect tense describes an action began in the past and continues into the present, but the context clue “last year” makes it clear that the action of the board meeting has ended.
Sentence version two correctly uses the past tense verb “met,” which indicates to us that the board met in the past, but no longer does so.
Let’s try another:
Incorrect: Before Marty and Scott won the championship, they have visualized success.
Correct: Before Marty and Scott won the championship, they visualized success.
Sentence version one can’t work.
Sentence version two correctly uses the past tense verb “visualized” to express that Marty and Scott’s visualization ended in the past.
Targeted Practice Determined whether each sentence is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now try some concept mastery.
Let’s now discuss when the present perfect is not mandatory, but common.

The reason is that, as we know, the present perfect tense describes an action that began in the past and continues to be happening in the present or continues to be affecting the present, so if the action ended in the past, using the present perfect is illogical.
title: 6.6.2 Situations in Which the Present Perfect Tense Can’t Be Used

contents:
Lesson Content There are a few other words that are clues indicating that the use of the present perfect tense may make sense.
For example:
Native Americans have lived in what is now the United States for at least the last 15,000 years.
Because Ale has lived in Vietnam for more than two years, she is fluent in Vietnamese.
Over the last one million years, human civilizations have undergone evolutionary change.
Over the course of last few days, it has become clear the people in this office have become a team.
During the semester, Juan has gone snorkeling in Mexico many times.
During this presidential election, our country has lost some of its cohesion.
💡MUST KNOW:
The words “for,” “over,” and “during” are clues indicating that the use of the present perfect may make sense.
Let’s now discuss the past perfect tense.
summary:
Lesson Content There are a few other words that are clues indicating that the use of the present perfect tense may make sense.
For example:
Native Americans have lived in what is now the United States for at least the last 15,000 years.
Because Ale has lived in Vietnam for more than two years, she is fluent in Vietnamese.
Over the last one million years, human civilizations have undergone evolutionary change.
title: 6.6.3 When the Present Perfect Is Not Mandatory, but Common

contents:
Lesson Content The past perfect tense is used in describing a past action that occurred before another past action.
The past perfect tense = “had” + the past participle of a verb.
For example, “had been,” ”had eaten,” and “had performed” are all in the past perfect.
💡MUST KNOW:
The past perfect tense is used with the action that occurred deeper in the past, and the simple past tense is used with the action that occurred in the more recent past.
Consider the following sentence:
The tour guide directed the boat toward a location where whales had surfaced.
It’s important to see that both events described in the sentence occurred in the past, but one of the events occurred before the other.
Notice that the past perfect “had surfaced” indicates that the whales surfaced before the past action “the tour guide directed the boat.”
Let’s consider another example:
She skipped the popcorn at the movies because she had eaten a big supper.
Here, the past perfect tense is used to let us know which of the two actions described in the sentence, her skipping and her eating, happened first.
Let’s look at another example:
The mayor had done surprisingly little to address city residents’ call for the reclamation of brownfields and was not reelected for a second term.
Here, the use of the past perfect tense makes clear the sequence of events and their cause-and-effect nature.
So how do we put this knowledge in action on test day? When we encounter the past perfect tense, we check to be sure that it is used correctly and that it makes sense given the context clues provided in the sentence.
Consider the following sentence:
Incorrect: The travel restrictions that were in place for nearly sixty years were finally repealed.
Correct: The travel restrictions that had been in place for nearly sixty years were finally repealed.
Sentence version one doesn’t make sense.
Sentence version two works well because the past perfect “had been” indicates that the nearly sixty-year period preceded the relaxing of the restrictions, which also occurred in the past.
Consider the following sentence:
Incorrect: Although the Green Party sank millions of dollars into its candidate, it lost the election, garnering less than two percent of the votes.
Correct: Although the Green Party had sunk millions of dollars into its candidate, it lost the election, garnering less than two percent of the votes.
We can eliminate sentence version one because it conveys that the investment of millions of dollars (sank) and the election (lost) occurred at the same time.
Notice that, in using the past perfect “had sunk” and the simple past “lost,” sentence version two correctly establishes the fact that there are two past actions, one in the deeper past and one in the more recent past.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Choose the tense that best fits each sentence. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now discuss a past perfect-related strategy.
