The past perfect tense = “had” + the past participle of a verb.
For example, “had been,” ”had eaten,” and “had performed” are all in the past perfect.
💡MUST KNOW:
The past perfect tense is used with the action that occurred deeper in the past, and the simple past tense is used with the action that occurred in the more recent past.
title: 6.7 The Past Perfect Tense

contents:
Lesson Content SC questions may try to trick us into choosing wrong answers that reverse the correct placement of the past perfect tense.
💡MUST KNOW:
SC questions may try to trick us into choosing wrong answers that reverse the correct placement of the past perfect tense. These questions, which have two past actions mentioned, utilize the past perfect tense with the more recent past action.
For example:
Incorrect: She had skipped the popcorn at the movies because she ate a big supper.
Notice that the correct placement of the past perfect tense has been reversed, creating a sentence that is illogical.
Incorrect: The mayor did surprisingly little to address city residents’ call for the reclamation of brownfields and had not been reelected for a second term.
Here again, notice that the correct placement of the past perfect tense has been reversed, creating a sentence that is illogical.
We can use this knowledge to score points on test day by eliminating any sentence version that reverses the correct placement of the past perfect tense.
Key Strategy: Eliminate any sentence version that reverses the correct placement of the past perfect tense.
Let’s give this strategy a try:
Incorrect: Although she missed some of the conferences, she had been able to negotiate an increase in compensation.
Correct: Although she had missed some of the conferences, she was able to negotiate an increase in compensation.
We can easily eliminate sentence version one because she must have missed the conferences before negotiating an increase in compensation.
Sentence version two, however, correctly places the past perfect “had missed” with the action further back in time and the simple past “was” with the more recent past action.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether the sentence is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Consider the following examples:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 6 The little girl was ecstatic when her grandfather presented her with the completed dollhouse, which he had spent months building, on her sixth birthday, but she hardly plays with it anymore. 1. [x] (A) dollhouse, which he had spent months building, on her sixth birthday 2. [ ] (B) dollhouse that he was building for months on her sixth birthday > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) dollhouse on her sixth birthday, he built it for months > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) dollhouse, which he has spent months building, on her sixth birthday > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) dollhouse on her sixth birthday, which he has built for months > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy YOUR CHOICE(A) The little girl was ecstatic when her grandfather presented her with the completed dollhouse, which he had spent months building, on her sixth birthday , but she hardly plays with it anymore.
The past tense verbs “was” and “presented” tell us that “was ecstatic” and “presented her with” are past events.
So, the use of the past perfect tense “had spent” makes sense in describing an event that occurred before those past events: her grandfather spent months building the dollhouse before he presented it to her and before she was ecstatic. The use of the past perfect “had spent” makes that time sequence clear.
Notice also that the relative clause “which he had spent months building” logically modifies the noun “dollhouse.”
(B) The little girl was ecstatic when her grandfather presented her with the completed dollhouse that he was building for months on her sixth birthday , but she hardly plays with it anymore.
It does not make sense to say that “her grandfather presented her with the completed dollhouse that he was building,” since “was building” indicates that he was still building the dollhouse at the time that he presented it to her, but the modifier “completed” tells us that he was not.
Also, the modifier following the noun “dollhouse,” “that he was building for months on her sixth birthday,” conveys the illogical meaning that the little girl’s grandfather was building a dollhouse for months on her sixth birthday. Of course, one cannot build something “for months” on a single day.
This structure is subtly different from the structure in (A). In (A), “which he had spent months building” is set off from the sentence by commas, and a comma separates “which he had spent months building” from “on her sixth birthday.” Thus, in (A), “which he had spent months building” is not connected to “on her sixth birthday” the way “that he was building for months” is connected to “on her sixth birthday” in (B).
(C) The little girl was ecstatic when her grandfather presented her with the completed dollhouse on her sixth birthday, he built it for months , but she hardly plays with it anymore.
This version connects two independent clauses using only a comma and, thus, is a run-on sentence. The independent clauses “The little girl was ecstatic when her grandfather presented her with the completed dollhouse on her sixth birthday” and “he built it for months …” should be joined by a semicolon or by a comma followed by a conjunction such as “and.”
Furthermore, the use of the simple past “built” doesn’t make sense. The girl’s grandfather’s building the dollhouse must have preceded his presenting it to her, but by using the simple past “built” and the simple past “presented,” the sentence conveys that these two events occurred at the same time.
(D) The little girl was ecstatic when her grandfather presented her with the completed dollhouse, which he has spent months building, on her sixth birthday , but she hardly plays with it anymore.
The use of the present perfect “has spent” does not make sense in this context.
The present perfect is used to communicate that an event began in the past and continues in the present. However, there are several clues in the sentence that tell us that the grandfather is no longer building the dollhouse. So, that event is not continuing in the present.
For one, the dollhouse is described as “completed” at the time that her grandfather “presented” (past tense) it to the little girl. Of course, the building of the dollhouse would not be ongoing in the present if the dollhouse was completed in the past.
Also, the past tense verbs “was” and “presented” tell us that the little girl’s sixth birthday was a past event, as does the fact that “she hardly plays with it (the dollhouse) anymore.” Thus, “has spent,” which indicates that the grandfather is still building the dollhouse, does not make sense in the context of what the rest of the sentence says.
(E) The little girl was ecstatic when her grandfather presented her with the completed dollhouse on her sixth birthday, which he has built for months , but she hardly plays with it anymore.
The use of the present perfect verb “has built” does not make sense. The present perfect is used to indicate that an event began in the past and continues in the present, but there are several clues in the sentence that tell us that the grandfather is no longer building the dollhouse. So, that event is not continuing in the present.
For one, the dollhouse is described as “completed” at the time that her grandfather “presented” (past tense) it to the little girl. Of course, the building of the dollhouse would not be ongoing in the present if the dollhouse was completed in the past.
Also, the past tense verbs “was” and “presented” tell us that the little girl’s sixth birthday was a past event, as does the fact that “she hardly plays with it (the dollhouse) anymore.” Thus, “has built,” which indicates that the grandfather is still building the dollhouse, does not make sense in the context of what the rest of the sentence says.
Furthermore, the placement of “which he has built …” directly after “sixth birthday” results in the sentence’s seeming to convey the nonsensical meaning that the little girl’s grandfather has built her sixth birthday for months. Notice that “on her sixth birthday” does not modify “dollhouse.” So, it does not make sense to say that “which he has built …” modifies the entirety of “dollhouse on her sixth birthday,” because “dollhouse” and “on her sixth birthday” are conceptually separate, not one noun phrase.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss another type of situation in which the past perfect must be used.
summary:
Lesson Content SC questions may try to trick us into choosing wrong answers that reverse the correct placement of the past perfect tense.
💡MUST KNOW:
SC questions may try to trick us into choosing wrong answers that reverse the correct placement of the past perfect tense. These questions, which have two past actions mentioned, utilize the past perfect tense with the more recent past action.
For example:
Incorrect: She had skipped the popcorn at the movies because she ate a big supper.
title: 6.7.1 Eliminate Sentences that Reverse the Correct Position of the Past Perfect Tense

contents:
Lesson Content A sentence must use the past perfect tense when the sentence is about an action or event completed by a specified date or time period in the past.
For example:
By late evening on Election Day, it had become clear that the upstart candidate would soon be the president of the country.
Late evening on Election Day is a time in the past, and we have an event, becoming, that was completed before that time.
💡MUST KNOW:
The past perfect tense must be used to indicate that an action or event was completed before a specified date or time period in the past.
How can we leverage this knowledge on test day? We can eliminate any sentence version that indicates that an action or event was completed before a specified date or time period in the past but does not correctly use the past perfect tense.
Key Strategy: Eliminate any sentence version that indicates that an action or event was completed before a specified date or time period in the past but does not correctly use the past perfect tense.
Let’s give this a try:
Incorrect: By 1910, everyone learned the jitterbug.
Correct: By 1910, everyone had learned the jitterbug.
We can quickly eliminate sentence version one because the sentence is about an action or event completed by a specified date or time period in the past, but it incorrectly uses the simple past “learned” instead of the past perfect “had learned.”
Sentence version two correctly uses the past perfect tense in describing an action or event completed by a specified date or time period in the past.
Let’s try another:
Incorrect: By the end of the month, all of the foreign troops left the war-torn nation.
Correct: By the end of the month, all of the foreign troops had left the war-torn nation.
We can quickly eliminate sentence version one because the sentence is about an action or event completed by a specified date or time period in the past, but it incorrectly uses the simple past “left” instead of the past perfect “had left.”
Sentence version two correctly uses the past perfect tense in describing an action or event completed by a specified date or time period in the past.
Be Careful: Not every sentence about something occurring by a time in the past has to use the past perfect.
By 1910, many people from all walks of life were using electric cars.
By the time the music started, the sun was setting.
These sentences are not about actions completed by certain points in time.
The first sentence is about an action, the use of electric cars, that was ongoing, rather than completed, at a certain point in time.
The second sentence is about an event, the setting of the sun, that was ongoing, rather than completed, at a certain point in time.
💡MUST KNOW:
Not every sentence about something occurring by a time in the past has to use the past perfect. If a sentence about something occurring by a time in the past is not about an action or event completed by a time in the past, it can use another tense.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each sentence is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 The polls opened promptly at 7 a.m., but by then, a line stretching around the block formed. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 2 By the time the game was over, many of the people in attendance were falling asleep in their seats. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 While John found taking the test to be exciting and fun, by the time he answered the last question, he was ready to stop and see his scores. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 Jack and Aretha ended up going to the miniature golf course for their date because the eight o’clock movie already started by the time they found a parking spot near the theater. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 5 By 1920, when Prohibition took effect, the American temperance movement was active for nearly 100 years. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 6 Seeing a herd of zebras running across the plain, the photographer was excited to take some shots, but by the time she was ready, they had disappeared from sight. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 7 Although he was largely self-taught, by his teenage years, Srinivasa Ramanujan understood more about math than many people with university degrees in the subject. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
We can tell that the sentence is meant to convey that the long line formed before 7 a.m., since the formation of the line was completed “by then.” So, the past perfect “had formed” is required.
Correct sentence:
The polls opened promptly at 7 a.m., but by then, a line stretching around the block had formed.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
This sentence is not about an action completed by a certain point in time. It’s about an action, “falling asleep,” that was ongoing by a certain point in time, “the time the game was over.” So, the use of the past continuous makes sense.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
This sentence is not about an event completed by a certain point in time. It’s about a person’s state of being, “ready to stop and see his scores,” at a certain point in time. So, the use of the simple past “was” makes sense.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
“By the time” and “already” tell us that first the movie started and then the couple found a parking spot and went to the golf course. Since the starting of the movie was completed by the point in time mentioned, when they found a parking space, the past perfect “had … started” should be used.
Correct sentence:
Jack and Aretha ended up going to the miniature golf course for their date because the eight o’clock movie had already started by the time they found a parking spot near the theater.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
This one is tricky, because, if the sentence did not include the last four words, “for nearly 100 years,” the use of the simple past “was” would be correct, since the sentence would be about the state of being, “active,” of the American temperance movement at a certain point in time.
However, since the sentence does include “for nearly 100 years,” it is about a completed action, the action having been active for nearly 100 years. So, the use of the past perfect “had been” is required.
Correct sentence:
By 1920, when Prohibition took effect, the American temperance movement had been active for nearly 100 years.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
Since the sentence is conveying that the zebras’ disappearing from sight was completed by a certain time, when “she was ready,” the use of the past perfect “had disappeared makes sense.
One thing that may seem odd is that the simple past “was excited” is used in describing an event that began before an action described via the use of the past perfect, “had disappeared.” This choice of tense does work, however, since it makes sense to say that she “was excited” while the other events were occurring.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 7 Explanation:
In this case, Srinivasa Ramanujan’s understanding is not an action that was completed by a certain point in time. Rather, he understood on an ongoing basis. So, the use of the simple past “understood” makes sense.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s practice applying what we just discussed in answering Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 7 By 1864, President Lincoln made public his plan for reconstruction of the Southern States, promising that the United States - at that time known as the “Union” - would legally recognize the government of any state, which promised to emancipate its slaves, pledge support to the Union as the only government of the United States, and fully abide by the Constitution. 1. [ ] (A) President Lincoln made public his plan for reconstruction of the Southern States, promising that the United States - at that time known as the “Union” - would legally recognize the government of any state, which promised > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) when President Lincoln had made public his plan for reconstruction of the Southern States and had promised that the United States - at that time known as the “Union” - would legally recognize the government of any state that had promised > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) President Lincoln had made public his plan for reconstruction of the Southern States, promising that the United States - at that time known as the “Union” - would legally recognize the government of any state that promised 4. [ ] (D) President Lincoln made public his plan for reconstruction of the Southern States and promised that the United States - at that time known as the “Union” – will legally recognize the government of any state, which promised > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) President Lincoln, making public his plan for reconstruction of the Southern States, promised that the United States - at that time known as the “Union” – will legally recognize the government of any state promising > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) By 1864, President Lincoln made public his plan for reconstruction of the Southern States, promising that the United States - at that time known as the “Union” - would legally recognize the government of any state, which promised to emancipate its slaves, pledge support to the Union as the only government of the United States, and fully abide by the Constitution.
Since Lincoln’s making his plan public is an action that was completed by a point in time in the past, 1864, the use of the simple past “made” is not correct. The past perfect “had made” should be used.
Also, the use of the construction “any state, which” does not make sense. As a result of using that construction, the sentence conveys the illogical meaning that Lincoln promised that the Union would recognize the government of any state, and then adds the information that “any state” promised to emancipate its slaves, etc. Notice, “any state” does not refer to a particular state. So, it does not make sense to modify “any state” by using the nonrestrictive “which promised.” To more clearly see why this construction does not work, we can consider a simpler example:
Incorrect: Roberto said that he likes any cat, which is black.
Since there isn’t a particular cat that the sentence is about, it does not make sense to describe the cat.
Now, what would make sense is the following:
The union would recognize any state that promised …
In the above construction, the restrictive modifier “that promised” logically restricts “any state” to those that promised.
(B) By 1864, when President Lincoln had made public his plan for reconstruction of the Southern States and had promised that the United States - at that time known as the “Union” - would legally recognize the government of any state that had promised to emancipate its slaves, pledge support to the Union as the only government of the United States, and fully abide by the Constitution.
In this version, everything that follows “by 1864” is a long modifier modifying “1864.” So, this version has no main clause and is not a complete sentence.
YOUR CHOICE(C) By 1864, President Lincoln had made public his plan for reconstruction of the Southern States, promising that the United States - at that time known as the “Union” - would legally recognize the government of any state that promised to emancipate its slaves, pledge support to the Union as the only government of the United States, and fully abide by the Constitution.
The past perfect “had made” is correctly used in expressing that Lincoln’s making the plan public occurred before a point in time in the past, 1864.
Also, the construction “any state that promised …” is logical because the use of the restrictive modifier “that promised …” to narrow down “any state” to “any state that promised …” makes sense.
(D) By 1864, President Lincoln made public his plan for reconstruction of the Southern States and promised that the United States - at that time known as the “Union” – will legally recognize the government of any state, which promised to emancipate its slaves, pledge support to the Union as the only government of the United States, and fully abide by the Constitution.
Since Lincoln’s making his plan public is an action that was completed by a point in time in the past, 1864, the use of the simple past “made” is not correct. The past perfect “had made” should be used.
Also, the use of the simple future tense “will … recognize” does not make sense. The promise was made in the past about an action to be taken in the past. So, the future in past “would … recognize” should be used.
Also, the use of the construction “any state, which” does not make sense. As a result of using that construction, the sentence conveys the illogical meaning that Lincoln promised that the Union would recognize the government of any state, and then adds the information that “any state” promised to emancipate its slaves, etc. Notice, “any state” does not refer to a particular state. So, it does not make sense to modify “any state” by using the nonrestrictive “which promised.” To more clearly see why this construction does not work, we can consider a simpler example:
Incorrect: Roberto said that he likes any cat, which is black.
Since there isn’t a particular cat that the sentence is about, it does not make sense to describe the cat.
Now, what would make sense is the following:
The union would recognize any state that promised …
In the above construction, the restrictive modifier “that promised” logically restricts “any state” to those that promised.
(E) By 1864, President Lincoln, making public his plan for reconstruction of the Southern States, promised that the United States - at that time known as the “Union” – will legally recognize the government of any state promising to emancipate its slaves, pledge support to the Union as the only government of the United States, and fully abide by the Constitution.
Since Lincoln’s promising to legally recognize is an action that was completed by a point in time in the past, 1864, the use of the simple past “promised” is not correct. The past perfect “had promised” should be used.
Also, the use of the simple future tense “will … recognize” does not make sense. The promise was made in the past about an action to be taken in the past. So, the future in past “would … recognize” should be used.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 8 By 2018, after studying the art and benefits of plant-based cooking, the chef decided to cook slowly marinated vegetables, with many of the restaurant’s guests who consume his food often commenting on how tender and delicious the vegetables are. 1. [ ] (A) decided to cook slowly marinated vegetables, with many of the restaurant’s guests who consume his food often commenting > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) had decided to cook marinated vegetables slowly, resulting in many of the restaurant’s guests consuming his food who often commented > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) has decided to cook marinated vegetables slowly, and many of the restaurant’s guests consuming his food often commented > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) had decided to cook marinated vegetables slowly, and many of the restaurant’s guests who consume his food comment often 5. [ ] (E) decided slowly to cook marinated vegetables with many of the restaurant’s guests who often consumed his food commenting > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: hard (A) By 2018, after studying the art and benefits of plant-based cooking, the chef decided to cook slowly marinated vegetables, with many of the restaurant’s guests who consume his food often commenting Since the chef’s deciding to cook marinated vegetables is an action that was completed by a point in time in the past, 2018, the use of the simple past “decided” is not correct. The past perfect “had decided” should be used.
Also, since “slowly” appears between “cook” and “marinated,” whether “slowly” is meant to modify “cook” or “marinated” is not clear.
Also, the use of “with” is not ideal, as it results in the sentence’s seeming to convey that the chef cooked with the guests.
Also, since “often” appears between “consume his food” and “commenting,” whether “often” is meant to modify “consume” or “commenting” is not clear.
(B) By 2018, after studying the art and benefits of plant-based cooking, the chef had decided to cook marinated vegetables slowly, resulting in many of the restaurant’s guests consuming his food who often commented In this version, the past perfect “had decided” is correctly used in expressing that the chef’s deciding occurred before a point in time in the past, 2018.
However, we have to be careful to check the meaning conveyed. In doing so, we see that this choice conveys the nonsensical meaning that, by deciding to cook marinated vegetables, the chef resulted in many guests.
(C) By 2018, after studying the art and benefits of plant-based cooking, the chef has decided to cook marinated vegetables slowly, and many of the restaurant’s guests consuming his food often commented The use of “has decided,” which is in the present perfect tense, which is meant to connect the past to the present, does not make sense in this situation, in which the sentence indicates that the chef’s deciding occurred by a point in time in the past, 2018. The past perfect “had decided” should be used instead.
Also, since “often” appears between “consuming his food” and “commented,” whether “often” is meant to modify “consuming” or “commented” is not clear.
YOUR CHOICE(D) By 2018, after studying the art and benefits of plant-based cooking, the chef had decided to cook marinated vegetables slowly, and many of the restaurant’s guests who consume his food comment often The past perfect “had decided” is correctly used in expressing that the chef’s deciding occurred before a point in time in the past, 2018.
Also, the adverbs “slowly” and “often” are placed so as to clearly modify “cook” and “comment,” and this version conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(E) By 2018, after studying the art and benefits of plant-based cooking, the chef decided slowly to cook marinated vegetables with many of the restaurant’s guests who often consumed his food commenting Since the chef’s deciding to cook marinated vegetables is an action that was completed by a point in time in the past, 2018, the use of the simple past “decided” is not correct. The past perfect “had decided” should be used.
Also, the use of “with” is not ideal, as it results in the sentence’s seeming to convey that the chef cooked with the guests.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss why it is unnecessary to use the past perfect tense when the time sequence is clear without it.
summary:
Lesson Content A sentence must use the past perfect tense when the sentence is about an action or event completed by a specified date or time period in the past.
For example:
By late evening on Election Day, it had become clear that the upstart candidate would soon be the president of the country.
Late evening on Election Day is a time in the past, and we have an event, becoming, that was completed before that time.
title: 6.7.2 Use the Past Perfect When Describing an Action/Event Completed by a Certain Time in the Past.

contents:
Lesson Content At this point, you know that, when we have two past actions with one of the actions occurring deeper in the past, we can use the past perfect tense to make the time sequence clear.
However, if a sentence makes the sequence of events clear without the use of the past perfect tense, there is no need to use the past perfect.
💡MUST KNOW:
If a sentence makes clear the time sequence of past events without the use of the past perfect tense, the use of the past perfect tense is not necessary.
There are two common situations in which the use of the past perfect is not necessary.
Kim inspected the airplane’s engine before she sat in the cockpit.
Cody brushed his teeth before he went to the dentist.
The word “before” makes the time sequences clear in these sentences, so we would not be required to say:
Incorrect: Kim had inspected the airplane’s engine before she sat in the cockpit.
Incorrect: Cody had brushed his teeth before he went to the dentist.
The use of the past perfect tense in the above variations is unnecessary, and the resulting sentences do not quite make sense, as we can see by considering the following.
When we say, “Kim inspected the airplane’s engine before she sat in the cockpit,” the sequence of events is clear.
Let’s now consider a couple of examples that use the word after:
Freddie purchased the sailboat after he learned to sail.
The stock’s value rose after the company’s disgraced CEO resigned.
Although each of the above sentences discusses two past events, with one event occurring before the next, the past perfect tense is not necessary because the word “after” indicates which event happened first.
Incorrect: Freddie purchased the sailboat after he had learned to sail.
Incorrect: The stock’s value rose after the company’s disgraced CEO had resigned.
The timeline of events is made clear by the sentences’ using “after,” and so, the past perfect tense is not required.
Other context clues can also make sequences of events clear.
Modest Mussorgsky was a prolific composer until his use of alcohol caught up with him.
Even though this sentence uses the simple past “was,” the context clue “until” indicates that “Modest Mussorgsky was” preceded “his use of alcohol caught up.” So, the use of the past perfect is not necessary.
Evy McDonald developed a method for solving the problem in 1988, but, even in 2018, few people were aware of the method’s existence.
Even though this sentence uses the simple past “developed,” the dates mentioned, “1988” and “2018,” clearly indicate that “Evy McDonald developed” preceded “few people were.” So, the use of the past perfect is not necessary.
How can we use this knowledge to score points on test day? We can resist the urge to eliminate sentence versions with two past actions on ONLY the grounds that they don’t use the past perfect.
Key Strategy: Resist the urge to eliminate sentence versions with two past actions on ONLY the grounds that they don’t use the past perfect.
Consider the following pair of sentences:
Incorrect: For years, Antonio had learned by watching his brother at work, and then he built a career, which was successful in music.
Correct: Before he built a successful career in music, Antonio, for years, learned by watching his brother at work.
Having noticed that the sentence is about two past events that occurred in sequence, a test-taker looking for the use of the past perfect tense might be tempted to choose version one and eliminate version two, because version one uses the past perfect tense and version two does not.
However, the use of the past perfect tense in version two is not necessary because the use of “before” in version two is sufficient for making clear the sequence of the events described.
Meanwhile version one is flawed, because “and then” does not do a good job of connecting the past “built” with the deeper past “had learned,” and because, in version one, “career, which was successful in music” conveys the illogical meaning that the career itself succeeded in music.
💡MUST KNOW:
Being aware that the use of the past perfect tense is not always necessary for making clear the sequence of past events can help us to avoid being fooled into choosing an incorrect choice.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each sentence is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s practice applying what we just learned in answering Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 9 Immediately after he had tasted the sauce, not containing enough salt, the head chef fired his sous chef. 1. [ ] (A) he had tasted the sauce, not containing > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) he tasted the sauce, because of not containing > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) tasting the sauce, for not containing > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) he tasted the sauce, which did not contain 5. [ ] (E) he had tasted the sauce, and it did not contain > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) Immediately after he had tasted the sauce, not containing enough salt, the head chef fired his sous chef.
The use of the past perfect tense “had tasted” is unnecessary because the phrase “immediately after” makes clear that the event “tasted” precedes the event “fired.” Thus, the simple past tense “tasted,” rather than the past perfect “had tasted,” is all that is required to convey a clear meaning in this context.
Furthermore, because the present participial phrase “not containing enough salt” is set off from the rest of the sentence by commas, we can tell that it does not modify the noun that directly precedes it, “sauce,” but rather modifies the subject of one or the other of the sentence’s two clauses. So, “not containing enough salt, modifies either “he” or “the head chef,” both of which name the same person. As a result, the sentence conveys the ridiculous meaning that the head chef did not contain enough salt and that, for some reason, his “not containing enough salt” prompted him to fire the sous chef.
(B) Immediately after he tasted the sauce, because of not containing enough salt, the head chef fired his sous chef.
“Because of not containing enough salt” does not make clear that it was the sauce that did not contain enough salt and instead seems to indicate that the head chef was “not containing enough salt,” and that his “not containing enough salt” somehow prompted him to fire the sous chef.
Notice, however, that the use of the simple past tense “tasted,” rather than the past perfect “had tasted,” is correct even though the event “tasted” precedes the event “fired,” because the sequence of events is made clear by the phrase “immediately after.”
(C) Immediately after tasting the sauce, for not containing enough salt, the head chef fired his sous chef.
The phrase“for not containing enough salt” does not make clear that it was the sauce that did not contain enough salt and instead seems to indicate that the reason the head chef fired the sous chef was that the sous chef did not contain enough salt. Of course, this meaning is nonsensical.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Immediately after he tasted the sauce, which did not contain enough salt, the head chef fired his sous chef.
This version effectively conveys a clear and logical meaning.
By using the simple past tense “tasted” along with the phrase “immediately after,” this version makes clear that the event “tasted” precedes the event “fired.”
Also, by using the relative pronoun “which” to clearly refer to “the sauce,” this version makes clear what “did not contain enough salt.”
(E) Immediately after he had tasted the sauce, and it did not contain enough salt, the head chef fired his sous chef.
The use of the past perfect tense “had tasted” is unnecessary because the phrase “immediately after” makes clear that the event “tasted” precedes the event “fired.” Thus, the simple past tense “tasted,” rather than the past perfect “had tasted,” is all that is required to convey a clear meaning in this context.
Furthermore, “Immediately after he had tasted the sauce, and it did not contain” seems to indicate that the head chef’s tasting the sauce and the sauce’s not containing enough salt were two separate events, after which the head chef fired the sous chef. The logical meaning that the sauce did not contain enough salt when the head chef tasted it is not conveyed by connecting these two clauses with the conjunction “and.”
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 10 Before having become a popular companion animal, collies, which are also known as “rough collies,” had served for centuries as a herding dog in Scotland. 1. [ ] (A) having become a popular companion animal, collies, which are also known as “rough collies,” had served for centuries as a herding dog > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) becoming popular as being a companion animal, collies that are also known as “rough collies” had for centuries served as herding dogs > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) its becoming popular as a companion animal, the collie, also known as “the rough collie,” for centuries served as a herding dog 4. [ ] (D) they became popular as a companion animal, collies, also known as “rough collies,” have served for centuries as herding dogs > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) they had become popular as companion animals, the collie, which is also known as “the rough collie,” had served for centuries as a herding dog > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard The use of the past perfect is not necessary for indicating the sequence of past events because “before” indicates that one past event occurred before the other. Having established that the presence of “before” means that the use of the simple past “served” is correct, we won’t be tempted into choosing an incorrect choice by the fact that it uses the past perfect.
(A) Before having become a popular companion animal, collies, which are also known as “rough collies,” had served for centuries as a herding dog in Scotland.
Since the sequence of past events is made clear via the use of “before,” the use of the past perfect “had served” for describing the earlier event is unnecessary. So, we have to avoid being tempted by the use of the past perfect into choosing this choice, which is flawed for the following reasons.
The plural “collies” does not work logically with the singular “companion animal” in the opening modifier, which modifies “collies.” In order to work together logically, both nouns have to be either singular or plural.
Also, it does not make sense to say that the plural “collies” served as “a herding dog.”
(B) Before becoming popular as being a companion animal, collies that are also known as “rough collies” had for centuries served as herding dogs in Scotland.
Since the sequence of past events is made clear via the use of “before,” the use of the past perfect “had served” for describing the earlier event is unnecessary. So, we have to avoid being tempted by the use of the past perfect into choosing this choice, which is flawed for the following reasons.
It does not make sense to say that the collie became popular as “being a companion animal.” The collie simply became popular as “a companion animal.”
Also, the plural “collies” does not work logically with the singular “companion animal” in the opening modifier, which modifies “collies.” In order to work together logically, both nouns have to be either singular or plural.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Before its becoming popular as a companion animal, the collie, also known as “the rough collie,” for centuries served as a herding dog in Scotland.
Since the sequence of past events is made clear via the use of “before,” the use of the simple past “served” for describing the earlier event makes sense.
Also, the singular “its” agrees with the singular “collie,” and this version effectively expresses a meaning that makes sense.
(D) Before they became popular as a companion animal, collies, also known as “rough collies,” have served for centuries as herding dogs in Scotland.
It does not make sense to say that the plural “they” became popular as the singular “a companion animal.”
Also, since the main clause is about an event that occurred before a point in time in the past, the point in time when collies became popular as companion animals, the use of the present perfect “have served,” which seems to connect the event the collie’s serving to the present, does not make sense.
(E) Before they had become popular as companion animals, the collie, which is also known as “the rough collie,” had served for centuries as a herding dog in Scotland.
The use of the past perfect “had become,” is perhaps not ideal, but is not strictly incorrect and does not constitute a strong reason to eliminate this choice. While the use of the simple past “became” would probably make more sense, tenses can be used in nuanced ways to express meanings that are slightly different from each other, and, in this case, by using the past perfect, a writer could express a meaning slightly different from that expressed when the simple past is used.
At the same time, since the sequence of past events is made clear via the use of “before,” the use of the past perfect “had served” for describing the earlier event is unnecessary and somewhat illogical. So, we have to avoid being tempted by the use of the past perfect “had served” into choosing this choice, which is clearly flawed for the following reason.
The singular “collie” does not work logically with the plural “they” or the plural “companion animals.”
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now move on and discuss the future perfect tense.
summary:
Lesson Content At this point, you know that, when we have two past actions with one of the actions occurring deeper in the past, we can use the past perfect tense to make the time sequence clear.
However, if a sentence makes the sequence of events clear without the use of the past perfect tense, there is no need to use the past perfect.
💡MUST KNOW:
If a sentence makes clear the time sequence of past events without the use of the past perfect tense, the use of the past perfect tense is not necessary.
title: 6.7.3 The Past Perfect Tense Is Unnecessary When the Time Sequence Is Clear Without It.

contents:
Lesson Content The future perfect tense indicates that an event that has not yet happened will be completed before another event that has not yet happened or before a specific time in the future.
💡MUST KNOW:
The future perfect tense indicates that an event that has not yet happened will be completed before another event that has not yet happened or before a specific time in the future.
The future perfect tense is formed by combining “will,” the helping verb “have,” and the past participle of the main verb.
💡MUST KNOW:
The future perfect tense = “will” + “have” + past participle.
Consider the following example:
I will have worked for 35 years by the time I retire.
In the above example, the use of the future perfect tense lets us know that the future action of working for 35 years will be completed before the retirement, which has not yet occurred.
I will have traveled to 86 countries before this trip is over.
In the above example, the future perfect tense lets us know that the future action of traveling to 86 countries will occur before the trip is over.
The following example uses the simple future tense:
By Monday, he will have a week to complete the assignment.
Here, when Monday comes, the person will have a week to complete the assignment.
What about the following, which uses the future perfect?
By Monday, he will have had a week to finish the assignment.
In this sentence, we see that by a future Monday, the person “he” will already have had a week to complete an assignment.
Let’s do some targeted practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether past perfect, present perfect, or future perfect should be used in the following sentences. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery:
Consider the following Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 11 The company will have cornered the market by the time the patents related to the product will expire. 1. [ ] (A) will have cornered the market by the time the patents related to the product will expire > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) corners the market by the time the patents related to the product will expire > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) will have cornered the market by the time the patents related to the product expire 4. [ ] (D) cornering the market by the time the patents related to the product expires > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) will corner the market by the time the patents related to the product will expire > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium (A) The company will have cornered the market by the time the patents related to the product will expire .
The use of the simple future tense “will expire” in “The company will have cornered the market by the time the patents … will expire” does not make sense.
The use of the future perfect tense “will have cornered” with “by the time” is meant to communicate that “the company will have cornered the market” by a particular point in time.
Notice, however, that “the patents … will expire” does not indicate a point in time. After all, “the patents … will expire” is not connected to any particular point in time. Rather, “the patents … will expire” communicates that, at some time in the future, the patents will expire.
So, saying, “The company will have cornered the market by the time the patents … will expire” doesn’t make sense because “by the time the patents … will expire” does not actually point to any particular point in time.
(B) The company corners the market by the time the patents related to the product will expire .
The use of the simple future tense “will expire” in “The company corners the market by the time the patents … will expire” does not make sense.
“The company corners the market by the time” is meant to communicate that the company corners the market by a particular point in time.
Notice, however, that “the patents … will expire” does not indicate a point in time. After all, “the patents … will expire” is not connected to any particular point in time. Rather, “the patents … will expire” communicates that, at some time in the future, the patents will expire.
So, saying, “The company corners the market by the time the patents … will expire” doesn’t make sense because “by the time the patents … will expire” does not actually point to any particular point in time.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The company will have cornered the market by the time the patents related to the product expire .
The future perfect tense “will have cornered” and the simple present tense “expire” logically pair with each other to convey that one future event will be completed (“the company will have cornered the market”) by the time another future event occurs (“patents related to the product expire”).
Although the sentence discusses two future events, the use of the present tense “patents … expire” makes sense as a marker of a point in time by which “the company will have cornered the market. In other words, the sentence effectively conveys that “by the time” marked by the event “the patents … expire,” the event “cornered the market” will have occurred.
For an explanation of why the future tense “will expire” would not work, see the explanation for choice (A), (B), or (E).
Furthermore, the future perfect tense “will have cornered” tells us that, at that future point when the patents expire, the event “cornered” will have already happened. The time sequence is clear and logical.
(D) The company cornering the market by the time the patents related to the product expires .
This version does not contain a main verb and, thus, is not a complete sentence.
Notice that “cornering,” a participle, cannot serve at the main verb of a sentence.
Furthermore, the verb “expires” is part of the prepositional phrase “by the time the patents related to the product expires.”
Thus, this version is composed of only “the company” and a long participial phrase, “cornering the market …,” which modifies “company.”
Additionally, the singular verb “expires” in “by the time the patents related to the product expires” does not agree with its plural subject “patents.” We have to be careful not be fooled by the presence of the singular noun “product” just before “expires” into thinking that the singular “expires” is correct. The subject is “patents,” and “related to the product” is merely a modifier that modifies “patents.”
(E) The company will corner the market by the time the patents related to the product will expire .
The use of the simple future tense “will expire” in “The company will corner the market by the time the patents … will expire” does not make sense.
The use of the future tense “will corner” with “by the time” is meant to communicate that “the company will corner the market” by a particular point in time.
Notice, however, that “the patents … will expire” does not indicate a point in time. After all, “the patents … will expire” is not connected to any particular point in time. Rather, “the patents … will expire” communicates that, at some time in the future, the patents will expire.
So, saying, “The company will corner the market by the time the patents … will expire” doesn’t make sense because “by the time the patents … will expire” does not actually point to any particular point in time.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 12 By the time more doctors will begin to prescribe to their patients a diet free from animal products, many people will have died as a result of complications related to the “Western Diet,” which is a diet high in meat, eggs, and dairy, yet low in fruits and vegetables. 1. [ ] (A) will begin to prescribe to their patients a diet free from animal products, many people will have died as a result of complications related to the “Western Diet,” which is a diet > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) begin to prescribe a diet to their patients free of animal products, many people will die as a result of complications related to the “Western Diet,” which is a diet > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) begin prescribing a diet to their patients free from animal products, many people will have died as a result of complications from the “Western Diet,” a diet > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) prescribe to their patients a diet free from animal products, many people were dying as a result of complications related to the “Western Diet” that is a diet > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) begin to prescribe to their patients a diet free of animal products, many people will have died as a result of complications related to the “Western Diet,” a diet View Solution Question Level: medium (A) By the time more doctors will begin to prescribe to their patients a diet free from animal products, many people will have died as a result of complications related to the “Western Diet,” which is a diet high in meat, eggs, and dairy, yet low in fruits and vegetables.
The use of the simple future tense “will begin” in “By the time more doctors will begin …, many people will have died” does not make sense.
The use of “by the time” with the future perfect “will have died” is meant to communicate that “many people will have died” by a particular point in time.
Notice, however, that “more doctors will begin” does not indicate a point in time. After all, “more doctors will begin” is not connected to any particular point in time. Rather, “more doctors will begin” communicates that, at some time in the future, more doctors will begin.
So, saying, By the time more doctors will begin …, many people will have died” doesn’t make sense because “by the time more doctors will begin” does not actually point to any particular point in time.
Also, because this version uses the preposition “from” rather than the preposition “of,” this version suggests the nonsensical idea that a diet could be free from animal products, as if the diet has escaped from or been liberated from animal products.
(B) By the time more doctors begin to prescribe a diet to their patients free of animal products, many people will die as a result of complications related to the “Western Diet,” which is a diet high in meat, eggs, and dairy, yet low in fruits and vegetables.
Because the modifier “free of animal products” is placed directly after “patients,” this version suggests the nonsensical idea that doctors’ patients may be free of animal products.
Also, the use of the simple future tense “will die” does not make sense, because people’s dying is an event that the sentence indicates will have occurred by a certain point in time in the future, “the time doctors begin to prescribe ….” The sentence should use the future perfect “will have died” instead.
YOUR CHOICE(C) By the time more doctors begin prescribing a diet to their patients free from animal products, many people will have died as a result of complications from the “Western Diet,” a diet high in meat, eggs, and dairy, yet low in fruits and vegetables.
Because the modifier “free from animal products” is placed directly after “patients,” this version suggests the nonsensical idea that doctors’ patients may be free from animal products.
Also, because this version uses the preposition “from” rather than the preposition “of,” this version suggests the nonsensical idea that patients could be free from animal products, as if the patients mentioned have escaped from or been liberated from animal products.
(D) By the time more doctors prescribe to their patients a diet free from animal products, many people were dying as a result of complications related to the “Western Diet” that is a diet high in meat, eggs, and dairy, yet low in fruits and vegetables.
Because this version uses the preposition “from” rather than the preposition “of,” this version suggests the nonsensical idea that a diet could be free from animal products, as if the diet has escaped from or been liberated from animal products.
Also, the context clue “by the time doctors prescribe” indicates that the sentence is about an event that will have occurred by a point in time in the future. So, the use of the past continuous “were dying” does not make sense.
Also, since the Western Diet is a particular diet, using the restrictive relative clause “that is a diet …” to narrow the Western Diet down to “the Western Diet that is high in meat, eggs, and dairy, yet low in fruits and vegetables” does not make sense.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) By the time more doctors begin to prescribe to their patients a diet free of animal products, many people will have died as a result of complications related to the “Western Diet,” a diet high in meat, eggs, and dairy, yet low in fruits and vegetables.
The context clue “by the time doctors begin to prescribe” indicates that the sentence is about an event that will have occurred by a point in time in the future. So, the use of the future perfect “will have died” makes sense.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now consider another way in which tenses can appear in Sentence Correction questions, the future in the past.
summary:
Lesson Content The future perfect tense indicates that an event that has not yet happened will be completed before another event that has not yet happened or before a specific time in the future.
💡MUST KNOW:
The future perfect tense indicates that an event that has not yet happened will be completed before another event that has not yet happened or before a specific time in the future.
The future perfect tense is formed by combining “will,” the helping verb “have,” and the past participle of the main verb.
title: 6.8 The Future Perfect Tense

contents:
Lesson Content The future in the past is used when sentences about past events refer to events that were in the future when the past events occurred.
Consider the following example:
At the rally, the candidate said that he would take campaign donations only from individuals.
The sentence conveys that, at a time in the past, the candidate said that he was going to do something in the future, “take campaign donations only from individuals.”
💡MUST KNOW:
The future in the past is used when sentences about past events refer to events that were in the future when the past events occurred.
Let’s look at some more examples:
Even though the student was unfamiliar with the concepts, she knew that she would understand them eventually.
The sentence is about what the student knew about the future, that she would understand, at a point in time in the past.
The director said that the cast would have a party after the final show.
This sentence is about plans for the future that the cast had in the past.
During yesterday’s meeting, the division manager explained that she would soon be releasing the long-awaited new product.
This sentence is about plans for the future that the division manager had in the past.
To create the simple future tense, we use “will.”
To create the future in the past, we can use the past tense version of “will,” which is “would.”
Consider the following examples:
Future Tense: They are convinced that they will find gold soon.
Future in the Past: They were convinced that they would find gold soon.
Future Tense: Eleni hopes that she will arrive in time to see the eclipse.
Future in the Past: Eleni hoped that she would arrive in time to see the eclipse.
How do we use this knowledge to score points on test day? We resist the urge to automatically decide that a sentence using the future tense “will” is correct because the sentence, at some point, describes an event in the “future.” If the future event described is actually the future in the past, we must use “would.”
Let’s give this strategy a try.
Incorrect: Having seen his beautiful creations, it was predicted that the choreographer will become well known by the end of the decade.
Correct: Having seen his beautiful creations, many predicted that the choreographer would become well known by the end of the decade.
Sentence version one doesn’t work because the opening modifier, “having seen his beautiful creations” doesn’t logically modify “it.” In other words, version one contains a misplaced introductory modifier.
The second version works well because the opening modifier “Having seen his beautiful creations,” correctly modifies “many.” Also, the sentence describes the future in the past and correctly uses “would” instead of “will.”
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice For each sentence, choose Incorrect, if the use of tense in the sentence does not make sense, or Correct, if the use of tense in the sentence makes sense. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now consider some Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 13 Because he felt that the artist will become widely known, the gallery owner purchased many of the artist’s works while selling them later, after the prediction proved to be correct. 1. [ ] (A) will become widely known, the gallery owner purchased many of the artist’s works while selling them later, > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) would become widely known, the gallery owner purchased many of the artist’s works and, later, sold them, 3. [ ] (C) is going to become widely known, the gallery owner was purchasing many of the artist’s works, selling them later, > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) was going to become widely known, the gallery owner, purchasing many of the artist’s works, sold them later, > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) is becoming widely known, the gallery owner purchased and sold many of the artist’s works later, > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy In this question, the non-underlined portion provides two context clues that we can use to determine which tenses the correct answer should use: “he felt” and “after the prediction proved.”
(A) Because he felt that the artist will become widely known, the gallery owner purchased many of the artist’s works while selling them later, after the prediction proved to be correct.
The use of the future tense “will become” doesn’t make sense with the past tense “he felt that.” The use of the future in the past “would become” makes more sense.
To confirm that this choice is incorrect, we can notice that “purchased many of the artist’s works while selling them later” conveys the nonsensical meaning that the gallery owner purchased the works simultaneously with “selling them later.” How could the owner perform a past action “purchased” and a future action “selling them later” at the same time?
YOUR CHOICE(B) Because he felt that the artist would become widely known, the gallery owner purchased many of the artist’s works and, later, sold them, after the prediction proved to be correct.
It makes sense to use the future in the past “would become widely known” with the past tense “he felt that.”
Also, the timeline presented by the remainder of the sentence “later, sold them, after the prediction proved to be correct” makes sense.
Notice that the heavy use of commas around “later” and “sold them” is employed to make this choice “sound funny” and, thus, obscure the fact that it is the correct answer.
(C) Because he felt that the artist is going to become widely known, the gallery owner was purchasing many of the artist’s works, selling them later, after the prediction proved to be correct.
The use of the future tense “is going to become” doesn’t make sense with the past tense “he felt that.” The use of the future in the past “would become” makes more sense.
To confirm that this choice is incorrect, we can notice that “was purchasing many of the artist’s works, selling them later” does not clearly present a logical timeline and seems to convey the nonsensical meaning that the gallery owner purchased the works simultaneously with “selling them later.”
(D) Because he felt that the artist was going to become widely known, the gallery owner, purchasing many of the artist’s works, sold them later, after the prediction proved to be correct.
It makes sense to use the future in the past “was going to become widely known” with the past tense “he felt that.”
However, the main clause, “the gallery owner, purchasing many of the artist’s works, sold them later,” conveys the illogical meaning that, when the gallery owner sold the works “later,” he was purchasing the works that he sold.
(E) Because he felt that the artist is becoming widely known, the gallery owner purchased and sold many of the artist’s works later, after the prediction proved to be correct.
It does not make sense to use the present tense “is becoming” in describing what the gallery owner felt in the past. Also, the present tense “is becoming” would not be used to express a prediction – it would make sense to use the future tense or the future in the past.
Also, what this version conveys does not really make sense, as it seems to indicate that the gallery owner predicted that the artist would become widely known, and that as a result of making that prediction, the gallery owner “later,” after the artist became widely known, both purchased and sold many of the artist’s works. Purchasing and selling the artist’s works after the artist became widely known is not a logical consequence of predicting that the artist would become widely known.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 14 While the organizers of the Race Across America were hoping for a safer version of the 3000 mile bike race that would supplant the original version, having proved it to be unpopular, the new version was phased out after being held for only two years. 1. [ ] (A) While the organizers of the Race Across America were hoping for a safer version of the 3000 mile bike race that would supplant the original version, having proved it to be unpopular, the new version was phased out > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) The organizers of the Race Across America, hoping that a safer version of the 3000 mile bike race would supplant the original version, the new version proved to be unpopular, and they phased it out > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Although the organizers of the Race Across America were hoping that a safer version of the 3000 mile bike race will supplant the original version, the new version proved to be unpopular, so they phased it out > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) The organizers of the Race Across America were hoping that a safer version of the 3000 mile bike race would supplant the original version, but proving to be unpopular, the new version was phased out 5. [ ] (E) The organizers of the Race Across America had hoped that a safer version of the 3000 mile bike race was going to supplant the original version, but because it proved to be unpopular, they phased out the new version > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium In this question, the sentence mentions multiple events, and the correct answer must present these events in a time sequence that makes sense.
“After being held for only two years” in the non-underlined portion is a context clue that provides some indication of what tenses should be used in the correct answer.
Also, we have to make sure that the part of the answer choice that precedes “after being held” would be logically modified by “after being held.” If we forget to make sure that the choice we choose works logically with “after being held,” we may choose a trap choice.
(A) While the organizers of the Race Across America were hoping for a safer version of the 3000 mile bike race that would supplant the original version, having proved it to be unpopular, the new version was phased out after being held for only two years.
This choice conflicts with itself, in that it first says that the organizers were “hoping for a safer version,” indicating that such a version did not exist, but then says that “the new version proved it to be unpopular,” a statement that indicates that, actually, the new version did exist.
Also, the modifier “having proved it to be unpopular” does not convey a clear meaning, since it’s not clear who or what is the agent of “having proved.” Did the organizers prove something to be unpopular? Did the new version of the race prove itself to be unpopular? Also, “it” does not have a clear referent.
(B) The organizers of the Race Across America, hoping that a safer version of the 3000 mile bike race would supplant the original version, the new version proved to be unpopular, and they phased it out after being held for only two years.
While, at first, this version may seem to flow nicely, by reading it carefully, we can see that it is illogical.
It starts off seeming to have as its subject “the organizers of the Race Across America,” which is followed by a modifier “hoping that a safer version ….” However, without including a verb to go with the seeming subject “the organizers …,” the sentence jumps to another subject, “the new version.” We can see the issue more clearly by removing the modifier “hoping that a safer version,” since we get the following, which is clearly nonsensical:
“The organizers of the Race Across America … the new version proved to be unpopular ….”
Also, the end of the answer choice does not work logically with the non-underlined portion. When we combine the answer choice with the non-underlined portion, we get the following clause:
“they phased it out, after being held for only two years.”
That part conveys the nonsensical meaning that the organizers phased out the new version after the organizers were held for two years.
(C) Although the organizers of the Race Across America were hoping that a safer version of the 3000 mile bike race will supplant the original version, the new version proved to be unpopular, so they phased it out after being held for only two years.
The use of the simple future tense “will supplant” in this version does not make sense, for two reasons. One is that the expression “were hoping” implies that the organizers’ hoping ended in the past. So, what they hoped for, the supplanting of the original race by the new version, should be expressed in the past tense, as “would supplant.” Also indicating that the new version’s supplanting the original version was in the past is past tense “the new version proved to be unpopular, so, they phased it out.” Since, when this statement was made, the possibility that the new version would supplant the old version was in the past, the use of the simple future “will supplant” does not make sense. The future in the past “would supplant” is required.
Also, the end of the answer choice does not work logically with the non-underlined portion. When we combine the answer choice with the non-underlined portion, we get the following clause:
“they phased it out, after being held for only two years.”
That part conveys the nonsensical meaning that the organizers phased out the new version after the organizers were held for two years.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The organizers of the Race Across America were hoping that a safer version of the 3000 mile bike race would supplant the original version, but proving to be unpopular, the new version was phased out after being held for only two years.
The use of the future in the past “would supplant” in this version makes sense, as the sentence indicates that the organizers were, in the past, hoping for the supplanting of the original version by the safer version.
Notice that the use in this version of the past continuous “were hoping,” rather than the past perfect “had hoped,” which use is correct but may seem incorrect, is employed to obscure the fact that the choice is correct.
(E) The organizers of the Race Across America had hoped that a safer version of the 3000 mile bike race was going to supplant the original version, but because it proved to be unpopular, they phased out the new version after being held for only two years.
The use of the future in the past “was going to supplant” is not entirely illogical. However, since “was going to” is usually used when events are planned or likely to occur, it would make more sense to use the future in the past “would supplant,” since the supplanting was hoped for rather than planned and was not clearly likely to occur.
Also, which noun “it” refers to is not entirely clear, although the most logical referent for “it” is “the new version.”
While those issues may make this choice not as good as the correct answer, neither of them is a strong reason to eliminate this choice. However, we can eliminate this choice with confidence because the end of the answer choice does not work logically with the non-underlined portion. When we combine the answer choice with the non-underlined portion, we get the following clause:
“they phased out the new version after being held for only two years.”
That clause conveys the nonsensical meaning that the organizers phased out the new version after the organizers were held for two years.
Notice, the use of the past perfect “had hoped” in this choice is employed to make this choice seem more correct than the correct answer, which uses the past continuous “were hoping” instead. Also, the use of the blatantly correct dependent clause structure “because it proved to be unpopular” is another move employed to make this incorrect choice “sound right.”
A key aspect of getting this question correct is to see that both this choice and choice (D) seem correct and then check to see how each one works with the non-underlined portion.
Correct Answer: (D)

title: 6.9 The Future in the Past

contents:
Lesson Content The continuous tenses, sometimes called “the progressive tenses,” are used in discussing events that are continuous.
💡MUST KNOW:
We use the progressive tenses to discuss actions that are, were, or will be in progress.
The purpose of these tenses is to bring the reader’s attention to the temporarily ongoing nature of an action.
💡MUST KNOW:
The purpose of the continuous tenses is to bring the reader’s attention to the temporarily ongoing nature of an action.
Let’s discuss the present continuous tense.
summary:

💡MUST KNOW:
We use the progressive tenses to discuss actions that are, were, or will be in progress.

💡MUST KNOW:
The purpose of the continuous tenses is to bring the reader’s attention to the temporarily ongoing nature of an action.
title: 6.10 The Continuous Tenses

contents:
Lesson Content The present continuous tense is used for describing events that are temporarily ongoing in the present.
The children are playing outside.
The dog is running on the beach.
The sun is setting over the Pacific Ocean.
The members are taking their placement tests.
Notice that, in each of the above sentences, the event is occurring “right now.” In addition, the events are continuous.
💡MUST KNOW:
The present continuous tense is used to describe events that are temporarily ongoing in the present. This tense is formed by joining a present tense form of the verb “to be” with the present participle (-ing form) of the base verb.
Let’s now take a look at the past continuous tense.
summary:
Lesson Content The present continuous tense is used for describing events that are temporarily ongoing in the present.
The children are playing outside.
The dog is running on the beach.
The sun is setting over the Pacific Ocean.
The members are taking their placement tests.
Notice that, in each of the above sentences, the event is occurring “right now.” In addition, the events are continuous.
💡MUST KNOW:
The present continuous tense is used to describe events that are temporarily ongoing in the present.
title: 6.10.1 The Present Continuous Tense

contents:
Lesson Content The past continuous tense is used to describe an action or event that was ongoing but has ended.
The children were playing outside yesterday.
The plane was flying over Paris one hour ago.
Last night, the sun was setting over the ocean.
Notice that the events described by these examples have already occurred and that, when the events occurred, they were ongoing, or continuous.
💡MUST KNOW:
The past continuous tense is used to describe an action or event that was ongoing but has ended.
This tense can also be used to describe an event that was occurring in the background when a more notable event occurred.
The woman was washing clothes when the police arrived.
The dog was chewing his bone when the kids came running into the room.
The plane was flying over Colombia when the pilot decided to descend quickly.
The tiger was watching the herd of buffalo as they swam in the lake.
Notice that, in the above examples, the past continuous tense is used in describing events that were already occurring when more notable events occurred, and that all of the events occurred in the past.
💡MUST KNOW:
The past continuous tense can also be used to describe an event that was occurring in the background when a more notable event occurred.
Let’s take a look at another continuous tense, the future continuous.
summary:
Lesson Content The past continuous tense is used to describe an action or event that was ongoing but has ended.
The children were playing outside yesterday.
The plane was flying over Paris one hour ago.
Last night, the sun was setting over the ocean.
Notice that the events described by these examples have already occurred and that, when the events occurred, they were ongoing, or continuous.
💡MUST KNOW:
The past continuous tense is used to describe an action or event that was ongoing but has ended.
title: 6.10.2 The Past Continuous Tense

contents:
Lesson Content The future continuous tense is used to describe an action or event that will be ongoing but has not yet begun; the action or event will occur in the future.
The children will be playing outside tomorrow.
The plane will be flying over Madrid in about five hours.
Astronomers will be studying the comet as it passes through the inner solar system.
Notice that the events described by these examples will be ongoing at some point in the future.
💡MUST KNOW:
The future continuous tense is used to describe an action or event that will be ongoing but has not yet begun.
Let’s now discuss the perfect continuous tenses.
summary:

title: 6.10.3 The Future Continuous Tense

contents:
Lesson Content Just as the simple present, simple past, and simple future tenses have continuous tense counterparts, the perfect tenses – the present perfect, past perfect, and future perfect – have continuous counterparts.
To compare the present perfect tense and the present perfect continuous tense, consider the following examples:
That eagle has flown overhead many times.
That eagle has been flying overhead for the last twenty minutes.
In the first example, the present perfect is used to convey that, over a period of time extending from the past to the present, the eagle has flown overhead on multiple occasions.
Now let’s compare the past perfect with the past perfect continuous:
John told us that some people had waited all day.
By the time we got in line, some people had been waiting for hours.
In the first sentence, the past perfect is used to convey that one event, people had waited, occurred before another, John told us.
Finally, let’s compare the future perfect with the future perfect continuous:
