When the runners complete the race, they will have run over 40 kilometers.
When the runners approach the finish line, they will have been running for hours.
In the first example, the future perfect “will have run” is used to convey that, at a point in the future, the runners will have completed a certain action, running over 40 kilometers.
💡MUST KNOW:
There are continuous versions of the present perfect, the past perfect, and future perfect tenses. They are the present perfect continuous, the past perfect continuous, and the future perfect continuous. Like other continuous tenses, these perfect continuous tenses are used to highlight the ongoing nature of events described.
Let’s now discuss determining whether to use a non-continuous tense or a continuous tense.
summary:
Lesson Content Just as the simple present, simple past, and simple future tenses have continuous tense counterparts, the perfect tenses – the present perfect, past perfect, and future perfect – have continuous counterparts.
To compare the present perfect tense and the present perfect continuous tense, consider the following examples:
That eagle has flown overhead many times.
That eagle has been flying overhead for the last twenty minutes.
In the first example, the present perfect is used to convey that, over a period of time extending from the past to the present, the eagle has flown overhead on multiple occasions.
title: 6.10.4 The Perfect Continuous Tenses

contents:
Lesson Content Correctly answering a Sentence Correction question can require determining whether using a non-continuous tense or using a continuous tense makes more sense.
When sentences involve reasons to highlight the ongoing nature of events, versions that use continuous tenses are the best.
When sentences do not involve reasons to highlight the ongoing nature of events, versions that use non-continuous tenses are the best.
💡MUST KNOW:
When sentences involve reasons to highlight the ongoing nature of events, versions that use continuous tenses are the best. When sentences do not involve reasons to highlight the ongoing nature of events, versions that use non-continuous tenses are the best.
When stating simple facts, such as “dogs bark” or “John plays tennis” the simple present is the correct tense to use.
Consider these sentences:
Jinus plays tennis.
Jinus is playing tennis.
Both sentences make sense, but they convey two different meanings.
Let’s consider another:
Scott is playing the piano.
If Scott is currently playing the piano, then the sentence is correct as written.
Scott plays the piano.
💡MUST KNOW:
Use the simple present rather than the present continuous in stating that events occur, if the point of the sentence is not to express that events are ongoing at the present time.
How can we use this knowledge to score points on test day? We can determine whether the use of a non-continuous tense or the use of a continuous tense makes more sense by considering context clues.
Key Strategy: Use context clues to determine whether the use of a non-continuous tense or the use of a continuous tense makes more sense.
When sentences involve reasons to highlight the ongoing nature of events, versions that use continuous tenses are the best.
When sentences do not involve reasons to highlight the ongoing nature of events, versions that use non-continuous tenses are the best.
Consider the following pair of versions:
Incorrect: When razorfish sense the presence of a predator, they are hiding in the sandy substrate of the seabed.
Correct: When razorfish sense the presence of a predator, they hide in the sandy substrate of the seabed.
We can eliminate version one because we can tell that what the sentence is meant to describe is what razorfish do when they sense the presence of a predator: they hide.
Version two, of course, makes sense because the use of the simple present tense “they hide,” effectively conveys what the razorfish do.
Here’s another pair:
Incorrect: Because average global temperatures are currently at record levels, sea levels rise at the rate of 3.4 millimeters per year.
Correct: Because average global temperatures are currently at record levels, sea levels are rising at the rate of 3.4 millimeters per year.
Context clues allow us to quickly eliminate sentence version one.
In version two, the use of the present continuous makes sense, because the present continuous expression “sea levels are rising at the rate of 3.4 millimeters per year” conveys that sea levels are rising at that rate at this moment but may not have risen at that rate in the past and may not rise at that rate in the future.
So, in this case, the version that uses the present continuous is more effective than the version that uses the simple present.
Deciding Whether to Use the Simple Past or the Past Continuous
Just as context clues tell us whether using the simple present or using the present continuous makes more sense, we can tell from context clues whether using the simple past or the past continuous makes more sense.
💡MUST KNOW:
Just as context clues tell us whether using the simple present or using the present continuous makes more sense, we can tell from context clues whether using the simple past or the past continuous makes more sense.
Unless there is a clear reason to highlight the continuous nature of a past action, we don’t use the past continuous tense.
💡MUST KNOW:
Unless there is a clear reason to highlight the continuous nature of a past action, we don’t use the past continuous tense.
How do we use this knowledge to score points on test day? We eliminate any sentence version that uses the past continuous tense when it does not make sense to describe a past action as continuous.
Key Strategy: Eliminate any sentence version that uses the past continuous tense when it does not make sense to describe a past action as continuous.
Consider the following example:
Incorrect: In 2018, Bryce Carlson was rowing across the Atlantic Ocean in 38 days 6 hours and 49 minutes, setting a record that stands to this day.
Correct: In 2018, Bryce Carlson rowed across the Atlantic Ocean in 38 days 6 hours and 49 minutes, setting a record that stands to this day.
We can eliminate version one because what makes sense is that Bryce Carlson completed the trip in 38 days 6 hours and 49 minutes.
Version two makes sense because the simple past “rowed” is what we would expect when a sentence describes a past action that was completed.
In contrast, when a sentence expresses a past action that was clearly ongoing, we can eliminate sentence versions that do not use the past progressive tense.
Key Strategy: When a sentence expresses a past action that was clearly ongoing, we can eliminate sentence versions that do not use the past progressive tense.
Consider the following:
Incorrect: The filming was delayed because, upon arriving on location, the crew saw that some bears played in the pond and waited until the bears left.
Correct: The filming was delayed because, upon arriving on location, the crew saw that some bears were playing in the pond and waited until the bears left.
We can eliminate sentence version one because what makes sense is not that the crew saw that bears played in the pond, but were not playing in the pond when the crew arrived, but, rather, that the bears were playing (the action was ongoing), when the crew arrived and while the crew waited.
We see that the use of the past continuous “were playing” in version two makes sense.
Deciding Whether to Use the Simple Future or the Future Continuous
Unless there is a clear reason to highlight the continuous nature of a future action, we don’t use the future continuous tense.
💡MUST KNOW:
Unless there is a clear reason to highlight the continuous nature of a future action, we don’t use the future continuous tense.
How do we use this knowledge to score points on test day? We eliminate any sentence version that uses the future continuous tense when it does not make sense to describe a future action as continuous.
Key Strategy: Eliminate any sentence version that uses the future continuous tense when it does not make sense to describe a future action as continuous.
OK, let’s consider a sentence about future events:
Incorrect: If the traffic doesn’t clear soon, by the time the musicians arrive, the couple will say their vows.
Correct: If the traffic doesn’t clear soon, by the time the musicians arrive, the couple will be saying their vows.
We can tell from what the sentence says about the events that this sentence is not meant to convey that the couple “will say” their vows.
Now, to wrap up our discussion, let’s choose between present perfect and present perfect continuous:
Since moving to New York, the pop singer (has attended/has been attending) the opera a few times.
In this case, the action, the pop singer’s attending the opera, has not been continuous but, rather, has occurred on a few discrete occasions.
Since moving to New York, the pop singer has attended the opera a few times.
In all of the above examples, context clues clearly indicate whether using a non-continuous tense or using a continuous tense makes more sense.
💡MUST KNOW:
When we answer Sentence Correction questions, we can use context clues to determine whether it makes more sense to use a non-continuous tense, it makes more sense to use a continuous tense, or either a non-continuous tense or a continuous tense works.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice For each sentence, choose Correct, if the sentence makes sense as written, or Incorrect, if the sentence does not make sense as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 Leo walks to school every day instead of taking the bus. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 In Yanis’s opinion, Nina is making a big mistake by moving to Los Angeles to pursue a career in acting, especially since she has no experience. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 Once the members of the team have been choosing a designer, they will meet with that designer to discuss how to proceed. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 4 The school principal’s first language is Spanish, but he also is speaking English, French, and some German, so, if necessary, he can easily communicate with members of our multinational community. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 5 The gubernatorial candidate has been running a campaign focused primarily on health care expansion and protections for workers’ unions, which, so far, has been very effective. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 6 By the time the reporters arrived at the event, the guest of honor had been leaving and was already back at her hotel. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 7 Because of the way in which the building is designed, it will be surviving the next major earthquake to hit this area. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 8 The swimmers were afraid of the circling whale until they realized that, by swimming around them, the whale was protecting them from the nearby sharks. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 9 Despite the damper that this news will put on everybody’s weekend, the CEO will announce the layoffs in the staff meeting on Friday morning because he thinks that the work week will be less productive if he is making the announcement on Monday. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 10 The problem with the chassis was unexpected, so the engineers work now, hoping that they can solve it quickly. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 11 By this time next year, the national debt will have been growing to $150,000 per citizen. 1. [ ] (A) Correct > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Incorrect ## Targeted Practice: 12 Because Dennis almost crashed the last time he had the radio turned up in rush-hour traffic, he now listens to loud music in the car only when he is on a long stretch of empty highway. 1. [x] (A) Correct 2. [ ] (B) Incorrect > Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
In stating facts or describing habitual actions, the use of the simple present tense makes sense. Thus, “walks” is the correct verb to use in describing how Leo gets to school each day.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
It makes sense to use the present continuous “is making” in describing an action in which Nina is currently engaging. Also, the simple present “has” is correct, because we use the simple present form of “to have” to indicate possession in the present.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
The context clue “they will meet with that designer” tells us that the choosing of the designer will not be ongoing but, rather, will have been completed. So, the use of the present perfect continuous “have been choosing” does not make sense. We need the regular present perfect instead.
Correct sentence:
Once the members of the team have chosen a designer, they will meet with that designer to discuss how to proceed.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
The sentence does not describe the current actions of the principal. So, the present continuous isn’t necessary. The simple present tense “speaks” is correct in a statement of fact about the principal’s language skills.
Correct sentence:
The school principal’s first language is Spanish, but he also speaks English, French, and some German, and so, if necessary, he can easily communicate with members of our multinational community.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
Given the context of the sentence, including the key phrase “so far,” we know that the candidate’s campaign is ongoing. Thus, the use of the present perfect continuous “has been running” makes sense.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
If the guest of honor “was already back at her hotel,” then she wasn’t in the process of leaving. So, the use of the past perfect continuous “had been leaving” does not make sense. We need instead the past perfect “had left,” which conveys that she had completed the act of leaving.
Correct sentence:
By the time the reporters arrived at the event, the guest of honor had left and was already back at her hotel.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 7 Explanation:
The point to be made is not that the building is going to survive on an ongoing basis but, rather, that, if or when the next major earthquake hits the area, the building will survive on that occasion. So, the use of the future continuous does not make sense. The meaning is effectively conveyed by the simple future.
Correct sentence:
Because of the way in which the building is designed, it will survive the next major earthquake to hit this area.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 8 Explanation:
Since it makes sense that, during the time period described, the whale was protecting the swimmers on a temporarily ongoing basis, it makes sense to use the past continuous “was protecting.”
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 9 Explanation:
The point to be made is that the entire work week would be affected by the CEO’s making the announcement on one occasion on Monday. The CEO wouldn’t be making the announcement on an ongoing basis. So, using the present continuous “is making” does not make sense. The simple present “makes” is required.
Correct sentence:
Despite the damper that this news will put on everybody’s weekend, the CEO will announce the layoffs in the staff meeting on Friday morning because he thinks that the work week will be less productive if he makes the announcement on Monday.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 10 Explanation:
The sentence is not about what the engineers do in general but, rather, about what the engineers are doing on a temporarily ongoing basis: working. So, the use of the simple present “work” does not make sense. The present continuous “are working” is required.
Correct sentence:
The problem with the chassis was unexpected, so the engineers are working now, hoping that they can solve it quickly.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 11 Explanation:
The point to be made in this case is that the national debt will have completed the act of growing to $150,000 per citizen, not that the debt will be somehow growing to $150,000 on an ongoing basis. So, the use of the future perfect continuous “will have been growing” does not make sense. The future perfect is required.
Correct sentence:
By this time next year, the national debt will have grown to $150,000 per citizen.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 12 Explanation:
The word “now” may make us gravitate toward the present continuous tense; however, we must consider the broader context of the sentence. In this case, “now” doesn’t indicate that an action is currently occurring. In other words, Dennis is not currently on a stretch of empty highway, listening to music (as far as we know). Rather, “now” (along with “only when”), points to a change in Dennis’s habits, and we use the simple present to describe habitual actions. So, the simple present “listens” is correct.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now answer some Sentence Correction questions:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 15 The oldest continental rocks that are about 4 billion years old are found in cratons, which are stable parts of the continental lithosphere that have been surviving several successive supercontinent cycles, each of which has been lasting 300 to 500 million years. 1. [ ] (A) rocks that are about 4 billion years old are found in cratons, which are stable parts of the continental lithosphere that have been surviving several successive supercontinent cycles, each of which has been lasting > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) rocks, which are about 4 billion years old, are found in cratons, stable parts of the continental lithosphere that have survived several successive supercontinent cycles, each of which has lasted 3. [ ] (C) rocks, about 4 billion years old, are found in cratons, stable parts of the continental lithosphere surviving several successive supercontinent cycles each of which was lasting > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) rocks, being about 4 billion years old, are found in cratons, stable parts of the continental lithosphere, to survive several successive supercontinent cycles, each of which were lasting > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) rocks are about 4 billion years old and are found in cratons, stable parts of the continental lithosphere, surviving several successive supercontinent cycles, each of them having been lasting > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) The oldest continental rocks that are about 4 billion years old are found in cratons, which are stable parts of the continental lithosphere that have been surviving several successive supercontinent cycles, each of which has been lasting 300 to 500 million years.
The oldest continental rocks are already in a specific category, “the oldest.” So, further restricting the category by using the restrictive modifier “that are about 4 billion years old,” which also restricts the rocks by age, does not make sense. What would make sense is adding the information that the oldest rocks are about 4 billion years old via the use of a nonrestrictive modifier, such as “which are about 4 billion years old.”
Also, using the present perfect continuous “have been surviving,” which expresses that the cratons have been surviving successive supercontinent cycles on an ongoing basis up to the present time clearly does not make sense, because, if the cycles are successive, they are not all ongoing at the present time. Rather, they occurred in succession, with one ending when the next began. So, all but the last of the cycles must have ended by the present time, and therefore there is no way for the cratons to be surviving the cycles on an ongoing basis up to the present time.
Also, using the present perfect continuous “has been lasting,” which expresses that the cycles have been lasting on an ongoing basis up to the present time does not make sense. After all, if the cycles are successive, they are not all ongoing at the present time. Rather, they have occurred in succession, with one ending before the next occurred. So, all but the last of the cycles must have ended by the present time, and, therefore, there is no way for the cycles to be “lasting” on an ongoing basis up to the present time.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The oldest continental rocks, which are about 4 billion years old, are found in cratons, stable parts of the continental lithosphere that have survived several successive supercontinent cycles, each of which has lasted 300 to 500 million years.
Using the nonrestrictive modifier “which are about 4 billion years old” to add information about “the oldest continental rocks” makes sense.
Using the present perfect “have survived,” which indicates that the survival of the cratons began in the past and continues in the present makes sense.
The reason why the use of the present perfect “has lasted” makes sense may not be obvious, since, clearly, if the cycles have been successive, then earlier cycles have not lasted to the present time but, rather, ended at some point in the past before later cycles occurred. At the same time, the sentence discusses the cycles as a group, and the lasting of cycles in the group, one after the other, has been occurring up to the present time. So, the use of the present perfect “has lasted” makes sense.
(C) The oldest continental rocks, about 4 billion years old, are found in cratons, stable parts of the continental lithosphere surviving several successive supercontinent cycles each of which was lasting 300 to 500 million years.
Describing the cratons as “surviving several successive supercontinent cycles” does not make sense, because, if the cycles are successive, they are not all ongoing at the present time. Rather, they occurred in succession, with one ending when the next began. So, all but the last of the cycles must have ended by the present time, and, therefore, there is no way for the cratons to be currently “surviving” several successive cycles.
Also, the use of the past continuous “was lasting” does not make sense. A cycle’s lasting for 300 to 500 million years is an event that is completed once it occurs. Once the cycle is over, it makes sense to say that the cycle lasted for 300 to 500 million years. It does not make sense to say that the cycle “was lasting for 300 to 500 million years.” It lasted that long once, not over a period of time.
(D) The oldest continental rocks, being about 4 billion years old, are found in cratons, stable parts of the continental lithosphere, to survive several successive supercontinent cycles, each of which were lasting 300 to 500 million years.
The use of the word “being” in “being about 4 billion years” old is unnecessary and somewhat illogical. As a result of the unnecessary use of “being,” this version seems to convey the illogical meaning that the oldest continental rocks are found in cratons because of the oldest continental rocks’ being about 4 billion years old.
Also, as a result of the wording “to survive several successive supercontinent cycles,” the sentence seems to convey that there is a purpose to the rocks’ being found in cratons, as if, on purpose, the rocks are found in cratons in order to survive supercontinent cycles.
Also, since “each” is always singular, the plural “were” does not agree with “each.”
Also, the use of the past continuous “were lasting” does not make sense. A cycle’s lasting for 300 to 500 million years is an event that is completed once it occurs. Once the cycle is over, it makes sense to say that the cycle lasted for 300 to 500 million years. It does not make sense to say that the cycle “was lasting for 300 to 500 million years.” It lasted that long once, not over a period of time. So, it does not make sense to say that the cycles “were lasting” for 300 to 500 million years.
(E) The oldest continental rocks are about 4 billion years old and are found in cratons, stable parts of the continental lithosphere, surviving several successive supercontinent cycles, each of them having been lasting 300 to 500 million years.
Because the participial phrase “surviving several successive supercontinent cycles” is set off by commas, it should modify the clause that it follows and have a logical connection to what that clause says. However, there is not a strong logical connection between what the phrase says and what the clause that precedes the phrase says. As a result, the sentence conveys the illogical meaning that, via being about 4 billion years old and being found in cratons, the oldest rocks are somehow “surviving successive supercontinent cycles.”
Also, whether “each of them having been lasting …” is meant to modify the rocks or the cycles is not clear.
Also, using the continuous participle “having been lasting” does not make sense. A cycle’s lasting for 300 to 500 million years is an event that is completed once it occurs. Once the cycle is over, it makes sense to say that the cycle lasted for 300 to 500 million years. It does not make sense to say that the cycle had been lasting for 300 to 500 million years. It lasted that long once, not over a period of time. So, it does not make sense to describe the cycles as “having been lasting” for 300 to 500 million years.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 16 In the teeth of an Aztec man who died in one of the two waves of disease that were occurring in 1545 and 1576 was bacterial DNA that matched that of salmonella. 1. [ ] (A) disease that were occurring in 1545 and 1576 > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) disease that were first in 1545 and then in 1576 > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) disease - it occurred in 1545 and again in 1576 – > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) disease then occurring in 1545 and 1576 > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) disease, which occurred in 1545 and in 1576, View Solution Question Level: easy (A) In the teeth of an Aztec man who died in one of the two waves of disease that were occurring in 1545 and 1576 was bacterial DNA that matched that of salmonella.
Because of the use of the past continuous “were occurring,” this version conveys the illogical meaning that two waves of disease, each of which occurred during different years, were occurring at the same time.
(B) In the teeth of an Aztec man who died in one of the two waves of disease that were first in 1545 and then in 1576 was bacterial DNA that matched that of salmonella.
What this version is meant to convey is not clear. What does waves “were first in 1545 and then in 1576” even mean?
Also, it is not the nature of waves that the same wave would exist in one year and then come back in another year. A wave would occur once, and then the next occurrence of a wave, in a later year, for instance, would be a different wave, not the same wave. So, what this version conveys by saying, “waves … were first in 1545 and then in 1576” does not make sense.
Further, it does not really make sense to say that waves of disease “were” in a year. Waves of disease may occur during a year, but would not simply be in a year.
(C) In the teeth of an Aztec man who died in one of the two waves of disease - it occurred in 1545 and again in 1576 – was bacterial DNA that matched that of salmonella.
While a bit awkward, this version might have worked if the pronoun “it” had a logical referent. However, “it” has nothing to refer to. The sentence mentions two waves, and the singular “it” would not logically refer to the plural “two waves.”
(D) In the teeth of an Aztec man who died in one of the two waves of disease then occurring in 1545 and 1576 was bacterial DNA that matched that of salmonella.
Because of the use of the present participle “occurring,” this version conveys the illogical meaning that two waves of disease, which occurred during two different years, were occurring at the same time.
YOUR CHOICE(E) In the teeth of an Aztec man who died in one of the two waves of disease, which occurred in 1545 and in 1576, was bacterial DNA that matched that of salmonella.
This version uses the simple past “occurred” to convey the logical meaning that two waves of disease occurred during two years.
Some people are reluctant to choose this choice because they see that the modifier “which occurred in 1545 and in 1576” is nonrestrictive and, therefore, merely mentions that the two waves of disease occurred in 1545 and in 1576 but does not specify that the two waves of disease were the ones that occurred in 1545 and in 1576. However, the truth is that the sentence works fine without restricting the waves of disease in that way. The sentence might seem better with a restrictive modifier, but it’s not incorrect with a nonrestrictive modifier. It still conveys a meaning that makes sense.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 17 During the 1800s, passenger pigeons traveled in flocks numbering in the millions, but by around the first of September, 1914, with the death of the last of them, the passenger pigeon had become completely extinct. 1. [x] (A) passenger pigeons traveled in flocks numbering in the millions, but by around the first of September, 1914, with the death of the last of them, the passenger pigeon had become 2. [ ] (B) although passenger pigeons were traveling in flocks numbering in the millions, by around the first of September, 1914, when the last of them died, passenger pigeons were becoming > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) passenger pigeons were traveling in flocks that were numbering in the millions, but by around the first of September, 1914, when the last of them was dying, the passenger pigeon had become > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) when passenger pigeons traveled in flocks that numbered in the millions, by around the first of September, 1914, the last of them died, and the passenger pigeon was becoming > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) passenger pigeons traveled in flocks that were numbered by the millions, but then, around the first of September, 1914, after the last of them had died, passenger pigeons became > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard CORRECT ANSWER(A) During the 1800s, passenger pigeons traveled in flocks numbering in the millions, but by around the first of September, 1914, with the death of the last of them, the passenger pigeon had become completely extinct.
This version uses the simple past “traveled” to clearly and concisely describe what passenger pigeons did in the 1800s.
Also, this version correctly uses the past perfect “had become” to indicate that, by a certain point in time, around the first of September, 1914, the passenger pigeons’ becoming extinct had been completed.
(B) During the 1800s, although passenger pigeons were traveling in flocks numbering in the millions, by around the first of September, 1914, when the last of them died, passenger pigeons were becoming completely extinct.
Logically, the opening modifier “during the 1800s” has to modify an event described by the sentence. Notice, everything that follows “during the 1800s” is either a modifying clause or a modifying phrase except for the independent clause “passenger pigeons were becoming completely extinct.” So, “during the 1800s” must modify “passenger pigeons were becoming completely extinct.” Meanwhile, that clause is also modified by “by around the first of September, 1914.” Because both “during the 1800s” and “by around the first of September, 1914” modify “passenger pigeons were becoming completely extinct, the sentence conveys the nonsensical meaning that passenger pigeons were becoming extinct by around the first of September, 1914, in the 1800s.
Also, the use of the past continuous “were traveling” results in the sentence’s expressing that the passenger pigeons were, on an ongoing basis, traveling in flocks numbering in the millions. As a result, the sentence conveys the illogical meaning that the passenger pigeons were, on an ongoing basis, traveling in flocks numbering in the millions when the last of the passenger pigeons died, as if they could have been traveling in flocks numbering in the millions when there was only one left and when that one died.
Also, the use of the past perfect continuous “were becoming” results in the sentence’s conveying the illogical meaning that, by around the first of September, 1914, when the last of the passenger pigeons died, the pigeons were becoming completely extinct on an ongoing basis. Of course, when the last of the passenger pigeons died, they would not have been becoming completely extinct on an ongoing basis, because, when the last one died, they would have become completely extinct, at which point the process of their becoming extinct would have ended, not continued on an ongoing basis.
(C) During the 1800s, passenger pigeons were traveling in flocks that were numbering in the millions, but by around the first of September, 1914, when the last of them was dying, the passenger pigeon had become completely extinct.
There is no reason to use the past continuous “were traveling.” While the use of the past continuous is not strictly incorrect, the use of the simple past “traveled” would make more sense, because the point to be made is not that the passenger pigeons were traveling on an ongoing basis but, rather, simply that, during the 1800s, they traveled in flocks numbering in the millions.
Also, the wording “that were numbering in the millions” is unnecessarily wordy. It is just as effective and more concise to say, “numbering in the millions.”
Also, the use of the past continuous “was dying” does not make sense, because the passenger pigeon would not have become completely extinct when the last of the passenger pigeons was in the process of dying. The passenger pigeon did not become completely extinct until the last one died.
(D) During the 1800s, when passenger pigeons traveled in flocks that numbered in the millions, by around the first of September, 1914, the last of them died, and the passenger pigeon was becoming completely extinct.
This version conveys the nonsensical meaning that, in the 1800s, by around the first of September, 1914, the last of the passenger pigeons died.
Also, this version conveys the illogical meaning that, when passenger pigeons traveled in flocks that numbered in the millions, the last of the passenger pigeons died.
Also, this version uses the past continuous “was becoming” to convey the illogical meaning that, when the last of the passenger pigeons died, the passenger pigeon was becoming completely extinct on an ongoing basis. Of course, when the last passenger pigeon died, the passenger pigeon would not have been going extinct on an ongoing basis, because the process of the passenger pigeon’s going extinct would have been completed.
YOUR CHOICE(E) During the 1800s, passenger pigeons traveled in flocks that were numbered by the millions, but then, around the first of September, 1914, after the last of them had died, passenger pigeons became completely extinct.
The wording “flocks that were numbered by the millions” conveys the nonsensical meaning that the flocks were numbered by some “millions,” as if there were “millions,” of what we don’t know, that or who numbered the flocks, whatever numbering the flocks would even be.
Also, the use of the connector “but then” doesn’t quite make sense. It does not make sense to connect “in the 1800s” to “around the first of September, 1914” by saying “but then,” because the 1800s were not directly followed by the first of September, 1914, as if the 1800s ended and “then” it was the first of September, 1914.
Also, the use of the past perfect “had died” in “after the last of them had died” is unnecessary and somewhat illogical, because “after” makes the time sequence clear. It would be sufficient and more logical to say, “after the last of them died.”
Also, it does not make sense to say, “after the last of them had died, passenger pigeons became extinct.” Passenger pigeons became extinct the moment the last of them died, not after the last of them died.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss verb mood.
summary:
Lesson Content Correctly answering a Sentence Correction question can require determining whether using a non-continuous tense or using a continuous tense makes more sense.
When sentences involve reasons to highlight the ongoing nature of events, versions that use continuous tenses are the best.
When sentences do not involve reasons to highlight the ongoing nature of events, versions that use non-continuous tenses are the best.
💡MUST KNOW:
When sentences involve reasons to highlight the ongoing nature of events, versions that use continuous tenses are the best.
title: 6.10.5 Determining Whether to Use a Non-Continuous or Continuous Tense

contents:
Lesson Content Some Sentence Correction questions will test our skill in assessing use of verb mood.
💡MUST KNOW:
Whereas verb tense lets us place events in time, verb mood conveys the attitude with which a statement is made.
Let’s begin with the indicative verb mood.
summary:
Lesson Content Some Sentence Correction questions will test our skill in assessing use of verb mood.
💡MUST KNOW:
Whereas verb tense lets us place events in time, verb mood conveys the attitude with which a statement is made.
Let’s begin with the indicative verb mood.
title: 6.11 Verb Mood

contents:
Lesson Content The indicative mood is the most commonly used verb mood and is used for stating facts, assertions, and observations.
The flowers smell good.
Tomorrow, we will go to the beach.
It may be necessary to change the design of the outer shell.
The panel concluded that the bank should stop financing oil projects completely by 2008.
Once ranging as far east as Arkansas and Louisiana, ocelots are currently found only in extreme southern Texas and northeastern Mexico.
💡MUST KNOW:
The indicative mood is the most common verb mood and is used to state facts, assertions, and observations.
The indicative mood is used in most GMAT Sentence Correction questions, and is the mood used in the examples we have seen so far in this chapter.
Let’s now discuss the subjunctive mood, which is very important to understand for the GMAT.
summary:
Lesson Content The indicative mood is the most commonly used verb mood and is used for stating facts, assertions, and observations.
The flowers smell good.
Tomorrow, we will go to the beach.
It may be necessary to change the design of the outer shell.
The panel concluded that the bank should stop financing oil projects completely by 2008.
Once ranging as far east as Arkansas and Louisiana, ocelots are currently found only in extreme southern Texas and northeastern Mexico.
title: 6.12 Indicative Mood

contents:
Lesson Content The subjunctive mood is used for statements that lack certainty, such as those that describe hypothetical or conditional situations, dreams, wishes, desires, or doubts.
In a sentence that describes a hypothetical situation, the form of “to be” that should be used is the verb “were,” never the verb “was.” At first, this usage will probably sound incorrect to you.
💡MUST KNOW:
Sentences that describe hypothetical situations must use the subjunctive mood. In describing these hypothetical situations with the verb “to be,” we use the verb “were,” never the verb “was.”
Consider the following example:
Correct: If I were a millionaire, I would buy my dream house.
Incorrect: If I was a millionaire, I would buy my dream house.
This sentence is meant to convey that the speaker is not actually a millionaire.
Consider the following example:
Correct: If I were a king, I would be rich.
Incorrect: If I was a king, I would be rich.
Notice that this sentence is meant to convey a hypothetical situation: I am not actually a king, but if I were I’d be rich.
Correct: Were I still living in South Africa, I would still be working to protect endangered wildlife.
Incorrect: Was I still living in South Africa, I would still be working to protect endangered wildlife.
Notice that this sentence is meant to convey a hypothetical situation: If I were still living in South Africa, I would be doing something… (working to protect endangered wildlife).
How can we use this knowledge to score points on test day? One powerful strategy we can use is to quickly eliminate any sentence version that expresses a hypothetical situation yet doesn’t use the subjunctive mood.
Consider the following:
Incorrect: If he was an experienced pilot, he would be aware that landing in such conditions is challenging.
Correct: If he were an experienced pilot, he would be aware that landing in such conditions is challenging.
Sentence version one is incorrect because it describes a hypothetical situation yet does not use the subjunctive mood.
How can we see that the sentence is describing something theoretical? We can notice that “he” is not actually a pilot.
Let’s look at another example:
Incorrect: I wish that I was done with my GMAT preparation.
Correct: I wish that I were done with my GMAT preparation.
Sentence version one is incorrect because it expresses a hypothetical situation yet does not use the subjunctive mood.
How can we see that the sentence is describing something theoretical? We can notice that “I” am not actually done with my GMAT preparation.
Additional Context Clues to Help spot Sentences Requiring the Subjunctive Mood
In identifying sentences that require the subjunctive mood, it’s helpful to know that these hypothetical clauses typically begin with “if,” “as if,” “were,” or “as though.”
Consider these example sentences using the subjunctive mood:
Correct: You act as if getting laid off were the end of the world.
Incorrect: You act as if getting laid off was the end of the world.
Correct: She looked at me as though I were a monster.
Incorrect: She looked at me as though I was a monster.
In all of the above examples, the subjunctive is necessary, and the use of the indicative is incorrect, because the sentences are about hypothetical, rather than actual, situations.
💡MUST KNOW:
In identifying sentences that require the subjunctive mood, it’s helpful to know that these hypothetical clauses typically begin with “if,” “as if,” “were,” or “as though.”
One Variation on the Subjunctive Mood So far, the examples that we have discussed use the subjunctive form of “to be,” which is “were.”
Another form of the subjunctive that can appear in a Sentence Correction question is formed via the combination of “were” and an infinitive.
Consider the following examples:
If Sheri were to arrive early, the surprise would be ruined.
If the owner of the building were to decide to fix it up, the entire neighborhood would benefit.
Bankruptcy could be avoided if the city were to manage its finances better.
If I were to purchase a plane ticket to Hawaii, I would surely go diving with Tiger Sharks.
Happiness could be found if the couple were to communicate better.
As it was in the previous examples, in these examples the subjunctive is used in clauses that describe situations that are hypothetical.
Incorrect: If Sheri was to arrive early, the surprise would be ruined.
Incorrect: If the owner of the building was to decide to fix it up, the entire neighborhood would benefit.
Incorrect: Bankruptcy could be avoided if the city was to manage its finances better.
Incorrect: If I was to purchase a plane ticket to Hawaii, I would surely go diving with Tiger Sharks.
Incorrect: Happiness could be found if the couple was to communicate better.
💡MUST KNOW:
Another form of the subjunctive that can appear in a Sentence Correction question is formed via the combination of “were” and an infinitive.
Let’s now discuss situations in which the subjunctive mood is not used.
summary:
Lesson Content The subjunctive mood is used for statements that lack certainty, such as those that describe hypothetical or conditional situations, dreams, wishes, desires, or doubts.
In a sentence that describes a hypothetical situation, the form of “to be” that should be used is the verb “were,” never the verb “was.” At first, this usage will probably sound incorrect to you.
💡MUST KNOW:
Sentences that describe hypothetical situations must use the subjunctive mood.
title: 6.13 The Subjunctive Mood: Hypothetical Situations

contents:
Lesson Content There is a major difference between a hypothetical situation and a situation in which the outcome is real, yet the speaker is unsure of that outcome.
💡MUST KNOW:
There is a major difference between a hypothetical situation and a situation in which the outcome is real, yet the speaker is unsure of that outcome.
Consider the following example sentence:
If this painting was painted by Rembrandt, then it is an amazing find.
In this situation, the painting mentioned was or was not painted by Rembrandt.
How can we use this knowledge to score points on test day? We can eliminate any sentence version that uses the subjunctive mood when describing a situation that is uncertain, not hypothetical.
Key Strategy: Eliminate any sentence version that uses the subjunctive mood when describing a situation that is uncertain, not hypothetical.
Consider the following:
Incorrect: If our representative were there that day, he can report what occurred.
Correct: If our representative was there that day, he can report what occurred.
Notice that this sentence is not describing something hypothetical.
Let’s consider another example:
Incorrect: If the song were written by Mike Posner, then some royalties should go to him.
Correct: If the song was written by Mike Posner, then some royalties should go to him.
This sentence is not about a hypothetical situation.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Choose either the subjunctive mood or the indicative mood for the verb in each of the following sentences. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now consider some Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 18 Investors suspect that it would increase the supply of government bonds if the government will pass another stimulus package. 1. [ ] (A) it would increase the supply of government bonds if the government will pass > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) the supply of government bonds would increase if the government is passing > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) it would increase the supply of government bonds if the government passes > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) the supply of government bonds would increase if the government were to pass 5. [ ] (E) the supply of government bonds would increase if the government has passed > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) Investors suspect that it would increase the supply of government bonds if the government will pass another stimulus package.
The two clauses of this version communicate conflicting meanings and do not work logically with each other.
The use of “would increase” in the first clause of the sentence indicates that the sentence is about a hypothetical outcome that investors suspect would occur if the government were to take a certain action, not about an outcome that may actually occur.
However, the use of the future indicative “will pass” in “if the government will pass another stimulus package” implies that the sentence is about events that may actually happen, not about a hypothetical situation.
Additionally, the pronoun “it” in “it would increase the supply of government bonds” does not have a logical antecedent. The sentence does not mention anything that “would increase the supply of government bonds.”
(B) Investors suspect that the supply of government bonds would increase if the government is passing another stimulus package.
The two clauses of this version communicate conflicting meanings and do not work logically with each other.
The use of “would increase” in the first clause of the sentence indicates that the sentence is about a hypothetical outcome that investors suspect would occur if the government were to take a certain action, not about an outcome that may actually occur.
However, the use of the present continuous indicative “is passing” in “if the government is passing another stimulus package” indicates that the sentence is about events that may actually be happening, not about a hypothetical situation.
(C) Investors suspect that it would increase the supply of government bonds if the government passes another stimulus package.
The two clauses in this version communicate conflicting meanings and do not work logically with each other.
The use of “would increase” in the first clause of the sentence indicates that the sentence is about a hypothetical outcome that investors suspect would occur if the government were to take a certain action, not about an outcome that may actually occur.
However, the use of the indicative “passes” in “if the government passes another stimulus package” indicates that the sentence is about events that may actually happen, not about a hypothetical situation.
Additionally, the pronoun “it” in “it would increase the supply of government bonds” does not have a logical antecedent. The sentence does not mention anything that “would increase the supply of government bonds.”
YOUR CHOICE(D) Investors suspect that the supply of government bonds would increase if the government were to pass another stimulus package.
The two clauses of this version work logically together.
The use of “would increase” in the first clause of the sentence indicates that the sentence is about a hypothetical outcome that investors suspect would occur if the government were to take a certain action.
Similarly, the use of the subjunctive “were to pass” in “if the government were to pass another stimulus package” communicates that the sentence is about a hypothetical situation.
Also, this version eliminates the antecedent-lacking pronoun “it” and, thus, conveys a logical meaning.
(E) Investors suspect that the supply of government bonds would increase if the government has passed another stimulus package.
The two clauses in this version communicate conflicting meanings and do not work logically with each other.
The use of “would increase” in the first clause of the sentence indicates that the sentence is about a hypothetical outcome that investors suspect would occur if the government were to take a certain action, not about an outcome that may actually occur.
However, the use of the present perfect indicative “has passed” in “if the government has passed another stimulus package” indicates that the sentence is about events that may actually happen, not about a hypothetical situation.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 19 If Babe Ruth, the professional baseball player who was a star in the early twentieth century, was placed into the league as it is now, in the twenty-first century, he will not have a size advantage, contributing to his dominance. 1. [ ] (A) If Babe Ruth, the professional baseball player who was a star in the early twentieth century, was placed into the league as it is now, in the twenty-first century, he will not have a size advantage, contributing > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) When Babe Ruth, the professional baseball player who was a star in the early twentieth century, is placed into the league as it is now, in the twenty-first century, he will not have a size advantage, which contributes > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) If Babe Ruth, the professional baseball player who was a star in the early twentieth century, was placed into the league as it is now, in the twenty-first century, he would not have a size advantage contributing > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) When Babe Ruth, the professional baseball player who was a star in the early twentieth century, were placed into the league as it is now, in the twenty-first century, he did not have the size advantage, which contributed > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) If Babe Ruth, the professional baseball player who was a star in the early twentieth century, were to be placed into the league as it is now, in the twenty-first century, he would not have the size advantage that contributed View Solution Question Level: easy Since the sentence is about placing someone who played baseball in the early twentieth century into the league as it is in the twenty-first century, the scenario is almost certainly hypothetical. So, unless, at over 100 years old, Babe Ruth might still be capable of playing baseball professionally, the correct voice to use in the “if” clause must be the subjunctive. Also, as the question is written, even if Babe Ruth’s playing baseball in the twenty-first century were possible, because of tense and modifier issues, none of the versions that use the indicative in the “if” clause would work anyway.
(A) If Babe Ruth, the professional baseball player who was a star in the early twentieth century, was placed into the league as it is now, in the twenty-first century, he will not have a size advantage, contributing to his dominance.
The meaning conveyed by the wording of this version, “if Babe Ruth … was placed into the league … he will,” is illogical, because the implications of the meaning are that Babe Ruth, who played baseball around 100 years before the statement was made, may have actually been placed into the league around the time the statement was made. Since the placing into the league of a baseball player who played around 100 years before the statement was made is almost certainly purely hypothetical, the use of the indicative “was placed” does not make sense. The subjunctive should be used instead.
Also, the use of the participial phrase “contributing to his dominance” after the clause “he will not have a size advantage” and separated from that clause by a comma results in the sentence’s conveying the illogical meaning that, by not having a size advantage, Babe Ruth will contribute to his dominance.
(B) When Babe Ruth, the professional baseball player who was a star in the early twentieth century, is placed into the league as it is now, in the twenty-first century, he will not have a size advantage, which contributes to his dominance.
The meaning conveyed by the wording of this version, “when Babe Ruth … is placed into the league … he will,” is illogical, because the implications of the meaning are that Babe Ruth, who played baseball around 100 years before the statement was made, is actually going to be placed into the league after the time the statement was made. Since the placing into the league of a baseball player who played around 100 years before the statement was made is almost certainly purely hypothetical, the use of the indicative “is placed” does not make sense. The subjunctive should be used instead.
Also, the use of the relative clause “which contributes to his dominance” does not make sense. “Which contributes to his dominance” seems to modify “size advantage.” However, the clause that precedes “which contributes to his dominance” says that Babe Ruth will not have a size advantage. So, there is no size advantage for “which contributes to his dominance” to modify, because the point that the preceding clause makes is that no such size advantage will exist.
(C) If Babe Ruth, the professional baseball player who was a star in the early twentieth century, was placed into the league as it is now, in the twenty-first century, he would not have a size advantage contributing to his dominance.
The meaning conveyed by the wording of this version, “if Babe Ruth … was placed into the league … he would not have,” is illogical for two reasons.
One reason is that the use of “he would” makes clear that the statement is about what “would” be the case in a hypothetical scenario, whereas the indicative “if he was” implies that the scenario presented is possibly actual.
The other reason is that the implications of the meaning conveyed by “if he was placed into the league” are that Babe Ruth, who played baseball around 100 years before the statement was made, may actually have been placed into the league around the time the statement was made. Since, the placing into the league of a baseball player who played around 100 years before the statement was made is almost certainly purely hypothetical, the use of the indicative does not make sense.
For the above two reasons, the use of the indicative “was placed” is incorrect, and the use of the subjunctive “were placed,’’ which would indicate that the scenario described is hypothetical, makes more sense.
(D) When Babe Ruth, the professional baseball player who was a star in the early twentieth century, were placed into the league as it is now, in the twenty-first century, he did not have the size advantage, which contributed to his dominance.
Since the use of “when” before a statement implies that the events described by the statement did or will in fact occur, the use of the subjunctive in a statement beginning with “when” does not make sense, because events described via the use of the subjunctive are understood to be hypothetical, not actual. So, saying “when Babe Ruth … were placed” is not logical. “Were” is either the subjunctive form or the plural form of “to be,” neither of which works in the context of this version.
Also, the statement “he did not have the size advantage, which contributed to his dominance” is not logical. “He did not have the size advantage, which contributed to his dominance” expresses (1) that, in the universe, there is one size advantage, “the size advantage;” (2) that Babe Ruth did not have that size advantage; and (3) that, apparently, somehow, that size advantage contributed to Babe Ruth’s dominance, perhaps at some time when he did have that size advantage.
YOUR CHOICE(E) If Babe Ruth, the professional baseball player who was a star in the early twentieth century, were to be placed into the league as it is now, in the twenty-first century, he would not have the size advantage that contributed to his dominance.
This version uses the subjunctive “were to be placed” in conveying the logical meaning, that, in a hypothetical situation in which Babe Ruth is placed into today’s league, he would not have “the sized advantage that contributed to his dominance.”
Notice, the use of the restrictive relative clause “that contributed to his dominance” effectively expresses that the size advantage mentioned is a specific one, the size advantage that contributed to Babe Ruth’s dominance.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss how the subjunctive mood is used in command situations.
summary:
Lesson Content There is a major difference between a hypothetical situation and a situation in which the outcome is real, yet the speaker is unsure of that outcome.
💡MUST KNOW:
There is a major difference between a hypothetical situation and a situation in which the outcome is real, yet the speaker is unsure of that outcome.
Consider the following example sentence:
If this painting was painted by Rembrandt, then it is an amazing find.
title: 6.14 The Subjunctive Mood is Not Used in Sentences that Express Uncertainty

contents:
Lesson Content Along with hypothetical situations, certain types of “command situations” require the use of the subjunctive mood, which, in these situations, is referred to as the “command subjunctive.”
In these situations, we’ll see a request, suggestion, demand, or command word followed by the word “that.”
💡MUST KNOW:
Request, suggestion, demand, or command words followed by the word “that” require the subjunctive mood, which, in these situations, is referred to as the command subjunctive.
This idea is illustrated in the sentences below:
Correct: I demand that you be respectful at dinner tonight.
Incorrect: I demand that you are respectful at dinner tonight.
Normally we would say “you are,” not “you be,” since we use the indicative mood in most cases.
Let’s look at another example sentence.
Incorrect: I demand that you are at the gym by noon.
Correct: I demand that you be at the gym by noon.
The good news is that we can use the command subjunctive with any verb, not just the verb “to be.” Consider the following sentence:
I insist that he respect me at dinner tonight.
Again, we have a command verb followed by the word “that,” which is our signal to use the command subjunctive – that is, the base form of the verb.
Correct: I insist that he respect me at dinner tonight.
Incorrect: I insist that he respects me at dinner tonight.
Let’s consider another:
Incorrect: I insist that she eats her lunch before the meeting.
Correct: I insist that she eat her lunch before the meeting.
Again, notice that we use the command subjunctive.
💡MUST KNOW:
The subjunctive form of the verb “to be” used in command sentences is always “be.” Other verbs used in command sentences will be expressed in their unconjugated form.
Let’s look at some other common situations:
Correct: The coach requests that uniforms be returned on time.
Incorrect: The coach requests that uniforms are returned on time.
Correct: The regulations require that everyone submit a customs form.
Incorrect: The regulations require that everyone submits a customs form.
Correct: I suggest that his candidacy be reevaluated.
Incorrect: I suggest that his candidacy is reevaluated.
It’s helpful to memorize this list of verbs that are often followed by the command subjunctive.
Now, to be clear, not every sentence that involves a request, suggestion, demand, or command must use the command subjunctive.
For instance, the following sentences are all correct:
The spokesperson asked that everyone wait for the formal announcement.
The spokesperson asked everyone to wait for the formal announcement
After the debacle, the prime minister ordered that the secretary conduct a full review.
After the debacle, the prime minister ordered the secretary to conduct a full review.
Often, a lender will require that a borrower purchase insurance.
Often, a lender will require a borrower to purchase insurance.
💡MUST KNOW:
Not every sentence that involves a request, suggestion, demand, or command must use the command subjunctive.
So, what we’re discussing in this lesson is simply that the command subjunctive is required in situations in which a request, suggestion, demand, or command word is followed by “that.”
How can we use this knowledge to score points on test day? We can eliminate any sentence version that contains a “that” preceded by request, suggestion, demand, or command word but does not use the subjunctive mood.
Key Strategy: Eliminate any sentence version that contains a “that” preceded by request, suggestion, demand, or command word but does not use the subjunctive mood, which, in these situations, is referred to as the command subjunctive.
Let’s practice eliminating such sentences.
Incorrect: The general requires you be at your command post from midnight to 8 a.m.
Incorrect: The general requires that you should be at your command post from midnight to 8 a.m.
Incorrect: The general requires that you will be at your command post from midnight to 8 a.m.
Incorrect: The general requires that you are to be at your command post from midnight to 8 a.m.
Incorrect: The general requires that you are at your command post from midnight to 8 a.m.
Correct: The general requires that you be at your command post from midnight to 8 a.m.
Notice that all of the sentence versions except the final version are incorrect because they don’t use the command subjunctive.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice For each sentence, choose Incorrect, if the sentence is flawed, or Correct, if the sentence is well constructed and conveys a meaning that makes sense. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s consider some Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 20 Advocates of longer terms for members of the U.S. 1. [ ] (A) that, rather than run for reelection every two years, each representative will run every four years > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) that each representative run for reelection every four years instead of every two 3. [ ] (C) that, instead of every two years, each representative should run for reelection every four years > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) that, rather than running for reelection every two years, each representative is running every four > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) that each representative runs for reelection every four years instead of two years > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) Advocates of longer terms for members of the U.S. House of Representatives propose that, rather than run for reelection every two years, each representative will run every four years , so that more time can be devoted to legislating than to campaigning.
The phrase “propose that” should not be followed by the indicative “will run.” Advocates would not propose what will happen.
“Propose that” should be followed by the command subjunctive “run.”
Thus, “advocates propose that … each representative will run every four years” is incorrect.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Advocates of longer terms for members of the U.S. House of Representatives propose that each representative run for reelection every four years instead of every two , so that more time can be devoted to legislating than to campaigning.
This version is the only one that correctly follows the phrase “propose that” with the command subjunctive “run.”
The sentence structure is logical, and the sentence effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(C) Advocates of longer terms for members of the U.S. House of Representatives propose that, instead of every two years, each representative should run for reelection every four years , so that more time can be devoted to legislating than to campaigning.
The phrase “propose that” should not be followed by the indicative “should run.” Advocates would not propose what should happen. The idea that something “should” happen is an opinion, and advocates would not propose an opinion.
“Propose that” should be followed by the command subjunctive “run.”
(D) Advocates of longer terms for members of the U.S. House of Representatives propose that, rather than running for reelection every two years, each representative is running every four , so that more time can be devoted to legislating than to campaigning.
The phrase “propose that” should not be followed by the indicative “is running.” The present continuous “is running” indicates that the event “each representative is running” is currently ongoing, and advocates would not propose what is currently ongoing.
“Propose that” should be followed by the command subjunctive “run.”
(E) Advocates of longer terms for members of the U.S. House of Representatives propose that each representative runs for reelection every four years instead of two years , so that more time can be devoted to legislating than to campaigning.
The phrase “propose that” should not be followed by the indicative “runs.” The present indicative “runs” indicates that the event “each representative runs” occurs routinely, and advocates would not propose what already occurs routinely.
“Propose that” should be followed by the command subjunctive “run.”
Notice that, even though “representative” is a singular noun, because the command subjunctive is needed, “run” not “runs” is correct.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 21 One long term solution to the nation’s problems, which have been discussed but have not been widely implemented, is mandating secondary education to include instruction focused on critical thinking. 1. [ ] (A) problems, which have been discussed but have not been widely implemented, is mandating secondary education to include > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) problems that has been discussed but not widely implemented is mandating that secondary education include 3. [ ] (C) problems, having been discussed but not widely implemented, is mandating that secondary education includes > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) problems being discussed but not widely implemented is mandating secondary education, so that it includes > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) problems that have been discussed but not widely implemented, is to mandate that secondary education should include > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium The expression “mandating that” should be followed by the command subjunctive.
(A) One long term solution to the nation’s problems, which have been discussed but have not been widely implemented, is mandating secondary education to include instruction focused on critical thinking.
Since the plural “have” agrees with the plural “problems,” the relative clause “which have been discussed but have not been widely implemented” seems to modify “problems.” However, while problems might be discussed, problems would not be implemented. So, if that relative clause is understood to modify “problems,” then the sentence does not make sense. Of course, a “solution” would be “discussed but … not widely implemented.” However, if the relative clause is understood to modify “solution,” the sentence is grammatically flawed, because the plural “have” does not agree with the singular “solution.”
Also, the expression “mandating secondary education to include” does not make sense. “Mandating” is not synonymous with “commanding” or “ordering.” One does not mandate someone or something to do something the way one would command someone to do something, such as include instruction focused on critical thinking. One mandates something. For instance, one would mandate “that secondary education include instruction focused on critical thinking.”
CORRECT ANSWER(B) One long term solution to the nation’s problems that has been discussed but not widely implemented is mandating that secondary education include instruction focused on critical thinking.
The relative clause “that has been discussed but not widely implemented” logically modifies “one solution to the nation’s problems.”
Also, “mandating that” is correctly followed by the command subjunctive expression “secondary education include.”
YOUR CHOICE(C) One long term solution to the nation’s problems, having been discussed but not widely implemented, is mandating that secondary education includes instruction focused on critical thinking.
The use of the present participial phrase “having been discussed but not widely implemented” results in the sentence’s expressing the illogical meaning that, in the process of having been discussed, a “solution to the nation’s problems” is what it is. For clarity, here’s an analogous sentence:
Jose, having been discussed, is my brother.
Clearly, Jose’s having been discussed has no connection to his being someone’s brother.
Similarly, the solution to the nation’s problems’ having been discussed has no connection to the solution’s being mandating that secondary education include instruction focused on critical thinking. So, adding the information “having been discussed but not widely implemented” via the use of a present participial phrase, which seems to connect that information to the main topic of the sentence, does not make sense.
Also, this version incorrectly follows “mandating that” with the indicative voice expression “secondary education includes.”
(D) One long term solution to the nation’s problems being discussed but not widely implemented is mandating secondary education, so that it includes instruction focused on critical thinking.
The use of the restrictive participial phrase “being discussed but not widely implemented” to modify “one solution to the nation’s problems” makes sense.
However, “mandating secondary education, so that it includes” does not make sense. Mandating secondary education would not somehow result in secondary education’s including something.
(E) One long term solution to the nation’s problems that have been discussed but not widely implemented, is to mandate that secondary education should include instruction focused on critical thinking.
Since the plural “have” agrees with the plural “problems,” the relative clause “that have been discussed but not been widely implemented” seems to modify “problems.” However, while problems might be discussed, problems would not be implemented. So, if that relative clause is understood to modify “problems,” then the sentence does not make sense. Of course, a “solution” would be “discussed but … not widely implemented.” However, if the relative clause is understood to modify “solution,” the sentence is grammatically flawed, because the plural “have” does not agree with the singular “solution.”
Also, this version incorrectly follows “mandating that” with “secondary education should include.” One would not mandate that something should happen. The fact that something should happen is conceptually somewhat separate from the concept of mandating it. One would simply mandate that something happens.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 22 Since, in recent years, local rivers’ overflowing their banks during spring and summer rainstorms has become more frequent, the city is urging all who own homes in the flood zone to take the flood damage mitigation measures listed on the city’s website. 1. [x] (A) has become more frequent, the city is urging all who own homes in the flood zone to take 2. [ ] (B) have become more frequent, the city urges that all who own homes in the flood zone take > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) having become more frequent, the city is urging all who own homes in the flood zone, so that they take > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) is becoming more frequent, the city has urged all who own homes in the flood zone that they should take > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) are becoming more frequent, the city has been urging all who own homes in the flood zone taking > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard Both of the following are correct.
The city urges that all who own homes in the flood zone take measures.
The city urges all who own homes in the flood zone to take measures.
So, the fact that one choice in this question is structured like one of the above examples and another choice is structured like the other of the above examples does not constitute a decision point that one can use to choose one of those choices over the other. Choosing one of those choices over the other requires finding another decision point.
Also, correctly answering this question takes avoiding becoming overly focused on whether “urged” or “urged that” is followed by something that works with it logically, because there are other issues that have to be considered as well, such as the subject-verb agreement issue in the version created via the use of choice (B).
YOUR CHOICE(A) Since, in recent years, local rivers’ overflowing their banks during spring and summer rainstorms has become more frequent, the city is urging all who own homes in the flood zone to take the flood damage mitigation measures listed on the city’s website.
The singular “has become” agrees with the singular subject “overflowing.” (The inclusion in the sentence before and after “overflowing” of multiple plural nouns – “years,” “rivers,” “banks,” and “rainstorms” – was done for the purpose of confusing test-takers into misperceiving that the subject of the sentence is plural, rather than singular.)
Also, “the city is urging all … to take …” is correctly structured and expresses a logical meaning.
(B) Since, in recent years, local rivers’ overflowing their banks during spring and summer rainstorms have become more frequent, the city urges that all who own homes in the flood zone take the flood damage mitigation measures listed on the city’s website.
“The city urges that all … take …” is correctly structured and expresses a logical meaning.
However, the plural “have become” does not agree with the singular subject “overflowing.” (The inclusion in the sentence before and after “overflowing” of multiple plural nouns – “years,” “rivers,” “banks,” and “rainstorms” – was done for the purpose of confusing test-takers into misperceiving that the subject of the sentence is plural, rather than singular.)
(C) Since, in recent years, local rivers’ overflowing their banks during spring and summer rainstorms having become more frequent, the city is urging all who own homes in the flood zone, so that they take the flood damage mitigation measures listed on the city’s website.
In this sentence, “since” seems to be used as a conjunction similar to “because.” So, “since” should connect two clauses. In this version, what follows “since” is “overflowing … having become more frequent.” That structure is not a clause but, rather, an absolute phrase. So, in this version, the conjunction “since” incorrectly connects an absolute phrase – a modifier – with a clause.
An alternative possibility is that, in this version, “since” is used as preposition indicating a starting point in time, in which case the meaning meant to be conveyed would be the following. Since the point in time when overflowing became more frequent, the city has urged. However, the wording of the sentence is not structured to correctly express that meaning. For one thing, in that case, “the city is urging” should be the present perfect continuous “the city has been urging,” as in “Since x point in time, the city has been urging ….”
Also, “the city is urging all who own homes in the flood zone, so that they take” is poorly structured and, therefore, does not effectively convey a clear meaning. It does not make clear what the city is urging all to do. Rather it simply says that the city is urging all, and then suggests that the purpose of this nonspecific urging is to get “all” to take certain measures. So, the sentence fails to logically connect the ideas of urging and getting people to take measures. Contrast the wording of this version with “urging all … to take” in the version created via the use of (A) and “urges that all … take” in the version created via the use of (B).
(D) Since, in recent years, local rivers’ overflowing their banks during spring and summer rainstorms is becoming more frequent, the city has urged all who own homes in the flood zone that they should take the flood damage mitigation measures listed on the city’s website.
