Since, “in recent years” is largely in the past, the use of the present tense “is becoming” is suboptimal. It makes more sense to use, as does (A), the present perfect “has become,” which matches what the sentence suggests, which is that the overflowing’s becoming more frequent began in the past and continues in the present.
Also, this version incorrectly follows “has urged … that” with “they should take.” An action would be urged. “They should take” does not describe an action. It expresses a judgment. “Has urged … that” should be followed by the command subjunctive expression “they take.”
(E) Since, in recent years, local rivers’ overflowing their banks during spring and summer rainstorms are becoming more frequent, the city has been urging all who own homes in the flood zone taking the flood damage mitigation measures listed on the city’s website.
The plural “are becoming” does not agree with the singular subject “overflowing.” (The inclusion in the sentence before and after “overflowing” of multiple plural nouns – “years,” “rivers,” “banks,” and “rainstorms” – was done for the purpose of confusing test-takers into misperceiving that the subject of the sentence is plural, rather than singular.)
Also, since, “in recent years” is largely in the past, the use of the present tense “are becoming” is suboptimal. It makes more sense to use, as does (A), the present perfect “has become,” which matches what the sentence suggests, which is that the overflowing’s becoming more frequent began in the past and continues in the present.
Also, “the city is urging all who own homes in the flood zone taking” is poorly structured and, therefore, does not effectively convey a clear meaning. It does not make clear what the city is urging all to do. Rather it says, basically, that the city is urging all taking measures. Thus, the sentence fails to logically connect the ideas of urging and getting people to take measures. Contrast the wording of this version with “urging all … to take” in the version created via the use of (A) and “urges that all … take” in the version created via the use of (B).
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss conditional sentences and the conditional mood.
summary:
Lesson Content Along with hypothetical situations, certain types of “command situations” require the use of the subjunctive mood, which, in these situations, is referred to as the “command subjunctive.”
In these situations, we’ll see a request, suggestion, demand, or command word followed by the word “that.”
💡MUST KNOW:
Request, suggestion, demand, or command words followed by the word “that” require the subjunctive mood, which, in these situations, is referred to as the command subjunctive.
title: 6.15 Use of the Subjunctive Mood in Command Situations

contents:
Lesson Content Like the subjunctive mood, the conditional mood is used in sentences about conditions and results that are purely hypothetical.
💡MUST KNOW:
The conditional mood is used in sentences about conditions and results that are purely hypothetical.
The conditional mood can be formed by combining “would” or “could” with a finite verb.
Consider the following examples, each of which describes a purely hypothetical situation:
If I knew how to speak French, I would move to Paris.
If they could see us now, they would be laughing.
If the fruit had been outside, it could have been eaten by rabbits.
Had the method been published, people could have been using it.
💡MUST KNOW:
The conditional mood is formed by combining “would” or “could” with a finite verb.
As we well know, when describing an action or event that is possible, we often use the verbs “will” or “can.” Notice that this usage is in contrast to the use of “would” or “could,” which are used in hypothetical situations.
💡MUST KNOW:
We often use the verbs “will” or “can” when describing an action or event that is actually possible, but we use “would” or “could” when describing actions or events that are purely hypothetical.
So how do we use this knowledge to score points on test day.
Key Strategy: We eliminate any sentence versions that use “will” or “can” in describing actions or events that are purely hypothetical.
Consider the following sentence versions:
Incorrect: If it were possible to fly by flapping one’s arms, I will get home faster.
Correct: If it were possible to fly by flapping one’s arms, I would get home faster.
We can quickly eliminate sentence version one because a context clue (if it were possible …) alerts us that this sentence is describing a purely hypothetical situation.
We see that in sentence version two, the clause “I would get home faster” correctly uses the conditional “would get” to express a proposition that would be true “if it were possible to fly by flapping one’s arms.”
Consider the following sentence versions:
Incorrect: If Ashanti had arrived early, the surprise will have been ruined.
Correct: If Ashanti had arrived early, the surprise would have been ruined.
We can quickly eliminate sentence version one because a context clue (if Ashanti had arrived early) alerts us that this sentence is describing a purely hypothetical situation.
In the second sentence version, the clause “the surprise would have been ruined” correctly uses the conditional “would have been ruined” to express a proposition that would be true under the condition “Ashanti had arrived early.”

title: 6.16 The Conditional Mood

contents:
Lesson Content Remember, the conditional mood is used in sentences describing purely hypothetical things.
💡MUST KNOW:
The conditional mood is used in sentences describing purely hypothetical things. It is not used in situations that could actually be true.
For review, consider the following sentence versions:
Incorrect: If the movie were as good as the trailer, it will be very popular.
Incorrect: If the movie were as good as the trailer, it should be very popular.
Correct: If the movie were as good as the trailer, it would be very popular.
In all three versions, the dependent clause that states the condition, “if the movie were as good as the trailer,” is in the subjunctive mood.
So, sentence versions one and two are incorrect because both of them use the indicative mood.
However, and this is important, not all conditional sentences must use the subjunctive mood.
💡MUST KNOW:
Not all conditional sentences must use the subjunctive mood. Many sentences are conditional yet at the same time do not describe something hypothetical.
For example, consider the following correct conditional sentences that use the indicative mood:
If Rene prefers to draw all day, then she should find a way to sell her art.
If the engineers solve this problem, the robot will be the best in its class.
If the CEO learns to meditate, everyone’s stress level may decrease significantly.
Notice that, in each case, the statement made in the main clause does not express something hypothetical.
Here’s another set:
Incorrect: If she wears normal clothes and sunglasses, she would go unrecognized.
Correct: If she wears normal clothes and sunglasses, she will go unrecognized.
Correct: If she wears normal clothes and sunglasses, she may go unrecognized.
Notice that each sentence version is conditional yet at the same time does not describe something hypothetical.
Sentence version one, which uses the conditional “would go,” is incorrect, because we don’t use the conditional mood for sentences that discuss things that are possible.
On the other hand, sentence version two, which uses the simple future “she will,” is correct.
So, in order to arrive at the correct answers to Sentence Correction questions involving conditional statements, we have to notice whether the statements are about actual or hypothetical situations and whether the sentences use the conditional or indicative mood appropriately.
💡MUST KNOW:
In order to arrive at the correct answers to Sentence Correction questions involving conditional statements, we have to notice whether the statements are about actual or hypothetical situations and whether the sentences use the conditional or indicative mood appropriately.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice For each sentence, choose Incorrect, if the sentence incorrectly uses a verb mood or is otherwise illogical, or Correct, if the sentence is grammatically correct and makes sense. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s answer some Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 23 If Sandeep will be chosen as next president of West Highlands Animal Shelter, a no-kill shelter that is committed to rehabilitating abused animals, he will surely bring much needed fresh energy to the position. 1. [ ] (A) will be chosen as next president of West Highlands Animal Shelter, a no-kill shelter that is committed to rehabilitating abused animals, he will > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) is chosen the next president of West Highlands Animal Shelter that is a no-kill animal shelter committed to rehabilitating abused animals, he would > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) were chosen to be the next president of West Highlands Animal Shelter, which is a no-kill shelter committed to rehabilitating animals of abuse, he will > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) was chosen as the next president of West Highlands Animal Shelter, being a no-kill animal shelter committed to the rehabilitation of abused animals, he would > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) were to be chosen as the next president of West Highlands Animal Shelter, a no-kill animal shelter committed to the rehabilitation of abused animals, he would View Solution Question Level: easy Correctly answering this question takes careful analysis of the relationships between the “if” clauses and the main clauses in the various versions and identifying the choice in which the “if” clause and the main clause work together logically.
Also, some versions have modifier issues.
YOUR CHOICE(A) If Sandeep will be chosen as next president of West Highlands Animal Shelter, a no-kill shelter that is committed to rehabilitating abused animals, he will surely bring much needed fresh energy to the position.
This version is about what will happen under certain conditions. It makes sense to use the future tense “he will … bring” in describing what will happen.
However, it does not make sense that Sandeep will bring fresh energy under the condition that he will be chosen. What makes sense is that Sandeep will bring fresh energy under the condition that he is chosen. So, the use of the future tense “will be chosen” does not make sense.
(B) If Sandeep is chosen the next president of West Highlands Animal Shelter that is a no-kill animal shelter committed to rehabilitating abused animals, he would surely bring much needed fresh energy to the position.
This version illogically uses in the “if” clause the indicative expression “Sandeep is chosen,” which indicates that there is an existing possibility that Sandeep will be chosen as the next president of the shelter, with the conditional expression “he would … bring,” which indicates that the sentence is about a hypothetical situation rather than an existing possibility.
Also, since presumably there is only one West Highlands Animal Shelter, using the restrictive modifier “that is a no-kill animal shelter …,” which seems to restrict the definition of “West Highlands Animal Shelter” to a particular shelter, does not make sense. It’s already clear that West Highlands Animal Shelter is a particular shelter.
(C) If Sandeep were chosen to be the next president of West Highlands Animal Shelter, which is a no-kill shelter committed to rehabilitating animals of abuse, he will surely bring much needed fresh energy to the position.
This version illogically uses in the “if” clause the subjunctive expression “Sandeep were,” which indicates that Sandeep’s being chosen as the next president of the shelter is merely hypothetical, with the indicative expression “he will … bring,” which indicates that the sentence is about, not a hypothetical situation but the actual possibility that Sandeep, having been chosen as the next president of the shelter, will bring fresh energy.
Also, “rehabilitating animals of abuse” does not really make sense. One might rid animals of something or cure animals of something, but one would not rehabilitate animals of something, such as abuse.
(D) If Sandeep was chosen as the next president of West Highlands Animal Shelter, being a no-kill animal shelter committed to the rehabilitation of abused animals, he would surely bring much needed fresh energy to the position.
This version illogically uses in the “if” clause the indicative expression “Sandeep was chosen,” which indicates that it is possible that Sandeep was actually chosen as the next president of the shelter, with the conditional expression “he would … bring,” which indicates that the sentence is about a hypothetical situation rather than an actually possible situation.
Also, since the logical target of the participial modifier “being a no-kill animal shelter committed …” is either the subject of the clause that precedes the modifier or the subject of the clause that follows the modifier, which subjects are “Sandeep” and “he,” respectively, read literally, the sentence conveys that “Sandeep” is “being a no-kill animal shelter.”
CORRECT ANSWER(E) If Sandeep were to be chosen as the next president of West Highlands Animal Shelter, a no-kill animal shelter committed to the rehabilitation of abused animals, he would surely bring much needed fresh energy to the position.
This version logically uses in the “if” clause the subjunctive expression “Sandeep were,” which indicates that Sandeep’s being chosen as the next president of the shelter is hypothetical, with the conditional expression “he would … bring,” which indicates that the sentence is about a hypothetical situation.
Also, the appositive “a no-kill animal shelter committed …” logically modifies “West Highlands Animal Shelter.”
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 24 If the company‘s car was damaged by the sandstorm, the company would have had the car repaired by its insurance company, but because the car was damaged through operator negligence, the insurance company has denied payment for the repairs. 1. [ ] (A) If the company‘s car was damaged by the sandstorm, the company would > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Were the company’s car damaged by the sandstorm, the company should > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) If the company’s car were damaged by the sandstorm, the company will > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) If the company’s car was damaged by the sandstorm, the company may > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Had the company’s car been damaged by the sandstorm, the company could View Solution Question Level: medium The non-underlined portion of the sentence says that the car was damaged through operator negligence. So, the sentence indicates that the car was not damaged by the sandstorm. Thus, the car’s being damaged by the sandstorm is a hypothetical scenario, not an actual possibility. Since the condition that the car was damaged by the sandstorm and the consequences of the condition are hypothetical, the subjunctive mood must be used in the “if” clause and the conditional mood must be used in the independent clause about the hypothetical consequences of the car’s being damaged in the sandstorm.
(A) If the company‘s car was damaged by the sandstorm, the company would have had the car repaired by its insurance company, but because the car was damaged through operator negligence, the insurance company has denied payment for the repairs.
Since the non-underlined portion of the sentence indicates that the car was not damaged by the sandstorm, the use of the indicative “was damaged,” which indicates that the car may in fact have been damaged by the sandstorm does not make sense.
(B) Were the company’s car damaged by the sandstorm, the company should have had the car repaired by its insurance company, but because the car was damaged through operator negligence, the insurance company has denied payment for the repairs.
“Were the company’s car damaged” is a subjunctive expression functionally equivalent to “if the company’s car were damaged.” So, while this version does not use the word “if,” it does have a clause that uses the subjunctive and functions as an “if” clause would.
However, since the scenario does not hypothetically exist in the present but, rather, hypothetically existed in the past, the use of “were … damaged” does not make sense. “Had … been damaged” should have been used instead.
Also, “should” is used in the expression of a recommendation or an expectation, not to indicate that a situation is hypothetical. So, “should” should not be used in this context. “Would” should be used instead.
(C) If the company’s car were damaged by the sandstorm, the company will have had the car repaired by its insurance company, but because the car was damaged through operator negligence, the insurance company has denied payment for the repairs.
Since the car’s having been damaged by the sandstorm is hypothetical, the use of the subjunctive makes sense. However, since the scenario does not hypothetically exist in the present but, rather, hypothetically existed in the past, the use of “were damaged” does not make sense. “Had been damaged” should have been used instead.
Also, since the condition that the car had been damaged by the sandstorm and the consequences of that condition are hypothetical, the conditional should be used in the main clause that describes the consequence of the condition. However, “will have had” is not in the conditional mood. The formation of the conditional uses “would,” not “will.”
(D) If the company’s car was damaged by the sandstorm, the company may have had the car repaired by its insurance company, but because the car was damaged through operator negligence, the insurance company has denied payment for the repairs.
Since the non-underlined portion of the sentence indicates that the car was not damaged by the sandstorm, the use of the indicative “was damaged,” which indicates that the car may in fact have been damaged by the sandstorm does not make sense.
Also, since the scenario in which the car was damaged by the sandstorm is purely hypothetical, it is not the case that the company may have had the car repaired by the insurance company, and so, the use of the indicative “may have had” does not make sense.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Had the company’s car been damaged by the sandstorm, the company could have had the car repaired by its insurance company, but because the car was damaged through operator negligence, the insurance company has denied payment for the repairs.
“Had the company’s car been damaged” is a subjunctive expression functionally equivalent to “if the company’s car had been damaged.” So, while this version does not use the word “if,” it does have a clause that uses the subjunctive and functions as an “if” clause would. That clause is correctly used in combination with the conditional expression “the company could have had” to express that, in a hypothetical situation in which the car was damaged by a sandstorm, the company could have had the car repaired by the insurance company.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 25 The actor refused to acknowledge the allegations made by the tabloid photographer, saying that doing so will only lend them credence and encourage copycats. 1. [ ] (A) doing so will only lend them > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) to do it would only lend it > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) to do this, he would only lend them > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) to do so would only lend them 5. [ ] (E) in so doing, it would be lending them > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: hard The underlined portion of the sentence refers to an action that has not happened and is not expected to happen. Therefore, the actor’s taking that action is hypothetical, not an actual possibility. So, while there is no “if” clause in the sentence, the sentence is about what would occur in a hypothetical situation, one in which the actor acknowledges the allegations. So, it makes sense to use of the conditional “would … lend” in describing the hypothetical result of the actor’s acknowledging the allegations.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The actor refused to acknowledge the allegations made by the tabloid photographer, saying that doing so will only lend them credence and encourage copycats.
“doing so” refers to “acknowledge the allegations,” an action that the actor does not plan to take. So, the sentence is about what would happen in a hypothetical situation in which the actor is “doing so.” Therefore, the use of the indicative “will … lend,” which expresses what will actually, not hypothetically, happen if the actor acknowledges the allegations does not make sense.
(B) The actor refused to acknowledge the allegations made by the tabloid photographer, saying that to do it would only lend it credence and encourage copycats.
While this version correctly uses the conditional “would … lend” in describing a hypothetical situation, it has pronoun use issues since, in neither instance in which the pronoun “it” appears, does “it” have a clear referent.
While, conceivably, the first “it” could refer to “acknowledge the allegations,” the expression “to do so” is considered correct, while the expression “to do it,” in which “do it” refers to an action, is not preferred by the GMAT.
The second “it” does not have any possible logical referent. If the second “it” were also to refer to “acknowledge the allegations,” the sentence would convey the nonsensical meaning that to acknowledge the allegations would lend acknowledging the allegations credence, and there is in the sentence no singular noun to which “it” could refer. “It” would not refer to “tabloid photographer,” because “it” is not used in referring to people.
(C) The actor refused to acknowledge the allegations made by the tabloid photographer, saying that to do this, he would only lend them credence and encourage copycats.
While this version correctly uses the conditional “would … lend” in describing a hypothetical situation, it has some clear flaws.”
While conceivably the “do this” could refer to “acknowledge the allegations,” the expression “to do so” is considered correct, while the expression “to do this,” in which “do this” refers to an action, is not preferred by the GMAT.
Also, “them” must refer to “allegations,” and “do this” must refer, though possibly not correctly, to “acknowledge the allegations.” Therefore, “to do this, he would only lend them credence and encourage copycats” conveys the nonsensical meaning that, in order to acknowledge the allegations, the actor would only lend the allegations credence and encourage copycats.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) The actor refused to acknowledge the allegations made by the tabloid photographer, saying that to do so would only lend them credence and encourage copycats.
“To do so” refers to “acknowledge the allegations,” an action that the actor does not plan to take. So, the sentence is about what would happen in a hypothetical situation in which the actor chooses “to do so.” Therefore, the use of the conditional “would … lend,” which expresses what would happen, but won’t actually happen, makes sense.
Also, “them” logically refers to “the allegations.”
(E) The actor refused to acknowledge the allegations made by the tabloid photographer, saying that in so doing, it would be lending them credence and encourage copycats.
While this version correctly uses the conditional “would be lending” in describing a hypothetical situation, it has a pronoun use issue since the pronoun “it” does not have a logical referent. Since the one “doing” something must be the actor, “in so doing” has to modify what the actor would achieve “in so doing.” Therefore, “would be lending” must be what the actor would achieve “in so doing.” However, “it,” the subject of “would be lending,” would not refer to “the actor.”
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss the topic of verb voice.
summary:
Lesson Content Remember, the conditional mood is used in sentences describing purely hypothetical things.
💡MUST KNOW:
The conditional mood is used in sentences describing purely hypothetical things. It is not used in situations that could actually be true.
For review, consider the following sentence versions:
Incorrect: If the movie were as good as the trailer, it will be very popular.
Incorrect: If the movie were as good as the trailer, it should be very popular.
title: 6.17 The Conditional Mood Is Not Used in Sentences that Are Not Purely Hypothetical

contents:
Lesson Content In addition to using variations in verb tense and verb mood to test our skill in assessing the effectiveness of sentences, SC questions use verb voice.
There are two verb voices, active voice and passive voice.
Active voice is the more commonly used verb voice.
💡MUST KNOW:
When the subject of a clause performs the action, the corresponding verb is in the active voice.
Consider the sentence below:
S V DO
The dog caught the ball. In this sentence, we observe the most common sentence structure in the English language: subject, verb, direct object.
In the passive voice, the subject of the clause has an action being done to it.
S V O
The ball was caught by the dog.
In this passive voice construction, what was initially the direct object, “the ball,” now acts as the subject, followed by a passive voice construction of the verb “to catch,” and the former subject, “dog,” becomes the object of a prepositional phrase.
Note that the passive voice form of the verb is always the past participle preceded by a form of the helping verb “to be.” So, in this case, the passive verb is “was caught.”
💡MUST KNOW:
When the subject of a sentence has an action being done to it, the corresponding verb is in the passive voice. The passive voice is composed of a form of “to be” followed by the past participle of the main verb.
Although both the active voice and passive voice versions of the example above are grammatically correct, the two sentences are different because they are focused on different things.
Let’s look at some other examples of passive voice vs.
This type of disruptive behavior is taken very seriously by the school.
The sentence is in the passive voice.
The school takes this type of disruptive behavior very seriously.
Now the subject is “the school,” the verb is “takes,” and the object is “this type of disruptive behavior.” Both sentences are grammatically correct, but you’ll notice that the second sentence—the one in the active voice—is a bit more concise.
Let’s look at one more example:
The paper was written by a PhD candidate, but its controversial premise earned it a lot of attention in the press.
Let’s focus on the main clause; it’s in the passive voice.
A PhD candidate wrote the paper, which had a controversial premise that earned it a lot of attention in the press.
Both sentences are grammatically correct.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Identify whether the main clause in each of the following sentences is in the passive voice or the active voice. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 All of the charges were denied by the suspect, who vociferously maintained his innocence. 1. [x] (A) Passive 2. [ ] (B) Active > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 Statements from local residents were heard in the last hour of the city council meeting, with each resident receiving three minutes of speaking time to voice his or her concerns about the proposed development. 1. [x] (A) Passive 2. [ ] (B) Active > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 As complaints about the growing size of the garbage dump have become more frequent and more vehement, municipal officials have tried to get some of the garbage carted off to dumps in neighboring counties. 1. [ ] (A) Passive > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Active ## Targeted Practice: 4 Each contestant on the game show was given a series of cryptic clues that, once decoded, would lead to a suitcase containing the prize money. 1. [x] (A) Passive 2. [ ] (B) Active > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 Although many now consider the transfer of surplus military equipment from the armed forces to police a Republican-backed initiative, the program allowing combat gear to gain a “second life” in the hands of state and local law enforcement was, in fact, originally instituted in the 1990s by Democratic President Bill Clinton. 1. [x] (A) Passive 2. [ ] (B) Active > Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
The main clause’s subject, “the charges,” is the receiver of the action of the passive verb “were denied.”
An active voice version of the sentence is:
The suspect denied all of the charges and vociferously maintained his innocence.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
The main clause’s subject, “statements,” is the receiver of the action of the passive verb “were heard.”
An active voice version of the sentence is:
The city council heard statements from local residents during the last hour of meeting …
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
The main clause’s subject, “officials,” takes the action “have tried.”
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
The main clause’s subject, “each contestant,” is the receiver of the action of the passive verb “was given.”
An active voice version of the sentence is:
Each contestant on the game show received a series of cryptic clues that, once decoded, would lead to a suitcase containing the prize money.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
The main clause’s subject “the program allowing …,” is the receiver of the action of the passive verb “was … instituted.”
An active voice version of the sentence is:
Although many now consider the transfer of surplus military equipment from the armed forces to police a Republican-backed initiative, in fact, Democratic President Bill Clinton originally instituted the program allowing combat gear to gain a “second life” in the hands of state and local law enforcement in the 1990s.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss how to go about deciding between answer choices that use the active voice or the passive voice.
summary:
Lesson Content In addition to using variations in verb tense and verb mood to test our skill in assessing the effectiveness of sentences, SC questions use verb voice.
There are two verb voices, active voice and passive voice.
Active voice is the more commonly used verb voice.
💡MUST KNOW:
When the subject of a clause performs the action, the corresponding verb is in the active voice.
Consider the sentence below:
title: 6.18 Verb Voice: Active Voice vs. Passive Voice

contents:
Lesson Content Most of the time, the active voice is preferred to the passive voice, because in most cases, sentences are more concise and convey their meanings more clearly when they are written in active voice than when they are written in passive voice.
💡MUST KNOW:
Most of the time, the active voice is preferred to the passive voice.
For example, notice that the active voice version of the two sentences below does a much better job of conveying what occurred than the convoluted and awkward passive voice version does.
Active Voice: In 1974, daredevil Evel Knievel mounted a rocket bike and sought to jump across the Snake River Canyon.
Passive Voice: In 1974, a rocket bike was mounted and to jump across the Snake River Canyon was sought by daredevil Evel Knievel.
In the following two sentences, the version written in the active voice is more clear and concise:
Passive Voice: By November of this year, the project will have been completely finished by the software developers.
Active Voice: By November of this year, the software developers will have completely finished the project.
How can we use this knowledge to score points on test day? Unless there is a clear reason (see the discussion below) to use the passive voice or unless each version written in the active voice contains clear mistakes, choose the active voice over the passive voice.
Let’s now talk about instances in which the passive voice is preferred.
The instances in which the passive voice is preferred over the active voice will tend be those in which the passive voice helps to draw attention to the more important noun in the sentence.
💡MUST KNOW:
The instances in which the passive voice is preferred over the active voice will tend be those in which the passive voice helps to draw attention to the more important noun in the sentence.
When a Sentence is About Findings or Scientific Research
When a sentence is about findings or scientific research, it may make sense to put the person or people doing the research in the background and bring the findings to the forefront.
The results of the groundbreaking research were released to the public this morning by university officials.
In the sentence above, the focus is appropriately placed on the “results of the groundbreaking research,” not on the unnamed officials who released them.
University officials released the results of the groundbreaking research to the public this morning.
The focus of the sentence has shifted from one sentence to the next, but both sentences are grammatically sound and effectively convey logical meanings.
💡MUST KNOW:
When we report on findings or scientific research, we typically want to put the person or people doing the research in the background and bring the findings to the forefront. The passive voice helps us to accomplish this goal.
When the Goal of a Sentence is to Emphasize the Receiver of an Action
Another instance in which it makes sense to use the passive voice is when the goal of a sentence is to emphasize the receiver of an action over the action itself.
Consider the following example:
The entire village was wiped out by the tsunami.
One could say, “The tsunami wiped out the entire village,” but because we have a natural event “doing” the action, we are just as likely to see the passive voice used in order to place the emphasis on what was affected by the event rather than on the event.
My car was totaled in the accident, so now I need to buy a new one.
The important point is that the person no longer has a car and has to purchase a new one, so it makes sense to keep the emphasis on “my car” rather than on what totaled the car.
💡MUST KNOW:
We often place the emphasis on the thing receiving the action, rather than on the doer, when the action is the result of an event or force of nature.
When the Doer of an Action is Unidentified in a Sentence
Sometimes the doer of an action is unidentified in a sentence, either because the doer is not important or not known.
Traditionally, stories are divided into three parts: beginning, middle, and end.
Steering wheel locks are used to prevent car theft.
In reading the first example, we perhaps can assume that the doer of the action is “people in general,” but it is not necessary to spell that fact out for the sentence meaning to be clear.
With the rise of McCarthyism during the 1950s, many people were blacklisted despite scant evidence that they belonged to the Communist Party.
We may know, based on our knowledge of history, who did the blacklisting, but the doer is not relevant to the meaning of the sentence.
In the 19th century, before its adverse effects were known, cocaine was used for everything from pain relief and appetite suppression to local anesthesia and anemia treatment.
The main point of the sentence above is to identify the many uses of cocaine during the 19th century, not to identify who was using the drug.
Another case in which we often use passive voice is when stating a general truth.
Rules are made to be broken.
Trust is earned, not bought.
In these instances, the doer is figurative and implied.
💡MUST KNOW:
Sometimes the doer of an action is unidentified in a sentence, because the doer is either not important or not known. In such situations, the passive voice is often used.
Sentences about the Ingredients or Contents of Something
Finally, the passive voice is often used in sentences about the ingredients or contents of something, which use phrases such as “made of,” “made up of,” “composed of,” and “filled with,” preceded by a form of the verb “to be.” We use the passive voice in these cases to keep the emphasis on the whole rather than its parts.
Our solar system is made up of eight planets rotating around a single sun.
Cakes are made of flour, sugar, butter, and baking soda.
The Greater Antilles is composed of Cuba, Haiti, the Dominican Republic, Puerto Rico, Jamaica, and the Cayman Islands.
The house was filled with love.
💡MUST KNOW:
The passive voice is often used in sentences about the ingredients or contents of something, which use phrases such as “made of,” “made up of,” “composed of,” and “filled with,” preceded by a form of the verb “to be.” We use the passive voice in these cases to keep the emphasis on the whole rather than on its parts.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Choose the preferred version from each of the following pairs, depending on whether the active voice wording or the passive voice wording is more effective. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Consider the following Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 26 In the Australian town of Coober Pedy, residents are allowed by subterranean homes and businesses to escape summer temperatures, where 110 degrees Fahrenheit is often reached. 1. [ ] (A) residents are allowed by subterranean homes and businesses to escape summer temperatures, where 110 degrees Fahrenheit is often reached > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) subterranean homes and businesses allow residents in escaping the 110 degrees Fahrenheit often reached by summer temperatures > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) summer temperatures that often reach 110 degrees Fahrenheit are escaped by residents, as allowed by subterranean homes and businesses > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) subterranean homes and businesses allow residents to escape summer temperatures that often reach 110 degrees Fahrenheit 5. [ ] (E) escaping summer temperatures by residents, which often reach 110 degrees Fahrenheit, is allowed by subterranean homes and businesses > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) In the Australian town of Coober Pedy, residents are allowed by subterranean homes and businesses to escape summer temperatures, where 110 degrees Fahrenheit is often reached .
We have to remember that, unless there is a clear reason to use the passive voice in a GMAT SC sentence, the active voice is preferred. For instance, if the use of the passive voice in a particular context helps to make the sentence meaning clear, then we would have good reason to use the passive voice. In this case, however, the use of the passive “residents are allowed by subterranean
homes and businesses to escape …” actually makes the sentence meaning somewhat unclear and even comical. The passive voice in this context seems to indicate that subterranean homes and businesses are actively giving residents permission to escape summer temperatures, a nonsensical meaning. This use of the passive voice is a big red flag that we should search for a better answer choice.
Additionally, it does not make sense to use “where” to refer to “summer temperatures,” since temperatures are not a place. The relative pronoun “which” would make sense in this context.
(B) In the Australian town of Coober Pedy, subterranean homes and businesses allow residents in escaping the 110 degrees Fahrenheit often reached by summer temperatures .
The statement that “subterranean homes and businesses allow residents in escaping the 110 degrees Fahrenheit” conveys the nonsensical meaning that homes and businesses themselves are escaping the temperatures and that in doing so, those homes and businesses “allow residents.” Logically, the residents, not the homes and businesses, are doing the escaping, and thus it would make sense to say that “subterranean homes and businesses allow residents to escape.”
Furthermore, it is rather strange to say that the thing being escaped is the “110 degrees Fahrenheit,” rather than the “summer temperatures.” One would seek to escape the temperatures, not the degrees. Rephrasing the sentence to say “summer temperatures that often reach 110 degrees Fahrenheit” or something similar would result in a more logical meaning conveyed.
(C) In the Australian town of Coober Pedy, summer temperatures that often reach 110 degrees Fahrenheit are escaped by residents, as allowed by subterranean homes and businesses .
Although it is not grammatically incorrect or illogical to use the passive voice in “summer temperatures that often reach 110 degrees Fahrenheit are escaped by residents,” unless we have a particular reason to do so (for instance, if no other answer choice results in a grammatically correct sentence), the active voice is preferred. Additionally, this choice actually does contain an error in meaning: saying that the temperatures “are escaped by residents, as allowed by subterranean homes and businesses” seems to indicate that the homes and businesses are actively deciding to allow residents to escape or granting the residents permission to escape. Clearly, such a meaning is nonsensical.
YOUR CHOICE(D) In the Australian town of Coober Pedy, subterranean homes and businesses allow residents to escape summer temperatures that often reach 110 degrees Fahrenheit .
This choice produces a sentence with a clear and logical meaning. Notice that this choice uses the active “allow” and “reach,” making crystal clear which nouns are performing which actions in the sentence, without attributing nonsensical actions to inanimate things, as we see in some other choices. The sentence contains no grammatical errors and effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense. (E) In the Australian town of Coober Pedy, escaping summer temperatures by residents, which often reach 110 degrees Fahrenheit, is allowed by subterranean homes and businesses .
This choice uses prepositions and pronouns in ways that create unclear and illogical meanings. First, “summer temperatures by residents” seems to indicate that the residents create the temperatures, a nonsensical meaning.
Moreover, since the modifier “which often reach 110 degrees Fahrenheit” is placed after “by residents,” rather than after “temperatures,” the sentence seems to convey the illogical meaning that the resident, rather than the temperatures “reach 110 degrees Fahrenheit.”
Even if we cross off the relative clause beginning with “which,” the phrasing “escaping summer temperatures by residents … is allowed by subterranean homes and businesses” does not make entirely clear who exactly is doing the “escaping.”
Thus, we should look for a version that conveys a clearer, more logical meaning.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 27 The process of refilling the automobile’s tank with hydrogen takes about five minutes, and once the process is complete, hydrogen will have stored a quantity of energy sufficient for powering the automobile for about 500 kilometers of travel in the automobile’s tank. 1. [ ] (A) hydrogen will have stored a quantity of energy sufficient for powering the automobile for about 500 kilometers of travel in the automobile’s tank > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) a quantity of energy sufficient for powering the automobile for about 500 kilometers of travel is stored in the automobile’s tank of hydrogen 3. [ ] (C) the hydrogen in the tank keeps in storage a quantity of energy sufficient for powering the automobile for about 500 kilometers of travel > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) hydrogen has stored a quantity of energy sufficient for powering the automobile for about 500 kilometers of travel in the automobile’s tank > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) a quantity of energy sufficient for powering the automobile for about 500 kilometers of travel will have been stored in the automobile’s tank by hydrogen > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard None of the versions of the sentence that use active voice main verbs convey a meaning as logical as that conveyed by the version that uses a passive voice main verb.
(A) The process of refilling the automobile’s tank with hydrogen takes about five minutes, and once the process is complete, hydrogen will have stored a quantity of energy sufficient for powering the automobile for about 500 kilometers of travel in the automobile’s tank .
Because it uses the active voice “hydrogen will have stored,” this version suggests that hydrogen will have actively stored energy in the automobile’s tank, as if hydrogen pulled the car up to the fueling station and filled it with fuel.
Also, the placement of the prepositional phrase “in the automobile’s tank” directly after “travel” results in the sentence’s seeming to convey that the 500 kilometers of travel would occur in the automobile’s tank.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The process of refilling the automobile’s tank with hydrogen takes about five minutes, and once the process is complete, a quantity of energy sufficient for powering the automobile for about 500 kilometers of travel is stored in the automobile’s tank of hydrogen .
This version uses the passive voice “is stored” to convey a logical meaning, that, once the fueling process is complete, a certain amount of energy “is stored” in the automobile’s tank. Notice that using the active voice, which would express who or what has stored the energy, is completely unnecessary, because who or what has stored the energy is not germane to what the sentence is expressing. The point is simply that the energy is stored. So, the passive voice works well, and of all the versions of the sentence, this one makes the most sense.
(C) The process of refilling the automobile’s tank with hydrogen takes about five minutes, and once the process is complete, the hydrogen in the tank keeps in storage a quantity of energy sufficient for powering the automobile for about 500 kilometers of travel .
The use of the active voice wording “hydrogen … keeps in storage” does not make sense in this context. Energy is stored in hydrogen or stored in the tank, but hydrogen does not keep energy “in storage,” as if hydrogen has a warehouse or a closet in which hydrogen keeps energy in storage while the car is operating.
(D) The process of refilling the automobile’s tank with hydrogen takes about five minutes, and once the process is complete, hydrogen has stored a quantity of energy sufficient for powering the automobile for about 500 kilometers of travel in the automobile’s tank .
Because it uses the active voice “hydrogen has stored,” this version suggests that hydrogen has actively stored energy in the automobile’s tank, as if hydrogen pulled the car up to the fueling station and filled it with fuel.
Also, the placement of the prepositional phrase “in the automobile’s tank” directly after “travel” results in the sentence’s seeming to convey that the 500 kilometers of travel would occur in the automobile’s tank.
(E) The process of refilling the automobile’s tank with hydrogen takes about five minutes, and once the process is complete, a quantity of energy sufficient for powering the automobile for about 500 kilometers of travel will have been stored in the automobile’s tank by hydrogen .
While energy can be stored in hydrogen, this version seems to suggest that filling the car with fuel and storing energy in the tank is done “by hydrogen,” is if the point to be made is not that energy is stored in hydrogen in the tank but rather that somehow hydrogen that is not necessarily in the automobile’s tank actively stores energy, not within itself, but in the automobile’s tank.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss being careful not to eliminate a choice just because it uses the passive voice.
summary:
Lesson Content Most of the time, the active voice is preferred to the passive voice, because in most cases, sentences are more concise and convey their meanings more clearly when they are written in active voice than when they are written in passive voice.
💡MUST KNOW:
Most of the time, the active voice is preferred to the passive voice.
For example, notice that the active voice version of the two sentences below does a much better job of conveying what occurred than the convoluted and awkward passive voice version does.
title: 6.18.1 Deciding Between Answer Choices That Use the Active Voice or Passive Voice

contents:
Lesson Content At this point, we know well that we’ll encounter situations in which the correct answers are written in the passive voice.
💡MUST KNOW:
In SC questions, we will encounter situations in which the correct answers are written in the passive voice, so don’t rule out a sentence simply because it is written in the passive voice.
In fact, one way in which a question writer can make the incorrectness or correctness of answer choices less obvious is to use the active voice in incorrect choices and the passive voice in the correct answer to a question, thus tempting test-takers to choose the more conventional sounding, active voice incorrect choice rather than the less conventional sounding, passive voice correct answer.
So, when answering Sentence Correction questions, we have to be careful not to be fooled by such a setup.
Consider the following pair of sentence versions:
Passive Voice: In the end, the more skilled candidate was elected by the citizens to the office of governor, an electoral outcome that boded well for the prospects of the state.
Active Voice: In the end, the citizens elected the more skilled candidate to the office of governor, resulting in an electoral outcome that boded well for the prospects of the state.
The active voice construction might sound a little more “normal,” or more “right,” than the slightly less concise but still correct passive voice construction.
The moral of the story is that we have to be careful not to be tempted into choosing an incorrect answer choice just because it uses the active voice and, therefore, sounds a little more “normal” than other choices.
💡MUST KNOW:
Be careful not to be tempted into choosing an incorrect Sentence Correction answer choice just because it uses the active voice and, therefore, sounds a little more “normal” than other choices.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 28 As one of history's greatest painters and an important contributor to the foundations of modern art, Vincent Van Gogh was not painting before his late twenties and produced most of his best-known works during his final two years. 1. [ ] (A) As one of history's greatest painters and an important contributor to the foundations of modern art, Vincent Van Gogh was not painting before his late twenties and produced most of his best-known works > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) As one of history's greatest painters and as an important contributor to the foundations of modern art, Vincent Van Gogh would not begin painting until his late twenties, and he produced most of his best-known works > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) One of history's greatest painters and an important contributor to the foundations of modern art, Vincent Van Gogh did not begin painting until his late twenties, and most of his best-known works were produced 4. [ ] (D) Though one of history's greatest painters and an important contributor to the foundations of modern art, Vincent Van Gogh had not begun to paint before his late twenties, producing most of his best-known works > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Even though he was one of history's greatest painters and an important contributor to the foundations of modern art, Vincent Van Gogh did not begin painting until his late twenties, and he produced most of his best-known works > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium There are a few tempting trap answers in this question. Choice (E) in particular seems attractive with “even though” setting up the sentence to indicate a contrast between Van Gogh’s accomplishments and his commencing painting at a relatively late age. Another aspect of choice (E) and some other incorrect choices that makes them tempting is the use of the straightforward active voice “he produced” rather than the passive voice “his best-known works were produced.” At the same time, only the choice that uses the passive voice makes sense, as all the other choices convey illogical meanings.
(A) As one of history’s greatest painters and an important contributor to the foundations of modern art, Vincent Van Gogh was not painting before his late twenties and produced most of his best-known works during his final two years.
This choice conveys the illogical meaning that, as one of history’s greatest painters, Vincent Van Gogh was not painting.
Also, the use of the past continuous wording “was not painting before his late twenties” does not convey a clear meaning. It doesn’t clearly indicate that he did not paint until his late twenties. Rather it expresses that, at some point before his late twenties, he was not painting.
(B) As one of history’s greatest painters and as an important contributor to the foundations of modern art, Vincent Van Gogh would not begin painting until his late twenties, and he produced most of his best-known works during his final two years.
With its nicely parallel wording, “as one of history’s … and as an important …,” this choice may be tempting. Also, it may “sound right” because it uses the active voice “he would not begin to paint.” At the same time, this version conveys the illogical meaning that, as one of history’s greatest painters, Van Gogh would not begin painting until his late twenties, as if he was already one of history’s greatest painters before he began.
YOUR CHOICE(C) One of history’s greatest painters and an important contributor to the foundations of modern art, Vincent Van Gogh did not begin painting until his late twenties, and most of his best-known works were produced during his final two years.
This choice may not seem as appealing as some others because it contains a somewhat “funny sounding” appositive “one of history’s greatest painters and an important contributor” and the passive voice wording “most of his best-known works were produced.” All the same, it effectively conveys a logical meaning.
(D) Though one of history’s greatest painters and an important contributor to the foundations of modern art, Vincent Van Gogh had not begun to paint before his late twenties, producing most of his best-known works during his final two years.
This version conveys the illogical meaning that, though Van Gogh was one of history’s greatest painters, he had not begun to paint before his late twenties, as if he had been one of history’s greatest painters when he had not begun to paint.
Also, the wording “had not begun to paint before his late twenties” conveys a muddled meaning that does not really make sense, and the sentence does not successfully indicate that he actually did begin to paint upon reaching his late twenties. All the sentence says is that, before his late twenties, he had not begun.
Also, as a result of the use of the closing modifier, “producing some of his best-known works …,” the sentence conveys the nonsensical meaning that Van Gogh had not begun painting, producing some of his best-known works.
(E) Even though he was one of history’s greatest painters and an important contributor to the foundations of modern art, Vincent Van Gogh did not begin painting until his late twenties, and he produced most of his best-known works during his final two years.
This choice may be tempting, as “even though” nicely sets up the sentence to describe a contrast between Van Gogh’s accomplishments and his commencing painting at a relatively late age, and as the active voice “he produced” may “sound right.” However, this version conveys the illogical meaning that, even though Van Gogh was one of history’s greatest painters, he did not start painting until his late twenties, as if he was one of the greatest painters before he started painting.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss verb voice issues being combined with other issues to create clearly incorrect choices.
summary:
Lesson Content At this point, we know well that we’ll encounter situations in which the correct answers are written in the passive voice.
💡MUST KNOW:
In SC questions, we will encounter situations in which the correct answers are written in the passive voice, so don’t rule out a sentence simply because it is written in the passive voice.
In fact, one way in which a question writer can make the incorrectness or correctness of answer choices less obvious is to use the active voice in incorrect choices and the passive voice in the correct answer to a question, thus tempting test-takers to choose the more conventional sounding, active voice incorrect choice rather than the less conventional sounding, passive voice correct answer.
title: 6.18.2 Don’t Eliminate a Sentence Version Just Because its Written in the Passive Voice

contents:
Lesson Content One theme that we’ve discussed often is how we’ll score the most points on SC questions when we seek out the most significant mistakes we can find and then eliminate the sentence versions containing them.
Strategic Reminder: We’ll score the most points on SC questions when we seek out the most significant mistakes we can find and then eliminate the sentence versions containing them.
This theme is often underscored in questions involving verb voice because there may be times when we’re unsure whether the use of one of the two verb voices alone is sufficient to make a SC answer choice clearly incorrect.
For example, in the following sentence, the use of the active voice, although not ideal, is not sufficient to make the version wrong.
Incorrect: While full of amazing images, publishing houses rejected J.
Yes, the above sentence would work better if it were to use the passive voice rather than the active voice, because if the passive voice were used, the emphasis would be on J.
Too often people get caught up with the verb voice issue in a sentence like that, and in doing so, they miss the easy out.
Let’s consider another example sentence:
Incorrect: Enrollment in institutions of higher education is being sought by an increasing number of people with aspirations for better lives, realizing that knowledge leads to prosperity.
While the above sentence might work better if the active voice rather than the passive voice were used, the use of the passive voice is not clearly incorrect.
Since the closing participial phrase would logically have the subject of the preceding clause, “enrollment,” as its agent, the sentence conveys the nonsensical meaning that “enrollment” is “realizing that knowledge leads to prosperity.” So, this version is clearly incorrect.
💡MUST KNOW:
Since use of one of the verb voices may not be sufficient to make an answer choice clearly incorrect, in answering these questions we can make the work lighter by seeking out other issues that make a choice clearly incorrect.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 Choose the correct version. 1. [x] (A) A scientist who once worked for NASA, Barbara Brennan now directs energetic flows to help people to heal. 2. [ ] (B) A scientist who once worked for NASA, energetic flows are now directed by Barbara Brennan to help people to heal. > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 Choose the correct version. 1. [ ] (A) A medium-sized star, hydrogen and helium, along with trace amounts of other elements, make up the sun. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) A medium-sized star, the sun is made up of hydrogen and helium, along with trace amounts of other elements. ## Targeted Practice: 3 Choose the correct version. 1. [ ] (A) Performance enhancing drugs having been used by her, which are known to cause serious health problems, the cyclist was suspended from the sport for a year. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Having used performance enhancing drugs, which are known to cause serious health problems, the cyclist was suspended from the sport for a year. ## Targeted Practice: 4 Choose the correct version. 1. [x] (A) After what scientists call “the seven minutes of terror," the probe finally landed on the surface of the planet Mars, where the probe will operate for the next two years. 2. [ ] (B) After what scientists call “the seven minutes of terror," the surface of the planet Mars was finally landed on by the probe, where it will operate for the next two years. > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 5 Choose the correct version. 1. [x] (A) Whereas, in the past, elephants could be found all over Africa, now they are restricted to ever shrinking pockets of land. 2. [ ] (B) Whereas, in the past, they could find elephants all over Africa, now they are restricted to ever shrinking pockets of land. > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 6 Choose the correct version. 1. [ ] (A) Over the past few decades, people have observed a clear association between the use of naval sonar and an event involving a beaked whale’s stranding itself and dying numerous times. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Over the past few decades, a clear association between the use of naval sonar and an event involving a beaked whale’s stranding itself and dying has been observed numerous times. Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
In this case, we could get caught up in deciding whether the active voice “Barbara Brennan now directs” or the passive voice “energy flows are now directed” is better.
However, we can get an easy out by noticing that, in (b), the opening modifier “a scientist who once worked for NASA” illogically modifies “energetic flows.”
So, (b) is clearly incorrect, and the correct version is (a).
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
In this case, we could get caught up in deciding whether the active voice “hydrogen and helium … make” or the passive voice “the sun is made up of” is better.
However, we can get an easy out by noticing that, in (a), the opening modifier “a medium-sized star” illogically modifies “hydrogen and helium.”
So, (a) is incorrect, and the correct version is (b).
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
In this case, we could get caught up in deciding whether the passive voice “performance enhancing drugs have been used” or the active voice “having used performance enhancing drugs, … the cyclist” is better.
However, we can get an easy out by noticing that, in (a), the modifier “which are known to cause serious health problems” is placed so as to illogically modify “her” rather than “drugs.”
So, (a) is incorrect, and the correct version is (b).
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
In this case, the active voice version, (a), conveys a meaning that makes sense, while the passive voice version, (b), illogically focuses on the surface of the planet Mars, as if the point of sending the probe to Mars was for the surface of the planet to be “landed on.” So, it’s pretty easy to tell that the active voice version, (a), is better.
Still, we can also notice that, in (b), “where it will operate” is placed so as to illogically modify “the probe,” rather than what it would logically modify, “the surface of the planet Mars.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
In this case, we could get caught up in deciding whether the passive voice “elephants could be found” or the active voice “they could find” is better.
However, we can get an easy out by noticing that, because of the way (b) uses “they” twice, the meaning conveyed by (b) is not clear. The second “they” could refer to whomever the first “they” refers to or to “elephants.”
So, (b) is clearly incorrect, and the correct version is (a).
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
In this case, we could get caught up in deciding whether the active voice “people have observed” or the passive voice “an event … has been observed” is better.
However, we can get an easy out by noticing that the wording in (a), “a beaked whale’s stranding itself and dying numerous times,” seems to suggest the nonsensical idea that a beaked whale can die numerous times.
So, (a) is clearly incorrect, and the correct version is (b).
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Consider the following Sentence Correction example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 29 In 1988, operatic singer Carlos Feller had made his debut at the Metropolitan Opera as Don Alfonso, who was regarded as his signature role, and returned there in 1990 as Dr. 1. [ ] (A) operatic singer Carlos Feller had made his debut at the Metropolitan Opera as Don Alfonso, who was regarded as his signature role, and returned > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) operatic singer Carlos Feller made his debut at the Metropolitan Opera as who was regarded as his signature role, Don Alfonso, and later he returned > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) the debut at the Metropolitan Opera of operatic singer Carlos Feller was made as Don Alfonso, regarded as his signature role, and returned > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) operatic singer Carlos Feller made his debut at the Metropolitan Opera, in what was regarded as his signature role, Don Alfonso, and he returned 5. [ ] (E) the debut at the Metropolitan Opera of operatic singer Carlos Feller had been made as Don Alfonso, which was regarded as his signature role, later returning > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium In this question, the active voice version of the main clause “Carlos Feller made his debut” is clearly superior to the passive voice version “the debut … of Carlos Feller was made” because the passive voice version puts the focus of the sentence on the debut rather than on the sentence’s main topic, Carlos Feller, the passive voice version is awkward, and the passive voice wording does not clearly convey that Carlos Feller was in the role of Don Alfonso.
Having noticed that the active voice versions are better, we can use other issues to choose between the active voice versions and find the one correct answer.
(A) In 1988, operatic singer Carlos Feller had made his debut at the Metropolitan Opera as Don Alfonso, who was regarded as his signature role, and returned there in 1990 as Dr. Bartolo in Rossini’s Il Barbiere di Siviglia.
While two events are mentioned, Carlos Feller’s making his debut and his returning, the use of the past perfect “had made” is unnecessary for making the time sequence clear and results in the sentence’s conveying a somewhat illogical meaning, as the dates of the events are mentioned, so the time sequence is clear without the use of the past perfect. Read literally, the main clause seems to convey that, in 1988, Carlos Feller had already made his debut, a meaning that does not seem to be what the author means to convey.
Also, since a role is a thing, not a person, it does not really make sense to use “who” to refer to the role “Don Alfonso.”
Also, since the sentence opens with “in 1988,” the sentence is essentially “in 1988 … Carlos Feller had made … and returned … in 1990,” conveying that, in 1988, Carlos Feller returned in 1990. This issue could easily be missed by a test-taker who does not consider the non-underlined portion along with the answer choice.
(B) In 1988, operatic singer Carlos Feller made his debut at the Metropolitan Opera as who was regarded as his signature role, Don Alfonso, and later he returned there in 1990 as Dr. Bartolo in Rossini’s Il Barbiere di Siviglia.
Since a role is a thing, not a person, it does not really make sense to use “who” to refer to the role “Don Alfonso” or to say that Don Alfonso was “who was regarded as his signature role.”
Also, “later” in “later he returned there in 1990” is redundant, and the use of “later” is illogical, as, the use of the dates 1990 and 1988 is sufficient for conveying that one event happened after the other.
(C) In 1988, the debut at the Metropolitan Opera of operatic singer Carlos Feller was made as Don Alfonso, regarded as his signature role, and returned there in 1990 as Dr. Bartolo in Rossini’s Il Barbiere di Siviglia.
The passive voice wording “the debut … was made” is suboptimal, since it places the focus of the sentence on the debut rather than on Carlos Feller, while Carlos Feller seems to be meant to be the main topic of the sentence, given what the sentence says about the debut, the role and his returning in 1990.
Also, the wording “the debut … was made as Don Alfonso” does not clearly convey that Carlos Feller was in the role of Don Alfonso, instead conveying the nonsensical meaning that the debut itself somehow “was made as Don Alfonso.”
Furthermore, “the debut … was made … and returned” conveys the nonsensical meaning that the debut returned in 1990.
Finally, since the sentence opens with “in 1988,” the sentence is essentially “in 1988, the debut … was made … and returned … in 1990,” conveying that, in 1988, the debut returned in 1990. This issue could easily be missed by a test-taker who does not consider the non-underlined portion along with the answer choice.
YOUR CHOICE(D) In 1988, operatic singer Carlos Feller made his debut at the Metropolitan Opera, in what was regarded as his signature role, Don Alfonso, and he returned there in 1990 as Dr. Bartolo in Rossini’s Il Barbiere di Siviglia.
The active voice wording “Carlos Feller made his debut … and returned” effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
Also, using the pronoun “what,” rather than “who,” to refer to “his signature role, Don Alfonso” makes sense, as a role is a thing, not a person.
(E) In 1988, the debut at the Metropolitan Opera of operatic singer Carlos Feller had been made as Don Alfonso, which was regarded as his signature role, later returning there in 1990 as Dr. Bartolo in Rossini’s Il Barbiere di Siviglia.
The passive voice wording “the debut … had been made” is suboptimal, as it places the focus of the sentence on the debut rather than on Carlos Feller, while Carlos Feller seems to be meant to be the main topic of the sentence, given what the sentence says about the debut, the role and his returning in 1990.
Also, the wording “the debut … had been made as Don Alfonso” does not clearly convey that Carlos Feller was in the role of Don Alfonso, instead conveying the nonsensical meaning that the debut itself somehow “had been made as Don Alfonso.”
Furthermore, while two events are mentioned, the making of Carlos Feller’s debut and his returning, the use of the past perfect “had been made” is unnecessary for making the time sequence clear and results in the sentence’s conveying a somewhat illogical meaning, since the dates of the events are mentioned, and so, the time sequence is clear without the use of the past perfect. Read literally, the main clause seems to convey that, in 1988, Carlos Feller’s debut had already been made, a meaning that does not seem to be what the author means to convey.
Finally, the sentence is essentially, “in 1988 … the debut … had been made … later returning … in 1990,” and thus this version conveys the illogical meaning that the debut returned in 1990.
Correct Answer: (D)
summary:
Lesson Content One theme that we’ve discussed often is how we’ll score the most points on SC questions when we seek out the most significant mistakes we can find and then eliminate the sentence versions containing them.
Strategic Reminder: We’ll score the most points on SC questions when we seek out the most significant mistakes we can find and then eliminate the sentence versions containing them.
This theme is often underscored in questions involving verb voice because there may be times when we’re unsure whether the use of one of the two verb voices alone is sufficient to make a SC answer choice clearly incorrect.
title: 6.18.3 In Questions Testing Verb Voice, it’s Often Smart to Seek Other Errors Too.

contents:
Lesson Content Parallelism is all around us, and it lends clarity and forcefulness to writing.
“Early to bed, early to rise, makes a man healthy, wealthy, and wise.”
In the above example, the three adjectives “healthy,” “wealthy,” and “wise” are parallel.
“Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears; I come to bury Caesar, not to praise him.”
In this second example, a parallel structure is made up of two verbs in infinitive form (“to + verb”).
“Today’s students can put dope in their veins or hope in their brains.”
This third example contains two noun phrases that are parallel: “dope in their veins” and “hope in their brains.”
As you move through this chapter, you’ll find that there is a natural harmony or cadence to parallel structure.
In this chapter, we’ll carefully discuss parallelism and various types of flaws in parallel structure that you could encounter in Sentence Correction questions.

title: 7.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content People often wonder when to check for parallelism.
💡MUST KNOW:
There are firmly established triggers that alert us to check for proper parallelism. Each time we encounter one of these triggers in a sentence, we check to be sure that the elements in that sentence are parallel.
In this chapter, we’ll cover each of these parallelism triggers, such as the FANBOYS conjunctions, correlative conjunctions, including “not only … but also,” and items found in a list.
For now, it’s important that we know not to rely on our “ears” when checking for parallelism.
summary:

In this chapter, we’ll cover each of these parallelism triggers, such as the FANBOYS conjunctions, correlative conjunctions, including “not only … but also,” and items found in a list.
title: 7.2 There are Common Triggers that Alert Us to Check for Proper Parallelism

contents:
Lesson Content A common theme we’ll encounter is that parallelism has little to do with how a sentence sounds.
We’ll encounter many sentences that “sound” parallel, yet are not.
💡MUST KNOW:
Parallelism has little to do with how a sentence sounds. Instead, parallelism has everything to do with how a sentence is structured. Don’t rely on your “ear”!
Let’s now discuss a concept that is foundational in understanding parallelism: structures built around coordinating conjunctions, also known as FANBOYS, must be parallel.
