title: 7.3 Parallelism Has Little to Do with How a Sentence Sounds to Your Ear

contents:
Lesson Content We already know that the coordinating conjunctions are “for,” “and,” “nor,” “but,” “or,” “yet,” and “so.” We use the mnemonic device FANBOYS to remember them.
💡MUST KNOW:
The coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS): For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So.
These FANBOYS conjunctions are always parallelism markers, and a structure built around one of the FANBOYS must be parallel.
In other words, any time we encounter one of these FANBOYS conjunctions, we must be vigilant in ensuring that parallelism is properly maintained in the sentence in which it’s found.
💡MUST KNOW:
The FANBOYS conjunctions are always parallelism triggers. A structure built around one of the FANBOYS must be parallel. Thus, whenever we encounter one of the FANBOYS, we must check to see whether the sentence uses proper parallelism.
We’ll soon see that the most common FANBOYS conjunction we’ll encounter is “and.” So, of course, whenever we see an “and” in a sentence, we must check to that see whether parallelism is maintained.
💡MUST KNOW:
The FANBOYS conjunction “and” is the most common type of parallelism marker that we’ll encounter. Whenever we see an “and” in a sentence, we must check to see whether proper parallelism is maintained.
Let’s now discuss an efficient method for determining whether elements built around FANBOYS are parallel.
summary:
Lesson Content We already know that the coordinating conjunctions are “for,” “and,” “nor,” “but,” “or,” “yet,” and “so.” We use the mnemonic device FANBOYS to remember them.
💡MUST KNOW:
The coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS): For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So.
These FANBOYS conjunctions are always parallelism markers, and a structure built around one of the FANBOYS must be parallel.
In other words, any time we encounter one of these FANBOYS conjunctions, we must be vigilant in ensuring that parallelism is properly maintained in the sentence in which it’s found.
title: 7.4 FANBOYS Always Alert Us to Check for Proper Parallelism

contents:
Lesson Content There are many methods that can be used to determine whether structures in a sentence are written with proper parallelism, but many of these are either just too grammatical or too complicated to work well on test day.
Instead, we’re going to use a method that is based on logic and is easy to use on test day.
To determine whether a structure built around a FANBOYS is parallel:
Step 1: Notice the FANBOYS conjunction.
As we know, FANBOYS conjunctions are always triggers to check for parallelism.
For example, if we encountered the following sentence, we would notice the FANBOYS conjunction it contains, which is “and”:
Computers must be fast and to be reliable.
Step 2: Determine what elements the FANBOYS conjunction connects.
Computers must be (fast) and (to be reliable.)
In this sentence, the FANBOYS conjunction connects the two things that computers must be.
Step 3: Locate the word(s) that introduce(s) the elements connected by the FANBOYS conjunction.
Computers must be (fast) and (to be reliable.)
We see that what the “and” connects are two things that computers must be.
Step 4: Determine whether the elements connected by the FANBOYS conjunction work logically with what introduces those elements.
The way to determine whether each of the elements connected by the FANBOYS conjunction works logically with what introduces those elements is to create two separate test sentences using the introductory word(s) and the elements themselves.
Computers must be fast.
Computers must be to be reliable.
Notice that, although the first sentence makes sense, the second does not.
Incorrect: Computers must be fast and to be reliable.
Notice that we needed to know almost nothing about grammar to see that this sentence uses faulty parallelism.
We could correct the sentence by fixing the element after the FANBOYS conjunction:
Correct: Computers must be fast and reliable.
Notice that, now, the sentence passes our parallelism test:
Computers must be fast.
Computers must be reliable.
Because both of our test sentences make logical sense, the parallelism across the FANBOYS conjunction is done correctly.
💡MUST KNOW:
Each of the elements connected by a FANBOYS conjunction must work logically with what introduces those elements.
Let’s try another sentence:
To succeed at this company, team members must communicate and having open minds.
Reading the sentence, we notice the use of the FANBOYS conjunction “and,” which alerts us to check for parallelism.
Next, we determine what the FANBOYS conjunction connects.
Next, we determine what introduces the elements connected by the FANBOYS conjunction.
We know that each of the elements connected by the FANBOYS conjunction must work logically with what introduces them.
Team members must communicate.
Team members must having open minds.
Notice that, although the first sentence makes sense, the second does not.
Incorrect: To succeed at this company, team members must communicate and having open minds.
We could correct the sentence by fixing the element after the FANBOYS conjunction:
To succeed at this company, team members must communicate and have open minds.
Now, the structure built around “and” is properly parallel.
💡MUST KNOW:
To determine whether a sentence uses proper parallelism across FANBOYS conjunctions:
So, we now know that the FANBOYS conjunctions are always triggers for us to check for parallelism and that, when one of the FANBOYS is used in a sentence, parallelism must be maintained.
Now, not every Sentence Correction question that includes one of the FANBOYS will involve flawed parallel structure.
So, how can we use this knowledge to score points on test day? Simple! We eliminate any sentence versions that don’t maintain proper parallelism across a FANBOYS conjunction.
Let’s use the following Targeted Practice set to build competency with this skill.
Targeted Practice --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now apply what we just learned in answering some Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 1 Although the critic Louis Leroy meant “Impressionists” to be derogatory, the artists found the term appropriate and adopting it for themselves. 1. [ ] (A) adopting > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) by adopting > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) adopted 4. [ ] (D) to adopt > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) to have adopted > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy In reading the sentence, we notice the FANBOYS conjunction “and.” Since “and” must connect parallel elements, what precedes “and” and what follows “and” must be parallel.
Since the underlined portion of the sentence follows “and,” the correct answer will serve to create an element following “and” that’s parallel to the one preceding “and.”
To determine which choice is correct, we identify what introduces the elements connected by “and” and find a choice such that both elements work with what introduces them.
(A) Although the critic Louis Leroy meant “Impressionists” to be derogatory, the artists found the term appropriate and adopting it for themselves.
By creating test sentences that combine what introduces the elements connected by “and” with each of the elements, we can see that, in this version, the elements are not parallel.
Although the critic Louis Leroy meant “Impressionists” to be derogatory, the artists found the term appropriate. – makes sense
Although the critic Louis Leroy meant “Impressionists” to be derogatory, the artists adopting it for themselves. – does not make sense
(B) Although the critic Louis Leroy meant “Impressionists” to be derogatory, the artists found the term appropriate and by adopting it for themselves.
By creating test sentences that combine what introduces the elements connected by “and” with each of the elements, we can see that, in this version, the elements are not parallel.
Although the critic Louis Leroy meant “Impressionists” to be derogatory, the artists found the term appropriate. – makes sense
Although the critic Louis Leroy meant “Impressionists” to be derogatory, the artists by adopting it for themselves. – does not make sense
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Although the critic Louis Leroy meant “Impressionists” to be derogatory, the artists found the term appropriate and adopted it for themselves.
By creating test sentences that combine what introduces the elements connected by “and” with each of the elements, we can see that, in this version, the elements are parallel.
Although the critic Louis Leroy meant “Impressionists” to be derogatory, the artists found the term appropriate. – makes sense
Although the critic Louis Leroy meant “Impressionists” to be derogatory, the artists adopted it for themselves. – makes sense
(D) Although the critic Louis Leroy meant “Impressionists” to be derogatory, the artists found the term appropriate and to adopt it for themselves.
By creating test sentences that combine what introduces the elements connected by “and” with each of the elements, we can see that, in this version, the elements are not parallel.
Although the critic Louis Leroy meant “Impressionists” to be derogatory, the artists found the term appropriate. – makes sense
Although the critic Louis Leroy meant “Impressionists” to be derogatory, the artists to adopt it for themselves. – does not make sense
(E) Although the critic Louis Leroy meant “Impressionists” to be derogatory, the artists found the term appropriate and to have adopted it for themselves.
By creating test sentences that combine what introduces the elements connected by “and” with each of the elements, we can see that, in this version, the elements are not parallel.
Although the critic Louis Leroy meant “Impressionists” to be derogatory, the artists found the term appropriate. – makes sense
Although the critic Louis Leroy meant “Impressionists” to be derogatory, the artists to have adopted it for themselves. – does not make sense
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 2 The new type of sailboat is capable of harnessing even the slightest breeze or to travel on foils to take advantage of heavy winds, so it is a versatile racing craft. 1. [ ] (A) is capable of harnessing even the slightest breeze or to travel > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) is able to harness even the slightest of breezes or travel 3. [ ] (C) has ability of harnessing even the slightest breezes or to travel > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) has the capability of harnessing even the slightest breeze or by traveling > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) can harness even the slightest of breezes or traveling > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy All versions of the sentence use the FANBOYS conjunction “or.” Since “or” must connect parallel elements, what precedes “or” and what follows “or” must be parallel.
To determine whether the elements connected by “or” are parallel, we can combine them with what introduces them and see whether the resulting constructions make sense.
(A) The new type of sailboat is capable of harnessing even the slightest breeze or to travel on foils to take advantage of heavy winds, so it is a versatile racing craft.
In this version, we have the following:
The new type of sailboat is capable of harnessing even the slighted breeze or to travel …
Combining each of the elements connected by “or” with what introduces them, we get the following:
The new type of sailboat is capable of harnessing even the slighted breeze – makes sense
The new type of sailboat is capable of to travel … – does not make sense
Since the second construction does not make sense, the elements are not parallel.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) The new type of sailboat is able to harness even the slightest of breezes or travel on foils to take advantage of heavy winds, so it is a versatile racing craft.
In this version, we have the following:
The new type of sailboat is able to harness even the slightest of breezes or travel …
Combining each of the elements connected by “or” with what introduces them, we get the following:
The new type of sailboat is able to harness even the slighted of breezes – makes sense
The new type of sailboat is able to travel … – makes sense
Since both constructions make sense, the elements are parallel, and this version is correct overall.
(C) The new type of sailboat has ability of harnessing even the slightest breezes or to travel on foils to take advantage of heavy winds, so it is a versatile racing craft.
In this version, we have the following:
The new type of sailboat has ability of harnessing even the slighted breeze or to travel …
In this case, the sentence simply doesn’t make sense, since “has ability of harnessing” is not logical or idiomatically correct. What would be correct is “has the ability to harness.”
Also, even “has ability … to travel” is a little off. It should be “has the ability … to travel.”
(D) The new type of sailboat has the capability of harnessing even the slightest breeze or by traveling on foils to take advantage of heavy winds, so it is a versatile racing craft.
In this version, we have the following:
The new type of sailboat has the capability of harnessing even the slightest breeze or by traveling …
Combining each of the elements connected by “or” with what introduces them, we get:
The new type of sailboat has the capability of harnessing even the slighted breeze – makes sense
The new type of sailboat has the capability of by traveling … – does not make sense
Since the second construction does not make sense (and by the way, even the first one is not quite idiomatically correct), the elements are not parallel.
(E) The new type of sailboat can harness even the slightest of breezes or traveling on foils to take advantage of heavy winds, so it is a versatile racing craft.
In this version, we have the following:
The new type of sailboat can harness even the slightest of breezes or traveling …
Combining each of the elements connected by “or” with what introduces them, we get:
The new type of sailboat can harness even the slighted of breezes – makes sense
The new type of sailboat can traveling … – does not make sense
Since the second construction does not make sense, the elements are not parallel.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now see how what we have been discussing relates to parallelism in lists.
summary:
Lesson Content There are many methods that can be used to determine whether structures in a sentence are written with proper parallelism, but many of these are either just too grammatical or too complicated to work well on test day.
Instead, we’re going to use a method that is based on logic and is easy to use on test day.
To determine whether a structure built around a FANBOYS is parallel:
title: 7.4.1 How to Determine Whether a Structure Built Around a FANBOYS Is Parallel

contents:
Lesson Content Without realizing it, you’ve been not only learning about parallelism in structures built across FANBOYS but also learning about parallelism in lists, which frequently appear in SC questions.
More specifically, you’ve been learning about parallelism in lists of two items because the structures built across FANBOYS that we have been discussing are essentially two-item lists.
💡MUST KNOW:
Structures built around FANBOYS conjunctions are two-element lists. These lists must maintain proper parallel structure.
For example:
Maria went swimming and took a bath.
Notice that we have a two-element list because Maria did two things:
(1) Maria went swimming.
(2) Maria took a bath.
For example:
The current in the river was strong yet manageable.
Notice that we have another two-element list because the current was two things:
(1) The current in the river was strong.
(2) The current in the river was manageable.
For example:
People living at high altitudes must learn to tolerate thin air or suffer the consequences.
Notice that we have another two-element list because people living at high altitudes have two choices:
(1) People living at high altitudes must learn to tolerate thin air.
(2) People living at high altitudes must suffer the consequences.
At this point, we’re going to discuss parallelism in lists in general, considering in more detail how to determine whether a list is properly parallel.
summary:
Lesson Content Without realizing it, you’ve been not only learning about parallelism in structures built across FANBOYS but also learning about parallelism in lists, which frequently appear in SC questions.
More specifically, you’ve been learning about parallelism in lists of two items because the structures built across FANBOYS that we have been discussing are essentially two-item lists.
💡MUST KNOW:
Structures built around FANBOYS conjunctions are two-element lists. These lists must maintain proper parallel structure.
title: 7.5 Structures Built Around FANBOYS are Essentially Lists of Two Items

contents:
Lesson Content A list consists of two or more items in a series, and the items in a list are generally separated by commas or coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS).
The items in a list must be parallel.
💡MUST KNOW:
A list consists of two or more items in a series, often separated by commas. The items in a list must be parallel.
The good news is that, because you already know a lot about checking for parallelism across the FANBOYS conjunctions, the skills needed for checking for parallelism in a list will be much easier to master.
Now, to evaluate sentences with lists, we must understand what structures are understood to “carry over” in a structure involving a list.
summary:
Lesson Content A list consists of two or more items in a series, and the items in a list are generally separated by commas or coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS).
The items in a list must be parallel.
💡MUST KNOW:
A list consists of two or more items in a series, often separated by commas. The items in a list must be parallel.
The good news is that, because you already know a lot about checking for parallelism across the FANBOYS conjunctions, the skills needed for checking for parallelism in a list will be much easier to master.
title: 7.6 Parallelism in Lists

contents:
Lesson Content “Carrying over” simply refers to the concept that the sentence structures that introduce a list are understood to be repeated throughout the list, although these elements are not explicitly rewritten.
💡MUST KNOW:
“Carrying over” refers to the concept that certain elements of a structure involving a list are understood to be repeated throughout the list, although these elements are not explicitly rewritten.
You saw this concept (although we didn’t use the term carrying over) a short while ago in the sections on parallelism across FANBOYS.
For example, in the sentence:
Bananas are healthy and satisfying.
“Bananas are” introduces the two elements in the list to convey the meaning that:
Bananas are healthy.
Bananas are satisfying.
Notice that “bananas are” carries over to each element in the list without being explicitly written because “bananas are” introduces the list.
Here is an easy way to visualize what’s happening.
Bananas are (healthy and satisfying).
Now, because “bananas are” carries over to each item in the list, we’ll distribute “bananas are” to each of the items in the list to see what the sentence communicates.
Bananas are healthy.
Bananas are satisfying.
Let’s apply this concept to a list of three elements.
Members post their comments, complaints, and suggestions on the appropriate message boards.
The same as in a list of two items, in a three-item list, whatever is connected to or introduces the list is understood to carry over to all the elements in the list without being explicitly written.
Here’s an easy way to visualize what’s happening.
Members post their (comments, complaints, and suggestions) on the appropriate message boards.
Now, we will distribute “members post their” to each of the items in the list to see what the sentence communicates:
Members post their comments, members post their complaints,and members post their suggestions on the appropriate message boards.
Notice that the sentence communicates three things via the use of the list:
Members post their comments.
Members post their complaints.
Members post their suggestions.
This exercise of using parentheses and then distributing (carrying over) is important because it’s an easy, effective way to determine whether items in a list make logical sense.
💡MUST KNOW:
The exercise of using parentheses and then distributing (carrying over) is important because it’s an easy, effective way to determine whether items in a list make logical sense.
For example, each of the items in the above list makes perfect sense with the structure that introduces the list, “members post their.”
Members post their comments.
Members post their complaints.
Members post their suggestions.
Now that we understand how carry over works in lists, let’s leverage this new knowledge and learn a simple strategy involving the idea of carry over that can be used to determine whether a list is parallel.
summary:
Lesson Content “Carrying over” simply refers to the concept that the sentence structures that introduce a list are understood to be repeated throughout the list, although these elements are not explicitly rewritten.
💡MUST KNOW:
“Carrying over” refers to the concept that certain elements of a structure involving a list are understood to be repeated throughout the list, although these elements are not explicitly rewritten.
You saw this concept (although we didn’t use the term carrying over) a short while ago in the sections on parallelism across FANBOYS.
title: 7.6.1 Carry Over in Lists

contents:
Lesson Content To determine whether the items in a list are parallel, we’ll follow the following steps:
Step1: Notice the list in the sentence.
Lists are always parallelism triggers.
Please leave a message including your name, your number, and explain why you called.
Step 2: Identify the elements of the list.
Our next move in analyzing a sentence for proper parallelism in a list is to identify the elements of the list.
Please leave a message including your name, your number, and explain why you called.
We see that the list in the sentence consists of three things that the message is including: “your name,” “your number,” and “explain why you called.”
Step 3: Determine the word(s) that introduce(s) the list.
Please leave a message including your name, your number, and explain why you called.
We have some flexibility here.
Step 4: Carry over the word(s) that introduce(s) the list to each element of the list and check to see whether the words that introduce the list work logically with each of the elements.
Let’s use some parentheses and distribute (carry over):
Please leave a message including (your name, your number, and explain why you called).
Please leave a message including your name, please leave a message including your number, and please leave a message including explain why you called.
The words that carry over to the items in the list must make logical sense with the items in the list.
Please leave a message including your name.
Please leave a message including your number.
Please leave a message including explain why you called.
Notice that, although the first two test sentences make sense, the third is illogical.
Incorrect: Please leave a message including your name, your number, and explain why you called.
Notice that we did not need to rely on knowledge of grammar to see that the third element does not work logically with the rest of the sentence.
We could fix the sentence by changing the last item to make it a noun phrase like the other two items.
Please leave a message including your name, your number, and your reason for calling.
Notice that now the list makes perfect sense:
Please leave a message including (your name, your number, and your reason for calling).
Please leave a message including your name, please leave a message including your number, and please leave a message including your reason for calling.
💡MUST KNOW:
The words that carry over to the items in the list must make logical sense with the items in the list.
Let’s consider another example sentence:
Incorrect: Above all else, the Governor’s new platform emphasized collective fiscal responsibility, mutual respect among all citizens, and the metropolitan school district to become the best in the nation.
We see that the sentence contains a list.
Above all else, the Governor’s new platform emphasized collective fiscal responsibility, mutual respect among all citizens, and the metropolitan school district to become the best in the nation.
Next we determine that “the Governor’s new platform emphasized” introduces the list.
Above all else, the Governor’s new platform emphasized (collective fiscal responsibility, mutual respect among all citizens, and the metropolitan school district to become the best in the nation).
So, the sentence says:
Above all else, the Governor’s new platform emphasized collective fiscal responsibility, the Governor’s new platform emphasized mutual respect among all citizens, and the Governor’s new platform emphasized the metropolitan school district to become the best in the nation.
Of course, the last element in the list does not make sense with what introduces the list.
What did the Governor’s new platform emphasize?
The Governor’s new platform emphasized collective fiscal responsibility.
The Governor’s new platform emphasized mutual respect among all citizens.
The Governor’s new platform emphasized the metropolitan school district to become the best in the nation.
Notice that, although the first two elements of the list work with the wording that introduces the list, the third does not.
So, how could we make the sentence logical?
Above all else, the Governor’s new platform emphasized collective fiscal responsibility, mutual respect among all citizens, and the need for the metropolitan school district to become the best in the nation.
Notice that now the list and the sentence are logically written.
Above all else, the Governor’s new platform emphasized the need for the metropolitan school district to become the best in the nation.
Remember, when a list is introduced, each of the elements in the list must work logically with the part of the sentence that introduces the list.
How can we use this knowledge to score points on test day? We can eliminate any sentence versions containing lists that do not maintain proper parallelism.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now consider some Sentence Correction examples:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 3 The historian argued that World War II was caused by Germany’s resentment of the onerous conditions imposed with the Treaty of Versailles, Italy hoped to expand its colonies in Africa, and Japan’s desire to increase their access to natural resources such as oil. 1. [ ] (A) with the Treaty of Versailles, Italy hoped to expand its colonies in Africa, and Japan’s desire to increase their > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) by the Treaty of Versailles, Italy’s hope to expand its colonies in Africa, and Japan desired to increase its > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) on the Treaty of Versailles, Italy’s hope to expand its colonies in Africa, and Japan’s desire to increase their > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) by the Treaty of Versailles, Italy’s hope to expand its colonies in Africa, and Japan’s desire to increase its 5. [ ] (E) from the Treaty of Versailles, Italy’s hope to expand its colonies in Africa, and Japan’s desire for increasing its > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy In this sentence, a three-item list is introduced by “World War II was caused by.” In the correct version of the sentence, all three items in the list work logically with “World War II was caused by.”
(A) The historian argued that World War II was caused by Germany’s resentment of the onerous conditions imposed with the Treaty of Versailles, Italy hoped to expand its colonies in Africa, and Japan’s desire to increase their access to natural resources such as oil.
The second item in the list does not work with “World War II was caused by,” as we can see by combining the two:
World War II was caused by Italy hoped to expand its colonies. – does not make sense
Also, it does not make sense to say that conditions were imposed “with” the treaty. They were imposed by the treaty.
Also, the plural pronoun “their” does not agree with its antecedent, the singular “Japan.”
(B) The historian argued that World War II was caused by Germany’s resentment of the onerous conditions imposed by the Treaty of Versailles, Italy’s hope to expand its colonies in Africa, and Japan desired to increase its access to natural resources such as oil.
The third item in the list does not work with “World War II was caused by,” as we can see by combining the two.
World War II was caused by Japan desired to increase its access to natural resources. – does not make sense
(C) The historian argued that World War II was caused by Germany’s resentment of the onerous conditions imposed on the Treaty of Versailles, Italy’s hope to expand its colonies in Africa, and Japan’s desire to increase their access to natural resources such as oil.
While the expression “imposed on” is idiomatically correct, using “imposed on” in this context does not make sense, as the conditions were not imposed on the treaty. They were imposed by the treaty.
Also, the plural pronoun “their” does not agree with its antecedent, the singular “Japan.”
CORRECT ANSWER(D) The historian argued that World War II was caused by Germany’s resentment of the onerous conditions imposed by the Treaty of Versailles, Italy’s hope to expand its colonies in Africa, and Japan’s desire to increase its access to natural resources such as oil.
In this version, all three items in the list work logically with “World War II was caused by.”
Also, it makes sense to say that conditions were imposed “by” the treaty of Versailles.
(E) The historian argued that World War II was caused by Germany’s resentment of the onerous conditions imposed from the Treaty of Versailles, Italy’s hope to expand its colonies in Africa, and Japan’s desire for increasing its access to natural resources such as oil.
The list in this version is parallel, but the meaning conveyed by this version doesn’t make sense.
It doesn’t make sense to say, “conditions imposed from the treaty.” The conditions were imposed “by” the treaty.
Also, it doesn’t make sense to say, “Japan’s desire for increasing its access to natural resources.” That phrase communicates that Japan desired the act of increasing its access rather than desired the actual increased access. The point to be made is that Japan desired increased access or that Japan desired to increase its access.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 4 While sharks are types of fish, sharks differ from most other fish in that sharks, unlike other fish, have skeletons composed of cartilage rather than bone, have been covered in sharp, toothlike scales called denticles rather than smooth scales, and have not a single covered gill slit, having up to seven exposed gill slits on each side. 1. [ ] (A) have been covered in sharp, tooth like scales called denticles rather than smooth scales, and have not a single covered gill slit, having up to seven exposed gill slits on each side > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) covered in sharp, tooth-like scales called denticles rather than smooth scales, and having up to seven gill slits on each side, rather than a single, covered gill slit > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) they are covered in sharp, tooth-like scales called denticles rather than smooth scales, and have not a single, covered gill slit but up to seven exposed gill slits on each side > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) are covered in sharp, tooth-like scales called denticles rather than smooth scales, and by having up to seven exposed gill slits on each side, rather than a single covered gill slit > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) are covered in sharp, tooth-like scales called denticles rather than smooth scales, and have, instead of a single, covered gill slit, up to seven exposed gill slits on each side View Solution Question Level: medium The sentence contains a list introduced by “sharks, unlike other fish.” In the correct version, all of the elements of the list work logically with “sharks, unlike other fish.”
(A) While sharks are types of fish, sharks differ from most other fish in that sharks, unlike other fish, have skeletons composed of cartilage rather than bone, have been covered in sharp, tooth like scales called denticles rather than smooth scales, and have not a single covered gill slit, having up to seven exposed gill slits on each side .
All elements of the list in this version work logically with what introduces the list, “sharks, unlike other fish.” However, the meaning conveyed by this version does not make sense.
It does not make sense to say that “sharks have been covered in denticles,” as that wording suggests that someone other than the sharks covered the sharks in denticles.
Also, the meaning conveyed by “have not a single covered gill slit, having up to seven exposed gill slits on each side” is unclear, and it seems to be the somewhat illogical meaning that, as a result of sharks’ having up to seven exposed gill slits, sharks don’t have a single, i.e., don’t even have one, covered gill slit.
(B) While sharks are types of fish, sharks differ from most other fish in that sharks, unlike other fish, have skeletons composed of cartilage rather than bone, covered in sharp, tooth-like scales called denticles rather than smooth scales, and having up to seven gill slits on each side, rather than a single, covered gill slit .
In this version, everything that follows “skeletons composed of cartilage rather than bone” appears to be a modifier modifying “skeletons composed of cartilage rather than bone.” Thus, since it does not make sense to describe a skeleton as covered in denticles and having gill slits, this version does not make sense.
(C) While sharks are types of fish, sharks differ from most other fish in that sharks, unlike other fish, have skeletons composed of cartilage rather than bone, they are covered in sharp, tooth-like scales called denticles rather than smooth scales, and have not a single, covered gill slit but up to seven exposed gill slits on each side .
In this version, the second element of the list does not work logically with “sharks, unlike other fish,” since it unnecessarily repeats the subject via the use of the pronoun “they,” as we can see by combining “sharks, unlike other fish” with the second element:
sharks, unlike other fish, they are covered in sharp, tooth-like scales called denticles rather than smooth scales
(D) While sharks are types of fish, sharks differ from most other fish in that sharks, unlike other fish, have skeletons composed of cartilage rather than bone, are covered in sharp, tooth-like scales called denticles rather than smooth scales, and by having up to seven exposed gill slits on each side, rather than a single covered gill slit .
In this version, the third element of the list does not work logically with “sharks, unlike other fish,” as we can see by considering the following:
… sharks, unlike other fish, (have skeletons composed of cartilage rather than bone, are covered in sharp, tooth-like scales called denticles rather than smooth scales, and by having up to seven exposed gill slits on each side, rather than a single covered gill slit).
… sharks, unlike other fish, have skeletons composed of cartilage rather than bone, sharks, unlike other fish, are covered in sharp, tooth-like scales called denticles rather than smooth scales, and sharks, unlike other fish, by having up to seven exposed gill slits on each side, rather than a single covered gill slit
CORRECT ANSWER(E) While sharks are types of fish, sharks differ from most other fish in that sharks, unlike other fish, have skeletons composed of cartilage rather than bone, are covered in sharp, tooth-like scales called denticles rather than smooth scales, and have, instead of a single, covered gill slit, up to seven exposed gill slits on each side .
All of the elements of the list in this version work logically with what introduces the list, “sharks, unlike other fish,” as we can see by considering the following:
… sharks, unlike other fish, (have skeletons composed of cartilage rather than bone, are covered in sharp, tooth-like scales called denticles rather than smooth scales, and have, instead of a single, covered gill slit, up to seven exposed gill slits on each side).
… sharks, unlike other fish, have skeletons composed of cartilage rather than bone, sharks, unlike other fish, are covered in sharp, tooth-like scales called denticles rather than smooth scales, and sharks, unlike other fish, have, instead of a single, covered gill slit, up to seven exposed gill slits on each side.
The fact that the first two elements of the list in this version use “rather than,” while the third element uses “instead of” does not render the elements non-parallel.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now continue with our mastery of parallel structure by considering repeating elements essential to what introduces a list cannot be haphazardly placed in a list.
summary:
Lesson Content To determine whether the items in a list are parallel, we’ll follow the following steps:
Step1: Notice the list in the sentence.
Lists are always parallelism triggers.
Please leave a message including your name, your number, and explain why you called.
Step 2: Identify the elements of the list.
Our next move in analyzing a sentence for proper parallelism in a list is to identify the elements of the list.
title: 7.6.2 How to Determine Whether the Items in a List are Parallel

contents:
Lesson Content From our discussion of carrying over, we know that we need not explicitly write a word such as “to” before every element in a list in a sentence like the following:
The members of the committee decided to reduce marketing expenditures, to increase headcount significantly, and to change the schedule.
In other words, one “to” in front of the first element of the list is perfect, as you’ve seen in sentences like the following:
The members of the committee decided to reduce marketing expenditures, increase headcount significantly, and change the schedule.
In other words, the “to” can introduce the list, and in such a case, the “to” is understood to carry over to every element in the list.
So, why are we bringing this concept up again? Well, test-takers are sometimes fooled into accepting as correct sentences like the following:
Incorrect: The members of the committee decided to reduce marketing expenditures, increase headcount significantly, and to change the schedule.
Notice that the “to”s are haphazardly placed in the list.
A repeating element, such as “to,” that is essential to what introduces a list must either appear once, before the first item in the list, OR appear in every one of the items in the list.
In the examples above, “to” is essential to what introduces the list, because “to” is necessary for connecting “decided” to the verbs that make up the list.
Now, the good news is that this rule applies to any repeating element that’s essential to what introduces a list, whether it be a preposition, such as “to” or “by,” or another part of speech.
💡MUST KNOW:
Repeating elements such as “to,” “from,” and “that” cannot be haphazardly placed in a list if they are essential to what introduces the list.
Consider the following examples:
Correct: The three-week expedition to Bolivia consisted mostly of taking photos of the country’s archaeology found in remote areas, enjoying the natural beauty of its mountains, lakes, and deserts, and spending time in the metropolitan city of La Paz.
Notice that, in the above example, the preposition “of” leads the three-element list and is understood to apply to each of the list elements.
Correct: The three-week expedition to Bolivia consisted mostly of taking photos of the country’s archaeology found in remote areas, of enjoying the natural beauty of its mountains, lakes, and deserts, and of spending time in the metropolitan city of La Paz.
Notice that “of” is now present in each of the items in the three-element list.
Incorrect: The three-week expedition to Bolivia consisted mostly taking photos of the country’s archaeology found in remote areas, of enjoying the natural beauty of its distant mountains, lakes, and desserts, and of spending time in the metropolitan city of La Paz.
“Of” can lead the list or be present in each element in the list.
Incorrect: The three-week expedition to Bolivia consisted mostly taking photos of the country’s archaeology found in remote areas, of enjoying the natural beauty of its distant mountains, lakes, and desserts, and spending time in the metropolitan city of La Paz.
“Of” can lead the list or be present in each element in the list.
Incorrect: The three-week expedition to Bolivia consisted mostly taking photos of the country’s archaeology found in remote areas, enjoying the natural beauty of its distant mountains, lakes, and desserts, and of spending time in the metropolitan city of La Paz.
“Of” can lead the list or be present in each element in the list.
Incorrect: The three-week expedition to Bolivia consisted mostly of taking photos of the country’s archaeology found in remote areas, enjoying the natural beauty of its mountains, lakes, and desserts, and of spending time in the metropolitan city of La Paz.
“Of” can lead the list or be present in each element in the list.
💡MUST KNOW:
A repeating element that is essential to what introduces a list must either appear once, before the first item in the list, OR appear in every one of the items in the list. Such a repeating element cannot appear haphazardly in the list.
Of course, not all repeating elements are essential to what introduces the lists in which they appear.
The groom was congratulated by his friends, his parents, and guests he was meeting for the first time.
In the above example, the fact that “his” appears in the first and second list elements but not in the third is not a flaw.
On the other hand, “by” is essential to what introduces the list, because “by” is what connects “congratulated” to the nouns that make up the list.
The groom was congratulated by his friends, by his parents, and by guests he was meeting for the first time.
💡MUST KNOW:
A repeating element that is NOT essential to what introduces a list does not have to carry over to or appear before every item in the list.
How can we use this knowledge to score points on test day? We can eliminate any sentence version in which words that are essential to what introduces a list are haphazardly placed in front of some but not all elements of a list.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Consider the following Sentence Correction example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 5 New teachers should expect that students will challenge their authority, that materials will be outdated or unavailable, and the administration will provide scant support. 1. [ ] (A) and the administration will provide scant support > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) while receiving scant support from the administration > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) and that the administration’s support will be scant 4. [ ] (D) as the administration will provide scant support > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) since they will receive scant support from the administration > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy Since “that” is necessary for connecting “new teachers should expect” to the statements that express what new teachers should expect, “that” cannot be placed at the beginning of some but not all of the list elements that express what new teachers should expect.
(A) New teachers should expect that students will challenge their authority, that materials will be outdated or unavailable, and the administration will provide scant support .
The list in this version is not logical. “That” is necessary for connecting “new teachers should expect” to the statements that express what new teachers should expect, but “that” is placed at the beginning of two elements that express what new teachers should expect but not at the beginning of the third element.
(B) New teachers should expect that students will challenge their authority, that materials will be outdated or unavailable, while receiving scant support from the administration .
The meaning conveyed by this version is ambiguous because this version contains a misplaced modifier. Because “while receiving scant support …” follows the clauses about students and materials, the sentence could be interpreted as conveying that students and materials will receive scant support.
Also, since in this version, “while receiving scant support …” is a modifier, rather than a list item, the list is composed of two items, “that students will …” and “that materials will ….” Therefore, those two noun clauses should be connected by “and,” but no “and” connects them.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) New teachers should expect that students will challenge their authority, that materials will be outdated or unavailable, and that the administration’s support will be scant .
This version is clear and concise, and correctly includes “that” at the beginning of all three list items that express what new teachers should expect.
(D) New teachers should expect that students will challenge their authority, that materials will be outdated or unavailable, as the administration will provide scant support .
This version would work if there were an “and” between the two clauses about students and materials. However, since there is not an “and” between those two clauses, “as the administration …” seems to be a third item in the list that is not parallel to the other two items and does not work with what introduces the list, as we can see by combining the two:
new teachers should expect as the administration will provide scant support
(E) New teachers should expect that students will challenge their authority, that materials will be outdated or unavailable, since they will receive scant support from the administration .
In this version, “since they will receive …” is a modifying clause, rather than a list item. So, the list is composed of two items, “that students will …” and “that materials will ….” Therefore, those two items should be connected by “and,” but no “and” connects them.
Also, what “they” in “since they will receive …” refers to is not clear.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss situations in which words in lists must be repeated for clarity.
