(D) The Piccirilli Brothers collaborated with Daniel Chester French on numerous occasions; not only did they carve his sculpture of Abraham Lincoln, which sits in the Lincoln Memorial, but also nearly all of the marble statues he designed.
Whatever appears after the first part of a “not only … but also” construction does not carry over (without being explicitly written) to after the second part. In this case, “did they carve,” which appears after “not only,” does not carry over to after “but also,” so we do not have a parallel construction.
Thus, the thought conveyed is incomplete. “Not only did they carve” seems to indicate that the Piccirilli Brothers performed some other action in addition to “carve,” but no additional action is mentioned after “but also.” (E) The Piccirilli Brothers collaborated with Daniel Chester French on numerous occasions, not only carving his sculpture of Abraham Lincoln, which sits in the Lincoln Memorial, but also nearly all of the marble statues he designed.
Whatever appears after the first part of a “not only … but also” construction does not carry over (without being explicitly written) to after the second part. In this case, “carving,” which appears after “not only,” does not carry over to after “but also,” so we do not have a parallel construction.
Thus, the thought conveyed is incomplete. “Not only carving” seems to indicate that the Piccirilli Brothers performed some other action in addition to “carving,” but no additional action is mentioned after “but also.”
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 15 Muhammad Ali, who was born Cassius Clay, is considered one of the greatest boxers and most well-known athletes in sports history, though his lasting fame can be attributed to not only his audacious style in the ring but also to his political activism, particularly by being a conscientious objector to the Vietnam War. 1. [ ] (A) one of the greatest boxers and most well-known athletes in sports history, though his lasting fame can be attributed to not only his audacious style in the ring but also to his political activism, particularly by being > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) as one of the greatest boxers and most well-known athletes in sports history, although his lasting fame can be attributed not only to his audacious style in the ring but also his political activism, particularly as > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) to be one of the greatest boxers and most well-known athletes in sports history, though his lasting fame can be attributed to not only his audacious style in the ring but his political activism as well, particularly by being > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) one of the greatest boxers and most well-known athletes in sports history, though his lasting fame can be attributed to not only his audacious style in the ring but also his political activism, particularly as 5. [ ] (E) to be one of the greatest boxers and most well-known athletes in sports history, although his lasting fame can be attributed not only to his audacious style in the ring but also to political activism, particularly > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium (A) Muhammad Ali, who was born Cassius Clay, is considered one of the greatest boxers and most well-known athletes in sports history, though his lasting fame can be attributed to not only his audacious style in the ring but also to his political activism, particularly by being a conscientious objector to the Vietnam War.
Since “to” appears before “not only,” “to” is understood to be carried over to what follows “but also.” So, repeating “to” after “but also” does not make sense.
Also, “particularly by being” has no logical modification target and seems to modify the preceding clause, with “being” having as its agent “his lasting fame.” As a result, the sentence seems to convey the nonsensical meaning that Muhammad Ali’s fame was being a conscientious objector.
(B) Muhammad Ali, who was born Cassius Clay, is considered as one of the greatest boxers and most well-known athletes in sports history, although his lasting fame can be attributed not only to his audacious style in the ring but also his political activism, particularly as a conscientious objector to the Vietnam War.
The correlative structure in this version is flawed, because “to,” which appears after “not only” is not repeated after “but also.” As a result of that omission, what appears after “but also” does not work logically with what precedes the correlative structure, as we can see by combining the two:
… his lasting fame can be attributed his political activism …
Also, “considered as one of the greatest boxers” is unidiomatic and not quite logical. The GMAT would prefer “considered one of the greatest boxers.”
(C) Muhammad Ali, who was born Cassius Clay, is considered to be one of the greatest boxers and most well-known athletes in sports history, though his lasting fame can be attributed to not only his audacious style in the ring but his political activism as well, particularly by being a conscientious objector to the Vietnam War.
Since “not only … but … as well” is an acceptable structure, and since both what follows “not only” and what is between “but” and “as well” work logically with what precedes the correlative structure, the correlative structure in this version works.
However, “considered to be one of the greatest boxers” is unidiomatic and not quite logical. The GMAT would prefer “considered one of the greatest boxers.”
Also, “particularly by being” has no logical modification target and seems to modify the preceding clause, with “being” having as its agent “his lasting fame.” As a result, the sentence seems to convey the nonsensical meaning that Muhammad Ali’s fame was being a conscientious objector.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) Muhammad Ali, who was born Cassius Clay, is considered one of the greatest boxers and most well-known athletes in sports history, though his lasting fame can be attributed to not only his audacious style in the ring but also his political activism, particularly as a conscientious objector to the Vietnam War.
Both what follows “not only” and what follows “but also” work logically with what precedes the correlative structure, “his lasting fame can be attributed to.”
Also, this version effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(E) Muhammad Ali, who was born Cassius Clay, is considered to be one of the greatest boxers and most well-known athletes in sports history, although his lasting fame can be attributed not only to his audacious style in the ring but also to political activism, particularly a conscientious objector to the Vietnam War.
Since both what follows “not only” and what follows “but also” work logically with what precedes the correlative structure, the correlative structure in this version works.
However, “considered to be one of the greatest boxers” is unidiomatic and not quite logical. The GMAT would prefer “considered one of the greatest boxers.”
Also, since “political activism” is not preceded by “his,” the sentence does not make clear that the political activism mentioned is that of Muhammad Ali.
Also, “particularly a conscientious objector” has no logical target, and seems to modify “political activism,” with the result that the sentence seems to convey the nonsensical meaning that political activism is somehow a conscientious objector.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss the placement of words between “but” and “also” can result in an illogical sentence..
summary:
Lesson Content In the last section, we saw that an essential element that precedes “not only” is understood to carry over, without being written, to what comes after “but also.”
In contrast, an element that comes directly after “not only” does not carry over to after “but also.” So, if any such element is necessary after “but also,” it must be repeated.
💡MUST KNOW:
In a “not only … but also” structure, elements that follow “not only” are not understood to carry over to after “but also.
title: 7.7.3 Whatever Appears After “Not Only” Does Not Carry Over to After “But Also.”

contents:
Lesson Content When “not only … but also” is used, we must pay careful attention to any words in between “but” and “also,” because the placement of words between “but” and “also” can mess up the parallelism.
💡MUST KNOW:
When “not only … but also” is used, we must pay careful attention to any words in between “but” and “also,” because the placement of words between “but” and “also” can mess up the parallelism.
Consider the following example:
Correct: The chef not only prepares dinners but also bakes pastries.
We can see that this sentence is logical by distributing “the chef” after “not only” and “but also.”
Not only the chef prepares dinners but also the chef bakes pastries.
However, look what happens when “he” is placed between the “but … also.”
Incorrect: The chef not only prepares dinners but he also bakes pastries.
When we distribute “the chef,” we get the following:
Not only the chef prepares dinners but he also the chef bakes pastries.
Now, the second part of the “not only … but also” construction is broken since “but he also the chef bakes pastries” does not make sense.
Let’s consider another set of examples:
Correct: People who solve world problems not only heal the world but also heal themselves.
The above example clearly and logically conveys that people who solve world problems accomplish two ends, as we can see by distributing “people who solve world problems” after “not only” and “but also.”
Not only people who solve world problems heal the world but also people who solve world problems heal themselves.
Now consider what happens if we put “they” between “but” and “also.”
Incorrect: People who solve world problems not only heal the world but they also heal themselves.
When we distribute “people who solve world problems,” we get the following:
Not only people who solve world problems heal the world but they also people who solve world problems heal themselves.
Notice that the parallelism is broken since “but they also people who solve world problems heal themselves” does not make sense.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s now consider a Sentence Correction example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 16 During her period of field study, the primatologist observed that not only do chimpanzees appear to have a sense of fairness but also that they seek to address unfair treatment of others. 1. [ ] (A) observed that not only do chimpanzees appear to have a sense of fairness but also that they seek > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) not only observed chimpanzees appearing to have a sense of fairness but also observed them seeking 3. [ ] (C) observed not only that chimpanzees appear to have a sense of fairness but observed also that they seek > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) not only observed chimpanzees appearing to have a sense of fairness but also seeking > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) not only observed that chimpanzees appear to have a sense of fairness but she also observed that they seek > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) During her period of field study, the primatologist observed that not only do chimpanzees appear to have a sense of fairness but also that they seek to address unfair treatment of others.
Since “that” appears before “not only,” it is understood to appear after “but also.” So, the repetition of “that” after “but also” does not make sense, as we can see by carrying over the “that” that appears before “not only”.
that (not only do chimpanzees appear to have a sense of fairness but also that they seek)
not only that chimpanzees (do) appear to have a sense of fairness but also that that they seek
We can see that “that that they seek” does not make sense.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) During her period of field study, the primatologist not only observed chimpanzees appearing to have a sense of fairness but also observed them seeking to address unfair treatment of others.
In this version, what appears after “not only” is parallel to what appears after “but also” and works with what introduces the correlative structure, as we can see by considering the following:
the primatologist (not only observed chimpanzees appearing to have a sense of fairness but also observed them seeking)
not only the primatologist observed chimpanzees appearing to have a sense of fairness but also the primatologist observed them seeking
So, the sentence is logically constructed.
(C) During her period of field study, the primatologist observed not only that chimpanzees appear to have a sense of fairness but observed also that they seek to address unfair treatment of others.
In this version, since “observed” appears before “not only,” the repetition of “observed” between “but” and “also” is illogical, as we can see by considering the following:
observed (not only that chimpanzees appear to have a sense of fairness but observed also that they seek)
not only observed that chimpanzees appear to have a sense of fairness but observed also observed that they seek
We can see that “observed also observed that they seek” does not make sense.
(D) During her period of field study, the primatologist not only observed chimpanzees appearing to have a sense of fairness but also seeking to address unfair treatment of others.
In this version, both “observed” and “chimpanzees” appear after “not only.” So, they are not understood to appear after “but also.” As a result, this version is illogical, as we an see by considering the following:
the primatologist (not only observed chimpanzees appearing to have a sense of fairness but also seeking to address unfair treatment of others.)
not only the primatologist observed chimpanzees appearing to have a sense of fairness but also the primatologist seeking to address unfair treatment of others.
We can see that “the primatologist seeking to address unfair treatment of others” does not make sense in this context.
(E) During her period of field study, the primatologist not only observed that chimpanzees appear to have a sense of fairness but she also observed that they seek to address unfair treatment of others.
In this version, since “the primatologist” appears before “not only,” the placement of “she” between “but” and “also” does not make sense, as we can see by considering the following:
the primatologist (not only observed that chimpanzees appear to have a sense of fairness but she also observed that they seek)
not only the primatologist observed that chimpanzees appear to have a sense of fairness but she also the primatologist observed that they seek
We can see that “she also the primatologist observed that they seek” does not make sense.
Correct Answer: (B) OK, now let’s discuss other correlative conjunctions.
summary:
Lesson Content When “not only … but also” is used, we must pay careful attention to any words in between “but” and “also,” because the placement of words between “but” and “also” can mess up the parallelism.
💡MUST KNOW:
When “not only … but also” is used, we must pay careful attention to any words in between “but” and “also,” because the placement of words between “but” and “also” can mess up the parallelism.
title: 7.7.4 The Placement of Words Between “But” and “Also” Can Result in an Illogical Sentence.

contents:
Lesson Content At this point, you’ve already learned a great deal about the correlative structure “not only … but also.” The good news, as promised, is that the patterns that apply to “not only … but also” apply almost identically to the other correlative conjunctions.
Let’s continue by talking about carry over and correlative conjunctions.
summary:
Lesson Content At this point, you’ve already learned a great deal about the correlative structure “not only … but also.” The good news, as promised, is that the patterns that apply to “not only … but also” apply almost identically to the other correlative conjunctions.
Let’s continue by talking about carry over and correlative conjunctions.
title: 7.7.5 Other Correlative Conjunctions

contents:
Lesson Content As you saw with the correlative conjunction “not only … but also,” whatever essential element precedes “not only” is understood to carry over to the “but also” part of the construction.
not only … but … as well
not … but
both … and
either … or
neither … nor
For example, consider the following sentence:
Incorrect: Each day, Sam is charged with either mopping the floors or he must take out the trash.
Just prior to “either,” we see the essential element “Sam is charged with.” Let’s use some parentheses and distribute this essential element.
Each day, Sam is charged with (either mopping the floors or he must take out the trash).
The essential element is understood to carry over to both what follows “either” and what follows “or.”
So, the sentence communicates:
Each day either Sam is charged with mopping the floors or Sam is charged with he must take out the trash.
Of course, this sentence does not make sense.
Each day, Sam is charged with mopping the floors.
Each day, Sam is charged with he must take out the trash.
The sentence could be correctly written as follows:
Each day, Sam is charged with either mopping the floors or taking out the trash.
Now, the correlative expression has two matching elements, expressed by two gerunds, both of which work logically with “Sam is charged with.”
Let’s look at another example of a sentence containing a correlative expression:
Incorrect: The embassy in Los Angeles is neither efficient with its visa processing system nor is it organized with its customer communications.
Remember that the essential elements prior to the “neither” carry over to after the “nor.” Thus, in this sentence, “the embassy in Los Angeles is” is understood to carry over to directly after the “nor.”
So, what does this sentence say?
The embassy in Los Angeles is (neither efficient with its visa processing system nor is it organized with its customer communications).
Neither the embassy in Los Angeles is efficient with its visa processing system nor the embassy in Los Angeles is is it organized with its customer communications.
We can see that the part of the sentence that follows “nor” does not work logically with “the embassy in Los Angeles is.”
A correct version of the sentence is:
The Embassy in Los Angeles is neither efficient with its visa processing system nor organized with its customer communications.
Notice that now, when we distribute “the embassy in Los Angeles is,” each part is logical:
Neither the embassy in Los Angeles is efficient with its visa processing system nor the embassy in Los Angeles is organized with its customer communications.
Let’s look at another example sentence:
Incorrect: The new park will be a place where residents can either walk their dogs or where they can play soccer.
Let’s break the sentence down, keeping in mind that whatever essential elements precede “either” are understood to come after “or.” In this case, the preceding elements are “the new park will be a place where residents can.”
The new park will be a place where residents can (either walk their dogs or where they can play soccer).
Either the new park will be a place where residents can walk their dogs or the new park will be a place where residents can where they can play soccer.
Of course, this sentence does not make sense.
The new park will be a place where residents can (either) walk their dogs.
The new park will be a place where residents can (or) where they can play soccer.
Notice that the second part of the sentence does not work logically with what precedes the correlative construction.
The new park will be a place where residents can either walk their dogs or play soccer.
Now the sentence is clear and logical.
The new park will be a place where residents can (either) walk their dogs.
The new park will be a place where residents can (or) play soccer.
💡MUST KNOW:
What precedes the first part of a correlative conjunction carries over to the second part of the correlative construction.
Now, here’s a version of the sentence that we have been discussing that’s illogical, not because of what does carry over, but because of what does not carry over:
Incorrect: The new park will be a place where either residents can walk their dogs or play soccer.
The key to seeing that this sentence is incorrect is to notice what carries over and what does not carry over.
The new park will be a place where (either residents can walk their dogs or play soccer).
Either the new park will be a place where residents can walk their dogs or the new park will be a place where play soccer.
The new park will be a place where (either) residents can walk their dogs.
The new park will be a place where (or) play soccer.
The sentence does not work because, since “residents can” appears after, rather than before, “either,” “residents can” does not carry over to what appears after “or.”
We could correct the sentence by repeating “residents can” after “or,” as in:
The new park will be a place where either residents can walk their dogs or residents can play soccer.
Remember that elements that follow the first part of a correlative conjunction do not carry over to what follows the second part of the correlative conjunction.
💡MUST KNOW:
Elements that follow the first part of a correlative conjunction do not carry over to what follows the second part of the correlative conjunction.
Let’s look at another example:
It was not his money that impressed her but his generosity with everyone he encountered that did.
We see the correlative conjunction “not … but.” Just prior to the “not” we see “it was.” Thus, this essential element will carry over to the second part of the correlative construction.
It was (not his money that impressed her but his generosity with everyone he encountered that did).
(Not) it was his money that impressed her (but) it was his generosity with everyone he encountered that did.
Notice that, while “not” and but” don’t really work as placed in this breakdown version, both parts of the sentence make sense with the “it was” that precedes the correlative conjunction.
What about the following?
It was not his money but that he was generous with everyone he encountered that impressed her.
We see the correlative conjunction “not …
It was (not his money but that he was generous with everyone he encountered) that impressed her.
(Not) it was his money (but) it was that he was generous with everyone he encountered that impressed her.
Notice that, while “not” and but” don’t really work as placed in this breakdown version, both parts of the sentence work with the essential elements that precede the correlative conjunction.
Let’s look at another example:
Peter cleaned his apartment both for his girlfriend’s comfort and for his own sanity.
We see that “Peter cleaned his apartment” precedes “both.” Therefore, “Peter cleaned his apartment” must carry over to the second part of the correlative construction.
Peter cleaned his apartment (both for his girlfriend’s comfort and for his own sanity).
Both Peter cleaned his apartment for his girlfriend’s comfort, and Peter cleaned his apartment for his own sanity.
If necessary, we can break the sentence into two sections to confirm that it makes sense.
Peter cleaned his apartment (both) for his girlfriend’s comfort.
Peter cleaned his apartment (and) for his own sanity.
Let’s consider another example:
Incorrect: The old steel town both was eerily quiet and economically depressed.
We see that “The old steel town” precedes the correlative conjunction “both … and.” Thus, “The old steel town” is understood to carry over to both parts of the correlative construction.
The old steel town (both was eerily quiet and economically depressed.)
The sentence communicates:
Both the old steel town was eerily quiet, and the old steel town economically depressed.
We see that this sentence does not make sense.
The old steel town was eerily quiet.
The old steel town economically depressed.
We could fix the sentence by moving was to before, rather than after, “both,” as in:
The old steel town was both eerily quiet and economically depressed.
Now “the old steel town was” precedes the correlative construction, and both “eerily quiet” and “economically depressed” work logically with “the old steel town was.”
Here’s an example that uses another correlative conjunction, “rather … than”:
Marshall joked that he would rather get a root canal than eat his wife’s cooking, but his wife didn’t find the joke the least bit amusing.
We see that “he would” introduces the correlative conjunction “rather … than.” Thus, “he would” carries over to the second part of the correlative construction.
Marshall joked that he would (rather get a root canal than eat his wife’s cooking).
We can break the part of the sentence that involves the correlative construction into two clauses:
… he would (rather) get a root canal …
… he would (than) eat his wife’s cooking …
We see that both of these clauses make perfect sense.
So, how do we use this knowledge to score points on test day? Easy! We eliminate any sentence versions that are illogical because they incorrectly utilize (or do not utilize correctly) essential elements that introduce correlative constructions.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s consider some Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 17 Modern airships, such as blimps, are filled not, as many assume, with hydrogen, the inflammable gas associated with the Hindenburg disaster, but filled with helium, a gas almost as buoyant as hydrogen yet completely inert. 1. [ ] (A) disaster, but filled > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) disaster; they are filled instead > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) disaster; instead, filled > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) disaster, but 5. [ ] (E) disaster, but are filled instead > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy When “not x … but y” is used, the item that comes after “not” has to be parallel to the item that comes after “but,” and both items have to work logically with what precedes the correlative structure.
(A) Modern airships, such as blimps, are filled not, as many assume, with hydrogen, the inflammable gas associated with the Hindenburg disaster, but filled with helium, a gas almost as buoyant as hydrogen yet completely inert.
Since “filled” appears in what precedes “not,” “filled” is understood to carry over to what follows “but.” Therefore, for “filled” to appear again after “but,” as it does in this version, does not make sense.
(B) Modern airships, such as blimps, are filled not, as many assume, with hydrogen, the inflammable gas associated with the Hindenburg disaster; they are filled instead with helium, a gas almost as buoyant as hydrogen yet completely inert.
The clause preceding the semicolon in this version does not work, as it includes the first part of the “not x but y” structure, “are filled not … with hydrogen,” but not the second part, and as a result, is awkward and somewhat illogical.
(C) Modern airships, such as blimps, are filled not, as many assume, with hydrogen, the inflammable gas associated with the Hindenburg disaster; instead, filled with helium, a gas almost as buoyant as hydrogen yet completely inert.
The clause preceding the semicolon in this version does not work, as it includes the first part of the “not x but y” structure, “are filled not … with hydrogen,” but not the second part, and as a result is awkward and somewhat illogical.
Also, the portion of the sentence following the semicolon is a fragment, rather than a complete clause. A semicolon should connect parallel structures, such as a clause and a clause, not a clause and a fragment.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) Modern airships, such as blimps, are filled not, as many assume, with hydrogen, the inflammable gas associated with the Hindenburg disaster, but with helium, a gas almost as buoyant as hydrogen yet completely inert.
The version created via the use of this choice makes sense, as we can see by considering the following:
Modern airships … are filled (not … with hydrogen, the inflammable gas associated with the Hindenburg disaster, but with helium …).
Not modern airships … are filled with hydrogen, the inflammable gas associated with the Hindenburg disaster, but modern airships … are filled with helium ….
(E) Modern airships, such as blimps, are filled not, as many assume, with hydrogen, the inflammable gas associated with the Hindenburg disaster, but are filled instead with helium, a gas almost as buoyant as hydrogen yet completely inert.
Since “are filled” appears in what precedes “not,” “are filled” is understood to carry over to what follows “but.” Therefore, for “are filled” to appear again after “but,” as it does in this version, does not make sense.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 18 Some of the oldest surviving examples of recorded sound reside in the archives of the Edison laboratory, having either been recorded on wax cylinder or on tin plate. 1. [ ] (A) laboratory, having either been recorded on wax cylinder or > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) laboratory, and this was recorded either on wax cylinder or recorded > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) laboratory and were recorded either on wax cylinder or 4. [ ] (D) laboratory, which were recorded on either wax cylinder or > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) laboratory, being either recorded on wax cylinder or recorded > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium In answering this question, we have to be careful to select a choice the use of which results in an “either … or” structure that is logically constructed and in a version that expresses a meaning that makes sense.
(A) Some of the oldest surviving examples of recorded sound reside in the archives of the Edison laboratory, having either been recorded on wax cylinder or on tin plate.
Because “recorded” comes after “either,” “recorded” is not understood to carry over to the part of the “either … or” structure that follows “or.” Therefore, the “either … or” structure is not logically constructed, as we can see by considering the following:
having (either been recorded on wax cylinder or on tin plate)
either having been recorded or having on tin plate
We can see that “having on tin plate” does not make sense.
Also, this version has a meaning issue, because it does not quite make sense to say that the examples of recorded sound “reside in the archives … having … been recorded.” The examples of recorded sound reside in the archives because someone put them there, not because of their having been recorded on wax cylinder or tin plate, but the use of the closing participial phrase in this version results it’s conveying that the examples’ residing in the archives is somehow a result of their “having … been recorded.”
(B) Some of the oldest surviving examples of recorded sound reside in the archives of the Edison laboratory, and this was recorded either on wax cylinder or recorded on tin plate.
Since there is nothing specific to which the demonstrative pronoun “this” would refer, the use of “this” does not make sense. While conceivably “this” could refer to “sound,” the sentence does not mention a particular “sound.” So, the use of a demonstrative pronoun, which is meant to refer to something specific, does not make sense.
Also, since “recorded” precedes “either,” “recorded” is understood to carry over to what appears after “or.” However, “recorded” appears also after “or.” So, the “either … or” structure illogically repeats “recorded,” as we can see by considering the following:
this was recorded (either on wax cylinder or recorded on tin plate)
this was either recorded on wax cylinder or recorded recorded on tin plate
We can see that “recorded recorded on tin plate” does not make sense.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Some of the oldest surviving examples of recorded sound reside in the archives of the Edison laboratory and were recorded either on wax cylinder or on tin plate.
This version conveys the logical meaning that some of the earliest examples of recorded sound reside in the archives and were recorded in two ways.
Also, the “either … or” structure is logically constructed, as we can see by considering the following:
were recorded (either on wax cylinder or on tin plate)
were either recorded on wax cylinder or recorded on tin plate
(D) Some of the oldest surviving examples of recorded sound reside in the archives of the Edison laboratory, which were recorded on either wax cylinder or on tin plate.
Because of the way in which “which were recorded …” is placed, it can modify only “archives.” The modifier is too far from “examples” to modify “examples.” However, “archives” would not be recorded. So, the modifier “which were recorded …” is misplaced.
Also, since “on” precedes “either,” “on” is understood to carry over to what appears after “or.” However, “on” appears also after “or.” So, the “either … or” structure illogically repeats “on,” as we can see by considering the following:
recorded on (either wax cylinder or on tin plate).
recorded either on wax cylinder or on on tin plate.
We can see that “on on tin plate” does not make sense.
(E) Some of the oldest surviving examples of recorded sound reside in the archives of the Edison laboratory, being either recorded on wax cylinder or recorded on tin plate.
In this version, what appears after “either” matches what appears after “or,” and both what appears after “either” and what appears after “or” work logically with what precedes the structure, “being.” So, the “either … or” structure in this version is blatantly logical.
However, we must not allow ourselves to be tempted into choosing this choice by the presence of the blatantly correct parallel structure, because the meaning conveyed by this version does not make sense. This version says, essentially, “Some of the oldest surviving examples of recorded sound reside in the archives … being recorded,” as if, even as surviving examples of recorded sound reside in the archives, they are “being recorded.” Of course, this meaning does not make sense.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss some other important structures that demand parallelism.
summary:
Lesson Content As you saw with the correlative conjunction “not only … but also,” whatever essential element precedes “not only” is understood to carry over to the “but also” part of the construction.
not only … but … as well
not … but
both … and
either … or
neither … nor
For example, consider the following sentence:
Incorrect: Each day, Sam is charged with either mopping the floors or he must take out the trash.
title: 7.7.6 Carry-Over and other Correlative Conjunctions

contents:
Lesson Content So far in this chapter, we’ve seen a number of situations in which parallelism must be maintained.
X rather than Y
mistake X for Y
prefer X to Y
range from X to Y
more X than Y (in certain contexts)
Consider the following example sentence:
The team traveled by rail rather than flew to the tournament.
Notice, what precedes “rather than,” “traveled,” is parallel to what follows “rather than,” “flew.”
The following is not correct:
Incorrect: The team traveled by rail rather than flying to the tournament.
While sentences like the above sentence are commonly used in speech and writing, the above sentence is not correct because the finite verb “traveled” is not parallel to the gerund “flying.”
Please don’t mistake my silence for anger; I’m simply thinking of what to say.
In the expression “mistake X for Y,” the nouns “silence” and “anger” are parallel.
Greg prefers cooking a meal for his family at home to eating in a crowded restaurant.
In the expression “prefer X to Y,” the two gerund phrases are parallel.
The offerings at the eco-friendly products show range from a solar powered grill to reusable makeup removing pads.
In the expression “range from X to Y,” the two noun phrases are parallel.
After Dr.
In the expression more X than Y, the nouns “beast” and “man” are parallel.
Of course, in some contexts, what follows “more” will not appear to be parallel to what follows “than.” Consider the following example:
Raul was more skilled than Alonso.
So, if we see the expression, “more X than Y,” we have to consider context in determining whether what follows “more” has to be parallel to what follows “than.”
💡MUST KNOW:
Each of the following structures demands parallelism.
Now, a key thing to notice about the parallel structures in the above examples is that, unlike the other parallel structures we’ve seen in this chapter, most of these structures don’t involve carry over that we can use to determine whether they are properly parallel.
For instance, in “Please don’t mistake my silence for anger,” “mistake my silence for anger” uses proper parallelism because “silence” and “anger” are both nouns.
Notice also that the elements don’t have to be exactly the same.
💡MUST KNOW:
In order for the structures “X rather than Y,” “mistake X for Y,” “prefer X to Y,” “range from X to Y,” or “more X than Y” to be parallel, X and Y must be grammatically similar.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Consider the following Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 19 While many office workers reported that they preferred working at home to they were commuting to work each day, many others reported that they missed the camaraderie they experienced in an office setting. 1. [ ] (A) to they were commuting > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) to they commute > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) more than they like to commute > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) to commuting 5. [ ] (E) instead of if they commute > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) While many office workers reported that they preferred working at home to they were commuting to work each day, many others reported that they missed the camaraderie they experienced in an office setting.
This version does not contain a parallel “prefer X to Y” construction.
What appears after “preferred” should be parallel to what appears after “to.” However, the gerund “working” is not parallel to the independent clause “they were commuting.”
Notice that the meaning conveyed is not quite logical because “they were commuting” conveys an action that “they” were doing, not a preference.
The sentence created with this version is neither parallel nor logical.
(B) While many office workers reported that they preferred working at home to they commute to work each day, many others reported that they missed the camaraderie they experienced in an office setting.
This version does not contain a parallel “prefer X to Y” construction.
What appears after “preferred” should be parallel to what appears after “to.” However, the gerund “working” is not parallel to the independent clause “they commute.”
Notice that the meaning conveyed is not quite logical because “they commute” conveys an action that “they” were doing, not a preference.
The sentence created with this version is neither parallel nor logical.
(C) While many office workers reported that they preferred working at home more than they like to commute to work each day, many others reported that they missed the camaraderie they experienced in an office setting.
This version does not contain a parallel “prefer X to Y” construction.
What appears after “preferred” should be parallel to the second element of the construction. However, the gerund “working” is not parallel to the infinitive verb “to commute.”
Additionally, “preferred X more than they liked Y” is redundant, since “preferred” and “more than they liked” convey essentially the same meaning in this context. The correct idiom is “preferred X to Y.”
CORRECT ANSWER(D) While many office workers reported that they preferred working at home to commuting to work each day, many others reported that they missed the camaraderie they experienced in an office setting.
This is the only version of the sentence that contains parallel elements in the “prefer X to Y” construction.
The gerund “working,” which appears after “preferred,” is parallel to the gerund “commuting,” which appears after “to.”
The sentence is grammatically correct, and the meaning makes sense.
(E) While many office workers reported that they preferred working at home instead of if they commute to work each day, many others reported that they missed the camaraderie they experienced in an office setting.
This version does not contain a parallel “prefer X to Y” construction.
What appears after “preferred” should be parallel to the second element of the construction. However, the gerund “working” is not parallel to the clause “if they commute ….”
Additionally, “preferred X instead of Y” is somewhat redundant and unnecessarily wordy, since “preferred” and “instead of” convey essentially the same meaning in this context. The correct idiom is “preferred X to Y.”
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 20 The TV critic suggested that the singing competition owed its high ratings more to viewers’ fear of missing out than their actually being interested in the outcome. 1. [ ] (A) to viewers’ fear of missing out than their actually being interested > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) because viewers are afraid of missing out than because they are actually interested > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) because of viewers’ fear of missing out than that they are actually interested > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) due to viewers being afraid of missing out than to their actual interest > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) to viewers’ fear of missing out than to their actual interest View Solution Question Level: medium In the context of a sentence like the one in this question, “more … than” is used in a way similar to the way in which a correlative conjunction such as “either … or” is used. So, what follows “more” must be parallel to what follows “than.”
(A) The TV critic suggested that the singing competition owed its high ratings more to viewers’ fear of missing out than their actually being interested in the outcome.
In this version, “more” and “than” are followed by two non-parallel elements, “to viewers’ fear of missing out” and “their actually being interested ….”
Also, the element that follows “than” does not work logically with what precedes the “more … than” structure, as we can see by considering the following:
the singing competition owed its high ratings … their actually being interested in the outcome
(B) The TV critic suggested that the singing competition owed its high ratings more because viewers are afraid of missing out than because they are actually interested in the outcome.
In this version, “more” and “than” are followed by two parallel elements, “because viewers are afraid …” and “because they are actually interested ….”
However, neither of the elements that follow “more” and “than” works logically with what precedes the “more … than” structure, as we can see by considering the following:
the singing competition owed its high ratings … because viewers are afraid of missing out
the singing competition owed its high ratings … because they are actually interested in the outcome
(C) The TV critic suggested that the singing competition owed its high ratings more because of viewers’ fear of missing out than that they are actually interested in the outcome.
In this version, “more” and “than” are followed by two non-parallel elements, “because of viewers’ fear …” and “that they are actually interested ….”
Also, neither of the elements that follow “more” and “than” works logically with what precedes the “more … than” structure, as we can see by considering the following:
the singing competition owed its high ratings … because of viewers’ fear of missing out
the singing competition owed its high ratings … than that they are actually interested in the outcome
(D) The TV critic suggested that the singing competition owed its high ratings more due to viewers being afraid of missing out than to their actual interest in the outcome.
In this version, “more” and “than” are followed by two non-parallel elements, “due to viewers’ being afraid …” and “to their actual interest …,” since “due to” is not parallel to “to.”
Also, the element that follows “more” does not work logically with what precedes “more,” as we can see by considering the following:
the singing competition owed its high ratings … due to viewers’ being afraid of missing out
CORRECT ANSWER(E) The TV critic suggested that the singing competition owed its high ratings more to viewers’ fear of missing out than to their actual interest in the outcome.
In this version, “more” and “than” are followed by two parallel elements, “to viewers’ fear …” and “to their actual interest ….”
Also, both of the elements that follow “more” and “than” work logically with what precedes the “more … than” structure, as we can see by considering the following:
the singing competition owed its high ratings … to viewers’ fear of missing out
the singing competition owed its high ratings … to their actual interest in the outcome
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 21 Almost universally acknowledged is that the most successful teachers are those who, rather than just convey the basic facts and figures to their students, inspire them to think on their own. 1. [x] (A) Almost universally acknowledged is that the most successful teachers are those who, rather than just convey the basic facts and figures to their students, inspire them to think on their own. 2. [ ] (B) Rather than just conveying the basic facts and figures, the most successful teachers are the ones who inspire students to think on their own, which is almost universally acknowledged. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Almost universally acknowledged is the fact that the teachers who are the most successful are not only conveying the basic facts and figures to their students but can inspire them also to think on their own. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) The most successful teachers are the ones who inspire students, a fact almost acknowledged universally, rather than the ones who just convey the basic facts and figures. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) It is almost universally acknowledged that the most successful teachers are those who inspire students to think on their own, rather than just conveying the basic facts and figures to them. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard Two sentence elements connected by the expression “rather than” should be parallel. Only the correct version uses parallel elements with “rather than” and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) Almost universally acknowledged is that the most successful teachers are those who, rather than just convey the basic facts and figures to their students, inspire them to think on their own. In this version, the expression “rather than” connects parallel elements “convey” and “inspire,” and this version effectively conveys a meaning that makes sense.
(B) Rather than just conveying the basic facts and figures, the most successful teachers are the ones who inspire students to think on their own, which is almost universally acknowledged. In this version, the expression “rather than” connects elements that are not parallel: “conveying,” a gerund, and “are,” a finite verb.
Also, the ideas connected by “rather than” do not work logically together, as it does not make sense to contrast teachers’ conveying facts and figures with teachers are the ones.
Also, the use of the relative clause “which is almost universally acknowledged” is not ideal, since the relative clause could be perceived as modifying “their own,” with the result that the sentence could be read as conveying the nonsensical meaning that “their own” is almost universally acknowledged. Furthermore, even if we were to decide that the relative clause modifies the clauses that precede it, that type of modification is not optimal and is not preferred by the GMAT.
(C) Almost universally acknowledged is the fact that the teachers who are the most successful are not only conveying the basic facts and figures to their students but can inspire them also to think on their own. The first sign that this choice is incorrect is its use of the present continuous “are … conveying.” By using that verb form, this choice suggests that the teachers mentioned are at this moment continuously “conveying” facts and figures. What makes more sense is to say that the teachers “convey” facts and figures, communicating that they do so in general rather than that they are doing so continuously at this moment.
Also, the “not only … but also” construction in this version is not properly parallel, as we can see by considering the following:
the teachers who are the most successful are (not only conveying the basic facts and figures to their students but can inspire them also to think on their own)
the teachers who are the most successful not only are conveying the basic facts and figures to their students but are can inspire them also to think on their own
We can see that “but are can inspire them also” does not make sense.
(D) The most successful teachers are the ones who inspire students, a fact almost acknowledged universally, rather than the ones who just convey the basic facts and figures. In this version, the modifier “a fact almost acknowledged universally” is poorly placed, with the result that the modifier modifies only the first clause of the sentence, rather than the entire sentence, even though the sentence expresses one coherent idea that would be better modified as a whole.
Also, the meaning conveyed by “a fact almost acknowledged universally” doesn’t really make sense. The point to be made is that the fact is almost universally acknowledged, but the modifier expresses that the fact is universally almost acknowledged, as if everyone everywhere almost, but not quite, acknowledges the fact.
(E) It is almost universally acknowledged that the most successful teachers are those who inspire students to think on their own, rather than just conveying the basic facts and figures to them. In this version “rather than” connects two elements that are not parallel: “inspire,” a finite verb, and “conveying,” a gerund.
Correct Answer: (A) Now that we have covered various types of parallel structures, let’s talk about an important general concept of parallel structures: parallel does not always mean exactly the same.
summary:
Lesson Content So far in this chapter, we’ve seen a number of situations in which parallelism must be maintained.
X rather than Y
mistake X for Y
prefer X to Y
range from X to Y
more X than Y (in certain contexts)
Consider the following example sentence:
The team traveled by rail rather than flew to the tournament.
Notice, what precedes “rather than,” “traveled,” is parallel to what follows “rather than,” “flew.
title: 7.8 Other Structures that Demand Parallelism

contents:
Lesson Content Sometimes in learning about parallelism, people come away with the idea that the elements of a parallel structure must always match each other closely.
Let’s review some common situations in which elements that may not seem parallel are considered parallel, starting with the fact that verbs in different tenses can be perfectly parallel.
summary:
Lesson Content Sometimes in learning about parallelism, people come away with the idea that the elements of a parallel structure must always match each other closely.
Let’s review some common situations in which elements that may not seem parallel are considered parallel, starting with the fact that verbs in different tenses can be perfectly parallel.
title: 7.9 Parallel Does Not Always Mean Exactly the Same.

contents:
Lesson Content Some people believe that verbs that are in different tenses are not parallel to each other.
💡MUST KNOW:
Verbs that are in different tenses can be parallel.
For example, consider the following sentence that has different tenses, past perfect and past continuous, in the same list:
The ecologist said that genetically modified fish had found their way into the lake, had grown quickly, and were eating the other fish.
Does the sentence work? It does, right? Let’s break it down:
The ecologist said that genetically modified fish (had found their way into the lake, had grown quickly, and were eating the other fish).
The ecologist said that genetically modified fish had found their way into the lake, that genetically modified fish had grown quickly, and that genetically modified fish were eating the other fish.
We can see that each element in the list works logically with “that genetically modified fish.”
Let’s look at another example:
Storms such as these affect critical infrastructure, topple trees, and will continue to pose serious problems for commuters if the city does not devise a comprehensive preparation plan.
The three elements in the series presented in the sentence above are parallel even though the first two verbs are in the simple present tense and the third is in the future tense.
Storms such as these (affect critical infrastructure, topple trees, and will continue to pose serious problems for commuters if the city does not devise a comprehensive preparation plan).
Storms such as these affect critical infrastructure, storms such as these topple trees, and storms such as these will continue to pose serious problems for commuters if the city does not devise a comprehensive preparation plan.
We see that each element in the list works logically with “storms such as these.”
Let’s consider one more example:
The school opened to voters at 7 a.m.
The simple past tense verb “opened” and the present perfect “has been” are elements of a parallel construction using the coordinating conjunction “and.”
The school (opened to voters at 7 a.m.
The school opened to voters at 7 a.m.
How can we use this knowledge to score points on test day? We can take care not to eliminate sentence versions just because they use different verb tenses in structures that must be parallel.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now consider a Sentence Correction example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 22 The organization helped to shape Brazilian ecological policy, promote rainforest restoration, is likely to restore thousands of hectares in the near future, and is made up of enterprises ranging from small farms to major NGOs. 1. [ ] (A) organization helped to shape Brazilian ecological policy, promote rainforest restoration, is likely to restore thousands of hectares in the near future, and > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) organization, having helped to shape Brazilian ecological policy, promote rainforest restoration, and being likely to restore thousands of hectares in the near future, > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) organization, which helped to shape Brazilian ecological policy, promote rainforest restoration, and likely restore thousands of hectares in the near future, > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) organization, which helps to shape Brazilian ecological policy, which promotes rainforest restoration, which is likely to restore thousands of hectares in the near future, and which > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) organization, which helped to shape Brazilian ecological policy, is promoting rainforest restoration, and will likely restore thousands of hectares in the near future, View Solution Question Level: hard To arrive at the correct answer to this question, we can first eliminate the versions that have errors in construction and then use meaning to choose between the choices that remain.
(A) The organization helped to shape Brazilian ecological policy, promote rainforest restoration, is likely to restore thousands of hectares in the near future, and is made up of enterprises ranging from small farms to major NGOs.
The list in this version is not logically constructed, as we can see by considering the following:
helped to (shape Brazilian ecological policy, promote rainforest restoration, is likely to restore thousands of hectares in the near future, and is made up of)
helped to shape Brazilian ecological policy, helped to promote rainforest restoration, helped to is likely to restore thousands of hectares in the near future, and helped to is made up of
We can see that what introduces the list, “helped to,” works logically with only the first two elements of the list and not with the last two.
(B) The organization, having helped to shape Brazilian ecological policy, promote rainforest restoration, and being likely to restore thousands of hectares in the near future, is made up of enterprises ranging from small farms to major NGOs.
The list in the modifier in this version is not parallel, as we can see by considering the following:
having helped to (shape Brazilian ecological policy, promote rainforest restoration, and being likely to restore thousands of hectares in the near future)
having helped to shape Brazilian ecological policy, having helped to promote rainforest restoration, and having helped to being likely to restore thousands of hectares in the near future
We can see that what introduces the list, “having helped to,” doesn’t work with the third list element since “having helped to being likely to restore” does not make sense.
(C) The organization, which helped to shape Brazilian ecological policy, promote rainforest restoration, and likely restore thousands of hectares in the near future, is made up of enterprises ranging from small farms to major NGOs.
Often people answering this question narrow the choices down to this one and choice (E). Thus, often, correctly answering the question requires carefully considering this choice and choice (E) to find a key reason to choose one or the other.
So, notice what we get if we distribute “helped to” throughout the list in this version.
helped to (shape Brazilian ecological policy, promote rainforest restoration, and likely restore thousands of hectares in the near future)
helped to shape Brazilian ecological policy, helped to promote rainforest restoration, and likely helped to restore thousands of hectares in the near future
The list is parallel, but there is a meaning issue since “helped to restore thousands of hectares in the near future” doesn’t make sense. After all, it doesn’t make sense to use the past tense “helped to” in discussing an event that will occur “in the near future.”
So, this choice is incorrect.
(D) The organization, which helps to shape Brazilian ecological policy, which promotes rainforest restoration, which is likely to restore thousands of hectares in the near future, and which is made up of enterprises ranging from small farms to major NGOs.
We have to be careful not to be tempted into choosing this choice just because it is so obviously parallel.
Notice that this version is composed of only “the organization” and a list of relative clauses that begins with the first “which” and runs all the way to the end of the sentence.
So, this version has no independent clause and thus is not a complete sentence.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) The organization, which helped to shape Brazilian ecological policy, is promoting rainforest restoration, and will likely restore thousands of hectares in the near future, is made up of enterprises ranging from small farms to major NGOs.
While the verbs in the list in this version are in different tenses, the list is perfectly parallel, as we can see by considering the following:
which (helped to shape Brazilian ecological policy, is promoting rainforest restoration, and will likely restore thousands of hectares in the near future)
which helped to shape Brazilian ecological policy, which is promoting rainforest restoration, and which will likely restore thousands of hectares in the near future
This version is logically constructed and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s continue by discussing that active voice verbs can be parallel to passive voice verbs.
summary:
Lesson Content Some people believe that verbs that are in different tenses are not parallel to each other.
💡MUST KNOW:
Verbs that are in different tenses can be parallel.
For example, consider the following sentence that has different tenses, past perfect and past continuous, in the same list:
The ecologist said that genetically modified fish had found their way into the lake, had grown quickly, and were eating the other fish.
title: 7.9.1 Verbs That Are in Different Tenses Can be Perfectly Parallel.

contents:
Lesson Content One might assume that it’s incorrect to have both active voice verbs and passive voice verbs in the same list.
💡MUST KNOW:
There is nothing wrong with having active voice verbs and passive voice verbs together in a list.
Consider the following example:
Having hit her GMAT target, Aisha applied to her first-choice business school, was invited for an interview, and was accepted.
As we already know well, each component of a list must work logically with the essential element or elements that introduce the list.
Having hit her GMAT target, Aisha (applied to her first-choice business school, was invited for an interview, and was accepted).
Does each element in the list work logically with “Aisha?” Definitely.
Having hit her GMAT target, Aisha applied to her first-choice business school, Aisha was invited for an interview, and Aisha was accepted.
Notice that it’s OK that the verb “applied” is in the active voice, while the verbs “was interviewed” and “was accepted” are in the passive voice.
Consider our example sentence with a small change:
Incorrect: Having hit her GMAT target, Aisha applied to her first-choice business school, was invited for an interview, and Aisha was accepted.
Let’s see what this sentence communicates:
Having hit her GMAT target, Aisha (applied to her first-choice business school, was invited for an interview, and Aisha was accepted).
Having hit her GMAT target, Aisha applied to her first-choice business school, Aisha was invited for an interview, and Aisha Aisha was accepted.
Notice that the third element in the list does not work logically with what introduces the list.
💡MUST KNOW:
When items appear in a series, verb voice can change from active to passive without violating the rules of parallelism, as long as each element in the list works logically with the essential element or elements that introduce the list.
Let’s look at a couple more examples.
On Friday, Spencer left the office at 2:30 p.m., picked up his daughter from school, took her to her ballet class, and was reprimanded by his boss for arriving ten minutes late to the 4:00 p.m.
Despite the switch from the active verbs “left,” “picked up,” and “took” to the passive “was reprimanded,” the sentence above is parallel and correct.
On Friday, Spencer (left the office at 2:30 p.m., picked up his daughter from school, took her to her ballet class, and was reprimanded by his boss for arriving ten minutes late to the 4:00 p.m.
On Friday, Spencer left the office at 2:30 p.m., Spencer picked up his daughter from school, Spencer took her to her ballet class, and Spencer was reprimanded by his boss for arriving ten minutes late to the 4:00 p.m.
Notice that each of the elements in the list work logically with “Spencer.”
However, if we were to change the fundamental structure of an element within the series, even without changing any of the verbs, the sentence would no longer be parallel.
Incorrect: On Friday, Spencer left the office at 2:30 p.m., picked up his daughter from school, he took her to her ballet class, and was reprimanded by his boss for arriving ten minutes late to the 4:00 p.m.
By placing the pronoun “he” at the beginning of the element “took her to her ballet class,” we have created an independent clause, which is not parallel to the other elements.
On Friday, Spencer (left the office at 2:30 p.m., picked up his daughter from school, he took her to her ballet class, and was reprimanded by his boss for arriving ten minutes late to the 4:00 p.m.
On Friday, Spencer left the office at 2:30 p.m., Spencer picked up his daughter from school, Spencer he took her to her ballet class, and Spencer was reprimanded by his boss for arriving ten minutes late to the 4:00 p.m.
Of course, the third item in the list does not make sense.
So, how do we use this knowledge to score points on test day? Easy! We don’t eliminate sentence versions just because they utilize both the active and passive verb voices.
Targeted Practice --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now consider some Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 23 The jewel thief is being pursued by Interpol, was last spotted at an outdoor market in Berlin, and will likely be caught when he attempts to cross the border into a neighboring country. 1. [x] (A) was last spotted at an outdoor market in Berlin, and will likely be 2. [ ] (B) is last spotted at an outdoor market in Berlin, and is likely being > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) is last spotted at an outdoor market in Berlin, and likely to be > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) being last spotted at an outdoor market in Berlin, and likely being > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) last spotted at an outdoor market in Berlin, and likely > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy CORRECT ANSWER(A) The jewel thief is being pursued by Interpol, was last spotted at an outdoor market in Berlin, and will likely be caught when he attempts to cross the border into a neighboring country.
Remember that verbs that are in different tenses can be perfectly parallel. In this case, there is a list of three elements describing actions related to “the jewel thief”: “is being pursued,” “was last spotted,” and “will likely be caught.”
We know that the final two elements must be parallel to the first element, since the first element appears in the non-underlined portion of the sentence and thus cannot be changed. Although the first element, “is being pursued,” is present progressive tense, “was last spotted” is past tense, and “will likely be caught” is future tense, the verbs are still parallel, and the timeline of events is logical.
This version is parallel, grammatically correct, and conveys a logical meaning.
(B) The jewel thief is being pursued by Interpol, is last spotted at an outdoor market in Berlin, and is likely being caught when he attempts to cross the border into a neighboring country.
Although this version may appear more parallel than others because “is being pursued,” “is last spotted,” and “is likely being caught” all begin with “is,” the meaning conveyed does not make sense.
Firstly, “is,” a present tense verb, conflicts with “last spotted,” which describes a past action.
Secondly, “is likely being caught” indicates an action that is currently underway, but “when he attempts to cross the border” indicates an action that hasn’t happened yet. Furthermore, it does not make sense that “the jewel thief is being pursued” and “is likely being caught” simultaneously. If the thief currently is being pursued, then he is not also currently being caught.
Remember, versions of a sentence that achieve parallelism by repeating the exact same words are not automatically correct; we must be sure that the meaning conveyed is logical.
(C) The jewel thief is being pursued by Interpol, is last spotted at an outdoor market in Berlin, and likely to be caught when he attempts to cross the border into a neighboring country.
Saying thatthe jewel thief “is last spotted” does not make sense because “is” indicates a present action while “last spotted” indicates a past action.
Additionally, the final element in the list, “likely to be caught” is not parallel to the first two elements because the adjective “likely” is not parallel to the verb “is.”
This version is not parallel or logical.
(D) The jewel thief is being pursued by Interpol, being last spotted at an outdoor market in Berlin, and likely being caught when he attempts to cross the border into a neighboring country.
Although this version may appear more parallel than others because “being pursued,” “being last spotted,” and “likely being caught” all include “being,” the meaning conveyed does not make sense.
If the thief is currently “being pursued,” then it is not possible that he is also currently “being caught.” Furthermore, the statement that “The jewel thief is … being caught when he attempts to cross the border” does not make sense because “being caught” indicates an action that is currently underway, but “when he attempts to cross the border” indicates an action that hasn’t happened yet.
Remember, versions of a sentence that achieve parallelism by repeating the exact same words are not automatically correct; you must be sure that the meaning conveyed is logical.
(E) The jewel thief is being pursued by Interpol, last spotted at an outdoor market in Berlin, and likely caught when he attempts to cross the border into a neighboring country.
If we carry over what precedes the start of the list to the other elements of the list, we can see that the meaning conveyed is illogical:
The jewel thief is being pursued, the jewel thief is last spotted at an outdoor market in Berlin, and the jewel thief is likely caught when he attempts to cross the border
Although the sentence is parallel and grammatically correct, it does not make sense to say that the jewel thief “islast spotted,” because “is” indicates a present action while “last spotted” indicates a past action.
Furthermore, if the thief currently “is being pursued,” then it does not make sense that he also currently is “likely caught.” Additionally, “is likely caught” indicates a present state, but “when he attempts to cross the border” indicates an action that hasn’t happened yet.
Thus, this version does not convey a logical timeline of events.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 24 John Francis Cook, Jr., a prominent educator, politician, and businessperson, served as a Howard University trustee, was appointed a Washington D.C. Justice of the Peace and appointed Chief Tax Collector, and partnered with his brother and a former legislator to found the manufacturing firm Cook, Cook, and White. 1. [x] (A) John Francis Cook, Jr. 2. [ ] (B) As a prominent educator, politician, and businessperson, John Francis Cook, Jr. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) John Francis Cook, Jr. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Because of being a prominent educator, politician, and businessperson, John Francis Cook, Jr. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) John Francis Cook, Jr. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard Correctly answering this question requires avoiding choosing one of the incorrect choices that may seem correct because it uses only the active voice, it uses only the passive voice, or it includes wording that is very clearly rather parallel. The list that appears in the correct answer sounds a little funny and uses both active voice verbs and passive voice verbs and yet is logically constructed.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) John Francis Cook, Jr. , a prominent educator, politician, and businessperson, served as a Howard University trustee, was appointed a Washington D. C. Justice of the Peace and appointed Chief Tax Collector, and partnered with his brother and a former legislator to found the manufacturing firm Cook, Cook, and White.
This version uses an outer list and a nested list to effectively convey a meaning that makes sense.
The outer list is:
John Francis Cook, Jr.…:
served …
was …
partnered …
The nested list is:
was:
appointed a Washington D.C. Justice of the Peace
appointed Chief Tax Collector
(B) As a prominent educator, politician, and businessperson, John Francis Cook, Jr. served as a Howard University trustee, as a Washington D. C. Justice of the Peace, and appointed as Chief Tax Collector, partnering with his brother and a former legislator to found the manufacturing firm Cook, Cook, and White.
The use of the opening modifier “as a prominent educator, politician, and businessman” is not quite logical. John Francis Cook, Jr. would not have been engaging in the activities listed in the sentence “as a prominent educator, politician, and businessperson,” i.e., “in the capacity of a prominent educator, politician, and businessperson.” He simply was a prominent educator, politician, and businessman who engaged in the listed activities.
Also, the list of John Francis Cook, Jr.’s activities is not logically constructed, since the third item, “appointed …,” does not work logically with what introduces the list, “John Francis Cook, Jr. served,” as we can see by combining the two:
John Francis Cook, Jr. served appointed …
Also, the use of the closing participial phrase, “partnering …,” results in the sentence’s conveying the illogical meaning that, in the process of or as a result of serving as a trustee and as a justice of the peace and of being appointed Chief Tax Collector, John Francis Cook, Jr. partnered with his brother and a legislator to found a manufacturing business.
(C) John Francis Cook, Jr. , a prominent educator, politician, and businessperson, who, serving as a Howard University trustee, was appointed a Washington D. C. Justice of the Peace, was appointed Chief Tax Collector, and was partnered with his brother and a former legislator to found the manufacturing firm Cook, Cook, and White.
This version may seem to be correct since all three verbs, “was appointed,” “was appointed,” and “was partnered,” in the list of events that appears in the sentence are in the passive voice. However, by looking at aspects of the sentence other than that parallelism, we can see that the sentence is rather flawed.
While “was partnered” may match the other passive voice verbs in the list, saying that John Francis Cook, Jr. was partnered with others to found the manufacturing firm is not logical, as it seems to convey that some outside force caused him to be partnered with the others.
Also, since everything after “John Francis Cook, Jr.” modifies “John Francis Cook, Jr.,” the sentence has no main verb.
Also, the use of the modifier “serving as a Howard University Trustee” results in the sentence’s conveying the somewhat illogical meaning that the events mentioned were somehow related to John Francis Cook, Jr.’s serving as a Howard University Trustee.
(D) Because of being a prominent educator, politician, and businessperson, John Francis Cook, Jr. served as a Howard University trustee, was appointed a Washington D. C. Justice of the Peace, and was appointed Chief Tax Collector, partnering with his brother and a former legislator to found the manufacturing firm Cook, Cook, and White.
This flawed choice contains a perfectly constructed list and is basically grammatically correct. So, it is a rather tempting trap choice.
“Because of being a …” is not effective, because it does not make clear that John Francis Cook, Jr. was the one being all those things.
Also, the use of the closing participial phrase, “partnering …,” results in the sentence’s conveying the illogical meaning that, in the process of or as a result of serving as a trustee and being appointed a justice of the peace and Chief Tax Collector, John Francis Cook, Jr. partnered with his brother and a legislator to found a manufacturing business.
(E) John Francis Cook, Jr. , who was a prominent educator, politician, and businessperson, served as a Howard University trustee, was appointed a Washington D. C. Justice of the Peace, and Chief Tax Collector, to partner with his brother and a former legislator to found the manufacturing firm Cook, Cook, and White.
The list in this version is not logical, because the third element “Chief Tax Collector” has no verb and, thus, does not work logically with what introduces the list, “John Francis Cook, Jr.
Also, “to partner …” conveys the illogical meaning that John Francis Cook, Jr. served as a trustee of a university and was appointed to government offices in order to partner with people to found a manufacturing firm.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss another idea that will help us to avoid eliminating correct answers in SC questions.
summary:
Lesson Content One might assume that it’s incorrect to have both active voice verbs and passive voice verbs in the same list.
💡MUST KNOW:
There is nothing wrong with having active voice verbs and passive voice verbs together in a list.
Consider the following example:
Having hit her GMAT target, Aisha applied to her first-choice business school, was invited for an interview, and was accepted.
As we already know well, each component of a list must work logically with the essential element or elements that introduce the list.
title: 7.9.2 Active Voice and Passive Voice in the Same List Can be Parallel

contents:
Lesson Content When considering parallelism of clauses in a sentence, there is one simple rule that we can follow:
Clauses in a sentence do not have to be structured in the same way to be parallel.
After all, clauses are parallel to each other in that they all have subjects and verbs.
💡MUST KNOW:
Clauses in a sentence do not have to be structured in the same way to be parallel because clauses are parallel to each other in that they all have subjects and verbs.
Consider the following sentence:
Glass is relatively hard, diamond is harder than glass, and lonsdaleite is the hardest naturally occurring substance known.
Notice that the three clauses in the sentence are parallel.
Consider the following slightly different sentence:
Glass is relatively hard, diamond is harder than glass, and the hardest naturally occurring substance known is lonsdaleite.
Notice that, while the three clauses are not laid out in the same way, they are still parallel, and the sentence is perfectly acceptable.
Consider the following two sentence versions:
While many people like traveling, many others prefer taking “staycations.”
While many people like traveling, taking “staycations” is preferred by many others.
