We might say that the first sentence looks more parallel.
How do we use this knowledge to score points on test day? When we encounter sentences versions composed of multiple clauses, remember that clauses are, by definition, parallel to each other.
Let’s consider some Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 25 Twenty percent of the teachers report that the new curriculum is working no better than the previous one, more than half report noticeable benefits generated by the change, and the change has not affected the remaining about thirty percent of the teachers. 1. [x] (A) more than half report noticeable benefits generated by the change, and the change has not affected the remaining about thirty percent of the teachers 2. [ ] (B) more than half reports that the change has generated noticeable benefits and that the change has not affected the remaining about thirty percent of them > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) the report by more than half is that the change has generated multiple benefits, the change not affecting the remaining about thirty percent of the teachers > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) more than half report that changing generates noticeable benefits, with the remaining about thirty percent of them not affected by the change > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) more than half reports that, by changing, noticeable benefits have been generated, and the remaining about thirty percent have not been affected by the change > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium In most versions, the sentence is composed of three clauses. Arriving at the correct answer requires realizing that those three clauses don’t have to be structured in the same way for the sentence to be correct.
Because the clauses in the correct version are not all structured in the same way, the correct answer may seem incorrect. All the same, careful analysis of all the versions reveals that the correct version is the only one that is logical.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) Twenty percent of the teachers report that the new curriculum is working no better than the previous one, more than half report noticeable benefits generated by the change, and the change has not affected the remaining about thirty percent of the teachers .
The first two clauses in this version begin with quantities, “twenty percent” and “more than half,” while the third clause begins with “the change” and ends with a quantity, “about thirty percent of the teachers.” Also, the first clause includes “report that,” while, in the second clause, “report” is not followed by “that. For these reasons, this version may seem a little off at first.
However, the three independent clauses that make up this version are basically parallel, even though they are not structured in exactly the same way, and since this version is grammatically correct and conveys a logical meaning, this version is correct.
(B) Twenty percent of the teachers report that the new curriculum is working no better than the previous one, more than half reports that the change has generated noticeable benefits and that the change has not affected the remaining about thirty percent of them .
Since “more than half” means “more than half of the teachers,” “more than half” needs a plural verb. So, the singular “reports” is not correct.
Also, because of the presence of “that” after “and,” the list in this version is not logically structured as we can see by summarizing it:
Twenty percent … report that …, more than half reports that … and that ….
We can see the sentence is composed of two independent clauses “twenty percent … report that” and “more than half reports that … and that” joined by a comma without a conjunction. The clause that appears after “and,” “that the change has not affected …,” is not an independent clause. It’s a noun clause that is a direct object of “reports,” in “more than half reports that the change has generated … and that the change has not affected ….”
Even though careful analysis reveals this choice to be clearly flawed, this choice could be tempting, because the repetition of the structure “that the change has” makes the choice look parallel. We have to be careful not to be tempted into choosing a choice like this one just because it has a parallel look to it.
(C) Twenty percent of the teachers report that the new curriculum is working no better than the previous one, the report by more than half is that the change has generated multiple benefits, the change not affecting the remaining about thirty percent of the teachers .
The wording “the report by more than half is that the change has generated multiple benefits” does not make sense. It conveys the illogical meaning that there is some report “by more than half” of the teachers, and that that “report” is “that the change has generated multiple benefits.”
Also, because this version ends with an absolute phrase, “the change not affecting …,” rather than with a third clause connected to the first two clauses by a comma + “and,” this version is incorrectly composed of two clauses, “twenty percent of the teachers report” and “the report by more than half is …,” joined by a comma without a conjunction.
(D) Twenty percent of the teachers report that the new curriculum is working no better than the previous one, more than half report that changing generates noticeable benefits, with the remaining about thirty percent of them not affected by the change .
The wording of this choice, “more than half report that changing generates noticeable benefits,” does not convey a meaning that is clearly connected to the rest of what the sentence says. It seems to convey that the teachers report that changing in general, rather than the previously mentioned change of curriculum, somehow generates benefits, and leaves the reader wondering, “Changing what?”
Also, because this version ends with a modifying phrase, “with the remaining about thirty percent of them …,” rather than with a third clause connected to the first two clauses by a comma + “and,” this version is incorrectly composed of two clauses, “twenty percent of the teachers report …” and “more than half report,” joined by a comma without a conjunction.
(E) Twenty percent of the teachers report that the new curriculum is working no better than the previous one, more than half reports that, by changing, noticeable benefits have been generated, and the remaining about thirty percent have not been affected by the change .
Since “more than half” means “more than half of the teachers,” “more than half” needs a plural verb. So, the singular “reports” is not correct.
Also, “by changing, noticeable benefits have been generated” conveys the nonsensical meaning that, by changing, the benefits have caused themselves to be generated.
We have to be careful not to be tempted into choosing an incorrect choice like this one simply because we see that the choice’s structure is clearly parallel.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 26 Most frogs and toads live for less than 10 years, many salamanders can live for up to 20 years, and an amphibian’s longest lifespan, the olm salamander, is more than 100 years. 1. [ ] (A) an amphibian’s longest lifespan, the olm salamander, is more than 100 years > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) the olm salamander can live for more than 100 years, being the longest lifespan of an amphibian > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) an amphibian’s longest lifespan is more than 100 years, which is the olm salamander > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) the olm salamander can live for more than 100 years, which is the amphibian that lives the longest > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) the longest lifespan of an amphibian is that of the olm salamander, which can live for more than 100 years View Solution Question Level: medium (A) Most frogs and toads live for less than 10 years, many salamanders can live for up to 20 years, and an amphibian’s longest lifespan, the olm salamander, is more than 100 years .
Although the three clauses in this version are parallel, in this version, “an amphibian’s longest lifespan, the olm salamander” conveys the ridiculous meaning that a lifespan is a salamander. After all, “an amphibian,” the logical target of the appositive “the olm salamander,” appears only as a possessive modifying the noun “longest lifespan.” Thus, the appositive “the olm salamander” incorrectly calls the lifespan, rather than the amphibian, a salamander.
(B) Most frogs and toads live for less than 10 years, many salamanders can live for up to 20 years, and the olm salamander can live for more than 100 years, being the longest lifespan of an amphibian .
Although the three clauses in this version are parallel, this version is incorrect because it has a meaning issue. The closing participial phrase “being the longest lifespan” takes the subject of the preceding clause, “the olm salamander,” as the agent of “being.” Thus, this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that “the olm salamander” is “being the longest lifespan.”
(C) Most frogs and toads live for less than 10 years, many salamanders can live for up to 20 years, and an amphibian’s longest lifespan is more than 100 years, which is the olm salamander .
Although the three clauses that make up the list in this version are parallel, the closing relative clause, “which is the olm salamander,” is misplaced, seemingly modifying the noun that directly precedes it, “100 years.” Of course, “100 years” cannot possibly be a salamander, so the meaning conveyed is nonsensical.
(D) Most frogs and toads live for less than 10 years, many salamanders can live for up to 20 years, and the olm salamander can live for more than 100 years, which is the amphibian that lives the longest .
Although the three clauses that make up the list in this version are parallel, the closing relative clause, “which is the amphibian that lives the longest,” is too far removed from the noun it logically should modify, “the olm salamander,” to properly refer to it. Instead, the relative clause seems to modify the noun that directly precedes it, “100 years,” conveying the nonsensical meaning that “100 years” is an amphibian.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Most frogs and toads live for less than 10 years, many salamanders can live for up to 20 years, and the longest lifespan of an amphibian is that of the olm salamander, which can live for more than 100 years .
Remember that clauses in a sentence do not have to be structured in the same way to be parallel. Although the final clause in the list of three clauses that appears in this version, “the longest lifespan of an amphibian is that of the olm salamander” is structured somewhat differently from the first two clauses, “most frogs and toads live …” and “many salamanders can live …,” notice that all three are independent clauses that begin with a subject + verb. In fact, there is nothing about the final clause that makes it not parallel to the first two.
The sentence is parallel and grammatically correct and conveys a logical meaning.
Correct Answer: (E)
summary:
Lesson Content When considering parallelism of clauses in a sentence, there is one simple rule that we can follow:
Clauses in a sentence do not have to be structured in the same way to be parallel.
After all, clauses are parallel to each other in that they all have subjects and verbs.
💡MUST KNOW:
Clauses in a sentence do not have to be structured in the same way to be parallel because clauses are parallel to each other in that they all have subjects and verbs.
title: 7.9.3 Clauses in a Sentence Do Not Have to Be Structured in the Same Way to Be Parallel.

contents:
Lesson Content We have discussed the fact that parallelism is largely based on logic and the fact that structures don’t have to match exactly in order to be acceptably parallel.
At the same time, we have seen that some structures are more parallel than others.
Rome is one of the few cities in the world where a great number of well-preserved ancient structures can be found and that has many examples of stunning Renaissance architecture.
Rome is one of the few cities in the world where a great number of well-preserved ancient structures can be found and where there are many examples of stunning Renaissance architecture.
Notice that the first version uses a relative clause beginning with “where” and a second relative clause beginning with “that has,” while the second version uses two relative clauses beginning with “where.” So, the second version is more parallel than the first.
All the same, the first version is not obviously incorrect.
So, what if these two versions were in a Sentence Correction question?
Here’s a strategy that we can employ in such a situation:
If you have narrowed your choices down to two that seem basically correct and have no significant difference other than degree of parallelism, go with the more parallel choice.
So, how do we use this knowledge to score points come test day? Easy! If we must choose between two choices, both of which seem basically correct, we choose the choice that creates the more parallel structure.
Here’s another pair of sentence versions:
Allobates zaparo, a frog species found in the rainforest and that resembles a poison dart frog, is not actually poisonous.
Allobates zaparo, a frog species found in the rainforest and resembling a poison dart frog, is not actually poisonous.
In the first version, “found in the rainforest” is paired in a list with “that resembles the poison dart frog.”
In the second version, “found in the rainforest” is paired in a list with “resembling a poison dart frog.”
Since “found in the rainforest,” “that resembles the poison dart frog,” and “resembling a poison dart frog” can all correctly modify “frog species” and there is no obvious error in either version, how could we choose between the two sentence versions?
We could use our strategy of choosing the more parallel version and choose the second version.
Let’s consider another pair:
Having considered the rate at which the project was progressing, they decided to hire a second developer, who would work closely with the web design team, and to hire a third developer in two months.
Having considered the rate at which the project was progressing, they decided to hire a second developer, who would work closely with the web design team, and that they would hire a third developer in two months.
Once again, we have two sentences that are basically the same, except in that the structure of the first version, which uses “to” twice, is more parallel than the structure of the second version, which uses “to” and then “that they would.” Since neither choice is obviously incorrect, faced with a choice between two versions like these in a Sentence Correction question, once again, we would use the strategy of simply choosing the more parallel version, which, in this case, is the first version.
Now, remember, we should use this strategy of choosing the choice that creates the more parallel version only in cases in which there is no other reason to choose either version.
💡MUST KNOW:
The strategy of choosing the choice that creates the more parallel version of a sentence should be used only as a last resort when we don’t see any other decision point that we can use to choose between two choices in an SC question.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now consider some Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 27 Wanting to develop creatively, Mikhail Baryshnikov’s defection to the United States from his native Russia was from hoping that he would experience artistic freedom and be able to explore a variety of forms of dance. 1. [ ] (A) Mikhail Baryshnikov’s defection to the United States from his native Russia was from hoping that he would experience artistic freedom and > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Mikhail Baryshnikov defected to the United States from his native Russia, hoping to experience artistic freedom and that he would > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Mikhail Baryshnikov defecting from his native Russia to the United States and hoping to experience artistic freedom and > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) the defection of Mikhail Baryshnikov to the United States from his native Russia was from his hoping to experience artistic freedom and to > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Mikhail Baryshnikov defected from his native Russia to the United States, hoping to experience artistic freedom and View Solution Question Level: medium Some of the sentence versions in this question have clear errors. At the same time, once we’ve eliminated the choices that create those versions, we’re left with two sentence versions that are not blatantly incorrect, the (B) version and the (E) version. So, to decide between choice (B) and choice (E), we have to scan the two sentence versions to find a significant difference that we can use as a decision point.
In doing so, we see that the only significant difference between the two versions is that the (E) version is a bit more parallel than the (B) version. Therefore, to get this question correct, we choose (E).
(A) Wanting to develop creatively, Mikhail Baryshnikov’s defection to the United States from his native Russia was from hoping that he would experience artistic freedom and be able to explore a variety of forms of dance.
We can readily eliminate this version because the opening modifier, “wanting to develop creatively,” is misplaced so as to illogically modify “Mikhail Baryshnikov’s defection.” After all, “Mikhail Baryshnikov’s defection” would not have been “wanting to develop creatively.”
Notice also that it’s not logical to say that “Mikhail Baryshnikov’s defection … was from hoping.” After all, while he defected because he was hoping to do certain things, his defection did not come “from hoping.”
(B) Wanting to develop creatively, Mikhail Baryshnikov defected to the United States from his native Russia, hoping to experience artistic freedom and that he would be able to explore a variety of forms of dance.
This version has no major issues. So, often, people answering this question are left with this choice and choice (E). At that point, arriving at the correct answer requires considering the differences between this version and the (E) version to find a reason to choose one choice over the other.
There are two differences between this version and the (E) version.
One is that, whereas this version says, “defected to the United States from his native Russia,” the (E) version says, “defected from his native Russia to the United States.” There’s no significant grammatical or logical difference between the two. So, that difference does not enable us to choose one choice over the other.
The other difference is that, whereas this version says, “hoping to experience artistic freedom and that he would be able to,” the (E) version says, “hoping to experience artistic freedom and be able to.” We see that what this version says is a little less parallel than what the (E) version says.
So, since there’s no other significant difference between what this version says and what the (E) version says, we can eliminate this choice and choose (E) because the (E) version is a bit more parallel.
(C) Wanting to develop creatively, Mikhail Baryshnikov defecting from his native Russia to the United States and hoping to experience artistic freedom and be able to explore a variety of forms of dance.
Since neither “defecting” nor “hoping” can on its own serve as a main verb, this version has no main verb and thus is not a complete sentence.
(D) Wanting to develop creatively, the defection of Mikhail Baryshnikov to the United States from his native Russia was from his hoping to experience artistic freedom and to be able to explore a variety of forms of dance.
We can readily eliminate this version because the opening modifier, “wanting to develop creatively,” is misplaced so as to illogically modify “the defection of Mikhail Baryshnikov.” After all, “the defection” would not have been “wanting to develop creatively.”
Notice also that it’s not logical to say that “Mikhail Baryshnikov’s defection … was from his hoping.” After all, while he defected because he was hoping to do certain things, his defection did not come “from his hoping.”
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Wanting to develop creatively, Mikhail Baryshnikov defected from his native Russia to the United States, hoping to experience artistic freedom and be able to explore a variety of forms of dance.
This choice is grammatically correct and logically constructed and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
Also, as discussed in the explanation of choice (B), this choice is a bit more parallel than choice (B), which does not have any major issues.
So, this choice is the best answer to this question.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 28 A system of drainage canals is being installed in the city for the purpose of mitigating flooding in the near term with prevention of it in the more distant future. 1. [ ] (A) mitigating flooding in the near term with prevention of it > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) mitigating flooding in the near term and preventing it 3. [ ] (C) flood mitigation in the near term and prevention of it > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) flood mitigating in the near term and its prevention > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) mitigating flooding in the near term and the prevention of it > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium (A) A system of drainage canals is being installed in the city for the purpose of mitigating flooding in the near term with prevention of it in the more distant future.
Notice that, in this version, “for the purpose of mitigating flooding in the near term with prevention of it in the more distant future” conveys a nonsensical meaning. After all, it seems to convey that the purpose is to mitigate flooding in the near term “with,” i.e., “by means of” prevention of it in the future. Of course, it’s not possible to mitigate flooding in the near term by preventing in the more distant future. So, we can eliminate this choice because this version has a meaning issue.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) A system of drainage canals is being installed in the city for the purpose of mitigating flooding in the near term and preventing it in the more distant future.
This choice is grammatically correct and logically constructed and conveys a meaning that makes sense.
Also, as discussed in the explanation of choice (E), this choice is a bit more parallel than choice (E), which does not have any major issues.
So, this choice is the best answer to this question.
(C) A system of drainage canals is being installed in the city for the purpose of flood mitigation in the near term and prevention of it in the more distant future.
This choice may appear correct at first glance because “mitigation” matches “prevention.”
However, notice that the pronoun “it” has no noun to refer to. After all, in this version “flood” is used as an adjective to modify “mitigation,” not as a noun. Also, “it” cannot logically refer to “flood mitigation” since it would not make sense to say that the system of canals is being installed for “prevention of flood mitigation.”
(D) A system of drainage canals is being installed in the city for the purpose of flood mitigating in the near term and its prevention in the more distant future.
The clearest reason to eliminate this choice is that “it” has no noun to refer to. After all, in this version “flood” is used as an adjective to modify “mitigating, not as a noun. Also, “it” cannot logically refer to “flood mitigating” since it would not make sense to say that the system of canals is being installed for “prevention of flood mitigating.”
Another sign that this choice is incorrect is that people don’t normally say, “flood mitigating.” They say, “flood mitigation.” Is that a clear reason to eliminate this choice? Perhaps not, but it’s a reason to suspect that this choice is not the correct answer.
(E) A system of drainage canals is being installed in the city for the purpose of mitigating flooding in the near term and the prevention of it in the more distant future.
This version has no major issues. So, often, people answering this question are left with this choice and choice (B). At that point, arriving at the correct answer requires considering the difference between this version and the (B) version to find a reason to choose one choice over the other.
The only difference is that, whereas this version says, “mitigating flooding in the near term and the prevention of it,” the (B) version says, “mitigating flooding in the near term and preventing it.” We see that what this version says is a little less parallel than what the (B) version says.
So, since there’s no other significant difference between what this version says and what the (B) version says, we can eliminate this choice and choose (B) because the (B) version is a bit more parallel.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now talk about the special cases of gerunds and infinitives in parallel structures.
summary:
Lesson Content We have discussed the fact that parallelism is largely based on logic and the fact that structures don’t have to match exactly in order to be acceptably parallel.
At the same time, we have seen that some structures are more parallel than others.
Rome is one of the few cities in the world where a great number of well-preserved ancient structures can be found and that has many examples of stunning Renaissance architecture.
title: 7.10 When, other than in degree of parallelism, two versions of a sentence seem equally correct, choose the more parallel version.

contents:
Lesson Content Gerunds, verb forms that end in –ing, such as “sailing” or “writing,” are essentially nouns, and infinitives, verbs preceded by “to,” such as “to sail” or “to write,” can also be used as nouns.
However, despite this similarity between gerunds and infinitives, the convention followed by the GMAT is that gerunds and infinitives are not parallel.
💡MUST KNOW:
The GMAT prefers versions that do not use gerunds and infinitives together in lists.
Consider the following example.
Incorrect: I like playing tennis, cycling in the mountains, and to relax at the end of the day.
Even though it may seem fine as it is, the sentence would not be preferred by the GMAT because it contains a list that includes two gerunds, “playing” and “cycling,” and an infinitive, “to relax.”
The GMAT would prefer one of the following.
A list composed of infinitives:
I like to play tennis, to cycle in the mountains, and to relax at the end of the day.
A list composed of gerunds:
I like playing tennis, cycling in the mountains, and relaxing at the end of the day.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s consider a Sentence Correction example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 29 Before he began work on a painting, the modern artist Balthus liked to meditate for long periods in front of the canvas and smoking to increase his concentration. 1. [ ] (A) to meditate for long periods in front of the canvas and smoking > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) meditating for long periods in front of the canvas and smoking 3. [ ] (C) to meditate, long periods in front of the canvas, and with smoking > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) meditating for long periods in front of the canvas and to smoke > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) for long periods to meditate in front of the canvas and smoking > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) Before he began work on a painting, the modern artist Balthus liked to meditate for long periods in front of the canvas and smoking to increase his concentration.
A list of two elements in which one element begins with an infinitive verb and the other element begins with a gerund would not be preferred on the GMAT. This version contains a list of two elements introduced by “liked”:
liked to meditate for long periods in front of the canvas and liked smoking
Since “to meditate” is an infinitive and “smoking” is a gerund, this construction is not preferred. However, since this version of the sentence does not contain any conclusive grammar or meaning errors, let’s not rule it out altogether before examining the other answer choices. Remember, the best of five possible answer choices in a GMAT question may not always be written in the best way imaginable.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) Before he began work on a painting, the modern artist Balthus liked meditating for long periods in front of the canvas and smoking to increase his concentration.
This version contains a list of two elements both of which begin with gerunds, “meditating” and “smoking”:
Balthus liked meditating for long periods in front of the canvas and Balthus liked smoking
The sentence contains no grammar or meaning errors. With all that considered, this answer choice is preferable to choices (A) and (D), which we can now eliminate, since two gerunds are considered more parallel than a gerund and an infinitive verb on the GMAT.
(C) Before he began work on a painting, the modern artist Balthus liked to meditate, long periods in front of the canvas, and with smoking to increase his concentration.
This third element in the list in this version, “with smoking,” is not parallel to the infinitive verb “to meditate” or the noun phrase “long periods in front of the canvas.”
Furthermore, if we carry over the what introduces the list, “Balthus liked,” we can see that the third element does not convey a logical meaning:
Balthus liked to meditate, Balthus liked long periods in front of the canvas, and Balthus liked with smoking to increase his concentration.
“Balthus liked with smoking to increase his concentration” is a nonsensical statement.
The sentence is not parallel or logical.
(D) Before he began work on a painting, the modern artist Balthus liked meditating for long periods in front of the canvas and to smoke to increase his concentration.
A list of two elements in which one element begins with a gerund and the other element begins with an infinitive verb would not be preferred on the GMAT. This version contains a list of two elements introduced by “liked”:
liked meditating for long periods in front of the canvas and liked to smoke
Since “meditating” is a gerund and “to smoke” is an infinitive, this construction is not preferred. However, notice that, as in (A), there are no conclusive grammar or meaning errors in this version. In fact, the only difference between this version and the version created with (A) is that the infinitive verb and the gerund are reversed in order. However, both choices feature an infinitive verb and a gerund as the two elements in a list, providing another clue that neither choice (since both are essentially the same) will end up being the best choice.
(E) Before he began work on a painting, the modern artist Balthus liked for long periods to meditate in front of the canvas and smoking to increase his concentration.
A list of two elements in which one element begins with an infinitive verb (“to meditate”) and the other element begins with a gerund (“smoking”) would not be preferred on the GMAT. This version contains a list of two such elements, introduced by “Balthus liked”:
Balthus liked for long periods to meditate in front of the canvas and Balthus liked smoking
Notice also that “for long periods” is a squinting modifier; it could modify “liked” or “to meditate.” Was the activity of meditation something that Balthus liked for long periods of his life, or was the activity something that Balthus liked to do for long periods at a time? Given the current placement of modifier, we can’t be sure which meaning is intended.
Since the sentence contains a list that is not parallel, and the sentence conveys an ambiguous, meaning this choice is incorrect.
Correct Answer: (B)
summary:
Lesson Content Gerunds, verb forms that end in –ing, such as “sailing” or “writing,” are essentially nouns, and infinitives, verbs preceded by “to,” such as “to sail” or “to write,” can also be used as nouns.
However, despite this similarity between gerunds and infinitives, the convention followed by the GMAT is that gerunds and infinitives are not parallel.
💡MUST KNOW:
The GMAT prefers versions that do not use gerunds and infinitives together in lists.
title: 7.11 Gerunds and Infinitives – Special Cases

contents:
Lesson Content It’s hard to get through the Verbal section of the GMAT without encountering Sentence Correction questions that involve comparisons.
In a comparison, two or more things, ideas, concepts, entities, or people are compared.
For example:
John is like Melissa; they both love Caribbean beaches.
The teeth of the gray wolf are similar in size to the teeth of the red wolf.
Federico can run the 40-yard dash as quickly as Johanna can.
The Magna Carta is a set of laws older than the Bill of Rights.
Swimming is similar to hiking in that both elevate a person’s heart rate.
We see that a comparison is easy to recognize.
Logical Unambiguous Properly constructed Grammatical A comparison is logical when it compares similar things, using two or more items.
Let’s begin by discussing how to identify comparisons.

title: 8.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content There are a number of signal words that can alert us that a comparison is being made.
in the same way than at the same rate similar to correspondingly much as so whereas while nevertheless nonetheless by the same token similarly just as likewise in similar fashion as … as 💡MUST KNOW:
Familiarize yourself with the common comparison signal words and be on the lookout for them.
Let’s now discuss the important concept that comparisons must be logical.

title: 8.2 Identifying Comparisons

contents:
Lesson Content Whenever we make a comparison, that comparison must be logical.
Let’s discuss how these illogical comparisons typically occur.
When an Item Is Illogically Compared With a Different Type of Item
Some illogical comparisons involve an item compared with a different type of item.
Consider the following sentence:
Incorrect: The cost of summer camp is much higher than summer school.
Let’s identify the two items being compared.
We can’t logically compare summer school with the cost of summer camp.
To correct this error, we must revise the sentence so that two similar items are compared.
The cost of summer camp is much higher than summer school’s.
The cost of summer camp is much higher than that of summer school.
Note that we could repeat “the cost” to clarify the comparison, but the GMAT typically finds this approach a little wordy.
The cost of summer camp is much higher than summer school’s cost.
Or
The cost of summer camp is much higher than the cost of summer school.
Although the above sentences are significantly better than sentences with illogical comparisons, keep in mind that the GMAT prefers concise answers, all else equal.
💡MUST KNOW:
When we make a comparison, the items compared must be logically comparable. If a comparison is made in a sentence, locate the items compared and make sure that it is logical to compare those items.
Let’s look at some more examples:
Incorrect: In comparison with the president’s views, the vice president is conservative.
By noting the words used, “in comparison," and the meaning that they convey, we can tell that the sentence is expressing a comparison of two things, “the president’s views” and “the vice president.” Is this comparison a logical one? Certainly not.
In comparison with the president’s views, the vice president’s views are conservative.
In comparison with the president, the vice president is conservative.
The following more concise sentence is also a correct version of the sentence.
In comparison with the president’s views, those of the vice president are conservative.
Now, the two items are logically comparable.
Incorrect: Last year, the cost of an MBA was greater than a house.
Notice that the sentence is illogically comparing the “cost of an MBA” to a “house.” We can re-write the sentence:
Last year, the cost of an MBA was greater than the cost of a house.
Last year, the cost of an MBA was greater than that of a house.
Now, each sentence conveys a logical meaning.
Incorrect: The maximum acceleration of the F-22 is much greater than the F-16.
We can’t compare the “acceleration of the F-22” with the “the F-16.” We can re-write the sentence:
The maximum acceleration of the F-22 is much greater than the maximum acceleration of the F-16.
The maximum acceleration of the F-22 is much greater than that of the F-16.
The F-22’s maximum acceleration is much greater than the F-16’s.
Now, each sentence conveys a logical meaning.
Incorrect: The colors used in Picasso’s lithographs are quite similar to those by Van Gogh.
Are certain colors “by Van Gogh?” Not really.
We can re-write the sentence:
The colors used in Picasso’s lithographs are quite similar to those used by Van Gogh. The colors used in Picasso’s lithographs are like those used in Van Gogh’s lithographs.
Now the comparison is logical.
Incorrect: Therapeutic treatment with antibiotic A produced fewer secondary sinus infections than antibiotic B.
The above sentence illogically compares therapeutic treatment with antibiotic A with antibiotic B.
Therapeutic treatment with antibiotic A produced fewer secondary sinus infections than were produced by therapeutic treatment with antibiotic B.
Incorrect: The students in this chemistry course are unlike other chemistry classes.
We cannot compare students to classes.
The students in this chemistry course are unlike those in other chemistry classes.
The students in this chemistry course are unlike the students in other chemistry classes.
Now the sentence is logically written.
Incorrect: Like many CEOs, Jack’s budget appeared well reasoned; however it was badly flawed.
Can we compare CEOs with budgets? For sure, no.
Like the budgets of many CEOs, Jack’s budget appeared well reasoned; however it was badly flawed.
Like many CEOs, Jack had a budget that appeared well reasoned; however it was badly flawed.
In this last sentence, we’ve changed the meaning slightly.
Also, notice that, in the correct versions, plural entities and singular entities are compared.
💡MUST KNOW:
Comparing singular and plural entities with each other is fine as long as doing so results in a logical comparison.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s practice applying what we just discussed in answering Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 1 In the United States, even after only the people who spend time at the beach are considered, the odds of being attacked by a shark are about 1 in 12 million, while being struck by lightning is much higher – about 1 in 700,000. 1. [ ] (A) after only the people who spend time at the beach are considered, the odds of being attacked by a shark are about 1 in 12 million, while being struck by lightning is > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) after the people only spending time at the beach are considered, the odds of being attacked by a shark are about 1 in 12 million, while that of being struck by lightning is > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) if the people only spending time at the beach are considered, the odds of being attacked by a shark are about 1 in 12 million, while that of lightning is > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) if only the people who spend time at the beach are considered, the odds of being attacked by a shark are about 1 in 12 million, while those of being struck by lightning are 5. [ ] (E) if the people who only spend time at the beach are considered, the odds of being attacked by a shark are about 1 in 12 million, while those of lightning are > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy Each version of the sentence in this question expresses a comparison between the odds of being attacked by a shark and something else. Only in the correct version does the comparison expressed compare the odds of being attacked by a shark with something with which the odds of being attacked by a shark can logically be compared.
(A) In the United States, even after only the people who spend time at the beach are considered, the odds of being attacked by a shark are about 1 in 12 million, while being struck by lightning is much higher – about 1 in 700,000.
The use of the preposition “after” does not make sense. The odds mentioned are not calculated “after” the people mentioned are considered. They are calculated via considering those people.
Also, this version illogically compares the odds of being attacked by a shark with “being struck by lightning.”
(B) In the United States, even after the people only spending time at the beach are considered, the odds of being attacked by a shark are about 1 in 12 million, while that of being struck by lightning is much higher – about 1 in 700,000.
The use of the preposition “after” does not make sense. The odds mentioned are not calculated “after” the people mentioned are considered. They are calculated via considering those people.
Also, “only” is misplaced. The point to be made is that the odds are calculated via considering only people who spend time at the beach, not via considering “people only spending time at the beach,” i.e., people who do nothing in life other than spend time at the beach.
Also, the use of the singular pronoun “that” does not make sense, because “that” is understood to be standing in for the plural “odds,” and can’t stand in for the singular “odd,” because the singular “odd” is never used in this type of context, one in which “odds” is used synonymously with “probability.”
(C) In the United States, even if the people only spending time at the beach are considered, the odds of being attacked by a shark are about 1 in 12 million, while that of lightning is much higher – about 1 in 700,000.
“Only” is misplaced. The point to be made is that the odds are calculated via considering only people who spend time at the beach, not via considering “people only spending time at the beach,” i.e., people who do nothing in life other than spend time at the beach.
Also, the use of the singular pronoun “that” does not make sense, because “that” is understood to be standing in for the plural “odds,” and can’t stand in for the singular “odd,” because the singular “odd” is never used in this type of context, one in which “odds” is used synonymously with “probability.”
Also, even if the use of “that” did make sense, this version would illogically compare the odds of being attacked by a shark with the “odds of lightning,” which comparison does not make sense because, while there are odds of a person’s being struck by lightning and odds that lightning will strike at a certain time or in a certain place, lightning itself does not have odds.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) In the United States, even if only the people who spend time at the beach are considered, the odds of being attacked by a shark are about 1 in 12 million, while those of being struck by lightning are much higher – about 1 in 700,000.
In this version “only” is placed so as to logically restrict the people considered to only those in the category “people who spend time at the beach.”
Also, this version logically compares the odds of being attacked by a shark with those, i.e., the odds of, being struck by lightning.
(E) In the United States, even if the people who only spend time at the beach are considered, the odds of being attacked by a shark are about 1 in 12 million, while those of lightning are much higher – about 1 in 700,000.
“Only” is misplaced. The point to be made is that the odds are calculated via considering only people who spend time at the beach, not via considering “people who only spend time at the beach,” i.e., people who do nothing in life other than spend time at the beach.
Also, this version illogically compares the odds of being attacked by a shark with the “odds of lightning,” which comparison does not make sense because, while there are odds of a person’s being struck by lightning and odds that lightning will strike at a certain time or in a certain place, lightning itself does not have odds.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 2 With the average yearly temperature increasing in the state, meteorologists predict that severe forest fires will damage this year a number of California’s state parks greater than last year. 1. [ ] (A) this year a number of California’s state parks greater than > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) this year a greater number of California’s state parks than such fires damaged 3. [ ] (C) a number of California’s state parks this year greater in comparison with such fires > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) a greater number of California’s state parks this year than the parks > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) California’s state parks this year in a number that will be greater than the parks damaged > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard Correctly answering this question takes being careful not to unconsciously mentally fix the flaws in the logic of the comparisons expressed by the incorrect versions.
(A) With the average yearly temperature increasing in the state, meteorologists predict that severe forest fires will damage this year a number of California’s state parks greater than last year.
This version is tricky, because it could seem to logically compare the number of parks predicted to be damaged this year with the number of parks damaged last year.
However, the truth is that, in saying “a number of California’s state parks greater than last year,” this version expresses an illogical comparison of “a number of … parks” with “last year.”
Of course, a number of parks would not be greater than “last year.”
CORRECT ANSWER(B) With the average yearly temperature increasing in the state, meteorologists predict that severe forest fires will damage this year a greater number of California’s state parks than such fires damaged last year.
This version logically compares the number of parks forest fires are predicted to damage this year with the number such fires damaged last year.
(C) With the average yearly temperature increasing in the state, meteorologists predict that severe forest fires will damage a number of California’s state parks this year greater in comparison with such fires last year.
In saying, “a number of California’s state parks this year greater in comparison with such fires last year,” this version suggests the illogical idea that “a number of … parks” is “greater” than “such fires.”
Of course, a number of parks would be greater than another number of parks, not greater than fires.
(D) With the average yearly temperature increasing in the state, meteorologists predict that severe forest fires will damage a greater number of California’s state parks this year than the parks last year.
In saying “a greater number of California’s state parks this year than the parks last year,” this version suggests the illogical idea that “a … number of parks” is greater than “the parks.”
What we have to notice is that a number would not be greater than “the parks.” A number would be greater than another number.
Also, “the parks last year” is not quite logical. Parks would not be in a year. “The parks damaged last year” would make sense, but “the parks last year” does not.
(E) With the average yearly temperature increasing in the state, meteorologists predict that severe forest fires will damage California’s state parks this year in a number that will be greater than the parks damaged last year.
In saying “a number that will be greater than the parks damaged,” this version illogically compares “a number” with “the parks.”
Although the comparison may seem to make sense since “a number” refers to a number of parks, it does not make sense to say that a number will be greater than parks.
Also, the wording of this version, “forest fires will damage California’s state parks this year in a number,” does not convey a meaning that makes sense. How exactly would forest fires damage parks “in a number”?
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now consider another type of illogical comparison – one in which something is mistakenly compared with itself.
summary:
Lesson Content Whenever we make a comparison, that comparison must be logical.
Let’s discuss how these illogical comparisons typically occur.
When an Item Is Illogically Compared With a Different Type of Item
Some illogical comparisons involve an item compared with a different type of item.
Consider the following sentence:
Incorrect: The cost of summer camp is much higher than summer school.
Let’s identify the two items being compared.
We can’t logically compare summer school with the cost of summer camp.
title: 8.3 Comparisons Must be Logical

contents:
Lesson Content Another type of illogical comparison involves the comparison of something with itself.
Incorrect: Julie is more skilled at singing than any singer I’ve encountered.
This sentence expresses a comparison of Julie with any singer that the speaker has ever encountered.
Since Julie cannot be more skilled than herself, we need to distinguish between Julie and “any singer.” We can do this by inserting the word “other,” as demonstrated below:
Julie is more skilled at singing than any other singer I’ve encountered.
This change seems like a small one, but it is not.
The word “other” is not the only word we can use to make a distinction between items or people compared.
Julie is more skilled at singing than anyone else I have encountered.
Now, the comparison is clear.
Let’s look at another example:
Incorrect: Regardless of whether they love or hate the Italian soccer club Juventus, many soccer fans would agree that Cristiano Ronaldo is better at soccer than any player in history.
Although we may be comfortable speaking that sentence, it’s illogical.
Regardless of whether they love or hate the Italian soccer club Juventus, many soccer fans would agree that Cristiano Ronaldo is better at soccer than any other player in history.
Now the logic of the sentence works.
Sometimes the fact that something is illogically compared with itself is harder to see.
Consider the following:
Incorrect: The bears that live in Pennsylvania survive longer than they do in Texas.
What’s wrong with that sentence? What does “they” stand for? “They” takes the place of “bears that live in Pennsylvania.”
So, let’s replace the pronoun with the noun phrase:
Incorrect: The bears that live in Pennsylvania survive longer than bears that live in Pennsylvania do in Texas.
What!? There is some madness for you, right? The sentence makes an extremely illogical comparison.
At this point, many people say, ‘Hey.
Possible corrections are:
The bears that live in Pennsylvania survive longer than the bears that live in Texas do.
The bears that live in Pennsylvania survive longer than those that live in Texas do.
Bears survive longer in Pennsylvania than they do in Texas
These sentences now make logical comparisons.
In general, we must be very vigilant for errors and ambiguities in meaning and logic when pronouns refer to nouns that are immediately followed by modifiers, such as “dogs that live in the house,” “women who attend Stanford University,” or “elephants that live in Africa.”
Let’s examine another example sentence:
Incorrect: Trees that are planted in rich soil grow faster than they do in poor soil.
What’s wrong with that sentence? What does the pronoun “they” represent? “They” represents “Trees that are planted in rich soil.” Let’s replace the pronoun with the noun that it represents:
Trees that are planted in rich soil grow faster than trees that are planted in rich soil do in poor soil.
The sentence is illogical.
Trees that are planted in rich soil grow faster than those planted in poor soil do.
Trees that are planted in rich soil grow faster than trees planted in poor soil do.
Now the sentences make comparisons that are logical.
Trees grow faster in rich soil than they do in poor soil.
In this last case, the prepositional phrase “in rich soil” no longer modifies the noun “trees,” and thus the pronoun “they” refers to trees in general, with the result that the comparison makes sense.
Let’s look at another example.
Incorrect: Although students have long considered Mr.
What is the meaning of the above sentence? Does the same group of kids behave better when they are in Ms.
Incorrect: Although students have long considered Mr.
The comparison is illogical.
Because the prepositional phrase “in Ms.
So, in order for the sentence to express a logical comparison, it has to be written in a different way.
Although students have long considered Mr.
Although students have long considered Mr.
Both sentences express a clear meaning and a logical comparison.
Let’s look at one more example.
Incorrect: While home values in many rural and suburban areas of the country plummeted during the 2008 economic crisis now known as the Great Recession, the real estate market in New York City proved far more resilient than it did even in the country’s second-largest city, Los Angeles.
Did you catch the error in the sentence above? The use of the pronoun “it” does not result in the sentence’s expressing a logical comparison between the real estate market in New York City and the real estate market in Los Angeles.
Incorrect: While home values in many rural and suburban areas of the country plummeted during the 2008 economic crisis now known as the Great Recession, the real estate market in New York City proved far more resilient than the real estate market in New York City did even in the country’s second-largest city, Los Angeles.
The real estate market in New York City can’t possibly be in Los Angeles.
While home values in many rural and suburban areas of the country plummeted during the 2008 economic crisis now known as the Great Recession, the real estate market in New York City proved far more resilient than the real estate market in the country’s second-largest city, Los Angeles.
💡MUST KNOW:
Be careful not to perceive as correct a comparison that illogically compares something with itself.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each sentence expresses a logical comparison and thus is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s practice applying what we just learned in answering Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 3 Although a hardy houseplant, aloe vera plants that are in the wild grow faster than when indoors. 1. [ ] (A) Although a hardy houseplant, aloe vera plants that are in the wild grow faster than when indoors. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Aloe vera plants grow faster in the wild than they do indoors, though they are hardy houseplants. 3. [ ] (C) Aloe vera plants that are in the wild grow faster than when they are indoors, although they are a hardy houseplant. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Aloe vera plants that are in the wild grow faster than they do when indoors, though they are hardy houseplants. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Even though they are hardy houseplants, aloe vera plants grow faster in the wild than what they do indoors. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium Correctly answering this question takes understanding the following.
Because the modifier “that are in the wild” restricts what it modifies, the expression “aloe vera plants that are in the wild” restricts the aloe vera plants mentioned to a certain category, ones that are in the wild.
So, when a sentence uses the expression “aloe vera plants that are in the wild,” it is naming only certain aloe vera plants, the ones that are in the wild.
Thus, if a pronoun, such as “they,” refers to “aloe verb plants that are in the wild,” that pronoun does not refer to “aloe vera plants.” It refers to the entire expression “aloe vera plants that are in the wild.”
By applying the above information, we can see that the comparisons expressed by some choices in this question illogically compare “aloe vera plants that are in the wild” with themselves.
(A) Although a hardy houseplant, aloe vera plants that are in the wild grow faster than when indoors. Since the opening modifier “although a hardy houseplant” is placed so as to modify “aloe vera plants that are in the wild,” this version has an agreement issue, because the singular “a hardy houseplant” does not match the plural “aloe vera plants.” Multiple aloe vera “plants” would not be “a hardy houseplant.”
Furthermore, “aloe vera plants that are in the wild” would not be “houseplants.” So, saying, “although a hardy houseplant, aloe vera plants that are in the wild” does not make sense.
Also, the comparison expressed by this version does not make sense. In saying, “aloe vera plants that are in the wild grow faster than when indoors,” this version seems to express a nonsensical comparison of aloe vera plants that are in the wild with a time, “when indoors.”
CORRECT ANSWER(B) Aloe vera plants grow faster in the wild than they do indoors, though they are hardy houseplants. Notice that, unlike in some other versions of the sentence, in this version, the logical referent of “they” is “aloe vera plants.”
As a result, this version logically compares how fast aloe vera plants grow in the wild with how fast aloe vera plants grow indoors, as we can see by substituting “aloe vera plants” for “they.”
aloe vera plants grow faster in the wild than they do indoors, though they are hardy houseplants
aloe vera plants grow faster in the wild than aloe vera plants do indoors, though aloe vera plants are hardy houseplants
(C) Aloe vera plants that are in the wild grow faster than when they are indoors, although they are a hardy houseplant. In saying, “Aloe vera plants that are in the wild grow faster than when they are indoors,” this version expresses a nonsensical comparison of aloe vera plants in the wild and a time, “when they are indoors.”
Also, since the logical referent of “they” is “aloe vera plants that are in the wild,” this version suggests the illogical ideas that aloe vera plants that are in the wild sometimes “are indoors” and that aloe vera plants that are in the wild “are hardy houseplants,” as we can see by substituting “aloe vera plants that are in the wild” for “they.”
aloe vera plants that are in the wild grow faster than when they are indoors, though they are hardy houseplants
aloe vera plants that are in the wild grow faster than when aloe vera plants that are in the wild are indoors, although aloe vera plants that are in the wild are hardy houseplants
Aloe vera plants in the wild would not be indoors or houseplants.
(D) Aloe vera plants that are in the wild grow faster than they do when indoors, though they are hardy houseplants. Since the logical referent of “they” is “aloe vera plants that are in the wild,” this version illogically compares “aloe vera plants that are in the wild” with themselves, as we can see by substituting “aloe vera plants that are in the wild” for “they.”
aloe vera plants that are in the wild grow faster than they do when indoors
aloe vera plants that are in the wild grow faster than aloe vera plants that are in the wild do when indoors
Notice also that this version suggests the illogical idea that aloe vera plants that are in the wild sometimes are indoors. Aloe vera plants that are in the wild would not be indoors.
Also, “Aloe vera plants that are in the wild grow faster …, though they are hardy houseplants,” conveys the nonsensical meaning that aloe vera plants that are in the wild are houseplants. Of course, aloe vera plants in the wild would not be houseplants.
(E) Even though they are hardy houseplants, aloe vera plants grow faster in the wild than what they do indoors. This version expresses a nonsensical comparison between aloe vera plants and “what they do indoors.”
Also, the use of “even though” in this context does not quite make sense. “Even though” is used to express that something occurs despite something else.
So, in this case, “Even though they are hardy houseplants, aloe vera plants grow faster in the wild” suggests the illogical idea that aloe vera plants grow faster in the wild despite the fact that they are hardy houseplants, as if aloe vera plants’ being hardy houseplants might be expected to keep aloe vera plants from growing faster in the wild. Aloe vera plants’ being hardy, in other words surviving well, as houseplants would not keep aloe vera plants from growing faster in the wild.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 4 High blood pressure in the U.S. population is significantly more prevalent than the Swiss population, although the Swiss eat more cheese from France and drink more beer from Belgium than Americans eat and drink. 1. [ ] (A) High blood pressure in the U. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) In the U. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) In the U. 4. [ ] (D) High blood pressure in the U. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) In the U. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium Correctly answering this question takes paying careful attention to pronoun reference, because the use of the pronoun “it” results in a version’s illogically comparing something to itself.
(A) High blood pressure in the U. S. population is significantly more prevalent than the Swiss population, although the Swiss eat more cheese from France and drink more beer from Belgium than Americans eat and drink.
This version illogically compares how prevalent high blood pressure is in the U.S. population with how prevalent the Swiss population is.
(B) In the U. S. , high blood pressure in the population is significantly more prevalent than in the Swiss, although the Swiss eat more cheese from France and drink more beer from Belgium than Americans eat and drink.
Because this version opens with “in the U.S.,” what is expressed by the clause that follows “in the U.S.” is understood to be the case in the U.S. So, this version is subtly illogical, because it conveys that it is the case in the U.S. that high blood pressure in the population is more prevalent than in the Swiss, as if both “the population” and “the Swiss” are in the U.S. and high blood pressure is more prevalent in the former than in the latter.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) In the U. S. population, high blood pressure is significantly more prevalent than it is in the Swiss population, although the Swiss eat more cheese from France and drink more beer from Belgium than Americans eat and drink.
Because, in this version, “it” refers to “high blood pressure,” this version logically compares how prevalent high blood pressure is in the U.S. population with how prevalent it is in the Swiss population, as we can see by substituting “high blood pressure” for “it” in the sentence.
In the U.S. population, high blood pressure is significantly more prevalent than it is in the Swiss population …
In the U.S. population, high blood pressure is significantly more prevalent than high blood pressure is in the Swiss population …
(D) High blood pressure in the U. S. population is significantly more prevalent than it is in the Swiss population, although the Swiss eat more cheese from France and drink more beer from Belgium than Americans eat and drink.
Since the logical referent of “it” in this version is “high blood pressure in the U.S. population,” this version illogically compares high blood pressure in the U.S. population with high blood pressure in the U.S. population, as we can see by substituting “high blood pressure in the U.S. population” for “it.”
High blood pressure in the U.S. population is significantly more prevalent than it is in the Swiss population …
High blood pressure in the U.S. population is significantly more prevalent than high blood pressure in the U.S. population is in the Swiss population …
Also, because “it” refers to “high blood pressure in the U.S. population,” this version suggests the nonsensical idea that high blood pressure in the U.S. population is in the Swiss population.
(E) In the U. S. , high blood pressure is significantly more prevalent than Switzerland, although the Swiss eat more cheese from France and drink more beer from Belgium than Americans eat and drink.
This version is blatantly illogical because it compares high blood pressure with Switzerland.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss verbs in comparisons.
summary:
Lesson Content Another type of illogical comparison involves the comparison of something with itself.
Incorrect: Julie is more skilled at singing than any singer I’ve encountered.
This sentence expresses a comparison of Julie with any singer that the speaker has ever encountered.
Since Julie cannot be more skilled than herself, we need to distinguish between Julie and “any singer.” We can do this by inserting the word “other,” as demonstrated below:
title: 8.4 When Something is Mistakenly Compared With Itself

contents:
Lesson Content So far, whether examples that we have considered have been logical has depended primarily on whether comparing the things compared, which are named by nouns, makes sense.
One thing that we can be sure of is that repeating in the second part of a comparison the verb or verb phrase that appears in the first part of a comparison is generally going to work.
Jim attends the club meetings less regularly than Andrew attends the club meetings.
Catherine Ndereba likes running more than most people like running.
Andrea worked more carefully than Shashank worked.
Franklin Delano Roosevelt held the office of President for longer than Teddy Roosevelt held the office.
Because the comparisons in the above examples basically repeat in the second parts of the comparisons the verbs or verb phrases from the first parts of the comparisons, the sentences rather obviously make sense.
Another way to create logical comparisons is to substitute “does” or “do” for present tense action verbs and “did” for past tense action verbs.
Jim attends the club meetings less regularly than Andrew does.
In the above example, “does” is substituted for “attends the club meetings.”
Catherine Ndereba likes running more than most people do.
In the above example, “do” is substituted for “like running”
Andrea worked more carefully than Shashank did.
In the above example, “did” is substituted for “worked.”
Franklin Delano Roosevelt held the office of President for longer than Teddy Roosevelt did.
In the above example, “did” is substituted for “held the office.”
💡MUST KNOW:
Logical comparisons can be created via the substitution of “does” or “do” for repeated present tense action verbs or verb phrases and “did” for repeated past tense action verbs or verb phrases.
We can also use multiple tenses in comparisons as long as the resulting comparisons make sense.
Michael Phelps swims faster than Mark Spitz swam.
Early space exploration excited people more than space exploration excites people currently.
Some of today’s healthcare practitioners are more aware of the mind-body connection than many of their predecessors were.
The first example expresses a logical comparison of how Michael Phelps swims in the present and how Mark Spitz swam in the past.
Multiple tenses can work in comparisons also when “did” is substituted for a past tense verb or “does” is substituted for a present tense verb, as in the following examples:
Michael Phelps swims faster than Mark Spitz did.
Early space exploration excited people more than space exploration does currently.
💡MUST KNOW:
Verbs of multiple tenses can appear in comparisons as long as the resulting comparisons make sense.
Now, here’s an example that doesn’t work:
Incorrect: Elisa is just as patient with the students as Colleen does.
The above example is incorrect because it compares how Elisa is with what Colleen does.
Elisa is just as patient with the students as Colleen is.
Here’s another example that erroneously compares what one person did with what another person was:
Incorrect: Yesterday, Alexander, who really wanted to win, played harder than Giulio was.
The above example almost sounds correct, but if we read it carefully, we see that it illogically compares what Alexander did with how Giulio was.
Yesterday, Alexander, who really wanted to win, played harder than Giulio did.
The above example logically compares what Alexander did yesterday with what Giulio did yesterday.
Here’s another example that doesn’t make sense:
Incorrect: Katina has less experience in managing projects than Sondra has been.
It doesn’t make sense to compare something that Katina has with “Sondra has been,” because, even though both parts of the comparison use the word “has,” “Katina has” expresses something that Katina does – she has – whereas “Sondra has been” would logically be used to express, not something that Sondra does, but Sondra’s state of being, how Sondra has been.
A correct version is:
Katina has less experience in managing projects than Sondra has.
Now, the sentence expresses a logical comparison of what Katina has with what Sondra has.
Now, the incorrect examples above are pretty blatantly incorrect.
To see how such obscuring can work, consider the following example:
Incorrect: A bright light flashing in the darkness is more likely to attract the attention of passing drivers than a similarly bright light shining steadily would.
The above example is illogical because it compares how likely a bright light flashing “is” to attract attention with what a bright light shining steadily “would.” However, because of the length and complexity of the sentence, the sentence could seem fine if read casually.
The issue becomes more obvious if we simplify the sentence:
Incorrect: A light flashing is more likely to attract attention than a light shining would.
It doesn’t make sense to say what that sentence is understood to convey, which is “a light shining would likely to attract attention.”
To correct the sentence, we have to make the comparison logical by replacing “would” with “is,” as in:
A bright light flashing in the darkness is more likely to attract the attention of passing drivers than a similarly bright light shining steadily is.
Now the sentence logically compares how likely a bright light flashing is to attract attention with how likely a bright light shining steadily is to attract attention.
Let’s consider another example:
Incorrect: The study found that professional athletes have greater ability to handle mental fatigue of the type experienced during long matches or endurance events than amateurs can.
While “have ability” and “can” are similar in meaning, this example is illogical because it compares what professional athletes have, “greater ability,” with what amateurs can.
The study found that professional athletes have greater ability to handle mental fatigue of the type experienced during long matches or endurance events than amateurs have.
💡MUST KNOW:
It’s important to pay attention to the verbs used in comparisons, because the use of the wrong verb in a sentence that expresses a comparison can render the sentence illogical.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s practice applying what we just learned in answering Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 5 Because of the new compensation scheme recently put into place, the people working on the assembly line are paid much better than they did in the past. 1. [ ] (A) paid much better than they did > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) much better paid than they could > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) being paid much better than it was > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) much better paid than they were 5. [ ] (E) paid much better than those had > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy Correctly answering this question takes finding a choice the use of which results in a version that expresses a logical comparison between how well paid the people mentioned are in the present and something “in the past.” Some of the choices are rather similar to each other, and choice of verb turns out to be what defines the difference between incorrect choices and the correct answer.
(A) Because of the new compensation scheme recently put into place, the people working on the assembly line are paid much better than they did in the past.
The most logical referent for “they” in this version is “people working on the assembly line.” Since presumably those people didn’t pay themselves, this version illogically compares how well those people “are paid” with something unnamed that the same people “did” in the past. So, the use of the verb “did” is what makes this choice incorrect.
(B) Because of the new compensation scheme recently put into place, the people working on the assembly line are much better paid than they could in the past.
The most logical referent for “they” in this version is “people working on the assembly line.” So, this version illogically compares how well those people “are paid” with something unnamed that the same people “could” in the past. So, the use of the verb “could” is what makes this choice incorrect.
(C) Because of the new compensation scheme recently put into place, the people working on the assembly line are being paid much better than it was in the past.
The word “being” is unnecessary. It is more concise and more direct to say simply “are paid.” While the use of “being” in this version is not a strong reason to eliminate this choice, it is a flaw in this version.
Also, “it” has no clear referent. While “it” may seem to refer to the compensation of the people mentioned, their compensation is not mentioned in the sentence. Further, even if we were to decide that “it” refers to their compensation, the comparison would be illogical, because the sentence mentions nothing that would be logically compared with their compensation. Read literally, with “it” understood to mean “their compensation,” this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that the workers are better paid than their compensation was paid in the past.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) Because of the new compensation scheme recently put into place, the people working on the assembly line are much better paid than they were in the past.
This version expresses a logical comparison between how well paid the workers “are” now with how well paid they “were in the past.” The use of the verb “were” rather than verbs used in some other choices is what makes this choice correct.
(E) Because of the new compensation scheme recently put into place, the people working on the assembly line are paid much better than those had in the past.
The most logical referent for “those” in this version is “people working on the assembly line.” So, this version illogically compares how well people working on the assembly line “are paid” with something unnamed that the same people, i.e., “those,” “had” in the past. So, the use of the verb “had” makes this choice incorrect.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 6 Analysts predict that, this year, the company’s revenue will grow 12 percent, a number much greater than it was in any of the previous five years. 1. [ ] (A) a number much greater than it was > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) which is much more than it was > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) much more than it did 4. [ ] (D) much more than they had > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) much more than it was > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard (A) Analysts predict that, this year, the company’s revenue will grow 12 percent, a number much greater than it was in any of the previous five years.
Since “it” would logically refer to “a number,” and since “a number” refers to “12 percent,” this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that 12 percent is much greater than 12 percent was in any of the previous five years.
(B) Analysts predict that, this year, the company’s revenue will grow 12 percent, which is much more than it was in any of the previous five years.
Since “which” would most logically refer to “12 percent,” this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that 12 percent is much greater than 12 percent was in any of the previous five years.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Analysts predict that, this year, the company’s revenue will grow 12 percent, much more than it did in any of the previous five years.
In this version “much more than it did in any of the previous five years” is an appositive renaming “12 percent.”
Also, “it” would most logically refer to “revenue,” since “did” is a logical substitute for “grew.”
So, this version conveys the logical meaning that analysts predict that revenue will grow 12 percent and that 12 percent is much more than revenue grew in any of the previous five years.
Notice, key to this choice’s being correct is the use of the verb “did” as a replacement for “grew,” which use results in the sentence’s expressing a logical comparison between how much revenue is predicted to grow this year and how much it “did,” i.e., grew, in the past.
(D) Analysts predict that, this year, the company’s revenue will grow 12 percent, much more than they had in any of the previous five years.
In this version, the logical referent for “they” is “analysts.” So, “they had” means “analysts had.” Thus, key to understanding this version is determining what is it that is “much more than analysts had in the past.”
We might be tempted to decide that the sentence is meant to convey that “12 percent” is much more than analysts had in the past. However, the sentence does not say that the analysts currently “have” 12 percent. Also, the sentence does not indicate what the analysts had in the past that 12 percent would be more than. So, it doesn’t make sense to say that 12 percent is what is “much more than they (analysts) had in the past.”
