An alternative interpretation is that the sentence is meant to convey that analysts now predict that revenue will grow 12 percent much more than analysts had in the past predicted that revenue will grow 12 percent. Of course this meaning is ridiculous, and the truth is that, while the wording of this version may sound as if it means something that makes sense, there is no possible interpretation of the wording of this version that makes sense.
(E) Analysts predict that, this year, the company’s revenue will grow 12 percent, much more than it was in any of the previous five years.
If we decide that “it” refers to “revenue,” then this version illogically compares how much revenue is predicted to “grow” this year with how much revenue “was” in any of the past five years.
If we decide that “it” refers to “12 percent,” then this version conveys the nonsensical meaning that 12 percent is much more than 12 percent was in any of the past five years.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss a topic related to verbs in comparisons, ellipsis in comparisons.
summary:
Lesson Content So far, whether examples that we have considered have been logical has depended primarily on whether comparing the things compared, which are named by nouns, makes sense.
One thing that we can be sure of is that repeating in the second part of a comparison the verb or verb phrase that appears in the first part of a comparison is generally going to work.
Jim attends the club meetings less regularly than Andrew attends the club meetings.
title: 8.5 Verbs in Comparisons

contents:
Lesson Content While the word “ellipsis” may not be familiar to you, ellipsis is not a complex concept.
Consider the following example:
The head of sales attends webinars more often than other team members.
When we read the above sentence, we understand it to mean the following:
The head of sales attends webinars more often than other team members attend webinars.
In the first version, the wording “attend webinars” is omitted after “other team members.” This omission, or “ellipsis,” is considered correct because, even though the sentence does not include “attend webinars” after “other team members,” a reader can readily tell what the sentence is meant to express.
In the previous section of this chapter, we discussed that “does” or “do” can be substituted for a verb phrase in a comparison, as “do” is in the following version of the sentence:
The head of sales attends webinars more often than other team members do.
So, what we are seeing now is that, in addition to substitution, in which words such as “does” or “do” are substituted for words that would otherwise be repeated, there is another option, complete omission of words that would otherwise be repeated.
Let’s consider another set of examples:
A racing sailboat can round the course faster than a cruising sailboat can round the course.
A racing sailboat can round the course faster than a cruising sailboat can.
A racing sailboat can round the course faster than a cruising sailboat.
Notice, we can omit some or all of the repeated words “can round the course” from the second part of the comparison without eroding the clarity of the comparison made.
To lock in this concept, let’s consider some more examples in which repeated words are omitted:
She is quoted in the news less often than her sister is quoted in the news.
She is quoted in the news less often than her sister is.
She is quoted in the news less often than her sister.
The second and third versions of sentence omit “quoted in the news” and “is” without sacrificing clarity.
Surprisingly, research has shown that highly plugged-in teens are just as likely to socialize in person as less plugged-in teens are to socialize in person.
Surprisingly, research has shown that highly plugged-in teens are just as likely to socialize in person as less plugged-in teens.
The second version of the sentence omits “are to socialize in person” without losing clarity.
💡MUST KNOW:
Words that would otherwise be repeated in a sentence that expresses a comparison can be omitted as long as the sentence effectively expresses its meaning without the omitted words.
Let’s now discuss ambiguous comparisons.
summary:
Lesson Content While the word “ellipsis” may not be familiar to you, ellipsis is not a complex concept.
Consider the following example:
The head of sales attends webinars more often than other team members.
When we read the above sentence, we understand it to mean the following:
The head of sales attends webinars more often than other team members attend webinars.
In the first version, the wording “attend webinars” is omitted after “other team members.
title: 8.6 Ellipsis in Comparisons

contents:
Lesson Content As we have seen, omitting repeated words in a sentence that expresses a comparison is OK as long as the sentence still expresses its meaning effectively.
Consider the following sentence:
Incorrect: Alex is much nicer to me than Alfredo.
This sentence could be interpreted in two different ways.
In order for the sentence to be effective, the sentence has to include words that help clarify the meaning.
To convey the first meaning unambiguously, we can say:
Alex is much nicer to me than Alfredo is.
Notice, even though “is” is repeated in this case, it can’t be omitted, because without the second “is” the sentence does not effectively convey one meaning.
To convey the second meaning unambiguously, we can say
Alex is much nicer to me than to Alfredo.
In this case, the second “to” is necessary, because, without the second “to,” the sentence does not effectively convey one meaning.
💡MUST KNOW:
Correcting ambiguous comparisons conveying two or more possible meanings can be accomplished via the addition of words that help clarify the meaning.
Incorrect: Sara loves shark diving more than Scott.
This meaning of the sentence is unclear.
With the addition of the substitute verb “does” to the sentence, the meaning conveyed becomes clear:
Sara loves shark diving more than Scott does.
Now, it’s clear that Sara loves Shark diving more than Scott loves Shark diving.
But, maybe the speaker meant:
Sara loves shark diving more than she loves Scott.
Ok, now not so great for Scott, right?
Sentences must always be precise in their meanings, and we must be ever watchful for sentences that convey ambiguous meanings.
Incorrect: Joanna knows less about the Chilean culture than Rudy.
What is being compared? Does Joanna know less about the Chilean culture than she knows about Rudy, or does Joanna know less about the Chilean culture than Rudy knows? As written, the sentence does not clearly convey one or the other.
Joanna knows less about the Chilean culture than she knows about Rudy.
The above sentence expresses a comparison between Joanna’s knowledge of Chilean culture and her knowledge of Rudy.
Joanna knows less about the Chilean culture than Rudy knows.
Now, we are comparing Joanna’s knowledge of Chilean culture with Rudy’s knowledge of Chilean culture.
Incorrect: The foundation donated more money to the Gentle Barn Animal Sanctuary than other animal sanctuaries.
Notice the ambiguity? Did the foundation donate more money to the Gentle Barn Animal Sanctuary than to other animal sanctuaries? Or, did the foundation donate more money to the Gentle Barn Animal Sanctuary than did other animal sanctuaries? The sentence does not clearly indicate which it is meant to convey.
The foundation donated more money to the Gentle Barn Animal Sanctuary than to other animal sanctuaries.
Now it’s clear that the foundation gave the Gentle Barn more money than the foundation gave other animal sanctuaries.
The foundation donated more money to the Gentle Barn Animal Sanctuary than did other animal sanctuaries
Now it’s clear that the foundation gave money to the Gentle Barn, and so did other animal sanctuaries, but the foundation gave more than the other animal sanctuaries gave.
Incorrect: The current chief economist has been more focused on the company’s investment portfolio than the junior economists.
Notice that it’s hard to determine what is being compared.
The current chief economist has been more focused on the company’s investment portfolio than the junior economists have been.
Now, the sentence makes clear that what’s being compared is the degree of focus of the chief economist and the degree of focus of the junior economists.
The current chief economist has been focused more on the company’s investment portfolio than on the junior economists.
Now, the sentence makes clear that it’s expressing a comparison of the degree to which the chief economist is focused on the investment portfolio and the degree to which the chief economist is focused on the junior economists.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether the meaning conveyed by each of the following sentences is ambiguous or unambiguous. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s consider some Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 7 So extensive is the critic’s knowledge, so thorough is his research, and his reviews often delve as deeply into the subjects of the books he reviews as the books themselves. 1. [ ] (A) knowledge, so thorough is his research, and his reviews often delve as deeply into the subjects of the books he reviews as > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) knowledge and so thorough his research that his reviews often delve as deeply into the subjects of the books he reviews as do 3. [ ] (C) knowledge, his research is so thorough that his reviews often delve as deeply into the subjects of the books he reviews as > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) knowledge and so thorough is his research, and so, his reviews often delve as deeply into the subjects of the books he reviews as are > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) knowledge, and his research is so thorough, thus his reviews often delve deeply into the subjects of the books he reviews as > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium In order for the sentence to be effective, it has to clearly indicate with what how deeply the reviews delve into the subjects is being compared. Only one version clearly indicates that how deeply the reviews delve into the subjects is being compared with how deeply the books themselves delve into the subjects.
(A) So extensive is the critic’s knowledge, so thorough is his research, and his reviews often delve as deeply into the subjects of the books he reviews as the books themselves.
The wording of this version is such that the version could be interpreted as expressing either of the following comparisons:
His reviews often delve as deeply into the subjects of the books he reviews as the books themselves delve.
His reviews often delve as deeply into the subjects of the books he reviews as his reviews delve into the books themselves.
Neither “delve” nor “reviews delve into” is repeated in this version, and either could be understood to be repeated. So, the meaning conveyed is ambiguous.
Also, a clause like “so extensive is the critic’s knowledge” has to be followed by a clause that begins with “that …,” as in, “so extensive is the critic’s knowledge that x is the case,” but this version illogically puts “so extensive is the critic’s knowledge” and “so thorough is his research” into a list with a third clause, “his reviews often delve …,” rather than use “that” to logically connect the first two clauses to the third.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) So extensive is the critic’s knowledge and so thorough his research that his reviews often delve as deeply into the subjects of the books he reviews as do the books themselves.
By adding the verb “do,” which substitutes for “delve,” this version makes clear that the comparison made is between how deeply the reviews delve into the subjects and how deeply the books themselves “do.”
This version may sound a bit off because “is” is not used in “so thorough his research.” However, “is,” having appeared in “so extensive is his knowledge” is understood without being written to be repeated in “so thorough (is) his research.”
(C) So extensive is the critic’s knowledge, his research is so thorough that his reviews often delve as deeply into the subjects of the books he reviews as the books themselves.
The wording of this version is such that the version could be interpreted as expressing either of the following comparisons:
His reviews often delve as deeply into the subjects of the books he reviews as the books themselves delve.
His reviews often delve as deeply into the subjects of the books he reviews as his reviews delve into the books themselves.
Neither “delve” nor “reviews delve into” is repeated in this version, and either could be understood to be repeated. So, the meaning conveyed is ambiguous.
Also, the way “so extensive is the critic’s knowledge” is merely placed at the opening of the sentence and set off from the rest of the sentence by a comma without any connecting word leaves that clause unconnected to the rest of the sentence and without a clear purpose in the sentence.
(D) So extensive is the critic’s knowledge and so thorough is his research, and so, his reviews often delve as deeply into the subjects of the books he reviews as are the books themselves.
The use of “are” results in this version’s illogically comparing how deeply the reviews “delve” with how the books themselves “are.”
Also, a clause like “so extensive is the critic’s knowledge” has to be followed by a clause that begins with “that …,” as in, “so extensive is the critic’s knowledge that x is the case,” but this version illogically uses “and so” to connect “so extensive is the critic’s knowledge and so thorough is his research” to an independent third clause, “his reviews often delve …,” rather than use “that” to logically connect the first two clauses to the third.
(E) So extensive is the critic’s knowledge, and his research is so thorough, thus his reviews often delve deeply into the subjects of the books he reviews as the books themselves.
If we read this version carefully, we see that there is no “as” before “deeply.” Thus, this version does not express a comparison but, rather, conveys that “his reviews delve deeply … as the books,” as if to say something along the lines of that the reviews are acting “as the books.”
Also, a clause like “so extensive is the critic’s knowledge” or “his research is so thorough” has to be followed by a clause that begins with “that …,” as in, “so extensive is the critic’s knowledge that x is the case,” but this version illogically uses “thus” to connect “so extensive is the critic’s knowledge, and his research is so thorough” to an independent third clause, “his reviews often delve …,” rather than use “that” to logically connect the first two clauses to the third.
Also, since “thus” cannot alone serve as a conjunction that connects two clauses, the sentence is incorrectly composed of clauses connected by a comma without a conjunction and, thus, is a comma splice.
Also, ideally, “so extensive is the critic’s knowledge” and “his research is so thorough” would be structured in the same way, rather than slightly differently, as these two clauses are in this sentence, though the fact that they are not structured in the same way is not a very clear reason to eliminate this choice.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 8 A major reason for the loss of urban owls, hawks, and even herons is people’s using advanced rodenticides, which spread throughout the food chain and are just as lethal to predators as to rats and mice. 1. [x] (A) A major reason for the loss of urban owls, hawks, and even herons is people’s using advanced rodenticides, which spread throughout the food chain and are just as lethal to predators as to rats and mice. 2. [ ] (B) People using advanced rodenticides, which spread throughout the food chain and are not only lethal to rats and mice but also to predators, are major reasons for the loss of urban owls, hawks, and even herons. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Spreading throughout the food chain and just as lethal to predators as rats and mice, a major reason for the loss of urban owls, hawks, and even herons is people’s use of advanced rodenticides. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) People’s use of advanced rodenticides that have been spread throughout the food chain and are just as lethal to predators as rats and mice is a major reason for the loss of urban owls, hawks, and even herons. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) A major reason for the loss of urban owls, hawks, and even herons, people’s use of advanced rodenticides spreads throughout the food chain and is lethal, not just to rats and mice, but predators as well. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium Some versions of the sentence, including the correct version, include comparisons of how lethal the rodenticides are to predators with something else, but only the correct version clearly indicates what that something else is and expresses an unambiguous meaning. The other versions express comparisons that could be interpreted in either of the following two ways:
The rodenticides are just as lethal to predators as the rodenticides are to rats and mice.
The rodenticides are just as lethal to predators as rats and mice are to predators.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) A major reason for the loss of urban owls, hawks, and even herons is people’s using advanced rodenticides, which spread throughout the food chain and are just as lethal to predators as to rats and mice. Because this version uses “to” twice in “just as lethal to predators as to rats and mice,” this version clearly indicates that the comparison it expresses is between how lethal the rodenticides are “to predators” and how lethal the rodenticides are “to rats and mice.”
While the use of the possessive + gerund structure “people’s using” may sound a little funny, this version is grammatically correct and effectively expresses one logical meaning.
(B) People using advanced rodenticides, which spread throughout the food chain and are not only lethal to rats and mice but also to predators, are major reasons for the loss of urban owls, hawks, and even herons. The clearest issue in this version is that the “not only … but also” structure it contains is not parallel, as we can see by considering the following:
are (not only lethal to rats and mice but also to predators)
not only are lethal to rats and mice but also are to predators
We can see that “lethal” which appears after “not only” and, therefore, is not carried over to after “but also” is missing after “but also,” and, as a result the sentence says the illogical “are to predators,” leaving the reader to ask, “Are what to predators?”
Also, the meaning conveyed by the wording “people using advanced rodenticides … are the reasons” does not make complete sense. It’s really the people’s use of or people’s using the pesticides that is the reason for the loss of owls, etc., not the people themselves.
(C) Spreading throughout the food chain and just as lethal to predators as rats and mice, a major reason for the loss of urban owls, hawks, and even herons is people’s use of advanced rodenticides. The meaning conveyed by “just as lethal to predators as rats and mice” is not clear, as that wording could be interpreted as expressing either of the following:
just as lethal to predators as to rats and mice
just as lethal to predators as rats and mice are to predators
Also, since the opening modifier “spreading throughout the food chain …” is a noun modifier, the target of the modifier is the subject of the clause that follows the modifier, which subject is “a reason.” Since a reason would not be spreading throughout the food chain, this modification does not make sense.
(D) People’s use of advanced rodenticides that have been spread throughout the food chain and are just as lethal to predators as rats and mice is a major reason for the loss of urban owls, hawks, and even herons. The meaning conveyed by “just as lethal to predators as rats and mice” is not clear, as that wording could be interpreted as expressing either of the following:
just as lethal to predators as to rats and mice
just as lethal to predators as rats and mice are to predators
Also, “people’s use of advanced rodenticides that have been spread throughout the food chain” seems to convey the illogical meaning that people use the rodenticides after the rodenticides have been spread throughout the food chain by some unnamed entity.
(E) A major reason for the loss of urban owls, hawks, and even herons, people’s use of advanced rodenticides spreads throughout the food chain and is lethal, not just to rats and mice, but predators as well. This version conveys the illogical meaning that people’s use of rodenticides, rather than the rodenticides themselves, spreads throughout the food chain and is lethal.
Also, the “not just … but … as well” structure is not parallel, as we can see by considering the following:
lethal, (not just to rats and mice, but predators as well)
not just lethal to rats and mice, but lethal predators as well
We can see that, because “to” follows “not just,” “to” is not carried over to what follows “but,” and so, the sentence is understood to say “lethal predators,” which does not make sense in this context. “Lethal predators” is missing “to.’
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss incomplete comparisons.
summary:
Lesson Content As we have seen, omitting repeated words in a sentence that expresses a comparison is OK as long as the sentence still expresses its meaning effectively.
Consider the following sentence:
Incorrect: Alex is much nicer to me than Alfredo.
This sentence could be interpreted in two different ways.
In order for the sentence to be effective, the sentence has to include words that help clarify the meaning.
To convey the first meaning unambiguously, we can say:
title: 8.7 Ambiguous Comparisons

contents:
Lesson Content Sometimes a sentence leaves a comparison incomplete.
Let’s look at an example:
Incorrect: This tent is just as waterproof.
The meaning of this sentence is ambiguous because of an incomplete comparison.
This tent is just as waterproof as more expensive models.
The comparison becomes more complete via the addition of a noun to which the existing noun is compared.
Incorrect: This project will cost less.
Cost less than what? Cookies? A new swimming pool? A trip to Honolulu? This comparison is incomplete.
This project will cost less than the prior project.
Notice that now the comparison is clear; the sentence is comparing the cost of this project with the cost of the prior project.
💡MUST KNOW:
When a sentence expresses a comparison, if the sentence does not mention at least two items that are compared, the comparison expressed is not complete.
Now, probably, the GMAT won’t simply use a sentence like “This tent is just as waterproof,” and call it incorrect, because a sentence like that one is not strictly incorrect.
My brother prefers more expensive tents over this tent, because he believes the others to be more waterproof.
In the above context, the meaning of “this tent is just as waterproof,” is fairly clear, because of what the previous sentence says.
So, rather than call a simple incomplete comparison incorrect, what the GMAT would probably do is make a sentence that expresses an incomplete comparison rather clearly incorrect by having the expression of an incomplete comparison result in the sentence’s being clearly illogical or ineffective.
Consider the following example:
The researchers believed that they had finally developed a method for determining the composition of faraway planets, but the method turned out not to be as effective.
The above sentence clearly does not make sense, because it indicates that the method developed was the first of its kind, so there was no other such method with which to compare the method developed by the researchers.
One possible correct version is the following:
The researchers believed that they had finally developed a method for determining the composition of faraway planets, but the method turned out not to be as effective as they had hoped.
OK, now we have a complete comparison.
An alternative corrected version would change the comparison to a description, as in:
The researchers believed that they had finally developed a method for determining the composition of faraway planets, but the method turned out to be not very effective.
Now, the sentence is not expressing an incomplete comparison because it’s not expressing a comparison at all.
Let’s consider another example:
The amateur astronomers had expected to easily find Alpha Centauri in the night sky, but, because it is not very different, they didn’t find it until they used a star chart.
The sentence says that Alpha Centauri is “not very different,” but doesn’t say what Alpha Centauri is not very different from.
The amateur astronomers had expected to easily find Alpha Centauri in the night sky, but, because it is not very different from other stars, they didn’t find it until they used a star chart.
Let’s consider one more example:
While commuting by bike can be enjoyable, if it takes much longer, people may be resistant to traveling that way.
The sentence does not indicate what commuting by bike may take much longer than.
While commuting by bike can be enjoyable, if it takes much longer than commuting by train, people may be resistant to traveling that way.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s practice applying what we just learned in answering Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 9 Unlike the weather in the Hawaiian Islands, which tends to be significantly cooler during the winter months than the summer, the weather in Panama is consistently warmer. 1. [ ] (A) Unlike the weather in the Hawaiian Islands, which tends to be significantly cooler during the winter months than the summer, the weather in Panama is consistently warmer. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) The weather in Panama is consistently warmer than that of the Hawaiian Islands, which tend to be significantly cooler during the winter months than the summer months are. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) The weather in Panama is consistently warmer than it is in the Hawaiian Islands, where it tends to be significantly cooler during the winter months than it is during the summer months. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Whereas, during the winter months, the weather in the Hawaiian Islands becomes significantly cooler than they are during the summer, the weather in Panama is consistently warmer. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) In Panama, the weather is consistently warmer than that of the Hawaiian Islands, which tends to be significantly cooler during the winter months than in summer. View Solution Question Level: hard Correctly answering this question requires finding a version that expresses comparisons that are both complete and logical. One has also to avoid choosing a choice in which well matched sentence elements are used for the purpose of distracting the test-taker from the fact that the version does not effectively express a meaning that make sense.
(A) Unlike the weather in the Hawaiian Islands, which tends to be significantly cooler during the winter months than the summer, the weather in Panama is consistently warmer. The relative clause “which tends to be significantly cooler during the winter months than the summer” does not effectively express a comparison that makes sense. Since “during” is not repeated before “summer,” the clause seems to express that the weather, which “which” refers to, is cooler than the summer, a meaning that obviously does not make sense.
Also the comparison “unlike the weather in the Hawaiian islands … the weather in Panama is consistently warmer” does not make sense, because “the weather in Panama is consistently warmer” is an incomplete comparison, since the sentence does not indicate what the weather in Panama is warmer than.
(B) The weather in Panama is consistently warmer than that of the Hawaiian Islands, which tend to be significantly cooler during the winter months than the summer months are. The relative clause “which tend to be significantly cooler during the winter months than the summer months are” illogically compares the Hawaiian Islands, which “which” refers to, with the summer months.
(C) The weather in Panama is consistently warmer than it is in the Hawaiian Islands, where it tends to be significantly cooler during the winter months than it is during the summer months. “It” appears three times in this version and seems to refer to the weather of the Hawaiian Islands. However, this version never actually mentions the weather of the Hawaiian Islands, and the only possible referent of “it” is “the weather in Panama.” As a result, this version conveys the following nonsensical meaning:
The weather in Panama is consistently warmer than the weather in Panama is in the Hawaiian Islands, where the weather in Panama tends to be significantly cooler during the winter months than the weather in Panama is during the summer months.
Notice “it” is repeated in this incorrect version to make this version appear coherent and logical even though it isn’t. Similarly, “during the winter months” is paired with the matching “during the summer months” to add further reason for test-takers to misperceive this version to be well-constructed and logical.
(D) Whereas, during the winter months, the weather in the Hawaiian Islands becomes significantly cooler than they are during the summer, the weather in Panama is consistently warmer. Since the only logical referent of “they” is “the Hawaiian Islands,” this version illogically compares how the weather in the Hawaiian Islands becomes in winter with the Hawaiian Islands themselves, as we can see by substituting “Hawaiian Islands” for “they.”
the weather in the Hawaiian Islands becomes significantly cooler than the Hawaiian Islands are during the summer
Also, “the weather in Panama is consistently warmer” does not express a complete comparison, since it does not indicate what the weather in Panama is consistently warmer than.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) In Panama, the weather is consistently warmer than that of the Hawaiian Islands, which tends to be significantly cooler during the winter months than in summer. This version expresses a logical comparison of the weather in Panama and “that,” i.e., “the weather,” of the Hawaiian Islands. It also effectively describes the weather of the Hawaiian Islands as cooler during the winter months than it is in summer.
Notice that the wording of this version is chosen with the intent of raising doubt in the mind of test-takers that this version is correct. To that end, weather is connected with Panama via the use of “in,” while weather is connected with the Hawaiian Islands via the use of “of.” Also, “during the winter months” and “in summer,” while logically similar, are not perfectly matched.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 10 The upstart battery company asserts that, although its batteries use a technology never before applied by a battery company, its batteries last longer than others. 1. [x] (A) before applied by a battery company, its batteries last longer than others 2. [ ] (B) applied in batteries made by other companies, its batteries last longer > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) applied by any battery company, its batteries last longer than others do > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) before applied by battery companies, its batteries last longer > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) applied by another battery company, its batteries last longer than theirs do > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard Correctly answering this question requires not only noticing whether comparisons are complete and logical but also avoiding being distracted by the differences in comparisons into choosing a choice that is flawed in a way that does not involve a comparison.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) The upstart battery company asserts that, although its batteries use a technology never before applied by a battery company, its batteries last longer than others .
The comparison expressed by this version is complete, because it compares the upstart company’s batteries with others, i.e., other batteries.
Also, the technology is logically described as “technology never before applied by a battery company.”
(Note: The use of the singular “battery company” with the plural “others” is meant to give the test-taker the impression that this correct version of the sentence may not be logically constructed.”)
(B) The upstart battery company asserts that, although its batteries use a technology never applied in batteries made by other companies, its batteries last longer .
The comparison expressed by this version is incomplete, because this version does not indicate what the upstart company’s batteries “last longer” than.
(C) The upstart battery company asserts that, although its batteries use a technology never applied by any battery company, its batteries last longer than others do .
While the comparison expressed by this version is blatantly complete, we have to be careful not to be distracted by the obvious correctness of the comparison into choosing this choice, because this version illogically describes the technology used by the upstart company’s batteries as “never applied by any battery company.” If the upstart company’s batteries use this technology, then it has been applied by one company, and so, saying that it has never been applied does not make sense.
(D) The upstart battery company asserts that, although its batteries use a technology never before applied by battery companies, its batteries last longer .
The comparison expressed by this version is incomplete, because this version does not indicate what the upstart company’s batteries “last longer” than.
(E) The upstart battery company asserts that, although its batteries use a technology never applied by another battery company, its batteries last longer than theirs do .
In this version, since “another battery company” is singular, there is no logical referent for “theirs.”
Also, “never applied by another battery company” seems meant to express that there is a a specific other battery company that has not used the technology. “Never applied by any other battery company” more effectively expresses that no other battery company of all battery companies has used the technology.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss sentences that express comparisons that are incomplete even though the items meant to be compared are all mentioned.
summary:
Lesson Content Sometimes a sentence leaves a comparison incomplete.
Let’s look at an example:
Incorrect: This tent is just as waterproof.
The meaning of this sentence is ambiguous because of an incomplete comparison.
This tent is just as waterproof as more expensive models.
The comparison becomes more complete via the addition of a noun to which the existing noun is compared.
Incorrect: This project will cost less.
Cost less than what?
title: 8.8 Incomplete Comparisons

contents:
Lesson Content There is another move involving incomplete comparisons that the GMAT can make.
Incorrect: When it is compared with eating butter, eating olive oil is healthier.
Notice what’s going on in the above example.
Yes, the sentence says “compared with eating butter,” but “compared with eating butter” is part of a modifying clause, “when it is compared with eating butter,” that modifies “eating olive oil is healthier.”
So, read literally, the sentence conveys that eating olive oil is healthier than something else – the sentence does not say what else – at certain times.
So, even though the sentence mentions both olive oil and butter, strictly speaking, the comparison is not complete.
Eating olive oil is healthier than eating butter.
Here’s another example:
Incorrect: Although tigers can run fast, when compared with tigers, cheetahs run much faster.
In this case, we have a sentence that may seem to express a comparison of how fast cheetahs run with how fast tigers run, but what it actually conveys is that, at certain times, those times when cheetahs are compared with tigers, cheetahs run faster than something not mentioned by the sentence.
A correct version would directly and clearly compare how fast cheetahs run with how fast tigers run, as does the following:
Although tigers can run fast, cheetahs run much faster than tigers.
Let’s consider one more example:
Incorrect: The bones of birds, if compared with those of other animals, are much different.
The above example expresses the illogical meaning that the bones of birds are much different from something unmentioned, but only if the bones of birds are compared with those of other animals.
The bones of birds are much different from those of other animals.
💡MUST KNOW:
In analyzing sentences that involve comparisons, we have to be careful not to be fooled by sentences that mention the items meant to be compared yet don’t actually express complete comparisons of those items.
Of course, not every sentence that uses wording like “when compared” or “if compared” expresses an incomplete comparison.
The Torre Eurosky, the tallest building in Rome, towers over buildings around it, but when the Torre Eurosky is compared with the Freedom Tower in New York City, the Torre Eurosky may seem rather small.
In the above example, the use of the modifying clause “when the Torre Eurosky is compared with the Freedom Tower in New York City” makes sense, because the point of the sentence is that, at certain times, the times when the Torre Eurosky is compared with the Freedom Tower, the Torre Eurosky may seem rather small.
💡MUST KNOW:
Not every sentence that uses wording like “when compared” or “if compared” expresses an incomplete comparison. In some cases, sentences that use such wording are perfectly logical.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now consider some Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 11 Some people claim that healthcare executives are not as concerned about patients as are nurses. 1. [x] (A) healthcare executives are not as concerned about patients as are nurses 2. [ ] (B) when comparing healthcare executives with nurses, they’re not as concerned about patients > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) healthcare executives are not as concerned about patients as nurses > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) nurses are more concerned about patients than healthcare executives > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) nurses are more concerned about patients when they are compared with healthcare executives > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium CORRECT ANSWER(A) Some people claim that healthcare executives are not as concerned about patients as are nurses .
Although this version may sound a little awkward because of the way in which the second “are” is placed, the use of the second “are” results in the sentence’s clearly expressing a comparison of how concerned about patients healthcare executives are with how concerned nurses are.
(B) Some people claim that when comparing healthcare executives with nurses, they’re not as concerned about patients .
Whether “they” refers to “healthcare executives,” to “nurses,” or to some other people who are “comparing healthcare executives with nurses” is not clear.
Also, regardless of whom “they” refers to, the sentence conveys the illogical meaning that “they,” whoever they are, are not as concerned about patients at times when “they” are comparing healthcare executives with nurses.
Also, the comparison expressed by this version is incomplete, as this version does not indicate whom or what “they” are not as concerned as.
(C) Some people claim that healthcare executives are not as concerned about patients as nurses .
Because neither “are” nor “about” is repeated in this version, the meaning conveyed by this version is ambiguous, and could be either of the following:
Healthcare executives are not as concerned about patients as nurses are about patients.
Healthcare executives are not as concerned about patients as healthcare executives are about nurses.
(D) Some people claim that nurses are more concerned about patients than healthcare executives .
Because neither “are” nor “about” is repeated in this version, the meaning conveyed by this version is ambiguous, and could be either of the following:
Nurses are more concerned about patients than healthcare executives are.
Nurses are more concerned about patients than about healthcare executives.
(Note: This choice is not incorrect because it expresses a meaning slightly different from that expressed by the other choices. It’s incorrect solely because it does not effectively express one clear meaning.)
(E) Some people claim that nurses are more concerned about patients when they are compared with healthcare executives .
The comparison expressed by this version is incomplete, as, although both nurses and healthcare executives are mentioned, the sentence does not express a comparison of the people in the two categories. Rather it expresses the illogical meaning that nurses are more concerned about patients than someone or something not mentioned at times when “they,” whoever “they” may be, are compared with healthcare executives.
Of course, the fact that “they” could refer to “nurses” or “patients” is another issue had by this version.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 12 Similar to a cat, a fox’s tail helps them to balance when they are running, jumping or pouncing. 1. [ ] (A) Similar to a cat, a fox’s tail helps them to balance when they are > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) When compared with that of a cat, a fox’s tail is similar, helping the fox to balance when > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) A fox’s tail is similar to that of a cat, which helps the fox to balance when > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Like a cat’s tail, a fox’s tail is similar, and helps it to balance when > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) A fox’s tail, similar to that of a cat, helps it to balance when View Solution Question Level: hard In answering this question, we have to be careful not to choose a choice that seems to express a complete comparison but doesn’t really, even though it uses comparison type wording and mentions both items meant to be compared.
(A) Similar to a cat, a fox’s tail helps them to balance when they are running, jumping or pouncing.
This version illogically describes a fox’s tail as “similar to a cat.”
Also, the plural pronouns “them” and “they” have no logical referent, since the fox’s tail would not help both a cat and a fox to balance, and so, there is no plural noun or combination of nouns to which “them” and “they” would refer. “Them” and “they” seem to stand in for “foxes,” but the sentence is about the tail of a single fox, not the tails of multiple foxes.
(B) When compared with that of a cat, a fox’s tail is similar, helping the fox to balance when running, jumping or pouncing.
While this version mentions both “a fox’s tail” and “that of a cat,” it does not actually compare the two. Rather it conveys the illogical meaning that a fox’s tail is similar to something not mentioned, at a time when a fox’s tail is compared with the tail of a cat.
Also, while the use of the participial phrase “helping the fox to balance” may seem correct, especially since it uses “fox” rather than “it,” the pronoun that the correct answer, (E), uses, the truth is that, via the use of that participial phrase, the sentence conveys the nonsensical meaning that, by being similar (to something not mentioned), the fox’s tail helps the fox to balance, as we can see more easily by considering that part of the sentence in isolation:
a fox’s tail is similar, helping the fox to balance
(C) A fox’s tail is similar to that of a cat, which helps the fox to balance when running, jumping or pouncing.
The comparison expressed by this version makes sense, and so, this version is perfect up to the comma. However, the modifier after the comma has no logical target, and seems to modify “cat,” with the result that the sentence seems to convey the nonsensical meaning that a cat helps the fox to balance.
Even if we were to decide that “which helps the fox” modifies the entire preceding clause, a type of modification that the GMAT does not prefer, it would not really make sense to say that the fact that a fox’s tail is similar to that of a cat helps the fox to balance.
(D) Like a cat’s tail, a fox’s tail is similar, and helps it to balance when running, jumping or pouncing.
This version mentions both a cat’s tail and a fox’s tail, but is illogical, because it says that “a fox’s tail is similar” without indicating what a fox’s tail is similar to. It may seem to convey that a fox’s tail is similar to a cat’s tail, but actually conveys that a fox’s tail is like a cat’s tail in that both a cat’s tail and a fox’s tail are similar to something not mentioned. So, the comparison expressed by “a fox’s tail is similar” is incomplete.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) A fox’s tail, similar to that of a cat, helps it to balance when running, jumping or pouncing.
This version clearly conveys that a fox’s tail helps a fox to balance.
The use of the modifier “similar to that of a cat” is perhaps slightly awkward, but “that” clearly refers to “tail” and the modifier does clearly modify “a fox’s tail,” in a manner similar to the manner in which “red and shiny” modifies “cherry” in the following example:
A cherry, red and shiny, sat on the plate.
Also, it may seem that which noun or pronoun the pronoun “it” refers to is ambiguous, since “fox’s,” “tail,” “that,” and “cat” all precede “it” in the sentence. However, since “fox’s tail” is the clearly the subject of “helps,” and since the fox’s tail would help the fox, we can tell that “it” refers to “fox.”
Also, although, in this version, the referent for the personal pronoun “it” is the possessive “fox’s,” having a personal pronoun refer to the possessive form of a noun is considered acceptable in GMAT Sentence Correction.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss how to properly use “like,” “such as,” and “including.”
summary:
Lesson Content There is another move involving incomplete comparisons that the GMAT can make.
Incorrect: When it is compared with eating butter, eating olive oil is healthier.
Notice what’s going on in the above example.
Yes, the sentence says “compared with eating butter,” but “compared with eating butter” is part of a modifying clause, “when it is compared with eating butter,” that modifies “eating olive oil is healthier.”
So, read literally, the sentence conveys that eating olive oil is healthier than something else – the sentence does not say what else – at certain times.
title: 8.9 Sentences That Express Comparisons That Are Incomplete Even Though the Items Meant to Be Compared Are All Mentioned

contents:
Lesson Content The word “like” can be used in a comparison, but not to introduce an example.
To see the difference between the use of “like” and the use of “such as” demonstrated, consider the example sentences below:
Incorrect: When traveling through the Everglades, one may see many animals, like snakes, mammals, and alligators.
Since “like” is strictly a comparison word on the GMAT, using it in this context would imply that one may see, not the animals mentioned, but rather animals similar to, or “like,” snakes, mammals, or alligators.
To correct the sentence, we can replace the word “like” with “such as,” as demonstrated below:
When traveling through the Everglades, one may see many animals, such as snakes, mammals, and alligators.
We could also say:
When traveling through the Everglades, one may see many animals, including snakes, mammals, and alligators
In the above two examples, “such as” and “including” introduce lists of examples of animals that one may actually see.
Note that when “such as” is used to introduce a set of examples, it is often preceded by a comma, as it is in the following example:
The boy received for his birthday many toys, such as action figures, a soccer ball, and a board game, and his favorite among them was a tricky puzzle.
It would also be correct to say:
The boy received for his birthday many toys, including action figures, a soccer ball, and a board game, and his favorite among them was a tricky puzzle.
Also, a sentence can be correct with “such” and “as” separated, as they are in the following version:
The boy received for his birthday such toys as action figures, a soccer ball, and a board game, and his favorite among them was a tricky puzzle.
💡MUST KNOW:
The word “like” is strictly a comparison word and cannot be used to introduce examples. To introduce examples, we can use “such as.” We can also use “including” to introduce examples.
One thing that helps with understanding this difference is the following: we use “like” to compare two nouns that are similar, yet not the same.
Raspberries are like blueberries.
Wolves are like dogs.
Tigers are like house cats.
Diamonds are like cubic zirconias.
In other words, “like” is used in discussing a noun or nouns similar to another noun (the reference noun), but the reference noun is not in the set to which the other noun or nouns belong.
Of course, using “such as” in such contexts would not make sense, as it doesn’t in the following nonsensical examples.
Incorrect: Raspberries are such as blueberries.
Incorrect: Wolves are such as dogs.
In contrast, “such as” is used to introduce a set of examples that are in the set mentioned before the list of examples:
Consumption of fruits high in antioxidants, such as raspberries and blueberries, can be good for one’s health.
The above sentence expresses that raspberries and blueberries are in the set “fruits high in antioxidants.
Canines, such as wolves and dogs, are social animals.
The above sentence expresses that wolves and dogs are in the set “canines.”
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s practice applying what we just learned in answering Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 13 The young scouts, such as many scouts before them, built character and learned much via making the hundred mile trek along a section of the Appalachian Trail. 1. [ ] (A) such as many scouts > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) such as many scouts who were > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) like many scouts 4. [ ] (D) like many scouts, who had been > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) such as many scouts, who had been > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy The sentence is about scouts who are similar to or “like” scouts before them. So, in this case, using “like” is correct.
(A) The young scouts, such as many scouts before them, built character and learned much via making the hundred mile trek along a section of the Appalachian Trail.
The “many scouts before them” are not examples of “the young scouts.” The “many scouts before them” are different scouts, scouts before the young scouts. So, using “such as,” which is used for introducing examples, does not make sense.
(B) The young scouts, such as many scouts who were before them, built character and learned much via making the hundred mile trek along a section of the Appalachian Trail.
The “many scouts who were before them” are not examples of “the young scouts.” The “many scouts who were before them” are different scouts, scouts who were before the young scouts. So, using “such as,” which is used for introducing examples, does not make sense.
Also, since it would not be the case that the “many scouts” mentioned literally “were before” the young scouts when the young scouts were making the trek, the meaning conveyed by “the many scouts who were before them” does not make sense. Yes, “many scouts” had gone before the young scouts, but it is not really logically to say that the many scouts “were before” the young scouts. Were what before the young scouts?
CORRECT ANSWER(C) The young scouts, like many scouts before them, built character and learned much via making the hundred mile trek along a section of the Appalachian Trail.
Since the point to be made is that the young scouts were like many scouts before them, the use of “like” makes sense in this case.
Also, the simple wording “like many scouts before them” effectively compares the young scouts with the other scouts without the complicating present of a verb, unlike the wording seen in other choices, which includes verbs such as “were” or “had been,” the use of which results in the versions expressing meanings that are not quite logical.
(D) The young scouts, like many scouts, who had been before them, built character and learned much via making the hundred mile trek along a section of the Appalachian Trail.
Since the point to be made is that the young scouts were like many scouts before them, the use of “like” makes sense in this case.
However, because “who had been” is set off from the sentence by commas, “who had been” is nonrestrictive and, therefore, does not narrow down the “many scouts” to a certain group of scouts. Rather, it adds some nonessential information about “many scouts.” As a result, the logic of what the sentence expresses is a bit off, because the sentence does not clearly indicate that the many scouts mentioned are scouts other than the young scouts. It does not say “like many other scouts.” It does not say “like many scouts who had been before the young scouts,” without a comma between “scouts” and “who.” It simply says, “the young scouts, like many scouts … built character and learned.” Since “the young scouts” could be included in “many scouts,” the sentence could be read as comparing the young scout with themselves.
Also, the meaning of “who had been before them” is not clear. Had been what before them?
(E) The young scouts, such as many scouts, who had been before them, built character and learned much via making the hundred mile trek along a section of the Appalachian Trail.
The “many scouts” mentioned are not examples of “the young scouts.” The “many scouts” mentioned are other scouts. So, using “such as,” which is used for introducing examples, does not make sense.
Also, the meaning of “who had been before them” is not clear. Had been what before them?
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 14 Wheat protein, often referred to as gluten, can disrupt the sleep of those who are intolerant to it by triggering an immune response similar to pathogens, like viruses, yeast, and bacteria, all of which have the ability to disrupt sleep. 1. [ ] (A) disrupt the sleep of those who are intolerant to it by triggering an immune response similar to pathogens, like > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) be disruptive to the sleep of intolerant people because it can trigger an immune response, which, like the responses triggered by pathogens, such as > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) disrupt the sleep of those intolerant to gluten by triggering an immune response like pathogens do, including > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) be disruptive for the sleep of those intolerant to it by triggering an immune response similar to that of the responses triggered by pathogens, including > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) disrupt the sleep of those who are intolerant to it by triggering an immune response like the one triggered by pathogens, such as View Solution Question Level: medium (A) Wheat protein, often referred to as gluten, can disrupt the sleep of those who are intolerant to it by triggering an immune response similar to pathogens, like viruses, yeast, and bacteria, all of which have the ability to disrupt sleep.
This version illogically compares an immune response with pathogens.
Also, it incorrectly uses “like” to introduce “viruses, yeast, and bacteria.” The sentence is not structured to state that pathogens are like viruses, yeast, and bacteria. Rather, it is set up to express that “viruses, yeast, and bacteria” are examples of pathogens. Therefore, “viruses, yeast, and bacteria” should be introduced by “such as,” not by “like.”
(B) Wheat protein, often referred to as gluten, can be disruptive to the sleep of intolerant people because it can trigger an immune response, which, like the responses triggered by pathogens, such as viruses, yeast, and bacteria, all of which have the ability to disrupt sleep.
The use of the expression “intolerant people” in this context does not make sense, because the sentence does not clearly indicate that the people mentioned do not tolerate gluten. Rather it merely describes them as “intolerant,” which adjective, when used in isolation, is generally understood to mean “unwilling to tolerate certain ideas, beliefs, or cultures.” So, this version expresses the illogical idea that gluten disrupts the sleep of people unwilling to tolerate certain ideas, beliefs, or cultures because gluten triggers an immune response.
Also, the relative clause that begins with “which” has no main verb and does not express a complete thought. Which do what?
(C) Wheat protein, often referred to as gluten, can disrupt the sleep of those intolerant to gluten by triggering an immune response like pathogens do, including viruses, yeast, and bacteria, all of which have the ability to disrupt sleep.
This version incorrectly uses “like” to connect two clauses “wheat protein can disrupt … by triggering an immune response” and “pathogens do.” As discussed in the next section of this chapter, “as” rather than “like” should be used in this type of situation.
Also, this version illogically jumps from “pathogens do” to introducing a list of examples of pathogens. Since “including viruses, yeast, and bacteria” provides a list of examples of pathogens, it should follow directly after “pathogens,” not after “pathogens do.”
(D) Wheat protein, often referred to as gluten, can be disruptive for the sleep of those intolerant to it by triggering an immune response similar to that of the responses triggered by pathogens, including viruses, yeast, and bacteria, all of which have the ability to disrupt sleep.
The preposition “for” is misused. Gluten is not disruptive “for” sleep. The idiomatically correct and relatively logical expression to use in this context is “disruptive to sleep.”
Also, since “that” could only refer to “the immune response,” this version conveys the illogical meaning that gluten triggers an immune response similar to the immune response of the responses triggers by pathogens.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Wheat protein, often referred to as gluten, can disrupt the sleep of those who are intolerant to it by triggering an immune response like the one triggered by pathogens, such as viruses, yeast, and bacteria, all of which have the ability to disrupt sleep.
This version clearly indicates that gluten triggers an immune response in people intolerant to gluten and correctly uses “such as” in introducing a list of examples of pathogens.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss “like” and “as.”
summary:
Lesson Content The word “like” can be used in a comparison, but not to introduce an example.
To see the difference between the use of “like” and the use of “such as” demonstrated, consider the example sentences below:
Incorrect: When traveling through the Everglades, one may see many animals, like snakes, mammals, and alligators.
Since “like” is strictly a comparison word on the GMAT, using it in this context would imply that one may see, not the animals mentioned, but rather animals similar to, or “like,” snakes, mammals, or alligators.
title: 8.10 “Like” versus “Such As” and  “Including”

contents:
Lesson Content Just as people often misuse the words “like” and “such as,” they also often use the word “like” when the word “as” is needed.
Incorrect: The teacher acts like she were a drill sergeant.
While “as” is a versatile word that can be used as a preposition or a conjunction, the comparison word “like” can be used only as a preposition.
So, the above example does not make sense, since, in the above example, the object of “like” is a clause, “she were a drill sergeant.”
A corrected version is:
The teacher acts as though she were a drill sergeant.
Now, we could change things a bit and use the preposition “like” along with a noun that serves as the object of “like”:
The teacher acts like a drill sergeant.
Notice that, in the above example, the object of “like” is the noun phrase “a drill sergeant.” So, the use of “like” makes sense.
When “like” is used in the way in which it is used in the above example, “like” means, essentially, “in the manner of.” So, the above example expresses, “The teacher acts in the manner of a drill sergeant.”
Here’s another example in which “like” is used to convey “in the manner of”:
The normally shy student was acting like an extrovert.
In the above example, “an extrovert” is the object of the preposition “like,” and “like” conveys the meaning “in the manner of.” So, the sentence conveys the following meaning:
The normally shy student was acting in the manner of an extrovert.
We could also say:
The normally shy student’s actions were like an extrovert’s.
Notice that, in the above example, “like” is appropriately used to compare “actions,” a noun, with “an extrovert’s” actions.
The normally shy student’s actions were like those of an extrovert.
Here actions (of the shy student) are compared with actions (of an extrovert).
💡MUST KNOW:
The word “like” can be used only in comparisons of nouns or pronouns. It cannot be used to connect verbs or clauses. In a comparison involving two clauses, “as” must be used.
Let’s consider another example:
The judge is acting as though she would prefer to be somewhere else today.
Notice that, in the sentence above, “as though” is used to connect the two clauses.
We also could follow “as” with “if,” as seen here:
The judge is acting as if she would prefer to be somewhere else today.
What about the following?
Samantha and Maria, like their sister Natalia, are away at college.
Notice that we are comparing nouns (“Samantha,” “Maria,” and “Natalia”), so “like” is the proper comparison word.
Incorrect: Just like eating a vegan diet is good for the body, drinking organic coffee regularly is good for the mind.
This sentence is incorrect because “like” is used in comparing two clauses.
Just as eating a vegan diet is good for the body, drinking organic coffee regularly is good for the mind.
What about the following?
Incorrect: Cosmologist Stephen Hawking revolutionized our understanding of time and space, like Albert Einstein and Max Plank had before him.
Notice that the comparison involves two clauses: “Stephen Hawking revolutionized our understanding of time and space” and “Albert Einstein and Max Plank had before him.” Because two clauses are compared, we need to use “as.” A correct version is:
Cosmologist Stephen Hawking revolutionized our understanding of time and space, as Albert Einstein and Max Plank had before him.
A final thought for this section: it’s important to be aware that either of the following sentences would be considered correct on the GMAT:
Like his father, Amaury drives race cars.
As his father does, Amaury drives race cars.
We can directly compare Amaury with his father, or we can compare what they do.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s practice applying what we just discussed in answering Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 15 Having merely voted to repeal deleterious legislation that she had previously voted in favor of, the legislator was carrying on as if she were a savior of the people. 1. [x] (A) as if she were 2. [ ] (B) like she was > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) like she were > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) as though she was > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) as if she was > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard Since the sentence expresses a comparison of two ideas expressed by clauses, using “like” is incorrect and using “as though” or “as if” makes sense.
Also, since the sentence is meant to convey that the legislator’s being a savior of the people is not an event that may actually have occurred but rather a hypothetical situation, the use of the subjunctive “she were” makes sense. If you didn’t catch that the subjunctive “were” works better than the indicative “was,” you might want to review the discussion of the use of the subjunctive in the Verb Tense, Mood, and Voice chapter.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) Having merely voted to repeal deleterious legislation that she had previously voted in favor of, the legislator was carrying on as if she were a savior of the people.
This version correctly uses “as if” in expressing a comparison of two ideas expressed by two clauses.
Also, the use of the subjunctive “she were” to indicate that the legislator’s being a savior of the people is a hypothetical situation makes sense.
(B) Having merely voted to repeal deleterious legislation that she had previously voted in favor of, the legislator was carrying on like she was a savior of the people.
This version incorrectly uses “like” in expressing a comparison of two ideas expressed by two clauses, “she was carrying on” and “she was ….”
Also, the use of the indicative “she was” does not make sense, because the point to be made is not that the legislator may have actually been a savior of the people but, rather, that the legislator’s being a savior is a hypothetical situation, and so, the use of the subjunctive “were,” rather than the indicative “was,” is necessary.
(C) Having merely voted to repeal deleterious legislation that she had previously voted in favor of, the legislator was carrying on like she were a savior of the people.
This version incorrectly uses “like” in expressing a comparison of two ideas expressed by clauses, “she was carrying on” and “she was ….”
(D) Having merely voted to repeal deleterious legislation that she had previously voted in favor of, the legislator was carrying on as though she was a savior of the people.
While this version correctly uses “as though” in expressing a comparison of two ideas expressed by two clauses, the use of the indicative “she was” does not make sense, because the point to be made is not that the legislator may have actually been a savior of the people but, rather, that the legislator’s being a savior of the people is a hypothetical situation, and so, the use of the subjunctive “were,” rather than the indicative “was,” is necessary.
(E) Having merely voted to repeal deleterious legislation that she had previously voted in favor of, the legislator was carrying on as if she was a savior of the people.
While this version correctly uses “as if” in expressing a comparison of two ideas expressed by two clauses, the use of the indicative “she was” does not make sense, because the point to be made is not that the legislator may have actually been a savior of the people but, rather, that the legislator’s being a savior of the people is a hypothetical situation, and so, the use of the subjunctive “were,” rather than the indicative “was,” is necessary.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 16 The fourteen-year-old girl playing bass with the band that was playing in the park; she was slapping the strings as would a veteran with decades of training and experience. 1. [ ] (A) park; she was slapping the strings as would a veteran with decades of training and experience > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) park was slapping the strings like a veteran with decades of training and experience 3. [ ] (C) park, slapping the strings as if she were a veteran with decades of training and experience > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) park was slapping the strings like a veteran with decades of training and experience would > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) park was slapping the strings as a veteran with decades of training and experience > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard (A) The fourteen-year-old girl playing bass with the band that was playing in the park; she was slapping the strings as would a veteran with decades of training and experience .
Using “as” to connect the clause “she was slapping …” with the inverted clause “would a veteran …” is logical. However, this version is incorrect because it uses a semicolon to connect a phrase, “the fourteen-year-old girl playing …,” with a clause, “she was slapping ….” A semicolon can be used to connect a clause with a clause, but not to connect a phrase with a clause.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) The fourteen-year-old girl playing bass with the band that was playing in the park was slapping the strings like a veteran with decades of training and experience .
This version correctly uses “like” to convey that the fourteen-year-old girl was slapping the strings in the manner of a veteran.
(C) The fourteen-year-old girl playing bass with the band that was playing in the park, slapping the strings as if she were a veteran with decades of training and experience .
While using “as if” to connect a clause ending with “slapping the strings” with the clause “she were a veteran …” would make sense, since there is no independent clause before “as if,” this version is composed of merely “the fourteen-year-old girl” followed by an extended modifier, “playing the bass …,” and, therefore, is a fragment rather than a complete sentence.
(D) The fourteen-year-old girl playing bass with the band that was playing in the park was slapping the strings like a veteran with decades of training and experience would .
This version incorrectly uses “like” to connect a clause, “the fourteen-year-old girl … was slapping the strings,” with a clause, “a veteran … would.”
(E) The fourteen-year-old girl playing bass with the band that was playing in the park was slapping the strings as a veteran with decades of training and experience .
This version conveys the illogical meaning that, as a veteran with decades of training and experience, the fourteen-year-old girl was slapping the strings. A fourteen-year-old girl would not be doing something as a veteran with decades of experience, since she has not lived for decades.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss comparisons that involve quantity words and countable or uncountable nouns.
summary:
Lesson Content Just as people often misuse the words “like” and “such as,” they also often use the word “like” when the word “as” is needed.
Incorrect: The teacher acts like she were a drill sergeant.
While “as” is a versatile word that can be used as a preposition or a conjunction, the comparison word “like” can be used only as a preposition.
So, the above example does not make sense, since, in the above example, the object of “like” is a clause, “she were a drill sergeant.
title: 8.11 “Like” versus “As”

contents:
Lesson Content Some nouns are countable, meaning that they have plural forms and that their plural forms name quantities made up of countable items.
Other nouns are uncountable, meaning that quantities named by these nouns are not made up of countable items.
One reason why understanding the distinction between countable and uncountable nouns is useful in GMAT Sentence Correction is that the words used in expressing comparisons involving quantities, called “quantity words,” vary along with the types of quantities compared.
So, let’s discuss the use of quantity words in comparisons, starting with the quantity words “fewer” and “less.”
“Fewer” versus “Less”
“Fewer” modifies a countable noun, whereas “less” modifies an uncountable noun.
Incorrect: If you relax your face, you will have less wrinkles than you have now.
Notice that “wrinkles” is a countable noun.
If you relax your face, you will have fewer wrinkles than you have now.
“Fewer” is correctly used with the countable noun, “wrinkles.”
Incorrect: You have less apples than John has.
“Apples” is a countable noun, so we cannot use “less.”
You have fewer apples than John has.
We use “fewer” with the countable noun “apples.”
Incorrect: You have fewer juice than John has.
We see that “juice” is uncountable, so we cannot use “fewer.”
You have less juice than John has.
Now the sentence makes sense, with “less” used with “juice.”
What about the following more complicated sentence?
Incorrect: As she prepares for the marathon, Alessandra is consuming less pasta and cookies than she normally consumes.
Many people would see that sentence as correct.
A possible correction is:
As she prepares for the marathon, Alessandra is consuming less pasta and fewer cookies than she normally consumes.
Now, “less” is used with the uncountable noun “pasta,” and “fewer” is used with the countable noun “cookies.”
We must always be on the lookout for such small but important mistakes in Sentence Correction questions.
What about the following?
Incorrect: As a result of its waste reduction program, the restaurant now discards much less uneaten food and empty containers than it discarded in the past.
Notice that we have “much less” used with both “uneaten food” and “empty containers.” We see that “uneaten food” is uncountable, so using “much less” with “uneaten food” is perfect.
A corrected sentence is:
As a result of its waste reduction program, the restaurant now discards much less uneaten food and far fewer empty containers than it discarded in the past.
Let’s look at one more example.
Incorrect: With each passing year, the minimum wage provides families with less security and opportunity, a trend that may result in less educational and economic opportunities for their children later in life.
Pay close attention to the words in the sentence above.
With each passing year, the minimum wage provides families with less security and opportunity, a trend that may result in fewer educational and economic opportunities for their children later in life.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now do some Concept Mastery.
Let’s consider a Sentence Correction example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 17 Because of changing tastes, in recent years, the bakery has sold fewer pies and cakes than it sold in the past, a development negatively affecting profits. 1. [x] (A) pies and cakes than it sold in the past, a development negatively affecting profits 2. [ ] (B) pies and cakes than they had sold in the past, negatively affecting profits as a development > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) pie and cake than it had sold in the past, which has been a development negatively affecting profits > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) pies and cakes than were sold in the past, and it has negatively affected profits as a development > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) pie and cake than was sold in the past, which had been a development negatively affecting profits > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy CORRECT ANSWER(A) Because of changing tastes, in recent years, the bakery has sold fewer pies and cakes than it sold in the past, a development negatively affecting profits .
“Fewer” is correctly used to modify the countable nouns “pies” and “cakes,” the singular “it” correctly refers to the singular “bakery,” and the closing abstract appositive “a development …” logically modifies the entire preceding clause.
(B) Because of changing tastes, in recent years, the bakery has sold fewer pies and cakes than they had sold in the past, negatively affecting profits as a development .
The plural “they” does not agree with what it seems to refer to, which is the singular “bakery.”
Also, the use of the past perfect “had sold,” while not 100 percent incorrect, does not make sense, since the phrase “in the past” clearly indicates the sequence of events, making the use of the past perfect unnecessary and somewhat illogical. The point to be made is not that bakery “had sold” more pies and cakes in the past. The point to be made is that the bakery “sold” more pies and cakes in the past.
Also, the expression “negatively affecting profits as a development” implies that either the bakery or the profits were a development, neither of which implications makes sense.
(C) Because of changing tastes, in recent years, the bakery has sold fewer pie and cake than it had sold in the past, which has been a development negatively affecting profits .
The combination of the non-underlined portion of the sentence with this answer choice creates “the bakery has sold fewer pie and cake.” Since “pie” and “cake” are uncountable nouns, the use of “fewer” to modify these nouns is incorrect.
Also, the use of the past perfect “had sold,” while not 100 percent incorrect, does not make sense, since the phrase “in the past” clearly indicates the sequence of events, making the use of the past perfect unnecessary and somewhat illogical. The point to be made is not that bakery “had sold” more pies and cakes in the past. The point to be made is that the bakery “sold” more pies and cakes in the past.
Also, the only possible noun to which “which” could refer is “past,” but the past would not be a development negatively affecting profits. Alternatively, “which” could refer to the entire idea expressed by the preceding clause, but the GMAT does not prefer this type of reference, especially if there is a noun that directly precedes “which” to which “which” could refer, as “past” does in this case.
