(D) Because of changing tastes, in recent years, the bakery has sold fewer pies and cakes than were sold in the past, and it has negatively affected profits as a development .
The wording “fewer pies and cakes than were sold in the past” does not clearly connect the pies and cakes mentioned to the bakery mentioned. Pies and cakes that “were sold in the past” could have been sold by any bakery, not just the one that is the subject of the sentence.
Also, “it” has no logical referent. While “it” could refer to “bakery,” that the bakery would have “negatively affected profits” by selling fewer pies and cakes does not quite make sense. Also, if “it” refers to “bakery,” then the sentence conveys the nonsensical meaning that the bakery negatively affected profits “as a development,” meaning that the bakery was “a development.”
(E) Because of changing tastes, in recent years, the bakery has sold fewer pie and cake than was sold in the past, which had been a development negatively affecting profits .
The combination of the non-underlined portion of the sentence with this answer choice creates “the bakery has sold fewer pie and cake.” Since “pie” and “cake” are uncountable nouns, the use of “fewer” to modify these nouns is incorrect.
Also, the only possible noun to which “which” could refer is “past,” but the past would not be a development negatively affecting profits. Alternatively, “which” could refer to the entire idea expressed by the preceding clause, but the GMAT does not prefer this type of reference, especially if there is a noun that directly precedes “which” to which “which” could refer, as “past” does in this case.
Also, the use of the past perfect “had been a development” does not make sense, since the use of the past perfect indicates that the event described occurred before other past events mentioned in the sentence, a sequence of events that conflicts with what the rest of the sentence expresses, which is that the development described is ongoing in the present.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss “many” and “much.”
“Many” versus “Much”
Although “many” and “much” both modify quantities, we use “much” with uncountable nouns and “many” with countable nouns.
For example:
She has much love in her heart.
How much time is remaining in the movie?
Sam has much more interest in mountain climbing than Sandeep has.
Since “love,” “time,” and “interest” are uncountable nouns, we use “much” when modifying them.
There are many ways to get to Moscow from Seoul.
How many countries have you visited?
Although elephants were once common in Africa, there aren’t many left.
Since, “ways,” “countries,” and “elephants” are countable nouns, we use “many” to modify or refer to them.
💡MUST KNOW:
Use “much” with uncountable nouns and “many” with countable nouns.
Consider the following more difficult example sentence:
Incorrect: The high waters caused by the storm had deposited so much sand and sea shells on the property that the house appeared to be standing in the middle of a beach.
Notice that we have “so much” used with “sand” and with “sea shells.” Using “so much” with “sand” is fine because “sand” is uncountable.
The high waters caused by the storm had deposited so much sand and so many sea shells on the property that the house appeared to be standing in the middle of a beach.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now consider a Sentence Correction example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 18 The campaign director estimated that over 3000 people were in attendance at the rally, which was many more than they had expected. 1. [ ] (A) which was many more than they had > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) much more than was > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) many more as > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) many more than had been 5. [ ] (E) much more people than had been > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium Since the sentence is about 3000 people, which is a countable quantity, the correct word to use is “many.”
(A) The campaign director estimated that over 3000 people were in attendance at the rally, which was many more than they had expected.
While this version correctly uses “many,” “which” has no logical referent, since a rally would not be “many more than they had expected.”
Also, “they” has no referent within the sentence, though that it does not is not a strong reason to eliminate this choice, because “they” could refer to people mentioned in a sentence previous to this one, unlike “which,” which must refer to something mentioned within the sentence in which “which” appears.
(B) The campaign director estimated that over 3000 people were in attendance at the rally, much more than was expected.
“Much” is incorrectly used to refer to the countable “3000 people.”
Also, the simple past “was expected” is not as good as the past perfect “had been expected,” because, presumably, the people were expected to attend before the campaign director estimated how many attended. So, since the campaign director “estimated” in the past, it makes sense to use the past perfect “had been expected” to indicate that the people were expected to attend before the campaign director estimated how many attended.
(C) The campaign director estimated that over 3000 people were in attendance at the rally, many more as expected.
Because this version uses “as” rather than “than” with “many more,” the comparison expressed by this version is not complete, leaving the reader wondering “Many more than what?”
CORRECT ANSWER(D) The campaign director estimated that over 3000 people were in attendance at the rally, many more than had been expected.
This version correctly uses “many” to refer to the countable “3000 people.”
Also, it uses the past perfect “had been expected” along with the simple past “estimated” to indicate that the people were expected before the campaign director estimated.
(E) The campaign director estimated that over 3000 people were in attendance at the rally, much more people than had been expected.
This choice could be tempting because the repetition of the word “people” in the final phrase of the sentence could appear to add clarity. However, the repetition of “people” is unnecessary, because “many more” is understood to mean “many more people,” and, meanwhile, the use of “much” with the countable “people” is incorrect.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss “number” and “amount.”
summary:
Lesson Content Some nouns are countable, meaning that they have plural forms and that their plural forms name quantities made up of countable items.
Other nouns are uncountable, meaning that quantities named by these nouns are not made up of countable items.
One reason why understanding the distinction between countable and uncountable nouns is useful in GMAT Sentence Correction is that the words used in expressing comparisons involving quantities, called “quantity words,” vary along with the types of quantities compared.
title: 8.12 Comparisons That Involve Quantity Words and Countable or Uncountable Nouns

contents:
Lesson Content We use “number” with countable nouns and “amount” with uncountable nouns.
For example:
The number of species worldwide is declining.
Notice that “species” is a countable noun.
The number of people at the beach was amazing.
Of course, “people” is a countable noun.
The amount of enthusiasm on display at the candidate’s rally was shocking.
“Enthusiasm” can’t be counted.
The amount of water in the beaker is not sufficient for this experiment.
“Water” can’t be counted.
💡MUST KNOW:
We use “number” with countable nouns, and we use “amount” with uncountable nouns.
Of course, the words number and amount are often used in sentences that express comparisons.
Incorrect: The amount of seals that inhabit Cape Cod is now greater than it was for decades.
The sentence incorrectly uses “amount” with the countable “seals.” “Number” must be used with countable nouns, as in:
The number of seals that inhabit Cape Cod is now greater than it was for decades.
Now, whenever these words are used, in addition to considering whether “number” or “amount” is used correctly, we must also check to see whether comparisons made are logical.
Incorrect: Currently, the number of cars on the road using electric power is far greater than five years ago.
Notice that this sentence illogically attempts to compare “the number of cars on the road using electric power” with “five years ago.” A corrected version is:
Currently, the number of cars on the road using electric power is far greater than it was five years ago.
Of course, the following is also incorrect:
Incorrect: Currently, the number of cars on the road using electric power is far greater than they were five years ago.
Notice that this sentence illogically attempts to compare “the number of cars on the road today using electric power” with “they were five years ago.” What does the “they” represent? Certainly not “the number.”
Let’s do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s consider some Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 19 Because, in rural areas, there is less ambient light than there is in urban areas, the amount of stars that one can see with the naked eye is greater in rural areas than in urban areas. 1. [ ] (A) amount of stars that one can see with the naked eye is greater in rural areas than > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) number of stars that one can see with the naked eye in rural areas is greater than it is > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) amounts of stars that one can see with the naked eye are greater in rural areas than they are > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) number of stars that one can see with the naked eye is greater in rural areas than it is 5. [ ] (E) amount of stars that one can see with the naked eye is greater in rural areas than that > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium (A) Because, in rural areas, there is less ambient light than there is in urban areas, the amount of stars that one can see with the naked eye is greater in rural areas than in urban areas.
This version is correct except in that it incorrectly uses “amount” with the countable “stars.”
(B) Because, in rural areas, there is less ambient light than there is in urban areas, the number of stars that one can see with the naked eye in rural areas is greater than it is in urban areas.
Since “it” would logically refer to “the number of stars that one can see with the naked eye in rural areas,” this version expresses the illogical idea that the number of stars that one can see with the naked eye in rural areas is greater than the number of stars that one can see with the naked eye in rural areas in urban areas, as we can clearly see by substituting “the number of stars that one can see with the naked eye in rural areas” for “it” in the sentence.
… the number of stars that one can see with the naked eye in rural areas is greater than the number of stars that one can see with the naked eye in rural areas is in urban areas.
(C) Because, in rural areas, there is less ambient light than there is in urban areas, the amounts of stars that one can see with the naked eye are greater in rural areas than they are in urban areas.
Even though “amounts” is plural, it still is not correct to use “amounts” with the countable stars.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) Because, in rural areas, there is less ambient light than there is in urban areas, the number of stars that one can see with the naked eye is greater in rural areas than it is in urban areas.
This version correctly uses “number” with the countable “stars.”
Also, this version expresses the logical idea that a quantity, the number of stars that one can see with the naked eye, is greater in rural areas than “it,” meaning that quantity, is in urban areas.
(E) Because, in rural areas, there is less ambient light than there is in urban areas, the amount of stars that one can see with the naked eye is greater in rural areas than that in urban areas.
This version incorrectly uses “amount” with the countable “stars.”
Also, there is no logical referent for “that.” The sentence does not mention something “in rural areas” that could be logically compared with “that in urban areas.” Rather, it mentions “the amount of stars that can be seen with the naked eye,” and then says that this quantity is greater in rural areas than “that in urban areas.” That what? To more clearly see the issue, compare this choice with choice (D).
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 20 To decorate the tables at the event, the caterers used an array of decoratively carved fruits, like kiwis, grapefruit, and melons, along with a large amount of chocolates in shapes such as stars, hearts, and flowers. 1. [ ] (A) To decorate the tables at the event, the caterers used an array of decoratively carved fruits, like kiwis, grapefruit, and melons, along with a large amount of chocolates in shapes such as stars, hearts, and flowers. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Decorating the tables at the event, an array of decoratively carved fruits, including kiwis, grapefruit, and melons, along with a large number of chocolates in shapes like stars, hearts, and flowers, used by the caterers. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Used by the caterers to decorate the tables at the event was an array of decoratively carved fruits, such as kiwis, grapefruit, and melons, along with a large amount of chocolate in shapes such as stars, hearts, and flowers. 4. [ ] (D) To decorate the tables at the event, an array of decoratively carved fruit, including kiwis, grapefruit, and melons, along with a large number of chocolates in shapes such as stars, hearts, and flowers, were used by the caterers. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) By using an array of decoratively carved fruits, like kiwis, grapefruit, and melons, along with a large amount of chocolate in shapes such as stars, hearts, and flowers, the tables at the event were decorated by the caterers. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard (A) To decorate the tables at the event, the caterers used an array of decoratively carved fruits, like kiwis, grapefruit, and melons, along with a large amount of chocolates in shapes such as stars, hearts, and flowers. This version incorrectly uses “like” in introducing a list of examples of fruits.
Also, this version incorrectly uses “amount” with the countable “chocolates.”
(B) Decorating the tables at the event, an array of decoratively carved fruits, including kiwis, grapefruit, and melons, along with a large number of chocolates in shapes like stars, hearts, and flowers, used by the caterers. This version incorrectly uses “like” in introducing a list of examples of shapes.
Also, this version includes no main verb, only nouns and modifiers, and, therefore, is not a complete sentence.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Used by the caterers to decorate the tables at the event was an array of decoratively carved fruits, such as kiwis, grapefruit, and melons, along with a large amount of chocolate in shapes such as stars, hearts, and flowers. This version may “sound funny” because it is an inverted sentence in which the subject, “an array,” follows the main verb, “was.” All the same, this version is correct, correctly using “such as” in introducing lists of examples of fruits and shapes and correctly using “amount” with the uncountable “chocolate.”
Notice that, since “along with a large amount of chocolate …” is an additive phrase, the subject of the sentence can be correctly presumed to be the singular “array,” which agrees with the singular “was.”
(D) To decorate the tables at the event, an array of decoratively carved fruit, including kiwis, grapefruit, and melons, along with a large number of chocolates in shapes such as stars, hearts, and flowers, were used by the caterers. This version suggests the illogical idea that an array of fruits had the intention of decorating the tables at the event and, therefore, was used by the caterers.
Also, the subject of the sentence is the singular “an array of decoratively carved fruit, which does not agree with the plural “were.” “Along with a large number of chocolates … is an additive phrase, and, therefore, its presence does not render the singular subject plural.
(E) By using an array of decoratively carved fruits, like kiwis, grapefruit, and melons, along with a large amount of chocolate in shapes such as stars, hearts, and flowers, the tables at the event were decorated by the caterers. Since the opening modifier, “by using an array …” must modify the subject of the clause that follows, which is “the tables,” this version expresses the nonsensical meaning that the tables at the event used an array of fruit and a large amount of chocolate in order to be decorated by the caterers.
Also, this version incorrectly uses “like” in introducing a list of examples of fruits.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now talk about quantities of time, money, or weight.
summary:
Lesson Content We use “number” with countable nouns and “amount” with uncountable nouns.
For example:
The number of species worldwide is declining.
Notice that “species” is a countable noun.
The number of people at the beach was amazing.
Of course, “people” is a countable noun.
The amount of enthusiasm on display at the candidate’s rally was shocking.
“Enthusiasm” can’t be counted.
The amount of water in the beaker is not sufficient for this experiment.
title: 8.13 “Number” versus “Amount”

contents:
Lesson Content Time, money, and weight may seem to be countable.
For instance, we know that we would use “less” with “water,” as does the following example:
The tank currently contains less than two gallons of water.
Money works similarly, as we can see in the following example:
The account currently holds less than two dollars.
Time works similarly, as we can see in the following example:
The sun will set less than five hours from now.
One would not say:
Incorrect: The sun will set fewer than five hours from now.
One would say:
Ten minutes is generally too much time to spend on one quant question when one is taking the GMAT.
One would not say:
Incorrect: Ten minutes are generally too many time to spend on one quant question when one is taking the GMAT.
Weight is also uncountable, and so, both of the following are correct:
Even though Michael consumes over 4000 calories per day, because he is so active, he has gained less than five pounds.
Eight kilograms is not much more than the weight of an average sized cat.
💡MUST KNOW:
Nouns that name quantities of time, money, or weight are treated as uncountable nouns.
Now, if a sentence is about the units of time, money, or weight themselves, the nouns are countable.
How many minutes are left?
The above sentence is correct because it’s about the number of minutes themselves.
Incorrect: How much minutes is left?
Similarly if a sentence is about units of money themselves, the noun naming the units is a countable noun, as in the following example:
Many Euros have been spent on the repairs of the classic cathedral.
The above sentence is correct because it’s about the number of Euros themselves.
Incorrect: Much Euros has been spent on the repairs of the classic cathedral.
On the other hand, the following, which is about a quantity of money rather than about the units of money themselves is correct:
Some people felt that 2 billion Euros was too much money to spend on rebuilding a cathedral.
We can see that the above sentence is not about the Euros themselves, but about a quantity of money, which is considered uncountable.
The following is correct as well:
Some people felt that 2 billion Euros was too much to spend on rebuilding a cathedral.
Even though the word “money” is not included in the above version, the sentence is understood to convey that 2 billion Euros is too much money to spend.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s consider some Sentence Correction examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 21 Weighing fewer than 100 pounds though armed with confidence, Jerrie Mock became the first woman flying around the world solo and returning to her hometown, Columbus Ohio, fewer than 30 days after she had departed. 1. [ ] (A) fewer than 100 pounds though armed with confidence, Jerrie Mock became the first woman flying around the world solo and returning to her hometown, Columbus Ohio, fewer than 30 days after she had > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) less than 100 pounds, Jerrie Mock was armed with confidence and became the first woman flying around the world solo, returning to her hometown, Columbus Ohio, fewer than 30 days after she > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) less than 100 pounds but armed with confidence, Jerrie Mock became the first woman to fly around the world solo, by returning to her hometown, Columbus Ohio, in less than 30 days after she had > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) fewer than 100 pounds though armed with confidence, Jerrie Mock became the first woman to fly around the world solo and return to her hometown, Columbus Ohio, by fewer than 30 days after she > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) less than 100 pounds but armed with confidence, Jerrie Mock became the first woman to fly around the world solo, returning to her hometown, Columbus Ohio, less than 30 days after she View Solution Question Level: hard Since weight and time are treated as if they are uncountable, any version of this sentence that uses “fewer” with “100 pounds,” which expresses Jerrie Mock’s weight, or uses “fewer” with “30 days,” which expresses a quantity of time, is probably incorrect. Further, and more importantly, any version that uses “less” with “100 pounds” or with “30 days” is certainly not incorrect because it uses “less.”
(A) Weighing fewer than 100 pounds though armed with confidence, Jerrie Mock became the first woman flying around the world solo and returning to her hometown, Columbus Ohio, fewer than 30 days after she had departed.
The use of “fewer” with “100 pounds” and with “30 days” does not make sense, because, in this sentence, “100 pounds” and “30 days” are used to express quantities of weight and time, both of which are considered uncountable. “Less” should be used instead.
Also, this version suggests the illogical idea that Jerrie Mock weighed less than 100 pounds even though she was armed with confidence, as if being armed with confidence would normally cause someone to weigh more than 100 pounds.
Also, the use of the continuous “flying” and “returning” results in the sentence’s conveying the illogical meaning that Jerry Mock became the first woman continuously flying around the world solo and returning to Jerry Mock’s hometown, as if she did so on an ongoing or nonstop basis.
Also, the use of the past perfect “had departed” is not necessary and somewhat illogical, because the sequence of events is indicated via the use of “after.” So, the use of the simple past “departed” would make more sense.
(B) Weighing less than 100 pounds, Jerrie Mock was armed with confidence and became the first woman flying around the world solo, returning to her hometown, Columbus Ohio, fewer than 30 days after she departed.
The use of “fewer” with “30 days” does not make sense, because, in this sentence, “30 days” is used to express a quantity of time, which is considered uncountable. “Less” should be used instead.
Also, this version seems to suggest that Jerrie Mock’s weighing less than 100 pounds is somehow connected to her being armed with confidence and becoming the first woman “flying around the world solo,” perhaps expressing that Jerry Mock was armed with confidence and flew around the world solo as a result of her weighing less than 100 pounds.
Also, the use of the continuous “flying” results in the sentence’s expressing the illogical meaning that Jerry Mock became the first woman continuously flying around the world solo, as if she did so on an ongoing or nonstop basis. This meaning is in conflict with the fact stated by the sentence that she returned to her hometown after 30 days, a fact that indicates that actually she flew around the world just once, not continuously.
(C) Weighing less than 100 pounds but armed with confidence, Jerrie Mock became the first woman to fly around the world solo, by returning to her hometown, Columbus Ohio, in less than 30 days after she had departed.
This version conveys the nonsensical meaning that by merely returning to her hometown, Jerrie Mock became the first woman to fly around the world solo.
Also, “in less than 30 days after she had departed” does not make sense. This modifier should be either “in less than 30 days,” which would express how long she took, or “less than 30 days after she had departed,” which would express when she returned. As written, the modifier seems to attempt to express both of those meanings at the same time, with the result that does not express any logical meaning.
Also, the use of the past perfect “had departed” is not necessary and somewhat illogical, because the sequence of events is indicated via the use of “after.” So, the use of the simple past “departed” would make more sense.
(D) Weighing fewer than 100 pounds though armed with confidence, Jerrie Mock became the first woman to fly around the world solo and return to her hometown, Columbus Ohio, by fewer than 30 days after she departed.
The use of “fewer” with “100 pounds” and with “30 days” does not make sense, because, in this sentence, “100 pounds” and “30 days” are used to express quantities of weight and time, both of which are considered uncountable. “Less” should be used instead.
Also, this version suggests the illogical idea that Jerrie Mock weighed less than 100 pounds even though she was armed with confidence, as if being armed with confidence would normally cause someone to weigh more than 100 pounds.
Also, this version suggests the illogical idea that the point to be made is that Jerrie Mock was not merely the first woman to fly around the world solo, but rather the first woman to fly around the world solo and return to Jerry Mock’s hometown “fewer than” 30 days after she departed. This meaning seems unlikely to be the one meant to be expressed by the author.
Also, the use of “by” is somewhat illogical. “By” is used to indicate a point in time, but the point to be made in this case is not that Jerry Mock returned to her hometown by a point in time. The point to be made is that she returned to her hometown within a period of time “fewer than” 30 days.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Weighing less than 100 pounds but armed with confidence, Jerrie Mock became the first woman to fly around the world solo, returning to her hometown, Columbus Ohio, less than 30 days after she departed.
This version correctly uses “less” with “100 pounds,” which expresses a weight, and with “30 days,” which expresses a quantity of time.
Also, “weighing less than 100 pounds but armed with confidence” expresses a logical contrast between Jerrie Mock’s weight and her level of confidence.
Also, this version clearly expresses that Jerrie Mock became the first woman to fly around the world solo, and that, in the process of doing so, she returned to her hometown less than 30 days after she left.
Notice that, even though this version uses the simple past “she departed” rather than the past perfect “she had departed,” the sequence of events is made clear via the use of “after.”
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 22 Unfortunately, a decline in the quality of people’s self care has resulted in many more dollars spent by them on healthcare than were spent in past years. 1. [ ] (A) many more dollars spent by them on healthcare than were > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) they spent much more dollars on healthcare than they > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) much more dollars that people spent on healthcare than were > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) people’s spending on healthcare many more dollars than they 5. [ ] (E) much more dollars being spent on healthcare than was > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: hard While “money” is an uncountable noun, in this case, the sentence is about dollars themselves, which are countable. So, the correct word to use is not “much” but “many.”
(A) Unfortunately, a decline in the quality of people’s self care has resulted in many more dollars spent by them on healthcare than were spent in past years.
While this version correctly uses “many” with the countable “dollars,” the meaning conveyed is not logical. The sentence conveys not that a decline in the quality of self care resulted in an increase in the number of dollars spent on healthcare but rather that the decline resulted in many more of the dollars spent on healthcare. Then, the dollars that somehow resulted from the decline are illogically compared with dollars that were spent in past years. It’s actually a bit challenging to say what this version conveys, because, while this version may “sound correct,” it’s actually a virtually meaningless jumble of words.
(B) Unfortunately, a decline in the quality of people’s self care has resulted in they spent much more dollars on healthcare than they spent in past years.
This version incorrectly uses “much” with the countable “dollars.”
Also, while “resulted in” should be followed by an object of the preposition “in,” which object has to be a noun or pronoun or something that acts as a noun or pronoun does, in this case “resulted in” is illogically followed by a clause, “they spent ….”
Of course, since unlike the virtually meaningless versions (A) and (C), this version does express a logical comparison, we have to be careful not to be tempted into choosing this version even though it has the flaws described above.
(C) Unfortunately, a decline in the quality of people’s self care has resulted in much more dollars that people spent on healthcare than were spent in past years.
This version incorrectly uses “much” with the countable “dollars.”
Also, like the version created via the use of (A), this version conveys the nonsensical meaning, that a decline in the quality of people’s self care resulted in dollars. Also the latter part of this version appears to express a comparison but, actually, is a virtually meaningless jumble of comparison words.
Notice also that the use of the plural “were,” the use of which implies that “dollars” is plural and countable, is in conflict with the use of “much,” the use of which implies that “dollars” is a single uncountable entity.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) Unfortunately, a decline in the quality of people’s self care has resulted in people’s spending on healthcare many more dollars than they spent in past years.
This version correctly uses “many” with the countable “dollars.
Also, in this version, “resulted in” is followed by something that can logically serve as the object of “in”: “people’s spending.”
Also, unlike the versions created via the use of (A) and (C), this version expresses a logical comparison, “many more dollars than they spent in past years.”
Of course, for the purpose of making the question challenging, this correct version is written in a way such that this version may at first “sound” less correct than other versions.
(While many people feel that a personal pronoun, such as “they,” should not have as its referent a noun in the possessive form, such as “people’s,” in GMAT Sentence Correction questions, such references have appeared in correct answers, as “they” referring to “people’s” does in this correct answer.)
(E) Unfortunately, a decline in the quality of people’s self care has resulted in much more dollars being spent on healthcare than was spent in past years.
This version’s use of “much” and “was” with the countable and plural “dollars” is clearly incorrect.
Also, while, unlike the versions created via the use of (A) and (C), this version does a halfway decent job of expressing a logical meaning, it still could be read as conveying the illogical meaning that a decline in the quality of people’s self care resulted in dollars.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now talk about “greater than” and “more than.”
summary:
Lesson Content Time, money, and weight may seem to be countable.
For instance, we know that we would use “less” with “water,” as does the following example:
The tank currently contains less than two gallons of water.
Money works similarly, as we can see in the following example:
The account currently holds less than two dollars.
Time works similarly, as we can see in the following example:
The sun will set less than five hours from now.
title: 8.14 Correctly Using Quantity Words When Discussing Quantities of Time, Money, or Weight

contents:
Lesson Content “Greater than” is used in comparing the sizes, levels, degrees, magnitudes, or extents of things.
“More … than” is not synonymous with “larger than” or “higher than.” “More” can be used in multiple ways, including as a determiner that directly modifies what follows it.
Consider the following example:
Her dislike of gambling is greater than her dislike of exercise.
Notice that, in this case, the magnitudes of her dislikes are compared.
The following is incorrect:
Incorrect: Her dislike of gambling is more than her dislike of exercise.
We wouldn’t use “more than” in this context, because “more than” is not synonymous with “larger than.” So, the above sentence does not make sense.
On the other hand, the following example correctly uses “more” as a determiner that modifies “space.”
Sara has more space than Olivia has.
Notice that, in this case, the sentence conveys that both Sara and Olivia have space and indicates that the quantity of space had by Sara is larger than the quantity had by Olivia by using the determiner “more” to modify the space that Sara has.
The following would be incorrect:
Incorrect: Sara has greater space than Olivia has.
Of course, the above sentence does not make sense.
Now, the following is correct:
The amount of space that Sara has is greater than the amount that Olivia has.
Why is the above example correct? Because it correctly uses “greater than” in comparing the sizes of two amounts.
Incorrect: The amount of space that Sara has is more than the amount that Olivia has.
Since “more than” is not synonymous with “larger than,” the above sentence does not make sense.
Notice, we can substitute “larger than” for “greater than” and still have a logical sentence:
The amount of space that Sara has is larger than the amount that Olivia has.
💡MUST KNOW:
“Greater than” is used in comparing the sizes, levels, degrees, magnitudes, or extents of things.
In Sentence Correction questions, we’ll sometimes see statistics compared.
For example:
Incorrect: The standard deviation of the test scores of the students in group A was more than that of the test scores of the students in group B.
Notice that the sentence is comparing the sizes of the standard deviations, which are statistics, so we cannot use “more than.” A correct version is:
The standard deviation of the test scores of the students in group A was greater than that of the test scores of the students in group B.
Notice, we could use “larger than” where “greater than” is and have a logical sentence:
The standard deviation of the test scores of the students in group A was larger than that of the test scores of the students in group B.
Here’s another example that expresses a comparison of statistics:
Incorrect: In Tribeca, the average selling price for a new home is more than the average selling price in the Dumbo area of Brooklyn.
We see that the level of the “average selling price” in one area is compared with the level of “average selling price” in a different area.
The following is a possible correction:
In Tribeca, the average selling price for a new home is greater than the average selling price in the Dumbo area of Brooklyn.
It is also correct to say the following:
In Tribeca, the average selling price for a new home is higher than the average selling price in the Dumbo area of Brooklyn.
Of course, we need to be sure that whenever a comparison is made via the use of “greater than” or “more than,” that comparison is done properly.
Incorrect: The standard deviation of the test scores of the students in group A was greater than the students in group B.
We see that the comparison is broken.
What about the following?
Incorrect: The standard deviation of the test scores of the students in group A was greater than those of group B.
We see that “the standard deviation of the test scores of the students in group A” is being compared with “those.” Presumably, “those” refers to the students in group B or the test scores of the students.
A corrected version of the sentence could be:
The standard deviation of the test scores of the students in group A was greater than that of the test scores of the students in group B.
or
The standard deviation of the test scores of the students in group A was greater than the standard deviation of the test scores of the students in group B.
💡MUST KNOW:
When comparing statistics, use “greater than”; do not use “more than.”
Let’s do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 23 The Parker Solar Probe can travel without melting through coronal material at temperatures greater than a million degrees Celsius. 1. [x] (A) temperatures greater 2. [ ] (B) temperatures that are more > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) temperatures more > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) temperatures, which are greater > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) temperatures, which are more > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy CORRECT ANSWER(A) The Parker Solar Probe can travel without melting through coronal material at temperatures greater than a million degrees Celsius.
Since the sentence expresses a comparison of the magnitudes of temperatures, the correct word to use is “greater.” Notice, if “higher” were substituted for “greater,” the sentence would still work.
The Parker Solar Probe can travel without melting through coronal material at temperatures higher than a million degrees Celsius.
(B) The Parker Solar Probe can travel without melting through coronal material at temperatures that are more than a million degrees Celsius.
This version incorrectly uses the determiner “more” in comparing the magnitudes of temperatures with a single magnitude, “a million degrees Celsius.” In order to be correct, it would have to use “greater” or “higher” in place of “more.”
(C) The Parker Solar Probe can travel without melting through coronal material at temperatures more than a million degrees Celsius.
This version incorrectly uses the determiner “more” in comparing the magnitudes of temperatures with a single magnitude, “a million degrees Celsius.” In order to be correct, it would have to use “greater” or “higher” in place of “more.”
(D) The Parker Solar Probe can travel without melting through coronal material at temperatures, which are greater than a million degrees Celsius.
While “greater” is the correct word to use in comparing the magnitudes of temperatures, this version does not make sense because it uses the nonrestrictive modifier “which are greater than million degrees Celsius.” Since the modifier is nonrestrictive, the sentence does not specify that the temperatures of the coronal material are temperatures greater than a million degrees Celsius. It instead conveys that the coronal material is at temperatures, without specifying what type of temperatures. Then, via the use of the relative clause “which …,” the sentence expresses that temperatures in general are greater than a million degrees Celsius.
(E) The Parker Solar Probe can travel without melting through coronal material at temperatures, which are more than a million degrees Celsius.
This version incorrectly uses the determiner “more” in comparing the magnitudes of temperatures with a single magnitude, “a million degrees Celsius.” In order to be correct, it would have to use “greater” or “higher” in place of “more.”
Also, even if “more” were the correct word to use, this version would not make sense because it uses the nonrestrictive modifier “which are more than million degrees Celsius.” Since the modifier is nonrestrictive, the sentence does not specify that the temperatures of the coronal material are temperatures greater (“more”), than a million degrees Celsius. It instead conveys that the coronal material is at temperatures, without specifying what type of temperatures. Then, via the use of the relative clause “which …,” the sentence expresses that temperatures in general are greater than a million degrees Celsius.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now continue to the next section.
summary:
Lesson Content “Greater than” is used in comparing the sizes, levels, degrees, magnitudes, or extents of things.
“More … than” is not synonymous with “larger than” or “higher than.” “More” can be used in multiple ways, including as a determiner that directly modifies what follows it.
Consider the following example:
Her dislike of gambling is greater than her dislike of exercise.
Notice that, in this case, the magnitudes of her dislikes are compared.
title: 8.15 “Greater Than” versus “More Than”

contents:
Lesson Content As we have seen, to a large degree, GMAT Sentence Correction is a test of skill in noticing issues.
Another way in which Sentence Correction questions test the same skills is through the use of incorrect combinations of words in comparison expressions.
So, in order to ensure that we will correctly answer Sentence Correction questions involving comparisons, we have to maintain awareness of whether the comparison expressions in the sentences use correct combinations of words.
Consider the following example:
Incorrect: The rate at which the tides rise in the Bay of Fundy is much greater as the rate at which tides rise in most other locations.
While, if read quickly, the above sentence may seem to be fine, if we look carefully, can see that the comparison in the above example incorrectly uses “as” with “greater.” Of course, with “greater,” “than” should be used instead of “as,” as in:
The rate at which the tides rise in the Bay of Fundy is much greater than the rate at which tides rise in other locations.
This following example has a similar issue:
Incorrect: There may be more life forms in a handful of earth as there are people living on the planet Earth.
The above sentence does not make sense.
There may be more life forms in a handful of earth than there are people living on the planet Earth.
Let’s consider one more example:
Incorrect: While people may believe poodles to be small dogs, the truth is that some poodles are as large in terms of height and weight than dogs of breeds commonly considered large.
In expressing a comparison of poodles and dogs of other breeds, this example incorrectly pairs “as large” with “than.” “As large” should be paired with “as,” as in:
While people may believe poodles to be small dogs, the truth is that some poodles are as large in terms of height and weight as dogs of breeds commonly considered large.
💡MUST KNOW:
Incorrect choices in Sentence Correction questions involving comparisons may include incorrect combinations of comparison words, such as “greater as” rather than the correct “greater than,” “more … as” rather than the correct “more … than,” “so … as,” rather than the correct “as … as,” “as … than,” rather than the correct “as … as,” or “such … like,” rather than the correct “such … as.”
Let’s do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correctly written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s practice applying what we just learned in answering Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 25 Analysis of the proportions of carbon isotopes present in the structure of a tomb located in Scotland have indicated that, contrary to conclusions based on analysis of artifacts, the tomb is thousands of years as old as similar tombs found in Greece. 1. [ ] (A) have indicated that, contrary to conclusions based on analysis of artifacts, the tomb is thousands of years as old as similar tombs found in Greece > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) has indicated, contrary to conclusions based on analysis of artifacts, the tomb, compared with similar tombs found in Greece, as being thousands of years older > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) indicate that, contrary to conclusions based on analysis of artifacts, the tomb is thousands of years older than similar tombs found in Greece > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) has indicated that, contrary to conclusions based on analysis of artifacts, the tomb is thousands of years older as similar tombs found in Greece > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) indicates that, contrary to conclusions based on analysis of artifacts, the tomb is older, by thousands of years, than similar ones found in Greece View Solution Question Level: medium (A) Analysis of the proportions of carbon isotopes present in the structure of a tomb located in Scotland have indicated that, contrary to conclusions based on analysis of artifacts, the tomb is thousands of years as old as similar tombs found in Greece .
The singular subject “analysis” does not agree with the plural verb “have indicated.”
Also, the expression “thousand of years as old as” does not make sense. Since “as old as” is used to express that two things are the same age, there is no reason to mention “thousands of years” along with saying “as old as.”
(B) Analysis of the proportions of carbon isotopes present in the structure of a tomb located in Scotland has indicated, contrary to conclusions based on analysis of artifacts, the tomb, compared with similar tombs found in Greece, as being thousands of years older .
This version does not effectively convey a logical meaning. If we consider the core sentence without any nonessential modifiers, we have “analysis … has indicated the tomb as being thousands of years older. That sentence basically means that analysis has pointed out the tomb “as being thousands of years older,” and does not effectively convey what exactly the analysis has indicated.
(C) Analysis of the proportions of carbon isotopes present in the structure of a tomb located in Scotland indicate that, contrary to conclusions based on analysis of artifacts, the tomb is thousands of years older than similar tombs found in Greece .
This version is correct except for the fact that the singular subject “analysis” does not agree with the plural verb “indicate.”
(D) Analysis of the proportions of carbon isotopes present in the structure of a tomb located in Scotland has indicated that, contrary to conclusions based on analysis of artifacts, the tomb is thousands of years older as similar tombs found in Greece .
The use of “as” with “thousand of years older” is incorrect. The correct expression is “thousands of years older than.”
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Analysis of the proportions of carbon isotopes present in the structure of a tomb located in Scotland indicates that, contrary to conclusions based on analysis of artifacts, the tomb is older, by thousands of years, than similar ones found in Greece .
The singular subject “analysis” agrees with the singular verb “indicates,” and this version correctly uses “than” with “older, by thousands of years.”
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 26 While the Chrysler Building in New York has twenty-five fewer floors than those of the more well-known Empire State Building, for eleven months following completion, the Chrysler building was as tall or taller than any other building in the city or the world. 1. [ ] (A) twenty-five fewer floors than those of the more well-known Empire State Building, for eleven months following completion, the Chrysler building was as tall or taller than any other building > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) twenty-five floors less than the more well-known Empire State Building has, for eleven months following completion, the Chrysler building was as tall as or taller than any building > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) twenty-five floors fewer than are had by the more well-known Empire State Building, for eleven months following its completion, the Chrysler building was as tall as or taller than any other building 4. [ ] (D) twenty-five fewer floors as the more well-known Empire State Building has, for eleven months following being completed, the Chrysler building was tall as or taller than any other building > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) floors that are twenty-five fewer than those of the more well-known Empire State Building, for eleven months following its completion, the Chrysler building was just as tall or taller than other buildings > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard (A) While the Chrysler Building in New York has twenty-five fewer floors than those of the more well-known Empire State Building, for eleven months following completion, the Chrysler building was as tall or taller than any other building in the city or the world.
“The Chrysler Building in New York has twenty-five fewer floors than those of the … Empire State Building” does not convey any meaning very well but seems to convey the nonsensical meaning that the Chrysler Building has twenty-five fewer floors than the floors of the Empire State Building, or “those,” have.
Also, “completion” does not effectively express that it was the Chrysler Building that was completed. This idea would be better expressed via “its completion,” which, via the use of “its,” indicates that what’s mentioned is the completion of the Chrysler Building.
Also, “as tall” is missing the second “as.” It should be “as tall as.” We have to avoid being fooled by the fact that “or taller than” follows “as tall” into thinking that the second “as” is not necessary.
(B) While the Chrysler Building in New York has twenty-five floors less than the more well-known Empire State Building has, for eleven months following completion, the Chrysler building was as tall as or taller than any building in the city or the world.
“Less” is incorrectly used with the countable noun “floors.”
Also, “completion” does not effectively express that it was the Chrysler Building that was completed. This idea would be better expressed via “its completion,” which, via the use of “its,” indicates that what’s mentioned is the completion of the Chrysler Building.
Also, since the Chrysler Building is a building that is “any building,” by saying “the Chrysler Building was as tall as or taller than any building,” the sentence illogically compares the Chrysler Building with itself.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) While the Chrysler Building in New York has twenty-five floors fewer than are had by the more well-known Empire State Building, for eleven months following its completion, the Chrysler building was as tall as or taller than any other building in the city or the world.
The comparisons expressed by this version are logical, and the comparison words used are used correctly.
(D) While the Chrysler Building in New York has twenty-five fewer floors as the more well-known Empire State Building has, for eleven months following being completed, the Chrysler building was tall as or taller than any other building in the city or the world.
This version incorrectly uses “as” with “twenty-five fewer.” With “fewer,” “than” should be used rather than “as.”
Also, “being completed” does not effectively express that it was the Chrysler Building that was completed. This idea would be better expressed via “its being completed” or “its completion,” either of which, via the use of “its,” indicates that what’s mentioned is the completion of the Chrysler Building.
Also, “tall as” is missing an “as” before “tall.” It should be “as tall as.”
(E) While the Chrysler Building in New York has floors that are twenty-five fewer than those of the more well-known Empire State Building, for eleven months following its completion, the Chrysler building was just as tall or taller than other buildings in the city or the world.
This version expresses the illogical idea that the floors of the Chrysler Building themselves are somehow “fewer than” the floors of the Empire State Building. The number of floors can be fewer than another number of floors, or the floors can be fewer in number than other floors, but floors themselves cannot simply be fewer than other floors, as if being few is an attribute that floors can have to a greater degree than other floors have that attribute.
Also, “just as tall” is missing the second “as.” It should be “just as tall as.” We have to avoid being fooled by the fact that “or taller than” follows “just as tall” into thinking that the second “as” is not necessary.
Also, while not a strong reason for eliminating this choice, the sentence’s saying that the Chrysler Building was taller than “other buildings” does not result in the expression of a clear meaning. Is “taller than other buildings” meant to express that the Chrysler Building was taller than some other buildings in the city or the world, perhaps as few as two buildings? Is it meant to express that the Chrysler Building was taller than all other buildings?
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss how “whereas,” “while,” and “although” differ from “unlike.”
summary:
Lesson Content As we have seen, to a large degree, GMAT Sentence Correction is a test of skill in noticing issues.
Another way in which Sentence Correction questions test the same skills is through the use of incorrect combinations of words in comparison expressions.
So, in order to ensure that we will correctly answer Sentence Correction questions involving comparisons, we have to maintain awareness of whether the comparison expressions in the sentences use correct combinations of words.
title: 8.16 Maintaining Awareness of Whether Comparison Expressions Use Correct Combinations of Words

contents:
Lesson Content It’s common for people to perceive “whereas” and “unlike” as somewhat synonymous or as functioning in similar ways in sentences.
Now, we’ve already learned that, when we see a comparison that employs “unlike,” the rules governing such a comparison are fairly strict and that the two elements related by “unlike” have to be rather similar.
Unlike Stacy Ann, Jovan enjoys photography.
Notice that two people named by proper nouns, “Stacy Ann” and “Jovan” are compared.
Unlike avocados, oranges do not contain significant quantities of omega-3 fatty acids.
In this case, two fruits named by nouns, “avocados” and “oranges,” are compared.
The above comparisons make sense because the items compared in each case are similar to each other.
Incorrect: Unlike computers made today, using a computer made fifty years ago required a fair amount of technical knowledge.
This last example illogically compares things that are quite different from each other: computers and using a computer.
So, we have been conditioned to look for similarity of items compared when we evaluate the logic of comparisons.
This conditioning can be problematic when we see words such as “whereas,” “while,” or “although” in sentences that express contrasts, because the way in which such words function is different from the way in which “unlike” functions, and, as a result, the type of similarity required when “unlike” is used is not required when words such as “whereas,” “while,” or “although” are used.
Let’s consider “whereas.” “Whereas” means “in contrast with the fact that” and is used to connect clauses, and clauses connected by “whereas” don’t have to be particularly similar to each other.
Whereas you will go to Venice this fall, I will go to Rio de Janeiro.
The clauses in the above example are similar to each other.
Whereas some people enjoy jewelry, jewelry is considered superfluous by others.
Whereas Lucas loves cookies, the likelihood that cookies will be popular with the other guests is low.
Notice that the clauses connected by “whereas” in the above two example sentences are quite different from each other.
So, we can see that, unlike sentence elements connected by “unlike,” clauses connected by “whereas” don’t have to be particularly similar to each other.
💡MUST KNOW:
Unlike sentence elements connected by “unlike,” clauses connected by “whereas” don’t have to be particularly similar to each other.
We see a similar phenomenon with the words “while” and “although.” Consider the following examples:
While roses are red, violets are blue.
While Edgar spends his time writing novels, Elizabeth’s days are spent writing software.
While we love the mountains, swimming in the ocean is a great way to spend a summer afternoon.
Although you love ice cream, I love cake.
Although she was running on the beach, no sand got into her hair.
Although the Earth is clearly round, some people believe it to be flat.
As is the case with clauses connected by “whereas,” while, in some sentences, clauses connected by “while” or “although” are similar to each other, in some well-constructed sentences, clauses connected by “while” or “although” are quite different from each other.
Let’s do some Targeted Practice:
Targeted Practice Determine whether each of the following sentences is correct as written. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s practice applying what we just discussed in answering Sentence Correction questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 27 Even though toothed whales have conical teeth and use them for catching fish or squid, baleen whales have fringe-like structures that they use for retaining the krill and plankton upon which they feed called plates of baleen. 1. [ ] (A) Even though toothed whales have conical teeth and use them for catching fish or squid, baleen whales have fringe-like structures that they use for retaining the krill and plankton upon which they feed called plates of baleen. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Toothed whales have conical teeth, and they are used for catching fish or squid, while, at the same time, baleen whales have plates of baleen, a fringe-like structure, and they are used for retaining the krill and plankton upon which they feed. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Unlike toothed whales, which have conical teeth that they use to catch fish or squid, plates of a fringe-like structure called baleen are used by baleen whales to retain the krill and plankton upon which they feed. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) When compared with the conical teeth that toothed whales use to catch fish and squid, plates of baleen are a fringe-like structure used by baleen whales to retain the krill and plankton upon which they feed. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Whereas toothed whales have conical teeth that they use to catch fish or squid, plates of baleen, a fringe-like structure, are used by baleen whales to retain the krill and plankton upon which they feed. View Solution Question Level: medium Correctly answering this question requires analyzing the logic of the comparisons and contrasts expressed by the versions and avoiding being tempted into choosing an illogical choice by wording that is structured in a parallel fashion that “sounds right.”
(A) Even though toothed whales have conical teeth and use them for catching fish or squid, baleen whales have fringe-like structures that they use for retaining the krill and plankton upon which they feed called plates of baleen. This choice may be tempting, because the first clause of this version begins with “toothed whales have” and the second clause begins with “baleen whales have.” So, this choice may appear to be correct at first.
However, careful analysis reveals that the use of “even though” results in the sentence’s suggesting the illogical idea that baleen whales have fringe-like structures even though toothed whales have teeth, as if toothed whales’ having teeth would somehow be expected to keep baleen whales from having fringe-like structures.
Also, the placement of “plates of baleen” at the end of the sentence after “krill and plankton upon which they feed” results in the sentence’s seeming to convey the nonsensical meaning that krill and plankton are called plates of baleen.
(B) Toothed whales have conical teeth, and they are used for catching fish or squid, while, at the same time, baleen whales have plates of baleen, a fringe-like structure, and they are used for retaining the krill and plankton upon which they feed. This choice may be tempting, because the clauses in this version are structured in a rather parallel fashion. So, this choice may appear to be correct at first. However, this version does not effectively convey a logical meaning.
“And they are used for catching fish or squid” does not clearly connect the use of the teeth to the toothed whales. It merely describes what the teeth are used for without indicating that it’s the toothed whales that use them in that way.
“They” in “they are used for catching” does not clearly refer to “teeth,” and could refer to “toothed whales.”
The use of the conjunction “while” would be sufficient. The use of “at the same time” is redundant and results in the sentence’s seeming to suggest that the point to be made is that toothed whales have teeth and baleen whales have plates of baleen at the same time, as if the timing of the possession of these features matters, an idea unlikely to be the author’s point.
“They are used for retaining the krill and plankton upon which they feed” does not clearly connect the use of the plates of baleen to the baleen whales. It merely describes what the plates of baleen are used for without indicating that it’s the baleen whales that use them in that way.
“They” in “they are used for retaining” does not clearly refer to “plates of baleen,” and could refer to “baleen whales.”
“They” in “upon which they feed” does not clearly refer to “baleen whales” and could refer to “plates of baleen.”
(C) Unlike toothed whales, which have conical teeth that they use to catch fish or squid, plates of a fringe-like structure called baleen are used by baleen whales to retain the krill and plankton upon which they feed. This version presents an illogical contrast between toothed whales and plates of a fringe-like structure called baleen.
(D) When compared with the conical teeth that toothed whales use to catch fish and squid, plates of baleen are a fringe-like structure used by baleen whales to retain the krill and plankton upon which they feed. This version may seem to present a logical comparison of conical teeth and plates of baleen. However, what it actually expresses is not a comparison but, rather, the illogical meaning that plates of baleen are a fringe-like structure used by baleen whales when plates of baleen are compared with conical teeth, as if plates of baleen are not a fringe-like structure when they are not compared with conical teeth.
Also, saying that the plural “plates of baleen are” the singular “a fringe-like structure” does not quite make sense.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Whereas toothed whales have conical teeth that they use to catch fish or squid, plates of baleen, a fringe-like structure, are used by baleen whales to retain the krill and plankton upon which they feed. This version uses two clauses connected by “whereas” to logically express a contrast. Even though the two clauses of the sentence are structured differently from each other, in that the subject of the first is “toothed whales,” while the subject of the second is “plates of baleen,” the meaning conveyed makes sense.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 28 Windsurfing is considered physically challenging, involves standing on a board and holding a sail, and is in contrast to kiteboarding, which involves standing on a board and being attached to a kite and can be done in ways to make it not very physically challenging, though still somewhat dangerous. 1. [ ] (A) Windsurfing is considered physically challenging, involves standing on a board and holding a sail, and is in contrast to kiteboarding, which involves standing on a board and being attached to a kite and can be done in ways to make it > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Whereas windsurfing, which involves standing on a board and holding a sail, is considered to be physically challenging, kiteboarding, which involves standing on a board and attached to a kite, can be done in ways in which it is > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Unlike the way in which windsurfing, which involves standing on a board and holding a sail, is considered physically challenging, there are ways in which kiteboarding, which involves standing on a board while attached to a kite, can be done so that it is > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Windsurfing, which involves standing on a board and holding a sail, is considered physically challenging, while there are ways in which kiteboarding, which involves standing on a board while attached to a kite, can be done such that it is 5. [ ] (E) Windsurfing, involving standing on board while holding a sail, is considered to be physically challenging, unlike ways in which kiteboarding, which involves standing on a board while being attached to a kite, can be done so that it is > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: hard This question includes multiple choices that are worded to seem correct but include illogical comparisons or convey illogical or nonsensical meanings. So, correctly answering the question takes using careful analysis of the logic of the comparisons expressed by the choices and of the meanings conveyed by the choices to eliminate the incorrect choices and arrive at the correct answer, which, though worded to sound a little off, uses two clauses connected by “while” to express a logical comparison and conveys meanings that make sense.
(A) Windsurfing is considered physically challenging, involves standing on a board and holding a sail, and is in contrast to kiteboarding, which involves standing on a board and being attached to a kite and can be done in ways to make it not very physically challenging, though still somewhat dangerous.
Saying simply “windsurfing … is in contrast to kiteboarding,” does not make sense. “In contrast to” is meant to be used as a modifier that modifies an entire idea, as in, “In contrast to windsurfing, kiteboarding is X.” This version instead lists “is in contrast to kiteboarding” as one of the attributes of windsurfing, as if being in contrast to something is an attribute that stands on its own.
Also, the wording “being attached to a kite” seems to convey the illogical meaning that the activity kiteboarding centers on the process of “being attached to a kite.”
Also, the wording “can be done in ways to make it” illogically mixes two concepts. The “ways” mentioned are either ways in which kiteboarding “can be done” or ways “to make it not very physically challenging. However, by using the wording “can be done in ways to make it,” this version seems to say both things at once and conveys neither. Conveying a logical meaning requires wording along the lines of the following, which don’t illogically mix the two ideas:
… kiteboarding can be done in ways such that it is not very physically challenging.
or
… there are ways to make kiteboarding not very physically challenging.
(B) Whereas windsurfing, which involves standing on a board and holding a sail, is considered to be physically challenging, kiteboarding, which involves standing on a board and attached to a kite, can be done in ways in which it is not very physically challenging, though still somewhat dangerous.
Since the first clause of this sentence begins with “whereas windsurfing, which involves …” and the second clause begins with “kiteboarding, which involves …,” this version may appear to be correct. However, this version is flawed in multiple ways.
“Considered to be” is not idiomatically correct and not quite logical. The point to be made is that windsurfing is considered X, not that windsurfing is considered to be X.
Also, “which involves standing on a board and attached to a kite” does not make sense no matter how it is read. It could be read as the following list:
which involves:
standing on a board
attached to a kite
“Which involves … attached to a kite” does not make sense.
Alternatively, the clause could be read as the following list:
which involves standing:
on a board
attached to a kite
“Standing … attached to a kite” is not quite logical.
Also, since “it” in “can be done in ways in which it is not very physically challenging” refers to “kiteboarding,” the sentence conveys the nonsensical meaning that kiteboarding is not very physically challenging “in” the ways in which it can be done.
(C) Unlike the way in which windsurfing, which involves standing on a board and holding a sail, is considered physically challenging, there are ways in which kiteboarding, which involves standing on a board while attached to a kite, can be done so that it is not very physically challenging, though still somewhat dangerous.
Since this version uses “unlike” to connect “the way in which windsurfing” with “there are ways in which kiteboarding,” it may appear to be correct at first. However, the comparison expressed is not logical.
One issue is that “the way” cannot be logically compared with “there are ways.”
Another is that the sentence is about two different types of ways, one of them “the way in which windsurfing is considered” something, and the other “ways in which kiteboarding … can be done.” So, even though both sides of the comparison involve “ways,” they don’t actually involve the same type of items.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) Windsurfing, which involves standing on a board and holding a sail, is considered physically challenging, while there are ways in which kiteboarding, which involves standing on a board while attached to a kite, can be done such that it is not very physically challenging, though still somewhat dangerous.
This version may appear to be incorrect, because the two clauses in this version are different from each other, in that the first is about windsurfing, while the second begins with “there are ways”. All the same, the two clauses effectively convey contrasting ideas, and connecting the two clauses via the use of “while” makes sense.
(E) Windsurfing, involving standing on board while holding a sail, is considered to be physically challenging, unlike ways in which kiteboarding, which involves standing on a board while being attached to a kite, can be done so that it is not very physically challenging, though still somewhat dangerous.
This version illogically contrasts “windsurfing” with “ways in which kiteboarding … can be done.”
Also, “considered to be” is not idiomatically correct and not quite logical. The point to be made is that windsurfing is considered X, not that windsurfing is considered to be X.
Also, the wording “which involves standing on a board while being attached to a kite” conveys the illogical meaning that the activity kiteboarding centers on the process of “being attached to a kite.”
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss avoiding being trapped by incorrect answers that include well written comparisons.
summary:
Lesson Content It’s common for people to perceive “whereas” and “unlike” as somewhat synonymous or as functioning in similar ways in sentences.
Now, we’ve already learned that, when we see a comparison that employs “unlike,” the rules governing such a comparison are fairly strict and that the two elements related by “unlike” have to be rather similar.
Unlike Stacy Ann, Jovan enjoys photography.
Notice that two people named by proper nouns, “Stacy Ann” and “Jovan” are compared.
title: 8.17 “Whereas,” “While,” and “Although” Are Different From “Unlike.”

contents:
Lesson Content As we have discussed previously, in order to force test-takers to use logic in choosing among Sentence Correction choices rather than simply choose a choice that “sounds correct,” Sentence Correction question writers often seek to make incorrect choices seem correct and correct choices seem incorrect.
Consider the following two sentence versions:
Version 1: Having been hunted to near extinction in the wild, there are now more tigers living in captivity in zoos, sanctuaries, and private hands than there are living in the wild.
Version 2: Wild tigers having been hunted to near extinction, there are now more tigers living in captivity, sanctuaries, and private hands than living in the wild.
A test-taker focused on the comparison part of the sentence may decide that Version 1 is the better version, because Version 1 words the comparison very clearly with its use of “there are more tigers living in … than there are living in,” while Version 2 uses more elliptical, minimal wording “there are now more tigers living in … than living in.”
However, the truth is that both ways of expressing the comparison are fine.
Meanwhile, Version 1 is clearly incorrect, because the opening modifier, “having been hunted …,” is a noun modifier, but there is no noun for it to logically modify.
The takeaway from this example is that we have to be careful not to be fooled into choosing an incorrect choice by the fact that, in expressing a comparison, that incorrect choice uses wording that seems a bit better than the, also acceptable, wording used by the correct answer to the question.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice.
Targeted Practice From each pair of sentence versions, choose the one that is correct. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Consider the following Sentence Correction example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Sentence Correction Example 29 Whereas the paintings of Jackson Pollock are completely non-representational, elements that represent real world objects are included among those of Henry Moore’s sculptures. 1. [x] (A) Whereas the paintings of Jackson Pollock are completely non-representational, elements that represent real world objects are included among those of Henry Moore’s sculptures. 2. [ ] (B) Unlike Jackson Pollock’s completely non-representational paintings, Henry Moore included in his sculptures some elements that represent real world objects. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) In contrast to the elements of Jackson Pollock’s paintings, which are completely non-representational, the elements of Henry Moore’s sculptures include some, which represent real-world objects. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) In comparison with Jackson Pollock’s completely non-representational paintings are the elements of Henry Moore’s sculptures, among which are some that represent real-world objects. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Unlike the paintings of Jackson Pollock, being completely non-representational, the sculptures of Henry Moore include some elements, representing real world objects. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard Correctly answering this question requires avoiding being tempted into choosing an incorrect choice by the fact that the elements of the comparison expressed by that incorrect choice match well. The correct answer to this question may not appear to be correct at first, but, via careful analysis of the meanings expressed by the choices, we can eliminate incorrect choices that may initially seem correct and arrive at the correct answer, which is the only choice that conveys a meaning that makes sense.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) Whereas the paintings of Jackson Pollock are completely non-representational, elements that represent real world objects are included among those of Henry Moore’s sculptures. Because the subject of the first clause of this choice is “paintings,” while the subject of the second clause is “elements,” this choice may at first seem to be incorrect. However, we have to remember that clauses of a sentence that uses “whereas” to express a comparison don’t have to match in the way that elements of a comparison that uses “unlike” do. As long as the clauses express contrasting ideas, the sentence can work.
In this case, the two clauses connected by “whereas” do effectively express a contrast between Jackson Pollock’s paintings and Henry Moore’s sculptures.
Another aspect of this version that may seem incorrect is the use of the pronoun “those,” which seemingly could refer to “paintings,” “elements,” or “objects.” However, since it does not make sense that Henry Moore’s sculptures would have paintings, and since it’s clear that the sentence is not expressing that Henry Moore’s sculptures have objects, the only logical referent of “those” is “elements.”
(B) Unlike Jackson Pollock’s completely non-representational paintings, Henry Moore included in his sculptures some elements that represent real world objects. This choice illogically compares Jackson Pollock’s paintings with Henry Moore.
(C) In contrast to the elements of Jackson Pollock’s paintings, which are completely non-representational, the elements of Henry Moore’s sculptures include some, which represent real-world objects. This choice may seem correct, because it contrasts elements with elements, and because this choice’s use of two relative clauses beginning with “which” may make this choice seem well laid out and balanced.
However, since “which represent real-world objects” is a nonrestrictive modifier, the clause “the elements of Henry Moore’s sculptures include some, which represent real-world objects” conveys the illogical meaning that the elements include some elements. Well, there’s a surprise!
