Then the nonrestrictive modifier “which represent real-world objects” merely adds nonessential information that the some elements included in the elements represent real-world objects.
This version would work if, instead of the nonrestrictive “which represent …,” a restrictive modifier, such as “that represent …,” were used, so that “some” would be extended to, for instance, “some (elements) that represent real world objects,” as we can see by considering the following:
… the elements of Henry Moore’s sculptures include some that represent real-world objects.
The above version of the clause clearly conveys that the elements include some elements that represent real-world objects.
(D) In comparison with Jackson Pollock’s completely non-representational paintings are the elements of Henry Moore’s sculptures, among which are some that represent real-world objects. The most blatant flaw in this version is that it seems to express an illogical comparison between Jackson Pollock’s paintings and elements of Henry Moore’s sculptures.
Another issue is that the version does not actually express a comparison, but rather expresses the illogical idea that the paintings are “in comparison” with the elements.
Additionally, “which” seems to refer to “sculptures” rather than to “elements,” though we know from the other versions that it is the elements, rather than the sculptures, that represent “real-world objects. This last issue does not, however, constitute a clear reason for eliminating this choice, since the idea that sculptures represent real-world objects does make sense.
(E) Unlike the paintings of Jackson Pollock, being completely non-representational, the sculptures of Henry Moore include some elements, representing real world objects. This choice may seem correct, because it logically compares the paintings of Jackson Pollock with the sculptures of Henry Moore, and because this choice’s use of two non-restrictive present participial modifiers, “being …” and “representing …,” may make this choice seem well laid out and balanced.
However, “paintings” is not the subject of a clause and is not the actor of any action mentioned by the sentence. Therefore, the present participial phrase “being non-representational” would most logically take as its agent, not “paintings,” but “sculptures,” which, unlike “paintings,” is the subject of a clause. As a result of the foregoing, the sentence conveys the meaning that, as a result of being non-representational, the sculptures include elements, which meaning is of course nonsensical, because all sculptures have elements, and being non-representational would not result in sculptures’ including elements.
Further, “the sculptures of Henry Moore include some elements, representing real-world objects” conveys the nonsensical idea that, by or in the course of including elements, the sculptures represent real-world objects.
So, we have to be careful not to be lured by the fact that the comparison expressed by this choice is obviously logical into choosing this choice, whose overall meaning is nonsensical.
Correct Answer: (A)
summary:
Lesson Content As we have discussed previously, in order to force test-takers to use logic in choosing among Sentence Correction choices rather than simply choose a choice that “sounds correct,” Sentence Correction question writers often seek to make incorrect choices seem correct and correct choices seem incorrect.
Consider the following two sentence versions:
Version 1: Having been hunted to near extinction in the wild, there are now more tigers living in captivity in zoos, sanctuaries, and private hands than there are living in the wild.
title: 8.18 What Appears to Be the Best Written, Clearest Comparison May Be in an Incorrect Choice.

contents:
Lesson Content In the verbal reasoning section of the GMAT, between 9 and 13 of the 36 questions that appear will be Critical Reasoning questions.
💡MUST KNOW:
In the verbal reasoning section of the GMAT, between 9 and 13 of the 36 questions that appear will be Critical Reasoning questions.
Critical Reasoning questions fall into the following eleven major question types:
(1) Identify the Assumption
These questions ask us to identify an assumption upon which an argument depends.
Which of the following is an assumption logically necessary for coming to the conclusion of the above argument?
(2) Weaken the Argument
These questions ask us to find a statement of fact that would weaken or hurt an argument.
Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above?
(3) Strengthen the Argument
These questions ask us to find a statement of fact that would help an argument or support a conclusion.
Which of the following, if true, most strongly supports the hypothesis?
(4) Resolve a Paradox
These questions ask us to find a statement of fact that would resolve what appears to be (but is not actually) a paradox.
Which of the following statements, if true, would best resolve the apparent contradiction described above?
(5) Inference
These questions ask us to find an unwritten conclusion that must be true given what a passage says.
If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred from them?
(6) Find the Conclusion
These questions ask us to find a conclusion that is supported by a passage.
If the statements above are true, which of the following is most strongly supported by them?
(7) Evaluate the Argument
These questions ask us to find an answer choice that helps us to determine whether an argument is well constructed.
In evaluating the argument, it would be most useful to establish which of the following?
(8) Logical Flaw
These questions ask us to find a flaw in the reasoning of an argument.
Which of the following best describes the error of reasoning contained in the argument above?
(9) Complete the Passage
These questions ask us to find an answer that best completes an uncompleted passage.
Which of the following best completes the passage?
(10) Method of Reasoning
These questions ask us to identify the way in which a passage is logically organized or structured.
The author develops the argument by
(11) Boldface
These questions ask us to determine what functions portions of passages play in arguments.
In the argument given, the two boldfaced portions play which of the following roles?
Don’t worry if any (or all) of these question types seem new to you.
Let’s take a look at the basic layout of Critical Reasoning questions.
summary:
Lesson Content In the verbal reasoning section of the GMAT, between 9 and 13 of the 36 questions that appear will be Critical Reasoning questions.
💡MUST KNOW:
In the verbal reasoning section of the GMAT, between 9 and 13 of the 36 questions that appear will be Critical Reasoning questions.
Critical Reasoning questions fall into the following eleven major question types:
(1) Identify the Assumption
These questions ask us to identify an assumption upon which an argument depends.
title: 1.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content Each Critical Reasoning question begins with a written stimulus, which is usually, though not always, in the form of a short passage of about 100 words or fewer.
💡MUST KNOW:
Each Critical Reasoning question begins with a written stimulus, usually in the form of a short passage of about 100 words or fewer. Accompanying the stimulus is a question and five answer choices. The question will always ask us to determine which of the five answer choices is logically related to the stimulus in a particular way.
Of course, the correct answer to a Critical Reasoning question will be the choice that matches what the question asks us to find.
To see how a Critical Reasoning question works, let’s consider an example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 1 Recently, sales of figs have dramatically increased in many areas of the country. Which of the following if true provides the most additional support for the conclusion of the argument? 1. [ ] (A) Because fig trees can thrive in a range of climates, it is possible to grow figs in many areas of the country. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Sales of blueberries also have increased recently. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Not only sales of fresh figs but also sales of dried figs have increased recently. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Sales of figs have increased only in areas of the country in which the new video game is popular. 5. [ ] (E) Figs are sources of important nutrients and of antioxidants. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy This question is a typical Critical Reasoning question.
It starts off with a passage, which, in this case, presents an argument about why sales of figs have increased.
The conclusion of the argument presented is the following:
Conclusion: The reason for the increases in sales of figs is people’s seeing figs portrayed as sources of power in the new video game.
The passage is followed by a question stem:
Question Stem: Which of the following, if true, provides the most additional support for the conclusion of the argument?”
So, to correctly answer the question, we need to find the choice that provides the most additional support for the argument’s conclusion.
(A) Because fig trees can thrive in a range of climates, it is possible to grow figs in many areas of the country. This choice does not provide support for the conclusion. It provides some information on growing figs but says nothing that indicates why fig sales increased or connects the increases in sales of figs to the release of the new video game.
(B) Sales of blueberries also have increased recently. The fact that sales of blueberries have increased does not support the conclusion at all. To support the conclusion, we need information that somehow indicates that the increases in sales of figs are related to the release of the new video game, and the fact that sales of blueberries increased does not indicate that the two are related.
(C) Not only sales of fresh figs but also sales of dried figs have increased recently. Notice that this choice provides additional information about the increases in sales of figs but does not provide more information on why the increases occurred. So, what it says does not indicate that the increases in sales of figs are related to the release of the new video game.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Sales of figs have increased only in areas of the country in which the new video game is popular. This choice provides support for the conclusion that the reason for the increases in sales of figs is people’s seeing figs portrayed as sources of power in the new video game.
The passage supports the conclusion by providing the information that the increases in sales of figs occurred just after the video game was released.
This choice provides further support by showing that the increases in sales of figs occurred not only just after the video game was released but also only in areas in which the video game is popular. After all, if sales of figs increased only in areas in which the video game is popular, then we have reason to believe that there is a connection between the video game and the increases in sales of figs.
So, since this choice provides additional support for the argument’s conclusion, this choice is the correct answer.
(E) Figs are sources of important nutrients and of antioxidants. This choice has the wrong effect.
We need a choice that provides support for the conclusion that the reason for the increases in sales of figs is people’s seeing figs portrayed as sources of power, but this choice instead provides a different reason why people would purchase figs, the fact that figs are sources of nutrients and antioxidants.
Correct Answer: (D) We will, of course, have much more to say about the structure of Critical Reasoning questions in the chapters to come.
Let’s now talk about the skills that Critical Reasoning questions test.
summary:
Lesson Content Each Critical Reasoning question begins with a written stimulus, which is usually, though not always, in the form of a short passage of about 100 words or fewer.
💡MUST KNOW:
Each Critical Reasoning question begins with a written stimulus, usually in the form of a short passage of about 100 words or fewer. Accompanying the stimulus is a question and five answer choices. The question will always ask us to determine which of the five answer choices is logically related to the stimulus in a particular way.
title: 1.2 The Basic Layout of Critical Reasoning Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Many students lack sufficient understanding of what GMAT Critical Reasoning questions test; they don’t understand the skills required to perform well on these questions.
GMAT Critical Reasoning questions are designed to test our skill in making, analyzing, and evaluating arguments and plans.
💡MUST KNOW:
GMAT Critical Reasoning questions are designed to test our skill in making, analyzing, and evaluating arguments and plans.
Correctly answering these questions requires using what the makers of the GMAT call higher-order thinking skills to reason your way to the correct choice.
It’s helpful to know what Critical Reasoning is not.
What GMAT Critical Reasoning is not:
GMAT Critical Reasoning is not a word matching game. GMAT Critical Reasoning is not really a test of how well we have memorized information, such as a list of Critical Reasoning question types. GMAT Critical reasoning is not a test of how well we use simple strategies to find correct answers. Now let’s discuss what GMAT Critical Reasoning is.
vision attention to detail. skill in the use of logic. avoiding falling into mental traps and cognitive biases. emphasizing careful, clear thinking and minimizing shallow thinking. good judgment. execution of best practices. We can distil all the above into the same three main aspects that are involved in answering GMAT questions of any type.
Arriving at the correct answers to Critical Reasoning questions depends on:
What you know. What you see. What you do. 💡MUST KNOW:
Arriving at the correct answers to GMAT Critical Reasoning questions generally involves three aspects, what we know, what we see, and what we do.
As this chapter progresses, and in the Critical Reasoning Chapters that follow, we’re going to help you to develop every skill you need to correctly and efficiently answer Critical Reasoning questions.
Let’s now turn our attention to the structure of an argument.

title: 1.3 The Skills That Critical Reasoning Questions Test

contents:
Lesson Content Most GMAT Critical reasoning questions are based on arguments.
💡MUST KNOW:
An argument is a series of statements that logically explain a point of view.
In order to effectively answer Critical Reasoning questions, we must understand how arguments are commonly structured.
The Conclusion The Evidence The Assumptions The Background Information Let’s begin by discussing the conclusion.
summary:
Lesson Content Most GMAT Critical reasoning questions are based on arguments.
💡MUST KNOW:
An argument is a series of statements that logically explain a point of view.
In order to effectively answer Critical Reasoning questions, we must understand how arguments are commonly structured.
The Conclusion The Evidence The Assumptions The Background Information Let’s begin by discussing the conclusion.
title: 1.4 The Structure of an Argument

contents:
Lesson Content The conclusion of the argument is the sentence that states the argument’s point of view.
An author’s conclusion is always a statement of opinion.
💡MUST KNOW:
The conclusion of the argument is the sentence that states the argument’s point of view. An author’s conclusion is always a statement of opinion. In other words, whenever an author sets forth a conclusion, she’s presenting what she believes to be true.
Conclusions come in the form of ideas, viewpoints, recommendations, suggestions, and plans of action, to name a few.
Consider the following simple argument:
Critical Reasoning is like a game. Games are fun. Critical Reasoning must be fun.
The conclusion of the above argument is “Critical Reasoning must be fun.” It’s the main point of the argument, the part of the argument that the other parts lead up to.
Authors often use specific keywords to indicate to the reader that a conclusion is about to follow.
Therefore…, In conclusion…, So…, Clearly…, Hence…, This shows that…, It can be concluded that…, Should…, Ought to…, Must…, As a result…, Thus…, Consequently…, As a consequence…, Accordingly…, It follows that…, For this reason…
For example, consider the following conclusions that could be a part of Critical Reasoning questions:
Therefore, the government of Verdantia should lower interest rates immediately.
In conclusion, plant-based diets are good for people and for the environment.
So, the city of Redville would be well advised to increase the number of miles of bicycle lanes found on city roadways. Clearly, the new university study is flawed. Hence, most people would benefit from increasing the amount of time they spend exercising each day. This finding shows that Great White Sharks are not the solitary animals that scientists once suspected them to be. It can be concluded that the conservation efforts are making a positive impact on the prosperity of local residents.
It is for this reason that the Rhino is one of the most respected animals in Africa. As a result, the nurses’ union should demand higher wages for its constituents.
Consequently, the economist recommended that the economy reopen. Accordingly, the bridge connecting the two islands should be immediately closed to all pedestrian traffic.
As a consequence, the wolves will soon be returning to their homes in Yellowstone. These keywords often signal that what follows will indeed be a conclusion.
Also, although quite often an argument’s conclusion is found at the end of the passage, the truth is that it can be found anywhere within the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
Although quite often an argument’s conclusion is found at the end of the passage, the truth is that it can be found anywhere within the passage. So, don’t expect the conclusion to always be the last sentence of a passage.
To effectively and efficiently answer a Critical Reasoning question that is based on an argument, we must relentlessly seek out and understand the argument’s conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
To effectively and efficiently answer a Critical Reasoning question that is based on an argument, we must relentlessly seek out and understand the argument’s conclusion.
We’ll have a lot more to say about conclusions as the course progresses.
Let’s now discuss evidence/premises.
summary:
Lesson Content The conclusion of the argument is the sentence that states the argument’s point of view.
An author’s conclusion is always a statement of opinion.
💡MUST KNOW:
The conclusion of the argument is the sentence that states the argument’s point of view. An author’s conclusion is always a statement of opinion. In other words, whenever an author sets forth a conclusion, she’s presenting what she believes to be true.
title: 1.5 The Conclusion

contents:
Lesson Content Evidence or premises are facts that support the conclusion and are used to build the argument.
Let’s consider our simple example again:
Critical Reasoning is like a game. Games are fun. Critical Reasoning must be fun.
In the argument above, the premises that support the conclusion are “Critical Reasoning is like a game,” and “Games are fun.” Those statements support the conclusion “Critical Reasoning must be fun.”
Evidence provided by authors of Critical Reasoning questions comes in many forms.
💡MUST KNOW:
Evidence or premises are facts that support the conclusion and are used to build the argument.
Remember that a statement of fact is never an opinion.
The study found that people who eat at least five servings of fruit each day have significantly lower rates of tooth decay than are had by people who do not. The town of West Hills recently began adding chlorine to the public water supply.
Starting tomorrow, park rangers will begin flying over the park aerial drones equipped with thermal cameras able to detect campfires.
The Mars Company saw a 300 percent increase in customer complaints last month. None of the students in the class have taken a course in logic.
Scientists found shark fossils in a dry lake located in what is now central Florida. The council of Gloucester passed a new law making it legal for police to stop and issue a traffic ticket to any driver seen not wearing a seat belt while driving in Gloucester.
Notice that each of the statements above is factual; there is no opinion component to them.
It is important to know that we never argue with evidence presented to us in Critical Reasoning questions.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Critical Reasoning questions, we never argue with evidence presented to us. If something is presented as a fact, we must consider it a fact.
We’ll have more to say about evidence as the course progresses.
Let’s now discuss background information.
summary:
Lesson Content Evidence or premises are facts that support the conclusion and are used to build the argument.
Let’s consider our simple example again:
Critical Reasoning is like a game. Games are fun. Critical Reasoning must be fun.
In the argument above, the premises that support the conclusion are “Critical Reasoning is like a game,” and “Games are fun.” Those statements support the conclusion “Critical Reasoning must be fun.”
Evidence provided by authors of Critical Reasoning questions comes in many forms.
title: 1.6 Evidence or Premises

contents:
Lesson Content Sometimes arguments contain background information that the author uses to frame the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
An author presents us with background information to “get us up to speed” on anything we may need to know in order to effectively understand the argument.
For example, in the following passage, the author uses the first sentence to convey background information to provide us with enough context to be able to completely understand the argument:
The Noodle House, which serves only noodles and has won the award for the best noodles in our state five years in a row, is opening a store in the Town of Redlands next month. The mayor of Redlands loves eating award-winning food. Therefore, the mayor of Redlands will love eating at the Noodle House. Notice that, without the background information about the Noodle House, the argument would not make much sense.
We’ll have a lot more to say about background information as the course progresses.
Let’s now discuss assumptions.
summary:

💡MUST KNOW:

For example, in the following passage, the author uses the first sentence to convey background information to provide us with enough context to be able to completely understand the argument:
title: 1.7 Background Information

contents:
Lesson Content A vital part of any argument is its assumptions.
An assumption is an unstated piece of information that must be true if the logic of an argument is to work.
💡MUST KNOW:
An assumption is an unstated piece of information that must be true if the logic of the argument is to work.
It can be helpful to think of an argument as a math problem:
(any background info)+ Evidence + Assumptions = Conclusion
In other words, think of assumptions as the unstated glue, the missing information, that connects the evidence to the conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
Think of assumptions as the unstated glue, the missing information, that connects the evidence to the conclusion. Without this glue, the argument will fall apart, because, without this glue, the evidence provided does not support the argument’s conclusion.
Because the entire next chapter is devoted to assumptions, we won’t say anything further here about assumptions.
Let’s apply what we have discussed about the structure of an argument by considering an example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 2 The Town of Seaford is projected to have a sizable budget deficit in the coming year. Which of the following does the author assume in arriving at the above conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) Tax rates set by the Town of Seaford could be increased substantially. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) If the Town of Seaford does not cover its budget deficit, it will be forced to declare bankruptcy in the coming year. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) People will continue to use the North Bay Bridge to get to Seaford even if they must pay a toll to do so. 4. [ ] (D) The Town of Seaford has been spending money on wasteful projects. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) The number of cars that cross the North Bay Bridge every day will not increase in the near future. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy The passage in this question starts with some background information:
Background: The Town of Seaford is projected to have a sizable budget deficit in the coming year.
This background information provides context for the argument that follows.
The passage then provides a premise to support a conclusion:
Premise: Every day, over 20,000 cars cross the North Bay Bridge into Seaford.
Finally, the passage states the conclusion of the argument:
Conclusion: By charging a toll to cross the North Bay Bridge, the Town of Seaford can generate enough revenue to cover its budget deficit.
We can see that the background information and premise are facts, while the conclusion is an opinion supported by the premise, and that the background information, premise, and conclusion work together to present an understandable, logical argument.
Having read the passage, we come to the question stem:
Question Stem: Which of the following does the author assume in arriving at the above conclusion?
So, to correctly answer the question we have to find an assumption that the author has made in arriving at the conclusion.
(A) Tax rates set by the Town of Seaford could be increased substantially. The author does not have to assume that tax rates could be increased in order to conclude that the Town of Seaford could cover its budget deficit by charging a toll for crossing the bridge. After all, it’s toll revenue, not tax revenue, that is expected to cover the deficit.
(B) If the Town of Seaford does not cover its budget deficit, it will be forced to declare bankruptcy in the coming year. This choice does not have the effect we need. This choice describes a consequence of not covering the budget deficit: the town will have to declare bankruptcy.
The author does not have to assume anything about consequences of not covering the deficit to conclude that the town can cover the deficit by charging a toll to cross the bridge.
YOUR CHOICE(C) People will continue to use the North Bay Bridge to get to Seaford even if they must pay a toll to do so. This choice states an assumption that the author must be making in arriving at the conclusion of the argument.
The author supports the conclusion that the Town of Seaford can cover its budget deficit by charging a toll for crossing the North Bay Bridge by using the premise that over 20,000 cars cross the bridge every day. Of course, the author is reasoning that, if the town were to receive a toll for each of 20,000 cars every day, the town would generate a significant amount of revenue over the coming year.
However, what if people use the North Bay Bridge only because there is no toll? In that case, it’s possible that, if the town were to charge a toll for crossing the bridge, people wouldn’t continue to use the bridge, in which case the town would not be able to cover its budget deficit by charging a toll to cross the bridge.
So, we can see that, in arriving at the argument’s conclusion, the author must be assuming that people will continue to use the North Bay Bridge even if they must pay a toll in order to do so.
(D) The Town of Seaford has been spending money on wasteful projects. This choice has the wrong effect. The correct answer will state something that the author is assuming in arriving at the conclusion, but what this choice does is provide a possible explanation for why the Town of Seaford is projected to have a sizable budget deficit: it has been spending money on wasteful projects.
Notice that the author does not have to assume that the Town of Seaford has been spending money on wasteful projects in order to conclude that the town can cover its budget deficit by charging a toll to cross the North Bay Bridge. After all, the town could cover the deficit by charging a toll regardless of what the town has spent money on.
(E) The number of cars that cross the North Bay Bridge every day will not increase in the near future. Since, presumably, the more cars cross the bridge, the more the Town of Seaford could collect in tolls, the author certainly does not have to assume that the number of cars that cross the bridge every day will not increase. In fact, if the number of cars that cross the bridge daily were to increase, it would be even more likely that the town could cover its deficit by charging a toll. So, the author is not assuming what this choice says.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss some words that we need to understand in order to be effective in answering Critical Reasoning questions.
summary:
Lesson Content A vital part of any argument is its assumptions.
An assumption is an unstated piece of information that must be true if the logic of an argument is to work.
💡MUST KNOW:
An assumption is an unstated piece of information that must be true if the logic of the argument is to work.
It can be helpful to think of an argument as a math problem:
(any background info)+ Evidence + Assumptions = Conclusion
title: 1.8 Assumptions

contents:
Lesson Content In this chapter, we’re helping you build a basic toolkit.
“A few” means “not many, but more than one.”
“Several” means “three or more but not many.”
“Some” means “more than one, but possibly all.”
“Many” means “a large number of.” Notice that “many” is a version of “some.” Notice also that “many” is not necessarily “most.”
“Most” means “more than 50 percent.”
“A majority” means “more than 50 percent.”
“A minority” means “less than 50 percent.”
“Solely” = “only” e.g., “John is solely responsible.” = “Only John is responsible.”
“At least one” = “one or more” e.g., “At least one pig can fly.” = “One or more pigs can fly.”
“Exclusively” = “only” e.g., “Bats are exclusively nocturnal.” = “Bats are only nocturnal.”
“Uniquely” = “in a way connected to only one person or thing” e.g., “The facility is uniquely equipped to recycle such materials.” = “Only that facility is equipped in that way to recycle such materials.”
“Always” = “all” e.g., “Radiation leaks are always dangerous.” = “All radiation leaks are dangerous.” “Martha is always late.” = “On all occasions, Martha is late.”
You’ll see these words come up often.
Let’s now discuss a key move for Critical Reasoning success.

“Most” means “more than 50 percent.”
“A majority” means “more than 50 percent.
title: 1.9 Some Essential Words to Understand

contents:
Lesson Content When answering a Critical Reasoning question, even if we think that we have found the correct answer, we should go through all the five choices before choosing a correct answer.
💡MUST KNOW:
Even if we think that we have found the correct answer, we should go through all the five options before choosing a correct answer.
Notice that Critical Reasoning question stems ask us to find the “best” choice among the five choices provided.
For example, Critical Reasoning question stems read:
“MOST seriously weakens”
“provides the MOST support”
“BEST explains”
“is MOST strongly supported by”
Therefore, even if we have found a choice that we think answers the question, there may be in the remaining choices another choice that answers the question in a much better way.
That said, to avoid any misconception, we’d like to say that our extensive research into Critical Reasoning questions has shown that, although the questions ask us to find the “best’ answer,” there will be only one truly correct answer for every question, and the difference between the correct answer and the “runner-up” will be significant.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Critical Reasoning questions, there will be only one truly correct answer for every question, and the difference between the correct answer and the “runner-up” will be significant.
Let’s now talk about what to do if we’ve eliminated all of the answer choices.

title: 1.10 When Answering a Critical Reasoning Question, Be Sure to Carefully Evaluate All Five of the Answer Choices

contents:
Lesson Content Sometimes we find that we have eliminated all the answer choices.
💡MUST KNOW:
If you have eliminated all the answer choices, simply take a moment to be sure that you have properly understood the passage. Then go back through the answer choices and carefully reevaluate them. This time through you need to really bear down on each answer choice and rigorously evaluate its merits.
By continuing to consider the differences between the choices and by going back to the passage to find details that you missed in previous readings, you’ll find that one choice that is the correct answer to the question.
Let’s now discuss what we have to see about the statements in passages and answer choices to do well in Critical Reasoning.
