title: 1.11 What to Do If you Have Eliminated All of the Answer Choices

contents:
Lesson Content To do well in Critical Reasoning, we must see the logical implications of statements in Critical Reasoning passages and answer choices.
A logical implication is simply the logical consequence of a set of facts or conditions.
💡MUST KNOW:
A logical implication is simply the logical consequence of a set of facts or conditions.
We can present a logical implication with an “if/then” statement.
In the above “if/then” statement, Y is the logical implication of X.
💡MUST KNOW:
We can present a logical implication with an “if/then” statement.
Let’s consider some examples:
If the service at a restaurant improves, then a smaller percentage of patrons will complain to management. It makes sense that, the better the service at a restaurant is, the smaller will be the percentage of its patrons complaining to management.
If it is easier to get to a store, then more people will shop there. We’d expect that, all else equal, as the ease of getting to a store goes up, the number of people who shop there will increase.
If the price of fuel for cars increases, then demand for more fuel-efficient cars will increase, and demand for less-fuel efficient cars will decrease. Increased demand for more fuel-efficient cars and decreased demand for less fuel-efficient cars are logical implications of an increase in price for fuel for cars.
If people can make something more efficiently, then they can produce more of that thing in the same amount of time. We can see that it makes perfect sense that, when people can make something more efficiently, they can produce more of it in the same amount of time.
If the number of vehicles a city owns increases, then its vehicle maintenance expenses will increase. We’d expect that, as the number of vehicles owned by a city increases, the cost to maintain those vehicles also increases.
In GMAT Critical Reasoning, we can, and are expected to, use common sense to determine what the logical implications of statements in the passages and answer choices are.
💡MUST KNOW:
In GMAT Critical Reasoning, we can, and are expected to, use common sense to determine what the logical implications of statements in the passages and answer choices are.
For example, let’s say a Critical Reasoning answer choice says the following:
It has become more difficult to park close to the restaurant.
We can use common sense to determine what the logical implications of that answer choice are.
Let’s consider another example:
It is more profitable for manufacturing companies to use method A than to use method B.
It’s common knowledge that, generally, companies seek to be profitable.
Let’s consider one more example of a statement we could see in a Critical Reasoning passage:
Cenospheres are microscopic spheres that form only when hydrocarbons burn.
In this case, the logical implication is that, if cenospheres form only when hydrocarbons burn, then if no hydrocarbons burn, no cenospheres will form.
Let’s now do some Targeted Practice to hone our skill in seeing logical implications.
Targeted Practice For each question, select the answer that most logically follows from the given information. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 Country X gets all its sugar from two sugar cane farms. One of these farms is destroyed during a hurricane, with the result that there is 50 percent drop in the number of kilograms of sugar produced per month in Country X. 1. [x] (A) The cost of sugar in Country X will increase. 2. [ ] (B) The cost of sugar in Country X will decrease. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) The cost of sugar in Country X will not change. > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 2 City water pipe construction has substantially decreased the number of parking spots available for patrons of Restaurant R. 1. [ ] (A) More people will dine at Restaurant R. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Fewer people will dine at Restaurant R. 3. [ ] (C) There will be no change in the number of people dining at Restaurant R. > Correct Answer: (B) ## Targeted Practice: 3 Munsford Inc. supplies paint to homebuilding companies. Recently, the companies to which Munsford Inc. supplies paint have been reporting decreases in orders for new homes. 1. [ ] (A) The amount of paint sold by Munsford Inc. will increase. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) The amount of paint sold by Munsford Inc. will decrease. 3. [ ] (C) The information provided does not indicate anything about the amount of paint sold by Munsford Inc. > Correct Answer: (B) ## Targeted Practice: 4 A hotel in the town of Morganville recently began offering its rooms for a 20 percent discount off the normal prices. 1. [ ] (A) The hotel’s daily income will increase. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) The hotel’s daily income will decrease. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) The information provided does not clearly indicate what will happen with regard to the hotel’s daily income. ## Targeted Practice: 5 The cost of electricity in City C has recently decreased by 15 percent while the cost of gasoline has increased by 10 percent. 1. [x] (A) Sales of fully electric vehicles will increase. 2. [ ] (B) Sales of fully electric vehicles will decrease. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) The information provided does not clearly indicate anything about what will happen to sales of fully electric vehicles in City C. > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 6 The wholesaler that supplies coffee to a coffee shop has run out of the type of coffee the shop normally sells. So, the shop is forced to sell coffee that tastes different from the coffee it normally sells. 1. [ ] (A) Sales of coffee will increase. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Sales of coffee will decrease. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) The information provided does not clearly indicate anything about sales of coffee at the coffee shop. ## Targeted Practice: 7 Consultants hired by the city of Oak Hills recommended that the city take three actions to reduce air pollution: restrict use of motor vehicles, install bike paths, and improve public transportation. However, Oak Hills has taken only one of the actions, installing bike paths. 1. [ ] (A) Levels of air pollution will increase. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Levels of air pollution will decrease. 3. [ ] (C) Levels of air pollution will remain the same. > Correct Answer: (B) ## Targeted Practice: 8 In a certain country, in each of the past five years, 70 percent of citizens responding to surveys have said they prefer to take high speed trains to travel long distances, and 30 percent have said they prefer to drive to travel long distances. 1. [ ] (A) The percentage will increase. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) The percentage will decrease. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) The percentage will remain the same. Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
Basic laws of supply and demand tell us that, when the supply of an item (such as sugar) drops, the price of the item rises. Thus, we’d expect an increase in the cost of sugar sold in Country X.
Notice that the price of sugar in Country X dropping would be going in the wrong direction. In other words, the price of sugar dropping would be counter to what we would expect when the supply of sugar decreases. Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
Since a drop in the number of available parking spots for Restaurant R makes it harder for people to get to it, the logical implication of the information provided is that fewer people will dine at Restaurant R.
Notice that we’d be going in the wrong direction if we said that more people would dine at Restaurant R.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
In this case, we can use common knowledge of how businesses operate to determine the likely result of the fact that the companies to which Munsford Inc. supplies paint reported a decrease in orders. The likely result is that the amount of paint sold by Munsford Inc. will decrease.
After all, if the companies have fewer orders for new homes, it’s logical that they will build fewer homes and therefore use less paint per month.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
Since a price decrease incentivizes people to make more purchases, we’d expect the hotel to sell more rooms.
At the same time, the hotel will receive 20 percent less than usual for each room it sells.
So, since the number of rooms sold will increase but the price per room will decrease, what will happen to the hotel’s daily income is unclear. After all, we don’t know which will have more of an effect, the increase in rooms sold or the decrease in prices.
Correct Answer: (C) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
Since the increase in the cost of gasoline would make gasoline powered cars less desirable while the decrease in the cost of electrify would make fully electric vehicles more desirable, we’d expect sales of fully electric vehicles to increase.
Notice that the idea that sales of fully electric cars would decrease goes in the opposite direction of what’s logical. Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 6 Explanation:
The information we have is that the shop is forced to sell coffee that tastes “different” from the coffee it normally sells.
Notice that coffee that tastes “different” could taste better, worse, or no better or worse to the shop’s customers. So, the information we have does not indicate how the shop’s customers will respond to the change in coffee.
Thus, the information provided does not clearly indicate anything about sales of coffee at the coffee shop.
Correct Answer: (C) Targeted Practice: 7 Explanation:
In this scenario, the consultants recommended each of the three actions as a way to reduce air pollution. So, even though Oak Hills took only one of the actions, installing bike paths, and did not take the other two, we would still expect levels of air pollution to decrease.
After all, although the city could have reduced air pollution more by taking all three actions, the city’s taking one of the actions can be expected to result in levels of air pollution going in a downward direction.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 8 Explanation:
The key thing to notice in this case is that the percentage of citizens saying they prefer to take high speed trains and the percentage saying they prefer to drive have remained the same for five years. So, the situation has been stable.
Thus, even though more citizens say they prefer to take high speed trains than say they prefer to drive, what makes the most sense is that the percentage of citizens who take high speed trains will remain the same.
After all, if every year, 70 percent of citizens say they prefer to take high speed trains and 30 percent say they prefer to drive, then it’s reasonable to expect that, all else equal, the situation will be stable, with around 70 percent of people taking high speed trains and 30 percent driving.
Let’s now use our skill in seeing logical implications to answer some example questions.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 3 In the United States, from 1920 to 1930, the number of automobiles in use increased significantly. Which of the following, if true about the United States between 1920 and 1930, most helps to explain why the price of crude oil decreased despite the increase in demand for gasoline? 1. [ ] (A) In the United States, the average automobile burned approximately the same amount of fuel per mile in 1930 as the average automobile burned in 1920. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Between 1920 and 1930, discoveries of new oil fields in the United States allowed producers to increase production of crude oil by over 100 percent. 3. [ ] (C) United States oil producers exported significantly more crude oil in 1930 than they did in 1920. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) From 1920 to 1930, the number of refineries that could produce gasoline from crude oil in the United States increased substantially. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) In the United States, from 1920 to 1930, it cost less per barrel for a large oil company to produce crude oil than it did for a small oil company to produce crude oil. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: N/A We see that the passage in this question presents a scenario in which demand for gasoline increased, but the price of crude oil, from which gasoline is derived, decreased.
Then, the question stem asks us to find a choice that “most helps to explain why the price of crude oil decreased despite the increase in demand for gasoline.”
If we think about this question in terms of logical implications, we see that the correct answer must have the following logical implication:
If the correct answer is true, a likely result is that the price of oil decreased.
So, let’s go through the choices to find one that has that implication.
(A) In the United States, the average automobile burned approximately the same amount of fuel per mile in 1930 as the average automobile burned in 1920. Notice that the implication of this choice is what we already know from the passage. After all, if the average automobile burned approximately the same amount of fuel per mile in 1930 as the average automobile burned in 1920, then we would expect that, as the number of automobiles increased, the amount of gasoline demanded would also increase. And in fact, the passage says, “the number of automobiles in use increased significantly. As a result, the demand for gasoline also increased.”
Since we don’t need to explain why the demand for gasoline increased as the number of automobiles increased, this choice doesn’t do what the correct answer must do.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Between 1920 and 1930, discoveries of new oil fields in the United States allowed producers to increase production of crude oil by over 100 percent. Let’s think about the implications of this choice.
What would be the logical result of the fact that discoveries of new oil fields in the United States allowed producers to increase production of crude oil by over 100 percent? All else equal, the result would be a reduction in the price of crude oil. Right?
After all, if producers were able to increase production of crude oil by over 100 percent, then the supply of crude oil grew significantly, and it’s common knowledge that, when the supply of something increases, all else equal, the price of that thing decreases.
Now, it’s true that all else was not equal, since demand for gasoline, which is derived from crude oil, increased. All the same, since an increase in the supply of crude oil would have had the effect of pushing the price of crude oil lower, this choice helps to explain why, even though demand for gasoline increased, the price of crude oil decreased.
(C) United States oil producers exported significantly more crude oil in 1930 than they did in 1920. Notice that an implication of this choice is that demand for crude oil was even stronger than the information in the passage indicated. After all, if United States oil producers exported significantly more crude oil in 1930 than they did in 1920, then not only did demand for gasoline, which is derived from crude oil, increase in the United States, but also the amount of United States crude oil used outside the United States increased.
We can see that the fact that there was demand for United States crude oil outside the United States would not explain why the price of crude oil decreased. The fact that there was an additional source of demand would be expected to cause the price to increase, not decrease.
(D) From 1920 to 1930, the number of refineries that could produce gasoline from crude oil in the United States increased substantially. The fact that the number of refineries that could produce gasoline from crude oil in the United States increased substantially doesn’t have clear implications with regard to the price of crude oil. Supply of and demand for crude oil itself would affect the price, not the fact that refineries could produce gasoline from crude oil.
That said, if anything, the increase in the number of refineries could perhaps have explained an increase in the price of crude oil. After all, an increase in the number of refineries could have resulted in an increase in the rate at which crude oil was used to make gasoline and thus resulted in an increase in demand for crude oil. So, if anything, this choice does the opposite of what the correct answer must do.
(E) In the United States, from 1920 to 1930, it cost less per barrel for a large oil company to produce crude oil than it did for a small oil company to produce crude oil. The fact that it cost less per barrel for a large oil company to produce crude oil than it did for a small oil company to produce crude oil doesn’t have any clear implications with regard to the price of crude oil. After all, we don’t have information about the sizes of the companies that produced crude oil between 1920 and 1930. So, we have no reason to believe that the sizes of oil companies had anything to do with the decrease in the price of crude oil.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 4 The teachers at Wildwood High School plan to begin using a method of teaching math in the classroom that has been shown to be much more effective than the method the school uses currently. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument? 1. [ ] (A) Teachers at Wildwood High School have been teaching math using the current method for over five years. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) The teachers at Wildwood High School do not have plans to change the methods they use for teaching reading or science. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Most of the students at Wildwood High School attend almost all of their math classes. 4. [ ] (D) Historically, the performance in math of Wildwood High School students has been higher than the national average. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) For some Wildwood High School students, doing homework thoroughly is much more important for their math performance than learning in the classroom. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: N/A Let’s break down the argument presented by the passage:
Premise: The teachers at Wildwood High School plan to begin using a method of teaching math in the classroom that has been shown to be much more effective than the method the school uses currently.
Conclusion: Clearly, therefore, the performance in math of Wildwood High School students will improve.
We see that the argument uses the fact that the teachers at Wildwood High School plan to use a more effective method of teaching math in the classroom to support the conclusion that the performance in math of the students will improve.
Then, the question asks, “Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?”
So, the correct answer to this question must strengthen the argument. In other words, it must provide additional support for the conclusion.
We can also think about the correct answer in terms of logical implications. To strengthen the argument, the correct answer must have the following logical implication:
If the correct answer is true, the likely result is that the performance in math of Wildwood High School Students will improve.
So, let’s consider the answer choices with these ideas in mind.
(A) Teachers at Wildwood High School have been teaching math using the current method for over five years. The fact that teachers at Wildwood High School have been using the current method to teach math for over five years has no implications for the effect of switching to a more effective method of teaching math. After all, regardless of how long the teachers at Wildwood High School have been using the current method, switching to the new method may or may not result in an improvement in students’ performance in math.
(B) The teachers at Wildwood High School do not have plans to change the methods they use for teaching reading or science. The fact that teachers at Wildwood High School do not have plans to change the methods they use for teaching reading or science has no implications for the effect of switching to a more effective method of teaching math. After all, regardless of whether the teachers change the methods they use for teaching reading or science, switching to the new method of teaching math may or may not result in an improvement in students’ performance in math.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Most of the students at Wildwood High School attend almost all of their math classes. Let’s think about the logical implications of this choice in the context of what we already know about Wildwood High School. The passage tells us that the teachers plan to begin using a more effective method of teaching math in the classroom.
Now, this choice tells us that students at Wildwood High attend most of their math classes. So, what are the implications of that fact?
Well, one of the implications is that the students will be in class when the teachers use the more effective method for teaching math. As a result, the teachers will be able to teach the students by using the new method in the classroom, and thus, there’s reason to believe that the student’s performance in math will indeed improve because of the teachers’ using the new method.
Thus, we can see that this choice strengthens the argument, because its implications give us more reason to believe that the students’ performance in math will improve.
(D) Historically, the performance in math of Wildwood High School students has been higher than the national average. The fact that the performance in math of Wildwood High School students has been higher than the national average has no clear implications for the effect of switching to a more effective method of teaching math. After all, regardless of whether the performance in math of the students has been higher than the national average, switching to the new method of teaching math may or may not result in an improvement in the students’ performance in math.
(E) For some Wildwood High School students, doing homework thoroughly is much more important for their math performance than learning in the classroom. Notice that, if anything, the implications of this choice are the opposite of what we need. After all, one implication of this choice is that changing the way math is taught in the classroom may not affect some students’ math performance. After all, if doing homework is much more important for these students’ math performance than learning in the classroom, then a change in what goes on in the classroom may not make much difference in how these students do.
In other words, an implication of this choice is that, at least in the cases of some students, the teachers’ using a more effective method may not result in improvements in math performance.
Correct Answer: (C) There are some common Critical Reasoning mistakes that we see people make again and again.
summary:
Lesson Content To do well in Critical Reasoning, we must see the logical implications of statements in Critical Reasoning passages and answer choices.
A logical implication is simply the logical consequence of a set of facts or conditions.
💡MUST KNOW:
A logical implication is simply the logical consequence of a set of facts or conditions.
We can present a logical implication with an “if/then” statement.
In the above “if/then” statement, Y is the logical implication of X.
title: 1.12 To Do Well in Critical Reasoning, See the Logical Implications of Statements

contents:
Lesson Content Many students have learned a gimmicky strategy for answering Critical Reasoning questions: read the question passage, read the question stem, and then “pre-think” what the correct answer will be.
Let’s make this very simple – do not pre-think.
💡MUST KNOW:
Do not “pre-think” answers by attempting to read the question passage, read the question stem, and then “pre-think” what the correct answer will be. Doing so will likely limit your ability to become skilled at answering Critical Reasoning questions.
Here’s why pre-thinking doesn’t serve us well.
First, pre-thinking takes time.
💡MUST KNOW:
Pre-think? Nah - you are not playing an elementary school game; you’re seeking to get into business school.
Second, even though we may be well aware that a pre-thought answer may not turn out to be the correct answer to a question, having a pre-thought answer in mind when we go through the answer choices can distract us and hamper our ability to find the correct answer.
💡MUST KNOW:
Often, a test-taker who pre-thinks goes through all the answer choices in a question looking for a pre-thought answer only to have to go through all the choices again when none of the choices matches the pre-thought answer.
Finally, the test-maker has massive amounts of data on the mistakes that we are likely to make if we pre-think answer choices, and this data very surely is used to create trap answer choices.
💡MUST KNOW:
When we pre-think, we’re liable to be affected by one of a number of extremely well-researched cognitive biases, and choose trap choices.
TTP also has large amounts of data on how students incorrectly “pre-think” the answers to the questions, and yes, to better prepare you, we’ve used this data to engineer trap answer choices in our Critical Reasoning questions.
So, pre-thinking can make it significantly harder for us to arrive at the correct answers to Critical Reasoning questions and, at the same time, make it significantly easier to fall into a trap.
💡MUST KNOW:
Pre-thinking can make it significantly harder for us to arrive at the correct answers to Critical Reasoning questions and, at the same time, make it significantly easier to fall into a trap.
Let your competition suffer with gimmicky strategies.
💡MUST KNOW:
Let your competition suffer with gimmicky strategies. Let them struggle. We’re going to show you how to get the job done.
Let’s now discuss another gimmicky strategy not to use, eliminating or choosing an answer choice on the basis of the presence of one word.
summary:
Lesson Content Many students have learned a gimmicky strategy for answering Critical Reasoning questions: read the question passage, read the question stem, and then “pre-think” what the correct answer will be.
Let’s make this very simple – do not pre-think.
💡MUST KNOW:
Do not “pre-think” answers by attempting to read the question passage, read the question stem, and then “pre-think” what the correct answer will be. Doing so will likely limit your ability to become skilled at answering Critical Reasoning questions.
title: 1.13 Do Not Attempt to “Pre-Think” the Answers to Critical Reasoning Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Since a single word in a Critical Reasoning answer choice can have a significant effect on the meaning conveyed by that choice, people often get the impression that it makes sense to eliminate or choose Critical Reasoning answer choices because of the presence of particular words in the choices.
For instance, since answer choices that use words such as “all,” “none,” “always,” or “never” state things that are extreme, people often use the strategy of eliminating any choice that uses such a word, terming such choices “extreme choices.”
Another word the presence of which people sometimes feel constitutes a reason to eliminate a choice is the word “some.” People eliminate choices that include “some” because they assume that a choice that uses “some” says something too vague to be a correct answer.
The issue with using such an approach is that, while it is true that “extreme choices” and some other types of choices that people often eliminate because of the presence of a particular word are more often than not incorrect choices, such choices are not always incorrect.
They aren’t mastering Critical Reasoning; they are playing the odds, and the results they get reflect what they are doing.
So, what should we do instead?
Rather than eliminate a choice because it uses a particular word, we can suspect that a choice is incorrect because it uses a particular word and carefully determine whether, in using that word, the choice does or does not do what the correct answer to the question must do.
💡MUST KNOW:
Eliminating Critical Reasoning answer choices on the basis of the presence of single words is an unreliable, low quality approach to answering Critical Reasoning questions. For reliable results and high scores, we have to carefully evaluate the effect of every Critical Reasoning answer choice regardless of what words it uses.
Remember, Critical Reasoning questions, and GMAT verbal in general, test the level of sophistication of the thinking we use in answering the questions, and eliminating a choice because the choice uses a certain word is not a sophisticated method; it’s a gimmick.
Let’s now discuss another move we should never make in answering a Critical Reasoning question.
summary:
Lesson Content Since a single word in a Critical Reasoning answer choice can have a significant effect on the meaning conveyed by that choice, people often get the impression that it makes sense to eliminate or choose Critical Reasoning answer choices because of the presence of particular words in the choices.
For instance, since answer choices that use words such as “all,” “none,” “always,” or “never” state things that are extreme, people often use the strategy of eliminating any choice that uses such a word, terming such choices “extreme choices.
title: 1.14 Do Not Eliminate or Choose a Critical Reasoning Answer Choice Because of the Presence of One Word

contents:
Lesson Content Often, because they want to save time, or simply because they don’t feel like reading every word, test-takers skip or gloss over words in Critical Reasoning passages, question stems, or answer choices or decide not to read the last several words of a passage, question stem, or answer choice.
In fact, what often happens is that a test-taker who has skipped words in a Critical Reasoning question flails around and repeatedly goes through the answer choices without finding the correct answer because, in skipping those words, the test-taker has failed to acquire the information necessary for answering the question.
Of course, a test-taker’s skipping words in a question can also result in the test-taker’s not finding the correct answer at all, choosing an incorrect choice, and missing the question.
So, we can see that carefully reading every word of a Critical Reasoning question can end up being not only the surest but also the shortest path to the correct answer.
💡MUST KNOW:
Skipping words in reading Critical Reasoning passages, question stems, or answer choices can be devastating to Critical Reasoning performance. So, to maximize performance, when answering a Critical Reasoning question, carefully read every word.
Let’s consider an example to see how our performance can be affected by whether we skip words or carefully read every word of a Critical Reasoning question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 5 In the past, most purchasers of automobiles prioritized performance over eco-friendliness when considering which automobile to purchase. Which of the following, if true, would cast the most doubt on the conclusion of the argument above? 1. [ ] (A) Automobiles are, on average, more eco-friendly than they were in the past. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) In general, using public transportation is more eco-friendly than using even the most eco-friendly automobile. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Currently, people are generally more aware of the ecological effects of automobile use than they were in the past. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) A large percentage of people consider durability the most important quality of an automobile. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Currently, many of the most eco-friendly new automobiles are also the highest performing new automobiles. View Solution Question Level: easy The passage in this question states the following conclusion:
Conclusion: It must be the case that, now, more people than ever prioritize eco-friendliness over performance in choosing among automobiles.
The passage provides the following premise to support the conclusion:
Premise: In recent years, the percentage of people choosing to purchase the most eco-friendly automobiles available has increased significantly.
We see that the question stem is the following:
Which of the following, if true, would cast the most doubt on the conclusion of the argument above?
So, to correctly answer the question we have to find the choice that casts the most doubt on the conclusion.
Now, here’s the thing.
If we read the conclusion in its entirety, we’re likely to answer this question correctly, but if we gloss over or fail to read some words of the conclusion, we could easily end up choosing an incorrect answer.
For instance, in reading the conclusion, we could gloss over the words “over performance,” reading the conclusion in the following way:
It must be the case that, now, more people than ever prioritize eco-friendliness over performance in choosing among automobiles.
Notice what happens if we gloss over “over performance.” The conclusion could seem to be that eco-friendliness is people’s top or main priority in choosing an automobile. So, by glossing over “over performance,” we could get the impression that the conclusion is different from what it is.
Another thing we could do in reading the conclusion is fail to read the last few words, reading the conclusion in the following way:
It must be the case that, now, more people than ever prioritize eco-friendliness over performance in choosing among automobiles.
See what happens if we don’t read the last few words? If we fail to read the last few words, we could get the impression that the conclusion is that people prioritize eco-friendliness over performance in general, not just “in choosing among automobiles.”
OK, let’s now review the answer choices and see how glossing over or failing to read some words of the conclusion could affect how we answer this question.
(A) Automobiles are, on average, more eco-friendly than they were in the past. Regardless of how eco-friendly cars are, the fact that the percentage of people choosing to purchase the most eco-friendly automobiles available has increased significantly still supports the conclusion that, now, more people than ever prioritize eco-friendliness over performance in choosing among automobiles.
So, the fact that cars are, on average, more eco-friendly than they were in the past does not cast any doubt on the conclusion.
(B) In general, using public transportation is more eco-friendly than using even the most eco-friendly automobile. Eliminating this choice should be fairly straightforward since the conclusion of the argument is about people’s prioritizing eco-friendliness in choosing among automobiles, while this choice expresses a comparison of the eco-friendliness of public transportation and the eco-friendliness of automobiles. In other words, the fact that using public transportation is more eco-friendly than using an automobile does not indicate that, in choosing among automobiles, people are not prioritizing eco-friendliness over performance.
Notice, however, what could happen if we were to fail to read the last few words of the conclusion and misread it in the following way:
It must be the case that, now, more people than ever prioritize eco-friendliness over performance in choosing among automobiles.
By failing to read the last few words of the conclusion, we could perceive the conclusion to be that, now, more people than ever prioritize eco-friendliness over performance in general. In that case, we could get the impression that this choice casts doubt on the conclusion.
After all, if it is true that using public transportation is more eco-friendly than using even the most eco-friendly automobile, then we could make the case that people who are purchasing automobiles, rather than using public transportation, are not prioritizing eco-friendliness over performance in general. Thus, we could make the case that this choice casts doubt on our mistaken version of the conclusion.
You may think that you would never choose a choice on the basis of such shaky reasoning, but remember, when taking the GMAT, you will be operating under time constraints and will have to make relatively quick decisions regarding which choices are incorrect or correct, and any choice that seems to cast doubt on the conclusion could be very tempting.
The moral of the story is that reading every word of the question is important for Critical Reasoning success.
(C) Currently, people are generally more aware of the ecological effects of automobile use than they were in the past. What this choice says is in line with the conclusion that more people than ever prioritize eco-friendliness over performance in choosing among automobiles.
In fact, what this choice says could explain why more people than ever prioritize eco-friendliness over performance in choosing among automobiles. After all, people’s being more aware of the ecological effects of automobile use could cause them to prioritize eco-friendliness over performance in choosing an automobile.
So, this choice certainly does not cast doubt on the conclusion.
(D) A large percentage of people consider durability the most important quality of an automobile. Eliminating this choice should be fairly straightforward.
The conclusion of the argument is that more people than ever prioritize eco-friendliness over performance in choosing an automobile, and more people than ever could do so regardless of how many people consider durability the most important quality of an automobile. After all, even if people’s order of priorities is (1) durability, (2) eco-friendliness, and (3) performance, they are prioritizing eco-friendliness over performance, even though durability is their number one priority.
So, what this choice says cast no doubt whatsoever on the conclusion.
Notice, however, what could happen if we were to gloss over the words “over performance” and misread the conclusion in the following way:
It must be the case that, now, more people than ever prioritize eco-friendliness over performance in choosing among automobiles.
If we gloss over “over performance,” the conclusion could seem to be that eco-friendliness is people’s top or main priority in choosing an automobile. Of course, in that case, we could easily get the impression that what this choice says, that a large percentage of people consider durability the most important quality of an automobile, casts doubt on our mistaken version of the conclusion. After all, if a large percentage of people consider durability the most important quality of an automobile, then it could seem plausible that eco-friendliness is not the top or main priority of more people than ever.
You may think that you would never choose an answer choice on the basis of such shaky reasoning, but remember, when taking the GMAT, you will be operating under time constraints and will have to make relatively quick decisions regarding which choices are incorrect or correct, and any choice that seems to cast doubt on the conclusion could be very tempting.
The moral of the story is that reading every word of the question is important for Critical Reasoning success.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Currently, many of the most eco-friendly new automobiles are also the highest performing new automobiles. What this choice says casts doubt on the conclusion.
The conclusion that, now, more people than ever prioritize eco-friendliness over performance in choosing among automobiles is supported by the premise that the percentage of people choosing to purchase the most eco-friendly automobiles available has increased significantly.
However, what if, as this choice says, many of the most eco-friendly new automobiles are also the highest performing ones? In that case, it could very well be that people are not prioritizing eco-friendliness over performance and are purchasing the most eco-friendly automobiles only because the most eco-friendly automobiles are also the highest performing automobiles.
So, what this choice says is a reason to believe that, even though the percentage of people choosing to purchase the most eco-friendly automobiles available has increased, it could very well be that the conclusion is not correct.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss why we should not eliminate Critical Reasoning answer choices just because they bring in new information.
summary:
Lesson Content Often, because they want to save time, or simply because they don’t feel like reading every word, test-takers skip or gloss over words in Critical Reasoning passages, question stems, or answer choices or decide not to read the last several words of a passage, question stem, or answer choice.
In fact, what often happens is that a test-taker who has skipped words in a Critical Reasoning question flails around and repeatedly goes through the answer choices without finding the correct answer because, in skipping those words, the test-taker has failed to acquire the information necessary for answering the question.
title: 1.15 Do Not Skip Words When Reading Critical Reasoning Passages, Question Stems, or Answer Choices in an Attempt to Save Time.

contents:
Lesson Content Many people get the impression that they should robotically eliminate any Critical Reasoning answer choice that brings in new information that is different from what is discussed in the passage.
“The author never said anything about ants in the argument. ”
“This answer choice brings in new information. ”
“What does the Mediterranean Sea have to do with Lake Michigan?”
“This answer choice seems off-topic. ”
“This answer choice came out of nowhere. ”
“This answer choice is out of scope. ” Sometimes, eliminating an answer choice that brings in information that was not discussed in the passage does make sense.
However, the truth of the matter is that many correct answers to Critical Reasoning questions bring in new information that is rather different from anything said by the passage, and if we eliminate choices just because they bring in new information, we won’t maximize our scores.
💡MUST KNOW:
Many correct answers to Critical Reasoning questions bring in new information that is different from anything said by the passage.
For example, correct answers to Weaken the Argument and Strengthen the Argument questions – the two most common types of Critical Reasoning questions – often bring in information that is quite different from anything said by the passages.
Therefore, we cannot eliminate an answer choice simply because it brings in new information that is different from what is discussed in the passage.
We’ll talk more about this idea later, but to get off to a good start, just remember that we cannot eliminate an answer choice simply because it brings in new information that is different from what is discussed in the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
We cannot eliminate an answer choice simply because it brings in new information that is different from what is discussed in the passage.
Let’s showcase this idea with a simple Weaken the Argument question (Be kind to yourself; if you don’t get it correct, it’s OK.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 6 Over the past year, brown bears that inhabit the nearby mountains have been coming into the Town of Silverton with greater regularity than ever before. Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument? 1. [x] (A) One year ago, a number of farms in the town began for the first time growing blueberries, which are a favorite food of brown bears. 2. [ ] (B) People living in the town have grown increasingly nervous because of the increased presence of the brown bears. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Over the past year, no more mountain lions have been sighted in the town than were sighted during previous years. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) The owners of the town’s honeybee farms have reported seeing brown bears opening their beehives to eat the honey found within. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Brown bears find honey irresistible because it is high in both sugar and calories, two things that they require in order to maintain a healthy weight. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy Let’s break down the argument.
Background: Over the past year, brown bears that inhabit the nearby mountains have been coming into the Town of Silverton with greater regularity than ever before.
Premise: About a year ago, three farms in the town began for the first time producing honey, which brown bears find irresistible.
Conclusion: It must be that the reason why brown bears are coming into the town with greater regularity is that they are coming to consume the honey produced by these farms.
The question asks, “Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument?”
So, our goal is to weaken the support for the conclusion that bears are coming into the town more regularly now because of the honey being produced there. Anything that casts some doubt on this conclusion will weaken the argument.
We need to keep in mind that we won’t disregard an answer choice simply because it presents new information different from anything the passage says. In other words, even if an answer choice presents information different from anything the passage says, if that answer choice weakens the argument, it will be correct.
YOUR CHOICE(A) One year ago, a number of farms in the town began for the first time growing blueberries, which are a favorite food of brown bears. Many people just starting to learn about Critical Reasoning quickly eliminate this answer choice. They say, “This is an argument about honey. Blueberries have nothing to do with honey. Therefore, what this answer choice says is out of scope.”
Not so fast.
The conclusion is that the reason why brown bears are coming into the town with greater regularity is that they are coming to consume the honey that farms have started to produce.
How could we cast doubt on this claim? Couldn’t we do so by finding another plausible reason why brown bears are coming into the town with greater regularity? Definitely, right?
Notice that, although this answer choice is about blueberries, something not mentioned in the passage, what this answer choice says absolutely weakens the argument, because it brings up an alternative possible reason why the brown bears are coming into the town with greater regularity.
In other words, the author claims that it is the honey. This answer choice casts doubt on that claim by bringing up the possibility that it might not be the honey at all; it might just be the blueberries!
(B) People living in the town have grown increasingly nervous because of the increased presence of the brown bears. Although what this answer choice says is very plausible given what the passage says, the fact that people living in the town have grown increasingly nervous because of the increased presence of the brown bears does not weaken the argument.
In other words, even if what this answer choice says is true, it still makes sense to conclude that the reason why bears are coming into the town is that they are coming to consume honey.
(C) Over the past year, no more mountain lions have been sighted in the town than were sighted during previous years. Notice that this answer choice is quite different from answer choice (A). Whereas choice (A) brings up new information, and that new information weakens the argument, this choice brings up new information, and that new information does NOT weaken the argument.
After all, even if, over the past year, no more mountain lions have been sighted in the town than were sighted during previous years, that fact does not cast doubt on the author’s claim about the brown bears and the honey. In fact, if anything, the fact that bears, which find honey irresistible, are coming into the town more, while mountain lions do not seem to be coming into the town more, tends to confirm that the presence of the honey is the reason for the change in the bears’ behavior.
So, yes, we will eliminate this answer choice. Notice, however, that we are not eliminating it because it brings in new information. We’re eliminating it because what it says does not weaken the argument.
(D) The owners of the town’s honeybee farms have reported seeing brown bears opening their beehives to eat the honey found within. What this answer choice says is in line with the conclusion of the argument. The fact that the owners of farms in the town have reported seeing brown bears opening their beehives to eat honey fits perfectly with the conclusion that the reason why brown bears are coming into the town with greater regularity is that they are coming to consume the honey produced by the farms. Therefore, what this answer choice says certainly does not weaken the argument.
(E) Brown bears find honey irresistible because it is high in both sugar and calories, two things that they require in order to maintain a healthy weight. What this answer choice says explains a premise in the argument.
Notice that the passage says that “brown bears find honey irresistible. ”
So, what this choice does is simply explain why brown bears find honey irresistible. They find it irresistible because it is high in both sugar and calories, two things that they require in order to remain at a healthy weight.
What this answer choice says certainly does not weaken the argument.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss why you must discover and then address habits that lead to choosing incorrect answers.
summary:
Lesson Content Many people get the impression that they should robotically eliminate any Critical Reasoning answer choice that brings in new information that is different from what is discussed in the passage.
“The author never said anything about ants in the argument. ”
“This answer choice brings in new information. ”
“What does the Mediterranean Sea have to do with Lake Michigan?”
“This answer choice seems off-topic. ”
“This answer choice came out of nowhere.
title: 1.16 Do Not Eliminate an Answer Choice Just Because it Brings in New Information.

contents:
Lesson Content Test-takers often miss Critical Reasoning questions for reasons that were predictable and avoidable.
💡MUST KNOW:
A key aspect of improving Critical Reasoning performance is discovering and then addressing habits that lead to choosing incorrect answers.
Look at the below list of common reasons people miss Critical Reasoning questions that they could have gotten correct.
I missed important details in the passage.
I failed to read the entire passage.
I failed to read entirely an answer choice that I eliminated / chose.
I attempted to answer the question without fully understanding the passage.
I chose an answer that answered a question different from the question asked.
I failed to note / lost track of the conclusion.
I eliminated the correct answer because it seemed irrelevant.
I attempted to pre-think the answer choice.
I made up a convoluted, unsupported story to support my choice.
I chose a trap choice because it was worded to sound like what the passage says.
I chose a trap choice because it seemed to have the effect I was looking for.
I ran out of time.
I did not keep all of the information straight.
Each time you get a question incorrect that you should have gotten correct, it’s extremely important to make a note of why you got the question incorrect.
💡MUST KNOW:
The TTP Course makes it easy for you to log your errors. Each time you get a question wrong, tell the course why. Then, we’ll create a powerful error log for you to study with.
Your goal is to track and recognize all of the reasons why you miss Critical Reasoning questions and then address those reasons to maximize your Critical Reasoning performance.
Let’s now discuss the fact that we don’t need any specialized, outside knowledge to answer critical reasoning questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Test-takers often miss Critical Reasoning questions for reasons that were predictable and avoidable.
💡MUST KNOW:
A key aspect of improving Critical Reasoning performance is discovering and then addressing habits that lead to choosing incorrect answers.
Look at the below list of common reasons people miss Critical Reasoning questions that they could have gotten correct.
I missed important details in the passage.
I failed to read the entire passage.
title: 1.17 To Improve Your Critical Reasoning Performance, You Must Discover and Then Address Habits That Lead to Choosing Incorrect Answers

contents:
Lesson Content Correctly answering Critical Reasoning questions does not require any outside, specialized knowledge.
💡MUST KNOW:
Correctly answering Critical Reasoning questions does not require any outside, specialized knowledge. Any outside information necessary for answering these questions is considered “common knowledge” among people with common sense and a basic level of education.
In other words, yes, we will encounter passages on economics, science, music theory, politics, and psychology, to name a few topics, but any outside information we’ll need to answer Critical Reasoning questions will be information that is considered generally known, and widely accepted, by the general population.
For example, we might need to know that profit = the money taken in by selling something – the cost of selling that thing, but we would never be required to know the relationship between mortgage interest rates and bond yields.
For example, we might need to know that depression is a condition that can involve a person’s feeling unhappy, but we would never need to know that depression can be caused by the environment or food choices.
Realizing that we are not expected to have any outside, specialized knowledge to answer these questions can help us a lot.
💡MUST KNOW:
Any answer choice that would require our having advanced or specialized knowledge in any particular field to choose that answer choice as the correct answer can’t be the correct answer to a Critical Reasoning question.
Now, that all said, Critical Reasoning questions do often discuss themes, concepts, plans, and processes that are well-outside of the range of what is considered common knowledge, but still, any specialized knowledge that we might need to correctly answer the question will be contained within the four corners of the question; we won’t be required to have that specialized knowledge committed to memory.
If Critical Reasoning questions don’t require any specialized, outside knowledge, then why are Critical Reasoning questions challenging? Well, what’s challenging is the logical reasoning we have to do in order to answer them.
Let’s now talk about timing and Critical Reasoning questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Correctly answering Critical Reasoning questions does not require any outside, specialized knowledge.
💡MUST KNOW:
Correctly answering Critical Reasoning questions does not require any outside, specialized knowledge. Any outside information necessary for answering these questions is considered “common knowledge” among people with common sense and a basic level of education.
In other words, yes, we will encounter passages on economics, science, music theory, politics, and psychology, to name a few topics, but any outside information we’ll need to answer Critical Reasoning questions will be information that is considered generally known, and widely accepted, by the general population.
title: 1.18 We Don’t Need Specialized, Outside Knowledge to Answer Critical Reasoning Questions

contents:
Lesson Content The verbal section of the GMAT allots 65 minutes for answering 36 questions, or an average time of 1.8 minutes per question (about 1 minute and 48 seconds per question).
Of course, if you’ll need to average 1.8 minutes per question and yet may spend 2 minutes per Critical Reasoning question, where will the extra time come from? In general, Sentence Corrections take less time, on average, than Critical Reasoning questions.
💡MUST KNOW:
On test day you’ll likely spend about 1.75 to 2 minutes on each Critical Reasoning question. However, some of the more difficult Critical Reasoning questions may take you up to three minutes to answer.
That said, answering Critical Reasoning questions when you are taking the GMAT and practicing answering Critical Reasoning questions when you are preparing to take the GMAT are two different activities.
💡MUST KNOW:
When you are practicing, you should answer the Critical Reasoning questions you see untimed.
This information is very important.
No matter what you have heard from anyone else about how to practice Critical Reasoning, do Critical Reasoning practice questions untimed, focus on understanding and accuracy, and let speed come with skill.
One of your main aims in practicing Critical Reasoning is to learn to see and clearly articulate what makes incorrect choices incorrect and correct answers correct.
As is the case with other types of GMAT questions, you won’t learn to succeed in Critical Reasoning by getting questions incorrect in two minutes each.
Let’s now talk about the fact that Critical Reasoning questions have been consistent over time.
summary:
Lesson Content The verbal section of the GMAT allots 65 minutes for answering 36 questions, or an average time of 1.8 minutes per question (about 1 minute and 48 seconds per question).
Of course, if you’ll need to average 1.8 minutes per question and yet may spend 2 minutes per Critical Reasoning question, where will the extra time come from? In general, Sentence Corrections take less time, on average, than Critical Reasoning questions.
title: 1.19 Timing and Critical Reasoning Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Many Critical Reasoning questions require that we determine what information is missing from the arguments presented to us.
💡MUST KNOW:
The ability to determine what information is missing from arguments is of significant importance to success in answering Critical Reasoning questions.
We’ll elaborate upon this point as the course proceeds, but we are about to see this important point front and center with assumptions.
summary:
Lesson Content Many Critical Reasoning questions require that we determine what information is missing from the arguments presented to us.
💡MUST KNOW:
The ability to determine what information is missing from arguments is of significant importance to success in answering Critical Reasoning questions.
We’ll elaborate upon this point as the course proceeds, but we are about to see this important point front and center with assumptions.
title: 2.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content As we discussed in the previous chapter, an assumption is an unstated piece of information that must be true in order for the logic of an argument to work.
To see more clearly how assumptions function in arguments, let’s consider a simple argument:
Dogs like to bark.
Therefore, Fido must like to bark.
Notice that we have one premise:
Dogs like to bark.
Given that premise, the author concludes the following:
Fido must like to bark.
In making this argument, the author has made an important assumption.
The author has assumed that Fido is a dog.
The author has used the premise that dogs like to bark to support the conclusion that Fido likes to bark, but the only way in which that premise supports the conclusion is by relying on the assumption that Fido is a dog.
Sure, dogs like to bark.
We see that the assumption that Fido is a dog is an unstated piece of information that must be true in order for the argument to work.
Let’s add that assumption to the argument:
Premise: Dogs like to bark.
Assumption: Fido is a dog.
Conclusion: Fido must like to bark.
OK, now the argument is more complete.
If this simple argument were turned into a simple Critical Reasoning question, it might look like the following:
Dogs like to bark. Therefore, Fido must like to bark.
Which of the following is an assumption upon which the author relies in arriving at the conclusion presented?
(A) Fido is a dog.
Pretty simple right? This question asks us to find an unstated piece of information upon which the argument relies.
Let’s look at a slightly harder example.
Today is not a holiday.
Therefore, the kids will be in school.
The author has one premise:
Today is not a holiday.
From that premise, he concludes the following:
The kids will be in school.
Does the author make any assumptions? He does, right? In making his argument, he assumes that there could not be another reason why the kids would not be in school.
Premise: Today is not a holiday.
Assumption: Holidays are the only days when kids are not in school.
Conclusion: Therefore, the kids will be in school.
Notice that, in order for this argument to work, it must be true that holidays are the only days when kids are not in school.
Today is not a holiday. Therefore, the kids will be in school.
Which of the following is an assumption upon which the author relies in arriving at the conclusion presented?
(A) Holidays are the only days when kids are not in school.
Not too bad, right? The question is simply asking us what the author assumes in making the argument.
Let’s now consider a somewhat more complex argument:
All people in houses in which communicable diseases are present should be quarantined. Two people in the yellow house on Maple Street came down with disease x yesterday. Therefore, all people in the yellow house on Maple Street should be quarantined.
We can break this argument down as follows:
Premise 1: All people in houses in which communicable diseases are present should be quarantined.
Premise 2: Two people in the yellow house on Maple Street came down with disease x yesterday.
Conclusion: All People in the yellow house on Maple Street should be quarantined.
As we will in answering any Assumption question, we must ask, “What is the missing information? What piece of information is unstated by the author yet must be true in order for the evidence presented to effectively support the conclusion?”
Can you tell what necessary piece of information is missing from this argument?
The missing piece of information is that disease x is a communicable disease.
Although the author does not explicitly state this information in making the argument, we can see that it is completely necessary to properly connect the premises to the conclusion, because without that assumption, there is a gap between the premises and the conclusion.
The only way this argument works is by including the assumption that disease x is in fact a communicable disease.
We can rewrite the structure of the argument:
Premise 1: All people in houses in which communicable diseases are present should be quarantined.
Premise 2: Two people in the yellow house on Maple Street came down with disease x yesterday.
Assumption: Disease x is a communicable disease.
Conclusion: All people in the yellow house on Maple Street should be quarantined.
Notice that this assumption is unstated information that MUST BE TRUE in order for the evidence presented to effectively support the conclusion.
Now, as you read that little argument about disease x, you may have on your own made the assumption that disease x is a communicable disease.
After all, can you imagine how hard it would be to have a conversation (or read an argument) if you had to stop every 10 seconds and ask for clarification on every assumption that was made? Having to do so would make communication almost impossible.
However, we have to become more conscious of assumptions when answering Critical Reasoning questions.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Critical Reasoning questions, we must be conscious of the assumptions upon which arguments depend, read actively, and seek to be aware of gaps in arguments presented in the questions.
Let’s now consider some example Assumption questions.
For best results, take your time in considering these questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 1 The Noodle House, which serves only noodles and has won the award for the best noodles in our state five years in a row, is opening a store in the Town of Redlands next month. Which of the following is an assumption upon which the support for the above conclusion depends? 1. [ ] (A) The mayor of Redlands does not eat any meals at home. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) The Town of Redlands has a small number of noodle restaurants. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Only award-winning restaurants open in the Town of Redlands. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) It costs less to dine at the Noodle House than at any other restaurant. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) The mayor of Redlands is not allergic to noodles. View Solution Question Level: easy Background: The Noodle House, which serves only noodles and has won the award for the best noodles in our state five years in a row, is opening a store in the Town of Redlands next month.
Premise: The mayor of Redlands loves eating award-winning food.
Conclusion: Therefore, the mayor of Redlands will love eating at the Noodle House.
Notice that there is a large gap in the argument. The author provides us with only one premise, that the mayor of Redlands loves eating award-winning food. From that one premise, she concludes that the mayor of Redlands will love eating at the Noodle House. On what grounds does the author feel justified in reaching her conclusion? What must be true in order for her argument to be valid?
(A) The mayor of Redlands does not eat any meals at home. Does it have to be the case that the mayor does not eat ANY meals at home in order for the mayor to love eating at the Noodle House? Of course not. Even if the mayor eats some meals at home, the mayor could still love eating at the Noodle House. So, what this choice says is not an assumption necessary in order for the argument to work.
(B) The Town of Redlands has a small number of noodle restaurants. Perhaps the Town of Redlands has a small number of noodle restaurants. Perhaps the Town of Redlands has a ton of noodle restaurants. Either way, the argument’s conclusion that the mayor will love eating at the Noodle House follows from the fact that the mayor loves eating award-winning food.
Thus, the argument does not depend on any assumptions about how many noodle restaurants are in Redlands.
(C) Only award-winning restaurants open in the Town of Redlands. It could be that only award-winning restaurants open in the Town of Redlands. Alternatively, it could be that a wide range of restaurants open in the Town of Redlands. Either way, the fact that the mayor loves eating award-winning food remains true, and the argument’s conclusion could still follow from that premise.
(D) It costs less to dine at the Noodle House than at any other restaurant. Given that the Noodle House has won awards and that the mayor loves eating award-winning food, would it make sense to conclude that the mayor will love eating at the Noodle House even if the Noodle House is not the least expensive restaurant in the entire universe? Sure it would.
The argument is not that the mayor loves eating inexpensive food. So, the argument could certainly work even if it costs a little more to dine at the Noodle House than it costs to dine at the cheapest restaurant that exists.
What this choice says does not have to be assumed in order for the argument to work.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The mayor of Redlands is not allergic to noodles. What this answer choice says must be true for the argument to work.
After all, if the mayor of Redlands WERE allergic to noodles, then the argument would fall apart because the conclusion that the mayor will love eating at the Noodle House would no longer follow from the premise.
Regardless of how many awards the Noodle House has won, if the mayor is allergic to noodles, he certainly will not be heading over to the Noodle House for a bowl of noodles. Therefore, the author MUST assume that the mayor is NOT allergic to noodles.
Notice that the argument cannot possibly work unless what this answer choice says is true.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 2 This past summer, the beach city of Hampton Falls began charging a ten dollar per person admission fee at one of its two public beaches, with the result that the city earned $50,000 in revenue during the summer beach season. Which of the following must be true in order for the above conclusion to follow from the evidence provided? 1. [ ] (A) The people who worked as lifeguards at the Hampton Falls beaches this past summer will be willing to do so again during the next summer beach season. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) During the next summer beach season, Hampton Falls will not be more crowded with beachgoers than it usually is. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) The next summer beach season in Hampton Falls will be two weeks shorter than this past summer’s beach season. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) The beaches in Hampton Falls will not have to be closed for all of next summer because of high bacteria counts in the ocean water along the beaches. 5. [ ] (E) Some people who went to a beach in Hampton Falls last summer will not go to a beach in Hampton Falls during the next summer beach season. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy Background: This past summer, the beach city of Hampton Falls began charging a ten dollar per person admission fee at one of its two public beaches, with the result that the city earned $50,000 in revenue during the summer beach season.
Premise: The city council has recently decided to begin charging the same admission fee at its other public beach.
Conclusion: Therefore, during the next summer beach season, Hampton Falls will be able to earn at least $100,000 by simultaneously charging admission to both of its public beaches.
(A) The people who worked as lifeguards at the Hampton Falls beaches this past summer will be willing to do so again during the next summer beach season. Unless the people going to the beaches are going almost exclusively to see the lifeguards, the author need not assume that the people who worked as lifeguards at the Hampton Falls beaches this past summer will be willing to do so again during the next summer beach season.
Even if none of the same lifeguards come back, the fact that the city council has recently decided to begin charging the same admission fee at the other public beach may support the conclusion that, during the next summer beach season, Hampton Falls will be able to earn at least $100,000 by charging admission to both of its public beaches.
(B) During the next summer beach season, Hampton Falls will not be more crowded with beachgoers than it usually is. Concluding that Hampton Falls will be able to earn at least $100,000 during the next summer beach season by charging admission to both beaches does not require assuming that Hampton Falls will not be more crowded with beachgoers than it usually is.
After all, if Hampton Falls IS more crowded with beachgoers than it usually is, then there should be plenty of people to pay admission to the two beaches, in which case it seems likely that Hampton Falls will be able to earn at least $100,000 by charging admission to both beaches.
So, what this choice says does not have to be true in order for the argument to work.
(C) The next summer beach season in Hampton Falls will be two weeks shorter than this past summer’s beach season. What this answer choices says goes in a direction opposite to the direction of the argument.
The conclusion is that Hampton Falls will be able to earn at least $100,000 by simultaneously charging admission to both of its public beaches. If next summer’s beach season in Hampton Falls will be two weeks shorter than this past summer’s beach season, then that is less time for Hampton Falls to earn $100,000. In that case, we have a bit less conviction that the plan will work.
Of course, we are not seeking an answer choice that weakens the argument. Rather, we’re seeking an assumption upon which the argument relies.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The beaches in Hampton Falls will not have to be closed for all of next summer because of high bacteria counts in the ocean water along the beaches. What this answer choice says MUST be true for the argument to be valid. After all, any time an author proposes a plan, he assumes that the plan will work and that there are not variables that will enter into the equation that will spoil the plan. In the TTP Course, we refer to such variables as “malicious variables.” When an author presents a plan, he assumes that there are no malicious variables that will ruin the plan.
However, imagine if the beaches in Hampton Falls WILL have to be closed for all of next summer because of high bacteria counts in the ocean water along the beaches. Wouldn’t that fact ruin the plan, rendering the author’s conclusion invalid? Definitely! After all, if the beaches will be closed for all of next summer, Hampton Falls certainly will NOT be able to earn at least $100,000 by charging admission to both of its public beaches.
Therefore, in order for this argument to work, the author MUST assume that the beaches in Hampton Falls WILL NOT have to be closed for all of next summer because of high bacteria counts in the ocean water along the beaches.
Notice that what this answer choice provides is an unwritten premise that MUST be true in order for the argument to work. In other words, this answer choice states an assumption upon which the argument relies.
(E) Some people who went to a beach in Hampton Falls last summer will not go to a beach in Hampton Falls during the next summer beach season. What this answer choice states is a reasonable prediction.
After all, it is unlikely that every person who went to a beach in Hampton Falls last summer will go to a beach in Hampton Falls next summer.
So, it would make sense to predict that, as this choice says, some people who went to a beach in Hampton Falls last summer will not go to a beach in Hampton Falls during the next summer beach season.
Of course, we are not seeking a choice that states a reasonable prediction. We’re seeking an assumption upon which the argument relies, and going from the premise that Hampton Falls is going to charge admission to both of its public beaches to the conclusion that Hampton Falls will be able to earn at least $100,000 certainly does not require assuming that some people who went to the beaches this past summer won’t go to the beaches next summer.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 3 MowGreen, a company that uses sustainable methods to maintain lawns, has been expanding quickly, and its services are now available in many areas of the country. Which of the following is an assumption logically necessary for coming to the conclusion of the above argument? 1. [x] (A) MowGreen’s management team considers profitability in determining whether to expand into an area. 2. [ ] (B) People in the area around Drawnville are not interested in using sustainable methods for maintaining their lawns. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) There remain in the country areas in which MowGreen is not currently operating into which it could profitably expand its operations. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) MowGreen operates profitably in all of the areas in which it currently operates. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Sustainable methods for maintaining lawns are generally more expensive to use than are less environmentally friendly methods. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: MowGreen has been expanding, and its services are available in many areas.
Premise: MowGreen would not be able to operate profitably in the Drawnville area.
Conclusion: One can be sure that MowGreen will not expand into the Drawnville area any time soon.
There is a gap in the argument. The premise says that MowGreen would not be able to operate profitably in the Drawnville area. The conclusion says that MowGreen will not expand into the area around Drawnville. What’s missing here? What must be true in order for the conclusion to be properly drawn from the premises? The correct answer to the question will fill in the gap between would not be able to operate profitably in the area and will not expand … into the area.
YOUR CHOICE(A) MowGreen’s management team considers profitability in determining whether to expand into an area. While it may seem obvious that, since MowGreen would not be able to operate profitably in the Drawnville area, MowGreen will not expand into that area any time soon, the truth is that going from the premise to the conclusion that MowGreen requires assuming that MowGreen’s management team considers profitability in determining whether to expand into an area.
After all, if MowGreen’s management team does NOT consider profitability in determining whether to expand into an area, then the fact that MowGreen would not be able to operate profitably in the Drawnville area does not support the conclusion, and this argument falls apart.
So, this choice states an assumption necessary for coming to the conclusion of the argument.
(B) People in the area around Drawnville are not interested in using sustainable methods for maintaining their lawns. While, if people in the Drawnville area were not interested in using sustainable lawn care methods, it would be unlikely that MowGreen would expand into that area, concluding that MowGreen will not expand into that area does not require assuming that people in that area are not interested in using sustainable lawn care methods.
After all, the fact that MowGreen would not be able to operate profitably in the Drawnville area could still support the conclusion even if, contrary to what this choice says, people in the area would be interested in using sustainable lawn care methods.
(C) There remain in the country areas in which MowGreen is not currently operating into which it could profitably expand its operations. Concluding that MowGreen will not expand into an area in which it cannot operate profitably does not require assuming that there are areas into which it could profitably expand. MowGreen does not have to expand at all.
(D) MowGreen operates profitably in all of the areas in which it currently operates. This choice may seem to be similar to choice (A), the correct answer, but there is a key difference.
Notice that expanding and operating are not the same thing.
Concluding that MowGreen will not expand into the area around Drawnville requires assuming that MowGreen does not expand into areas in which it cannot operate profitably.
However, it does not require assuming that MowGreen currently operates profitably in every area in which it already operates.
It could be that MowGreen expands into an area only if it can operate profitably in that area at the time when it expands into that area, yet for some reason, MowGreen is not currently operating profitably in all areas in which it operates.
