Perhaps things changed in certain areas in which MowGreen operates after it expanded into those areas, and as a result it no longer operates profitably there.
(E) Sustainable methods for maintaining lawns are generally more expensive to use than are less environmentally friendly methods. This choice is tempting because what it says both sounds plausible and may explain why MowGreen would not be able to operate profitably in Drawnville.
However, we are not looking for a statement that is merely plausible or for an explanation, and it is not necessary to assume that sustainable methods for maintaining lawns are more expensive to use than conventional methods in order to arrive the conclusion that MowGreen will not expand into an area in which it is not able to operate profitably.
Correct Answer: (A) Ok, at this point you’re beginning to develop a better understanding of what assumptions are and how to answer Assumption questions.
summary:
Lesson Content As we discussed in the previous chapter, an assumption is an unstated piece of information that must be true in order for the logic of an argument to work.
To see more clearly how assumptions function in arguments, let’s consider a simple argument:
Dogs like to bark.
Therefore, Fido must like to bark.
Notice that we have one premise:
Dogs like to bark.
Given that premise, the author concludes the following:
title: 2.2 Assumptions: Unstated Pieces of Information that Must Be True in Order for Arguments to Work

contents:
Lesson Content Most of the time, recognizing an Identify the Assumption question is easy, because these questions utilize predictable language to ask for answer choices that fill logical gaps in arguments.
Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends?
Which of the following is an assumption made in drawing the conclusion above?
The argument depends on which of the following?
The conclusion drawn above is based on the assumption that _______.
The conclusion above would be more reasonably drawn if which of the following were inserted into the argument as an additional premise?
The argument depends on assuming which of the following?
The argument above logically depends on which of the following?
In order for the conclusion to be true, which of the following must be true?
While not all of the questions above include the word “assumption,” you can tell that the right answer to a question like any of them will be an assumption that fills a gap in the logic of an argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
Learn to recognize the common and predictable language used by Identify the Assumption questions and understand what these questions are asking you to do.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 4 Side by side tests of fruits and vegetables grown using conventional and organic methods have found no significant differences between the levels of key nutrients in the conventionally grown produce and the levels of those nutrients in the organically grown produce. Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends? 1. [ ] (A) Fruits and vegetables that are grown using organic methods do not taste different from those grown using conventional methods. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Plants take in nutrients from the soil as a necessary aspect of their growth processes. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Conventionally grown produce does not contain significant levels of health harming chemicals that organically grown produce does not contain. 4. [ ] (D) The differences between the prices of most organically grown produce and those of most conventionally grown produce are not justified. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) The methods used in farming organically grown produce tend to be more labor intensive than those used in farming conventionally grown produce. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy From what’s being asked, we can clearly see that this is an Assumption question:
Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends?
Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: Side by side tests have found no significant differences between levels of certain nutrients in organically grown produce and the levels of those nutrients in conventionally grown produce.
Conclusion: There is no reason to believe that there is any health benefit to be derived from eating organically grown produce instead of conventionally grown produce.
The argument uses one specific fact about differences between organically grown produce and conventionally grown produce to support the general conclusion that there is no reason to believe that there is any health benefit to be derived from eating organically grown produce rather than conventionally grown produce.
Coming to that conclusion from that one fact requires assuming that the only way in which eating organically grown produce could be better for health than eating conventionally grown produce is through the organically grown produce’s having greater levels of certain nutrients.
What if there were some other way in which eating organically grown produce is better for one’s health? It would be naïve to think that there is only one way in which eating organically grown produce is better for one’s health.
(A) Fruits and vegetables that are grown using organic methods do not taste different from those grown using conventional methods. The conclusion is specifically about the health benefits of eating organically grown produce rather than conventionally grown produce, and is not that there is no difference at all between the two types of produce.
Concluding that the health benefits of eating the two types of produce are the same does not require assuming that there is no difference between the tastes of the two types of produce.
(B) Plants take in nutrients from the soil as a necessary aspect of their growth processes. This choice could be tempting because it mentions nutrients, and the support for the argument’s conclusion is that there are no significant differences between levels of certain nutrients in the two types of produce.
However, we have to remember that Critical Reasoning is not a word matching game. To arrive at correct answers consistently, we have to consider the logical implications of what choices say, not just look for choices that say things that sound like what the passages say.
The fact that there are no significant differences between levels of certain nutrients in the two types of produce supports the conclusion regardless of whether it is true that plants take in nutrients from the soil as a necessary aspect of their growth processes.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Conventionally grown produce does not contain significant levels of health harming chemicals that organically grown produce does not contain. Concluding that, since there are no significant differences between levels of certain nutrients in the two types of produce, eating organic produce is not better for health requires assuming that eating conventionally grown produce is not somehow harmful to health in a way that eating organically grown produce is not.
After all, what if it were the case that conventionally grown produce contains significant levels of health harming chemicals that organically grown produce does not contain? The argument would fall apart, as, while the premise would be true, the conclusion that there is no reason to believe that there is any health benefit to be derived from eating organically grown produce rather than conventionally grown produce would not be true.
Thus, the information presented by this choice MUST BE TRUE in order for the logic of the argument to work.
(D) The differences between the prices of most organically grown produce and those of most conventionally grown produce are not justified. This choice could be tempting, because what it says may seem to be supported by the statements that appear in the argument. However, we are not looking for something that can be concluded from what the argument says, but rather for an assumption integral to the argument.
(E) The methods used in farming organically grown produce tend to be more labor intensive than those used in farming conventionally grown produce. While what this choice says may be true in reality, we are looking not for a true statement but rather for an assumption necessary for arriving at the argument’s conclusion.
The evidence about the levels of nutrients in the two types of produce would support the conclusion about the health benefits of eating the two types of produce regardless of whether it is true that the methods used in farming organically grown produce tend to be more labor intensive than those used in farming conventionally grown produce.
Correct Answer: (C) We’ve already solved a few Assumption questions, so you’re getting better at solving them.
summary:
Lesson Content Most of the time, recognizing an Identify the Assumption question is easy, because these questions utilize predictable language to ask for answer choices that fill logical gaps in arguments.
Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends?
Which of the following is an assumption made in drawing the conclusion above?
The argument depends on which of the following?
The conclusion drawn above is based on the assumption that _______.
title: 2.3 Recognizing an Identify the Assumption Question

contents:
Lesson Content To answer Assumption questions correctly, follow this 6-step strategy.
Step 1 – Carefully read and understand the passage.
The foundation of arriving at the correct answer to an Assumption question is understanding the passage.
The importance of understanding the passage cannot be overemphasized.
So, your first job in answering an Assumption question is to carefully read the passage, developing understanding of what the passage says and noting key details of the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
The foundation for arriving at the correct answer to an Assumption question is understanding the passage.
Step 2 – Read the question stem and identify what it is asking you to do.
Next, read the question stem.
If the question is asking you to identify an assumption or idea upon which the conclusion or argument depends, it’s an Identify the Assumption question, and in order to get it correct, you have to choose the answer choice that expresses an assumption integral to the argument.
(Note: Some people prefer to read Critical Reasoning question stems before they read the passages.
Step 3 – Go back to the passage and determine exactly what the conclusion of the argument is.
At this point, you understand the passage and know that you have to find an assumption integral to the argument.
Of course, the assumption will be information that is necessary in order for the argument’s conclusion to be effectively supported by its premises.
So, the next step is to go back to the passage and identify exactly what the conclusion is.
Identifying the exact conclusion of the argument is possibly the single most crucial step you will take in answering an Assumption question.
If you have only a vague idea of what the conclusion is, you can easily fall for trap choices that support the wrong conclusion.
So, identify exactly what the conclusion is.
Step 4 – Identify how the conclusion is supported.
The correct answer to an Assumption question will be information that is necessary in order for the argument’s conclusion to be effectively supported by its premises.
So, of course, the next key step in answering an Assumption question is to identify the premises that support the conclusion and determine how they support it.
Step 5 – Remove choices that are rather obviously not assumptions integral to the argument.
Usually, in an Assumption question, there are three answer choices that are relatively clearly not assumptions integral to the argument.
Eliminating these choices is a good next step.
Notice something here.
On your first pass through the answer choices, keep choices that you are not sure about, even if doing so means that you are left with three or more choices with which to work.
Often people eliminate the correct answer first! Why? Because, often the correct answer says something that is not obviously connected to the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
Eliminating answer choices that are clearly not correct is not the same as eliminating choices that you are not sure about. On your first pass through the answer choices in an Assumption question, keep choices that you are not sure about.
Step 6 – Choose the answer choice that is most clearly an assumption integral to the argument.
After eliminating answer choices that are clearly not assumptions integral to the argument, you are at the final stage of finding the correct answer to an Assumption question.
If you find yourself stuck between two answer choices, ask yourself which really connects the premises to the conclusion? Remember, an assumption is a piece of missing information that MUST BE TRUE in order for the logic of the argument to work.
Also, if you are circling through answer choices with no clear reason to choose one over others, it may be that there is something in the passage that you need to see in order to choose between the choices you have left.
Most GMAT Critical Reasoning questions have one clearly correct answer that answers the question in a tightly logical way.
So, find that one choice that must be true in order for the argument’s logic to work.
Summary of the Strategy for Identify the Assumption Questions
To summarize, here is the 6-step strategy that you can use to answer Assumption questions correctly:
💡MUST KNOW:
Memorize and practice the 6-step strategy for attacking Identify the Assumption questions.
Let’s now apply the strategy to answering an Assumption question.
For best results, take your time in answering this question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 5 Until recently, quinoa was farmed via traditional methods that are relatively benign in terms of their effects on the environment. Which of the following is an assumption upon which the logic of the above argument relies? 1. [ ] (A) It would be possible for quinoa farmers to meet current demand by using traditional farming methods on farms that utilize large land areas. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) There are other foods that contain the nutrients that quinoa contains in the exact same concentrations. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) The commercial farming methods used for farming quinoa are more detrimental to the environment than are the methods used for farming other food crops. 4. [ ] (D) Quinoa farmers were better off before increasing demand for quinoa catalyzed the shift to farming quinoa via modern commercial farming methods. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Quinoa would not be as popular as it is if more people were aware of the environmental effects of quinoa farming. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium Step 1 – Carefully read and understand the passage.
We can see that the passage presents an argument and that the reasoning of the argument is the following.
Since, as quinoa has become increasingly popular, the farmers who produce it have been shifting to using methods that have significant negative effects on the environment, people should curtail their quinoa consumption and eat other foods instead.
Step 2 – Read the question stem and identify what it is asking you to do.
The question asks us to find an assumption on which the logic of the argument relies.
Step 3 – Go back to the passage and determine exactly what the conclusion of the argument is.
We can see that the statements in the passage lead up to and support the last statement in the passage:
Conclusion: People concerned about the environment should curtail their quinoa consumption and eat other foods instead.
So, that last statement is the conclusion of the argument.
Notice that we are using the exact conclusion in its entirety, rather than a paraphrase or summary of the conclusion. Even leaving out a few words, such as “and eat other foods instead,” could result in our choosing an incorrect choice.
Step 4 – Identify how the conclusion is supported.
Going through the passage, we see that the conclusion is supported by the following premise:
Premise: As quinoa, which contains key nutrients in high concentrations, has become increasingly popular, in order to produce quinoa in greater quantities, quinoa farmers have been shifting to using commercial farming methods that have significant negative effects on the environment.
So, the author has concluded that people concerned about the environment should curtail their quinoa consumption and eat other foods instead because the methods quinoa farmers are using have significant negative effects on the environment.
Step 5 – Remove choices that are rather obviously not assumptions integral to the argument.
Let’s examine each answer choice, remembering that we are looking to separate statements that are rather clearly not assumptions on which the argument relies from statements that could potentially be our correct answer.
(A) It would be possible for quinoa farmers to meet current demand by using traditional farming methods on farms that utilize large land areas.
The argument uses the premise that quinoa farmers have been using methods that have negative effects on the environment to support the conclusion that people should curtail their quinoa consumption.
Going from that premise to that conclusion does not require assuming that the farmers could use traditional methods instead of the methods that have negative effects.
Therefore, we can confidently eliminate choice (A).
(B) There are other foods that contain the nutrients that quinoa contains in the exact same concentrations.
The author of the argument concludes that people should curtail their consumption of quinoa, which “contains key nutrients in high concentrations,” and eat other foods instead.
Is it possible that the conclusion depends on what this choice says, that other foods contain the nutrients that quinoa contains in the exact same concentrations?
Perhaps.
After all, people eat food in order to take in nutrients. So, maybe they should not switch from eating quinoa to eating other foods unless what this choice says is true.
Let’s keep choice (B) for now.
(C) The commercial farming methods used for farming quinoa are more detrimental to the environment than are the methods used for farming other food crops.
This choice is interesting.
If what this choice said is not true and, thus, the methods used to farm quinoa are NOT more detrimental to the environment than are methods used to farm other foods, then, presumably, consuming quinoa is not more detrimental to the environment than consuming other foods.
In that case, even though the premise that quinoa farmers are using methods that have negative effects on the environment is true, that premise does not support the argument’s conclusion, because people’s curtailing quinoa consumption and consuming other foods instead wouldn’t make any difference in terms of negative effects on the environment.
So, it appears that what this choice says has to be true in order for the argument to work.
Let’s keep choice (C), which seems to be the best one so far.
(D) Quinoa farmers were better off before increasing demand for quinoa catalyzed the shift to farming quinoa via modern commercial farming methods.
The conclusion is that people concerned about the environment should curtail their quinoa consumption and eat other foods instead.
That conclusion is supported by the fact that, in order to produce quinoa in greater quantities, quinoa farmers have been shifting to using farming methods that have significant negative effects on the environment.
Does it have to be the case that farmers were better off before they started using the new methods in order for that evidence to support the conclusion? No, even if the farmers were NOT better off before, the premise could support the conclusion. After all, even if the farmers are just as well off as they were before they started using the new methods, the new methods have negative effects on the environment.
Choice (D) is another one that we can confidently eliminate.
(E) Quinoa would not be as popular as it is if more people were aware of the environmental effects of quinoa farming.
What this choice says is clearly connected to the argument, but does it fill a gap in the argument?
The conclusion is that people concerned about the environment should curtail their quinoa consumption and eat other foods instead.
Quinoa doesn’t have to become less popular in order for curtailing quinoa consumption to make sense.
In fact, this choice is a conclusion supported by the statements in the argument rather than an assumption that the logic of the argument depends on.
So, let’s eliminate choice (E).
Step 6 – Choose the answer choice that is most clearly an assumption integral to the argument.
We have eliminated choices (A), (D), and (E). We are left with choices (B) and (C).
Choice (B) does seem to be important in this situation, since people switching from eating one food to eating another might be rightly concerned about making sure that they are not missing out on any nutrients by doing so.
However, do the concentrations of nutrients in foods that people switch to have to be exactly the same as concentrations of nutrients in quinoa in order to conclude that people should switch? Not really. People eat many different foods, and they don’t all contain the exact same nutrients in the exact same concentrations.
Choice (C), on the other hand, fills a key gap in the argument.
The evidence provided is that, in order to produce quinoa in greater quantities, quinoa farmers have been shifting to using commercial farming methods that have significant negative effects on the environment.
The conclusion is that people concerned about the environment should curtail their quinoa consumption and eat other foods instead.
In order to go from that evidence to that conclusion, one has to assume what choice (C) says, that the commercial farming methods used for farming quinoa are more detrimental to the environment than are the methods used for farming other food crops.
After all, if the commercial methods used for farming quinoa are NOT more detrimental than the methods used for farming other food crops, then curtailing quinoa consumption and eating other foods instead won’t help.
So, choice (C) is the correct answer to this question.
Correct Answer: (C) OK, you can use this next example to further practice using the strategy for answering Assumptions questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 6 Generally, jazz musicians are expected to be able to read sheet music in order to learn new songs. The conclusion above is based on which one of the following assumptions? 1. [ ] (A) “Playing by ear” is an appropriate method of learning to play songs in any genre. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Most expert jazz musicians who “play by ear” are not good at reading sheet music. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) The songs that amateur jazz musicians play can be learned as easily via reading sheet music as via “playing by ear. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Amateur jazz musicians would rather “play by ear” than read sheet music. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Expert jazz musicians learn songs more effectively than do amateur jazz musicians. View Solution Question Level: medium The argument states that, while jazz musicians are expected to be able to learn new songs through reading sheet music, a recent query into methods has divided jazz musicians into two groups: amateurs, who rely on reading sheet music, and experts, who opt to “play by ear”.
Because “playing by ear” is the method used by the experts, the argument concludes that it must be the “more effective” method.
Of course, what experts do and what is most effective are necessarily not the same thing. So, in this argument, there is a gap between ‘expert jazz musicians learn new songs by “playing by ear”’ and ‘“playing by ear” is more effective.’
(A) “Playing by ear” is an appropriate method of learning to play songs in any genre. The author does not have to assume that “playing by ear” is an appropriate method of learning to play songs in ANY genre in order to go from the evidence, that expert jazz musicians play by ear, to the conclusion, that, for learning jazz songs specifically, “playing by ear” is more effective than reading sheet music.
(B) Most expert jazz musicians who “play by ear” are not good at reading sheet music. If true, what this choice says might partly explain why expert jazz musicians “play by ear,” but we are not looking for an explanation.
Meanwhile, since this choice provides an alternate reason for expert jazz musicians’ playing by ear – they aren’t good at reading sheet music – it actually weakens the support for the conclusion, by providing reason to believe that expert jazz musicians choose to “play by ear” for a reason other than the relative effectiveness of “playing by ear.”
YOUR CHOICE(C) The songs that amateur jazz musicians play can be learned as easily via reading sheet music as via “playing by ear. ” This choice is incorrect for two reasons.
One is that it discusses a subset of jazz music, the songs played by amateur musicians. Therefore, it does not address the need for an assumption the fills a gap in an argument about learning jazz music in general.
The second reason is that, by suggesting that reading sheet music works just as well as “playing by ear” for learning certain songs, this choice to a degree contradicts the conclusion.
(D) Amateur jazz musicians would rather “play by ear” than read sheet music. This choice could be tempting, because what it says lines up fairly well with what the passage says and with the conclusion of the argument. One might think that, in order to argue that “playing by ear” is more effective than reading sheet music, one would have to assume that amateur jazz musicians would rather “play by ear” than read sheet music.
However, the truth is that going from the premises that amateurs use sheet music and that experts tend to “play by ear” to the conclusion that “playing by ear” is the more effective method does not require assuming anything about the preferences of amateurs, which could be based on factors other than effectiveness.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Expert jazz musicians learn songs more effectively than do amateur jazz musicians. Think about the missing information that must be true.
We’re told that expert jazz musicians learn to play new songs by “playing by ear.” Then, we’re presented with the conclusion that this method of learning is more effective than what the others do (read sheet music). To make the logic of this argument work, we must assume that expert jazz musicians do, in fact, learn new songs more effectively than do amateur jazz musicians.
After all, if this assumption were not true, the evidence would not support the conclusion, and the argument would fall apart.
Certainly, more evidence may be necessary to prove this conclusion; what we have done here is provide one assumption that is necessary for the evidence provided to support the conclusion.
Correct Answer: (E) Now that you have a general sense of how Assumption questions work and how to find their answers, let’s build some depth of skill by considering some of the ways in which correct answer choice can fill gaps in arguments.
summary:
Lesson Content To answer Assumption questions correctly, follow this 6-step strategy.
Step 1 – Carefully read and understand the passage.
The foundation of arriving at the correct answer to an Assumption question is understanding the passage.
The importance of understanding the passage cannot be overemphasized.
So, your first job in answering an Assumption question is to carefully read the passage, developing understanding of what the passage says and noting key details of the passage.
title: 2.4 Strategy for Identify the Assumption Questions

contents:
Lesson Content In Critical Reasoning, we use the term “author” to refer to whoever is setting forth an argument in a question.
The “authors” of these arguments vary from question to question, but the one commonality that these authors all share is that they assume that they are correct, whatever their arguments may be.
💡MUST KNOW:
In making the arguments found in Critical Reasoning questions, the authors of these arguments assume that they are correct; they assume that their arguments are sound and logical.
This point is not a small one, and remembering it will provide us an edge in finding the assumptions made by authors.
Because authors assume that their arguments are sound, they also assume that they have considered every variable that could affect their arguments, and that, after considering those variables, they’ve reached conclusions that are supported by their evidence.
💡MUST KNOW:
Authors assume that they have considered every variable that could affect their arguments, and that, after considering those variables, they’ve reached conclusions that are supported by their evidence.
Another way of thinking about this is that authors assume that there will not be any unforeseen variables that will enter into the equation and render their arguments unsound.
In the TTP Course, we have a name for unforeseen variables that render arguments unsound.
💡MUST KNOW:
A malicious variable is a variable that an author has failed to consider in the construction of an argument and that enters into the equation and spoils that argument, rendering the argument unsound.
Likewise, when an author presents a plan, the author assumes that there are no malicious variables that will cause the plan not to work.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an author presents a plan, the author assumes that there are no malicious variables that will cause the plan not to work.
Malicious variables come in all shapes and sizes, but they have in common that they were not foreseen by the author, and that they ruin the author’s argument.
For example, consider the following mini-example:
Most residents of Spring Hills lack health insurance. Blue Health, a nationwide insurance company, just announced that it will offer health insurance for a deeply discounted price to all Spring Hills residents who do not have any preexisting conditions. Therefore, by next month, most Spring Hills residents will have health insurance.
Let’s list some malicious variables that would ruin the argument, if true.
Malicious Variable: It will take many months for most residents of Spring Hills to learn about the health insurance being offered by Blue Health.
Assumption: It will NOT take many months for most residents of Spring Hills to learn about the health insurance being offered by Blue Health.
Malicious Variable: Most residents of Spring Hills suffer from at least one medical condition that Blue Health categorizes as a preexisting condition.
Assumption: Most residents of Spring Hills DO NOT suffer from at least one medical condition that Blue Health categorizes as a preexisting condition.
Malicious Variable: Most residents of Spring Hills would prefer to use the money that they would otherwise spend on health insurance on wellness-promoting services, such as personal trainers, meditation coaches, and nutritionists.
Assumption: Most residents of Spring Hills would NOT prefer to use the money that they would otherwise spend on health insurance on wellness-promoting services, such as personal trainers, meditation coaches, and nutritionists.
Malicious Variable: Even at the deeply discounted price offered by Blue Health, the monthly cost of the health insurance it offers far exceeds what most residents of Spring Hills are able to pay.
Assumption: The deeply discounted price for the health insurance offered by Blue Health DOES NOT exceed what most residents of Spring Hills are able to pay.
Notice that each of those malicious variables, if true, would ruin the argument.
So, how can we use this information on test day? We can use it to find the correct answers to Assumption questions by looking for a choice that states the author’s assumption that a certain malicious variable is not true.
💡MUST KNOW:
The correct answer to an Assumption question will always say that some malicious variable that could wreck the argument or plan is not the case.
Let’s transform that mini-example into a full example, so you can put these ideas into action.
For best results, take your time answering this question and carefully analyze each choice to learn to clearly see which one presents the author’s assumption that a malicious variable is not the case.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 7 Most residents of Spring Hills lack health insurance. The conclusion above is based on which one of the following assumptions? 1. [ ] (A) No other health insurance companies will follow Blue Health’s lead and begin offering similar health insurance at a discounted price to residents of Spring Hills. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) It will take many months for most residents of Spring Hills to learn about the health insurance being offered by Blue Health. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Being covered by health insurance is an important part of any wealth management strategy. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Blue Health is able to offer health insurance for a deeply discounted price to Spring Hills residents because the cost of healthcare in Spring Hills is unusually low. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Most residents of Spring Hills do not suffer from at least one medical condition that Blue Health categorizes as a preexisting condition. View Solution Question Level: easy Let’s break down the argument.
Background: Most residents of Spring Hills lack health insurance.
Premise: Blue Health, a nationwide insurance company, just announced that it will offer health insurance for a deeply discounted price to all Spring Hills residents who do not have any preexisting conditions.
Conclusion: By next month, most Spring Hills residents will have health insurance.
The author assumes that his argument is sound. He also assumes that he has carefully and comprehensively analyzed every variable that could affect his argument, and that, after analyzing those variables, he has reached a conclusion supported by the evidence presented. He assumes that there are not any malicious variables that will enter into the equation and render his argument invalid.
We’ll be seeking an answer choice that says something that must be true for the conclusion to properly follow from the premise.
(A) No other health insurance companies will follow Blue Health’s lead and begin offering similar health insurance at a discounted price to residents of Spring Hills. Regardless of whether other health insurance companies will follow Blue Health’s lead and begin offering similar health insurance at a discounted price to residents of Spring Hills, the author’s argument could be sound.
After all, the argument’s conclusion is based upon the premise that Blue Health just announced that it would offer health insurance for a deeply discounted price to all Spring Hills residents who do not have any preexisting conditions.
We can see that, even if many other health insurance companies follow suit, his argument still supports his conclusion (and is in fact stronger).
(B) It will take many months for most residents of Spring Hills to learn about the health insurance being offered by Blue Health. What this answer choice says weakens the argument.
After all, if it will take many months for most residents of Spring Hills to learn about the health insurance being offered by Blue Health, then it does not make sense to conclude that most of the residents will have health insurance by next month.
If what this choice says it true, the argument falls apart.
(C) Being covered by health insurance is an important part of any wealth management strategy. Regardless of whether being covered by health insurance is an important part of any wealth management strategy, the argument could be sound.
After all, the argument’s conclusion is based upon the premise that Blue Health just announced that it would offer health insurance for a deeply discounted price to all Spring Hills residents who do not have any preexisting conditions.
To make his argument, the author does not need to assume that being covered by health insurance is an important part of any wealth management strategy. Even if being covered by health insurance is not an important part of any wealth management strategy, the evidence presented could support the conclusion.
This answer choice could be tempting because what it says is probably true in reality. However, in answering Assumptions questions we’re not seeking a choice that’s probably true in reality; we’re seeking one that must be true for the argument to work.
(D) Blue Health is able to offer health insurance for a deeply discounted price to Spring Hills residents because the cost of healthcare in Spring Hills is unusually low. This answer choice is tempting because it explains why Blue Health is able to offer health insurance for a deeply discounted price to Spring Hills residents. The cost of healthcare in Spring Hills is unusually low.
However, we are not seeking an explanation for why Blue Health is able to offer health insurance for a deeply discounted price to Spring Hills residents. The argument has already established as fact that Blue Health will offer health insurance for a deeply discounted price. We don’t need an explanation for why that fact is true. Rather, we need an assumption that allows the conclusion to properly follow from the evidence.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Most residents of Spring Hills do not suffer from at least one medical condition that Blue Health categorizes as a preexisting condition. Let’s consider what would happen if most residents of Spring Hills DO suffer from at least one medical condition that Blue Health categorizes as a preexisting condition. What happens in that situation? In that situation, even though it is true that Blue Health will offer health insurance for a deeply discounted price to all Spring Hills residents who do not have any preexisting conditions, the conclusion that, by next month most residents of Spring Hills will have health insurance does not follow from the evidence.
In that case, the argument falls apart. So, if it were true that most residents of Spring Hills do suffer from such a condition, that fact would constitute a malicious variable, for sure.
However, the author assumes that his argument is correct and that there are no malicious variables. Therefore, he must assume that, as this choice says, most residents of Spring Hills do NOT suffer from at least one medical condition that Blue Health categorizes as a preexisting condition.
Notice that, in general, this answer choice underscores the author’s belief that there are no malicious variables that will enter the equation and spoil the argument.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 8 People who do yoga tend to be healthier than people who do not do yoga. Which of the following has the author assumed in making the prediction? 1. [x] (A) People will sign up for and attend the yoga classes. 2. [ ] (B) People at the company do not have time to attend yoga classes. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Yoga improves people’s health by strengthening their hearts, muscles, and immunity. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) The healthier people are, the more productive they will be at work. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Doing yoga is the only way for a person to become healthier. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy Let’s break down the argument.
Premise: People who do yoga tend to be healthier than people who do not do yoga.
Premise: We are going to offer yoga classes at our company.
Conclusion: Therefore, the people who work at our company will become healthier.
Notice that the author has proposed a plan. Whenever an author proposes a plan, he or she inherently makes the assumption that the plan will work as it has been laid out, that there will not be malicious variables that enter the equation and derail the plan.
YOUR CHOICE(A) People will sign up for and attend the yoga classes. Notice that, if it is true that people WILL NOT sign up for and attend the yoga classes, then the argument falls apart. That fact would certainly constitute a malicious variable.
Of course, the author assumes that there are not any malicious variables that will enter into the equation and derail the plan. Therefore, the author must assume that people WILL sign up for and attend the yoga classes.
Notice that this choice underscores the idea that the author assumes that there will not be any malicious variables that enter the equation and derail the plan.
(B) People at the company do not have time to attend yoga classes. If people at the company do not have time to attend yoga classes, then the plan will fail. Therefore, what this answer choice says weakens the argument. In an Assumption question, an answer choice that weakens the argument can never be correct.
(C) Yoga improves people’s health by strengthening their hearts, muscles, and immunity. This answer choice is tempting because it explains how Yoga improves people’s health. In other words, it explains that Yoga increases people’s health by strengthening their hearts, muscles, and immunity.
However, we are not seeking an explanation for why Yoga improves health. The argument already establishes as fact that Yoga improves health. We don’t need an explanation for why that fact is true. Rather, we need an assumption that allows the conclusion to properly follow from the evidence.
(D) The healthier people are, the more productive they will be at work. The argument’s conclusion is that the people who work at our company will become healthier. Notice that this is a conclusion solely about health. To arrive at the conclusion, the author does not need to assume that there will be any particular results of that improved health, such as increased productivity. So, regardless of whether this answer choice is true, the argument could still be sound.
(E) Doing yoga is the only way for a person to become healthier. The argument’s conclusion that people who work at the company will become healthier is supported by the premises regardless of whether doing yoga is the only way for a person to become healthier.
After all, even if doing yoga is not the only way for a person to become healthier, doing yoga is a way for a person to become healthier. So, if the company is going to offer yoga classes, it makes sense to conclude that people who work at the company will become healthier.
So, the author does not have to assume what this choice says in order to arrive at the argument’s conclusion.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 9 150 years ago, the area where the Mininguela River meets the Dallora River was prime habitat for a variety of native species. Which of the following must be assumed in making the above prediction? 1. [ ] (A) Only after native plant species have reestablished significant populations in the area will many animals choose to reside there. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Factories did not discharge toxic wastes into the Mininguela River or the Dallora River. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Without being reintroduced by humans, most native species will not return to the area where the Mininguela River meets the Dallora River. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) The effects of years of manufacturing in the area where the rivers meet have not rendered the local soil inhospitable to native species. 5. [ ] (E) Currently, there are no native species inhabiting the region surrounding the intersection of the Mininguela River and the Dallora River. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy Here’s a breakdown of the argument.
Background: 150 years ago, the area where the Mininguela River meets the Dallora River was prime habitat for a variety of native species.
Background: As time went on, the region became an industrial center where dozens of factories were located, at which point, for many native species, the area was not suitable habitat.
Premise: The factories are being torn down.
The conclusion of the argument is a prediction.
Conclusion: Native plants and animals will repopulate this once ecologically vibrant region.
The reasoning underlying this prediction is that the area was previously prime habitat for native species, and that the factories that rendered the area inhospitable to native species are being removed. So, it seems natural that the species would repopulate the area.
Is the fact that the factories are being torn down evidence sufficient for concluding definitively that native species will repopulate the area, or does something else also have to be true?
One key thing that we will see time and again is that, whenever anyone makes a prediction, he or she inherently makes the assumption that there will not be malicious variables that enter the equation and render the prediction incorrect.
The correct answer to this question highlights the basic assumption that, with the factories gone, there will not be something else preventing native species’ repopulating the area.
(A) Only after native plant species have reestablished significant populations in the area will many animals choose to reside there. What this choice says makes sense, and someone making a prediction about species’ repopulating an area would likely agree with what this choice says.
However, we are looking specifically for an assumption necessary for the evidence to support the prediction, and it does not have to be the case that the repopulation process will work as described by this choice in order for the fact that the factories have been torn down to support the prediction that native species will repopulate the area.
(B) Factories did not discharge toxic wastes into the Mininguela River or the Dallora River. This choice could be tempting, in that one could make the case that, if factories DID discharge toxic wastes into the rivers, the area might be so contaminated that native species would not repopulate it.
Notice, however, that we would have to go beyond what this choice says in order to make the case that, if factories did discharge toxic wastes into the rivers, the prediction is incorrect. We don’t have information clearly indicating that past discharging of toxic waste into the rivers would contaminate the area enough to keep species from repopulating it.
So, we don’t have information clearly indicating that what this choice says is an assumption necessary for making the prediction.
(C) Without being reintroduced by humans, most native species will not return to the area where the Mininguela River meets the Dallora River. Notice that this answer choice indicates that there is a malicious variable – the fact that, without being reintroduced, the species will not return – that could render the prediction incorrect.
In an Assumption question, an answer that presents a malicious variable will never be correct because the fact that a malicious variable exists will weaken the argument.
In an Assumption question, a choice that weakens the argument can never be correct.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The effects of years of manufacturing in the area where the rivers meet have not rendered the local soil inhospitable to native species. Predicting that native species will repopulate the area requires assuming that manufacturing in the area has not rendered the soil in the area inhospitable to native species. Otherwise, the prediction would not make sense because, if the soil were inhospitable to native species, even after the removal of the factories, the species would not repopulate the area.
So, the fact that the factories will be removed is not sufficient evidence for concluding that native species will repopulate the region. In addition to the evidence provided in the passage, the argument requires the assumption stated by this choice.
Notice that the correct answer to this question highlights the basic assumption that, with the factories gone, there will not be something else, a malicious variable, preventing native species’ repopulating the area.
(E) Currently, there are no native species inhabiting the region surrounding the intersection of the Mininguela River and the Dallora River. What this choice says may seem to be supported by what the argument says.
However, we are not looking for a conclusion supported by the argument but rather for an assumption upon which the prediction depends, and the prediction that native species will repopulate the region does not depend on the assumption that absolutely no native species currently inhabit the region.
After all, even if some native species inhabit in the region, those species and others could still repopulate the area.
So, the prediction could be correct even this choice is not true.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 10 On the island of Lakahoa, numerous giant stone statues have been found. Which of the following is an assumption on which the argument relies? 1. [ ] (A) The ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa did not have the ability to transport the few remaining statues to sacred areas. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) There were no differences between the levels of quality of the statues used in sacred areas. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) The number of giant stone statues on Lakahoa in ancient times was the same as the number there now. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) The area around the quarry was not considered sacred by ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa. 5. [ ] (E) All of the ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa had the skills required for assessing the quality of a stone statue. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium Here’s a breakdown of the argument.
Background: On the island of Lakahoa, numerous giant stone statues have been found. It is believed that these statues were used for marking sacred areas by people who inhabited Lakahoa in ancient times.
Premise: A few of the statues remain in the area around the quarry where all of the statues were produced.
Conclusion: Clearly, therefore, ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa did not believe those few statues to be of sufficiently high quality for marking sacred areas.
We see that the author has gone from the simple fact that a few of the statues remain in the area around the quarry to a conclusion about what ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa believed about the quality of those statues.
In going from the premise to the conclusion, the author has assumed that a number of malicious variables that could wreck the argument are not the case.
To find the correct answer, we have to look for a choice that states the author’s assumption that a particular malicious variable that could wreck the argument is not the case.
(A) The ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa did not have the ability to transport the few remaining statues to sacred areas. Rather than state an assumption on which the argument depends, this choice weakens the argument.
After all, if, as this choice says, the ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa did not have the ability to transport the statues that remain around the quarry, then it could be that the reason those statues remain in that area is simply that there was no way to transport them.
In that case, the fact that those statues remain there would not be a reason to conclude that ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa did not believe those statues to be of sufficiently high quality for marking sacred areas.
In an Assumption question, a choice that weakens the argument can never be correct.
(B) There were no differences between the levels of quality of the statues used in sacred areas. Notice that, even if there WERE differences between the levels of quality of the statues used in sacred areas, the argument still works.
After all, even if there were differences between the levels of quality of the statues used in sacred areas, it could still be that the few statues that remain in the area around the quarry were lower in quality than all the rest. Also, it could still be that those few statues remain there because of what ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa believed about their quality.
Thus, even if this choice is not true, that fact is not a malicious variable that wrecks the argument.
So, the author doesn’t have to assume what this choice says in order for the argument to work.
(C) The number of giant stone statues on Lakahoa in ancient times was the same as the number there now. Notice that, even if the number of giant stone statues on Lakahoa in ancient times was NOT the same as the number there now, the argument still works.
After all, regardless of how many statues were on Lakahoa in ancient times, it could still be the case that the reason a few statues remain in the area around the quarry is that ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa did not believe those statues to be of sufficiently high quality for marking sacred areas.
Thus, even if this choice is not true, that fact is not a malicious variable that wrecks the argument.
So, the author doesn’t have to assume what this choice says in order for the argument to work.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The area around the quarry was not considered sacred by ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa. If the area around the quarry WAS considered sacred by ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa, the argument falls apart. So, that fact would certainly constitute a malicious variable.
After all, if the area around the quarry was considered sacred, then it doesn’t make sense to conclude that ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa did not believe the statues remaining there to be of sufficiently high quality for marking sacred areas. In that case, it could instead be that those few statues remain there because ancient inhabitants used them to mark that area.
So, in making the argument, the author must be assuming that the area around the quarry was NOT considered sacred by ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa, as this choice says.
(E) All of the ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa had the skills required for assessing the quality of a stone statue. This choice is tempting because it’s close to correct. After all, it makes sense that, in concluding that ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa did not believe some statues to be of sufficiently high quality for marking sacred areas, the author assumes that some of the ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa had the skills required for assessing the quality of a stone statue.
However, must the author assume that ALL of the inhabitants had the skills required for assessing the quality of a stone statue? No.
After all, if even it is not the case that all of the inhabitants had such skills, it could still be the case that SOME of the inhabitants were able to determine that the statues that remain around the quarry were not of sufficiently high quality for marking sacred areas.
So, even if NOT all of the ancient inhabitants of Lakahoa had the skills required for assessing the quality of a stone statue, that fact is not a malicious variable that wrecks the argument.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss common ways in which assumption question correct answers work.
summary:
Lesson Content In Critical Reasoning, we use the term “author” to refer to whoever is setting forth an argument in a question.
The “authors” of these arguments vary from question to question, but the one commonality that these authors all share is that they assume that they are correct, whatever their arguments may be.
💡MUST KNOW:
In making the arguments found in Critical Reasoning questions, the authors of these arguments assume that they are correct; they assume that their arguments are sound and logical.
title: 2.5 In Critical Reasoning Questions, Authors Assume that their Arguments are Correct.

contents: Lesson Content The correct answer to an assumption question will always fill a gap in an argument.
summary: Lesson Content The correct answer to an assumption question will always fill a gap in an argument.
title: 2.6 Common Ways in which Assumption Question Correct Answers Work

contents:
Lesson Content When an author sets forth an argument, plan, proposal, or course of action, she assumes that there will be no offsetting factors that will somehow render that argument, plan, proposal, or course of action invalid.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an author sets forth an argument, plan, proposal, or course of action, she assumes that there will be no offsetting factors that will somehow render that argument, plan, proposal, or course of action invalid.
For example, people taking an action may assume that other people will not seek to counter the effects of the action.
Let’s discuss some real life “offsetting factors” that have occurred and that spoiled some arguments, plans, proposals, and courses of action.
Plan 1: To help cigarette smokers quit smoking, a law was passed requiring cigarette manufacturers to lower the amount of nicotine, a highly addictive stimulant, found in cigarettes.
Assumption: There will be no offsetting factors that ruin this plan; for example, smokers WILL NOT smoke more cigarettes to compensate for the lower amounts of nicotine found in each cigarette.
Outcome: The law did not help smokers to quit smoking because of an unexpected offsetting factor: smokers actually did smoke more cigarettes to compensate for the lower amounts of nicotine found in each cigarette.
Plan 2: To help curb the consumption of sugary drinks, a state added a hefty tax to the price of all soda sold in the state.
Assumption: There will be no offsetting factors that ruin this plan.
Outcome: Soda sales did not decline because soda manufacturers did respond by lowering the price of their soda by exactly the amount of the tax, effectively keeping the cost of soda to consumers the same.
Plan 3: To lose weight, people significantly reduce the number of calories they consume.
Assumption: There will be no offsetting factors that ruin this plan; for example, a person’s body WILL NOT respond to the reduction in calories consumed by reducing the number of calories it burns.
Outcome: In fact, in some cases, though certainly not in all cases, people who reduce calorie intake do not lose weight because their bodies do respond by reducing the number of calories they burn.
The assumption that there will be no offsetting factors to ruin a plan is not always a good assumption (more on this in the Weaken the Argument Chapter).
Some of you are saying, “Hey – this section sounds a lot like the malicious variables section.” If you thought that, you are correct! The idea that an author assumes that there will not be any malicious variables that enter into the equation and spoil the plan is a general, overarching theme in Assumption questions.
Let’s put this idea into practice.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 11 Beer sold in the city of Hooksville has always contained seven percent alcohol by volume. Which of the following must be assumed in making the above prediction? 1. [ ] (A) Consuming too much alcohol is harmful for a person’s mental and physical wellbeing. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) The cost of beer sold in Hooksville will not substantially increase because of the new law. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Some residents of Hooksville consume wine but do not consume other alcoholic beverages. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Hooksville residents will not compensate for the lower alcohol volume in beer by drinking twice as much beer as they do now. 5. [ ] (E) Many nearby towns have enacted similar laws, which have resulted in residents of those towns consuming far less alcohol than they consumed prior to the laws’ being enacted. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) Consuming too much alcohol is harmful for a person’s mental and physical wellbeing. This answer choice is tempting because it explains why there is concern that residents of Hooksville are consuming too much alcohol.
However, we are not seeking an explanation for why there is concern that residents of Hooksville are consuming too much alcohol. The argument already establishes as fact that there is concern that residents of Hooksville are consuming too much alcohol. We don’t need an explanation for why that fact is true.
Rather, we need an assumption that allows the conclusion to properly follow from the evidence.
(B) The cost of beer sold in Hooksville will not substantially increase because of the new law. If the cost of beer sold in Hooksville were to substantially increase because of the new law, then we’d have more faith that, in the near future, Hooksville residents who drink beer will consume less alcohol than they currently do.
After all, simple laws of economics tell us that, as prices rise (especially substantially), demand decreases. In other words, if the cost of beer substantially increased, the plan would be even more likely to work.
So then, certainly the author is not assuming that the cost of beer sold in Hooksville will not substantially increase because of the new law.
(C) Some residents of Hooksville consume wine but do not consume other alcoholic beverages. Regardless of whether some residents of Hooksville consume wine but do not consume other alcoholic beverages, the argument could still work. After all, the argument centers on the people who DO drink beer in Hooksville.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Hooksville residents will not compensate for the lower alcohol volume in beer by drinking twice as much beer as they do now. Any time an author sets forth an argument, plan, proposal, or course of action, she assumes that there will be no offsetting factors that will somehow render that argument, plan, proposal, or course of action invalid. Therefore, the author must assume that Hooksville residents will not compensate for the lower alcohol volume in beer by drinking twice as much beer as they do now.
After all, if Hooksville residents DO compensate for the lower alcohol volume in beer by drinking twice as much beer as they do now, then the plan falls apart.
(E) Many nearby towns have enacted similar laws, which have resulted in residents of those towns consuming far less alcohol than they consumed prior to the laws’ being enacted. Although what this answer choice says is in line with the argument’s reasoning and conclusion, the author does not need to assume what this choice says. Even if no nearby town has enacted a similar law and seen results like those the author predicts will be achieved in Hooksville, the law could could still work in Hooksville.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 12 In the country of Junipira, mangos are being grown, processed, and shipped to markets in Junipira at the maximum possible rate of 2,000 mangos per day. Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends? 1. [ ] (A) Most residents of Junipira will begin to substantially reduce their mango consumption. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Mango production in Junipira is limited by the relatively small number of acres available in the country for growing mangos. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Almost all residents of Junipira will not begin to travel to and purchase mangos in the nearby country of Mangosteen, where mangos are less expensive than they are in Junipira. 4. [ ] (D) There are other fruits sold in Junipira that have the same taste, texture, and consistency as mangos. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Over the past few months, mango farmers have developed innovative ways to increase the number of mangos that they are able to grow per acre of farmland. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium (A) Most residents of Junipira will begin to substantially reduce their mango consumption. What this answer choice says weakens the argument.
After all if,most residents of Junipira will begin to substantially reduce their mango consumption, then there is a good chance that the level of demand will drop back below the level of supply. In this case, we would have less faith that the price of mangos sold in Junipira will soon increase.
In general, when we’re seeking an assumption upon which an argument rests, an answer choice that says something that weakens the argument will not be correct.
(B) Mango production in Junipira is limited by the relatively small number of acres available in the country for growing mangos. This answer choice is tempting because it helps explain why mangos are being grown, processed, and shipped to markets in Junipira at the maximum possible rate of 2,000 mangos per day: mango production in Junipira is limited by the relatively small number of acres available in the country for growing mangos.
However, the argument establishes as fact that mangos are being grown, processed, and shipped to markets in Junipira at the maximum possible rate of 2,000 mangos per day. We don’t need an explanation for why that fact is true.
Rather, we need an assumption that allows the conclusion to properly follow from the evidence.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Almost all residents of Junipira will not begin to travel to and purchase mangos in the nearby country of Mangosteen, where mangos are less expensive than they are in Junipira. Any time an author sets forth an argument, plan, proposal, or course of action, she assumes that there will be no offsetting factors that will somehow render that argument, plan, proposal, or course of action invalid. Therefore, the author must assume that almost all residents of Junipira will not begin to travel to and purchase mangos in the nearby country of Mangosteen. After all, if almost all residents of Junipira WILL begin to travel to and purchase mangos in the nearby country of Mangosteen, then the plan falls apart.
(D) There are other fruits sold in Junipira that have the same taste, texture, and consistency as mangos. If, contrary to what this choice says, there were NO other fruits sold in Junipira that have the same taste, texture, and consistency as mangos, then we’d have a little bit more faith in the argument. After all, if no other fruits sold in Junipira have the same taste, texture, and consistency as mangos, then probably there are not any viable substitutes for mangos, a situation in which mango demand is likely to remain strong.
So then, certainly the author is not assuming that there ARE other fruits sold in Junipira that have the same taste, texture, and consistency as mangos.
(E) Over the past few months, mango farmers have developed innovative ways to increase the number of mangos that they are able to grow per acre of farmland. The conclusion is supported by the premise that mangos are being grown, processed, and shipped to markets in Junipira at the maximum possible rate of 2,000 mangos per day.
The effect of this choice is to explain how farmers have achieved that maximum possible rate. They have developed innovative ways to increase the number of mangos that they are able to grow per acre of farmland.
Of course, the premise will be a fact that supports the conclusion regardless of how the farmers have achieved that maximum possible rate. So, the author does not have to assume anything about how the farmers have achieved that maximum possible rate in order to support the conclusion.
Correct Answer: (C) Now, let’s discuss correct answers that highlight assumptions that cause-and-effect arguments rely on.
summary:
Lesson Content When an author sets forth an argument, plan, proposal, or course of action, she assumes that there will be no offsetting factors that will somehow render that argument, plan, proposal, or course of action invalid.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an author sets forth an argument, plan, proposal, or course of action, she assumes that there will be no offsetting factors that will somehow render that argument, plan, proposal, or course of action invalid.
title: 2.6.1 Assumption Correct Answer Type 1: States an Assumption that there is no Offsetting Factor

contents:
Lesson Content Many arguments that appear in the GMAT involve cause-and-effect reasoning, something that we’ll often mention in the chapters that follow, but we’ll begin building our expertise in cause-and-effect reasoning here.
An author makes a cause-and-effect claim when she maintains that some factor x is the cause of some outcome y.
When an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she assumes that her cause-and-effect claim is correct; she assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observed events.
Another way of saying this is that, when an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she posits that the cause she has described is the ONLY possible cause of the effect.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she assumes that her cause-and-effect claim is correct; she assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observed events.
For example, let’s say that an author notices that, when the speed limit on a certain highway was reduced, the number of vehicular collisions on that highway decreased.
In other words, she assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for the decrease in the number of collisions.
Let’s list out some plausible alternative explanations for the decrease in the number of collisions that occurred after the speed limit on the highway was reduced.
The number of vehicles using the highway substantially decreased immediately after the speed limit was reduced.
For the first time, a new law mandated that all vehicles on the highway be equipped with advanced collision avoidance systems.
Large trucks, which had been responsible for most of the collisions on the highway, were for the first time not allowed to use the highway.
The highway surface, which had lapsed into a state of dangerous disrepair, was recently restored to like-new condition, a substantial improvement to the safety of the highway.
Notice that each of those could be a plausible reason why the number of collisions decreased.
So, if it were determined that any of those statements were true, her argument would fall apart.
For another example of cause-and-effect reasoning, consider the following argument:
In a recent medical study, all of the study participants who had abused alcohol in the past five years were found to be suffering with depression. Thus, it must be the case that abusing alcohol causes depression. Notice that the argument relies on cause-and-effect logic.
Does the author provide convincing evidence that abusing alcohol actually causes depression? No, the only premise is that the study participants who abused alcohol were found to be suffering with depression.
In fact, there are several possibilities here:
The author is correct; abusing alcohol does cause depression. The cause-effect relationship is reversed: the depression actually caused the alcohol abuse. A third factor caused both conditions. The conditions developed independently and are not related. So, think carefully here.
The cause-effect relationship is not actually reversed. There isn’t a third factor that is the true cause of both alcohol abuse and depression. The two conditions did not develop independently. In other words, the author is assuming that he is correct in his conclusion about the events that he has observed.
💡MUST KNOW:
An Assumption question correct answer can state an assumption that must be true in order for a cause-and-effect conclusion to be correct.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 13 Steven was asked to take a polygraph, i.e., lie detector, test prior to being hired as a security guard at Company X. In drawing the conclusion, Company X assumed which of the following? 1. [ ] (A) Using a polygraph test for hiring purposes is legal in the state where Steven lives. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Steven was not suffering from an illness that causes excess sweating, a common cause of polygraph failure. 3. [ ] (C) When he created his resume, Steven was not aware that he would be asked about its contents in the course of a polygraph test. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Company X has a legal interest in hiring security guards who demonstrate only the highest levels of integrity. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Steven had previously failed a polygraph test at another company, Company Y. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy First let’s identify the conclusion made by Company X.
Conclusion: Steven must have lied on his resume.
This conclusion is based on Steven’s failure on the section of the polygraph test in which he was asked about his resume.
Notice that the company is assuming that there could not be another reason why Steven failed that section of the polygraph test. In other words, we see that the Company assumes that the only potential cause of Steven’s polygraph failure is his lying on his resume. After all, if there is something else that could have caused Steven’s polygraph failure, then the logic of the argument no longer works.
So then, what unstated information is necessary for the logic of the argument to work? An assumption that there is not another potential cause for Steven’s polygraph failure.
(A) Using a polygraph test for hiring purposes is legal in the state where Steven lives. In deciding to use a polygraph test for hiring purposes, Company X may be assuming that is it legal to do so.
However, going from the fact that Steven failed the part of the test related to his resume to the conclusion that he lied on his resume does not require assuming that using the test is legal. The argument is sound regardless of whether using the test is legal.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Steven was not suffering from an illness that causes excess sweating, a common cause of polygraph failure. Going from the fact that Steven failed the test to the conclusion that he lied on his resume requires the assumption (among other assumptions), that he didn’t fail because of an illness that made him sweat. If Steven was in fact suffering from an illness that that causes excess sweating, a common cause of polygraph failure, then the fact that he failed the test would not be reason to believe that he was lying.
Notice that what this answer choice says serves to rule out an alternative explanation for why Steven failed the polygraph.
Company X must assume that Steven was not suffering from an illness that causes excess sweating, a common cause of polygraph failure, because if he WERE, then the argument would fall apart.
(C) When he created his resume, Steven was not aware that he would be asked about its contents in the course of a polygraph test. This choice could be tempting, since one could think that, had Steven been aware that he would be asked about his resume in the course of a polygraph test, he would not have lied on his resume, and that, therefore, concluding that he lied on his resume requires assuming that he was not aware that he would be asked about his resume in the course of a polygraph test.
However, what this choice says does not have to be true in order for the argument to work. Steven could have lied even though he was aware that he would be asked about his resume in the course of a polygraph test.
Overall, going from the evidence, the test results, to the conclusion, that Steven lied, does not require assuming anything about Steven’s thinking. Really, all one has to assume is that the cause of his failing the test was lying.
(D) Company X has a legal interest in hiring security guards who demonstrate only the highest levels of integrity. This statement makes sense, and may explain why Company X is using a polygraph test, but it is unrelated to the matter of concluding that Steven lied because he failed the polygraph test.
In answering Assumption questions, we must be careful to not choose as correct answer choices that say things that make sense yet are not assumptions upon which arguments depend.
In this case, the argument could work regardless of whether Company X has a legal interest in hiring security guards who demonstrate only the highest levels of integrity.
(E) Steven had previously failed a polygraph test at another company, Company Y. This statement tends to lend credibility to the conclusion that Steven is lying. Thus, it is a tempting answer choice.
However, we are not simply looking for more support for the conclusion that Steven lied. We are looking for an assumption necessary for connecting the results of the test Steven took for Company X with the conclusion that he lied on his resume, and the assumption that Steven failed a different test at a different company is not necessary for making that connection.
In other words, this argument could be sound regardless of whether Steven had previously failed a polygraph test at another company, Company Y.
Correct Answer: (B) Here’s another example with a cause-and-effect conclusion:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 14 In a recent medical study, all of the study participants who had abused alcohol in the past five years were found to be suffering with depression. Each of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends EXCEPT: 1. [x] (A) Both alcohol abuse issues and depression can be solved via the use of therapy specially designed for the treatment of alcohol abuse issues. 2. [ ] (B) The depression and alcohol abuse of the study participants were not both results of heavy use of illicit drugs. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) The study participants were not found to be suffering with depression before they started abusing alcohol. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) The study was not set up in a way such that people who were not suffering with depression would be very unlikely to participate. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) The concurrence of alcohol abuse and depression seen in the people studied was not merely a coincidence. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium The argument in this question has a cause-and-effect conclusion, which is based on the fact that people who were known to abuse alcohol were found to be suffering with depression.
Arriving at that conclusion from the evidence requires assuming that the observed pattern was not the result of something other than alcohol abuse causing depression. In other words, the author assumes that she is correct in her evaluation of the situation. She assumes that it is in fact the abuse of alcohol that is the cause of depression. She assumes that there is not an alternative explanation for the pattern of alcohol abuse and depression.
