Because this is an “EXCEPT” question, four of the answers will be assumptions upon which the argument depends; these will be incorrect answers. Our job is to find the one answer that is NOT an assumption upon which the argument depends
YOUR CHOICE(A) Both alcohol abuse issues and depression can be solved via the use of therapy specially designed for the treatment of alcohol abuse issues. This choice could seem to be an assumption that the author must be making, because it could seem necessary to assume that therapy designed for treatment for alcohol abuse issues could solve both alcohol abuse issues and depression in order to conclude that abusing alcohol causes depression.
Notice, however, that the truth is that this choice is not an assumption necessary for going from the evidence about alcohol abuse and depression to the conclusion that abusing alcohol causes depression. Rather, this choice is a conclusion that follows from the author’s conclusion. It makes sense to conclude that, if abusing alcohol causes depression, therapy designed to treat alcohol abuse issues could solve both issues.
Meanwhile, coming to the conclusion that alcohol abuse causes depression does not require assuming anything about therapy designed for treating alcohol abuse issues. One could arrive at the argument’s conclusion even if such therapy did not even exist.
Assumption upon which the argument depends? No.
(B) The depression and alcohol abuse of the study participants were not both results of heavy use of illicit drugs. Going from the evidence that the participants in the study both abused alcohol and were found to be depressed to the conclusion that alcohol abuse causes depression requires assuming that a third factor, use of illicit drugs, did not cause both the alcohol abuse and the depression. After all, if a third factor, use of illicit drugs, DID cause both the alcohol abuse and the depression, then the conclusion would be incorrect.
Thus, if the depression and alcohol abuse of the study participants WERE both results of heavy use of illicit drugs, the argument falls apart. Therefore, the author must assume that the depression and alcohol abuse of the study participants were NOT both results of heavy use of illicit drugs.
Assumption upon which the argument depends? Yes.
(C) The study participants were not found to be suffering with depression before they started abusing alcohol. The conclusion that alcohol abuse causes depression depends on the assumption that the depression seen in the study participants did not precede the alcohol abuse. After all, if the study participants WERE found to be suffering with depression before they started abusing alcohol, then it could not be the case that abusing alcohol caused the depression.
Assumption upon which the argument depends? Yes.
(D) The study was not set up in a way such that people who were not suffering with depression would be very unlikely to participate. If the study had been set up in such a way that people who were not depressed would have been very unlikely to participate, then it could very well be that the reason that all the participants who abused alcohol were depressed was that everyone who participated in the study was depressed. In other words, non-depressed people did not partake in the study. In this case, we’d have an alternative explanation for why all of the study participants who had abused alcohol in the past five years were found to be suffering with depression: everyone in the study was depressed anyway. In this case, the author’s argument would fall apart.
So, in order to connect the alcohol abuse and depression of the study participants to the conclusion that alcohol abuse causes depression, the author must assume that the study was not set up in a way such that people who were not depressed would have been very unlikely to participate.
Assumption upon which the argument depends? Yes.
(E) The concurrence of alcohol abuse and depression seen in the people studied was not merely a coincidence. When two events, such as alcohol abuse and depression, occur together, it is possible that there is no cause and effect relationship between the two events, that the pattern observed is merely a coincidence. After all, the world can be a random place, and there are many patterns to be found that may seem to result from cause-and-effect relationships but result from nothing more than chance.
In the case of this argument, if it were true that there really is no cause-and-effect relationship between alcohol abuse and depression, we’d have an alternative explanation for the observation made by the author, and the argument would fall apart.
Therefore, the author must assume that the concurrence of alcohol abuse and depression seen in the people studied was not merely a coincidence.
Assumption upon which the argument depends? Yes.
Correct Answer: (A)
summary:
Lesson Content Many arguments that appear in the GMAT involve cause-and-effect reasoning, something that we’ll often mention in the chapters that follow, but we’ll begin building our expertise in cause-and-effect reasoning here.
An author makes a cause-and-effect claim when she maintains that some factor x is the cause of some outcome y.
When an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she assumes that her cause-and-effect claim is correct; she assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observed events.
title: 2.6.2 Assumption Correct Answer Type 2: States an Assumption that Must be True in order for a Cause-and-Effect Conclusion to be Correct

contents:
Lesson Content In Assumption questions, we often encounter arguments that conclude that some plan will not work or that some outcome will not occur.
However, the evidence that she provides will not be sufficient alone for the argument to work.
💡MUST KNOW:
Whenever an author concludes that a plan will not work or that some outcome will not occur, she must assume that there is not some other way or some other path (that she has failed to consider) via which the plan could work or via which the outcome could occur.
In other words, whenever an author concludes that a plan will not work or that some outcome will not occur, she assumes that she is correct and that she has taken into consideration all possible variables that could have an impact on the plan or the outcome.
💡MUST KNOW:
Whenever an author concludes that a plan will not work or that, in general, some outcome will not occur, she assumes that she is correct and that she has taken into consideration all possible variables that could have an impact on the plan or the outcome. She assumes that she has not failed to consider any variable that could make her argument unsound.
In this type of question, the correct answer will be one that states (in one way or another) an assumption that there is not another way or path via which the plan could work or via which the outcome could occur.
In the following mini example, notice that the author concludes that a certain plan will not work.
Community leaders in Kanelston are encouraging residents to grow their own vegetables. However, most residents of Kanelston live in apartment buildings and thus do not have yards in which they could plant gardens. Therefore, what the community leaders are doing is not going to make much difference.
Which of the following is an assumption upon which the conclusion depends?
(Correct Answer) The roofs of most apartment buildings in Kanelston are not good locations for rooftop gardens.
Notice, the author’s conclusion that what the community leaders are doing is not going to make much difference is based on the fact that one path to having a garden, planting in a yard, is not available to the residents.
Let’s look at another mini example in which the assumption is that there is not another path via which an outcome could occur:
In each of the past three years, Dustin Manufacturing has succeeded in increasing production without adding to its work space. However, now, Dustin Manufacturing’s facilities are at a point such that there is no more room for either more employees or more equipment. Therefore, if Dustin Manufacturing does not add to its work space, its production will not increase this year.
Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument relies?
(Correct Answer) There is no way for employees at Dustin manufacturing to produce more per employee.
The author concludes that production will not increase without an increase in work space.
Notice that the correct answer states the argument’s unstated assumption that there is not an alternative path via which production could increase, an increase in production per employee.
In general, if the conclusion of the argument in an Assumption question is that a plan will not succeed, an outcome cannot occur, or that anything cannot, will not, or did not happen, it is likely that the correct answer choice will state an assumption that a certain alternative path is not or was not available.
💡MUST KNOW:
In an Assumption question, an argument may depend on the assumption that an alternative path to an outcome is not available.
Let’s practice identifying this type of assumption.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 15 Because residents of Mergate had complained for years about the noise associated with Mergate airport, the city took the radical step of closing the airport entirely. Which of the following is an assumption necessary for arriving at the conclusion drawn? 1. [ ] (A) Most businesses in Mergate depend on spending by vacationers for the majority of their income. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Residents of Mergate have not noticed a significant reduction in noise pollution since the airport was closed. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) The improvement in the ambience in Mergate resulting from the closing of the airport will not cause vacationers to feel that making some extra effort to get there is worthwhile. 4. [ ] (D) In Mergate, growth in business not dependent on spending by vacationers will not make up for any city tax revenue lost because of a reduction in vacationer related business. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) The closing of the airport has not benefited any businesses located in Mergate. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium The conclusion of the argument is the following:
Conclusion: Vacation related businesses in Mergate will no longer be able to successfully compete with similar businesses in Wave City.
The conclusion is supported by the fact that one path via which businesses in Mergate were able to compete with businesses in Wave City, vacationers’ having the ability to fly directly into Mergate, has been eliminated. Of course, in order to arrive at the conclusion from this one premise, the author must assume that there will not be another path via which businesses in Mergate will be able to successfully compete with businesses in Wave City. In other words, the author believes that she has carefully and completely evaluated the situation and that there is not another way that businesses in Mergate will be able to successfully compete with businesses in Wave City.
(A) Most businesses in Mergate depend on spending by vacationers for the majority of their income. This answer could be tempting, because it could seem to reinforce the idea that Mergate businesses will have difficulty competing with businesses in Wave City as a result of the closing of the airport.
Notice, however, that this choice supports the wrong conclusion. The conclusion is not that most Mergate businesses will have difficulty competing. It’s that certain businesses, the vacation related businesses, will have difficulty competing.
Going from the premise that traveling to Mergate by air requires flying into Wave City airport to the conclusion that vacation related businesses in Mergate will no longer be able to successfully compete does not require assuming that most businesses in Mergate depend on spending by vacationers. Even if only a small fraction of businesses in Mergate depend on spending by vacationers, the conclusion could be supported by the evidence.
(B) Residents of Mergate have not noticed a significant reduction in noise pollution since the airport was closed. It is not necessary to assume that closing the airport did not result in a noticeable change in noise pollution in order to conclude that closing the airport has made competing with businesses in Wave City difficult for certain businesses in Mergate. In other words, the argument could be sound regardless of whether what this answer choice says is true.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The improvement in the ambience in Mergate resulting from the closing of the airport will not cause vacationers to feel that making some extra effort to get there is worthwhile. The conclusion that closing the airport has created a situation such that vacation related business in Mergate cannot successfully compete with similar businesses in Wave City depends on the assumption that closing the airport has not made available a new path via which the Mergate businesses can compete.
Thus, the argument does not work unless what this choice says, that the closing of the airport has NOT given vacationers a reason to make some extra effort to get Mergate, is true.
After all, what if it were true that the closing of the airport HAS given vacationers a reason to make some extra effort to get Mergate? In that case, the premise that traveling to Mergate by air requires flying into Wave City airport would not support the conclusion.
(D) In Mergate, growth in business not dependent on spending by vacationers will not make up for any city tax revenue lost because of a reduction in vacationer related business. The conclusion is not that Mergate will experience a net loss in tax revenue but rather that certain businesses in Mergate will not be able to compete with businesses in Wave City. So, since this choice supports a conclusion about tax revenue rather than one about businesses competing, this answer choice supports the wrong conclusion.
(E) The closing of the airport has not benefited any businesses located in Mergate. Concluding that certain businesses in Mergate will not be able to compete as a result of the closing of the airport does not require assuming that no businesses at all in Mergate will benefit from the closing of the airport. In other words, the argument could be sound regardless of whether what this answer choice says is true.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 16 While diamonds are not as rare as some other types of gemstones, the companies that mine diamonds restrict the number of diamonds that they make available and thus create the impression that diamonds are rare and, therefore, valuable. Which of the following is an assumption upon which the above conclusion depends? 1. [ ] (A) Demand for diamonds will increase to a point such that diamond prices would remain at their current levels even if supply were not artificially restricted. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Demand for non-diamond gemstones, such as rubies and emeralds, will not increase substantially. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) It would not become more difficult for diamond mining companies to restrict the number of diamonds that they make available, were additional large, minable deposits of diamonds found. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Laboratories that make artificial diamonds will never produce and sell in significant quantities diamonds that are indistinguishable from mined diamonds. 5. [ ] (E) The price of a diamond not only depends on the supply of and demand for diamonds but also is affected by the quality of the diamond. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: hard Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Companies that mine diamonds restrict the number that they make available and thus create the impression that diamonds are rare and valuable.
Premise: Demand for diamonds is increasing.
Conclusion: As long as the companies that mine diamonds continue to restrict the number of diamonds that they make available, diamond prices will not decrease substantially.
Notice that author is concluding that a certain outcome will occur (diamond prices will not decrease substantially). This argument is based on what seems to be a straightforward case of restricted supply and increasing demand working together to support prices.
Remember that, whenever an author concludes that some outcome will not occur, she assumes that she is correct and that she has taken into consideration all possible variables that could have an impact on the outcome. She assumes that she has not failed to consider any variable that could make her argument unsound.
Therefore, this argument depends on the assumption that there is not some path other than via diamond mining companies via which additional supply of diamonds could come into the market and thus cause a decrease in the price of diamonds.
(A) Demand for diamonds will increase to a point such that diamond prices would remain at their current levels even if supply were not artificially restricted. Notice that the conclusion is that diamond prices will not decrease as long as companies that mine diamonds continue to restrict the number of diamonds that they make available.
So, arriving at the conclusion does not require assuming anything about what would happen if companies that mine were not to continue to restrict the supply of diamonds.
(B) Demand for non-diamond gemstones, such as rubies and emeralds, will not increase substantially. This answer might somehow appear to be correct, as one could get the impression that, if demand for other types of gemstones were to increase, demand for diamonds would decrease. Therefore, one might conclude that, in order for diamond prices to remain at their current levels, demand for other types of gemstones must not increase substantially.
However, the truth is that we have no reason to believe that demand for diamonds would decrease if demand for other types of stones were to increase. In fact, demand for all types of gemstones could increase simultaneously.
So, it is not the case that this answer has to be true in order for the conclusion to hold.
(C) It would not become more difficult for diamond mining companies to restrict the number of diamonds that they make available, were additional large, minable deposits of diamonds found. This choice is a trap, since what it is related to, the idea that companies that mine diamonds will be able to continue to restrict the number of diamonds that they make available, may seem to be a key aspect of the support for the conclusion of the argument. However, this choice is incorrect in multiple ways.
For one thing, difficulty restricting the number of diamonds available does not equate to restricting the number of diamonds available being impossible. The companies could still restrict the number of diamonds available even if it were more difficult to do so.
Secondly, and more importantly, the conclusion of the argument does not depend on diamond mining companies’ being able to continue to restrict the number of diamonds that they make available. Rather, the conclusion is that, IF the companies continue to restrict the number of diamonds that they make available, diamond prices will not decrease substantially.
So, this choice supports a conclusion different from the argument’s conclusion. This choice supports the conclusion that the diamond mining companies will be able to continue to restrict supply.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Laboratories that make artificial diamonds will never produce and sell in significant quantities diamonds that are indistinguishable from mined diamonds. In concluding that the price of diamonds will not decrease substantially as long as the companies that mine diamonds continue to restrict the number of diamonds that they make available, the argument depends upon the assumption that diamonds will not come into the marketplace via a new path, a path other than via the mining companies. If diamonds were to come from another source, then the supply of diamonds could increase even if the mining companies were to continue to restrict the number that they make available.
Therefore, the argument depends on the assumption that laboratories will not begin making significant quantities of diamonds that are indistinguishable from mined ones. After all, if laboratories WILL begin making significant quantities of diamonds that are indistinguishable from mined ones, then the evidence does not support the conclusion, and the argument falls apart.
(E) The price of a diamond not only depends on the supply of and demand for diamonds but also is affected by the quality of the diamond. This answer could be tempting because it is relevant to the argument, as it seems to be saying, basically, that the price of diamonds depends on factors other than the supply of and demand for diamonds, a statement that could potentially be seen as weakening the argument.
However, while such a statement might be relevant to the argument and might weaken it, we are looking, not for a weakener, but rather for an assumption integral to the argument.
In general, when we’re seeking an assumption upon which an argument relies, an answer choice that says something that weakens the argument will not be correct.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now talk about another assumption that could appear in a correct answer: that a solution to a problem will not cause the same problem or a similar one.
summary:
Lesson Content In Assumption questions, we often encounter arguments that conclude that some plan will not work or that some outcome will not occur.
However, the evidence that she provides will not be sufficient alone for the argument to work.
💡MUST KNOW:
Whenever an author concludes that a plan will not work or that some outcome will not occur, she must assume that there is not some other way or some other path (that she has failed to consider) via which the plan could work or via which the outcome could occur.
title: 2.6.3 Assumption Correct Answer Type 3: States an Assumption that There are No Other Paths to Success

contents:
Lesson Content As we now know, many arguments in the GMAT focus on plans or courses of action.
Another way of thinking about this is that, when an author sets forth a plan or course of action, she believes that she has correctly and completely analyzed whatever situation the plan or course of action centers on.
So then, in Assumption questions, authors typically make the assumption that a plan or a course of action they suggest for solving a problem will not itself result in the same problem in a new way.
💡MUST KNOW:
In an Assumption question, an argument may depend on the assumption that a plan or course of action considered the path to solving a problem will not itself result in the same problem.
Consider the following argument:
Overfishing in the waters around Seatown has begun to significantly deplete the populations of fish in them. WildWater Fisheries Inc. has proposed setting up fish farms in these waters. By focusing on farming fish rather than on catching the wild fish that live in the waters near Seatown, we will stop the depletion of the wild fish stocks.
The above argument makes the case for taking a course of action, setting up fish farms rather than fishing for wild fish.
If this argument were in an Assumption question, it might be followed by an answer choice like the following.
(Correct Answer) Crowding fish together in offshore farms does not create conditions that support the multiplication of organisms that infect and decimate wild fish populations.
Notice that the above answer choice states an assumption necessary for the logic of the argument to work.
In other words, if fish farms become breeding grounds for organisms that decimate wild fish populations, then setting up fish farms would cause the same problem that setting up fish farms was meant to solve.
Consider the following examples:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 17 Homeowners Association Member: Some members of our community have expressed concerns about the number of mice living here. Which of the following, if true, is an assumption upon which the above argument depends? 1. [ ] (A) Mice carry and transmit more diseases than are carried and transmitted by other types of rodents. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Mice would not become ill if they were to eat cayenne pepper placed around the foundation of a house. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) There are more mice living in the community in question than there were a few years ago. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Predators, which are necessary for keeping mouse populations controlled, would not completely avoid an area with a scent of cayenne pepper. 5. [ ] (E) Cayenne pepper repels not only mice but also ants. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy The homeowners association member is proposing a plan. What plan? To place cayenne pepper around foundations of houses.
The goal of the plan is to reduce the number of mice in the community.
Has she considered the plan well? Is there some way that the plan might result in the problem it is meant to solve or a similar one? Might placing cayenne pepper around houses actually result in an increase in the number of mice?
(A) Mice carry and transmit more diseases than are carried and transmitted by other types of rodents. Notice that the conclusion is not that reducing the number of mice in the community is important for some reason, such as that mice carry and transmit diseases.
The conclusion is that, by placing cayenne pepper around the foundations of houses, people in the community will reduce the number of mice in the community. In other words, the conclusion is simply that the plan for reducing the number of mice in the community will work.
Of course, the plan could work regardless of whether mice carry more diseases than other rodents carry.
So, what this choice says is not an assumption upon which the argument relies.
(B) Mice would not become ill if they were to eat cayenne pepper placed around the foundation of a house. The conclusion of the argument is that, by placing cayenne pepper around the foundations of their houses, people will reduce the number of mice in their community.
The evidence used is that mice are repelled by cayenne pepper. In other words, the evidence is that mice don’t like cayenne pepper and avoid it.
Could the plan work even if mice WOULD become ill if they were to eat the cayenne pepper?
Common sense tells us that mice would not eat the pepper, and that, even if they did, for some insane reason, eat the pepper and become ill, they would continue to be repelled by cayenne pepper, in which case the plan could work.
So, the argument does not depend on the assumption that mice WOULD NOT become ill if they were to eat the cayenne pepper placed around a house.
(C) There are more mice living in the community in question than there were a few years ago. The conclusion is simply that the plan for reducing the number of mice in the community will work.
Placing cayenne pepper around the foundations of houses could result in a reduction in the number of mice in the community regardless of whether there are more, fewer, or just as many mice as there were in the community a few years ago. In other words, the number of mice present does not affect whether mice in general are repelled by cayenne pepper.
So, arriving at the conclusion that the plan will work does not require assuming that there are more mice in the community than there were a few years ago.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Predators, which are necessary for keeping mouse populations controlled, would not completely avoid an area with a scent of cayenne pepper. As you now know, one common assumption that authors typically make is that a plan to solve a problem will not itself result in the same problem in a new way.
In this case, the homeowners association member assumes that placing cayenne pepper around the foundations of houses will not somehow exacerbate the mouse issue. After all, if placing cayenne pepper around the foundations of houses would somehow result in an increase in the number of mice, then the argument would fall apart.
So, imagine if predators, which are necessary for keeping mouse populations controlled, WOULD completely avoid an area with a scent of cayenne pepper? In that case, the plan to solve the problem would itself cause the same problem in a new way because, without the predators, which are necessary for keeping mouse populations controlled, the mouse population would be uncontrolled. The argument would fall apart.
Therefore, the author must assume that predators, which are necessary for keeping mouse populations controlled, would NOT completely avoid an area with a scent of cayenne pepper.
(E) Cayenne pepper repels not only mice but also ants. The author’s conclusion is that “if we all place cayenne pepper around the foundations of our houses, we will certainly reduce the number of mice in our community.”
Notice that this conclusion, and the argument in general, is completely about reducing the number of mice in the community, and that the plan for reducing the number of mice could work regardless of whether cayenne pepper repels ants.
Therefore, the author does not need to assume that cayenne pepper repels not only mice but also ants.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 18 Rowenton City Council Member: While crime rates in Rowenton continue to drop, the police still receive a steady stream of reports of people breaking into parked automobiles at night to steal items left in them. Which of the following is an assumption upon which the city council member relies in coming to the conclusion above? 1. [ ] (A) People generally feel safer at night if the streets via which they are travelling are well lit. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Putting out public service messages advising people to refrain from leaving items in automobiles at night would, at best, cause only a temporary change in habits. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) There are no reasons other than to deter stealing from parked automobiles for installing additional streetlights in Rowenton. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) In Rowenton, the rate of nighttime automobile break-ins has not been dropping as fast as have the rates of other types of crime. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) People who steal items from cars do not depend on light from streetlights in determining whether there is in a car anything worth breaking in for. View Solution Question Level: medium Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: People are breaking into parked cars at night to steal items left in them.
Proposal: Install streetlights on streets that are currently dark at night.
Expected Outcome: By doing so, we will achieve a reduction in the number of nighttime automobile break-ins.
In suggesting that installing streetlights on streets that are currently dark at night will result in a reduction in nighttime automobile break-ins, the city council member is assuming that the light from streetlights will deter breaking into cars and not somehow make the problem worse. After all, what if the light from the streetlights would somehow make the problem worse? In such a case, the argument would fall apart.
Remember that when an author sets forth a plan, he believes that that plan will work; he believes that he has correctly and completely analyzed the situation. In believing that the plan will work, the author assumes that the plan he suggests for solving a problem will not result in the same problem in a new way.
(A) People generally feel safer at night if the streets via which they are travelling are well lit. While what this choice says seems plausible and seems to be in line with the reasoning of the argument, the conclusion is specifically about reducing automobile break-ins by installing streetlights. Since it does not have to be the case that people feel safer on well-lit streets in order for it to be the case that installing streetlights would be effective in reducing automobile break-ins, what this choice says is not an assumption upon which the city council member relies in making the argument.
In answering Assumption questions, we must be careful to not choose as correct answer choices that say things that are plausible yet are not assumptions upon which the arguments depend.
(B) Putting out public service messages advising people to refrain from leaving items in automobiles at night would, at best, cause only a temporary change in habits. This choice could be tempting, since a test-taker might feel that, if the city council member is recommending installing street lights, the city council member must be assuming that simply advising people to refrain from leaving items in cars overnight would not do much to solve the break-in problem, possibly because people would only temporarily change their habits.
However, the truth is that the city council member does not have to assume anything about other strategies for reducing break-ins in order to propose adding streetlights to achieve a reduction in break-ins. In other words, the city council member is saying not that adding streetlights is the only strategy that would work but rather that adding streetlights is a strategy that would work.
One concept that we will see over and over as this course progresses is the concept of the “different plan” or the “better plan” trap answer. If we look carefully at what this answer choice says, we see that it mentions a different plan. In general, unless an author claims that his plan is the only plan or the best plan, he need not assume that there are not other plans that could work.
(C) There are no reasons other than to deter stealing from parked automobiles for installing additional streetlights in Rowenton. That the city council member is focusing on reducing break-ins by installing streetlights does not mean that the city council member must be assuming that reducing break-ins is the only reason for installing streetlights. In other words, he never makes the claim that reducing break-ins is the only reason for installing streetlights. His argument leaves open the possibility that there could be many other reasons for installing the streetlights.
(D) In Rowenton, the rate of nighttime automobile break-ins has not been dropping as fast as have the rates of other types of crime. The fact that the city council member said that crime rates continue to drop but is still suggesting installing streetlights in order to cause a reduction in nighttime automobile break-ins might seem to indicate that the city council member believes that the rate of nighttime automobile break-ins has not been dropping as fast as have the rates of other types of crime.
However, regardless of whether what the passage says even supports what this choice says, we are not looking for a conclusion that follows from what is stated in the passage. We are looking for an assumption necessary for coming to the city council member’s conclusion.
The city council member’s conclusion is simply that a reduction in nighttime automobile break-ins could be achieved by installing additional streetlights. The success of that plan does not depend on there being a difference between how fast the rate of nighttime automobile break-ins has been dropping and how fast the rates of other types of crime have been dropping.
YOUR CHOICE(E) People who steal items from cars do not depend on light from streetlights in determining whether there is in a car anything worth breaking in for. If people who break into cars at night depend on light from streetlights for determining whether to break into cars, then it could be the case that, rather than result in a decrease in nighttime break-ins, adding streetlights would result in an increase in break-ins.
Therefore, the city council member must be assuming that people don’t depend on the light from streetlights for determining whether to break into cars.
Notice that this assumption is a piece of missing information that MUST BE TRUE in order for the logic of the argument to work. Also, notice that this answer choice confirms that a course of action considered the path to solving a problem will not result in the same problem in a new way. Finally notice that if People who steal items from cars DO depend on light from streetlights in determining whether there is in a car anything worth breaking in for, then the argument falls apart.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 19 While there is often heavy traffic congestion on Coastal Highway in Dolya, the number of vehicles present on Coastal Highway during peak driving hours is actually slightly less than the estimated congestion free capacity of the highway. Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument relies? 1. [ ] (A) Even without the revenue generated via toll collection on Coastal Highway, Dolya would be able to maintain a balanced budget. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) The congestion around the toll collection stations is caused by cars entering Coastal Highway via entrance ramps near the stations rather than by the stations themselves. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Most people who drive to destinations readily accessible via Coastal Highway do not avoid Coastal Highway because of the tolls that they would have to pay to use it. 4. [ ] (D) Even during off peak driving hours, significant congestion is caused by the toll collection stations on Coastal Highway. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) The number of drivers driving at excessive speeds on Coastal highway would increase were the toll collection stations to be removed. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard The argument makes the following case:
Background: There is often heavy traffic congestion on Coastal Highway in Dolya.
Premise: The number of vehicles present on Coastal Highway during peak driving hours is actually slightly less than the estimated congestion free capacity of the highway.
Premise: Most of the congestion occurs around the toll collection stations.
Conclusion: Removal of the toll collection stations would solve most traffic congestion problems on Coastal Highway.
Notice that the author has set forth a plan, which is to remove the toll collection stations on Coastal Highway. When an author sets forth a plan, he believes that that plan will work. In believing that the plan will work, the author assumes that the plan he suggests for solving a problem will not result in the same problem in a new way.
So, in this argument, one assumption necessary for coming to the conclusion is that removing the toll collection stations would not itself somehow cause traffic congestion on the highway. After all, if the removal of the toll collection stations would cause congestion, then the implementation of the plan would cause in a new way the same problem that the plan is meant to solve.
(A) Even without the revenue generated via toll collection on Coastal Highway, Dolya would be able to maintain a balanced budget. While the revenue generated by the toll collection stations may be worth considering in evaluating a plan to remove them, the argument’s conclusion is not that Dolya will be able to maintain a balanced budget. It’s that removing the stations would solve traffic congestion problems.
So, this choice supports the wrong conclusion and does not have any effect on this argument.
(B) The congestion around the toll collection stations is caused by cars entering Coastal Highway via entrance ramps near the stations rather than by the stations themselves. What this choice says actually weakens the argument.
If cars entering the highway, rather than the toll collection stations, are causing the congestion, then removing the stations would have no effect on the congestion. So, the author can’t be making this assumption.
In general, when we’re seeking an assumption upon which an argument rests, an answer choice that says something that weakens the argument will not be correct.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Most people who drive to destinations readily accessible via Coastal Highway do not avoid Coastal Highway because of the tolls that they would have to pay to use it. If “most” people going to places accessible via Coastal Highway avoid using it because of the tolls, then more than half of the people who would otherwise use the highway are not using it because of the tolls. So, removing the tolls could result in a doubling of the number of cars using the highway.
Now, notice that a premise of the argument states that the number of cars on the highway is “slightly less than the highway’s congestion free capacity.” Therefore, doubling the number of cars on the highway seems certain to push the number well above the congestion free capacity, and thus cause congestion.
So, the argument relies on assuming that most people who drive to destinations readily accessible via Coastal Highway DO NOT avoid Coastal Highway because of the tolls, because if the tolls are keeping all those people from using the highway, then removing the tolls would cause congestion, rather than solve congestion problems.
Notice that what this choice says is a missing piece of information that must be true in order for the logic of the argument to work; this is the assumption. Notice also that this answer choice confirms that a course of action considered the path to solving a problem will not itself cause the same problem in a new way.
So, if what this choice says is not true, then the argument falls apart.
(D) Even during off peak driving hours, significant congestion is caused by the toll collection stations on Coastal Highway. This choice could be tempting, because one could follow a faulty line of thinking along the lines of the following: “If there is no traffic congestion around the toll collection stations during off peak hours, then removing the toll collection stations would not affect congestion during off peak hours, and maybe any congestion during off peak hours is somewhere else, and thus, hmm, removing them may not make much difference. So, maybe we have to assume that there is congestion around the toll collection stations even during off peak hours.” Of course, the truth is that the passage makes clear that most of the congestion occurs around the toll collection stations. So, it is not necessary to assume that there is congestion around the toll collection stations at any particular time, because that most of the congestion that occurs around the stations has been stated as fact.
In general, we must remember that the premises are always considered factual, so an answer choice that says something that appears to confirm a premise will never be correct.
(E) The number of drivers driving at excessive speeds on Coastal highway would increase were the toll collection stations to be removed. While what this choice says seems reasonable given what we know about the real world, we are not looking for a statement that says something reasonable. We are looking for an assumption integral to the argument, and assuming that more people will drive at excessive speeds if the stations are removed is not necessary for concluding that removing the stations will result in the elimination of most traffic congestion.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now talk about correct answers that highlight assumptions that things that are likely true are actually true.
summary:
Lesson Content As we now know, many arguments in the GMAT focus on plans or courses of action.
Another way of thinking about this is that, when an author sets forth a plan or course of action, she believes that she has correctly and completely analyzed whatever situation the plan or course of action centers on.
So then, in Assumption questions, authors typically make the assumption that a plan or a course of action they suggest for solving a problem will not itself result in the same problem in a new way.
title: 2.6.4 Assumption Correct Answer Type 4: Highlights an Assumption that a Plan for Solving a Problem will not Result in the Same Problem or a Similar One

contents:
Lesson Content Sometimes in an Assumption question, an author will base her argument on an idea that is probably true but is not guaranteed to be true.
💡MUST KNOW:
Sometimes, an argument in an Assumptions question depends on the assumption that something that is likely true is actually true.
Consider the following example:
Three years ago, some of the students attending Rockville High School began for the first-time attending weekend classes designed to help them pass a state-wide assessment test. Since that time, the percentage of Rockville High School students passing the test has been greater than it has been any other time in the school’s history. Clearly, the weekend classes are responsible for this increased pass rate of Rockville High School students. Upon which of the following does the logic of the argument above depend?
(Correct Answer) At least some of the Rockville High School students who have attended the weekend classes have taken the state-wide assessment test.
Notice that the author makes an inconspicuous, yet important, assumption.
Some of you are saying, “Wait – that statement is obvious.
Look back to the argument.
This is the point.
So, without stating that some of the students who have attended the weekend classes have actually taken the test, the author concludes that the weekend classes are responsible for the increased test pass rate of Rockville High School students.
In general terms, we see that the author assumes that what is likely true is actually true.
Notice what would happen if it were not true that at least some of the students who have attended the weekend classes have actually taken the state-wide assessment test.
After all, we’d be saying, “NONE of the students who have attended the weekend classes have taken the state-wide assessment test.” In this, case it would not make sense for the author to conclude that the weekend classes are responsible for the increased pass rate.
If you have a hard time seeing that the argument would be busted, add that fact into the argument:
Three years ago, some of the students attending Rockville High School began for the first-time attending weekend classes designed to help them pass a state-wide assessment test. Since that time, the percentage of Rockville High School students passing the test has been greater than it has been any other time in the school’s history. None of the students who have attended the weekend classes designed to help them pass the state-wide assessment test have taken the test.
Does it make sense to conclude from what the above passage says that the weekend classes are responsible for the increased pass rate of Rockville High School students? Clearly, it does not.
So, again, the author must be assuming that at least some of the students who began attending the weekend classes have taken the test.
Consider the following examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 20 The unemployment rate in Catunah was greater than 10 percent for years. Which of the following is an assumption necessary for arriving at the above conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) The government of Catunah will continue to offer free work skills training until the unemployment rate is at what is considered a normal level, 6 percent. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) The training offered by the government was designed to help people acquire key skills required for working at certain types of companies located in Catunah. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) The reason the unemployment rate in Catunah decreased is that many unemployed people left to find work in other countries. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Some people who received the work skills training offered by the government of Catunah subsequently found employment. 5. [ ] (E) The unemployment rate in Catunah will decrease further if the government continues to offer free work skills training. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy Let’s break down the argument.
Premise: The unemployment rate in Catunah was greater than 10 percent for years.
Premise: The government started offering free work skills training to unemployed people.
Premise: The unemployment has decreased to around 7 percent.
Conclusion: Helping people to acquire work skills facilitates their finding employment.
The premises indicate that the government offered work skills training and subsequently unemployment decreased. The conclusion is that helping people to gain work skills facilitates their finding employment.
Of course, there is a gap between the premises and the conclusion, as the argument does not clearly establish that the decrease in the unemployment rate was in fact related to the work skills training.
While it is likely that there was at least some connection between the two, the argument relies on the assumption that there was in fact such a connection.
In other words, the validity of this argument rests on the assumption that something that is likely true is actually true.
(A) The government of Catunah will continue to offer free work skills training until the unemployment rate is at what is considered a normal level, 6 percent. The argument is about the effects of work skills training and is based on evidence from the past. We don’t need to assume that the government will offer work skills training in the future in order to make an argument about the effect that offering work skills training had in the past.
(B) The training offered by the government was designed to help people acquire key skills required for working at certain types of companies located in Catunah. What this answer choice says may explain why it is that people who went through the training found employment. However, we are not looking for an explanation, and what this choice expresses is not an assumption that is necessary for arriving at the conclusion.
We don’t have to assume that the training was designed to help people gain key skills required for working at certain types of companies in order to go from the fact that work skills training was followed by a decrease in unemployment to the conclusion that helping people to develop work skills facilitates their finding employment.
In general, when we’re seeking an assumption upon which an argument rests, an answer choice that presents an explanation for one of the facts presented in the passage will not be the correct answer.
(C) The reason the unemployment rate in Catunah decreased is that many unemployed people left to find work in other countries. What this answer choice says actually weakens the argument.
If the reason Catunah’s unemployment rate decreased is that unemployed people left Catunah, then the government’s offering work skills training is not the reason why the unemployment rate decreased. In that case, the conclusion that helping people to acquire work skills facilitates their finding employment is not supported by the premises.
So, the argument definitely does not depend on what this answer choice says.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Some people who received the work skills training offered by the government of Catunah subsequently found employment. We know that the unemployment rate decreased, and given what is said in the passage, and what we know about the real world, one could say that it seems likely that people who acquired work skills through the training found employment.
However, there is a difference between what is likely and what is factual. In order to go from the premises of this argument to the conclusion that helping people to acquire work skills facilitates their finding employment, one has to assume that some people who took part in the training did in fact find employment. This piece of missing information must be true in order for the logic of the argument to work. After all, what would happen to the argument if we learned that none of people who took part in the training did in fact find employment? The argument would no longer work – right?
Notice that this answer choice confirms that something that is likely true is actually true, a key assumption in this argument.
(E) The unemployment rate in Catunah will decrease further if the government continues to offer free work skills training. Even if, for some reason, it were not the case that the unemployment rate in Catunah would continue to decrease if the government were to continue to provide free work skills training, it could still be the case that the decrease in the unemployment rate that did occur resulted from the government’s offering free work skills training. So, what this choice expresses is not an assumption necessary for arriving at the argument’s conclusion.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 21 Satellite Media, a new cellular phone service provider, will soon begin advertising heavily on the internet. Which of the following is an assumption logically necessary for coming to the conclusion of the above argument? 1. [ ] (A) Satellite Media will put competing cellular phone service providers out of business. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) No residents of Brookville have land lines that they use for telephone service. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Some of the residents of the town of Brookville use the internet. 4. [ ] (D) Satellite Media’s cellular phone plans cost less than the other cellular phone plans available to Brookville residents. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Some residents of Brookville are happy with the quality of the cellular phone reception provided by the cellular phone service providers they currently use. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Satellite Media, a new cellular phone service provider, will soon begin advertising heavily on the internet.
Premise: Many residents of the town of Brookville have long complained about the poor cellular phone reception they experience.
Conclusion: Once Satellite Media begins its internet advertising campaign, residents of Brookville will begin switching their cellular phone service to plans provided by Satellite Media.
In going from the argument’s premises to the conclusion, the author is making a number of assumptions. For instance, does Satellite Media even provide cellular phone service in Brookville? The author is assuming that it does.
Of course, we don’t have to come up with any assumptions the author is making on our own. The assumption we have to find will be stated by the correct answer, and in this question, the correct answer states something that is likely true that the author has assumed is actually true.
(A) Satellite Media will put competing cellular phone service providers out of business. This choice is tempting because it is clearly related to what the passage says. However, this choice is not correct because it’s not an assumption necessary for arriving at the argument’s conclusion. Instead, it’s a conclusion that is supported, at least somewhat, by what the passage says.
After all, if the argument’s conclusion that people in Brookville will switch to using Satellite Media’s cellular phone service is correct, then maybe others will switch to using Satellite Media as well. So, given what the passage says, it seems possible that, as this choice says, Satellite Media will put competing cellular phone service providers out of business.
Of course, we’re not looking for a conclusion supported by the passage. We’re looking for an assumption upon which the argument depends, and the conclusion that people in Brookville will start switching to using Satellite Media’s cellular phone service is supported by the premises regardless of whether Satellite Media will put other cellular phone service providers out of business.
In order to avoid choosing a choice like this one, we have to remember what we are looking for and not get tempted into choosing a choice just because it’s closely related to what the argument says.
YOUR CHOICE(B) No residents of Brookville have land lines that they use for telephone service. Notice that the argument is about people switching their cellular phone service providers. Does the author have to assume that no residents of Brookville have land lines in order to conclude that people complaining about cellular reception will switch providers? Not at all. Some people could have land lines, and it would still make sense to conclude that, once people learn from advertising that there is a new cellular phone service provider, people who are not happy with their cellular phone reception will begin switching to using the new provider.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Some of the residents of the town of Brookville use the internet. Notice that we’re told that Satellite Media will soon begin advertising heavily on the internet. Great. But what happens if none of the residents of Brookville use the internet? In this situation, the argument will be busted; it will fall apart, because the evidence that Satellite Media will soon begin advertising heavily on the internet will not support the conclusion.
Some are you are saying, “Of course some of the residents of Brookville use the internet.” However, that some of them do is not obvious at all. While it is likely true that some residents of Brookville use the internet, it is not guaranteed to be true.
Look back to the argument. Where do we see conclusive evidence that any of the residents of Brookville use the internet? Maybe none do, right? Although it is likely true that some of the residents of Brookville use the internet, that fact is not stated by the author.
So, without stating that some residents of Brookville use the internet, the author concludes that, once Satellite Media begins its internet advertising campaign, residents of Brookville will begin switching their cellular phone service to plans provided by Satellite Media.
Therefore, the author is assuming that at least some of the residents of the town of Brookville use the internet.
In general terms, we see that the author assumes that what is likely true is actually true.
(D) Satellite Media’s cellular phone plans cost less than the other cellular phone plans available to Brookville residents. The author need not assume that Satellite Media’s cellular phone plans cost less than the other cellular phone plans available to Brookville residents.
After all, the author tells us that many residents of the town of Brookville have long complained about the poor cellular phone reception they experience. Of course, if the residents are unhappy with their cellular phone reception, they may decide to switch providers even if the plans they would switch to do not cost less than the plans they have.
So, even if Satellite Media’s cellular phone plans DO NOT cost less than the other cellular phone plans available to Brookville residents cost, the author’s conclusion could still follow from the premises.
(E) Some residents of Brookville are happy with the quality of the cellular phone reception provided by the cellular phone service providers they currently use. We’re told that that many residents of the town of Brookville have long complained about the poor cellular phone reception they experience. The author bases her conclusion on this fact.
Certainly, she need not assume that some residents of Brookville are happy with the quality of the cellular phone reception provided by the cellular phone service providers they currently use.
After all, her conclusion is that the residents who are not happy will switch providers. Of course, the premises would support that conclusion even if it is not the case that some residents of Brookville are happy with their cellular phone service.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss another type of assumption an author can make – that changes occurring over time don’t somehow undermine the connection between the evidence used in making an argument and the conclusion to that argument.
summary:
Lesson Content Sometimes in an Assumption question, an author will base her argument on an idea that is probably true but is not guaranteed to be true.
💡MUST KNOW:
Sometimes, an argument in an Assumptions question depends on the assumption that something that is likely true is actually true.
Consider the following example:
Three years ago, some of the students attending Rockville High School began for the first-time attending weekend classes designed to help them pass a state-wide assessment test.
title: 2.6.5 Assumption Correct Answer Type 5: An Assumption that Something that is Likely True is Actually True

contents:
Lesson Content Arguments about topics such as plans, predictions, or explanations can include elements that are from different time periods.
What all of these arguments have in common are assumptions that a conclusion about something occurring in one time period can be logically based on evidence derived from events in another time period.
For instance, in arriving at a conclusion about events that occurred a thousand years ago by observing certain conditions that exist today, one may be assuming that the relevant conditions that exist today are similar to conditions that existed a thousand years ago.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an author uses data from one time period to support a conclusion about a different time period, she assumes that that data can actually be used to support her conclusion. That is, she assumes that things have not changed or will not change over time in a way that would invalidate her conclusion.
For example, imagine that in 1970 an author researching green technologies learned that, although they have tried hard for many years, scientists have not been able to create a battery that could store enough energy to power a car.
In making that argument, the author assumes that things will not change in such a way that at some point in the future scientists will be able to create a battery that stores enough energy to power a car.
Consider the following mini example:
Automobile Company CEO: The difficulty that we have had in selling the large cars that we make began when the price of fuel increased last year. Typically, when fuel prices have increased, people have switched to using smaller cars that use less fuel than our large cars use. Then, once fuel prices have decreased, people have switched back to using relatively large cars. So, once fuel prices decrease, our large cars will begin to sell again.
Upon which of the following does the CEO rely in making the prediction?
(Correct Answer) Most people have not recently decided to permanently stop driving relatively large cars because of environmental concerns.
In this example, the CEO makes a prediction that is based on patterns that occurred in the past.
💡MUST KNOW:
In an Assumption question, an argument may depend on the assumption that a change occurring over time does not render the conclusion invalid.
Ok, now consider how the fact that things may have changed over time is related to the correct answer to the following question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 22 Ten years ago, the city of Verdantville began a major campaign to reduce violence. Which of the following is logically necessary for arriving at the conclusion? 1. [x] (A) Ten years ago, people in Verdantville were not generally less likely to report acts of violence than they have been in more recent years. 2. [ ] (B) Similar campaigns in cities located near Verdantville have not resulted in reductions in numbers of reported acts of violence. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) The early childhood education program that was started five years ago in Verdantville has not had time to result in a reduction in violence. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) The number of police patrolling the streets of Verdantville is approximately the same today as it was ten years ago. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) If the campaign to reduce violence in Verdantville had not been undertaken, the number of acts of violence reported per year in Verdantville would have increased. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium In this argument, the fact that the number of acts of violence reported per year in Verdantville has remained approximately the same is taken as evidence that the campaign to reduce violence has not had any effect.
Conclusion: The campaign is having no effect on the amount of violence occurring in Verdantville.
There are, however, at least two gaps in this argument:
Gap: The evidence is about acts of violence reported, while the conclusion is about violence occurring. It could be that the number of acts of violence reported is not directly correlated with the amount of violence occurring.
Gap: The evidence used to support the conclusion compares the number of acts of violence reported ten years ago with the number reported today. Perhaps, such reporting is somehow different today from how it was ten years ago.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Ten years ago, people in Verdantville were not generally less likely to report acts of violence than they have been in more recent years. The conclusion is based on comparisons between the number of acts of violence reported for the year ten years ago and the numbers reported during the years that have followed. What if the proportion of acts of violence that are reported each year has changed over time, and, therefore, this comparison is faulty? If the proportion of acts of violence reported increased over the past ten years, then a decrease in the amount of violence occurring could result in no change in the number of acts of violence reported each year. So, the conclusion relies on the assumption that ten years ago people were not less likely to report acts of violence than they have been in recent years. In other words, if ten years ago people WERE less likely to report acts of violence than they have been in recent years, then the argument falls apart. Notice that this answer choice confirms that a change occurring over time does not render the conclusion invalid, a key assumption upon which this argument rests.
(B) Similar campaigns in cities located near Verdantville have not resulted in reductions in numbers of reported acts of violence. The fact that similar campaigns in other cities have not resulted in reductions in numbers of reported acts of violence could potentially be seen as adding slightly to the credibility of the conclusion that Verdantville’s campaign has not been effective.
However, we are looking not for more evidence to support the argument’s conclusion but, rather, for an assumption necessary for connecting the evidence that we already have to the argument’s conclusion.
In general, when we’re seeking an assumption upon which an argument rests, an answer choice that provides new support for the conclusion of the argument will not be correct.
(C) The early childhood education program that was started five years ago in Verdantville has not had time to result in a reduction in violence. What this choice says might help to explain why the program has not had an effect, if it indeed has not, but we are looking for an assumption, not an explanation. One doesn’t have to assume that a specific program has not yet had an effect in order to conclude that program as a whole has not affected the amount of violence occurring.
(D) The number of police patrolling the streets of Verdantville is approximately the same today as it was ten years ago. This choice could be tempting, as one might feel that, in order for the amount of violence occurring to remain at the same level, the number of police on the streets has to remain at the same level. We are not, however, looking for a reason why the amount of violence occurring didn’t change. We are looking for an assumption necessary for coming to the conclusion given the evidence already presented. In general, when we’re seeking an assumption upon which an argument rests, an answer choice that presents an explanation for one of the facts presented in the passage will not be the correct answer.
(E) If the campaign to reduce violence in Verdantville had not been undertaken, the number of acts of violence reported per year in Verdantville would have increased. Given what this choice says about an increase in violence, it could seem to be in line with what the argument says. However, careful analysis of this choice reveals that it weakens the argument.
The evidence presented in the argument seems to indicate that the amount of violence occurring has not increased. If it were really true, as this choice says, that the amount of violence occurring would have increased had the campaign not been undertaken, then the fact that the amount of violence occurring did not increase indicates that the campaign has been successful in at least keeping the amount of violence from increasing, which conclusion is counter to the conclusion that the campaign has had no effect on the amount of violence occurring. In general, when we’re seeking an assumption upon which an argument rests, an answer choice that says something that weakens the argument will not be correct.
Correct Answer: (A) Now that we are more familiar with some types of assumptions that we might see, let’s talk about a key aspect of many of them, how inconspicuous they can be.
summary:
Lesson Content Arguments about topics such as plans, predictions, or explanations can include elements that are from different time periods.
What all of these arguments have in common are assumptions that a conclusion about something occurring in one time period can be logically based on evidence derived from events in another time period.
For instance, in arriving at a conclusion about events that occurred a thousand years ago by observing certain conditions that exist today, one may be assuming that the relevant conditions that exist today are similar to conditions that existed a thousand years ago.
title: 2.6.6 Assumption Correct Answer Type 6: Highlights an Assumption that a Change Occurring over Time does not Render the Conclusion Invalid

contents:
Lesson Content One thing that often surprises people is how inconspicuous assumptions can be.
For example:
Henrietta bakes the best apple pies in Clay County. The best apple pie entries win the Clay County Southern Charm baking contest every year without fail. Therefore, Henrietta’s apple pie will win the Southern Charm baking contest this year.
Let’s look at the structure of the argument:
Premise: Henrietta’s apples pies are the best apple pies.
Premise: The best apple pie entries always win the contest.
Conclusion: Henrietta will win the contest.
If, after reading that argument, you said, “Of course Henrietta’s apple pie will win the Southern Charm baking contest this year,” you unconsciously (or maybe even consciously) filled in one major gap in the argument.
The problem with filling these gaps in as we read is that it becomes harder for us to spot assumptions, so when reading we need to be aware of gaps in arguments.
After all, she can have the best pies in the world, but if she does not enter them in the contest, she won’t win.
So, in reality, the argument works this way:
Premise: Henrietta’s apples pies are the best apple pies.
Premise: The best apple pie entries always win the contest.
Assumption: Henrietta will enter her apple pies in the contest.
Conclusion: Henrietta will win the contest.
Simply put, the author of the argument (and, perhaps, you), took for granted that Henrietta would actually enter one of her apple pies into the Southern Charm baking contest! Her pies may be known far and wide as the best in the county, but she won’t win if she doesn’t enter.
Now – stop right here.
Upon closer inspection, we can see that this “small” assumption – that Henrietta will actually enter the contest is not so small after all.
This next mini example will now be easier:
A TV show holds a televised talent contest for all eligible singers. Monica has a beautiful soprano voice. So, she will likely make it onto the show. The conclusion is that Monica will make it onto the show, and this conclusion is based on the evidence that the contest showcases eligible singers.
Yes, there is.
💡MUST KNOW:
Assumptions made in GMAT arguments are often inconspicuous.
So, an assumption is not always information connected to an argument in a fascinating, logically complex way.
Consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 23 The boom in the freshly brewed tea business in Krztanza will end when the consumer learns that a serving of Krztanzian style freshly brewed tea often contains an amount of caffeine equivalent to that in up to eight cans of a popular energy drink and has as many calories as does an entire meal. Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends? 1. [ ] (A) Consumers in Krztanza do not mind high levels of caffeine and high calorie counts in their freshly brewed coffee. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Although Krztanzian style freshly brewed tea is less healthy than packaged tea, it has become very popular. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Consumers in Krztanza do not prefer high calorie beverages that contain high levels of caffeine. 4. [ ] (D) Krztanzian style freshly brewed tea could be improved via reducing its levels of calories and caffeine. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Ultimately, the consumption of tea in Krztanza will decrease because packaged tea contains fewer calories and caffeine. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Packaged tea contains less caffeine and has fewer calories than does Krztanzian style freshly brewed tea and is available in most grocery stores and even in many stores where freshly brewed coffee is sold.
Conclusion: The boom in the freshly brewed tea business in Krztanza will end when the consumer learns that a serving of Krztanzian style freshly brewed tea often contains an amount of caffeine equivalent to that in up to eight cans of a popular energy drink and has as many calories as does an entire meal.
The reasoning of the argument is that, since packaged tea contains less caffeine and has fewer calories than Krztanzian style freshly brewed tea, and since packaged tea is widely available, the boom in the freshly brewed tea business in Krztanza will end when people find out about the high caffeine and caloric levels of Krztanzian style freshly brewed tea.
However, while, given the way it is structured and worded, the argument seems to imply that people want to avoid caffeine and calories, it never actually says that consumers prefer beverages with relatively low caffeine and caloric levels over beverages with high caffeine and caloric levels. After all, what if people DO NOT prefer beverages with relatively low caffeine and caloric levels over beverages with high caffeine and caloric levels? In such a case, the argument falls apart.
(A) Consumers in Krztanza do not mind high levels of caffeine and high calorie counts in their freshly brewed coffee. The wording of this choice makes it seem somehow relevant to the argument. However, Critical Reasoning is not a word matching game, and the fact that a choice seems to match what the argument says does not make that choice correct.
In fact, what we really need to notice about this choice is that its effect is basically the opposite of what we need in order to complete the argument.
The fact that people do not mind high levels of caffeine and calories in their coffee is in conflict with the conclusion that they will reduce their consumption of Krztanzian style freshly brewed tea once they become aware of its caffeine and calorie levels. After all, if they do not mind high levels of calories and caffeine in their coffee, then it’s quite possible that they don’t mind high levels of calories and caffeine in general.
In general, when we’re seeking an assumption upon which an argument rests, an answer choice that says something that weakens the argument will not be correct.
(B) Although Krztanzian style freshly brewed tea is less healthy than packaged tea, it has become very popular. This choice adds virtually nothing to the argument. From what we the argument says, we already have reason to believe that freshly brewed tea has become very popular.
If we were looking for a conclusion supported by what the passage says, this choice might work. However, we are looking for an assumption integral to the argument, not a conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Consumers in Krztanza do not prefer high calorie beverages that contain high levels of caffeine. The author must assume what this choice says.
Notice that, if, contrary to what this choice say, people in Krztanza DO prefer high caffeine beverages that are high in calories, then learning that Krztanzian style freshly brewed tea is higher in caffeine and calories than packaged tea won’t cause them to reduce their consumption of freshly brewed tea.
Thus, if consumers in Krztanza DO prefer high calorie beverages that contain high levels of caffeine, then the evidence that packaged tea contains less caffeine and has fewer calories than Krztanzian style freshly brewed tea does not support the conclusion, and the argument falls part.
So, the argument works only if what this choice says is true.
Notice how, even though what this choice says is rather basic and is obviously integral to the argument, it still constitutes an assumption without which the logic of the argument falls apart.
(D) Krztanzian style freshly brewed tea could be improved via reducing its levels of calories and caffeine. Concluding that the boom in the freshly brewed tea business in Krztanza will end does not require assuming that freshly brewed tea could be improved.
In fact, if it is the case that freshly brewed tea could be improved, then perhaps tea makers will improve freshly brewed tea and people will continue to consume it, or even increase their consumption of it, in which case, the boom will not end.
(E) Ultimately, the consumption of tea in Krztanza will decrease because packaged tea contains fewer calories and caffeine. This choice could be tempting, because the argument’s conclusion is that the boom in the freshly brewed tea business will end, and this choice is about a decrease in tea consumption.
Notice, however, that the point of the argument is not that tea in general will be consumed less. The point is that people will consume less Krztanzian style freshly brewed tea once they find out about its high caffeine and calorie levels.
So, the argument could still work even if, contrary to what this choice says, the overall consumption of tea in Krztanza will NOT decrease, as long as consumption of Krztanzian style freshly brewed tea in particular will decrease.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now consider the use of limiting words, such as “except” or “not,” in question stems.
summary:
