Lesson Content One thing that often surprises people is how inconspicuous assumptions can be.
For example:
Henrietta bakes the best apple pies in Clay County. The best apple pie entries win the Clay County Southern Charm baking contest every year without fail. Therefore, Henrietta’s apple pie will win the Southern Charm baking contest this year.
Let’s look at the structure of the argument:
Premise: Henrietta’s apples pies are the best apple pies.
title: 2.7 Assumptions Can Be Inconspicuous

contents:
Lesson Content Assumption questions will sometimes include in their question stems the word “except” or “not.” The use of question stems that use such words can make questions a bit more challenging.
The following two question stems are examples of Assumption question stems that contain “except” or “not”:
The logic of the argument above depends on all of the following EXCEPT:
Which of the following is not an assumption on which the argument depends?
When an Assumption question has the word “except” or “not” in the stem, four out of the five answer choices will be assumptions integral to the argument.
The correct answer will be the one piece of information that does not supply a link between the evidence and the conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an Assumption question has the word “except” or “not” in the stem, four out of the five answer choices will be assumptions integral to the argument. These answer choices will be incorrect. The correct answer will be the choice that does not link the evidence and the conclusion; the correct answer will not be an assumption upon which the argument depends.
Since an “except” or “not” question asks us to do the opposite of what we normally do in answering an assumption question, in order to avoid becoming confused when answering such a question, we can mark each of the choices with a “Yes,” if the choice states an assumption integral to the argument, or with a “No,” if what the choice says is not an assumption integral to the argument.
Consider the following examples:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 24 In order to reduce the number of vehicles traveling over the speed limit, the Town of Commerce recently installed on its highways automated cameras that detect speeding vehicles. Each of the following, if true, is an assumption upon which the argument depends EXCEPT: 1. [ ] (A) The number of vehicles using the highways in Commerce has not significantly declined since the introduction of the cameras. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Drivers of vehicles on the highways of Commerce do not slow to the speed limit when approaching the cameras, and then exceed the speed limit after passing the cameras. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) The cameras recently installed to detect speeding vehicles on the highways in Commerce have not critically malfunctioned since their installation. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Most drivers who speed on highways in Commerce have not changed their habits recently, going from doing most of their driving during daylight hours to doing most of it at night. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) The population of the Town of Commerce has not significantly increased over the period of time since the cameras to detect speeding vehicles were installed. View Solution Question Level: medium The argument is pretty simple.
Commerce recently installed on its highways automated cameras that detect speeding vehicles. Since the introduction of the cameras, the number of speeding vehicles detected each day by the cameras has sharply declined.
The author concludes from that evidence that the presence of the cameras is deterring from speeding those drivers who would otherwise speed.
Notice that the author makes a cause-and-effect claim. She concludes that the installation of the cameras is causing the decrease in the number of speeding vehicles detected each day.
Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, he or she assumes that there cannot be an alternative explanation for the observation made. In other words, in making this argument, the author assumes that there is not an alternative explanation for the decrease in the number of speeding vehicles detected each day. In her mind, the only explanation is the one she has posited.
Now, because this is an “EXCEPT” question, four of the five choices will be assumptions necessary in order for the premises of the argument to support the argument’s conclusion.
The correct answer will be the choice on which the argument does not depend.
(A) The number of vehicles using the highways in Commerce has not significantly declined since the introduction of the cameras. What this answer choice says is an assumption upon which the argument depends.
If, contrary to what this choice says, the number of vehicles using the highways in Commerce HAS significantly declined since the introduction of the cameras, then we have a pretty darn good alternative explanation for the decline in the number of speeding vehicles detected each day by the cameras.
After all, it makes sense that, as the number of vehicles using the highways declines, we’ll see a decline in the number of speeding vehicles.
Therefore, in making her argument, the author must assume that the number of vehicles using the highways in Commerce HAS NOT significantly declined since the introduction of the cameras. Otherwise, how could she make the claim that the cameras are deterring drivers from speeding?
Necessary assumption? Yes.
(B) Drivers of vehicles on the highways of Commerce do not slow to the speed limit when approaching the cameras, and then exceed the speed limit after passing the cameras. What this answer choice says is an assumption upon which the argument depends.
If, contrary to what this choice says, drivers of vehicles on the highways of Commerce DO slow to the speed limit when approaching the cameras, and then exceed the speed limit after passing the cameras, then we have a pretty darn good alternative explanation for the decline in the number of speeding vehicles detected each day by the cameras.
After all, in that case, there has not really been a decline in the number of vehicles speeding. Instead, the vehicles speed on the highways until they approach the cameras, at which point they slow down. Once past the cameras, the cars resume speeding.
Therefore, in making her argument, the author must assume that drivers on the highways of Commerce DO NOT slow to the speed limit when approaching the cameras, and then exceed the speed limit after passing the cameras. Otherwise, how could she make the claim that the cameras are deterring drivers from speeding?
Necessary assumption? Yes.
(C) The cameras recently installed to detect speeding vehicles on the highways in Commerce have not critically malfunctioned since their installation. What this answer choice says is an assumption upon which the argument depends.
If, contrary to what this choice says, the cameras recently installed to detect speeding vehicles on the highways in Commerce HAVE critically malfunctioned since their installation, then we have a pretty darn good alternative explanation for the decline in the number of speeding vehicles detected each day by the cameras: the cameras don’t work. After all, if the cameras don’t work, how can they detect speeding vehicles?
Therefore, in making her argument, the author must assume that the recently installed cameras HAVE NOT critically malfunctioned since their installation. Otherwise, how could she make the claim that the cameras are deterring drivers from speeding?
Necessary assumption? Yes.
(D) Most drivers who speed on highways in Commerce have not changed their habits recently, going from doing most of their driving during daylight hours to doing most of it at night. What this answer choice says is an assumption upon which the argument rests.
Notice that the passage tells us that the cameras cannot detect speeding vehicles at night. So, what if, contrary to what this choice says, most drivers who speed on highways in Commerce HAVE changed their habits, going from doing most of their driving during daylight hours to doing most of it at night?
In that case, we’d have a pretty darn good alternative explanation for the decline in the number of speeding vehicles detected each day by the cameras: most drivers who speed switched to driving at night, a time when the cameras can’t detect speeding vehicles.
Therefore, in making her argument, the author must assume that most drivers who speed on highways in Commerce HAVE NOT changed their habits from doing most of their driving during daylight hours to doing most of it at night. Otherwise, how could she make the claim that the presence of the cameras is deterring drivers from speeding?
Necessary assumption? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The population of the Town of Commerce has not significantly increased over the period of time since the cameras to detect speeding vehicles were installed. What this choice says is NOT an assumption upon which the argument depends.
What if, contrary to what this choice says, the population of the Town of Commerce HAS significantly increased over the period of time since the cameras were installed? How would that fact affect the validity of the argument?
If anything, that fact would indicate that, since there are more people in Commerce, there are likely more cars on the highways in Commerce than there were when the cameras were installed. If there are likely more cars on the highways, we would expect there to be more speeding vehicles on the highways, but the number of speeding vehicles detected each day by the cameras has declined.
So, if what this choice says were NOT true, we would have even more reason to suspect that something, such as the cameras, is deterring drivers from speeding.
So, what this choice says is certainly not an assumption upon which the argument depends.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 25 Fish that swim in water containing PCBs, which are chemicals toxic to many lifeforms, absorb PCBs via their gills. Each of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends EXCEPT: 1. [ ] (A) Fish cannot take in PCBs by feeding on fish that have previously inhabited water containing PCBs. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Fish do not manufacture PCBs internally via natural processes. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Plants that fish consume do not absorb PCBs from the soil in which they grow. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Fish in which PCBs have built up in fatty tissues do not represent only a small proportion of fish that have inhabited water containing PCBs. 5. [ ] (E) PCBs are never passed from a mother fish that has absorbed PCBs to her young. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium Premise: Fish that swim in water containing PCBs, which are chemicals toxic to many lifeforms, absorb PCBs via their gills.
Premise: Having entered fish’s systems, PCBs can build up in the fatty tissues of fish that swim in such water.
Conclusion: If PCBs are present in a fish’s fatty tissues, it must be the case that that fish has inhabited water containing PCBs.
Notice that the argument jumps from the premise that PCBs can build up in the fatty tissues of fish that swim in water containing PCBs to the conclusion that, if PCBs are present in a fish’s fatty tissues, it must be the case that that fish has inhabited water containing PCBs.
Making that jump requires assuming that the only way in which PCBs can get into the fatty tissues of fish is through fish’s inhabiting water containing PCBs.
After all, if there are other ways in which PCBs could get into the fatty tissues of fish, then it’s possible that PCBs could be present in a fish’s fatty tissues even if that fish did not inhabit water containing PCBs. In that case it would not make sense to conclude that, if PCBs are present in a fish’s fatty tissues, it must be the case that that fish has inhabited water containing PCBs.
So, without the assumption that only way in which PCBs can get into the fatty tissues of fish is through fish’s inhabiting water containing PCBs, the argument falls apart.
Now - because this is an “EXCEPT” question, four of the five answer choices will be assumptions that will be necessary in order for the premises of the argument to support the argument’s conclusion. These will be incorrect choices. The correct answer will be the answer choice that the argument does not depend on.
(A) Fish cannot take in PCBs by feeding on fish that have previously inhabited water containing PCBs. What this answer choice says is an assumption upon which the argument rests.
The conclusion is that, if PCBs are present in a fish’s fatty tissues, it must be the case that that fish has inhabited water containing PCBs. In order for the presence of PCBs to be sufficient evidence that a has fish inhabited such water, there have to be no ways other than inhabiting such water for a fish to take in PCBs.
Therefore, in order for the argument to work, it must be true that fish cannot take in PCBs by feeding on fish that have previously inhabited water containing PCBs. After all, imagine what would happen if fish CAN take in PCBs by feeding on fish that have previously inhabited water containing PCBs. The argument would fall apart.
Necessary assumption? Yes.
(B) Fish do not manufacture PCBs internally via natural processes. What this answer choice says is an assumption upon which the argument rests.
The conclusion is that, if PCBs are present in a fish’s fatty tissues, it must be the case that that fish has inhabited water containing PCBs. In order for the presence of PCBs to be sufficient evidence that a fish has inhabited such water, there have to be no ways other than through a fish’s inhabiting such water for PCBs to build up in a fish’s fatty tissues.
Therefore, in order for the argument to work, it must be true that fish do not manufacture PCBs internally via natural processes. After all, imagine what would happen if fish DO manufacture PCBs internally via natural processes. The argument would fall apart.
Necessary assumption? Yes.
(C) Plants that fish consume do not absorb PCBs from the soil in which they grow. What this answer choice says is an assumption upon which the argument rests.
The conclusion is that, if PCBs are present in a fish’s fatty tissues, it must be the case that that fish has inhabited water containing PCBs. In order for the presence of PCBs to be sufficient evidence that a fish has inhabited such water, there have to be no ways other than inhabiting such water for fish to take in PCBs.
Therefore, in order for the argument to work, it must be true that plants that fish consume do not absorb PCBs from the soil in which they grow. After all, imagine what would happen if plants that fish consume DO absorb PCBs from the soil in which they grow. The argument would fall apart.
Necessary assumption? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Fish in which PCBs have built up in fatty tissues do not represent only a small proportion of fish that have inhabited water containing PCBs. This choice may seem to be an assumption necessary for arriving at the conclusion. One might think something along the lines of, “If this statement were not true, and it were the case that fish in which PCBs have built up fatty tissues DO represent a only a small proportion of fish that have inhabited water containing PCBs, then the presence of PCBs in a fish’s fatty tissues would not necessarily indicate that that fish has inhabited water containing PCBs.”
Notice, however, that what this choice says does not have to be true in order for the presence of PCBs in a fish’s fatty tissues to indicate that that fish has inhabited water containing PCBs.
Yes, if it were the case that fish in which PCBs have built up fatty tissues DO represent a only a small proportion of fish that have inhabited water containing PCBs, then an absence of PCBs in a fish’s fatty tissues would not reliably indicate that that fish has not inhabited water containing PCBs.
However, even in such a case, the presence of PCBs in a fish’s fatty tissues could indicate that that fish has inhabited water containing PCBs.
Necessary assumption? No.
(E) PCBs are never passed from a mother fish that has absorbed PCBs to her young. What this answer choice says is an assumption upon which the argument depends. This choice, like choices (A), (B), and (C), eliminates an alternative way in which PCBs could become present in a fish’s fatty tissues. After all, if PCBs ARE sometimes passed from a mother fish that has absorbed PCBs to her young, then the argument falls apart.
Necessary assumption? Yes.
Correct Answer: (D) We’ll now consider a powerful tool for solving Assumption questions: the negation technique.
summary:
Lesson Content Assumption questions will sometimes include in their question stems the word “except” or “not.” The use of question stems that use such words can make questions a bit more challenging.
The following two question stems are examples of Assumption question stems that contain “except” or “not”:
The logic of the argument above depends on all of the following EXCEPT:
Which of the following is not an assumption on which the argument depends?
title: 2.8 Working with Tricky Question Stems That Contain Limiting Words

contents:
Lesson Content At this point, you know well that an assumption is an unstated piece of information that must be true in order for the given evidence to lead to the conclusion.
After all, what would happen to the argument if we were to discover that there WERE other ways in which a person could become exposed to Compound X? In such a case, the argument would fall apart – right? In other words, what would happen to the argument if we were to discover that NO people who gained work skills through the training subsequently found employment? The argument would fall apart – right? After all, if these lights WILL somehow aid breaking into cars, the plan will not work because the street lights will be making the problem that they are intended to help solve worse.
What have we been doing in these situations?
Once we’ve found the assumption on which an argument depends, we’ve been showing you – confirming for you – that if that assumption were NOT true (if the assumption were false), the argument would fall apart.
💡MUST KNOW:
Because an assumption is a piece of missing information that MUST BE true for the logic of an argument to work, if we were to find an assumption upon which an argument depends to be false, the argument would no longer work.
Why is all of this important? It’s important because we can use this understanding of how assumptions work as a powerful tool for finding the assumption upon which the argument depends.
How it works is simple.
On the other hand, if we negate a potential assumption and the argument is no worse for the wear (it’s unaffected), then that potential assumption must not have been necessary.
To see negation in action, let’s revisit our argument about disease x:
All people in houses in which communicable diseases are present should be quarantined. Two people in the yellow house on Maple Street came down with disease x yesterday. Therefore, all people in the yellow house on Maple Street should be quarantined.
Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends?
(A) Disease x is a communicable disease. (B) People should be quarantined only when they have a communicable disease.
We already know that the author assumes that disease x is a communicable disease.
Here’s how we can use the negation technique.
Choice (A): Disease x is a communicable disease.
Negated Choice (A): Disease x is NOT a communicable disease.
If the negated choice (A) were true, the argument would fall apart.
Let’s look now at choice (B) and negate it:
Choice (B): People should be quarantined only when they have a communicable disease.
Negated Choice (B): It is NOT the case that people should be quarantined only when they have a communicable disease.
Does this negation of choice (B) weaken the support for the conclusion? No, not at all, because, even if it is NOT the case that people should be quarantined only when they have a communicable disease, the conclusion that, all people in the yellow house on Maple Street should be quarantined could still make sense.
Let’s have another look at Monica and her beautiful voice:
A TV show holds a televised talent contest for all eligible singers. Monica has a beautiful soprano voice, so she will likely make it onto the show. Remember we said that the necessary assumption here is that Monica must be eligible for the TV show.
Think of it this way.
💡MUST KNOW:
If we want to tell whether something is necessary, then try getting rid of it. If the argument falls apart in its absence, that thing was necessary for the argument to be sound.
Here’s the four-step approach to successfully use the negation technique.
Step 1: Break down the Argument. Do so just as you would with any Critical Reasoning question, making sure to identify the conclusion and evidence.
Step 2: Write down the negation of each answer. It doesn’t have to be a complete sentence; shorthand is efficient and works well.
Negate the main verb (e.g. Negate a quantity word (e.g. Get rid of a “not” (e.g. Simply put “it is not the case that” before the statement. Step 3: Eliminate choices that do not hurt the conclusion when negated.
Step 4: Choose the answer that most specifically hurts the Argument. If we are down to two choices, we re-read the evidence and the conclusion again.
(Occasionally, the negation test may leave you with two choices the negations of which seem equally damaging to the conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
When we negate the answer choices in an Assumption question, the negated version of the correct answer will destroy the argument.
Consider the following examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 26 MowGreen, a company that uses sustainable methods to maintain lawns, has been expanding quickly, and its services are now available in many areas of the country. Which of the following is logically necessary for coming to the conclusion of the above argument? 1. [x] (A) MowGreen’s management team considers profitability in determining whether to expand into an area. 2. [ ] (B) People in the Drawnville area are not interested in using sustainable methods for maintaining their lawns. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) There remain in the country areas in which MowGreen is not currently operating into which it could profitably expand its operations. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) MowGreen operates profitably in all of the areas in which it currently operates. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Sustainable methods for maintaining lawns are generally more expensive to use than are less environmentally friendly methods. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: MowGreen has been expanding, and its services are available in many areas.
Premise: MowGreen would not be able to operate profitably in the area around Drawnville.
Conclusion: One can be sure that MowGreen will not expand into the area around Drawnville any time soon.
There is a gap in the argument. The premise says that MowGreen would not be able to operate profitably in the Drawnville area. The conclusion says that MowGreen will not expand into the Drawnville area. What’s missing here? What must be true in order for the conclusion to be properly drawn from the premise? The correct answer to the question will fill in the gap between “would not be able to operate profitably in the area” and “will not expand into the area.”
Let’s look at choice (A) and negate it:
MowGreen’s management team DOES NOT consider profitability in determining whether to expand into an area.
If this negation were true, the argument would fall apart. Thus, choice (A) presents an assumption necessary to the argument. This is the correct answer.
Now, let’s negate each of the incorrect choices.
Negated (B): People in the area around Drawnville ARE interested in using sustainable methods for maintaining their lawns.
Negated (C): There NO LONGER remain in the country areas in which MowGreen is not currently operating into which it could profitably expand its operations.
Negated (D): MowGreen DOES NOT operate profitably in all of the areas in which it currently operates.
Negated (E): Sustainable methods for maintaining lawns are NOT generally more expensive to use than are less environmentally friendly methods.
We can see that none of the negated versions of these incorrect options weaken the argument. Thus, none of these incorrect options is an assumption that is necessary to this argument.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 27 While the practice of allowing members of the public to donate to political campaigns may have corrupting effects, if candidates for elected office were not allowed to accept donations for covering campaign expenses, only the wealthy would have the financial resources generally necessary for winning elections, and people of more modest financial means would rarely be elected. Which of the following is an assumption necessary for arriving at the above conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) Wealthy people generally have unfair financial advantages over people of more modest personal financial means. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) People of relatively modest financial means never become wealthy and, thus, able to finance their own election campaigns. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Wealthy people elected to public offices will not safeguard the interests of citizens of modest financial means. 4. [ ] (D) The costs of running an election campaign have been increasing over time. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) A corrupt person of modest financial means will, if elected to public office, safeguard the interests of citizens of modest financial means. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium The argument is based on the following premise:
Premise: If candidates for elected office were not allowed to accept donations for covering campaign expenses, only the wealthy would have the financial resources generally necessary for winning elections, and people of modest financial means would rarely be elected.
It jumps from those premises to the following conclusion:
Conclusion: Donations to campaigns must be allowed in order to facilitate the election of people who will safeguard the interests of citizens of modest financial means.
There is a gap between the premise and the conclusion, as the argument jumps from evidence that is focused on people’s levels of financial means to a conclusion about safeguarding the interests of citizens of modest financial means, without making any sort of clear connection between a person’s level of financial means and whether that person will safeguard the interests of citizens of modest financial means.
Now, let’s negate each choice to see which is an assumption integral to the argument.
(A) Negated: Wealthy people generally DO NOT have unfair financial advantages over people of more modest personal financial means.
Incorrect. This statement does not destroy the argument.
Even if wealthy people do not have “unfair” financial advantages, the evidence presented, that, if candidates for elected office were not allowed to accept donations for covering campaign expenses, only the wealthy would have the financial resources generally necessary for winning elections, could still support the conclusion that donating to campaigns must be allowed in order to facilitate the election of people who will safeguard the interests of people of modest financial means.
(B) Negated: People of relatively modest financial means sometimes become wealthy and, thus, able to finance their own election campaigns.
Incorrect. This statement does not destroy the argument.
Even if people of modest financial means sometimes become wealthy, it could still be the case that the wealthy would win most elections if campaign donations were not allowed and that, therefore, donations to campaigns must be allowed in order to facilitate the election of people who will safeguard the interests of citizens of modest financial means.
After all, if a person of modest financial means becomes wealthy and, thus able to finance an election campaign, that person is no longer a person of modest financial means.
(C) Negated: Wealthy people elected to public offices WILL not safeguard interests of citizens of modest financial means.
Correct. This statement destroys the argument.
If wealthy people WILL safeguard the interests of citizens of modest financial means, then, even if wealthy people would be the only ones to have the financial resources necessary for winning elections, people who would safeguard the interests of citizens of modest financial means could be elected.
So, if what this choice says is NOT true, the conclusion is not supported and the argument falls apart. Thus, this choice must state an assumption upon which the argument depends.
(D) Negated: The costs of running an election campaign have NOT been increasing over time.
Incorrect. This statement does not affect the argument.
The conclusion of the argument is not based on the trend in the costs of running an election campaign. As long as significant financial resources are required for winning an election, the argument makes sense.
(E) Negated: A corrupt person of modest financial means will NOT, if elected to public office, safeguard the interests of citizens of modest financial means.
Incorrect. This statement does not affect the argument.
The conclusion is not that any person of modest financial means elected to office will safeguard the interests of citizens of modest financial means.
In other words, while the argument suggests that allowing donating to campaigns is necessary for enabling the election of someone who will safeguard the interests of citizens of modest financial means, it does not suggest that allowing donating to campaigns is sufficient for achieving that end.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s continue by discussing the general differences between trap choices and correct answers in assumption questions.
summary:
Lesson Content At this point, you know well that an assumption is an unstated piece of information that must be true in order for the given evidence to lead to the conclusion.
After all, what would happen to the argument if we were to discover that there WERE other ways in which a person could become exposed to Compound X? In such a case, the argument would fall apart – right?
title: 2.9 The Negation Technique

contents:
Lesson Content A key aspect of arriving at the correct answers to Assumption questions is noticing the difference between trap choices and correct answers.
It says something that is rather clearly not an assumption integral to the argument but is so obviously connected to the argument that a test-taker is tempted to choose it. It says something that may seem to be an assumption integral to the argument but is not actually an assumption integral to the argument. 💡MUST KNOW:
A trap choice in an Assumption question does one of two things:
Let’s now discuss how we can avoid choosing trap choices of the first type, the ones that are rather clearly not assumptions that the arguments depend on but are obviously connected to the arguments.
These choices may explain a part of the argument, may be conclusions that the argument supports, or may relate to the argument in some other way that makes them relevant to the discussion in the passage and, therefore, tempting.
Accordingly, key to avoiding choosing a trap choice that is obviously connected to the argument is to keep in mind exactly what we are looking for, an unstated piece of information necessary in order for the evidence to support the argument’s conclusion (we’re seeking an assumption).
As long as we are clear about what we are looking for and follow the strategy of identifying the conclusion and how it is supported and then seeking an assumption necessary for connecting the support to the conclusion, we’ll be unlikely to be fooled into choosing such a trap choice.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering an Assumption question, key to avoiding choosing a trap choice that is obviously connected to the argument is to keep in mind exactly what we are looking for, an unstated piece of information necessary in order for the evidence to support the argument’s conclusion. We’ll see that, as connected to the argument as such a choice may be, it simply does not do what the correct answer has to do.
Consider the following example:
The compensation of agents at the Home Squad real estate agency located in Verdantville comes only in the form of commissions that are based on the prices of the houses that they sell. This year the average selling price of a house in Verdantville is expected to be only half what it was last year. Therefore, this year, the average compensation of agents at the Home Squad agency will likely be around half what it was last year.
Which of the following is an assumption upon which the above conclusion depends?
(Trap Choice) Demand for houses in Verdantville plummeted because a large company relocated from Verdantville to a different city.
(Correct Answer) This year, the number of houses sold per agent at the Home Squad agency will not be significantly higher than it was last year.
If a test-taker were rushing through these choices without considering exactly what type of answer is needed, the trap choice could be tempting enough that the test-taker would choose it.
However, if we keep in mind what we need to find, an assumption, and ask ourselves what gap the trap choice fills in the argument, we’ll see that it doesn’t fill a gap and avoid being fooled into choosing it.
The conclusion of the argument is the following:
Conclusion: This year, the average compensation of agents at the Home Squad agency will likely be around half what it was last year.
In order to conclude that the agents will make less, we don’t have to assume that, as the trap choice says, a company relocated.
The correct answer, on the other hand, really is integral to the support for the conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
A test-taker can significantly positively affect performance in answering Assumption questions by simply keeping in mind that the correct answers have to be assumptions integral to support for the conclusions and, thus, avoid choosing trap choices that are temptingly connected to the arguments but clearly don’t do what’s required.
Now, let’s consider traps of the second type, the ones that can seem to be assumptions that arguments depend upon, even though they are not really.
Consider the following example:
It had long been believed that prehistoric cave paintings, which often depict animals, were created by men for reasons related to hunting animals for food. However, many of these paintings include stenciled images of hands, and, by analyzing the images, researchers have determined that most of the hands used in creating the stenciled images were women’s hands. So, to the surprise of many, it appears that most prehistoric cave paintings were created by women.
Which of the following is an assumption upon which the above argument relies?
(Trap Choice) The people who created the cave paintings did not participate in hunting the types of animals depicted in the paintings.
(Correct Answer) The hands depicted in images in cave paintings were not the hands of people other than the artists who created the paintings.
Notice the difference between the trap choice and the correct answer.
The trap choice says something that one could feel makes sense given what is said in the argument.
Notice, however, that while, after reading the argument, one might assume that the artists who created the cave paintings did not hunt, the argument does not depend on that assumption.
The correct answer, on the other hand, says something that must be true in order for the argument to work.
Notice also how the correct answer is worded to make it unappealing.
💡MUST KNOW:
An Assumption question can include trap choices that appear to be assumptions that the argument depends upon. In order to avoid picking a choice of this type, we have to look carefully to see which answer choice truly fits a gap in the argument and which does not.
Now, to get to the correct answers to the following examples, remember to:
read the passage carefully. identify exactly what the conclusion is. identify how the conclusion is supported. knock out the choices that are temptingly related to the argument but are not assumptions that the argument requires. choose the choice that actually fills a gap in the argument. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 28 People taking Pro-Max, a new plant-based protein powder, after each gym workout for three months gained more lean muscle than was gained by people taking Pro-Lean, a competing protein powder made from milk, after each gym workout for three months. Which of the following is an assumption upon which the above argument depends? 1. [x] (A) The people who used Pro-Max did not have gym workout routines significantly more effective than those had by the people who used Pro-Lean. 2. [ ] (B) A serving of Pro-Max protein powder does not cost more money than a serving Pro-Lean protein powder. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Pro-Lean protein powder does not taste significantly better than Pro-Max protein powder. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) None of the people who used Pro-Lean protein powder were also personal trainers in gyms. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Pro-Max protein powder is lower in calories than Pro-Lean protein powder. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy YOUR CHOICE(A) The people who used Pro-Max did not have gym workout routines significantly more effective than those had by the people who used Pro-Lean. Notice that the author draws a cause-and-effect link between using Pro-Max and gaining lean muscle.
To make this cause-and-effect claim, the author must be assuming that it is in fact the Pro-Max that is responsible for the gains in lean muscle. In other words, she is assuming that there is not another possible explanation for why the people taking Pro-Max gained more lean muscle than the people taking Pro-Lean gained.
What if the people who used Pro-Max did have gym workout routines significantly more effective than those had by the people who used Pro-Lean? In this case, wouldn’t we have a very plausible alternative explanation for why people taking Pro-Max gained more lean muscle than people taking Pro-Lean gained? Definitely! It could have been the more effective workout routines, rather than the Pro-Max, that resulted in greater lean muscle gains experienced by people who took Pro-Max.
In that case, the argument would fall apart.
Therefore, in drawing the conclusion that Pro-Max must be superior to Pro-Lean for building lean muscle after working out in a gym, the author assumes that the people who used Pro-Max did not have gym workout routines significantly more effective than those had by the people who used Pro-Lean.
(B) A serving of Pro-Max protein powder does not cost more money than a serving Pro-Lean protein powder. Notice that the author never discusses the costs of Pro-Max or Pro-Lean. Her conclusion is simply that Pro-Max must be superior to Pro-Lean for building lean muscle after working out in a gym.
Since the costs of the products would not affect the lean muscle gains achieved by people using the products, the author need not assume anything about the cost of either product.
In you chose this answer as correct, you may have done so because what it says is probably pertinent to choosing between two competing products. Costs may matter, right? However, we’re seeking not something that merely seems connected to the argument but, rather, an assumption upon which the argument depends.
(C) Pro-Lean protein powder does not taste significantly better than Pro-Max protein powder. Notice that the author never discusses the taste of Pro-Max or Pro-Lean. Her conclusion is simply that Pro-Max must be superior to Pro-Lean for building lean muscle after working out in a gym. One or both of them could taste like swamp water, and her argument could still be valid.
Be careful not to choose as correct things that sound good or things that jive well with reality. After all, you may know that some protein powders don’t taste very good. You might have considered the possibility that, if a protein powder does not taste very good, then perhaps people would not use it regularly. However, we know that, regardless of the taste, people used the protein powders “after each gym workout for three months.”
So, the argument does not rely on any assumptions about taste.
(D) None of the people who used Pro-Lean protein powder were also personal trainers in gyms. Remember, it was the people taking Pro-Max for three months after each gym workout who gained more lean muscle than was gained by people taking Pro-Lean. From that fact, the author concludes that Pro-Max must be superior to Pro-Lean for building lean muscle after working out in a gym.
If anything, a person’s being a personal trainer in a gym would contribute to that person’s gaining lean muscle. So, if, contrary to what this choice says, SOME of the people using Pro-Lean were personal trainers, we’d be even more convinced that Pro-Max is the superior product, because it would be the case that, even with the advantage of being personal trainers, the people using Pro-Lean did not gain as much lean muscle as the people using Pro-Max gained.
Thus, the argument certainly does not depend on the assumption that none of the people who consumed Pro-Lean protein powder were also personal trainers in gyms.
(E) Pro-Max protein powder is lower in calories than Pro-Lean protein powder. This choice is a tempting trap that may seem to be an assumption upon which the argument depends.
It would be easy to read this choice and decide that what this choice says, that Pro-Max is lower in calories than Pro-Lean, is an assumption necessary for arriving at the argument’s conclusion.
Why would it be easy to decide that? Because the conclusion is that Pro-Max is “superior,” and we may think that a powder that is “lower in calories” is superior, and that one not “lower in calories” would not be superior.
Remember, though, what we are looking for “an assumption upon which the above argument depends.” Does what this choice says have to be assumed in order for the argument to work?
The argument’s conclusion is that Pro-Max is “superior” to Pro-Lean. To support that conclusion, the argument uses the evidence that people taking Pro-Max gained more lean muscle than people taking Pro-Lean gained. So, the argument is based on RESULTS, and here’s the thing:
We don’t have to assume anything about calories in order for those results to support the conclusion. Even if Pro-Max were NOT lower in calories than Pro-Lean is, the results would still support the conclusion that Pro-Max is superior for building lean muscle.
So, the argument does not depend on the assumption that Pro-Max is lower in calories than Pro-Lean because, even if Pro-Max were not lower in calories, the evidence provided would still support the argument’s conclusion.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 29 Homeownership, as opposed to renting a home, can have many benefits, both for individuals who own homes and for society as a whole. Which of the following is an assumption on which the argument relies? 1. [ ] (A) Crime rates are lower in cities in which the ratio of homeowners to renters is relatively high because people who own homes tend to feel invested in their communities. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) People involved in government are interested in improving conditions for individuals and society at large. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Without government encouragement to buy homes, most people in the country will choose to rent the homes in which they live. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) The post retirement housing expenses of people who own homes are often lower than those of people who rent. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Some of the people who are currently renting homes or considering renting homes could afford to buy homes should they choose to do so. View Solution Question Level: hard Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: Homeownership can have many benefits, both for individuals and for society.
Premise: Fewer than 50 percent of the country’s citizens own homes.
Premise: The government does little to encourage homeownership.
Conclusion: By doing more to encourage homeownership by making people aware of the advantages homeownership has over renting, the government could improve conditions both for individuals and for society.
The reasoning of the argument is that, since homeownership can have benefits, and since fewer than 50 percent of the country’s citizens own homes, by encouraging people to buy homes via making them aware of the benefits of buying homes, the government can make everyone better off. Of course, there is an assumption in this argument, the assumption that people will buy homes if they are aware of the benefits of homeownership.
Remember, whenever a plan or course of action is presented, the author believes that that plan will work as she has proposed. She assumes that she has carefully and comprehensively analyzed the situation. She assumes that there are not any malicious variables that will spoil the plan. We’ll keep these ideas in mind as we look through the answer choices.
(A) Crime rates are lower in cities in which the ratio of homeowners to renters is relatively high because people who own homes tend to feel invested in their communities. This choice could be tempting, because what it says is related to the argument and because it could seem to support the argument’s premise that homeownership benefits society as a whole.
However, the effect of this choice is not the effect we need. This choice explains how homeownership benefits society, rather than states an assumption upon which the argument relies.
The argument is already supported by the fact that homeownership benefits society. So, making the argument does not require assuming that homeownership has any particular benefit to society, such as its resulting in reduced levels of crime.
(B) People involved in government are interested in improving conditions for individuals and society at large. While choices (A), (C), and (D) are clearly not assumptions upon which the argument relies, this answer choice could seem to be an assumption integral to the argument, as one could make the case that, in order for the benefits described in the argument to be generated, people in government would have to be interested in making that outcome happen.
However, if we look carefully, we can see that this choice supports the wrong conclusion.
This choice supports the conclusion that the government will encourage homeownership. After all, the fact that people in government are interested in improving conditions supports the conclusion that the government will encourage homeownership because of its benefits.
However, the conclusion of the argument is not that the government will encourage homeownership. The conclusion is that, by doing more to encourage homeownership, the government could improve conditions.
We can see that it’s not necessary to assume that people in government are interested in improving conditions in order to conclude that they could improve them by encouraging homeownership.
(C) Without government encouragement to buy homes, most people in the country will choose to rent the homes in which they live. This choice is tempting because it is related to what the passage says. In fact, it’s a conclusion supported by the passage.
By saying that fewer than 50 percent of people own homes and that the government has not encouraged homeownership, the passage supports the conclusion that, without government encouragement, most people will choose to rent.
However, this choice is not an assumption upon which the argument relies.
Notice that, even if, without government encouragement, most people would choose to buy rather than rent, it is still possible that, by encouraging homeownership, the government could get even more people to buy and thus benefit society.
So, even if what this choice says were not true, the argument could still work.
(D) The post retirement housing expenses of people who own homes are often lower than those of people who rent. This choice could be tempting since this choice may partially explain how homeownership benefits individuals who own homes: homeownership may help them to have lower post retirement housing expenses.
However, this choice is not an assumption upon which the argument relies, as the argument is already supported by the fact that homeownership benefits individuals who own homes, and that fact supports the argument without being explained.
In other words, arriving at the conclusion does not require assuming that homeownership has the particular benefit mentioned by this choice. Even if the post retirement housing expenses of people who own homes WERE NOT lower than those of people who rent, the argument could still work.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Some of the people who are currently renting homes or considering renting homes could afford to buy homes should they choose to do so. The support for the conclusion that conditions for everyone would improve if the government were to encourage homeownership by making people aware of the advantages homeownership has over renting depends on the assumption that some of the people who are renting or considering renting homes could afford to buy homes. After all, if nobody renting or considering renting could afford to buy a home, then making people aware that they would benefit from purchasing homes rather than renting would make no difference in the number who buy homes.
In other words, if NONE of the people who are currently renting homes or considering renting homes could afford to buy homes should they choose to do so, then we have a malicious variable, and the argument falls apart.
Notice that, in general, this answer choice helps underscore the author’s belief that there are no malicious variables that will enter the equation and spoil the plan.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss common incorrect answer choices in assumption questions.
summary:
Lesson Content A key aspect of arriving at the correct answers to Assumption questions is noticing the difference between trap choices and correct answers.
It says something that is rather clearly not an assumption integral to the argument but is so obviously connected to the argument that a test-taker is tempted to choose it. It says something that may seem to be an assumption integral to the argument but is not actually an assumption integral to the argument.
title: 2.10 The General Differences Between Trap Choices and Correct Answers in Assumption Questions

contents:
Lesson Content In the last section, we discussed how correct answers generally differ from trap choices.
💡MUST KNOW:
Knowing the most common types of incorrect choices that appear in Assumption questions can help us considerably.
Let’s begin by discussing an answer choice that supports the wrong conclusion.
summary:
Lesson Content In the last section, we discussed how correct answers generally differ from trap choices.
💡MUST KNOW:
Knowing the most common types of incorrect choices that appear in Assumption questions can help us considerably.
Let’s begin by discussing an answer choice that supports the wrong conclusion.
title: 2.11 Common Incorrect Answer Choices in Assumption Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Sometimes, Critical Reasoning incorrect answer choices will take important words or concepts from the stimulus and swap these with seemingly similar stand-ins that do not actually mean the same thing.
💡MUST KNOW:
Sometimes, Critical Reasoning incorrect answer choices will take important words or concepts from the stimulus and swap these with seemingly similar stand-ins that do not actually mean the same thing.
Of course, since what these incorrect choices discuss is similar to what the passage discusses, if we’re not paying attention, it’s easy to become ensnared by such a switch.
One way in which this type of swap can appear in an Assumption question is in an answer choice that helps to support a conclusion that is related to the conclusion of the argument but is not the conclusion of the argument.
Consider the following mini-example:
Zelda: I want to sell more of my watercolor paintings. Displaying some of them in the town library seems to be the best way to do so.
Tom: Your work would be seen by more people at Snazzy’s Gallery downtown. So, you would be more likely to achieve your goal if you were to display your work there.
Which of the following, if assumed, would allow Tom’s conclusion to be properly drawn?
(Choice that Supports the Wrong Conclusion) Paintings sold at Snazzy’s Gallery generally sell for prices significantly higher than are received for similar paintings that have been displayed at the library.
(Correct Answer) Some people who would see paintings at Snazzy’s Gallery would be interested in purchasing paintings of the type that Zelda wants to sell.
This question is tricky, because Tom’s conclusion is about Zelda’s goal, and Zelda’s goal is mentioned long before Tom’s conclusion is stated.
Zelda’s Goal: I want to sell more of my watercolor paintings.
Thus, Tom’s conclusion is:
Tom’s Conclusion: You would be more likely to achieve your goal (of selling more of your paintings) if you were to display them at Snazzy’s Gallery.
Note that the choice that supports the wrong conclusion may appear at first glance to be necessary for supporting Tom’s conclusion, as it mentions something that we might consider important in this situation, the prices received for paintings sold at each of the two venues.
However, we are seeking an assumption necessary for arriving at a specific conclusion, Tom’s conclusion that Zelda could sell more paintings at the gallery than she would at the library.
What Tom’s conclusion does depend on is the assumption that people who would see Zelda’s paintings at Snazzy’s Gallery would purchase paintings like hers.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering Assumption questions, beware of trap choices that support conclusions other than the conclusion of the argument in the stimulus.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 30 It is generally believed that, since plant matter generally does not contain in significant quantities vitamin B12, a nutrient found in many animal products, people following plant-based diets may become deficient in vitamin B12. Which of the following is an assumption upon which the author relies in arriving at the conclusion presented? 1. [ ] (A) A person consuming plant-based food to which vitamin B12 has been added cannot detect the presence of the added B12 by simply tasting the food. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) No people have health issues that involve their having difficulty absorbing and utilizing the B12 in the food they eat. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) It will not turn out that that the environmental consequences of people’s choosing to follow plant-based diets are worse than those of people’s consuming some animal products. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) All people who follow plant-based diets are aware that, by following plant-based diets, they may become deficient in vitamin B12. 5. [ ] (E) There are no necessary nutrients that are not present in plant material and that cannot be added to plant-based foods or consumed in supplement form. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium Remember, when answering an Assumption question, we must find an assumption necessary in order for the evidence to support the conclusion of the argument presented, not a different conclusion. So, we have to be sure to correctly identify the exact conclusion of the argument presented.
The following is the conclusion of the argument presented:
Conclusion: There is no reason to be concerned that the ongoing trend toward following plant-based diets will result in anyone’s experiencing vitamin B12 deficiency.
The following is the support provided for that conclusion:
Premise: People following plant-based diets can easily add vitamin B12 to plant-based foods or take supplements that provide vitamin B12.
So, the argument jumps from the premise that people following plant-based diets can add vitamin B12 to foods or take supplements to the conclusion that there is NO REASON to be concerned that the ongoing trend toward following plant-based diets will result in ANYONE’S experiencing vitamin B12 deficiency.
That’s a pretty big jump involving a variety of assumptions.
(A) A person consuming plant-based food to which vitamin B12 has been added cannot detect the presence of the added B12 by simply tasting the food. This choice supports the wrong conclusion. It supports a conclusion along the lines of that B12 added to plant-based foods is not detectable by someone eating those foods. Of course, that’s not the conclusion of the argument presented.
Meanwhile, regardless of whether what this choice says is true, the evidence provided supports the argument’s conclusion. After all, even if people CAN detect the presence of B12 added to food by simply tasting the food, the fact that people can add B12 to foods or take B12 supplements could support the conclusion that there is no reason to be concerned that the trend toward following plant-based diets will result in anyone’s experiencing B12 deficiency.
YOUR CHOICE(B) No people have health issues that involve their having difficulty absorbing and utilizing the B12 in the food they eat. This choice could be tempting because it supports a conclusion similar to the argument’s conclusion.
This choice provides some support for the conclusion that no people will experience B12 deficiencies. After all, the information that no people have health issues that involve their having difficulty absorbing and utilizing the B12 in the food they eat makes us more confident that nobody will experience a B12 deficiency.
However, what this choice says is not an assumption upon which the support for the conclusion of this argument depends.
After all, health issues resulting in B12 deficiencies and the following of plant-based diets resulting in B12 deficiencies are two separate matters.
So, even if, contrary to what this choice says, SOME people DO HAVE health issues that involve their having difficulty absorbing and utilizing the B12 in the food they eat, the fact that people can add B12 to foods or take B12 supplements could support the conclusion that there is no reason to be concerned that the trend toward following plant-based diets will result in anyone’s experiencing B12 deficiency.
(C) It will not turn out that that the environmental consequences of people’s choosing to follow plant-based diets are worse than those of people’s consuming some animal products. This choice supports the wrong conclusion. It supports a conclusion along the lines of that following plant-based diets is better for the environment than consuming animal products. Of course, that’s not the conclusion of the argument presented.
Meanwhile, regardless of whether what this choice says is true, the evidence provided supports the argument’s conclusion. After all, even if it WILL turn out that that the environmental consequences of people’s choosing to follow plant-based diets are worse than those of people’s consuming some animal products, the fact that people can add B12 to foods or take B12 supplements could support the conclusion that there is no reason to be concerned that the trend toward following plant-based diets will result in anyone’s experiencing B12 deficiency.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) All people who follow plant-based diets are aware that, by following plant-based diets, they may become deficient in vitamin B12. The conclusion that there is no reason to be concerned that the trend toward following plant-based diets will result in anyone’s experiencing vitamin B12 deficiency is supported by the premise that people following plant-based diets can easily add vitamin B12 to foods or take supplements.
Of course, in order for that premise to support the conclusion, the two have to be connected by the assumption that people are aware that, by following plant-based diets, they may become deficient in vitamin B12. After all, if people are NOT aware of that fact, then, even though they can add B12 to food or take supplements, some may not add B12 to food or take supplements. In that case, the conclusion that there is no reason to be concerned that the trend toward following plant-based diets will result in anyone’s experiencing vitamin B12 deficiency is not supported by the premise.
So, what this choice says is an assumption upon which the argument depends since, if NOT all people who follow plant-based diets are aware that, by following plant-based diets, they may become deficient in vitamin B12, the argument falls apart.
(Notice that, because this choice uses the word “all,” this choice may be considered an “extreme choice.” Nevertheless, it is the correct answer to this question. So, here we have a great example of a question that we would miss if we were to use the gimmicky method of eliminating all “extreme choices.”)
(E) There are no necessary nutrients that are not present in plant material and that cannot be added to plant-based foods or consumed in supplement form. This choice supports the wrong conclusion. It supports a conclusion along the lines of that people following plant-based diets get all the nutrients they need. Of course, that’s not the conclusion of the argument presented.
Meanwhile, regardless of whether what this choice says is true, the evidence provided supports the argument’s conclusion. After all, even if there are SOME necessary nutrients that are not present in plant material and that cannot be added to plant-based foods or consumed in supplement form, the fact that people can add B12 to foods or take B12 supplements could support the conclusion that there is no reason to be concerned that the trend toward following plant-based diets will result in anyone’s experiencing B12 deficiency.
Correct Answer: (D) In addition to looking out for answer choices that support the wrong conclusions, we need to be on the lookout for choices that do the reverse of what the correct answer does.
summary:
Lesson Content Sometimes, Critical Reasoning incorrect answer choices will take important words or concepts from the stimulus and swap these with seemingly similar stand-ins that do not actually mean the same thing.
💡MUST KNOW:
Sometimes, Critical Reasoning incorrect answer choices will take important words or concepts from the stimulus and swap these with seemingly similar stand-ins that do not actually mean the same thing.
Of course, since what these incorrect choices discuss is similar to what the passage discusses, if we’re not paying attention, it’s easy to become ensnared by such a switch.
title: 2.11.1 Assumption Incorrect Choice Type 1: Supports the Wrong Conclusion

contents:
Lesson Content Assumption questions often include answer choices that do basically the reverse of what a correct answer would do.
These “reverse” answer choices can be surprisingly seductive at first since they express something that is relevant to the argument in the passage and that has an effect on that argument.
Let’s return to Steven and his unfortunate experience with the polygraph test:
Steven was asked to take a polygraph, i.e., lie detector, test prior to being hired as a security guard at Company X. In the words of the polygraph examiner, Steven “absolutely failed” the section that asked whether the information on his resume was truthful. Company X determined that, since Steven failed the polygraph test, he must have lied on his resume.
In drawing the conclusion, Company X assumed which of the following? Consider the following as a possible answer choice:
A polygraph can be failed for a number of reasons that may be unrelated to the truth of the subject’s statements.
Notice that this answer choice weakens the support for the conclusion by calling into question the reliability of the polygraph test, thus doing the opposite of what a correct answer would.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering Assumption questions, look out for choices that have effects that are basically the reverse of the effect of an assumption that would help support an argument.
Consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 31 State Governor: In order to avoid having taxpayers pay for the lifestyles of people who could be working but choose instead to use illicit drugs that render them dysfunctional and unemployable, I propose that we regularly test for illicit drug use everyone receiving welfare benefits and that any such recipient testing positive for illicit drug use not be eligible to receive welfare benefits for two years following the test. Which of the following must the governor be assuming in coming to the above conclusion? 1. [x] (A) The savings achieved by making drug users ineligible to receive welfare benefits will be greater than the expense of testing all welfare benefit recipients for drug use. 2. [ ] (B) People who use illicit drugs and who know that they are going to be tested for drug use can take measures that result in their testing negative for drug use. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Everyone who engages in the use of illicit drugs becomes dysfunctional and unemployable. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Most people who become ineligible for welfare benefits because of their testing positive for use of illicit drugs will discontinue their drug use and find employment. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) The percentage of people receiving welfare benefits who use illicit drugs is greater than the percentage of people not receiving such benefits who use illicit drugs. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium The governor’s reasoning is that, since taxpayers pay for welfare benefits, taxpayer money can be saved via testing welfare benefit recipients for illicit drug use and denying welfare benefits to people whose test results indicate that they use illicit drugs.
Conclusion: By following this strategy we can save taxpayer dollars.
In order to choose the correct answer to this question, we have to keep the governor’s conclusion in mind. Otherwise, we could be tempted into choosing a trap choice that expresses something that is related to the argument but is not an assumption upon which the argument depends.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The savings achieved by making drug users ineligible to receive welfare benefits will be greater than the expense of testing all welfare benefit recipients for drug use. The point of using the strategy is to save taxpayer money, and denying welfare benefits would reduce the amount of money spent.
However, there’s another part to the plan, testing the recipients for illicit drug use. If the amount of money that would have to be spent to test all recipients for drug use exceeds the amount of money that would be saved by denying benefits to those who test positive, then the strategy will not be successful in saving taxpayer money.
In other words, if the savings achieved by making drug users ineligible to receive welfare benefits will NOT be greater than the expense of testing all welfare benefit recipients for drug use – a malicious variable for sure – then the argument falls apart.
So, the success of the plan depends on the assumption that the savings achieved through denying drug users benefits will be greater than the expense of testing.
(B) People who use illicit drugs and who know that they are going to be tested for drug use can take measures that result in their testing negative for drug use. This answer choice is relevant to the argument, but its effect on the argument is the opposite of what we are looking for. Rather than provide additional support for the conclusion, this choice weakens the argument.
After all, if people who use drugs can hide their drug use by taking measures that result in their testing negative for drug use, then the likelihood of success of the proposed strategy, which depends on correctly identifying those who use drugs, is questionable.
In general, when answering an Assumption question, be careful not to choose an answer choice that says something that weakens the argument.
(C) Everyone who engages in the use of illicit drugs becomes dysfunctional and unemployable. The governor’s conclusion is about a strategy for saving money, not about the effects of using illicit drugs.
The strategy of denying welfare benefits to people who test positive for illicit drugs could result in saving taxpayer dollars even if most people who use illicit drugs do NOT become dysfunctional and unemployable.
So, this choice does not state an assumption upon which the argument depends.
(D) Most people who become ineligible for welfare benefits because of their testing positive for use of illicit drugs will discontinue their drug use and find employment. This choice is a trap.
Since the governor did mention something about “people who could be working” if they weren’t using drugs, one could get the impression that the point of the strategy is to get people to stop using drugs and start working.
However, that is not exactly the point of the strategy. The point of the strategy is simply to save taxpayer money, and it is possible that implementing the strategy could result in saving taxpayer money even if most people who become ineligible for welfare benefits because of their testing positive for use of illicit drugs will NOT discontinue their drug use and find employment.
So, what this choice says does not have to be true in order for the strategy to work.
(E) The percentage of people receiving welfare benefits who use illicit drugs is greater than the percentage of people not receiving such benefits who use illicit drugs. While, in order for the strategy to work, at least some people receiving welfare benefits have to be using illicit drugs, it does not have to be the case that most people receiving welfare benefits are using illicit drugs.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now talk about an answer choice that doesn’t help to support the argument but instead merely explains an aspect of the scenario presented in the passage.
summary:
Lesson Content Assumption questions often include answer choices that do basically the reverse of what a correct answer would do.
These “reverse” answer choices can be surprisingly seductive at first since they express something that is relevant to the argument in the passage and that has an effect on that argument.
Let’s return to Steven and his unfortunate experience with the polygraph test:
Steven was asked to take a polygraph, i.
title: 2.11.2 Assumption Incorrect Choice Type 2: Choices that Do the Reverse of What the Question Asks For

contents:
Lesson Content Sometimes an incorrect choice in an Assumption question will provide an explanation for something discussed in the passage.
Let’s consider again our example of Henrietta and her pie:
Henrietta bakes the best apple pies in Clay County. The best apple pie entries win the Clay County Southern Charm baking contest every year without fail. Therefore, Henrietta’s apple pie will win the Southern Charm baking contest this year.
Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends?
(Incorrect Choice that Explains) Every time the Southern Charm baking contest has been held, the same judges have judged the pies entered into the contest.
(Correct Answer) Henrietta will enter her apple pie into the Southern Charm baking contest.
The incorrect choice that explains could be tempting.
Of course, the fact that an answer choice provides an explanation of an aspect of the scenario discussed in the passage does not make it the correct answer.
💡MUST KNOW:
The fact that an answer choice provides an explanation of an aspect of the scenario discussed in the passage does not make it the correct answer. If the question asks for an assumption upon which the argument depends, only an answer choice that provides such an assumption will be correct.
As we discussed previously, the correct answer in the Henrietta’s pie example is an assumption upon which the argument depends.
Be sure to differentiate any choices that are explanations from the choice that is an assumption as you consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 32 A study undertaken in Sweden found that, when people worked for six hours a day, they tended to be more productive and take fewer sick days than when they worked for the more traditional seven to eight hours per day. Which of the following must be true in order for the above conclusion to be correct? 1. [ ] (A) People who work for six hours per day generally have more energy for managing various aspects of their lives than is had by people who work for more hours per day. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Financial gains achieved by having people work more productively for fewer hours per day would exceed the costs of hiring additional people to make up for the reduction in hours worked by each person. 3. [ ] (C) People who go from working eight hours a day to working six hours per day generally report that they experience reductions in stress and increases in job satisfaction. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) It is always the case that a reduction in the number of hours per day a person works results in an increase in that person’s productivity. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Most workers would prefer working for only six hours per day over working seven to eight hours per day. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: The results of a study indicate that, when people worked for six hours a day, they tended to be more productive and take fewer sick days than when they worked for the more traditional seven to eight hours per day.
Conclusion: For companies seeking to maximize profits, it would make sense to have more workers, each of them working for six hours rather than for seven to eight hours per day.
While the question does not use the word “assumption,” in asking “Which of the following must be true in order for the conclusion to be correct?” the question is asking for an assumption upon which the argument depends.
The argument goes from the premise, which states simply that people involved in a study were more productive and took fewer sick days when they worked for six hours a day than when they worked seven to eight hours per day, to the conclusion that therefore companies seeking to maximize profits would be better off using more workers working for six hours per day rather than fewer workers working for seven to eight hours per day. Making this leap requires making various assumptions.
One basic assumption is that there are no malicious variables that will enter into the equation and spoil the plan.
(A) People who work for six hours per day generally have more energy for managing various aspects of their lives than is had by people who work for more hours per day. This choice may explain why people who work fewer hours per day are more productive: they have more energy. However, we don’t need such an explanation.
The argument has already established that when people work for six hours a day they are more productive than when they work seven to eight hours per day. Arriving at the argument’s conclusion from that evidence does not require explaining why they are more productive.
In general, when we’re seeking an assumption upon which an argument rests, an answer choice that presents an explanation for one of the facts presented in the passage will not be the correct answer.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Financial gains achieved by having people work more productively for fewer hours per day would exceed the costs of hiring additional people to make up for the reduction in hours worked by each person. Maximizing profits requires not just maximizing productivity but also taking costs into account.
While workers who work for six hours a day might be more productive than workers working seven to eight hours per day, companies whose workers are working fewer hours per day would probably have to take on more workers. Thus, in order for companies to become more profitable by having people work for fewer hours per day, the financial gains achieved by using that strategy have to be greater than the expenses associated with hiring additional people as necessary to compensate for the reduction in hours worked per person.
After all, if financial gains achieved by having people work more productively for fewer hours per day would NOT exceed the costs of hiring additional people to make up for the reduction in hours worked by each person, then the strategy of having people work for fewer hours per day would not result in profit maximization, and the argument would fall apart.
Notice that, in general, this answer choice underscores the author’s belief that there are no malicious variables that will enter the equation and spoil the plan.
(C) People who go from working eight hours a day to working six hours per day generally report that they experience reductions in stress and increases in job satisfaction. This choice may explain why people who work fewer hours per day are more productive: they experience reductions in stress and increases in job satisfaction. However, we don’t need such an explanation.
The argument has already established that people are more productive when they work six hours per day than they are when they work seven to eight hours per day. Arriving at the argument’s conclusion from that evidence does not require explaining why they are more productive.
(D) It is always the case that a reduction in the number of hours per day a person works results in an increase in that person’s productivity. The argument is specifically about having people work six hours per day rather than seven to eight hours per day. So, it does not have to be the case that any reduction in the hours a person works per day will result in increased productivity in order for the argument to work.
Even if, for instance, a person going from working for three hours a day to working for one hour a day would become much less productive, barely having time to start working before the workday ended, it could still be the case that companies could maximize profits by having people work for six hours rather than for seven or eight hours per day.
(E) Most workers would prefer working for only six hours per day over working seven to eight hours per day.
