The conclusion depends only on the connection between the productivity of people who work only six hours per day and profitability. Making this connection does not require assuming that people prefer to work six hours per day rather than eight hours per day. Even if they do not prefer working for fewer hours per day, having them work for fewer hours per day could be more profitable.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss a type of incorrect answer that does not help to support the argument in the stimulus but rather is itself a conclusion supported by the information in the stimulus.
summary:
Lesson Content Sometimes an incorrect choice in an Assumption question will provide an explanation for something discussed in the passage.
Let’s consider again our example of Henrietta and her pie:
Henrietta bakes the best apple pies in Clay County. The best apple pie entries win the Clay County Southern Charm baking contest every year without fail. Therefore, Henrietta’s apple pie will win the Southern Charm baking contest this year.
Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends?
title: 2.11.3 Assumption Incorrect Choice Type 3: Explains an Aspect of the Scenario Presented in the Passage

contents:
Lesson Content Another type of incorrect answer choice that can appear in an Assumption question is a choice that states a conclusion that is supported by the passage rather than an assumption upon which the argument depends.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering an Assumption question, be careful not to choose an answer choice that states a conclusion supported by what the passage says. We must find a choice that expresses an assumption integral to the argument.
If such an answer choice appears in an Assumption question, the choice will be both tempting and incorrect, because we don’t need a conclusion supported by the passage to answer an Assumption question.
Consider the following example:
Senator: The recent housing crisis occurred because a significant percentage of homeowners had taken on more debt than made sense given their income levels. Of course, doing so was only possible because lenders were loaning money to homebuyers who were unlikely to be able to repay the loans. In order to avoid repeating the housing crisis, we must enact regulations to prevent this type of lending.
Which of the following is an assumption upon which the above conclusion depends?
(Conclusion Supported by the Passage Choice) The recent housing crisis would not have occurred had homeowners better managed their debt levels.
(Correct Answer) Without regulations that prevent them from doing so, lenders will once again provide to homeowners loans that do not make sense given the homeowners’ incomes.
The conclusion supported by the passage choice is tempting, because what it says makes perfect sense given what is said in the passage.
We are not, however, looking for a choice that merely makes sense given what the passage says.
Does the Senator’s conclusion depend on assuming that the recent housing crisis would not have occurred had homeowners better managed their debt levels? It does not.
The correct answer, on the other hand, does express an assumption that the Senator must be making.
Now, beware of choices that are conclusions supported by what the passages say as you consider the following examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 33 In the City of Verdana, more than 50 percent of the population is considered obese. Which of the following must be true in order for the above conclusion to follow from the evidence provided? 1. [ ] (A) Many of the residents of Verdana are not over the age of 40. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Obesity is a common health problem among the residents of Verdana. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Doctors in Verdana agree that sufficient daily exercise is vital for health and well-being. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Residents of Verdana would be willing to engage in daily exercise. 5. [ ] (E) Public health officials in Verdana believe that the residents would benefit from daily exercise. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy Background: In the City of Verdana, more than 50 percent of the population is considered obese.
Premise: The main driver of this phenomenon is lack of sufficient daily exercise on the part of Verdana residents, most of whom are not fully aware of how beneficial exercise could be for their health.
Premise: Public health officials in Verdana plan to begin a campaign to educate the residents on the benefits of daily exercise.
Conclusion: Clearly, once this campaign is underway, the rate of obesity in Verdana will decrease.
The author has presented a plan. Any time an author presents a plan, he assumes that the plan will work. In other words, he assumes that there are not variables that will enter into the equation that will spoil the plan. In the TTP Course, we refer to such variables as “malicious variables.” When an author presents a plan, he assumes that there are no malicious variables.
Because this is an Assumption question, we’re seeking an answer choice that presents an unwritten premise that must be true in order for the argument to work.
(A) Many of the residents of Verdana are not over the age of 40. What is the significance of being over the age of 40? If you chose this answer as correct, you likely constructed an unsupported story that people over 40 will have a hard time exercising daily or losing weight and that, therefore, the author must be assuming that many of the residents of Verdana are not over the age of 40.
Of course, nothing in the passage, or in reality, supports that story. None, some, many, or all of the residents of Verdana could be over the age of 40, and the argument could still work perfectly. The author certainly does not need to assume that many of the residents of Verdana are not over the age of 40.
Remember, it is important not to create unsupported stories.
(B) Obesity is a common health problem among the residents of Verdana. The author states that more than 50 percent of the population in Verdana is considered obese. So, this choice could be tempting, because what this choice says is a conclusion supported by a fact stated by the author. In other words, the fact that more than 50 percent of the population of Verdana is obese indicates that obesity is common health problem in Verdana.
So, to avoid choosing this incorrect choice, we have to remember what we are looking for. We aren’t looking for a conclusion supported by the passage. We are looking for an assumption necessary in order for the argument’s evidence to support the argument’s conclusion. The author does not have to assume that obesity is a common health problem in Verdana in order for the argument to work.
The author has stated that more than 50 percent of Verdana’s population is obese. In doing so, he has indicated that obesity is common in Verdana. He certainly does not have to assume something that he has already indicated is the case.
In answering an Assumption question, we must be careful not to choose as correct a choice that is not a necessary assumption but rather a conclusion that follows from what the passage says.
(C) Doctors in Verdana agree that sufficient daily exercise is vital for health and well-being. The passage presents as fact that “the main driver of this phenomenon is lack of sufficient daily exercise.”
So, the passage establishes that lack of daily exercise is responsible for the high rate of obesity in Verdana. That fact, when presented in the context of the other facts provided, is more than sufficient to support the argument’s conclusion.
The author need not assume that doctors in Verdana agree that sufficient daily exercise is vital for health and well-being. That assumption would simply serve to magnify or clarify what the passage has already told us about daily exercise. Maybe doctors agree that daily exercise is vital for health and well-being, or maybe they don’t. Either way, what the argument says about daily exercise is a fact that supports the conclusion.
When answering an Assumption question, we must be careful not to choose as correct a choice that essentially magnifies or clarifies a fact provided in the passage.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Residents of Verdana would be willing to engage in daily exercise. Public health officials in Verdana plan to begin a campaign to educate the residents on the benefits of daily exercise. The author concludes that, once they do so, the rate of obesity in Verdana will decrease.
Any time an author concludes that a plan will work, he assumes that there will not be unanticipated variables – malicious variables, as we call them here at TTP – that spoil the plan.
However, what if it were true that residents of Verdana would NOT be willing to engage in daily exercise? In that case, the public health officials in Verdana could run a million campaigns to educate the residents on the benefits of daily exercise, yet nothing would change, because the residents of Verdana would NOT be willing to engage in daily exercise. In other words, the unwillingness of Verdana residents to engage in daily exercise would be a malicious variable that would spoil the public health officials’ plan.
Therefore, the author must be assuming that residents of Verdana would be willing to engage in daily exercise. Notice that the argument would fall apart without this assumption.
(E) Public health officials in Verdana believe that the residents would benefit from daily exercise. This choice could be tempting because it follows from what the passage says.
The passage says that public health officials in Verdana plan to begin a campaign to educate the residents on the benefits of daily exercise. What makes sense, then, is that the public health officials believe that the residents would benefit from daily exercise. After all, why would public health officials begin a campaign to educate the residents on the benefits of daily exercise if the public health officials didn’t believe that the residents would benefit from daily exercise?
To avoid choosing this incorrect choice, we have to remember what we are looking for. We aren’t looking for a conclusion supported by the passage. We are looking for an assumption necessary in order for the argument’s evidence to support the argument’s conclusion, and the author does not have to assume that the public health officials in Verdana believe that the residents would benefit from daily exercise in order for the argument to work.
The author states the fact that more than 50 percent of the population in Verdana is considered obese. He then mentions a plan, and concludes that implementing the plan will result in a reduction in the rate of obesity in Verdana.
Implementing the plan could result in a reduction in the rate of obesity whether public health officials in Verdana believe or don’t believe that the residents would benefit from daily exercise.
In answering an Assumption question, we must be careful not to choose as correct a choice that is not a necessary assumption but rather a conclusion that follows from what the passage says.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 34 In order to reduce smoking by citizens, the government both banned advertising designed to induce people to start smoking and began requiring that cigarette manufacturers place on packs of cigarettes graphic photographs depicting the damaging effects of smoking. Which of the following is an assumption upon which the above conclusion depends? 1. [ ] (A) Stores that sell cigarettes also sell sleeves that fit over cigarette packs, which can be used to cover any photographs that may appear on the packs. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) When two new actions have been taken simultaneously by a government, it can be difficult to determine which of the actions has caused the effects that follow. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Smokers and non-smokers have similar feelings about the damaging effects that smoking has on people’s health. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Advertising designed to induce people to start smoking does not have a significant effect on people’s decisions regarding whether to smoke cigarettes. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Smokers’ psychology is not such that a clear indication that someone is seeking to change their behavior causes them to increase engagement in the undesired behavior. View Solution Question Level: hard In the scenario presented in this argument, the reduction in smoking may or may not have resulted from the government’s having required that there be on cigarette packs graphic photographs depicting the effects of smoking. People are asked how they feel about the photographs, and because they say that they found the photographs “repellent,” it is determined that the photographs caused at least part of the reduction in smoking.
Conclusion: The photographs caused at least part of the reduction in smoking.
This conclusion make sense, as, if the photographs repel people, then it seems likely that the photographs cause people to smoke less.
However, what’s likely to be true is not necessarily true, and clearly there is a gap in this argument. The evidence is people’s opinions of the photographs, while the conclusion is that the photographs caused a reduction in smoking. What is being assumed in this situation?
(A) Stores that sell cigarettes also sell sleeves that fit over cigarette packs, which can be used to cover any photographs that may appear on the packs. This choice is tempting because it is quite relevant to the discussion. After all, if people can easily cover the photographs, then perhaps the photographs don’t have much effect.
However, this choice is incorrect because it weakens rather than supports the argument. The fact that the photographs can be covered and, therefore, may not have much effect undermines the conclusion that they caused part of the reduction in smoking.
(B) When two new actions have been taken simultaneously by a government, it can be difficult to determine which of the actions has caused the effects that follow. This answer is tempting because it is a conclusion supported by what the passage says about it not being clear whether the ban on advertising or the photographs were the cause of the reduction in smoking.
However, to correctly answer the question, we don’t need a conclusion supported by the passage; we need an assumption upon which the argument depends.
Concluding that the photographs caused part of the reduction does not depend on assuming that it’s difficult to determine what has caused a change when a government has taken two actions simultaneously.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Smokers and non-smokers have similar feelings about the damaging effects that smoking has on people’s health. This answer could be tempting because it is a conclusion supported by what the argument says about how smokers and non-smokers feel about the photographs.
However, since we don’t have to assume that they feel the same way about the health effects of smoking to conclude that the photographs caused part of the reduction, this choice is not the correct answer.
(D) Advertising designed to induce people to start smoking does not have a significant effect on people’s decisions regarding whether to smoke cigarettes. The conclusion is that the photographs caused part of the reduction in smoking, not all of it. So, the rest of the reduction could have been caused by the ban on advertising designed to induce people to start smoking.
Thus, arriving at the conclusion does not require assuming that such advertising does not have a significant effect on people’s decisions regarding whether to smoke cigarettes, because, even if such advertising does cause people to smoke cigarettes, and so, the ban on such advertising did cause a reduction in smoking, the photographs could still have caused part of the observed reduction in smoking.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Smokers’ psychology is not such that a clear indication that someone is seeking to change their behavior causes them to increase engagement in the undesired behavior. To people who already smoke, a ban on advertising designed to induce people to start smoking would not be a clear indication that someone is seeking to change their behavior.
On the other hand, repellent photographs required by the government on packs of cigarettes constitute a clear indication that someone is seeking to change the behavior of smokers.
So, if, contrary to what this choice says, smokers’ psychology IS such that a clear indication that someone is seeking to change their behavior causes them to increase engagement in the undesired behavior, then the photographs could have caused smokers to smoke more rather than smoke less.
Thus, the argument depends on the assumption that smokers’ psychology is NOT such that a clear indication that someone is seeking to change their behavior causes them to increase engagement in the undesired behavior. After all, if the presence of repellent photographs could have caused smokers to increase their smoking, then the conclusion is not supported by the evidence, and the argument falls apart.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss the better plan or alternative plan wrong answer.
summary:
Lesson Content Another type of incorrect answer choice that can appear in an Assumption question is a choice that states a conclusion that is supported by the passage rather than an assumption upon which the argument depends.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering an Assumption question, be careful not to choose an answer choice that states a conclusion supported by what the passage says. We must find a choice that expresses an assumption integral to the argument.
title: 2.11.4 Assumption Incorrect Choice Type 4: A Statement that is a Conclusion Supported by what is Said in the Passage

contents:
Lesson Content Often, in an Assumption question, the argument will be about a plan, and often the conclusion of the argument in such a question is essentially that the plan will work.
In many such questions, one of the incorrect answer choices will mention a plan that is an alternative to the plan the argument concludes will work, and often this alternative plan will appear to be better than the plan that the argument is promoting.
“Better plan” and “alternative plan” answer choices can be tempting, because they mention something that the author of the argument in passage may not have considered.
The truth is, however, that concluding that a plan will work does not require assuming that there is not a better or alternative plan.
💡MUST KNOW:
Concluding that a plan will work does not require assuming that there is not a better or alternative plan. A plan does not have to be the best possible plan or only possible plan in order for it to work.
Consider the following example:
To address the budgetary problems that Edentown’s Department of Health Services is experiencing, the city’s mayor has proposed imposing a tax on the sale of soda in stores. Since, not only could the tax discourage the consumption of soda, which results in costly health problems, but also any resulting tax revenue could be directed to the Department of Health Services, the mayor feels that imposing the tax would partially solve the budgetary problems of the Department of Health Services.
Which of the following is an assumption upon which the mayor’s reasoning depends?
(Better Plan Choice) The budgetary problems of the Department of Health Services could not be completely solved via negotiating better deals with suppliers.
(Correct Answer) The people in Edentown would not choose to completely avoid being affected by the soda tax by purchasing soda from stores in nearby Paradiseville.
The mayor feels that imposing the tax would partially solve the budgetary problems of the Department of Health Services.
Notice what the better plan choice does.
Of course, it’s tempting to choose this choice as the correct answer.
The correct answer, on the other hand, does express an assumption required for coming to the conclusion that the plan will work.
💡MUST KNOW:
An argument that concludes that a plan will work does not depend on the assumption that there is not another plan that would work even better.
Notice the “better plan” or “alternative plan” answer choices as you consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 35 CEO of Dreamy Beans Coffee Shop Chain: At our current staffing levels, people who work in our shops are often seen chatting with customers rather than working. Which of the following is an assumption upon which the CEO’s conclusion depends? 1. [ ] (A) Dreamy Beans could not reduce total expenses significantly by reducing the amount of supplies and inventory wasted every day. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Most people who patronize coffee shops do not cite social relationships that they have with staff members as key reasons for choosing a particular shop. 3. [ ] (C) People who have gained knowledge and experience over years of working at Dreamy Beans are not valuable assets to the company. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Adding new styles and flavors of coffee drinks to the menu would not increase profitability more than reducing staffing would. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Understaffing of Dreamy Beans shops would not at times result in customers’ waiting in excessively long lines. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: People who work in Dreamy Beans shops are often seen chatting with customers rather than working.
Premise: We estimate that the time staff members are seen chatting amounts to on average approximately 8 percent of their time on the job.
Conclusion: It is clear that we can increase profitability significantly by reducing labor costs via staffing our shops with up to 8 percent fewer people.
It seems to make sense that, if the shops’ staff members are spending 8 percent of their time on the job chatting rather than working, the shops could operate with 8 percent fewer staff members and thus operate more profitably. At the same time, it could be that the chatting somehow positively impacts profitability. So, in coming to the conclusion, the CEO is assuming that eliminating chatting will not be eliminating something that is important for the profitability of the shops.
(A) Dreamy Beans could not reduce total expenses significantly by reducing the amount of supplies and inventory wasted every day. This is an “alternative plan” answer choice. One might be tempted to assume that, if the CEO is considering one way of reducing expenses, he is assuming that an alternative way is not available.
The truth is, however, that the CEO’s conclusion is simply that reducing staffing would improve profitability. That conclusion would be supported by the evidence regardless of whether there are other ways to reduce expenses.
Remember that the conclusion that a plan will work does not require the assumption that there is not a better or alternative plan.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Most people who patronize coffee shops do not cite social relationships that they have with staff members as key reasons for choosing a particular shop. In coming to the conclusion that it is clear that Dreamy Beans can increase profitability by reducing staffing, the CEO is assuming that the positive effects on profitability of reducing staffing would be greater than any negative effects on profitability of eliminating time for chatting with customers.
If social relationships that customers have with staff members were key reasons for choosing a particular shop, then eliminating time for chatting with customers could result in significantly fewer people patronizing Dreamy Beans’ shops.
Thus, if it is true that, contrary to what this choice says, most people who patronize coffee shops DO cite social relationships that they have with staff members as key reasons for choosing a particular shop, then the conclusion is not supported by the evidence, and the argument falls apart.
Therefore, the CEO must be assuming that most customers do NOT cite such relationships as key reasons for choosing a particular shop.
(C) People who have gained knowledge and experience over years of working at Dreamy Beans are not valuable assets to the company. This is not an assumption upon which the argument depends.
For one thing, the CEO did not say that reducing staffing would involve laying off people who have gained knowledge and experience over years of working at Dreamy Beans. For all we know, reducing staffing would be done via reducing hiring of new employees.
Furthermore, even if reducing staffing would involve laying off people who have gained knowledge and experience over years of working at Dreamy Beans, the CEO could believe that those people ARE valuable assets of the company and still conclude that reducing staffing will result in increased profitability.
(D) Adding new styles and flavors of coffee drinks to the menu would not increase profitability more than reducing staffing would. This is a “better plan” choice.
The conclusion that reducing staffing would result in increased profitability does not depend on the assumption that there is not a way to increase profitability even more than reducing staffing would increase it. In other words, the CEO is not saying that there is not a better way to increase profitability; he’s simply saying that reducing staffing would result in increased profitability.
Remember that the conclusion that a plan will work does not require the assumption that there is not a better or alternative plan.
(E) Understaffing of Dreamy Beans shops would not at times result in customers’ waiting in excessively long lines. This choice is a tempting trap, because we could fall into the trap of thinking that the CEO’s plan would result in understaffing, and if understaffing WOULD at times result in customers’ waiting in excessively long lines, then the CEO’s plan could result in a reduction, rather than an increase, in profitability.
Notice, however, that we are not given reason to believe that reducing staffing by 8 percent would result in understaffing. On the contrary, what the CEO says seems to indicate that reducing staffing by 8 percent would result in a more appropriate level of staffing.
So, the CEO does not have to be assuming anything about understaffing, because the plan does not involve understaffing.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now wrap up our discussion of types of wrong answer choices that can appear in Assumption questions with our final potentially tricky type of choice, a choice that supports the conclusion of the argument but is not an assumption upon which the argument depends.
summary:
Lesson Content Often, in an Assumption question, the argument will be about a plan, and often the conclusion of the argument in such a question is essentially that the plan will work.
In many such questions, one of the incorrect answer choices will mention a plan that is an alternative to the plan the argument concludes will work, and often this alternative plan will appear to be better than the plan that the argument is promoting.
title: 2.11.5 Assumption Incorrect Choice Type 5: Mentions a Better, or Alternative, Plan

contents:
Lesson Content A tricky type of incorrect answer choice that can appear in an Assumption question is one that supports the conclusion but is not an assumption upon which the argument depends.
To avoid choosing such an incorrect choice, we have to keep in mind that providing support for a conclusion and being an assumption upon which an argument depends are not necessarily the same thing.
To see how this type of incorrect choice can work, consider the following example.
Anthropologist: Local storytellers, who allegedly base their stories on historical accounts passed down to them by other storytellers, say that the Anabara people were killed off by invaders. However, examination of remains found in areas where Anabara people lived indicates that the Anabara died off as a result of a smallpox epidemic. Clearly, in this case, the storytellers are not providing an accurate account of what occurred.
Upon which of the following does the anthropologist’s conclusion depend?
(Supports the Conclusion Choice) In areas where Anabara communities were located, no signs of invasions have been found.
(Correct Answer) Invaders did not take measures meant to cause the Anabara people to become infected with the smallpox virus.
Notice what’s going on here.
Let’s be clear though.
So, the supports the conclusion choice supports the conclusion without being an assumption required by the argument.
The correct answer, on the other hand is required by the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
There is a difference between an answer choice that simply supports the conclusion of an argument and one that is an assumption upon which the argument depends. To correctly answer an Assumption question, we need to find the answer choice that expresses an assumption upon which the argument depends.
Consider the choices in the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 36 Political Consultant: Rebecca Lim has twice run for the office of Mayor of Springville, and neither time did she win. Which of the following is assumption upon which the consultant’s conclusion depends? 1. [x] (A) The citizens of Springville consider the qualifications of candidates in deciding whom to vote for. 2. [ ] (B) Rebecca Lim will not be elected mayor next time she runs without more voters becoming aware of her qualifications. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) The candidate who beat Rebecca Lim in the previous two elections will not be eligible to run in the next election. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Rebecca Lim’s qualifications have not changed significantly since the last time she ran for Mayor of Springville. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Candidates other than Rebecca Lim who will be running for Mayor are generally perceived by voters to be not well qualified for the position of Mayor of Springville. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium Having found that voters were not aware of Rebecca Lim’s qualifications when she ran in and did not win previous elections, the consultant reasons that making voters aware that Rebecca Lim is the candidate most qualified to be mayor of Springville will result in her winning the election next time she runs.
Conclusion: You can be sure that Rebecca Lim will win next time.
In arriving at that conclusion, the consultant is assuming that making voters aware that Rebecca Lim is the candidate most qualified to be mayor is sufficient for assuring that she will win. But is it really sufficient? What has to be true in order for that plan to work?
YOUR CHOICE(A) The citizens of Springville consider the qualifications of candidates in deciding whom to vote for. In concluding that Rebecca Lim will win the election once voters are aware that she is the candidate most qualified to be mayor of Springville, the consultant must be assuming that voters will be swayed to vote for Rebecca Lim by the fact that she is most qualified.
In other words, the consultant’s reasoning depends on the assumption that the citizens of Springville consider the qualifications of candidates in deciding whom to vote for. Otherwise, making voters aware that Rebecca Lim is the candidate most qualified to be mayor of Springville would not make any difference.
(B) Rebecca Lim will not be elected mayor next time she runs without more voters becoming aware of her qualifications. This choice is a tempting trap, since the consultant’s choice of campaign strategy could be perceived as indicating that the consultant assumes that Rebecca Lim will not win unless more voters become aware of her qualifications.
Notice, however, that while it may be the case that the consultant believes that Rebecca Lim won’t win unless more voters become aware of her qualifications, concluding that she will win if voters are aware of her qualifications does not require assuming that she will not win if they are not aware of her qualifications.
After all, even if she WILL be elected mayor if no more voters become aware of her qualifications, she could win if voters become aware that she is the candidate most qualified to be mayor.
(C) The candidate who beat Rebecca Lim in the previous two elections will not be eligible to run in the next election. This choice may provide additional support for the conclusion that Rebecca Lim will win the election, but is it an assumption upon which the argument depends?
No.
The plan is to make voters aware that Rebecca Lim is the most qualified candidate. Implementing that plan could result in her winning the next election regardless of whether the candidate who won the last two times will be running the next time.
(D) Rebecca Lim’s qualifications have not changed significantly since the last time she ran for Mayor of Springville. Notice that changes in Rebecca Lim’s qualifications have not necessarily made it less likely that she will win. Her qualifications could have changed in a way such that she has become even more likely to win.
So, the conclusion that she is sure to win does not depend on the assumption that her qualifications have not changed significantly since the last time she ran.
(E) Candidates other than Rebecca Lim who will be running for Mayor are generally perceived by voters to be not well qualified for the position of Mayor of Springville. This choice could be tempting, as the fact that the other candidates running are perceived by voters as being not well qualified provides additional support for the conclusion that Rebecca Lim will win the election. However, we are not looking for additional support for the conclusion. We are looking for a statement that has to be true in order for the argument to work.
The consultant’s argument is not based on the premise that Rebecca Lim is merely well qualified. It’s based on the premise that she is the most qualified. Since only one candidate can be the most qualified, being sure that she will win does not require assuming that none of the other candidates are perceived as being well qualified.
So, the making voters aware that Rebecca Lim is the most qualified could result in her winning the next election even if, contrary to what this choice says, other candidates are NOT generally perceived by voters to be not well qualified.
So, this choice is not an assumption upon which the consultant’s argument depends.
Correct Answer: (A) Very shortly, we’re going to transition to the Weaken the Argument chapter.
summary:
Lesson Content A tricky type of incorrect answer choice that can appear in an Assumption question is one that supports the conclusion but is not an assumption upon which the argument depends.
To avoid choosing such an incorrect choice, we have to keep in mind that providing support for a conclusion and being an assumption upon which an argument depends are not necessarily the same thing.
To see how this type of incorrect choice can work, consider the following example.
title: 2.11.6 Assumption Incorrect Choice Type 6: Supports the Conclusion but is not an Assumption Upon Which the Argument Depends

contents:
Lesson Content Remember, when an author makes an argument, she assumes that that argument is correct.
In the next chapter, we’ll discuss ways to weaken an argument.
Before we get there, we want you to see that an argument’s assumptions are closely tied to statements that could weaken that argument.
Consider the following mini-example:
It tends to be the case that the higher the quality of the food a person eats, the better that person’s health will be. However, higher quality food generally costs more than lower quality food costs. Clearly then, people are forced to make a choice between lower expenses and better health.
OK, now notice that the following statement weakens the above argument.
In general, the added expense of a person’s eating higher quality food is completely offset by a reduction in that person’s medical expenses.
The statement that higher food expenses are completely offset by lower medical expenses is contrary to and weakens the support for the conclusion that people are forced to make a choice between lower expenses and better health.
Therefore, someone making the argument that people are forced to make a choice between lower expenses and better health must be assuming that the above statement that weakens the argument is not true.
Since arguing that people are forced to make a choice between lower expenses and better health requires assuming that the above statement that weakens the argument is not true, the negated version of that statement, which follows, must be an assumption upon which the argument depends.
In general, the added expense of a person’s eating higher quality food is NOT completely offset by a reduction in that person’s medical expenses.
Notice that this assumption is simply a statement that would weaken the argument combined with the word “not.” In other words, the “not” in the assumption neutralizes a statement that would otherwise weaken the argument.
Interestingly, many Assumption question correct answer choices include the word “not” and work in the following way:
The answer choice mentions something that would conflict with, or weaken, the argument. The word “not” appears in the answer choice in a way that negates the information that would conflict with, or weaken, the argument. The combination of the information that would weaken the argument with the word “not” makes the choice an assumption upon which the argument relies. So, many Assumption question correct answer choices are composed of information that would weaken the argument combined with the word “not.”
Of course, not every choice that includes “not” is an Assumption correct answer.
To determine which of the employees was responsible for the accounting fraud, the investigators plan to interview each employee. Since people tend to perspire when they lie, the investigators will be able to tell who is lying by noticing which employee perspires during the interview.
Which of the following is an assumption upon which the expectation that the plan will work relies?
(Incorrect Choice that Includes “not”) The employees who will be interviewed will not have time to prepare their stories.
(Correct Answer that Includes “not”) The location where the interviews will be held will not be so hot that everyone there will perspire regardless of whether they are lying.
The point of the investigation in this example is to determine who is responsible for the accounting fraud.
The incorrect choice that includes “not” is not required in order for the plan to work, as the plan is based entirely on the idea that, when people lie, they perspire.
On the other hand, the conclusion that the plan will work clearly relies on the correct answer that includes “not”.
💡MUST KNOW:
The correct answer to an Assumption question can be composed of information that would weaken the argument combined with the word “not.”
Let’s now practice noticing how “not” functions in correct answers to Assumption questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 37 Factory Floor Supervisor: I have heard from my counterparts at other factories that government inspectors are cracking down on factories violating safety codes. Which of the following is an assumption that the supervisor is making in choosing to prescribe the outlined course of action? 1. [ ] (A) Government inspectors will make an unannounced visit to the factory sometime in the near future. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) People working at the factory will not violate safety codes by acting in accordance with the factory’s standard operating procedures. 3. [ ] (C) All of the department managers of the factory are well versed in the factory’s standard operating procedures. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Adhering exactly to the factory’s standard operating procedures is the best way to make the factory safe for those who work there. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) If inspectors find a violation of safety codes, they will recommend relatively light consequences if they see that factory personnel are following standard operating procedures. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium The factory floor supervisor starts with some background information:
Government inspectors are cracking down on factories violating safety codes. The factory floor supervisor states a goal that is a response to the background information:
Ensure that we are not violating any safety codes. Finally, the factory floor supervisor prescribes a course of action for achieving the stated goal:
Make sure that everyone is following our standard operating procedures exactly. The question asks for an assumption that the factory floor supervisor is making in prescribing the outlined course of action. The purpose of the course of action that he outlines is to ensure that he and whomever he is speaking to are not violating any safety codes. So, in order to answer the question correctly, we need an answer that expresses an assumption that the factory floor supervisor must be making in concluding that following standard operating procedures exactly will serve the purpose of ensuring that they are not violating any safety codes.
(A) Government inspectors will make an unannounced visit to the factory sometime in the near future. While the factory floor supervisor does seem to be concerned that government officials may visit the factory floor, concluding that, by following standard operating procedures, they will achieve the goal of ensuring that they are not violating any safety codes does not require assuming that government officials will show up unannounced.
YOUR CHOICE(B) People working at the factory will not violate safety codes by acting in accordance with the factory’s standard operating procedures. This choice is interesting and includes the word “not”. So, let’s see whether the supervisor must be assuming what this choice says.
The purpose of following standard operating procedures exactly is to ensure that factory personnel are not violating any safety codes.
Now, notice what we get if we remove “not” from this choice:
People working at the factory will not violate safety codes by acting in accordance with the factory’s standard operating procedures.
Of course, if people working at the factory WILL violate safety codes by acting in accordance with the factory’s standard operating procedures, then following the factory’s standard operating procedures will cause what it is meant to prevent. So, without “not” this choice would weaken the argument.
Thus, this choice is a weakener + “not.”
Therefore, the factory supervisor must be assuming what this choice says, that people working at the factory will not violate safety codes by acting in accordance with the factory’s standard operating procedures.
(C) All of the department managers of the factory are well versed in the factory’s standard operating procedures. This choice could be tempting, in that a test-taker could feel that the plan will not work unless the managers are well versed in the standard operating procedures.
However, we are not looking for an assumption necessary for concluding that factory personnel are ready to make the plan work. We are looking for an assumption necessary for concluding that following standard operating procedures would serve the purpose of ensuring that factory personnel are not violating any safety codes.
In prescribing a course of action that he wants the factory personnel to follow, the factory supervisor does not have to assume that everyone is fully ready to follow that course of action. In other words, he can decide what he wants them to do and say what he wants them to do without assuming that they all already know how to do it.
(D) Adhering exactly to the factory’s standard operating procedures is the best way to make the factory safe for those who work there. The stated goal is not to make the factory safe for everyone who works there. So, prescribing the course of action of adhering exactly to standard operating procedures does not require assuming that adhering exactly to the factory’s standard operating procedures is the best way to make the factory safe.
(E) If inspectors find a violation of safety codes, they will recommend relatively light consequences if they see that factory personnel are following standard operating procedures. What this choice says sounds reasonable.
However, the purpose of the course of action is not to ensure that inspectors will recommend relatively light consequences if they find any violations of safety codes. The purpose of the course of action is to ensure that factory personnel are not violating any safety codes
So, this choice does not express an assumption integral to choosing to prescribe the course of action.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 38 New Owner of Joe’s Snack Shack: Any who are concerned about the future of Joe’s Snack Shack under our ownership might consider the following. Which of the following is an assumption upon which the new owner’s conclusion depends? 1. [ ] (A) Updating Joe’s Snack Shack will result in an increase in profits that will justify the costs of updating the restaurant. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Joe’s Snack Shack will not become more popular unless its exterior and interior are updated significantly. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Many of the restaurants that compete most directly with Joe’s Snack Shack will be updated extensively in the near future. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Joe’s Snack Shack’s antique décor and vintage look are not the most significant reasons for the restaurant’s popularity. 5. [ ] (E) Unless the exterior and interior of a restaurant are updated regularly, the restaurant will go out of business. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium Here’s a breakdown of the argument.
Premise: Neither the interior nor the exterior of Joe’s Snack Shack, which has been operating for 70 years, has been significantly updated since the restaurant first opened.
Premise: Over the next year, we plan to modernize virtually every aspect of the restaurant.
Conclusion: There is no reason to doubt that the restaurant will become more popular than ever.
There is a noticeable gap in the argument. The premises state that the restaurant has not been updated and will be modernized. The conclusion says that there is no reason to doubt that the restaurant will become more popular. More modern and more popular are not the same thing. So, there is a gap between modernizing the restaurant and the restaurant’s becoming more popular.
What piece of information is missing that must be true in order for this argument to work?
(A) Updating Joe’s Snack Shack will result in an increase in profits that will justify the costs of updating the restaurant. Notice that the conclusion of the argument is that there is no reason to doubt that the restaurant will become more popular. This answer choice, on the other hand, is about an increase in profits.
The fact that the restaurant will be modernized could support the conclusion that the restaurant will become more popular regardless of whether modernizing the restaurant will result in an increase in profits.
So, the argument could work even if, contrary to what this choice says, updating Joe’s Snack Shack will NOT result in an increase in profits. Thus, this choice is not an assumption upon which the argument depends.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Joe’s Snack Shack will not become more popular unless its exterior and interior are updated significantly. Notice that, even though this choice includes the word “not,” this choice is not an assumption upon which the argument depends.
After all, even if what this choice says were not true, and Joe’s Snack Shack would become more popular even if it were not updated, it could still be true that the Joe’s Snack Shack will become more popular than ever after it is modernized.
Since the argument’s conclusion could follow from the premises even if what this choice says were not true, this choice is not an assumption upon which the argument depends.
(C) Many of the restaurants that compete most directly with Joe’s Snack Shack will be updated extensively in the near future. The fact that competing restaurants will be updated in the near future could be seen as a reason for modernizing Joe’s Snack Shack.
However, we are not looking for a reason for updating the restaurant. We are looking for an assumption that connects the premise that the restaurant will be modernized to the conclusion that it will become more popular.
In fact, if anything, the fact that other restaurants will be updated might serve to call into question the conclusion that modernizing Joe’s Snack Shack will result in its becoming more popular. After all, if other restaurants will be updated, then maybe modernizing Joe’s Snack Shack will just help Joe’s Snack Shack keep up with other restaurants rather than result in its becoming more popular.
So, if anything, this choice weakens the argument, rather than states an assumption integral to the argument.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) Joe’s Snack Shack’s antique décor and vintage look are not the most significant reasons for the restaurant’s popularity. This choice is a weakener combined with “not.”
Without “not,” this choice would say the following:
Joe’s Snack Shack’s antique décor and vintage look are not the most significant reasons for the restaurant’s popularity.
If that statement were true, then, contrary to the conclusion of the argument, we would have reason to doubt that the restaurant will become more popular after it is modernized. After all, if Joe’s Snack Shack’s antique décor and vintage look ARE the most significant reasons for the restaurant’s popularity, then modernizing the restaurant is likely to result in the restaurant’s becoming less popular, rather than more popular.
So, without “not,” this choice is a weakener.
Thus, in order for the argument to work, it has to be true that Joe’s Snack Shack’s antique décor and vintage look are NOT the most significant reasons for the restaurant’s popularity.
So, this choice states an assumption upon which the argument depends.
(E) Unless the exterior and interior of a restaurant are updated regularly, the restaurant will go out of business. It could very well be that the new owner believes that, unless a restaurant is updated, it will go out of business.
However, it is not necessary to assume what this choice says in order to conclude that Joe’s Snack Shack will be more popular after it is modernized.
After all, even if it is NOT true that, unless a restaurant that is updated, it will go out of business, the fact that Joe’s Snack Shack will be modernized could support the conclusion that it will become more popular.
Correct Answer: (D)
summary:
Lesson Content Remember, when an author makes an argument, she assumes that that argument is correct.
In the next chapter, we’ll discuss ways to weaken an argument.
Before we get there, we want you to see that an argument’s assumptions are closely tied to statements that could weaken that argument.
Consider the following mini-example:
It tends to be the case that the higher the quality of the food a person eats, the better that person’s health will be.
title: 2.12 Assumptions and Weakening an Argument

contents:
Lesson Content As we’ve discussed, an argument is composed of a conclusion, the evidence that supports the conclusion, any background information, and the assumptions that connect the evidence with the conclusion.
Conclusion Evidence Background Assumptions On the GMAT, we’ll never find an answer choice that weakens an argument by attacking the premises (that is, the evidence) upon which the conclusion rests, because while in real life premises may turn out to be invalid, on the GMAT the premises are taken to be true.
💡MUST KNOW:
On the GMAT, never try to weaken an argument by attacking the premises (that is, the evidence) upon which the conclusion rests. The premises are always taken to be true.
So, for example, if an argument’s premise says,
Last year, 50 percent of all of Company Z’s sales came from product P.
we must assume that premise to be factual.
Because background information is also considered factual, we’ll never be able to weaken an argument by attacking background information.
💡MUST KNOW:
Because background information is also considered factual, we’ll never be able to weaken an argument by attacking background information.
Since the answer to a Weaken question can’t undermine the argument by attacking the evidence (or the background information), the answer has to undermine the support for the conclusion in another way, which is to attack the reasoning that links the evidence to the conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
The correct answer to a Weaken the Argument question will show that the conclusion does not necessarily follow from the premises, even though we take those premises to be true.
How do we show that the conclusion does not necessarily follow from the premises? We target the assumptions.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Weaken questions, we must be aware of assumptions made by authors because, in general, we weaken GMAT arguments by attacking the assumptions upon which the arguments rest.
In other words, correct answers to Weaken questions say things that indicate that assumptions authors have made are not valid.
💡MUST KNOW:
Correct answers to Weaken questions say things that indicate that assumptions authors have made are not valid.
From the Identify the Assumption chapter, you already know that an assumption is an unstated piece of information that must be true in order for the logic of the argument to work.
Let’s revisit the disease x argument from the chapter on Assumption questions:
All people in houses in which communicable diseases are present should be quarantined. Two people in the yellow house on Maple Street came down with disease x yesterday. Therefore, all people in the yellow house on Maple Street should be quarantined.
From the Assumption questions chapter, we already know that the author assumes that disease x is a communicable disease.
The only way this argument works is if it’s true that disease x is a communicable disease.
The structure of the argument is:
Premise 1: All people in houses in which communicable diseases are present should be quarantined.
Premise 2: Two people in the yellow house on Maple Street came down with disease x yesterday.
Assumption: Disease x is a communicable disease.
Conclusion: People in the yellow house should be quarantined.
How can we weaken this argument? Easy.
For instance, what if we were to provide a new fact, the fact that it is not the case that disease x is a communicable disease?
In that case, the assumption that holds the argument together would not be true, and the argument would fall apart.
So, an assumption functions as the unwritten information that connects the evidence to the conclusion.
Thus, if we can attack the author’s assumption, we can weaken the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
To weaken an argument, we can attack the assumption(s) made by the author. By breaking this critical glue that connects the evidence to the conclusion, we weaken the argument.
Let’s practice a bit.
Targeted Practice For each of the following arguments, choose the answer option that most weakens the argument. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now consider an example similar to one that we saw in the chapter on Assumption questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 1 MowGreen, a company that uses sustainable methods to maintain lawns, has been expanding quickly, and its services are now available in many areas of the country. Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above? 1. [x] (A) MowGreen’s management team does not consider profitability in determining whether to expand into an area. 2. [ ] (B) People in the Drawnville area are not interested in using sustainable methods for maintaining their lawns. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) There remain in the country areas in which MowGreen is not currently operating into which it could profitably expand its operations. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) MowGreen operates profitably in all of the areas in which it currently operates. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Sustainable methods for maintaining lawns are generally more expensive to use than are less environmentally friendly methods. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: MowGreen has been expanding, and its services are available in many areas.
Premise: MowGreen would not be able to operate profitably in the Drawnville area.
Conclusion: One can be sure that MowGreen will not expand into the Drawnville area any time soon.
Notice that the author is making the assumption that MowGreen’s management team considers profitability in determining whether to expand into an area. After all, if this assumption were found to be false, the argument would fall apart.
Which of the answer choices effectively attacks this assumption?
YOUR CHOICE(A) MowGreen’s management team does not consider profitability in determining whether to expand into an area. If MowGreen’s management team does not consider profitability in determining whether to expand into an area, this argument falls apart.
After all, if MowGreen’s management team does not consider profitability in determining whether to expand into an area, then the premise that MowGreen would not be able operate profitably in the Drawnville area doesn’t really support the conclusion that MowGreen will not expand into the Drawnville area.
So, this choice weakens the argument by effectively attacking an assumption integral to the argument.
(B) People in the Drawnville area are not interested in using sustainable methods for maintaining their lawns. Given that MowGreen uses sustainable methods to maintain lawns, the fact that people in the area around Drawnville are not interested in using sustainable lawn care methods is in line with the conclusion that MowGreen will not start operating in that area. So, this choice does not weaken the support for the conclusion.
(C) There remain in the country areas in which MowGreen is not currently operating into which it could profitably expand its operations. The fact that there are areas of the country into which MowGreen can profitably expand its operations does not change the fact that MowGreen would not be able to operate profitably in the Drawnville area. So, this choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion.
(D) MowGreen operates profitably in all of the areas in which it currently operates. This choice could be temping if a test-taker were to take the fact that MowGreen operates profitably in all of the areas in which it operates as an indication that actually MowGreen could somehow operate profitably in the Drawnville area.
Remember, though, in GMAT Critical Reasoning, the premises are taken to be true. So, we can’t weaken the argument by attacking the premise that MowGreen would not be able to operate profitably in the Drawnville area. That premise is a simple fact.
Notice also that the truth is that this choice does not indicate that MowGreen could operate profitably in the Drawnville area. All it indicates is that MowGreen operates profitably where it does operate.
(E) Sustainable methods for maintaining lawns are generally more expensive to use than are less environmentally friendly methods. The fact that the methods MowGreen uses are more expensive to use than are other methods has no effect on the argument, as it neither changes the fact that MowGreen would not be able to operate profitably in the Drawnville area nor provides reason to believe that MowGreen would operate in Drawnville unprofitably.
Correct Answer: (A) Ok – so we’ve seen that, if an argument’s assumptions are shown to be false, the argument will fall apart.
Instead, these correct answers simply cast some doubt on the validity of the assumptions made by authors, thereby casting some doubt on the efficacy of the arguments.
Notice how the correct answer in the following example simply casts a bit of doubt on the validity of the conclusion.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 2 The Games Plus franchise stocks the game TigerTrail at all of its stores. Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument? 1. [ ] (A) Preceding TigerTrail’s release, the Games Plus franchise mounted an extensive online advertising campaign announcing the game. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Many game buyers associate above eye-level shelf placement of a game with high popularity. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Most people who have bought TigerTrail from Store A have called in the order and picked up at the register. 4. [ ] (D) Every year since it opened, Store A has reported higher annual profits than any other Games Plus franchise store. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Patrons of Store A have no more discretionary income to spend on video games than do patrons of other stores in the Games Plus franchise. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy Here’s a breakdown the argument:
Premise 1: Store A always displays its copies of TigerTrail on shelves above eye-level.
Premise 2: The other stores in the franchise rotate their copies of TigerTrail between eye-level and above eye-level shelves.
Premise 3: Store A sells more copies of TigerTrail per year than does any other store in the franchise.
Assumption: The author makes the unstated logical leap that the comparatively higher sales at Store A are the result of shelf placement rather than of some other factor. In other words, she assumes that there is no other factor that was responsible for the comparatively higher sales at Store A.
Conclusion: The shelf placement of TigerTrail at Store A is responsible for the higher sales of TigerTrail at Store A.
Notice that the author is making a cause-and-effect claim. The author argues that the cause of Store A’s selling more copies of TigerTrail per year than any other store is the shelf placement of TigerTrail at Store A. As we already know, a cause-and-effect claim is a bold one, and whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she assumes that there will not be any malicious variables that enter the equation and ruin her argument.
Often, that assumption turns out to be a bad one. Let’s see …
(A) Preceding TigerTrail’s release, the Games Plus franchise mounted an extensive online advertising campaign announcing the game. Since there is no indication that this franchise-wide ad campaign would have had a special impact on Store A buyers in particular, this choice doesn’t affect an argument about the difference between Store A and other stores in the franchise.
In other words, this answer choice does not provide an alternate explanation for the difference between sales of TigerTrail at Store A and sales of TigerTrail at other stores.
So, this choice does not cast any doubt on the conclusion that the shelf placement is responsible for the difference in sales of TigerTrail.
(B) Many game buyers associate above eye-level shelf placement of a game with high popularity. Notice that what this answer choice says provides an explanation for why above eye-level shelf placement works: perhaps buyers are fooled into thinking that TigerTrail must be really popular.
We are not, however, seeking an explanation for why shelving above eye-level results in increased sales; we’re seeking an alternate explanation for why Store A sold so many copies of TigerTrail.
This choice does not weaken the connection between above eye-level shelf placement and high sales of TigerTrail in Store A – if anything, it strengthens it by having us believe even more strongly in the power of such shelf placement.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Most people who have bought TigerTrail from Store A have called in the order and picked up at the register. This choice weakens the link between shelf placement of TigerTrail and sales of TigerTrail, because, if most of the buyers of TigerTrail at Store A have picked up the game at the register, it is quite possible that most of the buyers did not ever see the shelf TigerTrail was on.
Thus, this choice suggests that the buyers may not have been influenced by the shelf placement and that there is another reason for the difference in levels of sales.
Notice that this answer attacks the author’s assumption that the customers were ever in position to be affected by the shelf placement of TigerTrail. Notice too that this answer choice presents a malicious variable: the people did not even see the shelf-placement!
(D) Every year since it opened, Store A has reported higher annual profits than any other Games Plus franchise store. What this answer choice says is not directly connected to sales of TigerTrail or even clearly connected to sales at all. The higher profits at Store A could be the result of factors other than sales, factors such as greater efficiency and keeping costs low.
Besides, the higher profits at Store A could also be the result of higher sales of TigerTrail. In such a case, this choice just presents a result of the higher sales of TigerTrail.
(E) Patrons of Store A have no more discretionary income to spend on video games than do patrons of other stores in the Games Plus franchise. If anything, this choice strengthens rather than a weakens the argument that shelf placement is responsible for the higher sales of TigerTrail at Store A, as it eliminates one alternative explanation for the higher sales, that there is a difference in the discretionary income of the customers.
In other words, if customers who shop at Store A have higher discretionary incomes than customers who shop at other stores, then that fact could provide an alternative explanation for why Store A sells so many copies of TigerTrail (its customer have more money to spend). What this answer choice says removes that alternative explanation as a possibility.
As we will see in greater detail in the Strengthen the Argument chapter, when a cause-and-effect claim is made, we can strengthen the support for that cause-and-effect claim by ruling out any alternative explanations for the observed events.
Correct Answer: (C) As the chapter progresses, we’ll continue to underscore strategies to effectively weaken arguments.
summary:
Lesson Content As we’ve discussed, an argument is composed of a conclusion, the evidence that supports the conclusion, any background information, and the assumptions that connect the evidence with the conclusion.
Conclusion Evidence Background Assumptions On the GMAT, we’ll never find an answer choice that weakens an argument by attacking the premises (that is, the evidence) upon which the conclusion rests, because while in real life premises may turn out to be invalid, on the GMAT the premises are taken to be true.
title: 3.2 Focus on Assumptions to Effectively Weaken an Argument.

contents:
Lesson Content Most of the time, recognizing a Weaken the Argument question is easy, because these questions utilize predictable language.
Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above?
Which of the following, if true, casts the most doubt on the conclusion drawn above?
Which of the following, if true, does the most to call into question the claim made?
Which of the following, if discovered, would be evidence against the speculation above?
Which of the following, if true, most seriously undermines the support for the above conclusion?
Which of the following, if true, would indicate a vulnerability of the speaker’s argument?
Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the force of the speaker’s evidence?
It’s pretty easy to see that all of these stems ask us to weaken, in some way, an argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
Learn to recognize the common and predictable language used by Weaken the Argument questions.
Let’s practice recognizing Weaken the Argument questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 3 Although campfires are illegal in Aurora Falls National Park, campfires often start forest fires in the park. Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above? 1. [ ] (A) Aurora Falls National Park has provided its rangers with extensive training in the safe operation of aerial drones as well as in the best practices in campfire extinguishment. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) A significant percentage of the forest fires that occur in Aurora Falls National park are not started by campfires. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Statistics compiled by Aurora Falls National Park rangers show that forest fires started by campfires have been responsible for the destruction of thousands of acres of park property. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Because of Aurora Falls National Park’s large size, park rangers require 30 minutes on average to reach most park locations, during which time campfire sparks can easily start forest fires. 5. [ ] (E) The aerial drones utilize advanced heat sensors that some militaries deploy on satellites used to detect the launch of surface-based missiles. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium Background: Although campfires are illegal in Aurora Falls National Park, campfires often start forest fires in the park.
Premise: Tomorrow, park rangers will begin using aerial drones that detect campfires and relay coordinates of campfire locations to park rangers, who will go to and extinguish the campfires.
Conclusion: Surely, going forward, only rarely will campfires start forest fires in the park.
Notice that this question is clearly a Weaken the Argument question, because the question stem asks, “Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above?”
The author has presented a plan. Any time an author presents a plan, she assumes that the plan will work. In other words, she assumes that there are no variables that will spoil the plan. In the TTP Course, we refer to such variables as “malicious variables.” When an author presents a plan, she assumes that there are no malicious variables.
What has the author failed to consider? What malicious variable has gone unnoticed?
(A) Aurora Falls National Park has provided its rangers with extensive training in the safe operation of aerial drones as well as in the best practices in campfire extinguishment. What this answer choice says strengthens the argument.
If Aurora Falls National Park has provided its rangers with extensive training in the safe operation of aerial drones as well as in the best practices in campfire extinguishment, then the rangers have some important training related to the plan presented in the argument. That fact makes it likely that the plan will be successful.
Of course, we’re not seeking a strengthener. We’re seeking an answer choice that weakens the argument.
(B) A significant percentage of the forest fires that occur in Aurora Falls National park are not started by campfires. Notice that the conclusion of the argument is not that, in general, forest fires will rarely occur in the park. It’s that, going forward, only rarely will campfires start forest fires in the park.
So, the fact that a significant percentage of the forest fires that occur in Aurora Falls National park are not started by campfires does not affect the support for the conclusion at all, because even if things other than campfires start forest fires in the park, the evidence presented still supports the conclusion, which is specifically about forest fires started by campfires.
So, this choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion.
(C) Statistics compiled by Aurora Falls National Park rangers show that forest fires started by campfires have been responsible for the destruction of thousands of acres of park property. The conclusion is simply that, going forward, only rarely will campfires start forest fires in the park.
Our job in answering the question is to weaken the support for that conclusion.
This choice tells us how much destruction forest fires caused by campfires have caused in the past. Does that information indicate that putting out campfires won’t prevent them from starting forest fires “going forward”? Of course not. No matter how much destruction campfires have caused in the past, putting them out could prevent them from causing forest fires in the future.
So, what this answer choice says does not affect the support for the conclusion or weaken the argument at all.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Because of Aurora Falls National Park’s large size, park rangers require 30 minutes on average to reach most park locations, during which time campfire sparks can easily start forest fires. What this answer choice says certainly weakens the argument.
The argument’s conclusion is that, going forward, only rarely will campfires start forest fires in the park.
So, imagine that one of the drones spots a campfire, and a park ranger mobilizes to get to that location. If, as this choice says, park rangers require 30 minutes on average to reach most park locations, during which time campfire sparks can easily start forest fires, then a campfire could easily start a forest fire prior to the ranger’s reaching the campfire.
So, this choice provides a good reason to question whether the plan will work. Notice also that this answer choice presents a malicious variable that the author has failed to consider.
(E) The aerial drones utilize advanced heat sensors that some militaries deploy on satellites used to detect the launch of surface-based missiles. If anything, what this answer choice says strengthens the argument.
After all, if the thermal cameras on the aerial drones utilize advanced heat sensors that some militaries deploy on satellites used to detect the launch of surface-based missiles, then these heat sensors must be pretty sophisticated. If the heat sensors are sophisticated, then the aerial drones equipped with these thermal cameras would likely do a good job of detecting campfires in the park. Therefore, given what this choice says, we have more conviction that the plan will work.
Of course, in answering this question, we’re not seeking a strengthener; we need to weaken the argument.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 4 It is much easier to cultivate, i.e., plough and otherwise prepare for planting, fields from which any rocks near the surface have been removed. Which of the following, if true, most seriously undermines the above conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) The people of Rapa Nui were dependent on farming, as there were on the island few trees from which could be built boats for fishing. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) When Europeans first arrived on Rapa Nui, they found a thriving population. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Many of the pieces of rock found in the fields used for farming on Rapa Nui were small enough that they could have easily been removed by an individual working alone. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) The people of Rapa Nui carved from volcanic rock large statues, which they placed at regular intervals on the island. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Volcanic rock gives off small amounts of phosphorus, a plant nutrient the levels of which are generally quite low in the soil found on Rapa Nui. View Solution Question Level: hard The question stem asks us to find the answer that “most seriously undermines the conclusion.” So, this question is a Weaken question, and we have to find a way to weaken the argument.
The conclusion that we have to undermine is:
Conclusion: It must be the case that the people who lived on Rapa Nui did not develop expertise in agriculture.
The conclusion is supported by that fact that fields on Rapa Nui used for centuries for farming were found to be full of rocks, even though it’s much easier to cultivate fields from which any surface rocks have been removed.
There is a gap in this argument, in that it jumps from the fact that the fields were found to be full of rocks to the conclusion that the people of Rapa Nui did not develop expertise in agriculture.
Notice that the author assumes that the only reason why there would be rocks in fields used for farming is that the people farming had not developed expertise in agriculture. However, if there was an alternative explanation for why there would be rocks in fields, then the argument would be weakened.
(A) The people of Rapa Nui were dependent on farming, as there were on the island few trees from which could be built boats for fishing. This choice could be very tempting, as one might be tempted to believe that, if the people of Rapa Nui were dependent on farming, it’s likely that they developed expertise in agriculture.
However, the truth is that what this choice says does not contradict what the passage says or attack the connection between the fact that there were rocks in the fields and the conclusion.
After all, the people of Rapa Nui could have been dependent on farming and yet not developed expertise in agriculture.
So, regardless of how dependent on farming the people of Rapa Nui were, the premise, that the fields were full of rocks, still indicates that they didn’t develop expertise in agriculture.
(B) When Europeans first arrived on Rapa Nui, they found a thriving population. This choice could be tempting, as one might be tempted to believe that, if the people of Rapa Nui were thriving, it’s likely that they developed expertise in agriculture.
However, the truth is that what this choice says does not contradict what the passage says or attack the connection between the fact that there were rocks in the fields and the conclusion.
After all, the people could have been thriving without developing expertise in agriculture.
So, regardless of whether the people of Rapa Nui were thriving, the premise, that the fields were full of rocks, still indicates that they didn’t develop expertise in agriculture.
(C) Many of the pieces of rock found in the fields used for farming on Rapa Nui were small enough that they could have easily been removed by an individual working alone. This choice strengthens the support for the conclusion, since it eliminates an alternative reason for the rocks’ being in the fields: they would have been difficult to remove.
By eliminating the possibility that the rocks were in the fields, not because people left them there out of lack of agricultural expertise, but because they would have been difficult to move, this choice strengthens the case for the conclusion that the people of Rapa Nui did not develop expertise in agriculture.
