(D) The people of Rapa Nui carved from volcanic rock large statues, which they placed at regular intervals on the island. This choice could be tempting, as one might be tempted to believe that the presence of the rocks in the fields was some consequence of the creation of the volcanic rock statues, and that, therefore, it somehow makes sense that the rocks would be in the fields even if the people of Rapa Nui had developed expertise in agriculture.
The truth is, however, that it is not clear that the presence of the rocks in the fields has anything to do with the creation of the statues.
Furthermore, even if the presence of the rocks in the fields was a consequence of the creation of the statues, the presence of the rocks in fields used for farming could still support the conclusion that the people of Rapa Nui did not develop expertise in agriculture. After all, regardless of how the rocks got there, the fields use for agriculture were full of rocks even though cultivating the fields would presumably have been much easier if the rocks had been removed.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Volcanic rock gives off small amounts of phosphorus, a plant nutrient the levels of which are generally quite low in the soil found on Rapa Nui. This choice severely undermines the support for the conclusion, as it very effectively attacks the assumption that the only reason why there would be rocks in fields used for farming is that the people farming had not developed expertise in agriculture.
The choice attacks that assumption by providing reason to believe that, actually, the rocks were in the fields because the people of Rapa Nui had developed expertise in agriculture.
By stating that the levels of the plant nutrient phosphorus are generally quite low in the soil found on Rapa Nui, and that volcanic rocks give off phosphorus, this choice provides a reason to believe that the rocks were purposely left in or placed in the fields to provide plants growing in the fields with phosphorus, an action that would be taken by farmers, not out of ignorance, but rather because they had expertise.
So, by showing that the fact that there were volcanic rocks in the fields could indicate that the people of Rapa Nui had expertise in farming, this choice seriously undermines the support for the conclusion.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss a strategy for answering Weaken the Argument questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Most of the time, recognizing a Weaken the Argument question is easy, because these questions utilize predictable language.
Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above?
Which of the following, if true, casts the most doubt on the conclusion drawn above?
Which of the following, if true, does the most to call into question the claim made?
Which of the following, if discovered, would be evidence against the speculation above?
title: 3.3 Spotting a Weaken the Argument Question

contents:
Lesson Content To find the correct answer to a Weaken question, follow this 6-step strategy.
Step 1 – Carefully read and understand the passage.
The foundation of arriving at the correct answer to a Weaken question is understanding the passage.
The importance of understanding the passage cannot be overemphasized.
So, your first job in answering a Weaken question is to carefully read the passage, developing understanding of what the passage says and noting key details of the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
The foundation for arriving at the correct answer to a Weaken question is understanding the passage.
Step 2 – Read the question stem and identify what it is asking you to do.
Next, read the question stem.
If the question is asking you to weaken the argument or to undermine the support for or call into question the conclusion, it’s a Weaken the Argument question.
Step 3 – Go back to the passage and determine exactly what the conclusion of the argument is.
At this point, you understand the passage and know that you have to weaken the argument.
Of course, the correct answer will be information that undermines the support for the conclusion or otherwise serves to call the conclusion into question.
So, the next step is to go back to the passage and identify exactly what the conclusion is.
Identifying the exact conclusion of the argument is possibly the single most crucial step you will take in answering a Weaken question.
If you have only a vague idea of what the conclusion is, you can easily fall for trap choices that weaken the support for the wrong conclusion.
So, identify exactly what the conclusion is.
Step 4 – Identify how the conclusion is supported.
Generally, the correct answer to a Weaken question will attack the connection between the premises and the conclusion or otherwise undermine the support for the conclusion.
So, of course, the next key step in answering a Weaken question is identifying the premises that support the conclusion and determining how they support it.
Step 5 – Go through the answer choices one by one, seeking to remove “strengtheners” and choices that do not affect the argument.
Usually in a Weaken question, there are only two reasonably “good” answer choices.
So, the next step in answering a Weaken question is to remove the easiest-to-eliminate choices.
💡MUST KNOW:
When evaluating the answer options in Weaken the Argument questions, it’s important to remove answer options that either strengthen the conclusion or have no impact on the conclusion.
Now – it’s important that we leave room for the fact that upon our initial analysis, we can sometimes eliminate the correct answer.
💡MUST KNOW:
Just as it is important to eliminate answers that we feel are incorrect, we must remain flexible, in that we may need to revisit answer options that we have eliminated.
Step 6 – Choose the answer choice with the clearest weakening impact.
Once you have eliminated the answer choices that either strengthen the argument or rather obviously do not affect the argument, in most case, you will be left with one or two choices.
If you find yourself left with two answer choices, both of which seem to have a deleterious effect on the support for conclusion, ask yourself, “Which one weakens the support for the conclusion the most clearly?”
There should be a recognizable difference between the effect on the argument of the correct answer and the effect of the second-best choice.
Look for subtle differences in logic.
The logic of the relationship between the second-best choice and the argument will be such that, even though the second-best choice may seem to weaken the argument, it does not really.
In contrast, generally there will be a tight logic to why, unlike any of the other answer choices, the correct choice answers the question.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering a Weaken question, once you are down to two final contenders for the correct answer, choose the one with the greatest and clearest negative impact on the support for the argument’s conclusion.
Summary of the Strategy for Weaken the Argument Questions
To summarize, here is the 6-step strategy that you can use to answer Weaken questions correctly:
💡MUST KNOW:
Memorize and practice the 6-step strategy for Weaken the Argument questions.
Let’s now apply the strategy to answering a Weaken the Argument question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 5 Three years ago, the town of Amity renewed two blocks on its waterfront by replacing abandoned warehouses with green, retail, and restaurant space, with the result that the town was able to collect substantial sales taxes from the new businesses. Which of the following, if true, would most seriously weaken the argument? 1. [ ] (A) If property tax rates in Amity were increased slightly, the resulting additional revenue would exceed the revenue currently collected in sales taxes from the renewal area businesses. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Most of the restaurants in the waterfront area are filled to capacity every weekend. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) The volume of restaurant and retail business decreases when the national economy slows down. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) In the nearby town of Friendship, neither retail business nor restaurant business has ever been very strong. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Since the completion of the first renewal project, it has become common for all streets leading to and in Amity’s waterfront area to be congested with slow moving traffic. View Solution Question Level: hard Step 1 – Carefully read and understand the passage.
We can see that the passage presents an argument and that the reasoning of the argument is the following.
Because the two-block waterfront renewal project done three years ago resulted in sales tax revenue and there are two more blocks available for renewal, the town can double sales tax revenue collected from waterfront renewal area businesses by renewing the other two blocks.
Step 2 – Read the question stem and identify what it is asking you to do.
We see that the question stem is “Which of the following, if true, would most seriously weaken the argument?”
So, this is a Weaken the Argument question, and our task is to undermine the support for or call into question the conclusion.
Step 3 – Go back to the passage and determine exactly what the conclusion of the argument is.
We can see that the statements in the passage lead up to and support the last statement in the passage:
Conclusion: The town of Amity can, by renewing the two available blocks, double the amount of sales tax revenue it will receive from businesses in waterfront renewal areas.
So, that last statement is the conclusion of the argument.
Notice that we are using the exact conclusion in its entirety, rather than a paraphrase or summary of the conclusion. Even leaving out a few words, such as “in waterfront renewal areas,” could result in our choosing an incorrect choice.
Step 4 – Identify how the conclusion is supported.
Going through the passage, we see that the conclusion is supported by the following premises:
Premise 1: Three years ago, the town of Amity renewed two blocks on its waterfront by replacing abandoned warehouses with green, retail, and restaurant space, with the result that the town was able to collect substantial sales taxes from the new businesses.
Premise 2: There are two more blocks available for renewal on the waterfront.
So, the author has seen what occurred when the first two blocks were renewed and concluded that, since there are two more blocks available for renewal, the town can repeat what happened the first time.
Thus, the argument is also supported by the following assumption:
Assumption: The author makes the unstated logical leap that the situation in Amity is as conducive to new business as it was when the first renewal project was completed.
In arriving at the conclusion, the author is assuming that what was true in the past will be true again. Is this a good assumption? Maybe. Maybe not. Such an assumption certainly is vulnerable to attack.
Step 5 – Remove choices that strengthen the argument or rather obviously have no effect on the argument.
Let’s examine each answer choice, remembering that we are looking to separate statements that either strengthen the argument or do not affect the argument from statements that seem to weaken the argument.
(A) If property tax rates in Amity were increased slightly, the resulting additional revenue would exceed the revenue currently collected in sales taxes from the renewal area businesses.
To see why this choice is not the correct answer, remember what the conclusion of the argument is:
Conclusion: The town of Amity can, by renewing the two available blocks, double the amount of sales tax revenue it will receive from businesses in waterfront renewal areas.
So, the conclusion of the argument is specifically that renewing the second set of two blocks will result in a doubling of sales tax revenue. The conclusion is not that some other plan would not generate more revenue.
So, this choice has absolutely no effect on the support for the conclusion. After all, regardless of how much revenue the town could generate by increasing property taxes, the fact that there are two more blocks in the waterfront area that could be renewed supports the conclusion that the town can double the amount of sales tax revenue it will receive from businesses in waterfront renewal areas.
Notice how important identifying the conclusion is. If we are not clear about what the conclusion is, we could easily choose this choice.
Since this choice focuses on another plan – not the plan mentioned – and thus does not affect the conclusion of the argument, we can put it aside with a high degree of confidence.
(B) Most of the restaurants in the waterfront area are filled to capacity every weekend.
This choice indicates that the restaurant business in the waterfront area is strong. Therefore, this choice supports the idea that repeating the renewal project will result in a doubling of business and of sales taxes. So, if anything, this choice is a strengthener rather than a weakener, and we can put it aside with a high degree of confidence.
(C) The volume of restaurant and retail business decreases when the national economy slows down.
This choice is interesting. If the national economy were to slow and the volume of restaurant and retail business therefore decrease, sales tax revenue would also decrease. So, this choice seems to have the potential to weaken an argument about increasing sales tax revenue.
Let’s keep this one.
(D) In the nearby town of Friendship, neither retail business nor restaurant business has ever been very strong.
At first this choice might seem to affect the argument by indicating something negative about prospects for new business.
Notice, however, that not only is this choice saying something about Friendship rather than Amity but also it states that the restaurant business in Friendship has “never been very good.”
Of course, if the restaurant business in Friendship has never been very good, then, even when the first waterfront renewal project was undertaken in Amity, the restaurant business in Friendship was not very good.
OK then, so, what this choice really means is that, even when the restaurant business in Friendship was not good, a renewal project in Amity worked.
Thus, this choice does not provide any reason to believe that a second renewal project would be any less successful than the first one, and therefore this choice does not affect the argument.
So, we can confidently eliminate this choice.
(E) Since the completion of the first renewal project, it has become common for all streets leading to and in Amity’s waterfront area to be congested with slow moving traffic.
What this choice discusses, streets congested with slow moving traffic, may not seem related to sales tax revenue at first glance. However, what this choice says seems to indicate that people get to the waterfront area by automobile and that their ability to do so has run up against a constraint, the capacity of the streets.
So, this choice indicates that the current situation is likely not as conducive to an increase in business as was the situation before the first renewal project was completed. In other words, this choice points out that times have changes since the renewal of the first two blocks on the waterfront.
This choice could be our answer.
Step 6 – Choose the answer choice that most clearly weakens the argument.
Choice (C) may seem to weaken the argument, as what it says seems to indicate that sales tax revenue could be negatively affected by economic factors.
However, let’s think about this.
If the second waterfront renewal were as financially successful as the first, in any economic environment, strong or weak, sales tax revenue would be approximately double what it would have been. So, even if the economy were to slow, it could be the case that, by renewing two more blocks, Amity can double the amount of sales tax revenue that it will receive, because the revenue could be double what it would have been in the same situation if the two blocks had not been renewed. So, what this choice says actually has no effect on the conclusion of the argument.
Choice (E), on the other hand, provides a clear reason to doubt the validity of the conclusion. How? It indicates that the author’s assumption was not a good one, by indicating that there is a difference between the situation preceding the prospective renewal of the second two blocks and the situation that preceded the renewal of the first two blocks.
Now, driving to the waterfront area has become difficult, and common sense tells us that it could be difficult to double the amount of business done in the waterfront renewal area if people would have trouble getting there.
Notice how clearly this choice weakens the argument by providing a clear reason for calling into question the idea that, by renewing the second two blocks, the town could double sales tax revenue from waterfront renewal area businesses.
Correct Answer: (E) Now that you understand the basic process of answering a Weaken question, let’s discuss in more depth some important strategies for weakening an argument.
Let’s discuss in more detail what a Weaken question asks you to do.
summary:
Lesson Content To find the correct answer to a Weaken question, follow this 6-step strategy.
Step 1 – Carefully read and understand the passage.
The foundation of arriving at the correct answer to a Weaken question is understanding the passage.
The importance of understanding the passage cannot be overemphasized.
So, your first job in answering a Weaken question is to carefully read the passage, developing understanding of what the passage says and noting key details of the passage.
title: 3.4 Strategy for Weaken the Argument Questions

contents:
Lesson Content When we hear the phrase “weaken the argument,” we may feel that our job is to destroy the argument.
In fact, much of the time, the correct answer to a Weaken question, especially a more difficult one, will not provide an earth-shattering attack.
Thus, we need to be adept at finding subtle ways to weaken an argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
Often, the correct answer to a Weaken the Argument question will not totally destroy the argument but instead weaken it in a subtle yet important way.
On legal television shows, lawyers talk about proving a case “beyond a reasonable doubt.” Think of Weaken the Argument questions in the following way: we don’t need to find an option that proves the conclusion false, just an option that provides reasonable doubt about the conclusion.
Casting even a small amount of doubt onto an argument’s conclusion is enough to weaken it.
💡MUST KNOW:
Casting even a small amount of doubt onto an argument’s conclusion is enough to weaken it.
Consider the following example:
Factory pollution of the water of Wattachen Lake has resulted in a reduction in the axolotl population that once thrived in the lake. Experts recommend that the factory be relocated in order to restore the ecological conditions in and surrounding Wattachen Lake. Once the factory is gone, they claim, the axolotl population will certainly return to its original pre-factory levels within two years.
Which of the following most seriously undermines the strength of the experts’ argument?
The experts’ conclusion is the following:
Conclusion: Once the factory is gone, the axolotl population will certainly return to its original pre-factory levels within two years.
What is one assumption made by the experts in arriving at that conclusion? As we know, any time someone presents a plan, he assumes that the plan will work.
For example, the experts assume that the pollution from the factory will not remain in the water after the factory has been relocated.
First, let’s look at an answer choice that clearly destroys the argument:
(Correct Answer) The factory pollutants in the water will begin to disintegrate only several years after removal of the source.
If it takes “several years” for removal of the factory to have an effect on levels of pollutants, then the relocation of the factory will have no effects within the first two years.
Ok – that correct answer pretty blatantly destroys the argument.
Here is a more difficult to analyze answer that also blatantly destroys the argument:
(Correct Answer) Once its habitat is threatened, the reproductive rate of the axolotl changes in a way such that each successive generation produces only half the number of offspring produced by the previous generation.
Using some soft math, we can deduce that the axolotl population as a whole will get smaller and smaller as the generations pass.
On the GMAT, we will rarely see such direct rebuttals of the argument.
The following answer choice provides a reason to doubt the experts’ conclusion without definitively ruling it out:
(Correct Answer) The population of tilapia, a natural predator of the axolotl, has been held in abeyance by the factory pollutants.
Now, we cannot say for sure whether the resurgence of the tilapia population that is suggested by this choice would impede growth of the axolotl population.
At the same time, we can see that there is reason to question whether, with the return of this predator, the axolotl population would quickly rebound.
Notice that this answer choice presents a malicious variable and, thus, weakens the argument in a subtle way.
So, we can see that, to weaken an argument, all we must do is cast a reasonable amount of doubt that the argument is sound.
💡MUST KNOW:
To weaken an argument, all we must do is cast a reasonable amount of doubt that the argument is sound.
Here is another answer choice that subtly casts doubt on the certainty of the resurgence of axolotls:
(Correct Answer) Genetic adaptations in the axolotls during the factory’s presence have rendered them and subsequent generations unable to subsist on their pre-factory food source.
This statement tells us that the axolotls have changed recently and can no longer eat what they used to eat.
Notice, again, that this statement does not clearly destroy the argument.
Consider the following examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 6 Last week, the Silver Bullet Tea Company, which recently opened a chain of high-end tea shops in the affluent City of Silverton, began a marketing campaign, which involved television, newspaper, and internet marketing, aimed at letting residents of Silverton know that its tea shops are now open for business. Which of the following, if true, most weakens the argument above? 1. [ ] (A) In the City of Silverton, the law permits tea shops to operate only if they do not violate city ordinances. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) The Silver Bullet Tea Company spent less than half of the budget that it had originally allocated for the public marketing campaign in the City of Silverton. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Most residents of the Town of Silverton would be willing to purchase the expensive tea sold at a high-end tea shop. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Because they are into active, outdoor activities, most of the residents of Silverton rarely watch television, read the newspaper, or use the internet. 5. [ ] (E) When asked in a city-sponsored survey what the City of Silverton needs most, many residents responded that it needs more high-end tea and coffee shops. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Last week, the Silver Bullet Tea Company, which recently opened a chain of high-end tea shops in the affluent City of Silverton, began a public marketing campaign, which involved television, newspaper, and internet marketing, aimed at letting residents of Silverton know that its tea shops are now open for business.
Conclusion: Its tea shops will soon be filled with residents of Silverton purchasing tea.
The Silver Bullet Tea Company has a marketing plan. Any time someone presents a plan, he assumes that the plan will work. In other words, he assumes that there are not variables that will enter into the equation that will spoil the plan.
In the TTP Course, we refer to such variables as “malicious variables.” When an author presents a plan, he assumes that there are no malicious variables.
What malicious variables might the author have missed? What things might the author have overlooked that would spoil the plan?
(A) In the City of Silverton, the law permits tea shops to operate only if they do not violate city ordinances. If you choose this choice as correct, you likely created an unsupported story involving the Silver Bullet Tea Company’s violating city ordinances.
Notice, however, that we do not have any evidence that the Silver Bullet Tea Company has violated or will violate any ordinances.
Since what this choice says is not itself a reason to question the conclusion that Silver Bullet’s tea shops will be filled with residents of Silverton purchasing tea, this choice does not weaken the argument.
We can’t make a choice into a weakener by making up an unsupported story.
(B) The Silver Bullet Tea Company spent less than half of the budget that it had originally allocated for the public marketing campaign in the City of Silverton. This choice is a pseudo-weakener that may seem to affect the support for the conclusion but doesn’t really.
If you chose this answer choice as correct, you likely assumed that, if the Silver Bullet Tea Company spent less than half of the budget that it had originally allocated for the public marketing campaign in the City of Silverton, then it must not have spent enough money, and thus the campaign will not work as planned.
Be careful not to do that. The Silver Bullet Tea Company’s marketing budget could be ginormous, and half of it could still be enormous. In other words, we really don’t know what “less than half” means, and so, this choice’s effect on the support for the conclusion is not clear.
Watch out for pseudo-weakeners that say things that seem to make a difference but don’t clearly weaken the support for the conclusion.
(C) Most residents of the Town of Silverton would be willing to purchase the expensive tea sold at a high-end tea shop. What this answer choice says strengthens the argument.
After all, the information that most residents of the Town of Silverton would be willing to purchase the expensive tea sold at a high-end tea shop makes us more confident that soon the Silver Bullet Tea Company’s tea shops will be filled with residents of Silverton purchasing tea.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Because they are into active, outdoor activities, most of the residents of Silverton rarely watch television, read the newspaper, or use the internet. Notice that what this answer choice says brings up a reason why the plan might not work.
After all, if, because they are into active, outdoor activities, most of the residents of Silverton rarely watch television, read the newspaper, or use the Internet, then most of the potential customers of the Silver Bullet Tea Company might never know the tea shops are open for business, because they would not be aware of the marketing campaign, which involves television, newspaper, and internet marketing.
Notice that this choice does not completely destroy the argument. The fact that most of the residents of Silverton rarely watch television, read the newspaper, or use the internet does not prove without a doubt that the marketing campaign won’t work.
At the same time, the fact that most of the residents of Silverton rarely watch television, read the newspaper, or use the internet is certainly a reason to question the conclusion that, as a result of the marketing campaign, Silver Bullet’s shops will soon be filled with residents of Silverton.
Notice that this answer choice presents us with a malicious variable. Thus, this choice weakens the argument.
(E) When asked in a city-sponsored survey what the City of Silverton needs most, many residents responded that it needs more high-end tea and coffee shops. Notice that what this answer choice says strengthens the argument.
After all, if when asked in a city-sponsored survey what the City of Silverton needs most, many residents responded that it needs more high-end tea and coffee shops, then we have more faith that the Silver Bullet Tea Company’s tea shops will soon be filled with residents of Silverton purchasing tea.
Of course, we’re not seeking a strengthener; we’re seeking a weakener.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 7 Blades of Glory skating rink, one of three skating rinks in Greenville, draws revenue from admissions and from food and drink concessions during its day and evening open-skate sessions. Which of the following, if true, most seriously calls into question the soundness of management’s plan? 1. [ ] (A) At some skating rinks, daytime food sales are a source of significant profits. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) One hungry member of a group of skaters can influence the group’s decision regarding which rink to go to. 3. [ ] (C) Sometimes when people go to the rink’s concessions stand to buy drinks, they decide to buy food as well. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Many of the people who skate at Blades of Glory go during evening sessions on some days and during daytime sessions on others. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Most people who would have bought both food and drinks at Blades of Glory will still buy drinks even if food is not available. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium Management’s decision is based on the following line of reasoning:
Premise: Daytime food sales not profitable.
Assumption: There is not some way in which offering food in the daytime is helping overall profitability.
Conclusion: Increase overall profitability by ceasing to offer daytime food.
Notice the important assumption in the argument: offering food during the daytime is not somehow helping overall profitability. After all, if the rink were somehow making more profit overall in the daytime because of offering food, even if food sales themselves were not profitable, it would no longer make sense to believe that ceasing to offer food during the day would increase overall profitability.
Remember, since the stated goal of the rink is to increase profitability, any considerations that are not somehow connected to profitability will not affect the conclusion. The correct answer will be somehow related to profitability.
At the same time, notice that the correct answer does not have to destroy the conclusion. It has only to provide a good reason to question the conclusion. Be on the lookout for subtle ways to weaken the argument.
(A) At some skating rinks, daytime food sales are a source of significant profits. Notice that the argument already states as fact that, at Blades of Glory skating rink, daytime food sales are not profitable. The fact that daytime food sales at other rinks are profitable does nothing to change the fact that daytime food sales are not profitable at Blades of Glory.
So, this answer choice does not affect the conclusion, which is about how Blades of Glory skating rink’s overall profitability will be affected by the rink’s ceasing to offer food in the daytime.
In general, we must remember that the premises of GMAT Critical Reasoning arguments are always considered factual. So, an answer choice that says something that appears to weaken a premise will never be correct.
YOUR CHOICE(B) One hungry member of a group of skaters can influence the group’s decision regarding which rink to go to. This choice offers a clear reason why ceasing to offer food during the day in order to increase profitability may not be a sound plan. If one hungry member of a group can influence where the entire group goes to skate, then offering food at Blades of Glory skating rink could influence entire groups of skaters to skate at Blades of Glory, and ceasing to offer food could result in their going to other rinks that offer food.
So, this choice casts doubt on the conclusion that ceasing to offer food will result in increased profitability.
Note that, while this choice does not destroy the argument, it certainly casts some doubt on the conclusion. This choice brings up factor that management may have overlooked and, thus, exposes a gap in the chain of reasoning. Notice that this answer choice directly (although subtly) attacks an assumption made by the author. Notice also that this answer choice presents us with a malicious variable.
(C) Sometimes when people go to the rink’s concessions stand to buy drinks, they decide to buy food as well. Regardless of what this choice says, we already know from the argument that daytime food sales are not profitable. Remember, we can’t weaken a GMAT argument by attacking the premises. This choice may seem to attack the premise that daytime food sales are not profitable, but it doesn’t affect the validity of that premise.
(D) Many of the people who skate at Blades of Glory go during evening sessions on some days and during daytime sessions on others. Regardless of what this choice says, we already know from the argument that daytime food sales are not profitable.
Remember, we can’t weaken a GMAT argument by attacking the premises. In other words, the fact that some of the same skaters who attend the evening session also attend the daytime sessions does not change the fact that most skaters do not buy food during the day.
Thus, this choice has no clear impact on the support for the conclusion that ceasing to offer food during the day will increase profitability.
(E) Most people who would have bought both food and drinks at Blades of Glory will still buy drinks even if food is not available. If anything, this option strengthens, rather than weakens, the management’s argument. By indicating that most people who would have bought food and drinks will still buy drinks even if food is not offered, it eliminates a potential weakener that the sales of drinks may decline if food is not offered.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss new information in Weaken the Argument questions.
summary:
Lesson Content When we hear the phrase “weaken the argument,” we may feel that our job is to destroy the argument.
In fact, much of the time, the correct answer to a Weaken question, especially a more difficult one, will not provide an earth-shattering attack.
Thus, we need to be adept at finding subtle ways to weaken an argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
Often, the correct answer to a Weaken the Argument question will not totally destroy the argument but instead weaken it in a subtle yet important way.
title: 3.5 Weaken Does Not Mean Destroy.

contents:
Lesson Content Students often get the impression that, in answering Weaken the Argument questions, they should eliminate any answer choice that seems to bring up a new topic.
The truth is, however, that all correct answers to Weaken the Argument questions will bring up new information, and it’s important to understand that information that does not resemble the information that makes up the argument is not the same as irrelevant information.
So, for sure don’t discount an answer choice simply because it brings up a new topic.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering a Weaken the Argument Question, do not discount an answer choice just because it brings up a new topic.
Test-takers using that strategy often eliminate the correct answer first! The reason people often eliminate the correct answer first is that the correct answer to a Weaken question is often the one that looks the most different from the information that makes up the argument.
Therefore, our job is clear; our only job is to find the answer choice that most seriously weakens the argument provided in the question, regardless of whether that answer resembles the information that makes up the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering a Weaken the Argument question, our only job is to find the answer choice that most seriously weakens the argument provided in the question, regardless of whether that answer resembles the information that makes up the argument.
Let’s practice noticing how new information can weaken an argument.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 8 The island province of Mar de Isla will begin offering a 5 percent discount on the tax its residents must pay yearly on any real estate they own. Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the above argument? 1. [ ] (A) Because most residents of the island know one another well, they are very willing to help each other out when needed. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) The rate of the tax residents of Mar de Isla must pay on real estate they own is slightly lower than that of similar taxes paid by residents of most nearby islands. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Over time, traditional passing of property from generation to generation has put almost all Mar de Isla real estate into the hands of less than 20 percent of its residents. 4. [ ] (D) Many residents of Mar de Isla could easily find 100 hours to volunteer over the course of a year. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Some Mar de Isla residents already donate some of their time to community endeavors, such as trash collection, dog walking, and drug counseling. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy Background:The island province of Mar de Isla will begin offering a 5 percent discount on the tax its residents must pay yearly on any real estate they own.
Background: To qualify, a resident seeking the discount must provide documentation indicating that he or she has completed at least 100 hours of community service during the tax year for which the discount is requested.
Premise: A 5 percent tax savings will be significant to Mar de Isla residents.
Conclusion: Most of the residents will choose to participate in the program.
The author has presented a plan. Anytime an author presents a plan, she assumes that the plan will work. In other words, she assumes that there are not variables that will enter into the equation that will spoil the plan. In the TTP Course, we refer to such variables as “malicious variables.” When an author presents a plan, she assumes that there are no malicious variables.
What might the author have failed to consider? What malicious variables might have gone unnoticed?
(A) Because most residents of the island know one another well, they are very willing to help each other out when needed. What this choice says is in line with what the argument says.
After all, if it is true that most residents of the island know one another well, so they are very willing to help each other out when needed, then we have more conviction that most of the residents will choose to participate in the program, performing at least 100 hours of community service.
Of course, we’re not seeking a choice that is in line with the argument’s conclusion. We’re seeking one that weakens the argument.
(B) The rate of the tax residents of Mar de Isla must pay on real estate they own is slightly lower than that of similar taxes paid by residents of most nearby islands. The passage states as fact that a 5 percent tax savings will be significant to Mar de Isla residents. So, that premise must be true even though the Mar de Isla tax rate is lower than tax rates of most nearby islands. Thus, the plan proposed by the argument could work.
After all, whatever the tax rate is, if a 5 percent savings is significant for residents, then most may very well be willing to do 100 hours of community service to get that 5 percent savings.
What this answer choice says does not weaken the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Over time, traditional passing of property from generation to generation has put almost all Mar de Isla real estate into the hands of less than 20 percent of its residents. The conclusion of the argument is that most of the residents will choose to participate in the program. This conclusion is based on the fact that Mar de Isla will begin offering a 5 percent discount on the tax its residents must pay yearly on any real estate they own.
Of course, this 5 percent discount will be an incentive to complete 100 hours of community service only to people who own real estate. People who don’t own real estate do not have to pay any real estate tax. So, the discount would not affect them.
Now, if what this answer choice says is true, and traditional passing of property from generation to generation has put almost all Mar de Isla real estate into the hands of less than 20 percent of its residents, then MOST of the residents (more than 80 percent) do not own real estate.
Therefore, the 5 percent discount on the real estate tax would be an incentive to less than 20 percent of the residents, and less than 20 percent of the residents is clearly not “most of the residents.”
Given what this choice says, the fact that Mar de Isla will begin offering a 5 percent discount on the real estate tax to residents who complete at least 100 hours of community service gives us very little reason to believe that “most of the residents will choose to participate in the program,” as the conclusion states.
The information in this answer choice clearly weakens the argument.
Notice that what this answer choice says about traditional passing of property from generation to generation is new information that is quite different from any information presented in the passage. That’s OK. Correct answers that present new information are normal in Weaken the Argument questions.
(D) Many residents of Mar de Isla could easily find 100 hours to volunteer over the course of a year. What this choice says is in line with what the argument says, and perhaps even slightly strengthens the argument.
After all, if it is true that many residents of Mar de Isla could easily find 100 hours to volunteer over the course of a year, then we have more conviction that the plan will work.
Imagine if the residents could NOT easily find 100 hours to volunteer over the course of a year. Wouldn’t that be a serious impediment to the success of the plan? So, if what this answer choice says is true, and residents CAN easily find the time to volunteer, then this information provides some support for the argument’s conclusion.
Of course, we’re not seeking a choice that is in line with the argument’s conclusion. We’re seeking one that weakens the argument.
(E) Some Mar de Isla residents already donate some of their time to community endeavors, such as trash collection, dog walking, and drug counseling. We can’t be sure exactly how what this choice says affects the argument.
Does the fact that some Mar de Isla residents already donate some of their time to community endeavors indicate that these people are willing to donate their time and make us more convinced that the plan will work, or does it indicate that people are already doing all the volunteering they can handle and won’t be willing to do more? We just don’t know, right?
Be careful to not choose as correct an answer choice that could be interpreted in multiple ways when what you’re looking for is an answer that weakens the argument.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 9 Administrators at City Hospital are concerned that their doctors have less time than ever to work on patient charts, claiming that the doctors devote more shift hours to seeing patients than they did two years ago. Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument? 1. [ ] (A) Two years ago, City Hospital implemented an electronic charts system that significantly improved the efficiency and effectiveness of the system used for recording patient information. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) City Hospital recently implemented a program that, using video diagnosis, outsources some aspects of handling certain patients’ cases to a satellite clinic. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) A hospital in a nearby county specializing in complex cases closed its doors two years ago, referring any future patients experiencing such situations to City Hospital. 4. [ ] (D) The hiring process at City Hospital has become notably more rigorous in the past two years. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) City Hospital has long experienced difficulty in matching each patient case with the doctor likely to handle that case most efficiently. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard Premise 1: The number of patients has not changed.
Premise 2: Doctors’ shift-lengths have not changed.
Premise 3: The number of doctors on shift at any given time has not changed.
Conclusion: This claim (that doctors devote more shift hours to seeing patients) is likely unwarranted.
What is the assumption being made here? The assumption is that there are no variables other than the ones mentioned that can affect how much time doctors can spend with their patients. Is this a good assumption? Probably not, right?
(A) Two years ago, City Hospital implemented an electronic charts system that significantly improved the efficiency and effectiveness of the system used for recording patient information. This introduces a topic that relates to something mentioned in the prompt: patient charts.
However, this choice is about the efficiency of and effectiveness of the system for recording patient information, while the argument is specifically about how much time doctors spend with patients.
Since the fact that there have been improvements to the chart system does not mean that doctors actually do spend more time with patients than they spent two years ago, this choice does not weaken the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(B) City Hospital recently implemented a program that, using video diagnosis, outsources some aspects of handling certain patients’ cases to a satellite clinic. By saying that some aspects of handling patients’ cases have been outsourced, this choice implies that the doctors have been relieved of some work and should be spending less time with patients, not more.
So, this choice is in line with what the argument says and, therefore, is not a weakener.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) A hospital in a nearby county specializing in complex cases closed its doors two years ago, referring any future patients experiencing such situations to City Hospital. This choice discusses a new topic and provides new information that weakens the argument.
Notice that, while this choice does not directly discuss patients, doctors, or shift length at City Hospital, what it says implies that, since the other hospital closed, City Hospital has been handling more cases that are complex.
Common sense tells us that complex cases could be particularly time consuming for the doctors at City Hospital. So, this choice provides reason to believe that it’s likely that the time spent per patient at City Hospital has increased.
Thus, the new information provided by this choice serves to cast doubt on the conclusion that City Hospital doctors are not spending more time with patients.
Another important thing to see is the shift in detail in the passage. Notice that the passage says that the administrators claim that “doctors devote more shift hours to seeing patients than they did two years ago.” However, the premise says, “The number of patients, the length of each shift, and the number of doctors on shift at City Hospital at any given time have not changed.”
Notice how the correct answer weakens the support for the conclusion by indicating that, even though what the premise says is true, it’s likely that the doctors do spend more shift hours seeing patients.
(D) The hiring process at City Hospital has become notably more rigorous in the past two years. This choice introduces a new topic, the hiring process.
So, we have to assess whether what this choice says affects the argument.
That over the past two years the hospital has been using a more rigorous hiring process suggests that the hospital has hired better doctors, but it is unclear whether a “better” doctor would spend more or less time with a patient than a less skilled doctor would.
Therefore, there is no clear connection between a rigorous hiring process and the number of hours spent on each patient, and so this choice has no clear effect on the argument.
(E) City Hospital has long experienced difficulty in matching each patient case with the doctor likely to handle that case most efficiently. This choice may appear, at first glance, to fall into our “weakener” category; inefficiency could cause doctors to spend more hours with patients.
Notice, however, that the argument is about a change that occurred two years ago. This choice, on the other hand, indicates that the inefficiency has been an ongoing problem. So, this choice, “City Hospital has long experienced…,” does not affect the argument.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss common ways in which correct answers to weaken the argument questions weaken arguments.
summary:
Lesson Content Students often get the impression that, in answering Weaken the Argument questions, they should eliminate any answer choice that seems to bring up a new topic.
The truth is, however, that all correct answers to Weaken the Argument questions will bring up new information, and it’s important to understand that information that does not resemble the information that makes up the argument is not the same as irrelevant information.
title: 3.6 New Topics in Weaken Question Answer Choices

contents:
Lesson Content The ways in which correct answers weaken arguments are predictable.
As we go through these answer types, there are, at least, three things that you can accomplish.
• You can learn about the types of answers discussed, any of which could easily show up in Critical Reasoning questions on the GMAT.
• You can note the logic that underlies each answer type, as that logic could show up in Weaken questions or in questions of other types.
• You can note how logically and convincingly the correct answers to Weaken questions generally weaken the arguments.

As we go through these answer types, there are, at least, three things that you can accomplish.

title: 3.7 Common Ways in which Correct Answers to Weaken the Argument Questions Weaken Arguments

contents:
Lesson Content One way in which authors of arguments fail to consider other possibilities is by relying on false dichotomies in coming to conclusions.
The term “false dichotomy” may sound complex, but what it means is actually rather simple.
💡MUST KNOW:
A false dichotomy occurs when an argument assumes that, just because one conclusion is impossible, another conclusion must be true. In other words, a false dichotomy occurs when an author incorrectly limits the number of possible conclusions to two.
In making a false dichotomy, the author assumes that there could not be other possibilities.
💡MUST KNOW:
In making a false dichotomy, the author assumes that there could not be other possibilities. We can weaken such an argument by showing that that assumption is a poor one; we can weaken the argument by showing that there are, in fact, other possibilities.
For example, imagine if someone were to say, “Since you are hungry but don’t want to eat a hamburger for dinner, you must be a diehard vegan.” Would this person’s conclusion be valid? Do you necessarily have to be a diehard vegan to not want to eat a hamburger for dinner? Certainly not – you could be a carnivore who just doesn’t feel like eating hamburgers now, or you could have an aversion to ground meat.
The secret to handling an argument that is attempting to pull us into a false dichotomy is to be careful not to get stuck in an either/or mindset and to be cognizant of the other possibilities that exist.
💡MUST KNOW:
Don’t get caught in an either/or mindset. If the conclusion in a weaken question is based on a choice between two alternatives, the correct answer to the question could weaken the argument by indicating that a third alternative exists.
For example:
Inspectors have informed the high school that three of the school’s buses need repairs that will take a week to complete. The school has just enough buses to cover all the routes via which students are picked up and brought to school. Since there is no practical way to temporarily change the routes so that they can be covered by a reduced number of buses, parents of students on three of the routes will have to drive those students to school for a week.
Which of the following, if true, weakens the argument?
(Correct Answer) Public buses on which high school students could safely travel run on routes almost exactly the same as the routes taken by some of the school’s buses.
The argument concludes that, because one method of transporting students with a reduced number of school buses – changing the routes – is not available, it will be necessary to use one particular alternative method – parents drive the students – to transport students to the school.
Notice that the correct answer attacks the author’s assumption that there are no other ways to get the students to school.
Here’s an example of how a false dichotomy can work in a Weaken question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 10 In 1932, the painting La Pose Enchantée, which had been completed only five years before by painter René Magritte, disappeared. Which of the following, if true, casts the most doubt on the above conclusion? 1. [x] (A) Examination of existing Magritte paintings has indicated that Magritte painted over some of his own paintings in the process of creating new paintings. 2. [ ] (B) Public awareness of the destruction of a well-known painting by a gallery’s personnel could do significant damage to that gallery’s reputation. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) La Pose Enchantée is similar in some aspects to certain works of Picasso, an artist who was a contemporary of Magritte’s. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) There are no currently available records that clearly indicate the exact location where La Pose Enchantée was last seen. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Although La Pose Enchantée had been shown at multiple galleries, the painting had not yet been purchased from Magritte at the time of its disappearance. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium The conclusion of this argument, that La Pose Enchantée was inadvertently destroyed by gallery personnel, is based on a false dichotomy. The author arrives at this conclusion by simply eliminating another alternative, the painting’s being found.
Of course, the truth is that, in making the argument, the author has assumed that there are no possibilities other than the painting’s having been found or its having been destroyed by gallery personnel. In making a false dichotomy, the author assumes that there could not be other possibilities.
We can weaken the argument by showing that there are, in fact, other possibilities; we can weaken the argument by showing that her assumption was a poor one.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Examination of existing Magritte paintings has indicated that Magritte painted over some of his own paintings in the process of creating new paintings. By saying that Magritte painted over some of his own paintings, this choice indicates that it could very well be that La Pose Enchantée disappeared because Magritte painted over La Pose Enchantée. By offering this third possibility, this choice weakens the argument, which is based on the idea that there are only two things that could have happened to the painting, its having been found or its having been destroyed by gallery personnel.
Notice that this answer choice weakens the argument by showing that the author’s assumption that there are only two possibilities is a poor one.
(B) Public awareness of the destruction of a well-known painting by a gallery’s personnel could do significant damage to that gallery’s reputation. This choice could be tempting, because it indicates that galleries at which La Pose Enchantée was displayed would have had a significant reason to make sure that it was not destroyed. However, the fact that galleries had a reason to make sure that the painting was not destroyed does not clearly indicate that the painting was not in fact destroyed at a gallery. It could be that La Pose Enchantée was destroyed at a gallery that subsequently hid the destruction of the painting from the public.
(C) La Pose Enchantée is similar in some aspects to certain works of Picasso, an artist who was a contemporary of Magritte’s. While a creative test-taker might find a way to spin this choice into a reason to believe that La Pose Enchantée was not destroyed, the truth is that there is no clear reason to believe that, since the painting resembled another artist’s work, it was not destroyed.
So, this choice does not cast any doubt whatsoever on the conclusion that the painting was destroyed.
(D) There are no currently available records that clearly indicate the exact location where La Pose Enchantée was last seen. The fact that the last location at which La Pose Enchantée was seen is not known does not indicate that the painting was not destroyed at that location. If the argument were that La Pose Enchantée was destroyed at a specific gallery, this choice might undermine support for the conclusion, but that conclusion is not the one that we are seeking to call into question.
(E) Although La Pose Enchantée had been shown at multiple galleries, the painting had not yet been purchased from Magritte at the time of its disappearance. This choice tends to eliminate one explanation for the disappearance of La Pose Enchantée, that it was purchased and taken to an unknown location by the buyer. In eliminating an alternative explanation for the disappearance, this choice slightly strengthens, rather than weakens, the support for the argument’s conclusion.
Correct Answer: (A) Since we have been discussing conclusions that are weakened by answers indicating that authors didn’t consider all the possibilities, let’s continue by discussing cause-and-effect arguments, which also can be flawed because their authors failed to consider possibilities.
lets talk about weakening answer type 2: an answer that calls into question a cause-and-effect conclusion .
summary:
Lesson Content One way in which authors of arguments fail to consider other possibilities is by relying on false dichotomies in coming to conclusions.
The term “false dichotomy” may sound complex, but what it means is actually rather simple.
💡MUST KNOW:
A false dichotomy occurs when an argument assumes that, just because one conclusion is impossible, another conclusion must be true. In other words, a false dichotomy occurs when an author incorrectly limits the number of possible conclusions to two.
title: 3.7.1 Weakening Answer Type 1: Exposes a False Dichotomy by Offering a Third Alternative

contents:
Lesson Content We’ve learned a bit about cause-and-effect arguments in the Assumptions chapter.
We must be careful whenever a speaker makes a cause-and-effect argument, because determining whether one thing actually causes another can be difficult.
💡MUST KNOW:
We must be careful whenever a speaker makes a cause-and-effect argument, because determining whether one thing actually causes another can be very difficult.
In fact, making such determinations is so difficult that, for decades, courts of law did not acknowledge that cigarette smoking causes lung cancer.
Cigarette companies argued that people who smoke were likely to also drink alcohol, eat poor diets, get insufficient exercise, and generally live stressful lives, all of which are known to cause lung cancer.
There are a number of unique, but related ways, in which cause and effect can appear in Critical Reasoning questions.
When an author confuses correlation with causation, that author claims that the fact that two events happen simultaneously or sequentially indicates that they must have a causal relationship.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an author confuses correlation with causation, that author claims that, because two events happen simultaneously or sequentially, they must have a causal relationship.
That is, the author assumes that one event must have caused the other event to occur.
💡MUST KNOW:
It’s important to remember that the fact that two events happen together does not mean that one of the events causes the other event.
Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she makes the assumption that her cause-and-effect claim is correct and that there is not an alternative explanation for the pattern that she seeks to explain.
💡MUST KNOW:
Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she assumes that her cause-and-effect claim is correct and that there is not an alternative explanation for the pattern that she seeks to explain.
Often, we’ll see that this assumption is a bad one.
💡MUST KNOW:
One way to weaken an argument based on cause-and-effect reasoning is to show that there is an alternative explanation for the observation made by the author.
To weaken such reasoning, we can provide the following types of alternate reasons for the existence of correlation:
For example, let’s say a study found that
People who eat an avocado for breakfast every day tend to be leaner than those who do not.
There are various ways in which this correlation can exist.
Eating an avocado for breakfast every day made these people leaner.
Alternatively, the correlation could exist in any of the three ways that we discussed above:
In a Weaken question, the correct answer can weaken the argument by providing reason to believe that a cause-and-effect relationship that an argument concludes exists does not actually exist.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Weaken the Argument questions, cause-and-effect arguments can be weakened by answer choices that indicate that the relationship that the argument concludes exists does not actually exist.
Consider the following examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 11 A recent study compared data on the overall health and lifespans of people who abstain from drinking alcoholic beverages with the overall health and lifespans of people who drink moderately. Which of the following, if true, most clearly calls into question the validity of the above conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) What constitutes consuming alcohol in moderation is not defined in exactly the same way by every researcher. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Many people who abstain from consuming alcohol do so because they are not healthy. 3. [ ] (C) The alcohol content of different types of alcoholic beverages varies widely. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) The people who consumed alcoholic beverages moderately did not do so in order to experience health benefits. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) The study was designed so that the effects of other lifestyle related variables, such as diet, did not distort the results. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy Here’s a breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Results of a study showed that, on average, people who drink alcohol moderately are in better health and live longer than people who abstain from drinking.
Conclusion: Consuming alcohol in moderation is beneficial for health.
A key detail to notice is that the argument mentions people who “regularly drink moderate amounts of alcohol.” So, people who consume large amounts of alcohol or people who rarely consume alcohol are not the subjects of the argument.
Notice that the author uses cause-and-effect reasoning in his argument. Any time an author makes an argument that rests on cause-and-effect reasoning, we must be skeptical of the cause-and-effect claim as well as the soundness of the argument. Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, he makes the assumption that his cause-and-effect claim is correct and that there is not an alternative explanation for the pattern that he seeks to explain. Often, we’ll see that this assumption is a bad one.
The argument posits that regular consumption of moderate amounts of alcohol is the cause of better health and longer lives. What is the author assuming? Is there more to the story?
(A) What constitutes consuming alcohol in moderation is not defined in exactly the same way by every researcher. The fact that different researchers define consuming alcohol in moderation in different ways does not clearly weaken the argument, because the fact that different researchers have different definitions of consuming alcohol in moderation does not give us a clear reason to doubt that the people studied were consuming alcohol moderately.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Many people who abstain from consuming alcohol do so because they are not healthy. What this answer says suggests that the reason people who drink alcohol moderately are generally healthier than people who do not is that people who are already unhealthy tend to refrain from drinking alcohol.
By providing reason to believe that people who consume alcohol in moderation are not healthy because of their alcohol consumption but, rather, consume alcohol because they are healthy, this choice indicates that the relationship between moderate alcohol consumption and better health is the reverse of the one that the argument concludes exists.
Thus, this choice weakens the argument.
Notice that, in general, what this answer choice shows is that the author’s assumption that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observation she cites is a poor one.
(C) The alcohol content of different types of alcoholic beverages varies widely. While a test-taker might be tempted to construe this choice as weakening the argument by somehow calling into question whether the people studied were actually consuming alcohol in moderation, what this choice says gives us no clear reason to believe other than that they were consuming alcohol moderately. In other words, in order for what this choice says to affect the argument, we would need additional information clearly connecting the varying amounts of alcohol in beverages to a problem in the study, such as that the researchers did not correctly account for the amount of alcohol in beverages consumed by people.
(D) The people who consumed alcoholic beverages moderately did not do so in order to experience health benefits. While this choice mentions things involved in the argument, drinking moderately and health, it does not affect the argument, because even if those consuming alcohol did not do so in order to gain health benefits, consuming alcohol could still have health benefits.
(E) The study was designed so that the effects of other lifestyle related variables, such as diet, did not distort the results. This choice supports the idea that the results of the study are not distorted by confounding factors Therefore, what it says strengthens rather than weakens the argument.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 12 Health Guru: For two years following their reaching the summit of Mount Everest, people who have safely returned from the summit of Mount Everest have an incidence of heart attacks lower than the incidence of heart attacks in people in general. Which of the following, if true, most calls into question the health guru's conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) There is little difference between the incidence of heart attacks in people who have viewed Mount Everest from airplanes and the incidence of heart attacks in other people. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) The incidence of heart attacks in people who regularly climb indoor climbing walls is lower than the incidence of heart attacks in people who do not. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) It is generally the case that only people who have achieved a high level of fitness succeed in climbing to the summit of Mount Everest. 4. [ ] (D) Climbing to the summit of Mount Everest costs much more than it costs to join a local gym for five years. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Many people who have experienced heart attacks could have prevented them by reducing their body weights or quitting smoking. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium The health guru concludes the following:
Conclusion: One can decrease one’s chances of experiencing a heart attack by climbing to the summit of Mount Everest every two years.
The conclusion is based on the correlation between people’s having climbed to the summit of Mount Everest and safely returned and those people’s having a lower-than-normal incidence of heart attacks over the two years following their climbs.
Notice that the author uses cause-and-effect reasoning in her argument. Any time an author makes an argument that rests on cause-and-effect reasoning, we must be skeptical of the cause-and-effect claim as well as the soundness of the argument. Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she makes the assumption that her cause-and-effect claim is correct and that there is not an alternative explanation for the pattern that she seeks to explain. Often, we’ll see that this assumption is a bad one.
Is the true cause of the lower incidence of heart attacks the people’s climbing to the summit of Mount Everest? Or could there be another cause?
(A) There is little difference between the incidence of heart attacks in people who have viewed Mount Everest from airplanes and the incidence of heart attacks in other people. While this choice mentions Mount Everest, it doesn’t weaken the argument, as viewing Mount Everest from an airplane is an activity significantly different from climbing Mount Everest. So, the fact that viewing Mount Everest from an airplane does not seem to prevent heart attacks does not affect the argument.
(B) The incidence of heart attacks in people who regularly climb indoor climbing walls is lower than the incidence of heart attacks in people who do not. This choice could be tempting because it discusses something that is somewhat similar to climbing Mount Everest, climbing indoor climbing walls.
However, this choice is not a weakener, as it shows that, not only is there a correlation between climbing Mount Everest and a lower incidence of heart attacks, but also there is a correlation between climbing indoor climbing walls and a lower incidence of heart attacks.
Bringing up another pattern similar to the one that supports the argument certainly doesn’t weaken the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(C) It is generally the case that only people who have achieved a high level of fitness succeed in climbing to the summit of Mount Everest. This choice indicates that people who climb to the summit of Mount Everest are generally fitter than the average person. It is common knowledge that fitter people are generally less likely to experience heart attacks. So, given what this choice says, it could be the case that climbing to the summit of Mount Everest does not prevent heart attacks, but rather, that the people who climb to the summit of Mount Everest are less likely to have heart attacks because they have achieved high levels of fitness.
Thus, this choice weakens the argument by showing that there is a third factor, high levels of fitness, that underlies both people’s reaching the summit of Mount Everest and those same people’s having a relatively low incidence of heart attacks.
Notice that, in general, what this answer choice shows is that the author’s assumption that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observation she cites is a poor one.
(D) Climbing to the summit of Mount Everest costs much more than it costs to join a local gym for five years. This answer could be tempting, as it indicates that preventing heart attacks by simply joining a gym may be better than preventing them by climbing Mount Everest, because joining a gym costs much less.
However, the conclusion is not that climbing Mount Everest is the best way to prevent heart attacks, but rather that it does prevent heart attacks. So, this choice does not affect the conclusion.
In general, unless an author claims that his plan is the only plan or the best plan, he need not assume that there are no other plans that could work or assume that there is not a better plan than his. Therefore, the mention of a different plan or a better plan will not weaken the argument.
(E) Many people who have experienced heart attacks could have prevented them by reducing their body weights or quitting smoking. The fact that there are various ways in which people can prevent heart attacks does not affect the conclusion that people can prevent heart attacks by climbing Mount Everest.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 13 Relationship Advisor: When people ask me about causes of divorce, I point to statistics, which show that couples who live together before getting married are more likely to get divorced than are those who do not live together before marriage. Which of the following, if true, is most damaging to the relationship advisor’s conclusion? 1. [x] (A) In comparison with more conservative minded people, more liberal minded people tend to be both more open to living together before marriage and more open to getting divorced. 2. [ ] (B) In some countries, the divorce rates have steadily decreased over the past 30 years. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Couples who live together before getting married have an opportunity to learn to work together as a team before they get married. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) For preventing divorce, there is no substitute for having good communication skills. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) For many couples who live together before getting married, marriage does not immediately lead to significant changes in their lifestyles. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium First, let’s break down the argument:
Premise: Couples who live together before marriage are more likely to divorce than are those who do not live together before marriage.
Conclusion: Living together before marriage is one of the causes of divorce.
Notice that the author uses cause-and-effect reasoning in her argument. Any time an author makes an argument that rests on cause-and-effect reasoning, we must be skeptical of the cause-and-effect claim as well as the soundness of the argument. Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she makes the assumption that her cause-and-effect claim is correct and that there is not an alternative explanation for the pattern that she seeks to explain. Often, we’ll see that this assumption is a bad one.
In order to weaken the argument, we need an answer choice that provides information that serves to call into question the causal relationship, that living together prior to marriage causes divorce.
YOUR CHOICE(A) In comparison with more conservative minded people, more liberal minded people tend to be both more open to living together before marriage and more open to getting divorced. What this answer choice says indicates that whether a couple’s mindset is conservative or liberal can affect both (1) whether the couple are likely to live together before marriage and (2) whether the couple are likely to get divorced.
So, this choice provides an alternate explanation for the correlation between living together before marriage and getting divorced: a third variable, liberal mindset, causes both of them.
Thus, this choice suggests that there is a strong possibility that the correct explanation for the observed relationship between living together before marriage and getting divorced is not the explanation upon which the author bases her claim. Thus, this choice weakens the argument.
It’s important to remember that a powerful way in which we can weaken a cause-and-effect argument is to show that perhaps there is a third variable (in this case, a liberal mindset), that causes both of the other two variables (in this case, living together and divorce).
Notice that this choice directly attacks the author’s assumption.
(B) In some countries, the divorce rates have steadily decreased over the past 30 years. Notice that the conclusion of the argument is based on the fact that there is a correlation between living together before marriage and getting divorced.
The fact that overall divorce rates in some countries have decreased does not change the fact that people who live together before marriage are more likely to get divorced than people who don’t live together before marriage, and the fact that overall divorce rates have decreased does not affect the support that the correlation between living together before marriage and getting divorced provides for the argument’s conclusion.
(C) Couples who live together before getting married have an opportunity to learn to work together as a team before they get married. This answer choice could be tempting, as it could seem to weaken the argument by presenting a reason why living together before marriage would prevent divorce rather than cause it: people living together before marriage have an opportunity to learn to work together as a team.
However, even though what is described by this choice seems to be an advantage to living together before marriage, it remains the case that couples who live together before marriage are more likely to get divorced than are couples who do not live together before marriage. So, what this choice says does not affect the argument.
In general, we must remember that the premises are always considered factual, so an answer choice that says something the appears to weaken a premise will never be correct.
(D) For preventing divorce, there is no substitute for having good communication skills. This choice may seem to suggest that a couple’s level of communication skills is more important than whether they live together before marriage in determining whether they will remain married.
However, even if level of communication skills is more important than whether a couple live together, living together before marriage could still cause divorce. So, what this choice says does not affect the argument.
It’s important not to choose as correct answer choices that say things that are probably true in reality yet do not weaken the argument.
(E) For many couples who live together before getting married, marriage does not immediately lead to significant changes in their lifestyles. Since this choice may be taken as suggesting that living together before marriage is little different from marriage, a test-taker might be tempted to select this choice as a result of reasoning that something similar to marriage would not be the cause of divorce.
However, the truth is that, regardless of whether marriage is significantly different from living together before marriage, for some reason, couples who live together before marriage are more likely to get divorced than are couples who don’t live together before marriage. So, what this choice says does not provide a clear reason to believe that the correct explanation for this correlation is not that living together before marriage causes divorce.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now continue by discussing another type of argument flaw that can be highlighted by a weakening correct answer, a faulty generalization.
summary:
Lesson Content We’ve learned a bit about cause-and-effect arguments in the Assumptions chapter.
