We must be careful whenever a speaker makes a cause-and-effect argument, because determining whether one thing actually causes another can be difficult.
💡MUST KNOW:
We must be careful whenever a speaker makes a cause-and-effect argument, because determining whether one thing actually causes another can be very difficult.
In fact, making such determinations is so difficult that, for decades, courts of law did not acknowledge that cigarette smoking causes lung cancer.
title: 3.7.2 Weakening Answer Type 2: An Answer that Calls into Question a Cause-and-Effect Conclusion

contents:
Lesson Content When an author uses a sample of a population as evidence in his argument, he assumes that that sample provides solid support for his conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an author uses a sample of a population as evidence in his argument, he assumes that that sample provides solid support for his conclusion. In Weaken the Argument questions, we’ll see that that assumption can be a poor one.
Authors sometimes make generalizations, drawing conclusions about populations by considering small samples of those populations.
For example, some hedge-fund managers engage in insider trading.
💡MUST KNOW:
Be cautious if a generalization made about all members of a group is based on characteristics of members of a sample taken from that group.
When the argument in a Weaken question makes a broad generalization about members of a group that is based on information on only some of the members of that group, be aware that the correct answer will likely demonstrate that the fact that some members of a group have attribute X does not mean that all members of the group have attribute X.
Flaws in Sampling One important theme that falls into this category is that, whenever we take a sample of a population, for example in setting up an experiment or taking a survey, and then make generalizations about the overall population, we may run into trouble.
We say that, in order for a conclusion based on characteristics of a sample to be well supported, we must have a “representative sample.” In other words, the sample must have characteristics that are the same as (or similar enough to) the characteristics of the overall population.
💡MUST KNOW:
In order for a conclusion based on characteristics of sample to be well supported, we must have a “representative sample.” In other words, the sample must have characteristics that are the same as (or similar enough to) the characteristics of the overall population.
If the sample is not representative of the population, then observations made about the sample may not be applicable to the population.
💡MUST KNOW:
If the sample is not representative of the population, then observations made about the sample may not be applicable to the population.
For example, imagine that we are selling shoes.
So, the arguments in GMAT Weaken questions can involve samples, and the correct answers to such questions can weaken the arguments by showing that the samples are not representative of the overall populations that the conclusions are about.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Weaken questions in which conclusions are supported by characteristics of samples of populations, the correct answers can weaken the arguments by showing that the samples are not representative of the populations that the conclusions are about.
Consider the following examples:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 14 Fitness Company Executive: Our new fitness program was designed to be easy to stick with. Which of the following, if true, would most undermine the executive’s conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) If a person discontinues using a fitness program, some of the results achieved by using the program can be retained for a relatively long period of time. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Generally, the people who volunteer for testing the effectiveness of a new fitness program are unusually motivated to become fit. 3. [ ] (C) When another fitness program was tested by the company, only 50 percent of the volunteers using it stuck with it for at least a year. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Often, a person following a fitness program will begin to see significant effects within about 45 days of starting to follow it. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Many of the volunteers who participated in the testing of the program had little previous experience with fitness programs. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy This argument concludes that, because almost 90 percent of the volunteers who tested the fitness program stuck it out for at least a year, almost 90 percent of the fitness company’s customers who use the program will stick with it for at least a year.
The author’s assumption is that the volunteers are not different in some important way from the company’s customers as a whole. Right? In other words, the argument overlooks the possibility that most customers may act differently from how the volunteers acted.
In general, one theme to think about is that, whenever we look at sample of a population and then seek to make sweeping generalizations applicable to the overall population, we may find that our sample is not representative of the population.
(A) If a person discontinues using a fitness program, some of the results achieved by using the program can be retained for a relatively long period of time. This choice could be tempting, because the fact one can retain some results of using a fitness program for a while after discontinuing use of it could be perceived as a reason why people would discontinue using a fitness program. Thus, this choice could be perceived as providing a reason to believe the less than 90 percent the customers who use the new program will stick with it.
The truth is, however, that this potential reason for discontinuing use of the fitness program was present when 90 percent of volunteers stuck with the program for at least a year. So, even with this potential reason to quit, 90 percent of volunteers stuck with the program, and this choice provides no reason to doubt that 90 percent of the company’s customers will also stick with it.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Generally, the people who volunteer for testing the effectiveness of a new fitness program are unusually motivated to become fit. This choice indicates that the people who volunteered to test the program are probably different from the company’s customers in general, as the people who volunteered were likely unusually motivated to become more fit. In such a case, the fact that a large proportion of them stuck with the program does not imply that a similarly large proportion of the customers, who may not be as motivated, will stick with the program.
Thus, what this answer choice says weakens the argument by highlighting that the sample of people upon which the conclusions is based is a sample that is likely unrepresentative of the company’s customers in general. That is, the sample was biased.
So, this choice attacks the author’s assumption that the volunteers are not different in some important way from the company’s customers as a whole.
Notice that this answer choice presents a malicious variable.
(C) When another fitness program was tested by the company, only 50 percent of the volunteers using it stuck with it for at least a year. This choice has virtually no bearing on the argument at hand. This choice is about a different program. How many volunteers stuck with a different program has no impact on the given argument.
(D) Often, a person following a fitness program will begin to see significant effects within about 45 days of starting to follow it. Whether it takes one month, six months, or more than a year to see benefits, such information will have no impact on the argument. Whatever number of days it takes to see significant benefits, 90 percent of the volunteers stuck with the program for at least a year, and the conclusion is based on this fact. Thus, this choice has no impact on the argument.
(E) Many of the volunteers who participated in the testing of the program had little previous experience with fitness programs. We might be tempted to make this choice seem to be a weakener by making up a story about why people who had no previous fitness experience would have acted differently from the company’s customers.
However, we have no information on whether the customers in general have previous experience with fitness programs. Thus, this choice has no clear impact on the argument.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 15 Presentiment is said to be a phenomenon characterized by a person’s bodily functions, such as heart rate, shifting into response mode before a stressful event actually occurs. Which of the following, if true, most seriously undermines the researchers’ conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) The results of the studies that indicated that presentiment is a real phenomenon did not all show that it is with the same statistical significance. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) People serving as subjects in studies of parapsychological phenomena such as presentiment are often compensated for their participation. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) The people studied who appeared to be experiencing presentiment often were not fully aware that they were, though monitoring equipment reported changes in bodily functions. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) The methods used in most of the studies reviewed were rigorous enough that the results that they generated could be considered reliable. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) When, in the process of studying a phenomenon, researchers get unexciting results, they often forgo publishing their findings. View Solution Question Level: medium Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: Presentiment is said to be a phenomenon characterized by a person’s body functions shifting into response mode before a stressful event actually occurs.
Background: A group of researchers reviewed all of the studies of presentiment the results of which were published during the previous fifty years .
Premise: The vast majority of the studies that the researchers reviewed provided statistical evidence confirming that presentiment is a real phenomenon.
Conclusion: The researchers concluded that presentiment is a real phenomenon.
We need to weaken the support for the conclusion, which is based on study results.
One key detail to notice is that the argument mentions that the results considered had been “published.” How could this detail be important?
(A) The results of the studies that indicated that presentiment is a real phenomenon did not all show that it is with the same statistical significance. Notice that this answer choice doesn’t say that the statistical significance of any study’s results was low. It just says that the statistical significance was not the same in all cases.
The fact that the statistical significance of results was not the same in all cases does not mean that the results were not solid evidence for the conclusion that presentiment is real.
So, what this choice says has no effect on the strength of the argument.
(B) People serving as subjects in studies of parapsychological phenomena such as presentiment are often compensated for their participation. This answer choice could be taken as indicating that the participants in the studies mentioned could have been influenced by compensation to do things to make presentiment seem to be real even if it is not.
The truth is, however, that we don’t have any clear reason to believe that compensation influenced the study participants in that way. In fact, for all we know, the compensation influenced the study participants to do their best to generate accurate results.
Furthermore, we have no clear reason to believe even that the participants had the ability to affect the study results.
So, there is no clear path via which this choice would weaken the argument.
When answering Weaken questions, we must be careful to not choose as correct answer choices that state facts that seem significant yet do not weaken the argument.
(C) The people studied who appeared to be experiencing presentiment often were not fully aware that they were, though monitoring equipment reported changes in bodily functions. Regardless of whether the people studied were aware that they were experiencing changes in bodily functions that appeared to constitute presentiment, they were experiencing those changes. Therefore, what this choice says does not affect the validity of the study results or affect the researchers’ conclusion based on the study results.
(D) The methods used in most of the studies reviewed were rigorous enough that the results that they generated could be considered reliable. Since what this choice says serves to confirm the reliability of the study results upon which the researchers’ conclusion is based, it strengthens, rather than weakens, the support for the researchers’ conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(E) When, in the process of studying a phenomenon, researchers get unexciting results, they often forgo publishing their findings. It is likely that study results that indicate that presentiment is not a real phenomenon would be considered unexciting.
Therefore, by saying that, when researchers studying a phenomenon get unexciting results, they often forgo publishing their findings, this choice provides a reason to believe that any researchers whose study results indicated that presentiment is not a real phenomenon may have decided not to bother publishing their results.
Thus, we can see that the set of published studies upon which the group of researchers’ conclusion is based may not have included some studies the results of which indicated that presentiment is not a real phenomenon. In other words, what this choice says indicates that the set of studies the group of researchers reviewed may have been a biased sample of studies of presentiment.
Thus, by indicating that the group of researchers may have based their conclusion on a sample that was biased in a way such that it would indicate that presentiment is a real phenomenon even if it isn’t real, this answer choice undermines the credibility of their conclusion.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss answers that weaken arguments that are based on faulty comparisons.
summary:
Lesson Content When an author uses a sample of a population as evidence in his argument, he assumes that that sample provides solid support for his conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an author uses a sample of a population as evidence in his argument, he assumes that that sample provides solid support for his conclusion. In Weaken the Argument questions, we’ll see that that assumption can be a poor one.
Authors sometimes make generalizations, drawing conclusions about populations by considering small samples of those populations.
title: 3.7.3 Weakening Answer Type 3: Shows that a Generalization that Supports an Argument is Faulty

contents:
Lesson Content When an author uses a comparison as evidence in an argument, she assumes that that comparison provides solid support for her conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an author uses a comparison as evidence in an argument, she assumes that that comparison provides solid support for her conclusion. Often, in Weaken the Argument questions, we’ll see that that assumption is a poor one, because authors often make faulty comparisons.
When an author of an argument makes a faulty comparison, she attempts to support the argument by comparing two things that are fundamentally different in an important way such that the comparison does not actually support the conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an author of an argument makes a faulty comparison, she attempts to support the argument by comparing two things that are fundamentally different in an important way such that the comparison does not actually support the conclusion.
For example, imagine someone supporting an argument by making a comparison between wild wolves and domesticated dogs, on the basis of the fact that the two are closely related genetically and look similar.
It’s generally risky to assume that, just because two things seem the same in one way or even in multiple ways, they are similar enough that one can be used as a valid analog for the other, and in Weaken questions, correct answers can weaken arguments based on comparisons by showing that the comparisons are faulty and, therefore, don’t really support the conclusions of the arguments.
💡MUST KNOW:
The correct answer to a Weaken question can weaken an argument based on a comparison by indicating that items in the comparison are different in key ways, and that, therefore, the comparison is faulty.
Consider the following examples:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 16 House cats that are adopted as kittens and experience at least three hours of human interaction per day are significantly more docile than cats that are adopted as adults or experience little day-to-day human interaction. Which of the following, if discovered to be true, would most undermine the conclusion above? 1. [ ] (A) Cheetahs are generally more docile than are wild cats of other species. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Wild animals born in captivity are more similar to domesticated animals than are wild animals born in nature preserves or in natural environments. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) In the last 50 years, there have been few deadly incidents involving zookeepers and wild cats that were born in captivity. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Since, unlike wild cats, house cats have been bred for generations to coexist with humans, they have a temperament that is easily influenced by human interaction. 5. [ ] (E) Zookeepers take safety precautions even when dealing with wild cats born in captivity. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium This argument bases its conclusion about wild cats on an analogous situation involving house cats. So, the argument is based on the assumption that house cats and wild cats are similar enough that they will respond in similar ways to factors in their environments.
Is this a good assumption?
Hint: If an argument is based on a comparison, often the correct answer will mention a comparison.
(A) Cheetahs are generally more docile than are wild cats of other species. This choice could be tempting because the passage mentions cheetahs, and because what this choice says could be taken as indicating that wild cats other than cheetahs are something along the lines of never docile. So, we could perceive this choice as weakening the argument by indicating that, unlike cheetahs, cats of other species raised in captivity would not be significantly more docile than non-captive cats of those species.
Notice, however, that this choice differentiates between cheetahs and other species of wild cats, while the argument is about differences between captive and non-captive wild cats. Thus, regardless of what this choice says, it could still be the case that wild cats that are born in captivity can be expected to be significantly more docile than non-captive wild cats.
After all, even if most wild cats born in captivity are not as docile as cheetahs, wild cats born in captivity may still be way more docile than non-captive wild cats.
So, this choice has no effect on the strength of the argument.
(B) Wild animals born in captivity are more similar to domesticated animals than are wild animals born in nature preserves or in natural environments. If anything, this option supports, rather than weakens the support for, the conclusion.
By saying that wild animals born in captivity are more similar to domestic animals than are wild animals born in more natural circumstances, this choice provides a little more reason to believe the conclusion that captive wild cats are likely to be more docile than non-captive wild cats.
Thus, this choice mildly strengthens, rather than weakens, the argument, doing the opposite of what the correct answer has to do.
(C) In the last 50 years, there have been few deadly incidents involving zookeepers and wild cats that were born in captivity. Because it is not clear why there have been few deadly incidents involving zookeepers and wild cats that were born in captivity, this choice does not really affect the argument.
Also, the fact that there have been few deadly incidents involving zookeepers and wild cats that were born in captivity certainly does not conflict with the argument’s conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Since, unlike wild cats, house cats have been bred for generations to coexist with humans, they have a temperament that is easily influenced by human interaction. The argument depends on the assumption that house cats and wild cats are influenced in a similar way by their environments. This choice points out the vulnerability of this assumption by saying that house cats have been bred for generations to coexist with humans and are thus different from wild cats: while house cats can be easily influenced by human interactions, given what this choice says, wild cats are probably not as easily influenced.
Thus, this choice casts doubt on the conclusion by showing that the comparison between wild cats and house cats is faulty.
(E) Zookeepers take safety precautions even when dealing with wild cats born in captivity. This choice implies that even captive wild cats are not particularly docile and is therefore tempting.
Notice, however, that the conclusion of the argument is not that captive wild cats are rather docile or somehow safe to interact with. The conclusion is that wild cats born in captivity can be expected to be more docile than non-captive wild cats. Of course, a captive wild cat could be significantly more docile than a non-captive wild cat and still not be safe to interact with.
So, this choice does not cast any doubt on the conclusion of the argument.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 17 By restoring key, previously longstanding relationships among various animal and plant species in the Yellowstone area of the United States, reintroducing wolves to the area resulted in a restoration of the area’s ecosystem. Which of the following, if true, most clearly serves to call the conclusion into question? 1. [ ] (A) While dingoes and wolves descended from common ancestors, they have evolved separately for thousands of years. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) In some areas of Australia where dingoes are not currently present, when dingoes were present, they killed livestock. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Because of the unstable nature of the climate in Australia, relationships among animal and plant species constantly change there. 4. [ ] (D) Like wolves, dingoes serve as apex predators in areas in which they are present. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Efforts aimed at restoring ecosystems in Australia by eradicating species of animals not native to Australia have been largely unsuccessful. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard Getting the correct answer to this question requires noticing a key difference between things being compared. To see what’s going on, let’s first analyze the argument.
The conclusion of the argument is the following:
Conclusion: Reintroducing dingoes where they have been missing in Australia would achieve results similar to those achieved by reintroducing wolves in the United States, restoring the ecosystems of the areas in which dingoes have been missing.
This conclusion is supported by two ideas:
Premise: Reintroducing wolves in an area restored longstanding relationships among plant and animal species and thus caused the restoration of the area’s ecosystem.
Premise: Dingoes are wild dogs similar to wolves.
Premise: Dingoes have been missing from areas of Australia.
This plan seems to make sense. So, how could the conclusion be called into question? Perhaps there is a key difference between things being compared.
Wolves and dingoes are obviously being compared. At the same time, there is another, less obvious, comparison being made, the comparison between the ecosystem restored in the United States and ecosystems in Australia.
(A) While dingoes and wolves descended from common ancestors, they have evolved separately for thousands of years. While this answer may seem to indicate that wolves and dingoes are somehow so different that they don’t have similar effects on the ecosystems of the areas that they inhabit, the truth is that this choice does not really tell us that wolves and dingoes are significantly different. Yes, they have evolved separately, but that this separate evolution has resulted in significant differences between the two is not clearly indicated by what this choice says. For all we know from what this choice says, even though they have evolved separately, they are still almost exactly the same.
Furthermore, the conclusion is based on the stated premise that dingoes “are wild dogs somewhat similar to wolves.” So, the argument has already addressed the possible issue highlighted by what this choice says by making it clear that, regardless of how long dingoes and wolves have evolved separately, the are somewhat similar.
Thus, given what this choice says, what we really know about wolves and dingoes remains that they are “somewhat similar.” So, this choice does not constitute a clear weakener.
(B) In some areas of Australia where dingoes are not currently present, when dingoes were present, they killed livestock. While this choice indicates that the plan may have a certain drawback, that reintroduced dingoes may kill livestock, indicating that there is a drawback is not the same as indicating that the plan won’t work.
We could say that this choice weakens the support for the wrong conclusion, the conclusion that the plan has no drawbacks.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Because of the unstable nature of the climate in Australia, relationships among animal and plant species constantly change there. By highlighting a key difference between the ecosystem restored in the United States and the ecosystems in Australia, this choice weakens the argument.
To understand how this choice weakens the argument, we have to notice a key detail.
In the Yellowstone area of the United States, reintroduction of wolves resulted in the restoration of the ecosystem because it restored “longstanding relationships among … species.”
On the other hand, this choice indicates that, in Australia, ecosystems are not characterized by longstanding relationships among species; rather, the relationships “constantly change.”
So, because of this key difference between the ecosystem of the Yellowstone area and ecosystems in Australia, what worked in the United States may not work in Australia.
Thus, this choice calls the argument’s conclusion into question by showing that the conclusion is based on a faulty comparison.
(D) Like wolves, dingoes serve as apex predators in areas in which they are present. By bringing up a similarity between wolves and dingoes, this choice strengthens, rather than weakens, the conclusion that reintroducing dingoes will have effects similar to those achieved via reintroducing wolves.
(E) Efforts aimed at restoring ecosystems in Australia by eradicating species of animals not native to Australia have been largely unsuccessful. This choice could be tempting, as what it says could be taken as indicating that restoring ecosystems in Australia has been made difficult by the presence of non-native species.
However, all this choice is really saying is that methods other than reintroducing dingoes have not worked. That those methods have not worked does not mean that reintroducing dingoes wouldn’t work.
Correct Answer: (C) Another mistake authors of arguments can make is confusing absolute numbers and percentages.
summary:
Lesson Content When an author uses a comparison as evidence in an argument, she assumes that that comparison provides solid support for her conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an author uses a comparison as evidence in an argument, she assumes that that comparison provides solid support for her conclusion. Often, in Weaken the Argument questions, we’ll see that that assumption is a poor one, because authors often make faulty comparisons.
When an author of an argument makes a faulty comparison, she attempts to support the argument by comparing two things that are fundamentally different in an important way such that the comparison does not actually support the conclusion.
title: 3.7.4 Weakening Answer Type 4: Shows that a Comparison Used to Support a Conclusion is Faulty

contents:
Lesson Content When an author presents as evidence statistical data, such as numbers and percentages, she assumes that (1) that data provides solid support for her conclusion and (2) that she has properly analyzed that data.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an author presents as evidence statistical data, such as numbers and percentages, she assumes that (1) that data provides solid support for her conclusion and (2) that she has properly analyzed that data. Often in Weaken the Argument questions, neither of those assumptions is a good one.
Critical reasoning questions often attempt to confuse us with numbers.
In general, if a set is small, a large percentage of that set is still a small number of items.
So, we have to be careful when answering questions involving percentages, because the real number behind a percentage may be large, small, or somewhere in between.
Furthermore, percentages can be misleading in other ways.
Consider the following:
This year Jack’s salary decreased 50 percent and Betty’s salary increased 15 percent.
Are we able to assume that Betty made more than Jack this year? No, because we have no information regarding the original salary for either Jack or Betty.
💡MUST KNOW:
Be extra careful when you encounter arguments centered on numbers and percentages; such arguments can lead to unfounded conclusions.
Consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 18 According to a recent study of hospital safety, 10 percent of patients who underwent cardiac bypass surgery at a local hospital experienced serious complications, while 30 percent of patients who underwent cardiac bypass surgery at a hospital in a neighboring town experienced similarly serious complications. Which of the following, if true, most seriously calls into question the conclusion above? 1. [ ] (A) The neighboring hospital has fewer patients who suffer serious complications after liver transplants than does the local hospital. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Since cardiac bypass surgeries are among the most complex ones, a complication rate below 35 percent is considered exemplary. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) The neighboring hospital performed more than twice as many cardiac bypass surgeries last year as in any previous year. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) The number of patients who underwent cardiac bypass surgery at the local hospital was eight times the number who underwent such surgery at the hospital in the neighboring town. 5. [ ] (E) The neighboring hospital is world-renowned for the quality of the work done there. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy The argument concludes the following:
Conclusion: The neighboring hospital must have had more patients (a greater number of patients) who experienced serious complications in undergoing cardiac bypass surgery than the local hospital had.
The support for the conclusion is that the neighboring hospital had a higher percentage of patients who suffered from serious complications following bypass surgery.
Remember, that a higher percentage does not always coincide with a larger number.
(A) The neighboring hospital has fewer patients who suffer serious complications after liver transplants than does the local hospital. This fact is irrelevant, as the argument discusses cardiac bypasses, not liver transplants. Don’t fall into the test-maker’s trap of presenting information about condition X when the argument is about condition Y.
(B) Since cardiac bypass surgeries are among the most complex ones, a complication rate below 35 percent is considered exemplary. This choice does not affect the support for the conclusion. It indicates that both hospitals have done exemplary work given that their complication rates are below 35 percent. However, the conclusion is not about the quality of the work done at the hospitals.
(C) The neighboring hospital performed more than twice as many cardiac bypass surgeries last year as in any previous year. This choice presents an irrelevant comparison; it compares a number from last year with numbers from previous years, rather than provides information that can be used for comparing one hospital with another. While this choice is slightly more appealing than (B), which contains no comparison at all, it ultimately has no bearing on the conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The number of patients who underwent cardiac bypass surgery at the local hospital was eight times the number who underwent such surgery at the hospital in the neighboring town. For the purpose of analyzing the effect of this choice, let’s assume that the local hospital has 80 bypass patients a year, meaning that it has 80(0.1) = 8 patients with severe complications.
In this case, the neighboring hospital has 1/8 * 80 = 10 bypass patients, meaning that it has 10(0.3) = 3 patients with serious complications.
So, we can see that, even though a larger percentage of patients experienced complications at the neighboring hospital, that percentage represented a smaller number of actual people.
Thus, what this choice says destroys the support for the conclusion that the neighboring hospital had “more patients” who experienced serious complications after cardiac bypass surgery.
(E) The neighboring hospital is world-renowned for the quality of the work done there. This option could be tempting, as one could perceive what it says about quality of work as providing reason to believe that patients at the hospital in the neighboring town would be less likely to experience complications than patients at other hospitals. Given that take, one could perceive this choice as casting doubt on the conclusion that the hospital in the neighboring town must have had more patients who experienced serious complications in undergoing cardiac bypass surgery than the local hospital had.
However, what this choice says neither changes the fact that 30 percent of patients who underwent cardiac bypass surgery at the hospital in the neighboring town experienced serious complications or gives us any clear reason to question the argument’s conclusion.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss weakening answers that indicate that plans may not generate the results that people are looking for.
summary:
Lesson Content When an author presents as evidence statistical data, such as numbers and percentages, she assumes that (1) that data provides solid support for her conclusion and (2) that she has properly analyzed that data.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an author presents as evidence statistical data, such as numbers and percentages, she assumes that (1) that data provides solid support for her conclusion and (2) that she has properly analyzed that data.
title: 3.7.5 Weakening Answer Type 5: Indicates that, in Arriving at the Conclusion, the Author Confused Numbers and Percentages

contents:
Lesson Content Remember, any time an author presents a plan, she assumes that the plan will work.
So, when an author presents a plan, she assumes that there are no malicious variables.
💡MUST KNOW:
Any time an author presents a plan, she assumes that there are no variables that will enter into the equation that will spoil the plan. In Weaken the Argument questions, this assumption often turns out to be a poor one.
One pattern commonly seen in Weaken questions is a passage that outlines a plan or a course of action that an author expects will generate some desired or expected results.
💡MUST KNOW:
When the argument in a Weaken question concludes that a plan will generate certain results, the correct answer will likely weaken the argument by indicating that the course of action will not generate those desired or expected results.
The answers indicating that plans will not achieve the desired or expected results tend to fall into the following categories:
The plan addresses one factor in a situation, but the correct answer indicates that there is another factor that the plan does not address. The correct answer indicates that the method that the plan uses to solve a problem makes the problem worse in another way. The argument concludes that the plan will work because a similar plan has worked before, but the correct answer shows that the plan will not work because the current situation is different from the situation existing when the plan was used previously. The correct answer shows that the logic underlying the expectation that the plan will work misses a key aspect of how a situation works. In order to be ready to recognize what’s going on in these types of questions, let’s look at examples with correct answers that fall into each of these categories.
Category:
The plan addresses one factor in a situation, but the correct answer indicates that there is another factor that the plan does not address. Example:
Surveys of people in Sartania indicate that people living in rural areas of the state are overwhelmingly in favor of one of the candidates running for governor. However, many of the people living in rural areas of Sartania are not registered to vote and therefore would not be able to vote in the upcoming election. Therefore, this candidate’s campaign manager is sending volunteers to the rural areas of Sartania to register people for voting, believing that doing so will significantly increase the number of votes for this candidate.
Which of the following, if true, would indicate that the plan will not achieve the expected result?
(Correct Answer) Since voters in rural Sartania must travel significant distances to get to polling places, few of the registered voters in those areas bother to vote.
In this example, the campaign manager assumes that by removing one impediment to voting, lack of registration, the plan will result in significantly more votes from people living in rural areas.
The correct answer indicates that the course of action will not produce the expected result because there is another factor, travel distances, that will keep those people from voting, even though they are registered.
Category:
The correct answer indicates that the method that the plan uses to solve a problem makes the problem worse in another way. Example:
Overfishing in the waters around Seatown has begun to significantly deplete the populations of fish in them. WildWater Fisheries Inc. has proposed setting up fish farms in these waters. By focusing on farming fish rather than on catching the wild fish that live in the waters near Seatown, we will stop the depletion of the wild fish stocks.
Which of the following, if discovered to be true, would most undermine the support for the conclusion above? (Correct Answer) Crowding fish together in offshore farms can create conditions that support the multiplication of organisms that can infect and decimate wild fish populations.
In this argument, which we discussed in the Assumption questions chapter, the author concludes that farming fish, rather than catching wild fish, will prevent the depletion of wild fish stocks.
Category:
The argument concludes that the plan will work because a similar plan has worked before, but the correct answer shows that the plan will not work because the current situation is different from the situation existing when the plan was used previously. Example:
CEO of Steak Restaurant Chain: The key to our success has been to follow the hip young crowd wherever they go. Young people with high incomes and low expenses have been great customers for us and have tended to become customers for life. Even as we speak, our development team is considering new locations in areas where up and coming young people live and to which they travel. So, you can be sure that our company will continue to grow strongly.
Which of the following, if true, provides a reason to believe that the CEO’s prediction will prove to be incorrect?
(Correct Answer) Among young people, steak is increasingly perceived as an unhealthy food consumed by unaware older people.
In this example, the CEO assumes that what has worked in the past will work again.
Category:
The correct answer shows that the logic underlying the expectation that the plan will work misses a key aspect of how a situation works. Example:
Owner of Stanton Toys: During the week, our location downtown is often full of children who come in after school, but most have little to no money with them, and, therefore, few make purchases. During the week, there is not much traffic at the location at the edge of town, but that location does a huge amount of business on weekends, when children accompanied by parents make purchases. Therefore, we would lose little business and could increase overall profitability by closing the downtown location.
Which of the following, if true, most clearly indicates that, if the owner’s proposal were implemented, the results would not be as expected?
(Correct Answer) Many children whose parents buy them toys from Stanton Toys on weekends have previously decided that they want those toys upon seeing them in the downtown store.
In this example, the owner assumes that, just because few purchases are made at the downtown store, closing that location would not result in the loss of much business.
Consider the following examples:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 19 President: Some countries to which we could have been exporting wind turbines over the past few years have had unfair tariffs blocking their citizens from importing our wind turbines. Which of the following, if true, provides reason to believe that the president’s actions will not have the results mentioned? 1. [ ] (A) Wind turbines produce energy only when winds are strong enough to cause the turbines to rotate. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) In the president’s country, the operations of wind turbine manufacturers are currently hampered by an ongoing shortage of copper, a key component of wind turbines. 3. [ ] (C) The removal of tariffs on solar panels, another source of renewable energy, is not expected to result from the trade deals that the president is negotiating. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) The price of oil, which is correlated with demand for wind turbines, is not expected to increase in the coming months. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Domestic demand for wind turbines in the president’s country is not expected to increase over the coming year. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: Countries have had tariffs preventing their citizens from importing our wind turbines.
Premise: I am currently negotiating trade deals that will result in the removal of those tariffs.
Conclusion: Within months we will see increased production and increased employment in our wind turbine industry.
The president plans to negotiate a trade deal that will result in the removal of tariffs blocking companies in her country from doing business with people in other countries. She expects these actions to result in increased production of wind turbines in her country.
One key thing that we will see time and again is that, whenever anyone proposes a plan, he or she makes the assumption that the plan will work as it has been laid out, that there will not be other variables that enter the equation and derail the plan. Often, such an assumption is not so good.
(A) Wind turbines produce energy only when winds are strong enough to cause the turbines to rotate. What this answer choice describes is a limitation of the usefulness of wind turbines, one that may affect overall demand for wind turbines. At the same time, even though wind turbines need wind in order to produce energy, people do purchase wind turbines. So, even though the usefulness of wind turbines may be limited by their need for wind in order to operate, it could still be the case that, once the tariffs are removed, the country’s wind turbine industry will do more business.
So, this choice does not weaken the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(B) In the president’s country, the operations of wind turbine manufacturers are currently hampered by an ongoing shortage of copper, a key component of wind turbines. This choice indicates that, in addition to the tariffs, there is another factor constraining the wind turbine industry, a copper shortage. As a result of this shortage, it seems likely that, even without the tariffs, the country’s wind turbine industry will not be able to increase production and hire more people, because the manufacturers need copper in order to produce wind turbines.
So, by indicating there is an additional factor hampering wind turbine production, a copper shortage, which the president’s actions will not address, this choice provides a reason to believe that the president’s actions will not have the expected results.
Notice that what this answer choice says weakens the argument by attacking a bad assumption made by the author.
(C) The removal of tariffs on solar panels, another source of renewable energy, is not expected to result from the trade deals that the president is negotiating. Tariffs on solar panels should not negatively affect foreign demand for wind turbines. Rather, tariffs restricting trade in solar panels might cause people to buy wind turbines instead. So, if anything, this choice may strengthen, rather than weaken, the support for the conclusion.
(D) The price of oil, which is correlated with demand for wind turbines, is not expected to increase in the coming months. Notice that the president has already provided support for the conclusion, the fact that the tariffs will be removed.
What this choice says indicates that another possible reason to believe that there will be increased production and increased employment in the country’s wind turbine industry – the expectation that oil prices will increase – does not exist.
OK, but so what? We don’t need more support for the conclusion. We already have a premise that supports the conclusion, and the fact that we don’t have a second premise does not weaken the support that we already have.
So, what this choice says has no effect on the support for the conclusion.
(E) Domestic demand for wind turbines in the president’s country is not expected to increase over the coming year. This choice could be tempting, because it could be perceived as indicating that demand in general for the country’s wind turbines will not increase, even after the tariffs are lifted.
However, the conclusion is based on the expectation of an increase in international trade in wind turbines not on an expectation of an increase in domestic demand.
So, all this choice really indicates is that there will not be an increase in domestic demand in addition the expected increase in international trade.
Thus, this choice does not have any effect on the support for the argument’s conclusion.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 20 The city of Tennico has announced that it plans to start a program involving periodic gun buybacks. Which of the following, if true, most calls into question the expected effectiveness of the program? 1. [ ] (A) By not questioning people selling guns to the city, officials may miss opportunities to gain information that could be used to prevent violence. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Sometimes a relative or friend of someone who owns a gun will sell the gun to the city in order to prevent the owner from using it to perpetrate violence. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) The money spent to buy back guns could be instead used to fund educational programs that would address the root causes of violence in the city. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Many people feel more comfortable purchasing new guns if they know that they can easily sell the guns should they no longer want them. 5. [ ] (E) Many of the guns sold to the city will come from people who would not have carried the guns had the guns remained in their possession. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: The city of Tennico has announced that it plans to start a program involving periodic gun buybacks .
Plan: On the date of each announced gun buyback, a person in possession of a gun will be able to sell it to the city, no questions asked.
Expected Outcome: The gun buyback program will serve to reduce the number of guns carried by people on the streets of Tennico.
The reasoning underlying the guy buyback program – if the city buys guns from people possessing them, the number of guns carried by people will be reduced – seems to be rather straightforward. To weaken the argument, we need a statement that calls this reasoning into question, by indicating that it could very well be the case that the program will result in either no reduction in the number of guns on the streets or an increase in the number of guns on the streets.
Remember, whenever anyone proposes a plan, he or she makes the assumption that the plan will work as it has been laid out, that there will not be other variables that enter the equation and derail the plan. Often, such an assumption is not so good.
(A) By not questioning people selling guns to the city, officials may miss opportunities to gain information that could be used to prevent violence. While this choice may bring up one disadvantage of the program, what this choice says does not go in the direction we need it to go in order for it to weaken the argument.
The point of the program is to reduce the number of guns on the streets of Tennico, not to gain information. So, this choice does not affect the argument.
(B) Sometimes a relative or friend of someone who owns a gun will sell the gun to the city in order to prevent the owner from using it to perpetrate violence. In indicating that people may seek to prevent violence by selling guns to the city, what this choice says is in line with the reasoning of the argument, and therefore, this choice does not weaken the argument.
(C) The money spent to buy back guns could be instead used to fund educational programs that would address the root causes of violence in the city. The argument is not about the relative merits of the gun buyback program or about whether the best way for the city to spend money is to buy back guns. The conclusion is simply that the program will serve to reduce the number of guns carried by people in Tennico.
So, as interesting or valid as what this choice says may be, it does not affect the argument.
Also notice that if we look carefully at what this answer choice says, we see that it mentions a different plan, a better plan. In general, unless an author claims that his plan is the only plan or the best plan, he need not assume that there are not other plans that could work or assume that there is not a better plan than his. Therefore, the mention of a different plan or a better plan will not weaken the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Many people feel more comfortable purchasing new guns if they know that they can easily sell the guns should they no longer want them. While this choice says nothing about the program or about Tennico specifically, if we thoughtfully consider the implications of what it says, we can see the following. If people are more comfortable purchasing new guns if they know that they can readily sell the new guns that they purchase, then by providing people with a way to sell guns, Tennico may encourage more purchases of guns than there would have been had Tennico not had the buyback program. As a result, there could be more guns, and, therefore, more guns carried, in Tennico with the program in place than without.
So, while we don’t know for sure that what this choice says means that the buyback program will not result in a reduction in guns carried by people in Tennico, this information does call into question the conclusion that the program will have the desired effect.
(E) Many of the guns sold to the city will come from people who would not have carried the guns had the guns remained in their possession. This answer choice could be tempting, since it seems to indicate that buying back guns will in many cases not make any difference in the number guns carried on the streets of Tennico.
However, even if many of the guns bought by the city would not have been carried anyway, if some of the guns bought by the city would have been carried, then the program could result in a reduction in the number of guns carried.
So, this choice does not fundamentally undermine the strength of the conclusion.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 21 In accordance with Home Squad real estate agency’s new plan for increasing sales, each month, the agency will hold a contest such that the agent who has generated the most sales for that month will get, in addition to standard sales commissions, a $10,000 bonus. Which of the following, if true, most calls into question the above prediction? 1. [ ] (A) The least productive agents at the Home Squad will only rarely see their incomes affected by the bonus. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Much of the sales success of the Home Squad agency has resulted from agents’ cooperating to generate sales. 3. [ ] (C) $10,000 is more than some agents’ average monthly total commission from sales. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) The commission that an agent would receive for closing a particularly large sale would be much greater than the $10,000 bonus. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) The housing market in the area served by the Home Squad agency is not expected to be strong over the next few years. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard The expected outcome:
Conclusion: Having the contest will have a positive effect on the sales of the agency.
How that expectation is supported:
Background: Each month, the Home Squad agency will hold a contest such that the agent who has generated the most sales for that month will get, in addition to standard sales commissions, a $10,000 bonus.
Premise: That bonus will constitute a significant incentive for even the most productive agents.
The reasoning of the argument is that, by offering a significant bonus to the agent who generates the most sales in any month, the agency will incentivize all of the agents to generate more sales for the agency. This argument could be weakened by information indicating that the author of the plan missed a key aspect of how the business works and that, therefore, having the contest could have unanticipated effects such that it would negatively rather than positively affect the sales of the agency.
Remember, whenever anyone proposes a plan, he or she makes the assumption that the plan will work as it has been laid out, that there will not be other variables that will enter the equation and derail the plan.
(A) The least productive agents at the Home Squad will only rarely see their incomes affected by the bonus. While this choice indicates that it is possible that some of the of the agents will not be significantly affected by or incentivized by the contest, as long as some of the agents are incentivized by the contest, having the contest could have a positive effect on the sales of the agency.
So, this choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Much of the sales success of the Home Squad agency has resulted from agents’ cooperating to generate sales. By considering the implications of what this choice says in the context of what the passage says, we can see the following.
The contest provides an incentive for the agents to compete. Meanwhile, this choice says that much of the agency’s sales success comes from agents’ cooperation. By incentivizing the agents to compete with each other, the contest may sabotage some of the cooperation among agents that, according to this choice, has resulted in the agency’s sales success. Thus, by indicating that much of the agency’s success comes from cooperation, this choice illuminates an aspect of the situation that seems to have been missed by the author.
Given what this choice says, having the contest could actually negatively affect sales, and thus this choice serves to call into question the conclusion that having the contest will have a positive effect on sales.
Notice that what this answer choice says weakens the argument by attacking a bad assumption made by the author.
(C) $10,000 is more than some agents’ average monthly total commission from sales. While perhaps we could come up with a complex convoluted story explaining why a monthly contest that involves awarding a bonus greater than the average monthly commissions of some of the agency’s agents would not positively affect sales, the truth is that there is no clear reason to believe that what this choice says affects the conclusion of the argument. If anything, what this choice says indicates that the bonus could be a significant incentive for some of the agents, and that, therefore, having the contest might cause them to generate more sales for the agency than they otherwise would have.
(D) The commission that an agent would receive for closing a particularly large sale would be much greater than the $10,000 bonus. This choice could be tempting, as what it says tends to somewhat diminish the relative value of the bonus. All the same, the passage stated as fact that the bonus would constitute a significant incentive for even the most productive agents, and, meanwhile, the fact that a particular commission might be greater than the bonus does not mean that the bonus would not be valued by the agents.
So, what this choice says does not significantly change what we know about the scenario and does not weaken the argument.
(E) The housing market in the area served by the Home Squad agency is not expected to be strong over the next few years. This choice could be tempting, because one could take what it says to mean that the agency will have difficulty generating sales over the next few years and decide that, therefore, the contest won’t have the expected effect.
However, the conclusion of the argument is not that the agency will increase sales or generate a lot of sales but rather that having the contest will have a positive effect on sales. In other words, the conclusion is that sales will be greater with the contest than they would have been without it.
Since, regardless of the housing market in general is strong, the contest could have a positive effect on sales, this choice does not affect the support for the conclusion.
Correct Answer: (B) OK, now that we have covered questions in which arguments are weakened by answer choices that indicate that plans won’t work, let’s discuss questions that work in the opposite way, those in which arguments are weakened by answers indicating that plans will work.
summary:
Lesson Content Remember, any time an author presents a plan, she assumes that the plan will work.
So, when an author presents a plan, she assumes that there are no malicious variables.
💡MUST KNOW:
Any time an author presents a plan, she assumes that there are no variables that will enter into the equation that will spoil the plan. In Weaken the Argument questions, this assumption often turns out to be a poor one.
title: 3.7.6 Weakening Answer Type 6: Brings Up New Information that Indicates that a Plan Won’t Work

contents:
Lesson Content Plans are common topics in GMAT Critical Reasoning questions, and Weaken questions can involve conclusions that plans will not work or don’t make sense.
💡MUST KNOW:
When authors claim that plans will not work or don’t make sense, support for such conclusions can be weakened by correct answers that provide new information that indicates that actually the plans will work or do make sense.
The correct answers to these questions generally weaken the arguments by providing new information that offsets or makes up for the information used to support the arguments’ conclusions that the plans will not work.
Notice how the correct answers to the following questions weaken the arguments by offsetting the premises that the arguments use to indicate that the plan will not work.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 22 Travel Analyst: To entice new wealthy customers to book stays, the Royal Sunset Hotel and Spa recently began offering free international business class commercial airfare for two people with the booking of any beachfront suite for seven days or longer. Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above? 1. [ ] (A) The Royal Sunset Hotel and Spa is well-known for owning and operating a private airport one mile from the hotel, and that airport caters to hotel guests arriving on private planes. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Over the past three years, more Royal Sunset Hotel and Spa beachfront suites have been booked on the average night than were during previous years. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Most customers who book Royal Sunset Hotel and Spa beachfront suites invite as nonpaying guests at least two people who live in countries far from where the clients live. 4. [ ] (D) Other luxury hotels near the Royal Sunset Hotel and Spa have begun offering customers who book luxury suites complimentary dinners and hot stone massages. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) The Royal Sunset Hotel and Spa recently underwent extensive renovations to both its interior and exterior and is now significantly more attractive than it was previously. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy Background: To entice new wealthy customers to book stays, the Royal Sunset Hotel and Spa recently began offering free international business class commercial airfare for two people with the booking of any beachfront suite for seven days or longer.
Premise: Almost all customers who could afford to pay for at least seven days in a beachfront suite at the Royal Sunset Hotel and Spa would certainly prefer to fly to the hotel in their own private planes, not in commercial airplanes.
Conclusion: By doing so (offering free international business class commercial airfare for two people), it is unlikely that the hotel will attract any new customers.
The hotel has a plan: begin offering free international business class commercial airfare for two people with the booking of any beachfront suite for seven days or longer. Why? To entice new wealthy customers to book stays.
Now, notice that the author claims that this plan will not work. Is the author correct? Might it be the case that the author has miscalculated, and that the plan will work after all? What information might come to light that would show that the author’s conclusion is incorrect, and the hotel’s plan will indeed work?
(A) The Royal Sunset Hotel and Spa is well-known for owning and operating a private airport one mile from the hotel, and that airport caters to hotel guests arriving on private planes. What this answer choice says does not weaken the argument.
The conclusion of the argument is that the hotel will not attract new customers by offering free international business class commercial airfare for two people with the booking of a beachfront suite. That conclusion is supported by the fact that almost all customers who could afford to pay for at least seven days in a beachfront suite at the hotel would prefer to fly to the hotel in their own private planes, not in commercial airplanes.
Certainly, the fact that the hotel is well-known for owning and operating a private airport that caters to hotel guests arriving on private planes does not weaken the argument by giving us more faith that the hotel’s plan will work. The fact that guests can arrive by private plane at a private airport isn’t a reason to believe that they will be enticed by complimentary commercial airfare.
(B) Over the past three years, more Royal Sunset Hotel and Spa beachfront suites have been booked on the average night than were during previous years. What this answer choice indicates is simply that, in recent years, the hotel has experienced in uptick in guests’ booking the beachfront suites. That fact alone does not weaken the support for the conclusion that the new plan will not attract more guests.
Regardless of how the hotel has been doing in general, it could still be the case that guests won’t be attracted by the offer of complimentary commercial airfare with the booking of a beachfront suite.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Most customers who book Royal Sunset Hotel and Spa beachfront suites invite as nonpaying guests at least two people who live in countries far from where the clients live. The hotel is attempting to entice wealthy customers by offering free international business class commercial airfare for two people with the booking of any beachfront suite for seven days or longer.
The author claims that this plan will not work because almost all customers who could afford to pay for at least seven days in a beachfront suite at the hotel would prefer to fly there in their own private planes, not in commercial airplanes.
However, if it is true that most customers who book the beachfront suites invite as nonpaying guests at least two people who live in countries far from where the clients live, then the hotel’s plan may very well work.
After all, if the nonpaying guests who are invited by the paying customers live far away from the paying customers, it would be reasonable to think that these nonpaying guests could take advantage of the free international airfare, using it to fly to the hotel to meet the paying customers who invited them. Thus, the free international airfare could very well be a nice enticement to the paying customers because most invite as nonpaying guests at least two people who live in countries far from where the customers themselves live.
So, this choice provides a reason to believe that the plan will in fact work, and thus weakens the support for the conclusion that the plan will not work.
(D) Other luxury hotels near the Royal Sunset Hotel and Spa have begun offering customers who book luxury suites complimentary dinners and hot stone massages. The fact that other luxury hotels near the Royal Sunset Hotel and Spa have begun offering customers who book luxury suites complimentary dinners and hot stone massages does not weaken the argument.
If anything, the fact that other hotels are offering enticements to attract guests would strengthen the case for believing that the Royal Sunset Hotel and Spa won’t attract new customers by offering complimentary commercial airfare. If multiple hotels in the same area are offering enticements, maybe the enticements won’t have much effect.
(E) The Royal Sunset Hotel and Spa recently underwent extensive renovations to both its interior and exterior and is now significantly more attractive than it was previously. Notice that what this answer choice says is a general statement about the hotel. As wonderful as it may be that the hotel recently underwent extensive renovations that made it significantly more attractive, does that fact give us more faith that the hotel’s plan will work, thereby weakening the argument?
In order to avoid choosing this incorrect choice, we have to pay attention to what exactly the conclusion of the argument is. The conclusion is not that the hotel will not entice new customers in general. The conclusion is that the hotel will not entice new customers by offering complimentary airfare to guests who book beachfront suites.
However attractive the newly renovated hotel may be, it could still very well be the case that, since customers would rather fly in their own private jets, offering complimentary airfare will not entice any new customers to book stays at the hotel.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 23 City Council Member: The administration proposes to improve the health of people living in the South Ward by getting one of the food store chains that sell healthy food to open a store in the South Ward. Which of the following, if true, provides the most compelling reason to call into question the above conclusion? 1. [x] (A) People whose diets consist mostly of low-quality food generally spend significant portions of their incomes on dealing with health problems that result from living on such diets. 2. [ ] (B) Many residents of the South Ward receive from the government financial benefits that supplement their incomes. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Many lower quality foods include ingredients chosen specifically for flavoring the foods in ways that cause people to want to continue to consume them. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) One food store chain that is considering opening a store in the South Ward offers in its stores a range of foods that includes both higher quality and lower quality foods. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) The administration has the ability to fast track permitting and take other measures to make opening a store that sells healthy food in the South Ward relatively easy. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium The City Council Member has concluded the following about the administration’s plan to improve the health of people living in the South Ward:
Conclusion: Most residents of the South Ward would not be able to afford to shop at a store that sells only healthy foods.
The conclusion is supported by two premises:
Premise: Healthy food is more expensive than the food that residents of the South Ward are currently buying.
Premise: Most residents of the South Ward are already living at or close to the limits of their financial means.
So, what the City Council member is getting at is that most residents of the South Ward don’t have extra money that they could spend in order to upgrade the quality of their food.
Do they need extra money though? What if there is an offsetting factor that the City Council member is not taking into account in coming to the conclusion? Is he or she making one or more poor assumptions that a correct answer choice will target?
YOUR CHOICE(A) People whose diets consist mostly of low-quality food generally spend significant portions of their incomes on dealing with health problems that result from living on such diets. By considering the implications of this answer choice, we can see that it is exactly what we’re seeking.
If people who eat lower quality food spend large portions of their incomes on dealing with the resulting health problems, then people who eat higher quality food may be able to offset the extra expense of that food by not having to spend money to deal with health problems caused by the food that they eat.
So, this answer choice provides reason to believe that, actually, residents of the South Ward could afford to buy higher quality food, thus indicating that the administration’s plan could work and thus weakening the argument.
(B) Many residents of the South Ward receive from the government financial benefits that supplement their incomes. This choice is a tempting trap, because it could seem to indicate that the residents of the South Ward have more money than the City Council member believes them to have.
However, as we have discussed, correct answers to weaken questions can’t successfully attack premises of arguments, and the truth is that the City Council member didn’t say that the residents’ incomes are not sufficient for them to handle the expense of the healthy food. He said that the residents are at the limits of their financial means, which would include any financial benefits that they get from the government.
So, this choice actually changes nothing.
(C) Many lower quality foods include ingredients chosen specifically for flavoring the foods in ways that cause people to want to continue to consume them. This choice is only tangentially related to the topic of the argument.
The flavor of lower quality food might be another reason, in addition to affordability, why people consume lower quality food. At the same time, the existence of this other reason to consume lower quality food does not affect the support for the conclusion.
Regardless of whether people consume lower quality food because of its flavor, the fact that residents of the South Ward are at living at or close to the limits of their financial means supports the conclusion that they would not be able to afford shop at a store that sells only healthy foods.
(D) One food store chain that is considering opening a store in the South Ward offers in its stores a range of foods that includes both higher quality and lower quality foods. This choice is a tempting trap that has no effect on the support for the conclusion.
Notice that the conclusion of the argument is not that most residents of the South Ward would not be able to afford to shop at any new store that opens in the South Ward. The conclusion is that they would not be able to afford to shop at a store that sells only healthy foods.
So, the fact that a store that sells both higher quality and lower quality foods may open in the South Ward makes no difference in the strength of the argument. Regardless of whether residents of the South Ward would be able to afford to shop at that store, the evidence still supports the conclusion that most residents would not be able to afford to shop at a store that sells only healthy foods.
(E) The administration has the ability to fast track permitting and take other measures to make opening a store that sells healthy food in the South Ward relatively easy. This choice affects the support for the wrong conclusion.
The argument’s conclusion is not that the administration has no ability to get a store chain to open a store that sells healthy foods in South Ward. The argument’s conclusion is that most residents of the South Ward would not be able to afford to shop at such a store.
Regardless of whether the administration can make opening a store in the South Ward relatively easy, the fact that most residents of the South Ward are living at or close to the limits of their financial means supports the argument’s conclusion.
So, this choice has no effect on the strength of the argument.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now take our skills up another notch by discussing the difference between trap choices and correct answers in Weaken Questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Plans are common topics in GMAT Critical Reasoning questions, and Weaken questions can involve conclusions that plans will not work or don’t make sense.
💡MUST KNOW:
When authors claim that plans will not work or don’t make sense, support for such conclusions can be weakened by correct answers that provide new information that indicates that actually the plans will work or do make sense.
The correct answers to these questions generally weaken the arguments by providing new information that offsets or makes up for the information used to support the arguments’ conclusions that the plans will not work.
title: 3.7.7 Weakening Answer Type 7: Indicates that a Plan that the Argument Concludes will not Work Actually will Work

contents:
Lesson Content One key to correctly answering Weaken questions is being able to see the difference between trap choices and correct answers.
Trap Choice: In general, a trap choice in a Weaken the Argument question says something that seems detrimental to the conclusion and that is somehow related to the argument, and at the same time, what the trap choice says is not logically connected to the argument in a way such that the choice actually weakens the argument.
Correct Answer: Often, at first glance, a correct answer may not seem to be directly related to the conclusion of the argument.
Consider the following example:
A survey has found that parents who smoke are more likely than parents who don’t smoke to have children who are involved in juvenile delinquency. Therefore, it’s clear that parental smoking causes children to engage in destructive behavior.
Which of the following, if true, would most seriously weaken the argument?
(Trap Choice) Some of the children who most actively engage in destructive behavior have parents who do not smoke.
(Correct Answer) Living in stressful circumstances can lead parents to smoke and children to engage in destructive behavior.
Notice the difference between the trap choice and the correct answer.
The trap choice says something about children who “most actively engage in destructive behavior,” and the wording of the trap choice makes it sound important.
Notice, however, that the fact that some children whose parents do not smoke engage in destructive behavior does not conflict with the conclusion that parental smoking causes children to engage in destructive behavior.
The correct answer, on the other hand, indicates that it could be that the true cause-and-effect relationship is not that parental smoking causes children’s destructive behavior but, rather, that a third variable, living in stressful circumstances, underlies both the parents’ smoking and the children’s destructive behavior.
Notice this choice attacks the assumption that a parent’s smoking is the cause of a child’s juvenile delinquency.
So, the correct answer says something that clearly serves to call the conclusion into question.
Now that we better understand the difference between a trap choice and a correct answer to a Weaken question, here is some Targeted Practice that includes some tricky trap choices.
Targeted Practice Each of the following arguments is followed by a trap choice and a correct answer. For each example, select the correct answer. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Targeted Practice: 1 Transportation Analyst: A study done over the last five years shows that using car seatbelts reduced the incidence of serious injury in crashes that occurred at a speed limit of 60 miles per hour by 48 percent. Once our state passes a law requiring the use of seatbelts, we can expect that, by wearing seatbelts, people will reduce their likelihood of being injured in crashes to around half of what it is now. 1. [ ] (A) The data used in the study came only from crashes that were written up in police reports. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) People using protective devices such as seatbelts often feel more comfortable exceeding the speed limit and driving less carefully than they otherwise would. ## Targeted Practice: 2 A home energy use consulting firm offers insulation installation among its services, which also include solar panel installation, windmill installation, and smart heating system design and installation.
