Partly because of competition from individuals specializing in insulation, the profitability of providing insulation services is relatively low for the firm. The firm would be better off not providing insulation services and focusing on the more profitable aspects of its business. 1. [x] (A) The firm often sells comprehensive energy systems to people who initially were interested only in its insulation services. 2. [ ] (B) Many individuals make above average incomes by providing home insulation services. > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 3 City Health Official: Recently, computer analysis of data from our city departments has discovered a disturbing trend. When street vendors’ sales of frozen fruit items increase, the rate of petty crime in the city increases as well. Apparently, along with the other issues associated with eating sweet foods is that eating frozen fruit products can result in a tendency to engage in criminal behavior. 1. [x] (A) A significant percentage of petty crimes are committed by teenagers, who are more likely to stay home on cold or rainy days. 2. [ ] (B) Some people report that after eating frozen fruit products they feel happy and peaceful. > Correct Answer: (A) ## Targeted Practice: 4 Consumer Goods Manufacturing Company CEO: The government has informed us that we have to take measures aimed at reducing the incidence of industrial accidents at our company. Compliance will take up management’s time, and installing the required equipment will involve substantial costs. Thus, implementing the safety measures is going to be hurtful to the profitability of our business. 1. [ ] (A) Implementing the safety measures will not affect the company’s ability to continue a recently begun project, the aim of which is to cut costs and increase efficiency by streamlining manufacturing processes. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Consumers prefer to buy from companies that have track records of low incidences of industrial accidents. ## Targeted Practice: 5 Tennis Center Owner: The trend in our region is that with each passing year people are spending more of their leisure time cycling. In fact, projections show that, whereas currently people in our region spend five percent of their leisure time playing tennis and one percent cycling, within five years they are expected to spend three percent of their leisure time playing tennis and five percent cycling. If these projections are correct, our business is in trouble. 1. [ ] (A) Because of manufacturing innovations, the cost of high quality tennis equipment, and thus the cost of taking up the sport, are expected to decrease substantially. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) With the increased use of automation, the amount of leisure time had by people living in the region surrounding the tennis club is expected to double. Targeted Practice Solutions Targeted Practice: 1 Explanation:
(A) Trap. This choice seems to provide a reason to doubt the value of the data used in the premise. However, unless we have a valid reason to believe that the data from police reports is biased, this choice doesn’t impact the argument.
CORRECT ANSWERCORRECT ANSWER
This choice clearly undermines the support for the conclusion.
If it is often the case that people who wear seatbelts drive faster and less carefully than they would were they not wearing seatbelts, then some of the protection offered by seatbelts would be offset by the less safe driving habits of those wearing seatbelts. So, the results of a law requiring seatbelt use would not likely match the results that wearing seatbelts achieved for people who were all driving at the same speed, 60 miles per hour.
Thus, what this choice says indicates that the fact that using car seatbelts reduced the incidence of serious injury in crashes that occurred at a speed limit of 60 miles per hour by 48 percent does not necessarily support the conclusion of the argument.
Notice that this choice attacks the author’s assumption that all people wearing seat belts would experience results the same as those achieved by a specific set of people, people who were wearing seatbelts of their own volition while driving at a certain speed.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 2 Explanation:
CORRECT ANSWERCORRECT ANSWER
While this answer choice says nothing directly about profitability, it indicates that, while providing insulation services is not as profitable for the firm as providing other services is, customers who interact with the firm because of its insulation services often end up making purchases that are more profitable for the firm. Thus, it is as a result of offering insulation services that the firm makes some its more profitable sales.
So, this choice casts doubt on the conclusion that the firm would be better off ceasing to provide insulation services.
Notice that this choice attacks the assumption that providing insulation services doesn’t somehow increase the overall profitability of the company.
(B) Trap. This choice is tempting, since it can be construed as indicating that providing insulation services can be a great way to make money. However, the argument has established that, for the firm, providing insulation services is less profitable than other lines of business in which the firm engages. The fact that individuals can make above average incomes by providing insulation services does not change the relative level of profitability experienced by the firm as a result of its providing insulation services. So, this choice does not affect the argument.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 3 Explanation:
CORRECT ANSWERCORRECT ANSWER
At first glance, an answer choice that discusses what people do when the weather is cold and rainy may seem totally unconnected to a discussion of food and petty crime. However, with a little thinking, we can find that actually there is a connection.
It’s common knowledge that people eat cold snacks more when the weather is hot and sunny and less when the weather is cold and rainy. Meanwhile, this choice indicates that teenagers, some of whom perpetrate petty crime, are out more when it is hot and sunny and less when it is cold or rainy.
With those facts in mind, we can clearly see that both sales of frozen fruit snacks and rates of petty crime are affected by, a third variable, the weather.
Thus, by indicating that it is quite likely that the reason why there is a correlation between frozen fruit sales and petty crime is not that eating frozen fruit products can result in a tendency to engage in criminal behavior but, rather that, the weather affects both frozen fruit sales and the rate of petty crime, this choice undermines the support for the health official’s conclusion.
(B) Trap. This is a pseudo-weakener, one that seems to disprove the conclusion while actually making a very weak statement.
Notice that it says that “some people report that … they feel happy and peaceful.” “Some people” is any number of people more than one. So, this answer choice could be true if only two people feel happy and peaceful after eating frozen fruit products and millions do not.
Further, people who feel happy and peaceful could commit petty crimes.
Thus, this choice does not clearly weaken the argument.
Correct Answer: (A) Targeted Practice: 4 Explanation:
(A) Trap. The conclusion of the argument is that implementing the safety measures will be hurtful to the profitability of the business, and, at first glance, this answer choice may seem to indicate otherwise.
Let’s look, though, at what this pseudo-weakener answer choice actually says.
It says that implementing the safety measures will not keep the company from following through on some plans that the company already had. The fact that the company can follow through on some plans that it already had does not mean that implementing the safety measures will not cost the company time and money and, thus, hurt profitability.
So, this choice, which may seem to weaken the argument, actually changes nothing.
CORRECT ANSWERCORRECT ANSWER
This answer, which is about consumer preferences, may not seem related to safety measures and the costs of implementing them. However, consumer preferences are related to sales, and sales affect profitability, which is what the conclusion is about. So, with a little analysis, we can see that this answer choice says something that offsets what the argument says.
Notice that what this choice says indicates that, even though implementing the safety measures may be costly, there could be a payoff to implementing them: if they actually serve to reduce industrial accidents, consumers may do more business with the company. Of course, consumers’ doing more business with the company could completely offset the costs of implementing the safety measures or even result in the company’s becoming more profitable than it would have been.
So this answer choice calls into question the conclusion that implementing the safety measures will hurt profitability.
Correct Answer: (B) Targeted Practice: 5 Explanation:
(A) Trap. The appeal of this answer lies in the fact that it seems to weaken the argument by attacking one of the premises, the premise that people are going to reduce the percentage of their leisure time that they spend playing tennis. One could think, “Wait, playing tennis is going to cost less. So, actually people will play more.”
However, the truth of the matter is that the argument is based on the projections, and regardless of any expected reduction in the cost of playing tennis, the projections are what they are.
Notice that the conclusion starts with, “If these projections are correct.”
So, this answer choice that seems to weaken the argument actually changes nothing, and is therefore a trap.
CORRECT ANSWERCORRECT ANSWER
Using some simple math, we can see that this answer choice destroys the argument.
If the percentage of leisure time spent playing tennis goes from 5% to 3%, but leisure time doubles, then 3% of future leisure time will be 2 x 3% of today’s leisure time.
Thus, given what this choice says, in the future, people will be spending 2 x 3% = 6% of today’s leisure time playing tennis.
So, if the projections are correct, the amount of time people spend playing tennis will increase rather than decrease, going from 5% of today’s leisure time to 6%.
So, this choice completely destroys the argument.
Correct Answer: (B) Now, consider the following example, and watch out for trap choices.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 24 Marketing Executive: We use independently administered customer satisfaction surveys to rate the performance of our company, and we recently switched from using one survey provider to using another. Which of the following, if true, would most undermine the executive’s interpretation of the survey results? 1. [ ] (A) The provider of the new survey has only half as many company clients as has the provider of the previous survey. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) The new survey has been in use for only five months, while the previous one was used for three years. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) The more positive a person’s relationship with a company the more likely it is that the person will take the time to respond to a long, detailed survey for the company. 4. [ ] (D) Designing a survey that accurately captures multiple aspects of the satisfaction of a company’s customers requires detailed analysis of what to ask. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) The first few questions in the new survey are nearly identical to the first few questions that were in the previous survey. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium The marketing executive’s conclusion, that the ratings generated by the new survey are more accurate than those generated by the previous survey, is based on the premise that the new survey is longer and more detailed.
Notice that the marketing executive is essentially making a cause-and-effect claim in saying that it was the accuracy of the longer survey that produced the higher ratings. The author makes the assumption that there could be no other explanation for the more favorable ratings. He believes that the only explanation is that the longer survey generates more accurate ratings.
Was it accuracy that was responsible? Could some other factor have caused the increase? The correct answer will somehow call into question the idea that the longer and more detailed survey has generated more accurate ratings.
(A) The provider of the new survey has only half as many company clients as has the provider of the previous survey. This choice is tempting, as one could take it to be indicating that the provider of the new survey is somehow not as good as the provide or the previous survey and that, therefore, the new survey is likely not as accurate as the previous survey.
However, the fact that one provider has more clients than the other has does not clearly indicate anything about the quality of either provider or about the quality of the survey provided by each.
So, this choice does not have a clear effect on the argument.
(B) The new survey has been in use for only five months, while the previous one was used for three years. This answer could be tempting since the fact that the new survey has been used for only five months could be taken as indicating that the new survey is somehow lacking something had by the previous survey, which had been used for three years. Any such lack could result in the new survey’s generating ratings that are less accurate than those generated by the previous survey.
However, the truth is that the fact that the new survey has been used for only five months does not provide any solid reason to doubt the accuracy of the ratings generated by the new survey. If the survey is well designed, it could have generated accurate ratings on the first day it was used.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The more positive a person’s relationship with a company the more likely it is that the person will take the time to respond to a long, detailed survey for the company. This choice casts doubt on the conclusion that the higher ratings generated by the longer, more detailed survey are more accurate than the ratings generated by the previous survey
After all, if people with more positive relationships with the company are more likely to respond to the longer, more detailed new survey, then the ratings generated by this new survey are not likely to include a representative cross section of customer opinions of the company’s performance. Instead, the ratings generated by the new survey are likely to be disproportionately positive.
So, this choice indicates that it may be that bias, rather than accuracy, is responsible for the fact that the ratings generated by the new survey are higher than those generated by the previous survey.
Notice how clearly this answer choice weakens the argument. No conjecture is necessary for linking this choice to the conclusion. Rather, this choice provides a clear reason to doubt that the conclusion follows from the premise.
In general, whenever an author uses survey evidence to support his conclusion, he assumes that that survey has produced evidence that can in fact support the conclusion; he assumes that there are no malicious variables lurking in the survey evidence.
This answer choice underscores that that assumption was a bad one.
(D) Designing a survey that accurately captures multiple aspects of the satisfaction of a company’s customers requires detailed analysis of what to ask. The fact that designing a survey requires detailed analysis of what to ask does not serve to call into question the accuracy of the new survey, as we are not given reason to believe that detailed analysis was not included in the process of creating the new survey.
Don’t be fooled into thinking that a general statement like this one casts doubt on the validity of a conclusion.
(E) The first few questions in the new survey are nearly identical to the first few questions that were in the previous survey. The fact that the first few questions of the two surveys are similar could be construed as indicating that the two surveys are similar and, therefore, that concluding that the second is more accurate does not make sense.
However, the fact remains that the second survey is different from the first in that it is longer and more detailed. So, this choice does not affect the argument at all.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss common incorrect answer choices in weaken questions.
summary:
Lesson Content One key to correctly answering Weaken questions is being able to see the difference between trap choices and correct answers.
Trap Choice: In general, a trap choice in a Weaken the Argument question says something that seems detrimental to the conclusion and that is somehow related to the argument, and at the same time, what the trap choice says is not logically connected to the argument in a way such that the choice actually weakens the argument.
title: 3.8 The Difference Between Trap Choices and Correct Answers in Weaken Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Forewarned is forearmed.
Let’s first look at the type of answer choice that strengthens rather than weakens the argument.
summary:
Lesson Content Forewarned is forearmed.
Let’s first look at the type of answer choice that strengthens rather than weakens the argument.
title: 3.9 Common Incorrect Answer Choices in Weaken Questions

contents:
Lesson Content At this point, you understand that a Weaken the Argument question asks us to find a choice that does just that.
Don’t think you’d ever select an answer choice that strengthens an argument when you’re being asked to weaken it? Don’t be so sure; these opposite answer choices can be seductive.
Consider the following sample conclusion and strengthening answer choice:
Conclusion: Clearly, eating bananas is the key move that resulted in powerlifter’s strength almost doubling last year.
(Answer Choice that Strengthens) The only change the powerlifter made to his routine last year was to add eating bananas daily.
Notice that the answer choice that strengthens strengthens the support for the conclusion because it shows that the only change that the powerlifter made was to add bananas to his regimen, increasing the likelihood that the bananas were in fact responsible for the change.
Selecting such an answer choice would be doing the opposite of what we want to do to correctly answer Weaken the Argument questions.
One reason that these “opposite” answers can be particularly tempting is that they stick to the scope of the argument; they have the virtue of being relevant.
Let’s look at one more example:
Conclusion: The city will benefit financially from providing tax incentives that encourage the production of Broadway style shows. (Answer Choice that Strengthens) People who come to the city to attend Broadway style shows tend to spend money in the city both before and after the shows.
Once again, this answer choice, if true, would support the conclusion above it – the opposite of what you want to do.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering Weaken the Argument questions, be on the lookout for choices that strengthen rather than weaken the support for the conclusions.
Consider the following examples:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 25 After determining that a low percentage of drivers were wearing seat belts while driving vehicles in Gloucester, the council of Gloucester passed a new law making it legal for police to stop and issue a traffic ticket to any driver seen not wearing a seat belt while driving in Gloucester. Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above? 1. [ ] (A) All car insurance companies selling insurance to drivers of vehicles in Gloucester will double the amount they charge to drivers ticketed for not wearing a seat belt. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Since the passage of the seat belt law, the Gloucester police department has increased the number of police patrols tasked with issuing traffic tickets. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Two years ago, the nearby town of Markham instituted a similar seat belt law and then experienced a significant increase in the percentage of drivers who wear seat belts there. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Wearing a seat belt while driving a vehicle has been shown to substantially decrease the risk of injury or death associated with vehicular collisions. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) A large proportion of the vehicles driven in Gloucester have dark-tinted windows, making it impossible for police to see into the vehicles. View Solution Question Level: easy A low percentage of drivers were wearing seat belts while driving vehicles in Gloucester. So, the council of Gloucester made it legal for police to stop and issue a traffic ticket to any driver seen not wearing a seat belt while driving in Gloucester. Of course, as we’re told, drivers do not like paying traffic tickets. So, the author concludes that the new law will be effective at compelling almost all drivers to wear their seat belts while driving.
The author has presented a plan. Any time an author presents a plan, she assumes that the plan will work. In other words, she assumes that there are not variables that will enter into the equation that will spoil the plan.
What might the author have failed to consider? What variables might have gone unnoticed?
(A) All car insurance companies selling insurance to drivers of vehicles in Gloucester will double the amount they charge to drivers ticketed for not wearing a seat belt. Notice that this choice strengthens the argument by giving us more reason to believe that the plan will work.
The passage says that police will be able to ticket drivers who are not wearing seat belts and that drivers do not like paying traffic tickets. Now, if what this choice says is true, then drivers in Gloucester will have even more reason not to want to be ticketed for not wearing seat belts. Not only will the tickets themselves be an incentive to wear a seat belt, but also the prospect of having to pay more for insurance will be an incentive.
Of course, we are not seeking a choice that strengthens the argument; we’re seeking one that weakens it.
(B) Since the passage of the seat belt law, the Gloucester police department has increased the number of police patrols tasked with issuing traffic tickets. Notice that this choice strengthens the argument by giving us more reason to believe that the plan will work.
After all, if the Gloucester police department has increased the number of police patrols tasked with issuing traffic tickets, then there are more police watching for drivers not wearing seat belts than there were before the passage of the seat belt law. Of course, an increase in the number of police tasked with finding these drivers increases the likelihood that these drivers will not go unnoticed.
However, we are not seeking a choice that strengthens the argument; we’re seeking one that weakens it.
(C) Two years ago, the nearby town of Markham instituted a similar seat belt law and then experienced a significant increase in the percentage of drivers who wear seat belts there. What this choice says goes in the same direction as the argument.
The GMAT may not consider this choice a clear strengthener, because there could be significant differences between Gloucester and the nearby town of Markham. All the same, the fact that Markham instituted a similar seat belt law and then experienced a significant increase in the percentage of drivers who wear seat belts there gives us more conviction that Gloucester’s plan will work.
Of course, we’re not seeking a choice that goes in the same direction as the argument; we’re seeking one that weakens it.
(D) Wearing a seat belt while driving a vehicle has been shown to substantially decrease the risk of injury or death associated with vehicular collisions. As always, we must pay close attention to the argument’s conclusion, which is that the new law will be effective at compelling almost all drivers to wear their seat belts while driving vehicles in Gloucester.
If anything, what this answer choices says, that wearing a seat belt while driving a vehicle has been shown to substantially decrease the risk of injury or death associated with vehicular collisions, slightly strengthens the argument. After all, if riding in a car is much safer with a seat belt on, then maybe people will wear seat belts.
Because what this answer choice says goes in the same direction as the argument, it certainly does not weaken the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(E) A large proportion of the vehicles driven in Gloucester have dark-tinted windows, making it impossible for police to see into the vehicles. Notice that the author’s plan depends upon the police’s ability to see that drivers are not wearing seat belts. Once a driver is “seen not wearing a seat belt,” police may stop that driver and issue a ticket.
So, what if the police can’t see many of the drivers? What if, as this answer choice says, a large proportion of the vehicles driven in Gloucester have dark-tinted windows, making it impossible for police to see into the vehicles?
The information provided by this choice makes the argument less convincing by indicating that the new law might NOT be effective at compelling almost all drivers to wear their seat belts while driving vehicles in Gloucester, because the police will not be able to see into a large proportion of vehicles, and thus will not have cause to issue tickets to drivers of those vehicles who are not wearing seat belts.
Notice that this answer choice presents a malicious variable that the author did not consider.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 26 Box office revenue from fantasy genre movies was at an all-time high last year, while the total sales revenue from fantasy genre books significantly decreased. Which of the following, if true, most calls the conclusion into question? 1. [ ] (A) Noting a history of consistently high sales in the fantasy book genre, marketers of fantasy films last year targeted potential fantasy book buyers. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Enthusiasts of the fantasy genre increasingly prefer the communal nature of movie-watching to the solitary nature of reading a book. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Many fantasy book publishing and film studios garner marketing funding from the same sources, which have limited funds to allocate. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Lending programs at libraries significantly increased their inventory of newly released fantasy books as a result of the high box office sales of fantasy movies. 5. [ ] (E) Fantasy book exchanges that facilitate low or no cost trading of fantasy books between readers of the books saw no increase in volume last year. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: hard The author gives a reason for an observed phenomenon:
Premise: Fantasy movie sales up, while fantasy book sales down.
Conclusion: Likely, this disparity occurred because potential fantasy book readers opted to watch fantasy movies instead.
What assumption is made by the author? One assumption is that a decrease in book revenue is the same as a decrease in the number of people reading the books. Does a decrease in book revenue necessarily equate to a decrease in the number of people reading books? Not necessarily – right? Could there be another cause of the observed change?
(A) Noting a history of consistently high sales in the fantasy book genre, marketers of fantasy films last year targeted potential fantasy book buyers. This is an opposite answer, a strengthener rather than a weakener, as it promotes the idea that potential fantasy book buyers were “stolen” by the movie industry. The idea of increased movie marketing efforts supports the conclusion.
Be careful not to select a choice that strengthens the argument. We need a choice that weakens it.
(B) Enthusiasts of the fantasy genre increasingly prefer the communal nature of movie-watching to the solitary nature of reading a book. This choice strengthens the idea that, as the years pass, (“increasingly”), fantasy book buyers turn into fantasy movie watchers.
Be careful not to select a choice that strengthens the argument. We need a choice that weakens it.
(C) Many fantasy book publishing and film studios garner marketing funding from the same sources, which have limited funds to allocate. This choice seems to indicate that, since marketing budgets for fantasy books and movies are limited, marketing budgets for fantasy books or movies would increase as marketing budgets for the fantasy products of the other type, movies or books, would simultaneously decrease.
So, a test-taker could use what this choice says to come up with a convoluted story about marketing budgets and sales that would seem to somehow weaken, or somehow strengthen, the argument.
However, regardless of what happened to marketing budgets, it would still be the case that sales of fantasy movies increased, while sales of fantasy books decreased, and it could still be that the changes occurred because people opted to watch the movies rather than read the books.
So, this choice has no effect on the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Lending programs at libraries significantly increased their inventory of newly released fantasy books as a result of the high box office sales of fantasy movies. This choice offers an alternate explanation for the lower sales of fantasy books: People weren’t buying them; they were reading them for free instead (but still reading them nonetheless).
Of course, this choice does not destroy the argument; despite the libraries’ efforts, fantasy book readers may still have opted for the movies in large enough numbers to allow the speaker’s conclusion to hold. Still, this choice casts enough doubt to weaken the argument.
Notice that this choice attacks the author’s assumption that a decrease in book revenue means that there was a decrease in the number of people reading the books.
(E) Fantasy book exchanges that facilitate low or no cost trading of fantasy books between readers of the books saw no increase in volume last year. This choice does the opposite of what we need, because what it says is a reason to eliminate an alternate theory of why books sales went down, the theory that people were still reading them but got them from book exchanges rather than purchasing them. By eliminating that alternative theory, it strengthens the case for believing the argument’s conclusion that sales went down because people were actually reading fantasy books less and going to fantasy movies more.Be on the lookout for subtle strengtheners like this one.
Opposite wrong answer choices like (E) are especially appealing if they happen to resemble any weakeners that you may have thought of; if the topic in an answer choice seems to resemble an idea that occurred to you on your own, be doubly careful to ensure that that choice weakens – rather than strengthens – the argument.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now consider a type of choice that does not really affect the strength of an argument but can be very tempting, a pseudo-weakener.
summary:
Lesson Content At this point, you understand that a Weaken the Argument question asks us to find a choice that does just that.
Don’t think you’d ever select an answer choice that strengthens an argument when you’re being asked to weaken it? Don’t be so sure; these opposite answer choices can be seductive.
Consider the following sample conclusion and strengthening answer choice:
Conclusion: Clearly, eating bananas is the key move that resulted in powerlifter’s strength almost doubling last year.
title: 3.9.1 Strengtheners - Choices That Are the Opposite of the Correct Answer

contents:
Lesson Content In a Weaken question, along with the correct answer choice, which actually weakens the argument, there can be one or more pseudo-weakener answer choices, which may give the impression that they weaken the argument but don’t really get the job done.
Consider the following example:
The football coach at Verdantville high school wants the players on the team to gain weight. So, he is providing the players with all the chocolate bars that they can eat. Because the players have this tempting food available, the average weight of the players is sure to increase.
Which of the following, if true, provides the strongest reason to question the conclusion drawn?
(Pseudo-Weakener Choice) Some of the players on the team who weigh the least don’t like the taste of chocolate.
(Correct Answer) The bars that the coach is providing to the players contain large amounts of caffeine, which people seeking to lose weight often use to reduce their appetites.
To see why the pseudo-weakener doesn’t weaken the argument, let’s look carefully at the conclusion of the argument.
Conclusion: The average weight of the players is sure to increase.
As long as we can expect the average weight of the players to increase, the conclusion is supported.
What if the pseudo-weakener is true, and some of the players that weigh the least don’t like the taste of chocolate? It might seem that, in that case, the conclusion is called into question, because if some of the players that weight the least don’t like chocolate, then some won’t eat it, and those players won’t gain weight.
But wait.
Contrast the effect of the pseudo-weakener with the effect of the correct answer.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering Weaken the Argument questions, beware of pseudo-weakener answer choices that seem to weaken the arguments but don’t really have the effect we are looking for.
Consider the following example.
For best results, take your time and carefully analyze the choices to determine which are pseudo-weakeners and which truly weakens the argument.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 27 The SwiftBrew coffee company has been building its customer base by selling at relatively low prices the pods that go into its coffee makers. Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the conclusion above? 1. [ ] (A) Some people who have bought SwiftBrew coffee makers have used them for a few months and then stopped because of the amount of waste created in using the pods. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Typically, some users of SwiftBrew pods stock up on enough SwiftBrew pods to last for months. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Sales of SwiftBrew’s coffee makers are not increasing as fast as they did during the first year of the company’s selling coffee makers. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) MorningFresh, a company that makes coffee pods virtually identical to SwiftBrew’s coffee pods, plans to continue selling pods at the price at which SwiftBrew currently sells its pods. 5. [ ] (E) Some SwiftBrew customers reuse pods, making coffee more than once from a single pod. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium In the scenario presented in the passage, many people own coffee machines that use SwiftBrew pods and are fans of SwiftBrew pods. Now, the company plans to increase the price of its pods from its current relatively low level.
To determine which choice casts doubt on the conclusion, we have to note exactly what the conclusion is.
Conclusion: After SwiftBrew increases the price at which it sells its pods, its profits are sure to increase substantially.
So, to correctly answer the question, we need an answer choice that clearly calls into question the conclusion that the company’s profits will increase substantially after it increases the price at which it sells the pods.
As we go through the choices, let’s note which are pseudo-weakeners and which actually clearly weakens the argument.
(A) Some people who have bought SwiftBrew coffee makers have used them for a few months and then stopped because of the amount of waste created in using the pods. This choice is a pseudo-weakener.
Yes, some people have stopped using the machines, but does that fact mean that the company’s profits won’t increase once the price of the pods is higher? Not really. The point is that, by selling pods at a higher price to people who do use the machines, the company will increase its profits.
(B) Typically, some users of SwiftBrew pods stock up on enough SwiftBrew pods to last for months. This choice is a pseudo-weakener, because the fact that some people stock up on enough pods to last for months could be perceived as meaning that SwiftBrew won’t see a profit increase after increasing prices, because people who have stocked up on pods won’t have to buy any.
However, while the fact that some people stock up on enough pods to last for months means that, probably, some people won’t have to buy pods immediately after SwiftBrew increases the price of pods, those people would not have had to buy pods at that point in time anyway. What matters is that the people who do buy pods at that point in time will pay more than they would have before the price increase, and thus, SwiftBrew’s profits will increase.
Furthermore, even those who will have pods stocked up when SwiftBrew increases prices will eventually run out of pods and have to pay the new higher price if they want to buy more.
(C) Sales of SwiftBrew’s coffee makers are not increasing as fast as they did during the first year of the company’s selling coffee makers. This choice is a pseudo-weakener. From what this choice says, one could get the impression that the company’s profits are not going to increase much, as coffee maker sales growth is slowing.
Notice, however, that what this choice says indicates that SwiftBrew’s coffee maker sales are still increasing, and meanwhile, the expected boost in profits is expected to result from the planned increase in the price of pods, which could cause profit growth even if there were no growth in sales of SwiftBrew’s coffee makers.
YOUR CHOICE(D) MorningFresh, a company that makes coffee pods virtually identical to SwiftBrew’s coffee pods, plans to continue selling pods at the price at which SwiftBrew currently sells its pods. This choice truly weakens the argument, since, if another company plans to continue to sell pods that are virtually identical to SwiftBrew’s pods at the price at which SwiftBrew currently sells its pods, then it seems likely that, after SwiftBrew increases the price of its pods, many of its customers will start buying pods from the other company instead of from SwiftBrew. Since the customers’ switching could be a significant detriment to SwiftBrew’s profits and could even result in a decrease in SwiftBrew’s profits, this choice clearly serves to call the conclusion into question.
Remember, any time an author sets forth an argument, plan, proposal, or course of action, she assumes that there will be no offsetting factors that will somehow render that argument, plan, proposal, or course of action invalid. Notice that what this answer choice says highlights that that assumption was a poor one, that in fact there is an offsetting factor that could ruin the plan. This answer choice presents a malicious variable.
(E) Some SwiftBrew customers reuse pods, making coffee more than once from a single pod. This choice is a pseudo-weakener.
We could perceive this choice as weakening the argument, because the fact that people reuse the pods may seem to be a factor that would keep SwiftBrew’s profits from increasing. After all, by reusing pods, people can make coffee without purchasing as many pods as they would have to purchase if they were using each pod just once.
Notice, however, that even a pod that is used multiple times must be purchased to be used. So, the fact that some people reuse the pods does not change the fact that people purchase the pods.
So, regardless of what this choice says, the fact that SwiftBrew plans to increase the price of the pods supports the conclusion of the argument.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss frameshifts.
summary:
Lesson Content In a Weaken question, along with the correct answer choice, which actually weakens the argument, there can be one or more pseudo-weakener answer choices, which may give the impression that they weaken the argument but don’t really get the job done.
Consider the following example:
The football coach at Verdantville high school wants the players on the team to gain weight. So, he is providing the players with all the chocolate bars that they can eat.
title: 3.9.2 Choices That are Pseudo-Weakeners

contents:
Lesson Content Consider an argument that supports Conclusion X.
When a frameshift occurs, an incorrect answer choice will cast doubt on a conclusion that is related to or very similar to Conclusion X but is not Conclusion X.
If we’re not careful, it’s quite easy to fall for a frameshift because of the similarity between the actual conclusion of the argument and the conclusion called into question by the incorrect choice.
Consider this simple example:
Kindergarten students who are introduced to classical music as infants test better in both math and reading than their peers who were not introduced to classical music do. Clearly, classical music has a positive effect on early development.
Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument?
(Frameshift Choice) High school students who listen to classical music at home score no higher on standardized tests than do students who don’t listen to classical music.
(Correct Choice) Parents who take the time to play classical music for their infants tend to be considerably more invested in the success of their children than those who do not. Notice what’s going on here.
The frameshift choice casts doubt on the wrong conclusion, one about high school students rather than early development.
The correct answer, on the other hand, clearly casts doubt on the argument’s conclusion by providing an alternative explanation for why kindergarten students who are introduced to classical music as infants test better in both math and reading than their peers who were not introduced to classical music do.
Let’s consider another:
A survey of local nursing homes found that more than 80 percent of residents preferred DVD players and video streaming services to VCRs. Thus, it must be that elderly people adapt to technology more easily than stereotypes suggest.
Which of the following, if true, most strongly calls into question the conclusion above?
(Frameshift Choice) A survey of nursing homes found that, on average, residents spend only a small percentage of their time watching movies on their own devices.
(Correct Answer) The survey was mailed only to nursing home residents who had recently purchased a monthly subscription to at least one magazine covering trends in home movie theater technology. Notice the shift to casting doubt on a different conclusion, one about how the residents spend their time, rather than the argument’s conclusion about ease of adapting to technology.
At the same time, notice that the correct answer does actually weaken the argument by casting doubt on the value of the survey results.
The correct answer underscores that that assumption was a bad one.
💡MUST KNOW:
A frameshift answer choice to a Weaken question casts doubt on a conclusion other than the conclusion of the argument, and it may mislead us into believing that the two conclusions are the same. Don’t fall for such a trick.
Consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 28 In the past, most personal computer users used the Circlix operating system. Which, if true, most seriously undermines the force of the evidence cited? 1. [x] (A) Whereas, before ten years ago, computer users tended to be experts, now many computer users are novices or occasional users. 2. [ ] (B) For many users of personal computers, ease of use is not as important as top notch security. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) The number of minutes spent on the phone per personal computer technical support call has not significantly increased over the past decade. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Marketers are not concerned that sales of the Orange operating system will be affected by concerns about difficulty using it. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) The company that produced Orange dedicates far more engineer hours to developing Orange now than the company that produced Circlix dedicated to developing Circlix in the past. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium This speaker argues the following:
Premise: Over the period of time during which Orange has become more popular, the number of calls made to support centers by people seeking help with operating their computers has increased.
Conclusion: It must be the case that Orange is more difficult to use than Circlix.
First, let’s think about what assumption this author is making. She’s assuming that it’s in fact a more difficult to use operating system (Orange versus Circlix), that is causing the increased number of calls. Because she utilizes cause-and-effect reasoning, she’s assuming that there is not an alternative explanation for the observation she makes. Is this a good assumption? Could there be an alternative reason for the increased number of calls?
A frameshift answer choice will focus on something other than the connection between the number of calls and the conclusion that Orange is more difficult to use, and such a choice may cast doubt on a conclusion different from the argument’s conclusion, perhaps by focusing either on the number of calls or on the ease-of-use issues, but not on both at the same time.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Whereas, before ten years ago, computer users tended to be experts, now many computer users are novices or occasional users. This choice provides an alternate explanation for the increased number of calls: more people unfamiliar with computers are using computers. These people may be calling the support centers because of their own unfamiliarity or ignorance, not necessarily because the Orange operating system is particularly difficult to use. Also, earlier, when mostly experts used computers, these experts probably resolved issues on their own, without calling support centers.
When an argument involves cause-and-effect reasoning (it’s the new operating system causing the increased number of calls), one solid way to weaken such an argument is to provide a plausible alternative cause (novices using computers today versus mostly expert users ten years ago).
Notice this answer choice attacks the author’s assumption that Orange is causing the increased number of calls.
(B) For many users of personal computers, ease of use is not as important as top notch security. This frameshift choice doesn’t cast doubt on the conclusion of the argument. Instead, it addresses a related issue, the importance of ease of use to computer users.
Notice that the fact that, for many users of personal computers, ease of use is not as important as top-notch security does not conflict with or call into question the conclusion that Orange is more difficult to use than Circlix.
(C) The number of minutes spent on the phone per personal computer technical support call has not significantly increased over the past decade. What this frameshift choice says about the minutes per call does not change the fact that the number of calls has increased. So, this choice does not affect the support for the argument’s conclusion.
(D) Marketers are not concerned that sales of the Orange operating system will be affected by concerns about difficulty using it. The conclusion is not about sales of the Orange operating system, but rather about how difficult Orange is to use. So, this frameshift choice, which is about concerns about sales, does not add any new information that undermines the support for the conclusion of the argument.
(E) The company that produced Orange dedicates far more engineer hours to developing Orange now than the company that produced Circlix dedicated to developing Circlix in the past. This choice may provide reason to believe that the Orange operating system is a high-quality product and, therefore, should not be difficult to use, but we don’t actually have information that clearly indicates a connection between engineer hours and ease of use. In fact, engineers spending all that time working on Orange could have created a very complicated, difficult to use product.
So, this choice is a tempting trap choice that doesn’t clearly affect the conclusion that we are seeking to call into question.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss answers that are pertinent to the situation but do not affect the argument.
summary:
Lesson Content Consider an argument that supports Conclusion X.
When a frameshift occurs, an incorrect answer choice will cast doubt on a conclusion that is related to or very similar to Conclusion X but is not Conclusion X.
If we’re not careful, it’s quite easy to fall for a frameshift because of the similarity between the actual conclusion of the argument and the conclusion called into question by the incorrect choice.
title: 3.9.3 Frameshifts – Choices that Weaken the Support for the Wrong Conclusion

contents:
Lesson Content Some of the most tempting incorrect choices in Weaken questions are choices that mention things that one would likely consider in a real-world review of a situation.
At the same time, while these answer choices may say things worthy of consideration, they do not weaken the arguments that they follow.
💡MUST KNOW:
We must be careful not to be tempted into choosing an answer choice that says something that is valid and is pertinent to what the passage discusses but does not affect the argument. Answer choices can – and often do – appeal to our sensibilities, and yet not affect the argument presented.
Consider the following example:
Several commonly used agricultural pesticides have been linked to increased incidence of cancer in people exposed to them. By banning these pesticides and getting farmers to switch over to using organic farming methods, which do not include the use of such pesticides, we can make food safer to eat.
Which of the following, if true, most seriously calls into question the conclusion drawn?
(Pertinent but Incorrect Choice) Because organically farmed food is often more costly than food farmed via use of the pesticides in question, many people would prefer to be able to choose which to buy.
(Correct Answer) Some forms of dangerous bacteria are more likely to be found on foods produced via the use of organic methods than on other foods.
There are two key attributes of the pertinent but incorrect answer choice above.
The argument is about making food safer to eat.
Notice that the correct answer does in fact weaken the argument by providing a reason why banning these pesticides and getting farmers to switch over to using organic farming methods might not actually make food safer to eat.
Let’s look at another example:
Test results indicate that the new online learning system is a much more effective way for students to learn about many subjects than is a traditional classroom setting. Once the system is implemented in schools nationwide, students will learn more quickly, more effectively, and at their own paces.
Which of the following, if true, is a reason to question the likelihood that the above prediction will prove to be accurate?
(Pertinent but Incorrect Choice) If an educational program is poorly designed, students may score high on tests while not learning the things that would be most useful to them.
(Correct Answer) Effective use of the new online learning system requires types of support that very few members of school staffs know how to provide.
You can see why the pertinent but incorrect choice could be very tempting.
However, the pertinent but incorrect choice does not affect the argument because that choice is not about what the conclusion of the argument is about.
The correct answer, on the other hand, does cast doubt on the conclusion of the argument by indicating that implementing the new online learning system may not produce the expected results because school staff members aren’t ready to support use of the system.
Consider the following example, which includes choices that say something pertinent to the situation yet do not affect the argument.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 29 Politician: Our nation was once a major manufacturing power, but now companies in other countries are competing with ours by manufacturing at much lower costs. Which of the following, if true, is most damaging to the politician’s argument? 1. [ ] (A) The gases and particulate matter put into the atmosphere via worldwide use of fossil fuels are harming air quality and destabilizing the environment. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) For the nation to which the politician is referring, focusing on developing service businesses may make more sense than seeking to compete in manufacturing. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Fuel costs constitute a substantial component of total operating expenses for many manufacturing companies. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Investing in harnessing renewable resources would generate more long-term benefits than would be generated via investing in new fossil fuel extraction technology. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) When one nation stops importing fossil fuels, the resulting change in demand for fuel results in a decrease in worldwide fossil fuel prices. View Solution Question Level: easy The politician is arguing the following:
By using lower cost domestically produced fuel rather than imported fuel, the nation’s manufacturing companies will be able to become competitive once again.
What assumption is being made here? Any time someone proposes a plan, he or she assumes that the plan will work as it has be laid out and that there are not variables that have not been properly considered that will enter the equation and derail the plan. However, as we will see time and time again, this assumption is often a bad one.
For example, what if the country’s companies’ switching to using domestically produced fossils causes a decrease in demand for worldwide fossil fuel that in turn causes a decrease in the price of fossil fuels for companies in other countries?
To arrive at the correct answer, we have to carefully navigate through a set of choices that includes some that seem rather pertinent to the situation and yet have no bearing on the conclusion of the argument.
(A) The gases and particulate matter put into the atmosphere via worldwide use of fossil fuels are harming air quality and destabilizing the environment. This answer choice could be just what someone is thinking while reading an argument about the benefits of using lower cost fossil fuels. Don’t let this answer’s real-life pertinence to the situation tempt you into choosing it though. This choice does not affect the argument, because it does not have anything to do with costs and competitiveness.
Remember – do not fall for an answer choice that appeals to your sensibilities yet does not affect the argument.
(B) For the nation to which the politician is referring, focusing on developing service businesses may make more sense than seeking to compete in manufacturing. This is another interesting but incorrect choice. Yes, it might make more sense to focus on service businesses rather than manufacturing businesses. However, this choice changes nothing about an argument stating that, by using relatively low cost domestically produced fuel, industrial companies can compete effectively.
Also, notice that, if we look carefully at what this answer choice says, we see that it mentions a different plan, a better plan. In general, unless an author claims that his plan is the only plan or the best plan, he need not assume that there are not other plans that could work or assume that there is not a better plan than his. Therefore, the mention of a different plan or a better plan will not weaken the argument.
(C) Fuel costs constitute a substantial component of total operating expenses for many manufacturing companies. By confirming that fuel costs are significant for manufacturing businesses, this answer choice strengthens an argument that concludes that, by reducing their fuel costs, manufacturing companies can become more competitive. So, if anything, this answer choice is a strengthener rather than a weakener.
(D) Investing in harnessing renewable resources would generate more long-term benefits than would be generated via investing in new fossil fuel extraction technology. Whether doing something else would be more beneficial than investing in new fossil fuel extraction technology is certainly pertinent to the discussion at hand but does not affect the support for the conclusion of the argument.
Notice that the conclusion of the argument is simply that, by using lower cost fossil fuels, the nation’s manufacturing companies will be able to become more competitive. Thus, what this choice says about the benefits of investing in harnessing renewable resources has absolutely no effect on the argument’s conclusion, because what this choice says has no direct connection to the relationship between using lower cost fossil fuels and becoming more able to compete.
So, this choice is another one that is pertinent to the discussion but does not affect the argument.
Don’t fall for a better plan trap choice in a Weaken the Argument question. If we’re about to choose an answer choice that mentions a better plan, we better be super sure that the argument will be weakened by the introduction of a better plan.
YOUR CHOICE(E) When one nation stops importing fossil fuels, the resulting change in demand for fuel results in a decrease in worldwide fossil fuel prices. This choice indicates that, if the nation’s industrial companies cease to use imported fuel, the reduction in demand for fuel will cause worldwide fuel prices to decrease. In other words, by using lower cost domestically produced fuel, the manufacturing companies will cause lower fuel prices for their competitors as well.
While it’s not clear whether the cost of fuel outside the nation will drop to the level of the cost of domestically produced fuel, that worldwide fuel prices will drop as a result of the nation’s manufacturing companies’ switching to domestically produced fuel calls into question the idea that the nation’s manufacturing companies will improve their competitive positions by using domestically produced fuel.
Notice that this answer choice presents a malicious variable that the politician did not consider.
Correct Answer: (E) Remember – we must be careful not to be tempted into choosing an answer choice that says something that is valid and that is related to the argument but does not affect the argument.
Let’s now discuss answer choices that appear to do what is not acceptable in the context of the GMAT – undermine premises.
summary:
Lesson Content Some of the most tempting incorrect choices in Weaken questions are choices that mention things that one would likely consider in a real-world review of a situation.
At the same time, while these answer choices may say things worthy of consideration, they do not weaken the arguments that they follow.
💡MUST KNOW:
We must be careful not to be tempted into choosing an answer choice that says something that is valid and is pertinent to what the passage discusses but does not affect the argument.
title: 3.9.4 Pertinent but Incorrect Choices

contents:
Lesson Content As we have discussed, the correct answer to a Weaken the Argument question will never be one that attacks a premise of the argument, because premises in GMAT arguments are taken to be true and therefore cannot be undermined.
💡MUST KNOW:
Premises in GMAT arguments are taken to be true and therefore cannot be undermined. Therefore, an answer choice that says something that appears to undermine a premise will not weaken the argument.
Consider this example:
MaraSport makes running gear meant for people who run in marathons. The number of people entering marathons has been increasing steadily over the past several years, and the trend is expected to continue. Certainly then, sales of MaraSport’s running gear will continue to increase.
Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above?
(Choice that Appears to Attack a Premise) No more marathons were held last year than were held in the previous year.
(Correct Answer) RunningRush, which makes running gear virtually identical to that sold by MaraSport, recently began selling its gear at prices much lower than the prices at which MaraSport sells its gear.
Notice what’s going on in the argument above.
The answer choice that appears to attack a premise says that no more marathons were held last year than were held in the previous year, an assertion that could be seen as conflicting with and therefore undermining the premise that more people are entering marathons.
If we weren’t careful, we might therefore take that answer choice to be a weakener.
Also, notice that the correct answer does weaken the argument, by showing that there is a factor that the author failed to consider.
Here’s another example in which an answer choice may appear to, but does not, undermine a premise.
There are advantages to using the new method to harvest apples on farms, advantages that make the new method clearly superior to any method now commonly used. Therefore, the new method will soon become the one most widely used.
Which of the following, if true, most seriously calls into question the above prediction?
(Choice that Appears to Attack a Premise) The difference between the effectiveness of apple harvesting methods currently used and the effectiveness of the new method is not as great when a particularly well-trained team uses the methods.
(Correct Answer) The cost of the new equipment necessary for apple farmers to switch to the new method is more than what most are willing to spend. If we were not paying close attention, we might determine that the choice that appears to attack a premise weakens the argument.
We’re told that “there are advantages to using the new method to harvest apples on farms, advantages that make the new method clearly superior to any method now commonly used.” In other words, we must accept as fact that the new method is clearly superior to any method commonly used.
Notice that the correct answer, on the other hand, does weaken the argument by providing a malicious variable that the author failed to consider.
💡MUST KNOW:
Beware of answer choices that pose as weakeners by appearing to undermine premises of arguments.
The following examples include multiple answer choices that appear to attack the premises of the arguments.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 30 When companies in growing industries experience profit growth, they generally increase their numbers of employees. Which of the following, if true, casts the most doubt on the prediction made? 1. [ ] (A) Until three years ago, SuperPower had not had a profitable year. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Overall battery industry growth is not as great as growth currently occurring in some other industries. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) The percentage growth in profits experienced by SuperPower last year was not as great as the profit growth the company experienced the year before last year. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Engineers at SuperPower recently figured out how to automate what has been one of the most labor-intensive aspects of SuperPower’s manufacturing processes. 5. [ ] (E) While SuperPower has five divisions, only one of the five is experiencing significant growth in profits. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy This argument is based on two key premises. The first premise is the existence of a pattern.
Premise: When companies in growing industries experience profit growth, they generally increase their numbers of employees.
The second premise is about SuperPower specifically.
Premise: SuperPower is experiencing a surge in profits as the battery industry grows as a result of certain trends.
Combined, the two premises support the conclusion.
Conclusion: SuperPower can be expected to increase its number of employees soon.
To weaken this argument, we have to call into question the idea that the conclusion follows from the premises. What will not work, because this is a GMAT question, is seeking to undermine the premises themselves. So, to get this question right, we have to be careful to avoid being tempted into choosing an answer choice that seems to, though doesn’t really, undermine one or more of the premises of the argument.
(A) Until three years ago, SuperPower had not had a profitable year. This choice could be tempting if one were to perceive it as weakening the premise that SuperPower’s profits are growing.
Of course, we can’t weaken a premise, and the truth is that, even if SuperPower has been profitable for only a few years, it is still profitable and still experiencing profit growth currently. So, this choice does not weaken the support for the conclusion.
(B) Overall battery industry growth is not as great as growth currently occurring in some other industries. This choice is written to seem to somehow undermine the premise that the battery industry is growing enough to warrant hiring new employees.
Notice, however, that this choice does not actually conflict with the idea that the battery industry is growing. While the battery industry is not growing as fast as some other industries are, it’s still growing.
So, this choice does not really affect the support for the conclusion.
(C) The percentage growth in profits experienced by SuperPower last year was not as great as the profit growth the company experienced the year before last year. This choice is an attempt to create the impression that SuperPower’s profits are not growing, and, thus, to attack the premise that SuperPower is experiencing profit growth.
However, slower profit growth is still profit growth, and the fact stated in the passage, that SuperPower has been experiencing a surge in profit growth, remains true.
So, this choice does not really affect the support for the conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Engineers at SuperPower recently figured out how to automate what has been one of the most labor-intensive aspects of SuperPower’s manufacturing processes. By saying that engineers at SuperPower have figured out how to automate what has been a labor-intensive aspect of SuperPower’s manufacturing processes, this choice indicates that SuperPower may be able to increase production without hiring more employees, or even while employing fewer people.
So, this choice clearly weakens the support for the prediction that SuperPower will soon increase the number of people it employs.
This answer choice presents us with a malicious variable that the author has failed to consider.
(E) While SuperPower has five divisions, only one of the five is experiencing significant growth in profits. By saying that only one of SuperPower’s five divisions is experiencing profit growth, this choice might seem to undermine the premise that SuperPower is experiencing profit growth. However, the argument states as fact that SuperPower is experiencing a surge in profit growth, and the fact that growth at only one of five divisions is responsible for that surge in profit growth does not change the fact that that surge in profit growth is occurring.
So, this choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 31 In the past, electric motorcycles were impractical because of their heavy weights, below average performance, and short ranges. Which of the following, if true, most serves to call into question the above prediction? 1. [ ] (A) By choosing a smaller, more basic gasoline powered motorcycle, a rider can come close to duplicating the low operating costs of an electric motorcycle. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Duplicating in an electric motorcycle the range of a gasoline powered motorcycle will require significant innovations in the design of batteries that power electric motorcycles. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) A significant percentage of motorcycle riders base motorcycle purchasing decisions on sensory qualities, such as the roaring exhaust sound made by some gasoline powered engines. 4. [ ] (D) Some of the best engineered gasoline powered motorcycles are not much more difficult to maintain than are electric motorcycles. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Past years’ sales data show that electric motorcycles have not been popular with younger riders, who will make up a large percentage of purchasers of motorcycles over the next decade. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium The speaker argues the following:
Premise: Electric motorcycle weight, performance, and range will soon match the weight, performance, and range of gasoline powered motorcycles.
Premise: Electric motorcycles are easier to maintain and cost less to operate than gasoline powered motorcycles.
Conclusion: Within a decade, electric motorcycles will have almost entirely replaced gasoline powered motorcycles on the roads.
What assumption is the author making here? What gap lies between the premises and the conclusion?
Since this is a Weaken question, we will have to find an answer that undermines the idea that the conclusion about electric motorcycles replacing gasoline powered motorcycles follows from the premises.
Also, in order to get this one correct, we’ll have to avoid choosing a choice that seems to undermine a premise.
(A) By choosing a smaller, more basic gasoline powered motorcycle, a rider can come close to duplicating the low operating costs of an electric motorcycle. This choice says something that could be construed as weakening the premise that electric motorcycles cost less to operate than gasoline powered motorcycles.
Notice, however, that, in spite of what this choice says, that premise is still true.
Also, by the way, this choice implies that only smaller, more basic motorcycles come close to electric motorcycles in terms of operating costs.
So, this choice does not weaken the argument.
(B) Duplicating in an electric motorcycle the range of a gasoline powered motorcycle will require significant innovations in the design of batteries that power electric motorcycles. This choice may appear to be counter to the premise that soon the range of electric motorcycles will match that of gasoline powered motorcycles, but is it really?
The truth is that this choice merely discusses the path to matching the range. The premise is still true.
So, this choice does not affect the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(C) A significant percentage of motorcycle riders base motorcycle purchasing decisions on sensory qualities, such as the roaring exhaust sound made by some gasoline powered engines. This choice does undermine the conclusion, because it indicates that, in spite of all the premises being true, there is reason to believe that a significant percentage of people who ride motorcycles will continue to ride gasoline powered motorcycles. Their choices of motorcycles are based on sensory qualities, rather than on the qualities, such as cost to operate, in which electric motorcycles have an advantage.
Notice that this answer choice presents us with a malicious variable that the author has failed to consider.
(D) Some of the best engineered gasoline powered motorcycles are not much more difficult to maintain than are electric motorcycles. This choice may appear to counter the premise that electric motorcycles are easier to maintain than are gasoline powered motorcycles.
Notice however, it does not truly conflict with the premise. It merely qualifies the situation, and the fact remains; electric motorcycles are easier to maintain. Besides, the conclusion says “almost entirely replace.” Even if a few people would continue to use “some of the best engineered” gasoline motorcycles, the support for the conclusion would not be weakened.
So, this choice does not affect the argument.
(E) Past years’ sales data show that electric motorcycles have not been popular with younger riders, who will make up a large percentage of purchasers of motorcycles over the next decade. The argument is based on characteristics electric motorcycles have now and will have in the future. So, data from sales of electric motorcycles in the past, when electric motorcycles did not have those characteristics and were “impractical,” does not affect the argument.
Correct Answer: (C) Now that we have discussed various parts of Weaken the Argument questions and how to work with them, let’s wrap up our discussion by considering some types of tricky question stems that we need to be on the lookout for.
summary:
Lesson Content As we have discussed, the correct answer to a Weaken the Argument question will never be one that attacks a premise of the argument, because premises in GMAT arguments are taken to be true and therefore cannot be undermined.
💡MUST KNOW:
Premises in GMAT arguments are taken to be true and therefore cannot be undermined. Therefore, an answer choice that says something that appears to undermine a premise will not weaken the argument.
title: 3.9.5 Answer Choices That Appear to Attack Premises

contents:
Lesson Content Weaken the Argument questions will sometimes include the word “except” or “not” in their question stems to make the questions a bit more challenging.
Any of the following, if true, undermines support for the speaker’s claim EXCEPT:
Which of the following, if true, does not weaken the argument above?
When a Weaken question has the word “except” or “not” in the stem, four out of the five answer choices will weaken the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
When a Weaken question has the word “except” or “not” in the stem, four out of the five answer choices will weaken the argument. These answer choices will be incorrect. The correct answer will be the one that does not weaken the argument.
Since an “except” or “not” question asks us to do the opposite of what we normally do in answering an Weaken question, in order to avoid becoming confused when answering such a question, we can mark each of the choices with a “Yes,” if the choice undermines the support for the argument (weakens the argument), or with a “No,” if the choice does not undermine the support for the argument (does not weaken the argument).
When we are done labeling, we will have labeled only one option “No” (assuming we did the labeling correctly), and that option will be the answer.
Consider the following complicated question stem:
Each of the following, if true, would counter the objection to the interpretation advanced above EXCEPT:
When we encounter a stem like this, we should follow the below four steps:
(1) Identify the “interpretation” advanced in the argument.
(2) Identify the objection to this interpretation in the argument.
(3) For every answer choice, ask yourself, “Does this answer choice counter (weaken the support for) the objection?” - YES or NO
(4) The answer choice for which you have written NO is the correct answer.
Below is another complicated question stem that asks for a single statement that simultaneously weakens one hypothesis while strengthening another:
Which of the following, if discovered in experiments, would support one of the two hypotheses and undermine the other?
Though the words “except” and “not” do not appear in the above question stem, the question demands a similar organized process.
(1) Identify the two hypotheses.
(2) For every option, ask yourself “Does this option support hypothesis 1?” H1-YES or H1-NO
(3) For every option, ask yourself “Does this option support hypothesis 2?” H2-YES or H2-NO
(4) The answer choice with one YES and one NO is the correct one.
💡MUST KNOW:
When a Weaken the Argument question contains the words “except” or “not,” follow a step-by-step approach in evaluating the options.
Let’s practice handling tricky question stems in Weaken questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 32 Caps and Crowns Dentistry plans to acquire new dental patients at its dental office by offering a free examination, dental cleaning, and set of x-rays for all new patients. All of the following, if true, weaken the argument, EXCEPT: 1. [ ] (A) Generally, people who go to a dental office as a result of its offering free examination, dental cleaning, and x-rays do not return to that office for other dental services. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) The materials used by Caps and Crowns Dentistry in performing an examination and a dental cleaning and creating a set of x-rays cost the office $2,600. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Caps and Crowns Dentistry is staffed with some of the most qualified dentists practicing in any office within a 50-mile radius of Caps and Crowns. 4. [ ] (D) Because they take unusually good care of their dental health, the vast majority of patients whom the plan would attract spend very little on dental procedures during any two-year period. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) People who would patronize Caps and Crowns Dentistry are unlikely to spend money on dental procedures for at least three years because of a downturn in the local economy. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Caps and Crowns Dentistry plans to acquire new dental patients at its dental office by offering a free examination, dental cleaning, and set of x-rays for all new patients.
Premise: Nationwide data indicates that, on average, each dental patient spends $2,500 on dental procedures during any two-year period.
Conclusion: Over the next two years, Caps and Crowns Dentistry will get from the new patients that it acquires by implementing the plan revenue that will exceed the cost of implementing the plan.
Caps and Crowns Dentistry has a plan. Any time someone presents a plan, he assumes that the plan will work. In other words, he assumes that there are not variables that will enter the equation that will spoil the plan. In the TTP Course, we refer to such variables as “malicious variables.” When an author presents a plan, he assumes that there are no malicious variables. What malicious variables might Caps and Crowns Dentistry have missed?
Since this question is a Weaken EXCEPT question, we have to go through the five choices asking ourselves in each case, “Does this choice present a factor that will weaken the conclusion?”
In four cases, the answer will be “Yes.” We will eliminate those choices.
In one case, the answer will be “No,” and that choice will be our answer.
(A) Generally, people who go to a dental office as a result of its offering free examination, dental cleaning, and x-rays do not return to that office for other dental services. What this choice says weakens the argument.
