The fact that, generally, people who go to a dental office as a result of its offering free examination, dental cleaning, and x-rays do not return to that office for other dental services is a reason to question the conclusion that Caps and Crowns Dentistry will get from the new patients revenue that will exceed the cost of implementing the plan.
After all, if the new patients won’t return for other services, Cap and Crowns Dentistry is likely to generate no revenue at all by offering free services for new patients.
Weakens the argument? Yes.
(B) The materials used by Caps and Crowns Dentistry in performing an examination and a dental cleaning and creating a set of x-rays cost the office $2,600. What this choice says weakens the argument.
The passage tells us that, on average, each dental patient spends $2,500 on dental procedures during any two-year period. So, if the materials used by Caps and Crowns Dentistry in performing an examination and a dental cleaning and creating a set of x-rays cost the office $2,600, then Caps and Crowns Dentistry would not earn over the next two years revenue that will exceed the cost of implementing the plan.
Weakens the argument? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Caps and Crowns Dentistry is staffed with some of the most qualified dentists practicing in any office within a 50-mile radius of Caps and Crowns. What this answer choice says goes in the wrong direction.
Rather than weaken the argument, this answer choice says gives us more faith in the conclusion.
After all, if Caps and Crowns Dentistry is staffed with some of the most qualified dentists practicing in any office within a 50-mile radius of Caps and Crowns, then we have more reason to think that new patients will stick around for long enough for Caps and Crowns Dentistry to earn its money back.
So, since this choice strengthens, rather than weakens, the support for the conclusion, this choice is the correct answer to this Weaken EXCEPT question.
Weakens the argument? No.
(D) Because they take unusually good care of their dental health, the vast majority of patients whom the plan would attract spend very little on dental procedures during any two-year period. What this answer choice says weakens the argument.
The fact that, because they take unusually good care of their dental health, the vast majority of patients whom the plan would attract spend very little on dental procedures during any two-year period is a reason to question the conclusion that Caps and Crowns Dentistry will earn over the next two years revenue that will exceed the cost of implementing the plan.
After all, if the new patients will spend very little, then Caps and Crowns Dentistry will earn very little revenue by acquiring them.
Weakens the argument? Yes.
(E) People who would patronize Caps and Crowns Dentistry are unlikely to spend money on dental procedures for at least three years because of a downturn in the local economy. What this answer choice says weakens the argument.
The fact that people who would patronize Caps and Crowns Dentistry are unlikely to spend money on dental procedures for at least three years because of a downturn in the local economy is a reason to question the conclusion that Caps and Crowns Dentistry will earn over the next two years revenue that will exceed the cost of implementing the plan.
How will Caps and Crowns Dentistry get revenue in the next two years from patients who won’t spend money for at least three years?
Weakens the argument? Yes.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 33 Bans on smoking inside public establishments have been shown to be more effective at reducing per capita cigarette sales in colder climates than in warmer ones. All of the following, if true, weaken the argument, EXCEPT: 1. [ ] (A) Boselton is known for its unique success in getting people to quit smoking through a program that has enabled all but the most diehard smokers living there to quit. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) A significant county-wide increase in taxes on cigarettes will go into effect simultaneously with the ban. 3. [ ] (C) In comparison with residents of other cities in the county, residents of Boselton spend a much smaller proportion of their time in public establishments. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) In Boselton, unlike in other cities in the county, it is generally the case that the people who smoke are not the same people who tend to frequent public establishments. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Unlike most public establishments located in other cities in the county, few in Boselton currently allow people to smoke inside. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard This argument is based on two premises:
Premise: Bans on smoking inside public establishments have been shown to be more effective at reducing per capita cigarette sales in colder climates than in warmer ones.
Premise: Boselton is much colder than other cities in the county.
Given these premises, it does seem logical to conclude the following:
Conclusion: Boselton will be the city in its county that experiences the greatest decrease in per capita cigarettes sales as the result of a ban on smoking in public establishments.
The truth is, however, that there could be, and probably are, factors other than climate that will affect the results of the ban.
Since this question asks for the one choice that does not weaken the argument, we have to go through the five choices, asking ourselves in each case, “Does this choice present a factor that will cast doubt on the conclusion about the ban affecting Boselton the most?” In four cases, the answer will be “Yes.” In one case, the answer will be “No,” and that choice will be our answer.
(A) Boselton is known for its unique success in getting people to quit smoking through a program that has enabled all but the most diehard smokers living there to quit. By mentioning that Boselton has had “unique success,” this choice differentiates Boselton from other cities. What’s different about Boselton? Most smokers there have quit, and only diehard smokers are left. Of course, there’s a good chance that a ban on smoking in public establishments will not cause diehard smokers to significantly reduce their smoking. So, this choice gives us reason to question the conclusion that Boselton will experience the greatest decrease in per capita cigarette sales as a result of the ban.
Furthermore, the conclusion is about a “per capita” decrease in cigarette sales. “Per capita” cigarette sales in a given town is defined as the following:
total cigarette sales in a given town / total population in that town
Because, in Boselton, most of the people have already quit smoking, per capita cigarette sales in Boselton would already be very low. In other words, the numerator of the fraction has already dropped significantly. Therefore, we would not expect to see another large drop in Boselton’s per capita cigarette sales.
Meanwhile, we know that Boselton is unique in this aspect. In other words, the other towns have not experienced already a large drop in the number of smokers, and so we’d expect per capita cigarette sales to be much higher in those towns than in Boselton. Thus, we’d expect the indoor smoking ban to have a larger impact on per capita cigarette sales in these towns than in Boselton.
Weakener? Yes.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) A significant county-wide increase in taxes on cigarettes will go into effect simultaneously with the ban. Since this tax will affect all cities in the county equally, there’s no clear reason to believe that this tax will somehow offset the fact that Boselton is the coldest city and thus should experience the biggest effect from the ban.
Weakener? No.
YOUR CHOICE(C) In comparison with residents of other cities in the county, residents of Boselton spend a much smaller proportion of their time in public establishments. The effect on per capita cigarette sales comes from people’s not smoking in public establishments. So, if people in Boselton don’t spend as much time in public establishments as people in other cities do, then they, and their purchases of cigarettes, won’t be affected by the ban as much as people in other cities will be. Thus, even though Boselton is colder, its per capita cigarette sales may not decrease as much as those of other cities will.
Weakener? Yes.
(D) In Boselton, unlike in other cities in the county, it is generally the case that the people who smoke are not the same people who tend to frequent public establishments. The effect on per capita cigarette sales comes from people’s not smoking in public establishments. If, unlike the smokers in other cities, the people in Boselton who smoke don’t even go to public establishment, then their smoking won’t be affected the way the smoking of people in other cities will. Thus, even though Boselton is colder, its per capita cigarette sales may not decrease as much as those of other cities will.
Weakener? Yes.
(E) Unlike most public establishments located in other cities in the county, few in Boselton currently allow people to smoke inside. If, unlike the public establishments in other cities in the county, most public establishments in Boselton have already banned smoking inside, then, in Boselton, unlike in the other cities, the ban won’t make much difference in people’s smoking in public establishments and, thus, won’t have much effect on per capita cigarette sales.
Weakener? Yes.
Correct Answer: (B)
summary:
Lesson Content Weaken the Argument questions will sometimes include the word “except” or “not” in their question stems to make the questions a bit more challenging.
Any of the following, if true, undermines support for the speaker’s claim EXCEPT:
Which of the following, if true, does not weaken the argument above?
When a Weaken question has the word “except” or “not” in the stem, four out of the five answer choices will weaken the argument.
title: 3.10 Tricky Weaken Question Stems: EXCEPT and NOT

contents:
Lesson Content Strengthen the Argument questions are among the most common types of GMAT Critical Reasoning Questions.
The good news is that, as promised, the skills that you learned in the Assumption Chapter – and reinforced in the Weaken the Argument Chapter – will be significant assets to you as you learn to handle Strengthen the Argument questions.
Let’s begin discussing how to effectively strengthen an argument.
summary:
Lesson Content Strengthen the Argument questions are among the most common types of GMAT Critical Reasoning Questions.
The good news is that, as promised, the skills that you learned in the Assumption Chapter – and reinforced in the Weaken the Argument Chapter – will be significant assets to you as you learn to handle Strengthen the Argument questions.
Let’s begin discussing how to effectively strengthen an argument.
title: 4.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content As just mentioned, strengthening an argument involves providing new information that strengthens the support for the argument’s conclusion.
To see strengthening in action, let’s consider an example:
Alice knows all about making smoothies, and she makes smoothies that all her friends like. Clearly, her new smoothie shop will be a success. We can strengthen that argument by providing new information that makes the case for the conclusion more convincing.
Her new smoothie shop will be a success.
So, to strengthen the argument, we need information that makes us more convinced that Alice’s new smoothie shop will be a success.
Alice’s new smoothie shop is going to be located right next to a bike path on which thousands of people who like smoothies bike every day.
Wow.
Now, as we saw in the case of the above example, before we can strengthen an argument, we must first understand the logic of that argument.
Also, in the Assumption chapter and the Weaken the Argument chapter, a major theme presented was that of identifying the missing information in arguments.
To see how identifying missing information applies to strengthening arguments, let’s revisit the disease x argument that we discussed in previous chapters:
All people in houses in which communicable diseases are present should be quarantined. Two people in the yellow house on Maple Street came down with disease x yesterday. Therefore, all people in the yellow house on Maple Street should be quarantined.
From the past chapters, you already know that the author assumes that disease x is a communicable disease.
So, one way in which we could strengthen this argument is to make it known that disease x is in fact a communicable disease.
So, as you progress through this chapter, it’s important to continue to look for missing information.
Consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 1 It has been discovered that the larvae of a certain kind of wax moth can eat polyethylene, a type of plastic. Which of the following, if true, most strongly supports the hypothesis? 1. [ ] (A) Wax moth larvae normally hatch and grow on beeswax, which is somewhat similar chemically to polyethylene. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) When wax moth larvae consume polyethylene, they convert it into a non-plastic compound. 3. [ ] (C) People create approximately 80 million tons of polyethylene per year. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) The large amounts of plastic in landfills and ocean dumps are reducing the quality of life on our planet. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) It may be that the wax moth larvae would not continue to consume plastic should they encounter it outside of the specific conditions created in laboratory experiments. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: The larvae of a certain kind of wax moth can eat polyethylene, a type of plastic.
Premise: The larvae can consume large quantities of polyethylene relatively quickly.
Conclusion: The use of such moth larvae in large numbers could significantly help in the reduction of plastic pollution.
The scientists’ hypothesis is supported by the facts that the larvae eat plastic and that they eat it quickly. Those facts provide some reason to believe that the use of such moth larvae in large numbers could significantly help in the reduction of plastic pollution.
Now, to correctly answer the question, we need to find the choice that provides additional support for that hypothesis.
(A) Wax moth larvae normally hatch and grow on beeswax, which is somewhat similar chemically to polyethylene. This choice doesn’t have the effect we need. It may help to explain why the larvae eat plastic, but we already know that they eat plastic, and explaining why that fact is true does not make the support for the conclusion any stronger.
So, this choice does not affect the support for the conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(B) When wax moth larvae consume polyethylene, they convert it into a non-plastic compound. The choice provides additional support for the conclusion.
We already know that the larvae eat plastic.
Now, by confirming that, when the larvae eat plastic, they change the plastic into something other than plastic, this choice provides additional support for the conclusion that the use of the larvae could significantly help in the reduction of plastic pollution.
After all, the fact that, when the larvae eat plastic, the larvae don’t just chop the plastic into little pieces of plastic but instead change it into something other than plastic makes the case for the conclusion more convincing than it was.
(C) People create approximately 80 million tons of polyethylene per year. While this choice tends to confirm that, if the larvae could be used to reduce plastic pollution, such use would be quite valuable, it does not provide information that confirms that the larvae could in fact be used in that way.
(D) The large amounts of plastic in landfills and ocean dumps are reducing the quality of life on our planet. While this choice provides a reason for reducing the amount of plastic on the planet, it does not confirm that using the larvae would be an effective way of reducing the amount of plastic.
(E) It may be that the wax moth larvae would not continue to consume plastic should they encounter it outside of the specific conditions created in laboratory experiments. This choice weakens, rather than strengthens, the argument, by providing a reason to question the effectiveness of using wax moth larvae to reduce plastic pollution.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s next examine how to recognize when we’re being asked to strengthen an argument.
summary:
Lesson Content As just mentioned, strengthening an argument involves providing new information that strengthens the support for the argument’s conclusion.
To see strengthening in action, let’s consider an example:
Alice knows all about making smoothies, and she makes smoothies that all her friends like. Clearly, her new smoothie shop will be a success. We can strengthen that argument by providing new information that makes the case for the conclusion more convincing.
title: 4.2 Understanding How to Effectively Strengthen an Argument

contents:
Lesson Content Most of the time, recognizing a Strengthen the Argument question is easy because these questions utilize predictable language.
Which of the following, if true, most strongly supports _____?
Which of the following, if true, provides the strongest grounds for _____?
Which of the following, if true, most helps to justify _____?
Which of the following, if true, provides the best reason for _____?
Which of the following, if true, would strengthen _____?
Which of the following, if true, provides the most support for _____?
Which of the following, if true, does the most to strengthen the argument?
Any of the following statements, if true, helps to justify _____ EXCEPT:
Which of the following, if true, is further evidence that _____?
It’s important that we be ready to recognize these common prompts.
💡MUST KNOW:
Learn to recognize common Strengthen the Argument question stems.
Consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 2 A major multinational company has instituted a program in which women who work in the factories that produce the garments that the company sells receive training in life skills and problem solving over a period of approximately three months. Which of the following, if true, provides the most significant support for the company’s expectation? 1. [ ] (A) The number of women who have indicated that they are interested in participating in the program is sufficient for filling every available slot. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Many of the women who work at the company’s factories have not previously had many opportunities to develop good life skills. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Even some of the women employed by the company who have more life experience than most others feel that they can gain from participating in the program. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) There is some question among experts regarding whether adults can develop their life skills significantly over a period of a few months. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Lack of life or problem solving skills can underlie the development of ongoing issues that can interfere with people’s ability to work or remain employed. View Solution Question Level: easy In order to answer the question, we need an answer choice that provides support for the following expectation:
Conclusion: The program will pay for itself by resulting in greater productivity at the factories.
It’s important to notice that this argument provides no evidence to support the conclusion. After all, a conclusion is just an author’s opinion; it is his or her interpretation of some information. What information does the passage provide? The passage says that the company “has instituted a program in which women who work in the factories that produce the garments that the company sells receive training in life skills and problem solving over a period of approximately three months.” Does that fact necessarily allow the author to conclude that the program will pay for itself by resulting in greater productivity at the factories? No, it does not. After all, from the same fact the author could have just as well concluded that the program WILL NOT pay for itself by resulting in greater productivity at the factories. Right? In either case, there would be no support presented in the passage.
We’ll often see in a Strengthen the Argument question a situation in which a fact is presented, followed by an author’s conclusion, yet there is no support provided to justify that conclusion. Of course, without support for the conclusion, the argument is weak. Our job then is to seek an answer choice that provides actual support for the conclusion.
(A) The number of women who have indicated that they are interested in participating in the program is sufficient for filling every available slot. While, in order for the program to work, women have to sign up for it, the fact that every slot can be filled does not clearly support the conclusion that the program will result in greater productivity at the factories.
(B) Many of the women who work at the company’s factories have not previously had many opportunities to develop good life skills. This choice supports the wrong conclusion. It supports the conclusion that the program could benefit the women who sign up for it. However, that’s not the conclusion that we have to support.
(C) Even some of the women employed by the company who have more life experience than most others feel that they can gain from participating in the program. While the women may be correct in thinking that they will gain from participating in the program, the conclusion that we have to support is not that the women will gain from participating in the program.
(D) There is some question among experts regarding whether adults can develop their life skills significantly over a period of a few months. If anything, the fact that there is some question among experts regarding whether adults can develop their life skills significantly over a period of a few months weakens rather than strengthens the support for the conclusion, because it provides reason to question whether the program will have much of an effect.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Lack of life or problem solving skills can underlie the development of ongoing issues that can interfere with people’s ability to work or remain employed. If, as this choice says, lack of life or problem solving skills can underlie the development of ongoing issues that can interfere with people’s ability to work or remain employed, then by helping the women to develop life skills, the program could prevent the development of issues detrimental to women’s productivity at the factories. Thus, this choice provides solid support for the expectation that offering the program will result in increased productivity at the company’s factories and that, thus, the program could pay for itself.
Notice that, if we now break this argument down, it’s considerable stronger with this answer choice added as a premise than the argument was as presented to us in the stem:
Background: A major multinational company has instituted a program in which women who work in the factories that produce the garments that the company sells receive training in life skills and problem solving over a period of approximately three months.
Premise: Lack of life or problem solving skills can underlie the development of ongoing issues that can interfere with people’s ability to work or remain employed.
Conclusion: Even though the skills that the women develop via the training are not directly related to the production of garments, the company expects the program to pay for itself by resulting in greater productivity at the factories.
We see that the addition of the premise has greatly strengthened the argument, giving us reason to believe that the author’s conclusion is valid.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss a systematic strategy that you can use when answering Strengthen the Argument questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Most of the time, recognizing a Strengthen the Argument question is easy because these questions utilize predictable language.
Which of the following, if true, most strongly supports _____?
Which of the following, if true, provides the strongest grounds for _____?
Which of the following, if true, most helps to justify _____?
Which of the following, if true, provides the best reason for _____?
Which of the following, if true, would strengthen _____?
title: 4.3 Recognizing a Strengthen the Argument Question

contents:
Lesson Content To find the correct answer to a Strengthen question, follow this 6-step strategy.
Step 1 – Carefully read and understand the passage.
The foundation of arriving at the correct answer to a Strengthen question is understanding the passage.
The importance of understanding the passage cannot be overemphasized.
So, your first job in answering a Strengthen question is to carefully read the passage, developing understanding of what the passage says and noting key details of the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
The foundation for arriving at the correct answer to a Strengthen question is understanding the passage.
Step 2 – Read the question stem and identify what it is asking you to do.
Sometimes people confuse Strengthen the Argument questions with Weaken the Argument questions.
Step 3 – Go back to the passage and determine exactly what the conclusion of the argument is.
At this point, you understand the passage and know that you have to strengthen the argument.
Of course, the correct answer will somehow strengthen the support for the conclusion.
So, the next step is to go back to the passage and identify exactly what the conclusion is.
Identifying the exact conclusion of the argument is possibly the single most crucial step you will take in answering a Strengthen question.
If you have only a vague idea of what the conclusion is, you can easily fall for trap choices that strengthen the support for the wrong conclusion.
So, identify exactly what the conclusion is.
Step 4 – Identify how the conclusion is supported.
In some Strengthen questions, there are no premises in the passages.
In other Strengthen questions, there are in the passages supporting premises that we have to consider in answering the questions.
Step 5 – Remove “weakeners” and the choices that most obviously do not affect the argument.
Just as in a Weaken the Argument question, in a Strengthen the Argument question, there are usually only two serious contenders for the “best” answer among the five answer choices.
Although we’ll often find that three of the answer choices will be fairly easily classified as “weakeners” or choices that have no impact, we must read all five of the answer choices carefully before we start eliminating.
Now – and this is a major point – often people quickly eliminate the correct answers to Strengthen questions because they don’t see the connections between the correct answers and the arguments.
In fact, often the correct answer to a Strengthen question can look irrelevant at first, especially if it discusses something that was not mentioned in the argument.
So, in your first pass through the answer choices in a Strengthen the Argument question, it may make sense to keep some choices that you are not really sure about, even if your doing so means that you are left with more than two choices to work with.
Step 6 – Choose the answer choice that most clearly strengthens the argument.
Once we’ve narrowed the answer choices down to the final two or three, we must ask ourselves the following: Which of these most clearly strengthens the support for the conclusion?
One choice will have a clear strengthening impact on the support for the conclusion.
As the chapter progresses, we’ll continue to talk about the importance of precision in reading, and we know not to underestimate the impact that a single word can have on an answer choice.
Also, keep in mind that, in Strengthen the Argument questions, the correct answer need not discuss only information similar to what was discussed in the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Strengthen the Argument questions, as in Weaken the Argument questions, the correct answer need not discuss information similar to what was discussed in the passage. In other words, Strengthen the Argument correct answers are famous for presenting new information.
In fact, a hallmark of a more difficult Strengthen the Argument question is that the correct answer may well appear to be “out of scope,” although it does in fact strengthen the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
A hallmark of a more difficult Strengthen the Argument question is that the correct answer may well appear to be “out of scope,” although it does in fact strengthen the argument.
Summary of the Strategy for Strengthen the Argument Questions
To summarize, here is the 6-step strategy that you can use to answer Strengthen questions correctly:
💡MUST KNOW:
Memorize and practice the strategy for answering Strengthen the Argument questions.
Now that you have a robust strategy for answering Strengthen questions, let’s discuss some ways to strengthen an argument.
summary:
Lesson Content To find the correct answer to a Strengthen question, follow this 6-step strategy.
Step 1 – Carefully read and understand the passage.
The foundation of arriving at the correct answer to a Strengthen question is understanding the passage.
The importance of understanding the passage cannot be overemphasized.
So, your first job in answering a Strengthen question is to carefully read the passage, developing understanding of what the passage says and noting key details of the passage.
title: 4.4 Strategy for Strengthen the Argument Questions

contents:
Lesson Content While there are many ways in which an argument can be strengthened, the following five ways show up consistently in GMAT Strengthen the Argument questions:
Provide a Premise to Support or Add Support for the Conclusion Explicitly State an Assumption upon which the Argument Depends Address an Issue that Could Affect the Validity of the Conclusion Rule out an Alternate Cause of an Effect Confirm a Conclusion by Changing, Adding, or Eliminating a Variable Let’s discuss each of these.
summary:
Lesson Content While there are many ways in which an argument can be strengthened, the following five ways show up consistently in GMAT Strengthen the Argument questions:
Provide a Premise to Support or Add Support for the Conclusion Explicitly State an Assumption upon which the Argument Depends Address an Issue that Could Affect the Validity of the Conclusion Rule out an Alternate Cause of an Effect Confirm a Conclusion by Changing, Adding, or Eliminating a Variable Let’s discuss each of these.
title: 4.5 Common Ways to Strengthen an Argument

contents:
Lesson Content In some Strengthen the Argument questions, the passages contain conclusions without any supporting premises.
💡MUST KNOW:
In some Strengthen the Argument questions, the passages contain conclusions without any supporting premises. In other questions, the premises within the arguments only partially support the conclusions.
In any of these cases, an effective way for an answer choice to strengthen the argument is to provide a premise, which will either provide the only support for the conclusion or provide further support for the conclusion alongside existing premises.
💡MUST KNOW:
An effective way for an answer choice to strengthen the argument is to provide a premise, which will either provide the only support for the conclusion or provide further support for the conclusion alongside existing premises.
Remember, it is not necessary for the premise provided by the correct answer to prove that the conclusion is definitely correct; rather the support provided by the answer choice just has to make the conclusion more likely to be true.
💡MUST KNOW:
It is not necessary for the premise provided by the correct answer to prove that the conclusion is definitely correct; rather the support provided by the answer choice just has to make the conclusion more likely to be true.
Consider the following example.
Breezetricity, a company that specializes in using windmills to generate electrical power, plans to install a large windmill generation facility in the developing country Urdall. Even though it is expected that, for at least the next decade, demand for electricity in Urdall will be exceeded by the generating capacity of three coal fired generating facilities located in Urdall, Breezetricity expects its new windmill generation facility in Urdall to be profitable within five years.
Which of the following, if true, mostly strongly justifies Breezetricity’s contention?
We see that in the passage there is essentially no support for Breezetricity’s contention that the plant will be profitable.
(Correct Answer) Several international corporations that are interested in building manufacturing plants in Urdall have stated that they will do so only if there is available to them in Urdall a source of pollution free power.
The above statement provides reason to believe that there will be demand for the power generated by Breezetricity’s windmill facility.
💡MUST KNOW:
An argument can be strengthened via the addition of a premise that supports the conclusion.
In the Breezetricity example, the passage contained no premises to support the contention made by Breezetricity.
In the following example, there is a premise that supports the conclusion, but there is still room for more support from a correct answer.
The government of Khartona has decided to address the high rate of diabetes in Khartona by adding a 20 percent tax to the purchase price of sugary drinks. Since sugary drinks are the source of most of the refined sugar consumed by people in Khartona, the government’s position is that imposing the tax will result in a significant reduction in people’s consumption of refined sugar.
Which of the following, if true, provides the most support for the government’s position?
In the above passage, there is some support for the government’s position.
Consider the following: What if people in Khartona would respond to the tax by reducing their consumption of sugary drinks but would then consume sugar in another way? Wouldn’t that situation be a problem for the plan?
Alternatively, what if sugary drinks were so inexpensive that a 20 percent tax added to the prices of sugary drinks would not have much effect on people’s consumption of such drinks? For example, what if these drinks were 10 cents each? Would people really stop consuming them if their price became 12 cents each?
In other words, although the author provides us with some support for her conclusion, there is still considerable room available for strengthening the argument.
An answer choice that provides more support for the idea that imposing the tax would result in reduced consumption of sugar would provide additional support for the government’s position.
For example:
(Correct Answer) Most people in Khartona who drink sugary drinks choose them instead of healthier drinks that contain less sugar simply because the sugary drinks are 15 percent less expensive than the healthier ones.
Notice how the above answer choice adds new information that makes the government’s position much more convincing.
Another correct answer could be:
(Correct Answer) If a 20 percent tax were added to the price of sugary drinks, people in Khartona would not begin consuming similar amounts of sugar via consuming candy and sweet desserts.
Notice how this answer choice also adds new information that makes the government’s position much more convincing.
Consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 3 The board of Centerville Hospital has decided to implement an extensive quality improvement program in order to reduce the number of medical mistakes that occur each year at the hospital. Which of the following, if true, provides the most support for the consultants’ claim? 1. [ ] (A) The amount that Centerville Hospital spends annually on employee compensation has been decreasing over the past several years. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) The consultants were hired by the board of Centerville Hospital to find ways for the hospital to reduce costs. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) The annual operating expenses of Centerville Hospital are greater than those of any other hospital in the same county. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Some of the employees of Centerville Hospital are paid less than some employees in similar positions in other hospitals. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Often, when medical mistakes occur, patients harmed because of them use legal means to obtain significant monetary compensation from those considered responsible for the mistakes. View Solution Question Level: easy The consultants’ claim is:
Conclusion: As a result of implementing the program, the hospital’s total annual costs will decrease.
Notice that, while the passage provides some background information related to the consultants’ claim, it does not include any evidence that supports the claim. So, to strengthen the argument, we need an answer that is a premise that supports the consultants’ claim.
(A) The amount that Centerville Hospital spends annually on employee compensation has been decreasing over the past several years. We have to be careful not to misconstrue the fact stated by this choice, that the amount that the hospital spends annually on employee compensation has been decreasing, as somehow supporting the conclusion that the hospital’s total annual costs will decrease as a result of implementing the program.
Notice that the passage states as fact that implementing the program will require increasing the amount that the hospital spends on employee compensation, and what this choice says does not change that fact.
(B) The consultants were hired by the board of Centerville Hospital to find ways for the hospital to reduce costs. The fact that the consultants were hired to reduce costs is not strong evidence that following a recommendation that they have made will actually result in a decrease in costs.
(C) The annual operating expenses of Centerville Hospital are greater than those of any other hospital in the same county. This comparison does not affect the conclusion. One might take what this choice says as evidence that there is a lot of room for reduction of Centerville Hospital’s expenses and, thus, decide that this choice strengthens the conclusion that implementing the program will result in a reduction of those expenses.
However, that line of reasoning is a stretch, in multiple ways. For one thing, it could be that the reason Centerville Hospital’s expenses are the greatest is simply that it is the biggest hospital.
(D) Some of the employees of Centerville Hospital are paid less than some employees in similar positions in other hospitals. Regardless of how much Centerville Hospital employees are paid, an increase in the amount that the hospital spends on employee compensation will result in an increase rather than a decrease in annual total costs.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Often, when medical mistakes occur, patients harmed because of them use legal means to obtain significant monetary compensation from those considered responsible for the mistakes. This choice is a premise that supports the consultants’ claim.
If patients harmed because of medical mistakes often use legal means to obtain significant monetary compensation from those considered responsible for the mistakes, then mistakes made at Centerville Hospital could be costly for the hospital. Thus, reducing the number of medical mistakes made at Centerville Hospital could result in a significant reduction in the hospital’s annual expenses.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s next discuss how we can strengthen an argument by explicitly stating an assumption upon which the argument depends.
summary:
Lesson Content In some Strengthen the Argument questions, the passages contain conclusions without any supporting premises.
💡MUST KNOW:
In some Strengthen the Argument questions, the passages contain conclusions without any supporting premises. In other questions, the premises within the arguments only partially support the conclusions.
In any of these cases, an effective way for an answer choice to strengthen the argument is to provide a premise, which will either provide the only support for the conclusion or provide further support for the conclusion alongside existing premises.
title: 4.6 Provide a Premise to Support or Add Support for the Conclusion

contents:
Lesson Content As you well know, an assumption is a piece of missing information that must be true in order for the logic of the argument to hold.
Every argument relies upon many assumptions; it isn’t possible to include every single piece of relevant information in an argument.
When an assumption is uncontroversial (it is something that “everyone would agree is true”), the presence in the argument of that unstated assumption has no impact on the strength of the argument.
For example, when an argument involves mathematics, there is no need to state that putting the same values into a particular formula produces the same results every time; leaving aside unusual cases, that is something upon which everyone agrees.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an assumption upon which an argument relies is uncontroversial (it is something that “everyone would agree is true”), the presence in the argument of that unstated assumption has no impact on the strength of the argument.
The problem lies with controversial assumptions, ones that may well be false.
When an argument relies upon a controversial assumption, that assumption is a point of weakness in the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an argument relies upon a controversial assumption, that assumption is a point of weakness in the argument. Since the assumption is effectively a premise in the argument (an unwritten one), if it were false, then the conclusion would not be fully supported, and the argument would not be sound.
Consider this simple example:
All men are mortal. Therefore, Socrates is mortal.
In this argument, that Socrates is a man is a controversial assumption.
Therefore, we could strengthen this argument by explicitly stating as fact the author’s assumption that Socrates is a man:
All men are mortal. Socrates is a man. Therefore, Socrates is mortal.
Now the argument is much stronger.
Let’s look at another more detailed example:
Biochemical X contains estrogen-mimicking hormones that decrease the fertility of several species of fish, including salmon, trout, and bass. Therefore, allowing the biochemical to leak into Lake Garda will have a permanent and irreversible impact, wiping out all of its fish stocks.
This argument depends on two major assumptions – first, that Lake Garda contains only species of fish that will be affected by the biochemical, and second, that the fish cannot adapt.
So, how do we strengthen an argument that relies upon controversial assumptions? The most effective method is to explicitly state that the assumptions are true.
Lake Garda’s fish are all vulnerable to biochemical X and would be unable to adapt in such a way as to neutralize its effects.
Adding the above information makes the argument much stronger.
💡MUST KNOW:
An argument can be strengthened via the explicit stating of an unstated assumption upon which the argument relies.
Consider the following examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 4 A study was done and found that, when CEOs were dealing with negative situations, they were much more likely to take action when they were presented with the consequences of doing nothing. Which of the following statements, if true, most supports the conclusion drawn from the study? 1. [ ] (A) Many CEOs seek the counsel of others when they encounter negative problems. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Sometimes, when a CEO is faced with a negative situation, doing nothing is a viable option, as it allows the passage of time to positively affect the situation. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) It is generally the case that, when a CEO is faced with a negative situation, taking action produces better results than does doing nothing. 4. [ ] (D) CEOs tend to make decisions more quickly when information they receive comes from people whom they trust. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) It is normal in business for CEOs to base decisions that they make on secondhand information. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium The conclusion of the argument is the following:
Conclusion: CEOs faced with negative situations should always be made aware of the consequences of doing nothing.
The conclusion is based on the following premise:
Premise: A study has found that CEOs are more likely to take action when they are made aware of the consequences of doing nothing.
Notice that an assumption connects the premise to the conclusion. The argument assumes that a CEO’s taking action is beneficial.
Is this a good assumption? Maybe not, right? After all, it could very well be the case that doing nothing is more beneficial than doing something, right?
So, if this were a Weaken the Argument question, our job would be to attack that assumption. However, in this case, our job is to help the author bolster the argument. We can do so by confirming (as factual) one of her assumptions.
(A) Many CEOs seek the counsel of others when they encounter negative problems. The fact that CEOs seek the counsel of others indicates that CEOs would likely be open to hearing from others about the consequences of doing nothing. However, it does not add support to the conclusion that CEOs should be made aware of the consequences of doing nothing.
(B) Sometimes, when a CEO is faced with a negative situation, doing nothing is a viable option, as it allows the passage of time to positively affect the situation. This choice clearly does not strengthen the argument. Whereas the argument is that CEOs should be made aware of the consequences of doing nothing, because, when CEOs are made aware of those consequences, they are more likely to take action, this choice provides a reason not to take action. In other words, what this answer choice says weakens the argument, doing the opposite of what this question asks us to do.
YOUR CHOICE(C) It is generally the case that, when a CEO is faced with a negative situation, taking action produces better results than does doing nothing. This choice directly states an assumption upon which the argument depends, that a CEO’s taking action generally results in an outcome better than one that results from a CEO’s doing nothing.
If taking action did NOT generally result in a better outcome, then concluding that one should always do something that increases the likelihood of a CEO’s taking action would not make sense.
So, by confirming that taking action DOES generally result in a better outcome, this choice significantly strengthens the support for the argument’s conclusion.
(D) CEOs tend to make decisions more quickly when information they receive comes from people whom they trust. The fact that CEOs make decisions more quickly when the information they receive comes from people whom they trust does not strengthen the support for the conclusion, which is specifically that CEOs faced with negative situations should be made aware of the consequences of doing nothing.
The conclusion is not about whether people whom CEOs trust should be the ones to provide CEOs with information. It’s about the effect of providing them with information.
(E) It is normal in business for CEOs to base decisions that they make on secondhand information. This is a trap choice, because it could be taken as implying that a CEO’s becoming aware of the consequences of a course of action and acting on that information is normal in business, and that, therefore, making CEOs aware of those consequences makes sense.
The truth is that the value of a CEO’s being made aware of the consequences of doing nothing does not depend on whether it is normal for CEOs to base decisions on secondhand information. What’s normal and what should be done are not the same thing.
So, this choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss how we can strengthen an argument by addressing an issue that could affect the validity of the conclusion.
summary:
Lesson Content As you well know, an assumption is a piece of missing information that must be true in order for the logic of the argument to hold.
Every argument relies upon many assumptions; it isn’t possible to include every single piece of relevant information in an argument.
When an assumption is uncontroversial (it is something that “everyone would agree is true”), the presence in the argument of that unstated assumption has no impact on the strength of the argument.
title: 4.7 Explicitly State an Assumption Upon Which the Argument Depends

contents:
Lesson Content Often, in a scenario described in a Strengthen the Argument passage, there is room for issues that could undermine the conclusion.
For example, imagine if the CEO of an electric car company made the claim that his electric cars are much more environmentally friendly than their gas-powered counterparts because, while gas-powered cars emit greenhouse gases, electric cars do not.
In making that argument, he makes many assumptions, one of which is that there is no part of the electric car production process that could cause enough harm to the environment that his argument would be unsound.
For example, he’s assuming that the gas-powered heavy equipment (trucks, diggers, earth movers, cranes, backhoes, etc.) used to mine the metals used in the production of his car batteries don’t emit as much, or more, greenhouse gas as the gas-powered cars emit over their lifetimes.
To see how this type of answer choice works in a Strengthen the Argument question, consider the following mini-example:
The Endless Summer cruise line washes its ships regularly, with the result that a large amount of soap goes into the ocean. Endless Summer’s management has decided to switch soaps, from the soap that the company currently uses to Super Soap, which, unlike the soap that the company currently uses, has essentially no negative environmental effects in the concentrations that result from washing ships. Management feels that, by making this change, Endless Summer will be taking a step toward reducing the negative environmental effects of the company’s operations.
Which of the following, if true, serves to confirm that management’s plan will have the desired effect?
In the scenario outlined above, Endless Summer’s management feels that changing soaps will result in reduced negative effects on the environment.
Think about what assumption is being made.
So, the following could be a strengthening answer choice:
(Correct Answer) The process of manufacturing Super Soap has fewer negative effects on the environment than does the process of manufacturing the soap currently used by Endless Summer.
That answer choice addresses the issue of the effects of manufacturing Super Soap and provides some confirmation that using Super Soap is indeed better for the environment than using the soap that Endless Summer currently uses.
💡MUST KNOW:
An argument can be strengthened via addressing an issue that could affect the validity of the argument’s conclusion.
Now, here are some examples in which the correct answers address issues that could affect the conclusions of the arguments:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 5 Because birds eat grapes, vineyards often use methods that are expensive or harmful to birds to keep birds from consuming the vineyards’ grapes before the grapes can be harvested. Which of the following, if true, is most supportive of the idea that the plan will have the desired outcome? 1. [ ] (A) For many types of birds that live in the area where the Grape Estate vineyard is located, grapes are not preferred food. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Netting that can be used to cover grapevines and keep birds from eating the grapes not only is expensive but also must be replaced every few years. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) If dogs in a vineyard are relatively inactive, some birds may feel that it is safe to eat grapes in the vineyard. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Generally, dogs that roam in vineyards do not either chew on grapevines or eat grapes. 5. [ ] (E) Most of the birds that would consume grapes from the vines at Grape Estates are migratory birds that pass through the area just twice a year. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy In the scenario presented, the vineyard plans to use dogs to keep birds from eating the grapes and thus to preserve the grapes for harvesting.
Conclusion: Using this low-cost approach that does not harm birds will result in most grapes remaining on the vines unharmed until they are ready for harvest.
In proposing the use of dogs, is there an assumption being made? Definitely, right? A major assumption is that the dogs themselves won’t somehow damage the grapes. We could weaken this argument by attacking that assumption. But here our job is to bolster the argument, and we can do so by showing that this assumption is a good one and that, in fact, the dogs will not cause harm to the grapes.
Always remember that, whenever anyone proposes a plan, he or she makes the assumption that the plan will work as it has be laid out, that there are not unaddressed issues that will derail the plan. Our job in answering a question like this one is to show that that assumption is a good one.
(A) For many types of birds that live in the area where the Grape Estate vineyard is located, grapes are not preferred food. This choice could be tempting, because, since it indicates that many birds do not prefer to eat grapes, it could be perceived as providing additional support for the conclusion that the grapes will remain on the vines.
However, the passage has made clear that birds, at least some of them, do eat grapes, and what this choice says does not negate that stated fact.
So, this choice does not add information that changes the scenario or strengthens the argument, which is about keeping the birds that are interested in eating grapes from eating grapes.
(B) Netting that can be used to cover grapevines and keep birds from eating the grapes not only is expensive but also must be replaced every few years. This answer is a tempting trap because, by mentioning some disadvantages of using netting to keep birds from eating grapes, this answer choice presents some reasons for using dogs to keep birds away from the grapes and makes the dogs approach look good.
However, it is not the correct answer because it does not provide additional support for the specific conclusion for which we are seeking support, which is not that the dogs approach is good in general but, rather, that the dogs approach will work.
(C) If dogs in a vineyard are relatively inactive, some birds may feel that it is safe to eat grapes in the vineyard. What this choice says does not support the conclusion that the plan will work. If anything, it provides a reason to believe that the plan may not work. So, this choice is not a strengthener.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Generally, dogs that roam in vineyards do not either chew on grapevines or eat grapes. This choice addresses a possible issue.
While dogs may scare away birds that would eat the grapes, it’s possible that the dogs themselves would damage the vines or eat the grapes. By addressing this issue by indicating that the dogs will not chew the vines or eat the grapes, this choice supports the idea that the plan for preserving the grapes will work.
Notice that this answer choice strengthens the argument by addressing an issue that could affect the validity of the conclusion.
(E) Most of the birds that would consume grapes from the vines at Grape Estates are migratory birds that pass through the area just twice a year. This answer choice is one that gives us room to make up a convoluted story supporting the idea that the plan will work.
A test-taker might think, for instance, “Birds that pass through the area only twice a year will be more likely to be scared by the dogs than would be birds that have time to get accustomed to the dogs. So, this choice confirms that the plan will work.”
We really don’t have any reason to believe such a story though, and the truth is that this choice does not actually say anything that clearly supports the idea that the plan will work.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 6 The Roasted Coffee Company (RCC) is widely known for both its expensive coffees and its colorful ads featuring happy employees. Which of the following, if true, provides the most effective support for the claim made by RCC? 1. [ ] (A) Field workers employed by RCC work 4 to 5 hours less per week than do field workers doing the same basic work for other coffee manufacturers. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) RCC’s profit margins are considerably higher than those of the coffee manufacturers that dominate the market. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) RCC has promised to consider field workers when hiring people for management positions. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) RCC field workers who were asked to rate their level of job satisfaction knew that they could do so anonymously. 5. [ ] (E) Field workers employed by RCC tend to voluntarily leave the company after fewer years than are typically spent at one coffee manufacturer by workers employed by other coffee manufacturers. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium RCC makes the following claim:
Conclusion: The field workers whom the company employs have a work environment that is relatively relaxed in comparison with the work environments of similar companies.
This claim is based on the evidence that RCC workers’ reported job satisfaction is higher than that of other coffee workers.
The Company’s claim rests on survey evidence, which is statistical in nature. Therefore, the credibility of the Company’s claim is tied to the validity of the statistics cited, and there is room for an issue that could affect the validity of the conclusion. In other words, remember that whenever an author cites statistical data – such as the results of a survey – she assumes that that data is actually effective as evidence in her argument. Therefore, any answer choice that provides additional evidence that underscores that that assumption is a good one will strengthen the argument.
(A) Field workers employed by RCC work 4 to 5 hours less per week than do field workers doing the same basic work for other coffee manufacturers. Be careful here. This evidence supports the wrong conclusion.
While the workers at RCC may be less stressed than workers at other companies because the workers at RCC work for fewer hours per week, working for fewer hours is not the same as working in a more relaxed environment. After all, workers could be working fewer hours per week in a high-pressure, stress-filled environment.
(B) RCC’s profit margins are considerably higher than those of the coffee manufacturers that dominate the market. This information is not clearly connected to the conclusion.
While profit margins and work environment could somehow be connected, in this case, we are given no information that indicates that the fact that the company has higher profit margins means that the company has a more relaxed work environment.
Don’t do the GMAT test-makers any favors by making up some suppose-suppose-suppose chain of logic in your own mind, one that connects profit margins to work environment, or the like. That type of reasoning is too hypothetical. Don’t shoehorn in an answer. Remember, as soon as you say, “Well, if I look at it that way…” likely you’re looking at the wrong answer.
(C) RCC has promised to consider field workers when hiring people for management positions. Promotions and the degree to which the work environment is relaxed are two different subjects. So, while this choice might support a different conclusion, such as that RCC is a great place to work, it does not support the specific conclusion that we have to support, which is that RCC has a relatively relaxed work environment.
YOUR CHOICE(D) RCC field workers who were asked to rate their level of job satisfaction knew that they could do so anonymously. By addressing an issue related to how the statistics were generated, this fact lends credibility to the conclusion.
Anonymous worker surveys may be considered more reliable than worker surveys in which the respondents are not anonymous, because survey respondents may answer more honestly when they remain anonymous, feeling that any negative answers can’t be held against them by their supervisors or by the company in general. Thus, a conclusion based on the results of an anonymous survey may be better supported than one based on those of a non-anonymous survey.
Notice that this answer choice strengthens the support for the conclusion by addressing an issue that could affect the validity of the conclusion.
Notice also that, in general, what this answer choice says underscores that the author’s assumption that the results of the survey are actually effective as evidence in her argument is a good one.
(E) Field workers employed by RCC tend to voluntarily leave the company after fewer years than are typically spent at one coffee manufacturer by workers employed by other coffee manufacturers. This answer choice actually weakens the argument. It doesn’t make much sense to say that employees experience high job satisfaction, yet they also have a high turnover rate. Common sense indicates that, if a person is happy with his or her job, that person stays with the job. This choice does not support the Company’s claim.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss how we can strengthen an argument by ruling out an alternative cause of an effect.
summary:
Lesson Content Often, in a scenario described in a Strengthen the Argument passage, there is room for issues that could undermine the conclusion.
For example, imagine if the CEO of an electric car company made the claim that his electric cars are much more environmentally friendly than their gas-powered counterparts because, while gas-powered cars emit greenhouse gases, electric cars do not.
In making that argument, he makes many assumptions, one of which is that there is no part of the electric car production process that could cause enough harm to the environment that his argument would be unsound.
title: 4.8 Address an Issue That Could Affect the Validity of the Conclusion

contents:
Lesson Content In the arguments found in Strengthen the Argument questions, authors will often use cause-and-effect reasoning; in other words, they will link an effect to a cause.
💡MUST KNOW:
Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, he assumes that there cannot be an alternative explanation for the effect observed. In other words, he assumes that his cause-and-effect claim is completely accurate.
In other words, whenever we can rule out an alternative explanation for an observed effect, we have strengthened the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
One way to strengthen the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion is to rule out alternative causes.
Remember, we don’t have to prove a conclusion correct to strengthen support for it.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an argument uses cause-and-effect reasoning, by eliminating even one credible alternative cause for the observed effect, we can strengthen the support for the conclusion.
For example, let’s say that an author has found that lizards living in Madagascar are, on average, significantly larger than lizards of the same species living in other locations.
Ok – it’s clear that the author believes that it is the UV light that is causing the Madagascan lizards to grow so large.
Definitely, right? Let’s think of some:
In Madagascar, in contrast to other locations, there are unusually abundant food sources that promote lizard growth.
In Madagascar, unlike in other areas, there are unusually many predators that eat large quantities of baby lizards, which tend to be significantly smaller than older lizards.
In Madagascar, there are significantly better sources of camouflage, which protect large lizards from predators, than there are in other locations.
Unlike in other locations, the river water found in Madagascar, from which the lizards drink, contains an unusually high level of potent growth-promoting vitamins and minerals.
Any of those explanations (and others) are possible alternative explanations for why the Madagascan lizard are growing so large, but of all the thousands of possible explanations for why these lizards living in Madagascar are growing so large, the author believes (1) that none of those alternative explanations are true, and (2) that UV light is the only possible explanation.
So, therefore, we could strengthen the author’s cause-and-effect argument by ruling out any alternative explanation.
There ARE NOT unusually abundant food sources that promote lizard growth in Madagascar than there are in other locations.
In Madagascar, there ARE NOT unusually many predators that eat large quantities of baby lizards, which tend to be significantly smaller than older lizards.
In Madagascar, there ARE NOT significantly better sources of camouflage, which protect large lizards from predators, than there are in other locations.
The river water found in Madagascar, from which the lizards drink, DOES NOT contains an unusually high level of potent growth-promoting vitamins and minerals.
The more alternative explanations that we can rule out, the stronger the argument becomes.
Consider the following mini-example:
If pipistrelle bats are exposed to noise at levels greater than 50 decibels, they are unable to navigate effectively. If the bats cannot navigate, they cannot feed and starve to death. Over the past year, there has been a 75 percent increase in the number of dead bats found within a 2-kilometer radius of Lincoln town center. Therefore, it is likely that there has been noise above 50 decibels in that area.
Which of the following, if true, would add the most support to the above conclusion?
The conclusion of this argument is not unreasonable, but the premises do not provide particularly strong support.
Therefore, the argument could be strengthened via the addition of a premise that rules out explanations other than noise.
Here is a possible correct answer:
(Correct Answer) When many of the dead bats were tested for the presence of pathogens associated with diseases that kill bats, only two percent of the tested bats harbored one or more of those pathogens.
It’s important to see what is happening here.
With that idea in mind, you can rule out alternate causes to correctly answer this example question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 7 A year ago, Greenville Elementary School’s playground was renovated, with the goal of making it safer. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the conclusion? 1. [x] (A) Last year, the supervisors at Greenville Elementary School’s playground and the playground rules were the same as they had been the previous year. 2. [ ] (B) After becoming aware of the decrease in injuries on Greenville Elementary School’s playground, the administration of a nearby school decided to renovate that school’s playground. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) The students who used the playground both years said that they preferred using the newly renovated playground. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Because of unusually rainy weather last year, students spent less time on Greenville Elementary’s playground last year than students did the previous year. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Last year, fewer injuries occurred at Greenville Elementary’s renovated playground than occurred at the playground at Amity Elementary School. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy The author concludes the following.
Conclusion: The changes made Greenville Elementary School’s playground a safer place.
This is clearly a cause-and-effect argument, as the author argues that the changes to the playground directly contributed to the reduction of injuries.
Notice the assumption made in this argument, that there is no other explanation for the playground becoming a safer place. Is this a good assumption? Maybe, but maybe not – right?
Isn’t it possible that the changes made to the playground did not in fact cause the reduction in injuries? Couldn’t there be another, different, cause? Couldn’t there be an alternative explanation that would explain the decrease in the number of injuries? Couldn’t the author be missing something?
One way to strengthen this argument is to eliminate other potential causes.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Last year, the supervisors at Greenville Elementary School’s playground and the playground rules were the same as they had been the previous year. This information removes year-to-year differences in supervision and playground rules as possible explanations for the difference in rates of playground injuries. In other words, this fact rules out two alternative causes for the reduction in injuries.
Notice that this answer choice doesn’t eliminate all potential alternative explanations for the reduction in injuries, but it does address two factors that could affect student safety.
By providing reasons for ruling out alternative explanations for the reduction in injuries, this answer strengthens support for the conclusion.
(B) After becoming aware of the decrease in injuries on Greenville Elementary School’s playground, the administration of a nearby school decided to renovate that school’s playground. While the fact that another school’s administration decided to renovate that school’s playground after seeing the reduction in injuries at Greenville Elementary’s playground may be a vote of confidence in the idea that renovating the playground made it safer, that fact does not truly indicate that the renovation caused the change.
(C) The students who used the playground both years said that they preferred using the newly renovated playground. This choice does not support the conclusion of this argument. The conclusion we have to support is a cause-and-effect conclusion about safety, not one about what students prefer.
(D) Because of unusually rainy weather last year, students spent less time on Greenville Elementary’s playground last year than students did the previous year. This information weakens the argument, by providing a possible alternative reason for the decrease in injuries, since it could be that fewer injuries occurred at the playground, not because of the renovation, but because of what this choice mentions: students spent less time on the playground last year than students did the previous year.
(E) Last year, fewer injuries occurred at Greenville Elementary’s renovated playground than occurred at the playground at Amity Elementary School. This choice supports the wrong conclusion. The conclusion is not that the renovations made Greenville Elementary’s playground safer than other schools’ playgrounds.
Also, for all we are aware, fewer injuries occurred at Greenville Elementary School’s playground than occurred at Amity Elementary School’s playground even during the years before the renovation of Greenville Elementary School’s playground. So, the comparison made in this choice does not affect the support for the argument’s conclusion.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss confirming a conclusion by changing, adding, or eliminating a variable.
summary:
Lesson Content In the arguments found in Strengthen the Argument questions, authors will often use cause-and-effect reasoning; in other words, they will link an effect to a cause.
💡MUST KNOW:
Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, he assumes that there cannot be an alternative explanation for the effect observed. In other words, he assumes that his cause-and-effect claim is completely accurate.
In other words, whenever we can rule out an alternative explanation for an observed effect, we have strengthened the argument.
title: 4.9 Rule Out an Alternate Cause of an Effect

contents:
Lesson Content You are probably familiar with how many scientific experiments work: a hypothesis is presented, and then experiments are done in which variables are changed to see how changes in variables affect results.
For instance, a researcher who feels that children’s eating sugar makes learning difficult for them could confirm that hypothesis by having a control group and an experimental group.
If the children in the control group were to learn more easily than the children in the experimental group, the researcher could say that the results support the hypothesis that sugar consumption was causing the children in the experimental group to have difficulty learning.
Strengthen the Argument questions also can use the results of changes in variables to support hypotheses or conclusions.
To see how this type of confirmation can work in a Strengthen question consider the following example:
An extraordinarily high percentage of the people in the coastal town of Mergate suffer with symptoms of mercury poisoning. Since tuna can contain mercury in large amounts, and since the people who live in Mergate are known for regularly catching and eating tuna, it is likely that the mercury poisoning symptoms seen in people who live in Mergate result from people’s accumulating mercury in their bodies via tuna consumption.
Which of the following, if true, most helps to confirm the conclusion above?
Here’s the conclusion that we have to confirm in order to correctly answer the question:
Conclusion: It is likely that the symptoms result from people’s accumulating mercury in their bodies via tuna consumption.
The conclusion is already supported by the following facts:
Premise: Tuna can contain mercury in high levels.
Premise: The people who live in Mergate are known for regularly catching and eating tuna.
How could we further confirm this conclusion? One way in which we could do so is by eliminating a variable.
What if we were to find all the people in Mergate who do not eat tuna and see whether they exhibit symptoms of mercury poisoning? If they didn’t exhibit such symptoms, then we would have some confirmation that the high incidence of mercury poisoning symptoms in Mergate results from all the tuna eating.
Here’s a correct answer to the question:
(Correct Answer) No people in Mergate who avoid eating tuna because of allergic reactions they have had to tuna show any symptoms of mercury poisoning.
Showing the results of eliminating the tuna eating variable is just what the correct answer does for us, by telling us that people who avoid eating tuna because of allergic reactions they have had don’t exhibit symptoms of mercury poisoning.
Notice that, for all intents and purposes, the correct answer demonstrates there is a control group of people who do not have mercury poisoning.
Let’s look at this example from another angle, an important angle.
💡MUST KNOW:
The correct answer to a Strengthen question can strengthen an argument by providing information regarding what happens when a variable is changed, added, or eliminated.
Consider how the correct answer to the following question uses the results of changing a variable to strengthen the argument.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 8 The population of frogs in the Andes has declined precipitously. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument? 1. [ ] (A) It is widely believed that chytrid fungus was introduced into the ecosystem of the Andes by human activity. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) The greatest concentrations of frogs remaining in the Andes can be found at elevations at which chytrid fungus cannot survive. 3. [ ] (C) When air temperatures are above a certain maximum level, chytrid fungus generally does not infect frogs. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Researchers have yet to determine what proportion of frog species have developed any effective resistance to infection by chytrid fungus. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) The geographic spread of chytrid fungus can be accelerated by warmer temperatures resulting from planetary climate change. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium The argument that we have to strengthen supports the following conclusion:
Conclusion: The likely cause of the decline in the frog population in the Andes is a spreading of chytrid fungus.
The passage includes some evidence to support the conclusion:
Premise: Chytrid fungus is now common in the Andes.
Premise: Chytrid fungus can fatally infect frogs.
Notice that the author is making a cause-and-effect claim; she claims that it is the chytrid fungus that is the cause of the decline in the frog population. How could we strengthen the case for her claim?
(A) It is widely believed that chytrid fungus was introduced into the ecosystem of the Andes by human activity. In saying that it is widely believed that chytrid fungus was introduced into the ecosystem of the Andes by human activity, this choice presents an explanation for why chytrid fungus can be found in the Andes. At the same time, we already know from the passage that chytrid fungus can be found in the Andes, and explaining how it got there does not add new evidence that supports the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The greatest concentrations of frogs remaining in the Andes can be found at elevations at which chytrid fungus cannot survive. This choice strengthens the argument by showing what happens when a variable, the fungus, is eliminated.
By saying that where the fungus cannot survive is where the greatest concentrations of frogs are, this answer choice indicates that, when we take away the cause (the chytrid fungus), the effect (the frog population decline) also goes away, thus strengthening the argument.
Another way of thinking about this situation is that the frogs living at elevations at which chytrid fungus cannot survive represent a sort of control group because we eliminate the variable of interest (the fungus) from this group of frogs. Because this population of frogs is not showing the same decline (in fact, the greatest concentrations are found here), we have more faith that it is in fact the fungus that is responsible for the precipitous declines in frog populations elsewhere in the Andes.
(C) When air temperatures are above a certain maximum level, chytrid fungus generally does not infect frogs. This choice could be tempting, because it incorporates some things mentioned in the passage, warmer temperatures and chytrid fungus.
However, the truth is that describing conditions under which chytrid fungus does not infect frogs is not a way to support the conclusion that chytrid fungus is in fact infecting frogs and causing a decline in the frog population.
