(D) Researchers have yet to determine what proportion of frog species have developed any effective resistance to infection by chytrid fungus. This answer choice could be tempting, because it could be perceived as indicating that it is likely that some species of frogs in the Andes have not developed effective resistance to infection by chytrid fungus. Thus, this choice could be perceived as expressing the unstated assumption that frogs in the Andes can in fact be infected by chytrid fungus.
Notice, however, that this choice says only that researchers have yet to determine what proportion of species of frogs have developed effective resistance to infection by chytrid fungus. In other words, this choice does not clearly indicate what proportion of frogs have developed such resistance and leaves open the possibility that all species of frogs in the Andes have developed such resistance.
So, this choice does not add any information that clearly supports the conclusion.
(E) The geographic spread of chytrid fungus can be accelerated by warmer temperatures resulting from planetary climate change. This choice could be tempting, because it incorporates two topics from the passage, rising temperatures and chytrid fungus, and because it seems to indicate that rising temperatures make chytrid fungus more prevalent.
However, the implications of “can be accelerated by warmer temperatures” are not clear. How fast was the fungus spreading before the warmer temperatures existed? Was there chytrid fungus in the Andes all along? Who knows? Right?
Moreover, the passage has already stated that chytrid fungus is now common in the Andes. If we already have the information that chytrid fungus is common in the Andes, then adding the information that there exists presently a factor, rising temperatures, that causes chytrid fungus to spread faster does not make the scenario clearly different. After all, the fact that the fungus is common in the Andes means that it has spread there.
Thus, adding the fact that the geographic spread of chytrid fungus can be accelerated by warmer temperatures doesn’t result in a significant change in the scenario presented or a strengthening of the support for the conclusion.
Correct Answer: (B) Now before we extensively discuss some types of incorrect answers that can appear in Strengthen questions, let’s review the general difference between trap choices and correct answers in Strengthen questions.
summary:
Lesson Content You are probably familiar with how many scientific experiments work: a hypothesis is presented, and then experiments are done in which variables are changed to see how changes in variables affect results.
For instance, a researcher who feels that children’s eating sugar makes learning difficult for them could confirm that hypothesis by having a control group and an experimental group.
If the children in the control group were to learn more easily than the children in the experimental group, the researcher could say that the results support the hypothesis that sugar consumption was causing the children in the experimental group to have difficulty learning.
title: 4.10 Confirming a Conclusion by Changing, Adding, or Eliminating a Variable

contents:
Lesson Content In most Strengthen the Argument questions, along with the correct answer, there are one or more answer choices that can appear to strengthen the argument while not actually doing so.
Noticing the difference between these so called “trap” choices and the correct answer is generally the key final move in choosing the correct answer to a Strengthen the Argument question.
Trap Choice: A trap choice in a Strengthen the Argument question says something that is somehow related to the argument and that seems to strengthen the argument, and at the same time, what the trap choice says is not logically connected to the argument in a way such that it strengthens the argument.
Correct Answer: Often, at first glance, a correct answer to a Strengthen the Argument question may not seem to be directly related to the conclusion of the argument.
Consider the following example:
The city council of Argentium wants to reduce the number of pedestrians struck by motor vehicles every year. In order to achieve their goal, they have decided to replace the white stone borders of many of the pedestrian crosswalks on the busiest streets in the city with borders of flashing lights.
Which of the following, if true, provides the most support to the idea that the council’s plan will fulfill their aim?
(Trap Choice) Because the borders of flashing lights will be installed as part of scheduled street repaving, the installation of the new borders will not significantly impact traffic flow on the city’s busiest streets.
(Correct Answer) Research conducted by engineers in Argentium has shown crosswalk borders of flashing lights to be 220 percent more effective in getting the attention of speeding or distracted drivers than white stone borders.
Notice the difference between the trap choice and the correct answer.
The trap choice says something that sounds positive about the plan and that is related to what the argument said about the busiest streets in the city, “the installation of the new borders will not significantly impact traffic flow on the city’s busiest streets.” The choice has some appeal, as trap choices always do, because what it says about the plan indicates that the plan is practical.
Notice, however, that the fact that implementing the plan will not significantly affect traffic flow on the city’s busiest streets does not add support to the idea that the plan will achieve the council’s goal of reducing the number of pedestrians struck by cars.
The correct answer, on the other hand, says something that clearly supports the idea that the plan will work – the lighted crosswalk borders are more effective than are white stone borders in getting the attention of drivers.
💡MUST KNOW:
Differentiating between trap choices and the correct answer is the final key step in correctly answering a Strengthen the Argument question.
Now that we better understand the difference between a trap choice in a Strengthen question and a correct answer to a Strengthen question, let’s do some Targeted Practice that includes some tricky trap choices.
Targeted Practice Each of the following arguments is followed by a trap choice and a correct answer. For each argument, select the correct answer. --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- Targeted Practice Solutions Let’s now practice telling the difference between trap choices and correct answers in complete Strengthen questions.
For best results, take your time and carefully analyze every choice to develop skill.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 9 Mayor: I propose that we no longer allow the use of paper money at local businesses. Which of the following, if true, most strongly indicates that implementing the Mayor’s plan would have the desired effect? 1. [ ] (A) Some citizens of the city have access to neither credit cards nor smartphones. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) The mayor does not own stock in any company that produces and sells contactless forms of payment. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Most citizens carefully wash their hands with soap and hot water immediately after handing paper money. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Scientists have discovered that paper money can easily transmit diseases among people handling it. 5. [ ] (E) Most local businesses are already equipped to accept contactless forms of payment. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy Breakdown of the argument:
Background: I propose that we no longer allow the use of paper money at local businesses.
Background: Instead, all business will accept only contactless forms of payment, such as credit cards and payments via smartphones.
Conclusion: Implementing this plan would help protect and preserve the health of our citizens.
Notice that the mayor has not provided us with any evidence to support her argument. She moves from information on what her plan is – that all business will accept only contactless forms of payment, such as credit cards and payments via smartphones – to her conclusion that the plan would help protect and preserve the health of our citizens. Why does she believe her plan will help protect and preserve the health of our citizens?
Because no evidence has been provided in the argument, we’ll be seeking an answer choice that provides a premise that supports the mayor’s conclusion.
(A) Some citizens of the city have access to neither credit cards nor smartphones. What this choice says goes in the wrong direction.
What this choice says indicates that the plan might not be easy to pull off. That fact does not weaken the argument, but it certainly does not give us more faith that the plan will work.
If anything, what this answer choice says seems to be a very small vote against the plan’s working. However, we’re seeking a choice that increases our faith that the plan will work.
(B) The mayor does not own stock in any company that produces and sells contactless forms of payment. This choice is a trap choice. It sounds like something that would strengthen the argument, but it doesn’t really strengthen the argument.
This choice is tempting because, by saying that the mayor does not own stock in any company that produces and sells contactless forms of payment, this choice tends to confirm that the mayor does not have an ulterior motive for coming up with the plan of using only contactless forms of payment, and it’s easy for us to go one step further and decide that confirmation that the mayor does not have an ulterior motive for coming up with the plan indicates that the plan is likely to work.
However, the truth is that the fact that the mayor does not own stock in any company that produces and sells contactless forms of payment does not really help to confirm the mayor’s conclusion.
After all, even if the mayor did own stock in a company that produces and sells contactless forms of payment, switching to using only contactless forms of payment could help to protect and preserve the health of the citizens. Meanwhile, the plan could be totally useless even if the mayor does not own stock in any company that produces and sells contactless forms of payment
Since the fact that the mayor does not own stock in any company that produces and sells contactless forms of payment does not provide any clear indication that the plan will work, this choice does not strengthen the support for the mayor’s conclusion.
(C) Most citizens carefully wash their hands with soap and hot water immediately after handing paper money. The fact that most citizens carefully wash their hands with soap and hot water immediately after handing paper money would certainly not strengthen the argument.
So what if they do? How would that fact give us any more faith that their ceasing to use paper money would help protect and preserve their health?
If you choose this choice as correct, you likely did so because what it says seems plausible.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Scientists have discovered that paper money can easily transmit diseases among people handling it. As mentioned, the mayor provides no evidence that accepting only contactless forms of payment would help protect and preserve the health of the citizens. She assumes that there is something about paper money that would be harmful to the health of citizens, but she never provides that evidence.
Notice that what this answer choice says, that scientists have discovered that paper money can easily transmit diseases among people handling it, provides that missing evidence, strengthening the argument.
If it is true that scientists have discovered that paper money can easily transmit diseases among people handling it, then we have more reason to believe that the mayor’s plan will work. After all, if paper money can easily transmit diseases among people handling it, then doing away with paper money transactions should help protect and preserve the health of the citizens.
Notice that what this answer choice provides is a fact that the mayor, in making her argument, assumed to be true but did not state directly.
(E) Most local businesses are already equipped to accept contactless forms of payment. This choice is tempting trap because it supports a conclusion related to the mayor’s conclusion. It supports a conclusion along the lines of that the plan will be easy to execute. So, this choice could seem to be a strengthener because it says something that seems positive for the prospects of the plan’s working.
However, since the mayor’s conclusion is not that the plan will be easy to execute, this choice does not strengthen the argument.
The mayor’s conclusion is that implementing this plan would help protect and preserve the health of citizens, and the fact that most local businesses are already equipped to accept contactless forms of payment does not help to confirm that implementing the plan would help protect and preserve the health of citizens.
As always, to avoid choosing a trap choice, we must pay careful attention to the conclusion.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 10 In an effort to make college education more affordable, the government of Grendania plans to subsidize college education by paying up to fifteen percent of college tuition for any Grendanian who attends college full time. Which of the following most clearly supports the conclusion above? 1. [ ] (A) The government plans to leave in place programs that fund research engaged in by university professors. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Universities in Grendania base the levels of their tuition on the amounts that people are willing to pay. 3. [ ] (C) When college education is more affordable, more people choose to attend college full time. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) In Grendania, the cost of attending college has been increasing at a rate much faster than the general rate of price inflation. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Even if the average college tuition in Grendania were fifteen percent lower, many people would still have difficulty affording to attend college. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: The government of Grendania plans to subsidize college education by paying up to fifteen percent of college tuition.
Conclusion: Rather than make college more affordable for students, implementing this plan will serve only to increase the revenue of universities.
Notice, there is in the passage no support for the conclusion. All the passage provides is a description of the government’s plan and a conclusion about the results of the plan. So, the correct answer to the question will be a premise that supports the conclusion.
In order to figure out which choice supports the conclusion, we have to clearly understand what the conclusion is stating. The conclusion is stating two things about the results of implementing the plan: that affordability of college will not improve and that the revenue of universities will increase.
The correct choice will give us reason to believe that those effects will result from implementing the plan.
(A) The government plans to leave in place programs that fund research engaged in by university professors. This choice could be a tempting trap, as it gives us reason to believe that universities will receive funding, or revenue, from the government.
Notice, however, that this choice merely indicates that a source of revenue that is not directly related to the subsidy plan will remain unchanged. Therefore, it does not really provide reason to believe that revenue will increase as a result of implementing the plan.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) Universities in Grendania base the levels of their tuition on the amounts that people are willing to pay. At first, this choice may seem to be irrelevant to the argument, as the argument is about the government’s subsidizing education, while this choice is about what people are willing to pay. If, however, we carefully consider the implications of what this choice says, we can see the following.
Without the government subsidy, universities in Grendania base their tuition levels on what students are willing to pay.
Once the government starts subsidizing college education, the universities will base their tuition levels on the sum of what the students are willing to pay and what the government is willing to pay.
So, notice what will happen.
Once the government starts subsidizing education, the universities will increase tuition. As a result, the students will be paying what they are willing to pay, and the government will pay some as well.
Thus, college won’t become any more affordable for students. The students will be paying what they have been paying all along. At the same time, since the government will be paying some as well, the revenue of the universities will increase.
So, we can see that this choice provides very strong support for the conclusion of the argument.
(C) When college education is more affordable, more people choose to attend college full time. This answer is a tempting trap, as it could seem to indicate that university revenue would increase given the implementation of a government plan with a purpose of making college more affordable. If more people attend college because college is more affordable, then universities should receive more tuition.
However, this choice clearly does not support the conclusion, since this choice is about what happens when college education is more affordable, and the conclusion is about a situation in which revenue increases even though college education is not more affordable.
YOUR CHOICE(D) In Grendania, the cost of attending college has been increasing at a rate much faster than the general rate of price inflation. This choice could be tempting, because it provides a reason to believe that, even with government subsidies, people might still have difficulty affording college tuition.
Also, this choice could be taken as indicating that university revenue will increase because colleges will continue to increase the costs of attendance faster than the rate of inflation.
Notice, however, that the conclusion is about the effects of the plan, and this choice does not give us reason to believe that the subsidies would not make college at least more affordable than it would have been without the subsidies.
Also, this choice does not support the idea that the implementation of the plan will itself result in an increase in university revenue. The increase mentioned in this choice is not occurring because of the planned subsidies.
(E) Even if the average college tuition in Grendania were fifteen percent lower, many people would still have difficulty affording to attend college. While this choice may give us reason to believe that affording college would be difficult for some Grendanians even with the subsidies, it does not give us reason to believe that the subsidies would not at least make college more affordable that it would have been without the subsidies.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss common incorrect choices in strengthen questions.
summary:
Lesson Content In most Strengthen the Argument questions, along with the correct answer, there are one or more answer choices that can appear to strengthen the argument while not actually doing so.
Noticing the difference between these so called “trap” choices and the correct answer is generally the key final move in choosing the correct answer to a Strengthen the Argument question.
Trap Choice: A trap choice in a Strengthen the Argument question says something that is somehow related to the argument and that seems to strengthen the argument, and at the same time, what the trap choice says is not logically connected to the argument in a way such that it strengthens the argument.
title: 4.11 The Difference Between Trap Choices and Correct Answers in Strengthen Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Over time, the types of common incorrect choices that appear in Strengthen questions have been predictable.
Let’s begin by discussing incorrect choices that do not affect the strength of arguments in any way.
summary:
Lesson Content Over time, the types of common incorrect choices that appear in Strengthen questions have been predictable.
Let’s begin by discussing incorrect choices that do not affect the strength of arguments in any way.
title: 4.12 Common Incorrect Choices in Strengthen Questions

contents:
Lesson Content The most blatantly wrong answer choices in Strengthen questions simply do not affect the arguments.
In many cases, such choices are fairly easy to eliminate.
Consider the following example:
The government of Golado has recently increased the taxes imposed on the sale of all alcoholic beverages in Golado. While the tax increase is sure to result in a significant decrease in consumption of alcohol in Golado, government officials believe that increasing the taxes on sales of alcohol will result in a reduction in Golado’s government budget gap.
Which of the following, if true, provides evidence suggesting that the government officials’ belief is correct?
(Choice That Does not Affect the Argument) In Golado, the portion of the prices paid for alcohol that is represented by taxes is greater than the portion of cigarette prices represented by taxes.
(Correct Answer) In Golado, all of the organizations involved in dealing with health problems resulting from the consumption of alcohol are funded by the government.
Here’s the conclusion for which we need support:
Conclusion: Increasing the taxes on sales of alcohol will result in a reduction in Golado’s government budget gap.
Notice that the comparison made in the choice that does not affect the argument makes no difference in the support of the conclusion.
The comparison might sound interesting and important, but this comparison of alcohol taxes with cigarette taxes does not tell us what the effect of the increase in alcohol taxes will be, and the effect of that increase is what matters.
Now let’s consider the correct answer.
(Correct Answer) In Golado, all of the organizations involved in dealing with health problems resulting from the consumption of alcohol are funded by the government.
The passage states that the tax increase is sure to result in a significant decrease in the consumption of alcohol in Golado.
Therefore, by saying that organizations involved in dealing with health problems resulting from the consumption of alcohol are funded by the government, the correct answer strengthens the support for the conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
Some incorrect choices in Strengthen questions simply do not affect the arguments, because what they say is either not material to the conclusions or not even directly related to the foci of the arguments.
As mentioned above, these choices that have no effect on arguments are the ones that are most blatantly wrong.
One reason why people choose answers that don’t affect the arguments is that they can sound important.
A second reason why people choose choices that have no effect on the arguments is that these choices can be spun into convoluted stories that do affect the arguments.
For example we could say:
“If the portion of the prices paid for alcohol that is represented by taxes is greater than the portion of cigarette prices represented by taxes, the new alcohol taxes will definitely help with closing the budget gap, because everyone knows that cigarette taxes help with closing budget gaps, and these alcohol taxes are even bigger than cigarette taxes!”
Is that story supported by the information that we have? Does it really make sense? No, but it does connect that answer choice to the conclusion.
Convoluted, unsupported stories are not the paths to choosing the right answers to Strengthen questions.
💡MUST KNOW:
To avoid choosing incorrect answers that have no effect on arguments, be careful not to spin convoluted stories that are not really supported by the information presented or have little to no basis in common sense.
Now with that said, it is VERY IMPORTANT that we are not under the bad assumption that the lines of reasoning we’ll need to use in answering Strengthen the Argument questions will not be involved; many questions, especially those on the more difficult end of the difficulty spectrum WILL utilize fairly involved, often sophisticated, lines of reasoning, BUT those lines of reasoning will be completely logical, and no illogical or unsupported leaps will be necessary for deciding that a choice supports a prediction.
💡MUST KNOW:
We must not be under the bad assumption that the lines of reasoning we’ll need to use in answering Strengthen the Argument questions will not be involved; many questions, especially those on the more difficult end of the difficulty spectrum WILL utilize fairly involved, often sophisticated, lines of reasoning, BUT those lines of reasoning will be completely logical, and no illogical or unsupported leaps will be necessary for deciding that a choice supports a prediction.
Consider the following examples:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 11 It must be the case that consuming excessive amounts of caffeine results in damage to interpersonal relationships. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument? 1. [ ] (A) Mindfulness practice and guided meditation have both been shown to substantially improve a person’s ability to demonstrate self-control. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Consuming excessive amounts of alcohol can result in damage to interpersonal relationships. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) It is well-documented that, when a person acts impulsively and irrationally during a discussion between two people, the relationship between them becomes damaged. 4. [ ] (D) Most people who become impulsive and irrational while under the effects of caffeine would act impulsively and irrationally in the same situation if not under the effects of caffeine. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Not all people who consume excessive amounts of caffeine act impulsively and irrationally during interpersonal interactions. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Caffeine is well-documented to reduce a person’s ability to demonstrate self-control.
Premise: During a discussion between two people, a person who may act calm and controlled without the use of caffeine may act impulsively and irrationally while under the effects of caffeine.
Conclusion: It must be the case that consuming excessive amounts of caffeine results in damage to interpersonal relationships
Notice that there is a gap in the reasoning of the argument. The author says that caffeine is well-documented to reduce a person’s ability to demonstrate self-control and thus causes the person to act impulsively and irrationally. From those facts, she concludes that it must be the case that consuming excessive amounts of caffeine results in damage to interpersonal relationships.
Is she making an assumption? Definitely. She assumes that it is true that acting impulsively and irrationally during a discussion between two people WILL damage their relationship. Notice that the author does not provide us with any proof that acting in that way damages relationships.
Furthermore, if it is NOT true that acting impulsively and irrationally during a discussion between two people WILL damage their relationship, then the argument will fall apart.
(A) Mindfulness practice and guided meditation have both been shown to substantially improve a person’s ability to demonstrate self-control. The fact that mindfulness practice and guided meditation have both been shown to substantially improve a person’s ability to demonstrate self-control does not give us more conviction that excessive consumption of caffeine can result in damage to interpersonal relationships.
Regardless of what the effects of mindfulness practice and guided meditation may be, excessive consumption of caffeine may or may not result in damage to interpersonal relationships.
So, this choice has absolutely no effect on the strength of the argument.
(B) Consuming excessive amounts of alcohol can result in damage to interpersonal relationships. Notice that the argument’s conclusion is that consuming excessive amounts of caffeine results in damage to interpersonal relationships.
The fact that consuming a different compound, alcohol, can result in damage to interpersonal relationships does not give us any more conviction in the validity of the argument related to caffeine. After all, there may be material differences between caffeine and alcohol; in fact, common knowledge tells us that there are.
So, this choice has absolutely no effect on the strength of the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(C) It is well-documented that, when a person acts impulsively and irrationally during a discussion between two people, the relationship between them becomes damaged. The author assumes that it is true that acting impulsively and irrationally during a discussion between two people WILL actually damage the interpersonal relationship between them. However, her argument provides no evidence to support that assumption.
What this answer choice says, that it is well-documented that when a person acts impulsively and irrationally during a discussion between two people, the relationship between them becomes damaged, confirms the assumption made by the author. In other words, what this answer choice says provides as fact a piece of evidence that was missing from and necessary to the argument.
As you progress through this course, you’ll see that one common way to strengthen arguments is to confirm as fact an assumption made by an author.
(D) Most people who become impulsive and irrational while under the effects of caffeine would act impulsively and irrationally in the same situation if not under the effects of caffeine. Notice that what this choice says weakens the argument.
If most people who become impulsive and irrational while under the effects of caffeine would also act impulsively and irrationally in the same situation while not under the effects of caffeine, then we are less sure that consuming caffeine makes any difference in people’s behavior or affects whether people’s interpersonal relationships are damaged by their behavior.
After all, what this answer choice says tends to indicate that these people consuming caffeine would have interpersonal issues even without the caffeine consumption.
Of course, we are not looking for information that weakens the argument; we’re looking for a strengthener.
(E) Not all people who consume excessive amounts of caffeine act impulsively and irrationally during interpersonal interactions. What this choice says does not affect the argument.
The author has supported the conclusion by stating as fact that caffeine is well-documented to reduce a person’s ability to demonstrate self-control. Furthermore, the author has provided the example that, during a discussion between two people, a person who may act calm and controlled without the use of caffeine may act impulsively and irrationally while under the effects of caffeine.
The fact that not all people who consume excessive amounts of caffeine act impulsively and irrationally during interpersonal interactions does not negate those premises. The argument is based on the fact that caffeine consumption can, at least sometimes, lead to impulsive and irrational behavior. Caffeine consumption need not always lead to such behavior in order for the argument to work.
So, what this choice says neither weakens nor strengthens the support for the conclusion. The information has no impact on the argument.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 12 Unlike the cost of the corn from which cornflakes are made, the cost of the fuel used in producing and transporting cornflakes is only a small fraction of the total costs involved in bringing cornflakes to market. Which of the following, if true, provides the strongest support for the economists’ prediction? 1. [x] (A) The higher the price of fuel, the more profitable it becomes to convert corn into ethanol, which can be used to fuel many types of machines. 2. [ ] (B) The wholesale price of cornflakes has not increased significantly for several years. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) When fuel prices increase, people generally end up with less money to spend on products other than fuel. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Cornflakes have consistently been one of the most popular types of cereal among the many types that people have to choose from. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Before last year’s increase in fuel prices, the wholesale prices of both cornflakes and fuel had been lower than their averages for recent times. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard The question asks us to find a choice that provides support for the following prediction:
Conclusion: If fuel prices remain at the level that they reached when they increased substantially over the past year, the wholesale price of cornflakes will increase by a large percentage.
Notice, the passage does not provide any support for that prediction. Rather, the prediction is made despite what the passage says about the fraction of the cost of bringing cornflakes to market that is represented by the cost of fuel.
In analyzing the answer choices to determine which provides support for the prediction, we have to be careful not to spin a convoluted story that makes a choice seem to support the prediction even though that choice does not really.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The higher the price of fuel, the more profitable it becomes to convert corn into ethanol, which can be used to fuel many types of machines. This choice indicates that an increase in fuel prices results in increased use of corn in the making of fuel. Of course, increased use of corn in the making of fuel means increased demand for corn, which will likely result in an increase in the price of corn.
What the passage says indicates that the cost of corn constitutes a significant proportion of total the cost of bringing cornflakes to market. So, the combination of what this choice says with what the passage says clearly supports the prediction that the price of cornflakes will increase if the price of fuel remains at its current level. Notice that, while the line of reasoning that makes this choice correct is fairly involved, it is completely logical, and no illogical or unsupported leaps are necessary for deciding that this choice supports the prediction.
(B) The wholesale price of cornflakes has not increased significantly for several years. This choice could be tempting, because a test-taker could make it seem to support the conclusion by coming up with a story like the following:
“Everyone knows that prices go up eventually. So, the fact that the wholesale price of cornflakes has not increased in years means that it’s ready to go up now. So, this choice supports the prediction that the price is going to increase.”
Of course, that story is not really supported by common sense or by what the passage or this choice says. In fact, one could just as easily run the other way with this choice, by saying that the fact that cornflake prices have not increased for several years indicates that they won’t increase any time soon.
So, this choice has no effect on the strength of the argument.
(C) When fuel prices increase, people generally end up with less money to spend on products other than fuel. The combination of what the passage says about fuel prices having increased with what this choice says about people having less money to spend on products other than fuel when fuel prices increase tends to indicate that, currently, people are relatively low on money to spend on products other than fuel, such as cornflakes. Of course, people’s being low on money to spend on cornflakes would not support the prediction that the price of cornflakes will increase.
(D) Cornflakes have consistently been one of the most popular types of cereal among the many types that people have to choose from. This choice could be tempting, because a test-taker could make it seem to support the conclusion by coming up with a story like the following:
“Since cornflakes have consistently been one of the most popular types of cereal, they are due for a price increase. So, this choice supports the prediction that the price is going to increase.”
Of course, that story is not really supported by common sense or by what the passage or the answer choice says. In fact, one could just as easily run the other way with this choice, by saying that the fact that cornflakes have been consistently popular indicates that their popularity has not changed recently and that, therefore, there is no reason to expect their price to increase.
So, this choice has no effect on the strength of the argument.
(E) Before last year’s increase in fuel prices, the wholesale prices of both cornflakes and fuel had been lower than their averages for recent times. This choice could be tempting, because a test-taker could make it seem to support the conclusion by coming up with a story like the following:
“Since the price of cornflakes was below its average, it is sure to increase now, because things tend to revert to their averages.”
Of course, that story is not really supported by common sense or by what the passage or the answer choice says. Not everything that is below its past average level reverts to that level.
Correct Answer: (A)
summary:
Lesson Content The most blatantly wrong answer choices in Strengthen questions simply do not affect the arguments.
In many cases, such choices are fairly easy to eliminate.
Consider the following example:
The government of Golado has recently increased the taxes imposed on the sale of all alcoholic beverages in Golado. While the tax increase is sure to result in a significant decrease in consumption of alcohol in Golado, government officials believe that increasing the taxes on sales of alcohol will result in a reduction in Golado’s government budget gap.
title: 4.13 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 1: Provides Information that Does Not Affect the Argument in any Way

contents:
Lesson Content In Strengthen questions, there can be choices that seem to strengthen arguments by providing information that seems to confirm the validity of premises.
💡MUST KNOW:
Some incorrect answers to Strengthen questions are incorrect because their only function is to seem to confirm the validity of premises. Since the premises are already considered facts, these answer choices do not affect the strength of arguments.
Consider the following example:
Over the past decade, most people in Ostazia have significantly increased their consumption of fresh fruit. Over the same time period, the incidence of heart disease in Ostazia has decreased. Clearly then, consuming fresh fruit is an effective way to prevent heart disease.
Which of the following, if true, strengthens the argument?
(Choice that Seems to Confirm the Validity of a Premise) Survey results indicate that over 80 percent of residents of Ostazia have significantly increased their consumption of fresh fruit.
(Correct Answer) The incidence of heart disease in residents of areas of Ostazia where most people continue to follow a traditional diet that includes little fruit has not changed significantly over the past decade.
Here’s the conclusion for which we need support:
Conclusion: Consuming fresh fruit is an effective way to prevent heart disease.
Notice the effect of the choice that seems to confirm the validity of a premise.
One of the premises of the argument is “Most people in Ostazia have significantly increased their consumption of fresh fruit.” The choice that seems to confirm the validity of a premise provides survey results, “80 percent of residents of Ostazia have significantly increased their consumption of fresh fruit,” that seem to confirm that most people in Ostazia have increased their consumption of fresh fruit.
However, this choice does not strengthen the argument, because such confirmation makes no difference, as, even without this confirmation, we already consider the premise “most people in Ostazia have significantly increased their consumption of fresh fruit” a fact.
Now let’s consider the Correct Answer.
(Correct Answer) The incidence of heart disease in residents of areas of Ostazia where most people continue to follow a traditional diet that includes little fruit has not changed significantly over the past decade.
This choice adds support to the conclusion by showing what happened without the presence of the variable increased consumption of fresh fruit: the incidence of heart disease did not decrease.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 13 Approximately 65 million years ago, Earth experienced the largest mass extinction of plant and animal species in its history. Which of the following, if true, most strongly supports the researchers’ hypothesis? 1. [ ] (A) Approximately 75 percent of Earth’s plant and animal species went extinct 65 million years ago. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Fossil records indicate that species that required very little sunlight to live did not go extinct at the time of the asteroid impact. 3. [ ] (C) Evidence suggests that the asteroid that impacted Earth approximately 65 million years ago could have been 81 kilometers wide, about as wide as the country of Luxembourg is long. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Approximately 65 million years ago, massive volcanoes in what is now India began erupting, sending large quantities of soot and debris into Earth’s atmosphere. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Even relatively small amounts of soot or debris in the atmosphere can cause detectable changes in the amount of sunlight that reaches Earth’s surface. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Approximately 65 million years ago, Earth experienced the largest mass extinction of plant and animal species in its history.
Conclusion: Researchers hypothesize that the cause of that mass extinction was the impact of an asteroid that occurred approximately 65 million years ago.
Premise: Because the asteroid was one of the most massive asteroids known to have impacted Earth, its impact sent enormous amounts of soot and debris into Earth’s atmosphere, causing the amount of sunlight reaching Earth to suddenly decrease.
(A) Approximately 75 percent of Earth’s plant and animal species went extinct 65 million years ago. This choice is a tempting trap, like a shiny object that we might see and want to pick up, because what it says is right in line with what the passage says.
However, in saying that approximately 75 percent of Earth’s plant and animal species went extinct 65 million years ago, this answer choice merely provides some further detail about what we already know.
In other words, the passage states as fact that, approximately 65 million years ago, Earth experienced the largest mass extinction of plant and animal species in its history. So, by saying that approximately 75 percent of Earth’s plant and animal species went extinct, all this choice does is clarify what exactly constitutes “the largest mass extinction.”
In answering Strengthen the Argument questions, be careful to not choose as correct a choice that says something that simply magnifies or clarifies a fact that the passage has already provided.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Fossil records indicate that species that required very little sunlight to live did not go extinct at the time of the asteroid impact. What this choice says strengthens the case for the researchers’ hypothesis.
The researchers’ hypothesis is, essentially, that the asteroid impact prompted the largest mass extinction of plant and animal species in Earth’s history by causing the amount of sunlight reaching Earth to suddenly decrease.
So, we can tell that many plants and animals went extinct at that time.
At the same time, this choice tells us that species that required very little sunlight to live did not go extinct at the time of the asteroid impact. Of course, this information indicates that what determined whether organisms went extinct or were able to survive was their ability to live without much sunlight, and what follows from that reasoning is that lack of sunlight is what caused the mass extinction.
While this information does not prove for sure that a sudden decrease in sunlight caused the mass extinction, it does tend to confirm the researchers’ hypothesis. Remember, we’re not looking for information that creates an airtight case; we simply need information that strengthens the case.
(C) Evidence suggests that the asteroid that impacted Earth approximately 65 million years ago could have been 81 kilometers wide, about as wide as the country of Luxembourg is long. This choice is very tempting because, by providing information on how massive the asteroid that impacted Earth may have been, it could seem to confirm that the asteroid impact caused the mass extinction.
Notice, however, that we already have the following facts:
The asteroid was one of the most massive asteroids known to have impacted Earth.
The impact sent enormous amounts of soot and debris into Earth’s atmosphere, causing the amount of sunlight reaching Earth to suddenly decrease.
So, the truth is that the additional information provided by this choice doesn’t really make any difference in the strength of the argument. This choice provides further detail about what the approximate size of the asteroid may have been, but we already knew that it was “one of the most massive asteroids known to have impacted Earth,” massive enough that its impact caused a sudden decrease in the amount of sunlight reaching Earth. The additional detail this choice provides about the asteroid’s massiveness does nothing to strengthen the argument that the decrease in sunlight is what caused the mass extinction.
Since a choice that magnifies a fact stated in the passage doesn’t affect the strength of an argument, this choice has no effect on the strength of the support for the researchers’ hypothesis.
(D) Approximately 65 million years ago, massive volcanoes in what is now India began erupting, sending large quantities of soot and debris into Earth’s atmosphere. What this choice says weakens the argument.
The researchers’ hypothesis is that it was the asteroid impact that caused the mass extinction of plants and animals.
What this answer choice says, that approximately 65 million years ago, massive volcanoes in what is now India began erupting, sending large quantities of soot and debris into Earth’s atmosphere, provides an alternative explanation for the mass extinction of plants and animals, because the soot and debris from the volcanoes could have blocked sunlight or caused other catastrophic issues that prompted the mass extinction.
By providing reason to believe that volcanic eruptions, instead of the asteroid impact, may have been responsible for the mass extinction, this choice serves to call into question the researchers’ hypothesis.
Of course, in answering this question, we’re seeking an answer choice that provides information that strengthens, not weakens, the argument.
(E) Even relatively small amounts of soot or debris in the atmosphere can cause detectable changes in the amount of sunlight that reaches Earth’s surface. This choice is tempting because it seems to confirm the researcher’s hypothesis by confirming that soot and debris block sunlight.
Notice, however, that we don’t have to confirm that soot and debris block sunlight, because the passage states as fact that soot and debris from the asteroid impact caused the amount of sunlight reaching Earth to suddenly decrease.
An answer choice that confirms or magnifies a fact that has already been stated in the passage doesn’t affect the strength of the argument.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 14 The ancient Mayan city of Tikal collapsed relatively suddenly after thriving for over 1000 years. Which of the following adds further support to the researchers’ hypothesis? 1. [ ] (A) The ancient Mayans were not aware that clearing of forests could affect meteorological conditions in ways such that sustained extreme drought would result. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Clearing of forests to make way for city development was for Mayans a common practice during the time period extending from the founding to the collapse of Tikal. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Tikal was able to grow to an especially large size because of a sophisticated infrastructure that allowed for extensive use of water from rainfall. 4. [ ] (D) Archeological evidence suggests that, in order to sustain their lifestyles, residents of Tikal would have had to fell the majority of the trees in the Tikal area. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) El Zotz, a city located close to Tikal at the time of Tikal’s collapse, continued to thrive after that point in time. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard The passage states that researchers have hypothesized the following:
Conclusion: Tikal collapsed because of sustained extreme drought conditions resulting from the clearing of forests.
The conclusion is supported by one premise:
Premise: Residents of the Tikal had cleared approximately two-thirds of the forest in the area in which the city was located.
The passage also provides the following background information:
Background Information: The ancient Mayan city of Tikal collapsed relatively suddenly after thriving for over 1000 years.
In order to correctly answer the question, we need additional information that supports the hypothesis that drought caused by clearing of forests resulted in the collapse of the city.
In considering the choices, we have to be careful not to fall for trap choices that seem to add support to the argument by confirming the validity of the premise that appears in the passage. Since that premise is considered true, information that serves only to confirm the validity of that premise does not affect the strength of the argument.
(A) The ancient Mayans were not aware that clearing of forests could affect meteorological conditions in ways such that sustained extreme drought would result. This choice could be tempting, because we could use it as the basis of a narrative about why the Mayans would clear forests even though doing so would result in drought, and then construe that narrative as somehow supporting the hypothesis that Tikal collapsed because the clearing of forests led to drought conditions.
However, the truth is that the fact that Mayans were not aware of the consequences of clearing forests does not add support to the hypothesis that Tikal did in fact collapse because of such consequences.
(B) Clearing of forests to make way for city development was for Mayans a common practice during the time period extending from the founding to the collapse of Tikal. This choice could be tempting because it seems to confirm the premise that the Mayans cleared forests in the area in which Tikal was located.
However, since that premise is already considered factual, what this choice says has no effect on the strength of the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Tikal was able to grow to an especially large size because of a sophisticated infrastructure that allowed for extensive use of water from rainfall. In order to see why this choice is correct, we have to see that, while it does not indicate that clearing of forests led to drought, it does tend to indicate that drought would have led to the collapse of Tikal, as it tends to indicate that, without water from rainfall, the existence of Tikal was not sustainable.
In other words, by using common sense, we can tell that, if a city was able to grow to an especially large size only because of the extensive use of water from rainfall, it is likely that, in the absence of rainfall, that city would collapse.
So, what this choice says makes it seem likely that sustained drought would have resulted in the collapse of Tikal, and thus this choice provides additional support for the hypothesis that drought caused the collapse.
(D) Archeological evidence suggests that, in order to sustain their lifestyles, residents of Tikal would have had to fell the majority of the trees in the Tikal area. This choice could seem to be correct because it could be perceived as strengthening the argument by confirming that residents of Tikal did in fact clear forests.
However, the truth is that, since the premise “the residents of the Tikal had cleared approximately two-thirds of the forest in the area in which the city was located” is considered factual, confirmation that the residents cleared forests has no effect on the validity of the premise or the strength of the argument.
(E) El Zotz, a city located close to Tikal at the time of Tikal’s collapse, continued to thrive after that point in time. What this choice says could be spun into a convoluted story that would weaken or strengthen the support for the hypothesis. However, since El Zotz could have continued to thrive regardless of whether drought caused the collapse of Tikal, this choice neither weakens nor strengthens the argument.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss answers that support conclusions, but the wrong conclusions.
summary:
Lesson Content In Strengthen questions, there can be choices that seem to strengthen arguments by providing information that seems to confirm the validity of premises.
💡MUST KNOW:
Some incorrect answers to Strengthen questions are incorrect because their only function is to seem to confirm the validity of premises. Since the premises are already considered facts, these answer choices do not affect the strength of arguments.
Consider the following example:
Over the past decade, most people in Ostazia have significantly increased their consumption of fresh fruit.
title: 4.14 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 2: Information That Seems to Confirm the Validity of a Premise

contents:
Lesson Content As we know, to arrive at the correct answer to a Strengthen question, we have to clearly understand exactly what conclusion we are seeking to support.
💡MUST KNOW:
To arrive at the correct answer to a Strengthen question, we have to clearly understand exactly what conclusion we are seeking to support. If we don’t, we might fall for an answer choice that supports a conclusion that is similar to the conclusion we are seeking to support but is not that conclusion.
Like the blatantly wrong answer choices that we discussed in an earlier section, an answer choice that supports the wrong conclusion does not affect the argument.
We can have a tendency – if we are not careful – to selectively remember parts of or put our own spin on what we read.
For example, the argument’s conclusion could be about revenue, while an answer choice could support a conclusion about expenses.
In each of these cases, by presenting us with an answer choice that supports a conclusion related to the conclusion that we are seeking to support, the test makers set a mental trap for us to fall into.
So how do we avoid falling into such a trap and supporting the wrong conclusion? By remembering what conclusion we do need to support.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering a Strengthen question, to avoid choosing an incorrect answer choice that supports the wrong conclusion, be careful to remember exactly what conclusion you are seeking to support.
To see this type of answer choice in action, let’s revisit our Golado tax increase example:
The government of Golado has recently increased the taxes imposed on the sale of all alcoholic beverages in Golado. While the tax increase is sure to result in a significant decrease in consumption of alcohol in Golado, government officials believe that increasing the taxes on sales of alcohol will result in a reduction in Golado’s government budget gap.
Which of the following, if true, provides evidence suggesting that the government officials’ belief is correct?
(Choice that Supports the Wrong Conclusion) The budget gap represents a relatively small percentage of the total government budget.
What this tricky choice that supports the wrong conclusion says is related to the argument, but it doesn’t support the argument’s conclusion.
This choice could support a different conclusion, such as that revenue from the tax on alcohol will be sufficient for completely closing Golado’s budget gap.
So, this choice does not provide evidence suggesting that the government of Golado will achieve its goal of reducing the gap.
Here’s another example:
A new type of roofing material reflects almost all of the sunlight that hits it. As a result, cooling systems of houses with roofs composed of this material generally do not have to run as much on hot days as do cooling systems of houses with roofs composed of other materials. Therefore, having a roof composed of this new material will help a household to save energy.
Which of the following, if true, adds the most support to the conclusion?
(Choice that Supports the Wrong Conclusion) A roof composed of the new material costs less to install than does a roof composed of other materials normally used in roof construction.
(Correct Answer) Because roofs composed of the new material hold heat in, in houses with such roofs, the heating systems run less on cold days than they would were the roofs composed of other materials.
OK, what conclusion do we have to support in order to get this one correct?
Conclusion: Having a roof composed of this new material can help a household to save energy.
Now, what conclusion does the choice that supports the wrong Conclusion support? It supports multiple possible conclusions.
Households using the new material will save money. Because roofs composed of the new material are less expensive to install, they will be popular. Of course, neither of these conclusions is the one that we need to support.
Now, let’s consider the correct answer.
(Correct Answer) Because roofs composed of the new material hold heat in, in houses with such roofs, the heating systems run less on cold days than they would were the roofs composed of other materials.
This choice adds support for the argument’s conclusion, because, if the heating systems of houses with roofs composed of the new material run less than they would were the roofs composed of other materials, the heating system of such a house will use less energy than it would were the new material not used.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering a Strengthen question, we must be careful to choose an answer choice that supports the conclusion of the argument, not a choice that supports some other conclusion.
Consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 15 While, ordinarily, software companies hire customer service specialists to handle inbound calls made by people having trouble using the companies’ software, JustDoIt, a tax preparation software company, has the people who develop its software handle such calls. Which of the following, if true, provides the strongest confirmation that JustDoIt’s strategy will work as predicted? 1. [ ] (A) JustDoIt receives fewer support calls per user of its software than other software companies typically receive per user of their software. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) JustDoIt’s software developers can help customers resolve software use issues as quickly as can customer service specialists. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Surveys indicate that JustDoIt’s customers are more satisfied with the support that JustDoIt provides than other software companies’ customers are with the support that those companies provide. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Software developers who handle calls from people having trouble using software gain insights they can use to make future versions of that software easy for virtually anyone to use. 5. [ ] (E) JustDoIt employs enough software developers that their taking time to handle support calls does not make impossible the timely development of software products. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium In the scenario presented, JustDoIt is having relatively highly paid software developers handle customer support calls rather than having customer service specialists who are paid less per hour than software developers handle the calls. In doing so, JustDoIt expects to achieve the following outcome.
Conclusion: JustDoIt expects to save money in the long run by having its software developers handle the calls.
In order to get this question correct, we have to remember what conclusion we are seeking to support, since some of the incorrect choices are trap choices that support related conclusions.
It’s important to notice that the author of this argument provides no evidence to support JustDoIt’s conclusion. Why does JustDoIt expect to save money in the long run?
So, to correctly answer the question, we have to find an answer choice that indicates that, by using the strategy, JustDoIt will save money in the long run.
(A) JustDoIt receives fewer support calls per user of its software than other software companies typically receive per user of their software. This choice supports the wrong conclusion. It supports a conclusion along the lines of that JustDoIt will not spend more money on customer support than other companies typically spend even though JustDoIt pays the people handling calls more per hour than the other companies pay. Why? Because, even though JustDoIt pays more per hour, it gets fewer calls per user.
On the other hand, this choice does not support the conclusion that JustDoIt will save money by having software developers handle calls.
After all, regardless of how few calls per user JustDoIt receives, by having software developers handle the calls, it is spending more money than it would if people who get paid less per hour were handling the calls.
So, the fact that JustDoIt receives fewer support calls per user than other companies receive does not indicate that JustDoIt will save money in the long run by having software developers handle the calls.
(B) JustDoIt’s software developers can help customers resolve software use issues as quickly as can customer service specialists. This choice supports the wrong conclusion. The conclusion is not that the software developers can handle calls just as well as or just as quickly as customer service specialists.
The fact that software developers can handle calls as quickly as customer service specialists can does not indicate that JustDoIt will save money by having software developers take the calls. After all, if it costs JustDoIt more per hour to have software developers handle the calls, and if the software developers handle the calls as quickly as customer service specialists do, then JustDoIt is still spending more money than it would spend by hiring customer service specialists, not saving money.
(C) Surveys indicate that JustDoIt’s customers are more satisfied with the support that JustDoIt provides than other software companies’ customers are with the support that those companies provide. This choice supports the wrong conclusion. The conclusion is not that JustDoIt can satisfy its customers by having software developers take support calls.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Software developers who handle calls from people having trouble using software gain insights they can use to make future versions of that software easy for virtually anyone to use. If, in the process of taking support calls, JustDoIt’s software developers gain insights that they can use to make future versions of JustDoIt’s software easy for virtually anyone to use, then it’s likely that, in the future, JustDoIt’s software will be so easy to use that JustDoIt will receive few support calls.
Thus, given what this choice says, it seems likely that, by having product development professionals take support calls now, JustDoIt can reduce the amount of money it will have to spend on taking support calls in the future.
Notice that, with what this answer choice says, the author actually has support for her argument.
(E) JustDoIt employs enough software developers that their taking time to handle support calls does not make impossible the timely development of software products. This choice supports the wrong conclusion. The conclusion is not that JustDoIt can develop products in a timely manner even though its software developers handle support calls.
Notice that the fact that JustDoIt employs “enough software developers” does not indicate that JustDoIt will save money. On the contrary, in order to employ “enough software developers,” JustDoIt has to spend enough money.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss answer choices that explain rather than add support.
summary:
Lesson Content As we know, to arrive at the correct answer to a Strengthen question, we have to clearly understand exactly what conclusion we are seeking to support.
💡MUST KNOW:
To arrive at the correct answer to a Strengthen question, we have to clearly understand exactly what conclusion we are seeking to support. If we don’t, we might fall for an answer choice that supports a conclusion that is similar to the conclusion we are seeking to support but is not that conclusion.
title: 4.15 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 3: Choice That Supports the Wrong Conclusion

contents:
Lesson Content We must always remember that the premises in Critical Reasoning arguments are considered factual.
💡MUST KNOW:
The premises in Critical Reasoning arguments are considered factual. In other words, the premises are facts. Since there is no way to strengthen a fact, there is no way to strengthen a premise.
Therefore, it’s important to know that answer choices that explain part of a passage do not strengthen an argument because, as we just said, premises are facts, and even if we were to provide a 100-page essay explaining a fact, we couldn’t strengthen it.
So we need to be careful not to be tricked into choosing as correct a choice that says something that explains part of the passage instead of providing information that actually strengthens the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering a Strengthen question, we must make sure that we don’t get tricked into choosing a choice that explains part of the passage rather than supports the conclusion of the argument.
Let’s continue with our roofing material example:
A new type of roofing material reflects almost all of the sunlight that hits it. As a result, cooling systems of houses with roofs composed of this material generally do not have to run as much on hot days as do cooling systems of houses with roofs composed of other materials. Therefore, having a roof composed of this new material will help a household to save energy.
Which of the following, if true, adds the most support for the conclusion?
(Choice that Explains Part of the Passage) Sunlight not reflected by a house’s roof can heat the house, an effect that the house’s cooling system must work to counteract.
(Correct Answer) Because roofs composed of the new material hold heat in, in houses with such roofs, the heating systems run less on cold days than they would were the roofs composed of other materials.
The choice that explains part of the stimulus seems to support the argument.
Because, we ALREADY KNOW that, by reflecting sunlight, the new material makes it easier for a cooling system to cool a house, and all this choice does is explain how that process works.
So, this choice doesn’t add support for the conclusion.
The correct answer, on the other hand, provides entirely new support, by indicating that, not only do roofs composed of the new material make cooling houses easier but also, they help to save energy on cold days when houses have to be heated.
Now, let’s see how a choice that explains part of the passage could work with the Super Soap scenario:
The Endless Summer cruise line washes its ships regularly, with the result that a large amount of soap goes into the ocean. Endless Summer’s management has decided to switch soaps, from the soap that the company currently uses to Super Soap, which, unlike the soap that the company currently uses, has essentially no negative environmental effects in the concentrations that result from washing ships. Management feels that, by making this change, Endless Summer will be taking a step toward reducing the negative environmental effects of the company’s operations.
Which of the following, if true, serves to confirm that management’s plan will have the desired effect?
(Choice that Explains Part of the Passage) All of the ingredients in Super Soap are substances that exist naturally in the ocean.
(Correct Answer) The process of manufacturing Super Soap has fewer negative effects on the environment than does the process of manufacturing the soap currently used by Endless Summer.
Once again, the choice that explains part of the passage seems to support the conclusion.
However, the issue with this choice is that we already know from the passage that Super Soap “has essentially no negative environmental effects.” All the choice that explains part of the passage does is provide information that may explain why Super Soap has essentially no negative environmental effects.
As we discussed previously, the correct answer, on the other hand, does add new support for the conclusion, by showing an additional way in which Super Soap is less harmful to the environment than the soap that the company has been using.
Consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 16 Over the last forty years, the crime rate in the nation of Zundinia has steadily decreased. Which of the following, if true, provides the best support for the hypothesis? 1. [ ] (A) Lead in water can be absorbed into a person’s body through the person’s digestive system if the person drinks water that contains lead. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) While, in Zundinia, paint that contains lead is no longer used, there is still paint that contains lead on the walls of some older buildings. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Forty years ago, a major initiative to increase quality of education in Zundinia was the start of a trend of improvement in education there that has continued to this day. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) The adding of lead to gasoline meant for use in automobiles in Zundinia has been almost completely phased out over the past four decades. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) In Zundinian cities in which most water is still transported via pipes containing lead, the crime rates are little changed from what they were 40 years ago. View Solution Question Level: medium We are looking for support for the following hypothesis:
Conclusion: It is likely that the decrease in the crime rate in Zundinia can be at least partly attributed to the simultaneous decrease that has occurred in the amount of lead in the air and water in Zundinia.
There is already some support for the hypothesis:
Premise: People exposed to air or water that contains lead absorb some lead into their bodies, where it has negative impacts on their cognitive functioning.
We must keep in mind that this premise must be considered a fact, and any explanation of this fact (how it came to be, what’s causing it, the physiology of lead poisoning, etc.) will not strengthen the argument.
At the same time, the above premise is a general principle, rather than information that clearly connects the decrease in lead in the air and water in Zundinia with the decrease in the crime rate. Thus, information that indicates more clearly that a reduction in lead exposure is indeed what underlies the decrease in the crime rate in Zundinia would strengthen the support for the hypothesis.
Notice that the author is making the cause-and-effect claim that the steady decrease in the crime rate has been caused by the decrease that has occurred in the amount of lead in the air and water. In answering this question, our job is to find an answer choice that strengthens the case for that cause-and-effect claim, but in answering question, we have to be careful not to choose a choice that simply explains an aspect of the scenario described in the passage.
(A) Lead in water can be absorbed into a person’s body through the person’s digestive system if the person drinks water that contains lead. This choice does not add support for the hypothesis but, rather, explains how lead from water gets absorbed into people’s bodies. The passage has already stated as fact that lead in water can be absorbed into people’s bodies. Explaining how it is absorbed does not strengthen the support for the hypothesis.
(B) While, in Zundinia, paint that contains lead is no longer used, there is still paint that contains lead on the walls of some older buildings. This detail does not add support for the conclusion, as the fact that there is still lead to which people may be exposed certainly does not help to support the conclusion that a reduction in lead exposure is the reason for a decrease in the crime rate. At the same time, this choice does not affect the validity of the premise, which is the stated fact that the amount of lead in the air and water has decreased.
(C) Forty years ago, a major initiative to increase quality of education in Zundinia was the start of a trend of improvement in education there that has continued to this day. This choice presents another change that has occurred over the past 40 years and that could underlie the decrease in the crime rate, an improvement in education. So, given what this choice says, it may be that better education rather than a reduction in environmental lead underlies the decrease in the crime rate. Thus, by presenting a plausible alternative hypothesis, this choice weakens the support for the hypothesis presented in the passage.
(D) The adding of lead to gasoline meant for use in automobiles in Zundinia has been almost completely phased out over the past four decades. This choice explains, at least partly, why the amount of lead in the air and water in Zundinia has decreased: it has decreased because automobiles are no longer burning leaded gasoline and thus spreading lead everywhere.
At the same time, we already know that the amount of lead in the air and water has decreased over the past 40 years.
Explaining why it has decreased does not strengthen the support for the hypothesis.
YOUR CHOICE(E) In Zundinian cities in which most water is still transported via pipes containing lead, the crime rates are little changed from what they were 40 years ago. This choice strengthens the support for the hypothesis by showing what happens when we eliminate a variable, the decrease in the presence of lead.
It seems likely that, in cities in which most water is still transported via pipes containing lead, the water still contains lead. Thus, those cities likely do not have the variable “decrease of the amount of lead in the water.” It is also the case that in those cities the crime rates didn’t decrease.
Thus, without the variable “decrease in the amount of lead in the water,” we also don’t have the result “decrease in the crime rate.” Therefore, what this choice says tends to confirm that crime rates decreased because of decreases in the amount of lead in the water and, thus, strengthens the support for the hypothesis.
Remember, when we have a cause-and-effect conclusion, if we can show that, when the cause is eliminated, the effect is also, then we can strengthen the support for the conclusion.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now consider choices that don’t strengthen arguments because the information that the choices present is not well connected to the scenarios in the arguments.
summary:
Lesson Content We must always remember that the premises in Critical Reasoning arguments are considered factual.
💡MUST KNOW:
The premises in Critical Reasoning arguments are considered factual. In other words, the premises are facts. Since there is no way to strengthen a fact, there is no way to strengthen a premise.
Therefore, it’s important to know that answer choices that explain part of a passage do not strengthen an argument because, as we just said, premises are facts, and even if we were to provide a 100-page essay explaining a fact, we couldn’t strengthen it.
title: 4.16 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 4: Explains a Part of the Stimulus

contents:
Lesson Content A typical argument in a Strengthen question is based on a scenario that involves, for example, a particular place, company, phenomenon, or point in time.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering a Strengthen question, we must take care not to select as correct choices that aren’t well-connected to the scenario in the argument.
So, what does it mean to be “not well-connected?” The truth of the matter is that what the GMAT considers not well-connected might be different from what we’d expect (sorry – don’t kill the messenger).
For instance, an argument could be about the expected effects of increasing education spending in one large city, while the answer choice could be about the effects of increasing education spending in a small town.
While, in the real world, we might consider the effects of actions taken in one city or town when considering what the effects of similar actions taken in a different city or town might be, in GMAT Critical Reasoning, the point of view generally taken is that such comparisons do not provide reliable evidence for conclusions.
So, in this example, the GMAT would take the point of view that, since conditions in the two places could be different, the fact that increasing education spending in the city had a certain effect is not strong support for the conclusion that increasing education spending in the small town will have the same effect.
Here are some more examples of information that the GMAT would not consider strong support for a conclusion:
The fact that a storm that occurred five years ago was followed by an increase in the price of tomatoes probably would not be considered strong support for the argument that, because there was a storm last month, the price of tomatoes will soon increase. The fact that one large company was able to increase profitability by shutting down half its factories would probably not be considered the correct answer supporting a conclusion that a relatively small company should shut down factories. The fact that a discount store’s profits did not decrease when a recession occurred would not provide good support for the conclusion that a high-end department store’s profits will not decrease during a recession. The fact that a trucking company was able to use truck driving simulators to help its drivers reduce their yearly number of driving accidents does not provide good support that an airline would be able to use flight simulators to reduce the yearly number of accidents experienced by its airplane pilots.
