Notice that, while each of these questions resembles what we have to explain, neither is exactly what we have to explain.
(Trap Choice) Most citizens of Pondville were too busy to take ducks to another town to get a reward.
Notice that this choice would explain why the plan didn’t work: citizens of Pondville were too busy to take ducks to another town to get the reward.
However, this choice does not explain what we have to explain.
Here’s another trap choice that we could fall for if we didn’t correctly identify what we have to explain:
(Trap Choice) Most ducks released in the neighboring town did not like the neighboring town and found their way back to Pondville.
This choice could help to explain why the number of ducks in Pondville didn’t decrease: most ducks released in the neighboring town returned to Pondville.
Once again, however, this choice does not explain what we actually have to explain.
All the same, we can see how we could end up choosing one of the trap choices if we weren’t careful to identify exactly what we have to explain.
OK, so, what would explain what we have to explain?
(Correct Answer) In order to easily get the reward multiple times, some people in Pondville began breeding ducks in their back yards, from which many ducks escaped.
We can see that this correct answer does explain what we have to explain.
At the same time, the key thing to notice here is that the correct answer would not look any better than the trap choices if we hadn’t identified exactly what we have to explain.
So, we can see that a very important step in answering a Paradox question is identifying exactly what the paradox is that we must resolve.
💡MUST KNOW:
A very important step in answering a Paradox question is identifying exactly what the paradox is that we must resolve.
Now, often, to correctly identify what the correct answer to a Paradox question has to explain, we have to notice the direction of what the correct answer has to explain.
For instance, in the case of our example about the town overrun with ducks, what the correct answer has to explain is, basically, why there was an increase.
In other questions, the correct answers may have to explain why something decreased, why something did not increase, or why something remained the same.
💡MUST KNOW:
Often, to correctly answer a Paradox question, we have to notice the direction of what the correct answer has to explain.
Alternatively, we may have to explain why two things that seem the same have different effects or why one variable has a surprising effect on another variable.
In each case, identifying exactly what we have to explain is going to be a key aspect of arriving at the correct answer.
Let’s now apply what we have just discussed in answering an example question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 5 In the small town of Oakville, the proprietors of the three locally owned coffee shops located there were dismayed to see that a major coffee shop chain with a big marketing budget had decided to open a shop in town. Which of the following does the most to explain the surprising outcome? 1. [ ] (A) Most of the people who regularly drink coffee in Oakville prefer the taste of the locally owned shops’ coffee over that of the major chain’s coffee. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) During peak hours, the length of time customers spend in line at the major chain’s shop is often longer than some people are willing to wait. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) The proprietors of the locally owned coffee shops have run the shops for many years and are familiar to residents of Oakville. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) The marketing messages that the major chain’s shop puts out to potential customers have convinced many that drinking coffee is enjoyable and life enhancing. 5. [ ] (E) The major chain’s shop serves not only coffee but also gourmet sandwiches and desserts. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium What is the apparent paradox presented here?
Fact 1: A major coffee shop chain with a big marketing budget opened a shop in Oakville.
Fact 2: After the major coffee chain’s shop opened, three locally owned shops experienced significant increases in business.
Rephrased as a question, the apparent paradox is the following:
What has to be explained: Why would it be that, after a major coffee chain with a big marketing budget opened a shop in Oakville, three locally owned shops experienced significant increases in business?
Now, the key thing we have to note in this case is that only an answer choice that explains why the three locally owned shops experienced increases in business will be correct. A choice that does not explain increases in business will not be correct.
So, if we look for a choice that explains something vaguely similar to what we have to explain, such as why the locally owned coffee shops have not experienced decreases in business or why the local coffee shops are still doing OK, we could easily miss this question.
(A) Most of the people who regularly drink coffee in Oakville prefer the taste of the locally owned shops’ coffee over that of the major chain’s coffee. This choice could be tempting, because it describes a reason why locally owned coffee shops could successfully compete with the major chain’s shop.
Notice, however, that the fact that people prefer the locally owned shops’ coffee does not explain why the shops would experience increases in business. After all, people’s liking the locally owned shops’ coffee is not a new thing. Presumably they have liked these shops’ coffee all along. So, while this choice would explain why the locally owned shops have not experienced decreases in business, there is no reason why people’s preferring these shops’ coffee would result in increases in business.
So, this choice does not explain what we have to explain.
YOUR CHOICE(B) During peak hours, the length of time customers spend in line at the major chain’s shop is often longer than some people are willing to wait. This choice could be tempting, because it could be taken as indicating that, at times, in order to avoid the line at the major chain’s shop, some people might decide to go to locally owned coffee shops.
Notice, however, that what the fact that there are long lines at the major chain’s shop really indicates is that the major chain’s shop has many customers. That fact that major chain’s shop has many customers would not explain why the locally owned shops experienced increases in business.
Also, for all we know, the locally owned shops also have long lines during peak hours.
So, this choice does not explain what the correct answer has to explain.
(C) The proprietors of the locally owned coffee shops have run the shops for many years and are familiar to residents of Oakville. This choice could be tempting, because it describes a reason why locally owned coffee shops could successfully compete with the major chain’s shop.
Notice, however, that the fact that people are familiar with the locally owned shops’ owners does not explain why the shops would experience increases in business. People’s knowing the locally owned shops’ owners is not a new thing. So, there is no reason why it would cause increases, in other words, something new.
Thus, this choice does not explain what we have to explain.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) The marketing messages that the major chain’s shop puts out to potential customers have convinced many that drinking coffee is enjoyable and life enhancing. This choice could explain why the locally owned shops experienced increases in business.
The marketing done by the major chain’s shop convinced people to drink coffee. So, people in Oakville increased the amount of coffee that they drink, and all of the shops, including the locally owned shops, experienced increases in business.
Since this choice explains exactly what we have to explain, it is the correct answer.
(E) The major chain’s shop serves not only coffee but also gourmet sandwiches and desserts. There is no clear reason to believe that the major chain’s shop’s serving gourmet sandwiches and desserts would result in an increase in business at the other shops. So, this choice does not explain what we have to explain.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss the fact that we don’t seek a conclusion in a Resolve the Paradox question.
summary:
Lesson Content Precision in reading is one of the most important Critical Reasoning skills to master.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Resolve the Paradox questions, every word matters.
Accordingly, when test-takers get Paradox questions incorrect, they often do so because, when reading the passage of a Paradox question, they do not develop a precise understanding of what the paradox is that they need to resolve.
In answering a Paradox question, we must have a precise understanding of exactly what the paradox is that we must resolve.
title: 5.5 In answering a Paradox question, we must understand exactly what the paradox is that we must resolve.

contents:
Lesson Content As we answer a Paradox question, there is no need to look for a conclusion either in the stimulus or in the correct answer, because the author is not making an argument in the way in which he or she does in a Strengthen or Weaken question.
💡MUST KNOW:
We don’t need to find a conclusion to correctly answer a Resolve the Paradox question. So, don’t look for one!
In the stimulus of a Resolve the Paradox question, the author is presenting us with a paradox; the stimulus is simply an observation.
Let’s look at a simple example:
Antibiotics are ineffective against viruses. Yet, doctors routinely prescribe a course of antibiotics to patients infected with certain viruses that weaken the immune system.
Let’s look at the paradox here.
Fact 1: Antibiotics are ineffective against viruses.
Fact 2: Doctors prescribe antibiotics to patients infected with viruses that weaken the immune system.
If you’re struggling to find a conclusion in the above passage, the reason you are is because there isn’t one in the passage.
One way out of this paradox is to point out the following:
Patients who have compromised immune systems (such as those infected with these viruses) are likely to acquire secondary bacterial infections that antibiotics are effective against.
Meanwhile, just as there is no point in looking for a conclusion in a Paradox question stimulus, we don’t need to look for a conclusion among the answer choices.
In fact, in some cases, incorrect choices that appear in a Resolve the Paradox question are simply conclusions that follow from the information in the stimulus.
Here’s an example of this type of incorrect answer choice in action:
In Verdantia, there are websites that allow consumers to compare prices at which goods are sold by various retailers. Even though the presence of such sites fosters competition between retailers, as the number of price comparison sites in Verdantia has increased to a point such that there are a very large number of such sites, the prices of goods that appear on them also have increased.
Which of the following, if true, would best explain this unexpected result of the proliferation of price comparison sites?
(Incorrect Choice that States a Conclusion) Consumers are best off when there are some, but not a very large number of, price comparison sites.
(Correct Answer) When there are many price comparison sites, retailers, which pay to appear on the sites, incur significant expenses, which they pass on to consumers in the form of higher prices.
Notice how tempting the incorrect choice that states a conclusion could be.
The correct answer, on the other hand, has the effect that we are looking for, since it explains how it could be the case that a proliferation of price comparison sites could result in price increases even though such sites engender competition.
Let’s look at an example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 6 Online dating apps have made it easy for people to get in touch with many potential partners. Which of the following, if true, provides the best explanation for these paradoxical results? 1. [ ] (A) For many people, using more traditional methods of meeting potential partners might work better than using online dating apps. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) A person using an online dating app can get in touch with likeminded people whom the person probably would not have met without using such an app. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) A person faced with even slight imperfections in a potential partner may be tempted to continue looking if many other potential partners seem to be available. 4. [ ] (D) Having many options does not always make choosing among options easier than it is when fewer options are available. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Whether a person is using dating apps or using more traditional methods of meeting people, in order for a relationship to work, the person has to communicate well. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy What is the apparent paradox presented here?
Fact 1: Online dating apps have made it easy for people to get in touch with many potential partners.
Fact 2: In many cases, the use of such apps has resulted in people’s remaining single for much longer than they would have had they not used such apps.
Notice that the passage does not include a conclusion. That it doesn’t is not a problem, as we don’t need a conclusion. What we do need to see clearly is a paradox.
Rephrased as a question, the apparent paradox looks something like this:
What has to be explained: How could it be that using an app that makes getting in touch with potential partners easy results in people’s remaining single for much longer than they otherwise would have?
(A) For many people, using more traditional methods of meeting potential partners might work better than using online dating apps. This choice states a conclusion that follows from the statements in the stimulus.
If, even though online dating apps make getting in touch with potential partners easy, many people using them stay single for longer than they would have were they not using such apps, then it follows that, for many people, using more traditional methods of meeting potential partners might work better than using online dating apps.
All the same, we don’t need a conclusion. We need an explanation, and this choice does not explain how the two facts can be true simultaneously.
(B) A person using an online dating app can get in touch with likeminded people whom the person probably would not have met without using such an app. This choice simply extends what we already know, that people using online dating apps can get in touch with people easily. It doesn’t explain how using the apps can result in people’s staying single for longer than they otherwise would have.
YOUR CHOICE(C) A person faced with even slight imperfections in a potential partner may be tempted to continue looking if many other potential partners seem to be available. This choice explains how using an app that makes getting in touch with potential partners easy could result in a person’s staying single for much longer than that person otherwise would have. Feeling that a huge number of choices are easily available, a person using such an app might become so picky that the person doesn’t end up in a relationship with anyone.
(D) Having many options does not always make choosing among options easier than it is when fewer options are available. This choice might seem to state a conclusion that follows from the information presented in the stimulus.
Given the fact that using apps that make getting in touch with many people easy results in people staying single longer than they otherwise would have, one might conclude that having more options from which to choose does not always make choosing easier.
All the same, we don’t need a conclusion. We need an explanation that reconciles the two facts, and this choice does not explain why apps that make it easy to get in touch with many people results in people staying single for longer than they otherwise would have.
(E) Whether a person is using dating apps or using more traditional methods of meeting people, in order for a relationship to work, the person has to communicate well. This choice does not explain the right thing.
While this choice might explain why someone using online dating apps would stay single for just as long as a person using other methods would, it does not explain why a person using such apps would stay single for even longer than that person would without using such apps.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss why, in Resolve the Paradox questions, we should look for what could be true, not what must be true.
summary:
Lesson Content As we answer a Paradox question, there is no need to look for a conclusion either in the stimulus or in the correct answer, because the author is not making an argument in the way in which he or she does in a Strengthen or Weaken question.
💡MUST KNOW:
We don’t need to find a conclusion to correctly answer a Resolve the Paradox question. So, don’t look for one!
title: 5.6 Don’t Look for a Conclusion in a Resolve the Paradox Question.

contents:
Lesson Content For any Resolve the Paradox question, there will likely be many possible ways in which we could resolve the paradox.
💡MUST KNOW:
For any Resolve the Paradox question, there will likely be many possible ways in which we could resolve the paradox. Our job is just to find one of them.
We’re not trying to find the one answer that must be true.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering a Resolve the Paradox question, we’re looking for an answer that could be true. We’re not seeking an answer that must be true.
For example, let’s say we were given the following stimulus:
The South County region has received virtually no rainfall for several years. However, regional water managers have decided not to impose restrictions on residential water use.
What’s the paradox here? Well, on the one hand, there’s no rain.
Here are some possible explanations:
Explanation 1: South County imports most of its water from North County, which has received above average rain.
Reason: If South County gets its water from North County, which has lots of water, then the lack of rain may not result in water shortfall and thus may not be grounds for restrictions on residential water use.
Explanation 2: South County has received unusually high amounts of snow during the last few winters.
Reason: If South County has received lots of snow, then it could meet its water needs even though there hasn’t been rain.
Explanation 3: South County recently opened a desalination plant that converts seawater into drinkable fresh water.
Reason: If South County is making fresh water from seawater, then it doesn’t need rain to meet its water needs.
Explanation 4: South County regional water managers have imposed severe water restrictions on industrial and agricultural water use.
Reason: If the water managers are restricting other kinds of water uses, then they might not need to restrict residential water use.
Explanation 5: South County has water reserves sufficient for meeting its needs for the next decade.
Reason: If South County has lots of extra water in reserve, then a period without rain isn’t necessarily a problem that would require restrictions on water use.
We should notice that none of these statements has to be true.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 7 Estimates of life expectancy made via use of mortality tables are, on average, found to be fairly accurate, and many companies successfully utilize such tables for business purposes. Which of the following, if true, best explains the discrepancy between the common experience of the predictive utility of the tables and the experiences financial planners have had with using the tables? 1. [x] (A) People who hire financial planning consultants tend to take unusually good care of all aspects of their lives, including their health. 2. [ ] (B) A financial planning consultant who makes recommendations on the basis of life expectancy predictions based entirely on standard mortality tables could cause clients to experience significant financial difficulties. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) In addition to using mortality tables, many financial planners use interactive online tools to predict client lifespans. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Financial planners who base predictions of clients’ lifespans entirely on data from mortality tables are more likely to underestimate than overestimate clients’ lifespans. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Most financial planning consultants are aware that using mortality tables can result in underestimating life expectancy, and therefore they adjust their predictions accordingly. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium What is the apparent paradox presented here?
Fact 1: Estimates of life expectancy made via use of mortality tables are, on average, found to be fairly accurate.
Fact 2: Financial planning consultants who have used mortality tables in determining the life expectancies of clients have found that, on average, their clients significantly outlive the projections that have been made via use of the tables.
Rephrased as a question, the apparent paradox looks something like this:
What has to be explained: Why would it be that, even though estimates of life expectancy made via use of mortality tables are, on average, found to be fairly accurate, financial planning consultants find that, on average, their clients significantly outlive such estimates?
Notice, the answer does not have to be true given what is said in the stimulus. Rather, the answer will have to show why the clients tend to outlive the estimates.
YOUR CHOICE(A) People who hire financial planning consultants tend to take unusually good care of all aspects of their lives, including their health. This choice shows that there is something about the clients of financial planning consultants that makes the clients unusual and that makes them likely to live longer than most people do. They tend to take unusually good care of their health.
This fact would explain why, even though life expectancies predicted via use of mortality tables are generally fairly accurate, clients of financial planning consultants would, on average, outlive such life expectancies.
(B) A financial planning consultant who makes recommendations on the basis of life expectancy predictions based entirely on standard mortality tables could cause clients to experience significant financial difficulties. It must be true that a financial planning consultant who makes recommendations on the basis of life expectancy predictions based entirely on data from mortality tables could cause clients to experience significant financial difficulties, because clients of financial planning consultants tend to outlive such projected life expectancies, and financial planning consultants help people to plan for retirement.
However, in order to correctly answer the question, we don’t need an answer that must be true, but rather one that explains why the clients tend to outlive such predictions, and this choice does not help to explain why they do.
(C) In addition to using mortality tables, many financial planners use interactive online tools to predict client lifespans. Given that lifespan predictions based on mortality tables often turn out to be inaccurate, it’s not surprising that financial planning consultants would use tools other than mortality tables for predicting lifespans.
However, the fact that financial planning consultants use other tools does not explain why their clients tend to outlive lifespans predicted via the use of mortality tables.
(D) Financial planners who base predictions of clients’ lifespans entirely on data from mortality tables are more likely to underestimate than overestimate clients’ lifespans. It must be true that financial planners who base predictions of clients’ lifespans entirely on data from mortality tables are more likely to underestimate than overestimate clients’ lifespans, because, on average, financial planning consultant’s clients outlive projected lifespans based on data from mortality tables.
However, we don’t need a choice that expresses something that must be true, but rather a choice that explains why the clients tend to outlive such predictions.
(E) Most financial planning consultants are aware that using mortality tables can result in underestimating life expectancy, and therefore they adjust their predictions accordingly. This choice does not explain the right thing.
While this choice helps to explain how financial planning consultants avoid underestimating their clients’ lifespans, it doesn’t explain why their clients tend to outlive projected lifespans based on data from mortality tables.
Correct Answer: (A) Now that we have a solid understanding of how Paradox questions work, let’s continue.
summary:
Lesson Content For any Resolve the Paradox question, there will likely be many possible ways in which we could resolve the paradox.
💡MUST KNOW:
For any Resolve the Paradox question, there will likely be many possible ways in which we could resolve the paradox. Our job is just to find one of them.
We’re not trying to find the one answer that must be true.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering a Resolve the Paradox question, we’re looking for an answer that could be true.
title: 5.7 In Answering Resolve the Paradox Questions, Look for What Could be True, Not What Must be True.

contents:
Lesson Content The more skilled we are at recognizing incorrect choices in Resolve the Paradox questions, the better we’ll be at working through these questions efficiently and accurately, and the less likely we’ll be to fall for trap choices.
Let’s start our discussion of common Resolve the Paradox incorrect choices by reviewing a tricky type of incorrect choice: a choice that explains the wrong thing.
summary:
Lesson Content The more skilled we are at recognizing incorrect choices in Resolve the Paradox questions, the better we’ll be at working through these questions efficiently and accurately, and the less likely we’ll be to fall for trap choices.
Let’s start our discussion of common Resolve the Paradox incorrect choices by reviewing a tricky type of incorrect choice: a choice that explains the wrong thing.
title: 5.8 Common Resolve the Paradox Incorrect Choices

contents:
Lesson Content As we have discussed, our job in answering a Resolve the Paradox question is to find a choice that explains how the facts that make up the apparent paradox can coexist.
Incorrect choices that explain the wrong thing, in other words, choices that explain something other than how the facts can coexist, are common in Paradox questions.
Such a choice can be rather tempting because, even though it doesn’t explain what the correct answer has to explain, it does explain something related to the information presented by the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Resolve the Paradox questions, trap choices often explain the wrong thing.
Let’s look at an example:
In South America, many fruit crops have traditionally been pollinated by native bumblebees. In the late 1990’s European bumblebees were introduced into areas of South America, to assist with crop pollination. However, in some cases, following the introduction of European bumblebees, crop yields decreased rather than increased.
Which of the following, if true, does the most to explain why this unexpected result occured?
So, what’s the paradox here?
Fact 1: European bumblebees were introduced into areas of South America, to assist with crop pollination.
Fact 2: Following the introduction of European bumblebees, yields of some crops decreased rather than increased.
The correct answer to this Paradox question will explain how both facts could be true even though they seem to be in conflict.
For instance, a choice that explains something related to only one side of the paradox will be incorrect.
(Choice that Explains the Wrong Thing) Since European bumblebees are known to successfully pollinate crops in Europe, it was expected that they would be helpful in pollinating crops in South America.
The above choice explains why European bumblebees were introduced into areas of South America.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Resolve the Paradox questions, an option that explains something related to only one side of the paradox will be incorrect.
A choice that explains something related to the entire stimulus but does not explain how the two facts can coexist will also be incorrect.
(Choice that Explains the Wrong Thing) Pollination of most crop plants by bees is necessary, because, without such pollination, these plants will not bear fruit.
The above statement explains something related to the entire stimulus: why pollination by bees is necessary in order for crop plants to yield fruit.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Resolve the Paradox questions, an option that explains something related to the entire stimulus but does not explain how the two facts can coexist will be incorrect.
The following choice has the effect that we are looking for:
(Correct Answer) European bumblebees carry bee killing parasites to which bumblebees native to South America have little resistance.
Finally, we have a statement that explains why crop yields decreased: while European bumblebees were assisting with pollination, the native bees that had been pollinating crops before the arrival of European bees were being killed by parasites carried by the European bees.
💡MUST KNOW:
Beware of choices that explain things other than how the two facts that make up the paradox can coexist.
Consider the following examples:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 8 Dolphins have been observed swimming at speeds in excess of 50 kilometers per hour in ocean water. Which of the following, if true, resolves the apparent paradox described above? 1. [x] (A) Dolphins can ride waves, utilizing the waves’ energies to propel themselves through water. 2. [ ] (B) When dolphins swim at their maximum speeds, they can readily catch fast moving fish and squid, which are staple foods in dolphins’ diets. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Scientists have determined that sharks of some species generate enough energy to swim at speeds in excess of 50 kilometers per hour in ocean water. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Dolphins have been observed swimming at speeds far lower than 50 kilometers per hour in ocean water. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Dolphins navigate through ocean water using echolocation, a process involving emitting and receiving sound waves, allowing them to accurately map their surroundings. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy Fact 1: Dolphins have been observed swimming at speeds in excess of 50 kilometers per hour in ocean water.
Fact 2: However, when biologists analyze the muscles of dolphins, all agree that dolphins do not have sufficient muscle mass to generate enough energy to propel their bodies at 50 kilometers per hour through water.
Think about it. Dolphins have been observed swimming at speeds in excess of the speeds one would expect them to be able to achieve given their muscle mass.
What could explain this peculiar situation? What information would resolve this apparent paradox?
YOUR CHOICE(A) Dolphins can ride waves, utilizing the waves’ energies to propel themselves through water. What this answer choice says resolves the apparent paradox.
If dolphins can ride waves, utilizing the waves’ energies to propel themselves through water, then this fact would explain why dolphins have been observed swimming at speeds in excess of 50 kilometers per hour in ocean water, even though their own bodies can’t produce enough power for them to reach those speeds.
Imagine, for example, that a dolphin is swimming at, let’s say, 25 kilometers per hour and riding a wave moving at 25 kilometers per hour. In this case, the dolphin could be moving through the ocean at 50 kilometers per hour.
We see that there is no paradox after all. Dolphins ride waves to make use of power that they cannot produce on their own.
(B) When dolphins swim at their maximum speeds, they can readily catch fast moving fish and squid, which are staple foods in dolphins’ diets. Notice that this choice explains the wrong thing.
This choice may explain why dolphins swim so fast: maybe they swim fast to catch fast moving fish and squid.
Of course, in answering this question, we’re not seeking to explain why they swim that fast. Rather, we’re seeking to explain how they swim that fast.
(C) Scientists have determined that sharks of some species generate enough energy to swim at speeds in excess of 50 kilometers per hour in ocean water. We’re seeking to explain how dolphins can travel more than 50 kilometers per hour through the ocean despite the fact their bodies can’t produce energy sufficient for them to reach such speeds.
What this answer choice says, that scientists have determined that sharks of some species generate enough energy to swim at speeds in excess of 50 kilometers per hour in ocean water, can’t help us resolve the paradox because it doesn’t change what we know about dolphins.
Given that this answer choice says, we’re still wondering how dolphins reach speeds in excess of 50 kilometers per hour in the ocean.
(D) Dolphins have been observed swimming at speeds far lower than 50 kilometers per hour in ocean water. The fact that dolphins have been observed swimming at speeds far lower than 50 kilometers per hour in ocean water does not help us explain how dolphins have been observed moving at speeds in excess of 50 kilometers per hour in the ocean.
OK, so they don’t always swim faster than 50 kilometers per hour. That fact does not change the fact that we have to explain, that sometimes dolphins do swim at speeds greater than 50 kilometers per hour.
We can’t resolve a paradox by showing that not all situations are characterized by the paradox that we have to explain.
(E) Dolphins navigate through ocean water using echolocation, a process involving emitting and receiving sound waves, allowing them to accurately map their surroundings. What this choice says explains the wrong thing.
What it says may explain how dolphins navigate effectively while moving at high speeds: maybe they use echolocation to navigate effectively at high speeds.
However, it does not explain how dolphins move at speeds in excess of 50 mph in the ocean.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 9 Email scams work by convincing people that they will benefit from engaging in business transactions with people who have contacted them by email. Which of the following best helps to explain this surprising pattern? 1. [ ] (A) For many people who perpetrate email scams, the language that they use in composing emails pitching sham transactions is not their first language. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) A single person can efficiently send thousands of emails pitching sham transactions by using bulk email management software. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Many of the introductory emails sent by people perpetrating email scams are based on patterns that have been used many times. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) The people who do not notice the low quality of an email are those least likely to realize that a transaction is a scam in the middle of the process of completing it. 5. [ ] (E) Many of the people who write emails pitching scams have much experience in perpetrating scams and thus know which methods are most effective. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium What is the apparent paradox presented here?
Fact 1: The introductory emails meant to get people to consider engaging in scam transactions are often written in ways such that recognizing that the senders are pitching scams is not difficult.
Fact 2: The low quality of the emails can result in scammers being more rather than less productive.
Rephrased as a question, the apparent paradox looks something like this:
What has to be explained: How could it be that the fact that it is not difficult to recognize that emails are pitching scam transactions results in scammers’ being more productive?
Only a choice that answers the above question will work. A choice that explains anything else will not work.
(A) For many people who perpetrate email scams, the language that they use in composing emails pitching sham transactions is not their first language. This choice explains the wrong thing.
Rather than explain how the low quality of the emails results in scammers’ being more productive, it seems to explain why the wording of the emails is of such poor quality: the writers are composing emails in languages other than the writers’ first languages.
(B) A single person can efficiently send thousands of emails pitching sham transactions by using bulk email management software. This choice explains the wrong thing.
Rather than explain how the low quality of the emails results in scammers’ being more productive, it explains their productivity in another way: they use software to send emails in bulk.
(C) Many of the introductory emails sent by people perpetrating email scams are based on patterns that have been used many times. This choice does not explain the right thing.
This choice could perhaps help to explain why scammers often use poorly worded email messages that discuss transactions that sound unlikely to be legitimate: the scammers are sending messages based on patterns that have been used many times, and perhaps those patterns aren’t very good.
However, this choice does not explain why sending poorly worded messages about transactions that sound unlikely to be legitimate results in relatively high productivity. After all, the fact that the patterns have been used many times does not explain why use of messages based on the patterns results in relatively high productivity. In other words, simply using a pattern many times does not cause it to work well.
In fact, what this choice says leaves us with a new thing to explain: Why have email scammers been basing their messages on these patterns if doing so results in poorly worded messages about transactions that sound unlikely to be legitimate?
YOUR CHOICE(D) The people who do not notice the low quality of an email are those least likely to realize that a transaction is a scam in the middle of the process of completing it. This choice explains why sending low quality messages results in relatively high productivity: people who would use scammers’ time by starting to engage in transactions and then stopping when they realized that the transactions are fraudulent won’t even start to engage in the transactions, because the poor quality of the messages tips them off from the outset.
Thus, as a result of the low quality of the messages, the scammers end up dealing with the people least likely to realize that the transactions are scams and who are, thus, most likely to complete the transactions.
In other words, as a result of the low quality of the messages, scammers spend less time dealing with people unlikely to complete the scam transactions and more time dealing with people likely to complete the transactions and, thus, are more productive than they would be if the messages were of higher quality.
(E) Many of the people who write emails pitching scams have much experience in perpetrating scams and thus know which methods are most effective. This choice could be very tempting, since it almost seems to explain why sending low quality emails results in relatively high productivity. However, it explains the wrong thing.
Rather than explain how the low quality of the emails results in scammers’ being more productive, this choice seems to explain why scammers use low quality emails: the scammers have learned that using low quality emails results in relatively high productivity via their experiences with various methods.
Correct Answer: (D) So, an answer choice in a Paradox question will be correct only if it explains how the two facts can be true simultaneously.
💡MUST KNOW:
An answer choice in a Paradox question will be correct only if it explains how the two facts can be true simultaneously.
Let’s now apply that understanding to seeing why another type of choice does not get the job done.
summary:
Lesson Content As we have discussed, our job in answering a Resolve the Paradox question is to find a choice that explains how the facts that make up the apparent paradox can coexist.
Incorrect choices that explain the wrong thing, in other words, choices that explain something other than how the facts can coexist, are common in Paradox questions.
Such a choice can be rather tempting because, even though it doesn’t explain what the correct answer has to explain, it does explain something related to the information presented by the passage.
title: 5.8.1 Paradox Incorrect Choice Type 1: A Choice that Explains the Wrong Thing

contents:
Lesson Content We can never use a similarity between two things to explain a paradoxical difference between them.
💡MUST KNOW:
We can never use a similarity between two things to explain a paradoxical difference between them.
A stimulus may discuss things that are similar in some ways but are different in a way that seems paradoxical.
Now, one good strategy for approaching a question involving such a scenario is to look for a choice that explains how the two seemingly similar things are actually different.
On the other hand, any answer choice that shows how the two are similar will not resolve the paradox.
Let’s look at an example:
Many of the most profitable movies in recent years have been based on well-known television shows. Last year, Global Pictures released two such movies: Star Command and Space Explorers. Even though both were based on well-known television shows, Star Command was a commercial success, while Space Explorers was a commercial failure.
Which of the following best explains the surprising difference between the two outcomes?
So, what’s the paradox here?
Fact 1: Both Star Command and Space Explorers were based on well-known television shows.
Fact 2: Star Command was a success and Space Explorers was not.
The correct answer has to point out some relevant difference between the two movies that explains why one succeeded and the other failed.
A choice that focused on how the two movies were similar would be incorrect.
For example:
(Incorrect Choice that Shows How the Two Things are Similar) Critics reviewing the movies had expected both of them to be commercial successes.
The incorrect choice that shows how the two things are similar fits with the fact that the two movies were created in similar ways, but we aren’t looking for a choice that simply fits well with the fact that the movies were created in similar ways.
In other words, we can’t resolve a paradox that involves a difference between two things by saying, “Well, actually, they aren’t so different.”
💡MUST KNOW:
A paradox involving a difference between two things cannot be resolved by simply showing that the things are actually somehow similar.
Consider the following examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 10 Although cardiovascular exercises, such as running, tend to burn significantly more calories per hour than strength training exercises, such as weight lifting, a recent study has shown that people who engage in only strength training exercises tend to lose more body fat than people who engage in only cardiovascular exercises lose. Which of the following, if true, resolves the apparent paradox described above? 1. [ ] (A) Both cardiovascular exercises and strength training exercises have been shown to increase the endurance of those who engage in them. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Both cardiovascular exercises and strength training exercises have been shown to help people live longer. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Most people who engage in cardiovascular exercises do so for significantly longer time intervals than people who engage in strength training exercises do so for. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Both cardiovascular exercises and strength training exercises provide valuable benefits for heart health, blood sugar control, and joint stability. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) People who burn relatively high numbers of calories per hour while exercising are triggered to consume more post-exercise calories than people who burn fewer calories per hour while exercising consume. View Solution Question Level: easy What is the apparent paradox presented here?
Fact 1: Cardiovascular exercises tend to burn significantly more calories per hour than strength training exercises.
Fact 2: A recent study has shown that people who engage in only strength training exercises tend to lose more body fat than people who engage in only cardiovascular exercises lose.
Rephrased as a question, the apparent paradox looks something like this:
What has to be explained: Why would it be that, even though cardiovascular exercises tend to burn significantly more calories per hour than strength training exercises, a study has shown that people who engage in only strength training exercises tend to lose more body fat than people who engage in only cardiovascular exercises lose?
Notice that the correct answer has to explain a difference between the results of engaging in strength training exercises and the results of engaging in cardiovascular exercises: people who engage in the first lose more body fat than people who engage in the second.
So, a choice that explains how the two forms of exercise are similar will not be correct, because we can’t explain a difference by showing how two things are similar.
(A) Both cardiovascular exercises and strength training exercises have been shown to increase the endurance of those who engage in them. Notice that this choice explains how the two forms of exercise are similar: both increase the endurance of those who engage in them.
A choice that explains how the two are similar will not explain the difference in results.
(B) Both cardiovascular exercises and strength training exercises have been shown to help people live longer. Notice that this choice explains how the two forms of exercise are similar: both have been shown to help people live longer.
A choice that explains how the two are similar will not explain the difference in results.
(C) Most people who engage in cardiovascular exercises do so for significantly longer time intervals than people who engage in strength training exercises do so for. The effect of this choice is the opposite of the effect the correct answer must have. This choice makes the scenario even more paradoxical.
After all, the information that we already have makes it surprising that a study has shown that people who engage in only strength training exercises tend to lose more body fat than people who engage in only cardiovascular exercises lose. Now, the information provided by this choice makes those study results even more surprising.
After all, if most people who engage in cardiovascular exercises do so for significantly longer time intervals than people who engage in strength training exercises do so for, then we have even more reason to expect that the people who engage in cardiovascular exercises would be the ones to lose more body fat.
But they aren’t the ones. Why?
(D) Both cardiovascular exercises and strength training exercises provide valuable benefits for heart health, blood sugar control, and joint stability. Notice that this choice explains how the two forms of exercise are similar: both provide valuable benefits for heart health, blood sugar control, and joint stability.
A choice that explains how the two are similar will not explain the difference in results.
YOUR CHOICE(E) People who burn relatively high numbers of calories per hour while exercising are triggered to consume more post-exercise calories than people who burn fewer calories per hour while exercising consume. This choice perfectly explains what we have to explain.
After all, if what this choice says is true, then we can see why a study has shown that people who engage in only strength training exercises tend to lose more body fat than people who engage in only cardiovascular exercises lose: the people who engage in only cardiovascular exercises burn relatively high numbers of calories per hour and, therefore, are triggered to consume relatively high numbers of post-exercise calories. So, even though they burn more calories while exercising, they make up for burning those calories by consuming more calories after exercising.
Thus, by showing how the two forms of exercise are different, this choice explains the difference in results and resolves the apparent paradox.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 11 The primary ingredient in both dark chocolate and milk chocolate is cocoa. Which of the following, if true, best explains the difference in health benefits? 1. [ ] (A) Dark chocolate and milk chocolate both contain sugar, which, when consumed in sufficient quantities, can cause health problems. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Unlike dark chocolate, milk chocolate often contains large quantities of milk, cream, butter, and certain oils, each of which is well-documented to cause significant damage to the lining of arteries. 3. [ ] (C) Both dark chocolate and milk chocolate have been found to contain pesticide residues, which have been shown to damage the lining of arteries when present in large amounts. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) A large percentage of the calories found in dark chocolate come from saturated fat, heavy consumption of which has been linked to artery dysfunction. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Because both dark chocolate and milk chocolate contain a relatively large number of calories per ounce, both can cause weight gain in those who consume them. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy What is the apparent paradox presented here?
Fact 1: The primary ingredient in both dark chocolate and milk chocolate is cocoa, which contains compounds called flavonoids, which have been demonstrated to improve the health of the lining of arteries.
Fact 2: Research shows that, while the consumption of dark chocolate offers health benefits to arteries, the consumption of milk chocolate does not offer such benefits.
Rephrased as a question, the apparent paradox looks something like this:
What has to be explained: Why does research show that, while the consumption of dark chocolate offers health benefits to arteries, the consumption of milk chocolate does not offer such benefits, even though the primary ingredient in both dark chocolate and milk chocolate contains flavonoids, which have been demonstrated to improve the health of the lining of arteries?
Notice that the correct answer has to explain the difference between the health benefits of the consumption of dark chocolate and those of the consumption of milk chocolate. So, a choice that explains how the two forms of chocolate are similar will not be correct, because we can’t explain a difference by showing how two things are similar.
(A) Dark chocolate and milk chocolate both contain sugar, which, when consumed in sufficient quantities, can cause health problems. Notice that this choice explains how the two forms of chocolate are similar: both contain sugar, which can cause health problems.
A choice that explains how the two are similar will not explain the difference in health benefits.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Unlike dark chocolate, milk chocolate often contains large quantities of milk, cream, butter, and certain oils, each of which is well-documented to cause significant damage to the lining of arteries. This choice perfectly explains what we have to explain.
After all, if what this choice says is true, then we can see why research shows that, while the consumption of dark chocolate offers health benefits to arteries, the consumption of milk chocolate does not offer such benefits: milk chocolate is “unlike” dark chocolate, in that milk contains ingredients not contained in dark chocolate that are well-documented to cause significant damage to the lining of arteries.
Thus, by showing how the two forms of chocolate differ, this choice explains the difference in health benefits and resolves the apparent paradox.
(C) Both dark chocolate and milk chocolate have been found to contain pesticide residues, which have been shown to damage the lining of arteries when present in large amounts. Notice that this choice explains how the two forms of chocolate are similar: both have been found to contain pesticide residues.
A choice that explains how the two are similar will not explain the difference in health benefits.
(D) A large percentage of the calories found in dark chocolate come from saturated fat, heavy consumption of which has been linked to artery dysfunction. The key issue with this choice is that it provides information on dark chocolate only and does not tell us whether it is also the case that a large percentage of the calories found in milk chocolate come from saturated fat.
We can’t explain the difference between the health benefits of consumption of dark chocolate and the health benefits of consumption of milk chocolate by using information on dark chocolate without corresponding information on milk chocolate.
Since we don’t have information on whether this characteristic of dark chocolate makes it similar to or different from milk chocolate, what this choice says about dark chocolate does not help us to explain the difference in health benefits.
Of course, there’s also another issue: this choice seems to explain why dark chocolate, which contains saturated fat, could be detrimental, rather than beneficial to artery health.
So, this choice does not explain the difference in health benefits.
(E) Because both dark chocolate and milk chocolate contain a relatively large number of calories per ounce, both can cause weight gain in those who consume them. Notice that this choice explains how the two forms of chocolate are similar: both contain a relatively large number of calories per ounce.
A choice that explains how the two are similar will not explain the difference in health benefits.
Correct Answer: (B) Remember, choices that explain how things are similar will not resolve paradoxes involving differences between those things.
summary:
Lesson Content We can never use a similarity between two things to explain a paradoxical difference between them.
💡MUST KNOW:
We can never use a similarity between two things to explain a paradoxical difference between them.
A stimulus may discuss things that are similar in some ways but are different in a way that seems paradoxical.
Now, one good strategy for approaching a question involving such a scenario is to look for a choice that explains how the two seemingly similar things are actually different.
title: 5.8.2 Paradox Incorrect Choice Type 2: When the Paradox Involves a Difference Between Two Things but an Answer Choice Shows how the Two are Similar

contents:
Lesson Content Be on the lookout for choices that actually make the situation described even more paradoxical.
💡MUST KNOW:
Beware of choices that actually make the situation described even more paradoxical, that deepen the paradox. A situation can be made more paradoxical by information making the two facts appear to be more contradictory or by the addition of what seems to be more conflict to the scenario presented.
Remember, our job is to resolve the paradox, not to add to it.
Let’s look at an example:
Currently, the Beach City Gazette, a local newspaper, has more subscribers to its printed version than it has had at any other point in its 80-year history. However, the paper reported significant operating losses and this week had to lay off 30 percent of its staff.
Which of the following, if true, would explain what underlies this paradoxical situation?
So, what’s the paradox here?
Fact 1: More subscribers to its printed version than ever before.
Fact 2: Operating losses and layoffs.
The correct answer has to provide information that could be used to explain why, even though the paper has a record number of subscribers to its printed version, it is experiencing operating losses.
Meanwhile, a choice that adds to the paradoxical nature of the situation is incorrect.
In addition to having a record number of subscribers, the Gazette has been selling a record number of copies at newsstands.
The cost of the paper on which the Beach City Gazette is printed has decreased.
Advertising rates received by the Beach City Gazette are the highest they have ever been.
Notice the effects of these incorrect choices.
Could a choice that, like the three above, does the opposite of what the correct answer has to do ever really be tempting? Yes, and here’s why.
Consider this statement:
The online version of the Beach City Gazette has become even more popular than the printed version.
The above statement adds to the paradoxical nature of the scenario, by mentioning another way in which the Beach City Gazette is succeeding.
The moral of this story is that, to get Paradox questions right, we have to pay close attention to the effects of what answer choices say.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering a Paradox question, be careful not to choose an answer that actually augments, rather than resolves, the paradox.
Consider the following examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 12 Five years ago, after determining that few cyclists wore helmets while riding bikes in Rockport, the Town of Rockport passed a new law making the use of helmets mandatory for all cyclists riding in Rockport. Which of the following, if true, provides the best explanation for these paradoxical results? 1. [x] (A) Wearing a helmet while cycling increases the likelihood that a cyclist will ride more recklessly than he or she would if he or she were not wearing a helmet. 2. [ ] (B) As of five years ago, all automobiles registered in the Town of Rockport have been required to be equipped with advanced collision avoidance safety systems. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) The number of people who cycle in the Town of Rockport has decreased over the last five years. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Since the passage of the new helmet law, the Town of Rockport has increased its number of bicycle-only lanes, in which neither cars nor pedestrians are permitted. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Manchester, a town close to Rockport, saw a decrease in cycling-related injuries after passing a similar law, which required the use of helmets while cycling. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy Why would the passage of a law mandating the use of helmets, which one might reasonably expect to cause a decrease in the number of injuries to cyclists, be followed by an increase in the number of such injuries?
YOUR CHOICE(A) Wearing a helmet while cycling increases the likelihood that a cyclist will ride more recklessly than he or she would if he or she were not wearing a helmet. We need to explain why the number of injuries sustained by cyclists in Rockport each year increased after the passage of the helmet law, which would have been expected to lead to a decrease in injuries sustained by cyclists.
If it is true that wearing a helmet while cycling increases the likelihood that a cyclist will ride more recklessly than he or she would if he or she were not wearing a helmet, then we have a very good explanation for the paradox presented in the passage. If cyclists began to ride more recklessly while wearing helmets, then that reckless riding would certainly explain why the number of injuries sustained by cyclists increased despite the helmet law.
(B) As of five years ago, all automobiles registered in the Town of Rockport have been required to be equipped with advanced collision avoidance safety systems. What this answer choice says deepens the paradox, leaving us more confused.
If, as of five years ago, all automobiles registered in the Town of Rockport have been required to be equipped with advanced collision avoidance safety systems, then why did the number of injuries sustained by cyclists increase?
We would expect advanced collision avoidance safety systems to cause a decrease in the number of injuries sustained by cyclists, or at the very least, we certainly would not expect that the safety systems would lead to an increase in the number of injuries sustained by cyclists.
We must always be careful not to choose as correct a choice that deepens the paradox.
(C) The number of people who cycle in the Town of Rockport has decreased over the last five years. What this answer choice says deepens the paradox, leaving us more confused.
If it is true that the number of people who cycle in the Town of Rockport has decreased over the last five years, then we would expect to see a decrease in the number of injuries sustained by cyclists, because a decrease in the number of cyclists on the road should result in a decrease in injuries.
Instead, the number of injuries increased.
We must always be careful not to choose as correct a choice that deepens the paradox.
(D) Since the passage of the new helmet law, the Town of Rockport has increased its number of bicycle-only lanes, in which neither cars nor pedestrians are permitted. What this choice says deepens the paradox, leaving us more confused.
If it is true that, since the passage of the new helmet law, the Town of Rockport has increased its number of bicycle-only lanes, in which neither cars nor pedestrians are permitted, then we would expect to see a decrease in the number of cycling injuries, because it is common knowledge that bicycle-only lanes make riding safer, not more dangerous, for cyclists.
If you, for some reason, didn’t agree that it is common knowledge that bike lanes should make cycling safer, fine. In any case, an increase in bicycle-only lanes would not explain the increase in the number of cycling injuries.
(E) Manchester, a town close to Rockport, saw a decrease in cycling-related injuries after passing a similar law, which required the use of helmets while cycling. Although the Town of Manchester might be materially different from the Town of Rockport, what this answer choice says goes in the direction of deepening the paradox.
After all, if Manchester, a town close to Rockport, saw a decrease in cycling-related injuries after passing a similar law, which required the use of helmets while cycling, then we have some information that seems to indicate that helmet laws make cycling safer.
So then, why did the number of injuries sustained by cyclists in Rockport each year increase? The results of the helmet law in Manchester give us even further reason to believe that the number of cycling injuries in Rockport should have decreased rather than increased.
We must always be careful not to choose as correct a choice that deepens the paradox.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 13 In an effort to increase the amount of tax revenue it collects each year, three years ago, the City of Newland Falls doubled the rate of the property tax that its residents must pay on the ownership of real estate within city limits from 5 percent to 10 percent. Which of the following statements, if true, would best explain the paradoxical outcome described above? 1. [x] (A) Because of the high property tax rate, residents of Newland Falls have been abandoning their houses and moving to other towns, leaving the houses unoccupied and without owners. 2. [ ] (B) Newland Falls used much of the property tax revenue it collected to pay for marketing campaigns in nearby states designed to entice people to move to, and purchase new homes in, Newland Falls. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Over the past decade, the average annual income of residents of Newland Falls has consistently been nearly three times as high as that of residents of all adjacent cities. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Census surveys administered by Newland Falls show that the average age of all residents of Newland Falls has increased over the past three years. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Over the past three years, the amount of sales tax revenue collected by Newland Falls each year has increased by more than 20 percent. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy Three years ago, the City of Newland Falls doubled the rate of the property tax that its residents must pay on the ownership of real estate within city limits from 5 percent to 10 percent. Of course, we would expect such a change to result in an increase in the tax revenue collected by the city. However, paradoxically, since that time, the total tax revenue collected by the city has decreased by 15 percent each year.
Our job is to find an answer choice that helps us to explain why the tax revenue collected by the city has decreased in the face of an increase in the real estate tax.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Because of the high property tax rate, residents of Newland Falls have been abandoning their houses and moving to other towns, leaving the houses unoccupied and without owners. A key detail of the passage to notice is, “the City of Newland Falls doubled the rate of the property tax that its residents must pay on the ownership of real estate within city limits from 5 percent to 10 percent.” In other words, if people do not own real estate, they do not pay the real estate tax.
Thus, if it is true that, because of the high property tax rate, residents of Newland Falls have been “abandoning their houses and moving to other towns, leaving the houses unoccupied and without owners,” then this fact would certainly resolve the paradox. After all, if residents are abandoning their houses and moving to other towns, leaving the houses without owners, then, probably, there are fewer owners of real estate paying real estate tax in Newland Falls than there were before.
Notice that this answer choice gives us that “Aha!” feeling. We think, “Right. That is why the total tax revenue collected by the city has decreased by 15 percent each year even though the city increased the property tax rate.”
(B) Newland Falls used much of the property tax revenue it collected to pay for marketing campaigns in nearby states designed to entice people to move to, and purchase new homes in, Newland Falls. What this choice says deepens the paradox.
After all, if Newland Falls used much of the property tax revenue it collected to pay for marketing campaigns designed to entice people to move to, and purchase new homes in, Newland Falls, then we would expect to see more people purchasing homes in Newland Falls. As more people purchased homes in Newland Falls, they would be subject to the increased tax rate. Thus, we would expect to see the city collect more, not less, tax revenue. So, then, why did the tax revenue decline?
We are left more puzzled by what this choice says.
(C) Over the past decade, the average annual income of residents of Newland Falls has consistently been nearly three times as high as that of residents of all adjacent cities. This choice is not clearly connected to the paradoxical decrease in tax revenue in Newland Falls. While some government entities do collect taxes on income, we have no clear reason to believe that the level of income of Newland Falls residents is directly connected to the amount of tax revenue collected by the City of Newland Falls each year.
Furthermore, even if there exists some connection between the level of Newland Falls residents’ income and the amount of tax revenue collected by the city, this choice does not say that the level of income of Newland Falls residents changed. Rather, it indicates that the ratio of the average income of Newland Falls residents to the average income of residents of other towns has remained the same for a decade.
The fact that this ratio has not changed would not explain why the amount of tax revenue collected by the City of Newland Falls has changed.
(D) Census surveys administered by Newland Falls show that the average age of all residents of Newland Falls has increased over the past three years. We’re seeking a choice that helps us to explain why the tax revenue collected by the city has decreased in the face of an increase in the real estate tax.
The fact that the average age of all residents of Newland Falls has increased over the past three years does not help us to explain that paradoxical outcome. How can an increase in the average age of all residents explain that outcome? Without more information, we don’t know how average age affects the tax collected by the city.
If you choose this answer choice as correct, you likely made up an unsupported story about how average age affects the real estate tax collected by the city. However, no such link is provided by the passage or by this choice. As always, we must avoid concocting unsubstantiated stories.
(E) Over the past three years, the amount of sales tax revenue collected by Newland Falls each year has increased by more than 20 percent. What this choice says deepens the paradox.
It was already paradoxical that, since the City of Newland Falls doubled its property tax rate from 5 percent to 10 percent, the total tax revenue collected by the city has decreased by 15 percent each year.
Now, this choice says that the amount of sales tax revenue collected by Newland Falls each year has increased significantly over the past three years, a fact that makes it even more surprising that the total tax revenue collected by the city has decreased by 15 percent each year.
This choice leaves us more confused.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 14 A certain Midwestern American town had only two hardware stores. Which of the following, if true, best explains why the result was counter to the result that might have been reasonably expected? 1. [ ] (A) Over the past year, the town invested in the district in which the remaining hardware store is located, making the district more attractive to shoppers. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) The remaining hardware store is run by a national corporation with no close ties to the community in which the store is located. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) In a town near the town in which the hardware store is located, a store that had been selling some hardware type items in one department closed that department. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) After the other hardware store closed, the store manager at the remaining store did not make any significant changes to that store’s operations. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) The store that closed had been offering free classes via which people could learn how to undertake projects requiring tools and supplies available at hardware stores. View Solution Question Level: hard Generally, demand for goods sold in a store is inversely related to competition in that store’s market. In a situation in which the number of stores in a market decreases, demand for a remaining store’s goods would be expected to increase.
So, the scenario presented in this question seems paradoxical, in that, when one hardware store of only two in a town closed, demand for goods from the remaining store did not increase but, rather, decreased.
Rephrased as a question, the apparent paradox would look something like:
What has to be explained: Why would it be that, after one of only two hardware stores in a town closed, demand for goods sold in the remaining store decreased?
(A) Over the past year, the town invested in the district in which the remaining hardware store is located, making the district more attractive to shoppers. This choice adds to the paradoxical nature of the situation, because the district’s having become more attractive to shoppers would be expected to cause demand for the hardware store’s goods to increase rather than decrease. After all, a more attractive shopping district should attract more shoppers.
So, what this choice says makes the fact the demand decreased even more surprising.
(B) Incorrect. This choice could be tempting, because it could be perceived as providing a reason why demand for the remaining store’s goods would be relatively low: people in the community don’t feel that the store is connected to the community.
Remember, however, that what we need to explain is not why demand for the remaining store’s goods is relatively low, if it even is low, but rather why demand decreased after the other store closed, and this choice does not explain why this change in demand occurred.
After all, presumably the remaining store was run by a national corporation all along. Something that was the case all along would not have suddenly caused a decrease in demand after the other store closed.
(C) In a town near the town in which the hardware store is located, a store that had been selling some hardware type items in one department closed that department. This answer choice adds to the paradoxical nature of the situation.
The fact that a nearby store that sold hardware type items has ceased selling such items means that competition in the hardware market has decreased. Since this reduction in competition would be expected to cause demand for the remaining hardware store’s goods to increase, what this choice says make the decrease in demand for the remaining hardware store’s goods even more surprising.
(D) After the other hardware store closed, the store manager at the remaining store did not make any significant changes to that store’s operations. This choice could be tempting, as the fact that the manager made no changes could be perceived as indicating that the manager at the remaining store has been somehow remiss in his management of the store and has thus driven down demand for the store’s goods.
However, the truth is that, by confirming that nothing has changed at the remaining store, this choice adds to the paradoxical nature of the scenario, because it eliminates what could have been one possible reason for the decrease in demand for goods sold at the remaining store, some kind of change in the way in which the store has been operating.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The store that closed had been offering free classes via which people could learn how to undertake projects requiring tools and supplies available at hardware stores. While, at first glance, this choice may seem unrelated to demand at the remaining hardware store, if we consider the following, we can see how this choice explains why demand at the remaining store decreased.
By offering classes via which people could learn to undertake projects requiring items available at hardware stores, the store that closed was generating demand for goods sold at all hardware stores in the town in which it was located. Then, when it closed, and, thus, ceased to generate demand for goods sold at hardware stores, demand fell for the goods sold by the hardware store that remained.
Correct Answer: (E) Remember, our job is to explain the paradox, not enhance it.
summary:
Lesson Content Be on the lookout for choices that actually make the situation described even more paradoxical.
💡MUST KNOW:
Beware of choices that actually make the situation described even more paradoxical, that deepen the paradox. A situation can be made more paradoxical by information making the two facts appear to be more contradictory or by the addition of what seems to be more conflict to the scenario presented.
Remember, our job is to resolve the paradox, not to add to it.
title: 5.8.3 Paradox Incorrect Choice Type 3: A Choice that Makes the Situation More Paradoxical

contents:
Lesson Content In answering Resolve the Paradox questions, our job is not to call into question the credibility of any statement, nor is it to reinforce the credibility of any statement.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Resolve the Paradox questions, we accept the statements in the passages as true, even though they apparently conflict.
Accordingly, we never resolve the paradox in a Paradox question by challenging one of the facts stated in the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
We never resolve the paradox in a Paradox question by challenging one of the facts stated in the passage. Our job is to find a choice that explains how the two seemingly contradictory facts can be true at the same time.
Consider this example:
The moon is only 4.5 billion years old. Nevertheless, Apollo astronauts found on the moon rocks that are over 5 billion years old. The above situation is paradoxical since we wouldn’t expect rocks on the moon to be older than the moon itself.
We cannot resolve the above paradox by saying/indicating any of the following:
Apollo astronauts did not find any rocks on the moon. The rocks that were found on the moon are not 5 billion years old. The moon is older than 4.5 billion years. The reason each of the above statements does not resolve the paradox is that we accept the statements in the passage to be true, and thus we don’t challenge them.
Of course, since a Paradox question is based on the idea that the statements in the passage are true, we won’t actually see choices that directly contradict the statements in the passage.
(Incorrect Choice that Could be Perceived as Contradicting) Before advanced methods for calculating the moon’s age became available, some scientists thought it to be much older than other scientists did.
(Incorrect Choice that Could be Perceived as Contradicting) Some rocks brought back from the moon by astronauts were lost before their ages could be accurately determined.
(Incorrect Choice that Could be Perceived as Contradicting) Evidence indicates that it is possible for a moon much older than 4.5 billion years to exist.
Notice, none of those choices truly contradicts the original statements about the ages of the moon and the rocks.
So, what could resolve this paradox?
A choice that says:
(Correct Answer) The rocks found by the astronauts had been flying through space until they crashed into the moon after it was formed.
Such a choice would resolve the paradox, as it explains how both of the facts could be true: some rocks could have formed before the formation of the moon, and later crashed into moon.
💡MUST KNOW:
An answer that seems to contradict one of the two facts presented in a Paradox question will be incorrect.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 15 It is common knowledge that a diet that includes many high calorie foods can be a cause of weight gain. Which of the following, if true, resolves the apparent paradox described above? 1. [ ] (A) A recent study found that some people who strictly adhered to a ketogenic diet gained rather than lost weight. > Correct Answer: (E) 2.
