[ ] (B) Nutritionists who recommend ketogenic diets base their recommendations partly on information that is not common knowledge. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) A ketogenic diet is not made up exclusively of foods high in fats and oils but, rather, also includes vegetables. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Most experts agree that following an extreme diet such as a ketogenic diet is not as effective for maintaining an ideal weight as simply consistently eating in healthy ways. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Generally, when a person’s diet is composed mostly of fatty foods, the person develops a heightened ability to burn fat. View Solution Question Level: easy The stimulus presents the paradox that consumption of foods that would normally be expected to cause weight gain results in weight loss. Rephrased as a question, this paradox would look something like:
What has to be explained: How could it be that, even though consuming foods high in calories is known to cause weight gain, people following diets made up mostly of foods that are high in calories would, generally, be successful in losing weight?
(A) A recent study found that some people who strictly adhered to a ketogenic diet gained rather than lost weight. This choice might seem to contradict the fact that people who adhere to ketogenic diets tend to lose weight, as it indicates that some people who followed a ketogenic diet did not lose weight.
However, even though some people gain weight when following ketogenic diets, we know from the passage that generally people who follow such diets are successful in losing weight. Thus, the paradox stands as is.
(B) Nutritionists who recommend ketogenic diets base their recommendations partly on information that is not common knowledge. The fact that nutritionists who recommend ketogenic diets base their recommendations partly on information that is not common knowledge does not contradict the common knowledge that diets that include many high calorie foods tend to cause weight gain and does not explain why ketogenic diets work. To resolve the paradox, we need some information like the information that the nutritionists have!
(C) A ketogenic diet is not made up exclusively of foods high in fats and oils but, rather, also includes vegetables. This choice could be perceived as somehow contradicting the fact that a ketogenic diet is made up mostly of fats and oils.
Notice, however, that what this choice actually says is that a ketogenic diet involves eating vegetables in addition to fats and oils. Of course, that fact does not contradict the fact that a ketogenic diet is made up mostly of fats and oils.
If a person were to replace fatty foods with vegetables, then, given common knowledge about calories and weight, we might expect the person to lose weight. However, if a person eats both vegetables and fat and oils, we don’t expect the person to lose weight.
Thus, the paradox stands as is.
(D) Most experts agree that following an extreme diet such as a ketogenic diet is not as effective for maintaining an ideal weight as simply consistently eating in healthy ways. This choice could be perceived as somehow contradicting the fact that people adhering to ketogenic diets lost weight.
However, the truth is that, even if following a ketogenic diet is not the most effective way to maintain an ideal weight, people still lost weight while following ketogenic diets. So, this choice has no effect on the paradox.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Generally, when a person’s diet is composed mostly of fatty foods, the person develops a heightened ability to burn fat. This choice explains why, even though high calorie foods are known to cause weight gain, people following diets made up mostly of fats and oils, which are high calorie foods, generally lose weight. By following such diets, the people develop heightened abilities to burn fat and, thus, burn fat and lose weight.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 16 To solve a rental housing affordability problem, the city of Coastal West passed a rent control law, making it illegal for landlords in Coastal West to increase the rent they charge on any rental housing occupied by a tenant. Which of the following, if true, provides the best explanation for these paradoxical results? 1. [ ] (A) Rental housing is more affordable in Coastal West than it is in many other cities that have the same population as Coastal West. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Some property owners responded to Coastal West’s rent control law by converting rental housing to commercial space, thus significantly reducing the supply of rental housing in Coastal West. 3. [ ] (C) For many decades, the city of Coastal West did not have in place any law that prevented landlords from increasing the rent that they charged on rental housing occupied by tenants. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) From time to time, the city of Coastal West passes laws that have effects opposite to those that the city hopes will result. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Soon after passing the rent control law, the city of Coastal West passed a historic stimulus package that provides all renters with a $2,000 check on the first day of each new year. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy The passage presents a paradoxical situation:
Fact 1: The city of Coastal West passed a rent control law, making it illegal for landlords in Coastal West to increase the rent they charge on any rental housing occupied by a tenant.
Fact 2: After the passage of the law, affordability of rental housing in Coastal West quickly declined to its lowest point in the city’s history.
In answering this question, we have to keep in mind that the correct answer will not be correct because it somehow contradicts one of the above two facts. We have to take the facts as true and find a choice that explains how it can be that both are true.
(A) Rental housing is more affordable in Coastal West than it is in many other cities that have the same population as Coastal West. This choice could seem to contradict the fact that affordability of rental housing in Coastal West quickly declined to its lowest point in the city’s history. After all, if what this choice says is true, then maybe the affordability of rental housing in Coastal West isn’t too terrible.
Notice, however, that the fact that rental housing is more affordable in Coastal West than it is in other cities does not actually contradict the fact that affordability of rental housing in Coastal West declined. After all, even if rental housing is more affordable in Coastal West than it is in other cities, affordability of rental housing in Coastal West could have declined.
So, this choice does not really affect the paradox at all.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Some property owners responded to Coastal West’s rent control law by converting rental housing to commercial space, thus significantly reducing the supply of rental housing in Coastal West. This choice perfectly explains what we have to explain.
After all, if what this choice says is true, then we can see why, after the passage of the law, affordability of rental housing in Coastal West quickly declined: property owners responded to the rent control law by converting rental housing to commercial space, thus significantly reducing the supply of rental housing. Of course, a significant reduction in supply would likely cause an increase in rent levels, even if landlords could not increase the rent they charge on any rental housing occupied by a tenant. After all, landlords could still increase the rent they charge new tenants.
Thus, by showing why the affordability of rental housing declined, this choice resolves the apparent paradox.
(C) For many decades, the city of Coastal West did not have in place any law that prevented landlords from increasing the rent that they charged on rental housing occupied by tenants. This choice could seem to somehow negate the fact that the city of Coastal West passed a rent control law. We might be tempted to think something along the lines of, “Coastal West passed a rent control law, but it didn’t have one before. So, maybe affordability declined because Coastal West didn’t have a rent control law before.”
However, the truth is that, regardless of whether Coastal West did not have any rent control law for decades, it did pass such a law. So, regardless of what this choice says, the fact that affordability declined is still paradoxical.
So, this choice does not really affect the paradox at all.
(D) From time to time, the city of Coastal West passes laws that have effects opposite to those that the city hopes will result. The relationship between this choice and the statements in the passage is not that this choice explains how the statements in the passage could be true. Rather, this choice states a conclusion that follows from the statements in the passage.
After all, what this choice says follows from the fact that, after the city of Coastal West passed a rent control law to fix a rental housing affordability problem, affordability of rental housing in Coastal West quickly declined to its lowest point in the city’s history.
Since this choice follows from rather than explains the facts, it does not have the effect we seek.
(E) Soon after passing the rent control law, the city of Coastal West passed a historic stimulus package that provides all renters with a $2,000 check on the first day of each new year. If anything, what this choice says deepens the paradox, leaving us more confused.
After all, if what this choice says is true, then soon after the rent control law was passed, renters in Coastal West should have been better able to afford rental housing, because the city was giving them $2000 more to spend on rent each year than they had previously. So, given what this choice says, we would have expected affordability to improve, not decline.
Correct Answer: (B) Now let’s discuss telling the difference between the correct answer to a Paradox question and a choice that presents information that has no effect on the scenario described.
summary:
Lesson Content In answering Resolve the Paradox questions, our job is not to call into question the credibility of any statement, nor is it to reinforce the credibility of any statement.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Resolve the Paradox questions, we accept the statements in the passages as true, even though they apparently conflict.
Accordingly, we never resolve the paradox in a Paradox question by challenging one of the facts stated in the passage.
title: 5.8.4 Paradox Incorrect Choice Type 4: A Choice that Seems to Contradict One of the Two Facts

contents:
Lesson Content Among the answer choices in a Paradox question can be choices that have no effect on the paradox presented by the stimulus.
💡MUST KNOW:
Information that has no effect on the paradox will never resolve the paradox.
To tell the difference between incorrect choices that have no effect on the paradox and the correct answer, we have to carefully consider what effect each choice has on the scenario presented in the stimulus and be careful not to make up convoluted stories that give answer choices effects that they don’t really have.
Let’s look at an example:
On a short stretch of highway in Dolya along which there were many billboards, many car crashes were occurring during the afternoon rush hour. Since drivers can be distracted by billboards, Dolya’s government decided to ban the use of billboards along that stretch. Amazingly, once the billboards were taken down, the number of crashes per day increased.
Which of the following, if true, best explains why the outcome was the opposite of what might have been reasonably expected?
So what’s the paradox here?
Fact 1: Potentially distracting billboards were removed from a stretch of highway on which many car crashes had been occurring during the afternoon rush hour.
Fact 2: The number of crashes increased.
What has to be explained: Why would it be that, once potentially distracting billboards were taken down, the number of crashes on the stretch of highway along which the billboards had been standing increased?
Let’s consider some answer choices, starting with the following:
(Incorrect Choice that Has No Effect) Without the billboards, the land bordering that stretch of highway looks more natural.
We could somehow spin this choice into a story explaining why the number of crashes increased, perhaps a story about how people in natural surroundings are more likely to fall asleep while driving home in the afternoon.
Let’s consider another:
(Incorrect Choice that Has No Effect ) The billboards often showed advertisements for retail stores in communities further along the highway.
We could spin this choice into a story about how people became so excited to go to the stores that they became more alert in their driving, but that story is just way too farfetched.
OK, now for a more interesting choice:
(Correct Answer Choice) The billboards had been positioned in a way such that they had blocked light from the afternoon sun from going directly into the eyes of drivers.
Notice that the correct answer choice resolves the paradox.
If once the billboards were taken down, there was more afternoon sunlight going into the eyes of drivers, and so, the drivers had difficulty seeing, that fact could for sure explain why the number of crashes increased after the billboards were taken down.
So, we found a choice that has the effect we need without our coming up with a farfetched story.
When answering a paradox question, we haven’t found the correct answer until we have found one that is connected to the paradox in a way like the way in which this last choice is.
💡MUST KNOW:
To avoid choosing a Paradox question answer choice that introduces information that has no effect on the paradox, carefully consider whether the way in which you are connecting a choice to the paradox really makes sense.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 17 The First-Sale Doctrine holds that someone who has legally purchased copyrighted material, such as music or a novel, is legally entitled to dispose of that material in whatever way he or she sees fit. Which of the following, if true, best explains this seemingly contradictory action? 1. [x] (A) When digital content is sold on a secondary market, there is no way to verify that the seller has “disposed of,” and thus no longer possesses, the material. 2. [ ] (B) Not all potential secondary-market consumers own the equipment necessary to play MP3s or to read e-books. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Book publishers and record companies have attempted to shut down secondary markets for books and CDs as well. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Digital content did not exist when the First-Sale Doctrine was created. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Many modern authors and performance artists typically make their content available in both digital and physical forms. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy Rephrased as a question, the paradox presented in the passage would look something like:
What has to be explained: Why is it that, even though the First-Sale Doctrine allows for the re-sale of copyrighted materials, it is illegal to sell some copyrighted materials (digital copies)?
We are looking for an answer that explains the difference in how the two types of materials are treated legally, and that explanation has to work in a way that doesn’t require a convoluted story.
YOUR CHOICE(A) When digital content is sold on a secondary market, there is no way to verify that the seller has “disposed of,” and thus no longer possesses, the material. This answer choice describes a fundamental difference between the two types of copyrighted materials in question and explains why the term “disposed” does not apply to digital materials in the way in which the term applies to physical materials. Given that a concept, disposal, that plays a key part in the First-Sale Doctrine doesn’t apply to copyrighted digital content, it reasonably follows that the Doctrine could not be used to create a legal secondary market for copyrighted digital content. Thus, this answer choice resolves the paradox.
(B) Not all potential secondary-market consumers own the equipment necessary to play MP3s or to read e-books. This choice presents information that does not explain the difference between how sales of digital content are treated and how sales of other types of materials are treated legally.
While we could potentially make up a convoluted story about how the government wants to protect people from buying things that they can’t use, the truth is that that story does not make sense given what we know about the real world. After all, in the real world, not everyone has equipment necessary for playing CDs or for using other types of non-digital copyrighted materials. So, the characteristic of digital content described by this choice is also a characteristic of other types of copyrighted materials, meaning that what this choice describes is not a difference between the two types of materials, but rather, a characteristic that they share. Of course, the fact that the two types of materials are the same in this way would not explain why they are treated differently.
We can’t explain why two things are treated different by mentioning something that is a characteristic of both of them.
(C) Book publishers and record companies have attempted to shut down secondary markets for books and CDs as well. This choice shows how two different things (physical content and digital content) are similar. Remember, the paradox is that one type of secondary market is illegal and the other is legal. We need to figure out what makes these two types of content different from one another. Bringing up a way in which they are similar does not help to explain why they are treated differently.
(D) Digital content did not exist when the First-Sale Doctrine was created. This fact could seem to affect the paradox if we come up with a convoluted story.
We could come up with a story about how, since the First-Sale Doctrine was created before the advent of digital content, it does not apply to digital content.
However, is that really the way things work? Is it really the case that, by not applying to something, a law makes that thing illegal? Not usually, and not necessarily.
So, the truth is that only by making up something that we don’t really have good reason to believe can we use this choice to resolve the paradox.
(E) Many modern authors and performance artists typically make their content available in both digital and physical forms. We could come up with a convoluted story like the following:
“Authors and performance artists who make their content available in both digital and physical forms shouldn’t have to deal with both types being of media being resold. So, reselling digital material is not legal.”
However, the truth is that that story is not supported by what is said in the stimulus or what goes on in the real world. So, this information presented by this choice does not affect the paradox.
Correct Answer: (A) Now, let’s wrap up our discussion of incorrect choices in Paradox question by discussing choices that are not the correct answers to Paradox questions even though they discuss the facts that make up the apparent paradoxes.
summary:
Lesson Content Among the answer choices in a Paradox question can be choices that have no effect on the paradox presented by the stimulus.
💡MUST KNOW:
Information that has no effect on the paradox will never resolve the paradox.
To tell the difference between incorrect choices that have no effect on the paradox and the correct answer, we have to carefully consider what effect each choice has on the scenario presented in the stimulus and be careful not to make up convoluted stories that give answer choices effects that they don’t really have.
title: 5.8.5 Paradox Incorrect Choice Type 5: A Choice That Introduces Information That Has No Effect On The Paradox

contents:
Lesson Content Perhaps the trickiest type of incorrect choice that can appear in a Resolve the Paradox question is one that discusses the facts or aspects of the facts that make up the apparent paradox but does not resolve the paradox.
Consider the following example:
In order to spur scientific research, the government of Golado instituted a new program that involved giving significant financial awards and recognition to researchers who made breakthrough discoveries. However, in the years that followed the institution of the program, the pace of scientific discovery in Golado significantly decreased.
Which of the following, if true, would do the most to explain the surprising outcome?
(Incorrect Choice that Discusses Without Explaining) The government did not make an effort to inform the scientific community that the purpose of the program was to increase the pace of scientific discovery.
(Correct Answer) Researchers given strong incentives to compete with each other often respond by reducing the degree to which they share information that could lead to breakthrough discoveries.
So what’s the paradox here?
Fact 1: The government of Golado instituted a new program that involved giving significant financial awards and recognition to researchers who made breakthrough discoveries.
Fact 2: After the program was instituted the pace of scientific discovery decreased.
What has to be explained: Why would it be the case that, after the government started giving more awards and recognition to researchers who made breakthrough discoveries, the pace of scientific discovery decreased?
To make sure that we get the correct answer to a question like this one, we have to keep in mind that we need a choice that explains a specific thing: why the pace of scientific discovery decreased.
The incorrect choice that discusses without explaining does discuss both the program and the pace of scientific discovery.
Now, let’s contrast the incorrect choice that discusses without explaining with the correct answer.
So, as we are answering Paradox questions, we must be sure to:
Identify exactly what the correct answer has to explain. Read the answer choices carefully. Make sure that the answer choice that we choose doesn’t just mention the facts discussed in the passage but, rather, clearly serves to explain what has to be explained. 💡MUST KNOW:
In answering a Resolve the Paradox question, beware of answer choices that discuss the facts mentioned in the stimulus without actually resolving the paradox.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 18 In experiments, when trees, which use carbon dioxide when they grow, are exposed to air in which carbon dioxide is at a higher than normal level, they grow faster than they normally do. Which of the following, if true, provides the best explanation for why the most common result is different from what might have been expected? 1. [ ] (A) Increased atmospheric carbon dioxide is correlated with increased rates of tree growth in tropical forests more than it is with increased rates of tree growth in forests located in temperate areas. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Rising temperatures caused by increased levels of atmospheric carbon dioxide have resulted in a reduction in the amounts of water available to trees in temperate areas. 3. [ ] (C) Plants eventually adjust to an increased level of atmospheric carbon dioxide, their growth rates returning to what they were originally. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) In areas in which levels of nitrogen in the soil are low, trees have difficulty growing. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) The growth rates of trees of some species accelerate more than do those of trees of other species when the trees are exposed to air with higher than normal levels of carbon dioxide. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy What is the paradox presented here?
Fact 1: In experiments, when trees are exposed to air in which carbon dioxide is at a higher than normal level, they grow faster than they normally do.
Fact 2: In forests located in temperate areas, rates of tree growth have slowed from what had been normal as the level of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere has increased.
Rephrased as a question, the paradox presented in the passage looks something like this:
What has to be explained: How could it be that, even though, in experiments, trees exposed to air containing extra carbon dioxide grow faster than they normally do, the growth of trees in temperate forests has slowed to below normal as the level of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere has increased?
Only a choice that answers the above question will work. A choice that discusses increased atmospheric carbon dioxide and decreasing tree growth rates without explaining the how the two facts can coexist will not work.
(A) Increased atmospheric carbon dioxide is correlated with increased rates of tree growth in tropical forests more than it is with increased rates of tree growth in forests located in temperate areas. This choice could be tempting, because it discusses both increased atmospheric carbon dioxide and rates of tree growth and because it indicates that the growth rates of trees in tropical areas are more positively correlated with levels of atmospheric carbon dioxide than are the growth rates of trees in temperate areas.
Notice, however, this choice discusses only increases in growth rates. So, nothing in this choice explains why the rates of growth of trees in temperate areas have slowed as the level of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere has increased.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Rising temperatures caused by increased levels of atmospheric carbon dioxide have resulted in a reduction in the amounts of water available to trees in temperate areas. This choice explains why the rates of growth of trees in temperate areas have slowed as the level of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere has increased: as the level of carbon dioxide has increased, the amounts of water available to the trees have decreased.
(C) Plants eventually adjust to an increased level of atmospheric carbon dioxide, their growth rates returning to what they were originally. This choice is a tricky trap, as it could seem to explain why the tree growth rates have decreased. Perhaps they had already increased and have more recently been decreasing in the process of returning to normal.
However, notice what we have to explain. The passage says that “rates of tree growth have slowed from what had been normal.” So, whereas the phenomenon described in this choice involves growth rates returning to normal, the passage indicates, not that rates are returning to normal, but rather that rates are slowing to below normal. Thus, this choice cannot be correct.
(D) In areas in which levels of nitrogen in the soil are low, trees have difficulty growing. This choice could seem to explain why growth rates of trees in temperate areas are decreasing. Perhaps, the soil in those areas is low in nitrogen.
However, since we are not provided with a reason to believe that the levels of nitrogen in the soil in temperate areas have changed as the level of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere has increased, this choice does not explain why the growth rates of trees in temperate areas have decreased, i.e., changed.
(E) The growth rates of trees of some species accelerate more than do those of trees of other species when the trees are exposed to air with higher than normal levels of carbon dioxide. This choice could be tempting because it discusses both tree growth rates and carbon dioxide levels.
Notice, however, that we can easily eliminate this choice, as it mentions only differences in acceleration of growth rates and says nothing that would explain decreasing growth rates.
Correct Answer: (B)
summary:
Lesson Content Perhaps the trickiest type of incorrect choice that can appear in a Resolve the Paradox question is one that discusses the facts or aspects of the facts that make up the apparent paradox but does not resolve the paradox.
Consider the following example:
In order to spur scientific research, the government of Golado instituted a new program that involved giving significant financial awards and recognition to researchers who made breakthrough discoveries.
title: 5.8.6 Paradox Incorrect Choice Type 6: Discusses the Facts that Make up the Apparent Paradox Without Explaining How they can Coexist

contents:
Lesson Content A cause-and-effect relationship is a relationship in which one event, X, causes another event, Y, to occur.
💡MUST KNOW:
A cause-and-effect relationship is one in which one event causes another event to occur.
Many arguments found in Critical Reasoning questions involve cause-and-effect relationships, or relationships that appear to be cause-and-effect relationships.
In fact, you’ve already encountered arguments of this type several times in the preceding chapters.
Let’s begin this chapter by reviewing, and elaborating on, the assumptions that are inherent to any cause-and-effect argument.
summary:
Lesson Content A cause-and-effect relationship is a relationship in which one event, X, causes another event, Y, to occur.
💡MUST KNOW:
A cause-and-effect relationship is one in which one event causes another event to occur.
Many arguments found in Critical Reasoning questions involve cause-and-effect relationships, or relationships that appear to be cause-and-effect relationships.
In fact, you’ve already encountered arguments of this type several times in the preceding chapters.
Let’s begin this chapter by reviewing, and elaborating on, the assumptions that are inherent to any cause-and-effect argument.
title: 6.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content As we learned in prior chapters, when an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she assumes that her cause-and-effect claim is actually correct; she assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observed events.
In other words, when an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she posits that the cause she has put forth is the ONLY possible cause.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she assumes that her cause-and-effect claim is actually correct; she assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observed events.
Consider the following example:
Psychologists who studied the smartphone usage of 5,000 children determined that, as the daily number of hours a child spent using a smartphone increased, so too did the likelihood of the child experiencing attention and learning issues. On this basis, the psychologists concluded that smartphone use causes attention and learning issues in children. ‘
Which of the following is an assumption made by the psychologists in arriving at the above conclusion?
(Correct Answer) Children experiencing attention and learning issues are not likely to spend much of the day using smartphones as a means of coping with these issues. In this example, the psychologists are concluding that smartphone usage causes attention and learning issues in children.
Notice that the correct answer highlights a piece of information that must be true in order for the logic of the argument to work.
Often the key assumption stated by the correct answer to an Assumption question involving a cause-and-effect argument will be, essentially, that there is not something other than the cause mentioned in the argument that could be the cause of the effect mentioned in the argument.
Consider the following example:
Ever since new owners took over the beachfront building and changed it from a restaurant with outdoor dining to a dance club with outdoor dancing to loud music, the birds that used to flock around the building have disappeared. It must be that the loud music disturbs the birds.
Which of the following is an assumption made in arriving at the above conclusion?
(Correct Answer) Birds are not less attracted to the building than they were previously because there is much less food at the club than there was at the restaurant.
In this example, the conclusion that loud music caused the disappearance of the birds is based on the premise that the birds stopped flocking around the building once the building became the location of a club at which loud music is played.
The correct answer expresses such an assumption, the assumption that the birds have not disappeared because of a reduction in the amount of food at the building.
What’s being assumed in the following example?
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 1 Administrators in the Red Valley School District have decided that a new math curriculum being used for teaching math to elementary school students in the district is not as good as the curriculum that was used previously. Which of the following must the administrators be assuming? 1. [ ] (A) Math test scores of high school students in the district have not increased following a switch to a curriculum similar to the new curriculum given to the elementary school students. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) The training that the teachers received to prepare them to apply the new math curriculum was not inadequate for preparing them to apply the curriculum effectively. 3. [ ] (C) The math tests currently given to the students are substantially different from tests that they took when they were using the older math curriculum. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Red Valley School District elementary school students’ English test scores did not decrease when the school switched to using a new curriculum for teaching English. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) The reviews of the new math curriculum that were read by Red Valley School District faculty members before they chose the new curriculum were not accurate. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium The administrators have concluded the following:
Conclusion: The new math curriculum being used for teaching math to elementary school students is not as good as the curriculum that was used previously.
Their conclusion is based on the fact that the district’s elementary school students’ math test scores have decreased following the implementation of the new curriculum.
In arriving at their conclusion, the administrators are assuming that the use of the new curriculum has caused the decrease in test scores and that there is not some other factor that has caused the decrease in the test scores.
(A) Math test scores of high school students in the district have not increased following a switch to a curriculum similar to the new curriculum given to the elementary school students. This choice could be tempting since, if math tests scores of high school students using a similar curriculum increased, then it would make sense to wonder whether the change to the new curriculum was truly the cause of the decrease in elementary school test scores.
At the same time, in arriving at their conclusion, the administrators don’t have to be assuming that test scores of high school students did not increase following a switch to using a similar curriculum. The switch could have been a downgrade for the elementary school students even if a switch to a similar curriculum was an upgrade for the high school students.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The training that the teachers received to prepare them to apply the new math curriculum was not inadequate for preparing them to apply the curriculum effectively. In deciding that the new curriculum is not as good as the one that was used previously, the administrators must be assuming that there was not some other reason for the decrease in test scores.
For instance, they must be assuming that the training that the elementary school teachers received to prepare them to apply the new curriculum was not inadequate for preparing them to use it effectively. After all, if the training was inadequate, then inadequacy of the training, rather than the quality of the curriculum, could explain the decrease in test scores. In other words, if the training was inadequate then the argument would fall apart.
So, the administrators must be assuming what this choice says.
(C) The math tests currently given to the students are substantially different from tests that they took when they were using the older math curriculum. This choice is tricky, because it is the opposite of what we are looking for. So, if we are not careful, we can be tempted into picking a choice like this one, particularly because, as we are going through the choices, we might already be thinking that something changed about the tests.
Notice that, the administrators must be assuming that the tests given currently are not different from but rather similar to, at least in terms of difficulty, the tests that students took when they were using the old curriculum.
The administrators certainly aren’t assuming that the tests are substantially different. After all, if the tests were different, that difference, rather than the change in curriculum, could be responsible for the decrease in test scores.
(D) Red Valley School District elementary school students’ English test scores did not decrease when the school switched to using a new curriculum for teaching English. The administrators are not comparing the new math curriculum with the new English curriculum but, rather, the new math curriculum with the previous math curriculum. So, they aren’t assuming that the switch to a new English curriculum produced results better than those resulting from the switch to the new math curriculum.
(E) The reviews of the new math curriculum that were read by Red Valley School District faculty members before they chose the new curriculum were not accurate. This choice could be tempting because, in our minds, we could add to this choice things that it does not say, such as the following:
The reviews of the new math curriculum said that it is better than any other.
The reviews of the new math curriculum said that it is better than the previous math curriculum.
At the same time, the truth is that this choice does not say what the reviews actually said. So, there is no reason for us to believe that the reviews said anything that might be considered not accurate by the administrators.
Furthermore, even if the reviews did include comparisons of the new curriculum with the previous curriculum, the administrators don’t have to assume anything about reviews in order to go from the test scores to the conclusion about the curriculum. The administrators don’t even have to be aware that such reviews exist.
Correct Answer: (B) You may recall the following example from the Assumption question chapter.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 2 In a recent medical study, all of the study participants who had abused alcohol in the past five years were found to be suffering with depression. Each of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends EXCEPT: 1. [x] (A) Both alcohol abuse issues and depression could be solved via the use of therapy specially designed for the treatment of alcohol abuse issues. 2. [ ] (B) The depression and alcohol abuse of the study participants were not both results of heavy use of illicit drugs. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) The study participants were not found to be suffering with depression before they started abusing alcohol. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) The study was not set up in a way such that people who were not suffering with depression would be very unlikely to participate. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) The concurrence of alcohol abuse and depression seen in the people studied was not merely a coincidence. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium The argument has a cause-and-effect conclusion.
Conclusion: It must be the case that abusing alcohol causes depression.
The conclusion is based on the fact that all of the people studied who had abused alcohol were found to be depressed.
Going from that evidence to that cause-and-effect conclusion requires assuming that the observed pattern was not the result of something other than alcohol abuse causing depression. In other words, the author must be assuming that the above conclusion is the only possible explanation for the fact that all of the people studied who had abused alcohol were found to be depressed.
Now, in this case, because the question stem says, “Each of the following is an assumption … EXCEPT,” we have to look for the one answer that is NOT an assumption upon which the argument depends. The four choices that ARE assumptions upon which the argument depends are incorrect.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Both alcohol abuse issues and depression could be solved via the use of therapy specially designed for the treatment of alcohol abuse issues. This choice could seem to be an assumption that the author must be making, because it could seem necessary to assume that therapy designed for treatment for alcohol abuse issues could solve both alcohol abuse issues and depression in order to conclude that abusing alcohol causes depression.
However, the truth is that this choice is not an assumption necessary for going from the evidence to the conclusion. Rather, this choice is a conclusion that follows from the author’s conclusion. After all, if abusing alcohol causes depression, then it makes sense to conclude that therapy designed to treat alcohol abuse issues could solve depression as well.
At the same time, concluding that alcohol abuse causes depression does not require assuming anything about therapy designed for treating alcohol abuse issues. After all, one could arrive at the argument’s conclusion even if such therapy did not even exist.
Assumption upon which the argument depends? No.
(B) The depression and alcohol abuse of the study participants were not both results of heavy use of illicit drugs. Going from the evidence that the participants in the study both abused alcohol and were found to be depressed to the conclusion that alcohol abuse causes depression requires assuming that a third factor, use of illicit drugs, is not the actual cause of both the alcohol abuse and the depression.
Assumption upon which the argument depends? Yes.
(C) The study participants were not found to be suffering with depression before they started abusing alcohol. The conclusion that alcohol abuse causes depression depends on the assumption that the depression did not precede the alcohol abuse. After all, if the participants were found to be suffering with depression before they started abusing alcohol, then clearly abusing alcohol did not cause their depression.
Assumption upon which the argument depends? Yes.
(D) The study was not set up in a way such that people who were not suffering with depression would be very unlikely to participate. If the study had been set up in a way such that people who were not depressed would have been very unlikely to participate, then it could very well be that the reason that all the participants who abused alcohol were depressed was simply that everyone who participated in the study was depressed. So, in order to connect the pattern of alcohol abuse and depression among study participants to the conclusion that alcohol abuse causes depression, we need the assumption that the study was not set up in such a way that people who were not depressed would not have been very unlikely to participate.
Assumption upon which the argument depends? Yes.
(E) The concurrence of alcohol abuse and depression seen in the people studied was not merely a coincidence. Going from the fact that all study participants who abused alcohol were found to be depressed to the conclusion that alcohol abuse causes depression requires the assumption that it was not mere coincidence that the study participants who abused alcohol were also depressed.
Assumption upon which the argument depends? Yes.
Correct Answer: (A) Now, having reviewed the assumptions inherent to cause-and-effect arguments, let’s broaden our understanding of cause-and-effect reasoning.
summary:
Lesson Content As we learned in prior chapters, when an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she assumes that her cause-and-effect claim is actually correct; she assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observed events.
In other words, when an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she posits that the cause she has put forth is the ONLY possible cause.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she assumes that her cause-and-effect claim is actually correct; she assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observed events.
title: 6.2 The Assumptions Inherent to All Cause-and-Effect Arguments

contents:
Lesson Content Humans tend to seek to see patterns and relationships in situations.
💡MUST KNOW:
Humans often make incorrect assumptions about cause-and-effect relationships.
For example, there’s a story of an old woman who lived in a tiny village.
The old woman made a classic mistake in cause-and-effect reasoning.
Causes and effects are very difficult to assign.
💡MUST KNOW:
Causes and effects are very difficult to assign. So, we must be very careful any time we encounter a question dealing with cause and effect.
Of course, you’d never make a reasoning error as silly as the one that the old woman made, would you? Let’s see.
Let’s now discuss some ways in which one can arrive at erroneous conclusions about causes and effects.
Let’s look at sequencing first.
summary:
Lesson Content Humans tend to seek to see patterns and relationships in situations.
💡MUST KNOW:
Humans often make incorrect assumptions about cause-and-effect relationships.
For example, there’s a story of an old woman who lived in a tiny village.
The old woman made a classic mistake in cause-and-effect reasoning.
Causes and effects are very difficult to assign.
💡MUST KNOW:
Causes and effects are very difficult to assign. So, we must be very careful any time we encounter a question dealing with cause and effect.
title: 6.3 Cause-and-Effect Reasoning

contents:
Lesson Content Mistakes involving sequencing happen when we notice that one event, X, occurs before another event, Y, and, on this basis alone, we conclude that the first event, X, must have caused the second, Y.
💡MUST KNOW:
We’re apt to make an error in reasoning any time we notice that one event, X, occurs before another event, Y, and, on this basis alone, we conclude that the first event, X, must have caused the second, Y.
For example, you have a headache, and then you take an aspirin.
It may seem intuitively correct that the aspirin caused the headache to disappear, especially given that your taking the aspirin immediately preceded the disappearance of your headache.
Perhaps the headache would have disappeared without the aspirin.
Perhaps the headache was caused by dehydration, and the water you drank with the aspirin cured the headache.
Perhaps your roommate stopped playing the drums soon after you took the aspirin.
Perhaps you received a call that you were just accepted into the business school of your choice, and since you are now relieved about a matter that had you deeply concerned and stressed, the headache has disappeared.
Perhaps, just prior to taking the aspirin, you were at your favorite juice bar and consumed a shot of turmeric, a plant substance the consumption of which has been shown to reduce headaches.
We could keep building this list of alternative possibilities, but you get the idea.
For another example, think back to the example of the old woman and the rooster.
Here’s the takeaway: the simple fact that one event precedes another does not necessarily imply that the first event caused the second event.
💡MUST KNOW:
The fact that some event X occurred before some event Y does not necessarily indicate that event X caused event Y.
In later sections of this chapter, we’ll carefully discuss how to weaken arguments that involve cause-and-effect reasoning, but for now let’s consider a Weaken the Argument example question that underscores how this sequencing reasoning error can make its way into Critical Reasoning questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 3 In 2012, the Muscat Sound, which is inhabited by manatees, was struck by an outbreak of algae blooms. Which of the following, if true, most weakens the argument? 1. [ ] (A) The populations of many types of fish that inhabit Muscat Sound have not declined at all since the outbreak of algae blooms began. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Far fewer motor boats have been present on the sound since the outbreak of the algae blooms. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) The increase in algae blooms is the result of increased levels in the sound of pollutants that are known to cause fatal illnesses in manatees. 4. [ ] (D) The algae release toxins that kill the manatees’ main food source, seagrass. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) A similar outbreak of algae blooms has occurred in a different area, which has not experienced increased mortality in marine life. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium The argument presents two events that occurred, one before the other: the outbreak of the algae blooms and the death of the manatees. The argument concludes that the outbreak of the algae blooms is the cause of the death of the manatees.
Did the algae blooms really cause the deaths of the manatees? Might there be an alternative explanation? In order to weaken the argument, we can present a plausible alternative cause for the manatees’ deaths. In other words, we need to be on the lookout for another cause that could have the effect of the manatees’ deaths.
(A) The populations of many types of fish that inhabit Muscat Sound have not declined at all since the outbreak of algae blooms began. By reasoning that, if populations of fish do not seem to be affected negatively by the algae, the algae may not negatively affect manatees either, one might mistakenly decide that this choice weakens the argument.
However, what we have to see is that, while populations of many types of fish that inhabit Muscat Sound are not declining, the passage states as fact that the manatees are dying off, and what this choice says does not change that fact.
So, regardless of whether the algae affects fish negatively, something is affecting the manatees negatively, and what this choice says provides no reason to believe that the algae is not what’s causing the manatees to die off.
Remember, we must take the premises provided to us as factual.
(B) Far fewer motor boats have been present on the sound since the outbreak of the algae blooms. This choice could weaken the argument by providing an alternate reason for the increase in the manatee death rate if we had a connection between the presence of motor boats and the welfare of manatees. For example, if food thrown by people on motorboats were the primary source of sustenance for the manatees, then a decrease in the number of boats would cause a decrease in food supply and, therefore, an increase in the manatee death rate.
However, since there is no information presented by this question or any common knowledge that clearly supports any such scenario, this choice doesn’t weaken the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The increase in algae blooms is the result of increased levels in the sound of pollutants that are known to cause fatal illnesses in manatees. This choice successfully weakens the argument by establishing an alternate cause for the rising mortality rate of the manatees: pollutants.
By saying that levels of pollutants that cause fatal illnesses in manatees have increased, this choice indicates that the manatee deaths may be being caused by pollutants.
Thus, this choice indicates that the algae blooms may not be the cause of the deaths of the manatees.
(D) The algae release toxins that kill the manatees’ main food source, seagrass. This choice strengthens the argument by explaining how algae blooms cause the deaths of manatees. If the algae kill the manatees’ main food source, the manatees are likely to die. This choice, thus, supports, rather than weakens, the conclusion that algae blooms must be causing the deaths of manatees.
Now, here, one may raise a doubt that it is not the algae blooms directly that are causing the deaths of the manatees and that it is probably the absence of the manatees’ main food source that is causing the deaths.
However, it doesn’t matter whether the algae blooms are “very directly” causing the deaths as long as they are causing something that eventually leads to the deaths of the manatees.
Think about it.
Even if the algae were not killing the manatees’ food source but “directly” killing the manatees, we could still say that algae are damaging the organs of the manatees, and the damage is leading to the deaths of the manatees. We could still argue that not the algae but the damage of the organs is killing the manatees. Couldn’t we?
So, as long as the algae blooms are eventually leading to the deaths of the manatees, we can say that algae blooms are the cause.
(E) A similar outbreak of algae blooms has occurred in a different area, which has not experienced increased mortality in marine life. This choice says that the outbreak of algae blooms in another area hasn’t caused the deaths of marine life. If there were a manatee population that hadn’t declined in that area, this choice would provide a reason to believe that algae blooms don’t cause the deaths of manatees. In such a case, this choice would have weakened the argument.
However, this choice doesn’t include any information about whether there are manatees in that other area. So, this choice does not indicate whether algae blooms affect manatees.
Thus, this choice has no impact on the argument.
Correct Answer: (C) As the chapter progresses, we’ll also cover in great detail how to strengthen arguments involving cause-and-effect reasoning, but for now let’s consider a Strengthen the Argument example question that underscores how sequencing can make its way into Critical Reasoning questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 4 Many states relaxed their fireworks laws two years ago by allowing a wider variety of fireworks to be sold. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the people’s position? 1. [ ] (A) The variety of fireworks recently allowed to be sold doesn’t include more powerful fireworks. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Fireworks companies have expanded their distribution in the last two years to reach areas that were not catered to by any fireworks company before. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Some of the states that relaxed their fireworks laws did not see an increase in the number of fireworks sold. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) The study also found that the number of severe fireworks related burns in adults neither increased nor decreased over the past two years. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) States that did not relax their fireworks laws did not see an increase in the number of severe fireworks related burns among minors. View Solution Question Level: medium People are urging lawmakers to roll back their recent relaxations in the fireworks laws. These people are doing so because they have concluded that, X, allowing a greater variety of fireworks to be sold caused, Y, an increase in serious burns to minors. This cause-and-effect claim is supported by the fact that X directly preceded Y.
One way to strengthen this argument is to provide additional support for the conclusion that X caused Y.
(A) The variety of fireworks recently allowed to be sold doesn’t include more powerful fireworks. This choice eliminates a potential strengthener.
Think about it. If the recently allowed fireworks did include more powerful fireworks, we could reasonably expect to see more injuries, such as severe burns. In such a case, the argument would have been strengthened, because our belief would have been increased that the relaxations are leading to an increase in severe firework burns.
However, this choice says that it was NOT more powerful fireworks that were recently allowed to be sold. Thus, this choice eliminates this potential strengthener. Therefore, it certainly does not strengthen the argument.
(B) Fireworks companies have expanded their distribution in the last two years to reach areas that were not catered to by any fireworks company before. This choice weakens the argument because it presents an alternative cause for the increase in the number of severe burns.
If more areas are now getting access to fireworks, it could be that this increased access, rather than the relaxations, that caused the increase in the number of severe burns. Thus, this choice indicates that the relaxations might not be the cause of the increase in the number of burns and thus weakens the people’s position regarding the roll back of the relaxations.
Remember, we can weaken a cause-and-effect argument by presenting a plausible alternative cause for the effect.
(C) Some of the states that relaxed their fireworks laws did not see an increase in the number of fireworks sold. This choice has no effect on the argument.
Notice that, according to the passage, the changes in laws allowed for a greater variety of fireworks to be sold. So, the conclusion is not that the increase in the number of burns was caused by an increase in the number of fireworks sold. It’s that the increase in the number of burns was caused by a change in the variety of fireworks sold.
So, the fact that the number of fireworks sold did not increase does not affect the support for the conclusion, which is about the results of allowing a greater variety of fireworks to be sold.
(D) The study also found that the number of severe fireworks related burns in adults neither increased nor decreased over the past two years. This answer neither strengthens nor weakens the support for the conclusion.
Notice that, regardless of what this choice says about adults, the fact remains that the number of severe fireworks related burns among minors has increased. Notice also that the conclusion of the people mentioned in the passage is simply that that increase in the number of burns was caused by the relaxation of the laws.
In other words, since the argument is restricted to supporting a conclusion about why the increase in the number of severe burns among minors occurred, this information about adults affects nothing about the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(E) States that did not relax their fireworks laws did not see an increase in the number of severe fireworks related burns among minors. This choice successfully strengthens the argument.
By saying that the states that did not relax their fireworks laws did not see an increase in the number of severe fireworks burns among minors, this choice indicates that, when X (relaxations) was not there, Y (increased number of severe burns) was also not there.
In doing so, this choice strengthens the case for concluding that there is a cause-and-effect link between the relaxations and the increase in the number of burns, because it indicates that only where there have been relaxations has there been an increase in the number of burns.
In general, when we are trying to support a conclusion that X caused Y, we can support that conclusion by saying that, when X did not occur, Y also didn’t occur.
Correct Answer: (E) Now, let’s discuss co-occurrence.
summary:
Lesson Content Mistakes involving sequencing happen when we notice that one event, X, occurs before another event, Y, and, on this basis alone, we conclude that the first event, X, must have caused the second, Y.
💡MUST KNOW:
We’re apt to make an error in reasoning any time we notice that one event, X, occurs before another event, Y, and, on this basis alone, we conclude that the first event, X, must have caused the second, Y.
title: 6.3.1 Cause-and-Effect Reasoning Error One: Sequencing

contents:
Lesson Content A second mistake in reasoning happens when two or more events occur simultaneously, and we conclude, on this basis alone, that they are causally linked.
💡MUST KNOW:
We’re apt to make an error in reasoning any time two or more events occur simultaneously, and we conclude, on the basis of their simultaneous occurrence alone, that they are causally linked.
For example, consider two events, X and Y, that occurred at the same time.
For example, many people who abuse illegal drugs are also found to suffer from depression.
Are there other possible ways in which drug abuse and depression could be related? Isn’t it possible that people suffering from depression take illegal drugs to self-medicate? Perhaps, then, it’s the depression that causes the illegal drug use.
Alternatively, maybe it’s the case that drug use does not cause depression and depression does not cause drug use.
Here’s the point: the fact that two events happen at the same time (that is, they co-occur) does not necessarily mean that a cause-and-effect relationship exists between the two events.
💡MUST KNOW:
The simultaneous occurrence of two events does not necessarily indicate that one causes the other.
Again, in later sections of this chapter, we’ll carefully discuss how to weaken arguments involving cause-and-effect reasoning, but for now, let’s consider a Weaken the Argument example question that underscores how this co-occurrence reasoning error can make its way into Critical Reasoning questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 5 In 1994, it was found that 98 percent of homicides in Country X occurred inside the victims’ own homes. Which of the following, if true, calls into question the politicians’ conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) Between 1978 and 1994, gun ownership and overall homicide rates in Country X remained the same. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) In Country X, while the number of homicides occurring annually in the victim’s home decreased from 1994 to 2000, the overall rate of domestic violence did not change. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) The years 1994 to 2000 saw a significant decline in all forms of serious crime in Country X. 4. [ ] (D) The suicide rate in Country X rose 2 percent between 1994 and 2000. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) From 1994 to 2000, there was no decrease in the number of guns sold annually to members of households in Country X. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard The politicians concluded the following:
Conclusion: The decrease in the homicide rate must certainly be due to the decrease in household gun ownership.
The conclusion is supported by the co-occurrence of (1) the drop in gun ownership as a result of the gun buy-back program and (2) the decrease in homicide rate.
An excellent way to weaken this argument would be to show that, despite the fact that two events occurred simultaneously, something else may be the cause of the decrease in homicides.
(A) Between 1978 and 1994, gun ownership and overall homicide rates in Country X remained the same. This choice tempts us with discussion of dates, gun ownership, and the homicide rate.
However, the events mentioned in this choice occurred during a time period different from the one that the argument is about.
Further, if anything, what this choice indicates, that the homicide rate did not decrease when household gun ownership did not decrease, would tend to confirm the conclusion that the decrease in the homicide rate was the result of the buyback program, as we would have the following pattern:
From this choice: no decrease in gun ownership – no decrease in homicide
From the passage: decrease in gun ownership – decrease in homicide
In other words, when we don’t have X, a decrease in gun ownership, we don’t have Y, a decrease in homicide, and when we do have X, we do have Y.
(B) In Country X, while the number of homicides occurring annually in the victim’s home decreased from 1994 to 2000, the overall rate of domestic violence did not change. This choice is a tempting trap, in that one could take it as conflicting with and somehow undermining the premise that there was a decrease in homicides that occurred in the victim’s own home.
However, as discussed in the chapter on Weaken the Argument questions, in GMAT Critical Reasoning questions, premises are taken as true and cannot be undermined.
Meanwhile, the truth is that domestic violence and homicide in homes are not necessarily the same thing. So, the fact that overall domestic violence did not decrease does not mean that homicide in homes did not decrease.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The years 1994 to 2000 saw a significant decline in all forms of serious crime in Country X. If all serious crime, not just homicide in the victim’s own home, declined in Country X from 1994 to 2000, then it could be that some universal factor, such as a change in culture, is at play here.
If a universal factor caused this overall decline in serious crime, that universal factor may also have caused the decline in the number of homicides in the victims’ own homes. Thus, given what this choice says, the decline in gun ownership or the buy-back policy may not have caused that decline in homicides.
Thus, this choice calls into question the politicians’ conclusion.
Remember, any time we provide an alternative cause of the effect mentioned in a cause-and-effect argument, we are weakening that argument. This choice suggests that there is an alternative cause for the decline in the number of homicides.
(D) The suicide rate in Country X rose 2 percent between 1994 and 2000. Like choice (B), this choice attempts to tempt us with information that could be perceived as undermining the premise that the homicide rate decreased.
However, as discussed in the chapter on Weaken the Argument questions, in GMAT Critical Reasoning questions, premises are taken as true and cannot be undermined.
In this case, since suicide and homicide are not the same thing, the fact that the suicide rate increased does not affect the strength of the argument at hand, which is focused on homicide.
(E) From 1994 to 2000, there was no decrease in the number of guns sold annually to members of households in Country X. This answer could tempt us, as it could be perceived as undermining the premise that the number of households with guns decreased.
However, in GMAT Critical Reasoning questions, the premises are taken to be true as stated, and the passage states as fact that number of households with guns dropped by 30 percent.
In other words, the fact that sales of guns to members of households did not decrease does not mean that the number of guns owned by members of households did not decrease. After all, it could be that more guns were bought back by the government than were bought by members of households.
So, this choice has no effect on the argument.
Correct Answer: (C) In later sections of this chapter, we’ll carefully discuss how to strengthen arguments that involve cause-and-effect reasoning, but for now let’s consider a Strengthen the Argument question that underscores how co-occurrence can make its way into Critical Reasoning questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 6 A recent study found that people living in areas with high levels of outdoor nighttime light reported more dissatisfaction with the quality and quantity of their sleep than was reported by people living in areas with low levels of outdoor nighttime light. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the support for the researchers’ conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) Municipalities with high levels of outdoor nighttime light could save money by reducing the numbers of street lights that they have to maintain. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Residents of areas with high levels of outdoor nighttime light can block the light from going into their homes by using heavy shades on windows. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) People who watch television before bed are reported to have more trouble sleeping than is had by people who read before bed. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Of the areas mentioned in the study, those with higher levels of outdoor nighttime light did not have higher numbers of noisy disturbances at night. 5. [ ] (E) Residents living in areas with high levels of outdoor nighttime light tend to value quality of sleep over quantity of sleep. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium The researchers have concluded the following:
Conclusion: Municipalities with high levels of outdoor nighttime light could improve both the quality and the quantity of sleep of their residents by reducing the municipalities’ numbers of street lights.
The evidence that the researchers have is the co-occurrence of (1) high levels of outdoor nighttime light and (2) more dissatisfaction with quality and quantity sleep.
The researchers’ conclusion depends on the assumption that high levels of outdoor nighttime light actually cause people to be dissatisfied with both the quality and quantity of their sleep. In other words, the researchers make the assumption that there can be no other explanation for the observation made. Our job in answering the question is to confirm the validity of that assumption.
(A) Municipalities with high levels of outdoor nighttime light could save money by reducing the numbers of street lights that they have to maintain. This choice is tempting, because it adds another reason for reducing the number of streetlights in an area, saving money.
However, since the purpose of the researchers’ conclusion is solely about improving “the quality and quantity of sleep of … residents,” this choice, which brings up a different benefit of reducing the numbers of streetlights, does not strengthen the support for the researchers’ conclusion.
(B) Residents of areas with high levels of outdoor nighttime light can block the light from going into their homes by using heavy shades on windows. The fact that residents can block light from streetlights from entering their homes is not a reason to believe that municipalities with high levels of outdoor nighttime light could improve both the quality and the quantity of sleep of their residents by reducing the municipalities’ numbers of street lights. If anything, it tends to weaken the support for that conclusion, by indicating that residents may have blocked the light from entering their homes, in which case the light would not be the reason for the sleep related problems experienced by the residents. Something else would have to be causing the problems.
(C) People who watch television before bed are reported to have more trouble sleeping than is had by people who read before bed. This choice could be tempting, because it compares the troubles in sleeping of two sets of people: people who read before bed and people who watch TV before bed. So, it could be perceived as indicating something connected to the scenario in the argument, which is also about two sets of people and how they sleep.
However, since both reading and watching television involve light, and since reading and watching television are both different from attempting to sleep, there is no clear connection between this comparison and the conclusion about reducing numbers of streetlights to help people to sleep.
So, this choice does not affect the argument.
The way to avoid choosing a choice like this one is to be careful not to come up with convoluted stories that seem to create logical connections where none really exist.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Of the areas mentioned in the study, those with higher levels of outdoor nighttime light did not have higher numbers of noisy disturbances at night. This choice strengthens the argument because it can be used to eliminate a possible alternative reason why people living in areas with high levels of outdoor nighttime light reported more dissatisfaction with the quality and quantity of their sleep than was reported by people living in areas with low levels of outdoor nighttime light.
By saying that, of the areas mentioned in the study, those with higher levels of outdoor nighttime light did not have higher numbers of noisy disturbances at night, this choice indicates that it’s not the case that noisy disturbances, rather than outdoor light, are causing the dissatisfaction with sleep of people in areas with relatively high levels of outdoor nighttime light. The elimination of that possibility makes it more likely that the outdoor nighttime light is indeed the cause of the sleep related problems.
(E) Residents living in areas with high levels of outdoor nighttime light tend to value quality of sleep over quantity of sleep. This choice says that residents value quality of sleep over quantity of sleep. However, the argument is not about the effects of artificial lighting on one type of sleep or the other. Rather, both the quantity and the quality have always been mentioned together. Thus, this choice has no impact on the argument.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss another key concept related to avoiding making errors in reasoning about cause and effect: correlation versus causation.
summary:
Lesson Content A second mistake in reasoning happens when two or more events occur simultaneously, and we conclude, on this basis alone, that they are causally linked.
💡MUST KNOW:
We’re apt to make an error in reasoning any time two or more events occur simultaneously, and we conclude, on the basis of their simultaneous occurrence alone, that they are causally linked.
For example, consider two events, X and Y, that occurred at the same time.
title: 6.3.2 Cause-and-Effect Reasoning Error Two: Co-Occurrence

contents:
Lesson Content We say that two variables are correlated when a change in one variable accompanies a change in the other.
💡MUST KNOW:
Two variables are correlated when the variables change together in a consistent manner. In a positive correlation, as one variable goes up (or down), so does the other. In a negative correlation, as one variable goes up (or down) the other does the opposite.
Now, the fact that two variables are correlated (i.e.
While a correlation between two variables may in some cases lend support to the conclusion that there is a causal relationship between the variables, it would be incorrect to infer that, because the variables are correlated, one must cause the other.
However, it is not uncommon for humans to make this error – that is, mistakenly to take correlation for causation.
Between 2000 and 2009, there was a very strong positive correlation between (1) the divorce rate in Maine and (2) the per capita consumption of margarine in the U.S.
Wow – look at the lines on the graph, which show an almost perfect upward and downward connected movement between the two variables.
Can we therefore conclude that eating more margarine causes an increase in the divorce rate in Maine? Should we all stop eating margarine to save the marriages of the citizens of Maine? Or, could the reverse cause and effect be at play? Is divorce making people somehow consume margarine? Should we investigate why getting divorced may make people crave margarine?
What’s happening here? Surely, we have uncovered a newsworthy cause-and-effect relationship? Right? Not exactly.
The point here is that, when two variables show an apparent correlation (either positive or negative), we cannot on the basis of that correlation alone conclude that a cause-and-effect relationship links them.
💡MUST KNOW:
The fact that two variables show a correlation (either positive or negative), does not allow us – on the basis of that fact alone – to conclude that a cause-and-effect relationship links them.
Let’s now practice noticing correlation in cause-and-effect arguments.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 7 Banquet halls that want to do more business should increase their prices. Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument? 1. [ ] (A) A banquet hall can increase its profits by carefully choosing the food and beverage items it offers so as to maximize margins. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) The more popular a month is for holding events, the less banquet halls have to offer discounts to generate sales for that month. 3. [ ] (C) A banquet hall that does more business than another banquet hall is not necessarily more profitable than the other banquet hall. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Often people perceive a service priced higher than other similar services to be of higher quality than the other services. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Some banquet halls are so busy that they do not want to do more business than they are already doing. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium The author has concluded the following:
Conclusion: Banquet halls that want to do more business should increase their prices.
The author has arrived at that conclusion after observing a correlation between the prices banquet halls charge and the number of events they host: the higher the prices, the more events they host.
In going from that observation to the conclusion, the author has assumed that the higher prices cause the greater numbers of events hosted. Hmm …
A choice that provides an alternative explanation for the correlation will weaken the argument.
(A) A banquet hall can increase its profits by carefully choosing the food and beverage items it offers so as to maximize margins. The author has presented a strategy for banquet halls that want to “do more business.”
Now, this choice presents a strategy via the use of which a banquet hall can “increase its profits.”
While doing more business and increasing profits are related, the fact that there is a strategy that a banquet hall can use to “increase its profits” does not mean that a banquet hall won’t “do more business” by using another strategy. After all, there can be multiple strategies that work.
So, this choice does not weaken the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The more popular a month is for holding events, the less banquet halls have to offer discounts to generate sales for that month. This choice provides an alternative explanation for the correlation observed by the author: the cause and effect are the reverse of what the author believes them to be.
In other words, this choice indicates that, rather than higher prices causing banquet halls to have busier months, what’s going on is that, during more popular months, banquet halls offer fewer discounts and thus charge prices that are on average higher. Simply put, this choice indicates that, rather than higher prices cause busier months, busier months cause higher prices.
By providing a plausible alternative explanation for the observed events, this choice attacks the author’s assumption that higher prices cause banquet halls to do more business and, thus, weakens the argument.
(C) A banquet hall that does more business than another banquet hall is not necessarily more profitable than the other banquet hall.
