The conclusion of the argument is specifically about a strategy that banquet halls can use if they “want to do more business.” Whether a banquet hall will become more profitable by doing more business is another matter. So, what this choice says about profitability has no effect on the support for the conclusion.
To avoid choosing a choice like this one, we have to be careful not to be fooled into perceiving two different things, such as doing more business and being more profitable, to be the same thing.
(D) Often people perceive a service priced higher than other similar services to be of higher quality than the other services. This choice has completely the wrong effect.
While what this choice says is related to the argument, the effect of this choice is to explain why the strategy proposed by the author would work. After all, the fact that, often, people perceive a service priced higher than other similar services to be of higher quality than the other services would explain why a banquet hall could do more business by increasing its prices: people seeing the higher prices would perceive that banquet hall’s services to be of higher quality and, therefore, choose to use that banquet hall.
Of course, a choice that explains why the strategy proposed by the author would work does not weaken the argument.
(E) Some banquet halls are so busy that they do not want to do more business than they are already doing. Notice that the conclusion of the argument is about a strategy that banquet halls that “want to do more business” can use. The fact that some banquet halls are so busy that they do not want to do more business than they are already doing does not indicate that the strategy that the author has proposed will not work for banquet halls that do want to do more business.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 8 Studies have shown that, the higher the average level of education of members of a society, the fewer children are had on average by members of that society. Which of the following, if true, does the most to confirm the above conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) Parents who have many children often have difficulty paying for their children to be educated. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) On average, the more educated people are, the more knowledgeable they are about how to optimally rear children. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) It is not the case that the governments that do the most to promote education are also those that do the most to advocate having small families. 4. [ ] (D) Children who are better educated tend to cause their parents less trouble than children who are less well educated. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) People who have children as teenagers are more likely to drop out of school than and tend to have more children than people who start having children later in life. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard The author concludes the following:
Conclusion: We can slow population growth by improving access to education.
The author bases that conclusion on a negative correlation between (1) average level of education of members of a society (2) average number of children had by members of that society. In other words, the author has observed that when one variable, level of education, is higher, a second variable, number of children, is lower.
Of course, in basing that conclusion on that observation, the author has assumed that education is the cause of people’s having fewer of children. So, information that helps to confirm that education is in fact the cause of people’s having fewer children will strengthen the argument.
(A) Parents who have many children often have difficulty paying for their children to be educated. This choice provides reason to believe that there may be an alternative explanation for the negative correlation between level of education and number of children had by members of a society: perhaps members of a society in which people have many children cannot afford to educate their children.
In other words, this choice indicates that the cause-and-effect relationship may be the reverse of what the author has assumed it to be. Perhaps it is not the case that education causes members of a society to have fewer children but, rather, the case that having fewer children causes members of a society to be more educated, because parents who have fewer children are better able to pay for their children to be educated.
By providing a plausible alternative explanation for the observed events, this choice weakens rather than strengthens the argument.
(B) On average, the more educated people are, the more knowledgeable they are about how to optimally rear children. This choice supports the wrong conclusion. It supports a conclusion along the lines of that education benefits the members of a society.
Meanwhile this choice does not support this argument’s conclusion, because it provides no clear reason to believe that parents’ being knowledgeable about rearing children will lead to a slowing of population growth.
Since this choice provides support for the wrong conclusion, it does not strengthen the argument.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) It is not the case that the governments that do the most to promote education are also those that do the most to advocate having small families. This choice strengthens the argument by serving to eliminate a plausible alternative explanation.
Imagine if the governments that do the most to promote education are also those that do the most to advocate having small families. Wouldn’t that fact serve as an alternative explanation for the observed pattern?
Yes, it certainly would, because that fact, if true, would be a very plausible alternative reason why, the higher the average level of education of members of a society, the fewer children are had by members of that society. Both the relatively high levels of education and the relatively low numbers of children would be the results of efforts of the same governments.
So, by getting rid of this potential alternate explanation for the observed pattern, this choice strengthens the argument.
If this choice doesn’t sound like a strengthener to you, try reading it without “not.” Without “not” this choice provides an alternate explanation for the observed pattern and weakens the argument.
Thus, with “not,” this choice eliminates that alternative explanation and strengthens the argument.
(D) Children who are better educated tend to cause their parents less trouble than children who are less well educated. This choice supports the wrong conclusion. It supports a conclusion along the lines of that education of children benefits parents.
Meanwhile this choice does not support this argument’s conclusion, because there is no clear reason to believe that children’s causing less trouble will lead to a slowing of population growth.
Since this choice provides support for the wrong conclusion, it does not strengthen the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(E) People who have children as teenagers are more likely to drop out of school than and tend to have more children than people who start having children later in life. This choice provides reason to believe that there may be an alternative explanation for the negative correlation between level of education and number of children had by members of a society: perhaps what’s going on is that, in societies in which people tend to have children as teenagers, people both drop out of school and have relatively high numbers of children.
In other words, this choice indicates that the cause-and-effect relationship between education and having fewer children the author has assumed exists may not exist, and that it may instead be the case that people’s having children as teenagers both causes them to be less educated and causes them to have more children.
Since this choice provides a reason to question the author’s assumption, it weakens rather than strengthens the argument.
Correct Answer: (C) Now that we better understand cause-and-effect reasoning, let’s continue.
summary:
Lesson Content We say that two variables are correlated when a change in one variable accompanies a change in the other.
💡MUST KNOW:
Two variables are correlated when the variables change together in a consistent manner. In a positive correlation, as one variable goes up (or down), so does the other. In a negative correlation, as one variable goes up (or down) the other does the opposite.
Now, the fact that two variables are correlated (i.
title: 6.3.3 Cause-and-effect Reasoning Error Three: Confusing Correlation and Causation

contents: Lesson Content As you already know, we tend to see cause-and-effect arguments appear in Weaken the Argument questions.
summary: Lesson Content As you already know, we tend to see cause-and-effect arguments appear in Weaken the Argument questions.
title: 6.4 Weakening A Cause-and-Effect Argument

contents:
Lesson Content Let’s say that you were to express a wish to a friend that you want to attain a v-shaped physique.
One way to weaken the case for your friend’s conclusion would be to show that perhaps the cause and the effect are in fact reversed.
In other words, what if we had information that indicates that it may be that only people who are somehow “naturally” built with v-shaped physiques thrive as professional swimmers? In such a case, it could be that professional swimmers have v-shaped physiques not because swimming helps in building v-shaped physiques but because only people with v-shaped physiques can do that well in swimming.
Regardless of the degree to which either of the foregoing cause-and-effect relationships has any basis in reality, the point to understand here is that the original conclusion about the cause and effect was drawn entirely on the basis of the co-occurrence of the two variables.
Remember that, when an author concludes that one thing causes another thing, she is assuming that there can be no other explanation for the observed pattern.
So then, one way to weaken a cause-and-effect argument is to show that perhaps the cause and effect are actually reversed.
💡MUST KNOW:
It is possible to weaken a cause-and-effect argument by showing that the cause-and-effect relation could be reversed. That is, if the argument assumes or concludes that that A caused B, showing that B may have caused A would successfully weaken the argument.
As another example, some researchers have long hypothesized that marijuana use causes depression in adolescents.
Consider the following examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 9 A biology professor noticed that, among his students, those who drink the most coffee also appear to be the most anxious. Which of the following, if true, most weakens the professor’s conclusion? 1. [x] (A) People often attempt to deal with anxiety by drinking hot beverages such as coffee. 2. [ ] (B) Coffee does not contain substances other than caffeine that may lead to increased anxiety levels. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) The students who drank decaffeinated coffee experienced lower levels of anxiety. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Biology students tend to drink more coffee than is drunk by students in other disciplines. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Biology students tend to report experiencing levels of anxiety higher than those experienced by students in other disciplines. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy The professor concludes that the high level of caffeine in coffee causes an increase in the anxiety levels of people. The conclusion is based on the co-occurrence of higher amounts of coffee and higher levels of anxiety.
One way to weaken the argument is by showing that the causal relationship might actually be in the opposite direction. In other words, greater anxiety may be causing greater consumption of coffee.
YOUR CHOICE(A) People often attempt to deal with anxiety by drinking hot beverages such as coffee. If people routinely drink hot beverages to deal with anxiety, then the cause-and-effect relationship may be the reverse of what the professor has concluded it to be. In this case, the higher levels of anxiety and the greater consumption of coffee co-occur not because coffee leads to anxiety but because anxiety leads people to drink more coffee.
Notice that this choice casts doubt on the conclusion by showing that perhaps the cause and effect are actually reversed.
(B) Coffee does not contain substances other than caffeine that may lead to increased anxiety levels. This choice strengthens the professor’s argument.
The opposite of this statement – coffee contains other substances that may lead to increased anxiety levels – would cast doubt on the professor’s conclusion that the caffeine is leading to increased anxiety. After all, in that case, those other substances, rather than caffeine, could be leading to higher anxiety levels.
This choice strengthens the argument by serving to rule out these alternative causes.
Remember, whenever we can eliminate a potential weakener, we strengthen the argument.
(C) The students who drank decaffeinated coffee experienced lower levels of anxiety. This choice strengthens the argument by showing that, when you remove caffeine from coffee, the anxiety levels also go down. In general, a valid way of strengthening a causal relation “X led to Y” is saying that, when X doesn’t happen, Y also doesn’t happen.
(D) Biology students tend to drink more coffee than is drunk by students in other disciplines. Because the conclusion is that anxiety is caused by coffee, in order for this choice to have any impact on the argument, we’d need to know not only what this choice says but also whether biology students are more anxious than students from other disciplines. Given that we do not have this information, this choice does not have any impact on the argument.
(E) Biology students tend to report experiencing levels of anxiety higher than those experienced by students in other disciplines. Because the conclusion is that anxiety is caused by coffee, in order for this choice to have any impact on the argument, we’d need to know not only what this choice says but also the coffee consumption of biology students relative to the coffee consumption of students in other disciplines. After all, the information that this choice provides does not indicate whether caffeine is the cause of biology students’ relatively high levels of anxiety.
Since we don’t have information on the coffee consumption of biology students, this choice does not have any impact on the argument.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 10 While studying traffic patterns in a major city, an urban planner observed that areas that had the most traffic congestion also had the highest numbers of vehicular crashes. Which of the following, if true, would cast the most doubt that implementation of the urban planner’s recommendation would achieve its intended objective? 1. [ ] (A) Many crashes occur because the drivers involved are distracted. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Reducing traffic congestion in major cities is expensive. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Many of the worst vehicular crashes occur late at night. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Traffic congestion tends to be preceded by vehicular crashes. 5. [ ] (E) When another city expanded the city’s roads to reduce traffic congestion, the city experienced fewer crashes. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy The recommendation that the city reduce traffic congestion to decrease the number of crashes is based on the co-occurrence of traffic congestion and vehicular crashes. The assumption underlying the recommendation is that the co-occurrence is actually the result of a causal relationship in which traffic congestion led to crashes.
One way in which the argument can be weakened is by showing that the causal relationship may in fact be the other way around: crashes led to traffic congestion – a very plausible scenario. In other words, one way to weaken this cause-and-effect argument is to find an alternative explanation.
(A) Many crashes occur because the drivers involved are distracted.
Regardless of whether distraction causes many crashes, there is a co-occurrence of congestion and crashes. So, it could be that, while many crashes are caused by distraction, many others are caused by congestion, or it could be that distraction causes crashes when there is congestion. In either scenario, congestion still seems to be a factor causing crashes. So, this choice does not really weaken the argument
(B) Reducing traffic congestion in major cities is expensive.
We are concerned not with the costs of reducing congestion but, rather, with the effects of reducing congestion. The cost of reducing congestion does not affect the effects of reducing congestion.
(C) Many of the worst vehicular crashes occur late at night.
This choice could be tempting, because what it says about the worst crashes occurring late at night, when, in theory, there would be less congestion, could be perceived as contradicting the premise that congestion and vehicular crashes co-occur and, thus, as weakening the argument.
However, as we are well aware, premises in Critical Reasoning questions are to be taken as fact. So, choices that seem to weaken arguments by attacking premises cannot be correct.
In this case, the fact that many of the worst crashes occur at night does not change the fact that crashes and congestion co-occur and, therefore, does not weaken the support for the recommendation.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Traffic congestion tends to be preceded by vehicular crashes.
If traffic congestion tends to be preceded by traffic crashes, then traffic congestion is not generally the cause of crashes, as the crashes happen before the traffic congestion does.
In such a case, it seems likely that crashes lead to congestion, and thus, the co-occurrence of crashes and congestion may be the result of this reverse causality. If so, the whole basis of the recommendation is challenged.
Thus, this choice casts doubt on the idea that reducing congestion will achieve the desired outcome.
(E) When another city expanded the city’s roads to reduce traffic congestion, the city experienced fewer crashes.
This choice can only strengthen the urban planner’s recommendation.
If another city experienced fewer crashes by reducing congestion, then it seems likely that the city in the argument will be able to achieve the same. Even though there could be differences in the cities because of which reducing congestion may not have the same impact in both cities, this choice still provides some support for the urban planner’s recommendation.
Correct Answer: (D) Next, let’s discuss how to weaken a causal argument by finding an alternative cause to explain the correlation.

One way to weaken the case for your friend’s conclusion would be to show that perhaps the cause and the effect are in fact reversed.
In other words, what if we had information that indicates that it may be that only people who are somehow “naturally” built with v-shaped physiques thrive as professional swimmers?
title: 6.4.1 Weakening a Cause-and-Effect Argument by Reversing the Relationship

contents:
Lesson Content Often, there can be an alternative explanation for an event.
💡MUST KNOW:
One way to weaken the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion is to show that there is an alternative explanation for the observed effect.
Consider the following argument:
In the past, almost all personal computer users used the MT operating system. Two years ago, a new operating system called AX was introduced, and, since then, AX has become increasingly popular. Over the period during which AX has become more popular, the number of emails made to support centers by people seeking help with operating their computers has increased. It must be the case that AX is more difficult to use than MT.
Notice the cause-and-effect reasoning set forth in the argument.
But – could there be an alternative explanation for the observed events? Yes, there could.
Whereas, before two years ago, computer users tended to be experts, now many computer users are novices or occasional users.
This fact would weaken the argument, since it provides an alternative explanation for the increase in the number of emails from people seeking help with operating their computers.
Consider the following examples in which support for cause-and-effect conclusions is weakened by choices that suggest alternative causes.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 11 Surfer: I read that, over the last five years, there have been, on average, 82 shark attacks on humans per year worldwide. Which of the following, if true, most seriously calls into question the surfer’s conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) While educational programs have succeeded in raising awareness of the importance of sharks for the health of the oceans, every year large numbers of sharks are killed for their fins. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) During the most recent year, more shark attacks on humans were reported than were reported in any previous year. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) The percentage of shark attacks that are fatal has been decreasing as methods for treating the resulting wounds have improved. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) The human population and the popularity of going to beaches and swimming in the ocean have been increasing steadily. 5. [ ] (E) Occasionally, a shark attack that occurs in a remote area goes unreported. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy The surfer arrives at the following conclusion:
Conclusion: There are more sharks in the water now than there were 20 years ago.
The conclusion is based on the fact that the number of shark attacks per year has increased. We could weaken this argument by presenting another reason why the number of shark attacks on humans has increased.
(A) While educational programs have succeeded in raising awareness of the importance of sharks for the health of the oceans, every year large numbers of sharks are killed for their fins. This choice is a trap.
Because this choice says that every year sharks are killed for their fins, a test-taker might construe what it says as indicating that shark populations have not rebounded.
However, even if what this choice says is true, conservation measures could still have resulted in an increase in the number of sharks in the oceans. After all, presumably, conservation measures have had some effect even if it is still the case that every year large numbers of sharks are killed for their fins. Also, regardless of what this choice says, the fact remains that the number of shark attacks has increased. Thus, concluding that there are more sharks in the oceans still makes sense even if what this choice says is true.
So, this answer choice does not weaken the argument.
(B) During the most recent year, more shark attacks on humans were reported than were reported in any previous year. What this choice says basically just confirms what the argument says, that the number of attacks has increased. So, it does not at all weaken the argument.
(C) The percentage of shark attacks that are fatal has been decreasing as methods for treating the resulting wounds have improved. The outcomes of shark attacks do not affect the number of shark attacks or the number of sharks. So, what this choice says does not affect the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The human population and the popularity of going to beaches and swimming in the ocean have been increasing steadily. The information provided by this choice indicates that there are more people in the world than there were twenty years ago and that going to the beach and swimming in the ocean are more popular than they were twenty years ago. Thus, this choice provides reason to believe that there are more people in the oceans than there were twenty years ago.
An increase in the number of people in the oceans would explain an increase in shark attacks on people.
So, this choice presents an alternate reason for the increase in shark attacks and thus weakens the support for the conclusion that there are many more sharks in the oceans than there were twenty years ago.
(E) Occasionally, a shark attack that occurs in a remote area goes unreported. This choice is a trap, because reporting, or lack of reporting, of shark attacks could affect the number of shark attacks that the surfer is aware of, and therefore, this choice could be perceived as affecting the argument.
Notice, however, that the surfer has mentioned an increase from an average of 50 shark attacks per year to an average of 82 per year. That 32 shark attacks per year increase would not have resulted from shark attacks’ “occasionally” going unreported.
Furthermore, we are given no reason to believe that the number of shark attacks that go unreported in a typical year has changed over the past twenty years. So, presumably around the same number have gone unreported each year recently as went unreported each year twenty years ago, in which case the average number of shark attacks, both reported and unreported, per year has still increased by about 32.
So, this choice does not provide information that clearly affects the support for the surfer’s conclusion.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 12 The number of elm trees in Zobrinia had been decreasing steadily for decades. Which of the following, if true, provides information that is a reason to call into question the validity of the above conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) Some elm trees in Zobrinia have recently become infected with a virulent strain of the fungus that causes the disease. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Leaf scorch, which is a generally non-fatal tree condition caused by environmental factors, can be mistaken for Dutch elm disease. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) While tree experts in other countries have used inoculation to cause elm trees there to develop increased resistance to Dutch elm disease, such practices have not been used in Zobrinia. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Attempts to control the spread of the elm bark beetle, which is the carrier of the fungus that causes Dutch elm disease, have been largely unsuccessful. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Furniture made from the wood of elm trees, which had long been popular in Zobrinia, went out of favor in Zobrinia several years ago. View Solution Question Level: medium The argument arrives at the following cause-and-effect conclusion:
Conclusion: It must be that the elm trees in Zobrinia have somehow developed increased resistance to Dutch elm Disease.
The conclusion is based on the facts that Dutch elm disease has been killing elm trees and that, recently, the decline in the number of elm trees has slowed markedly.
We could call into question the conclusion that the cause of the slowing is increased resistance to the disease by bringing up something else that could be causing the slowing of the decline in the number of elm trees.
(A) Some elm trees in Zobrinia have recently become infected with a virulent strain of the fungus that causes the disease. This choice could be tempting, as it could be perceived as indicating that increased resistance to Dutch elm disease can’t be the reason for the slowing of the decline in the number of elm trees, because some elm trees have recently become infected with the disease.
However, the truth is that, even if some elm trees have become infected with a virulent strain of the fungus that causes the disease, it could still be the case that the slowing of the decline in the number of elm trees in Zobrinia is the result of elm trees in general having developed increased resistance to Dutch elm disease.
(B) Leaf scorch, which is a generally non-fatal tree condition caused by environmental factors, can be mistaken for Dutch elm disease. This choice could be perceived as indicating that it’s possible that the elm trees in Zobrinia were experiencing leaf scorch, rather than Dutch elm disease and as, therefore, casting doubt on the conclusion that the decline in the number of elm trees has slowed because the elm trees have developed increased resistance to Dutch elm disease.
Of course, that story doesn’t really make sense, because the passage states as fact that Dutch elm disease has been killing elm trees in Zobrinia. The fact that leaf scorch could be mistaken for Dutch elm disease does not mean that Dutch elm disease hasn’t been killing elm trees in Zobrinia or that increased resistance to the disease is not the reason for the slowing of the decline in the number of elm trees.
(C) While tree experts in other countries have used inoculation to cause elm trees there to develop increased resistance to Dutch elm disease, such practices have not been used in Zobrinia. This choice could be perceived to be a blatant weakener, because the fact that inoculation has not been used in Zobrinia could be perceived as indicating that the elm trees in Zobrinia must not have developed increased resistance to Dutch elm disease.
However, since the elm trees may have developed increased resistance without inoculation, this choice does not affect the strength of the argument.
(D) Attempts to control the spread of the elm bark beetle, which is the carrier of the fungus that causes Dutch elm disease, have been largely unsuccessful. This choice provides information that can be used to eliminate an alternative reason for the slowing of the decline in the number of elm trees: beetle control.
By serving to eliminate an alternative reason for the slowing of the decline, this choice strengthens, rather than weakens, the support for the conclusion that the slowing of the decline is the result of the elm trees’ having developed increased resistance to Dutch elm disease.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Furniture made from the wood of elm trees, which had long been popular in Zobrinia, went out of favor in Zobrinia several years ago. If furniture made from the wood of elm trees is no longer popular in Zobrinia, then it’s likely that the cutting down of elm trees for the purpose of to be making them into furniture has decreased in Zobrinia. Since such a decrease in the cutting down of elm trees could explain the slowing of the decline in the number of elm trees, this choice presents an alternative explanation for the slowing of the decline and thus serves to call into question the conclusion that increased resistance to Dutch elm disease is the cause.
Correct Answer: (E)
summary:
Lesson Content Often, there can be an alternative explanation for an event.
💡MUST KNOW:
One way to weaken the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion is to show that there is an alternative explanation for the observed effect.
Consider the following argument:
In the past, almost all personal computer users used the MT operating system. Two years ago, a new operating system called AX was introduced, and, since then, AX has become increasingly popular.
title: 6.4.2 Weakening a Cause-and-Effect Argument by Finding an Alternative Cause For the Effect

contents:
Lesson Content Another way to weaken a cause-and-effect argument is to find a third variable that causes both of the observed events.
💡MUST KNOW:
We can weaken a cause-and-effect argument by showing that there is a common cause for the two variables in question, rather than a direct cause-and-effect link between the two variables in question.
For example, let’s say that a statistician notices that, during the winter months, hospital admissions for frostbite increase, as does the number of people wearing coats.
Of course not.
Of course, the third variable here is the temperature.
While few people would make the mistake of assuming that wearing coats causes frostbite, here’s a more interesting example, one about the handwriting of children.
On an average, children with larger shoe sizes tend to have better handwriting.
Let’s see how this type of answer works in context:
In recent years in Pyortau, air pollution has increased and, simultaneously, the average level of physical fitness of the residents of the city has decreased. Some people noticing these two trends have declared that air pollution is clearly causing people to become less fit.
Which of the following, if true, provides the best reason to question the validity of the conclusion drawn?
(Correct Answer) In recent years, the use of automobiles has supplanted bicycling as the main way in which people get around Pyortau.
In the scenario presented, people have observed trends in two inversely correlated variables, air pollution and physical fitness, and concluded that an increase in one, air pollution, must be causing the decrease in the other, physical fitness.
Consider the following example, in which there is a common cause for both the observed outcomes.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 13 Epidemiologist: Most people would agree that smoking causes lung cancer. Which of the following, if true, most serves to call into question the epidemiologist’s conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) Substances called flavonoids, which are found in red wine, a popular alcoholic beverage, have been found to prevent cancer. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) The incidence of lung cancer in people who consume alcohol moderately is lower than the incidence in people who consume alcohol more heavily. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Studies of people who are occasional consumers of alcohol show no difference between the incidence of lung cancer in such people and that in the general population. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) While inhaled smoke does readily visible damage to lung tissue with which it comes into contact, no similarly visible lung tissue damage results from alcohol consumption. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) People often smoke or consume alcohol in order to cope with the types of emotional stress that are believed to be factors contributing to the development of lung cancer. View Solution Question Level: easy Premise: Our studies show the rate of lung cancer in people who regularly consume alcohol in significant quantities to be 50 percent higher than the rate in the general population.
Conclusion: Our research has led to the discovery of another habit that can cause lung cancer, alcohol consumption.
(Notice, in this argument, the conclusion is stated before the evidence is presented.)
In making the argument, the epidemiologist uses an observed correlation between regular consumption of significant quantities of alcohol and increased incidence of lung cancer as evidence that such alcohol consumption causes lung cancer. The argument could be weakened by a choice that provides information that indicates that the relationship between alcohol consumption and lung cancer may not be causative, and that instead another factor underlies both the alcohol consumption and lung cancer.
(A) Substances called flavonoids, which are found in red wine, a popular alcoholic beverage, have been found to prevent cancer. This choice could be tempting, because it could be taken as weakening the argument, in that it seems to indicate that alcohol consumption could prevent rather than cause lung cancer, at least as long as the alcohol is in red wine.
However, the fact that one substance in one type of alcoholic beverage, red wine, may prevent cancer does not either negate the fact that the study results show a strong correlation between consumption of alcohol and lung cancer or provide a clear reason to come to a conclusion different from the one arrived at by the epidemiologist.
In other words, while one substance in red wine may prevent cancer, the evidence provided still seems to support the conclusion that consumption of alcohol causes lung cancer.
(B) The incidence of lung cancer in people who consume alcohol moderately is lower than the incidence in people who consume alcohol more heavily. By indicating that greater alcohol consumption is correlated with a higher incidence of lung cancer, this choice provides additional support for the conclusion that regular alcohol consumption causes lung cancer.
(C) Studies of people who are occasional consumers of alcohol show no difference between the incidence of lung cancer in such people and that in the general population. The fact that occasional consumption of alcohol is not correlated with increased incidence of lung cancer does not cast doubt on the conclusion of this argument, which is, essentially, that regular consumption of alcohol causes lung cancer.
(D) While inhaled smoke does readily visible damage to lung tissue with which it comes into contact, no similarly visible lung tissue damage results from alcohol consumption. While what this choice says indicates that alcohol consumption does not affect lung tissue in the way that smoking does, the fact that alcohol consumption does not have the same effect that smoking has does not mean that alcohol consumption does not cause lung cancer. So, what this choice says doesn’t really cast doubt on the conclusion of the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(E) People often smoke or consume alcohol in order to cope with the types of emotional stress that are believed to be factors contributing to the development of lung cancer. At first glance, what this choice says may seem to have no way to weaken the argument, since it is about reasons for smoking and alcohol consumption rather than effects of smoking and alcohol consumption.
However, in saying that people consume alcohol in order to cope with the types of emotional stress believed to be factors underlying the development of lung cancer, this choice provides an alternative reason for the correlation between alcohol consumption and lung cancer: the same factor, emotional stress, underlies both the alcohol consumption and the development of lung cancer.
By presenting a third variable that could be causing both alcohol consumption and lung cancer, this choice serves to call into question the conclusion that alcohol consumption causes lung cancer.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 14 It has been consistently found that children who attend preschool are more likely to graduate from high school than children who don’t attend preschool. Which of the following, if true, would most seriously weaken the argument above? 1. [ ] (A) Some forms of early childhood enrichment activities are not associated with higher high school graduation rates. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) High school graduation rates are not the only measures of academic performance. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Parents who are actively involved in their children’s academic progress are much more likely to send their children to preschool than parents who are not. 4. [ ] (D) Children who do not attend preschool are more likely to score below grade level on state assessments. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Many school districts currently do not have the financial resources to implement universal preschool. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy The argument concludes that making preschool universal would increase high school graduation rates. The conclusion is supported by the first sentence, which indicates that there is a correlation between attending preschool and high school graduation.
One way to weaken the argument is to show that a third variable may be causing both preschool attendance and the greater high school graduation rates of children who have attended preschool.
(A) Some forms of early childhood enrichment activities are not associated with higher high school graduation rates. This choice could be tempting because it presents information that seems to indicate that at least some early childhood enrichment activities, such as preschool, do not contribute to greater academic success.
However, the argument has already made clear that attending preschool is associated with a higher rate of high school graduation, and what this choice says does not somehow negate that premise. It could simply be that, whereas some other early childhood enrichment activities are not associated with higher high school graduation rates, preschool does contribute to academic success.
So, this choice neither negates the premise upon which the argument is based nor gives us any other reason to doubt the argument’s conclusion.
(B) High school graduation rates are not the only measures of academic performance. Who cares? While there could be other measures of academic performance in addition to high school graduation rates, the argument is specifically about the effect of attending preschool on high school graduation rates. Thus, this choice does not affect the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Parents who are actively involved in their children’s academic progress are much more likely to send their children to preschool than parents who are not. If parents who are actively involved in the academic performance of their children are much more likely to send their children to preschool, then it is possible that parental involvement results in both children’s going to preschool and the relatively high rate of high school graduation of children who went to preschool.
By presenting information indicating that parental involvement may cause both preschool attendance and greater high school graduation rates, this choice weakens the support for the conclusion that preschool attendance results in greater rates of high school graduation. In other words, this choice weakens the argument by presenting an alternative explanation for the correlation between preschool attendance and greater rates of high school graduation.
(D) Children who do not attend preschool are more likely to score below grade level on state assessments. This choice tends to strengthen the argument by showing that children who do not go to preschool are more likely to underperform in academics.
(E) Many school districts currently do not have the financial resources to implement universal preschool. This choice weakens the support for the wrong conclusion.
It weakens the support for the conclusion that making preschool universal is practical or the support for the conclusion that preschool will be made universal.
The conclusion of this argument is specifically that making preschool universal would lead to a greater rate of high school graduation.
The fact that some school districts do not have the resources to implement universal preschool does not have any impact on the cause-and-effect relationship that is the basis of the argument. The argument can be weakened only by attacking the link between making preschool universal and increasing the high school graduation rate.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss another way to weaken the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion.
summary:
Lesson Content Another way to weaken a cause-and-effect argument is to find a third variable that causes both of the observed events.
💡MUST KNOW:
We can weaken a cause-and-effect argument by showing that there is a common cause for the two variables in question, rather than a direct cause-and-effect link between the two variables in question.
For example, let’s say that a statistician notices that, during the winter months, hospital admissions for frostbite increase, as does the number of people wearing coats.
title: 6.4.3 Weakening a Cause-and-Effect Argument by Finding a Third Variable That Causes Both of the Observed Events

contents:
Lesson Content In some cases, the correct answer to a Weaken question involving a cause-and-effect argument will weaken the support for the conclusion by showing that the supposed cause occurs without the effect occurring.
💡MUST KNOW:
When dealing with a cause-and-effect argument, showing that the supposed cause occurs without the effect occurring will weaken the argument. After all, if there is a cause-and-effect relationship at play, when we see the cause, we should also see the effect.
In many situations, the fact that an effect does not occur when a supposed cause does occur is a good reason to question whether the supposed cause actually causes the effect.
For example, if someone were to say that the reason that a palm tree in Cannes died was that snow fell on the tree, the fact that snow had fallen on that palm tree many times before without it’s dying or the fact that snow had fallen on many other palm trees in Cannes without their dying would be a good reason to question the conclusion that snow caused the tree to die.
To see how this type of reasoning would appear in a Weaken question involving a cause-and-effect argument, consider the following example:
The CEO of The Health Group was astonished to notice that, during the past week, the number of employees who had arrived for work later than the company’s stated start of business was triple what it had been during any other week in the company’s history. However, when he found out that much of the subway system of the city in which The Health Group is located had been closed that week for repairs, he concluded that employees had arrived late because of issues related to the subway system closures.
Which of the following, if true, provides a clear reason to question the validity of the CEO’s conclusion?
(Correct Answer) The same parts of the city’s subway system had been closed in that same way for six entire weeks out of the previous fifteen weeks.
You can see how the correct answer weakens the CEO’s argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
One way to weaken the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion is to show that the supposed cause occurs without the effect occurring.
Consider the following examples in which the arguments are weakened by information indicating that the supposed cause occurs, but the effect does not.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 15 Last spring, an algae bloom, a rapid increase in algae density, occurred in Lake Farmington, turning the water in the lake from blue to green. Which of the following, if true, would most clearly call into question the above explanation for the algae bloom in Lake Farmington? 1. [x] (A) The reclaimed groundwater from the farm near Lake Farmington was also released in similar amounts into Lake Williamson, which is ecologically similar to Lake Farmington, yet no algae bloom occurred. 2. [ ] (B) Officials have known for some time that an algae bloom in Lake Farmington was possible, yet they did not take measures to prevent a bloom from occurring. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) After the reclaimed groundwater from the farm near Lake Farmington was stopped from entering the lake, the algae bloom immediately ceased. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Although the algae that bloomed last spring have lived in Lake Farmington for hundreds of years, prior to last spring, Lake Farmington had never experienced an algae bloom. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) When the density of algae in a lake reaches a critical value, toxins produced by the algae can harm crops on farms that irrigate with water from that lake. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy Background: Last spring, an algae bloom, a rapid increase in algae density, occurred in Lake Farmington, turning the water in the lake from blue to green.
Premise: Just before the algae bloom occurred, a large farm near the lake began releasing into the lake reclaimed groundwater containing nitrogen and phosphorus in high concentrations.
Premise: Nitrogen and phosphorus are key nutrients for algae.
Conclusion: Therefore, it must be that the reclaimed groundwater from the farm was the cause of the algae bloom.
Notice that the author is making the cause-and-effect claim that the reclaimed groundwater from the farm was the cause of the algae bloom. As always, we must be skeptical any time we are presented with a cause-and-effect claim. In this case, we must ask ourselves, “Is the reclaimed groundwater from the farm really the cause of the algae bloom?”
What might the author have failed to consider? Could there be more to the story?
YOUR CHOICE(A) The reclaimed groundwater from the farm near Lake Farmington was also released in similar amounts into Lake Williamson, which is ecologically similar to Lake Farmington, yet no algae bloom occurred. What this choice says weakens the argument.
After all, if it is true that the reclaimed groundwater from the farm near Lake Farmington was also released in similar amounts into Lake Williamson, which is ecologically similar to Lake Farmington, yet no algae bloom occurred, then we are a lot less sure that the reclaimed groundwater from the farm is actually the cause of the algae bloom.
We must ask ourselves, if this reclaimed groundwater from the farm is really to blame for the algae bloom, then why did an algae bloom not occur in Lake Williamson, which is ecologically similar to Lake Farmington? We certainly would have expected an algae bloom to occur in both lakes.
This answer choice shows a situation in which the supposed cause (reclaimed groundwater) is present, but the effect (an algae bloom) does not occur. Thus, the author’s cause-and-effect claim is weakened.
(B) Officials have known for some time that an algae bloom in Lake Farmington was possible, yet they did not take measures to prevent a bloom from occurring. While it may have been shortsighted and even negligent for officials to do nothing to prevent an algae bloom that they knew was possible, that fact does not weaken case for the cause-and-effect claim made by the author.
Does their knowing that an algae bloom in Lake Farmington was possible indicate that the reclaimed groundwater from the farm was not the cause of the algae bloom? No, not at all.
In fact, their knowing doesn’t indicate anything about the cause of the algae bloom, nor does their lack of action to prevent the algae bloom. Of course, knowing something is possible and doing nothing to prevent that thing are not the same as actually causing something to happen.
Thus, the reclaimed groundwater from the farm could very well still be the cause of the algae bloom, and the author’s argument is not weakened.
(C) After the reclaimed groundwater from the farm near Lake Farmington was stopped from entering the lake, the algae bloom immediately ceased. What this choice says strengthens the argument.
If, after the reclaimed groundwater from the farm was stopped from entering Lake Farmington, the algae bloom immediately ceased, then we have more conviction in the author’s claim that the reclaimed groundwater was the cause of the algae bloom, because when the cause (reclaimed groundwater) was removed, the effect (algae bloom) disappeared.
After all, if a cause-and-effect link exists between two variables, when the cause goes away, we expect that the effect does also.
Of course, we are not looking for information that strengthens the argument; we need information that calls into question the author’s cause-and-effect claim.
(D) Although the algae that bloomed last spring have lived in Lake Farmington for hundreds of years, prior to last spring, Lake Farmington had never experienced an algae bloom. What this choice says strengthens the argument.
If the algae that bloomed last spring have lived in Lake Farmington for hundreds of years, yet Lake Farmington never experienced an algae bloom before last spring, then we have more conviction in the strength of the argument. After all, just after the reclaimed groundwater from the farm went into the lake, the algae bloom occurred, even though the algae have lived in the lake for hundreds of years without blooming.
That situation would certainly make us more willing to accept that there is a cause-and-effect relationship between the release of groundwater into the lake and the algae bloom.
Of course, we are not looking for information that strengthens the argument; we need information that calls into question the author’s cause-and-effect claim.
(E) When the density of algae in a lake reaches a critical value, toxins produced by the algae can harm crops on farms that irrigate with water from that lake. This choice could be tempting because it mentions farms, algae, and a lake, and therefore this choice may seem relevant to the argument.
However, this answer choice is about something that occurs once the density of algae gets too high. That information does not help us understand what caused the algae level in Lake Farmington to get so high.
In other words, this choice simply states a fact about algae; it doesn’t weaken the case for the cause-and-effect claim made by the author.
Correct Answer: (A) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 16 For decades, paleontologists struggled to explain why the saber-toothed cat died out approximately 10,000 years ago, after having thrived for approximately 2 million years. Which of the following, if true, would most clearly call into question the above explanation for the saber-toothed cat’s extinction? 1. [ ] (A) The largest extinction on record, the Permian extinction, was the result of volcanic activity. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Though recent scientific research indicates that Earth’s climate is now becoming warm enough to cause extinction of some species, populations of many species seem unaffected by the warm conditions. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) The geological record tends to confirm that 15,000 years ago, the global average temperature started increasing considerably. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Evidence suggests that the earth quickly warmed considerably around 50,000 years ago before it started cooling off again around 30,000 years ago. 5. [ ] (E) Many other species of megafauna, such as the bison, continued to thrive during the period during which the saber-toothed cat died out. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: hard Since this argument concludes that a rapid shift in climate caused the saber-toothed cat to go extinct, a good way to weaken the case for this conclusion is to show that there were other, similar periods of climate change that did not cause extinction of the saber-toothed cat.
(A) The largest extinction on record, the Permian extinction, was the result of volcanic activity. The argument is not concerned with the reasons for other extinctions. The argument is solely concerned with the reason for the extinction of the saber-toothed cat.
The largest extinction may have been the result of volcanic activity, but we have no information that indicates that this largest extinction included the extinction of the saber-toothed cat. Therefore, this choice does not affect the argument.
(B) Though recent scientific research indicates that Earth’s climate is now becoming warm enough to cause extinction of some species, populations of many species seem unaffected by the warm conditions. This choice could tempting, as the fact that populations of some species seem unaffected by the current warm conditions could be perceived as providing a reason to question the conclusion that warming in the past caused the extinction of the saber-toothed cat.
However, the truth is that, unlike the species that seem unaffected by the warmer conditions existing currently, the saber-toothed cat did go extinct, and it went extinct when temperatures increased. Overall, the fact that some species don’t seem to be affected by warm conditions now does not mean that warm conditions in the past did not cause the extinction of one particular species, the saber-toothed cat.
(C) The geological record tends to confirm that 15,000 years ago, the global average temperature started increasing considerably. This information provides some confirmation of an aspect of the explanation put forth, the aspect that the Earth’s climate warmed up. Thus, this choice only strengthens the argument.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) Evidence suggests that the earth quickly warmed considerably around 50,000 years ago before it started cooling off again around 30,000 years ago. If considerable warming that quickly occurred 50,000 years ago did not result in the extinction of the saber-toothed cat (since the cat thrived for 2 million years before it went extinct around 10,000 years ago, we know that it existed 50,000 years ago), then the conclusion is weakened.
After all, if climate change was enough to cause the extinction of the cat, then the climate change that occurred around 50,000 years ago should have led to the extinction of the cat. So, given that the extinction did not happen 50,000 years ago, we have a reason to believe that climate change was not the reason for the extinction.
So, this choice casts doubt on the conclusion that climate change led to the extinction of the saber-toothed cat because similar climate change happened before but did not lead to the extinction of the cat.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Many other species of megafauna, such as the bison, continued to thrive during the period during which the saber-toothed cat died out. Notice that, regardless of what this choice says about “many other species of megafauna,” the saber-toothed cat did go extinct, and thus there must be some explanation for the extinction of the saber-toothed cat.
The passage presents an explanation for the extinction of the saber-toothed cat: the climate changed, and the saber-toothed cat could not adapt quickly enough to survive.
The fact that other megafauna thrived does not indicate that that explanation is incorrect. After all, it’s quite possible that the other megafauna were able to adapt to climate change and, thus, to thrive under the same conditions that led to the extinction of the saber-toothed cat.
Thus, this choice does not impact the argument.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 17 Health Expert: The prevalence of dementia has reached epidemic proportions, with nearly one in three people over the age of 85 experiencing the issue. Which of the following, if true, does the most to undermine the support for the above conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) The neurons of people who go on to experience dementia start to form protein plaques long before any symptoms of the disease appear. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) By the time they reach age 5, the vast majority of people become infected with herpes viruses, which then remain in the people’s nervous systems. 3. [ ] (C) To date, no known medical method has been successful in completely clearing a person’s nervous system of herpes viruses. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Not all protein in the neurons of people experiencing dementia clumps into plaques. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Herpes viruses that infect humans exist in several different forms, each of which is associated with different types of health issues. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard The question asks us to find the choice that does the most to undermine the support for the following conclusion:
Conclusion: It is clear that the cause of dementia is herpes viruses.
The conclusion is supported by the fact that neurons exposed to herpes viruses in the lab have formed protein plaques of the type associated with the development of dementia.
But are herpes viruses really the cause of the condition?
Information indicating that it is often the case that the supposed cause, herpes viruses, is present in a person, but that that person does not experience the effect, dementia, would be a reason to question the validity of the conclusion.
(A) The neurons of people who go on to experience dementia start to form protein plaques long before any symptoms of the disease appear. This choice could be tempting, because, since it appears to disconnect the formation of the plaques from the onset of the condition, it could be perceived as indicating that, even if herpes viruses trigger the formation of plaques, the viruses don’t cause dementia.
However, the truth is that the fact that the formation of plaques begins long before symptoms appear does not affect the strength of the argument, as the plaques are still associated with the health issue. All this choice indicates is that plaques exist in a person’s system before it becomes apparent that the person is experiencing dementia. So, this choice does not really contradict the understanding of dementia that is the basis of the support for the conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(B) By the time they reach age 5, the vast majority of people become infected with herpes viruses, which then remain in the people’s nervous systems. This choice weakens the argument by indicating that the cause, herpes viruses, is often present when the effect, dementia, is not. In order to see why, we have to notice a key detail in the passage.
The passage says that nearly one in three people over the age of 85 experience the issue.
This choice says that the vast majority of people become infected with herpes viruses, which then remain in the people’s nervous systems.
What we can notice is that “the vast majority of people” is much more than half of all people, while “nearly one in three people” is less than a third of people, and much less than “the vast majority of people.” Thus, the information we have indicates that many more people harbor herpes viruses in their nervous systems than experience dementia.
By providing information indicating that it is often the case that people harbor herpes viruses in their nervous systems yet don’t experience dementia, this choice serves to call into question the conclusion that herpes viruses cause dementia.
(C) To date, no known medical method has been successful in completely clearing a person’s nervous system of herpes viruses. This choice weakens the support for the wrong conclusion.
If the conclusion were that, because it has been discovered that herpes viruses cause dementia, people are on the verge of solving dementia, this choice would cast doubt on the conclusion. After all, if people have not found a way to clear people’s nervous systems of herpes viruses, then, even if it were known that herpes viruses cause dementia, there might not be any way to take advantage of that knowledge.
However, that conclusion is not the conclusion that we have to weaken, and the fact that no known medical method has been successful in completely clearing a person’s nervous system of herpes viruses does not contradict or weaken the support for the conclusion that herpes viruses cause dementia.
(D) Not all protein in the neurons of people experiencing dementia clumps into plaques. This choice could be tempting since it uses words that appear in the passage, “protein,” “neurons,” “dementia,” and “plaques,” and since what this choice says could be perceived as somehow contradicting the premise that protein plaques are associated with dementia.
However, a correct answer to a weaken question will never contradict a premise, because the premises are taken to be factual, and the truth is that this choice is merely a pseudo-weakener worded to seem to conflict with what the passage says.
After all, regardless of what this choice says, the passage states as fact that protein plaques are associated with dementia. In other words, even if “not all protein in the neurons of people experiencing dementia clumps into plaques,” it is still the case that protein that does clump into plaques is associated with dementia.
So, regardless of what this choice says, protein plaques are associated with dementia, neurons exposed to herpes viruses have formed protein plaques of the type associated with the development of dementia, and therefore, there is reason to believe that herpes viruses cause dementia.
Thus, this choice does not affect the support for the conclusion.
(E) Herpes viruses that infect humans exist in several different forms, each of which is associated with different types of health issues. This choice could be temping, because the implications of what it says could be twisted in a way along the lines of the following:
Herpes viruses are associated with different types of health issues. Thus, some types of herpes viruses cause health issues that are not dementia. So, we can’t be sure that herpes viruses cause dementia.
However, that reasoning is not really logical. Even if we were not sure that a particular herpes virus would cause dementia, it could still be the case that, when dementia occurs, it is caused by some form of herpes virus. So, this choice has no effect on the strength of the argument.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss another way to weaken the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion.
summary:
Lesson Content In some cases, the correct answer to a Weaken question involving a cause-and-effect argument will weaken the support for the conclusion by showing that the supposed cause occurs without the effect occurring.
💡MUST KNOW:
When dealing with a cause-and-effect argument, showing that the supposed cause occurs without the effect occurring will weaken the argument. After all, if there is a cause-and-effect relationship at play, when we see the cause, we should also see the effect.
title: 6.4.4 Weakening a Cause-and-Effect Argument by Showing That the Effect Does Not Occur When the Supposed Cause Occurs

contents:
Lesson Content Just as we can weaken the support for a conclusion about a cause-and-effect relationship by showing that the supposed cause happens without the effect’s happening, we can also weaken the support for such a conclusion by showing that the effect happens even when the supposed cause does not.
💡MUST KNOW:
We can weaken the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion by showing that the effect happens even when the supposed cause does not.
Of course, it is possible that something is not the only cause of an effect.
At the same time, there are situations in which cause-and-effect conclusions can be called into question by the fact that the effect happens when the supposed cause does not.
To see how reasoning of this type could be involved in a Weaken question, consider the following example:
Researchers studying a rare metabolic disorder found a family in which thirty-one members were experiencing or had experienced that rare disorder. Genetic testing of many members of that family revealed that the vast majority of them carried the same mutated version of a gene related to metabolism. Therefore, the scientists concluded that the disorder is caused by the mutated gene.
Which of the following, if true, would most seriously weaken the support for the scientists’ conclusion?
(Correct Answer) Further tests revealed that a third of the family members who were experiencing the disorder did not carry the mutated version of the gene.
Since many members of one family are experiencing what is usually a rare metabolic disorder, and since the vast majority of them carry a mutated version of a gene related to metabolism, concluding that the mutated gene is the cause of the disorder may seem to make sense.
However, what the correct answer says seriously weakens the argument since, once we find out that a third of the family members who are experiencing the disease don’t carry the gene, the conclusion that the gene is the cause no longer seems to make sense.
Here’s an example question in which the argument is weakened in this way.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 18 Since, in 1989, rates of violent crime across Verdantia had been increasing for over two decades, the administrations of some cities in Verdantia decided to get tough on crime by taking actions such as increasing the strictness with which police handled even minor crimes and pushing prosecutors to seek lengthy sentences for all people convicted of crimes. Which of the following, if true, does the most to undermine the force of the evidence cited by the administrations? 1. [ ] (A) Many of the crimes of which people were convicted as a result of the get tough strategies were non-violent crimes such as small-scale drug dealing and shoplifting. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Some of the cities employing get tough on crime strategies were unprepared for the increases in arrests that ensued, with the result that their court systems became clogged. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) After the use of get tough strategies began, not just the rates of violent crime, but the rates of crime in general decreased in many cities in Verdantia. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) It has been found that education and community renewal can be more effective in causing reductions in violent crime and less costly than get tough on crime strategies. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) In cities in Verdantia in which get tough on crime strategies were not employed, rates of violent crime declined precipitously starting around 1990. View Solution Question Level: easy The administrations of the cities in which get tough on crime strategies were used concluded the following:
Conclusion: The get tough strategies have been successful.
In other words, since, when get tough strategies were used, rates of violent crime decreased, the administrations arrived at the cause-and-effect conclusion that get tough strategies caused decreases in rates of violent crime.
That conclusion could be called into question by information indicating that the same effect occurred even when the supposed cause was not present.
(A) Many of the crimes of which people were convicted as a result of the get tough strategies were non-violent crimes such as small-scale drug dealing and shoplifting. This choice could be tempting, as one could perceive it as indicating that the strategies were somehow affecting non-violent crime rather than having the desired effect, reduced rates of violent crime.
However, the truth is that the passage states as fact that rates of violent crime did decrease in the cities using the get tough strategies. So, what this choice says indicates that the strategies may have had effects in addition to the desired effects, not that the desired effects did not occur or did not result from the use of the strategies.
(B) Some of the cities employing get tough on crime strategies were unprepared for the increases in arrests that ensued, with the result that their court systems became clogged. The fact that the cities ran into problems in the process of implementing get tough strategies does not mean that using those strategies did not result in a reduction in violent crime.
(C) After the use of get tough strategies began, not just the rates of violent crime, but the rates of crime in general decreased in many cities in Verdantia. While the purpose of using get tough strategies was to reduce violent crime, the fact that the rates of other types of crime decreased as well does not mean that the strategies did not work. It could simply indicate that the strategies resulting in reductions in crime of all types.
(D) It has been found that education and community renewal can be more effective in causing reductions in violent crime and less costly than get tough on crime strategies. This is a typical “better plan” trap answer. The fact that there might be a way to reduce violent crime (education and community renewal) that is better than the way that was used (get-tough strategies) does not mean that the use of get tough strategies didn’t cause the reduction in violent crime.
YOUR CHOICE(E) In cities in Verdantia in which get tough on crime strategies were not employed, rates of violent crime declined precipitously starting around 1990. What this choice says is a reason to question the conclusion that the get tough strategies were the reason rates of violent crime decreased.
After all, if the effect (rates of violent crime declined) happened even without the supposed cause (get tough strategies), then maybe the supposed cause was not what caused the effect. For instance, it could be that something else, such as a general shift in mindset in Verdantia, resulted in the reduction in rates of violent crime, and that the use of get tough strategies made no difference.
So, this choice undermines the support that the administrations’ premise provides for their conclusion.
Correct Answer: (E) Now, let’s discuss some ways to strengthen the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion.
summary:
Lesson Content Just as we can weaken the support for a conclusion about a cause-and-effect relationship by showing that the supposed cause happens without the effect’s happening, we can also weaken the support for such a conclusion by showing that the effect happens even when the supposed cause does not.
💡MUST KNOW:
We can weaken the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion by showing that the effect happens even when the supposed cause does not.
title: 6.4.5 Weakening a Cause-and-Effect Argument by Showing That the Effect Occurs Even When the Supposed Cause Does Not Occur

contents:
Lesson Content As we saw in the Strengthen the Argument chapter, there are many ways to strengthen an argument.
Ruling out alternative causes. Showing that when the cause is not present the effect does not occur. Showing that the relationship cannot be reversed. 💡MUST KNOW:
Three ways that are commonly used to strengthen the support for cause-and-effect conclusions are the following: 1) Ruling out alternative causes, 2) Showing that when the cause is not present the effect does not occur, 3) Showing that the relationship cannot be reversed.
Let’s start with the first way: ruling out alternative causes.
summary:
Lesson Content As we saw in the Strengthen the Argument chapter, there are many ways to strengthen an argument.
Ruling out alternative causes. Showing that when the cause is not present the effect does not occur. Showing that the relationship cannot be reversed. 💡MUST KNOW:
Three ways that are commonly used to strengthen the support for cause-and-effect conclusions are the following: 1) Ruling out alternative causes, 2) Showing that when the cause is not present the effect does not occur, 3) Showing that the relationship cannot be reversed.
title: 6.5 Strengthening the Support for a Cause-and-Effect Conclusion

contents:
Lesson Content Since one way to weaken a cause-and-effect argument is to present an alternate cause for the observed effect, one way to strengthen a cause-and-effect argument is to rule out an alternative cause for the effect.
💡MUST KNOW:
One way to strengthen the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion is to rule out alternative causes.
Remember that, whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, he assumes that his claim is accurate.
Therefore, one way to strengthen the case for a cause-and-effect claim is to add more reason to believe that that assumption is a good one.
Remember, we don’t have to prove a conclusion correct to strengthen support for it.
Let’s look at a simple example:
While testing a new multivitamin on a group of research subjects with low vitamin D levels, Company X saw a significant increase in the vitamin D levels in all the subjects during the period of the study. Company X then concluded that its multivitamin had surely caused the rise in the vitamin D levels in the test subjects.
These people who have low vitamin D levels are given a multivitamin.
While Company X’s conclusion may very well be valid, there could be other factors that might have led to the increase in Vitamin D levels in the subjects.
For example, the period of the study might have coincided with a very sunny period of weather, and, given that sunlight is known to boost natural production of vitamin D, the sunlight, not the multivitamin, might have caused the increase in vitamin D levels in the subjects.
Or, during the period of the study, the subjects may have increased their intakes of vitamin D rich foods.
There are many other factors that could have increased the vitamin D levels of the subjects.
So, one way to strengthen the support for the conclusion that it is the multivitamin that has caused the higher vitamin D levels is to rule out as many such alternative causes as possible.
For example, imagine that we determined that the subjects did not increase their sun exposure.
Now, what if we were also to discover that the subjects’ diets remained unchanged? Wouldn’t the original cause-and-effect conclusion be even more believable? For sure, it would.
Now, imagine that we could rule out all other possible variables that could have affected the subjects’ vitamin D levels.
Thus, one way to strengthen a cause-and-effect argument is to rule out alternative causes of the observed effect.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 19 200 college seniors were recruited to participate in a study. Which of the following, if true, does the most to confirm the conclusion stated above? 1. [ ] (A) When the study participants were recruited, they agreed to fill out a second survey ten years after they graduated from college. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Many colleges could add conflict resolution classes to their curricula without hiring additional faculty. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) The relationships of a person who has strong conflict resolution skills will tend to be relatively low in stress. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) It may make sense for college students who want to earn high incomes to take conflict resolution classes. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) The participants’ survey responses showed that those given the conflict resolution training were, on average, no more motivated to have high incomes than those not given the training. View Solution Question Level: easy The author of this argument observes the pattern that the average income of people who were given thirty hours of conflict resolution training was significantly higher than the average income of people not given that training. Given that information, the author arrives at the following conclusion.
Conclusion: It must be that conflict resolution skills help people increase their incomes.
Of course, the author may have missed another factor that could have caused the correlation between people’s being given the conflict resolution training and their having higher incomes. So, one way to strengthen the case for the author’s conclusion is ruling out an alternative cause for the observed effect.
(A) When the study participants were recruited, they agreed to fill out a second survey ten years after they graduated from college. This choice has the wrong effect. Rather than strengthen the case for the conclusion, this choice simply explains part of the scenario in the passage. It explains how it came to be that the study participants filled out a second survey ten years after they graduated.
Of course, information on how it came to be that they filled out the second survey does not help to confirm a conclusion based on the results of that survey. After all, information on why all 200 participants filled out the second survey does not help us to determine what caused the difference in incomes.
