(B) Many colleges could add conflict resolution classes to their curricula without hiring additional faculty. This choice supports the wrong conclusion. Rather than support the conclusion of the argument presented by the passage, this choice supports a conclusion along the lines of that it would be easy for many colleges to add conflict resolution classes to their curricula.
To correctly answer a Strengthen question, we have to be careful to find support for the conclusion of the argument in the passage, not for a related conclusion, and this choice does not provide any confirmation for the conclusion that conflict resolution skills help people increase their incomes.
(C) The relationships of a person who has strong conflict resolution skills will tend to be relatively low in stress. This choice is tempting because it has an effect similar to the effect we need.
We need information that confirms that conflict resolution skills help people increase their incomes. On the other hand, this choice may explain how conflict resolution skills help people increase their incomes. After all, the fact that the relationships of a person who has strong conflict resolution skills will tend to be relatively low in stress may explain why the people who were given conflict resolution training had higher incomes: they somehow did better in their careers because their relationships were relatively low in stress.
Notice, however, that this choice does not really provide confirmation for the argument’s conclusion. After all, do we know for sure that having relationships that are relatively low in stress causes people to have relatively high incomes? Not really. It could be, for instance, that people who have relationships that are relatively high in stress work all the time to get away from other people and therefore have relatively high incomes.
So, this choice does not really provide additional confirmation for the conclusion.
(D) It may make sense for college students who want to earn high incomes to take conflict resolution classes. Notice that, rather than provide confirmation for the conclusion, this choice follows from the conclusion. After all, if it is true that conflict resolution skills help people increase their incomes, then it may make sense for college students who want to earn high incomes to take conflict resolution classes.
On the other hand, what this choice says about what it may make sense for college students to do does not confirm that conflict resolution skills do in fact help people increase their incomes.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The participants’ survey responses showed that those given the conflict resolution training were, on average, no more motivated to have high incomes than those not given the training. This choice provides additional confirmation for the conclusion by serving to rule out an alternative cause.
It could have been that motivation to have high incomes, rather than conflict resolution skills, caused the people who were given the conflict resolution training to have incomes that were higher, on average, than those of the people not given the training.
Thus, what this choice does by saying that the survey responses showed that those given the training were no more motivated to have high incomes than those not given the training is indicate that a difference in motivation was not responsible for the difference in incomes. With the ruling out of that alternative cause, the conclusion that conflict resolution skills caused the people given the training to have higher incomes becomes a bit more believable.
So, by serving to rule out a possible alternative cause, motivation, this choice helps to confirm that conflict resolution skills help people to increase their incomes.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 20 Every year the students who go to Middletown Elementary School and Greeneville Elementary School are tested for the purpose of assessing their progress in a variety of academic areas, and every year the test scores reveal that, on average, the students at Middletown Elementary have made more progress than have the students at Greeneville Elementary. Which of the following, if true, provides the most additional support to the conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) The administration at Middletown Elementary is known for being particularly effective in determining how best to use technology to facilitate learning. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) The parents of students at Greeneville Elementary are as supportive of the students’ academic progress as the parents of students at Middletown Elementary are. 3. [ ] (C) Sometimes teachers focus their efforts on getting students’ test scores to increase, to the detriment of students’ making well rounded academic progress. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) School administrators often consider student test scores in evaluating and rewarding teacher performance. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Some teachers at Middletown Elementary have received awards for excellence in teaching. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: Every year, the students at Middletown Elementary and Greenville Elementary are tested to assess their academic progress.
Premise: Every year the test scores reveal that the students at Middletown Elementary have made more progress than have the students at Greenville Elementary.
Conclusion: The teachers at Middletown Elementary must be more effective than the teachers at Greenville Elementary.
The argument goes from the premise that every year the students at Middletown Elementary make more progress than is made by the students at Greenville Elementary to the conclusion that the teachers at Middletown Elementary are more effective than the teachers at Greenville elementary.
Of course, the reason for the difference in student progress could be something other than levels of teacher effectiveness. So, information that helps rule out another reason for the difference would make the argument stronger by increasing the likelihood that the difference in student performance is indeed due to a difference in levels of teacher effectiveness.
(A) The administration at Middletown Elementary is known for being particularly effective in determining how best to use technology to facilitate learning. It might be tempting to make up a story about why the fact that the administration at Middletown Elementary is known for being particularly effective in determining how best to use technology to facilitate learning means that the teachers at Middletown elementary are more effective than the teachers at Greenville Elementary.
However, the truth is that we have no clear reason to believe that there is such a connection. In fact, for all we know, effective use of technology could be making up for ineffective teaching at Middletown Elementary.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The parents of students at Greeneville Elementary are as supportive of the students’ academic progress as the parents of students at Middletown Elementary are. Differences in levels of parental support of student progress could be the reason for the difference between the academic progress of students at Middletown Elementary and that of students at Greenville Elementary. So, by saying that parents of Greenville Elementary students are as supportive as parents of Middletown Elementary students, this choice rules out the possibility that a difference in parental support underlies the difference in student performance.
Thus, by ruling out an alternative explanation for the difference in performance, this choice increases our confidence in the conclusion that a difference in teacher effectiveness underlies the difference in student performance.
(C) Sometimes teachers focus their efforts on getting students’ test scores to increase, to the detriment of students’ making well rounded academic progress. This answer choice could be tempting, even though it does not strengthen the argument, because it states a fact about how teachers teach that could cross a test-taker’s mind as the test-taker reads the passage.
All the same, adding the general fact that some teachers focus on getting students to score higher on tests does make any stronger the case that the teachers at Middletown are more effective than those at Greenville Elementary.
(D) School administrators often consider student test scores in evaluating and rewarding teacher performance. The fact that school administrators use student test scores in evaluating teacher performance could be construed as lending weight to the argument, which is based on test scores. However, the truth is that the argument has already used test scores to make its case. To strengthen the argument, we need new information that we can combine with the information on test scores.
(E) Some teachers at Middletown Elementary have received awards for excellence in teaching. While what this choice says may seem to indicate that teachers at Middletown Elementary are especially effective and, therefore, more effective than the teachers at Greenville Elementary, the truth is that the fact that some teachers at Middletown Elementary have received awards does not clearly indicate that teachers at Middletown are in general more effective than those at Greenville. It could even be that many teachers at Greenville Elementary have also received such awards.
Correct Answer: (B) In questions involving cause-and-effect reasoning, you may encounter scientifically designed experiments.
summary:
Lesson Content Since one way to weaken a cause-and-effect argument is to present an alternate cause for the observed effect, one way to strengthen a cause-and-effect argument is to rule out an alternative cause for the effect.
💡MUST KNOW:
One way to strengthen the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion is to rule out alternative causes.
Remember that, whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, he assumes that his claim is accurate.
title: 6.5.1 Strengthening a Cause-and-Effect Argument by Ruling Out Alternative Causes

contents:
Lesson Content The major goal of an experiment is to isolate a single variable and measure its effect on another variable.
💡MUST KNOW:
The major goal of an experiment is to isolate a single variable and measure its effect on another variable.
In scientific studies, the method most commonly used for ruling out alternative causes is to have a control group alongside the true test group, which is also referred to as the experimental group.
💡MUST KNOW:
To rule out alternative causes, in an experiment, a control group is used alongside an experimental group. In a perfect experiment, the only difference between the control group and the experimental group would be the variable of interest.
For example, if we were interested in how a certain multivitamin affects people’s vitamin D levels, we’d have two groups of people.
Meanwhile, the experiment would be designed so that, except for the multivitamin, the two groups would be as identical as possible.
Now, if, at the end of the experiment, the control group and the experimental group were to exhibit a similar change in vitamin D levels, the results would be strong evidence that the change in vitamin D levels was not caused by the multivitamin.
Be on the lookout for questions that utilize the concept of the control group and the scientific experiment.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 21 The Timas are a tribe of relatively closely related people with similar lifestyles who have for centuries occupied an area now located partly within the nation of Gredaria and partly within the nation of Lexica. Which of the following, if true, would provide further evidence supporting the above conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) Among the 62 percent of Timas living in Gredaria who are not experiencing diabetes are many who consume large amounts of heavily processed foods. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) When asked, Timas who eat heavily processed foods and are experiencing diabetes generally indicate that they are not aware that there could be a connection between diet and diabetes. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Technological advances have made treating the symptoms of diabetes relatively simple. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) In the Timas who live in Lexica, who consume a traditional diet that includes no heavily processed foods, the rate of diabetes is 7 percent. 5. [ ] (E) The Timas who maintain healthy body weights experience diabetes only rarely. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy The argument arrives at the following conclusion:
Conclusion: The high rate of diabetes in the Timas who live in Gredaria results from their dietary choices.
The conclusion is supported by the fact that the Timas in Gredaria consume large amounts of heavily processed foods.
Notice that the author makes a cause-and-effect claim. Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she makes the assumption that her cause-and-effect claim is correct and that there cannot be an alternative explanation for her observation she seeks to explain. In this question, our job is to confirm the validity of that assumption.
An experiment set up to isolate the variable in question, diet, and eliminate other variables would help to confirm that the dietary choices cause the high rate of diabetes.
(A) Among the 62 percent of Timas living in Gredaria who are not experiencing diabetes are many who consume large amounts of heavily processed foods. Since this choice indicates that many Timas who consume large amounts of heavily processed foods do not experience diabetes, it actually weakens the link between consuming heavily processed foods and experiencing diabetes, and therefore weakens rather than strengthens the argument. We’re not seeking an answer choice that weakens the argument.
(B) When asked, Timas who eat heavily processed foods and are experiencing diabetes generally indicate that they are not aware that there could be a connection between diet and diabetes. While a person may be more likely to choose an unhealthy diet if that person is not aware that that diet causes health problems, the fact that a person is not aware of the effects of his diet does not somehow make it more likely that that diet is causing diabetes. Therefore, what this choice says has no effect on the conclusion.
(C) Technological advances have made treating the symptoms of diabetes relatively simple. The fact that treating the symptoms of diabetes has become relatively simple does not strengthen the connection between the heavily processed food laden diet of Timas in Gredaria and the higher than normal incidence of diabetes in those people.
YOUR CHOICE(D) In the Timas who live in Lexica, who consume a traditional diet that includes no heavily processed foods, the rate of diabetes is 7 percent. This choice provides us with information that is like the information provided by a controlled experiment.
From the passage, we know the following about the Timas in Gredaria and the Timas in Lexica:
They are closely related.
Their lifestyles are similar.
So, in considering the differences in health of the two groups of Timas, we can rule out, at least to a degree, genetics and lifestyle.
Thus, to a degree, we have isolated one variable: diet.
So, we have our experiment. Other variables have been eliminated, and we can look for the effects of diet.
From the passage and this choice, we know the following:
An abnormally high percentage of the Timas who eat heavily processed foods (the Timas in Gredaria) experience diabetes.
A much lower percentage of the Timas who don’t eat heavily processed foods (the Timas in Lexica) experience diabetes.
By providing us with information that is like the results of a scientific experiment, this choice adds further support to the conclusion that the high rate of diabetes in the Timas who live in Gredaria results from their dietary choices, i.e., from their eating heavily processed foods.
(E) The Timas who maintain healthy body weights experience diabetes only rarely. This choice is a possible trap, as we could see that it mentions body weight and perceive it as indicating something about diet.
However, this choice does not give us any specifics about diet, and it does not indicate whether the Timas who maintain healthy body weights do or do not eat lots of heavily processed foods.
Therefore, since the argument is about the effects of diets that include lots of heavily processed foods, this choice has no clear effect on the strength of the argument.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss how to strengthen an argument by showing that, when the variable that the argument presents as the cause in a cause-and-effect relationship is not present, the effect does not occur.
summary:
Lesson Content The major goal of an experiment is to isolate a single variable and measure its effect on another variable.
💡MUST KNOW:
The major goal of an experiment is to isolate a single variable and measure its effect on another variable.
In scientific studies, the method most commonly used for ruling out alternative causes is to have a control group alongside the true test group, which is also referred to as the experimental group.
title: 6.5.2 Scientifically Designed Experiments

contents:
Lesson Content In the question above that involved the diets of the two groups of Timas, the central way in which the correct answer strengthened the argument was to show what happens when the diet of heavily processed foods is not present.
Showing that, when the cause is not present, the effect does not occur is one of the ways in which correct answers to Strengthen questions commonly strengthen support for conclusions about cause-and-effect relationships.
💡MUST KNOW:
An argument with a cause-and-effect conclusion can be strengthened by an answer choice that shows that, when the cause is not present, the effect does not occur.
Here’s another example in which the argument is strengthened by an answer choice that shows that, when the cause is not present, the effect does not occur.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 22 Recently, bee populations have been declining worldwide. Which of the following, if true, provides the most support for the hypothesis stated above? 1. [x] (A) In areas in which bees are able to gather nectar and pollen mainly from wildflowers, bee populations are thriving. 2. [ ] (B) The current decline in bee populations involves a greater percentage of bees than were involved in similar declines in bee populations that occurred in the past. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) When neonicotinoid pesticides first came into use, it was believed that they would not have significant negative effects on bees. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Study results indicate that bees that get pollen from a variety of plant species may have immune systems healthier than those of bees that get pollen from a single species. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Neonicotinoid pesticides get into the pollen and nectar of crop plants by traveling through the plants after being applied to the seeds from which the plants are grown. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy Conclusion: It is the use of neonicotinoid pesticides that underlies the decline in bee populations.
The argument supports the conclusion by linking the use of the pesticides to the decline in bee populations by using the fact that the use of the pesticides coincides chronologically with the decline and the fact that the pesticides have been found in nectar and pollen of crop plants, which bees use for food.
The conclusion could be further supported by information indicating that, when the cause, pesticides, is not present, the effect, bee population decline, does not occur.
YOUR CHOICE(A) In areas in which bees are able to gather nectar and pollen mainly from wildflowers, bee populations are thriving. The argument indicates that the pesticides are present in the nectar and pollen of crop plants as a result of the pesticides’ having been used on crops. So, it is reasonable to presume that the pesticides are not found in the nectar and pollen of non-crop plants, such as wildflowers.
Thus, this choice indicates that populations of bees are thriving when the pesticides, which are used on crops but not on wildflowers, are not present in the bees’ food sources.
So, by indicating that, when the cause, the pesticides, is not present, the effect, bee populations declining, does not occur, this choice strengthens the support for the conclusion.
(B) The current decline in bee populations involves a greater percentage of bees than were involved in similar declines in bee populations that occurred in the past. This choice could be a tempting trap, as the argument indicates that this bee population decline and the use of neonicotinoid pesticides have occurred simultaneously. So, we could be tempted to decide that the fact that the current decline is more pronounced than other declines in bee populations somehow indicates that the pesticides that are currently in use are the cause.
However, the fact that the current decline is particularly pronounced does not clearly indicate that there is a link between this decline and the pesticides. After all, no matter how pronounced this decline is, some factor other than the pesticides could be the cause of this decline.
(C) When neonicotinoid pesticides first came into use, it was believed that they would not have significant negative effects on bees. The fact that it was believed that the pesticides would not have significant negative effects on bees clearly does not indicate that the pesticides do have significant negative effects on bees.
(D) Study results indicate that bees that get pollen from a variety of plant species may have immune systems healthier than those of bees that get pollen from a single species. Be careful not to spin a choice like this into a convoluted story that seems to connect a cause to an effect. Yes, this choice mentions pollen and bee health, but what it says about these topics does not link bee health or a bee population decline to the use of certain pesticides.
(E) Neonicotinoid pesticides get into the pollen and nectar of crop plants by traveling through the plants after being applied to the seeds from which the plants are grown. This choice could be a tempting trap, because it seems to provide confirmation that the pesticides get into the nectar and pollen that the bees make into food.
However, we don’t need such confirmation, because the argument has already stated that the pesticides have been found in crop plant nectar and pollen.
All this choice does is explain how the pesticides get into nectar and pollen. We have to watch out for choices that explain rather than strengthen.
Correct Answer: (A) Next let’s discuss another way to strengthen a cause-and-effect argument: showing that the relationships cannot be reversed.
summary:
Lesson Content In the question above that involved the diets of the two groups of Timas, the central way in which the correct answer strengthened the argument was to show what happens when the diet of heavily processed foods is not present.
Showing that, when the cause is not present, the effect does not occur is one of the ways in which correct answers to Strengthen questions commonly strengthen support for conclusions about cause-and-effect relationships.
title: 6.5.3 Strengthening a Cause-and-Effect Argument by Showing That, When the Cause is Not Present, the Effect Does Not Occur

contents:
Lesson Content Remember that, when an argument claims that A is the cause of B, we can weaken that argument by showing that it’s likely that B causes A.
Therefore, another way in which we can strengthen the conclusion in a cause-and-effect argument is to show that the relationship cannot be reversed.
💡MUST KNOW:
One way to strengthen a cause-and-effect conclusion is to show that the relationship between the cause and the effect cannot be reversed.
For example, having an underactive thyroid gland, or hypothyroidism, is associated with tiredness and weight gain.
Here’s an example in context:
In the Middle Ages it was observed that people who were feverish had fewer lice on their bodies than people who were not feverish had on their bodies. It must be the case that fever causes lice to leave a person’s body.
Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?
(Correct Answer) People in the Middle Ages who removed all lice from their bodies were no more likely to become feverish than were people who allowed lice to remain on them.
In this example, the conclusion of the argument is that fever causes the absence of lice.
Interestingly, in the Middle Ages, some people observed the relatively low numbers of lice on feverish people and came to the opposite conclusion, the conclusion that lack of lice causes people to become feverish.
The correct answer supports the argument’s conclusion over the conclusion arrived at by some people in the Middle Ages by indicating that the cause-and-effect relationship presented in the argument’s conclusion cannot be reversed; the removal of lice does not result in a person’s becoming feverish.
Consider this example in which the argument is strengthened by a choice indicating that the cause-and-effect relationship cannot be reversed.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 23 A sociological study of adults aged 24 years to 45 years shows that, the lower a person’s socioeconomic status, the greater is the likelihood of the person’s suffering from a chronic illness. Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the argument? 1. [ ] (A) The study included just as many participants whose ages were at the lower end of the age range as participants whose ages were at the higher end of the age range. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) A person’s socioeconomic status does not usually decline after a person develops a chronic illness. 3. [ ] (C) Some chronic illnesses, including post-viral fatigue syndrome, have a higher rate of incidence amongst high earners. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) A person’s income and profession usually determine her or his socioeconomic status. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) People with low socioeconomic status are more likely to suffer from psychological chronic illnesses than to suffer from physical chronic illnesses. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy In this argument, a correlation between relatively low socioeconomic status and relatively high incidence of chronic illness is used to support the conclusion that low socioeconomic status leads to chronic illness.
The argument can be strengthened by information showing that the reverse relationship does not hold; that is, chronic illnesses do not lead to low socioeconomic status.
(A) The study included just as many participants whose ages were at the lower end of the age range as participants whose ages were at the higher end of the age range. This information on the distribution of the ages of the participants does not affect the argument at all.
After all, regardless of the distribution of the ages of the participants, the fact that, the lower a person’s socioeconomic status, the greater is the likelihood of the person’s suffering from a chronic illness supports the conclusion that a person’s having a relatively low socioeconomic status results in that person’s being susceptible to chronic illness.
Thus, information on the ages of the participants neither weakens nor strengthens the support that the premise provides for the conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(B) A person’s socioeconomic status does not usually decline after a person develops a chronic illness. This answer shows that the reverse cause-and-effect relationship does not hold: developing a chronic illness does not lead to a drop in socio-economic status.
By indicating that the explanation for the correlation between low socioeconomic status and chronic illness is not that developing a chronic illness leads to a drop in socio-economic status, this choice eliminates an alternative explanation for the observed correlation between socioeconomic status and chronic illness and, thus, strengthens the argument.
(C) Some chronic illnesses, including post-viral fatigue syndrome, have a higher rate of incidence amongst high earners. While the fact that some forms of chronic illness are more common in high earners doesn’t negate the premise upon which the conclusion is based, this fact is somewhat counter to the premise that chronic illness is more common in people of low socioeconomic status.
Thus, while this choice doesn’t really weaken the support for the conclusion, it certainly doesn’t strengthen it.
(D) A person’s income and profession usually determine her or his socioeconomic status. Since the fact that income and profession are related to socioeconomic status is not clearly connected to the relationship between socioeconomic status and chronic illness, this choice has no impact on the argument.
(E) People with low socioeconomic status are more likely to suffer from psychological chronic illnesses than to suffer from physical chronic illnesses. We may be able to somehow spin the comparison outlined by this choice into a convoluted story that seems to strengthen the support for the conclusion.
However, the truth is that neither the passage nor this answer choice provides information indicating that the types of chronic illness people experience indicate anything about whether people’s experiencing chronic illness is related to people’s socioeconomic status.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss the role that cause-and-effect reasoning can play in Resolve the Paradox questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Remember that, when an argument claims that A is the cause of B, we can weaken that argument by showing that it’s likely that B causes A.
Therefore, another way in which we can strengthen the conclusion in a cause-and-effect argument is to show that the relationship cannot be reversed.
💡MUST KNOW:
One way to strengthen a cause-and-effect conclusion is to show that the relationship between the cause and the effect cannot be reversed.
title: 6.5.4 Strengthening a Cause-and-Effect Argument by Showing That the Relationship Cannot Be Reversed

contents:
Lesson Content When a cause-and-effect scenario appears in a Paradox question, we won’t have to find a choice that affects the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion, since Paradox questions don’t focus on support for conclusions.
Consider the following example:
In Australia, a fence was built to keep dingoes out of areas in which ranchers have livestock. It was expected that small marsupials, upon which dingoes prey, would thrive in the areas without dingoes, but instead the numbers of small marsupials in areas without dingoes are much lower than the numbers of such marsupials in areas where there are still dingoes.
Which of the following, if true, most helps to explain why the results have been the opposite of those expected?
(Correct Answer) When dingoes are not present in an area in Australia, foxes, which prey heavily on small marsupials, become much more common.
In this question, the stimulus presents us with an unexpected effect.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Resolve the Paradox questions involving cause-and-effect scenarios, the correct answers often explain effects mentioned in the passages.
Notice how the correct answer in the following question explains an effect.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 24 In the practice of snake charming, the charmer plays a wind instrument, called a pungi, and moves in time to the music. Which one of the following, if true, does the most to explain this surprising phenomenon? 1. [ ] (A) Since, because of the use of circular breathing, there are no pauses when the pungi is played, the sound created via the playing of the pungi is trance like. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) A cobra in close proximity to a person will take a defensive posture in which it stands up and follows the person’s movements to protect itself. 3. [ ] (C) Snake charmers are experienced in working with cobras, and use a variety of proven methods, some of them cruel, to get the results that the snake charmers want. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) The cobra is the type of snake most commonly used by modern snake charmers. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) The hissing sounds that cobras make must be for the purpose of chasing off other animals, not for communicating with other cobras. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy The unexpected situation presented is the following:
Fact 1: Cobras do not have ears or eardrums.
Fact 2: A cobra sitting in front of a snake charmer stands up and sways in time with the music, appearing to follow the rhythm.
If cobras don’t have ears or eardrums, and thus presumably cannot hear music, then how can the cobra’s actions be explained?
(A) Since, because of the use of circular breathing, there are no pauses when the pungi is played, the sound created via the playing of the pungi is trance like. This choice could seem to explain why the cobra moves in time to the music, if we were to lose track of the fact that the cobra has no way to hear the music and take the fact that the sound created by the playing of the pungi is “trance like” as indicating that the reason that the cobra moves in time to the music is that the cobra is entranced.
However, since the cobra cannot hear the music, the fact that the music is trance like makes no difference and does not explain why the cobra moves in time to it.
YOUR CHOICE(B) A cobra in close proximity to a person will take a defensive posture in which it stands up and follows the person’s movements to protect itself. This fact provides a link between the cobra’s standing up and swaying and the pungi music. Seeing this link requires noticing a key detail of the passage: the passage says that the charmer … moves in time to the music. So, this choice indicates that what is likely going on is that the cobra appears to detect the music, but really just sees the charmer’s movements and moves along with them in the process of defending itself.
Thus, by providing a cause, the cobra’s feeling that it needs to defend itself, for the observed effect, the movement of the cobra, this choice provides the explanation that we need.
(C) Snake charmers are experienced in working with cobras, and use a variety of proven methods, some of them cruel, to get the results that the snake charmers want. This choice could seem to explain why the cobra moves in time to the music: snake charmers know how to make cobras move in time to music.
However, it does not explain exactly what we are seeking to explain, which is how, even if they are experienced, snake charmers get cobras to move in time to music that the cobras do not hear.
(D) The cobra is the type of snake most commonly used by modern snake charmers. While it could be that cobras are the snakes that are most commonly used by snake charmers because they are the ones most likely to move in time to the music, the fact that they are the most commonly used does not explain why they move in time to the music even though they don’t have ears with which to hear the music.
(E) The hissing sounds that cobras make must be for the purpose of chasing off other animals, not for communicating with other cobras. This choice tempts the test-taker to choose it by providing a conclusion supported by what the passage says.
What the passage says, that cobras do not have ears, supports what this choice says, that the hissing sounds that cobras make must be for the purpose of chasing off other animals, not for communicating with other cobras. After all, if cobras cannot hear, then they would not hear the hissing of other cobras.
Of course, since we are looking for an explanation for the phenomenon, rather than a conclusion that is supported by the passage, this choice does not answer the question.
Correct Answer: (B)
summary:
Lesson Content When a cause-and-effect scenario appears in a Paradox question, we won’t have to find a choice that affects the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion, since Paradox questions don’t focus on support for conclusions.
Consider the following example:
In Australia, a fence was built to keep dingoes out of areas in which ranchers have livestock. It was expected that small marsupials, upon which dingoes prey, would thrive in the areas without dingoes, but instead the numbers of small marsupials in areas without dingoes are much lower than the numbers of such marsupials in areas where there are still dingoes.
title: 6.6 Cause-and-Effect Reasoning in Resolve the Paradox Questions

contents: Lesson Content Now that we have extensively discussed cause-and-effect reasoning and how such reasoning can appear in various types of Critical Reasoning questions, let’s continue with our exploration of GMAT Critical Reasoning.
summary: Lesson Content Now that we have extensively discussed cause-and-effect reasoning and how such reasoning can appear in various types of Critical Reasoning questions, let’s continue with our exploration of GMAT Critical Reasoning.
title: 6.7 Cause-and-Effect Reasoning in Other Types of Critical Reasoning Questions

contents:
Lesson Content An inference is a conclusion that can be drawn from a set of facts.
💡MUST KNOW:
An inference is a conclusion that can be drawn from a set of facts.
On the GMAT, a Critical Reasoning Inference question is a question that presents a set of facts and then asks which of the answer choices states a conclusion that can be properly inferred from that set of facts.
As you read the above description, you may have wondered why the word “properly” was included.
Unlike the conclusions in other types of GMAT questions, which, as we have discussed, may not be fully supported by the facts given, the correct answer to an Inference question is a conclusion that is fully supported by the facts given, and, thus, must be true.
💡MUST KNOW:
Unlike the conclusions in other types of GMAT questions, which, as we have discussed, may not be fully supported by the facts given, the correct answer to an Inference question is a conclusion that is fully supported by the facts given, and, thus, must be true.
If, given the facts presented in the stimulus, an answer could be true sometimes but not true at other times, that option is not the correct answer to an Inference question.
💡MUST KNOW:
If, given the facts presented in the stimulus, an answer could be true sometimes but not true at other times, that option is not the correct answer to an Inference question.
Let’s say that again: In GMAT Critical Reasoning, a properly drawn inference is not just something that could be reasonably concluded given what is said in the stimulus.
💡MUST KNOW:
The correct answer to an Inference question is the choice that must be true given what is stated in the passage. An answer option that makes a statement that may not be true will not be the correct answer to an Inference question.
Since an Inference question requires us to choose an answer that can be proven true solely on the basis of the information presented in the stimulus, correctly answering Inference questions requires determining exactly what the statements in the stimuli say and do not say.
💡MUST KNOW:
Correctly answering an Inference question requires noting exactly what the statements in the stimulus say and do not say.
Inference Questions Don’t Really Contain “Arguments.” While we’ve gotten into the habit of referring to the stimulus of a Critical Reasoning question as an “argument,” the stimuli in Inference questions generally do not contain arguments in the purest sense of the word “argument,” wherein conclusions based upon some premises are presented.
💡MUST KNOW:
Inference questions generally do not contain arguments in the purest sense of the word “argument,” wherein conclusions based upon some premises are presented. Instead, the stimulus of an Inference question simply presents us with a set of facts. Then, from that set of facts, our job is to determine what can be properly inferred.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 1 Research has shown that engaging in conversation while driving requires that a driver engage in two cognitive processes simultaneously, and, thus, that drivers talking on cell phones are not as focused on handling challenging moments in driving as are drivers who are not engaging in conversation. If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred from them? 1. [ ] (A) Drivers who have passengers in their cars are less likely to engage in cell phone conversations than are drivers without passengers in their cars. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) It is generally the case that drivers who converse with passengers in their cars are more focused on handling challenging moments in driving than are drivers talking on cell phones. 3. [ ] (C) The driving of drivers who have extensive practice in handling multiple tasks simultaneously is not affected by those drivers’ conversing on cell phones. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) If a driver conversing on a cell phone while driving were to stop conversing in order to handle a challenging driving situation, the other person conversing might hang up. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Driving while talking on a cell phone is comparable in dangerousness to driving while intoxicated. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium Information Given:
Fact 1: Engaging in conversation while driving requires that a driver engage in two cognitive processes simultaneously.
Fact 2: Drivers talking on cell phones are not as focused on handling challenging moments in driving as are drivers who are not engaging in conversation.
Fact 3: It is generally the case that a driver and a passenger will take breaks from conversing when the driver most needs to concentrate on driving.
To get the correct answer to this question, we have to be careful not to choose a choice that is plausible but not truly supported by the passage. The passage discusses the effect that conversing on a cell phone has on driving and provides a key fact about conversations between drivers and passengers in cars. What we can infer will have to be based on these specific facts, and no others.
(A) Drivers who have passengers in their cars are less likely to engage in cell phone conversations than are drivers without passengers in their cars. While what this choice says may be true in the real world, the passage does not say anything about how having a passenger in a car affects whether a driver talks on a cell phone. So, the information in the passage does not support this choice. In answering an Inference question, we need to be vigilant not to choose as correct answer choices that say things that are true in the real world yet at the same time are not valid inferences.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) It is generally the case that drivers who converse with passengers in their cars are more focused on handling challenging moments in driving than are drivers talking on cell phones. The passage makes clear that drivers talking on cell phones are not as focused on handling challenging moments in driving as are drivers not engaged in conversation. It also says that drivers and passengers in the same car who are talking will take breaks from conversing when the driver most needs to concentrate on driving, meaning, effectively, that during challenging moment in driving, such a driver will not be conversing. Since drivers not conversing are more focused on driving than are drivers conversing on cell phones, we can infer from what the passage says that it is generally the case that drivers who converse with passengers in their cars are more focused on handling challenging moments in driving than are drivers talking on cell phones.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The driving of drivers who have extensive practice in handling multiple tasks simultaneously is not affected by those drivers’ conversing on cell phones. The passage does not provide any information regarding how practice in simultaneously handling multiple tasks affects a driver’s ability to drive while conversing on a cell phone.
(D) If a driver conversing on a cell phone while driving were to stop conversing in order to handle a challenging driving situation, the other person conversing might hang up. While what this choice says seems plausible, the passage does not give us any reason to believe that it is true.
(E) Driving while talking on a cell phone is comparable in dangerousness to driving while intoxicated. The passage provides no information that we can use to make a comparison between the dangerousness of conversing on a cell phone while driving and that of driving while intoxicated.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss some basic guidelines for correctly answering Inference questions.
summary:
Lesson Content An inference is a conclusion that can be drawn from a set of facts.
💡MUST KNOW:
An inference is a conclusion that can be drawn from a set of facts.
On the GMAT, a Critical Reasoning Inference question is a question that presents a set of facts and then asks which of the answer choices states a conclusion that can be properly inferred from that set of facts.
title: 7.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content There are a number of smart strategies that we can use when answering Inference questions.
Never Bring in Outside Information or Knowledge We must answer Inference questions using only the facts provided to us in the stimuli; we must not bring in any outside information.
💡MUST KNOW:
We must answer Inference questions using only the facts provided to us in the stimulus; we must not bring in any outside information. Do not fall for answer options that state facts that are true but are not inferences supported by the facts provided in the stimulus.
For example, let’s again consider the following choice from the question in the previous section on driving while talking on a cell phone:
(E) Driving while conversing on a cell phone is comparable in dangerousness to driving while intoxicated.
A test-taker might be aware that research has indeed indicated that conversing on a cell phone can be as detrimental to a person’s driving effectiveness as intoxication.
In Inference questions, trap choices like this one prey upon our natural tendency to seek answer options that sound familiar or logical to us.
💡MUST KNOW:
The fact that an answer choice sounds familiar or logical to us does not make that answer choice correct.
An Inference Need Not Summarize the Passage or Provide a Main Conclusion to an Argument
Often, people feel that an inference must either summarize the passage or provide a logical main conclusion to an argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
Often, people feel that an inference must either summarize the passage or provide a logical main conclusion to an argument. In fact, an inference need not do either of these things.
OK – wait a minute.
Given an argument, an inference is simply any conclusion that can be properly drawn from the set of facts that make up that argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
Given an argument, an inference is simply any conclusion that can be properly drawn from the set of facts that make up that argument.
Consider the following passage:
No new houses have been built in Verdantville in the past eight years, and the values of houses in Verdantville have not increased. Over the same time period, the number of children living in Verdantville has increased substantially. The costs of operating the schools in Verdantville are paid for entirely by property taxes, which are based on house values.
Given what the passage says about the number of houses in Verdantville, the value of those houses, the number of children living in Verdantville, and the way the costs of operating the schools are covered, the following statement could be the main conclusion of the argument:
(Possible Main Conclusion of the Argument) Thus, in order to cover the expenses associated with educating all the children, Verdantville will have to increase property tax rates.
Notice, while this possible main conclusion is supported by the information in the passage, it does not have to be true given what the passage says.
Contrast that main conclusion with the following inferences that we can make given what the passage says.
(Inference) The aggregate value of the houses in Verdantville is no greater than it was eight years ago.
Given the fact that neither the number of houses nor the values of houses in Verdantville have increased, we can infer that the aggregate value of the houses in Verdantville is no greater than it was eight years ago.
(Inference) On average, there are more children per house in Verdantville than there were eight years ago.
Given the fact that the number of houses in Verdantville has not increased and the fact that the number of children living in Verdantville has increased, it must be true that the average number of children per house has increased.
Notice, while neither of the two inferences is the main conclusion of the argument, both are conclusions that one could draw from what the passage says.
So, in answering an Inference question, we don’t necessarily need to seek out what would be the author’s main conclusion to the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
An inference need not be the main conclusion of an argument; if the facts presented are true – and they always will be in Critical Reasoning Questions – any conclusion that can be properly drawn from them will be a valid inference. It’s important to be flexible when looking for inferences.
Let’s practice applying what we just discussed.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 2 In a recent survey, 1,000 professional poker players were asked whether they had any formal training in statistics. If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred from them? 1. [ ] (A) Most professional poker players do not have time to respond to surveys. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Having formal training in statistics is helpful for professional poker players. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Most professional poker players opt to informally study statistics on their own. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) A person can become a professional poker player without having any formal training in statistics. 5. [ ] (E) Most professional poker players do not attend college at any time during their careers. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy To correctly answer this question, we must stick closely to the facts provided to us in the passage and resist the urge to bring in unsupported outside information. Remember, we are seeking an answer choice that says something that MUST be 100-percent true given what the passage says.
(A) Most professional poker players do not have time to respond to surveys. The information provided by the passage contradicts what this statement says. After all, of the 1,000 professional poker players who were asked whether they had any formal training in statistics, 900 players responded. In other words, 90 percent of those surveyed responded.
Thus, given what the passage says, it seems likely that most professional poker players do have time to respond to surveys.
When an answer choice is contradicted by information in the passage, that answer choice will never be an inference.
(B) Having formal training in statistics is helpful for professional poker players. Notice that the passage never provides us with information regarding how helpful formal training in statistics is to professional poker players.
The only information about formal training in statistics that the passage provides is that 400 of the 900 professional poker players who responded to the survey indicated that they did not have formal training in statistics.
The passage says nothing about whether the other 500 players who responded to the survey had formal training in statistics.
Furthermore, even if some of the players did have formal training in statistics, the passage does not say that it was useful to them.
So, the passage does not support what this choice says.
If you choose this choice as correct, you may have done so because it seemed quite plausible. After all, poker is a game of numbers and probabilities. So, what this choice says may have resonated with you. Remember, however, when answering inference questions, we are seeking something that MUST be true given what the passage says, not something that is simply plausible.
(C) Most professional poker players opt to informally study statistics on their own. Notice that the survey is about professional poker players who have formal training in statistics. We are not provided with any information about whether these players opt to informally study statistics on their own.
The fact that 400 players said that they do not have formal training in statistics does not mean that those 400 players have studied statistics informally.
For all we know from what the passage says, many of the players have not studied statistics at all.
YOUR CHOICE(D) A person can become a professional poker player without having any formal training in statistics. Notice that 400 of the 900 professional poker players who responded to the survey did not have any formal training in statistics, and yet, they are professional poker players.
Of course, then, those 400 poker players are living proof that one can become a professional poker player without having any formal training in statistics.
Therefore, given what the passage says, we see that it must be true that a person can become a professional poker player without having any formal training in statistics.
(E) Most professional poker players do not attend college at any time during their careers. We don’t have any information about whether professional poker players do or do not attend college at any time during their careers. For all we know from what the passage says, 100 percent of poker players attend college during their careers.
Thus, this choice is completely unsupported.
If you picked this choice as correct, you may have done so because what it says seems plausible. Perhaps you felt that a person who was a professional poker player would have avoided attending college. Remember, though, we’re seeking a choice that MUST be true given what the passage says, not a choice that just sounds plausible.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 3 In nuclear fusion, the nuclei of two atoms join to form a nucleus of a different element and, in the process, release energy that could be used to produce electrical energy. If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true? 1. [ ] (A) Fusion will not be a viable means of solving the world’s energy related problems in the near future. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) If temperatures of 50 million degrees Celsius are reached, nuclear fusion will occur. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) If the nuclei of two atoms joined to form a nucleus of a different element, that process occurred in a situation characterized by temperatures around 50 million degrees Celsius. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Cold fusion cannot occur at temperatures above room temperature. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) If, in an experiment, energy from a nuclear reaction was used to produce electrical energy, cold fusion did not drive that process. View Solution Question Level: hard Facts Presented in the Passage:
Fact 1: In nuclear fusion, the nuclei of two atoms join to form a nucleus of a different element and, in the process, release energy that could be used to produce electrical energy.
Fact 2: Generally, temperatures around 50 million degrees Celsius are needed to produce nuclear fusion, but such temperatures are extremely difficult to reach.
Fact 3: Now, scientists are studying cold fusion, which could theoretically produce energy through nuclear fusion occurring at room temperature, helping to solve the world’s energy related problems.
Fact 4: However, to date, no one has demonstrated that cold fusion can actually occur.
To get this question correct, we have to differentiate between a main conclusion that could be true given what is said in the passage and an inference that must be true if the statements made in the passage are true.
(A) Fusion will not be a viable means of solving the world’s energy related problems in the near future. It is true that the passage says that temperatures around 50 million degrees Celsius are needed to produce nuclear fusion, that such temperatures are extremely difficult to reach, and that, to date, no one has proven that cold fusion can actually occur. So, a reasonable main conclusion that follows from what the passage says is that fusion will not be a viable means of solving the world’s energy related problems in the near future.
However, we are not looking for a reasonable main conclusion. We are looking for a choice that says something that must be true given what the passage says, and, for all we know, even though the facts presented indicate that, currently, there is no known way to practically harness fusion to generate energy, someone will discover and apply a way to do so in the near future, whatever the “near future” even is. Is the near future next month, next year, within the next decade? So, the facts presented do not mean that it must be true that fusion will not be a viable means of solving the world’s energy related problems in the near future.
(B) If temperatures of 50 million degrees Celsius are reached, nuclear fusion will occur. Notice that the passage does not say that temperatures of 50 million degrees Celsius alone are sufficient to cause nuclear fusion to occur. Rather it says that, generally, temperatures around 50 million degrees Celsius are needed to produce nuclear fusion. We must be careful to not confuse the necessary with the sufficient.
YOUR CHOICE(C) If the nuclei of two atoms joined to form a nucleus of a different element, that process occurred in a situation characterized by temperatures around 50 million degrees Celsius. The passage says, “In nuclear fusion, the nuclei of two atoms join to form a nucleus of a different element and, in the process, release energy that could be used to produce electrical energy. Generally, temperatures around 50 million degrees Celsius are needed to produce nuclear fusion.” It’s important to notice, however, that the passage does not say that nuclear fusion occurring at 50 million degrees Celsius is the only process via which nuclei of two atoms join to form a nucleus of a different element. Therefore, we can’t conclude that, if the nuclei of two atoms joined to form a nucleus of a different element, that process occurred in a situation characterized by temperatures around 50 million degrees Celsius. After all, there may be processes, such as cold fusion, that result in the nuclei of two atoms’ joining to form a nucleus of a different element at temperatures not around 50 million degrees Celsius.
(D) Cold fusion cannot occur at temperatures above room temperature. The passage says that scientists are studying cold fusion, which could theoretically produce energy through nuclear fusion occurring at room temperature, helping to solve the world’s energy related problems. However, we don’t have evidence that cold fusion cannot occur at temperatures above room temperature. For all we know, cold fusion could occur at temperatures somewhat higher than room temperature. We must be careful to stick as closely as possible to the information provided to us in the passage.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) If, in an experiment, energy from a nuclear reaction was used to produce electrical energy, cold fusion did not drive that process. The passage says, “to date, no one has demonstrated that cold fusion can actually occur.” Therefore, given what the passage says, it must be true that, if, in an experiment, energy from a nuclear reaction was used to produce electrical energy, cold fusion did not drive that process. After all, cold fusion has never been accomplished to date.
Notice, what this choice says is not an obvious main conclusion that follows from what the passage says. At the same time, it is an inference that is fully supported by what the passage says.
Correct Answer: (E) Do Not Seek to Predict Answers to Inference Questions
Given even a few facts, the number of valid inferences that can be drawn from them is large.
💡MUST KNOW:
Given even a few facts, the number of valid inferences that can be drawn from them is large. Therefore, it’s not a good use of time to seek to predict an answer to an inference question. Instead, our job is to intimately understand the facts presented as well as their relationship to one another.
Consider the following passage:
The deer carrying capacity of an area is the number of deer that can inhabit that area long term. If an area’s deer population exceeds the area’s carrying capacity, the deer will consume plants in that area faster than the plants can replenish themselves. Two years ago, all wolves in Johnson County, which had always kept the area’s deer population below the area’s carrying capacity, were chased off, and none have returned. As a result, the area’s deer population now exceeds the area’s carrying capacity. If we were to start coming up with inferences that we could make given the information presented in the above passage, we could quickly have multiple inferences.
There are currently more deer in Johnson County than there were two years ago.
The deer in Johnson County are currently consuming plants faster than the plants can replenish themselves.
Currently, there are more deer in Johnson County than can inhabit the area long term.
There are fewer wolves in Johnson County than there were two years ago.
Wolves in an area can keep the population of deer in that area lower than it would be without the presence of the wolves.
Remember, it’s not a good use of time to seek to predict an answer to an inference question.
Don’t Confuse Assumptions and Inferences
Although assumptions and inferences are similar, in that an assumption is an unwritten premise that must be true in order for a conclusion to be properly drawn from a set of provided premises and an inference is an unwritten conclusion that must be true if the facts provided are true, as you can see, assumptions and inferences are also quite different; an assumption serves as a bridge between the premises and the conclusion, whereas an inference is an unstated conclusion that must be true if the premises provided are true.
💡MUST KNOW:
An assumption is an unwritten premise that must be true in order for a conclusion to properly stem from a set of provided premises, while an inference is an unwritten conclusion that must be true if the facts provided are true.
Furthermore, we’ll never need to identify an assumption in an Inference question, and we’ll never need to identify an inference in an Assumption question.
Let’s now discuss the types of Inference question stems that you’re likely to see.
summary:
Lesson Content There are a number of smart strategies that we can use when answering Inference questions.
Never Bring in Outside Information or Knowledge We must answer Inference questions using only the facts provided to us in the stimuli; we must not bring in any outside information.
💡MUST KNOW:
We must answer Inference questions using only the facts provided to us in the stimulus; we must not bring in any outside information.
title: 7.2 Basic Guidelines for Correctly Answering Inference Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Every Inference question on the GMAT begins with a passage or other stimulus, which is followed by a question that asks, in one way or another, which of the answer choices states something that must be true given what is said in the stimulus.
While Inference questions can be worded in various ways, GMAT Critical Reasoning questions tend to utilize predictable language, a fact that we can exploit by familiarizing ourselves with the types of questions we will see.
Which of the following can be properly inferred from the statements above?
If the statements above are all true, which of the following can properly be inferred on the basis of them?
If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?
Which of the following must be true about … ?
Which of the following can be properly concluded from the statements in the passage?
If the statements above are true, which of the following conclusions can be properly drawn?
Which of the following is information provided by the passage?
💡MUST KNOW:
Learn to recognize that a question stem is asking you for an answer that must be true given what is said in the passage.
Let’s now discuss a strategy for attacking Inference questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Every Inference question on the GMAT begins with a passage or other stimulus, which is followed by a question that asks, in one way or another, which of the answer choices states something that must be true given what is said in the stimulus.
While Inference questions can be worded in various ways, GMAT Critical Reasoning questions tend to utilize predictable language, a fact that we can exploit by familiarizing ourselves with the types of questions we will see.
title: 7.3 Recognizing Inference Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Step 1 – Carefully Read and Completely Understand the Passage
As is the case in answering any other type of Critical Reasoning question, the foundation of choosing the right answer to an Inference question is understanding the passage.
Also, remember that, when answering an Inference question, misreading a single word in the stimulus can significantly change the implications of what is said.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering an Inference question, misreading a single word in the stimulus can significantly change the implications of what is said. Thus, we MUST pay extremely close attention as we read so as not to misinterpret what is being expressed.
Step 2 – Identify the Question Type (What type of information is the question asking for?)
Identifying the question type is relatively easy in the case of an Inference question.
Step 3 – Carefully Analyze the Facts
Stimuli that accompany Inference questions won’t be arguments so much as lists of facts.
The point is to internalize the given information and to express the meanings and the effects of the statements in your own words and/or symbols, so that you can fully understand the information in the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
Inference question stimuli typically don’t contain conclusions. Don’t spend your time searching for them.
To get a sense of the types of things you might take away from an Inference question stimulus, consider the following example of the type of passage you might see:
From the end of 2007 to the end of 2017, the number of actors working at Magic Movie Studios increased by 20 percent. Over the same period, the number of set designers working at Magic Movie Studios decreased by 10 percent. Nevertheless, over the same period, the total number of people working at the studios remained unchanged.
Here’s an analysis of the statements in the passage:
Effect of First Statement: Defines the change in the number of actors working at Magic Movie Studios over the time period.
Effect of Second Statement: Defines the change in the number of set designers working at Magic Movie Studios over the time period.
Effect of Third Statement: Makes clear that the total number of people working at the studios did not change over the period.
Notice, the statements tell us only about direction of change and degree of change in the numbers of three groups of people over a particular period.
In order to correctly answer Inference questions, we have to be clear about what information the statements provide and what information the statements do not provide.
Step 4 – Eliminate “Sometimes True,” “Could Be True,” and “Definitely Not True” Options
Some answers may make sense or seem reasonable.
💡MUST KNOW:
Remember, something that is possibly true (but likewise possibly false) cannot be the correct answer to an Inference question.
Also, the fact that an answer choice does not conflict with the information in the passage does not make it correct.
💡MUST KNOW:
The fact that an answer choice does not conflict with the information in the passage does not make it correct.
Always read all five answer choices first and then go through them more carefully on the second pass, eliminating the options that are more obviously wrong.
Let’s consider our example some more:
From the end of 2007 to the end of 2017, the number of actors working at Magic Movie Studios increased by 20 percent. Over the same period, the number of set designers working at Magic Movie Studios decreased by 10 percent. Nevertheless, over the same period, the total number of people working at the studios remained unchanged.
Which of the following can be properly inferred from the statements above?
Here’s an example of an answer choice that is plausible but not necessarily true:
(Incorrect Answer) At the end of 2017, there were more actors than set designers working at Magic Movie Studios.
Is it possible that more actors than set designers were working at the studios? Yes.
Does the passage clearly indicate that it is the case that more actors than set designers were working at the studios? No.
As we outlined above, all the passage does is define degrees of change in numbers.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering an Inference question, never select as correct an answer choice that could be true but doesn’t have to be true. Remember, an inference must be true.
Step 5 – Select the Choice that MUST be True
We now pick an answer option that must be true.
Negate the statement: if that statement were false, would it contradict information in the paragraph? The answer that conflicts the most with the statements in the passage when negated is most likely the choice that “must” be true.
If a statement or condition, when negated, contradicts basic information contained in the original passage, then this statement must be important.
Remember, we are looking for an answer that must be true.
Let’s go back to our example:
From the end of 2007 to the end of 2017, the number of actors working at Magic Movie Studios increased by 20 percent. Over the same period, the number of set designers working at Magic Movie Studios decreased by 10 percent. Nevertheless, over the same period, the total number of people working at the studios remained unchanged.
Which of the following can be properly inferred from the statements above?
Here’s an example of an answer choice that must be true:
(Correct Answer) From the end of 2007 to the end of 2017, the ratio of actors to set designers at Magic Movie Studios increased.
Given what the passage says, there is no way to deny what this choice says.
💡MUST KNOW:
Practice with the Inference Question Strategy.
Let’s practice by answering a question, using this strategy.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 4 Whenever demand by travelers for hotel rooms in Mennerton decreases, the rates that hotels charge decrease. Which of the following can be properly inferred regarding the past three years from the statement above? 1. [ ] (A) Mennerton hotel operators’ fears that allowing people to rent out their apartments for short stays would hurt the hotel business were unfounded. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) There have been more empty hotel rooms in Mennerton than there were in previous years. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Overall traveler demand for lodging in Mennerton has increased. 4. [ ] (D) People renting out their apartments for short term stays in Mennerton have been charging rates similar to those charged by hotels. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) The number of hotels operating in Mennerton has decreased over the past three years. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium Step 1 – Carefully Read and Completely Understand the Passage
At this point, we have carefully read the passage, making sure that we have correctly interpreted every word and each sentence.
Step 2 – Identify the Question Type (What type of information is the question asking for?)
Since the question asks “Which of the following can be properly inferred … ?” we know that we have to find an inference based on the information in the passage.
Step 3 – Carefully Analyze the Facts
Information Given:
Fact 1: Whenever traveler demand for hotel rooms in Mennerton decreases, rates charged by hotels decrease.
Fact 2: Over the past three years, the availability of places for travelers to stay in Mennerton has increased.
Fact 3: Over the past three years, people renting out their apartments for short stays have increasingly provided hotel type lodging to many of the travelers to Mennerton who otherwise would have stayed in hotels.
Fact 4: Over the same period of time, rates charged by hotels have not changed.
The correct answer will be one that is fully supported by what the passage says, even if it would not be considered the main conclusion of the passage. Given the facts stated in the passage, we know:
how demand for hotel rooms in Mennerton affects rates: when demand decreases, rates do too.
that the availability of places for travelers to stay in Mennerton has increased.
that people in Mennerton have been increasing renting out apartments for short stays.
what has happened with rates charged by hotels: no change.
Step 4 – Eliminate “Sometimes True,” “Could Be True,” and “Definitely Not True” Options
(A) What this choice says sounds as if it could be a main conclusion supported by what the passage says. The fact that the rates charged by hotels did not decrease even though the availability of places for travelers to stay in Mennerton has increased since people were renting out their apartments for short stays could be taken as evidence the hotel operators’ fears related to people’s renting out their apartments were unfounded. However, it does not have to be the case that hotel operators’ fears were unfounded. For one thing, the fact that rates have not changed over this time period does not necessarily indicate that the hotel business has not been hurt. For all we know, if people hadn’t been renting out their apartments for short stays over that time period, hotel rates would have increased substantially. Thus, while what this choice says could be true, it does not have to be true.
We can eliminate this choice.
(B) All we know from what the passage says is that people some people who would have stayed in hotels stayed in people’s apartments instead. That they did so does not mean that the number of empty hotel rooms increased. For all we know, there were many empty hotel rooms during the year four years ago, and, since then, hotels have seen an increase in business, even though people have been renting out their apartments to travelers.
We can eliminate this choice.
(C) Given the fact that hotel rates in Mennerton always decrease when demand for hotel rooms decreases, we can conclude that, even though the availability of places for travelers to stay in Mennerton has increased since people are renting out their apartments to travelers for short stays, demand for hotel rooms in Mennerton has not decreased. If demand for hotel rooms has not decreased even though people with apartments are taking business from hotels, it seems to make sense that overall traveler demand for lodging in Mennerton must have increased.
This choice could be our answer.
(D) While what this choice says seems plausible. If hotel rates have not changed even though, by renting out their apartments, people are providing lodging similar to that provided by hotels, it might make sense to conclude that the people who are renting out their apartments must be charging rates similar to those charged by hotels.
Keep for now.
(E) Given what the passage says about people’s staying in private apartments and hotel rates’ remaining at the same level, what this choice says seems to make sense. However, since the passage does not provide information that clearly supports any conclusion about the number of hotels in Mennerton, this choice cannot be the correct answer.
We can eliminate this choice.
Step 5 – Select the Choice that MUST be True
Although choice (D) seems to make sense, it does not have to be true that people renting out their apartments are charging rates similar to those charged by hotels. For all we know, people would rather stay in apartments than in hotels, and so people renting out apartments charge rates higher than those charged by hotels. On the other hand, it could be that people prefer to stay in hotels, and so the rates paid for apartments are lower than hotel rates.
Choice (C), on the other hand, must be true. If rates for hotel rooms have not decreased even though the availability of places for travelers to stay in Mennerton has increased since people are taking business from hotels by renting out their apartments, then overall traveler demand for lodging in Mennerton must have increased.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss common inference question trap answer types.
summary:
Lesson Content Step 1 – Carefully Read and Completely Understand the Passage
As is the case in answering any other type of Critical Reasoning question, the foundation of choosing the right answer to an Inference question is understanding the passage.
Also, remember that, when answering an Inference question, misreading a single word in the stimulus can significantly change the implications of what is said.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering an Inference question, misreading a single word in the stimulus can significantly change the implications of what is said.
title: 7.4 Inference Question Strategy

contents:
Lesson Content While we know that there is only one correct answer to each Inference question, in most Inference questions, as we have already seen, some of the incorrect answers may somehow rather convincingly seem to be correct and, thus, may tempt test takers for a variety of reasons.
In order to mentally prepare you to handle these trap answers, and thus reduce the likelihood of your falling for them, let’s go over some of the types of trap answers that can appear in Inference questions.
Let’s begin by looking at answers that could be true or is likely to be true.
summary:
Lesson Content While we know that there is only one correct answer to each Inference question, in most Inference questions, as we have already seen, some of the incorrect answers may somehow rather convincingly seem to be correct and, thus, may tempt test takers for a variety of reasons.
In order to mentally prepare you to handle these trap answers, and thus reduce the likelihood of your falling for them, let’s go over some of the types of trap answers that can appear in Inference questions.
title: 7.5 Common Inference Question Trap Answer Types
