contents:
Lesson Content Remember that an Inference question requires us to find an answer that must be true.
If we select an answer that only could be true or is only likely to be true, we are by definition selecting an incorrect answer.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering an Inference question, if we select an answer that only could be true or is only likely to be true, we are by definition selecting an incorrect answer.
For the purposes of GMAT Inference questions, there are three categories of truth: absolute truth (whatever must be true); mixed truth (whatever could be true, but might possibly be false); and un-truth (whatever is always false).
Let’s revisit our earlier example and consider into what categories of truth we can put each of five answer choices:
From the end of 2007 to the end of 2017, the number of actors working at Magic Movie Studios increased by 20 percent. Over the same period, the number of set designers working at Magic Movie Studios decreased by 10 percent. Nevertheless, over the same period, the total number of people working at the studios remained unchanged.
Which of the following can be properly inferred from the statements above?
(A) The ratio of set designers to actors at Magic Movie Studios was greater at the end of 2017 than it was at the end of 2007.
What choice (A) says is clearly false.
(B) From the end of 2017 to the end of 2027, the number of actors working at Magic Movie Studios will increase by 20 percent.
While what choice (B) says does not conflict with what the passage says, it is almost completely unsubstantiated, as the passage gives us no reason to believe that the increase in actors that occurred from 2007 to 2017 will be repeated from 2017 to 2027.
(C) At the end of 2017, there were more actors than set designers working at Magic Movie Studios.
Notice the difference between choice (C), which we saw earlier, and choices (A) and (B).
(D) In 2017 Magic Movie Studios needed fewer set designers than it needed in 2007.
Choice (D) is even more dangerous than choice (C), as the fact that fewer set designers were working at the studios at the end of 2017 than were working there at the end of 2007 could be taken as a reasonably clear indication that fewer set designers were needed at the studios in 2017 than were needed in 2007.
(E) From the end of 2007 to the end of 2017, the ratio of actors to set designers at Magic Movie Studios increased.
(E) is our correct answer.
When we are answering Inference questions, the type of solid support that there is for choice (E) is the type of support to look for in determining which answer is correct.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Inference questions, answers that only could be true or are only likely to be true are incorrect; an inference must be true 100 percent of the time. When seeking out the correct inference, look for an answer choice that is fully supported by the information presented in the stimulus.
In going through the following example, pay particular attention to why each of the incorrect answers could be true, but does not have to be true.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 5 City X has always provided a certain number of subsidized housing units in the downtown area. Given the information above, which of the following statements must be true? 1. [ ] (A) Some subsidized housing residents will have to move because the rent will not be affordable. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Residents of subsidized housing will have to take on second jobs in order to pay the additional rent. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) The increase in the supply of subsidized housing units will result in a decrease in cost of rent for such units. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) The portion of the rent for subsidized housing paid for by the tenants of such housing will increase. 5. [ ] (E) Local non-profits will make more funds available to assist low-income families in paying their rent. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy Fact 1: City X has been paying 1/2 of the rent paid for subsidized housing units.
Fact 2: City X will increase the number of subsidized housing units.
Fact 3: City X will pay only 1/3 of the rent.
What must be true?
The facts provided mention only what the city plans to do in terms of the number of subsidized housing units and the proportion of the rent for such units that the city will pay. There are many possible results of the changes that the city plans to make, results such as some residents of subsidized housing moving out of their units because of their not being able to afford to pay the rent without the city’s paying half, and some of these possible results are likely to occur. However, we are looking for a choice that describes something that must be true, not something that just may occur. We need a choice that states something clearly supported by the facts in the stimulus.
(A) Some subsidized housing residents will have to move because the rent will not be affordable. While some residents may have to move out, the passage never described the financial situations of the residents of subsidized housing. Could the residents easily afford to pay more for rent? Will the landlords lower the rents because they conclude that having occupied units with lower rents is better than having vacant units with higher rents? Since any of these alternate outcomes are quite possible, what this choice says is not necessarily true.
(B) Residents of subsidized housing will have to take on second jobs in order to pay the additional rent. This option is similar to (A). We don’t know what the residents can and cannot afford. Furthermore, maybe the residents currently don’t have jobs. In such a case they wouldn’t be taking second jobs; they may be taking first jobs.
(C) The increase in the supply of subsidized housing units will result in a decrease in cost of rent for such units. This is in fact very possible. Since the supply of such units will increase, the average cost to rent such a unit may decrease. However, we don’t know that this will definitely happen. Maybe, demand for such units is expected to increase, and so, adding more will only keep the rent levels stable. Alternatively, maybe the landlords will raise rents and seek to attract higher income renters. Again, the passage doesn’t give us information via the use of which these possibilities can be ruled out. So, what this says is not necessarily true.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The portion of the rent for subsidized housing paid for by the tenants of such housing will increase. Given that the city will pay a smaller fraction of the rent, what this choice says must be true in every case. For example, even if landlords decide to charge lower rents, the tenants will still end up paying a larger fraction of this decreased rent. In other words, regardless of what happens with rents, because the city will end up paying a smaller fraction of the rent, the tenants will pay a greater portion of the rent. Notice that this fact must be true.
(E) Local non-profits will make more funds available to assist low-income families in paying their rent. This is especially tricky because, in real life, what this choice describes has often occurred, and so, this option may easily appeal to our sensibilities. It most definitely could be true! However, there is nothing in the passage that suggests that what this choice describes will necessarily happen. Maybe these residents will just be forced to move out. Maybe some other group, such as the state or federal government, might step in and cover the portion that the city is no longer paying for. Either way, we don’t know for sure what will occur. So, what this choice says does not have to be true.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s look at another common type of trap answer: an answer that presents information from the stimulus in a distorted fashion.
summary:
Lesson Content Remember that an Inference question requires us to find an answer that must be true.
If we select an answer that only could be true or is only likely to be true, we are by definition selecting an incorrect answer.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering an Inference question, if we select an answer that only could be true or is only likely to be true, we are by definition selecting an incorrect answer.
title: 7.5.1 An Answer That Could be True or is Likely to be True

contents:
Lesson Content Unless we are paying very close attention when answering a Critical Reasoning question, it’s easy to forget and or twist some of what we read.
💡MUST KNOW:
Unless we are paying very close attention when answering a Critical Reasoning question, it’s easy to forget and or twist some of what we read. Thus, we may create a distorted picture of what we read. Such a distorted picture makes it a lot easier for a test-taker to fall for trap answers that include unsupported information.
This kind of trap answer – one that presents information from the stimulus in a distorted fashion – is attractive because it sounds very similar to information presented.
The information in the answer will be distorted just enough to make the answer incorrect.
When we go through the answers, we must carefully and directly compare them to the information in the original stimulus (rather than simply relying upon our memory).
💡MUST KNOW:
Look out for answers that present information from the original argument in a distorted fashion. It only takes a small change to render incorrect an otherwise correct answer. Remember, “almost correct” is “completely wrong.” It is incredibly easy to fall for trap answers that use language taken directly from the passage.
Here’s an inside tip: the correct answer will sometimes use different words to discuss the same ideas that are in the passage.
Let’s review the following stimulus and answer choices, which present information from the stimulus in a distorted fashion.
Limiting animal agriculture and switching from using fossil fuel to using hydroelectric power are two ways in which Vertland could reduce its national carbon emissions. Limiting animal agriculture would serve to reduce carbon emissions more than would switching to using hydroelectric power.
Which of the following must be true if all of the statements above are true?
(A) For Vertland, switching to hydroelectric power would not be a cost effective method of reducing carbon emissions.
Choice (A) distorts the information provided in the stimulus.
Let’s consider another choice.
(B) For Vertland, limiting animal agriculture is the method that would be most effective in reducing the nation’s carbon emissions. This answer distorts the information provided in the stimulus, in that it takes what the passage says about the effect of limiting animal agriculture versus the effect of switching to using hydroelectric power and distorts it into the conclusion that limiting animal agriculture is the method that would be most effective of all possible methods.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 6 When soil is low in calcium, crops can have difficulty growing in it. Which of the following is information provided by the statements above? 1. [x] (A) A farmer who adds too much lime to a calcium deficient field may initially see improvements in crop growth and then see declines in crop growth. 2. [ ] (B) Crop growth in soil in which calcium levels are too high will generally be stronger than crop growth in soil in which calcium levels are too low. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Foods grown in soil with relatively high calcium levels are more nutritious than foods grown in soil with relatively low calcium levels. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Three years after lime has been added to the soil in a field, it becomes necessary to reapply lime to maintain calcium levels sufficient for strong crop growth. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Of all the nutrients that may be present in soil, calcium is the nutrient that is most important for strong crop growth. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium The passage provides four related pieces of information.
Fact 1: When soil is low in calcium, crops can have difficulty growing in it.
Fact 2: To remedy a soil calcium deficiency, a farmer can add lime to the soil.
Fact 3: Once lime has been applied, soil calcium levels increase gradually.
Fact 4: Overly high soil calcium levels also can inhibit crop growth.
In order to get this question right, we have to be careful to stick to the facts and not to get sucked in by trap answers that present conclusions that are arrived at by distorting or going beyond what the passage says.
YOUR CHOICE(A) A farmer who adds too much lime to a calcium deficient field may initially see improvements in crop growth and then see declines in crop growth. Since, once lime has been applied, soil calcium levels increase gradually, and since overly high soil calcium levels can inhibit crop growth, a farmer who adds too much lime to a calcium deficient field may initially see improvements in crop growth as the calcium levels begin to increase and then see declines in crop growth as the calcium levels continue to increase to levels higher than are optimal for crop growth. Thus, this choice follows exactly from what the passage says.
(B) Crop growth in soil in which calcium levels are too high will generally be stronger than crop growth in soil in which calcium levels are too low. This choice distorts what the passage says, as, while the passage does say that an increase in calcium levels can help crops to grow, it does not follow from that fact that overly high calcium levels are better for crop growth than are calcium levels that are too low.
(C) Foods grown in soil with relatively high calcium levels are more nutritious than foods grown in soil with relatively low calcium levels. This choice could be tempting, because what it says is plausible. However, the passage discusses only the effects of soil calcium on crop growth and says nothing that we can use to arrive at a firm conclusion about the effects of soil calcium values on the nutritional value of crops grown in the soil.
(D) Three years after lime has been added to the soil in a field, it becomes necessary to reapply lime to maintain calcium levels sufficient for strong crop growth. This choice expresses a conclusion arrived at by distorting a fact presented in the stimulus. While the stimulus does say that, after the application of lime, the full effects of adding the lime can take three years to appear, it does not indicate that, once those effects appear, lime has to be added to the soil to maintain calcium levels sufficient for strong crop growth. It could be that, once the effects appear, the calcium levels will remain optimal for years.
(E) Of all the nutrients that may be present in soil, calcium is the nutrient that is most important for strong crop growth. What this choice says goes beyond what the passage says. While the facts in the passage do indicate that calcium is important for crop growth, they don’t provide information that we can use to conclude that calcium is the nutrient most important for crop growth.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s take a look at another common type of trap answer: an answer that is out of proportion to the information presented in the stimulus.
summary:
Lesson Content Unless we are paying very close attention when answering a Critical Reasoning question, it’s easy to forget and or twist some of what we read.
💡MUST KNOW:
Unless we are paying very close attention when answering a Critical Reasoning question, it’s easy to forget and or twist some of what we read. Thus, we may create a distorted picture of what we read. Such a distorted picture makes it a lot easier for a test-taker to fall for trap answers that include unsupported information.
title: 7.5.2 An Answer that Presents Information from the Stimulus in a Distorted Fashion

contents:
Lesson Content An “out of proportion” answer is one that exaggerates information presented in the stimulus.
For example, if a passage were to suggest that “some people in Group A like basketball,” while an answer choice implies that “all people in Group A are basketball fans,” the choice would be out of proportion and incorrect.
Let’s say there are ten people in Group A, then “some” could be three people, four people, or even ten people.
We must be very careful to not select answers that are out of proportion with the information presented in the stimulus.
💡MUST KNOW:
We must be very careful to not select answers that are out of proportion with the information presented in the stimulus.
Some key words that frequently go together are:
some/many/most/all never/seldom/rarely/sometimes/often good/better/best bad/worse/worst In the following sentences, consider how the information presented in the stimulus is different from the out of proportion answer.
Stimulus: Some of the senators who attended the meeting had salad for lunch.
Out of Proportion: Most of the senators who attended the meeting had salad for lunch.
Notice that the words “some” and “most” mean very different things.
💡MUST KNOW:
“Some” means “one or more,” while “most” means more than half.
Stimulus: After the passage of the Stokes-Miller act, that there will be new tariffs on goods from China is not guaranteed. Out of Proportion: After the passage of the Stokes-Miller act, that there will be new tariffs on goods from China is unlikely.
Similarly, notice that the words “not guaranteed” and “unlikely” have very different meanings.
💡MUST KNOW:
“Not guaranteed” means “a probability of less than 100 percent,” while “unlikely” means “a probability of less than 50 percent.”
Stimulus: A three percent decrease in the sales tax would be sufficient to boost revenue by twenty percent.
Out of Proportion: A three percent decrease in the sales tax is necessary to boost revenue by twenty percent.
It is easy to confuse “sufficient” with “necessary,” even though these two words have quite different meanings.
💡MUST KNOW:
Remember, “sufficient” means “enough,” while “necessary” means “required.”
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 7 Scientists studying populations of monarch butterflies and domestic honeybees between 1976 and 2014 noticed that a decline in the population of butterflies was always followed by a decline in the population of honeybees within two weeks. Which of the following conclusions can be properly drawn from information above? 1. [ ] (A) A decrease in the population of butterflies will always be followed by a decrease in the population of honeybees. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Factors that cause declines in the honeybee population do not necessarily cause declines in the butterfly population. 3. [ ] (C) The size of the honeybee population is more closely connected to the size of the monarch butterfly population than to the size of the population of any other insect. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) If monarch butterflies become extinct, domestic honeybees are likely to become extinct as well. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) The size of the honeybee population does not impact the size of the butterfly population. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium Fact 1: Butterfly population decline was always followed by honeybee decline.
Fact 2: Honeybee population decline was not always followed by butterfly decline.
What must be true?
Whenever the population of butterflies declined, the honeybee population also declined. Therefore, it is likely that there is some kind of relationship between the two species. Either the factor – such as weather, pollution, or disease - that caused the butterfly population declines also caused the honeybee population declines, or the butterfly population declines caused the honeybee population declines.
However, since the converse did not hold true (honeybee population declines were not always followed by butterfly population declines) we can’t merely assume that, for example, pollution kills both butterflies and honeybees or that weather kills both butterflies and honeybees. The information in the passage suggests that the order matters; that is, if the honeybee population declines first, then the butterfly population will not always decline.
YOUR CHOICE(A) A decrease in the population of butterflies will always be followed by a decrease in the population of honeybees. While what this choice says might seem reasonable given the fact that, in the past, declines in the butterfly population were always been followed by declines in the honeybee population, the truth is that, since the passage does not provide information on why the pattern manifested in the past, we don’t have a clear reason to believe that the pattern will hold in the future. In other words, since the passage mentions only a past pattern, while this choice is about the future, this choice is out of proportion with the information that is presented in the passage.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) Factors that cause declines in the honeybee population do not necessarily cause declines in the butterfly population. This choice is fully supported by the information presented in the passage. Since honeybee population declines are not always followed by butterfly population declines, whatever causes honeybee population declines doesn’t necessarily cause butterfly population declines.
(C) The size of the honeybee population is more closely connected to the size of the monarch butterfly population than to the size of the population of any other insect. This choice goes beyond what’s supported by the passage. While the passage indicates that the size of the honeybee population is somehow related to the size of the monarch butterfly population, the passage provides no basis for comparing the closeness of that connection with the closeness of the connection between the size of the honeybee population and the size of the population of any other insect. For all we know from what the passage says, there is, for instance, some other insect the size of the population of which both always increases and always decreases with the size of the honeybee population.
(D) If monarch butterflies become extinct, domestic honeybees are likely to become extinct as well. This choice is out of proportion with what the passage says. The passage says merely that a decline in the population of butterflies was always followed by a decline in the population of honeybees. It does not say that a decline in the butterfly population always will be or even always was followed by a decline of the same magnitude in the honeybee population. So, it could be that, when the monarch butterfly population declines a lot, the honeybee population also declines, but not as much. Alternatively, it could be that, in the future, butterfly population declines will not be followed by honeybee population declines. So, given what the passage says, it could be that, even if the butterflies were to die out completely, the honeybee population would decline by only a small percentage or not at all.
(E) The size of the honeybee population does not impact the size of the butterfly population. This answer uses the words “honeybee population” and “butterfly population” to distract us. It is also quite tempting because it seems to recognize that the size of honeybee population is more dependent upon that of the butterfly population than the size of the butterfly population is upon that of the honeybee population. However, the fact that declines in the honeybee population are not always followed by declines in the butterfly population does not mean that the size of honeybee population “does not impact” the size of the butterfly population. The size of the honeybee population could somehow affect the size of the butterfly population without there being a clear, constant pattern to the interaction between the two. So, we can’t say that there is no impact. What this choice says does not have to be true.
Correct Answer: (B) Changes in Tense Can Make an Answer Out of Proportion to the Stimulus
Pay close attention to changes in tense, which will often make the answer out of proportion to the information in the original paragraph.
Stimulus: There has never been a successful invasion of Country X.
Out of Proportion: There will never be a successful invasion of Country X.
Notice the change in verb tense.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering Inference questions, pay close attention to changes in tense, which will often make the answer out of proportion to the information in the original paragraph.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 8 In the United States, a customer has to pay sales tax to a state only when goods purchased are purchased from a retailer with a physical presence in the state in which the customer is located. If the above statements are true, which of the following must also be true? 1. [ ] (A) States received more revenue in the form of sales taxes before the advent of online retailing. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Most purchases made online do not involve a customer’s having to pay sales tax to a state. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) It is possible that an online retailer located in the United States would never have to charge sales tax on any purchase made from it. 4. [ ] (D) As the number of purchases made online increases, states will see decreases in sales tax revenue. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Customers usually make online purchases from retailers without physical locations in the states in which the customers are located. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard Effects of the Statements in the Passage:
Effect of the First Statement: Defines when a customer has to pay sales tax – only when goods purchased are purchased from a retailer with a physical presence in the state in which the customer is located.
Effect of the Second Statement: Indicates that many purchases involve a customer in one state purchasing from a retailer in another state.
Effect of the Third Statement: Describes the results of customers’ making purchases from retailers in other states – in such cases, customers do not pay sales tax.
The correct answer to this question will follow directly from the information provided in these three statements. To avoid choosing an incorrect choice, we have to be careful not to choose a choice that says something out of proportion with what is said in the statements. For instance, a choice that said that most customers making purchases online do not pay sales tax would be out of proportion with the statement made in the passage that says that “in many cases, customers do not pay sales tax.”
YOUR CHOICE(A) States received more revenue in the form of sales taxes before the advent of online retailing. This choice could be tempting, as, if we were to assume that online retailing involving customers and retailers in different states has taken business away from physical store retailing, then, given what the passage says about how sales taxes work, we might conclude that states received more revenue in the form of sales taxes before the advent of online retailing. However that assumption about online retailing taking business away from physical store retailing is not supported by the information in the passage. Thus, fully supporting this answer choice requires using information from outside the passage.
(B) Most purchases made online do not involve a customer’s having to pay sales tax to a state. What this choice says is out of proportion with what the passage says. The passage says that, “in many cases, customers do not pay sales tax on online purchases.” “Many” simply means “a great number.” A great number is not necessarily “most,” i.e., “more than half.” So, what this choice says about “Most purchases made online” is not supported by the passage.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) It is possible that an online retailer located in the United States would never have to charge sales tax on any purchase made from it. The passage says that a customer has to pay sales tax to a state only when goods purchased are purchased from a retailer with a physical presence in the state in which the customer is located. Since it is possible that all of an online retailer’s customers would be located in states other than any state in which the retailer has a physical location, it is possible that the retailer would never have to charge sales tax on any purchase made from it.
(D) As the number of purchases made online increases, states will see decreases in sales tax revenue. This choice could be tempting, as, if one were to assume that online retailing is taking business away from physical store retailing, one might feel that it would make sense to infer from what the passage says that, as more purchases are made online, state sales tax revenue will decrease. However, the idea that online retailing is taking business away from physical store retailing is not supported by anything said in the passage. So, arriving at the conclusion stated in this choice would require adding information from outside the passage to the scenario.
(E) Customers usually make online purchases from retailers without physical locations in the states in which the customers are located. What this choice says is out of proportion with what the passage says. While the passage says that “many online purchases are made by customers in one state from retailers with physical locations only in other states,” “many online purchases” could be a small percentage of all online purchases. So, the fact that many online purchases involve customers’ purchasing from retailers in states other than those in which the customers are located does not necessarily mean that customers usually make online purchases from retailers without physical locations in the states in which the customers are located.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss an answer that seems to be supported by the stimulus but actually depends on new information.
summary:
Lesson Content An “out of proportion” answer is one that exaggerates information presented in the stimulus.
For example, if a passage were to suggest that “some people in Group A like basketball,” while an answer choice implies that “all people in Group A are basketball fans,” the choice would be out of proportion and incorrect.
Let’s say there are ten people in Group A, then “some” could be three people, four people, or even ten people.
title: 7.5.3 An Answer that is out of Proportion to the Information that is Presented in the Stimulus

contents:
Lesson Content In answering Inference questions, we must be extremely careful with answer choices that bring in new information.
💡MUST KNOW:
In an Inference question, an answer choice that depends on support from information beyond what’s presented in the stimulus can’t be correct.
Consider the following example:
While surgery to treat cataracts restores vision clarity quickly, it is somewhat expensive. At the same time, there are relatively inexpensive holistic methods that some people who experience cataracts could use to clear their vision within a reasonable timeframe, just as effectively though not as quickly as surgery does. Nevertheless, even people experiencing cataracts who could clear their vision within a reasonable timeframe via use of holistic methods generally opt for surgery instead.
If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred on the basis of them?
(Trap Choice) People are more concerned about the time involved in dealing with cataracts than the expense involved.
(Correct Answer) More money is spent in the process of solving problems involving cataracts than necessarily has to be spent to solve them.
Notice, the trap choice and the correct answer are both related to the discussion in the passage, and both seem to be supported by the statements.
The trap choice, however, takes a step beyond what is fully supported when it makes an assertion about people’s concerns, because the passage defines only what people choose but not what motivates them.
The correct answer, on the other hand, is completely supported by the facts presented in the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
Watch out for answers that depend on new information not supported by what’s said in the stimuli of Inference questions.
Let’s look at an example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 9 Even as Yrstania’s population has increased, the number of people who use public libraries in Yrstania has been declining steadily since the 1950s. Which of the following can be properly inferred from the statements above? 1. [ ] (A) For young people in Yrstania, accessing information through home computers and mobile phones has to a large degree replaced library use. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) The money that Yrstania spends on public libraries would be better spent on infrastructure improvements. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) In Yrstania, since the 1950’s, public library use by people under age 25 has declined more than has public library use by people over age 65. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) People over 65 years of age prefer reading printed materials over using electronic media. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) In the 1950s, the percentage of people living in Yrstania who used public libraries was greater than the percentage who do so today. View Solution Question Level: medium Fact 1: Yrstania’s population has increased.
Fact 2: The number of people who use public libraries in Yrstania has been declining.
Fact 3: In Yrstania, the people over age 65 are those most likely to use public libraries.
Fact 4: In Yrstania, the people under age 25 are those least likely to use public libraries.
The key to getting the right answer to this question is to stick with only the facts provided in the stimulus. The stimulus has provided us with only numerical facts about library use. The passage does not go beyond this numerical information to provide information such as why library use has been declining, how much it has been declining for each age category, or why younger people are less likely to use the library than older people are. So, to get this question right, we have to be careful not to get tempted into picking an answer choice that requires the support of information other than the specific information that the passage provides.
(A) For young people in Yrstania, accessing information through home computers and mobile phones has to a large degree replaced library use. This option is not supported by the passage. It introduces two new concepts: home computers and mobile phones. Did we see anything about home computers and mobile phones in the passage? Not a thing – right? This wrong answer is seductive because it might seem to be a reasonable explanation for why younger people use libraries the least. However, nothing in the passage supports this explanation. Remember, we cannot bring outside information into Inference questions.
(B) The money that Yrstania spends on public libraries would be better spent on infrastructure improvements. This answer introduces two new concepts: money and infrastructure. The passage never discusses how governments make decisions about how to spend money or whether infrastructure would be a better use of money than libraries. Therefore, this answer also goes beyond what is supported by the passage. Notice that this answer employs a common GMAT trick: comparing two things in a way that the passage never does. How do we know which use of money is “better” or “worse?”
(C) In Yrstania, since the 1950’s, public library use by people under age 25 has declined more than has public library use by people over age 65. This answer introduces a new concept: how much public library use has changed for each age category. While what this choice says may seem to be follow from what the passage says about library use by people over age 65 and people under age 25, the truth is that, even though people under age 25 are currently those who use public libraries the least, it does not have to be the case that library use by that group has declined more than has library use by those over age 65. It could be, for instance, that those under age 25 rarely used public libraries even in the 1950’s, perhaps because most of these people had access to school libraries, and that now, for some reason, people under age 25 use public libraries more than they did back then, even though they don’t use them much now either. So, for all we know from what the passage says, use of public libraries by people over age 65 decreased, while use of public libraries by people under age 25 increased.
(D) People over 65 years of age prefer reading printed materials over using electronic media. This choice introduces an idea not addressed in the passage, the preferences of people over 65 years of age. While what this choice says may be true, the passage gives us no basis for determining whether people over 65 years of age prefer reading printed material or using electronic media.
YOUR CHOICE(E) In the 1950s, the percentage of people living in Yrstania who used public libraries was greater than the percentage who do so today. If today the population today is higher than it was in the 1950s, but a smaller number people use the library today, then the percentage of people who used the library in the 1950s must have been higher than it is today. That is, today we have a smaller number of library-using people divided by a larger total population, which is clearly a smaller percentage than the original.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss answers that are true in the real world but are not supported by the information provided in the stimulus.
summary:
Lesson Content In answering Inference questions, we must be extremely careful with answer choices that bring in new information.
💡MUST KNOW:
In an Inference question, an answer choice that depends on support from information beyond what’s presented in the stimulus can’t be correct.
Consider the following example:
While surgery to treat cataracts restores vision clarity quickly, it is somewhat expensive. At the same time, there are relatively inexpensive holistic methods that some people who experience cataracts could use to clear their vision within a reasonable timeframe, just as effectively though not as quickly as surgery does.
title: 7.5.4 An Answer That Seems to Be Supported by the Stimulus but Actually Depends on Other Information

contents:
Lesson Content In reading Inference questions, you may encounter incorrect answer choices that say things that are true in the real world but are not completely supported by the information presented to us in the stimulus.
Consider the following example:
The cost of solar panels that can be used by homeowners to generate electricity has been decreasing. Over the past seven years, the cost per watt of power generation capacity has decreased by 50 percent, and this trend is expected to continue.
Which of the following must be true if the statements above are true?
(Trap Choice That is True in the Real World) As a result of lower panel costs, installing a solar generating system now makes sense financially for many households.
(Correct Answer) With a certain amount of money, a homeowner can purchase twice the solar power generating capacity that that homeowner could have purchased seven years ago with that amount of money.
The trap choice could be tempting, because, not only is what the trap choice says plausible given what the passage says, but also, what it says is true in the real world.
What the correct answer says, on the other hand, is clearly true.
💡MUST KNOW:
Inference questions may include incorrect answer choices that say things that are true in the real world but are not supported by the statements in the stimuli. Be careful not to select these answer choices.
Now, for some practice, here are some examples that include answer choices that are true in the real world but are not supported by the stimulus.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 10 Recently, the Fitness Factory, a health club, for the first time began promoting its memberships to professionals who work in the high-rise buildings on the city block on which it is located. If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred from them? 1. [ ] (A) When professionals who work in high-rise buildings join health clubs, they use considerably more complimentary towels, soaps, and lotions than professionals who do not work in high-rise buildings use. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) The Fitness Factory’s expenses have increased since it began promoting its memberships to professionals who work in the high-rise buildings on the city block on which it is located. 3. [ ] (C) Much of the new revenue generated via the Fitness Factory’s promoting its memberships to professionals who work in the high-rise buildings has come from selling personal training packages. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Recently, the management of the Fitness Factory has made a number of bad decisions, which have led to the sharp decline in its profits. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Once it began promoting its memberships to professionals who work in the high-rise buildings on the block on which it is located, the Fitness Factory stopped all its other marketing. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy (A) When professionals who work in high-rise buildings join health clubs, they use considerably more complimentary towels, soaps, and lotions than professionals who do not work in high-rise buildings use. What this answer choice says sounds very plausible. After all, soaps, lotions, and towels are available at most high-end fitness clubs today, and these amenities might not be cheap.
However, nothing in the passage would allow us to infer what this answer choice says. There could be thousands of reasons why the Fitness Factory saw its profits decline sharply. For example, perhaps the rent that the Fitness Factory pays doubled recently.
When solving inference questions, we must resist the urge to choose as correct a choice that matches what goes on in the real world yet is not an inference based only on what the passage says.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The Fitness Factory’s expenses have increased since it began promoting its memberships to professionals who work in the high-rise buildings on the city block on which it is located. The passage tells us that the Fitness Factory has seen its sales revenue increase significantly, but its profits have declined sharply.
The following is common knowledge:
Profit = Revenue – Expenses
We can see from that equation that, if revenue went up but profit went down, the Fitness Factory’s expenses must have increased.
What this choice says must be true given what the passage says.
(C) Much of the new revenue generated via the Fitness Factory’s promoting its memberships to professionals who work in the high-rise buildings has come from selling personal training packages. What this answer choice says sounds very plausible. After all, it could very well be that, in reality, fitness clubs generate a lot of revenue by selling personal training to their clients.
However, nothing in the passage would allow us to infer what this answer choice says. We are not provided with any information about how the new revenue has been generated.
When solving inference questions, we must resist the urge to choose as correct a choice that fits what goes on in real world yet is not an inference based only on what the passage says.
(D) Recently, the management of the Fitness Factory has made a number of bad decisions, which have led to the sharp decline in its profits. What this answer choice says sounds very plausible. After all, the bad decisions of a company’s management often lead to sharp declines in the company’s profitability; we see such phenomena on a regular basis in the business world.
However, the passage does not fully support what this answer choice says. We are not provided with any information that clearly indicates that the company’s management has made bad decisions.
There could be thousands of reasons why the Fitness Factory saw its profits decline sharply. For example, perhaps the insurance that the Fitness Factory pays doubled in price recently.
When solving inference questions, we must resist the urge to choose as correct an answer choice that fits what goes on in the real world yet is not an inference based only on the passage.
(E) Once it began promoting its memberships to professionals who work in the high-rise buildings on the block on which it is located, the Fitness Factory stopped all its other marketing. Nothing at all in the passage would lead us to what this answer choice says. All we know is that the Fitness Factory, a health club, for the first time began promoting its memberships to professionals who work in the high-rise buildings on the city block on which it is located.
We don’t know about any of the company’s other marketing efforts.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 11 A “Use by” date on a food item is meant to indicate the last day on which the food should be consumed. If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred from them? 1. [ ] (A) In many cases, food marked with a “Use by” date is still good for consumption for a period of time after that date. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) On food items marked with both “Sell by” and “Use by” dates, the “Sell by” dates always precede the “Use by” dates. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) People generally pay more attention to “Use by” dates on food than to “Sell by” dates on food. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Sometimes, marking food with “Sell by” dates results in food going to waste. 5. [ ] (E) When people are shopping, they often use “Sell by” dates as indicators of the freshness of food items that they are considering purchasing. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: hard Fact 1: A “Use by” date indicates the last day on which a food item should be consumed.
Fact 2: A “Sell by” date indicates the last day on which a food item should be sold.
Fact 3: Not understanding the distinction, people often discard food that they would otherwise have consumed, because the “Sell by” date has been passed.
The key to getting the right answer to this question is to stick with only the facts provided in the stimulus and avoid choosing trap choices that are true in the real world but not supported by the stimulus.
The stimulus provides details on meanings of “Use by” and “Sell by” dates, contrasts the two types of dates, and discusses one consequence of marking food with “Sell by” dates. The correct answer will be supported by these specific facts.
(A) In many cases, food marked with a “Use by” date is still good for consumption for a period of time after that date. This choice could be tempting because it is true in the real world. However, the passage says nothing that clearly indicates that sometimes food items are good for consumption after their “Use by” dates.
(B) On food items marked with both “Sell by” and “Use by” dates, the “Sell by” dates always precede the “Use by” dates. This choice could be tempting because the passage indicates that, in most cases, foods are expected to be good for consumption after their “Sell by” dates. In other words, in most cases, foods can be used after their “Sell by” dates, and, therefore, in most cases, “Sell by” dates would precede “Use by” dates. However, “most cases” is not all cases, and so, it’s conceivable that the last day on which a food item should be sold would be the last day on which it should be consumed, in which case it could be marked with “Sell by” and “Use by” dates that are the same date.
(C) People generally pay more attention to “Use by” dates on food than to “Sell by” dates on food. While what this choice says may be true in the real world, and, while what the passage says about “Use by” dates and “Sell by” dates could be taken as indicating that “Use by” dates are more important than “Sell by” dates, the passage does not provide any information that clearly indicates that people pay more attention to “Use by” dates than they do to “Sell by” dates.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Sometimes, marking food with “Sell by” dates results in food going to waste. The passage indicates that food is expected to be good for a period of time after its “Sell by” date. It also indicates that people who do not understand the difference between “Sell by” dates and “Use by” dates often discard food that is good for consumption when they see that the food’s “Sell by” date has passed. Of course, food that is discarded even though it is good for consumption goes to waste. Thus, from what the passage says, we can infer that, sometimes, marking food with “Sell by” dates results in food going to waste.
(E) When people are shopping, they often use “Sell by” dates as indicators of the freshness of food items that they are considering purchasing. While what this choice says is true in the real world, the passage does not tell us anything about how people use the dates other than that they often discard food upon seeing the food’s “Sell by” date. So, this choice is not supported by what the passage says.
Correct Answer: (D) Now that we’ve discussed how to recognize trap answers in Inference Questions, let’s discuss types of correct answers that appear in these questions.
summary:
Lesson Content In reading Inference questions, you may encounter incorrect answer choices that say things that are true in the real world but are not completely supported by the information presented to us in the stimulus.
Consider the following example:
The cost of solar panels that can be used by homeowners to generate electricity has been decreasing. Over the past seven years, the cost per watt of power generation capacity has decreased by 50 percent, and this trend is expected to continue.
title: 7.5.5 An Answer that is True in the Real World but is not Supported by the Stimulus

contents: Lesson Content Just as knowing how incorrect answers may work can be helpful, a solid understanding of how correct answers may look can help us to efficiently and correctly answer Inference questions.
summary: Lesson Content Just as knowing how incorrect answers may work can be helpful, a solid understanding of how correct answers may look can help us to efficiently and correctly answer Inference questions.
title: 7.6 Correct Answer Types in Inference Questions

contents:
Lesson Content The first type of Inference correct answer that we’ll discuss is an answer that is a conclusion based on the entirety of the information presented in the passage.
Often, what is conveyed by the statements in the passage in an Inference question adds up logically to a conclusion.
Consider the following example:
The presence on Mars of certain clay minerals believed to form only in water gave scientists reason to believe that life may have once existed on Mars, in the water in which the clay minerals were formed. However, further investigation revealed that the clay minerals were formed in water present in molten magma. Since molten magma is extremely hot, any water present in magma would be too hot to support life.
Which of the following can be properly concluded from the statements above?
(Trap Choice) Life has never existed on Mars.
(Correct Answer) The presence on Mars of certain clay minerals that form only in water is no reason to believe that life existed on Mars.
Notice, both the trap choice and the correct answer are connected to all the information given in the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
In some cases, the correct answers to Inference questions are supported by the entirety of what is said in the passages.
Consider the following examples:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 12 Crop rotation is the practice of growing in the same area a series of different types of crops, one type of crop one season and another type the next season. Which of the following can be properly inferred from the statements above? 1. [ ] (A) If crop rotation is not used in an area, the presence of crop damaging organisms will not decrease in that area. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Whenever organisms cause extensive damage to crops in an area for two consecutive seasons, it must be the case that crop rotation was not employed. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Crop rotation can be just as effective in reducing the presence of crop damaging organisms as using chemical treatments generally is. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Crop rotation would not be effective in reducing the presence of organisms of a type that could live equally well on any type of crop. 5. [ ] (E) Farms that practice crop rotation tend to experience greater crop yields than are achieved by farms that do not practice crop rotation. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy The passage presents us with a clear picture of what crop rotation is and how it affects organisms that may cause damage to crops.
Effect of the First Statement: Describes crop rotation as a practice involving planting different crops in the same area, one crop one season and another crop the next.
Effect of the Second Statement: Explains how crop rotation can make it difficult for crop damaging organisms to persist in an area – crop damaging organisms that live on one type of crop may not be able to live on another type, and so they die out.
Effect of the Third Statement:Presents the logical outcome of crop rotation – a reduction the presence of crop damaging organisms in a given area.
The correct answer to this question is an inference that is based on all three of the statements in the stimulus.
(A) If crop rotation is not used in an area, the presence of crop damaging organisms will not decrease in that area. While the passage indicates that crop rotation can result in a reduction in the presence of crop damaging organisms, the passage does not suggest that, without crop rotation, the presence of such organisms never decreases. So, it does not support the conclusion that, if crop rotation is not used in an area, the presence of crop damaging organisms will not decrease in that area.
(B) Whenever organisms cause extensive damage to crops in an area for two consecutive seasons, it must be the case that crop rotation was not employed. This choice could be tempting, because it seems to follow from what the passage says about the effects of crop rotation on the ability of crop damaging organisms to live in an area. However, the passage does not say that crop rotation always makes living in an area impossible for crop damaging organisms. So, for all we know from what the passage says, sometimes crop damaging organisms can persist in an area and damage crops even if crop rotation is practiced.
(C) Crop rotation can be just as effective in reducing the presence of crop damaging organisms as using chemical treatments generally is. While what this choice says seems likely to be true, this choice goes beyond what the passage supports. The facts in the passage indicate that crop rotation has certain effects on crop damaging organisms. It does not give us information that we can use to definitively compare those effects with the effects of chemical treatments.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) Crop rotation would not be effective in reducing the presence of organisms of a type that could live equally well on any type of crop. The passage indicates that the reason that crop rotation makes persisting in an area difficult for crop damaging organisms is that, often, organisms that can live on one kind of crop are not able to live on another type of crop. So, it follows that a kind of organism that could live on any type of crop would be able to continue living in an area even if crop rotation were practiced in that area. Thus, crop rotation would not be effective in reducing the presence of such an organism.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Farms that practice crop rotation tend to experience greater crop yields than are achieved by farms that do not practice crop rotation. The passage indicates only that crop rotation has the effect of reducing the presence of crop damaging organisms. While it seems likely that, given the effects of crop rotation, farms that practice crop rotation tend to experience greater crop yields than are achieved by farms that do not practice crop rotation, the passage does not fully support this conclusion. In spite of what the passage says, it could be that some farmers do not practice crop rotation, and yet achieve similar results through the use of other methods.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 13 The degree to which smoking cigarettes is harmful to health is directly related to the amounts of damaging substances inhaled from cigarettes. Which of the following can be properly concluded from the information provided by the passage? 1. [ ] (A) Many smokers who smoke cigarettes with features designed to reduce amounts of damaging substances in inhaled smoke are not truly concerned about the harmful effects of smoking. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Cigarette companies that produce cigarettes with features designed to reduce amounts of damaging substances in inhaled smoke consciously intend to mislead via their marketing messages. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) It is difficult to design a cigarette such that any person smoking that cigarette would avoid to a large degree the ill effects of smoking. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Certain implications of marketing messages associated with cigarettes with features designed to reduce amounts of damaging substances in inhaled smoke do not hold true for all smokers. 5. [ ] (E) There is no actual difference between smoking regular cigarettes and smoking cigarettes with features designed to reduce amounts of damaging substances in inhaled smoke. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium The passage includes information:
that relates the harmful effects of smoking to the damaging substances inhaled from cigarettes.
indicating that cigarette companies produce cigarettes with features designed to reduce the amounts of such damaging substances present in inhaled smoke.
indicating that cigarette company marketing messages imply that people smoking such cigarettes will largely avoid the ill effects of smoking.
indicating that people who smoke such cigarettes, by inhaling more smoke, defeat the features.
By combining the information provided by the statements in the passage, one can arrive at a variety of conclusions. However, not all of them will have to be true given what the passage says.
(A) Many smokers who smoke cigarettes with features designed to reduce amounts of damaging substances in inhaled smoke are not truly concerned about the harmful effects of smoking. While the passage says that smokers who smoke cigarettes with features designed to reduce amounts of damaging substances in inhaled smoke defeat the features by inhaling more smoke, the passage does not clearly indicate anything about the concerns of such smokers with regard to the harmful effects of smoking. It could be that, even though they defeat the features, they are concerned about the harmful effects of smoking.
(B) Cigarette companies that produce cigarettes with features designed to reduce amounts of damaging substances in inhaled smoke consciously intend to mislead via their marketing messages. This answer could be tempting, because it seems plausible that cigarette companies intentionally mislead via their marketing messages associated with cigarettes with the features, given that many smokers defeat the features and so, in a way, the features don’t really make a difference. However, we are not looking for a statement that is merely plausible, but rather one that must be true, and, while the messages do seem to be misleading, nothing in the passage clearly indicates that the companies consciously intend to mislead.
(C) It is difficult to design a cigarette such that any person smoking that cigarette would avoid to a large degree the ill effects of smoking. This choice could be tempting, given that the passage indicates that features apparently designed to make smoking less harmful have been defeated by some people smoking the cigarettes. However, the fact that those particular features have been defeated does not necessarily mean that it is difficult to design cigarettes such that any person smoking them would largely avoid the ill effects of smoking.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Certain implications of marketing messages associated with cigarettes with features designed to reduce amounts of damaging substances in inhaled smoke do not hold true for all smokers. One might be tempted to eliminate this choice because the way in which it is written obscures its connection to what is said in the passage. All the same, it is connected in the following way.
The passage says that the degree to which smoking is harmful is related to the amounts of damaging substances inhaled while smoking. The passage indicates that many people who smoke cigarettes with the features defeat the features by inhaling more smoke. We can conclude that, therefore, when smoking the cigarettes with the features, those people do not avoid the ill effects of smoking cigarettes.
If many people do not avoid the ill effects, then not all people avoid the ill effects. Therefore, the marketing messages’ implications that, by choosing the cigarettes with the features, one will avoid the ill effects of smoking do not hold true for all people.
(E) There is no actual difference between smoking regular cigarettes and smoking cigarettes with features designed to reduce amounts of damaging substances in inhaled smoke. This answer is a trap, because the statements made in the passage do indicate that smoking regular cigarettes and smoking cigarettes with the features are likely similar in terms of their harmful effects, and so it’s easy to decide that there is no difference between the smoking one of the types of cigarettes and smoking the other. However, the passage does not say that the two are the same in all ways, and, further, the passage tells us ways in which they are different. The smoke from one is different from the smoke from the other, and people inhale differently when smoking one type from how they would inhale if smoking the other type.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 14 When people are doing work that requires intense focus or involves learning new things, background music without lyrics may enhance their productivity, while background music with lyrics generally distracts them and thus negatively affects productivity. Which of the following can be properly inferred from the statements above? 1. [ ] (A) When most of the people in a workspace are doing repetitive work, background music without lyrics will enhance the total productivity of the people in the workspace. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) The same background music that enhances the productivity of some people may cause a reduction in the productivity of others. 3. [ ] (C) Background music without lyrics enhances the productivity of people regardless of the type of work they are doing. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Music with lyrics enhances the productivity of people doing repetitive tasks more than music without lyrics does. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Managers interested in maximizing company productivity should be focused on the type of background music played in company workspaces. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard Fact 1: Background music without lyrics may enhance the productivity of people doing work that requires intense focus or involves learning new things.
Fact 2: Background music with lyrics generally distracts and thus negatively affects the productivity of people doing work that requires intense focus or involves learning new things.
Fact 3: When people are doing repetitive work, background music with or without lyrics may enhance productivity.
The facts presented involve two kinds of background music and the effects of background music on the productivity of people doing two types of work.
The correct answer to this question, while not a summary of the passage, is based on all of the facts combined.
(A) When most of the people in a workspace are doing repetitive work, background music without lyrics will enhance the total productivity of the people in the workspace. This answer could be tempting, because the passage indicates that music without lyrics may enhance the productivity of people doing repetitive work, and may even enhance the productivity of people doing work that requires intense focus or involves learning new things. However, this choice is out of proportion with what the passage says, as this choice says “will enhance,” while the passage says “may enhance.” Since saying that something “may enhance” productivity does not mean that that thing definitely “will enhance” productivity, what this choice says is not fully supported by what the passage says.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The same background music that enhances the productivity of some people may cause a reduction in the productivity of others. The passage says that music with lyrics may enhance the productivity of people doing repetitive work and that music with lyrics generally distracts people doing work requiring intense focus. Thus, one can infer from the facts in the passage that a type of music, music with lyrics, that enhances the productivity of some people may cause a reduction in the productivity of others.
(C) Background music without lyrics enhances the productivity of people regardless of the type of work they are doing. This choice could be tempting, because the passage indicates that background music without lyrics may enhance the productivity of both people doing work that requires intense focus and people doing repetitive work. However, in saying that background music without lyrics enhances the productivity of people regardless of the type of work they are doing, this choice goes too far. What if people were working on creating music? Would music playing while they were working cause them to be more productive? Probably not.
(D) Music with lyrics enhances the productivity of people doing repetitive tasks more than music without lyrics does. While one might be tempted to believe that what this choice says is true, the passage does not provide information that we can use to differentiate the effect of music with lyrics from that of music without lyrics.
(E) Managers interested in maximizing company productivity should be focused on the type of background music played in company workspaces. This choice goes well beyond anything said in the passage. The passage does not provide information that we can use to compare the importance to productivity of background music with the importance of any other factor that could affect productivity. So, it provides no basis for concluding that a manager interested in maximizing productivity should be focused on the type of background music played in company workspaces, rather than on something else.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now look at correct answers that are fully supported by small portions of the passages.
summary:
Lesson Content The first type of Inference correct answer that we’ll discuss is an answer that is a conclusion based on the entirety of the information presented in the passage.
Often, what is conveyed by the statements in the passage in an Inference question adds up logically to a conclusion.
Consider the following example:
The presence on Mars of certain clay minerals believed to form only in water gave scientists reason to believe that life may have once existed on Mars, in the water in which the clay minerals were formed.
title: 7.6.1 Answers that are Conclusions Based on the Entirety of What is Said in the Passage

contents:
Lesson Content Another type of correct answer that often appears in Inference questions is an answer that is fully supported by a small portion of the stimulus.
💡MUST KNOW:
In some cases, the correct answers to Inference questions are fully supported by small portions of or even single sentences of the passages. Understand that a correct answer to an Inference question need not be supported by the entire stimulus and that an answer will be correct as long as some portion of the stimulus fully supports the answer.
Consider the following example:
Ever since the first solar power generation facility was installed in Utrandia, people have been anticipating a day when the air in the nation would be cleaner. Since the primary culprit in creating air pollution is the burning of coal, having cleaner air requires that coal fired plants be replaced by solar generation facilities. In the past year the amount of electricity generated by solar panels in Utrandia exceeded for the first time the amount generated via burning coal.
Which of the following is information provided by the passage?
(Trap Choice) The air in Utrandia is now cleaner, because most coal fired plants in the nation have been shut down and replaced with solar generation facilities.
(Correct Answer) Two years ago, in Utrandia, the amount of electricity generated via the burning of coal was greater than or equal to the amount generated by solar panels.
In this case, the support for the correct answer comes entirely from one sentence out of the three sentences in the passage.
The trap choice may appear to be a true statement supported by information synthesized from the passage in its entirety, but it isn’t really.
In order to make sure that we identify the correct answer when it is supported by only a small portion of the passage, and don’t get tricked into picking an incorrect choice, we must keep our mind open to the idea that the correct answer could be something quite different from what we expect, and we have to make sure that we don’t choose an incorrect choice just because it resembles what we expected to see.
It could be that only after we have eliminated some answer choices that we know can’t be correct will it start to become apparent to us that the correct answer can’t be one that is supported by the entirety of the passage.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 15 Many doctors simply prescribe blood pressure medication to any patient whose blood pressure exceeds a certain level and do little to help such patients learn to use natural methods to reduce their blood pressure. If the statements above are true, which of the following conclusions can be properly drawn? 1. [ ] (A) Natural methods for reducing blood pressure are as effective as blood pressure medications prescribed by doctors. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) It is more likely that people’s blood pressure will be reduced via natural methods in cases in which the people’s blood pressure is dangerously high. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) There are natural methods that are effective in reducing the blood pressure of at least some people. 4. [ ] (D) All people who have been prescribed blood pressure medication should reduce body weight, reduce sodium intake, and meditate to lower blood pressure to a safe levels. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Many doctors would not prefer that patients working with them seek to reduce blood pressure via using natural methods rather than continue to use blood pressure medication. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy A test-taker might expect the correct answer to this question to be the result of synthesizing into a single conclusion all of the information about methods for reducing blood pressure that is given in the statements in the passage. However, it is also possible that the correct answer is based entirely on information found in just one of the sentences that make up the passage.
(A) Natural methods for reducing blood pressure are as effective as blood pressure medications prescribed by doctors. While, in saying that many people have succeeded in using natural methods to reduce blood pressure, the passage indicates that natural methods can be effective, the passage does not provide information comparing the effectiveness of natural methods with that of medication. Therefore, while what this choice says may be true, it goes beyond what is supported by the passage.
(B) It is more likely that people’s blood pressure will be reduced via natural methods in cases in which the people’s blood pressure is dangerously high. This choice takes what the passage says about people with dangerously high blood pressure and distorts it. The passage uses the examples of people with dangerously high blood pressure reducing their blood pressure through the use of natural methods to make a point about the effectiveness of natural methods. However, the passage does not indicate that the reason for choosing to discuss people with dangerously high blood pressure rather than people with somewhat overly high blood pressure is that it is most likely that people’s blood pressure will be reduced via natural methods in cases in which the people’s blood pressure is dangerously high. For all we know from what the passage says, people who have dangerously high blood pressure are those least likely to succeed in reducing their blood pressure.
YOUR CHOICE(C) There are natural methods that are effective in reducing the blood pressure of at least some people. The second sentence of the passage fully supports what this answer choice says, and the other sentence does not contribute any support to what this answer choice says. If, as the second sentence says, many people have succeeded in reducing their blood pressure levels by using natural methods, then it must be true that there are natural methods that are effective in reducing the blood pressure levels of at least some people.
(D) All people who have been prescribed blood pressure medication should reduce body weight, reduce sodium intake, and meditate to lower blood pressure to a safe levels. This choice could be tempting because it includes elements from all three sentences of the passage, and, therefore, it could seem to be a conclusion fully supported by what the three sentences convey. However, although the passage mentions that taking one or more of the actions listed in this choice can result in a reduction in blood pressure, the passage does not fully support the idea that all people who have been prescribed blood pressure medication should take all of these actions. For one thing, for all we know, some people who have been prescribed blood pressure medication would be hurt by taking all of these actions, and the passage does not give us reason to believe otherwise.
(E) Many doctors would not prefer that patients working with them seek to reduce blood pressure via using natural methods rather than continue to use blood pressure medication. While the passage mentions what many doctors do, it does not say anything that clearly indicates what doctors prefer that patients do.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now look at how numbers can be used in Inference questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Another type of correct answer that often appears in Inference questions is an answer that is fully supported by a small portion of the stimulus.
💡MUST KNOW:
In some cases, the correct answers to Inference questions are fully supported by small portions of or even single sentences of the passages. Understand that a correct answer to an Inference question need not be supported by the entire stimulus and that an answer will be correct as long as some portion of the stimulus fully supports the answer.
title: 7.6.2 Answers that are Fully Supported by Small Portions of the Passages

contents:
Lesson Content A third type of correct answer that we may encounter in an Inference question is an answer that is supported by numerical information located throughout the passage.
Finding the correct answer to such a question may require understanding the implications of a ratio or deciphering the meaning of data presented in percentage terms.
Consider the following example:
Roberta’s Chocolate has always used two ounces of cocoa in making each of its chocolate bars. Recently, the company decided to decrease the size and weight of its chocolate bars. However, the company has continued to include two ounces of cocoa in each bar.
If the above statements are true, which of the following conclusions can be properly drawn from them?
(Correct Answer) The ratio of cocoa to the other ingredients in the chocolate bars made by Roberta’s Chocolate has increased.
In saying that Roberta’s Chocolate made the bars smaller but didn’t reduce the amount of cocoa per bar, the passage indicates that the amounts of other ingredients in the bars must have decreased.
Trap answers to questions containing numerical data may offer statements that may seem to follow from the numerical information presented but are not actually supported by what the passage says.
Consider the following example:
The FrootZoomer juicer company makes two types of juicers, Zoomers, on each of which the company makes a 33 percent gross margin, and SooperZoomers, on each of which the company makes a 41 percent gross margin. Last year’s average gross margin on sales of the two types of juicers was higher than the gross margin on sales of the two types of juicers for the year before last year.
Which of the following can be properly inferred about FrootZoomer’s juicer sales in the past two years?
(Trap Choice) FrootZoomer sold more SooperZoomers last year than it sold during the year before last year.
(Correct Answer) The ratio of SooperZoomers sold to Zoomers sold last year is greater than the same ratio for the year before last year.
The key to finding the correct answer to this question is seeing that, since the average gross margin increased, the company’s sales must have changed in a way such that the ratio of SooperZoomers to Zoomers increased.
The key to avoiding choosing the trap choice is catching the fact that there are multiple ways in which the ratio of SooperZoomers sold to Zoomers sold could have increased, and that, therefore, while it’s possible that the sales of SooperZoomers increased, it does not have to be the case that sales of SooperZoomers increased.
Ways in which the ratio of SooperZoomers sold to Zoomers sold could have increased include:
Scenario 1: The number of SooperZoomers sold remained the same, and the number of Zoomers sold decreased.
Scenario 2: The number of SooperZoomers sold increased, and the number of Zoomers sold remained the same.
Scenario 3: The number of SooperZoomers sold decreased, and the number of Zoomers sold also decreased, but by a percentage greater than the percentage decrease in SooperZoomer sales.
