Yes, Scenario 2 above matches what the trap choice says.
On the other hand, since, for the average gross margin to increase, the ratio of SooperZoomers sold to Zoomers sold has to increase, what the correct answer says must be true.
💡MUST KNOW:
In some cases, the correct answers to Inference questions are supported by numerical information located throughout passages.
Consider the following examples:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 16 Miracle Walnut Company sells two products – bags of walnuts in their shells, for each of which the company charges $6, and bags of walnuts unshelled, for each of which the company charges $11. Which of the following is information provided by the statements above? 1. [ ] (A) Compared with Miracle Walnut Company’s total revenue for the year two years ago, the company’s total revenue last year was greater. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Last year the average price Miracle Walnut Company received for a bag of walnuts was greater than the average price it received the year before last. 3. [ ] (C) Last year, Miracle Walnut Company’s competitors sold walnuts in their shells at prices lower than that charged by Miracle Walnut Company for such walnuts. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Most of Miracle Walnut Company’s customers prefer unshelled walnuts over walnuts in their shells. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Last year, the number of bags of walnuts sold by Miracle Walnut Company was the same as the number it sold during the year before last year. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium Analysis of the Information Given:
Effect of the First Statement: Defines that Miracle Walnut Company sells two products and provides descriptions of the products and the price of each. Products and Prices: Bags of Walnuts in Shells, $6. Bags of Walnuts Unshelled, $11.
Effect of the Second Statement: Defines only the directions of changes in the quantities of the two products sold in each of two years. Last year, fewer bags of walnuts in shells were sold than were in the year before last. Last year more bags of walnuts unshelled were sold than were in the year before last.
To arrive at the correct answer to this question, we’ll have to be careful not to draw unwarranted conclusions from the information given. We know that the quantities sold of the two products changed, and we know the directions of the changes, but we don’t know the magnitudes of the changes. Each of the changes could have been large or small.
(A) Compared with Miracle Walnut Company’s total revenue for the year two years ago, the company’s total revenue last year was greater. If one were to assume that the decrease in the number of bags of walnuts in shells sold matched the increase in the number of bags of unshelled walnuts sold, then one might believe that this statement is true. For example, if the number of bags of walnuts in shells sold decreased by 1000, then revenue decreased by $6000. If the number of bags of unshelled walnuts increased by 1000, revenue increased by $11,000. Since the increase would be greater than the decrease in this scenario, the net change would be an increase in revenue. However, since the passage does not tell us anything about the magnitudes of the decrease and the increase, it does not support this type of calculation. So, it does not support what this choice says.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Last year the average price Miracle Walnut Company received for a bag of walnuts was greater than the average price it received the year before last. No matter how many bags of walnuts in shells and bags of unshelled walnuts were sold in each of the two years, if the number of bags of walnuts in shells decreased from the first year to the second year, and the number of bags of unshelled walnuts increased, then the average price of a bag of walnuts sold by the company must have increased. To see why this increase in the average price must have occurred, you could consider the following. The first year’s average price would be calculated using a certain number of 6’s and a certain number of 11’s. Then the second year’s average would be based on fewer 6’s and more 11’s, as the number of bags sold for $6 decreased year over year, and the number of bags sold for $11 increased. So, from the first year to the second, the average price would move away from $6 and toward $11.
(C) Last year, Miracle Walnut Company’s competitors sold walnuts in their shells at prices lower than that charged by Miracle Walnut Company for such walnuts. One might take the fact that fewer bags of walnuts in shells were sold last year than were the year before as an indication that competitors sold the same product at a lower price and, thus, took some business from Miracle Walnut Company. However, the passage does not give us a clear reason to believe that this specific factor caused the decrease.
(D) Most of Miracle Walnut Company’s customers prefer unshelled walnuts over walnuts in their shells. One might take the fact that the price charged for a bag of unshelled walnuts is greater than the price charged for a bag of walnuts in their shells as an indication that customers prefer unshelled walnuts. However, it isn’t necessarily the case that customers prefer unshelled walnuts. It could be that the price for unshelled walnuts is higher because they cost more to produce, or for some other reason other than customer preference.
(E) Last year, the number of bags of walnuts sold by Miracle Walnut Company was the same as the number it sold during the year before last year. What this choice says would be true only if the decrease in the number of bags of walnuts in shells sold exactly matched the increase in the number of bags of unshelled walnuts sold. Since the passage does not include information on the magnitudes of the changes in the number of bags sold, the passage does not provide support for the conclusion that the decrease matched the increase.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 17 The number of tourists from Japan who visited Paris during the first half of 2017 was 40.5 percent greater than the number from Japan who visited Paris during the first half of 2016. Which of the following can be properly concluded from the statements above? 1. [ ] (A) The total number of tourists from Japan and the United Kingdom who visited Paris during the first half of 2017 exceeded the same total for the first half of 2016. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) During the first half of 2017 more people from Japan than from the United Kingdom visited Paris. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) The ratio of tourists from Japan who visited Paris to tourists from the United Kingdom who visited Paris was greater for the first half of 2017 than for the first half of 2016. 4. [ ] (D) Of all tourists who visited Paris, the proportion from Japan was greater in the first half of 2017 than the proportion from Japan was in the first half of 2016. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) The amount of money spent in Paris by tourists from the United Kingdom during the first half of 2017 was less than that spent by such tourists during the first half of 2016. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium Analysis of the Information Given:
Effect of the First Statement: States the percentage difference in the number of tourists from Japan visiting Paris during two time periods, first half of 2016 and first half of 2017. Direction: Increase from the first period to the second period. Numerical Value: 40.5 percent.
Effect of the Second Statement: States the percentage difference in the number of tourists from the United Kingdom visiting Paris during two time periods, first half of 2016 and first half of 2017. Direction: Decrease from the first period to the second period. Numerical Value: 1.7 percent.
To get the correct answer to this question, we have to be careful about the conclusions that we draw, as we have only information on percentages, not information on absolute numbers. So, for instance, even though the percentage change in people visiting from the United Kingdom was much smaller than the percentage change in the number from Japan, for all we know, the numerical change in people visiting from the United Kingdom was much greater than the numerical change in people from Japan.
(A) The total number of tourists from Japan and the United Kingdom who visited Paris during the first half of 2017 exceeded the same total for the first half of 2016. Given that the passage says that the number from Japan increased substantially in percentage terms, while the number from the United Kingdom barely changed, one could be tempted to come to the conclusion that the total number visiting from the two countries increased. However, since the passage does not indicate how the absolute numbers of those visiting from the two countries compare, it does not support this conclusion. Given what the passage says, there could have been many more tourists from the United Kingdom than from Japan. So, a small percentage decrease in the tourists from the United Kingdom could actually represent a large decrease in absolute number terms, one that could outweigh a relatively small absolute number increase in those visiting from Japan.
(B) During the first half of 2017 more people from Japan than from the United Kingdom visited Paris. The statements provide neither absolute numbers of tourists from Japan and the United Kingdom nor any comparison of the numbers of tourists from the two places. Therefore, they don’t support any conclusions about comparisons in absolute numbers of tourists from Japan and the United Kingdom.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The ratio of tourists from Japan who visited Paris to tourists from the United Kingdom who visited Paris was greater for the first half of 2017 than for the first half of 2016. No matter what the actual numbers of tourists from Japan and the United Kingdom were in the two periods, if, as the passage says, the number from Japan increased from the first period to the second, and the number from the United Kingdom decreased, then the ratio of tourists from Japan to tourists from the United Kingdom must have increased.
(D) Of all tourists who visited Paris, the proportion from Japan was greater in the first half of 2017 than the proportion from Japan was in the first half of 2016. Since the number of tourists from Japan increased by a substantial percentage, it might be tempting to conclude that the proportion of all tourists visiting Paris represented by the tourists from Japan increased. However, for all we know, the total number tourists increased by, for example, 100 percent, in which case the proportion represented by tourists from Japan would have decreased.
(E) The amount of money spent in Paris by tourists from the United Kingdom during the first half of 2017 was less than that spent by such tourists during the first half of 2016. Since the passage does not provide information indicating how much tourists from the United Kingdom spent, it does not support any conclusion about how much they spent during the two time periods. For all we know from what the passage says, while the number of tourists from the United Kingdom decreased, it could be that the average amount spent per tourist from the United Kingdom increased, in which case the total amount spent by tourists from the United Kingdom could have increased rather than decreased.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 18 Last year, Shoes Unlimited, which has always sold only shoes, sold online more shoes than it sold online during any previous year in the company’s history. If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred from them? 1. [ ] (A) Last year, Shoes Unlimited’s expenses were greater than its expenses were during any other year in its history. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Last year, Shoes Unlimited sold via brick and mortar stores a larger number of shoes than it sold via brick and mortar stores during any previous year. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Over the last two years, Shoes Unlimited has seen its share of the shoe market decline. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Shoes Unlimited does not sell shoes to its customers via online transactions only. 5. [ ] (E) At the beginning of last year, Shoes Unlimited stopped aggressively marketing its products. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: hard Fact: Last year, Shoes Unlimited, which has always sold only shoes, sold online more shoes than it sold online during any previous year in the company’s history.
Fact: The average price at which the company sold shoes last year was higher than the average price at which it sold shoes during any previous year.
Fact: The company’s sales revenue for last year was the lowest sales revenue for any year in the company’s history.
In answering this question, we’re seeking a conclusion that MUST be true given the facts presented.
(A) Last year, Shoes Unlimited’s expenses were greater than its expenses were during any other year in its history. Notice that the passage tells us only about the number of shoes the company sold, the prices of the shoes, and the company’s sales revenue. None of that information tells us anything concrete about the company’s expenses. So, this choice is not supported by what the passage says.
(B) Last year, Shoes Unlimited sold via brick and mortar stores a larger number of shoes than it sold via brick and mortar stores during any previous year. The passage provides information only on Shoes Unlimited’s online sales of shoes.
Furthermore, the passage says that the company sold online more shoes than it sold online during any previous year in the company’s history, and that the average price at which the company sold shoes last year was higher than the average price at which it sold shoes during any previous year, but the company’s sale revenue for last year was the lowest sales revenue for any year in the company’s history.
If, even though the company sold a record high number of shoes online and sold its shoes at a record high average price, the company’s sales revenue was at a record low, the company must have seen a decrease in revenue from sales other than online sales, such as sales in brick and mortar stores.
So, while, given what the passage says, we don’t have sufficient information to be completely certain that, last year, the company sold via brick and mortar stores fewer shoes than it sold via brick and mortar stores during any previous year, the passage certainly does not support the conclusion that last year the company sold via brick and mortar stores a larger of number shoes than it sold via brick and mortar stores during any previous year.
(C) Over the last two years, Shoes Unlimited has seen its share of the shoe market decline. The passage provides information only on Shoes Unlimited’s sales. It does not provide any information on the shoe market as a whole or any information on Shoes Unlimited’s share of the market. So, the passage provides no support for what this choice says.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Shoes Unlimited does not sell shoes to its customers via online transactions only. We know that (Number of Units Sold) x (Price) = (Total Sales Revenue).
The passage says that Shoes Unlimited sold online more shoes than it sold online during any previous year in the company’s history.
The passage says also that the average price at which the company sold shoes last year was higher than the average price at which it sold shoes during any previous year.
Of course, if the company sells shoes online only, then the information we have would lead the following results for last year:
(Most Shoes Ever Sold) x (Highest Ever Average Price) = (Highest Ever Total Sales Revenue)
However, the passage tells us that the company’s sales revenue for last year was the lowest sales revenue for any year in the company’s history.
In order for the company’s sales revenue for last year to have been the lowest sales revenue for any year in the company’s history, the company must have seen a reduction in revenue somewhere, and we know that, if the company sold shoes online only, it would have had its highest ever sales revenue last year.
So, given what the passage says, we can tell that the company must sell shoes in ways other than via online transactions.
Thus, what this choice says, that Shoes Unlimited does not sell shoes to its customers via online transactions only, must be true.
(E) At the beginning of last year, Shoes Unlimited stopped aggressively marketing its products. While the passage says that Shoes Unlimited’s sales revenue decreased, it provides no information, such as information on Shoes Unlimited’s marketing, on why the company’s sales revenue decreased. So, the passage does not support what this choice says.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 19 During each of the past two years, Bonanvia consumed 50 percent of the oranges that it produced that year and exported the other 50 percent, either in the form of whole fruit or in the form of orange juice. If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true? 1. [ ] (A) Last year, Bonanvia consumed less than 50 percent of the orange juice that it produced. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Last year, the average price of oranges in Bonanvia was higher than it was the year before. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Last year, Bonanvia exported a smaller quantity of oranges in the form of whole fruit than it exported during the year before. 4. [ ] (D) It is more profitable for Bonanvian orange producers to export orange juice than to export oranges in the form of whole fruit. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Bonanvian orange producers’ revenue from exported orange juice last year was the same as the revenue from exported orange juice the year before. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard Analysis of the Information Given:
Effect of the First Statement: Establishes that, during each of the past two years, Bonanvia consumed 50 percent of the oranges that it produced that year and exported the other 50 percent.
Effect of the Second Statement: Defines the two ways in which Bonanvia consumed and exported oranges – in the form of whole oranges and in the form of orange juice.
Effect of the Third Statement: Compares the orange production of last year with the orange production of the year before – last year’s production was 15 percent lower than that of the year before.
Effect of the Fourth Statement: Makes clear that the same amount of orange juice was exported in each of the two years.
In order to answer this question correctly, we have to be careful not to distort the information presented in the passage or to make unwarranted assumptions. Given the fact that there are a variety of interrelated numerical facts in the passage, it would be easy to arrive at an erroneous conclusion.
For instance, we have to be careful not to take the fact that Bonanvia consumed 50 percent of the oranges that it produced each year and exported the other 50 percent as indicating that it is also the case that whole oranges and orange juice each represented 50 percent of the oranges consumed and exported.
Overall, we have to be very careful and organized in our thinking in order to arrive at a conclusion that is truly supported by what the passage says.
(A) Last year, Bonanvia consumed less than 50 percent of the orange juice that it produced. This answer is very tempting. Since the passage says that orange production was lower last year than it was the year before, that Bonanvia exported 50 percent of the oranges that it produced each year, and that Bonanvia exported the same amount of juice that it had exported the year before, we might be tempted to conclude that last year Bonanvia exported more than half of the juice that it produced. But, wait a minute. We really don’t know how much juice was exported either year. All we know is that, each year, Bonanvia exported half of the oranges that it produced, either in the form of whole oranges or in the form of juice. For all we know, Bonanvia exported no juice two years ago and no juice last year, all the while making juice out of all the oranges that it did not export. So, we can’t conclude that Bonanvia consumed less than half of the juice it produced last year.
(B) Last year, the average price of oranges in Bonanvia was higher than it was the year before. This choice could be tempting, because, since we know that the quantity of oranges produced last year was smaller than the quantity produced the year before, we might be tempted to conclude that price of oranges increased. However, even though it is plausible that a price increase would have occurred, the passage does not provide information that we can use to confirm that a price increase occurred.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Last year, Bonanvia exported a smaller quantity of oranges in the form of whole fruit than it exported during the year before. The total quantity of oranges produced last year was smaller than the quantity produced the year before. Meanwhile, each year, Bonanvia exported half of the oranges it produced that year. Half of a smaller quantity is smaller than half of a larger quantity. So, the quantity of oranges exported in the form of whole oranges and juice last year was smaller than the quantity exported the year before.
Since the quantity of juice exported last year was the same as the quantity of juice exported the year before, the quantity of whole oranges exported last year must have been smaller than the quantity exported the year before. If the quantity of oranges exported were not smaller, then the total quantity of juice and oranges exported last year would not be smaller than the quantity exported the year before, and we know that the total quantity was, in fact, smaller.
(D) It is more profitable for Bonanvian orange producers to export orange juice than to export oranges in the form of whole fruit. This cannot be concluded from what the passage says. The passage does not give us any indication that it is more profitable to export orange juice.
(E) Bonanvian orange producers’ revenue from exported orange juice last year was the same as the revenue from exported orange juice the year before. This choice is tempting, because the passage says that, last year, Bonanvia exported the same amount of orange juice that it exported the year before. However, the price of orange juice could have change from one year to the next, and Revenue = Quantity x Price. So, since the passage does not tell us anything about the prices of orange juice in the two years, even though it provides information on the quantity of orange juice exported, it does not support any conclusion about revenue from exported orange juice.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss correct answers that are supported by mathematical information located in small portions of the passages.
summary:
Lesson Content A third type of correct answer that we may encounter in an Inference question is an answer that is supported by numerical information located throughout the passage.
Finding the correct answer to such a question may require understanding the implications of a ratio or deciphering the meaning of data presented in percentage terms.
Consider the following example:
Roberta’s Chocolate has always used two ounces of cocoa in making each of its chocolate bars.
title: 7.6.3 Answers that are Supported by Numerical Information Located Throughout Passages

contents:
Lesson Content One variation that can make an Inference question a little trickier is a question’s having a correct answer that is based on some but not all the mathematical information present in a passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
When looking for the correct answer to an Inference question involving numerical information, be sure to be open to choosing any choice, not just the ones that seem to be based on all the information presented by the passage.
We saw this type of answer in the Verdantville example earlier.
No new houses have been built in Verdantville in the past eight years, and the values of houses in Verdantville have not increased. Over the same time period, the number of children living in Verdantville has increased substantially. The costs of operating the schools in Verdantville are paid for entirely by property taxes, which are based on house values.
(Correct Answer) On average, there are more children per house in Verdantville than there were eight years ago.
In that example, the inference about the number of children per house is based on just two facts out of several facts presented in the passage.
Since some of the information presented by the passage will not be necessary for supporting the correct answer, in order to find the correct answer to this type of question, we have to remember that any choice that is proven true by the information in the passage will be correct and not focus only on choices that incorporate all of the information present in the passage.
Here’s another example:
In Meklerton, the number of people who drive cars to work has decreased by 10 percent over the past two years, while, over the same time period, the number who ride bicycles to work has increased by 30 percent. In rush hour traffic in Meklerton, a person on a bicycle can travel 150 percent faster than can a person in a car.
Which of the following can be properly concluded from the information provided in the passage?
(Trap Choice) In Meklerton, the average amount of time spent by a person travelling to work each day has decreased over the past two years.
(Correct Answer) In Meklerton, the ratio of people who travel to work by bicycle to people who travel to work by car has increased.
A test-taker looking for an answer that is based on all the information in the passage might be tempted to choose the trap choice, as the trap choice may seem to follow from the information presented in the passage.
The correct answer is based on only a portion of the information in the passage, the decrease in people using cars and the increase in people cycling.
So, the correct answer to an Inference question that includes numerical information can be a simple deduction that can be proven using just a small portion of the information presented by the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
The correct answer to an Inference question that includes numerical information can be a simple deduction that can be proven using just a small portion of the information presented by the passage. It’s not always the case that we must use all the information presented in the stimulus to arrive at the correct answer.
Consider the following examples:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 20 Over the past ten years, the debt of the government of Vesornia has increased by 39 percent. Which of the following can be properly concluded from the information provided by the statements above? 1. [ ] (A) If the leading economist’s belief is correct, Vesornia will go bankrupt in a little over ten years. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) In order to avoid bankruptcy, Vesornia must achieve a higher rate of growth of gross domestic product. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) If the rate of gross domestic product growth in Vesornia increases by 25 percent, Vesornia will not go bankrupt. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) During the past ten years, the government debt of Vesornia has increased at a rate faster than the rate at which it increased during the ten years previous to the past ten. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Government debt per capita in Vesornia is greater than it was ten years ago. View Solution Question Level: medium Analysis of the Information Given:
Effect of the First Statement: Defines the change in size of government debt in Vesornia over a time period. Time Period: Past ten years. Direction: Increase. Numerical Value: 39 percent.
Effect of Second Statement: Defines change in the size of population of Vesornia. No change.
Effect of Third Statement: Defines change in size of gross domestic product of Vesornia. Direction: Increase. Numerical Value: 32 percent.
Effect of Fourth Statement: Describes the belief of leading economist – If debt increases an additional 40 percent, Vesornia will immediately go bankrupt.
This passage is one with which we have to be careful because all the numbers provided might tempt us to draw unwarranted conclusions. The truth is that the passage does not provide us with specific information on how the various numbers relate to the economist’s prediction. For instance, we are given values for percentage growth of gross domestic product and government debt, but we can’t say for sure to what degree growth in gross domestic product or growth in government debt would have to change in order for bankruptcy to be avoided.
So, to find the correct answer to this question, we have to be careful not to fall for trap answers that seem to be supported by the numerical information provided but are not really.
(A) If the leading economist’s belief is correct, Vesornia will go bankrupt in a little over ten years. This answer could be tempting, because a test-taker might jump from the idea that the debt increased by 39 percent over the past ten years to the conclusion that the debt will increase by another 40 percent in a little over ten years. However, we really don’t know that this will occur. For all we know, the debt will not grow further, or other trends will change. So, this answer is not supported.
(B) In order to avoid bankruptcy, Vesornia must achieve a higher rate of growth of gross domestic product. Given the information on debt and growth in gross domestic product presented in the passage, we might be tempted to believe that what this choice says is supported. It isn’t really, however. There could be ways for Vesornia to avoid bankruptcy without an increase in growth in gross domestic product.
(C) If the rate of gross domestic product growth in Vesornia increases by 25 percent, Vesornia will not go bankrupt. Since, if the country’s gross domestic product had grown 25 percent more than it did over the past ten years, it would have grown 40 percent rather than 32 percent, and, since 40 percent is greater than the growth in government debt, which was 39 percent, one might be tempted to decide that a 25 percent increase in gross domestic product growth would save Vesornia from bankruptcy. However, we don’t really have sufficient information for making that prediction. For all we know, Vesornia would go bankrupt even if gross domestic product were to grow 100 percent per year. So, this choice is not supported.
(D) During the past ten years, the government debt of Vesornia has increased at a rate faster than the rate at which it increased during the ten years previous to the past ten. While Vesornia’s government debt grew a lot in the past ten years, the passage does not indicate that it did not grow just as fast during the ten years that preceded the past ten years.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Government debt per capita in Vesornia is greater than it was ten years ago. With only two pieces of information out of the several pieces in the passage we can support this choice. We know that government debt increased by 39 percent. We know that size of the population was steady. Since the percentage increase in government debt was positive, while there was no increase in the number of people in Vesornia, it has to be the case that debt per capita, i.e. debt per person, increased.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 21 Last month, the average interest rate charged for a commercial loan made in Mennerton was lower than the average for any month in twenty years. Which of the following can be properly inferred from the statements above? 1. [ ] (A) In Mennerton, had only commercial equipment loans been made last month, last month’s average commercial loan rate would not have been the lowest monthly average seen in twenty years. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) 4.5 percent is the lowest commercial loan rate seen in Mennerton in twenty years. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) The month before last month, the average rate charged for a commercial loan in Mennerton was over 5 percent. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) If no commercial loans had been made last month in Mennerton, the average commercial loan rate for the past nine months would have been higher than it was. 5. [ ] (E) For each of the twelve months preceding last month, the average rate on commercial equipment loans was greater than 9 percent. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: hard Analysis of the Information Given:
Effect of the First Statement: Defines that monthly average interest rates on commercial loans in Mennerton were at a 20 year low last month.
Effect of Second Statement: Defines last month’s averages for rates on two types of commercial loans, 4.5 percent for real estate loans and 9 percent for equipment loans.
The passage provides limited information: a comparison between the average rate last month and averages rate for all months for the preceding 20 years, and the exact values of last month’s average levels of interest rates on two categories of loans. It does not provide any specific information on levels of interest rates in other time periods. One fact that is clear, though, is that we can compare last month’s average commercial loan rate with the average for any other month during the past 20 years and be sure that last month’s average is lower than the other month’s average.
(A) In Mennerton, had only commercial equipment loans been made last month, last month’s average commercial loan rate would not have been the lowest monthly average seen in twenty years. This choice could be tempting, because, last month’s average commercial equipment loan rate is so much higher than the average commercial real estate loan rate that one could get the impression that, if only equipment loans had been made last month, there’s no way that last month’s average rate would have been lower than the 20 year average. However, the truth is that the passage does not provide any information from which one can properly infer that the average commercial loan rate in Mennerton was not higher than 9 percent every month for over 20 years. So, it does not have to be true that 9 percent would not be the lowest rate in over 20 years, and so, what this choice says does not have to be true given what the passage says.
(B) 4.5 percent is the lowest commercial loan rate seen in Mennerton in twenty years. This answer could be tempting, because, since last month’s commercial rates were the lowest, on average, in twenty years, and since, last month, the average rate for commercial real estate loans was much lower than the average rate for commercial equipment loans, one might be tempted to decide that the average rate for commercial real estate loans was the lowest commercial loan rate seen in twenty years. The truth is, however, that another month’s average for all commercial loans could have been higher than this month’s average even though the other month’s average for commercial real estate loans was lower. So, what this choice says does not have to be true.
(C) The month before last month, the average rate charged for a commercial loan in Mennerton was over 5 percent. Even though last month’s mix of loans included commercial equipment loans whose rates averaged 9 percent, it is still possible that last month’s average rate, which was based also on commercial real estate loans whose rates averaged 4.5 percent, was under 5 percent. So, it is not necessarily the case that the average rate for the month before last month was over 5 percent.
YOUR CHOICE(D) If no commercial loans had been made last month in Mennerton, the average commercial loan rate for the past nine months would have been higher than it was. If last month’s average was lower than that of any other month of the last twenty years, then last month’s average was lower than that of any other month of the last nine months. So, last month’s average brought down the average for the past nine months. Therefore, if no commercial loans at all had been made last month, then the lowest monthly average, last month’s, would not have brought down the nine month average, which therefore would have been higher than it was. Notice, this choice is completely supported by what is said in only the first statement in the passage.
(E) For each of the twelve months preceding last month, the average rate on commercial equipment loans was greater than 9 percent. This choice could be tempting, as the fact that the average commercial equipment loan rate was 9 percent for the month that had the lowest average commercial loan rate in over 20 years could be perceived as indicating that 9 percent is the lowest monthly average commercial equipment loan rate seen in 20 years, and thus, in 12 months as well. However, since a monthly average for all commercial loans could be higher than this month’s even with a lower average for commercial equipment loans, one of the 12 months preceding this month could have had an average commercial equipment loan rate lower than this month’s.
Correct Answer: (D) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 22 In Derask, the total number of gasoline burning automobiles on the roads doubled from 1977 to 2017. Which of the following is a conclusion that can be properly drawn from the information in the passage? 1. [x] (A) The average number of miles travelled by an automobile burning a liter of fuel in Derask was in 2017 twice what it was in 1977. 2. [ ] (B) In Derask, the average annual cost of operating an automobile in 1977 was nearly twice the average annual cost of operating an automobile in 2017. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) In Derask, in 2017, more automobiles traveled more miles than were traveled per automobile in 1977 than traveled fewer miles than were traveled per automobile in 1977. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) No automobile used in Derask in 2017 burned more gasoline per mile traveled than did any automobile used in Derask in 1977. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) The total number of miles traveled by automobiles in Derask in 2017 was 10 percent greater than the total number of miles traveled by automobiles in Derask in 1977. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard Analysis of the Information Given:
Effect of the First Statement: Defines the change in number of automobiles over the time period – the number doubled.
Effect of Second Statement: Defines change in average fuel burned per mile – decrease by 50 percent.
Effect of Third Statement: Defines difference in quantity of gasoline used in one year and used in another – 10 percent more used in 2017 than in 1977.
We are presented with a few aggregate data points. Notice, we don’t know the actual number of automobiles or exactly what underlies the differences between the averages. To get the right answer, we have to be careful not to imagine that we have more information than we actually have.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) The average number of miles travelled by an automobile burning a liter of fuel in Derask was in 2017 twice what it was in 1977. What this choice says is very basic, and it happens to be completely supported by the second statement in the passage. If the average amount of fuel each automobile burned in going a mile decreased by 50 percent, then the average distance an automobile would go in burning a certain quantity, such as a liter, of fuel must have doubled.
(B) In Derask, the average annual cost of operating an automobile in 1977 was nearly twice the average annual cost of operating an automobile in 2017. While we know that the average amount of fuel burned by an automobile going a certain distance was double in 1977 what it was in 2017, since we don’t know the cost of fuel in the two years, we don’t have a basis for saying that the cost to operate a vehicle in 1977 was nearly twice the cost to operate one in 2017. Also, there are costs involved in operating an automobile other than fuel costs, and since we are given no information on trends in these other costs, what this choice says is not supported.
(C) In Derask, in 2017, more automobiles traveled more miles than were traveled per automobile in 1977 than traveled fewer miles than were traveled per automobile in 1977. This choice could be tempting, because it might seem to make sense that, if the number of miles traveled per car in 2017 was 10 percent greater than the number traveled per car in 1977, then it must be the case that, in 2017, more automobiles traveled more miles than were traveled per automobile in 1977 than traveled fewer miles than were traveled per automobile in 1977. There are many ways to increase an average though. It could be that a small number of automobiles traveled many miles in 2017, bringing the 2017 average up even though, in 2017, most cars traveled no more miles than were traveled per car in 1977.
(D) No automobile used in Derask in 2017 burned more gasoline per mile traveled than did any automobile used in Derask in 1977. The fact that the average amount of fuel burned per mile traveled by an automobile was greater in 1977 than in 2017 does not indicate that in 2017 no automobile burned more fuel per mile traveled than any automobile did in 1977. For one thing, in 2017, someone could still be using an automobile from 1977. The passage gives us aggregate information on average quantities of fuel burned, but not information on how much fuel any individual automobile burned.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The total number of miles traveled by automobiles in Derask in 2017 was 10 percent greater than the total number of miles traveled by automobiles in Derask in 1977. This choice could seem to fit the fact that 10 percent more fuel was burned in 2017 than in 1977. However, in 2017, on average, automobiles burned less fuel per mile than automobiles burned per mile in 1977. So, in order to burn 10 percent more fuel in 2017, automobiles would have had to travel more than 10 percent more in total than they did in 1977. We don’t need to calculate the exact difference in travel (120 percent) to eliminate this choice. We just have to notice that, since in 2017 automobiles used less fuel per mile than they did in 1977, a 10 percent increase in fuel use would require a greater than 10 percent increase in miles traveled.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now discuss Inference questions that contain the word “Except.”
summary:
Lesson Content One variation that can make an Inference question a little trickier is a question’s having a correct answer that is based on some but not all the mathematical information present in a passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
When looking for the correct answer to an Inference question involving numerical information, be sure to be open to choosing any choice, not just the ones that seem to be based on all the information presented by the passage.
title: 7.6.4 Answers that are Supported by Mathematical Information Located in Small Portions of Passages

contents:
Lesson Content As is the case with other types of Critical Reasoning questions, the test-makers can increase the difficulty of Inference questions by using the word “EXCEPT” or “not” in question stems.
If the statements above are true, all of the following can be properly inferred from them EXCEPT:
or
Given that the statements above are all true, which of the following is not an inference that can be properly made on the basis of them?
When an Inference question stem uses the word “EXCEPT” or “not,” four of the five answers will be inferences that can be made given the information in the passage.
Meanwhile, since, in most cases, they wouldn’t want to make it too easy to find the correct answer by giving you a choice that is clearly untrue, the correct answers to such questions tend to be possibly, but not necessarily, true.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an Inference Question uses the word “EXCEPT” or “not,” four of the five answers must be true. These four answers will be incorrect answers.
In these types of questions, the premises in the stimulus tend to make heavy use of words like “sometimes,” “always,” and “never.” They create conditions that must be met by the statements.
This concept might be easier to conceptualize with an example:
Animal Shelter Director: In our animal shelter we have only dogs and cats. None of our dogs is over 2 years old but many of our cats are.
All of the following statements must be true EXCEPT:
Remember, for a must be true (Inference) EXCEPT question, four of the answers will definitely be true, and these answers are incorrect.
Let’s consider a few possible answers:
All the dogs are at most 2 years old
This answer must be true. If none of the dogs is over 2 years old, then all of them must be 2 years old or younger.
No dog is older than the oldest cat
This answer must be true.
No cat is younger than the youngest dog
This answer could be true or could be false.
Pay attention to the words as you analyze the answers, because the correct answer may well be a distortion of a sentence containing similar words.
The essential technique for this type of question is the negation technique: ask yourself whether it is possible for all the information in the stem to be true and for each answer to be false.
In fact, the correct answer in these question types may in fact contain any of the traps discussed earlier in the chapter.
💡MUST KNOW:
In EXCEPT or not Inference questions, the correct answer will be the one that must be false or could be true. The four incorrect answers all must be true .
Consider the following examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 23 Delivereez and Boxrunners are the only two delivery services in town. If the statements above are true, which of the following is not an inference that can be made on the basis of them? 1. [ ] (A) A large package delivered uptown by van was delivered by Boxrunners. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) A small package delivered to midtown by bicycle may have been delivered by Delivereez. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) A large package delivered to midtown by van was delivered by Delivereez. 4. [ ] (D) A medium sized package delivered to midtown by pedicab was delivered by Delivereez. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) A large package delivered downtown by van was not delivered by Boxrunners. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium The stimulus to this question seems complicated and contains many facts. However, if we are organized and methodical, we can efficiently go through the answers to find the one that does not have to be true.
Facts Presented:
There are only two delivery services in town, Delivereez and Boxrunners.
Delivereez:
Bicycles – Small Packages
Pedicabs – Medium Packages
Vans – Large Packages
Delivers to: Downtown, Midtown
Boxrunners: Bicycles – Small Packages
Vans – Package of All Other Sizes
Delivers to: Midtown, Uptown
(A) A large package delivered uptown by van was delivered by Boxrunners. Of the only two delivery services, Boxrunners is the only one that delivers to uptown.
Must be true? Yes.
(B) A small package delivered to midtown by bicycle may have been delivered by Delivereez. Delivereez delivers to midtown and uses bicycles to deliver small packages.
Must be true? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(C) A large package delivered to midtown by van was delivered by Delivereez. Both services deliver to midtown, and both use vans for large packages. So, either one could have delivered a large package to midtown by van.
Must be true? No.
(D) A medium sized package delivered to midtown by pedicab was delivered by Delivereez. Of the only two delivery services in town, the only one that delivers by pedicab is Delivereez.
Must be true? Yes.
(E) A large package delivered downtown by van was not delivered by Boxrunners. Only Delivereez delivers to downtown.
Must be true? Yes.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 24 Last year, Manufacturer W sold more units than it sold during the previous year, but its total revenue was the same as its total revenue for the previous year. All of the following can be properly inferred from the information presented above EXCEPT: 1. [ ] (A) The average price per unit sold by Manufacturer W last year was lower than the average price per unit sold the previous year. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) The ratio of Manufacturer T’s profits to Manufacturer W’s profits for last year was greater than the same ratio for the year before. 3. [ ] (C) The ratio of the number of units sold by Manufacturer W to the number of units sold by Manufacturer T was greater last year than it was two years ago. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) The ratio of Manufacturer T’s revenue to Manufacturer W’s revenue was greater last year than it was two years ago. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Manufacturer T received last year an average price for each of its units sold higher than that received in the previous year. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium Fact 1: Manufacturer W’s total number of units sold increased.
Fact 2: Manufacturer W’s revenue remained the same.
Fact 3: Manufacturer T’s total number of units sold decreased.
Fact 4: Manufacturer T’s revenue increased.
What must be true?
From the information presented, we can make various inferences about the prices and sales of Manufacturer W and Manufacturer T.
In order to work with these statements, we must understand that total revenue is the product of number of units sold and price charged per unit.
U x P = R
So, if, for instance, U increases, and P increases, then R increases.
If U decreases, and P decreases, then R decreases.
If U remains the same, and P increases, R will increase even though U didn’t.
It is also the case that, if R has increased, but U has not changed, then we know that P must have increased.
On the other hand, if R has remained the same while U has increased, then P must have decreased.
So, for instance, since Manufacturer W’s units sold increased while Manufacturer W’s revenue remained the same, then the average price per unit sold by Manufacturer W must have decreased.
(A) The average price per unit sold by Manufacturer W last year was lower than the average price per unit sold the previous year. As outlined above, since the number of units sold by Manufacturer W increased while Manufacturer W’s revenue remained the same, then the average price per unit sold by Manufacturer W must have decreased.
Must be true? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The ratio of Manufacturer T’s profits to Manufacturer W’s profits for last year was greater than the same ratio for the year before. This choice could seem to be true, as we know that Manufacturer T’s revenue increased last year while Manufacturer W’s revenue remained the same. However, revenue and profit, while related, are not the same thing. Profit is revenue minus cost. So, with only revenue information we have no way to make this inference about profits.
Must be true? No.
(C) The ratio of the number of units sold by Manufacturer W to the number of units sold by Manufacturer T was greater last year than it was two years ago. Since we know that the number of units sold by Manufacturer W increased, while the number of units sold by Manufacturer T decreased, we can infer that the ratio of units sold by Manufacturer W to units sold by Manufacturer T increased, as, when we increase the number on the top of a ratio and decreased the number on the bottom of the ratio, we increase the ratio.
Must be true? Yes.
(D) The ratio of Manufacturer T’s revenue to Manufacturer W’s revenue was greater last year than it was two years ago. Since we know that Manufacturer T’s revenue increased, while Manufacturer W’s revenue remained the same, we can infer that the ratio of Manufacturer T’s revenue to Manufacturer W’s revenue must have increased, as, when we increase the number on the top of a ratio and don’t change the number on the bottom of the ratio, we increase the ratio.
Must be true? Yes.
(E) Incorrect. If Manufacturer T was able to increase its revenue even as its sales declined, it must have received a higher price for each unit it sold. Remember, revenue is the product of number of products sold and price charged per product. If unit sales declined, yet revenue increased, then the price must have increased.
Must be true? Yes.
Correct Answer: (B)
summary:
Lesson Content As is the case with other types of Critical Reasoning questions, the test-makers can increase the difficulty of Inference questions by using the word “EXCEPT” or “not” in question stems.
If the statements above are true, all of the following can be properly inferred from them EXCEPT:
or
Given that the statements above are all true, which of the following is not an inference that can be properly made on the basis of them?
title: 7.7 “Except” Inference Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Many of the Critical Reasoning questions that we’ve examined up to this point have introduced arguments that contain clearly-stated conclusions.
Both names are fitting since these questions ask us to find the author’s logical conclusion or main point.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Find the Conclusion/Main Point questions, our job is to select an answer choice that best captures the author’s conclusion, the main point of the argument.
Consider the following simple example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 1 Once the root cause of an illness has been fully addressed, a person who has been experiencing that illness will no longer experience it. If the statements above are true, which of the following is most strongly supported by them? 1. [ ] (A) Stress management counseling has no effect on people experiencing illness. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Some people experiencing illness become healthier without any treatment. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) There is no way to address the root causes of certain illnesses. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) In some cases, stress management counseling does not fully address the root cause of an illness. 5. [ ] (E) Stress is the sole cause of all forms of illness. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy The argument in this question has three premises:
Premise 1: Once the root cause of an illness has been fully addressed, a person who has been experiencing that illness will no longer experience it.
Premise 2: In some cases, weekly stress management counseling will cause the progression of an illness to cease.
Premise 3: Often in such cases, once people experiencing illness stop undergoing the counseling, the progression will recommence.
What conclusion do these premises support?
They start off by telling us that, once the root cause of an illness is fully addressed, the person experiencing the illness will no longer experience it. Then they tell us that, while stress management counseling may cause the progression of an illness to cease, once the counseling is no longer going on, in some cases, the progression recommences.
Thus, the premises support the conclusion that, in at least some cases, stress management counseling does not fully address the root cause of an illness.
(A) Stress management counseling has no effect on people experiencing illness. This answer choice conflicts with what the passage says. The premises don’t support the conclusion that stress management counseling has no effect at all. Actually, they indicate that stress management does have some effects, though the effects may be somewhat temporary in some cases.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Some people experiencing illness become healthier without any treatment. This answer choice states a conclusion with no support in the premises. They don’t say anything about people getting healthier without any treatment.
(C) There is no way to address the root causes of certain illnesses. What this choice says goes too far. The fact that stress management counseling may not always fully address the root causes of illnesses does not mean that there is no way to address the root causes of certain illnesses.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) In some cases, stress management counseling does not fully address the root cause of an illness. As discussed above, the first premise of the argument says that, when the root cause of an illness has been fully addressed, a person who has been experiencing that illness will no longer experience it. Thus, since, in some cases, when a person stops undergoing stress management counseling, the progression of an illness that the person has been experiencing recommences, the premises support the conclusion that, in some cases, stress management counseling does not fully address the root causes of illnesses.
(E) Stress is the sole cause of all forms of illness. This choice goes well beyond what the passage supports. The fact that, in some cases, stress management counseling causes the progression of an illness to cease, does not, on its own, support the conclusion that stress is the sole cause of all forms of illness.
Correct Answer: (D) At this point, you’ve already developed strong skills in finding the conclusion of an argument.
summary:
Lesson Content Many of the Critical Reasoning questions that we’ve examined up to this point have introduced arguments that contain clearly-stated conclusions.
Both names are fitting since these questions ask us to find the author’s logical conclusion or main point.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Find the Conclusion/Main Point questions, our job is to select an answer choice that best captures the author’s conclusion, the main point of the argument.
Consider the following simple example:
title: 8.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content Find the Conclusion questions will be based on passages that always contain premises (evidence), and, from time to time, include background information or an intermediate conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
An argument’s conclusion must be closely connected to its premises, assumptions, and any intermediary conclusions that may be present.
It’s important to keep in mind that a conclusion must follow from only the information presented in the argument and any assumptions made by the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
An argument’s conclusion must follow only from the information presented in the argument and any assumptions made by the argument. The conclusion cannot be based upon outside information that was never mentioned or implied.
It’s also important to keep in mind that the correct answer must do the best job, among the five answers, of capturing the author’s main point.
💡MUST KNOW:
The correct answer to a Find the Conclusion question must do the best job, among the five answers, of capturing the author’s main point, her conclusion.
Consider the following example:
Peterville’s library is seeking to raise money to make up a deficit in its budget by having a book drive in which local citizens will donate their unwanted books to the library. The library staff has decided to sell off any donated book of which the library already has a copy. Since people enjoy buying books at cheap prices, the book drive will allow the library to make up its funding deficit. Here is what we have, in shorthand notation:
Premise 1: Library has deficit
Premise 2: Will sell duplicate book copies
Premise 3: People tend to buy cheap books
Conclusion: Book drive = no more deficit
But what if we were to remove the conclusion from this argument? It would look like this:
Peterville’s library is seeking to raise money to make up a deficit in its budget by having a book drive in which local citizens will donate their unwanted books to the library. The library staff has decided to sell off any donated book of which the library already has a copy. People enjoy buying books at cheap prices. Now, with the conclusion missing, rather than providing facts that lead to an opinion, this argument now lists only facts.
💡MUST KNOW:
Don’t expect the conclusion to be provided in a Find the Conclusion question; in answering such a question, our task is to find the choice that best articulates the author’s logically justified but unstated conclusion.
One powerful strategy that we can use is the “therefore test.” As you know, “therefore” is a word commonly used to indicate that a conclusion is about to follow.
Peterville’s library is seeking to raise money to make up a deficit in its budget by having a book drive in which local citizens will donate their unwanted books to the library. The library staff has decided to sell off any donated book of which the library already has a copy. People enjoy buying books at cheap prices. Therefore:
Whatever follows the “therefore” must be the logical conclusion of the argument.
Peterville’s library is seeking to raise money to make up a deficit in its budget by having a book drive in which local citizens will donate their unwanted books to the library. The library staff has decided to sell off any donated book of which the library already has a copy. People enjoy buying books at cheap prices. Therefore, the book drive will allow the library to make up its funding deficit.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Find the Conclusion questions, consider using the “therefore test” to help you efficiently and accurately locate an argument’s conclusion.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 2 Smithtown Roast is the best-selling brand of coffee in grocery stores. The most reasonable conclusion based on the information above is that 1. [ ] (A) most people who buy their coffee from grocery stores buy the least expensive brand. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) most coffee drinkers buy their coffee at grocery stores, rather than from specialty markets. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) the tastes of coffee experts who judge at tasting competitions are different from the tastes of the general coffee drinking public. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) coffee tasting competitions tend to include all of the best-selling coffee brands. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) the best-selling brand of coffee is not necessarily the best tasting brand. View Solution Question Level: medium This argument has two premises:
Premise 1: Smithtown Roast is the best-selling brand at grocery stores
Premise 2: Smithtown Roast ranks last in tasting competitions.
Notice that there is no conclusion given. It’s our job to find argument’s conclusion, which will logically from these two premises. One way to conceptualize this argument, and the correct answer we seek is to say:
Smithtown Roast is the best-selling brand of coffee in grocery stores. However, Smithtown Roast is consistently rated poorly by coffee experts during coffee-tasting competitions. Therefore,
Whatever comes after “therefore” must be the logical conclusion to the argument.
(A) most people who buy their coffee from grocery stores buy the least expensive brand. This answer choice provides information that could explain the mismatch between the buying patterns at grocery stores and the results of tasting competitions if we assume that Smithtown Roast is least expensive brand. However, this information on the relative price of Smithtown Roast is new, outside information that does not logically follow from the stated premises. This is not the argument’s conclusion.
(B) most coffee drinkers buy their coffee at grocery stores, rather than from specialty markets. This answer might seem to follow from what the argument says if we were not really paying attention to what is said and we were to arrive at an erroneous association between the popularity of Smithtown Roast in grocery stores and the popularity of the stores themselves as places for buying coffee. However, the truth is that the fact that the most popular coffee in grocery stores does not taste very good does not mean that most people buy coffee in grocery stores.
YOUR CHOICE(C) the tastes of coffee experts who judge at tasting competitions are different from the tastes of the general coffee drinking public. This answer choice is very temping, as it seems to follow from what the passage says. The judges say the coffee doesn’t taste good but people buy it anyway. Maybe people who buy coffee don’t have the same taste in coffee that judges have. The truth is though that this conclusion is not really supported by what the argument says, as there could be many reasons other than taste for the popularity of Smithtown Roast, reasons such as price, marketing, or convenience.
(D) coffee tasting competitions tend to include all of the best-selling coffee brands. While the passage supports the conclusion at least one best-selling brand, Smithtown Roast, has been found in coffee tasting competitions, in saying that “coffee tasting competitions tend to include all of the best-selling coffee brands” this answer choice goes beyond what is supported.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) the best-selling brand of coffee is not necessarily the best tasting brand. This answer synthesizes the information in the premises. It offers a logically justified yet unstated final point: a brand of coffee’s sales is not necessarily related to the quality of its taste. Notice that we could do the following:
Smithtown Roast is the best-selling brand of coffee in grocery stores. However, Smithtown Roast is consistently rated poorly by coffee experts during coffee-tasting competitions. Therefore, the best-selling brand of coffee is not necessarily the best tasting brand.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss how to recognize a Find the Conclusion question.
summary:
Lesson Content Find the Conclusion questions will be based on passages that always contain premises (evidence), and, from time to time, include background information or an intermediate conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
An argument’s conclusion must be closely connected to its premises, assumptions, and any intermediary conclusions that may be present.
It’s important to keep in mind that a conclusion must follow from only the information presented in the argument and any assumptions made by the argument.
title: 8.1.1 Finding the Conclusion of an Argument

contents:
Lesson Content The question stem for a Find the Conclusion question will ask us to find the best conclusion to an argument among the five possible answer choices.
Which of the following conclusions is best supported by the information above?
The most reasonable conclusion based on the premises above is that
If the statements above are all true, they most strongly support which of the following conclusions?
The information above provides the most support for which of the following hypotheses?
The experimental results support which of the following hypotheses?
Which of the following is most strongly supported by the statements given?
Which one of the following most accurately states the main point of the argument?
Which of the following most logically concludes the argument?
Which one of the following best expresses the main point of the argument?
Which of the following most accurately expresses the author’s main idea in the argument?
The author is arguing that
💡MUST KNOW:
Be able to quickly recognize Find the Conclusion questions.
Consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 3 In legislative elections in Country Z, the incumbent usually wins re-election when the economy in that legislator’s district is doing well. The statements above are logically structured to lead to which of the following conclusions? 1. [ ] (A) In Country Z, if the economy in a district is doing poorly in an election year, the incumbent legislator in that district probably will not be reelected. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) In Country Z, the economic expertise of a legislator is the factor that has the most impact on whether that legislator will be reelected. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) In Country Z, any incumbents who have been reelected when economic conditions have been poor must have been in “safe” districts structured to make it easier for incumbents to win. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) In Country Z, when economic conditions are poor, the impact on electoral outcomes of candidates’ qualifications will tend to be greater than it will be when economic conditions are strong. 5. [ ] (E) Voter turnout in Country Z can be expected to be greater for elections held when the economy is doing poorly than for those held when the economy is doing well. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: hard This argument has two premises:
Premise 1: When the economy is doing well, the incumbent in a district usually re-elected.
Premise 2: When the economy is not doing well, or when people are worried about it, the incumbent does not have this advantage.
The correct answer will find some way to combine these two pieces of information to lead to a final point that logically follows from them. Remember, we can visualize this problem as:
In legislative elections in Country Z, the incumbent usually wins re-election when the economy in that legislator’s district is doing well. However, when the economy is doing poorly or when a majority of citizens in a legislator’s district express concerns about the strength of the economy, simply being the incumbent no longer provides an advantage. Therefore, The correct answer must logically fit into the space after “therefore.”
YOUR CHOICE(A) In Country Z, if the economy in a district is doing poorly in an election year, the incumbent legislator in that district probably will not be reelected. This answer goes too far. The premises state that, when the economy is not doing well, an incumbent no longer has an advantage. However, lacking an advantage is not the same as losing. The passage gives us no reason to believe that when economic conditions in a district are poor, an incumbent will probably lose.
(B) In Country Z, the economic expertise of a legislator is the factor that has the most impact on whether that legislator will be reelected. This choice goes beyond the information provided in the premises. The premises describe a relationship between reelection of legislators and the condition of the economy. The fact that that relationship exists does not mean that the economic expertise of a legislator is the factor that has the most impact on whether that legislator will be reelected. So, this statement might be true, but, since it adds “economic expertise,” which is only loosely related to what the premises discuss, and says “factor that has the most impact,” making an unsupported comparison between factors, it goes beyond what is supported by the premises.
(C) In Country Z, any incumbents who have been reelected when economic conditions have been poor must have been in “safe” districts structured to make it easier for incumbents to win. This conclusion, essentially that a specific single factor, “safe” districts, underlies all cases of legislators being reelected in Country Z when economic conditions are poor, goes well beyond what is supported by the premises. The premises indicate that incumbency is not an advantage when economic conditions are poor, but do not indicate that “safe” districts or any other factor is the only factor that matters when incumbency is not an advantage.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) In Country Z, when economic conditions are poor, the impact on electoral outcomes of candidates’ qualifications will tend to be greater than it will be when economic conditions are strong. This answer synthesizes the information in the premises and offers a logically justified but unstated final point. If incumbency is a great advantage in elections when the economy of a district is doing well but provides no advantage when economic conditions are poor, then it stands to reason that, when economic conditions are poor, the impact on electoral outcomes of candidates’ qualifications will tend to be greater than it will be when economic conditions are strong, because, when economic conditions are strong, incumbency tends to override any other factor, such as candidates’ qualifications.
(E) Voter turnout in Country Z can be expected to be greater for elections held when the economy is doing poorly than for those held when the economy is doing well. This answer might well be true, but it goes beyond the information provided in the premises. The premises never mention voter turnout or what factors might influence it, and the fact that economic factors influence election outcomes does not clearly indicate that more voters turn out when economic conditions are poor than turn out when the economy is doing well.
Notice that the full argument is as follows:
In legislative elections in Country Z, the incumbent usually wins re-election when the economy in that legislator’s district is doing well. However, when the economy is doing poorly or when a majority of citizens in a legislator’s district express concerns about the strength of the economy, simply being the incumbent no longer provides an advantage. Therefore, in Country Z, when economic conditions are poor, the impact on electoral outcomes of candidates’ qualifications will tend to be greater than it will be when economic conditions are strong.
Correct Answer: (D) At this point, you’re already getting much stronger in answering Find the Conclusion questions.
summary:
Lesson Content The question stem for a Find the Conclusion question will ask us to find the best conclusion to an argument among the five possible answer choices.
Which of the following conclusions is best supported by the information above?
The most reasonable conclusion based on the premises above is that
If the statements above are all true, they most strongly support which of the following conclusions?
The information above provides the most support for which of the following hypotheses?
title: 8.2 Spotting a Find the Conclusion Question

contents:
Lesson Content As there are for answering Critical Reasoning questions of other types, there are best practices that we can follow when answering Find the Conclusion questions.
Step 1 – Carefully Read and Completely Understand the Passage
The foundation of choosing the right answer to a Conclusion question is understanding the passage.
Step 2 – Identify the Question Type (What type of information is the question asking for?)
Read the question stem to determine what role the correct answer to the question must play.
“Which of the following most logically concludes the argument?”
Step 3 – Carefully Analyze the Argument
Once you’ve determined that you’re working on a Find the Conclusion question, your next job is to analyze the argument presented in the stimulus.
Step 4 – Eliminate Wrong Answers
Once we have analyzed the argument, we go through the answer choices, carefully asking, “Does this answer follow logically from the statements, and is it the best possible conclusion to the argument?” Use the obvious to your advantage: there can only be one correct answer choice.
💡MUST KNOW:
Use the obvious to your advantage: there can be only one correct answer choice. One of the answers will be the best conclusion to the argument.
In a Find the Conclusion question, there are usually two, perhaps even three, answer choices that we can eliminate relatively quickly because they make statements that are too general, focus on only one sentence of the argument, make extreme logical leaps, conflict with all or part of the argument, or simply say something that is relatively obviously not supported by the passage.
It’s worth writing “A, B, C, D, E” on your dry erase pad.
💡MUST KNOW:
To avoid eliminating an answer choice only mistakenly to choose it as correct, write out “A, B, C, D, E” on your dry erase pad. As you eliminate the choices that are obviously wrong, physically cross them out.
Now – as you already know, one of the most common mistakes that people make is to eliminate the correct answer in their initial assessment of the answer choices.
💡MUST KNOW:
One of the most common mistakes that people make is to eliminate the correct answer in their initial assessment of the answer choices. So, even after you’ve crossed off an answer, you must stay open to the fact that you may have crossed off the correct answer. Always be willing to reassess the situation.
Step 5 – Carefully Analyze the Final Answer Choices
Of the answers remaining, the correct answer must do the best job of capturing the author’s logical conclusion to the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
In a Find the Conclusion question, the correct answer will be the choice that most logically follows from the specific evidence provided in the argument.
At this point, one fundamental error that many people make is to simply re-read the two final choices and rely on “gut instinct” as to which is correct.
💡MUST KNOW:
When you are down to two answers, you must not become complacent and make your best guess. No way. Now is the time to shine, to turn the jets to overdrive, and to relentlessly seek out the correct answer.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 4 English Dictionary Editor: Many grammarians have recently stated that our dictionary should not have changed the definition of “they” to allow it to be used as a gender neutral singular pronoun on the grounds that using it in such a way is grammatically incorrect. Which of the following most logically concludes the dictionary editor’s argument? 1. [ ] (A) Contrary to the grammarians’ claim, the dictionary’s new definition of “they” is not grammatically incorrect. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Many other languages have a gender neutral, singular pronoun that is similar to the new definition of “they.” > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Grammarians eventually accept most changes to the ways in which words are used as grammatically correct. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) The grammarians were incorrect to claim that the dictionary should not have changed the definition of “they.” 5. [ ] (E) All dictionaries should change their definitions of “they” to allow it to be a gender neutral, singular pronoun. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: hard Let’s use the five-step process on this question.
Step 1 – Carefully Read and Completely Understand the Passage
At this point we have carefully read and understood what the passage says.
Step 2 – Identify the Question Type
The question stem asks: “Which of the following most logically concludes the dictionary editor’s argument?” We clearly see that we’re working with a Find the Conclusion question.
Step 3 – Carefully Analyze the Argument
