Evaluate the Argument questions present a plan, a proposal, or an argument and then ask us to find an answer that highlights information that helps us to evaluate whether the plan or proposal will work or whether the conclusion of the argument is valid.
title: 9.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content Many Evaluate the Argument questions can be solved via the use of what’s called the Yes/No test.
In many cases, the answer choices in Evaluate the Argument questions pose questions that can be answered in two ways, either “Yes” or “No.”
In such a case, the correct answer to an Evaluate the Argument Question must be a question such that, if it is answered “Yes”, the argument is affected in one way (either strengthened or weakened), and, if it is answered “No,” the argument is affected in the reverse way.
💡MUST KNOW:
In many cases, the answer choices in Evaluate the Argument questions pose questions that can be answered in two ways, either “Yes” or “No.” In such a case, the correct answer to an Evaluate the Argument Question must be a question such that, if it is answered “Yes”, the argument is affected in one way (either strengthened or weakened), and, if it is answered “No,” the argument is affected in the reverse way.
For instance, if the “Yes” answer strengthens the argument, then the “No” answer will weaken the argument, and, if the “Yes” answer weakens the argument, then the “No” answer will strengthen it.
On the other hand, in the case of a wrong answer, whether the response to the question posed by the answer is “Yes” or “No,” the answer has no effect on the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
When using the “Yes/No Test” on Evaluate the Argument questions, in the case of a wrong answer, whether the response to the question posed by the answer is “Yes” or “No,” the answer has no effect on the argument. In other words, a “Yes” makes us say “So what?” Similarly, a “No” makes us say “So what?”
Considered in the context of an example, this concept will be much easier to understand.
All people in houses in which communicable diseases are present should be quarantined. Two people in the yellow house on Maple Street came down with disease x yesterday. Therefore, all people in the yellow house on Maple Street should be quarantined.
Which of the following pieces of information would be most useful in evaluating the logic of the argument presented above?
(A) Whether disease x is a communicable disease (B) Whether people in a neighboring house have been infected by disease x
Let’s begin with answer (A).
What happens to the argument if the answer to the question is “Yes”? In that case, the argument is sound; it makes sense to quarantine the people in the yellow house on Maple Street.
For (B), what if the answer is “Yes”? Who cares? The fact that people in a neighboring house have been infected by disease x does not impact this argument.
Let’s look at another example with which you already have some experience:
Factory pollution has resulted in a reduction in the axolotl population that once thrived in Wattachen Lake. When present even in small amounts, chemical runoff from the factory causes reproductive problems for the axolotls. Experts recommend that the factory be re-located elsewhere in order to restore the ecological conditions in and surrounding Wattachen Lake. Once the factory is gone, they claim, the axolotl population will certainly return to its original pre-factory levels within two years.
In evaluating the argument, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?
(A) Whether axolotls ever live for longer than ten years
(B) Whether the factory pollutants in the water will begin to disintegrate only several years after removal of the factory
The experts’ argument is essentially the following:
Relocate factory.
What is one assumption made by the author? One major assumption is that this chemical runoff from the factory will not persist in the water after the factory has been relocated.
What if the answer to (A) is “Yes”? Is the support for the conclusion affected? No.
Now, let’s look at answer (B).
Let’s look at another one:
A survey has found that parents who smoke are more likely than parents who don’t smoke to have children who are involved in juvenile delinquency. Therefore, it’s clear that parental smoking causes children to engage in destructive behavior.
The answer to which of the following would be most useful in evaluating the argument?
(A) Whether some of the children who most actively engage in destructive behavior have parents who do not smoke
(B) Whether living in difficult or stressful circumstances can lead parents to smoke and children to engage in destructive behavior
The author concludes that there is a cause-and-effect relationship between parental smoking and juvenile delinquency.
If the answer to (A) is “Yes,” and, thus, some of the children who most actively engage in destructive behavior have parents who do not smoke, is the support for the conclusion affected? No.
If the answer to (B) is “Yes,” and living in difficult or stressful circumstances can lead parents to smoke and children to engage in destructive behavior, then the argument is weakened, because it may be the case that living in difficult or stressful circumstances causes people to smoke and causes juvenile delinquency, but there is no cause-and-effect link between smoking and juvenile delinquency.
💡MUST KNOW:
Consider using the Yes/No test to solve Evaluate the Argument questions.
To ensure that your learning is smooth, let’s look at an example that you’ve already seen, but this time it’s in the form of an Evaluate the Argument question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 3 Side by side tests of fruits and vegetables grown using conventional and organic methods have found no significant differences between the levels of key nutrients in the conventionally grown produce and the levels of those nutrients in the organically grown produce. In order to evaluate the argument, it would be most useful to determine which of the following? 1. [ ] (A) Whether fruits and vegetables that are grown using organic methods taste different from those grown using conventional methods > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Whether plants take in nutrients from the soil as a necessary aspect of their growth processes > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Whether conventionally grown produce contains significant levels of health harming chemicals that organically grown produce does not contain 4. [ ] (D) Whether the differences between the prices of most organically grown produce and those of most conventionally grown produce are justified > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Whether the methods used in farming organically grown produce are more labor intensive than those used in farming conventionally grown produce > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: Side by side tests have found no significant differences between levels of certain nutrients in organically grown produce and the levels of those nutrients in conventionally grown produce.
Conclusion: There is no reason to believe that there is any health benefit to be derived from eating organically grown produce instead of conventionally grown produce.
The argument goes from one specific fact about differences between organically grown produce and conventionally grown produce to concluding that there is no reason to believe that there is any health benefit to be derived from eating organically grown produce rather than conventionally grown produce.
Coming to that conclusion from that one fact requires assuming that the only way that eating organically grown produce could be better for health than eating conventionally grown produce is through the organically grown produce having greater levels of certain nutrients.
Information indicating that there is in fact a way in which eating organically grown produce could be better for health than eating conventionally grown produce would weaken the argument.
Information ruling out a possible way in which eating organically grown produce rather than conventionally grown produce could be beneficial for health would strengthen the argument.
(A) Whether fruits and vegetables that are grown using organic methods taste different from those grown using conventional methods The argument is specifically about the health benefits of eating organically grown produce rather than conventionally grown produce. Information indicating that “Yes,” the two types taste different or that “No,” they don’t taste different does not affect the conclusion about health benefits.
(B) Whether plants take in nutrients from the soil as a necessary aspect of their growth processes The conclusion is based on the levels of nutrients present in the types of produce. Regardless of whether “Yes,” plants take in nutrients from the soil as a necessary aspect of their growth processes or “No,” they don’t, the nutrients in the produce and the health benefits associated with eating the produce remain the same.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Whether conventionally grown produce contains significant levels of health harming chemicals that organically grown produce does not contain Concluding that, since the levels of certain nutrients are the same in the two types of produce, eating organic produce is not better for health requires assuming that eating conventionally grown produce is not somehow harmful to health in a way that eating organically grown produce is not. So, if the answer to the question posed by this choice is “Yes, conventionally grown produce contains significant levels of health harming chemicals that organically grown produce does not contain,” then the argument is seriously weakened, as that answer indicates that there is reason to believe that, contrary to what the conclusion says, there is reason to believe that eating organically grown produce is better for health than is eating conventionally grown produce. On the other hand, if the answer is “No, conventionally grown produce does not contain significant levels of such chemicals,” then the support for the conclusion is made even stronger, as with this information we can rule out a possible way in which eating organically grown produce could be better for health than is eating conventionally grown produce.
(D) Whether the differences between the prices of most organically grown produce and those of most conventionally grown produce are justified Information on whether differences between prices of the two types of produce are justified does not affect the conclusion, which is about the effects on health of eating the types of produce. So, neither a “Yes” answer nor a “No” answer to the question posed by this choice affects the argument.
(E) Whether the methods used in farming organically grown produce are more labor intensive than those used in farming conventionally grown produce Since the degree of use of labor in farming the two types of produce does not affect the health related effects of eating the produce, neither a “Yes” answer nor a “No” answer to the question posed by this choice would affect the conclusion.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now look at some common question stems for Evaluate the Argument questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Many Evaluate the Argument questions can be solved via the use of what’s called the Yes/No test.
In many cases, the answer choices in Evaluate the Argument questions pose questions that can be answered in two ways, either “Yes” or “No.”
In such a case, the correct answer to an Evaluate the Argument Question must be a question such that, if it is answered “Yes”, the argument is affected in one way (either strengthened or weakened), and, if it is answered “No,” the argument is affected in the reverse way.
title: 9.2 The Yes/No Test for Evaluate the Argument Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Evaluate the Argument questions use predictable language, such as:
In evaluating the argument, it would be most useful to establish which of the following?
Which of the following pieces of information would be most useful in evaluating the logic of the argument presented above?
Which of the following would be most useful in determining whether the conclusion is valid?
To evaluate the strength of the argument, it would be most helpful to know which of the following?
In order to evaluate the force of the evidence presented, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?
In assessing whether the data cited provide support for the position taken, it would be most useful to determine which of the following? Which of the following is most likely to yield information that would help in the evaluation of the researcher’s hypothesis?
Which of the following is most likely to yield information that would help in the evaluation of the effectiveness of the new method?
In deciding whether the plan will work as predicted, it would be most helpful to determine which of the following?
The answer to which of the following questions would be most useful for evaluating the likelihood of success of these initiatives?
In determining the impact of implementing the new strategy, it would be important to research which of the following?
In assessing the plan’s chances of achieving its aim, it would be most useful to know which of the following?
In relation to the claim above, the answer to which of the following questions would be most valuable in assessing the significance of the study?
In evaluating the argument, it would be most useful to compare
Which of the following, if true, would be most important to take into account in evaluating the result?
Investigating which of the following would be most useful in choosing between the competing explanations described above?
💡MUST KNOW:
Review and become familiar with the common question stems that are indicative of Evaluate the Argument Questions.
Let’s practice applying what we just learned.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 4 Each year, Steam and Beans holds its annual coffee contest in which coffee roasters from around the country enter their coffees to compete for the title of best new coffee. In evaluating the argument, it would be most useful to establish which of the following? 1. [ ] (A) Whether Witchtown Roasters’ coffee is the most expensive coffee that will be entered into the Steam and Beans coffee contest this year > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Whether some of the coffee roasters that have entered but never won the Steam and Beans coffee contest will decide not to enter coffees into the contest this year > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Whether Witchtown Roasters will enter its coffee into the Steam and Beans coffee contest this year 4. [ ] (D) Whether the people who own and operate Steam and Beans enjoy the taste and aroma of Witchtown Roasters’ coffee > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Whether the Steam and Beans coffee contest will be judged by spectators who attend the event or by coffee experts who will serve as formal judges > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy Notice that the question stem makes clear to us that this question is an Evaluate the Argument question. In answering this question, our job will be to determine which answer choice mentions the thing that would be most useful to establish for the purpose of evaluating the argument.
Background: Each year, Steam and Beans holds its annual coffee contest in which coffee roasters from around the country enter their coffees to compete for the title of best new coffee.
Premise: Witchtown Roasters has won that title every year for the last five years.
Conclusion: Witchtown Roasters will win the title again this year.
Does the fact that Witchtown Roasters has won the title every year for the last five years allow the author to conclude that Witchtown Roasters will win the title again this year? In making the argument, what assumptions is the author making? What information might help us to determine whether those assumptions are sound?
(A) Whether Witchtown Roasters’ coffee is the most expensive coffee that will be entered into the Steam and Beans coffee contest this year What if Witchtown Roasters’ coffee IS the most expensive coffee that will be entered into the Steam and Beans coffee contest this year? Would that fact allow us to evaluate the argument? No, because we are not provided with any information that indicates that the prices of the coffees have any bearing on whether a coffee will win the contest this year.
Maybe it is true that the most expensive coffee entered into the contest always wins. However, maybe it’s true that the least expensive always wins. Or, perhaps there is no correlation whatsoever between the prices of the coffees and which coffee wins.
We must resist the urge to make any unwarranted assumptions.
(B) Whether some of the coffee roasters that have entered but never won the Steam and Beans coffee contest will decide not to enter coffees into the contest this year What if some of the coffee roasters that have entered but never won the Steam and Beans coffee contest will decide not to enter coffees into the contest this year? Would that change make it more or less likely that Witchtown Roasters’ coffee will win the contest this year? The effect is not clear, right?
Similarly, what if all of the coffee roasters that have entered but never won the Steam and Beans coffee contest will enter coffees into the contest again this year? Would that fact make it any more or less likely that Witchtown Roasters’ coffee will win the contest this year? The effect is not clear, right?
If you chose this choice, you may have done so because you made up a story such as that, if the roasters that have not won drop out of the contest, new roasters that might beat Witchtown Roasters will enter in place of the roasters that dropped out. However, a story like that one is not supported by the information we have. For all we know from what the passage says, if some roasters that have never won drop out, their dropping out won’t make much of a difference at all. After all, these roasters have never won anyway.
We have to be careful not to take information like the information provided by this choice and make up a story that seems to affect the support for the conclusion of the argument but isn’t really supported by the information we have.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Whether Witchtown Roasters will enter its coffee into the Steam and Beans coffee contest this year If Witchtown Roasters will enter its coffee into the Steam and Beans coffee contest this year, then the author’s argument is pretty solid. In fact, that information would make her argument stronger because it would serve to eliminate the possibility that Witchtown Roasters will not even enter its coffee into the Steam and Beans coffee contest this year, in which case the conclusion would clearly be invalid.
However, if Witchtown Roasters will NOT enter its coffee into the Steam and Beans coffee contest this year, then the argument falls apart, because, if Witchtown Roasters’ coffee is not even entered into the contest this year, it would not be possible for Witchtown Roasters to win the contest this year.
Notice that what this answer choice says allows us to determine whether an assumption made by the author is sound or unsound, allowing us to evaluate the validity of author’s argument. In other words, if we look back at the argument, we can see that the author assumes that Witchtown Roasters WILL actually enter its coffee into the Steam and Beans coffee contest this year.
(D) Whether the people who own and operate Steam and Beans enjoy the taste and aroma of Witchtown Roasters’ coffee Notice that the passage does not say that the people who own and operate Steam and Beans have anything to do with which coffee wins the contest. So, for all we know from what the passage says, whether the people who own and operate Steam and Beans enjoy the taste and aroma of Witchtown Roaster’s coffee has absolutely no impact on whether that coffee will win the contest.
Furthermore, it could be that the people who own and operate Steam and Beans enjoy the taste and aroma of all the coffees that are entered into the contest, and that, therefore, the fact that they enjoy the taste and aroma of a coffee does not mean that that coffee will win the contest.
We have no information indicating that, if the people who own and operate Steam and Beans’ enjoy a coffee, that coffee is likely to win the contest. So, we have no reason to believe that establishing whether the people who own and operate Steam and Beans enjoy the taste and aroma of Witchtown Roasters’ coffee will help us to evaluate the argument.
(E) Whether the Steam and Beans coffee contest will be judged by spectators who attend the event or by coffee experts who will serve as formal judges Notice that we do not have any information on how the contest’s being judged by either spectators or coffee experts would impact which coffee will win.
For example, if the contest were judged by spectators would it be any more or less likely that Witchtown Roasters’ coffee will win or lose the contest this year? We can’t say.
Alternatively, if the contest were judged by coffee experts would it be any more or less likely that Witchtown Roasters’ coffee will win or lose the contest this year? We can’t say.
We must resist the urge to make unwarranted assumptions.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 5 People often blame increased consumption of high calorie foods for the increase in the percentage of people considered obese that has occurred over the past 100 years. The answer to which of the following questions would be most helpful for evaluating the validity of the above conclusion? 1. [ ] (A) Is it the case that most people who consume greater quantities of high calorie foods than people generally consumed in the past do jobs that involve little physical activity? > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Is the percentage of people in jobs that involve significant physical activity who are obese significantly greater today than it was 100 years ago? 3. [ ] (C) Has the percentage of people considered obese increased steadily over the past 100 years? > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Over the past 100 years have any medical methods that are effective in countering weight gain become available? > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Are there currently any people who are both in jobs that involve little physical activity and not obese? > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard The passage ends with the following conclusion:
Conclusion: It is the trend away from doing jobs that involve physical activity and toward doing jobs that involve little physical activity, rather than an increase in consumption of high calorie foods, that is responsible for the increase in the percentage of people considered obese.
The passage mentions two possible causes for the increase in the percentage of people considered obese: increased consumption of high calorie foods and a trend away from doing jobs that involve physical activity and toward doing jobs that involve little physical activity. The conclusion is that the second possible cause, the trend away from doing jobs that involve physical activity, is the one responsible for the increase in obesity. However, the passage provides no information that supports the conclusion.
The correct answer will express a question such that, depending on how the question is answered, the answer will provide support for the conclusion or provide a reason to question the validity of the conclusion.
(A) Is it the case that most people who consume greater quantities of high calorie foods than people generally consumed in the past do jobs that involve little physical activity? No matter how this question is answered, the answer does not provide information that connects or disconnects the trend in obesity to either the trend in consumption of high calorie foods or the trend away from doing jobs that involve significant physical activity. All we can get from a “Yes” or “No” answer to this question is some sense of the degree to which people who do jobs involving little physical activity consume greater quantities of high calorie foods than people generally consumed in the past. That information does not help us to understand which of the two lifestyle trends is connected to the increase in the percentage of people considered obese.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Is the percentage of people in jobs that involve significant physical activity who are obese significantly greater today than it was 100 years ago? The answer to this question tells us what happens when we eliminate the variable “the trend away from doing jobs that involve significant physical activity,” because the question is about only people in jobs that involve significant physical activity.
So, if the answer to this question is “Yes, the percentage of people in jobs that involve significant physical activity who are obese is significantly greater today than it was 100 years ago,” then the answer weakens the argument, as, if even the percentage of people in jobs that involve significant physical activity who are obese is increasing, then there must be some factor other than lack of work related physical activity that is causing an increase in the percentage of people considered obese.
On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “No, the percentage of people in jobs that involve significant physical activity who are obese is not significantly greater today than it was 100 years ago,” then the answer strengthens the argument by showing that, when the suspected cause, the trend away from doing jobs that involve signidficant physical activity, is not present, the effect, an increase in the percentage of people considered obese, is not present either.
(C) Has the percentage of people considered obese increased steadily over the past 100 years? Since there is no reason to believe that either increased consumption of high calorie foods or a trend away from doing jobs that involve significant physical activity would result in either a steady or not steady increase in the percentage of people considered obese, neither a “Yes” answer nor a “No” answer to this question points to a particular cause of the increased rate of obesity.
(D) Over the past 100 years have any medical methods that are effective in countering weight gain become available? This question addresses the wrong conclusion. Regardless of whether medical methods for countering weight gain have become available, people are more likely to be obese today than they were 100 years ago. So, the answer to this question does not affect the argument.
(E) Are there currently any people who are both in jobs that involve little physical activity and not obese? The answer to this question does not allow us to separate the effects of the two trends mentioned. Regardless of whether the answer to this question is that there are or are not people who do jobs that require little physical activity who are not obese, the answer gives us no way of determining whether consumption of high calorie foods or lack of physical activity is the cause of obesity among those who are obese and do jobs involving little physical activity. If, for instance, the answer is “No, there are no people who do jobs that involve little physical activity and are not obese,” we still don’t have information indicating whether the cause of everyone who does jobs involving little physical activity being obese is lack of physical activity or consumption of high calorie foods.
Correct Answer: (B) You already have a good understanding of how to solve Evaluate the Argument questions, but to enhance your ability to get these questions correct, let’s now discuss a formal strategy for attacking Evaluate the Argument questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Evaluate the Argument questions use predictable language, such as:
In evaluating the argument, it would be most useful to establish which of the following?
Which of the following pieces of information would be most useful in evaluating the logic of the argument presented above?
Which of the following would be most useful in determining whether the conclusion is valid?
To evaluate the strength of the argument, it would be most helpful to know which of the following?
title: 9.3 Common Question Stems for Evaluate the Argument Questions

contents:
Lesson Content To solve Evaluate the Argument Questions, follow this strategy:
Step 1 – Carefully Read, Break Down, and Completely Understand the Argument
In an Evaluate the Argument question, the passage will present a plan, proposal, or argument.
So, your first job in answering an Evaluate the Argument question is to carefully and methodically read the passage to determine what the point of the passage is and how any argument made in the passage is supported.
💡MUST KNOW:
The foundation for getting the right answer to an Evaluate the Argument question is understanding the plan, proposal, or argument presented in the passage.
In reading the passage of an Evaluate the Argument question, you will generally find two main components of an argument:
Conclusion – Since correctly answering an Evaluate the Argument question requires identifying an assumption upon which the conclusion of the argument depends, identifying with precision the conclusion of the argument is paramount to correctly answering the question. Evidence – We must also determine how the argument uses evidence to support the conclusion. Remember that the conclusion and any evidence presented are always explicitly stated in the passage in an Evaluate the Argument question.
Assumptions – Of course, in these questions, there will be missing information that must be true if the logic of the argument is to work. It can be helpful to make short, organized notes on the scratch pad, using abbreviations and symbols.
Remember - Critical Reasoning questions are not word matching or pattern identification questions.
Step 2 – Read the Question Stem and Identify What it is Asking You to Do
Read the question stem.
(Note: Some people prefer to read Critical Reasoning question stems before they read the passages.
Step 3 – Remove the Most Clearly Incorrect Answer Choices by Using the Yes/No Test or Other Methods.
In an Evaluate the Argument question, there are usually three answer choices that relatively clearly do not highlight information that would help us to evaluate the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
The correct answer to an Evaluate the Argument question highlights additional information that has the potential to strengthen or weaken the argument. Any choice that does not highlight such information is incorrect.
Notice something here.
On your first pass through the answer choices, keep choices that you are not sure about, even if doing so means that you are left with three or more choices with which to work.
It also makes sense to be open to revisiting answer choices that you have eliminated.
💡MUST KNOW:
Eliminating answer choices that are clearly not right is not the same as eliminating choices that you are not sure about. On your first pass through the answer choices, keep choices that you are not sure about. Also, be open to revisiting answer choices that you have eliminated. If you are having trouble finding an answer choice that works, it may be that you have eliminated the correct answer.
As you read each choice, seek to demonstrate how the information that it highlights affects the support for the argument’s conclusion or that the information does not affect the support.
The information highlighted by four out of the five answer choices will not affect the support for the argument’s conclusion at all, and often you can prove that it does not.
💡MUST KNOW:
As you read each choice, seek to demonstrate how the information that it highlights affects the argument or that the information does not affect the argument.
The key to getting Critical Reasoning questions right is to SEE.
💡MUST KNOW:
It is much better to SEE WHY the information highlighted by an answer choice cannot affect an argument than to say that you don’t see how it could. In the former case, you are eliminating the choice because you see something. In the latter case you are eliminating it because you do not see something.
At all times in answering GMAT questions of any type you must seek to SEE as much as possible.
If you find that you are not seeing reasons to eliminate or keep choices, it may help to go back to the passage and look through it to see what it might say that you have yet to see in a way that results in your choosing the correct answer.
Step 4 – Choose the Answer Choice That Is Most Clearly What We Would Want to Know in Order to Properly Evaluate the Argument. After eliminating answer choices that are clearly incorrect, you are at the final stage of getting the right answer to an Evaluate the Argument question.
💡MUST KNOW:
Memorize and practice the 4-step strategy for attacking Evaluate the Argument questions.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 6 Since, in order for polar bears to effectively hunt seals, their main food source, polar bears must use arctic sea ice as a platform, and, since, as global temperatures increase, arctic sea ice is disappearing, many people are concerned that polar bears will die out. Which of the following would it be most useful to establish in order to evaluate the argument? 1. [x] (A) Whether the caloric needs of polar bears can be satisfied via the bears’ consumption of things other than seals 2. [ ] (B) Whether the seal population will increase if polar bears are unable to use sea ice as a platform for hunting seals > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Whether arctic animals other than polar bears have successfully adjusted their eating habits in response to climate related environmental changes > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Whether it is likely that humans will change their activities in such a way as to cause global temperature increases to slow or cease > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Whether there are animals that can effectively hunt for seals without using sea ice as a platform > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy Step 1 – Carefully Read, Break Down, and Completely Understand the Argument
Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: Polar bears must use arctic sea ice as a platform for hunting seals.
Background: Arctic sea ice is disappearing, and therefore people are concerned that polar bears will die out.
Premise: Polar bears have been observed eating things other than seals.
Conclusion: Even if sea ice disappears entirely, polar bears will survive.
In this argument, the conclusion that polar bears will survive is based on the fact that polar bears have been observed eating things other than seals.
Step 2 – Read the Question Stem and Identify What it is Asking You to Do
Since the question stem is asking us to find a choice that highlights information that helps with evaluating the argument, it’s an Evaluate the Argument question.
Step 3 – Remove the Most Clearly Incorrect Answer Choices by Using the Yes/No Test Or Other Methods
(A) Keep. This choice is interesting. If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then we have some confirmation that polar bears could survive by consuming things other than seals. If the answer is “No,” then we have information that weakens the argument, because, if the caloric needs of polar bears cannot be satisfied via the bears’ consumption of things other than seals, then the conclusion is likely incorrect. Notice that this choice highlights an assumption on which the support for the conclusion depends, that the bears’ caloric needs can be satisfied via the bears’ consumption of things other than seals.
(B) Whether the seal population will increase if polar bears are unable to use sea ice as a platform for hunting seals This choice is a little tempting, because we might think that, if the answer is “Yes,” then maybe there will be so many seals that the polar bears will be able to eat them even without sea ice to use as a platform for hunting. However, given that the passage says that, in order for polar bears to effectively hunt seals, polar bears must use arctic sea ice as a platform, it seems highly unlikely that an abundance of seals would save the polar bears.
(C) Keep. This choice is interesting. If the answer is “No,” then maybe, for some reason, arctic animals in general have difficulty changing their eating habits. If the answer is “Yes,” then perhaps this choice helps to confirm that polar bears can do the same.
(D) Whether it is likely that humans will change their activities in such a way as to cause global temperature increases to slow or cease This choice could be temping, because one could perceive a “Yes,” answer as providing reason to believe that polar bears will survive. However, the point of the argument is not that polar bears will survive in general. The conclusion is that polar bears can survive even if arctic sea ice disappears entirely. Information indicating that the sea ice will not disappear would not strengthen this conclusion, which is about what will happen if sea ice does disappear.
(E) Whether there are animals that can effectively hunt for seals without using sea ice as a platform Regardless of whether the answer to the question posed by this choice is “Yes” or “No,” we already know from the passage that polar bears must have arctic ice in order to hunt effectively. Even if other animals can hunt seals without sea ice, polar bears won’t do so, and, if other animals don’t hunt seals without using sea ice, we don’t have information that affects the support for the conclusion. Thus, neither a “Yes” answer nor a “No” answer to this question provides information that affects the support for the conclusion.
Step 4 – Choose the Answer Choice That Is Most Clearly What We Would Want to Know in order to Properly Evaluate the Argument.
Choice (A) clearly matters here. If the answer is “Yes,” we have some additional support for the conclusion that polar bears could survive without arctic ice by eating things other than seals. If the answer is “No,” then support for the conclusion that polar bears could survive even if arctic sea is disappears completely is essentially destroyed.
Choice (C) is interesting, but the truth is that, even if other arctic animals have not been able to adapt in this way, polar bears might be able to, and even if other animals have been able to adapt in this way, polar bears could easily be different from those animals in some key way that would keep them from surviving by changing their eating habits. So, the answer to this question doesn’t really tell us much.
Therefore, the correct answer is (A).
Correct Answer: (A) So far, we have discussed one method, using the Yes/No test, for handling the answer choices of Evaluate the Argument questions.
summary:
Lesson Content To solve Evaluate the Argument Questions, follow this strategy:
Step 1 – Carefully Read, Break Down, and Completely Understand the Argument
In an Evaluate the Argument question, the passage will present a plan, proposal, or argument.
So, your first job in answering an Evaluate the Argument question is to carefully and methodically read the passage to determine what the point of the passage is and how any argument made in the passage is supported.
title: 9.4 Strategy for Attacking Evaluate the Argument Questions

contents:
Lesson Content In Evaluate the Argument questions, there are four forms in which answer choices can appear:
Questions that can be answered “Yes” or “No” Questions that can be answered in two ways that are not “Yes” or “No” Questions of other types Statements, rather than questions We have already discussed how to handle answer choices that can be answered “Yes” or “No.” We use the Yes/No test and consider whether the “Yes” and “No” answers strengthen or weaken the arguments.
summary:
Lesson Content In Evaluate the Argument questions, there are four forms in which answer choices can appear:
Questions that can be answered “Yes” or “No” Questions that can be answered in two ways that are not “Yes” or “No” Questions of other types Statements, rather than questions We have already discussed how to handle answer choices that can be answered “Yes” or “No.” We use the Yes/No test and consider whether the “Yes” and “No” answers strengthen or weaken the arguments.
title: 9.5 Ways to Handle the Four Types of Answer Choices That Appear in Evaluate the Argument Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Many Evaluate the Argument answer choices allow us to apply the Yes/No test, because the choices pose questions that can be answered “Yes” or “No.” However, other answer choices can be structured in ways such that the Yes/No test doesn’t work if we apply the test literally.
Consider the following question:
What proportion of lower income New York City residents travel to Manhattan by subway rather than by car?
That question cannot be answered “Yes” or “No.” So, we couldn’t use the Yes/No test to handle it were it to appear as an answer choice.
Almost all lower income New York City residents travel to Manhattan by subway rather than by car. Only two percent of lower income New York City residents travel to Manhattan by subway rather than by car. Let’s see how extreme answers like these could be useful in answering an Evaluate the Argument question:
A proposed new law would mean that anyone driving a car into the Manhattan borough of New York City would have to pay an $11 fee. The revenue raised as a result of imposing the fee would be used to improve subway service going into Manhattan. However, the mayor asserts that imposing the fee would hurt the interests of the lower income residents of New York City, for whom the new fee would represent a significant outlay.
The answer to which of the following questions would be most useful for evaluating the accuracy of the mayor’s claim?
(A) What percentage of New York City residents are considered “lower income”?
(B) What proportion of lower income New York City residents travel to Manhattan by subway rather than by car?
OK, now let’s use extreme answers to determine which answer choice highlights the most useful information.
We could answer the question posed by choice (A) in the following two extreme ways:
10 percent. 90 percent. Does either extreme answer provide information that is useful for evaluating the accuracy of the mayor’s claim? No.
Now, here again are our extreme answers to the question posed by choice (B):
Almost all lower income New York City residents travel to Manhattan by subway rather than by car. Only two percent of lower income New York City residents travel to Manhattan by subway rather than by car. These two answers definitely provide information that we can use to evaluate the accuracy of the mayor’s claim.
So, by using extreme answers, we can determine that choice (B) is the correct answer to this question.
💡MUST KNOW:
In many cases, by using extreme answers to questions posed by Evaluate the Argument answer choices, one can determine which answer is correct.
Here are some examples of questions that can be answered in two extreme ways along with possible extreme answers:
Let’s practice applying what we just learned.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 7 For years, students at Dearborn High School have scored below the national average on standardized tests of math and science. In evaluating the argument, it would be most useful to have the answer to which of the following? 1. [ ] (A) What percentage of the students at Dearborn High School are currently enrolled in math or science courses? > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Do teachers at the Dearborn High School who teach English and History classes offer free after school tutoring in English and History? > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) How far below the national average have the math and science test scores of students at Dearborn High School been? > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Will the world recognized experts offering tutoring at some point also become full time teachers at Dearborn High School? > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) What percentage of the students at Dearborn High School are able to attend the free after school tutoring? View Solution Question Level: easy Background: For years, students at Dearborn High School have scored below the national average on standardized tests of math and science.
Premise: Dearborn High School recently began offering to all students enrolled in its math and science classes free after school tutoring with world recognized experts in the topics currently being taught in those classes.
Conclusion: Surely, this approach will help its math and science students improve their scores on standardized tests of math and science.
As we go through the answer choices that follow, we’ll see that many cannot be answered using yes/no responses. That’s ok! We will be creative in how we use each answer choice.
The author has presented a plan. Any time an author presents a plan, he assumes that the plan will work. In other words, he assumes that there are not variables that will enter into the equation that will spoil the plan.
In the TTP Course, we refer to these variables as “malicious variables. ”
Is the author’s assumption a good one?
(A) What percentage of the students at Dearborn High School are currently enrolled in math or science courses? As always, it is important to focus on the conclusion of the argument, which, in this case, is that the approach of offering after school tutoring will help Dearborn High School’s “math and science students improve their scores on standardized tests of math and science. ”
Notice that the conclusion is about the math and science students only. It is not about students who are not enrolled in math or science classes. So, in evaluating the argument we have to consider only the math and science students.
Now, let’s try answering the question posed by this choice in two different ways.
First let’s answer that 90 percent of Dearborn High School students are enrolled in math and science classes. Are we now any more or less convinced that offering those students after school tutoring will help them? No.
OK, what if the answer is that 10 percent of Dearborn High School students are enrolled in math and science classes. Does that information mean that the after school tutoring will or will not be helpful? No.
We can see that, regardless of what the answer to the question posed by this choice is, the answer does not indicate whether the tutoring will be helpful. So, the answer to this question would not help us to evaluate the argument.
(B) Do teachers at the Dearborn High School who teach English and History classes offer free after school tutoring in English and History? As always, it is important to pay attention to the conclusion of the argument, which, in this case, is that the approach of offering after school tutoring will help Dearborn High School’s “math and science students improve their scores on standardized tests of math and science. ”
Now, let’s answer this question in two different ways.
What if the answer is “Yes, teachers at the Dearborn High School who teach English and History classes offer free after school tutoring in English and History”? Does that fact mean that the physics and math tutoring will, or will not, help physics and math students to improve their standardized test scores? No.
Similarly, if the answer to this question is “No, the English and History teachers do not offer free tutoring,” that fact has no impact on whether the physics and math tutoring will help students to improve their standardized test scores.
So, the answer to the question posed by this choice does not help us to evaluate the argument.
(C) How far below the national average have the math and science test scores of students at Dearborn High School been? Let’s answer this question in two different ways and see whether either answer affects the support for the conclusion.
What if the answer to this question is “Far below”? Would that fact give us any more or less faith that the plan will work? Not at all, right? After all, the test scores of Dearborn High students could have been far below the national average, and the plan could still work perfectly, or not work at all.
What if the answer to this question is “Not much lower”? Would that fact give us any more or less faith that the plan will work? Not at all, right? After all, the test scores of Dearborn High students could be just a bit below the national average, and the plan could still work perfectly.
So, regardless of how we answer this question, the answer does not affect the support for the conclusion.
(D) Will the world recognized experts offering tutoring at some point also become full time teachers at Dearborn High School? What if, at some point, the world recognized experts who do the tutoring will become full time teachers at Dearborn High School? Would that fact make the argument any stronger or any weaker? No, because what the world recognized experts do at a future point in time has no bearing on the plan proposed by the school, which is to have these experts work as tutors. What these experts go on to do after they leave their jobs as tutors will not impact the plan.
YOUR CHOICE(E) What percentage of the students at Dearborn High School are able to attend the free after school tutoring? Let’s answer this question in two different ways.
First, what would happen to the argument if the answer to this question were “Zero percent”? The argument would fall apart, right? After all, the author’s conclusion is that “this approach will help its math and science students improve their scores on standardized tests of math and science. ” However, if none of the students at Dearborn High School are able to attend the free after school tutoring, then the plan falls apart.
Alternatively, what would happen to the argument if the answer to this question were “Almost 100 percent”? In this case, the argument would be stronger because the more students who can actually attend the after school tutoring, the more faith we have that the plan will actually work as described.
Since answers to the question posed by this choice can weaken or strengthen the argument, this choice is the correct answer.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 8 In many urban areas called food deserts, there are no local stores that offer large selections of fresh, healthy to eat food. The answer to which of the following questions would be most useful for evaluating the likelihood of success of these initiatives? 1. [ ] (A) What percentage of the population of cities that would offer the incentives is represented by people who reside in food desert type areas? > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) How many studies of the effectiveness of offering incentives to encourage the opening of large food stores with comprehensive selections in food deserts have been undertaken? > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Do companies that operate large food stores cite lack of demand as the reason they have not previously opened stores in food desert type areas? 4. [ ] (D) Can the cities with the greatest percentages of their areas considered food deserts afford to offer incentives to encourage the opening of food stores with comprehensive selections in food deserts? > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) How often does the average person who lives in an urban area considered a food desert shop for food? > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard In order to evaluate the likelihood of success, first we must determine what constitutes success.
Goal of the Plan: Improve the health of residents of urban areas that are currently food deserts.
So, the correct answer choice will help us to determine whether offering incentives to encourage the opening of large food stores with comprehensive selections of food in areas that are currently considered food deserts will result in improved health of people living in those areas. Is the author making any assumptions? Think about this: what if these companies have not opened stores in these areas because there is not sufficient demand for the goods that they would sell? If there were a lack of demand for such goods, then even if the stores opened, residents would not shop in them. So, the author is assuming that there is a demand for the goods that these stores would sell, that people in these food desserts would actually purchase these more healthy foods.
(A) What percentage of the population of cities that would offer the incentives is represented by people who reside in food desert type areas? Notice that the Yes/No test cannot be applied to this choice. However, we can use extreme answers.
For example, let’s assume that a very small percentage of the population of cities that would offer the incentives is represented by people who reside in food desert type areas. Would this assumption either strengthen or weaken the claim that such programs could result in improved health of the people in those areas? No – it would not.
Now, let’s go to the other extreme. Let’s say that a very high percentage of the population of cities that would offer the incentives is represented by people who reside in food desert type areas. Would this information either strengthen or weaken the claim? No – it would not.
(B) How many studies of the effectiveness of offering incentives to encourage the opening of large food stores with comprehensive selections in food deserts have been undertaken? This choice is a tempting trap, as it could be perceived as basically asking whether offering such incentives has been effective in the past. To avoid choosing this choice, we have to see that the truth is that this choice is not asking whether offering such incentives has been effective. It is asking simply how many studies have been done.
We could answer:
0 studies have been done.
1000 studies have been done.
Since, either way, we have no information on what the results of any such studies are, neither answer is information that indicates whether offering the incentives will be effective.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Do companies that operate large food stores cite lack of demand as the reason they have not previously opened stores in food desert type areas? If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then, even if such stores were to open in food desert areas, there would be no demand for the healthy food that they sell. If residents of those areas would not buy healthy food even if it were available, then encouraging stores to open in those areas would not make any difference in people’s health. If, on the other hand, the answer to this question is “No,” then it is possible that encouraging such stores to open in food desert areas would result in improvement in the health of residents of those areas.
(D) Can the cities with the greatest percentages of their areas considered food deserts afford to offer incentives to encourage the opening of food stores with comprehensive selections in food deserts? Neither a “Yes” answer nor a “No” answer to this question affects the support for the conclusion of the argument, because the conclusion is not that all cities can offer or will offer the incentives but, rather, that offering the incentives will have a particular result in cities where they are offered.
(E) How often does the average person who lives in an urban area considered a food desert shop for food? Does it really matter how often a person shops for food? Let’s try two fairly extreme answers.
Once a day.
Once a month.
What’s the difference? Not much. No matter how often a person shops for food, the person could benefit from having healthier choices available.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s continue by discussing answer choices that pose questions that can’t be either answered “Yes” and “No” or answered with two extreme answers.
summary:
Lesson Content Many Evaluate the Argument answer choices allow us to apply the Yes/No test, because the choices pose questions that can be answered “Yes” or “No.” However, other answer choices can be structured in ways such that the Yes/No test doesn’t work if we apply the test literally.
Consider the following question:
What proportion of lower income New York City residents travel to Manhattan by subway rather than by car?
title: 9.5.1 Answer Choices That Pose Questions That Can Be Answered in Two Ways That Are Not “Yes” or “No”

contents:
Lesson Content In most cases, Evaluate the Argument answer choices will pose questions that can be either answered “Yes” and “No” or answered with two extreme answers.
For example:
Why did the divorce rate decrease?
What other types of plants are attacked by the fungus?
There is not one particular way in which to handle all such answer choices.
Does the reason for the divorce rate decrease affect the support for the argument? Does it matter what types of plants are attacked by the fungus? If you are stumped by such an answer choice, you can consider a few ways in which the question could be answered.
For example:
Why did the divorce rate decrease?
Because the laws changed. Because people started waiting to get married. After coming up with these answers, you would consider whether either of these answers, would affect the support for the conclusion.
The key to not getting into trouble with answer choices that are in the form of open ended questions like the ones above is to avoid using them to make up convoluted, unsupported stories.
Let’s consider such a choice in the context of an example:
The serious air pollution problems of San Alonso are to a large degree caused by the approximately 400,000 fossil fuel powered motor vehicles that are used in the city each day. Fortunately, it is estimated that, within five years, 200,000 of the motor vehicles used in San Alonso each day will be non-air-polluting electric vehicles. Therefore, within five years, the amount of air pollution typically seen in San Alonso will be greatly reduced.
The answer to which of the following questions would be most useful for evaluating the likelihood that the prediction will prove to be correct?
(A) What types of motor vehicles do people use in San Alonso? (B) Is the number of motor vehicles used in San Alonso each day five years from now predicted to be greater than 600,000?
Here’s the prediction we are evaluating:
Conclusion: Within five years, the amount of air pollution typically seen in San Alonso will be greatly reduced.
Now, let’s consider choice (A).
(A) What types of motor vehicles do people use in San Alonso?
Does the answer to the question posed by choice (A) matter? Let’s say that all the motor vehicles used are cars.
The key in this case is to make sure that we don’t come up with a story along the lines of that replacing fossil fuel powered cars with electric cars will reduce pollution, but replacing fossil fuel powered scooters with electric scooters would not.
Now let’s consider choice (B):
(B) Is the number of motor vehicles used in San Alonso each day five years from now predicted to be greater than 600,000?
Choice (B) is a question that can be answered “Yes” or “No.” If the answer is “Yes,” then, in five years, there will be more than 600,000 motor vehicles used in San Alonso.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an Evaluate the Argument answer choice is in the form of an open-ended question, carefully consider how answers to that question could affect the support for the argument, and be careful not to make up convoluted or unsupported stories that make that choice seem correct even though it isn’t really.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 9 The Logic Truck Company has unveiled its first model, the TrucaTruk, a battery-powered electric truck meant to be used by people living in rural Atruca. Knowing which of the following is most important for determining the likelihood that the above prediction will prove to be correct? 1. [ ] (A) Why a truck that can handle rough roads would be popular in rural Atruca > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Whether farmers in rural Atruca currently sell agricultural goods to buyers from urban areas > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Whether anyone currently living in rural Atruca could afford to buy a truck that sells at a price higher than the price at which the TrucaTruk is expected to sell > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) What percentage of people in rural Atruca have access to sources of electrical power with which they could charge electric vehicles 5. [ ] (E) What types of loads people in rural Atruca generally haul in trucks > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: The TrucaTruk is designed specifically to meet the transportation needs of people living in rural Atruca and can handle rough roads.
Premise: Unlike any other full sized truck currently available, the TrucaTruk will be priced at a level quite affordable for many residents of rural Atruca
Conclusion: As soon as the TrucaTruk is offered for sale, many will be bought.
The prediction that many TrucaTruks will be bought is based on the fact that the TrucaTruk is designed to perform well in rural Atruca and on the fact that it is priced affordably. Of course, there are other factors that figure into whether many of the trucks will be bought.
To correctly answer this question, we have to avoid choosing trap choices that highlight information that seems to matter but does not really and to make sure that the choice that we do choose highlights information that clearly affects the prospects for demand for TrucaTruks.
(A) Why a truck that can handle rough roads would be popular in rural Atruca This choice poses an open ended question that we can’t answer either with “Yes” and “No” or with two extreme answers. So, we have to carefully consider how answers to the question posed might affect the argument.
What if the answer were, “Because heavy rains often damage roads in rural Atruca”? Does that information affect the argument? Not for any clear reason.
As long as the truck is suited to the transportation needs of potential customers, demand should be strong, and so, it really doesn’t matter why a truck that can handle rough roads would be popular in rural Atruca.
(B) Whether farmers in rural Atruca currently sell agricultural goods to buyers from urban areas A “No” answer to this question might seem to indicate that there would not be much demand for TrucaTruks, as a test-taker could assume that, if farmers in rural Atruca do not sell agricultural goods to buyers in urban areas, they would have little use for trucks. However, this line of reasoning goes beyond what is supported by the information we have. For one thing, the trucks could be useful in ways other than for transporting agricultural good to urban areas. So, a “No” answer has no effect on the prediction.
Similarly, we have no basis for thinking that a “Yes” answer to this question would affect demand for TrucaTruks. If the answer is “Yes,” then farmers might want trucks for transporting goods to urban areas, but, on the other hand, they may already have ways of transporting goods to urban areas and have no use for trucks for this purpose.
(C) Whether anyone currently living in rural Atruca could afford to buy a truck that sells at a price higher than the price at which the TrucaTruk is expected to sell The passage has made clear that other trucks are not “affordable for many residents of rural Atruca.” So, regardless of whether this question is answered “Yes” or “No,” it will be the case that many people who live in rural Atruca are not able to afford other trucks and will be able to afford to buy a TrucaTruk.
YOUR CHOICE(D) What percentage of people in rural Atruca have access to sources of electrical power with which they could charge electric vehicles The TrucaTruk is described in the passage as “battery powered.” A battery powered vehicle has to be charged regularly. Therefore, if the answer to this question is “1 percent,” then most people in rural Atruca would have no way to charge the batteries in TrucaTruks, and thus the trucks would be useless to them. Thus a “1 percent” answer would weaken the argument. If, on the other hand, the answer to this question is “100 percent,” then an assumption upon which the argument relies, that there are ways in which people in rural Atruca can charge batteries, is correct, and so, that answer strengthens the support for the conclusion.
(E) What types of loads people in rural Atruca generally haul in trucks This is an open ended question that we cannot either answer “Yes” and “No” or answer in two extreme ways. So, let’s just consider how an answer to this question could affect the strength of the argument. While we may be able to come up with a convoluted story about why the types of loads matter, the truth is that we have no clear reason to believe that information on the types of loads that people carry in trucks in rural Atruca has implications for the demand for TrucaTruks.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss our final category of Evaluate the Argument answer choices.
summary:
Lesson Content In most cases, Evaluate the Argument answer choices will pose questions that can be either answered “Yes” and “No” or answered with two extreme answers.
For example:
Why did the divorce rate decrease?
What other types of plants are attacked by the fungus?
There is not one particular way in which to handle all such answer choices.
Does the reason for the divorce rate decrease affect the support for the argument?
title: 9.5.2 Answer Choices that Pose Questions of Other Types

contents:
Lesson Content So far, in all of the Evaluate the Argument questions that we have considered, the answer choices have been in the form of questions.
All the same, to answer Evaluate questions of this type, we can use a method that is similar to the methods that we have been using.
Consider the following example:
In response to residents’ complaints about constant noise, the city council of Mergate is considering permanently closing Mergate Airport. However, many businesses in Mergate depend on spending by vacationers, and so, in deciding whether to close the airport, the members of the city council are concerned about the effect that doing so would have on the profitability for such businesses in Mergate.
Which of the following, if true, would be most important for the city council to take into account in arriving at a decision regarding whether to close the airport?
(A) Businesses in Mergate that depend on spending by vacationers have not been as profitable in recent years as they had been in the past.
(B) When a small private airport was shut down in Boselton, some businesses in Boselton experienced increases in revenue.
(C) Without the constant noise made by airplanes taking off and landing at Mergate Airport, Mergate would be a much more attractive destination for vacationers.
In this example, the conclusion isn’t stated outright, but we can tell that the city council is seeking to evaluate the following conclusion:
Conclusion: It makes sense to close Mergate Airport.
We could have decided the conclusion is the opposite conclusion, that it doesn’t make sense to close the airport, but since the same answer choice will affect either conclusion, we can just go with the above conclusion.
Now let’s consider the effects of the choices.
(A) Businesses in Mergate that depend on spending by vacationers have not been as profitable in recent years as they had been in the past.
Incorrect. Choice (A) is just like certain trap answers that we saw in the Weaken the Argument chapter.
(B) When a small private airport was shut down in Boselton, some businesses in Boselton experienced increases in revenue.
Incorrect. As we discussed when we saw an example similar to this one in the Strengthen chapter, this choice is only loosely connected to the conclusion of the argument.
(C) Without the constant noise made by airplanes taking off and landing at Mergate Airport, Mergate would be a much more attractive destination for vacationers.
Correct. This choice definitely affects the support for the conclusion.
Before we found the correct answer, we didn’t know that the correct answer would be a strengthener.
💡MUST KNOW:
To find the answer to an Evaluate the Argument question that has answer choices that are statements, identify the conclusion of the argument, and then, find a choice that either weakens or strengthens the support for the conclusion.
The correct answer to the following question is the one that provides information that either weakens or strengthens the support for the conclusion.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 10 The city of Neurtai has added dozens of miles of protected bicycle lanes over the past six years, and yet the number of cyclists involved in collisions with motor vehicles has not decreased but, rather, increased slightly over the same time period. Which of the following, if true, is the most significant for determining whether it makes sense for Neurtai to discontinue adding protected bicycle lanes? 1. [x] (A) In recent years, as people in Neurtai have become more interested in fitness and ecology, there has been a significant increase in the popularity of cycling there. 2. [ ] (B) The number of miles of protected bicycle lanes added so far in Neurtai is not as great as the number that planners had expected to have been added by now. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Even in areas of Neurtai that have protected bicycle lanes, cyclists are not protected when they are crossing intersections. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) As the Neurtai public works department has gained experience in adding protected bicycle lanes, the cost per mile of adding the lanes has decreased. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) Many cyclists who ride in areas of Neurtai in which protected lanes have not been added have never gotten into collisions with motor vehicles. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard Since the answer choices in this Evaluate question are in the form of statements, we can treat this question as a hybrid of Weaken and Strengthen and look for a choice that either weakens or strengthens the support for the conclusion.
Conclusion: The Neurtai city of Neurtai should save money by discontinuing the process of adding protected bicycle lanes.
The support that the passage provides for the conclusion is that, even though the city has added dozens of miles of protected bike lanes, the number of collisions between cyclists and motor vehicles has increased slightly, rather than decreased.
Now, we need information that would either undermine or strengthen the support for the conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(A) In recent years, as people in Neurtai have become more interested in fitness and ecology, there has been a significant increase in the popularity of cycling there. At first, since this choice mentions popularity of cycling rather than something about bike lanes or collisions, it might seem to make sense to dismiss this choice. However, careful consideration of this choice reveals that its implications are that the number of cyclists in Neurtai has increased significantly. This information undermines the support for the conclusion by attacking a key assumption of the argument.
The conclusion is based on the fact that collisions between cyclists and motor vehicles increased slightly. If we assume that the number of cyclists has remained the same, then a slight increase in such collisions tends to indicate that safety has not increased. However, this choice attacks that assumption, by implying that the number of cyclists has not remained the same, but rather increased significantly. If the number of cyclists has increased significantly, but the number of collisions between cyclist and motor vehicles has increased only slightly, then it seems likely that the protected lanes are contributing to the safety of cyclists.
Since this choice thus weakens the conclusion, it is key information that can be used in determining whether to continue adding protected lanes.
(B) The number of miles of protected bicycle lanes added so far in Neurtai is not as great as the number that planners had expected to have been added by now. This choice is a pseudo-weakener, as it could be construed as indicating that the reason that cyclist safety has not improved is not that the lanes are not effective, but rather than not enough such lanes have been completed. However, since we are concerned with the effectiveness of the lanes that have been completed, this choice does not actually affect the support for the conclusion.
(C) Even in areas of Neurtai that have protected bicycle lanes, cyclists are not protected when they are crossing intersections. This choice could be construed as strengthening the support for the conclusion by showing that the protected bicycle lanes don’t truly protect cyclists, or as weakening the support for the conclusion by showing that the lanes actually do protect cyclists, and that the reason for the slight increase in collisions with motor vehicles is not that the lanes don’t make cyclists safer but that, even with the lanes, cyclists remain unprotected when they cross intersections. However, those lines of thinking are not logical, and the truth is that this choice neither strengthens nor weakens the support for the conclusion. It merely indicates that, even in areas in which there are protected bicycle lanes, cyclists are not always protected. Regardless of that fact, it could be that, by protecting cyclists some of the time, the lanes make cyclists safer, and what this choice says provides no information that can be used to determine whether, by protecting cyclists when they do protect cyclists, the lanes make cyclists safer.
(D) As the Neurtai public works department has gained experience in adding protected bicycle lanes, the cost per mile of adding the lanes has decreased. This choice might seem to attack the rationale for discontinuing the process of adding protecting cycling lanes, because it indicates that the cost of doing so is decreasing. However, a decreasing cost is still a cost, and meanwhile, if the lanes are not preventing collisions between cyclists and motor vehicles, then, regardless of how much they cost, they are not serving their purpose. So, this choice does not really affect the support for the conclusion.
(E) Many cyclists who ride in areas of Neurtai in which protected lanes have not been added have never gotten into collisions with motor vehicles. This choice is another pseudo-strengthener, in that it could be construed as indicating that the lanes are not necessary for preventing collisions between cyclists and motor vehicles. The truth is however, that, even if many cyclists who are not riding in such lanes do not get into collisions with motor vehicles, the lanes could still be helping to prevent collisions between cyclists and motor vehicles. Another way to look at this scenario is to consider the following. In a city with 100,000 cyclists, if 90 percent of cyclists were to get into collisions with motor vehicles, we would still have many cyclists, 10,000, who didn’t. So, that fact that many cyclists don’t get into collisions with motor vehicles does not mean that there are no collisions to prevent.
Correct Answer: (A) Now that we have discussed how to go about answering various types of Evaluate the Argument questions, let’s continue improving our game.
summary:
Lesson Content So far, in all of the Evaluate the Argument questions that we have considered, the answer choices have been in the form of questions.
All the same, to answer Evaluate questions of this type, we can use a method that is similar to the methods that we have been using.
Consider the following example:
In response to residents’ complaints about constant noise, the city council of Mergate is considering permanently closing Mergate Airport.
title: 9.5.3 Answer Choices that are Statements rather than Questions

contents:
Lesson Content As we have discussed, the correct answer to an Evaluate the Argument question is a question or statement that highlights information that we could use to weaken or strengthen the argument.
In writing Evaluate the Argument questions, question writers come up with all kinds of ways to create trap choices that seem to highlight information that affects the arguments but don’t really do so.
carefully note the conclusion the validity of which is being evaluated. refrain from making up convoluted stories that seem to make incorrect choices work even though the truth is that the choices don’t really highlight information that affects the arguments. relentlessly use the Yes/No test or the other methods that we have discussed until you find a choice that clearly does highlight information that affects the argument. Notice the how the trap choices work in the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 11 A survey of Springfield homeowners revealed that many who were selling their homes were doing so without the help of a real estate agent because they thought agent commissions to be too high. In evaluating the argument, it would be most useful to determine which of the following? 1. [ ] (A) Whether going to a 4 percent commission rate would have helped the real estate agents to attract more clients > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Whether the houses that were sold with the help of an agent sold at an average price higher than that of the houses that were sold without the help of an agent > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Whether the agents who did not reduce their commission rates experienced a reduction in the number of houses that they sold 4. [ ] (D) Whether a new survey of Springfield homeowners indicates that they feel that the new rates are more reasonable than the higher rates > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Whether the agents who reduced their rate sold more than half of the houses sold in Springfield during the year after the agents reduced their rate > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium Conclusion: The real estate agents who reduced their rate didn’t realize any benefit from doing so.
The fact that the real estate agents sold the same number of houses after reducing their commission rate as before they did could suggest that the reduction did not benefit them. However, this conclusion relies on the assumption that the number of houses that they sold after the reduction was unaffected by the reduction in commission, an assumption that is not necessarily correct.
