(A) Whether going to a 4 percent commission rate would have helped the real estate agents to attract more clients This answer is a trap, as the answer “Yes, they would have attracted more clients,” could be construed as indicating that reducing rates in general is beneficial, and the answer “No, they would not have attracted more clients,” could be construed as indicating that, in general, reducing rates does not result benefit agents. However, the argument claims that the agents did not benefit from the specific move, reducing their rate from 8 percent to 6 percent. Information on the results of a possible further decrease, to 4 percent, does not affect the conclusion about whether the agents benefited from the actual decrease. It could be that Springfield homeowners selling their houses are no more likely to use an agent if agents in general charge 4 percent than they are to use an agent if agents in general charge 6 percent commission.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Whether the houses that were sold with the help of an agent sold at an average price higher than that of the houses that were sold without the help of an agent The answers to this question don’t help at all with the evaluation of the argument. The correct answer should indicate something about the benefits for agents of changing rates. Comparing the price of houses sold by real estate agents with those of houses sold without the help of real estate agents might provide information useful for evaluating the benefits of working with an agent but does not help with evaluating whether reducing the commission rate was beneficial for the agents who did so.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Whether the agents who did not reduce their commission rates experienced a reduction in the number of houses that they sold If the answer is “Yes,” and, thus, the agents who did not decrease their commission rate sold fewer houses than they did the year before, then the agents who did decrease their commission rate may have benefited from doing so. If the agents who did not reduce their rate sold fewer houses, and those who did reduce their rate sold the same number of houses, then the total number of houses sold must have decreased. So, those who reduced their rate sold the same number of houses they sold before they reduced their rate, even though the total number of houses sold that year was lower than the number sold the year before. The agents who reduced their rate selling the same number of houses even though the total number of houses sold decreased could be evidence that reducing their rates benefited them
Likewise, if the answer is “No,” then the agents who did not decrease their rate sold the same number of houses as they sold the year before, the agents who did reduce their rate sold the same number of houses as they sold the year before, and the total number of houses sold remained the same. In that case, the only apparent result of the commission reduction is that the agents who reduced their rate received lower commissions, while the agents who didn’t reduce their rate received just as much as they received the year before. Thus, a “No” answer tends to be a reason to doubt that the reduction in commission rate generated benefits.
(D) Whether a new survey of Springfield homeowners indicates that they feel that the new rates are more reasonable than the higher rates This choice could trap a test-taker who does not notice that this choice is not directed at the correct conclusion. The correct answer needs to help in the evaluation of whether there was a benefit to the real estate agents who lowered their commissions. What is relevant to evaluating the results of the real estate agents’ decision is homeowners’ behavior/decisions and the actual commissions from sales facilitated by those agents. Regardless of whether the answer to the question posed by this choice is “Yes” or “No,” the opinions of homeowners do not constitute information indicating whether the agents with lower rates actually derived any benefit from reducing their rates.
(E) Whether the agents who reduced their rate sold more than half of the houses sold in Springfield during the year after the agents reduced their rate The answer to this question reveals nothing, because we have nothing relevant to compare the answer to, as we don’t have the proportion of houses sold by the agents who reduced their rate before they reduced their rate. Even though half the agents reduced their rate, it could be that, even before they reduced their rate, those agents who went on to reduce their rate sold well over half the houses sold, or well under half the houses sold. Neither the passage nor this choice says what the proportion was. So, regardless of whether the answer to this question is “Yes” or “No,” we have no way of using the information provided by that answer to determine whether reducing the rate had any effect.
Correct Answer: (C) To further prepare you to tell the difference between trap choices and correct answers to Evaluate the Argument questions, let’s go over some types of incorrect answers that you could encounter in Evaluate the Argument questions.
summary:
Lesson Content As we have discussed, the correct answer to an Evaluate the Argument question is a question or statement that highlights information that we could use to weaken or strengthen the argument.
In writing Evaluate the Argument questions, question writers come up with all kinds of ways to create trap choices that seem to highlight information that affects the arguments but don’t really do so.
carefully note the conclusion the validity of which is being evaluated.
title: 9.6 Trap Choices in Evaluate the Argument Questions

contents:
Lesson Content There is a difference between information that is pertinent to a topic and information that affects the support for a specific conclusion about that topic.
💡MUST KNOW:
There is a difference between information that is pertinent to a topic and information that affects the support for a specific conclusion about that topic.
For instance, in a general discussion of a problem, information on how to solve the problem would be pertinent.
Consider the following example:
Doctors often determine without investigating thoroughly that people experiencing occasional trouble breathing are experiencing asthma, a health issue characterized by airway constriction, and prescribe medication that helps with keeping airways open. However, occasional trouble breathing is not always caused by airway constriction and can be caused instead by a person’s being overweight. Therefore, it must be the case that people experiencing occasional trouble breathing often take medication that does nothing to alleviate their symptoms.
Determining which of the following would be most helpful in assessing the validity of the claim made?
(Incorrect Choice that Highlights Information Pertinent to the Discussion) Whether, by simply losing weight, an overweight person can solve occasional breathing problems caused by being overweight
(Correct Answer) Whether using medications commonly used to keep airways open causes the person using such medications to lose weight
Here’s the conclusion the validity of which we are seeking to assess:
Conclusion: It must be the case that people experiencing occasional trouble breathing often end up taking medication that does nothing to alleviate their symptoms.
Notice how the incorrect choice that highlights pertinent information could trap a test-taker.
The correct answer, on the other hand, clearly highlights information that would affect the support for the conclusion.
Here’s another example in which the incorrect answer highlights information that is certainly pertinent to what’s discussed in the passage and yet does not affect the argument:
The serious air pollution problems of San Alonso are to a large degree caused by the approximately 400,000 fossil fuel powered motor vehicles that are used in the city each day. Fortunately, it is estimated that, within five years, 200,000 of the motor vehicles used in San Alonso each day will be non-air-polluting electric vehicles. Therefore, within five years, the amount of air pollution typically seen in San Alonso will be greatly reduced.
The answer to which of the following questions would be most useful for evaluating the likelihood that the prediction will prove to be correct?
(Incorrect Choice that Highlights Information Pertinent to the Discussion) Is there an ecologically sound method for disposal of spent batteries from electric vehicles? (Correct Answer) Is the number of motor vehicles used in San Alonso each day five years from now predicted to be more than 600,000?
You can see how the incorrect choice could tempt a test-taker.
As discussed when we saw this example earlier, the answer to the question posed by the correct answer, on the other hand, could make or break the support for the conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Evaluate the Argument questions, beware of incorrect choices that highlight information that is pertinent to the topics that arguments are about but does not affect the support for the arguments.
When answering an Evaluate question, to avoid choosing a choice that highlights information that is pertinent to the topic discussed in the passage but does not affect the conclusion of the argument, make sure that you identify the conclusion of the argument and then keep the conclusion in mind as you consider the choices.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering an Evaluate question, to avoid choosing a choice that highlights information that is pertinent to the topic discussed in the passage but does not affect the conclusion of the argument, make sure that you identify the conclusion of the argument and then keep the conclusion in mind as you consider the choices.
Notice the difference between information that is pertinent to the topic discussed and information that actually affects the conclusion as you consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 12 Researchers in the United States found that the percentage of people diagnosed by physicians as having kidney stones is significantly higher in the southern states than in the northern states. Which of the following would it be most useful to establish in order to evaluate the researchers' hypothesis? 1. [ ] (A) How much higher the rate of diagnosis of kidney stones in the southern states is than the rate of diagnosis of kidney stones in the northern states > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) Whether it would be possible for people who live in the southern states to prevent entirely the formation of kidney stones by drinking large amounts of water consistently > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) How the rate of diagnosis of kidney stones in the southern states compares with the rate of diagnosis of kidney stones worldwide > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Whether people diagnosed by physicians as having kidney stones are ever misdiagnosed and do not actually have kidney stones > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Whether, in years when temperatures in the southern states are unusually low, the southern states’ kidney stones diagnosis rate decreases View Solution Question Level: easy Here’s the hypothesis that we have to evaluate:
Conclusion: The higher incidence of kidney stones in the southern states is the result of people’s experiencing dehydration at a relatively high rate in those states as a result of the relatively high temperatures in those states.
In order to find the correct answer, we have to avoid getting sucked in by a trap answer that brings up information that is pertinent to the topic at hand but doesn’t affect the support for the argument.
(A) How much higher the rate of diagnosis of kidney stones in the southern states is than the rate of diagnosis of kidney stones in the northern states We could answer this question with two extreme answers:
Ten times as high.
Only slightly higher.
Does it matter how much higher the diagnosis rate is in the southern states? No. All we need in order to support the conclusion is information indicating that there is a significantly higher diagnosis rate in the southern states, and the passage provides that information. So, while the degree of difference in rates of diagnosis might be pertinent in general to the topic discussed in the passage, it is not information that affects the support for the conclusion.
(B) Whether it would be possible for people who live in the southern states to prevent entirely the formation of kidney stones by drinking large amounts of water consistently A “Yes” or “No” answer to the question posed by this choice could be pertinent to a general discussion of dehydration and kidney stones. However, the support for the conclusion that the cause of the difference between the kidney stones diagnosis rates of the northern states and the southern states is not affected by information on whether people could entirely prevent kidney stones by drinking large amounts of water. Regardless of whether yes, drinking large amounts of water would entirely prevent kidney stones or no, it wouldn’t prevent all kidney stones, the fact remains that dehydration could be contributing to the relatively high kidney stones diagnosis rate in the southern states.
(C) How the rate of diagnosis of kidney stones in the southern states compares with the rate of diagnosis of kidney stones worldwide The comparison brought up by this choice does not matter. Regardless of how the rate of diagnosis in the southern states compares with the worldwide rate of diagnosis, the rate of diagnosis in the southern states is higher than that in the northern states, and so, any answer to the question posed by this choice says has no effect on the scenario presented in the passage or on the support for the conclusion.
(D) Whether people diagnosed by physicians as having kidney stones are ever misdiagnosed and do not actually have kidney stones The information highlighted by this choice is certainly pertinent to a discussion of rates of kidney stones diagnosis and could be perceived as affecting the support for the conclusion, because the support is based on rates of diagnosis of kidney stones. So, the answer “Yes, sometimes people diagnosed by physicians as having kidney stones do not actually have kidney stones,” could seem to undermine the force of the evidence presented by indicating that a kidney stones diagnosis is not reliable. However, since, even if the answer to this question is “Yes,” we don’t have a clear reason to believe that all kidney stones diagnoses are incorrect, and there is still a difference between diagnosis rates in the northern and southern states, neither the scenario nor the support for the conclusion is really changed by the answer to this question.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Whether, in years when temperatures in the southern states are unusually low, the southern states’ kidney stones diagnosis rate decreases The answer, “Yes, in years when temperatures in the southern states are unusually low, the southern states’ kidney stones diagnosis rate decreases,” would serve to strengthen the cause-and-effect conclusion of the argument, by showing that, when the cause, relatively high temperatures in the southern states, is not present, the effect, a relatively high kidney stones diagnosis rate, is not present either. A “No” answer, on the other hand, would weaken the support for the argument, because it would indicate that, even when the cause is not present, we still have the effect.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now consider another type of Evaluate the Argument incorrect choice, a choice that brings up an irrelevant comparison.
summary:
Lesson Content There is a difference between information that is pertinent to a topic and information that affects the support for a specific conclusion about that topic.
💡MUST KNOW:
There is a difference between information that is pertinent to a topic and information that affects the support for a specific conclusion about that topic.
For instance, in a general discussion of a problem, information on how to solve the problem would be pertinent.
title: 9.7 Evaluate Incorrect Choice Type 1: Highlights Information that is Pertinent to the Topic that the Argument is About but Doesn’t Affect the Strength of the Argument

contents:
Lesson Content Comparisons can often be used to strengthen or weaken support for conclusions and, thus, can be helpful for evaluating arguments.
💡MUST KNOW:
Comparisons can often be used to strengthen or weaken support for conclusions and, thus, can be helpful for evaluating arguments. Of course, only a relevant comparison will be useful in this way, and the incorrect answer choices to Evaluate questions often highlight comparisons that indicate nothing that is useful for evaluating the arguments.
Consider, for example, an argument with the conclusion that too much water is bad for the health of tomato plants.
Let’s consider an answer choice that highlights an irrelevant comparison in the context of the San Alonso air pollution example:
The serious air pollution problems of San Alonso are to a large degree caused by the approximately 400,000 motor vehicles that are used in the city each day. Fortunately, it is estimated that, within five years, 200,000 of the motor vehicles used in San Alonso each day will be non-air-polluting electric vehicles. Therefore, within five years, the amount of air pollution typically seen in San Alonso will be greatly reduced.
The answer to which of the following questions would be most useful for evaluating the likelihood that the prediction will prove to be correct?
(Incorrect Choice that Brings Up an Irrelevant Comparison) Is the level of air pollution in San Alonso greater than the levels of air pollution in most cities? (Correct Answer) Is the number of motor vehicles used in San Alonso each day five years from now predicted to be fewer than 600,000?
We can see how a test-taker’s mind could start running to create a story that explains how the comparison brought up by the incorrect choice would affect the argument.
The truth is, however, that that line of thinking doesn’t really make sense.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Evaluate the Argument questions, beware of incorrect choices that highlight irrelevant comparisons.
When answering an Evaluate question, to avoid choosing a choice that highlights an irrelevant comparison, we have to be careful not to come up with a convoluted story that seems to connect that comparison to the argument but doesn’t really make sense.
As you answer the following question, notice the difference between the relevance of the comparisons made in the incorrect answers and that of the comparison made in the correct answer.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 13 Elephants, like certain other animals, disperse the seeds of trees, thus enabling the parent trees whose seeds the elephants disperse to reproduce in areas at a distance from the locations of the parent trees. Determining which of the following would be most helpful in assessing the degree to which the conservationists’ concerns are well founded? 1. [ ] (A) Whether elephants more effectively disperse seeds than do animals of other species > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) How much decreases in elephant populations that have been resulting from hunting by poachers who sell their body parts will slow in the near future. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Whether trees that grow far from the locations of their parent trees are healthier than trees that grow near to their parent trees 4. [ ] (D) Whether the types of trees that grow in forests historically inhabited by elephants tend to grow taller than do trees that grow in forests never inhabited by elephants > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Which approach to maintaining elephant populations is the most likely to result in success > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard We have to evaluate the support for the following conclusion:
Conclusion: The reduction in seed dispersal that has resulted from decreases in elephant populations will negatively impact the numbers of healthy trees in forests that elephants inhabited historically.
The correct answer will highlight information that will add support for or serve to call into question the link between the reduction in seed dispersal and the numbers of healthy trees.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Whether elephants more effectively disperse seeds than do animals of other species This choice could be tempting, because the comparison that it brings up is related to the topic of the argument. Notice, however, that the argument is about the effects of the reduction in seed dispersal that has resulted from reductions in elephant populations. This reduction and its effects will be the same regardless of whether elephants disperse seeds more effectively than do animals of other species. If elephants are relatively great at dispersing seeds, the reduction in seed dispersal will have a certain effect. If elephants do not disperse seeds as effectively as do other animals, the reduction in seed dispersal will still have the same effect.
(B) How much decreases in elephant populations that have been resulting from hunting by poachers who sell their body parts will slow in the near future. This choice is tempting, because the degree to which decreases in elephant populations will slow could seem to affect the degree to which the conservationist’s concerns are well founded. However, by noticing a key detail in the passage, we can eliminate this choice. The concerns are about the reduction in seed dispersal that has already resulted from decreases in elephant populations that have already occurred. This choice is about the degree to which decreases in elephant populations will slow in the future. The degree to which decreases in elephant populations will slow in the future has no clear connection to the effects of a reduction in seed dispersal that has already occurred. Regardless of whether the decreases will slow a lot or not at all, the reduction that has already occurred will have the effects that it will have.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Whether trees that grow far from the locations of their parent trees are healthier than trees that grow near to their parent trees Let’s apply the Yes/No test to this choice. If the answer is “Yes,” and trees that grow far from their parent trees are healthier, then seed dispersal helps trees to be healthier. Thus, a “Yes” answer to the question posed by this choice adds to the support for the concerns. If the answer is “No,” then we have reason to believe that dispersing seeds does not benefit the health of trees, and thus, a “No” answer weakens support for the conclusion.
(D) Whether the types of trees that grow in forests historically inhabited by elephants tend to grow taller than do trees that grow in forests never inhabited by elephants This choice could be very tempting, because a test-taker could associate “taller” with “healthier” and conclude that having elephants around makes trees healthier. Notice, however, that a “Yes” answer to this question merely indicates that the types of trees in forest in which elephants live tend to grow taller than the trees that grow in other forests. All that information would indicate is that the best place for elephants may be the best place for the types of trees that grow tall. A “No” answer would indicate the opposite, that shorter types of trees thrive where elephants also thrive. Whatever the case, the truth is that neither “shorter” nor “taller’ is the equivalent of “healthier.” So, this comparison gives us no information that is useful for evaluation of the support for the conclusion.
(E) Which approach to maintaining elephant populations is the most likely to result in success The question posed by this choice is pertinent in general to a discussion of the effects of decreases in elephant populations, but the answer to the question posed by this choice does not provide information that affects the support for the specific concerns of the conservationists. What we have to see in order to eliminate this choice are that the concerns discussed in the passage are about the effects of decreases in elephant populations that have occurred, and that the answer to the question of how best to stem future decreases will not affect the degree to which concerns about the effects of past decreases are well founded.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now review another key type of Evaluate incorrect answer choice, a choice that misses the point of the argument.
summary:
Lesson Content Comparisons can often be used to strengthen or weaken support for conclusions and, thus, can be helpful for evaluating arguments.
💡MUST KNOW:
Comparisons can often be used to strengthen or weaken support for conclusions and, thus, can be helpful for evaluating arguments. Of course, only a relevant comparison will be useful in this way, and the incorrect answer choices to Evaluate questions often highlight comparisons that indicate nothing that is useful for evaluating the arguments.
title: 9.8 Evaluate Incorrect Choice Type 2: Brings Up an Irrelevant Comparison

contents:
Lesson Content In reviewing Weaken the Argument questions and Strengthen the Argument questions, we saw that some answer choices affect conclusions that are related to the conclusions of the arguments but are not the conclusions that we need to affect.
For example, we could have an argument the conclusion of which is that, by educating the public, avant garde artists could increase the market for their work.
Consider the following example:
Historically, Marvelo Chocolate Company sold its limited production, high end chocolate only in specialty stores, but, a year ago, Marvelo started offering its chocolate in some regular grocery stores in addition to continuing to offer it in specialty stores. After this change was made, sales of Marvelo Chocolate decreased rather than increased. Marketing experts theorized that this decrease occurred because Marvelo’s offering its chocolate in ordinary grocery stores had reduced the appeal of the brand to its traditional customer base.
The answer to which of the following would be most useful for evaluating the validity of the marketing experts’ conclusion?
(Choice that Misses the Point) To what degree do grocery stores look out for the interests of major chocolate companies over those of smaller, high end chocolate companies?
(Correct Answer) Are the members of Marvelo’s traditional customer base aware of what brands of chocolate are offered in grocery stores?
Here’s the conclusion the validity of which we need to evaluate:
Conclusion: The decrease in Marvelo’s sales occurred because Marvelo’s offering its chocolate in ordinary grocery stores had reduced the appeal of the brand to its traditional customer base.
So, we are concerned with why the decrease occurred.
The incorrect choice that misses the point goes into a whole other discussion, one that could end up providing information that would explain why Marvelo Chocolate didn’t sell much chocolate in grocery stores.
The correct answer, on the other hand, poses a key question.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering an Evaluate the Argument question, beware of incorrect choices that bring up information that misses the point of the argument and affects the wrong conclusion.
So, if these answer choices highlight information that is not actually related to the points of arguments, why would test-takers ever pick such choices? Because test-takers aren’t always clear about the points of the passages.
It’s easy to read a passage and come away with a general sense of what the passage is about rather than a clear understanding of what the exact point of the passage is.
Notice the difference between the trap choices that miss the point and the correct answer as you answer the following question.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 14 A recent study has found that people who have been consistently using mouthwash at least twice a day are more likely to experience diabetes than are people who have been using mouthwash once a day or less. To evaluate whether the approach mentioned would be likely to achieve its goal, it would be most useful to know which of the following? 1. [ ] (A) Whether, by having a healthy diet, a person could avoid experiencing diabetes even if that person were to use mouthwash at least twice a day > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Whether the symptoms that indicate that a person is likely to soon experience diabetes include persistent bad breath 3. [ ] (C) Whether the use of mouthwash results in the prevention of any health issues that are more detrimental than diabetes > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Whether most people who consistently use mouthwash at least twice a day do not eventually experience diabetes > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Whether there are products that can be substituted for mouthwash and the use of which is not associated with higher incidences of diabetes > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard The passage suggests the following:
Conclusion: Limiting use of mouthwash can help to prevent diabetes.
We have to find a choice that highlights information that would help with evaluating whether limiting use of mouthwash really helps with preventing diabetes.
The argument is based on the correlation between people’s using mouthwash at least twice a day and a higher than normal likelihood of developing diabetes. So, we need a choice that highlights information that either strengthens or weakens the link between that correlation and the conclusion that limiting mouthwash use can help to prevent diabetes.
In seeking the correct answer, we have to be careful not to get trapped by a choice that sounds right but misses the point of the argument.
(A) Whether, by having a healthy diet, a person could avoid experiencing diabetes even if that person were to use mouthwash at least twice a day This choice misses the point of the argument. The passage is not about whether there is a way to use mouthwash without developing diabetes. The passage’s focus is on the possibility that limiting use of mouthwash could help to prevent diabetes. Regardless of whether one could somehow use mouthwash without developing diabetes, one could still help to prevent diabetes by limiting use of mouthwash.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Whether the symptoms that indicate that a person is likely to soon experience diabetes include persistent bad breath A “Yes” answer to the question posed by this choice would seriously weaken the support for the argument, by indicating that the actual cause-and-effect relationship may be the reverse of the one that the author of the argument assumes exists. If bad breath is a symptom that typically precedes the onset of diabetes, then, rather than mouthwash is causing diabetes, maybe the cause and effect relationship is the onset of diabetes causes people to use mouthwash. Since a “Yes” answer would thus seriously undermine the support for the conclusion, this choice is the correct answer.
(C) Whether the use of mouthwash results in the prevention of any health issues that are more detrimental than diabetes The conclusion of the argument is very specific, that one can prevent diabetes by limiting one’s use of mouthwash. The author does not claim that one should limit one’s use of mouthwash or that limiting one’s use of mouthwash is beneficial overall. So, since one may or may not be able to prevent diabetes by limiting one’s use of mouthwash regardless of whether the use of mouthwash prevents health issues more detrimental than diabetes, neither a “Yes” answer nor a “No” answer to this question has any affect on the support for the conclusion.
(D) Whether most people who consistently use mouthwash at least twice a day do not eventually experience diabetes This choice could be tempting, because the answer “Yes, most people who consistently use mouthwash at least twice a day do not eventually experience diabetes,” could seem to weaken the support for the conclusion that limiting one’s use of mouthwash can help to prevent diabetes.
Notice, however, regardless of whether most people who use mouthwash at least twice a day do so without developing diabetes, the fact remains that people who use mouthwash at least twice a day are more likely to experience diabetes than are people who don’t use mouthwash a least twice a day, and so, the support provided by that fact is not affected by the answer to the question posed by this choice.
(E) Whether there are products that can be substituted for mouthwash and the use of which is not associated with higher incidences of diabetes This choice misses the point of the argument. The focus of the argument is not on ways to get the effects of mouthwash without experiencing diabetes. It’s on preventing diabetes by limiting mouthwash use. Regardless of whether, “Yes,” there are such products or, “No,” there are not, if mouthwash does indeed cause diabetes, then one could help to prevent diabetes by limiting use of mouthwash.
Correct Answer: (B)
summary:
Lesson Content In reviewing Weaken the Argument questions and Strengthen the Argument questions, we saw that some answer choices affect conclusions that are related to the conclusions of the arguments but are not the conclusions that we need to affect.
For example, we could have an argument the conclusion of which is that, by educating the public, avant garde artists could increase the market for their work.
Consider the following example:
title: 9.9 Evaluate Incorrect Choice Type 3: Highlights Information that Misses the Point and Affects the Wrong Conclusion

contents:
Lesson Content In our review of GMAT Critical Reasoning questions, we have seen many arguments that are flawed.
Logical Flaw questions also present us with stimuli containing flawed arguments, but the focus of Logical Flaw questions is somewhat different from the foci of the other types of questions, in that Logical Flaw questions are more directly focused on the flaws in the arguments.
choose an answer that best describes a flaw found in the argument (in the case of a pure Logical Flaw question). or
choose an answer that, if true, would weaken the flawed argument (in the case of a hybrid Logical Flaw/Weaken question). 💡MUST KNOW:
In answering a Logical Flaw question, our job is to do one of two things: (1) choose an answer that best describes a flaw found in the argument (in the case of a pure Logical Flaw question), or (2) choose an answer that, if true, would weaken the flawed argument (in the case of a hybrid Logical Flaw/Weaken question).
The good news is that many, if not most, Logical Flaw questions are just advanced Assumption questions, and, in many of the arguments we’ll encounter, the flaws we’ll find center squarely on assumptions made by the authors.
💡MUST KNOW:
Many Logical Flaw questions are just advanced Assumption questions.
Let’s continue by discussing the close connection between assumptions and logical flaws.
summary:
Lesson Content In our review of GMAT Critical Reasoning questions, we have seen many arguments that are flawed.
Logical Flaw questions also present us with stimuli containing flawed arguments, but the focus of Logical Flaw questions is somewhat different from the foci of the other types of questions, in that Logical Flaw questions are more directly focused on the flaws in the arguments.
choose an answer that best describes a flaw found in the argument (in the case of a pure Logical Flaw question).
title: 10.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content Remember – an assumption is a piece of missing information that must be true for the logic of an argument to be sound.
💡MUST KNOW:
An assumption is a piece of missing information that must be true for the support for an argument to be sound.
As you know well by this point, authors make assumptions when making arguments.
Let’s look at a few examples:
Health insurance for those living in major cities costs significantly more than does health insurance for those living in small towns. However, empirical data shows that people who live in large cities are no less healthy than people who live in small towns. It follows that health insurance companies profit more from people who live in major cities than from those who live in small towns. Which of the following best describes the error of reasoning contained in the argument above?
(A) The argument assumes without justification that the costs associated with providing health care are not significantly higher in major cities than in small towns.
Is there an important, and questionable, assumption being made by the author in this argument? For sure, there is.
💡MUST KNOW:
Remember, in solving Logical Flaw questions, it’s very helpful to seek out the assumptions made by authors.
Let’s look at another example:
Unlike other major automobile companies, all of which produce mostly automobiles that are fossil fuel powered, The Blitz Automobile Company makes only electric automobiles. Because electric automobiles are easier to maintain, less costly to power, and more environmentally friendly, they are superior to fossil fuel powered vehicles. Thus, soon, the Blitz Automobile Company will put all of the other automobile companies out of business.
Which of the following best describes the error of reasoning contained in the argument above?
(A) It neglects to consider the possibility that the other automobile companies may shift to producing electric automobiles. Is that argument presented a good one? Has the author made any questionable assumptions? He has, right? An assumption in this argument is that the other automobile companies are going to continue producing the products that they currently produce.
Now, it’s important to see that we can’t say for certain that the other companies will make this change, but we don’t need to be certain.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering logical flaw questions, be alert for answer choices that point out questionable assumptions made by authors. Many times, the flaw in the argument results from an author’s questionable assumption.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 1 Music Columnist: Over the past several years, there has been a huge upswing in the number of young people listening to modern jazz music. The music columnist’s argument is most vulnerable to criticism on which of the following grounds? 1. [ ] (A) It fails to consider the possibility that the majority of young people will not gravitate toward listening to modern jazz music. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) It assumes without warrant that the cause of the ongoing decline in listenership of radio stations that play “pop” music is the trend toward listening to modern jazz music. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) It ignores the fact that, because of the cyclical nature of musical trends, increases in listenership of jazz music are typically followed by decreases. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) It presupposes that the increase in the population of modern jazz music listeners is not associated with an increase in total music listenership. 5. [ ] (E) It overlooks the fact that the modern jazz music of the current era is similar in many ways to “pop” music. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium The music columnist concludes the following:
Conclusion: Radio stations that play the more commercial, “pop” forms of music are going to experience a significant decline in listenership.
The music columnist’s support for that conclusion is the fact that the number of young people listening to modern jazz music has increased significantly.
How is this argument flawed? The correct answer to this question points to the fact that the music columnist has relied on a questionable assumption in arriving at the conclusion.
(A) It fails to consider the possibility that the majority of young people will not gravitate toward listening to modern jazz music. While it is true that the argument does not include consideration of the possibility that the majority of young people will not gravitate toward listening to modern jazz, that the argument does not include consideration of that possibility is not a flaw, because, even if it is the case that the majority of young people will not gravitate toward listening to modern jazz, the conclusion could still be valid.
The conclusion is simply that the radio stations will experience a significant decline in listenership, and the stations’ experiencing a significant decline in listenership does not require all young people’s or most young people’s gravitating toward listening to modern jazz and, therefore, ceasing to listen to pop music radio. So, the music columnist’s argument works even though it does not include consideration of the possibility that the majority of young people will not gravitate toward listening to modern jazz.
(B) It assumes without warrant that the cause of the ongoing decline in listenership of radio stations that play “pop” music is the trend toward listening to modern jazz music. What this choice says misses the point of the argument. The point of the argument is not to explain why an ongoing decline in pop music radio listenership is occurring. It is to support a prediction about a future significant decline in pop music radio listenership. To avoid choosing a choice like this one, be careful to note exactly what the conclusion of the argument is.
(C) It ignores the fact that, because of the cyclical nature of musical trends, increases in listenership of jazz music are typically followed by decreases. While the argument does seem to ignore the fact that increases in listenership of jazz are typically followed by decreases, even if the argument does so, that the argument does so is not a flaw. The conclusion is not that the number of young people listening to modern jazz will remain relatively high forever, or that pop music radio listenership will remain relatively low forever. The conclusion is simply that pop music radio will experience a significant decline in listenership. Even if that decline were likely to be reversed at some point in the future, the conclusion could still be valid. So, the fact that jazz listenership is typically cyclical and may decrease at some point in the future does not affect the strength of the argument.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) It presupposes that the increase in the population of modern jazz music listeners is not associated with an increase in total music listenership. While it is true that modern jazz music listenership is increasing, the music columnist provides no evidence clearly indicating that this increase will result in a decrease in pop music radio listenership. It is entirely possible that an increase in modern jazz music listenership will involve a group of people mostly different from the group of people who listen to pop music radio. In such a case, the increase in modern jazz listenership may result not in a significant decrease in pop music radio listenership but, rather, in an increase in total music listenership.
So, the weakness in the music columnist’s argument is that it relies on the questionable assumption that the increase in the population of modern jazz listeners is not associated with an increase in total music listenership.
(E) It overlooks the fact that the modern jazz music of the current era is similar in many ways to “pop” music. This choice could be tempting, as one could come up with some convoluted reasoning that explains that, since modern jazz and pop music are similar, the increase in modern jazz listenership somehow won’t result in a decrease in pop music listenership. The truth is, however, that we have no clear reason to believe that overlooking the fact that these two forms of music are similar in some ways constitutes a flaw in the argument. The two forms of music could be quite different from each other, and listeners could gravitate from listening to one to listening to the other, even if the two are similar in some ways. So, the fact that the two forms of music are similar in some ways does not affect the strength of the support for the conclusion.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now look at common Logical Flaw Question stems.
summary:
Lesson Content Remember – an assumption is a piece of missing information that must be true for the logic of an argument to be sound.
💡MUST KNOW:
An assumption is a piece of missing information that must be true for the support for an argument to be sound.
As you know well by this point, authors make assumptions when making arguments.
Let’s look at a few examples:
Health insurance for those living in major cities costs significantly more than does health insurance for those living in small towns.
title: 10.1.1 The Connection Between Assumptions and Logical Flaws

contents:
Lesson Content As you already know, Critical Reasoning question stems are predictable, and the more quickly we can determine what we have to do in order to correctly answer the question we’re facing, the more time we’ll have for answering it.
The following are common ways in which Logical Flaw Questions are worded:
Which of the following best describes the error of reasoning contained in the argument above?
Which of the following best describes a weakness present in the argument above?
The argument is flawed because it fails to consider that
The reasoning in the argument above is flawed because it presumes without justification that
The argument above is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it
The author’s conclusion is based on the error in reasoning that
Which of the following points to the most serious logical flaw in the argument?
The CFO’s response is flawed as a refutation of the COO’s argument because it
Of the following, the best criticism of the argument above is that it fails to account for
A serious flaw in the reasoning of the argument is that
💡MUST KNOW:
Become familiar with the language that commonly appears in Logical Flaw question stems and able to quickly recognize that a question is asking you to identify a logical flaw.
At this point, you are beginning to understand how Logical Flaw questions work.

The following are common ways in which Logical Flaw Questions are worded:
Which of the following best describes the error of reasoning contained in the argument above?

title: 10.2 Common Logical Flaw Question Stems

contents:
Lesson Content The more basic type of Logical Flaw question is a pure Logical Flaw question.
💡MUST KNOW:
Some Logical Flaw Questions are pure Logical Flaw questions, meaning that they ask us to point out a flaw in the argument as it currently stands and without bringing in new information in the correct answer.
Let’s look at an example stimulus of a pure Logical Flaw question:
In a recent medical study, all of the study participants who had in the past five years abused barbiturates, powerful prescription sedatives commonly used to treat anxiety, were found to be suffering with depression. Thus, it must be the case that abusing barbiturates causes depression. Which of the following best describes the error of reasoning contained in the argument above?
(A) The argument overlooks the possibility that the observed correlation between barbiturate abuse and depression is not the result of the cause-and-effect relationship presented.
This argument has a cause-and-effect conclusion, that abusing barbiturates causes depression, which is based on the observation that people who were known to abuse barbiturates were also found to be suffering from depression.
After all, does the author provide us with any strong evidence to support her conclusion? No – she does not.
Notice that the correct answer highlights a potential vulnerability in an assumption made by the author.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 2 For the first four years of Technology Inc.’s being in business, the CEO handled all aspects of recruiting and hiring for the company, and, on average, revenue increased by close to 50 percent per year. The argument is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it 1. [ ] (A) assumes without sufficient warrant that the CEO’s strongest skills lie in recruiting and hiring. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) overlooks the possibility that, as the company has grown, the CEO has had to delegate more responsibilities in order for the company to operate efficiently. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) does not include consideration of the fact that human resources professionals are likely better versed in employment law than the CEO is. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) takes the change in the hiring process to be the cause of the change in revenue growth without considering other possible causes. 5. [ ] (E) fails to establish that the trend in Technology Inc. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy The argument is based on the fact that, when human resource professionals took over the handling of most aspects of hiring and recruiting at the company, revenue growth slowed. Given that fact, the argument arrives at the following cause-and-effect conclusion:
Conclusion: The CEO is better at recruiting and hiring than are the human resources professionals at the company.
There is a gap in the argument, in that the evidence is a change in revenue growth, while the conclusion is about the difference between the CEO’s level of skill in hiring and recruiting and that of the human resources professionals. So, the argument depends on an assumption that fills that gap.
(A) assumes without sufficient warrant that the CEO’s strongest skills lie in recruiting and hiring. This choice describes something that does not actually go on in the argument. The argument neither states nor implies that the CEO’s strongest skills lie in recruiting and hiring. It indicates only that the CEO is more skilled in recruiting and hiring than are the human resource professionals.
(B) overlooks the possibility that, as the company has grown, the CEO has had to delegate more responsibilities in order for the company to operate efficiently. This choice is incorrect because what it describes is not logically connected to the support for this argument’s conclusion. The conclusion is simply that the CEO is better at recruiting and hiring than are the human resources professionals. The reason why the CEO handed off responsibility for recruiting and hiring does not affect the support for that specific conclusion.
(C) does not include consideration of the fact that human resources professionals are likely better versed in employment law than the CEO is. If the conclusion were that there are no benefits to having human resources professionals handle recruiting and hiring, this choice could bring up a flaw in the support for the conclusion. However, since the conclusion is simply that the CEO is better at recruiting and hiring than are the human resources professionals, the fact that the argument does not include consideration of the fact that the human resources professionals might have certain knowledge that could be valuable to the company is not a flaw in the argument.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) takes the change in the hiring process to be the cause of the change in revenue growth without considering other possible causes. In going from the evidence that revenue growth slowed when human resources professionals started handling recruiting and hiring to the conclusion that the revenue growth slowed because the human resources professionals starting handling recruiting and hiring, the argument fails to consider other potential reasons for the decline in revenue growth, reasons such as the fact that growing revenue by large percentages can be easier when a company is small than it is once the company has become much bigger.
(E) fails to establish that the trend in Technology Inc. ’s revenue growth is significantly different from the trends in revenue growth of the company’s peers. This choice could be tempting, as one could perceive it as implying that the author has assumed that there is not some industry-wide factor that is causing multiple companies, including Technology Inc. and its peers, to experience similar declines in revenue growth. So, this choice could be perceived as indirectly saying that the author has assumed that the decline in revenue growth at Technology Inc. is not due to a factor other than the change in who handles recruiting and hiring.
The truth is, however, that that interpretation of this what this choice says is not supported. This choice simply says that the author has failed to establish that the trend in Technology Inc.’s revenue growth is significantly different from the trends in revenue growth of the company’s peers, and arriving at the argument’s conclusion does not require establishing that these trends are different. There are many reasons why the trends could be similar even if the conclusion of this argument is correct. As a matter of fact, it’s possible that, even if the trends are similar, the reason why the trends are the similar is that the CEOs of Technology Inc.’s peers are also better at recruiting and hiring than human resources professionals.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss the major difference between a Logical Flaw question and a Weaken the Argument Question.

Lesson Content The more basic type of Logical Flaw question is a pure Logical Flaw question.
💡MUST KNOW:
Some Logical Flaw Questions are pure Logical Flaw questions, meaning that they ask us to point out a flaw in the argument as it currently stands and without bringing in new information in the correct answer.
Let’s look at an example stimulus of a pure Logical Flaw question:
In a recent medical study, all of the study participants who had in the past five years abused barbiturates, powerful prescription sedatives commonly used to treat anxiety, were found to be suffering with depression.
title: 10.2.1 Pure Logical Flaw Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Logical Flaw questions may seem to be rather similar to Weaken the Argument questions.
As we’ve discussed, almost always, in order to weaken the conclusion in a Weaken the Argument question, the correct answer option brings in new, outside information.
On the other hand, in answering a pure Logical Flaw question, we are not seeking to weaken the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering a pure Logical Flaw question, we are not seeking to weaken the argument. Rather, our goal is to highlight an aspect of the argument that is flawed.
In other words, in answering questions of this type, our job is not to bring in outside, new information that weakens the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
In a pure Logical Flaw question, a flaw in the argument must always exist within the argument, and without the interjection of outside information.
Okay, having reviewed the difference between pure Logical Flaw questions and Weaken questions, let’s now discuss the second type of Logical Flaw question, a question that is a hybrid of a Weaken the Argument question and a Logical Flaw question.

On the other hand, in answering a pure Logical Flaw question, we are not seeking to weaken the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering a pure Logical Flaw question, we are not seeking to weaken the argument.
title: 10.2.2 Reviewing the Difference Between a Logical Flaw Question and a Weaken the Argument Question

contents:
Lesson Content The second Logical Flaw question type contains questions that are hybrids of Weaken the Argument questions and pure Logical Flaw questions.
💡MUST KNOW:
Some Logical Flaw Questions are hybrids of Weaken the Argument questions and pure Logical Flaw questions. These questions also ask us to point out a flaw in the author’s argument, but they bring in new information in the correct answer, in a way quite similar to the way in which regular Weaken the Argument questions do.
Let’s look at a stimulus from a hybrid Logical Flaw/Weaken the Argument question.
In order to reduce crowding in the parking lots at Listria University, the university’s facilities director has proposed an increase in parking fees of one dollar per hour in all university lots. The facilities director believes that, once the fee for even one hour of parking exceeds the cost of a round-trip tram ticket to the university from nearby Studentville, most students will choose to take the tram rather than park their cars at the university.
Which of the following statements, if true, provides the best evidence that the facilities director’s reasoning is flawed?
In this question, a proposal is introduced: increase the university’s parking fees.
Is this proposal completely logical? Hmm – maybe not, right? As presented, there is no clear link allowing us to see that this proposal would change the situation for the students, in other words, that the increase in parking fees would provide a strong reason for a change in the students’ parking behavior.
Here’s a possible correct answer to the question:
(Correct Answer) The vast majority of Listria University students come from local towns and, therefore, do not reside in Studentville while studying at the university.
The correct answer serves to point out that the author, in making her argument, has made an assumption that may not be valid, that using the tram to travel from Studentville to the university is a viable option for students.
Notice that the correct answer focused on the vulnerability of an assumption made by the author.
💡MUST KNOW:
Be prepared to answer questions that are blends of Logical Flaw and Weaken the Argument questions.
Below are two examples of question stems that blur the distinction between Logical Flaw and Weaken the Argument question types.
Which of the following, if true, would point to a possible flaw in the plan described?
Which of the following, if true, points to the most serious logical flaw in the argument?
Notice, while the correct answers to pure Logical Flaw questions are always true, since they describe characteristics of the arguments presented, the two stems above ask for answers that, “if true,” point to flaws.
Let’s practice answering questions that are blends of Logical Flaw and Weaken the Argument questions.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 3 Analyst: Two years ago, Redcorp, a consumer products company, hired a new chief marketing officer. Which of the following, if true, points to a possible flaw in the analyst’s argument? 1. [ ] (A) Over the last two years, the costs of the materials that Redcorp uses in producing the products it sells have not increased significantly. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) Over the course of the past two years, the unemployment rate of the country in which Redcorp sells most of its products went from 25 percent to 5 percent. 3. [ ] (C) Redcorp’s sales revenue had been increasing by about 5 percent each year over the decade prior to the arrival of the new chief marketing officer. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) The new chief marketing officer was able to significantly increase the sales revenue at the company at which she worked prior to joining Redcorp. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) The new chief marketing officer raised the prices of the consumer products sold by Redcorp. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium Premise: Two years ago, Redcorp hired a new chief marketing officer.
Premise: Since that time, Redcorp’s sales revenue has increased by 50 percent each year.
Conclusion: Clearly, the new chief marketing officer is responsible for the increase in Redcorp’s sales revenue.
Notice that the author is making the cause-and-effect claim that the new chief marketing officer is responsible for the increase in Redcorp’s sales revenue. As always, we must be skeptical anytime we are presented with a cause-and-effect claim. In analyzing this argument, we must ask ourselves whether the new chief marketing officer is really responsible for the increase in Redcorp’s sales revenue. Might there have been another cause? What might the analyst have failed to consider?
(A) Over the last two years, the costs of the materials that Redcorp uses in producing the products it sells have not increased significantly. The argument’s conclusion is that the new chief marketing officer is responsible for the increase in Redcorp’s sales revenue.
What this answer choice says, that, over the last two years, the costs of the materials that Redcorp uses in producing the products it sells have not increased significantly, is not clearly connected to the increase in the company’s sales revenue.
Yes, the fact that costs of materials have not increased might be a reason why Redcorp would be a healthy or profitable company. However, the fact that the costs of materials have not increased would not serve as an alternative explanation for the increase in sales revenue, and, in general, this information about materials costs is not clearly connected to the increase in sales revenue.
All this choice really tells us is that a variable, costs of materials, has not increased. The fact that a variable related to Redcorp’s business has not increased does not highlight any flaw in the reasoning via which the author arrived at the conclusion that the new chief marketing officer is responsible for the increase in Redcorp’s sales revenue. Regardless of the fact that materials costs did not increase, the fact that sales revenue increased after the new chief marketing officer was hired supports the conclusion that the new chief marketing officer is responsible for the increase in sales revenue.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) Over the course of the past two years, the unemployment rate of the country in which Redcorp sells most of its products went from 25 percent to 5 percent. What this answer choice says points to a possible flaw in the analyst’s argument by highlighting that there could be an alternative explanation for why Redcorp’s sales revenue has increased by 50 percent each year since the arrival of the new chief marketing officer.
After all, if over the course of the past two years, the unemployment rate of the country in which Redcorp sells most of its products went from 25 percent to 5 percent, then way more people have jobs now than had jobs two years ago. If way more people have jobs now than had jobs two years ago, then many people have more money to spend than they had two years ago. Of course, it is common knowledge that, when people have more money to spend, they spend more on goods and services, and so, the huge decrease in the unemployment rate could explain why Redcorp’s sales volume increased.
Notice that this answer choice weakens the support for the conclusion by providing new information on a topic not discussed by the analyst who made the argument. Also, notice that this question is a hybrid between a Weaken the Argument question and a Logical Flaw question.
(C) Redcorp’s sales revenue had been increasing by about 5 percent each year over the decade prior to the arrival of the new chief marketing officer. If anything, the fact that Redcorp’s sales revenue had been increasing by about 5 percent each year prior to the arrival of the new chief marketing officer strengthens the case for the analyst’s conclusion by showing that, when the cause (the new chief marketing officer) was not at Redcorp, the effect (sales revenue increased by 50 percent each year) did not occur.
Of course, a fact that strengthens the case for the conclusion does not point to a flaw in the argument.
(D) The new chief marketing officer was able to significantly increase the sales revenue at the company at which she worked prior to joining Redcorp. If anything, what this is choices says is in line with the reasoning presented in the argument.
After all, if the new chief marketing officer was able to significantly increase the sales revenue at the company at which she worked prior to joining Redcorp, then we are a little bit more willing to accept that she may have also been responsible for the increase in Redcorp’s sales revenue.
Of course, in answering this question, we are not seeking an answer choice that is in line with the argument; we are seeking one that, if true, points to a possible flaw in the argument.
(E) The new chief marketing officer raised the prices of the consumer products sold by Redcorp. The argument’s conclusion is that the new chief marketing officer is responsible for the increase in Redcorp’s sales revenue.
What this answer choice says is in line with the argument because it provides a potential explanation for how she may have increased Redcorp’s sales revenue: she may have increased the revenue by raising prices.
Of course, in answering this question, we are not seeking an answer choice that is in line with the argument; we are seeking one that, if true, points to a possible flaw in the argument.
Correct Answer: (B) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 4 In Creos, even people employed full time at the previous minimum wage would not earn incomes above the official poverty level. Which of the following, if true, would serve to call attention to a flaw in the above argument? 1. [ ] (A) Not all people in Creos whose incomes are below the official poverty level feel that they live in poverty. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) In Creos there has been an increase in the difference between the incomes of those earning the most and those earning the least. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) Past increases in the minimum wage in Creos have not been sufficient to eradicate poverty in the country. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) Employers surveyed have indicated that further increases in the minimum wage would cause them to reduce staffing. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Some people in Creos are experiencing chronic illnesses of severity such that the people experiencing the illnesses feel that they are unable to work. View Solution Question Level: hard Conclusion: There will not be anyone living in poverty in Creos.
The conclusion of the argument is based on the fact that someone working full time at the current minimum wage would have an income greater than the income officially considered a poverty level income. Is this one fact sufficient for arriving at the conclusion that absolutely nobody in Creos will be living in poverty? Not really. There could be other factors that would cause a person to live in poverty, even if working full time at minimum wage would theoretically result in a person’s not living in poverty. So, the argument depends on an unwarranted assumption, the assumption that there is only one factor, the level of the minimum wage, that can cause a person to live in poverty.
(A) Not all people in Creos whose incomes are below the official poverty level feel that they live in poverty. This choice is worded to sound as if it makes a difference. By indicating that people earning below poverty level incomes don’t necessarily feel that they are living in poverty, this choice seems to disconnect the concept of poverty from the concept of income level and, thus, from the minimum wage. Notice, however, that, since what this choice says does not conflict with the idea that nobody in Creos will be living in poverty, it does not serve to call attention to a flaw in the argument.
(B) In Creos there has been an increase in the difference between the incomes of those earning the most and those earning the least. Since an increase in the difference between the incomes of those earning the most and those earning the least does not change the fact that, at the current minimum wage, one can earn an above poverty level income, this choice does not highlight a flaw in the argument.
(C) Past increases in the minimum wage in Creos have not been sufficient to eradicate poverty in the country. Regardless of whether past increases in the minimum wage have been sufficient to eradicate poverty in the country, this minimum wage, at which one can earn an above poverty level income, may be sufficient.
(D) Employers surveyed have indicated that further increases in the minimum wage would cause them to reduce staffing. This choice could be tempting, because it points to a flaw in the support for a conclusion that is related to the conclusion of this argument. If the conclusion of the argument were that minimum wage increases always benefit workers, then this choice would highlight a possible flaw, by indicating that the fact that employers continued hiring after this minimum wage increase is no guarantee that they will continue hiring no matter how much the minimum wage increases. However, since the conclusion is simply that this particular minimum wage increase will cause an end to poverty in Creos, this choice does not point out a flaw in the argument.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Some people in Creos are experiencing chronic illnesses of severity such that the people experiencing the illnesses feel that they are unable to work. By indicating that a person in Creos may not work because the person is experiencing an illness, this choice calls attention to an unwarranted assumption upon which the argument relies. This choice calls attention to the fact that, even if, by working at the minimum wage, one can earn an above poverty level income, people in Creos could still live in poverty for reasons other than the level of the minimum wage, such as illness. So, this choice calls attention to the fact that the conclusion of the argument relies on the unwarranted assumption that there are no factors other than the level of the minimum wage that could cause a person in Creos to live in poverty.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 5 In 2006 an outbreak of E. Which of the following, if true, would point to a possible flaw in the farmers’ plan? 1. [x] (A) Plants and animals that live in natural habitats support crop yields by purifying water and preying on pests that damage crops. 2. [ ] (B) Many wild animals that harbor E. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) The 2006 E. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) It is possible to keep wildlife from entering farms by erecting fences, even if there are wildlife habitats near the farms. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) In many cases litigation related to food borne infections would be directed at wholesale buyers of produce rather than at farmers. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: hard By clearing areas of wildlife habitat that are near their farms, the farmers hope to achieve the following outcome:
Projected Outcome of the Plan: Farmers will safeguard their profits.
The question stem asks us to find an answer choice that points to a possible flaw in the farmers’ plan. A choice that indicates that clearing wildlife habitat near the farms would not necessarily lead to safeguarding the farmers’ profits would point out a flaw in the plan.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) Plants and animals that live in natural habitats support crop yields by purifying water and preying on pests that damage crops. This choice calls attention to a possibly incorrect assumption that the farmers have made in clearing the areas of natural habitat near their farms, the assumption that doing so will only serve to safeguard profitability. If the plants and animals living in those areas support the crop yields of the farms, then by clearing those areas, the farmers could be reducing the yields of their farms and thus reducing profitability. Thus, this choice calls attention to a possible flaw in the farmers’ plan.
(B) Many wild animals that harbor E. Coli are extremely unlikely to contaminate crops, even if the animals inhabit areas near farms. This choice could be tempting, because it could be perceived as attacking the assumption that clearing the habitats near the farms will make a difference in the probability of crops’ becoming contaminated with E. Coli. However, even if many animals would not contaminate crops, some animals still might. So, even given what this choice says, it could make sense to conclude that, by clearing the areas, farmers could be safeguarding their profits.
(C) The 2006 E. Coli outbreak was linked only to bagged spinach and to no other types of produce. This choice could be perceived as indicating that farmers who don’t farm spinach would be wasting their time by seeking to keep their produce from being contaminated. However, while that outbreak was linked to spinach only, the next could be linked to another type of produce. So, any produce farmer might be safeguarding profits by clearing areas of wildlife habitat near that farmer’s farm.
(D) It is possible to keep wildlife from entering farms by erecting fences, even if there are wildlife habitats near the farms. This is a typical alternative plan answer choice, one that could seem to point out a weakness in a plan via indicating that the plan may not be necessary, as there is an alternative plan that would work. In this case, the alternative plan is the use of fences. The truth is, however, that, regardless of whether, via the use of fences, it may be possible to keep wildlife out of farms without clearing wildlife habitat near farms, clearing wildlife habitat could serve to safeguard farmers’ profits.
(E) In many cases litigation related to food borne infections would be directed at wholesale buyers of produce rather than at farmers. This choice misses the point of the argument. The farmers are seeking to safeguard profits because wholesale buyers of produce are extremely wary of buying produce that may have been contaminated with E. Coli. So, regardless of whether litigation related to E. Coli infections would be directed at farmers, seeking to safeguard profits by taking measures to ensure that produce is not contaminated could make sense.
Correct Answer: (A) At this point, you may wish to review the Weaken the Argument chapter, because many of the common flaws in reasoning that we looked at in the context of Weaken the Argument questions appear also in Logical Flaw questions.
Let’s now discuss some common flaws in reasoning that appear in Logical Flaw questions.
summary:
Lesson Content The second Logical Flaw question type contains questions that are hybrids of Weaken the Argument questions and pure Logical Flaw questions.
💡MUST KNOW:
Some Logical Flaw Questions are hybrids of Weaken the Argument questions and pure Logical Flaw questions. These questions also ask us to point out a flaw in the author’s argument, but they bring in new information in the correct answer, in a way quite similar to the way in which regular Weaken the Argument questions do.
title: 10.2.3 Logical Flaw Questions Hybridized with Weaken the Argument Questions

contents:
Lesson Content There are several mistakes (flaws) in reasoning that authors commonly make in their arguments.
Errors in Reasoning Involving Cause and Effect Overgeneralizing and Making Sampling Errors Confusion Related to Numbers, Percentages or Other Numerical Information Conclusions that are not Supported by the Evidence Confusing Necessary or Significant Information with Sufficient Information The good news is that any of these concepts that are fair game in Weaken the Argument questions are also fair game in Logical Flaw questions.
But, in adhering to the deep learning mastery principles of Target Test Prep, we’re going to reinforce these concepts once again.
Let’s begin by discussing logical flaws in cause-and-effect reasoning.
summary:
Lesson Content There are several mistakes (flaws) in reasoning that authors commonly make in their arguments.
Errors in Reasoning Involving Cause and Effect Overgeneralizing and Making Sampling Errors Confusion Related to Numbers, Percentages or Other Numerical Information Conclusions that are not Supported by the Evidence Confusing Necessary or Significant Information with Sufficient Information The good news is that any of these concepts that are fair game in Weaken the Argument questions are also fair game in Logical Flaw questions.
title: 10.2.4 Common Flaws in Reasoning Found in Logical Flaw Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Authors of arguments often misconstrue or misperceive the nature of cause-and-effect relationships.
In Logical Flaw questions, we often see authors and speakers making mistakes associated with cause-and-effect reasoning.
The most common mistakes center on the following three flaws in reasoning:
Here’s an error in cause-and-effect reasoning in the context of a Logical Flaw question:
The transportation secretary of the city of Niezque noticed that, in areas of the city in which there were many bicycle repair shops, there tended to be great numbers of people using bicycles. Therefore, she proposed that, in order to increase the use of bicycles in the city, the city offer tax breaks to encourage the opening of more bicycle repair shops.
A flaw in transportation secretary’s reasoning is that she failed to consider the possibility that
(Correct Answer) the actual relationship between great numbers of cyclists and the presence of many bicycle repair shops is the reverse of the one upon which the success of the plan depends.
The transportation secretary observed the correlation between the presence of many bicycle repair shops and great numbers of cyclists and arrived at a cause-and-effect conclusion, the conclusion that the presence of many bicycle repair shops causes there to be great numbers of cyclists.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Logical Flaw questions, we often see authors and speakers making mistakes associated with cause-and-effect reasoning.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 6 The Dental Association maintains that adding fluoride to drinking water in order to prevent cavities is a practice that is safe for human health. Which of the following is the best criticism of the argument above? 1. [ ] (A) It fails to consider the possibility that people may feel that avoiding experiencing cavities is worth an increased risk of experiencing attention and learning issues. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) It assumes without sufficient warrant that all people experience the same effects from consuming fluoridated drinking water. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) It overlooks the possibility that the regions in which water is fluoridated share other characteristics that underlie the observed attention and learning issues. 4. [ ] (D) It ignores the fact that dentists’ supporting fluoridation conflicts with some of their own interests, as the need for their services might increase were drinking water not fluoridated. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) It fails to establish that, before water fluoridation began, no children in the areas in which water is now fluoridated experienced attention and learning issues. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy Conclusion: Adding fluoride to drinking water is not safe for human health.
The conclusion is based on an observed correlation between fluoridation of drinking water in certain regions and relatively high incidences of children’s experiencing attention and learning issues in those regions.
