Without specifically saying so, the author is indicating that fluoridation is a cause of these issues. What could be wrong with the author’s reasoning?
(A) It fails to consider the possibility that people may feel that avoiding experiencing cavities is worth an increased risk of experiencing attention and learning issues. What if it were true that people feel that avoiding experiencing cavities is worth an increased risk of experiencing attention and learning issues? Would the author’s reasoning be undermined? Not at all. It could still be the case that water fluoridation causes attention and learning issues, and is, therefore, not safe for human health.
(B) It assumes without sufficient warrant that all people experience the same effects from consuming fluoridated drinking water. Does the author really assume what this choice says the author assumes? What if the author did not assume that all people experience the same effects from consuming fluoridated drinking water? Would the argument still work? Yes, it would. Even if we assume that people experience various different effects from drinking fluoridated water, drinking such water could be unsafe for human health.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) It overlooks the possibility that the regions in which water is fluoridated share other characteristics that underlie the observed attention and learning issues. The author observes the correlation between water fluoridation in certain regions and relatively high incidences of attention and learning issues in those regions and assumes that the fluoridation must be causing the issues. In doing so, the author is overlooking the possibility that there is some other characteristic that these regions share that is the actual cause of the attention and learning issues. For instance, it could be that regions in which drinking water is fluoridated are also regions in which children experience more stress than is experienced by children in other regions. In such a case, stress, rather than fluoridation, could be the cause of the attention and learning issues, and, therefore, fluoridation may be safe for human health.
(D) It ignores the fact that dentists’ supporting fluoridation conflicts with some of their own interests, as the need for their services might increase were drinking water not fluoridated. This answer could be tempting, because it is true that supporting fluoridation does, at least in a way, conflict with certain interests of dentists, and the existence of this conflict of interest could be perceived as supporting the idea that the opinion about fluoridation expressed by the dentists is correct, as in, “Why would dentists say something incorrect if saying it actually conflicts with their own interests?” However, even if one were not to ignore the existence of this apparent conflict of interest, one could still conclude from the evidence that fluoridation causes attention and learning issues, and that, as sincere as the dentists may be in expressing their opinion, their opinion is incorrect.
(E) It fails to establish that, before water fluoridation began, no children in the areas in which water is now fluoridated experienced attention and learning issues. The conclusion of the argument is not that fluoridation is the sole cause of attention and learning issues. Therefore, there is no need for the author to establish that, before fluoridation began, there were no attention and learning issues. The evidence employed is that the incidences of attention and learning issues are higher where there is fluoridation, and this evidence could still be used to support the conclusion, even if, before fluoridation began, some children experienced attention and learning issues.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss the second type of logical flaw, those involving overgeneralization and sampling mistakes.
summary:
Lesson Content Authors of arguments often misconstrue or misperceive the nature of cause-and-effect relationships.
In Logical Flaw questions, we often see authors and speakers making mistakes associated with cause-and-effect reasoning.
The most common mistakes center on the following three flaws in reasoning:
Here’s an error in cause-and-effect reasoning in the context of a Logical Flaw question:
The transportation secretary of the city of Niezque noticed that, in areas of the city in which there were many bicycle repair shops, there tended to be great numbers of people using bicycles.
title: 10.2.5 Logical Flaw One: Errors in Reasoning Involving Cause and Effect

contents:
Lesson Content As we have already discussed in another chapter, when making a generalization, we base a broad claim about a larger population upon observations of a small sample of a population.
For example, let’s say that we know ten people who live in Dallas, and all vote Republican.
In answering Logical Flaw questions, be on the lookout for broad generalizations made from small samples; such generalizations will be suspect.
💡MUST KNOW:
When we analyze data from a relatively small sample, we risk making incorrect conclusions. In answering Logical Flaw questions, be on the lookout for broad generalizations made from small samples; such generalizations will be suspect.
Consider the following simple example:
Help Desk Manager: We use independently administered customer satisfaction surveys to rate the performance of our technical help team, and we recently switched from using one survey provider to using another. After the switch, the average customer satisfaction rating of our technical team immediately increased. Since the new survey provider is using a survey that is longer and more detailed than the survey used by the previous provider, clearly the higher ratings from the new survey are more accurate than were the lower ratings from the previous survey.
Which of the following statements, if true, provides the best evidence that the help desk manager’s reasoning is flawed?
(Correct Answer) The more positive a person’s relationship with a technical help team the more likely the person will take the time to respond to a long, detailed survey for the company.
In previous questions, you’ve seen the flaws inherent to using surveys to collect data.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 7 Bicycle Company Representative: It’s clear that, when we release our new model for sale to the general public, it will become one of the most popular bicycles in the marketplace, because, when we gave bicycles of our new model to ten professional cyclists to try for a month, ten out of ten liked the bicycles so much that they asked to keep them after the trial period ended. Which of the following best describes the greatest weakness in the bicycle company representative’s argument? 1. [ ] (A) It assumes without warrant that professional cyclists are good judges of the quality of a bicycle. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) The evidence provided in support of the claim made is not consistent with that claim. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) The support for its conclusion is based in part on the presupposition that its conclusion is valid. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) The support for its conclusion is based on data unrelated to that conclusion. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) Its conclusion is based on a sample that may be unrepresentative of the set that the conclusion is about. View Solution Question Level: easy The bicycle company representative has arrived at the following conclusion:
Conclusion: When we release our new model for sale to the general public, it will become one of the most popular bicycles in the marketplace.
In arriving at that conclusion, the bicycle company representative has made the assumption that the opinions of a sample of people (ten professional cyclists) are representative of the opinions of people in general. Is this assumption a good one? Well, it may be correct, but also, it very well may not be – right? Notice how the bicycle company representative considers a sample that is both very small and not necessarily representative of the opinions of people in general yet draws a sweeping conclusion about popularity of the bicycle with the general public. Beware of Critical Reasoning arguments that base broad conclusions on very specific evidence.
(A) It assumes without warrant that professional cyclists are good judges of the quality of a bicycle. This choice may seem to be correct, because the bicycle company representative’s argument does rely on the opinions of the ten professional cyclists without questioning whether those cyclists are good judges of the quality of a bicycle. The reason this choice is, nevertheless, incorrect is that the assumption that the professional cyclists are good judges of the quality of a bicycle is not “unwarranted.” Since it’s reasonable to presume that professional cyclists are knowledgeable about bicycles, assuming that they are good judges of the quality of a bicycle is not unwarranted but, rather, reasonable.
(B) The evidence provided in support of the claim made is not consistent with that claim. While the sample used to support the argument may not be representative of the opinions of the general public, what this choice says about the evidence is subtly incorrect. The word “inconsistent” does not correctly describe the logical flaw here. The evidence that the professional cyclists like the bicycle isn’t inconsistent with the conclusion that the bicycle will become one of most popular bicycles in the marketplace.
(C) The support for its conclusion is based in part on the presupposition that its conclusion is valid. This answer choice is tempting, because the fact that the conclusion, that the new bicycle will become one of the most popular bicycles in the marketplace, appears at the beginning of the passage could be perceived as indicating that argument uses the conclusion to support the conclusion. However, the truth is that the support for the conclusion is not the conclusion itself but, rather, the opinions of the professional cyclists. As is the case with choice (B), to avoid choosing this trap, we must read carefully.
(D) The support for its conclusion is based on data unrelated to that conclusion. This choice uses words that seem to describe this argument, “employs data unrelated to the conclusion,” and, since a test-taker may sense that something is wrong with basing a conclusion about the popularity of a bicycle with the general public on professional cyclists’ opinions of that bicycle, this answer could seem to be correct.
However, some answer choices sound “smart” unless you really understand what they are saying. The GMAT often strings together words and phrases that sound very intelligent while saying practically nothing – or worse, something just a tiny bit incorrect but not incorrect enough that you would notice unless you look carefully. In this case, while the sample may not be representative of the opinions of the general public, the opinions of the professional cyclists are certainly related to the conclusion about the popularity of the bicycle. So, saying that the data is “unrelated to the conclusion” is incorrect.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Its conclusion is based on a sample that may be unrepresentative of the set that the conclusion is about. This answer sums up the argument’s greatest weakness. A claim regarding the popularity of the new bicycle with the general public is based on a sample of opinions of professional cyclists, which may not be representative of the opinions of people in general. Since professional cyclists may want bicycles with characteristics quite different from the bicycle characteristics preferred by most members of the general public, it does not make sense to assume that the fact that ten professional cyclists like a bicycle of a certain model means that that bicycle will become one of the most popular bicycles in the marketplace. Notice that this correct answer highlights a flaw in an assumption made by the author.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss how confusion related to numerical information can result in flaws in arguments.
summary:
Lesson Content As we have already discussed in another chapter, when making a generalization, we base a broad claim about a larger population upon observations of a small sample of a population.
For example, let’s say that we know ten people who live in Dallas, and all vote Republican.
In answering Logical Flaw questions, be on the lookout for broad generalizations made from small samples; such generalizations will be suspect.
title: 10.2.6 Logical Flaw Two: Overgeneralization and Sampling Flaws

contents:
Lesson Content As has been discussed in other chapters, authors commonly make errors in reasoning based on numbers and percentages.
Consider the following example:
During the past 25 years, the percentage of Greysoft employees who invest their retirement monies in the company mutual fund has decreased by fifteen percent. Since a certain number of investors withdraw their money from the company mutual fund each year, to function optimally, the mutual fund requires a consistent number of new investors. Therefore, this decrease will have a significant, deleterious effect on Greysoft’s mutual fund program.
A serious flaw in the reasoning of the argument is that
Is this a sound argument? Is the author making any questionable assumptions? Notice that an assumption in this argument is that the fact that there was a decrease in the percentage of Greysoft employees who invest in the company’s mutual fund necessarily means that there has been a decrease in the number of investors in the company’s mutual fund.
While the conclusion would be true if the number of Greysoft employees has remained the same, it is entirely possible that the company has expanded.
(Correct Answer) the author assumes without sufficient warrant that a percentage decrease represents a decrease in an absolute number.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Logical Flaw questions, be on the lookout for errors involving numbers, percentages, or other numerical information.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 8 Campaign Director: In the last election, our candidate lost by just a few percentage points. Which of the following best describes a weakness in the support for the prediction made? 1. [ ] (A) It neglects to explain why more people would vote for the candidate mentioned in this election than voted for this candidate in the previous election. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) It does not include consideration of whether voter approval for the candidate mentioned has increased or voter approval for the opposing candidate has decreased. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) It fails to establish exactly what number of voters would have had to vote differently in order for the candidate mentioned to have won the previous election. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) It fails to account for the possibility that the number of people planning to vote in the current election is significantly greater than the number who voted in the previous election. 5. [ ] (E) It does not include consideration of the possibility that many voters surveyed had only recently decided to vote for the candidate mentioned. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium Conclusion: Our candidate is going to win the current election by a comfortable margin.
The conclusion is based on what seems to be compelling mathematical information. The candidate mentioned lost the previous election by just a few percentage points, and voter surveys indicate that 9 percent more voters plan to vote for this candidate in the current election. So, it seems likely that this candidate will win by a comfortable margin.
What could be wrong with this reasoning?
(A) It neglects to explain why more people would vote for the candidate mentioned in this election than voted for this candidate in the previous election. Does the support for the conclusion have to include an explanation for why more people will vote for the candidate mentioned in the current election than voted for this candidate in the previous election? Not at all. Regardless of why more people will vote for this candidate, if there is reason to believe that more people will vote for this candidate, a reason such as the survey results mentioned, the prediction is supported.
(B) It does not include consideration of whether voter approval for the candidate mentioned has increased or voter approval for the opposing candidate has decreased. This choice is similar to choice (A), in that in order to support the conclusion that the candidate mentioned will win, we don’t need to know whether the number of votes for this candidate increased as a result of increased approval for this candidate. Regardless of what underlies the increase in votes for this candidate, as long as there is a clear reason to believe that this candidate will get enough votes to win, the prediction is supported.
(C) It fails to establish exactly what number of voters would have had to vote differently in order for the candidate mentioned to have won the previous election. It ‘s true that the argument does not establish how many voters would have had to vote differently in the previous election in order for the candidate mentioned to have won. At the same time, in failing to do so, is the argument flawed? No. We don’t need that information to conclude that, since this candidate lost by only a few percentage points last time, a 9 percent increase in votes for this candidate will be enough to result in this candidate’s winning the election.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) It fails to account for the possibility that the number of people planning to vote in the current election is significantly greater than the number who voted in the previous election. If the number of voters who plan to vote in this election is significantly greater than the number who voted in the last election, it could be that a 9 percent increase in votes for the candidate mentioned will mean that this candidate will still lose the election, or that this candidate will not win by a “comfortable margin.” For instance, if the total number of people planning to vote in this election is 20 percent greater than the number who voted in the previous election, just to get the same percentage of all votes that this candidate got in the previous election, this candidate would have to get 20 percent more votes. In such a case, a 9 percent increase in the number of voters planning to vote for this candidate would mean a decrease in the percentage of all voters who are for this candidate. If the total number of people who plan to vote in the current election is 7 percent greater than the total number who voted in the previous election, with a 9 percent increase in votes, this candidate might just barely win. So, a weakness in the support for the prediction is that the support fails to account for the possibility that the total number of people planning to vote in this election is significantly greater than the number who voted in the previous election, in which scenario, a 9 percent increase in the number of votes for the candidate mentioned could still result in a loss for this candidate.
(E) It does not include consideration of the possibility that many voters surveyed had only recently decided to vote for the candidate mentioned. This choice could be tempting if a test-taker perceives it as pointing out the possibility that, since many voters only recently decided to vote for the candidate mentioned, they could easily change their minds and vote for a different candidate. The truth is, however, that, even if many voters only recently decided to vote for this candidate, there is no clear reason to believe that they will change their minds and vote differently. So, failing to consider the possibility that many voters surveyed had only recently decided to vote for the candidate mentioned is not clearly a weakness in the support for the prediction.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s continue.
summary:
Lesson Content As has been discussed in other chapters, authors commonly make errors in reasoning based on numbers and percentages.
Consider the following example:
During the past 25 years, the percentage of Greysoft employees who invest their retirement monies in the company mutual fund has decreased by fifteen percent. Since a certain number of investors withdraw their money from the company mutual fund each year, to function optimally, the mutual fund requires a consistent number of new investors.
title: 10.2.7 Logical Flaw Three: Confusion Related to Numbers, Percentages, or Other Numerical Information

contents:
Lesson Content Sometimes an author will draw a conclusion that’s simply not supported by the evidence provided.
For example, an author could seek to support the conclusion that a product is safe to use by going on about all the testing of the product that has been done.
Notice how the evidence in the following example doesn’t really support the conclusion.
Professor: Over the past few years, I have noticed that the students coming to our school are less motivated than students here were in previous years. It seems that the school has become more focused on admitting students who can pay full tuition than it is on attracting students who actually want to learn.
Admissions Director: Your assessment is incorrect. Over the past few years we have been using the same application that we have always used, have received approximately the same number of applications that we have received in the past, and have continued to interview applicants as we always have.
The admissions director’s response is flawed in that
(Correct Answer) the evidence provided does not conflict with the assertion that the response is meant to refute.
The admissions director asserts that the professor’s assessment is incorrect, but then provides evidence that is only tangentially related to the issue.
💡MUST KNOW:
Arguments can be flawed because their conclusions are not actually supported by the evidence provided.
The impact of the evidence in such a flawed argument is not such that it supports the conclusion.
Consider the following examples:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 9 Vehicle Safety Products CEO: Our car seats for infants have undergone thousands of hours of testing over the last five years. The reasoning of the argument is most vulnerable to criticism that the argument 1. [ ] (A) fails to prove that the company’s car seats for infants will work well in trucks also. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) bases a general conclusion on characteristics of a small, unrepresentative sample. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) fails to consider the possibility that the most effective way to keep an infant in a car safe is to drive carefully. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) relies on the unwarranted assumption that all drivers of cars need seats for infants. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) presents as evidence a premise that is not clearly linked to its conclusion. View Solution Question Level: easy Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Vehicle Safety Products CEO: Our car seats for infants have undergone thousands of hours of testing over the last five years.
Conclusion: Clearly, then, our car seats for infants must be the safest possible infant seats that drivers can purchase for their cars.
We must always take care not to be “blinded by the light. ”
Does the fact that a product has been extensively tested indicate that the product is well made or that the product will do exactly what it is intended to do? Of course not, right? In fact, the fact that a product has been rigorously tested over many years could even indicate that the creators of the product are having a hard time getting to the product to do what they want it to do.
We should be skeptical when the CEO says that, because her car seats for infants have undergone thousands of hours of testing over the last five years, her car seats for infants must be the safest possible infant seats that drivers can purchase for their cars. There is not a clear connection between the evidence that she presents and her conclusion.
(A) fails to prove that the company’s car seats for infants will work well in trucks also. The CEO’s conclusion is that her company’s car seats for infants must be the safest possible infant seats that drivers can purchase for their cars.
Notice that she does not make any claims regarding trucks. Her claim is about cars. So, she need not prove that the company’s car seats for infants will work well in trucks also.
So, while it is true that the CEO does not prove that the company’s car seats for infants will work well in trucks also, the fact that she does not do so is not a flaw in the argument.
(B) bases a general conclusion on characteristics of a small, unrepresentative sample. The CEO says that her car seats for infants have undergone thousands of hours of testing over the last five years. “Thousands of hours of testing over five years” certainly would not be considered “a small, unrepresentative sample. ” In fact, that testing seems extensive.
So, what this answer choice says is not a flaw in the CEO’s argument, because this choice does not match what the CEO does in making her argument.
(C) fails to consider the possibility that the most effective way to keep an infant in a car safe is to drive carefully. The CEO’s conclusion is that her company’s car seats for infants must be the safest possible infant seats that drivers can purchase for their cars.
So, she certainly need not consider the possibility that the most effective way to keep an infant in a car safe is to drive carefully. Her conclusion is not about what the best way to keep an infant safe is. Her conclusion is about which car seats for infants are the safest.
So, while it may be true that the CEO has not considered the possibility that the most effective way to keep an infant in a car safe is to drive carefully, the fact that she has not done so is not a flaw in her argument.
(D) relies on the unwarranted assumption that all drivers of cars need seats for infants. The CEO does not assume that all drivers of cars need seats for infants. She’s simply making the claim that her company’s car seats for infants must be the safest possible infant seats that drivers can purchase for their cars. This claim could be true regardless of whether all drivers of cars need seats for infants.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) presents as evidence a premise that is not clearly linked to its conclusion. Notice that there is a big disconnect in the CEO’s argument.
Think about the following.
Does the fact that her car seats for infants have undergone thousands of hours of testing over the last five years allow her to conclude that her car seats for infants must be the safest possible infant seats that drivers can purchase for their cars? No, right? All that testing could have shown anything, including that the car seats are utterly unsafe.
In words, the fact that something has been heavily tested does not allow us to conclude that that thing works as it should. Perhaps the thing is being tested so much because whoever is making it can’t quite get it right.
Whatever the case may be, the fact that something has been tested tells us nothing about that thing other than that it has been tested.
So, what this answer choice says, that the argument presents as evidence a premise that is not clearly linked to its conclusion, captures the issue that the fact that the car seats have been tested does not provide any support for the conclusion that the seats are the safest possible infant seats that drivers can purchase for their cars.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 10 Companies that sell food products made with ingredients derived from genetically modified organisms (GMOs) oppose enactment of laws requiring that such products be labeled as containing GMOs. The reasoning of the argument is most vulnerable to criticism that the argument 1. [ ] (A) provides no evidence to support the assertion that there is nothing wrong with eating foods that contain ingredients derived from GMOs. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) presents as evidence an assertion that is not clearly linked to its conclusion. 3. [ ] (C) includes no consideration of the fact that companies that sell food products made with ingredients derived from GMOs have a vested interest in maintaining positive consumer perceptions of such foods. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) simply contradicts the assertion made by the companies without providing evidence that supports its point of view. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) makes a distinction between companies that sell food products made with GMO derived ingredients and companies that do not when, in reality, companies may sell both types of products. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard The passage presents a scenario in which companies that make foods containing GMO ingredients oppose enactment of laws requiring that the foods be labeled in such a way as to indicate that they contain such ingredients. Then, it presents a premise and a conclusion.
Premise: The companies assert that there is nothing wrong with eating foods that contain ingredients derived from GMOs.
Conclusion: The companies have no reason to believe that the labeling will discourage consumers from purchasing products that contain GMO derived ingredients.
Does the conclusion follow from the premise? Not really. Even though the companies assert that there is nothing wrong with eating foods that contain ingredients derived from GMOs, and even if there were in fact nothing wrong with eating such foods, the companies could have many reasons to believe that the labeling will discourage consumers from purchasing their products. For example, consumers could take the presence of the labels as an indication that there is in fact something wrong with eating such foods.
(A) provides no evidence to support the assertion that there is nothing wrong with eating foods that contain ingredients derived from GMOs. While the assertion that there is nothing wrong with eating foods that contain ingredients derived from GMOs appears in the argument, the argument does not depend on that assertion’s being valid. The argument is not making that assertion. Rather, the case being made is that the companies making that assertion have nothing to be concerned about.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) presents as evidence an assertion that is not clearly linked to its conclusion. As discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the argument jumps from saying that the companies have asserted that there is nothing wrong with eating foods made with GMO derived ingredients to concluding that the companies have no reason to believe that consumers will be discouraged from buying such foods by GMO labeling. The evidence and the conclusion are essentially statements about two different topics, and there is no clear link via which the evidence, the companies’ assertion, supports the conclusion.
(C) includes no consideration of the fact that companies that sell food products made with ingredients derived from GMOs have a vested interest in maintaining positive consumer perceptions of such foods. The conclusion of the argument is not about the motivations of the companies or about the validity of any assertions made by the companies. So, the fact that the companies have a vested interest in maintaining positive consumer perceptions of such foods would not undermine the support for the conclusion. Therefore, the fact that the argument includes no consideration of the fact that the companies have this vested interest is not a flaw in the argument.
(D) simply contradicts the assertion made by the companies without providing evidence that supports its point of view. This choice could be temping, because the argument seems to be in opposition to the position of the companies. However, the truth is that, if we look carefully, we see that the argument does not contradict the assertion that it says has been made by the companies.
(E) makes a distinction between companies that sell food products made with GMO derived ingredients and companies that do not when, in reality, companies may sell both types of products. This choice is blatantly incorrect. The argument makes no such distinction. Companies that sell both type of products are in fact companies that sell products made with GMO derived ingredients, and the argument does not say anything that is inconsistent with this fact.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now examine logical flaws that result from confusing necessary or significant information with sufficient information.
summary:
Lesson Content Sometimes an author will draw a conclusion that’s simply not supported by the evidence provided.
For example, an author could seek to support the conclusion that a product is safe to use by going on about all the testing of the product that has been done.
Notice how the evidence in the following example doesn’t really support the conclusion.
Professor: Over the past few years, I have noticed that the students coming to our school are less motivated than students here were in previous years.
title: 10.2.8 Logical Flaw Four: Conclusions that are Not Supported by the Evidence

contents:
Lesson Content Necessary information, significant information, and sufficient information differ from each other in key ways.
💡MUST KNOW:
Necessary information, significant information, and sufficient information differ from each other in key ways.
At the same time, since they are somewhat similar, these types of information can be misused in arguments, in which cases the arguments are flawed.
A key thing to understand about these types of information is that information that is necessary or significant is not necessarily the same as information that is sufficient for supporting a conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
Necessary information or significant information is not necessarily the same as information that is sufficient for supporting a conclusion.
To clearly see how these types of information differ, let’s consider the difference between conditions that are necessary and conditions that are sufficient.
A necessary condition is one that has to be satisfied in order for a conclusion to be correct or an outcome to occur.
💡MUST KNOW:
A necessary condition is one that has to be satisfied in order for a conclusion to be correct or an outcome to occur.
Here are two examples of necessary conditions:
Water is necessary for the growth of plants. All mammals are animals. A sufficient condition, on the other hand, is one that is sufficient to ensure that a conclusion is correct or an outcome will occur.
💡MUST KNOW:
A sufficient condition, on the other hand, is one that is sufficient to ensure that a conclusion is correct or an outcome will occur.
Are our examples of necessary conditions also examples of sufficient conditions? Let’s consider them again.
Is the presence of water sufficient for concluding that a plant will grow? No. Is lack of evidence indicating that something is not an animal sufficient for concluding that it is a mammal? No. Here are two examples of sufficient conditions:
If we know that an animal is a shark, then we have evidence sufficient for concluding that it is a fish. If we were to know for sure that a person will refuse to read any biography, then we would have evidence sufficient for concluding that a plan involving having that person read the biography of Max Weber will not work. When an argument that requires sufficient information for arriving at its conclusion uses necessary information or significant information instead, that argument will be flawed.
💡MUST KNOW:
When an argument that requires sufficient information for arriving at its conclusion uses necessary information or significant information instead, that argument will be flawed.
For instance, an argument that says that, since a company has a great product, that company must be profitable is likely flawed.
We saw an issue involving the difference between a necessary condition and a sufficient condition earlier in this chapter in the Creos minimum wage question.
In Creos, even people employed full time at the previous minimum wage would not earn incomes above the official poverty level. At the same time, while the income of a person working full time at the new, higher minimum wage would exceed the official poverty level, some economists felt that employers would be reluctant to hire people at the new wage level. Fortunately, the increase in the minimum wage has not deterred employers from hiring. Therefore, there will not be anyone living in poverty in Creos.
Which of the following, if true, would serve to call attention to a flaw in the above argument? (Correct Answer) Some people in Creos are experiencing chronic illnesses of severity such that the people experiencing the illnesses feel that they are unable to work.
A minimum wage that is above poverty level is significant, and it’s even fairly accurate to say that having the opportunity to earn an above poverty level wage is a condition necessary for people’s avoiding living in poverty.
💡MUST KNOW:
Arguments that appear in Logical Flaw questions can be flawed as a result of their using significant or necessary information where sufficient information is required for arriving at the arguments’ conclusions.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 11 Guatama Elementary School Principal: Our students’ reading scores have tended to be below the statewide average for many years. Which of the following best describes the error of reasoning made by the principal in the argument above? 1. [ ] (A) He fails to establish that the surfaces of the schoolyards of many other schools in the state do not contain toxic heavy metals. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) He overlooks the fact some students at Guatama Elementary School already score higher than the statewide average on reading tests. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) He fails to consider that, as a result of any increases in the reading scores of students at Guatama Elementary School, the statewide average will increase. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) He assumes without warrant that the impact of remedying the issue involving toxic heavy metals will be sufficient to eliminate the difference in test scores. 5. [ ] (E) He fails to specify how the issue involving toxic heavy metals in the surface of the schoolyard will be remedied. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy The principal states the following conclusion:
Conclusion: Our students’ reading scores will increase to the statewide average.
Is the conclusion well supported?
(A) He fails to establish that the surfaces of the schoolyards of many other schools in the state do not contain toxic heavy metals. This choice could be tempting, as it could be perceived as implying that the heavy metals may not be the cause of the relatively low test scores, since many other schools in the state may also have heavy metals in the surfaces of their schoolyards. One could reason that, if students at many other schools may also be exposed to heavy metals, and yet their scores are higher, then maybe heavy metals aren’t the issue.
The truth is, however, that that story is rather convoluted. For one thing, it could be that students at other schools whose schoolyard surfaces contain toxic heavy metals are also scoring below the statewide average on tests. Also, even if there are heavy metals in the surfaces of schoolyards of many other schools, reducing Gautama Elementary students’ exposure to heavy metals could result in their test scores increasing to match the statewide average.
(B) He overlooks the fact some students at Guatama Elementary School already score higher than the statewide average on reading tests. What if the principal had considered this fact? Would his doing so make any difference in the support for the conclusion? Even if some students at Guatama Elementary School already score higher than the statewide average on reading tests, it would still be the case that reading scores at Guatama Elementary have tended to be lower than the statewide average and that remedying the issue involving toxic metals could result in Guatama Elementary students’ reading scores increasing to match the statewide average.
(C) He fails to consider that, as a result of any increases in the reading scores of students at Guatama Elementary School, the statewide average will increase. This answer could be tempting, as one could take it as indicating that, even if reading scores of Guatama Elementary students increase, they may not increase enough to match the statewide average. However, this choice does not call attention to any real flaw in the argument. Even though the statewide average would increase in this way, it could still be that, because of the toxic metals issue’s being remedied, the difference between Guatama Elementary students’ scores and the statewide average will be eliminated.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) He assumes without warrant that the impact of remedying the issue involving toxic heavy metals will be sufficient to eliminate the difference in test scores. While it may be that Gautama Elementary students’ reading scores will increase as a result of remedying the toxic metals issue, concluding that remedying the issue will result in the elimination of the difference between their scores and the statewide average is going too far given the limited information available.
While it is true that toxic heavy metals can interfere with cognition and that such heavy metals have been discovered in the surface of the schoolyard, there could be multiple factors contributing to the existence of the difference between the test scores of students at Guatama Elementary School and the statewide average. So, while remedying the heavy metals issue may cause Guatama Elementary’s students test scores to increase, the principal’s argument is flawed, in that he assumes that the impact of remedying the issue will be sufficient for eliminating the difference in test scores without evidence indicating that it will be sufficient and that there are not additional factors that would have to be dealt with in order for the difference in test scores to be eliminated.
Remedying the toxic metals issue may be significant, but, in concluding that doing so is sufficient for eliminating the difference in test scores, the principal is making an unwarranted assumption.
(E) He fails to specify how the issue involving toxic heavy metals in the surface of the schoolyard will be remedied. Concluding that remedying the toxic metals issue will result in Guatama Elementary students’ test scores increasing to match the statewide average does not require specifying exactly how the remedying will be accomplished.
Correct Answer: (D) Now that you have a sense of the types of logical flaws that can be the bases of the correct answers to Logical Flaw questions, let’s get this Logical Flaw game down by considering some types of incorrect and trap answers with which Logical Flaw questions tempt test-takers.
summary:
Lesson Content Necessary information, significant information, and sufficient information differ from each other in key ways.
💡MUST KNOW:
Necessary information, significant information, and sufficient information differ from each other in key ways.
At the same time, since they are somewhat similar, these types of information can be misused in arguments, in which cases the arguments are flawed.
A key thing to understand about these types of information is that information that is necessary or significant is not necessarily the same as information that is sufficient for supporting a conclusion.
title: 10.2.9 Logical Flaw Five: Confusing Necessary or Significant Information with Sufficient Information

contents:
Lesson Content A key aspect of choosing correct answers to Logical Flaw questions is, of course, not getting tempted into choosing answer choices that are correct sounding but incorrect.
Let’s go through some types of Logical Flaw incorrect answers to better prepare you to see them for what they are.
summary:
Lesson Content A key aspect of choosing correct answers to Logical Flaw questions is, of course, not getting tempted into choosing answer choices that are correct sounding but incorrect.
Let’s go through some types of Logical Flaw incorrect answers to better prepare you to see them for what they are.
title: 10.3 Types of Incorrect Answers in Logical Flaw Questions

contents:
Lesson Content The most obviously wrong answer choices in Logical Flaw questions are those that describe things that authors or arguments don’t actually do.
Consider the following example:
Unlike other major automobile companies, all of which produce mostly automobiles that are fossil fuel powered, The Blitz Automobile Company makes only electric automobiles. Because electric automobiles are easier to maintain, less costly to power, and more environmentally friendly, they are superior to fossil fuel powered vehicles. Thus, soon, the Blitz Automobile Company will put all of the other automobile companies out of business.
Which of the following best describes the error of reasoning contained in the argument above?
(A) It neglects to consider that the other automobiles companies may shift to producing electric automobiles.
(B) It relies on an arbitrary distinction between products that does not reflect the realities of the automobile market.
(C) It presents as evidence in support of its conclusion information that is in conflict with other evidence presented.
We have already discussed the fact that choice (A) is correct because it brings up an unwarranted assumption upon which the argument relies.
(B) It relies on an arbitrary distinction between products that does not reflect the realities of the automobile market.
Choice (B) is blatantly incorrect.
OK, now for choice (C).
(C) It presents as evidence in support of its conclusion information that is in conflict with other evidence presented.
Let’s review the evidence presented:
Premise: Other major automobile companies all produce mostly automobiles that are fossil fuel powered.
Premise: The Blitz Automobile Company makes only electric automobiles.
Premise: Because electric automobiles are easier to maintain, less costly to power, and more environmentally friendly, they are superior to fossil fuel powered vehicles.
With a quick review of the evidence, we can see that no part of it conflicts with any other part.
💡MUST KNOW:
Some answer choices to Logical Flaw questions are blatantly incorrect because what they describe is not what the authors or arguments actually do.
Incorrect answer choices of this type are fairly easy to eliminate.
As you go through the following examples, you can develop your vision by noticing the characteristics of the blatantly incorrect choices, the characteristics of the more tempting but still incorrect choices, and the characteristics of the correct answer.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 12 An independent consumer agency has concluded that, although it is overly complicated to install and run on computers, Smart Suite, a next generation computer anti-virus software package, provides computer users the most protection against infection by computer viruses provided by any anti-virus software. The consumer agency’s reasoning is most vulnerable to criticism on which of the following grounds? 1. [ ] (A) It assumes without sufficient warrant that computer viruses that have infected computers cause problems for the users of those computers. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) It presents a cause-and-effect conclusion without providing any evidence to support that cause and effect conclusion. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) It overlooks the possibility that the computer users able to install and run overly complicated software would also be those most knowledgeable about avoiding computer viruses in the first place. 4. [ ] (D) It relies on an arbitrary distinction between the appearance of Smart Suite’s user interface and the appearances of user interfaces of other computer anti-virus software packages available to consumers. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) It neglects to consider that computer anti-virus software packages sold by other companies can help computer users prevent computer viruses from infecting their computers. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy Breakdown of the argument:
Conclusion:An independent consumer agency has concluded that, although it is overly complicated to install and run on computers, Smart Suite, a next generation computer anti-virus software package, provides computer users the most protection against infection by computer viruses provided by any anti-virus software.
Premise: Numerous independent surveys show that the computers of users of Smart Suite have the lowest rate of infection with computer viruses of any group of computers.
Notice that the author is essentially making the cause-and-effect claim that the Smart Suite software package is the cause of “the lowest rate of infection with computer viruses. ”
As usual, we must be skeptical of this cause-and-effect claim. In analyzing this argument, we must ask, “Is it actually the Smart Suite program that is responsible for computers of users of Smart Suite having the lowest rate of infection with computer viruses of any group of computers?”
Might there have been another cause? Could there be alternative explanation for the observation made? What might the author have failed to consider?
(A) It assumes without sufficient warrant that computer viruses that have infected computers cause problems for the users of those computers. Notice that the consumer agency’s argument says absolutely nothing about computer viruses causing problems. The argument is entirely about how effective Smart Suite is in providing protection against infection by computer viruses.
The consumer agency does not claim that such protection is important or that it prevents any problem.
The agency’s conclusion is a specific one, that Smart Suite provides computer users the most protection against infection by computer viruses provided by any anti-virus software. In arriving at that conclusion, the agency does not have to assume that computer viruses that have infected computers cause problems for the users of those computers, because, regardless of whether the viruses cause problems, Smart Suite could still provide the most protection against infection by them.
So, what this choice describes is something that the argument simply does not do.
(B) It presents a cause-and-effect conclusion without providing any evidence to support that cause and effect conclusion. Notice that what this answer choice says conflicts with what the argument does.
After all, the argument does provide evidence for the cause-and-effect conclusion; the last sentence of the passage says, “The agency offers as evidence the fact that numerous independent surveys show that the computers of users of Smart Suite have the lowest rate of infection with computer viruses of any group of computers. ”
We must be careful not to choose as correct a choice that describes something that the argument simply does not do.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) It overlooks the possibility that the computer users able to install and run overly complicated software would also be those most knowledgeable about avoiding computer viruses in the first place. Notice that the passage tells us that Smart Suite is overly complicated to install and run on computers. Common sense logic would tell us that the users of computers most apt to use a software product that is “overly complicated to install and run on computers” would be those who have above average knowledge of and skills with computers and computer programs. After all, we would not expect those with novice or even intermediate knowledge of computers to use such a program.
What other characteristics would people with above average knowledge of and skills with computers and computer programs have? Logic tells us that such users might also be those most knowledgeable about avoiding computer viruses in the first place. Right?
So, this choice brings up an interesting point.
That point is that, in arriving at its conclusion, the argument has overlooked an important possibility, the possibility that the reason for the correlation between use of Smart Suite and “the lowest rate of infection with computer viruses” is not that Smart Suite offers the most protection against infection by computer viruses but, rather, that the people who use Smart Suite are those who would understand how to avoid computer viruses in the first place.
In other words, this answer choice brings up the fact that the argument is flawed, in that it has overlooked an alternative explanation for pattern present in the survey results.
(D) It relies on an arbitrary distinction between the appearance of Smart Suite’s user interface and the appearances of user interfaces of other computer anti-virus software packages available to consumers. Notice that the passage simply does not do what this choice describes. If you need to, read the passage again to prove to yourself that the passage does not rely on any distinction having to do with user interfaces.
In answering a Flaw question, we must be careful not to choose as correct a choice that mentions something that the argument simply does not do.
(E) It neglects to consider that computer anti-virus software packages sold by other companies can help computer users prevent computer viruses from infecting their computers. Notice that the argument simply does not do what this choice describes.
The conclusion is that Smart Suite provides the most protection against infection by computer viruses provided by any anti-virus software.
Of course, in concluding that Smart Suite provides the most protection, the argument does not have to be neglecting to consider that computer anti-virus software packages sold by other companies can help computer users prevent computer viruses from infecting their computers. In fact, in saying that Smart Suite provides “the most protection,” the argument seems to be making a comparison and implying that other packages provide some protection, just not as much protection as Smart Suite provides.
In answering a Flaw question, we must be careful not to choose as correct a choice that mentions something that the argument simply does not do.
Correct Answer: (C) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 13 People suffering with kidney stones can be treated in various ways, such as lithotripsy, which involves using shock waves to break up the stones, or drug therapy. The argument is most vulnerable to criticism on which of the following grounds? 1. [ ] (A) Evidence it presents in support of its conclusion conflicts with other information it presents in support of the same conclusion. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) It overlooks the possibility that drugs used to treat kidney stones cause reductions in blood pressure. 3. [ ] (C) It arrives at a conclusion about the negative side effects of lithotripsy without considering those of other treatment methods. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) It makes an unwarranted generalization about the overall population from a small sample composed only of people treated for kidney stones. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) It assumes without sufficient warrant that there is no way in which to perform lithotripsy without damaging the treated kidneys. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard The purpose of the argument is to support the following conclusion.
Conclusion: Lithotripsy causes damage to the kidneys that results in high blood pressure.
The support for the conclusion consists of one premise.
Premise: People whose kidney stones were treated via lithotripsy are more likely to experience excessively high blood pressure following treatment than are people who use drug therapy.
Notice that the premise is a simple correlation between treatment via lithotripsy rather than drug therapy and a greater incidence of excessively high blood pressure. Clearly, the reason for the existence of this correlation could be different from the reason mentioned, that lithotripsy causes high blood pressure.
(A) Evidence it presents in support of its conclusion conflicts with other information it presents in support of the same conclusion. This choice is blatantly incorrect. The argument presents only one piece of information as evidence. That information cannot conflict with itself. So, this choice describes something that the argument does not do.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) It overlooks the possibility that drugs used to treat kidney stones cause reductions in blood pressure. The conclusion that lithotripsy “must” be the cause of the excessively high blood pressure is based on the correlation between higher blood pressure and use of lithotripsy rather than drug therapy. In arriving at this conclusion, the argument overlooks other possibilities, including the possibility that the incidence of excessively high blood pressure is lower in those who use drug therapy because drug therapy causes reductions in blood pressure.
(C) It arrives at a conclusion about the negative side effects of lithotripsy without considering those of other treatment methods. This choice is a little tricky, because it is true that the argument arrives at a conclusion about the negative side effects of lithotripsy without considering those of other treatment methods. However, since, in supporting its specific conclusion, which is simply that lithotripsy causes excessively high blood pressure, the argument does not have any reason to consider the side effects of other treatment methods, the fact that the argument does not consider the side effects of other treatment methods is not a flaw.
(D) It makes an unwarranted generalization about the overall population from a small sample composed only of people treated for kidney stones. This choice is blatantly incorrect, because the discussion in the argument is restricted to experiences of people treated for kidney stones, and, thus, no generalization about the overall population is made.
(E) It assumes without sufficient warrant that there is no way in which to perform lithotripsy without damaging the treated kidneys. This choice could be tempting, because the statements in the argument could be read as indicating that there is no way to perform lithotripsy without damaging the treated kidneys. The truth is, however, that the argument simply states that lithotripsy damages kidneys, without asserting that there is not some way in which lithotripsy could be refined or used differently so that it would not cause damage to the treated kidneys. So, what this choice describes is something that the argument does not actually do.
Correct Answer: (B) Now let’s consider a type of answer choice that is less obviously incorrect.
summary:
Lesson Content The most obviously wrong answer choices in Logical Flaw questions are those that describe things that authors or arguments don’t actually do.
Consider the following example:
Unlike other major automobile companies, all of which produce mostly automobiles that are fossil fuel powered, The Blitz Automobile Company makes only electric automobiles. Because electric automobiles are easier to maintain, less costly to power, and more environmentally friendly, they are superior to fossil fuel powered vehicles.
title: 10.3.1 Incorrect Choices that Describe Things that the Arguments Do Not Actually Do

contents:
Lesson Content In order for a Logical Flaw answer choice to be correct, it has to have two characteristics.
💡MUST KNOW:
In order for a Logical Flaw answer choice to be correct, it has to have two characteristics. It has to describe something that actually goes on in the argument, and what it describes has to be a flaw in the argument. Many Logical Flaw incorrect answer choices satisfy the first criterion, as they describe things that the authors or arguments actually do, but do not satisfy the second one, as what they describe are not flaws.
For instance, an answer choice could say that the author fails to establish that a certain idea is correct, and it could be the case that the author does not establish that that idea is correct.
Consider the following example:
Health insurance for those living in major cities costs significantly more than does health insurance for those living in small towns. However, empirical data shows that people who live in large cities are no less healthy than people who live in small towns. It follows that health insurance companies profit more from people who live in major cities than from those who live in small towns. Which of the following best describes the error of reasoning contained in the argument above?
(A) The argument assumes without justification that the costs associated with providing health care are not significantly higher in major cities than in small towns.
(B) The argument fails to establish whether people in cities have incomes higher than those of people who live in small towns.
Notice that what choice (B) describes is a characteristic of the argument.
In contrast, as we discussed previously, if the assumption mentioned in choice (A) is not correct, then the argument falls apart.
💡MUST KNOW:
A Logical Flaw question may include one or more incorrect answer choices that describe things that are characteristics of the argument but are not flaws.
You can find some incorrect answer choices of this type in the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 14 Emotional freedom therapy, or EFT, is a method purported to heal via tapping on various parts of a person’s body. The logic of the argument above is most vulnerable to the criticism that the argument 1. [ ] (A) concludes that EFT is not effective without considering the results of studies that show other healing methods to be effective. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) fails to explain why EFT has become increasingly popular even though no studies have generated solid evidence indicating that it has any healing effects. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) takes a lack of evidence indicating that EFT is effective as proof that it is not. 4. [ ] (D) fails to establish that no people who have used EFT have seen improvements in their health. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) provides no evidence indicating that the tapping associated with EFT has ever had any negative effects on any person’s health. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium Conclusion: EFT is not effective in solving health problems.
The argument supports the conclusion with the following premise:
Premise: No study has generated solid evidence that EFT has any healing effects.
Notice that the premise doesn’t really say anything convincing. For all we know from what it says, there have been no studies of the effects of EFT at all.
To get the right answer to this question, we have to distinguish between choices that describe things that are characteristics of the passage but are not flaws and the correct answer, which brings up an actual flaw in the argument.
(A) concludes that EFT is not effective without considering the results of studies that show other healing methods to be effective. It is true that the argument does not include consideration of the results of studies that show other healing methods to be effective. All the same, while the results of studies of other healing methods may be somehow related to the efficacy of EFT, making the argument that EFT is not effective does not require considering the results of studies of other methods. All one really needs is solid evidence that EFT is not effective. So, while this choice does describe a characteristic of the argument, this choice does not describe a flaw.
(B) fails to explain why EFT has become increasingly popular even though no studies have generated solid evidence indicating that it has any healing effects. This choice is tempting because it is true that the argument does not include any such explanation, and also, one might wonder why EFT would be so popular if it is not effective. However, explaining why EFT is popular is not necessary for proving that EFT is not effective. So, even though it is true that the argument does not include such an explanation, that it does not is not a flaw.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) takes a lack of evidence indicating that EFT is effective as proof that it is not. The author concludes that EFT is not effective, but offers no actual proof to support this conclusion. The premise is simply that no studies have indicated that EFT is effective. It could be that the effectiveness of EFT has not been studied at all. Lack of study generated evidence that EFT has health benefits does not mean that EFT does not have health benefits.
(D) fails to establish that no people who have used EFT have seen improvements in their health. This choice is tricky. For one thing, it is true that the argument does not establish that no people who have used EFT have seen improvements in their health, and, meanwhile, the possibility that some people who have used EFT have seen improvements in their health could be perceived as undermining this argument. Thus, this choice could be perceived as pointing out a flaw. However, the truth is that, even if some people using EFT have seen improvements in their health, the method could still be totally useless, as it could be that those people healed for reasons other than the use of EFT. So, even though what this choice says is true of the argument, this choice does not describe a flaw in the argument.
(E) provides no evidence indicating that the tapping associated with EFT has ever had any negative effects on any person’s health. It is true that the argument provides no evidence indicating that the tapping associated with EFT has ever had any negative effects on any person’s health. However, the conclusion is not that EFT is harmful. So, the argument does not require evidence indicating that EFT has negative effects. So, while what this choice describes is a characteristic of the argument, it is not a flaw.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss wrong answers that would point out flaws if the arguments were meant to support conclusions different from the ones that they actually support.
summary:
Lesson Content In order for a Logical Flaw answer choice to be correct, it has to have two characteristics.
💡MUST KNOW:
In order for a Logical Flaw answer choice to be correct, it has to have two characteristics. It has to describe something that actually goes on in the argument, and what it describes has to be a flaw in the argument. Many Logical Flaw incorrect answer choices satisfy the first criterion, as they describe things that the authors or arguments actually do, but do not satisfy the second one, as what they describe are not flaws.
title: 10.3.2 Incorrect Choices that Bring Up Things that the Argument Does but are not Flaws

contents:
Lesson Content As we have seen in reviewing various types of Critical Reasoning questions, one way in which the creator of a question can create a tempting wrong answer choice is to have the choice address a conclusion that is closely related to the conclusion of the argument but is not actually the conclusion of the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
One way to create a tempting wrong answer choice is to have the choice address a conclusion that is closely related to the conclusion of the argument but is not actually the conclusion of the argument.
These types of choices appear in Logical Flaw questions as well, and the way they work is by bringing up something that would be a flaw in the argument if the argument were meant to support a conclusion different from the one that it actually supports.
💡MUST KNOW:
Answer choices that center on the wrong conclusion appear in Logical Flaw questions, and the way they work is by bringing up something that would be a flaw in the argument if the argument were meant to support a conclusion different from the one that it actually supports.
For instance, if an argument’s conclusion is about the effects of a person’s following a certain diet, a related conclusion would be that a person will actually manage to stick with the diet.
So, in answering a Logical Flaw question, correctly and exactly identifying the conclusion of the argument is a key step.
Consider the following example:
Education Secretary: The results of a recent study show that, the more years of high school education a person has, the less likely it is that that person will experience drug addiction. Therefore, if we can get a greater percentage of students to complete all of high school, we will succeed in reducing the incidence of drug addiction in young people in our country.
The argument is most vulnerable to which of the following criticisms?
(Choice that Describes a Flaw in the Support for the Wrong Conclusion) It assumes without sufficient warrant that the Department of Education has strategies that would be effective in getting more students to complete all of high school.
(Correct Answer) It overlooks the possibility that drug use is the cause of rather than the effect of students’ ceasing to attend high school before completing all of it.
What’s the conclusion that the argument has to support?
Conclusion: If we can get a greater percentage of students to complete all of high school, we will succeed in reducing the incidence of drug addiction in young people in our country.
So, in order to be effective, the argument has to support the conclusion that there will be a cause-and-effect relationship between getting students to complete high school and reducing drug addiction.
The answer that describes a flaw in the support for the wrong conclusion doesn’t find a flaw in the support for this cause-and-effect conclusion.
The correct answer, on the other hand, describes a key flaw in the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
A Logical Flaw question may include one or more incorrect answer choices that describe flaws in support for conclusions that are related to the conclusion of the argument but are not actually the conclusion of the argument.
The good news is that by, correctly and exactly identifying the conclusions of the arguments in Logical Flaw questions, a move that is not particularly difficult to make, you can avoid choosing many of the trap answers that appear in these questions.
To develop your vision, as you consider the following example, notice the answer choices that describe flaws in the support for conclusions other than the conclusion of the argument presented.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 15 One of the responsibilities of the Verdantville police force is to ensure that the streets of Verdantville are safe for travel. Of the following, the best criticism of the argument above is that it overlooks the possibility that 1. [ ] (A) over the past few years, the Verdantville police force has had to reallocate resources to the criminal investigations department, in response to a rash of burglaries. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) many automobile collisions in Verdantville happen at times of day when traffic is so heavy that speeding is difficult. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) fines for speeding in Verdantville have increased, with the result that revenue from speeding fines has remained the same even as the number of citations has declined. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) all of the town’s departments have cut back in some areas in order for the town to maintain a balanced budget in times that are slow economically. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) the decline in the number of drivers cited for speeding in Verdantville is the result of the police force’s effectively handling its street travel related responsibilities. View Solution Question Level: easy In order to be sure that we find a choice that describes a flaw in the support for the conclusion of the argument presented, we have to identify that conclusion exactly.
Conclusion: The Verdantville police are neglecting their responsibility of ensuring that the streets of Verdantville are safe for travel.
Notice that there is a gap in this argument. The support for the conclusion is the number of citations issued, while the conclusion is about safety. Safety and citations issued may be related matters, but they are not exactly the same.
(A) over the past few years, the Verdantville police force has had to reallocate resources to the criminal investigations department, in response to a rash of burglaries. This choice describes a flaw in the support for a conclusion that is related to the conclusion of the argument presented. If the conclusion of the argument were that the police have cited fewer drivers for speeding because the police are neglecting their responsibilities in general, then what this choice says would describe a flaw in the argument. However, even if the police have a good reason for citing fewer drivers for speeding – they have been dealing with a rash of burglaries – they could be neglecting their street travel related responsibilities. So, this choice does not highlight a flaw in the argument.
(B) many automobile collisions in Verdantville happen at times of day when traffic is so heavy that speeding is difficult. This choice may seem to provide a valid reason for the decrease in the number of drivers cited for speeding and, thus, to highlight a flaw in the argument. However, the fact that many collisions occur when drivers are not speeding anyway is not an indication that, in citing fewer drivers for speeding, the police are not neglecting their responsibility of making the streets safe for travel.
If this choice were a little different and said that speeding had, over the past few years, become difficult in Verdantville, then perhaps it would highlight a flaw in the argument, but since it does not describe a change that has occurred over the past few years but rather a situation that seems to have been the same all along, it does not highlight a flaw in the support for the conclusion presented.
(C) fines for speeding in Verdantville have increased, with the result that revenue from speeding fines has remained the same even as the number of citations has declined. This choice could be tempting, because its topic, revenue from speeding fines, is related to the conclusion of the argument. However, since the conclusion is not that the police have been neglecting generating revenue, this choice’s pointing out the fact that revenue may not have decreased does not highlight a flaw in the argument presented in this question.
(D) all of the town’s departments have cut back in some areas in order for the town to maintain a balanced budget in times that are slow economically. This choice highlights a flaw in the support for a conclusion that is not the conclusion of the argument presented in this question. If the conclusion of the argument were that the police department is being neglectful because of poor management, this choice would highlight a flaw in the support for the conclusion. However, since, even if the police are cutting back services to stay within budget, the police could be doing so by choosing to cut back on street safety related activities, what this choice says doesn’t highlight a flaw in the support for the argument’s conclusion.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) the decline in the number of drivers cited for speeding in Verdantville is the result of the police force’s effectively handling its street travel related responsibilities. By bringing up the possibility that the number of citations issued has decreased as a result of the police department’s having very effectively handled the responsibility of making the streets safer, this choice highlights a flaw in the argument. It could be that the reason that the number of drivers cited for speeding has decreased over the past few years is that fewer drivers are speeding because the police have been doing a great job of making the streets safe for travel. In that case, the conclusion would be incorrect. Therefore, the argument’s overlooking this possibility is a flaw.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s wrap up our discussion of Logical Flaw incorrect answers by discussing incorrect answers that present mathematical information that doesn’t matter.
summary:
Lesson Content As we have seen in reviewing various types of Critical Reasoning questions, one way in which the creator of a question can create a tempting wrong answer choice is to have the choice address a conclusion that is closely related to the conclusion of the argument but is not actually the conclusion of the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
One way to create a tempting wrong answer choice is to have the choice address a conclusion that is closely related to the conclusion of the argument but is not actually the conclusion of the argument.
title: 10.3.3 Incorrect Choices that Describe Flaws in the Support for the Wrong Conclusions

contents:
Lesson Content The argument in a Logical Flaw question can have a conclusion that is math related, such as a conclusion based on population data, a conclusion based on research results, or a conclusion about company profits.
💡MUST KNOW:
Logical Flaw questions involving mathematical arguments present an opportunity for a question writer to create answer choices that are tempting but incorrect because they bring up mathematical information that doesn’t matter for the support of the conclusion presented.
For instance, in a case in which the conclusion of an argument is about the average earnings of a person in a country, an answer choice that makes a point about the rate of unemployment in that country might seem to highlight a flaw in the argument and yet actually have nothing to do with the support for the conclusion presented.
Consider the following example:
Education Secretary: The results of a recent study show that, the more years of high school education a person has, the less likely it is that that person will experience drug addiction. Therefore, if we can get a greater percentage of students to complete all of high school, we will succeed in reducing the incidence of drug addiction in young people in our country.
The argument is most vulnerable to which of the following criticisms?
(Choice that Brings Up Mathematical Information that Doesn’t Matter) It overlooks the possibility that young people represent a smaller proportion of the country’s total population than they represented in the past.
(Correct Answer) It overlooks the possibility that drug use is the cause of rather than the effect of students’ ceasing to attend high school before completing all of it.
The conclusion is about reducing the incidence of drug addiction in young people.
