So how could a choice that brings up mathematical information that doesn’t matter be tempting? If a test-taker loses track of exactly what has to be supported, information that doesn’t matter could seem to matter.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Logical Flaw questions, beware of incorrect answer choices that bring up mathematical information that seems to matter but doesn’t really matter.
The keys to avoiding choosing such a choice are:
to be very clear about what the argument is seeking to support. and
to go beyond choosing a choice that merely “sounds right” to carefully considering the logical relationships between any mathematical information presented in answer choices and what the argument is seeking to support. 💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Logical Flaw questions, to avoid incorrect answer choices that bring up mathematical information that seems to matter but doesn’t really matter, we must (1) be very clear about what the argument is seeking to support, and (2) go beyond choosing a choice that merely “sounds right” to carefully considering the logical relationships between any mathematical information presented in answer choices and what the argument is seeking to support.
As you go through the following example, pay attention to the ways in which answer choices can seem to be correct even though the mathematical information that they bring up does not matter.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 16 A few decades ago, when only around 10 percent of people in England would go through life without having children, the growth in the country’s population was minimal. The argument is most vulnerable to which of the following criticisms? 1. [ ] (A) It overlooks the possibility that the percentage of recent immigrants into England who are choosing to have children is greater than the percentage of all people in England who are. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) It overlooks the possibility that the number of children per parent in England has increased significantly over the past few decades. 3. [ ] (C) It presupposes that a decrease in the population of England would be considered negative rather than positive for the country. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) It neglects to consider the fact that England’s population is higher currently than it was a few decades ago. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) It assumes without sufficient warrant that, at its current pace, immigration into England will cause the population to remain at the same level. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium Conclusion: The population of England would decrease significantly without immigration.
The conclusion is supported via a comparison of the percentage of people in England who go through life without having children currently with the percentage of people in England who would go through life without having children a few decades ago. Since, when the percentage of people who would have children was much lower, population growth was minimal, the argument concludes that, currently, the population of England would decrease significantly without immigration.
So, what’s wrong with this argument?
(A) It overlooks the possibility that the percentage of recent immigrants into England who are choosing to have children is greater than the percentage of all people in England who are. This choice could be tempting, as it could be perceived as indicating that immigrants are increasing the birth rate in England. However, the truth is that, regardless of what percentage of recent immigrants are choosing to have children, the percentage of all people in England who are choosing to have children remains exactly what the argument says it is. So, the mathematical information brought up by this choice doesn’t actually matter.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) It overlooks the possibility that the number of children per parent in England has increased significantly over the past few decades. The support for the conclusion centers on one fact, that the percentage of people in England who do not have children has increased. In doing so, the argument overlooks the fact that the birth rate in England is the result of, not just the percentage of people who have children, but also other factors, such as the number of children each parent has. It could very well be that the number of children per parent has increased significantly, in which case the population of England could increase even without immigration. By overlooking this possibility, the author of the argument may have arrived at an incorrect conclusion.
(C) It presupposes that a decrease in the population of England would be considered negative rather than positive for the country. While the argument could be perceived as implying that a decrease in population would be negative for England, the truth is that the argument does not actually include such a position. What this choice describes is something that the argument does not do at all.
(D) It neglects to consider the fact that England’s population is higher currently than it was a few decades ago. While it is true that the argument does not mention the fact that England’s population is higher currently than it was a few decades ago, that it does not do so is not a weakness. Making the case that the population will decrease as a result of an increase in the percentage of people who go through life without having children does not require taking into account the current size of the population. Regardless of whether the population of England is greater than, smaller than, or the same size as it was a few decades ago, an increase in the percentage of people who don’t have children will have the same effect on the current population, whatever that population may be. So, the mathematical information that this choice brings up does not matter for the support for the conclusion.
(E) It assumes without sufficient warrant that, at its current pace, immigration into England will cause the population to remain at the same level. This choice is blatantly incorrect, as what this choice describes is something that the argument does not do. The argument does not include any assumption that, at its current pace, the immigration into England will cause the population to remain the same. All the argument says is that, without immigration, the population would decrease significantly. Saying that is not the same as assuming that the population will remain the same.
Correct Answer: (B)
summary:
Lesson Content The argument in a Logical Flaw question can have a conclusion that is math related, such as a conclusion based on population data, a conclusion based on research results, or a conclusion about company profits.
💡MUST KNOW:
Logical Flaw questions involving mathematical arguments present an opportunity for a question writer to create answer choices that are tempting but incorrect because they bring up mathematical information that doesn’t matter for the support of the conclusion presented.
title: 10.3.4 Incorrect Choices that Bring Up Mathematical Information that Doesn’t Matter

contents:
Lesson Content Complete the Passage questions are among the easiest Critical Reasoning questions to identify, principally because they end with a blank space.
💡MUST KNOW:
Complete the Passage questions are among the easiest Critical Reasoning questions to identify, principally because they end with a blank space.
When we see a passage with a long, underlined segment at the end, “__________,” we know that we’re dealing with a Complete the Passage Question.
Complete the Passage questions generally begin with a question stem that is some variation of:
Which of the following most logically completes the passage?
The question stem is followed by a passage, and the last sentence of the passage ends with a blank space, as described above.
💡MUST KNOW:
The correct answer to a Complete the Passage question is the one that does the best job of filling the blank space and, thus, logically completing the passage.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 1 Which of the following best completes the passage? Paradoxical intervention is a healing technique that involves having a person engage in a behavior that the person wishes to discontinue. 1. [ ] (A) the person would prefer to get sufficient sleep rather than experience chronic insomnia > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) insomnia often develops as a result of behaviors that are only indirectly related to sleeping > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) a person experiencing chronic insomnia generally does not sleep that much anyway > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) without sufficient sleep, people generally experience physical health issues and cognitive problems > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) fear of failure, in this case, failure to sleep, is much of what has kept the person from succeeding View Solution Question Level: medium The passage in this question describes a method, paradoxical intervention, that counselors use in order to get people experiencing chronic insomnia to sleep.
The method involves having a person experiencing chronic insomnia seek to stay awake rather than seek to sleep.
The last sentence of the passage says that the rationale for using the method is that, through using this method, the person will end up sleeping because of whatever is said by the part that fills the blank.
So, the correct answer, the one that fills the blank, will tell us why, through seeking to stay awake, the person will sleep.
(A) the person would prefer to get sufficient sleep rather than experience chronic insomnia This choice adds almost nothing to the passage. From the fact that the person is working with a counselor to address the chronic insomnia, we can already reasonably conclude that the person would rather get sufficient sleep than experience chronic insomnia. Moreover, even if the fact that the person would rather get sufficient sleep were new information, that information would not explain why, through seeking to stay awake, the person would end up sleeping.
(B) insomnia often develops as a result of behaviors that are only indirectly related to sleeping This choice merely provides some background on how insomnia develops, and that background does not explain why, through seeking to stay awake, a person experiencing chronic insomnia would end up sleeping.
(C) a person experiencing chronic insomnia generally does not sleep that much anyway The fact that a person experiencing chronic insomnia doesn’t sleep much anyway might make staying awake seem to be fairly easy for a person experiencing chronic insomnia. However, that fact does not explain why, through seeking to stay awake, a person experiencing chronic insomnia would end up sleeping.
(D) without sufficient sleep, people generally experience physical health issues and cognitive problems While preventing physical health issues and cognitive problems might be a good reason for a person to sleep, a person experiencing chronic insomnia has been having difficulty sleeping, regardless of the fact that the person would be better off sleeping. So, the fact that lack of sleep results in physical health issues and cognitive problems does not explain why, through seeking to stay awake, a person experiencing chronic insomnia would end up sleeping.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) fear of failure, in this case, failure to sleep, is much of what has kept the person from succeeding If fear of failure has been keeping a person from succeeding at sleeping, changing the person’s goal from sleeping to staying awake could remove an impediment to the person’s falling asleep, because, if the person’s goal is to stay awake rather than to fall asleep, then, theoretically, the person would fear the failure to stay awake, rather than fear failure to sleep. At that point, without the fear of failure to sleep, which was keeping the person from sleeping, the person would sleep. Thus, this choice explains why, through seeking to stay awake, a person experiencing chronic insomnia would sleep.
Correct Answer: (E)
summary:
Lesson Content Complete the Passage questions are among the easiest Critical Reasoning questions to identify, principally because they end with a blank space.
💡MUST KNOW:
Complete the Passage questions are among the easiest Critical Reasoning questions to identify, principally because they end with a blank space.
When we see a passage with a long, underlined segment at the end, “__________,” we know that we’re dealing with a Complete the Passage Question.
title: 11.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content The good news is that, while the format of a Complete the Passage question may seem new, in answering a Complete the Passage question, we won’t actually have to do anything particularly different from what other types of Critical Reasoning questions ask us to do.
For example, a Complete the Passage question could ask us to do any one of the following:
· State an assumption made by the author
· Strengthen the argument
· Weaken the support for a claim presented in the argument
· State the conclusion that the author has been leading up to
· State an inference that can be made
· Resolve a seeming paradox outlined in the passage
· Evaluate a plan
💡MUST KNOW:
Complete the Passage questions won’t ask us to do anything particularly different from what other types of Critical Reasoning questions have already asked us to do.
This concept is easy to demonstrate with an example:
There are only two candidates for mayor in next month’s election: Thompson and McGuire. Since McGuire was recently arrested for tax evasion, Thompson will most likely win the election because __________.
Let’s break down this argument.
Premise 1: Thompson and McGuire are the only candidates for mayor
Premise 2: McGuire was recently arrested
Conclusion: Thompson will win
The blank line follows the conclusion and is connected to it with the word “because.” The word “because” signals that evidence is coming.
How could we strengthen this argument? An answer stating:
candidates who experience negative publicity in the period immediately before an election tend to lose that election
would strengthen the argument because it would directly connect “tax evasion” in the premise with election results.
Similarly, an answer stating:
candidates currently facing criminal charges are removed from the ballot
would also strengthen the argument because it explains how McGuire’s arrest would directly influence the result of the election.
💡MUST KNOW:
Read the last sentence of the stimulus carefully. The cue words that appear before the blank are excellent indicators of the type of role that the answer that fills that blank performs in the argument.
Let’s now discuss a way to expose the trap choices in Complete the Passage questions.
summary:
Lesson Content The good news is that, while the format of a Complete the Passage question may seem new, in answering a Complete the Passage question, we won’t actually have to do anything particularly different from what other types of Critical Reasoning questions ask us to do.
For example, a Complete the Passage question could ask us to do any one of the following:
· State an assumption made by the author
title: 11.2 The Skills Used in Answering Complete the Passage Questions are Similar to those Used for Answering Other Types of Critical Reasoning Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Much of what makes Complete the Passage questions tricky is the fact that the answer choice completes a sentence, and so, an incorrect choice that sounds as if it flows well with the sentence, even though it doesn’t effectively complete the passage, can be very tempting.
Consider the following example:
Which of the following most logically completes the set of facts presented below?
In response to residents’ complaints about constant noise made by airplanes’ taking off and landing, the city council of Mergate is considering permanently closing Mergate Airport. Many businesses in Mergate depend on spending by vacationers. Nevertheless, the members of the city council feel that the profitability of these businesses would not be negatively impacted by the closing of the city’s airport, since __________.
(A) these businesses represent a significant proportion of all businesses in Mergate
(B) these businesses have tended to be some of the most profitable in the city
(C) without the constant noise made by airplanes taking off and landing, Mergate would be a much more attractive destination for vacationers
In the scenario described in the passage, the city council members are considering closing the city’s airport and feel that the profitability of businesses that depend on spending by vacationers would not be negatively affected by the closing of the airport.
If we fill the blank with choice (A), we get the following:
Nevertheless, the members of the city council feel that the profitability of these businesses would not be negatively impacted by the closing of the city’s airport, since these businesses represent a significant proportion of all businesses in Mergate.
That sentence flows pretty nicely and almost seems to make sense.
Now, let’s fill the blank with choice (B):
Nevertheless, the members of the city council feel that the profitability of these businesses would not be negatively impacted by the closing of the city’s airport, since these businesses have tended to be some of the most profitable in the city.
That sentence also flows well, and, if you weren’t paying close attention, you might not notice that, no matter how profitable these businesses have been, their profitability could be negatively impacted by the closing of the airport.
So, because, in merely plugging a choice into the end of a sentence, the wrongness of an incorrect choice can be camouflaged by the length and sound of the sentence, a choice that is not quite right can sound pretty good.
Now, notice what happens if we convert the sentence that ends with a blank into a question.
Here’s the sentence that ends with a blank converted into a question.
Why is it that the city council members feel that the profitability of businesses in Mergate that depend on spending by vacationers would not be negatively impacted by the closing of the city’s airport?
Does choice (A) make sense as the answer to that question?
(A) these businesses represent a large proportion of businesses in Mergate
As we discussed above, that answer clearly makes no sense, but now, by making the sentence that ends with a blank into a question, we have removed the camouflage that hid the wrongness of the choice when the choice was part of a long sentence.
Choice (B) also makes little to no sense as the answer to our question:
(B) these businesses have tended to be some of the most profitable in the city
Now that choice (B) is an answer to a question rather than part of a long sentence, we can more easily see that it is incorrect.
Now, let’s answer our question with choice (C).
Why is it that the city council members feel that the profitability of businesses in Mergate that depend on spending by vacationers would not be negatively impacted by the closing of the city’s airport?
(C) without the constant noise made by airplanes’ taking off and landing, Mergate would be a much more attractive destination for vacationers
Positioned as an answer to a question, choice (C) is clearly correct.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering a Complete the Passage question, by converting the sentence with the blank into a question, you can make the differences between the wrong answers and the correct answer more obvious.
Notice how converting the sentence that ends with a blank into a question starting with “Why” exposes the trap answers in the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 2 Which of the following most logically completes the passage? Four blocks of a certain street in Trenditon are known as Restaurant Row, because, along those four blocks, most buildings have restaurants on their ground floors. 1. [x] (A) whether, to make space for trees, restaurants on Restaurant Row would have to significantly reduce the numbers of dining tables that they have on the sidewalk 2. [ ] (B) what types of trees would be the healthiest if they were planted along Restaurant Row > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) whether regulations in Trenditon allow for planting of trees in openings in public sidewalks > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) whether working with the town to change parking regulations would do more to enhance the profitability of restaurants on Restaurant Row than would planting trees > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) what proportion of owners of restaurants on Restaurant Row are in favor of planting trees in openings in the sidewalk there > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: easy If we convert the sentence ending with the blank into a question, we get the following:
What would the Chamber of Commerce determine in order to confirm that having trees growing in openings in the sidewalk along Restaurant Row would lead to the desired results?
Of course, in order to answer this question, we need to clearly identify what the “desired results” are. By looking through the passage we can find that the desired results are “make the street more attractive, thus resulting in greater profits for the restaurants.”
To get this question right, we have to find the answer choice that helps to confirm that the implementing the strategy will lead to those specific desired results.
(Notice, this question is essentially an Evaluate question in the form of a Complete the Passage question.)
CORRECT ANSWER(A) whether, to make space for trees, restaurants on Restaurant Row would have to significantly reduce the numbers of dining tables that they have on the sidewalk Since the desired results include “greater profits for the restaurants,” determining whether, to make space for trees, restaurants on Restaurant Row would have to significantly reduce the numbers of dining tables that they have on the sidewalk is important. If the restaurants would have to significantly reduce their numbers of tables, then planting trees might result in reduced profits rather than increased profits.
(B) what types of trees would be the healthiest if they were planted along Restaurant Row While the success of the project would depend on planting trees that would actually grow in that area, confirming that planting trees would result in greater profits for the restaurants does not really require determining what types of trees would be healthiest if they were planted in that area. As long as some reasonably attractive trees would be reasonably healthy in that area, the plan could work.
(C) whether regulations in Trenditon allow for planting of trees in openings in public sidewalks This answer could be tempting, as, if regulations prohibit planting trees in the sidewalks, then implementing the plan outlined may be virtually impossible. However, we have to remember what specifically has to be confirmed, which is not whether the plan can be implemented but, rather, whether, if implemented, the plan will result in greater profits for the restaurants.
(D) whether working with the town to change parking regulations would do more to enhance the profitability of restaurants on Restaurant Row than would planting trees This is a typical “better plan” answer choice. The question at hand is not whether there is some other plan, such as working with the town to change parking regulations, that is better than planting trees. The question is whether implementing the plan of planting trees in the sidewalks will result in greater profits for the restaurants.
(E) what proportion of owners of restaurants on Restaurant Row are in favor of planting trees in openings in the sidewalk there Since the proportion of restaurant owners in favor of planting trees along Restaurant Row is not clearly a factor that would affect whether doing so will result in greater profits for the restaurants, determining the proportion of owners in favor of planting trees is not necessary for confirming that planting the trees would result in greater profits for the restaurants.
Correct Answer: (A) So far, we have discussed examples of Complete the Passage questions such that, in answering them, it makes sense to convert the sentence ending with the blank into a question beginning with “Why.” Converting the sentence ending with the blank into a question beginning with “Why” makes sense if the word preceding the blank is a connecting word that functions similarly to “because,” in other words a word such as “because,” “since,” or “as.”
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering a Complete the Passage question, converting the sentence with the blank into a question beginning with “Why” makes sense if the blank is preceded by “because,” “since,” or “as.”
In some Complete the Passage questions, however, the blank is preceded by something other than a connecting word that functions like “because.” In those cases, we can rephrase the sentence that ends with a blank as a question beginning with “What.”
Consider the following example:
It had long been believed that prehistoric cave paintings, which often depicted animals, were created by men for reasons related to hunting animals for food. However, many of these paintings include stenciled images of hands, and, by analyzing the images, researchers have determined that most of the hands used in creating the stenciled images were women’s hands. So, we can conclude that most prehistoric cave paintings were created by women, assuming that __________.
(A) the people who created the cave paintings did not participate in hunting the types of animals depicted in the paintings.
(B) the hands depicted in images in cave paintings were not the hands of people other than the artists who created the paintings.
To answer this question, we can convert the sentence ending with a blank into the following question:
What has to be assumed in order to arrive at the conclusion that the cave paintings were created by women from the evidence that hands used in creating stenciled images included in the paintings were the hands of women?
Let’s consider choice (A).
On the other hand, it is true that, in order to use the fact that the hands used in making the hand images were women’s hands as evidence that the cave paintings were created by women, we have to assume what choice (B) says, that the hands used to make the images were not the hands of people other than the artists who created the paintings.
So, choice (B) answers our “What has to be assumed … ?” question.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering a Complete the Passage question in which the blank is preceded by something other than a connecting word that functions like “because,” we can convert the sentence ending with the blank into a question beginning with “What.”
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 3 Which of the following most logically completes the passage? Personalized learning involves allowing students great latitude to determine what topics to study and in what order. 1. [ ] (A) schools have found that effectively applying personalized learning methods can require hiring additional teaching staff members > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) learning can be difficult when the order in which topics are studied is inexpertly chosen, as understanding of one topic can be the basis for learning about another 3. [ ] (C) students in a group studying the same topics at the same time can motivate each other and help each other to learn > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) some schools that go from using from traditional methods to using personalized learning methods see student achievement gains, while others see reductions in achievement > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) some students are more excited than are others to take advantage of the benefits of personalized learning programs > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium The passage describes personalized learning and explains why, in theory, students would learn more and learn faster when using personalized learning than in groups directed by teachers. It then brings up the issue that, in reality, students in personalized learning programs find their learning efforts frustrated and lose motivation.
The last sentence of the passage can be converted into the following question, the logical answer to which will correctly fill the blank:
Why is it that students in personalized learning programs can find their efforts frustrated and lose motivation?
(A) schools have found that effectively applying personalized learning methods can require hiring additional teaching staff members That fact that schools have found that effectively applying personalized learning methods requires hiring additional staff members would not cause students to find their learning efforts frustrated and lose motivation.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) learning can be difficult when the order in which topics are studied is inexpertly chosen, as understanding of one topic can be the basis for learning about another The fact that learning can be difficult when the order in which topics are studied is inexpertly chosen could explain why students in personalized learning programs can find their efforts frustrated and lose motivation. Because the students are likely not experts at choosing the order in which topics are studied, students in personalized learning programs may be doing poor jobs of choosing the orders in which they study topics, and may thus be making their learning difficult.
(C) students in a group studying the same topics at the same time can motivate each other and help each other to learn This choice is a very tempting trap, but if you carefully consider what this choice says, you can see that the fact that students learning in groups can motivate each other does not explain why students in personalized learning programs can find their efforts frustrated and lose motivation. In other words, this choice explains why students in groups may be especially motivated rather than explains what is holding back students in personalized learning programs.
(D) some schools that go from using from traditional methods to using personalized learning methods see student achievement gains, while others see reductions in achievement The fact that some schools that go from using from traditional methods to using personalized learning methods see student achievement gains, while other schools see reductions in achievement does not explain why students in personalized learning programs can find their efforts frustrated and lose motivation.
(E) some students are more excited than are others to take advantage of the benefits of personalized learning programs The fact that some students are more excited than others to take advantage of the benefits of personalized learning programs does not explain why students in personalized learning programs can find their efforts frustrated and lose motivation.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss a general strategy for answering Complete the Passage questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Much of what makes Complete the Passage questions tricky is the fact that the answer choice completes a sentence, and so, an incorrect choice that sounds as if it flows well with the sentence, even though it doesn’t effectively complete the passage, can be very tempting.
Consider the following example:
Which of the following most logically completes the set of facts presented below?
In response to residents’ complaints about constant noise made by airplanes’ taking off and landing, the city council of Mergate is considering permanently closing Mergate Airport.
title: 11.3 Exposing the Traps in a Complete the Passage Question by Converting the Sentence that Ends with a Blank into a Question Beginning with “Why” or “What”

contents:
Lesson Content Here is a 5-step strategy for handling Complete the Passage questions.
Step 1 – Read the question stem and identify what it is asking you to do.
In most Complete the Passage questions, the question stems precede the passages.
Step 2 – Carefully read, break down, and completely understand the passage.
The foundation of choosing the correct answer to a Complete the Passage question is understanding the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
The foundation for choosing the correct answer to a Complete the Passage question is understanding the argument presented in the passage.
Step 3 – Convert the sentence that ends with the blank into a question.
Turn the sentence that ends with the blank into a “Why …” question or a “What …” question.
Trap answers can seem to fill the blank at the end of the passage, but often, once you turn the sentence ending with the blank into a question, that those traps are not correct answers becomes rather apparent.
Step 4 – Eliminate incorrect answer choices.
As you know by now, eliminating incorrect answer choices is at least equally as useful as selecting the correct answer choice.
In this step, you’ll be using many of the skills you’ve previously learned.
Step 5 – Test the final answer by reading it back into the passage.
Once we’ve picked the best answer, we fill in the blank with the answer choice and read the entire passage to make sure that, with the blank filled, the passage flows logically.
💡MUST KNOW:
Always plug your chosen answer choice back into the passage to make sure that this choice makes the passage flow logically.
Let’s apply our strategy to an example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 4 Which of the following best completes the passage below? The technique known as hydraulic fracturing, or “fracking,” involves pumping pressurized liquid into an oil well. 1. [ ] (A) oil company profits are heavily influenced by factors such as the level of economic activity and seasonal variations in temperature > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) over time, the cost to revive a previously exhausted oil well through fracking has declined to almost half the cost to drill a new one > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) the supply of oil produced by fracking has far exceeded consumer demand, causing oil prices to collapse 4. [ ] (D) the technique known as fracking has been criticized by environmentalists for contaminating ground water sources > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) getting approval to construct additional pipelines for transporting oil has proven difficult > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy In order to answer this question, let’s go through our five-step process.
Step 1 – Read the Question Stem and Identify What it is Asking You to Do.
The question stem that precedes the passage asks us to find the answer that best completes the passage, and the passage ends with a blank. So, to correctly answer this question, we have to find the choice that most logically completes the passage.
Step 2 – Carefully read, break down, and completely understand the passage.
This stimulus presents a situation in which people expected that fracking would increase profits, while in fact profits have gone down. This sort of puzzling situation is a classic example of the type of scenario that we would see presented in a Resolve the Paradox question.
Step 3 – Convert the sentence that ends with the blank into a question.
In the sentence that ends with the blank, the blank is preceded by the word “because.” So, we can convert that sentence into a question beginning with “Why.”
Why did oil company profits fall in the past decade, after fracking became widespread, even though they were expected to increase?
Step 4 – Eliminate incorrect answer choices.
Let’s take a look at the answer choices:
(A) oil company profits are heavily influenced by factors such as the level of economic activity and seasonal variations in temperature This choice is tempting, because it mentions two factors, economic activity and seasonal variations in temperature, that could affect oil company profits. Notice, however, the fact that these factors can affect oil company profits does not mean that they have caused this particular decrease in profits. In other words, this choice does not say that, for instance, economic activity has slowed and, that this slowdown has caused a decrease in oil profits. The choice merely indicates that a slowdown, if there were one, could affect oil company profits.
(B) over time, the cost to revive a previously exhausted oil well through fracking has declined to almost half the cost to drill a new one Rather than explain why profits have decreased, this answer choice does the opposite. It provides a reason why profits would have increased: one of the oil companies’ costs, the cost of fracking, has decreased. So, the effect of this choice goes in the wrong direction.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) the supply of oil produced by fracking has far exceeded consumer demand, causing oil prices to collapse Since a collapse in oil prices precipitated by the use of fracking could be what has caused oil company profits to decline, this choice answers the question of why oil company profits have declined as fracking has become widespread. So, this choice appears to be the correct answer.
(D) the technique known as fracking has been criticized by environmentalists for contaminating ground water sources Since we have no clear reason to believe that criticism of fracking would result in a decrease in oil company profits, this choice does not answer the question of why oil company profits fell in the past decade, after fracking became widespread, even though they were expected to increase.
(E) getting approval to construct additional pipelines for transporting oil has proven difficult This choice could be tempting, as difficulties related to constructing new pipelines could be a drag on oil companies’ ability to increase profits. However, since it does not make sense that difficulty in increasing the number of pipelines would result in a decrease in profits, this choice does not explain why oil company profits decreased.
Step 5 – Double-check the Final Answer by Reading it Back into the Passage.
Here’s the complete passage, with choice (C) filling the blank:
The technique known as hydraulic fracturing, or “fracking,”involves pumping pressurized liquid into an oil well. It was long believed that, through the use of fracking, oil companies could increase their profits by reviving previously exhausted wells. However, in the past decade, as fracking has entered widespread use, oil companies have seen their profits fall, because the supply of oil produced by fracking has far exceeded consumer demand, causing oil prices to collapse.
Since the passage with choice (C) filling the blank makes complete sense, choice (C) is the correct answer.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss some characteristics of correct answers to Complete the Passage questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Here is a 5-step strategy for handling Complete the Passage questions.
Step 1 – Read the question stem and identify what it is asking you to do.
In most Complete the Passage questions, the question stems precede the passages.
Step 2 – Carefully read, break down, and completely understand the passage.
The foundation of choosing the correct answer to a Complete the Passage question is understanding the passage.
title: 11.4 Strategy For Answering Complete the Passage Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Because a Complete the Passage Question asks us to select an answer option that best completes the passage, it’s easy to focus too much on the last sentence of the stimulus (the one with the blank line in it).
Consider the following example:
Miracle Walnut Company sells two products – bags of walnuts in their shells, and bags of walnuts unshelled. The machinery that the company uses to remove the shells of walnuts has broken down, and will be replaced with new, more efficient machinery rather than repaired. Since delivery and installation of new machinery will take about a week, Miracle Walnut Company will not produce unshelled walnuts for a week while the replacement of the shell removing machinery is in progress. Nevertheless, it is expected that all of Miracle Walnut Company’s orders will be filled during that week, because __________.
(Choice that Could Work with the Last Sentence Only) there is no reason to believe that the orders that the company receives during that week will not follow the usual pattern
(Correct Answer) the company has, in inventory, bags of unshelled walnuts sufficient to cover at least a week’s worth of orders
Notice that if we were to read just the last sentence without considering the rest of the passage, the choice that could work with the last sentence only might seem correct.
Nevertheless, it is expected that all of Miracle Walnut Company’s orders will be filled during that week, because there is no reason to believe that the orders that the company receives during that week will not follow the usual pattern. The sentence above sounds pretty reasonable.
The correct answer, on the other hand, fills the blank in a way that addresses the entirety of what is presented in the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
The answer to a Complete the Passage question must work with, and logically fit with, the passage as a whole. Any answer choice that fits only with the final sentence, and does not fit logically with the passage as a whole, will be incorrect.
The following example includes multiple answer choices that could be perceived as working with the last sentence of the passage but don’t work with the entire passage.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 5 Which of the following best completes the passage? In areas in which there are many people who are addicted to illicit drugs that they inject intravenously, people seeking to inject these drugs often seek out places that are secluded and have plenty of lighting that drug users can use to see their veins in order to inject drugs. 1. [ ] (A) drug users tend to prefer not to be visible to others when they are injecting drugs > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) these bulbs consume no more energy than is consumed by ordinary light bulbs > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) in blue light, intravenous drug users often have difficulty seeing their veins 4. [ ] (D) these bulbs provide just as much light as is provided by ordinary light bulbs > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) the bulbs provide light sufficient for safely walking through or engaging in most activities in these spaces > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy In the passage above, in the sentence that ends with the blank, the blank is preceded by “because.” Therefore, our task is to find the answer choice that explains why the installation of the light bulbs is expected to be effective in achieving its purpose.
From the information provided in the passage, we can see that the bulbs to be installed emit blue light and that the purpose of the installation of the bulbs is to achieve a reduction in the use of certain spaces by drug users.
So, the correct answer will answer the following question:
Why will replacing ordinary light bulbs in certain spaces with bulbs that emit blue light result in a reduction in the use of those spaces by drug users?
In answering that question, we have to be careful to consider all of the information provided by the passage.
(A) drug users tend to prefer not to be visible to others when they are injecting drugs If we were to consider this choice in the context of the last sentence only, this choice could be very tempting, as it could seem perfectly logical that installing light bulbs could deter drug users from using certain spaces because the drug users prefer not to be visible. However, if we consider everything said in the passage, we can see that the first sentence of the passage says that drug users actually choose spaces that have “plenty of lighting”. Since there is no reason to believe that drug users would be more visible in blue light than they were in spaces that already had “plenty of lighting,” this choice does not fit what is said in the passage.
(B) these bulbs consume no more energy than is consumed by ordinary light bulbs While this choice could conceivably fit with the last sentence of the passage, since it does not fit with the entirety of the passage, and does not answer the question outlined in the introduction to this explanation that the correct answer has to answer, this choice is not correct.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) in blue light, intravenous drug users often have difficulty seeing their veins This choice fits with the entirely of what is said in the passage and answers the question outlined in the introduction to this explanation. The passage says that intravenous drug users seek out spaces where there is plenty of light that they can use to see their veins. If replacing ordinary light bulbs in these spaces with blue light bulbs makes seeing veins difficult, then doing so should result in a reduction in the use of these spaces by intravenous drug users.
(D) these bulbs provide just as much light as is provided by ordinary light bulbs This choice could be tempting to a test-taker seeking to find a choice that works only with the sentence that ends with the blank and not with the entire passage. However, since there is no reason to believe that providing in certain spaces the same amount of light that has been provided in those spaces all along would result in a reduction in use of those spaces by drug users, this choice is not correct.
(E) the bulbs provide light sufficient for safely walking through or engaging in most activities in these spaces This choice might be tempting to a test-taker seeking to find a choice that works only with the sentence that ends with the blank and not with the entire passage. However, since there is no reason to believe that providing light sufficient for safely walking through or engaging in activities in spaces would result in a reduction in use of those spaces by drug users, this choice is not correct.
Correct Answer: (C)
summary:
Lesson Content Because a Complete the Passage Question asks us to select an answer option that best completes the passage, it’s easy to focus too much on the last sentence of the stimulus (the one with the blank line in it).
Consider the following example:
Miracle Walnut Company sells two products – bags of walnuts in their shells, and bags of walnuts unshelled. The machinery that the company uses to remove the shells of walnuts has broken down, and will be replaced with new, more efficient machinery rather than repaired.
title: 11.5 The Correct Answer to a Complete the Passage Question Must Work With the Entire Passage, Not With Just the Last Sentence.

contents:
Lesson Content Trap answers to Complete the Passage questions may utilize words, phrases, and syntax from the passage.
We must be vigilant not to choose such wrong answers.
Consider the following example:
Since the introduction of e-books, many have speculated that traditional “hard copy” book publishing will become largely obsolete in the near future because the book-purchasing public will prefer the more convenient e-books to traditional books. However, this prediction may have been premature, as __________.
(Choice with Matching Wording) the public’s purchases of traditional books have generated the majority of the revenue of local book stores
(Correct Answer) in recent years, physical book sales have actually increased
To correctly answer this question, we need a choice that answers the following question:
Why is it that the prediction that “hard copy” book publishing will be largely obsolete in the near future may have been premature?
With what it says about “the public’s purchases of traditional books,” the choice with wording that matches that of the passage sounds like the passage and could seem to fit in the blank space.
The correct answer, on the other hand, uses the expression “physical books” rather than the wording used in the passage, “traditional books,” but does a much better job of completing the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
To correctly answer a Complete the Passage question, we must find an answer choice that properly completes the passage by fitting into the logic of the argument as a whole and that is not simply a statement that includes wording similar to that found in the passage.
Notice how the wording in incorrect answer choices in the following example matches the wording of the passage without actually expressing something that completes the logic of the passage, while the correct answer actually completes the logic of the passage.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 6 Which of the following most logically completes the passage? Since every megawatt of power generated via a sustainable, non-polluting method, such as wind or solar power generation, theoretically represents a megawatt of power that would otherwise have been generated via burning fossil fuels, which creates pollution, the creation of a certain quantity of pollution is, in theory, avoided each time a megawatt of power is generated via a sustainable method. 1. [ ] (A) government subsidies of sustainable power generation have resulted in a reduction in the end cost to the consumer of such power > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) technological advances have resulted in reductions in the amount of pollution created in generating a megawatt of power via burning fossil fuels 3. [ ] (C) the cost of generating a megawatt of power via burning fossil fuels has declined > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) the proportion of power generated via burning fossil fuels has been steadily declining > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) some countries are in the process of passing laws capping the total quantities of fossil fuel related pollution allowed to be created annually within their borders > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium To correctly answer this question, we need a choice that is a logical answer to the following question:
Why has the quantity of pollution avoided by generating a megawatt of power via a sustainable method rather than via the use of fossil fuels been declining?
Choices that merely resemble the wording of the passage but do not answer that question will not be correct.
(A) government subsidies of sustainable power generation have resulted in a reduction in the end cost to the consumer of such power Since a reduction in cost to the consumer would not change how much pollution is avoided by generating power via sustainable methods, this choice does not answer the question.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) technological advances have resulted in reductions in the amount of pollution created in generating a megawatt of power via burning fossil fuels The passage says that, in burning fossil fuels to generate a megawatt of electricity, pollution is created and that the creation of that pollution can be avoided by generating that megawatt of power via a sustainable method instead.
If the amount of pollution created in generating a megawatt of power via burning fossil fuels has declined, as this choice says, then substituting a megawatt of power generated via a sustainable method for a megawatt of power generated via burning fossil fuels won’t prevent the creation of as much pollution as it would have in the past.
Thus, this choice answers our question and completes the passage, by explaining why the quantity of pollution avoided by generating a megawatt of power via a sustainable method, rather than via the use of fossil fuels, has been declining.
(C) the cost of generating a megawatt of power via burning fossil fuels has declined There is no clear reason to connect the cost of generating power via burning fossil fuels to the amount of pollution created in the process of generating a megawatt of power via burning fossil fuels. Thus, although the wording of this choice, which includes “generating a megawatt of power via burning fossil fuels” and “declining,” sounds like the wording in the passage, this choice does not answer the question discussed in the introduction to this explanation or logically complete the passage.
(D) the proportion of power generated via burning fossil fuels has been steadily declining This choice could be tempting, as, like the passage, it mentions “power generated via burning fossil fuels” and “declining.” However, the fact that a smaller proportion of the power generated is generated via the burning of fossil fuels does not explain why less pollution is avoided by generating a particular quantity of power via using sustainable methods rather than via burning fossil fuels.
(E) some countries are in the process of passing laws capping the total quantities of fossil fuel related pollution allowed to be created annually within their borders This choice could be tempting, as one might be inclined to conclude that, if countries are capping the amount of power allowed to be generated via burning fossil fuels, then, using sustainable methods for generating power won’t make any difference in the amount of pollution being made, because fossil fuel use will just remain at its capped level regardless of whether sustainable methods are used, and that, therefore, this choice correctly completes the passage.
However, following that line of reasoning requires making multiple assumptions, such as that the caps have been reached. Further, even if that line of reasoning did not rely on such assumptions, what this choice says is that some countries are in the process of passing laws capping the total quantities of fossil fuel related pollution allowed to be created annually within their borders. In other words, the laws have not yet been passed. So, what this choice says would not explain why the quantity of pollution avoided by generating a megawatt of power via a sustainable method has been declining.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s continue.
summary:
Lesson Content Trap answers to Complete the Passage questions may utilize words, phrases, and syntax from the passage.
We must be vigilant not to choose such wrong answers.
Consider the following example:
Since the introduction of e-books, many have speculated that traditional “hard copy” book publishing will become largely obsolete in the near future because the book-purchasing public will prefer the more convenient e-books to traditional books. However, this prediction may have been premature, as __________.
title: 11.6 The Answer to a Complete the Passage Question Must Work with the Logic of the Passage and not Merely Match the Wording of the Passage

contents:
Lesson Content As you already know, Complete the Passage questions are, for the most part, just altered forms of questions of types that you already know well.
Therefore, you already have a solid understanding of many of the common types of wrong answers that appear in Complete the Passage questions.
At the same time, since Complete the Passage questions have their own unique format, they also have some fairly unique types of incorrect answers.
summary:
Lesson Content As you already know, Complete the Passage questions are, for the most part, just altered forms of questions of types that you already know well.
Therefore, you already have a solid understanding of many of the common types of wrong answers that appear in Complete the Passage questions.
At the same time, since Complete the Passage questions have their own unique format, they also have some fairly unique types of incorrect answers.
title: 11.7 Common Incorrect Answer Types for Complete the Passage Questions

contents:
Lesson Content As you know very well at this point, many trap answers in Critical Reasoning questions sound good and appear to be correct, but, at the same time, they are incorrect.
In Complete the Passage questions, trap answers are often incorrect because, although they sound correct, they head in directions subtly different from the direction in which the passages were headed.
Notice the difference between the answers in the following example:
Which of the following best completes the passage?
Overfishing in the waters around Seatown has begun to significantly deplete the populations of fish in them. The government of Seatown has proposed setting up in these waters fish farms for raising fish for human consumption, in hopes that focusing on farming fish rather than on catching wild fish will give wild fish populations a chance to recover. However, fish farms become breeding grounds for fish parasites. So, implementing this plan may not bring about the desired outcome, because __________.
(A) medicated food fed to farmed fish in order to combat parasites can negatively affect some forms of marine life that inhabit the sea floor
(B) parasites that proliferate in fish farms attack young wild fish passing by the fish farms, thus decimating wild fish populations
To complete the sentence ending with the blank, we need a choice that explains why “implementing the plan may not bring about the desired outcome.” So, we have to make sure that we know what the “desired outcome” is.
Now, let’s consider the answer choices.
Notice the problem with choice (A).
Choice (B), on the other hand, effectively completes the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Complete the Passage questions, trap answers are often wrong because, although they sound correct, they head in directions subtly different from the directions in which the passages were headed. We must be careful not to choose such answers.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 7 Which of the following best completes the passage? In many states, young drivers are given restricted driver’s licenses that permit legal driving only during certain times of day, such as after 5 am and before 9 pm. 1. [ ] (A) most restricted licenses permit legal driving at any time of day, if the license holder is driving straight from home to work or work to home > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) the fact that a person has attained an age at which one can get an unrestricted driver’s license does not necessarily indicate that the person is a skillful driver > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) in some states it is possible for a young driver to gain an unrestricted license by completing a driver education course > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) young drivers sometimes feel that the need to arrive at home before the time of day when driving becomes illegal for them justifies their taking risks while driving 5. [ ] (E) there are times of day when restricted licenses permit legal driving even though many car crashes occur during those times of day > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy The passage discusses driver’s licenses that permit legal driving only during certain time of day. The conclusion presented is that “parents of these young drivers should be made aware that the presence of the restrictions can result in dangerous behavior.”
The blank in the passage follows the conclusion and is connected to the conclusion by the word “as.” Therefore, the answer choice that completes the passage will support a statement made, as the correct answer to a Strengthen question would.
We can convert the sentence that ends with a blank into the following question:
Why does the presence of restrictions on the times when a teenage driver can legally drive result in dangerous behavior?
Notice, the question is specifically about the fact that the presence of the restrictions can result in dangerous behavior. So, the correct answer will address this particular attribute of the restrictions, and not some other attribute of the restrictions or the restricted licenses that is subtly different from the fact that the presence of the restrictions can result in dangerous behavior.
(A) most restricted licenses permit legal driving at any time of day, if the license holder is driving straight from home to work or work to home At first glance, this choice may seem to be correct. Notice, however, the subtle difference between this choice and what we need. This choice indicates that having a restricted license may not prevent a teenage driver’s being exposed to the dangers of driving during the times of day when driving is the most dangerous.
(B) the fact that a person has attained an age at which one can get an unrestricted driver’s license does not necessarily indicate that the person is a skillful driver This choice goes in a subtly incorrect direction. It explains why licensing regulations may not prevent a person’s getting an unrestricted license without being skilled at driving. In contrast, the answer choice that we need is one that indicates that the restrictions cause dangerous behavior.
(C) in some states it is possible for a young driver to gain an unrestricted license by completing a driver education course This choice indicates that a teenage driver can get an unrestricted license. In contrast, we need a choice that explains why the restrictions on driving by a person holding a restricted license can cause dangerous behavior.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) young drivers sometimes feel that the need to arrive at home before the time of day when driving becomes illegal for them justifies their taking risks while driving This choice answers the question outlined in the introduction to this explanation and explains why the presence of restrictions on licenses can result in dangerous behavior, because it indicates that the presence of restrictions on licenses can result in teenage drivers’ taking risks while driving in order to arrive at home before the time of day when driving becomes illegal for them.
(E) there are times of day when restricted licenses permit legal driving even though many car crashes occur during those times of day At first glance, this choice may seem to be correct. Notice, however, the subtle difference between what this choice does and what we need. This choice indicates that having a restricted license may not completely prevent a teenage driver’s driving during times of day when great numbers of crashes occur. In contrast, the answer choice that we need is one that indicates that the restrictions cause dangerous behavior.
Correct Answer: (D)
summary:
Lesson Content As you know very well at this point, many trap answers in Critical Reasoning questions sound good and appear to be correct, but, at the same time, they are incorrect.
In Complete the Passage questions, trap answers are often incorrect because, although they sound correct, they head in directions subtly different from the direction in which the passages were headed.
Notice the difference between the answers in the following example:
title: 11.7.1 An Answer that Sounds Correct, but Heads in a Subtly Incorrect Direction

contents:
Lesson Content Remember, in answering a Complete the Passage Question, our goal is to find the answer that best completes the passage.
Consider the following example:
Miracle Walnut Company sells two products – bags of walnuts in their shells, and bags of walnuts unshelled. The machinery that the company uses to remove the shells of walnuts has broken down, and will be replaced with new, more efficient machinery rather than repaired. Since delivery and installation of new machinery will take about a week, Miracle Walnut Company will not produce unshelled walnuts for a week while the replacement of the shell removing machinery is in progress. Nevertheless, during that week, it is expected that all of Miracle Walnut Company’s orders will be filled, because __________.
(Choice that Follows from What the Passage Says) Miracle Walnut Company will, after that week, be producing unshelled walnuts with increased efficiency (Correct Answer) the company has, in inventory, bags of unshelled walnuts sufficient to cover at least a week’s worth of orders
Notice how the choice that follows from what the passage says could be tempting.
Of course, this choice doesn’t really work, because the fact that the company will be producing unshelled walnuts with increased efficiency after a week of having no machinery does not explain why it is that the company’s orders will be filled during that week.
The correct answer, in contrast, does not merely follow from what the passage says, but rather, provides additional information that supports the conclusion that the company will fill its orders and, thus, effectively completes the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
Some trap answers in Complete the Passage questions will sound plausible or inferable given what is stated in the passages, and yet these answers don’t logically complete the passages. We must be careful not to choose such answers.
To avoid choosing such an incorrect answer, keep in mind what you are seeking to do in answering a Complete the Passage question, which, unless the passage is set up to be completed with an inference or a conclusion, is choosing a choice that provides new information that completes the logic of the passage, not one that merely states a conclusion based on the information that the passage already includes.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 8 Which of the following best completes the discussion? The number of years that a person spends in school is negatively correlated with the likelihood of that person’s becoming involved in criminal activity. 1. [ ] (A) people who complete high school are less likely to become involved in crime than those who quit before they graduate > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) some people who have spent many years in school are no more educated than others who have spent relatively few years in school > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) the majority of people who do not complete high school do not become involved in any significant criminal activity > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) states that increased the number of years people are legally required to attend school saw reduced levels of criminal activity from the people who therefore attended school for more years 5. [ ] (E) the percentage of college graduates who become involved in crime is even lower than the percentage of high school graduates who become involved in crime > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: hard To correctly answer this question, we need a choice that answers the following:
What has been found that is a reason to discount the possibility that the reason for the observed correlation is not that, in general, education reduces a person’s likelihood of becoming involved in crime, but, rather, that people who are relatively more motivated to pursue education are relatively less motivated to become involved in crime?
Since we are seeking information that is a reason to discount a possibility mentioned in the passage, and since the passage mentions nothing that would constitute a reason to discount that possibility, the correct answer to this question has to provide new information rather than simply state a conclusion that is supported by information that the passage already includes.
(A) people who complete high school are less likely to become involved in crime than those who quit before they graduate This choice simply states a conclusion already supported by the information in the passage. If, as the passage says, the more years of education a person has the less likely it is that that person will engage in crime, then it makes sense to conclude that people who complete high school are less likely to become involved in crime than those who quit before they graduate. So, this choice does not really add new information to the passage and do what the information that logically completes the passage has to do, provide a reason to discount the possibility mentioned.
(B) some people who have spent many years in school are no more educated than others who have spent relatively few years in school Because this choice is about the results of spending years in school, which topic is a key component of the what the passage is about, this choice may seem relevant and therefore correct. The truth is, however, that the fact that some people who have spent many years in school are no more educated than others who have spent relatively few years in school has little to no effect on the support for the theory that education is the factor underlying the observed negative correlation or on the support for the alternative theory that motivation is the factor underlying the observed negative correlation. Presumably, people other than the “some” mentioned by this choice become more educated when they spend more years in school, in which case, we are still left wondering whether education or motivation is what underlies the observed negative correlation between years spent in school and likelihood of becoming involved in criminal activity.
Further, even if we were to decide that what this choice says could indicate that few people who attend many years of school are more educated than people who attend relatively few years of school, we would not have the answer we need. If it were the case that few people who attend many years of school are more educated than people who attend relatively few years of school, then a person’s attending school for more years generally would not result in that person’s becoming more educated, in which case, clearly, education would not be the factor underlying the negative correlation between years spent in school and likelihood of becoming involved in crime, and so, the factor underlying that negative correlation could be motivation. We need a choice that indicates that motivation is not the factor underlying the negative correlation, not one that indicates that it could very well be that factor.
(C) the majority of people who do not complete high school do not become involved in any significant criminal activity This choice provides reason to discount the wrong possibility. It provides reason to discount the possibility that most people who do not complete high school will become involved in significant criminal activity, and it doesn’t provide reason to discount the possibility mentioned in the passage. The fact that the majority of people who don’t complete high school, in other words, the majority of people who do not complete as many years of school as many others complete, do not become involved in any significant criminal activity does not help us to decide whether the factor underlying the negative correlation between years spent in school and likelihood of becoming involved in crime is education or motivation. The fact that the majority of people who do not complete high school do not become involved in any significant criminal activity is just a data point, a data point characterized by people’s completing a certain number of years of school and having a certain likelihood of becoming involved in criminal activity. Of course, from the information in the passage, we already knew that many such data points, data points involving certain amounts of time spent in school and certain likelihoods of becoming involved in crime, exist. The fact that this one exists changes nothing.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) states that increased the number of years people are legally required to attend school saw reduced levels of criminal activity from the people who therefore attended school for more years People who attend school for an increased number of years only because they are legally required to do so are likely not very motivated to become educated. Therefore, the fact that states saw “reduced levels of criminal activity” from these people indicates that education itself, without motivation, can lead to decreased levels of crime. Therefore, what this choice says is a reason to discount the possibility that motivation is the factor underlying the negative correlation between years spent in school and likelihood of becoming involved in criminal activity.
(E) the percentage of college graduates who become involved in crime is even lower than the percentage of high school graduates who become involved in crime If the number of years a person attends school is negatively correlated with the likelihood of that person’s becoming involved in criminal activity, then what this choice says about college graduates in comparison with high school graduates is a conclusion that follows from what the passage says. So, this choice does not really add new information to the passage and does not do what the information that logically completes the passage has to do, serve as a reason to discount the possibility mentioned.
Correct Answer: (D)
summary:
Lesson Content Remember, in answering a Complete the Passage Question, our goal is to find the answer that best completes the passage.
Consider the following example:
Miracle Walnut Company sells two products – bags of walnuts in their shells, and bags of walnuts unshelled. The machinery that the company uses to remove the shells of walnuts has broken down, and will be replaced with new, more efficient machinery rather than repaired.
title: 11.7.2 An Answer that States Something that is Plausible or Inferable Given What is Stated in the Passage, but is Not the Logical Completion of the Passage.

contents:
Lesson Content When answering a Complete the Passage question that discusses a plan or a course of action, it’s important that we do not select as correct an answer choice that addresses a material issue that is not, however, the issue that is the focus of the passage.
In a situation, there can exist multiple issues.
Consider the following example:
Miracle Walnut Company sells two products – bags of walnuts in their shells, and bags of walnuts unshelled. The machinery that the company uses to remove the shells of walnuts has broken down, and will be replaced with new, more efficient machinery rather than repaired. Since delivery and installation of new machinery will take about a week, Miracle Walnut Company will not produce unshelled walnuts for a week while the replacement of the shell removing machinery is in progress. Nevertheless, during that week, it is expected that all of Miracle Walnut Company’s orders will be filled, because __________.
(Choice that Addresses the Wrong Issue) the costs of the business disruption necessary for replacing the shell removing machinery will be significantly outweighed by the benefits of having new machinery
(Correct Answer) the company has, in inventory, bags of unshelled walnuts sufficient to cover at least a week’s worth of orders
Notice that the choice that addresses the wrong issue could be tempting because, as a test-taker reads through the passage, the issue of the costs versus the benefits of replacing the machinery might come to mind.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering a Complete the Passage question that discusses a plan or a course of action, it’s important that we do not select an answer choice that addresses a material issue that is not, however, the issue that is the focus of the passage.
To avoid choosing a choice that addresses a material issue that is not, however, the issue that is the focus of the passage, make sure that, rather than consider the choices in light of what might matter in the situation presented, you consider the choices in the context of the exact focus of the passage.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 9 Which of the following most logically completes the passage? Increases in temperatures on Earth are expected to exacerbate the drought conditions that exist in areas that already experience little rainfall. 1. [ ] (A) the substances that would be used to block sunlight are not the same substances that when placed into the atmosphere cause rainfall > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) a country could decide on its own to spray sunlight reflecting substances into the atmosphere without the help of or approval of other countries > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) much of the atmospheric moisture that becomes rain enters the atmosphere through evaporation caused by sunlight hitting the surfaces of oceans 4. [ ] (D) blocking sunlight from reaching the planet attacks the symptoms of the problem of increased temperatures on Earth without addressing the underlying causes > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) many of the countries in which drought conditions are present do not have financial resources to fund spraying sun blocking substances into the atmosphere > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy If we convert the sentence ending with the blank into a question, we get the following:
How could reducing temperatures on Earth by spraying into the atmosphere substances that would reflect sunlight that otherwise would have reached the planet’s surface exacerbate problems involving drought?
In order to correctly answer this Complete the Passage question, we need a choice that logically answers the specific question above. A choice that is about an issue that might arise from blocking sunlight but is not about exacerbation of problems involving drought will not be correct. So, for instance, a choice that says that spraying substances into the atmosphere would be harmful to birds, while relevant to the discussion, would not logically complete the passage.
(A) the substances that would be used to block sunlight are not the same substances that when placed into the atmosphere cause rainfall The point of the passage is not that droughts would not be solved by spraying substances into the atmosphere. The point is that problems involving drought could be exacerbated by spraying substances into the atmosphere. So, this choice goes in the wrong direction and, thus, does not logically complete the passage.
(B) a country could decide on its own to spray sunlight reflecting substances into the atmosphere without the help of or approval of other countries In mentioning that countries could spray sunlight blocking substances into the atmosphere without the help or approval of other countries, this choice brings up an issue other than the issue that is the focus of the passage, which is the exacerbation of problems involving drought.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) much of the atmospheric moisture that becomes rain enters the atmosphere through evaporation caused by sunlight hitting the surfaces of oceans This choice explains how blocking sunlight from reaching Earth’s surface could exacerbate problems involving drought. Rain is in part the result of sunlight hitting the surfaces of the oceans. So, if sunlight is blocked from hitting the surface of Earth, there could be a reduction in rainfall, and, thus, an exacerbation of problems involving drought. Thus, this choice answers the question discussed in the introduction to this explanation and logically completes the passage.
(D) blocking sunlight from reaching the planet attacks the symptoms of the problem of increased temperatures on Earth without addressing the underlying causes This choice could be tempting because it brings up an issue, addressing symptoms rather than underlying causes, that is relevant to what’s discussed in the passage. However, that issue is not the focus of the passage.
