The correct answer must complete the passage by explaining why problems involving drought could be exacerbated if sunlight is blocked from reaching Earth’s surface, and this choice does not do so.
(E) many of the countries in which drought conditions are present do not have financial resources to fund spraying sun blocking substances into the atmosphere This choice could be tempting because it brings up an issue, the fact that many of the countries in which drought conditions are present do not have financial resources to fund spraying sun blocking substances into the atmosphere, that is relevant to what’s discussed in the passage. However, that issue is not the focus of the passage. The correct answer must complete the passage by explaining how problems involving drought could be exacerbated via the use of the sunlight blocking approach described, and this choice does not do so.
Correct Answer: (C)
summary:
Lesson Content When answering a Complete the Passage question that discusses a plan or a course of action, it’s important that we do not select as correct an answer choice that addresses a material issue that is not, however, the issue that is the focus of the passage.
In a situation, there can exist multiple issues.
Consider the following example:
Miracle Walnut Company sells two products – bags of walnuts in their shells, and bags of walnuts unshelled.
title: 11.7.3 A Choice That Addresses a Material Issue That is Not, However, the Issue That is the Focus of the Passage.

contents:
Lesson Content We’ll wrap up our discussion of incorrect answers in Complete the Passage questions by discussing a type of choice that can be surprisingly tempting, a choice that explains part of the passage rather than completes the passage.
Consider the following example:
Which of the following best completes the passage?
Overfishing in the waters around Seatown has begun to significantly deplete the populations of fish in them. The government of Seatown has proposed setting up in these waters fish farms for raising fish for human consumption, in hopes that focusing on farming fish rather than on catching wild fish will give wild fish stocks a chance to recover. However, fish farms become breeding grounds for fish parasites. So, implementing this plan may not bring about the desired outcome, because __________.
(Choice that Explains but does not Complete the Passage) fish parasites can quickly multiply in fish farms, because of the high density and low genetic diversity of the farmed fish
(Correct Answer) parasites that proliferate in fish farms attack young wild fish passing by the fish farms, thus decimating wild fish populations
In order to correctly answer this question, we need a choice that answers the following:
Why wouldn’t setting up fish farms in the waters near Seatown give wild fish stocks a chance to recover?
Notice what the choice that explains but does not complete the passage does.
However, the answer that explains could seem to answer our question and complete the passage, because it makes the fish farms seem pretty bad for fish health.
As we have discussed, the correct answer actually does answer our question and complete the passage, as it indicates that the parasites that breed in fish farms end up decimating wild fish populations.
Overfishing in the waters around Seatown has begun to significantly deplete the populations of fish in them. The government of Seatown has proposed setting up in these waters fish farms for raising fish for human consumption, in hopes that focusing on farming fish rather than on catching wild fish will give wild fish stocks a chance to recover. However, fish farms become breeding grounds for fish parasites. So, implementing this plan may not bring about the desired outcome, because parasites that proliferate in fish farms attack young wild fish passing by the fish farms, thus decimating wild fish populations.
💡MUST KNOW:
When answering a Complete the Passage question, beware of incorrect answers that explain things described in the passage but do not actually complete the passage.
Notice the choices that explain but don’t complete in the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 10 Which of the following best completes the passage below? Claims by Hollywood studios that they lose millions of dollars annually in ticket sales when their movies are copied and sold by “pirates” are greatly exaggerated. 1. [ ] (A) some people who might not otherwise pay to see films purchase pirated films because they sell at prices lower than must be paid to view films legally > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) movie theaters can eject any ticket-holder who is illegally recording a film being screened and are under no obligation to return the ticket price to the ejected person > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) the primary cause of Hollywood’s financial difficulties is expensive union contracts that must be re-negotiated > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) movie pirates pay for tickets in order to film movies in theaters before studios release those movies for home viewing > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) potential revenue from the few purchasers of pirated films who truly represent “lost ticket sales” is minimal View Solution Question Level: easy The passage expresses an argument that can be summarized as follows:
A person pirating a film pays for a ticket, and most people who purchase pirated copies of films would not otherwise have paid to see the films.
We come to the last sentence, and can convert it into the following question:
Why is the loss to Hollywood attributable to this type of movie piracy small?
The correct answer will answer the above question.
(A) some people who might not otherwise pay to see films purchase pirated films because they sell at prices lower than must be paid to view films legally This choice could be tempting because what it says about people who purchase pirated films spending less than they would have had to spend to see the movies legally could be perceived as indicating that the studios are not losing much money by missing out on these people’s business.
The truth is, however, that all this choice really does is explain something mentioned in the passage, that most people who purchase pirated films would not otherwise pay to see the films. Explaining why people who purchase pirated films would not otherwise pay to see the films does not add information that answers the question of why the loss to Hollywood attributable to this type of movie piracy is small.
(B) movie theaters can eject any ticket-holder who is illegally recording a film being screened and are under no obligation to return the ticket price to the ejected person This answer could be tempting because, since it describes a way in which movie piracy could be prevented, it could be perceived as explaining why the losses to studios resulting from this type of piracy are small. However, since it’s not clear from this choice that movie piracy is in fact prevented in this way, this choice does not do a great job of completing the passage by explaining why the losses attributable to such piracy are small.
(C) the primary cause of Hollywood’s financial difficulties is expensive union contracts that must be re-negotiated This answer goes in a direction clearly different from the direction of the passage. The fact that there are expensive union contracts that must be re-negotiated does absolutely nothing to explain why the losses resulting from this type of piracy are small.
(D) movie pirates pay for tickets in order to film movies in theaters before studios release those movies for home viewing This choice explains why movie pirates pay for tickets, but we already know from the passage that movie pirates pay for tickets. So, this choice does not add information that explains why the losses to studios resulting from this type of piracy are small.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) potential revenue from the few purchasers of pirated films who truly represent “lost ticket sales” is minimal This answer choice perfectly answers our question and fits logically into the passage. Since a person pirating a film pays for a ticket, and most of the people who purchase a pirated copy of the film would never have purchased a ticket even if the pirated copy had not been available, the loss to Hollywood attributable to this type of movie piracy is small, because, as this choice says, potential revenue from the few purchasers of pirated films who truly represent “lost ticket sales” is minimal.
Correct Answer: (E)
summary:
Lesson Content We’ll wrap up our discussion of incorrect answers in Complete the Passage questions by discussing a type of choice that can be surprisingly tempting, a choice that explains part of the passage rather than completes the passage.
Consider the following example:
Which of the following best completes the passage?
Overfishing in the waters around Seatown has begun to significantly deplete the populations of fish in them. The government of Seatown has proposed setting up in these waters fish farms for raising fish for human consumption, in hopes that focusing on farming fish rather than on catching wild fish will give wild fish stocks a chance to recover.
title: 11.7.4 A Choice that Explains Something Discussed in the Passage but does not Complete the Passage

contents:
Lesson Content Unlike many other types of Critical Reasoning questions, Method of Reasoning questions focus on how an argument is structured, not on the merits of the argument.
In other words, when we consider the stimulus of a Method of Reasoning question, we don’t need to focus on whether the argument is sound or convincing or weak.
💡MUST KNOW:
When solving a Method of Reasoning question, our job is to focus on how the argument is organized and analyze the argument’s structure.
A simple analogy is as follows: in answering Strengthen or Weaken questions, we act as construction workers; we repair buildings or tear them down.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Strengthen or Weaken questions, for example, we act as construction workers; we repair buildings or tear them down. To answer Method of Reasoning questions, we act more as inspectors; inspecting the arguments, we figure out how the parts fit together.
For example, imagine that we were given the following question:
Letter to the Editor: A recent article in your newspaper stated that coffee is the most popular hot beverage in the United States. However, every American I know drinks tea rather than coffee. Therefore, this article must be false and should be corrected.
The argument above employs which of the following reasoning techniques?
(A) A claim is introduced, evidence that calls that claim into question is offered, and a contrary claim is made.
(B) A claim is introduced, evidence that supports that claim is offered, and a similar claim is made.
This is clearly a flawed argument.
However, determining how the argument is flawed isn’t our concern right now.
So, what does this argument say?
This argument has three sentences.
A recent article in your newspaper stated that coffee is the most popular hot beverage in the United States.
The second sentence provides evidence that seeks (not necessarily successfully) to undermine that claim:
However, every American I know drinks tea rather than coffee.
The third sentence offers the author’s conclusion:
Therefore, this article must be false and should be corrected.
Considering the two choices, we can clearly see that choice (A) is correct.
(A) A claim is introduced, evidence that calls that claim into question is offered, and a contrary claim is made.
In answering Method of Reasoning questions, it’s important to keep in mind that these questions contain arguments.
Let’s now discuss a simple yet effective strategy that we can deploy to help us correctly answer Method of Reasoning questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Unlike many other types of Critical Reasoning questions, Method of Reasoning questions focus on how an argument is structured, not on the merits of the argument.
In other words, when we consider the stimulus of a Method of Reasoning question, we don’t need to focus on whether the argument is sound or convincing or weak.
💡MUST KNOW:
When solving a Method of Reasoning question, our job is to focus on how the argument is organized and analyze the argument’s structure.
title: 12.1 Introduction

contents:
Lesson Content It’s obvious that, for an answer choice to be correct, that answer choice must be completely correct.
We can leverage this fact to help us solve Method of Reasoning questions.
The answers to Method of Reasoning questions describe the logical structures of authors’ arguments.
💡MUST KNOW:
Since the answers to Method of Reasoning questions describe the logical structures of arguments, a correct answer must describe exactly how a given argument is structured. If even one part of an answer choice says something untrue about the argument, that answer is incorrect.
A key thing to realize is that, sometimes, a Method of Reasoning answer choice will start off sounding correct, even though it isn’t really.
Notice the key differences between the answer choices in the following example:
Surprisingly, the results of a recent study showed that, on average, those who regularly drink moderate amounts of alcohol are in better health and live longer than those who abstain. However, these results should not necessarily be taken as indicating that consuming alcohol in moderation is beneficial to health, as many people who abstain from consuming alcohol do so because they are already not healthy.
The passage above proceeds by
(A) making the case that a conclusion should be rejected because it is completely unsupported.
(B) weakening the link between evidence and a cause-and-effect conclusion that seems to be supported by that evidence by bringing up an alternate reason for the observed pattern.
The passage definitely recommends against concluding that drinking alcohol in moderation is beneficial to health, but how exactly does it proceed? Let’s consider choice (A) first.
Choice (A) starts off seeming to be correct, as it says that the passage proceeds by making the case that a conclusion should be rejected, and rejecting a conclusion sounds something like what the passage does when it says that the results “should not necessarily be taken as indicating that consuming alcohol in moderation is beneficial to health.” As we continue to read choice (A), however, it becomes apparent that it is incorrect, because choice (A) describes the passage as saying that the conclusion is “completely unsupported.” The truth is that the passage does mention some support for the conclusion that consuming alcohol in moderation is beneficial to health, when it says, “those who regularly drink moderate amounts of alcohol are in better health and live longer than those who abstain.” Also, if we reconsider the beginning of choice (A), we can see that the passage does not even really make the case that the conclusion that consuming alcohol is beneficial for health should be rejected.
So, unlike what (A) says, the passage doesn’t really recommend rejecting the conclusion that consuming alcohol in moderation is beneficial for health and does mention support for that conclusion.
Choice (B), on the other hand, exactly matches how the passage proceeds.
💡MUST KNOW:
By reading Method of Reasoning answer choices completely and by carefully comparing them with how the stimuli proceed, you can consistently eliminate incorrect answers.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 1 Prison sentences are meant to reduce crime, by serving as disincentives to engage in crime. The author develops the argument by 1. [ ] (A) using key differences between the actions of two groups of people categorized by age to illuminate a phenomenon. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) demonstrating the lack of the effect of a factor by comparing situations that include and do not include that factor. 3. [ ] (C) undermining the force of the evidence supporting a common belief by providing a reasonable alternative interpretation of the same information. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) outlining a theory and then providing evidence to support that theory. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) questioning the support for a hypothesis by showing that the data used to support the hypothesis is skewed. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: hard In order to answer this question correctly, we have to find the choice that correctly describes how the author develops the argument. A choice that only partly matches how the argument is developed will be incorrect.
Let’s review how the argument proceeds:
It describes how prison sentences are meant to work.
It presents a theory that seems to follow from the information on how prison sentences are meant to work.
It presents evidence that conflicts with that theory.
It concludes that the theory is incorrect.
(A) using key differences between the actions of two groups of people categorized by age to illuminate a phenomenon. What this choice describes is nearly the opposite of how the author develops the argument. The conclusion is supported by a similarity between rather than by differences between the actions of people in two different age groups.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) demonstrating the lack of the effect of a factor by comparing situations that include and do not include that factor. This choice describes exactly how the passage proceeds. It demonstrates that longer prison sentences do not have a material crime reducing effect by showing that a situation in which the factor, threat of longer prison sentences, is present is not significantly different from a situation in which that factor is not present. In other words, since young people aged eighteen and older are no less likely to commit crimes than people younger than eighteen, even though the people aged eighteen and older face longer prison sentences if caught, the factor, the threat of longer prison sentences, has no effect.
(C) undermining the force of the evidence supporting a common belief by providing a reasonable alternative interpretation of the same information. This choice could be tempting, because its first word, “undermining,” could be perceived as describing what the argument does when it presents information conflicting with the theory that the threat of longer prison sentences serves to reduce crime more than shorter sentences do. However, since the passage proceeds by providing new information rather than by providing an alternative interpretation of already presented information, this choice does not accurately describe how the author develops the argument.
(D) outlining a theory and then providing evidence to support that theory. This choice could be tempting, because it mentions a theory and the passage does start off with an idea that might be true in “theory.” However, by reading the choice completely, we can see that it does not accurately describe how the author develops the argument, as the author does not provide evidence to support a theory, but rather provides evidence indicating that what seems true in theory is not actually true.
(E) questioning the support for a hypothesis by showing that the data used to support the hypothesis is skewed. This choice could seem correct initially, as it mentions “questioning the support for a hypothesis,” which seems at least somewhat like what the passage does when it provides information indicating that something that might be true “in theory” is not actually true. However, since the passage does not bring up any skewed data, this choice turns out to be incorrect.
Correct Answer: (B) Ok, now that we have a basic understanding of what Method of Reasoning questions ask us to do, let’s turn our attention to common question stems for this question type.
summary:
Lesson Content It’s obvious that, for an answer choice to be correct, that answer choice must be completely correct.
We can leverage this fact to help us solve Method of Reasoning questions.
The answers to Method of Reasoning questions describe the logical structures of authors’ arguments.
💡MUST KNOW:
Since the answers to Method of Reasoning questions describe the logical structures of arguments, a correct answer must describe exactly how a given argument is structured.
title: 12.1.1 Each Part of the Correct Answer Must Be, Well, Correct.

contents:
Lesson Content Method of Reasoning questions use predictable language, such as:
The argument proceeds by
The father responds to his daughter’s criticism by doing which of the following?
The exchange above supports the claim that the two parties disagree about
Which one of the following most accurately describes the relationship between Jane’s argument and Mark’s argument?
The Attorney General uses which of the following techniques in responding to the cheese importer?
The method of the argument is to
The author develops the argument above by
The argument above employs which of the following reasoning techniques?
Which of the following best describes the argument above?
💡MUST KNOW:
Familiarize yourself with the common question stems that indicate of Method of Reasoning questions, but as always, be flexible.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 2 Burrito Express CEO: Burrito Express has been in business in Country X for over a decade. The CEO proceeds by 1. [ ] (A) using the example of one company’s experience in Country X to make the case that Country X would benefit from making changes in its economic policies. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) describing a general economic situation and outlining its impact on a company’s global operations. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) evaluating the impact of current economic conditions on an industry and recommending how the industry should respond. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) assuming without providing justification that a course of action makes sense. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) describing certain consequences of current economic conditions and outlining a conclusion that is based on that information. View Solution Question Level: medium In answering Method of Reasoning questions, it’s especially important to focus on structure – or how the argument fits together. The structure of the argument is distinctly different from the content of the argument (its subject matter). Trap answers in Method of Reasoning questions may temptingly include content that is similar to the content of the passage and yet does not accurately reflect how the passage proceeds. Also, remember that a correct answer choice must be completely correct.
Let’s look at the structure of the stimulus:
A business needs certain items to make some of its products. Because of economic conditions, the business cannot get those items. Therefore, the business is shutting down, rather than scaling back its operations. That’s all. We need to find an answer choice that describes this basic structure.
(A) using the example of one company’s experience in Country X to make the case that Country X would benefit from making changes in its economic policies. This answer relies on content to tempt you. The stimulus discusses a company and economic conditions in Country X, and so does this answer. However, this answer doesn’t accurately describe the structure. While what the CEO describes could be taken as evidence that a change in Country X’s economic policy is warranted, the CEO is not making a case for a change in economic policy in Country X.
(B) describing a general economic situation and outlining its impact on a company’s global operations. This answer’s structure is closer to being correct, but it is still incorrect. For one thing, “describing a general economic situation” does not actually occur in the passage. Economic upheaval is mentioned, but the economic situation is not actually described. Similarly, the passage does not describe the impact that the situation in Country X has had on Burrito Express’s global operations. Remember, we can go back to the passage and ask “Does the argument do what the answer choice says it does?” If the answer to that question is “No,” then the answer can’t be correct.
(C) evaluating the impact of current economic conditions on an industry and recommending how the industry should respond. The passage does not describe the impact on an industry as a whole; it only describes the impact on Burrito Express. Likewise, the passage only states how Burrito Express is choosing to respond, and, unlike what this choice says, does not make a recommendation. Always go back to the stimulus and check!
(D) assuming without providing justification that a course of action makes sense. This answer could be tempting, as the CEO does mention a course of action that will be taken. However, since, in the passage, the CEO does provide some justification, the lack of ingredients, for the course of action, in saying that the CEO is “assuming without providing justification,” this choice does not accurately reflect what the CEO does.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) describing certain consequences of current economic conditions and outlining a conclusion that is based on that information. The CEO describes the consequences of current economic conditions (the inability to acquire ingredients for some items), and states a conclusion that is based on that information (shut down all operations rather than just eliminating those items). Notice that when we go back to the argument and check, we see that each part of this answer is correct.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now discuss a second type of Method of Reasoning question, Two-Part Method of Reasoning Questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Method of Reasoning questions use predictable language, such as:
The argument proceeds by
The father responds to his daughter’s criticism by doing which of the following?
The exchange above supports the claim that the two parties disagree about
Which one of the following most accurately describes the relationship between Jane’s argument and Mark’s argument?
The Attorney General uses which of the following techniques in responding to the cheese importer?
title: 12.1.2 Common Question Stems for Method of Reasoning Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Many Method of Reasoning questions are based on dialogues between two speakers.
Each part of the dialogue is considered a separate argument, generally with its own evidence, assumption(s), and conclusion(s).
The first speaker’s argument may or may not be sound.
On the other hand, the second speaker may respond effectively to the first speaker.
The two speakers could believe that they agree (or disagree), when in fact they are talking about different things.
So, in answering a Method of Reasoning question that involves two speakers, our first job is read through the exchange, noting any conclusions made by each speaker and getting a sense of how the speakers support those conclusions.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering a Method of Reasoning question that involves two speakers, our first job is read through the exchange, noting any conclusions made by each speaker and getting a sense of how the speakers support those conclusions.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 3 Researcher: Our study of the Mediterranean diet shows that people who followed that diet were healthier than and lived longer than did people who followed other diets. Editor: I talked with your assistant, and he told me that, to make his work easier, he had all the people in one area follow the Mediterranean diet and people in other areas follow other diets. The editor responded to the researcher’s assertion by doing which of the following? 1. [ ] (A) Challenging the assumption that there are not other diets via which people would achieve results better than those achieved via following the Mediterranean diet. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Pointing out that aggregate results of people’s following a particular diet may not indicate that that diet is optimal for any particular individual. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Pointing out that the research team failed to adequately control for a variable other than the one that was the focus of the study. 4. [ ] (D) Questioning the ethics of having people in different areas follow diets that the researchers believe to be of varying levels of quality. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) Asserting that the variations in health outcomes observed resulted from factors characteristic of areas in which people lived rather than from differences in diet. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: medium The researcher recommends following the Mediterranean diet. The basis for this recommendation is a set of study results that show that people who followed the Mediterranean diet experienced better health outcomes than were experienced by people following other diets.
The editor responds by declining to publish the findings. The basis for the editor’s decision is a fact that the editor points out, the fact that, because of the way the study was set up – the people following different diets lived in different areas – there could have been a cause, characteristics of areas in which people lived, other than diet that was responsible for the observed pattern in health outcomes.
(A) Challenging the assumption that there are not other diets via which people would achieve results better than those achieved via following the Mediterranean diet. While challenging the assumption that there are not other diets via which people would achieve results better than those achieved via following the Mediterranean diet might have been a valid way in which the editor could have responded, the editor did not challenge this assumption.
(B) Pointing out that aggregate results of people’s following a particular diet may not indicate that that diet is optimal for any particular individual. While the point brought up by this answer choice seems valid, it is not the point that the editor brought up.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Pointing out that the research team failed to adequately control for a variable other than the one that was the focus of the study. By pointing out that characteristics of areas in which people lived, rather than diet, could have been responsible for the observed variation in health outcomes, the editor does essentially what this choice describes, points out that the research team did not adequately control for a variable, area in which people lived, other than the one that was the focus of the study, diet.
(D) Questioning the ethics of having people in different areas follow diets that the researchers believe to be of varying levels of quality. While this choice brings up an interesting issue, the editor did not do what this choice describes.
(E) Asserting that the variations in health outcomes observed resulted from factors characteristic of areas in which people lived rather than from differences in diet. This choice is a little tricky, because the editor did bring up the possibility that the variable area in which people lived, rather than diet, was responsible for the variation in health outcomes. However, unlike what this choice describes, the editor did not assert that factors related to areas in which people lived were in fact responsible for the variation in health outcomes.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss how we can use the verbs used by the speakers to arrive at the correct answers to Two Part Method of Reasoning questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Many Method of Reasoning questions are based on dialogues between two speakers.
Each part of the dialogue is considered a separate argument, generally with its own evidence, assumption(s), and conclusion(s).
The first speaker’s argument may or may not be sound.
On the other hand, the second speaker may respond effectively to the first speaker.
The two speakers could believe that they agree (or disagree), when in fact they are talking about different things.
title: 12.2 Two-Part Method of Reasoning Questions

contents:
Lesson Content In answering two-part questions, we must determine what type of response the second speaker makes to the first speaker’s argument.
The journalist responds to the editor’s criticism by
(A) rejecting the editor’s conclusion while proposing …
(B) providing evidence that the editor’s argument has significantly …
(C) objecting to the editor’s introducing into the discussion …
(D) pointing out a benefit of maintaining the board, which …
(E) shifting the discussion from the argument at hand to an attack on …
Notice the five introducing verbs in these answer choices, and remember that, in answering a Two-Part Method of Reasoning question, we always can refer to the stimulus and determine whether the speakers do what answer choices say they do.
💡MUST KNOW:
The verbs used by the speakers in Two-Part Method of Reasoning questions can help us arrive at correct answers. However, as always, we must analyze these questions carefully and completely.
Let’s look at another example:
Film School President: It is essential that students in our Screenwriting program have exposure to all aspects of the film business, from location scouting to editing to still photography, so that they understand how their work fits into the film industry at large. This knowledge is vital to being able both to network and to work successfully alongside other industry professionals.
Screenwriting Instructor: That statement is false. Every hour spent learning other crafts is an hour not spent writing, and it is well known that a screenwriter must spend hundreds, if not thousands, of hours writing before his or her work reaches a professional standard. Therefore, any time spent on subjects other than writing will put my screenwriting students at a disadvantage in their careers.
The Screenwriting Instructor responds to the Film School President’s statement by
(A) pointing out a flaw in the Film School President’s argument while conceding that its main conclusion is valid.
(B) rejecting the Film School President’s conclusion and presenting evidence to support a contrary conclusion.
(C) rejecting the Film School President’s conclusion and providing statistical evidence that supports a contrary conclusion.
Notice that the two speakers’ claims are not necessarily incompatible; it is possible that a screenwriter must spend hundreds (or thousands) of hours writing before his/her work reaches a professional standard, and yet that it is still important that a screenwriting student understand various non-writing parts of the film industry.
Nevertheless, put this issue aside.
First, we see the Film School President’s statements indicating that a broad education in film is essential for screenwriting students to network and to understand their place in the industry.
Since the stem asks us how the Screenwriting Instructor responds to the President, our correct answer must correctly describe the relationship between the two arguments.
The Screenwriting Instructor responds to the Film School President’s statement by pointing out a flaw in the Film School President’s argument while conceding that its main conclusion is valid.
Does the Screenwriting Instructor take these actions? If we refer back to the stimulus, we see that the Screenwriting Instructor does neither of these two things.
Now, let’s look at the verbs in option (B).
The Screenwriting Instructor responds by rejecting the Film School President’s conclusion and presenting evidence to support a contrary conclusion.
Does the Screenwriting Instructor take both actions? Yes, for sure he does.
Now, let’s look at the two verbs in option (C).
The Screenwriting Instructor responds to the Film School President’s statement by rejecting the Film School President’s conclusion and providing statistical evidence that supports a contrary conclusion.
If you were not reading carefully, you may say that option (C) could also be correct.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 4 Marco: Many people claim that, within a decade, the majority of automobiles on the road will be powered by alternative energy sources such as electricity, natural gas, or hydrogen. Ted: As alternative energy powered vehicles become more common, their prices will significantly decrease as used vehicles enter the resale market and manufacturers take advantage of economies of scale. Ted responds to Marco by 1. [ ] (A) arguing that Marco has based his claim on an unrepresentative sample. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) suggesting that Marco has insufficiently defined the term “alternative. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) rejecting a premise upon which Marco’s argument depends. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) questioning Marco’s motives for opposing alternative energy powered vehicles. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) pointing out additional factors that Marco may not have considered. View Solution Question Level: medium Marco claims that it is unlikely that, in the future, the majority of automobiles on the road will be powered by alternative energy sources, basing his conclusion on the fact that such vehicles are more expensive than comparable gasoline powered vehicles. Notice that Ted does not say that Marco is wrong. Rather, he presents reasons that the prices of these vehicles are likely to decrease. The correct answer will certainly focus on his pointing out these additional considerations.
(A) arguing that Marco has based his claim on an unrepresentative sample. Marco does not base his claim on a sample. Furthermore, Ted never questions Marco’s basic claim. Ted only states that there is additional information to be considered. Remember, go back and check. Use the verbs.
(B) suggesting that Marco has insufficiently defined the term “alternative. ” Note that Ted never questions Marco’s use of the word “alternative.” Even though Marco never explicitly defines “alternative,” he gives examples of it that Ted appears to accept.
(C) rejecting a premise upon which Marco’s argument depends. This answer is tempting: Ted does in a sense claim that Marco’s evidence is incomplete. However, Ted never states that Marco’s premise that alternative-energy vehicles are more expensive is incorrect. Rather, he simply states that their prices will decrease over time. Keep in mind that, even if Ted is correct and the prices will decrease, Marco’s claim that alternative-energy cars are more expensive may be valid at this point in time.
(D) questioning Marco’s motives for opposing alternative energy powered vehicles. Ted never addresses Marco’s motivations, nor does he say that Marco is wrong. Ted merely offers additional information that may be pertinent to the argument.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) pointing out additional factors that Marco may not have considered. Ted claims that the prices of alternative energy powered vehicles will decline and supports that claim with evidence. That is, he points out additional information that might undermine the support for conclusion of the argument.
Correct Answer: (E) At this point, you already have a good idea of how to correctly answer one-part and two-part Method of Reasoning questions.
summary:
Lesson Content In answering two-part questions, we must determine what type of response the second speaker makes to the first speaker’s argument.
The journalist responds to the editor’s criticism by
(A) rejecting the editor’s conclusion while proposing …
(B) providing evidence that the editor’s argument has significantly …
(C) objecting to the editor’s introducing into the discussion …
(D) pointing out a benefit of maintaining the board, which …
(E) shifting the discussion from the argument at hand to an attack on …
title: 12.2.1 The Verbs Used by the Speakers Can Help Us Arrive at Correct Answers

contents:
Lesson Content To solve Method of Reasoning Questions, follow this simple strategy:
Step 1 - Carefully Read, Break Down, and Completely Understand the Argument
The foundation of choosing the right answer to a Method of Reasoning question is understanding the argument presented in the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
Do whatever you must to ensure that you understand the argument presented in a Method of Reasoning question. Go slowly. Read carefully. Take short notes on what you read. Be an active, engaged reader. Force yourself to become interested in the information presented. Pretend that you are Sherlock Homes solving a mystery. Remember, your goal is to carefully consider the structure of the argument, not the merits of the argument.
Remember to Identify the Conclusion and Premises.
As you analyze the argument, ask yourself: What is the author’s conclusion? What specific evidence does he or she use to support that conclusion? If there are two arguments, do the authors agree or disagree?
Since these questions are based on arguments, it’s important to use the skills you’ve already developed in analyzing arguments; you must continue to seek out the argument’s conclusion and premises, and, in some cases, consider any assumptions that are made.
Step 2 – Identify How to Answer the Question
To identify how to answer the question, read the question stem to see what type of answer you have to find.
The Attorney General uses which of the following techniques in responding to the Chief Technology Officer?
Step 3 – Summarize the Method in Simple Terms.
Since the question asks us to define the way in which the argument proceeds, it’s important that we have a clear sense of how the argument is structured before we look at the answer choices.
Describe the logical flow of the argument in your own words.
It’s important to keep in mind that we are unlikely to see our description exactly matched by an answer choice.
Remember, the GMAT rewards careful reading.
Step 4 - Go Through the Answer Choices One By One, Seeking to Remove the Most Obviously Incorrect Options
Your next step is to carefully go through the answer choices one by one, seeking to remove the most obviously incorrect options.
It’s worth writing “A, B, C, D, E” on your dry erase pad.
💡MUST KNOW:
To prevent your crossing out an answer choice only mistakenly to choose it as correct, write out “A, B, C, D, E” on your dry erase pad. As you eliminate the choices that are obviously wrong, physically cross them out.
Now – as you are already aware, one of the most common mistakes that people make is to cross off the correct answer in their initial assessment of the answer choices.
💡MUST KNOW:
One of the most common mistakes that people make is to cross off the correct answer in their initial assessment of the answer choices. So, even after you’ve crossed off an answer, you must stay open to the fact that you may have crossed off the correct answer. Always be willing to reassess the situation.
At this point, remember that you can go back to the argument and check on anything said in an answer choice! The passage is right in front of you.
💡MUST KNOW:
If even one part of an answer choice describes something untrue about the argument, that answer is incorrect.
Also, remember that the verbs used by the speakers can help us arrive at correct answers.
Step 5 – Carefully Analyze the Final Answer Choices
Of the answers remaining, the correct answer must do the best job of capturing the logical structure of the argument or arguments.
At this point, one fundamental error that many people make is simply to re-read the two final choices and rely on “gut instinct” as to which is correct.
💡MUST KNOW:
When you are down to two answers, you must not become complacent and make your best guess. No way. Now is the time to shine, to turn the jets to overdrive, and to relentlessly seek out the correct answer.
Consider the following examples.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 5 During the past ten years, the fastest growing segment of the video game market has been so-called “mobile gaming.” The graphics and game play of these video games, which are played on mobile devices such as tablet computers and smartphones, are becoming increasingly sophisticated and complicated. The argument above proceeds in which of the following ways? 1. [ ] (A) The author considers two different viewpoints and, in light of a set of facts, chooses one of the viewpoints. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) The author uses an analogy between situations as the evidence upon which to base a prediction about a category of technology. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) The author mentions a commonly held view and then, using recent developments as evidence, demonstrates that that view is no longer correct. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) The author arrives at a cause-and-effect conclusion using as evidence characteristics of a limited sample. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) The author bases a prediction about certain technology upon recent trends. View Solution Question Level: easy Step 1 - Carefully Read, Break Down, and Completely Understand the Argument
The argument supports the following conclusion:
Conclusion: At some point in the near future, stand-alone video game consoles will seem as antiquated as pinball machines.
The premises are that mobile gaming is the “fastest growing” part of the video game market and that mobile games have graphics and game play that “are becoming increasingly sophisticated and complicated.”
Step 2 – Identify How to Answer the Question
The question stem asks which way the argument proceeds. So, this is a Method of Reasoning question, and we have to choose the choice that correctly describes how the argument works.
Step 3 – Summarize the Method in Simple Terms.
What does the author do? The author describes recent trends (“fastest growing”) and (“increasingly sophisticated and complicated” “graphics and game play”) before making a prediction about the future (“at some point in the near future”).
A correct answer to this Method of Reasoning question might be:
The author brings up recent trends and uses those trends as the basis for making a prediction about video games.
Obviously, there might be different ways to describe the author’s method, but this summarizes the basic idea fairly well.
Step 4 - Go Through the Answer Choices One By One, Seeking to Remove the Most Obviously Incorrect Options
(A) The author considers two different viewpoints and, in light of a set of facts, chooses one of the viewpoints. This choice is fairly obviously incorrect, as the author does not consider two viewpoints.
(B) Keep. Since the author does make a prediction and does use an analogy, let’s keep this one for now.
(C) The author mentions a commonly held view and then, using recent developments as evidence, demonstrates that that view is no longer correct. While this choice could be tempting, as the author does use recent developments as evidence, the author does not actually challenge any particular view.
(D) The author arrives at a cause-and-effect conclusion using as evidence characteristics of a limited sample. This jumps out as the one of the most obviously incorrect. While in a sense the argument does mention a cause, developments in the video game market, and an effect, the obsolescence of video game consoles, the conclusion is not a cause-and-effect conclusion. It is simply a prediction.
(E) Keep. This choice sounds correct. The author does make a prediction about certain technology and bases that prediction on recent trends.
Step 5 – Carefully Analyze the Final Answer Choices
Choice (B) looked possibly correct at first, mostly because of its wording, but, upon careful review, we can see that, while the author does make a prediction, the author does not use the analogy as the basis for making that prediction. The author uses the analogy merely as a way of illustrating the predicted outcome.
Choice (E), on the other hand, accurately describes what the author does.
Correct Answer: (E) --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 6 Eleni: Most of the owners of hybrid cars whom I know are much healthier than the average person. Lee: People who drive hybrid cars tend to be concerned about ecology. Lee responds to Eleni’s argument by doing which of the following? 1. [ ] (A) Providing reason to question the premise that Eleni uses as support for her conclusion. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) Demonstrating that owning hybrid car is necessary for experiencing better than average health but likely not sufficient for doing so. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Pointing out that, because Eleni uses a limited sample that may not be representative of people in general, the support for her conclusion is weak. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Undermining the force of the evidence that Eleni uses by showing that a factor that Eleni did not mention may be causing the pattern she cites. 5. [ ] (E) Showing that the approach that Eleni is considering for achieving her goal is not the only way to achieve that goal. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium Step 1 - Carefully Read, Break Down, and Completely Understand the Argument
Eleni concludes that, in order to be healthy, she should get a hybrid car, rather than a typical internal combustion engine only car. Her basis for the conclusion is the fact that most of the owners of hybrid cars whom she knows are much healthier than the average person.
Lee brings up an alternative explanation for the correlation between hybrid car ownership and better than average health, the fact that people who own hybrid cars are generally concerned about ecology, and that, therefore, on average, they likely travel by bicycle more than the average person does and include in their diets more locally grown organic fruits and vegetables than the average person includes, lifestyle habits likely to result in better than average health. In doing so, he makes a case for doubting that Eleni’s choice of car will make any difference.
Step 2 – Identify How to Answer the Question
The question stem asks how Lee responds. So, this is a Method of Reasoning question, and we have to choose the choice that correctly describes the way in which Lee responds.
It is not necessary to determine whether Lee responds effectively to Eleni’s argument. We must only identify Lee’s method of reasoning.
Step 3 – Summarize the Method in Simple Terms.
What does Lee do? Lee brings up a characteristics of hybrid car owners, concern for ecology and the lifestyle habits that result from that concern, that constitute an alternative cause for the correlation that Eleni has observed.
He then states the conclusion that he doubts that her choice will make any difference.
A correct answer to this Method of Reasoning question might be:
Lee brings up an alternative cause for the observed correlation upon which Eleni bases her conclusion and, on the basis of the likelihood that that alternative cause is what actually underlies the correlation, concludes that there is reason to doubt Eleni’s conclusion.
Obviously, there are different ways to describe what Lee did, but the above summarizes the basic idea fairly well.
Step 4 - Go Through the Answer Choices One By One, Seeking to Remove the Most Obviously Incorrect Options
(A) Providing reason to question the premise that Eleni uses as support for her conclusion. This choice is fairly obviously incorrect, as Lee does not provide a reason to question Eleni’s premise that most of the owners of hybrid cars whom she knows are much healthier than the average person.
(B) Demonstrating that owning hybrid car is necessary for experiencing better than average health but likely not sufficient for doing so. Since Lee says that he doubts that Eleni’s choice of car will make any difference, it’s clear that he is not suggesting that owning a hybrid car is necessary for experiencing better than average health, because, if owning a hybrid car were necessary for experiencing better than average health, the choice to get a hybrid car would be important for experiencing health and, therefore, would make a difference. So, we can be rather confident that this choice is not the correct answer.
(C) Pointing out that, because Eleni uses a limited sample that may not be representative of people in general, the support for her conclusion is weak. This choice could be tempting, since Eleni does use a limited sample that may not be representative of people in general. However, since Lee says nothing at all about the size or nature of the sample that Eleni uses, we can be rather confident that this choice is incorrect.
(D) Keep. Lee does bring up a factor that Eleni did not mention, concern about ecology and the lifestyle habits that result from that concern, that may be causing the correlation upon which Eleni bases her conclusion. In doing so, he undermines the force of the evidence that Eleni provides, because, what he says makes clear that, even though there is a correlation between owning a hybrid car and better than average health, it is likely that, concern about ecology, and lifestyle habits associated with that concern, rather than owning a hybrid car, is the cause of better than average health. So, this choice could be our answer.
(E) Keep. Lee does bring up ways other than owning a hybrid car that would likely result in people’s experiencing better than average health. So, let’s keep this choice in case it’s correct.
Step 5 – Carefully Analyze the Final Answer Choices
Choice (E) may seem possibly correct at first, but if we carefully consider what Lee says, we can see that he never asserts or implies that owning a hybrid car is one way, along with other ways, to experience better than average health. Rather he mentions lifestyle habits that are likely to result in better than average health and then indicates that he doubts that one’s choice of car, which could be a choice to get a hybrid, will make any difference. So, through careful examination of choice (E), we can see clearly that it does not accurately describe what Lee does.
Choice (D), on the other hand, accurately describes what Lee does.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss incorrect answers in method of reasoning questions.
summary:
Lesson Content To solve Method of Reasoning Questions, follow this simple strategy:
Step 1 - Carefully Read, Break Down, and Completely Understand the Argument
The foundation of choosing the right answer to a Method of Reasoning question is understanding the argument presented in the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
Do whatever you must to ensure that you understand the argument presented in a Method of Reasoning question. Go slowly. Read carefully. Take short notes on what you read.
title: 12.3 Strategy for Attacking Method of Reasoning Questions

contents: Lesson Content Just as is the case with Critical Reasoning questions of all other types, Method of Reasoning questions can include various types of incorrect answers, some of which are tricky trap answers that almost seem correct.
summary: Lesson Content Just as is the case with Critical Reasoning questions of all other types, Method of Reasoning questions can include various types of incorrect answers, some of which are tricky trap answers that almost seem correct.
title: 12.4 Incorrect Answers in Method of Reasoning Questions

contents:
Lesson Content Remember, Method of Reasoning questions specifically ask us to analyze how a given argument is structured.
In other words, our analysis and identification will necessarily stick to the structure and wording of the argument presented.
For instance, in a question involving an argument that questions the effectiveness of a plan, an answer choice that might say something about questioning the motives of the planners.
Consider the following example:
Many people consider homeschooling inferior to traditional classroom education because they perceive homeschooling as failing to provide children with a means for learning to interact socially. However, while it is true that children who participate in traditional schooling often have more interaction with peers than do homeschooled children, traditional schooling is rather unlike the “real” world that comes after graduation. So, there is no reason to believe that children who participate in traditional schooling end up any better prepared to interact socially than homeschooled children.
The author of the above argument proceeds by
(A) basing its conclusion on the assumption that one type of educational experience is more similar to the “real” world than another.
(B) conceding that there is evidence in favor of the conclusion he opposes and then providing an additional consideration that supports his conclusion
In reading the argument above, one sees that the author compares the experience of traditional education to the “real” world.
The truth is, however, that choice (A) goes beyond what we can tell from what the passage says, as the passage does not indicate that the author assumes that the experience of homeschooling is “more similar” to the “real” world than is the experience of traditional schooling.
Choice (B), on the other hand, matches exactly how the author proceeds when he first concedes that children who participate in traditional schooling often have more interaction with peers than do homeschooled children, and then provides the additional consideration that traditional schooling is rather unlike the “real” world that comes after graduation.
💡MUST KNOW:
Answering Method of reasoning questions involves considering the structure and wording of the arguments presented; therefore, an answer choice that presents new components cannot be correct.
Often, in the arguments presented in Method of Reasoning questions, there will be gaps.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 7 City planners have traditionally sought ways to make cities more accessible to drivers by building parking garages and lots on valuable city real estate. Which of the following statements made by a city planner best illustrates the reasoning that underlies the change in policy described in the argument above? 1. [ ] (A) “As our cities become denser, the prevailing attitude that it is practical to own a car in an urban area must be broken down. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) “This city has long done a disservice to its core taxpayer base in favor of attracting entertainment dollars from the surrounding suburbs. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) “New bicycle sharing systems reduce the parking lot space necessary to support commerce in this area, thereby opening up valuable real estate for use by restaurants and shops. 4. [ ] (D) “More young people can afford to buy in our city now than could in the recent past, but, without entertainment options, we won’t be able to interest them in moving here. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) “Parking lots are unattractive and waste valuable space that could be used by retailers and other business that will generate more tax revenue for the city. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard In this Method of Reasoning question, we are asked to mimic the structure of the argument. If we break the argument down in simple terms, we see that
Fact One: City planners have traditionally sought ways to make cities more accessible to drivers by building parking garages and lots on valuable city real estate.
Fact Two: Now, city planners are ignoring the preferences of automobile drivers in favor of accommodating the preferences of urban residents who use alternate forms of transportation.
That’s all. We need to find a statement that reflects these ideas. Be careful not to select an answer that brings up any assumptions or additional ideas. Such components are not asked for in this case.
(A) “As our cities become denser, the prevailing attitude that it is practical to own a car in an urban area must be broken down. ” While this may be true in reality, this statement’s focus on density is unsupported as nowhere in the stimulus is density mentioned as a reason for the policy shift. Remember – in answering these questions, go back and check!
(B) “This city has long done a disservice to its core taxpayer base in favor of attracting entertainment dollars from the surrounding suburbs. ” Again, this statement may very well be true in reality. However, the stimulus never mentions drivers from the suburbs, nor does it imply that city dwellers have been “done a disservice.”
CORRECT ANSWER(C) “New bicycle sharing systems reduce the parking lot space necessary to support commerce in this area, thereby opening up valuable real estate for use by restaurants and shops. ” This answer essentially repeats the stimulus. That is, of course, exactly what the question asks us to do. The city dwellers want things that they can walk or bike to, rather than parking lots.
(D) “More young people can afford to buy in our city now than could in the recent past, but, without entertainment options, we won’t be able to interest them in moving here. ” The stimulus never, of course, mentions affordability. Also, while the stimulus does discuss the lifestyle that urban dwellers prefer, the answer fails to mention changes in transportation.
(E) “Parking lots are unattractive and waste valuable space that could be used by retailers and other business that will generate more tax revenue for the city. ” Again, this may very well be true; however, the stimulus never mentions tax revenue.
Correct Answer: (C) Next let’s discuss partially correct answers.
summary:
Lesson Content Remember, Method of Reasoning questions specifically ask us to analyze how a given argument is structured.
In other words, our analysis and identification will necessarily stick to the structure and wording of the argument presented.
For instance, in a question involving an argument that questions the effectiveness of a plan, an answer choice that might say something about questioning the motives of the planners.
Consider the following example:
title: 12.4.1 An Answer that Presents New Components

contents:
Lesson Content Partially correct answer choices are always tempting because they typically begin by accurately, or somewhat accurately, describing part of the argument.
Notice how the partially correct answer in the following example could be tempting:
It is common knowledge that overconsumption of salt can result in excessively high blood pressure. What is not as well known is that heavy consumption of sugar can have similar effects. Results of a 2010 study indicate that consuming a diet high in sugar can lead to an increase in blood pressure greater than that typically resulting from consumption of salt. This effect can be attributed in part to the body’s responding to the sugar consumption by producing large amounts of insulin, the presence of which, in turn, causes an increase in blood pressure.
In which of the following ways does the author of the above passage proceed?
(Partially Correct Answer) The author presents a claim supported by scientific evidence and explains how that claim conflicts with common knowledge. (Correct Answer) The author uses supporting information to back up a claim that supplements common knowledge.
In this example, the partially correct answer starts off correctly, as the author does present a claim supported by scientific evidence.
The correct answer, on the other hand, accurately describes how the argument proceeds.
💡MUST KNOW:
Every part of an answer must be correct, otherwise it is wrong.
To avoid choosing a partially correct answer, be sure to compare all parts of Method of Reasoning answer choices with what the passage says.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 8 Eugene: The highways around our city are chronically congested with traffic. Edda: When Houston, Texas responded to traffic congestion by widening a main highway, the availability of new lanes induced more drivers to use the highway. Edda responds to Eugene’s argument in which of the following ways? 1. [ ] (A) She agrees with the conclusion but shows that there is reason to question the choice of evidence used. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) She demonstrates that there is reason to question the final claim made by bringing up a specific case. 3. [ ] (C) She challenges the assumption that what has worked elsewhere will work in the situation mentioned. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) She refutes the conclusion by establishing that two disparate situations are analogous. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) She uses the example of a particular case to illustrate the inadvisability of assuming that all such cases are the same. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy Eugene’s conclusion is that that “our city” must commit to widening its highways. The conclusion is supported by the fact that highways around “our city” are congested and the fact that, if the highways had more lanes, there would be more highway space per car.
Edda responds to Eugene’s argument by bringing up a specific case in which the approach that Eugene suggests the city must commit to, widening highways, resulted in an increase, rather than a reduction, in congestion.
Think about Edda’s response. While some of the answer choices are entirely incorrect, some of the answer choices start off saying things that could describe how Edda responded. Here’s how the choices start off:
A. She agrees with the conclusion …
B. She demonstrates that there is reason to question the final claim made …
C. She challenges the assumption …
D. She refutes the conclusion …
E. She uses the example of a particular case …
Choice (A) is clearly incorrect, because she does not indicate that she agrees with the conclusion.
Choice (D) is more subtly incorrect, because, while she does provide a reason to question the conclusion, she does not directly refute the conclusion.
At the same time, choices (B), (C), and (E) all start off in ways that match what Edda does.
She does demonstrate that there is reason to question the final claim made, as (B) says.
What she does could be seen as challenging an assumption, as (C) says she does.
She does use an example of a particular case, as (E) says.
So, to answer this question correctly, we have to be sure to carefully consider all of what each choice says, so that we don’t end up choosing an answer that starts off by matching what Edda does but ends up saying something that does not match what she does.
(A) She agrees with the conclusion but shows that there is reason to question the choice of evidence used. Edda does not indicate that she agrees with Eugene’s conclusion. Rather, she brings up an example the existence of which is a reason to question Eugene’s conclusion. This choice is blatantly incorrect.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) She demonstrates that there is reason to question the final claim made by bringing up a specific case. Edda does exactly what this choice says she does, demonstrates that there is reason to question the final claim made by Eugene, “Our city must commit to widening its highways,” by bringing up an example of a situation in which the use of the approach that Eugene says “our city” must commit to resulted in an increase in congestion.
(C) She challenges the assumption that what has worked elsewhere will work in the situation mentioned. This answer choice starts off seeming to be correct, since the case that Edda brings up is a reason to question assumptions implicit in Eugene’s argument, such as the assumption that widening the highways will not result in an increase in the number of cars on the highways. However, this choice turns out to be incorrect, as the assumption that it mentions would not be an assumption that Edda challenges.
Eugene’s argument does not depend on the assumption that what worked elsewhere will work in this situation. His argument depends on the simple math he mentioned, and on assumptions that connect the implications of that math to his conclusion, none of which assumptions involves the fact that the approach of widening highways worked elsewhere. Widening existing highways could work in the situation described by Eugene even if it has never been done anywhere else.
Further, nothing that either Eugene or Edda says indicates that either believes that the approach worked elsewhere. So, it does not make sense to describe Edda’s response as challenging any assumption involving the approach’s having worked elsewhere.
(D) She refutes the conclusion by establishing that two disparate situations are analogous. While Edda’s response does indicate that there is reason to question Eugene’s conclusion, she does not refute his conclusion.
Also, while Edda does mention a situation that is similar in some ways to the situation discussed by Eugene, she does not establish that the two situations are analogous. Rather she merely mentions the situation in Houston, Texas without establishing that it is analogous to the one discussed by Eugene.
(E) She uses the example of a particular case to illustrate the inadvisability of assuming that all such cases are the same. The beginning of this choice matches exactly what Edda does; she does use the example of a particular case. However, while Edda does not directly say why she has brought up this example – she merely brings up the example without saying why – what Edda says is, presumably, a response to what Eugene says. So, since Eugene’s argument involves only the specifics of one case and clearly does not require assuming that all cases like the one he discusses are the same, we safely presume that Edda did not bring up the example in order to illustrate the inadvisability of assuming that all cases like the one Eugene discusses are the same, because there is no logical reason why she would have done so.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss another tricky type of incorrect Method of Reasoning answer choice, answers that describe methods that arguments could use but don’t use.
summary:
Lesson Content Partially correct answer choices are always tempting because they typically begin by accurately, or somewhat accurately, describing part of the argument.
Notice how the partially correct answer in the following example could be tempting:
It is common knowledge that overconsumption of salt can result in excessively high blood pressure. What is not as well known is that heavy consumption of sugar can have similar effects. Results of a 2010 study indicate that consuming a diet high in sugar can lead to an increase in blood pressure greater than that typically resulting from consumption of salt.
title: 12.4.2 Partially Correct Answers

contents:
Lesson Content Another tricky type of incorrect answer that you could see in a Method of Reasoning question is one that describes a method that the argument could use but doesn’t use.
Consider the following example:
Commercial landlords in Mennerton are excited about the completion of the new high speed rail line between Mennerton and Boselton, because they believe that, since the new line will make Mennerton more easily accessible, large businesses that would have located in Boselton will now locate instead in Mennerton, where rents are lower. They may be disappointed, as plans are now being made to extend the new line from Boselton to Verdantville. Once that extension is complete, Boselton will be an even better location for large businesses than it is now, and thus Mennerton may lose commercial tenants rather than gain them.
The passage above proceeds by
(Incorrect Answer) attacking the assumption that a change in the accessibility of Mennerton would be important to large businesses considering whether to locate there.
(Correct Answer) providing additional information that serves as a reason to doubt the validity of a conclusion made.
The landlords have concluded that, because the high speed rail line will make Mennerton more accessible, more businesses will want to locate there.
One way to weaken the support for the conclusion would be, as the incorrect answer says, attacking the assumption that a change in the accessibility of Mennerton would be important to large businesses considering whether to locate there.
The correct answer, on the other hand, describes exactly how the argument proceeds.
Here’s an example of this type of answer choice in the context of a Two-Part Method of Reasoning Question:
School Principal: Recently we have discovered that there is lead in the surface of the main schoolyard. Clearly, this lead is to blame for the lower than average reading scores of students at our school.
Reading Specialist: While lead can impact cognitive ability, it seems unlikely that the lead in that schoolyard is to blame for the low reading scores, as even students who haven’t ever used that schoolyard are scoring lower than average in reading.
The reading specialist indicates that there is reason to doubt the validity of the principal’s conclusion by doing which of the following?
(Incorrect Answer) Bringing up another factor that could be the cause of the effect that the principal mentions.
(Correct Answer) Showing that the effect that the principal mentions is still present even when the cause is not.
In this example, the principal arrives at a cause-and-effect conclusion, that the lead in the schoolyard is the reason for the students’ scoring lower than average in reading.
The reading specialist could have taken various paths in indicating that there is reason to doubt the validity of the principal’s conclusion.
The method that the reading specialist used is correctly described in the correct answer.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Method of Reasoning questions, don’t fall for incorrect answers that describe methods that the arguments could have used but didn’t.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 9 Editorial: The red-legged beetle burrows its way into hardwood trees, damaging the wood in such a way as to reduce its value should it be harvested. The editorialist’s argument proceeds by 1. [ ] (A) undermining the case for the use of a potential solution by refuting the claim that the red-legged beetles are present in the state. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) offering reason to believe that the red-legged beetle will not cause any damage in the editorialist’s state. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) pointing out that a particular course of action may be unnecessary since the conditions that would call for it are not present. 4. [ ] (D) contending that the destruction caused by insecticides would be worse than the damage to trees caused by beetles. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) arguing that, if a particular course of action is not appropriate in all cases, it is not appropriate in this particular case. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: easy The editorialist begins by providing background information about the beetle and indicating that some forestry officials wish to use insecticides because the beetle has no natural predators. The editorialist then states that the use of insecticides is somewhat destructive and, furthermore, may not be needed, since virtually no hardwood is harvested in that state.
The editorialist could have responded to the forestry officials’ assertion by questioning whether the beetles are present in the state, whether the beetles would cause damage, or whether the use of insecticides is worth its drawbacks. However, the editorialist followed none of these paths. So, we have to be careful to avoid choosing a choice that says that the editorialist took one of these paths that he could have taken but didn’t.
(A) undermining the case for the use of a potential solution by refuting the claim that the red-legged beetles are present in the state. While the editorialist could have taken the path of making the case that the use of insecticides may be unwarranted by questioning whether the beetles are present in the state, he didn’t.
(B) offering reason to believe that the red-legged beetle will not cause any damage in the editorialist’s state. While the editorialist could have taken the path of making the case that the use of insecticides may be unwarranted by offering a reason to believe that the beetles will cause no damage in the state, he didn’t.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) pointing out that a particular course of action may be unnecessary since the conditions that would call for it are not present. This answer describes how the editorialist responds. He points out that a “particular course of action” (the use of insecticides) “may be unnecessary” (may be unwarranted) since “the conditions that would call for it” (harvesting of hardwood trees) “are not present” in the state.
(D) contending that the destruction caused by insecticides would be worse than the damage to trees caused by beetles. While the editorialist could have taken the path of making the case that the use of insecticides may be unwarranted by contending that the destruction caused by insecticides would be worse than the damage to trees caused by beetles, he didn’t.
(E) arguing that, if a particular course of action is not appropriate in all cases, it is not appropriate in this particular case. While the editorialist could have taken the path of making the case that the use of insecticides may be unwarranted by claiming that, if a particular course of action is not appropriate in all cases, it is not appropriate in this particular case, he didn’t.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now discuss one more type of incorrect answer – an answer choice that describes a method that resembles what the argument does but is not what the argument does.
summary:
Lesson Content Another tricky type of incorrect answer that you could see in a Method of Reasoning question is one that describes a method that the argument could use but doesn’t use.
Consider the following example:
Commercial landlords in Mennerton are excited about the completion of the new high speed rail line between Mennerton and Boselton, because they believe that, since the new line will make Mennerton more easily accessible, large businesses that would have located in Boselton will now locate instead in Mennerton, where rents are lower.
title: 12.4.3 Answers that Describe Methods that Arguments Could Use but Don’t Use

contents:
Lesson Content Perhaps the trickiest incorrect answers in Method of Reasoning questions are the ones that describe methods that somehow resemble methods used in the arguments although the methods described are not actually the methods used in the arguments.
💡MUST KNOW:
Perhaps the trickiest incorrect answers in Method of Reasoning questions are the ones that describe methods that somehow resemble methods used in the arguments although the methods described are not actually the methods used in the arguments.
For example, the method that is actually used in an argument could be to bring up additional evidence to show that implementing a plan is unlikely to have the desired effect.
Let’s consider such a choice in context:
