Reintroducing wolves to the Yellowstone area of the United States resulted in a restoration of the area’s ecosystem by restoring key, previously longstanding relationships among various animal and plant species. Some ecologists have theorized that similar results could be achieved by reintroducing dingoes, which are wild dogs somewhat similar to wolves, to areas of Australia from which they have been missing. However, the types of longstanding relationships between species that have existed in the Yellowstone area are not as common in Australia. Therefore, reintroducing dingoes would not necessarily have effects similar to those achieved via reintroducing wolves.
In making the given argument, the author proceeds by
(Incorrect Choice) demonstrating the effect of a factor by using a comparison of situations that include and do not include that factor.
(Correct Answer) bringing up evidence that indicates that a comparison upon which a theory is based may not take into account a key difference.
Notice the resemblance between the incorrect choice and the correct answer.
However, the exact truth is that the author does not demonstrate the effect of any factor.
The correct answer describes exactly how the argument proceeds.
To choose a choice like the correct answer rather than one like the incorrect choice, you have to be careful not to go for a choice that sounds like what is going on in the argument but doesn’t really match.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Method of Reasoning questions, don’t fall for incorrect answers that are worded to sound much like what the arguments do but don’t actually describe the methods used by the arguments.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 10 University President: If we loan Dr. Admissions Official: Dr. The admissions official responds to university president’s argument in which of the following ways? 1. [ ] (A) She agrees with the president on the extent of the benefits that would result from engaging in the proposed deal but brings up potential drawbacks. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) She introduces considerations that serve to call into question the validity of the president’s conclusion. 3. [ ] (C) She disagrees on principle with engaging in the proposed deal, as students who have already enrolled in the school expect to work with Dr. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) She challenges the judgment that the benefits of engaging in the deal are outweighed by the negative effects of engaging in the deal. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) She disagrees with the president’s conclusion that master classes taught by famous alumni of the New Actors Group would attract students to the school. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: medium The university president’s conclusion is that giving away the drama department’s star professor for a year will actually increase enrollment in the drama department, because the organization to which they are lending the professor will send famous alumni to give master classes.
The admissions official counters the university president’s argument by introducing the consideration that the president’s plan will eliminate one of the major reasons that students enroll in the drama department – Dr. Spencer, that is – and the consideration that nine master classes may not be sufficiently attractive to offset any decrease in the attractiveness of the school that would result from Dr. Spencer’s absence.
To correctly answer this question, we have to be careful to choose a choice that accurately describes what the admissions official does and to avoid choosing a choice that seems to describe what she does but doesn’t really do so.
(A) She agrees with the president on the extent of the benefits that would result from engaging in the proposed deal but brings up potential drawbacks. This choice could be tempting because it to a degree resembles what the admissions official does. It seems reasonable to believe that she at least agrees that students would be attracted by the master classes, and she does bring up considerations that could conceivably be described as drawbacks. However, she does not actually agree with the president on the extent of the benefits that would result from engaging in the proposed deal. The president believes that trading the professor for the once a month master classes would cause enrollment to increase, while the admissions official is not so sure that it would.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) She introduces considerations that serve to call into question the validity of the president’s conclusion. As discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the admissions official counters the university president’s argument by introducing the consideration that the president’s plan will eliminate one of the major reasons that students enroll in the drama department – Dr. Spencer, that is – and the consideration that nine master classes may not be sufficiently attractive to offset any decrease in the attractiveness of the school that would result from Dr. Spencer’s absence.
If more students fail to enroll because of Dr. Spencer’s absence than do enroll because of the master classes, there will be a net decrease. Thus, given what the admissions official says, the president’s conclusion could be invalid. All the same, whether the president’s conclusion is actually invalid is irrelevant; we must be concerned with only how the admissions official’s argument addresses the president’s argument structurally.
(C) She disagrees on principle with engaging in the proposed deal, as students who have already enrolled in the school expect to work with Dr. Spencer. While bringing up the fact that students may be expecting to work with Dr. Spencer could be a valid way undermine the case for engaging in the deal, bringing up that fact is not what the admissions official does.
(D) She challenges the judgment that the benefits of engaging in the deal are outweighed by the negative effects of engaging in the deal. What this choice says certainly sounds like what the Admissions Official does: she challenges a judgment about benefits, drawbacks, and the degree to which one outweighs the other. So, you can see how, in the heat of taking the GMAT, a test-taker might either choose this answer or stare at it wondering whether it is correct. However, there’s a major problem with this choice: what this choice describes is basically the opposite of what the Admissions Official does. She doesn’t challenge the judgment that the benefits of engaging in the deal are outweighed by the negative effects of engaging in the deal. She challenges the judgment that the costs involved are outweighed by the benefits.
(E) She disagrees with the president’s conclusion that master classes taught by famous alumni of the New Actors Group would attract students to the school. This choice could be tempting, because it sounds somewhat like what the admissions official does. However, while she does disagree that there would be a net gain in the attractiveness of the school, she doesn’t disagree that the master classes themselves would be attractive.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss another type of Method of Reasoning Question, Boldface Method of Reasoning Questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Perhaps the trickiest incorrect answers in Method of Reasoning questions are the ones that describe methods that somehow resemble methods used in the arguments although the methods described are not actually the methods used in the arguments.
💡MUST KNOW:
Perhaps the trickiest incorrect answers in Method of Reasoning questions are the ones that describe methods that somehow resemble methods used in the arguments although the methods described are not actually the methods used in the arguments.
title: 12.4.4 An Answer Choice That Describes a Method That Resembles What the Argument Does but Is Not What the Argument Does

contents:
Lesson Content Some Method of Reasoning Questions present us with stimuli that include one or two sections that are in boldface type.
💡MUST KNOW:
Some Method of Reasoning Questions present us with stimuli that include one or two sections that are in boldface type. In answering such a question, our job is to identify the role that any section in boldfaced type plays in the argument.
Thus, in Boldface Method of Reasoning questions, the correct answer is the one that accurately describes the role, or roles, of any sections in boldface.
💡MUST KNOW:
In Boldface Method of Reasoning questions, when there are two boldfaced sections, each of the five answer choices provides a description of the role played by the first boldfaced section followed by a description of the role played by the second boldfaced section.
As in answering any Method of Reasoning question, in answering a Boldface question, we are far less concerned with the merits of the argument than we are with the structure of the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
As in answering any Method of Reasoning question, in answering a Boldface question, we are far less concerned with the merits of the argument than we are with the structure of the argument. In other words, in answering these questions, our goal is not to strengthen, weaken, or evaluate the argument. Instead, our goal is to assess how the argument is structured and determine what roles the bolded sections play in the argument.
Consider the following example:
The use of body cameras by police was expected to result in a reduction in inappropriate use of force by police officers. However, as the use of body cameras has increased, the annual number of citizen complaints about use of force by police officers has remained the same. Clearly, the presence of body cameras does not affect police behavior.
The portion in boldface plays which of the following roles in the argument above?
(A) It serves as evidence supporting the argument’s main conclusion.
We can analyze the flow of this argument and see that it begins by mentioning an expectation, then provides information on what has actually occurred, and, finally, arrives at a conclusion based on that information.
Let’s consider another example:
People have perceived the markings on an ancient Mayan calendar as indicating that the Maya had predicted that the world would end in December of 2012. This perception is likely incorrect. For the Maya of the time of the creation of the calendar, time was considered infinite. Further, other Mayan inscriptions indicate that the ancient Mayas considered a date 4000 years after December 2012 a significant date in the future of the world.
In the argument given, the two boldfaced portions play which of the following roles?
(A) The first is evidence supporting the author’s main conclusion; the second is that main conclusion.
(B) The first is the author’s main conclusion; the second provides support for that conclusion.
Now, we’re being asked to determine what roles two bolded portions play in an argument.
Since the first portion in boldface precedes the second, we might be tempted to go with choice (A), since, in most of the arguments that we have seen so far in GMAT Critical Reasoning, the evidence has preceded the conclusions.
However, if we carefully read what the bolded portions say and consider them in the context of the argument as a whole, it becomes apparent that, actually, the first portion in boldface states the author’s main conclusion and the second portion in boldface provides support for that conclusion.
As we will discuss further, the conclusion’s being placed in a position other than at the end of the passage is typical of Boldface questions.
💡MUST KNOW:
The arguments in Boldface questions are usually structured in ways meant to make the roles of the statements in the passages non-obvious. So, correctly answering boldface questions requires careful reading.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 11 Many people experiencing chronic back pain opt to undergo surgery, hoping to solve the pain issues. In the passage above, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles? 1. [ ] (A) The first is a conclusion opposed by the argument’s main conclusion; the second supports the main conclusion stated in the passage. > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) The first is evidence in support of the main conclusion stated in the passage; the second is also used to support the main conclusion. 3. [ ] (C) The first is a consideration that supports the main conclusion stated in the passage; the second is that main conclusion. > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) The first is a consideration that conflicts with the author’s main conclusion; the second is evidence that the author uses to support the main conclusion. > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) The first is a consideration that weighs in favor of the author’s position; the second is a consideration that weighs against that position. > Correct Answer: (B) View Solution Question Level: easy Let’s go through this passage to see what’s going on in it.
It starts off by mentioning a choice that people commonly make, to undergo surgery to address back pain issues.
Then the author states a position on this choice: “In many cases this choice is misguided.”
Then, the next two sentences discuss issues with surgery for back pain.
The only statement that is supported by any other statements is the one that states the author’s position, which is supported by the last two sentences of the passage. Thus, that position is the main conclusion of the passage, and the two sentences that follow it are supporting evidence.
(A) The first is a conclusion opposed by the argument’s main conclusion; the second supports the main conclusion stated in the passage. While the position of the author is in opposition to many people’s choosing to undergo back surgery, the choice that the author opposes is not mentioned in the first portion in boldface. So, the first part of this answer choice is incorrect.
Since the second bolded portion does support the main conclusion, the second part of this answer choice is correct. However, since the first part of this answer choice is incorrect, this choice cannot be the correct answer to the question.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) The first is evidence in support of the main conclusion stated in the passage; the second is also used to support the main conclusion. Since, as discussed in the introduction of this explanation, both the first portion in boldface and the second portion in boldface provide support for the author’s main conclusion, this choice is correct.
(C) The first is a consideration that supports the main conclusion stated in the passage; the second is that main conclusion. The first part of this answer choice is correct, as the first portion in boldface does support the main conclusion stated in the passage. However, while the second boldface portion does appear at the end of the passage, it is not the conclusion of the argument. Thus, the second part of this choice is incorrect, and, therefore, this choice is not the correct answer.
(D) The first is a consideration that conflicts with the author’s main conclusion; the second is evidence that the author uses to support the main conclusion. The first portion in boldface does not conflict with the author’s main conclusion, but, rather, supports it. Therefore, even though the second part of this choice correctly describes the second bolded portion as evidence that supports the conclusion, this choice is not the correct answer.
(E) The first is a consideration that weighs in favor of the author’s position; the second is a consideration that weighs against that position. As this choice says, the first bolded portion is indeed a consideration that weights in favor of the author’s position. However, unlike what this choice says, the second bolded portion supports rather than weighs against the author’s position. Therefore, this choice is not the correct answer.
Correct Answer: (B) Let’s now discuss the question stems that appear in Boldface Method of Reasoning Questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Some Method of Reasoning Questions present us with stimuli that include one or two sections that are in boldface type.
💡MUST KNOW:
Some Method of Reasoning Questions present us with stimuli that include one or two sections that are in boldface type. In answering such a question, our job is to identify the role that any section in boldfaced type plays in the argument.
Thus, in Boldface Method of Reasoning questions, the correct answer is the one that accurately describes the role, or roles, of any sections in boldface.
title: 12.5 Boldface Method of Reasoning Questions

contents:
Lesson Content The question stems that appear in Boldface questions generally ask us to determine which answers accurately describe the bolded sections of the passages.
In the argument given, the two boldfaced portions play which of the following roles?
In the argument above, the portion in boldface plays which of the following roles?
In the ecologist’s reasoning, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?
Which of the following, if true, would serve the same function in the argument as the statement in boldface?
💡MUST KNOW:
Be ready to identify what you have to do when you see a question stem referring to sections of a passage that are in boldface type.
Let’s now discuss what is probably the most important move you can make in handling Boldface questions, finding conclusions.
summary:
Lesson Content The question stems that appear in Boldface questions generally ask us to determine which answers accurately describe the bolded sections of the passages.
In the argument given, the two boldfaced portions play which of the following roles?
In the argument above, the portion in boldface plays which of the following roles?
In the ecologist’s reasoning, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?
Which of the following, if true, would serve the same function in the argument as the statement in boldface?
title: 12.5.1 Boldface Method of Reasoning Question Stems

contents:
Lesson Content The stimuli of Boldface questions can be hodgepodges of background information, premises, conclusions, and information of other types, and often it’s not obvious what roles the various parts of the passages play.
So, what’s the key to determining what roles the statements in a Boldface stimulus play? The key is finding any conclusions that appear in the passage, and in particular, the main conclusion.
The roles of all the statements in an argument tend to depend on how the statements are related to the conclusion of the argument.
Generally there is one main conclusion in a Boldface stimulus, and there are sometimes intermediate conclusions as well.
💡MUST KNOW:
A key move in the process of answering a Boldface question is locating the main conclusion of the argument. By finding the main conclusion in the passage of a Boldface question, you set yourself up with a reference point for determining what roles the other parts play.
Consider the following example:
Ever since certain password recommendations were released in 2003, many people have decided that they should use passwords that are based on single words, except that digits and symbols have been substituted for some of the words’ letters. However, people would be better off using passwords that are simply long strings of random words. Not only can password cracking software be set up to look for symbols typically substituted for letters of words but also, the longer the password the more difficult it is to crack, and long strings of random words are easier to memorize than long strings made up of letters, digits, and symbols.
In the argument given, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?
(A) The first outlines a recommendation made by the author; the second is information that the author feels should be considered in following that recommendation.
(B) The first is a practice that the author proceeds to recommend against; the second is evidence that the author uses in the process of supporting that recommendation.
If the passage above were to appear in a Boldface question, the roles played by each of the bolded sections might not be immediately clear.
The main conclusion of the passage is not the clause found at the end of the passage, but is, rather, the following:
people would be better off using passwords that are simply long strings of random words.
We can tell that the above is the main conclusion by noticing the following.
The entire first sentence, “Ever since … substituted for some of the words’ letters,” neither serves as support for any conclusion nor is supported by any other statement made.
The entire last sentence, “Not only can password cracking software … long strings made up of letters, digits, and symbols,” serves as support for the second sentence, “However, people … long strings of random words.” So, the second sentence states the main conclusion of the passage.
To confirm that the second and third sentences work together in this way, let’s put them together and add the word “Therefore” before the conclusion:
Not only can password cracking software be set up to look for symbols typically substituted for letters of words but also, the longer the password the more difficult it is to crack, and long strings of random words are easier to memorize than long strings made up of letters, digits, and symbols. Therefore, people would be better off using passwords that are simply long strings of random words.
Now that we clearly see what the main conclusion is, we can readily see the following:
(B) The first is a practice that the author proceeds to recommend against; the second is evidence that the author uses in the process of supporting that recommendation.
In other words, the main conclusion of the argument is a recommendation against using the practice described in the first bolded portion, and the second bolded portion is part of the evidence used to support the main conclusion.
Now, to efficiently find the correct answer to the following question, locate the main conclusion of the argument made in the passage.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 12 In recent years, so many people have been opting to attend college that the number of college graduates now exceeds the number of employment positions that require college educations. In the argument given, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles? 1. [ ] (A) The first is evidence used to support an intermediate conclusion of the argument; the second is that intermediate conclusion. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) The first is evidence used to support a conclusion opposed by the argument; the second is an intermediate conclusion drawn to support the argument’s main conclusion. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) The first is a judgment advanced in support of a position that the argument opposes; the second is the argument’s conclusion. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) The first is a position that the argument opposes; the second is evidence used to support the argument’s conclusion. 5. [ ] (E) The first is a conclusion drawn to support the main conclusion of the argument; the second is that main conclusion. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: medium To correctly answer this question, let’s start off by finding the main conclusion of the argument in the passage.
There are four main statements made in the passage:
In recent years, so many people have been opting to attend college that the number of college graduates now exceeds the number of employment positions that require college educations.
Some people, therefore, have made the case that much of the large investment that has been made in college education will not earn returns in terms of increased productivity.
This assessment is incorrect
in many positions that do not require college education, people who have knowledge gained through college education are generally more productive than are those without college education in the same positions.
The first and last of the statements are facts. The author does not conclude that the number of college graduates exceeds the number of employment positions that require college educations or that people who have knowledge gained through college education are more productive. Rather, the author presents these pieces of information as facts.
So, we are left with two more statements.
The second statement, the one about the case that “some people” have made, states a conclusion, but that conclusion is not the author’s conclusion; it’s the conclusion at which “some people” have arrived.
So, we are left with “This assessment is incorrect,” which is the main conclusion of the argument, as we can tell by noticing how it is supported by the last statement in the passage and noticing that it is the focus of all the information presented.
Now that we have located the main conclusion, choosing the correct answer will be rather straightforward.
(A) The first is evidence used to support an intermediate conclusion of the argument; the second is that intermediate conclusion. Since there is no intermediate conclusion, the first boldfaced portion clearly does not support an intermediate conclusion. As discussed, the second boldfaced portion is a fact that supports the main conclusion, and it is not, itself, a conclusion.
(B) The first is evidence used to support a conclusion opposed by the argument; the second is an intermediate conclusion drawn to support the argument’s main conclusion. This choice is a little more tempting, because the first boldfaced portion does oppose the main conclusion of the argument, and the second boldfaced portion does support the main conclusion of the argument. However, since the first boldfaced portion does not support anything but rather, on its own, opposes the main conclusion, and, since the second boldfaced portion is stated as fact rather than supported as an intermediate conclusion, this choice does not accurately describe the two bolded sections.
(C) The first is a judgment advanced in support of a position that the argument opposes; the second is the argument’s conclusion. The first boldfaced portion is itself a judgment, rather than support for such a judgment, and, as we discussed in our analysis in the introduction to this explanation, the second boldfaced portion is a statement of fact, rather than the argument’s conclusion.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) The first is a position that the argument opposes; the second is evidence used to support the argument’s conclusion. The first boldfaced portion is clearly a position that the argument opposes, since, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the main conclusion says of the first boldfaced portion, “This assessment is incorrect.” The second boldfaced portion is indeed a statement of fact that supports that main conclusion.
(E) The first is a conclusion drawn to support the main conclusion of the argument; the second is that main conclusion. The first boldfaced portion does not support the main conclusion, but rather, is a position to which the conclusion is in opposition. Also, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the second boldfaced portion is not the main conclusion of the argument.
Correct Answer: (D) Let’s now discuss how the answer options to Boldface questions provide us with important clues that we can use to our advantage.
summary:
Lesson Content The stimuli of Boldface questions can be hodgepodges of background information, premises, conclusions, and information of other types, and often it’s not obvious what roles the various parts of the passages play.
So, what’s the key to determining what roles the statements in a Boldface stimulus play? The key is finding any conclusions that appear in the passage, and in particular, the main conclusion.
The roles of all the statements in an argument tend to depend on how the statements are related to the conclusion of the argument.
title: 12.5.2 Identifying Conclusions in order to Efficiently and Correctly Answer Boldface Questions

contents:
Lesson Content As you now know, each of the five answer choices for a Boldface Method of Reasoning question either provides a description of the role played by one bolded section or provides a description of the role of the first of two bolded sections followed by a description of the role played by the second bolded section.
The good news is that these answer choices provide us with clear ways to eliminate incorrect answers.
Consider the following Boldface Method of Reasoning question answer choice:
(A) The first provides indisputable facts in support of a position; the second presents a consideration weighing against taking such a course of action.
Notice that the answer choice describes, not necessarily correctly, the role that the first bolded sentence plays in the argument and then describes the role that the second bolded sentence plays in the argument.
Now – in the stimulus, it is either completely true that the first bolded sentence “provides indisputable facts in support of a position” and the second bolded sentence “presents a consideration weighting against taking such a course of action,” or it is not true! There is no middle ground.
When we return to the stimulus, we ask “Does the first bolded statement provide indisputable facts in support of a position?” If, upon returning to the stimulus, we find that it is not true that the first bolded sentence provides indisputable facts in support of a position, then that answer choice cannot be correct.
💡MUST KNOW:
In answering Boldface Method of Reasoning questions, carefully compare the answer choices with the bolded sections of the passages to eliminate choices that cannot be correct.
Consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 13 While the appendix was long considered a useless part of the human body that somehow remained as evolution occurred, it is now apparent that the appendix is a useful piece of anatomy that plays a specific role. The previously accepted position on the appendix becomes suspect when one becomes aware of the discovery that at least thirty-two animal species have independently developed anatomy similar to the human appendix, as the likelihood that evolution made the same oversight in all of these cases is not great. Moreover, careful examination of the structure of the appendix reveals that it is well designed to play a unique role in the maintenance of gut flora. In the argument above, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles? 1. [x] (A) The first is the main conclusion of the argument; the second is a consideration in opposition to a conclusion that the main conclusion opposes. 2. [ ] (B) The first is a consideration in opposition to a conclusion that the main conclusion of the argument opposes; the second is the argument’s main conclusion. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) The first is an intermediate conclusion that is used to support the argument’s main conclusion; the second is the argument’s main conclusion. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) The first and second each provide evidence for the argument’s main conclusion. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) The first is a position taken by the author; the second is a fact that author concedes conflicts with the position taken. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium Once we have read the entire passage, our next move is to locate the main conclusion of argument, if there is one.
The main conclusion of this argument is:
Conclusion: it is now apparent that the appendix is a useful piece of anatomy that plays a specific role.
That the statement above is the main conclusion becomes apparent if we notice the following.
The first clause, “the appendix has long been considered … as evolution occurred,” is just background information that neither supports nor is supported by any other statement in the passage.
The last two sentences, “The previously accepted position … is not great,” and “Moreover, careful examination … gut flora,” serve as support for the statement made in the second sentence, “it is now apparent that the appendix is a useful piece of anatomy that plays a specific role.”
So, it’s clear that the statement made in the second sentence is the main conclusion of the argument. Now, let’s check the answer choices.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) The first is the main conclusion of the argument; the second is a consideration in opposition to a conclusion that the main conclusion opposes. As discussed above, the first is the main conclusion of the argument. The second is a consideration in opposition to the conclusion mentioned in the first statement made in the passage, the conclusion that the appendix is “a useless part of the human body that somehow remained as evolution occurred.”
(B) The first is a consideration in opposition to a conclusion that the main conclusion of the argument opposes; the second is the argument’s main conclusion. While the first is in opposition to a position that the main conclusion opposes, the first is not a consideration but, rather, the main conclusion itself. The second is not the conclusion, but rather, evidence that supports the main conclusion.
(C) The first is an intermediate conclusion that is used to support the argument’s main conclusion; the second is the argument’s main conclusion. The first is not an intermediate conclusion but, rather, the argument’s main conclusion. The second is not the main conclusion but rather a consideration that supports the main conclusion.
(D) The first and second each provide evidence for the argument’s main conclusion. The first half of this answer is incorrect. As discussed above, the first bolded section is not evidence supporting the main conclusion but, rather, the main conclusion of the argument. The second half of this choice is correct, as the second bolded portion does support the main conclusion.
(E) The first is a position taken by the author; the second is a fact that author concedes conflicts with the position taken. The first half of this choice is correct, as the first bolded portion is the main conclusion of the argument, and, as such, is a position taken by the author. The second half is not correct, as the second bolded portion supports rather than conflicts with the author’s position.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s continue.
summary:
Lesson Content As you now know, each of the five answer choices for a Boldface Method of Reasoning question either provides a description of the role played by one bolded section or provides a description of the role of the first of two bolded sections followed by a description of the role played by the second bolded section.
The good news is that these answer choices provide us with clear ways to eliminate incorrect answers.
title: 12.5.3 In Boldface Questions, the Answer Choices Provide Us with Powerful Clues

contents:
Lesson Content If the passages in Boldface questions were structured in the way in which the passages in typical Weaken the Argument questions are structured, with some background information, perhaps two premises, and a conclusion at the end, determining the roles of the sections of the arguments would be rather easy.
Here are some of the reasons why correctly identifying the roles of statements in and choosing the correct answers to Boldface questions can be tricky:
The conclusions are inconspicuous. The bolded portions can be worded and positioned to look as if they play roles other than the roles that they play. The passages often contain multiple conclusions, along with premises to support the conclusions. Incorrect answer choices often say things that are partly correct or closely related to what is correct. Let’s cover each of these.
summary:
Lesson Content If the passages in Boldface questions were structured in the way in which the passages in typical Weaken the Argument questions are structured, with some background information, perhaps two premises, and a conclusion at the end, determining the roles of the sections of the arguments would be rather easy.
Here are some of the reasons why correctly identifying the roles of statements in and choosing the correct answers to Boldface questions can be tricky:
title: 12.5.4 Common Characteristics of Boldface Questions that Make Choosing the Correct Answers Tricky

contents:
Lesson Content In most of the Critical Reasoning arguments that we have seen, the conclusions have appeared at or near to the ends of the passages, and the fact that they are conclusions has often been signaled by words such as “Therefore” or “Clearly.” This pattern does not hold for most arguments seen in Boldface questions.
💡MUST KNOW:
Often, in the arguments used in Boldface questions, main conclusions are not preceded by conclusion signaling words like “therefore,” and the conclusions are worded so as to be barely noticeable.
For instance, in an argument about saving coral reefs in a Strengthen question, the conclusion might be something like the following:
Therefore, actually, there is much that can be done to help coral reefs survive.
In an argument about the same topic in a Boldface question the conclusion could be one more like the following.
That perception is incorrect.
The above Boldface question conclusion is not preceded by a signaling word and barely says anything.
Often, another factor making the main conclusion in a Boldface question tricky to find will be the fact that the conclusion does not appear at the end of the argument.
💡MUST KNOW:
Another factor making the main conclusion in a Boldface question tricky to find will be the fact that the conclusion does not appear at the end of the argument.
Consider the following example:
There is a common perception that, because higher than normal water temperatures can kill off coral reefs, and because climate change related emissions are causing increases in ocean water temperatures, other than reducing climate change causing emissions, there is not much that can be done to save reefs. That perception is incorrect. Factors such as overfishing and pollution from farm fertilizer runoff result in much more damage to coral reefs than is being caused by high temperature water, and such factors can be addressed via local policy initiatives.
In the argument given, the portion in boldface plays which of the following roles?
(A) It states the argument’s main conclusion.
Notice how, in the example above, the conclusion, which is stated by the portion in boldface, is expressed in just a few words found in the middle of the passage, and is not preceded by a connecting word like “therefore.” Then, the next sentence is the support for the conclusion.
So, when you are looking for the main conclusion in the passage in a Boldface question, remember that it could be anywhere in the passage.
💡MUST KNOW:
Often, in the passages in Boldface questions, the conclusions are obscured by their wording and positioning.
Notice how placement and wording make the main conclusion inconspicuous in the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 14 Supplementing fossil fuels with ethanol made from corn has been touted as a way to reduce the negative environmental impacts of internal combustion engines. Of the following, which provides the most accurate description of the roles played by the portions of the passage in boldface? 1. [ ] (A) The first is the author’s main conclusion; the second is evidence in support of the main conclusion. > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) The first is a consideration in support of a conclusion that the author takes a position against; the second is evidence that the author uses to support his position. > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) The first states a conclusion that the author questions; the second presents proof that the conclusion that the author questions is irrefutable. > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) The first is a position that the author supports; the second is evidence that the author uses to support his main conclusion. > Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) The first is a conclusion that the author argues against; the second is a consideration that weighs in favor of a conclusion that the author opposes. View Solution Question Level: medium Let’s find the author’s main conclusion, if there is one. In this case, there is one, and it’s very short and hidden at the end of a sentence.
Conclusion: it is not.
Yes, those three words found at the end of the second sentence are the main conclusion, and what they mean in full is that the approach outlined in the first sentence of the passage is not sound. OK, now for the portions in boldface.
Now that we know what the argument’s main conclusion is, we can see that the first portion in boldface is a conclusion that the author argues against or opposes.
The second portion in boldface is supportive of the conclusion stated by the first boldfaced portion that the main conclusion opposes, and if we read the second boldfaced portion in context, we can see that it states a concession that the author makes in the process of opposing the conclusion stated by the first portion in boldface.
(A) The first is the author’s main conclusion; the second is evidence in support of the main conclusion. This choice could be tempting, as the second bolded portion does support the first. However, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the first bolded portion is not the main conclusion.
(B) The first is a consideration in support of a conclusion that the author takes a position against; the second is evidence that the author uses to support his position. This first portion in boldface is not support for the conclusion that the author takes a position against but, rather, that conclusion itself. The second is not support for the author’s position but, rather, support for the conclusion stated by the first boldfaced portion that the author opposes.
(C) The first states a conclusion that the author questions; the second presents proof that the conclusion that the author questions is irrefutable. This choice seems almost correct, but notice there are clear differences between what it says and what actually goes on in the passage.
The first part of this choice is a little off, because the author doesn’t just, as this choice says, question the conclusion presented by the first bolded portion. The author argues against it.
The second part of this choice is also off, because, while the second bolded portion does provide support for the conclusion that the author argues against, it does not provide proof that that conclusion is irrefutable.
(D) The first is a position that the author supports; the second is evidence that the author uses to support his main conclusion. The first bolded portion does not state a position that the author supports. The first bolded portion states a conclusion that the author argues against. Then, the second bolded portion is not evidence that the author uses to support the main conclusion. Rather, it supports the conclusion stated in the first boldfaced portion, which conclusion the author argues against.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) The first is a conclusion that the author argues against; the second is a consideration that weighs in favor of a conclusion that the author opposes. Both parts of this choice describe exactly what goes on in the passage, which is discussed further in the introduction to this explanation.
Correct Answer: (E) Let’s now consider how characteristics of the boldface portions can add to the trickiness of Boldface questions.
summary:
Lesson Content In most of the Critical Reasoning arguments that we have seen, the conclusions have appeared at or near to the ends of the passages, and the fact that they are conclusions has often been signaled by words such as “Therefore” or “Clearly.” This pattern does not hold for most arguments seen in Boldface questions.
💡MUST KNOW:
Often, in the arguments used in Boldface questions, main conclusions are not preceded by conclusion signaling words like “therefore,” and the conclusions are worded so as to be barely noticeable.
title: 12.5.5 The conclusions are inconspicuous.

contents:
Lesson Content Another way in which the creators of Boldface questions trick test-takers by taking advantage of common perceptions of how arguments work is by making the boldfaced portions look as if they play roles other than the roles that they play.
💡MUST KNOW:
In the passages in Boldface questions, the portions in boldface can be worded and positioned to appear to play roles other than the roles that they play.
One obvious way in which this can be done is by bolding the last sentence in the passage.
Consider the following example:
There is a common perception that, because higher than normal water temperatures can kill off coral reefs, and because climate change related emissions are causing increases in ocean water temperatures, other than reducing climate change causing emissions, there is not much that can be done to save reefs. That perception is incorrect. Factors such as overfishing and pollution from farm fertilizer runoff result in much more damage to coral reefs than is being caused by high temperature water, and such factors can be addressed via local policy initiatives.
In the argument given, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?
(Trap Choice) The first provides support for the main conclusion of the argument; the second is that main conclusion.
(Correct Answer) The first states a consideration in support of a conclusion that the argument opposes; the second provides support for the argument’s main conclusion.
While the portions in boldface play the roles described in the correct answer above, notice what would happen if we were to just put the two together without considering the rest of the passage.
because climate change related emissions are causing increases in ocean water temperatures, such factors can be addressed via local policy initiatives.
Now we have something that looks almost like an argument, one in which the two portions play the roles described in the trap choice: the first portion provides support for the second portion, and the second portion is the conclusion.
To avoid getting taken in by such a trap, remember that wording and placement of statements in boldface can be misleading.
Notice how the portions in boldface could be perceived as playing roles other than the roles that they play in the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 15 Since plant foods generally do not contain vitamin B12, the accepted wisdom is that, in order to maintain sufficient levels of vitamin B12, humans must either consume animal products, such as meat or dairy products, or take supplements. This view may be incorrect. The two sections in boldface play which of the following roles in the argument above? 1. [x] (A) The first is a conclusion that the author questions; the second is evidence that the author uses to support the author’s position. 2. [ ] (B) The first is a position that the author argues against; the second presents evidence that supports that position. > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) The first is a fact that the author uses to provide context; the second is an example presented by the author to illustrate the point made in the first. > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) The first and second are both used by the author to support the author’s main conclusion. > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) The first is a conclusion that is not the only conclusion present in the passage; the second is an example used to support the first. > Correct Answer: (A) View Solution Question Level: medium First, let’s find the main conclusion, if there is one. In this case, the main conclusion is:
Conclusion: This view may be incorrect.
Now, let’s consider the roles of the portions in boldface.
The first portion in boldface precedes the main conclusion and presents the “accepted wisdom” that the main conclusion calls a “view” and says may be incorrect.
The second portion is part of the evidence that the author uses to support the main conclusion, which is that the view that, in order to maintain sufficient levels of vitamin B12, humans must either consume animal products or take supplements may be incorrect.
This question is a little tricky though, as the fact that vegetarians experienced B12 deficiencies could be perceived as supporting the view that, in order to maintain sufficient levels of vitamin B12, humans must either consume animal products or take supplements. Only by carefully considering the second bolded portion in the context of the entire argument can we see that the fact about vegetarians is actually a key part of the support for questioning the view that, in order to maintain sufficient levels of vitamin B12, humans must either consume animal products or take supplements.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) The first is a conclusion that the author questions; the second is evidence that the author uses to support the author’s position. As discussed above, the first portion in boldface is a conclusion that the author questions, and the second portion in boldface is evidence that supports the author’s questioning of the conclusion stated by the first portion in boldface.
(B) The first is a position that the author argues against; the second presents evidence that supports that position. This first half of this choice is basically correct, as the first portion in boldface does present a conclusion that the author questions, and thus basically argues against. The second half of this choice is a trap, since, taken out of context, the second bolded portion could seem to support the first. However, the truth is that, rather than support the first bolded portion, the second bolded portion is part of the support for questioning the first bolded portion.
(C) The first is a fact that the author uses to provide context; the second is an example presented by the author to illustrate the point made in the first. This first is not just a fact that the author uses to provide context. It is the conclusion that the argument is set up to question. Furthermore, the second is not an example used to illustrate the point made in the first; it is part of the evidence supporting the case for questioning the first.
(D) The first and second are both used by the author to support the author’s main conclusion. This choice could be tempting, as, since the first bolded portion and the second bolded portion seem to be logically related, a test-taker could get the impression that they play similar roles, and that, thus, both support the author’s main conclusion. However, as discussed above, only the second bolded portion provides support for the author’s conclusion.
(E) The first is a conclusion that is not the only conclusion present in the passage; the second is an example used to support the first. This choice starts off correctly, as the first bolded portion is indeed a conclusion. However, it is not the only conclusion presented in the passage. Meanwhile, while the second portion in boldface might seem to support the first, the truth is that it is actually evidence that supports the case for questioning the conclusion stated in the first portion in boldface.
Correct Answer: (A) Let’s now consider how the content of the passages in Boldface questions can add to the challenges involved in arriving at the correct answers to Boldface questions.
summary:
Lesson Content Another way in which the creators of Boldface questions trick test-takers by taking advantage of common perceptions of how arguments work is by making the boldfaced portions look as if they play roles other than the roles that they play.
💡MUST KNOW:
In the passages in Boldface questions, the portions in boldface can be worded and positioned to appear to play roles other than the roles that they play.
title: 12.5.6 The bolded portions can be worded and positioned to look as if they play roles other than the roles that they play.

contents:
Lesson Content Another factor that can make sorting through the statements in a Boldface question and keeping everything straight challenging is the presence of multiple conclusions and the premises that support them.
Differentiating between premises and conclusions. Determining which conclusions are supported by the arguments and which ones the arguments oppose. Determining which premises support which conclusion. Consider the following example:
There is a common perception that, because higher than normal water temperatures can kill off coral reefs, and because climate change related emissions are causing increases in ocean water temperatures, other than reducing climate change causing emissions, there is not much that can be done to save reefs. That perception is incorrect. Factors such as overfishing and pollution from farm fertilizer runoff result in much more damage to coral reefs than is being caused by high temperature water, and such factors can be addressed via local policy initiatives.
In the argument given, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?
(Trap Choice) The first provides support for the main conclusion of the argument; the second also provides support for the main conclusion.
(Correct Answer) The first states a consideration in support of a conclusion that the argument opposes; the second provides support for the argument’s main conclusion.
In this example, the wording of the two portions in boldface looks similar, and the information that they provide seems to support one conclusion, the conclusion that coral reefs are in trouble.
Of course, the truth is that, as the correct answer says, the two portions in boldface support two different conclusions: a conclusion that the main argument opposes and the argument’s main conclusion.
💡MUST KNOW:
The arguments in Boldface questions can contain multiple conclusions and premises to support the conclusions.
Of course, the way to keep everything straight is to identify the main conclusion of the argument and work from that reference point to determine what roles the other parts play.
Consider the following example.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 16 A recent study showed a correlation between the quality of wine and the price of wine. In the passage above, the portions in boldface play which of the following roles? 1. [ ] (A) The first is a conclusion that is opposed by the main conclusion of the passage; the second is the main conclusion. > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) The first is a pattern that could be taken as support for a conclusion to which the main conclusion is in opposition; the second is the main conclusion. > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) The first could be taken as support for a conclusion that the main conclusion opposes; the second provides support for the main conclusion. 4. [ ] (D) The first is a specific case used to support a questionable assertion; the second is a more general case that follows from that assertion. > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) The first is an objection that has been raised against a position defended in the argument; the second is that position. > Correct Answer: (C) View Solution Question Level: hard The passage contains multiple conclusions and support for those conclusions. To get the question correct, let’s get them all straight. We’ll start with the main conclusion:
Main Conclusion: it would be wrong to conclude from the existence of this pattern that a winery will benefit financially from improving the quality of its wine.
The main conclusion contains within itself a conclusion that it opposes:
Conclusion Opposed by the Main Conclusion: a winery will increase its profitability by improving the quality of its wine.
The conclusion opposed by the main conclusion follows from the following conclusion about a pattern:
a winery’s improving the quality of its wine will enable that winery to increase the price that it receives per bottle
That conclusion is supported by the following:
A recent study showed a correlation between the quality of wine and the price of wine.
Finally, the main conclusion is supported by the last two sentences of the passage:
There are costs to improving wine quality, such as the extra expense of higher quality grapes, money spent on new technology, and salaries of top-notch winemakers, and, therefore, wineries that improve wine quality often see lower returns on investment than do wineries that produce wine of lower quality.
Now that we are clear about the functions of every part of the passage, let’s check the answer choices.
(A) The first is a conclusion that is opposed by the main conclusion of the passage; the second is the main conclusion. The first bolded portion is not a conclusion opposed by the main conclusion. As discussed above, it provides support for a conclusion opposed by the main conclusion. Similarly, the second portion in boldface is not the main conclusion of the passage. It is support for the main conclusion.
(B) The first is a pattern that could be taken as support for a conclusion to which the main conclusion is in opposition; the second is the main conclusion. The first half of this choice is correct, as the pattern mentioned in the first bolded portion could be taken as support for the conclusion opposed by the main conclusion of the passage. The second half is incorrect, however, as the second bolded portion is not the main conclusion but rather support for the main conclusion.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) The first could be taken as support for a conclusion that the main conclusion opposes; the second provides support for the main conclusion. This choice exactly matches the analysis discussed in the introduction to this explanation.
(D) The first is a specific case used to support a questionable assertion; the second is a more general case that follows from that assertion. The first half of this choice is close to being correct, as the first bolded portion does provide some support for a conclusion to which the argument is in opposition, which conclusion could therefore be considered “questionable.” However, the second bolded portion clearly does not follow from the assertion that the argument opposes, which assertion is that a winery will increase its profitability by improving the quality of its wine.
(E) The first is an objection that has been raised against a position defended in the argument; the second is that position. This first half of this choice is almost correct in a convoluted way, as the first bolded portion does support a conclusion that conflicts with the conclusion supported by the argument. However, the first bolded portion is not an objection, in other words a response, to the position that the argument defends. It is simply information that happens to support a different conclusion. The second part of this choice is clearly incorrect, as the second bolded portion is stated as fact and, thus, is not a position defended by the argument.
Correct Answer: (C) Let’s now consider how the incorrect answer choices in Boldface questions can be constructed to make them tempting.
summary:
Lesson Content Another factor that can make sorting through the statements in a Boldface question and keeping everything straight challenging is the presence of multiple conclusions and the premises that support them.
Differentiating between premises and conclusions. Determining which conclusions are supported by the arguments and which ones the arguments oppose. Determining which premises support which conclusion. Consider the following example:
There is a common perception that, because higher than normal water temperatures can kill off coral reefs, and because climate change related emissions are causing increases in ocean water temperatures, other than reducing climate change causing emissions, there is not much that can be done to save reefs.
title: 12.5.7 The passages often contain multiple conclusions, along with premises to support the conclusions.

contents:
Lesson Content As we just discussed, sorting out the roles of the statements in Boldface question passages can be complicated by the fact that there can be multiple conclusions with premises that support each of them.
For instance, if a portion in boldface is a premise that supports a conclusion opposed by the main conclusion of the argument, an answer choice that says that that boldfaced portion is itself a conclusion opposed by the conclusion of the argument could be a tempting trap.
Consider the following example:
There is a common perception that, because higher than normal water temperatures can kill off coral reefs,and because climate change related emissions are causing increases in ocean water temperatures, other than reducing climate change causing emissions, there is not much that can be done to save reefs. That perception is incorrect. Factors such as overfishing and pollution from farm fertilizer runoff result in much more damage to coral reefs than is being caused by high temperature water, and such factors can be addressed via local policy initiatives.
In the argument given, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?
(Trap Answer) The first is a common perception with which the main conclusion of the argument is in disagreement; the second is the argument’s main conclusion.
(Correct Answer) The first states a consideration in support of a conclusion that the argument opposes; the second is the argument’s main conclusion.
In the above example, it would be easy to fall for the trap answer’s description of the first portion in boldface, because what the trap answer says is closely related to what is correct.
The correct answer, on the other hand, correctly describes the roles of the two portions in boldface.
💡MUST KNOW:
Boldface questions can include incorrect answers that are tempting because they say things that are partly correct or are somehow closely related to what is correct.
To avoid choosing a trap answer that says something partly correct or closely related to what is correct, be careful to differentiate between parts of the arguments, such as conclusions and the premises that support those conclusions.
Consider the following example:
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Example 17 The city of Mantrose currently pays for the maintenance of its public rail system by charging tolls to use the circular bypass road that surrounds the city. In the argument given, the two boldfaced portions play which of the following roles? 1. [ ] (A) The first presents a plan that the argument seeks to defend against a particular criticism; the second is that criticism. > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) The first presents a plan whose consequences are at issue in the argument; the second is the argument’s conclusion about those consequences. > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) The first presents a reason that a plan is being opposed; the second is evidence undermining the case for that opposition. > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) The first outlines a plan to which there is opposition; the second presents a reason to discount the case for that opposition. 5. [ ] (E) The first describes certain consequences of a plan to which there is opposition; the second describes other consequences of the plan that will offset the consequences described in the first. > Correct Answer: (D) View Solution Question Level: easy First let’s identify the main conclusion of the argument, if there is one. In this case, there is one.
Conclusion: The proposed change is not likely to affect maintenance of Mantrose’s public rail system.
Now that we have a reference point, let’s identify the roles of the portions in boldface.
The first portion is clearly a statement of fact, because it provides clear and verifiable information. More specifically, the first presents a plan that the argument seeks to defend.
The prediction made in the second statement is part of the support for the main conclusion. The support for the main conclusion is developed from that prediction.
To choose the correct answer, we have to keep our facts straight, and not be fooled by choices that say things that are partially correct or are somehow closely related to what is correct.
(A) The first presents a plan that the argument seeks to defend against a particular criticism; the second is that criticism. Notice that the first part of answer (A) is correct; the first statement does present a plan that the argument seeks to defend. So far, so good. But, we must keep focused and analyze the second part of answer (A). Is the second bolded statement criticism? No, it is not.
(B) The first presents a plan whose consequences are at issue in the argument; the second is the argument’s conclusion about those consequences. This choice is somewhat tempting, for two reasons: the first half is correct, and the second half almost sounds correct. While the second bolded portion is not, as the second half of the choice says, the argument’s conclusion, the second bolded portion does support the conclusion. So, it would be pretty easy to lose track and choose this choice, which isn’t quite correct but reads almost as if it were correct.
(C) The first presents a reason that a plan is being opposed; the second is evidence undermining the case for that opposition. This is another choice that could trick a test-taker. The second half of the choice does correctly describe the second bolded portion. Meanwhile, while the first bolded portion does not, as this choice says, present a reason why a plan is being opposed, it does present the plan that is being opposed. So, if you weren’t paying close attention, this partially correct choice could be tempting.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) The first outlines a plan to which there is opposition; the second presents a reason to discount the case for that opposition. As this choice says, the first portion in boldface is the plan to which there is opposition, and the second presents a reason to discount the case for that opposition.
(E) The first describes certain consequences of a plan to which there is opposition; the second describes other consequences of the plan that will offset the consequences described in the first. A test-taker might get tricked by this choice for the following reasons. While the first portion does not, as this choice says, describe the consequences of the plan, it does describe the plan. In addition, while the second bolded portion does not, as this choice says, describe offsetting consequences of the plan, it does describe an offsetting factor. So, this choice sounds somewhat correct.
Correct Answer: (D)
summary:
Lesson Content As we just discussed, sorting out the roles of the statements in Boldface question passages can be complicated by the fact that there can be multiple conclusions with premises that support each of them.
For instance, if a portion in boldface is a premise that supports a conclusion opposed by the main conclusion of the argument, an answer choice that says that that boldfaced portion is itself a conclusion opposed by the conclusion of the argument could be a tempting trap.
title: 12.5.8 Incorrect answer choices often say things that are partly correct or closely related to what is correct.

contents:
A modifier describes/provides extra information about something else in the sentence. Incorrectly used modifiers lead to ambiguity or illogical meaning.
💡 Many modifiers answer the questions who, what, when, where, or why Adjectives & Adverbs These are one-word modifiers.
Adjective → ONLY Noun/Pronoun
Adverb → Almost anything EXCEPT Noun/Pronoun
Verbs, Adjectives Prepositional Phrases Entire Clauses Many adverbs are formed by adding “-ly” to the adjective
💡 Often Adjective ↔ Adverb use will be interchanged as the sentence error. These errors are often grammatically correct but can be found out on logical basis.
Eg: Max's grandmother is his supposed Irish ancestor. ☑️ | Max's grandmother is his supposedly Irish ancestor. ✅✅ Max’s grandmother is not actually his ancestor. (Doesn’t make sense)
Max’s grandmother is not actually Irish (plausible).
Some Common Adjective ↔ Adverb Words corresponding frequent independent rare recent seeming separate significant supposed usual Noun Modifiers Apart from adjectives, there are some phrases that act as noun modifiers too.
Type Example Meaning Preposition Sue placed the CAT on the counch. Where if the cat? (On the couch) Past Participle The CAT owned by Sue is playful. Whose cat? (the one owned by Sue) Present Participle without Commas The CAT sleeping on the rug belongs to Sue. Which cat? (the one sleeping on the rug) 💡 Noun modifiers answer: WHO, WHAT, WHICH, OR WHERE Essential Vs. Non-Essential Modifiers Removing essential modifiers from a sentence may compromise its meaning. The meaning is still retained on removing non-essential modifiers. “comma-which” is a non-essential modifier. Position of Noun Modifiers The reader must know exactly which noun is the modifier modifying.
🚨 Noun Modifiers & Nouns should be very close to each other. Eg:
❌ A hard worker and loyal team player, the new project was managed by Sue.
✅ A hard worker and loyal team player, Sue managed the new project.
💡 In case of multiple modifiers, the essential modifier is kept closer to the noun.
☑️ Jim biked along an old dirt road to get to his house, which cut through the woods. ✅✅ To get to his house, Jim biked along an old dirt road, which cut through the woods.
Possessive Nouns ≠ Nouns 💡 Possessive Nouns can never be the subject of a modifier. Possessive Nouns = Adjectives
Eg: ❌ Happy about his raise, Bill’s celebration included taking his friends out to dinner. ✅ Happy about his raise, Bill celebrated by taking his friends to dinner. In the above sentence the modifier “happy about his raise” should be referring to Bill. But it seems like it’s modifying “Bill’s Celebration”.
Noun Modifier Markers Relative Pronouns Which That Who Whose Whom Where When Except for that, these words almost always signal noun modifiers.
Who/Whom: Only people. That/Whose: People or Things. The town whose water supply was contaminated The [son of a gun] that stole my lunch deserves death. Which/Whom: Prepositions. “The canal through which water flows” & “the senator for whom we worked” 🚨 Which ≠ “people” (Only use it for prepositions) Where: Noun Place. 🚨 “Where” ≠ metaphorical place: condition, situation, case, circumstances, or arrangement
Use “Which” in these situations.
❌ We had an arrangement WHERE he cooked and I cleaned. ✅ We had an arrangement IN WHICH he cooked and I cleaned.
When/in which: Noun event/time. Prepositions and Participles Can be noun/adverbial modifiers.
💡A preposition after a noun modifies the noun. Participles Participle Forms
Verb Present Participle (-ing) Past Participle to play playing played to manage managing managed to begin beginning begun -ing word functioning as part of the verb form will have another verb immediately before it. -ing words that are NOT verbs & not separated from the rest of the sentence by a comma will either be a noun/noun modifier. Past participles, or - ed words, can be verb by themselves. But CANNOT function as a noun. Eg: Exhausted from her job, she bought a lottery ticket with hopes of winning big. (exhausted…job = adverbial modifier) Adverbial Modifiers These modify anything that isn’t just a plain noun. (Everything ≠ Noun)
💡 They answer questions like how, where, when, or why an action occurred. In general adverbial modifiers can be placed anywhere in the sentence and don’t have the positional bound like noun modifiers.
It is possible to place adverbial modifiers poorly, though.
❌ He walked and caught up with his sister more rapidly.
✅ He walked more rapidly and caught with his sister.
Sequence in Participle Modifiers Adverbial modifiers that use participles. So the the cause-effect relationship of these should be clearly visible.
✅ Slipping on the ice, she broke her ankle. ❌ Breaking her ankle, she slipped on the ice. ✅ She slipped on the ice, breaking her ankle.
Here Slipping on Ice→Breaking her ankle & not the other way round.
Subordinators These clauses modify the main clause to which they are attached.
Eg: Although the economy is strong, the RETAIL INDUSTRY IS STRUGGLING.
It is almost like a complete sentence but has a subordinator (although) at the beginning.
although before unless because that so that if yet after while since when 💡 The subordinator and the clause-subbordinator relationship should be consistent.
Although the economy is strong, the retail industry is doing well. ❌
Errors Multiple Connecting words
ALTHOUGH I need to relax, YET I have so many things to do! ❌
Connecting with the right meaning
If the sentence parts are an opposition we should expect contrasting connecting words between them.
She is not interested in playing sports, AND she likes watching them on TV. ❌
She is not interested in playing sports, BUT she likes watching them on TV. ✅
ALTHOUGH she is not interested in playing sports, she likes watching them on TV.✅
💡 Main Clause + (Not a Clause)
Citizens of many countries are expressing concern about the environmental damage cause by the widespread release of greenhouse gases may be impossible to reverse. ❌
Here there is no subject for the clause “may be impossible to reverse”. You may want to think that environmental damage is the subject. But its part of a prepositional phrase & can never be the subject of a clause.
So we use either remove the prepositional phrase:
Citizens of many countries are expressing concern THAT the environmental damage cause by the widespread release of greenhouse gases may be impossible to reverse
Or make the 2nd clause an additional clause:
Citizens of many countries are expressing concern about the environmental damage cause by the widespread release of greenhouse gases, DAMAGE THAT may be impossible to reverse.
🚨Which🚨 (Difficult to trap) WHICH always refers to the closest preceding noun.
Eg:
❌ Crime has recently decreased in our neighborhood, WHICH has led to a rise in property values.
But here it is being used to refer to an entire clause.
🚨 Use WHICH only to refer to nouns — never to refer to an entire clause. Corrections Turn the first thought into a noun phrase and make this phrase the subject of the verb, eliminating WHICH altogether:
The recent decrease in crime in our neighbourhood has led to a rise in property values.
Use an adverbial modifier to refer to the whole clause:
Crime has recently decreased in our neighbourhood, leading to a rise in property values.
Modifiers Vs. Core 2 long non-essential modifiers in a row.
Eg:
❌ George Carlin, both shocking and entertaining audiences across the nation, who also struggled publicly with drug abuse, influenced and inspired a generation of comedians.
We can place 1 modifier before & 1 after the noun: (Modifier ↔ Noun ↔ Modifier)
✅ Both shocking and entertaining audiences across the nation, George Carlin, who also struggled publicly with drug abuse, influenced and inspired a generation of comedians.
On the tougher questions, one modifier can be made part of the sentence:
✅ Both shocking and entertaining audiences across the nation, George Carlin influenced and inspired a generation of comedians yet struggled publicly with drug abuse.
💡 **LONG UNDERLINED PORTIONS** ⇒ **Sentence portions moving about/ Change complete roles** Quantity Countable Vs. Uncountable Things 💡 Try counting on the object to check if it’s Un/countable
1 Hat, 2 hat, 3 hat ✅
1 Patience, 2 Patience, 3 Patience ❌ Countable Modifiers MANY hats FEW stores FEWER children FEWEST shoes NUMEROUS books NUMBER of chairs Uncountable Modifiers MUCH patience LITTLE mechandise LESS money LEAST greed AMOUNT of furniture GREAT courage 💡 “MORE”, “MOST”, & “ALL“ work for both countable & uncountable. 🚨 LESS is being used too frequently for countable things. Instead FEWER must be used.
❌ There were less Numidian KINGS than Roman emperors. ✅ There were fewer Numidian KINGS than Roman emperors.
Money & Volume Dollars and gallons (unit nouns) by nature, are countable. But these nouns represent uncountable quantities like money and volume: one money, two moneys,… (?)
Comparative Vs. Superlative (Words Relating 2 Things Vs. > 2 Things) Comparative form of adjectives/adverbs (better, worse, more, less) for 2 things. Superlative form of adjectives/adverbs (best, worst, most, least) for > 2 things. 💡 2 Things ⇒ BETWEEN | >2 Things ⇒ AMONG
✅ I mediated a dispute BETWEEN Maya and Kalen. ✅ I mediated a dispute AMONG Maya, Logan, and Kalen.
The Word “Numbers” (COUNTABLE) Use Greater Than Vs. More than
✅ The rare Montauk beaked griffin is not extinct; its NUMBERS are now suspected to be much GREATER than before.
Increase/Decrease Vs. Greater/Less Increase/Decrease - Signal change of a thing over time. Greater/Less - Signals comparison between 2 things. Watch out for redundancies with increase/decrease:
❌ The price of silver FELL by a more than 35% DECREASE. ✅ The price of silver DECREASED by more than 35%. ✅ The price of silver FELL by more than 35%.
“THAT” in a sentence “That” is a modifier 🚨 “That”, “Which”, “Who”, & “Where” modify the immediately preceding noun. “That of” is used for comparison ❌ The debt-ratio of Greece is greater than Japan. (Greece’s Debt Ratio ↔ Japan) ✅ The debt-ratio of Greece is greater than THAT OF Japan. (Debt-Ratios of 2 countries compared)
Parallelism in “that” Care must be taken to use that in the parallel clauses.
❌ I believe that Santa Clause is real and the Knicks will win the NBA title.  ✅ I believe that Santa Clause is real and THAT the Knicks will win the NBA title. Reason Here in the 1st portion, the “Santa Clause is real” is part of the belief but that Knicks will be treated as an absolute truth. While in the 2nd sentence, the “that” creates a correct parallel and the Knicks winning is also part of the belief now. Exception of “that” modifier rule (Idiomatic Phrases) Although the general rule to “that” as a modifier holds, there are cases where it should not be blindly applied.
Eg: Here we can’t simply use “gun” as the subject of our modifier.
❌ The son of a gun THAT stole my lunch deserves to die.
✅ The son of a gun THAT stole my lunch deserves to die.
Practice-1 Each of the following sentences contains one or more underlined modifiers. For each of these modifiers, (a) identify the word or words, if any, that it modifies, and (b) indicate whether the modifier is correct. If the modifier is incorrect, suggest a way to correct the error.
Angola, which was ravaged by civil war for many years after it gained independence from Portugal, which is now one of Africa’s success stories, has an economy that grew by 21% last year, where parliamentary elections are to be held later this week.
Solution Which was ravaged .. from Portugal: CORRECT. This relative clause modifies the noun Angola. which is now one of Africa’s success stories: INCORRECT. This relative clause illogically modifies Portugal (which is in Europe). where parliamentary … this week: INCORRECT.
