A relative clause that begins with where must modify a noun that names a physical place, so this clause cannot modify year. The clause is too far away from Angola, however, to perform its intended role of modifying Angola. People, who talk loudly on their cell phones in crowded trains, show little respect for other passengers.
Solution who talk … crowded trains: INCORRECT. This clause is wrong because the commas that enclose it make it a nonessential clause. So if we remove it & see the sentence, we get “People show little respect for other passengers” which falsely claims for every human being. To correct remove the commas. Of all the earthquakes in European history, the earthquake, which destroyed Lisbon in 1755, is perhaps the most famous.
Solution which destroyed Lisbon in 1755: INCORRECT. The 2 commas enclosing “the earthquake” make it a nonessential clause. But because of the which we require a noun for it to modify Unaccustomed to the rigours of college life, James’s grades dropped.
Solution Unaccustomed here is being used for James himself & not James’s grades.
✅ Unaccustomed to the rigours of college life, James allowed his grades to drop.
Practice-2 The following four sentences contain highlighted sections. Use the rules in this chapter to correct any errors that you can find in them. Do not change anything that is not highlighted.
Jim is trying to reduce the number of soda that he drinks , at last night’s party, however, his resolve to drink fewer soda was sorely tested , he found himself quaffing many of the sodas.
Solution Jim is trying to reduce the AMOUNT of soda that he drinks ; at last night’s party, however, his resolve to drink LESS soda was sorely tested , ANDhe found himself quaffing A NUMBER of the sodas.
Number should be amount or quantity. Here soda is an uncountable substance-otherwise, soda would be sodas. The comma after drinks should be a semicolon, which would appropriately separate two independent clauses: Jim is trying … that he drinks and at last night’s party … sorely tested. Fewer should be Jess. Once again, regard soda as an uncountable substance. An and should be inserted after the comma after tested. This placement of and appropriately separates two main clauses: his resolve … was sorely tested and he found … sodas. Many should be a number. Since there is an -son the end of sodas, you know that sodas are now thought of as countable things-presumably servings of soda. A number of is an appropriate modifier for countable things. Between 1998 and 2003, there was heavy fighting in Parthia between numerous armed factions yet this conflict, so much more complicated than a conventional war between two states, involved no less that eight countries and twenty-five militias.
Solution Between 1998 and 2003, there was heavy fighting in Parthia AMONG numerous armed factions ; this conflict, so much more complicated than a conventional war between two states, involved no FEWER that eight countries and twenty-five militias.
The first between is correct, since only two dates are mentioned. The second between should be among, since the fighting involved more than two factions. Yet should be a semicolon. Yet is illogical because the action in the second clause (this conflict … militias) did not happen despite the action in the first clause (Between .. factions), as a word such as yet suggests. The third between is correct, since only two states are mentioned. Less should be fewer, because countries and militias are countable entities. Most legislators—including MUCH in the governor’s own party—realise that the governor’s budget would imperil the state’s finances , nonetheless, the budget is likely to be approved , because few legislators want to anger voters by cutting spending or raising taxes.
Solution Most legislators—including MANY in the governor’s own party—realise that the governor’s budget would imperil the state’s finances ; nonetheless, the budget is likely to be approved, because few legislators want to anger voters by cutting spending or raising taxes.
Much should be many, because legislators are countable. Nonetheless should be preceded by a semicolon. The clauses it connects are Most legislators … state’s finances and the budget is likely to be approved. The comma before because is correct. Because is a subordinating conjunction; therefore, it can be separated from a main clause by a comma. Nuanced Practice --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Simple Regular *“that”* {MODIFIER} example Sixty-five million years ago, according to some scientists, an asteroid bigger than Mount Everest slammed into North America, which, causing plant and animal extinctions, marks the end of the geologic era known as the Cretaceous Period. > Try solving it :) 1. [ ] (A) which, causing plant and animal extinctions, marks > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) which caused the plant and animal extinctions marking > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) and causing plant and animal extinctions that mark > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) an event that caused plant and animal extinctions, which marks > <
Correct Answer: (E) 5. [x] (E) an event that caused the plant and animal extinctions that mark ## *”that of”* example Retail sales rose 8/10 of 1 percent in August, intensifying expectations that personal spending in the July-September quarter more than doubled that of the 1.4 percent growth rate in personal spending for the previous quarter. > Try solving it :) 1. [ ] (A) that personal spending in the July-September quarter more than doubled that of > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) that personal spending in the July-September quarter would more than double 3. [ ] (C) of personal spending in the July-September quarter, that it more than doubled > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) of personal spending in the July-September quarter more than doubling that of > <
Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) of personal spending in the July-September quarter, that it would more than double that of > Correct Answer: (B) ## *”that”* in parallelism) However much United States voters may agree that there is waste in government and that the government as a whole spends beyond its means, it is difficult to find broad support for a movement toward a minimal state. 1. [x] (A) However much United States voters may agree that 2. [ ] (B) Despite the agreement among United States voters to the fact > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) Although United States voters agree > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Even though United States voters may agree > <
Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) There is agreement among United States voters that > Correct Answer: (A) ## 🚨 *”that”* modifier Exception) For many revisionist historians, Christopher Columbus has come to personify devastation and enslavement in the name of progress that has decimated native peoples of the Western Hemisphere. 1. [x] (A) devastation and enslavement in the name of progress that has decimated native peoples of the Western Hemisphere > Correct Answer: (E) 2. [ ] (B) devastation and enslavement in the name of progress by which native peoples of the Western Hemisphere have been decimated > Correct Answer: (E) 3. [ ] (C) devastating and enslaving in the name of progress those native peoples of the Western Hemisphere that have been decimated > Correct Answer: (E) 4. [ ] (D) devastating and enslaving those native peoples of the Western Hemisphere which in the name of progress are decimated > <
Correct Answer: (E) 5. [ ] (E) the devastation and enslavement in the name of progress that have decimated the native peoples of the Western Hemisphere Solutions Q1-Solution Correct Answer: (E) “which” is modifying “North America” when it should be referring to the “asteroid … slammed …” event. (A) (B) Mark Vs. Marks: Plant and Animal Extinctions (Plural) ⇒ mark (D) (C) → No parallelism to use here with the “and” Q2-Solution (B) The “that of” Comparison doesn’t add up. (A) (D), & (E) “that it” comparison is not making a whole lot of sense. (C). Q3-Solution (A) Parallelism: We need a “that” at the end to complete the parallelism. (B), (C), & (D) (E) → Has no contrasting words in the sentence. We can also use meaning to eliminate these answer choices. Q4-Solution (E) personify requires a noun. so “devastating” & “enslaving” cant be used. (C) & (D) (B) → “were decimated” & NOT “decimated” “devastation and enslavement” form a plural therefore “have” should be used here. (A)
summary:
A modifier describes/provides extra information about something else in the sentence. Incorrectly used modifiers lead to ambiguity or illogical meaning.
💡 Many modifiers answer the questions who, what, when, where, or why Adjectives & Adverbs These are one-word modifiers.
Adjective → ONLY Noun/Pronoun
Adverb → Almost anything EXCEPT Noun/Pronoun
Verbs, Adjectives Prepositional Phrases Entire Clauses Many adverbs are formed by adding “-ly” to the adjective
💡 Often Adjective ↔ Adverb use will be interchanged as the sentence error.
title: SC-4 Modifiers

contents:
Every sentence must have a noun (subject) that performs the action (verb).
Subject-Verb Errors Subject/Verb Missing A sentence missing the subject/verb is just a fragment. In GMAT, the correct answer must be a complete sentence or independent clause.
E.g: The cat sitting by the stairs. ❌
🚨 Verbs ≠ Complete Sentence Here although sitting looks like a verb, its a working verb that can run a sentence by itself.
The cat sitting by the stairs WATCHED the mouse ✅ | watched is a working verb here. The cat WAS SITTING by the stairs. ✅ | sitting is called a present participle 💡 “-ing” word by itself is never a working verb. Subject-Verb Number Disagreement Singular Subject ⇒ Singular Verb | Plural Subject ⇒ Plural Verb
The GMAT test-maker sets the paper in a way that masks the subject.
E.g.
The discovery of new medicines (was/were) vital to the company's growth. “The discovery of new medicines was vital to the company’s growth.”
Here both discovery & new medicines can be confused as the subjects. But the noun medicines is part of the prepositional phrase of new medicines.
Lin and Guy *drive* to work. ✅ | Lin, as well as Guy, *drive* to work every day. ❌ In the 2nd sentence, as well as Guy become the modifier and leaves only Lin as the subject.
So the correct sentence would be: “Lin, as well as Guy, drives to work everyday”
🚨 X and Y ⇒ Plural (Subject= X and Y) X as well as Y ⇒ Singular (Subject = X) A complicated right answer:
Lin and Guy drive to work together every morning and greet their co-workers cheerfully. Red: Subjects | Yellow: Verbs | Green: Conjunctions Mitigation Strategies Inserting middlemen words between the subject & the verb is the most common way to hide a subject. Remove the middlemen words.
Furthermore, a lot of warmup words can be used in front of the subject to obscure it.
There are a few common types of middlemen and warmups.
🚨Prepositional Phrases🚨 Group of words headed by a preposition
of mice for milk by 1800 in Zambia with her at that level to the store on their orders from the office Prepositions modify/describe a noun.
🚨 A noun in a prepositional phrase can NEVER be the subject of the sentence. Eg: Near Galway, the houses on the road to Spiddle is/are gorgeous Near Galway, the HOUSES on the road to Spiddle ARE gorgeous. Dependent Clauses Clauses that begin with who/because and cannot stand alone as sentences.
💡 Stripping away the dependent clause still leaves out a complete sentence. Eg: Because she studied hard, she earned a good score on the test Dependent: because she studied hard
Independent: She earned a good score on the test.
Modifiers Will be covered in a later section
Practice Focus on getting the error. The error can be fixed under multiple rewrites.
In the waning days of the emperor’s Life, the conquest of new lands on the borders of the empire WAS/WERE considered vital.
Solution In the waning days of the emperor’s life, the CONQUEST of new lands on borders of the empire WAS considered vital. The proliferation of computer games designed to involve many players at once were first developed before the widespread availability of high-speed internet connections.
Solution Computer games designed to involve many players at once were first developed before the widespread availability of high-speed internet connections. [The subject and verb have to make sense together, but the original sentence says that the proliferation … were first developed; this is illogical. Rather, the computer games were developed. The corrected sentence is just one possible rewrite.] Complex Sentences That The word “that” in a sentence signals that more information is coming.
💡 The word “THAT” acts as a reset in a sentence. A new subject-verb-object structure will follow. The teacher is confident that | her students mastered the lesson.
Red: Subjects | Yellow: Verbs | Green: object
Sentences without “that” might seem fine in the real world, they don’t follow grammar conventions. These are called run-off sentences.
Eg:
❌ The teacher is confident her students mastered the lesson.
✅ The teacher is confident that her students mastered the lesson.
Without “that”, the sentence has an ambiguous meaning.
❌ I know Meryl Streep is an actor.
Do we know Meryl Streep herself? Or something about her?
✅ I know that Meryl Streep is an actor.
Conjunctions Conjunctions can be used to connect 2 complete sentences.
Lin drove to work, but Guy rode his bike Yellow: Independent Clause | Green: conjunction
💡 Ind. Clause ↔ Comma-Conjunction ↔ Ind. Clause But just a comma to join the sentences is not enough. This is called a run-on sentence.
❌ Lin drove to work, Guy rode his bike
🚨 Despite a conjunction being used, we need to ensure that the 2 parts being joined are in fact independent clauses. Eg:
Andrew & Lisa are inseparable; doing everything together ❌
Andrew & Lisa are inseparable; they do everything together ✅
💡 Commonly used 7 conjunctions for this purpose: **F**or | **A**nd | **N**or | **B**ut | **O**r | **Y**et | **S**o (FANBOYS) In summary take care:
Both sides of the comma-FANBOY ⇒ Ind. Clause Don’t connect 2 Ind. Clauses using ONLY a comma. Connecting Punctuation Semicolon (;) {Ind. Clause ↔ (;) ↔ Ind. Clause} Semicolon → Transition Expression (however, therefore, in addition)
NOTE: Conjunctions ⇒ comma (,) | Transitional Elements ⇒ semicolon (;)
💡 Commonly used together with “however”, “”therefore”, or “in addition” to establish relationship beyond the (;) Lists A rare but correct use is to separate items that themselves contain commas:
❌ I listen to Earth, Wind & Fire, Wow, Owls, and Blood, Sweat & Tears.
✅ I listen to Earth, Wind & Fire; Wow, Owls; and Blood, Sweat & Tears.
Colon (:) {Ind. Clause ↔ (;) ↔ Dep./Ind. Clause} Provides further explanation for what comes before it.
The sentence before it must be a stand alone.
❌ I love listening to: classical, rock, rap, and pop music.
✅ I love listening to many kinds of music: classical, rock, rap, and pop.
“Namely” or “that is” can be inserted after the colon.
Whatever needs the explanation should be kept close to the colon.
We can put a main clause after a colon as well. But it should explain the preceding clause.
💡 Semicolon: Connects 2 related independent clauses Colon: Connects a sentence with examples/explanation Dash (—) A strong version of a comma, semicolon, colon.
Sometimes it helps in retaining the meaning. You can think of it as brackets too.
❌ My three best friends, Danny, Enrico, and Joey, and I went skiing. (maybe 7 people are going)
✅ My three best friends — Danny, Enrico, and Joey — and I went skiing.
It can also be used to restate/explain an earlier part of the sentence.
✅ Post-MBA compensation for investment bankers tends to surge far ahead that for management consultants — by tens, if not hundreds, of thousand dollars a year.
Practice 1 In each of the following sentences:
(a) highlight the verb and (b) underline the subject.
(c) determine whether the subject and the verb make sense together
(d) determine whether the subject agrees in number with the verb. If the subject is singular, the verb form must be singular. If the subject is plural, the verb form must be plural.
If the sentence is a fragment, or if the subject and verb do not make sense together, or if the subject and verb do not agree, (e) rewrite the sentence correcting the mistake. If the sentence is correct as it is, mark it with the word CORRECT.
A venomous snake designated the emblem of the rebellion by the insurgency.
Solution A venomous snake WAS designated the emblem of the rebellion by the insurgency.
The original is a fragment, with designated as a past participle. A simple fix is to add a form of the verb to be, such as was.
A number of players on the team have improved since the last season.
Solution A number of players on the team have improved since the last season.
CORRECT since “A number of” is plural
The decision to place the beautiful artefacts in out-of-the-way nooks around the mansion’s various rooms was inspiring.
Solution The decision to place the beautiful artefacts in out-of-the-way nooks around the mansion’s various rooms was inspiring.
The various middlemen phrases beginning with to, in, and around are to be omitted.
Just around the corner is a fantastic bakery and a small supermarket.
Solution Just around the corner ARE a fantastic bakery and a small supermarket.
The word and has created a compound subject which now requires a plural verb.
Practice 2 Fix the following sentences if necessary:
The petroleum distillates were so viscous, the engineers had to heat the pipe by nearly 30 degrees.
Solution The petroleum distillates were so viscous THAT the engineers had to heat the pipe by nearly 30 degrees. (run-on Sentence) The municipality’s back-to-work program has had notable success, nevertheless, it is not suitable for a state-wide rollout for several reasons.
Solution The municipality’s back-to-work program has had notable success ; nevertheless, it is not suitable for a state-wide rollout for several reasons. The Bentley trench, situated more than a mile and a half below sea level and completely covered by Antarctic glaciers, and it is the lowest point on the planet not under the oceans.
Solution The Bentley trench, situated more than a mile and a half below sea level and completely covered by Antarctic glaciers, IS the lowest point on the planet not under the oceans.
Explanation: The original sentence has an independent clause linked to a sentence fragment by the use of and. In the corrected version, one main clause combines all of the information given.
summary:
Every sentence must have a noun (subject) that performs the action (verb).
Subject-Verb Errors Subject/Verb Missing A sentence missing the subject/verb is just a fragment. In GMAT, the correct answer must be a complete sentence or independent clause.
E.g: The cat sitting by the stairs. ❌
🚨 Verbs ≠ Complete Sentence Here although sitting looks like a verb, its a working verb that can run a sentence by itself.
The cat sitting by the stairs WATCHED the mouse ✅ | watched is a working verb here.
title: SC-3 Sentence Structure

contents:
Must-Know Grammar Terms Clause Set of words containing a subject and a working verb
Eg: She applied for the job. She- subject & applied- working verb 💡 Using the question “Who did what?” will point to the subject and working verb Independent Clause: Standalone correct sentence.
Dependent Clause: Also contains a verb. But not standalone in a sentence. These are your sentence fragments.
Modifier Provides additional information in a sentence. Adjectives are a modifier.
Essential Modifiers: They are essential to understanding the sentence meaning
Eg: “The job that she started last week is much harder than her previous job” Here, “that she started last week” is an essential modifier.
Since without it, “The job that is much harder than her previous job”
We are left with the question “What Job?”
Non-essential Modifiers: These modifiers can be removed from the sentence without any change to its meaning.
Eg: “The cat, which lives next door, is very noisy.” In this sentence, “which lives next door” is a non-essential clause. If we know which cat is being referred to we can directly remove it to make the sentence “The cat is very noisy.” 💡 The “comma which” structure is an example of a non-essential modifier. Sentence Core Combination of independent clauses along with some essential modifiers forms a are the bare minimum needed in order to have a coherent sentence. All non-essential modifiers are removed from the sentence. Conjunction Words that help to stick parts of the sentences together.
Coordinating Conjunctions: (Ind. Clause ↔ Ind. Clause) Join independent clauses together. Subordinating Conjunctions: (Ind. Clause ↔ Modifier) Marker (Not an official grammar term) A flag to show that a certain kind of issue is being tested.
E.g: Unlike is a comparison marker.
💡 Keep a record of all markers & the grammar issue they point at. Meaning: A Closer Look The correct answer choice should convey a logical & clear meaning.
Meaning errors can primarily be classified into:
Choose your words Place your words Match your words Choose Your Words Is the right word being used for the right meaning?
Vocabulary Based E.g: ✅ “My decision to drive a hybrid car was motivated by ECONOMIC considerations.”
❌ “ECONOMICAL considerations motivated my decision to drive a hybrid car.”
Economical mean “thrifty, efficient” here. It’s not distant from the original meaning economic considerations- that is, monetary considerations.
Some Common Word Discrepancies Aggravate (Worsen) Aggravating (Irritating) known as (named) known to be (acknowledged as) loss of (no longer in possession of) loss in (decline in value) mandate (command) have a mandate (have authority from voters) native of (person of) native to (species that originated in) range of (variety of) ranging (varying) rate of (speed/frequency of) rates for (prices for) rise (general increase) raise (a bet/ salary increase) try to do (seek to accomplish) try doing (experiment with) May, Will, Must, Should These express the various levels of certainty, obligation & reality. The meaning changes entirely on swapping these words with each other.
Eg:
✅ The court ruled that the plaintiff MUST pay full damages.
❌ The court ruled that the plaintiff SHOULD pay full damages.
A court never suggests its final decision. It always gives a ruling which is strictly followed by everyone. But the “should” in the second sentence makes the decision a suggestion.
🚨 On the GMAT, “SHOULD” → Moral Obligation Place Your Words Placement of some words can change the entire meaning of a sentence.
Eg:
“ALL the children are covered in mud.”
”The children are ALL covered in mud.”
Here, the intent shifts from “how many” children (all of them) to “how much” the children were covered in mud (all over).
💡 Word Place Change ⇒ Check for possible meaning change The correct word placement should resolve all sentence ambiguity. Eg:
❌ The council granted the right to make legal petitions TO CITY OFFICIALS.
✅ The council granted CITY OFFICIALS the right to make legal petitions.
✅ The right to make legal petitions TO CITY OFFICIALS was granted by the council.
Match Your Words Sentences contain pairs of words or phrases that must match.
Eg: ❌ “Unlike Alaska, where the winter is quite cold, the temperature in Florida rarely goes below freezing.”
Here the state Alaska is being compared with the temperature in Florida. It’s illogical to compare a state to the temperature in another state.
💡 Connected words must always make sense together. Avoiding Redundancy Words which can be removed without changing the sentence meaning should be removed.
Words with same meaning Most common redundancy trap.
❌ The value of the stock ROSE by a 10% INCREASE.
✅ The value of the stock INCREASED by 10%.
✅ The value of the stock ROSE by 10%.
Expressions of time PAST Previously Formerly In the past Before now PRESENT Now Currently Presently At present YEARLY Annual Each year A year (3 launches a year) These are the most sneaky. It is easy to sneak in 2 redundant time expressions.
💡 One of the expressions being in the non-underlined part are specially sneaky. But there can be representations, provided the meaning supports it.
Contrast Words These are also sneaky since they are quite spaced apart.
❌ “Although she studied night and day for three months, yet she did not do well on her exam.”
Practice-I The underlined portion of each sentence below may contain one or more errors. Each sentence is followed by a boldface sample answer choice that may change the meaning. Select (A) if the original version is correct, (B) if the boldface version is correct, (C) if neither is correct, and (D) if both are correct.
If you select (A), explain what is wrong with the boldface version. If you select (B), explain how the boldface version corrects the original version. If you select (C), explain why both versions are incorrect. Some questions refer to rules and distinctions that will be discussed in upcoming chapters.
--- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Practice She is the most dedicated gardener on the block, watering the more than 50 plants in her yard every day. **(watering more than the 50 plants in her yard every day)** > Try solving it :) 1. [ ] (A) The original version is correct > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) The boldface portion is correct > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Neither of the 2 portions is correct > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Both the portions are correct > Right Answer! ## Practice Students at Carver High School are encouraged to pursue only those extracurricular activities from which stems success in college applications. **(success in college applications stems)** > Try solving it :)) 1. [ ] (A) The original version is correct > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) The boldface portion is correct > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Neither of the 2 portions is correct > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Both the portions are correct > Right Answer! ## Practice Hector remembers San Francisco as it was when he left 10 years ago. **(as though he had left ten years ago)** > Try solving it :)) 1. [ ] (A) The original version is correct > Correct Answer: (D) 2. [ ] (B) The boldface portion is correct > Correct Answer: (D) 3. [ ] (C) Neither of the 2 portions is correct > Correct Answer: (D) 4. [x] (D) Both the portions are correct > Right Answer! Solutions Q1 She is the most dedicated gardener on the block, watering the more than 50 plants in her yard every day. (watering more than the 50 plants in her yard every day)
(D) The original version contains the phrase the more than 50 plants. Here the words more than modify the number 50. The sentence therefore means that she waters her plants, of which there are more than 50. In the boldface version, the phrase changes to watering more than the 50 plants. Here the words more than are separated from the number 50, and therefore do not modify that number. The new version says that she waters something more than (i.e., in addition to) the plants-for instance, she might water her gravel walkway or her garden gnomes or the neighbours’ plants. Both versions potentially make sense (though one implies she is a very dedicated gardener), so either version could be correct.
Q2 Students at Carver High School are encouraged to pursue only those extracurricular activities from which stems success in college applications. (success in college applications stems)
(D) Both versions are correct!
The original sentence inverts the normal order of subject (success) and verb (stems = “originates”), but this inversion is possible after which or in similar positions. There is no change in meaning.
Q3 Hector remembers San Francisco as it was when he left 10 years ago. (as though he had left ten years ago)
(D) The boldface version changes to the original version, but the new version could make sense.
The original sentence says that Hector actually DID leave San Francisco 10 years ago. The revised version says that he did NOT leave San Francisco 10 years ago: the expression as though is used to discuss things that are untrue or did not happen (You behave as though you were richer than Bill Gates!). Another important change in meaning comes because the revised version takes out the words it (i.e., San Francisco) was, and therefore does not refer directly to the state of affairs in San Francisco 10 years ago. Practice-II Fix the following four sentences. Look for issues outlined in the above sections.
After the test format was changed, scores subsequently dropped by more than a 25% decrease. Solution Redundancy (Same Meaning Words): “dropped” & “decrease”.
After the test format was changed, scores subsequently dropped by more than 25%.
Though canals have experienced a severe decline in barge traffic over the past several decades, yet with the rise in fuel costs, “shipping” by actual ships may once again become an important means of transporting goods within the country. Solution Redundancy (Contrast Words): “Though” & “yet”
Canals have experienced a severe decline in barge traffic over the past several decades, yet with the rise in fuel costs, “shipping” by actual ships may once again become an important means of transporting goods within the country.
summary:
Must-Know Grammar Terms Clause Set of words containing a subject and a working verb
Eg: She applied for the job. She- subject & applied- working verb 💡 Using the question “Who did what?” will point to the subject and working verb Independent Clause: Standalone correct sentence.
Dependent Clause: Also contains a verb. But not standalone in a sentence. These are your sentence fragments.
Modifier Provides additional information in a sentence. Adjectives are a modifier.
title: SC-2 Grammar and Meaning

contents:
Question Format Although William Pereira first gained national recognition for his movie set designs, including those for Reap the Wild Wind and Jane Eyre, future generations remember him as the architect of the Transamerica Tower, the Malibu campus of Pepperdine University, and the city of Irvine.
(A) including those for Reap the Wild Wind and Jane Eyre, future generations remember
(B) like that for Reap the Wild Wind and Jane Eyre, future generations will remember
(C) like those for Reap the Wild Wind and Jane Eyre, future generations remembered
(D) including that for Reap the Wild Wind and Jane Eyre, future generations remembered
(E) including those for Reap the Wild Wind and Jane Eyre, future generations will remember
💡 Option (A) is the original sentence always. 20% of the time the answer is (A). So we shouldn’t neglect it completely. Q Timing (40s - 80s) Guess on the remaining options beyond the 2 min mark.
Solution Process 🚨 Destroy all sense of “The answer sounds good.” Use grammar rules to find answers. Step 1: 1st Glance Notice the length of the underline. What’s happening at the start of the underline? Notice the first word split of the options Clue Possible Implication Length of Underlined Section Long: Posssible problems with sentence structre (meaning, modifiers, parallelism)
Short: Compare the answers in full before reading the original sentence every will most always many not always 💡 There will always be a split between the first word of the 5 options. Step 2: Read for meaning Keep an eye on both grammar and meaning issues. Objective: To spot overall issues with meaning or sentence structure Identify the subject, verb, preposition, conjunctions & participles. Don’t have to identify grammar function of each word through. Eg: “Anne and Millie went to the movies in her car.”
This sentence is grammatically correct. But doesn’t convey the meaning clearly as to who’s car did they go in.
Step 2.5 Predict Answer Predict the correction in the answer choice. Might not be possible at all times but should be tried since it saves a lot of time. 🚨 DO NOT READ THE 1ST ANSWER CHOICE Step 3: Finding A Starting Point An SC question will be testing us on multiple fronts and will have many discrepancy points that could be identified.
There are likely 2 starting points:
An error (or suspected error) in the original sentence Splits/differences in the answers Ensure that there is at least 1 split for the suspected issue.
Step 4: Eliminate All Incorrect Choices During practice, if we’re down to 2 answer choices, we should simply pick of the two and move on.
💡🚨 But during its review, make the following considerations:
Why is the right answer right? Why are each of the 4 others wrong? How would someone mistakenly justify eliminating the right answer? How would someone mistakenly justify picking any of the wrong answers? 🚨 DO NOT go for shorter answer always. Focus on grammar & meaning
(Most of the times concision errors have a meaning/idiom error)
Step 5: Repeat 🚨 The correct answer doesn’t necessarily have to sound perfect to us too Step 6: Read the Answer Choice back into the sentence This will help you decide which answer communicated the meaning of the sentence most effectively.
When Stumped Read the answers back into the sentence, again. Shorter answers are generally better. Strike-off passive voice options. They are seldom the right answer. Avoid answers with redundancy. Answers with “being” are to be avoided.
summary:
Question Format Although William Pereira first gained national recognition for his movie set designs, including those for Reap the Wild Wind and Jane Eyre, future generations remember him as the architect of the Transamerica Tower, the Malibu campus of Pepperdine University, and the city of Irvine.
(A) including those for Reap the Wild Wind and Jane Eyre, future generations remember
(B) like that for Reap the Wild Wind and Jane Eyre, future generations will remember
title: SC-1 Question Format and Solution Process

contents:
Real-life data has fatter tails, that is a very high probability of shorter losses.
In EVT, we fit the distribution to only the tail of the data. It’s also called the semi-parametric method. Applicable only at the tails and for random variables >0
💡 so we multiply the -ve returns with (-1) and fit it. Method We have to fit a model to the tail of the dataset beyond a cutoff
Take the lowest 90% of the data. Then find the 5% Let’s have a CDF for the losses
So we now choose a value $u$, the values to the right of which, we fit the distribution to.
$$ P(u<L<u+y)=F(u+y)-F(u)\ P(L>u)=1-F(u) $$
where $y>0$.
Now let y be a function of u:
$$ F_u(y)=P(u<L<u+y|L>u)\ \Rightarrow F_u(y)=\frac{F(u+y)-F(u)}{1-F(u)} $$
As $u \to \infty$
$$ F_u(y) \to G_{\xi, \beta}(y)=1-[1+\xi\cdot \frac{y}{\beta}]^{-1/\xi} $$
Where $G$ is the Generalized Pareto Distribution
In a GPD:
$\xi$: Is the shape parameter & defines the fatness of the tails $\beta$: On the other hand is the scale parameter So the pdf for G is:
$$ g_{\xi, \beta}=\frac{1}{\beta}(1+\xi\cdot \frac{y}{\beta})^{-1/\xi -1} $$
Finding $\xi$ & Beta $\xi\ \epsilon\ [0.1,0.3]$
Sort the returns in ascending order. Take a return value $u$ such that cutoff %(say 10) of the data lies above it. Take the Maximum Likelihood Function Maximum Likelihood Function $$ \text{Max. Likelihood Function}:\ \pi[\frac{1}{\beta}(1+ \xi\cdot \frac{R_i-u}{\beta})^{-\frac{1}{\xi}-1}] $$
Maximize $\sum \ln(l)$ w.r.t. $(\xi, \beta)$
Finding VaR after GPD estimation The VaR satisfies the following condition:
$$P(L>VaR)=[1-F(u)][1-G_{\xi, \beta}(VaR-u)]$$ $$1-CL=\frac{n_u}{n}[1+\xi\cdot \frac{VaR-u}{\beta}]^{-1/\xi}$$
Where:
$n_u$: No. of datapoints above cutoff $n$: Total no. of datapoints Thus VaR becomes:
$$VaR= u + \frac{\beta}{\xi}[(\frac{n}{n_u}(1-CL)^{-\xi})-1]$$
Similarly conditional VaR:
$$CVaR=\frac{VaR+\beta+\xi u}{1-\xi}$$
Choice of u $n$ should be large ~ 1000 data points Approx 4 years of daily data At least 10%/100-datapoints (whichever is lower) of the total data should have -ve returns u should contain more than (C%) of data points where (1-C)% is the confidence level Time Aggregation of VaR $$ \text{(T-day EVT VaR)} = T^{\xi}\cdot \text{(Daily EVT VaR)} $$
Now:
Daily EVT VaR > Daily ND VaR $\xi$ < 0.5 💡 So for values >10, the T-Day VaR is almost the same for EVT & ND.
summary:
Real-life data has fatter tails, that is a very high probability of shorter losses.
In EVT, we fit the distribution to only the tail of the data. It’s also called the semi-parametric method. Applicable only at the tails and for random variables >0
💡 so we multiply the -ve returns with (-1) and fit it. Method We have to fit a model to the tail of the dataset beyond a cutoff
title: Extreme Value Theory (EVT)

contents:
It’s a statistical measure of potential loss. This is the amount we should be prepared to cover for.
VaR is the worst loss, over a target horizon, such that there is a low pre-specified probability that the actual loss will be higher.
$$ P(l>VaR)\leq 1-C $$
where $P(l\leq VaR)= C$
Discrete Example of calculating VaR Gains Prob 10 Cr 98% -2 Cr 1.5% -5 Cr 0.5% $\text{Expected Gain}\ E(G) = (0.98\ast10) - (0.015\ast2) - (0.005\ast5)$ Absolute VaR: The min. loss which has a X% prob. of getting a higher loss than OR (1-X)% of getting a lower loss
Relative VaR (to the expected value): Is the distance between the Expected Gains & Absolute VaR
Here for 99% VaR:
Absolute VaR= 2 Cr (1% CDF is contained withing this loss) Relative VaR= 11.745 Cr Continuous Example of VaR A uniform distribution between the gains [-30,70]. 💡 $z=\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}$ Normal Distribution X~[25,100], 99.9 VaR Expected Gains= 25 For VaR calculations:
$$ P(x>k)=P(\frac{x-25}{30}>\frac{K-25}{30})=99.9%\ \implies P(z>\frac{K-25}{30})=0.999 $$
Now the value corresponding to 0.001 (.1% area to the left) is -3.1 as inferred by the z-table.
$$ \therefore z>-3.1\ \implies \frac{K-25}{30}>-3.1\ \implies K=-68 $$
Absolute VaR= -68 Relative VaR= 25 - (-68) = 93 VaR for a Stock Process:
Mark to market our current exposure $[W_0]$ Set the time horizon $[1\ day]$ Get $\sigma$ of the underlying risk factor $[Returns]$ 💡 Jarque-Bera Test of Normality
$JB=[\frac{Skew^2}{6}+\frac{(kurt-3)^2}{24}]=\chi^2_{2DoF}$
where:
$\sigma=\sqrt{\frac{\sum(x-\bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ for a sample with population “n”
$Skew=\frac{1}{N-1}\sum \frac{(x_i-\bar{x})^3}{\sigma^3}$ AND $Kurt=\frac{1}{N-1}\sum \frac{(x_i-\bar{x})^4}{\sigma^4}$
For a distribution to be normal $JB>\chi^2_{crit}$
Assume a distribution of the risk factor Set a CL Report the worst loss VaR in terms of Returns $R^\ast$: Is the worst return for VaR
VaR (Abs): $W_0 - W^\ast = W_0 - W_0(1 + R^\ast)= -W_0\cdot R^\ast = -W_0(\bar{R}+Z^\ast\sigma)$
Here $W_0(1+R^\ast)$ is the Worst Asset Value Tomorrow
VaR (Rel): $W_0(1+\bar{R})-W^\ast=W_0[\bar{R}-R^\ast]=W_0[-Z^\ast\sigma]$
Here $W_0(1+\bar{R})$ is the Tomorrow’s Expected Value
Time Aggregation of Returns Let $R_i=$ Daily Returns
Assuming the markets to be efficient i.e.: Prices follow Random Walk Returns are uncorrelated over successive time intervals Therefore $E(R_{annual})=\sum E(R_i)$
Now $Var(R_{annual})=Var(\sum R_i)$
💡 $Var(X_1+X_2)= Var(X_1) + Var(X_2) + CoVar(X_1,X_2)$ Assuming the Daily Returns to be Identically Distributed, Therefore, if the Daily Returns follow a Normal ~ $Dist(\mu, \sigma^2)$
Then for the returns for a time window of T days:
$$ R_{(T-Days)}\approx Dist(T\mu, T\sigma^2) $$
Empirical VaR (Non-Parametric VaR) Sort the gains/returns including the signs. Take the gain/returns that have 5% of data above them. This value is your sort of like your 5% median types & is your 95% VaR 💡 Non-Parametric VaR doesn’t assume any distribution for your data. Correlated Returns Over Time In these we assume that
$$ R_t=\rho R_{t-1} + \epsilon_t $$
Then for some returns
$$ R_2=\rho R_{1} + \epsilon_2\ |||^{ly}\ R_3=\rho R_{2} + \epsilon_3\ \Rightarrow R_3 = \rho^2 R_{1} + \rho\epsilon_2 + \epsilon_3\ |||^{ly}\ R_4 = \rho^3 R_1 + f_4(\epsilon) $$
So for the variance of these returns, i.e. $V(R_1+R_2+R_3+R_4)=V(\sum_{i=1}^{4}R_i)$
$$ V(\sum_{i=1}^{T}R_i) = \sigma^2[T + 2(T-1)\rho + 2(T-2)\rho^2 + 2(T-3)\rho^3\ …\ 2\rho^{T-1} ] $$
Since $\rho<1$, we need not calculate beyond a few terms here since the terms start diminishing quickly.
Conditional VaR (Expected Shortfall) VaR asks how bad things can get (with a 95% confidence). Conditional VaR answers the questions “What is the expected loss if things get bad?” i.e.
$$ CVaR=R^{**}=E(R|R<R^*) $$
Worked Example Value P(Value) -20 0.0004 -11 0.014 -11 0.014 -2 0.49 So now the 97.5% VaR = 11 Cr So for 97.5% CVaR
$$CVaR = \frac{20 \ast 0.004 + 11 \ast (0.025-0.004)}{0.025}\ = 12.44$$
CVaR for a Normal Distribution Since $CVar=E(R|R<R^*)$, using bayes theorem where $[R\sim N(\mu,\sigma)]$:
$$CVaR=\frac{\int_{-\infty}^{R^\ast}rP(r)dr}{P(R<R^\ast)}$$
Thus our conditional return value would be where $[Z\sim N(0,1)]$:
$$CZ=\frac{\int_{-\infty}^{Z^\ast}zP(z)dz}{F(Z<Z^\ast)}$$
If CL=95%, then $F(Z<Z^*)=1-CL$
$$CZ=\frac{\int_{-\infty}^{Z^*}zP(z)dz}{1-CL}$$
Z being a normal distribution:
$$P(z)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}}$$
Solving for this value of $P(Z)$ {Hint: Use $-z^2/2=y$}
$$CZ = \frac{-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}}|_{-\infty}^{Z^\ast}}{1-CL}\ \implies CZ=\frac{-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-\frac{(Z^\ast)^2}{2}}}{1-CL}$$
💡 CVaR always follows the sub-additivity property. But if the $R\sim N$, then all $\sigma$, $VaR$, $CVaR$ hold true Precision VaR (P% Confidence) 1. Bootstrapping VaR For a dataset with 5000 data values:
Sample (with replacing) Get mean, variance & C% VaR Repeat steps 1-2 some (say 10K) times We now get a sample of VaR (10K datapoints).
Make a distribution of these VaR 2. Mean for Bootstrapped Dataset The mean of the bootstrapped Dataset $(\bar{\bar{x}})$, is assumed to follow the normal distribution here
$$ \bar{x}\sim N(\bar{\bar{x}},\frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}) $$
3. Standard Deviation for Bootstrapped Dataset In a bootstrapped dataset, the standard distribution $(s)$ follows:
$$ \frac{(T-1)s^2}{\sigma^2}\sim \chi^2 $$
As $T\to \infty$
$$ s^2 \sim N(\sigma^2,\sigma^2\sqrt{\frac{2}{T-1}})\ \Rightarrow s \sim N(\sigma,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{2T}}) $$
The returns & standard deviation of the bootstrapped dataset will be:
$$ \bar{r} \sim N(\bar{r}, \frac{s}{\sqrt{T}})\ s \sim N(s, \frac{s}{\sqrt{2T}}) $$
Now the VaR is $W_0Z\sigma$
Now the Z here is from the C% confidence value that comes from our input $(Z_c)$.
So now the VaR is:
$$ VaR=W_0Z_c\sigma\ \Rightarrow VaR = W_0Z_c(s\ \pm\ Z_p\frac{s}{\sqrt{2T}}) $$
Where:
$W_0$: The initial cost $Z_c$: The Z-Score corresponding to %VaR $s$: Standard Deviation of the Dataset $Z_p$: The Z-Score corresponding to confidence Score

VaR is the worst loss, over a target horizon, such that there is a low pre-specified probability that the actual loss will be higher.
$$ P(l>VaR)\leq 1-C $$
where $P(l\leq VaR)= C$
