title: Value At Risk (VaR)

contents:
Defining Risk Risk is the probability of a loss.
Financially Risky Decision Some examples of risky decisions in finance:
Investment Options: Where to invest in a financial instrument? Debt/Equity Balances: How much to invest in a financial instrument? Financial Risk Sources Sources of these risk are mostly macro-economic sources like:
Business cycles leading to changing interest rates Govt. Policies (Demonetization) Technological Changes (Crypto, FinTech) Wars & Natural Calamities Risk Management Steps Identify the risk Assessing & Measuring the risk Monitoring & Mitigate the risk Using Cash/Savings Derivatives (covered in DRM) Share/Transfer(partially) risk through (re)insurance Types of Risk 1. Market Risk Risk associated with relevant markets for the investments a company holds.
Equity/Commodity Exchange Rate: If you are invested in foreign currencies Interest Rate: If you are invested in bonds 2. Liquidity Risk The risk of your assets not being able to be sold.
3. Credit Risk Risk of a debt being defaulted on by your creditors
4. Operational Risks Risk associated with any of your operations:
ATMs & Banks → Thefts Online Banking → Frauds Measures of Risk Standard Deviation (Both upside/downside risk): Avg. Dispersion around the mean. Quantile (Downside Risk) Properties of a good loss measure (Artzner) Monotonicity: A portfolio generating overall worse returns should be riskier. $$[L(P)>L(Q)\Rightarrow R(P)>R(Q)]$$ Translation Invariant: Adding cash to your portfolio should decrease risk. $$[R(P + Cash)] < R(P) + Cash$$ Homogeneity: The riskiness of a factor increases by a same factor as that of the principle. $$[R(kP)=kR(P)]$$ Sub-Additivity: This is called diversification effect. $$[R(P+Q)<R(P)+R(Q)]$$
summary:
Defining Risk Risk is the probability of a loss.
Financially Risky Decision Some examples of risky decisions in finance:
Investment Options: Where to invest in a financial instrument? Debt/Equity Balances: How much to invest in a financial instrument? Financial Risk Sources Sources of these risk are mostly macro-economic sources like:
Business cycles leading to changing interest rates Govt. Policies (Demonetization) Technological Changes (Crypto, FinTech) Wars & Natural Calamities Risk Management Steps Identify the risk Assessing & Measuring the risk Monitoring & Mitigate the risk Using Cash/Savings Derivatives (covered in DRM) Share/Transfer(partially) risk through (re)insurance Types of Risk 1.
title: Introduction to Financial Risk

contents:
Futures Data.xlsx
Forward Contract Contract Farming Contract farming can be defined as agricultural production carried out according to an agreement between a buyer and farmers, which establishes conditions for the production and marketing of a farm product or products. Typically, the farmer agrees to provide agreed quantities of a specific agricultural product.
Why is the farmer entering into the contract? The farmer fears drop in prices they fix a price to ensure certain profits.
Why is the company entering into the contract? The company fears a lack of supply & rising prices so fixes on a somewhat fixed price.
Nomenclature $F_{0_T}$: The fixed price set in the contract at the end of the maturity period $T$. $Spot_T$: The market price at the end of maturity period $T$. 💡 Gain of one party = Loss of the other party It’s a zero-sum gain where in aggregate, the parties are at no loss. Long Vs. Short Forward Position Asset Ownership Long Forward Position: The party that owns the assets
Short Forward Position: The party that is “short” on the assets
Forwards Contract Long Forward Position: The party that buys the underlying goods
Short Forward Position: The party delivering the underlying goods.
Exercise A party ___________ on an underlying asset fears _______ price. It will mitigate the risk by taking a _______ position in forwards/futures market.
Solution Long, Price Dec., Short Short, Price Inc., Long Counter-party/ Default Risk The party which is incurring loss, has a propensity to default.
Futures Contract Sell/Deliver the goods through an exchange traded contract. There is no counter-party risk. These are standardised contracts.
Some features of futures contract:
There is a fixed delivery location. The product has certain quality requirements (with a degree of tolerance). The commodity supplier approaches the exchange taking a short futures position at a certain price to be delivered within a maturity date. The commodity buyer approaches the exchange taking a long futures position at a certain price to be delivered within. Once an agreement on the trade is made between buyer & supplier. Then novation of contracts occurs. 💡Novation is the replacement of one of the parties in an agreement between two parties, with the agreement of all three parties involved. To novate is to replace an old obligation with a new one. So the exchange now takes care of counter-party risk Indian Commodity Exchanges MCX NCDEX How is the risk mitigated? The exchange contracts have a margin (an amount the buyer/seller has to pay before the contract)
Margins Initial Margin: The percent of a purchase price that must be paid with cash when using a margin account. Paid by both parties. Kind of like caution money. Mark-to-Market (MTM) Margin: It’s the difference between our initial price and the settling price each day. It may be +ve in which case we receive the margin. If the margin is -ve, we pay the margin. Stock Futures Close Price: The weighted average price of the stocks traded in the last 30 mins of spot market. Settle Price: The weighted average price of the stocks traded in the last 30 mins of futures market. Lot Size: The no. of stocks that will be bought on the expiry date. It’s a measure of the liquidity of the stock’s futures contract. Open: The decided price of the 1st contract Open Interest (applicable on derivative contracts only): OI is the total number of outstanding derivative contracts that have not been settled. Consider only 1 of long/short futures positions. Change in Open Interest 💡 Open Interest is always +ve. but Change in OI can be -ve Open Interest Calculations Eg.-
Time Long Futures Position Lot Amount Short Futures Position Lot Amount Open Interest 9:30 A 25 X 25 25 10:00 C 75 25 D 75 50 100 12:00 E 100 F 100 200 12:20 L 50 C 50 200 12:20 M 25 N 25 225 2:25 D 25 A 25 200 Open Interest Trends When 2 new parties enter into a futures contract, open interest increases. When only 1 existing contract party enters into a contract with a new party, open interest remains the same. When 2 existing contract parties square off each other’s contract, the open interest decreases. What Happens if we don’t meet a margin call? Your broker will pay your margin. They will do so through closing your other open positions. And if the broker themselves are not able pay it, the exchange uses their settlement guarantee fund it has to square off the margin call.
Practice Solution Being in a short position, we’ll incur losses if the prices increase. But to maintain our maintenance margin, the max. loss we can incur before a margin call is 1000$ (Initial-Maintenance Margin). For 5000 ounces, this amounts to 0.2$ max rise per ounce.
summary:
Futures Data.xlsx
Forward Contract Contract Farming Contract farming can be defined as agricultural production carried out according to an agreement between a buyer and farmers, which establishes conditions for the production and marketing of a farm product or products. Typically, the farmer agrees to provide agreed quantities of a specific agricultural product.
Why is the farmer entering into the contract? The farmer fears drop in prices they fix a price to ensure certain profits.
title: Forwards and Future Contracts

contents:
An assumption is simply an unstated premise that must be true in order for the argument to be true. It is an integral component of the argument that the author takes for granted and leaves unsaid.
💡 The answer to an assumption question must be something that the author must believe in order for the conclusion to be properly drawn.
The assumptions are minimal & can’t contain extraneous statements
Diff. b/w Strengthen & Assumption Questions Strengthen questions ask us to support the argument in any way possible. The support can be anything from 1% - 100%.
On the other hand, assumption questions ask you to identify a statement that the argument assumes or supposes.
Example
Argument: That teenager is an outstanding golfer.
Assumption: The teenager almost always hits the ball. The teenager almost never swings and misses the ball.
Strengthen: The teenager won a local club tournament.
Assumption Vs. Must Be True The difference can be described as Before Vs. After. In an assumption question, we are required to find the statements that were used to make the conclusion. Whereas, in a Must Be True question we select the answers that follow from the argument made in the stimulus.
Question Stems “The argument in the passage depends on which of the following assumptions?” “The argument above assumes that” “The conclusion above is based on which of the following assumptions?” “Which of the following is an assumption made in drawing the conclusion above?” “The conclusion of the argument above cannot be true unless which of the following is true?” The Supporter/Defender Model Supporter/Defender are the two roles that assumptions play in GMAT.
Supporter Assumptions (Easier to identify): It is the traditionally linking role where an assumption connects the pieces of the argument. They often connect “new”/”rogue” pieces of information in the argument that appears only in the conclusion or the premise. Example “All male citizens of Athens had the right to vote. Therefore, Socrates had the right to vote in Athens.”
The linking assumption here is that Socrates was a male citizen of Athens. It connects the premise element of male citizens have Right to vote and the conclusion that Socrates had the same right.
💡 Should you ever see a gap/new element in the conclusion, a Supporter assumption answer will close the gap/connect the new element. Defender Assumptions: They protect the argument by eliminating ideas that could weaken the argument. Example “People who read a lot are more intelligent than other people. Thus, reading must cause a person to be intelligent.”
There are a lot of ideas that can undermine the author’s argument:
Sleeping more than eight hours causes a person to be intelligent. Regular exercise causes a person to be intelligent. But they are assumed to be not possible by the authors. They, therefore, make the following assumptions in the original argument:
Sleeping more than eight hours does not cause a person to be intelligent. Regular exercise does not cause a person to be intelligent. These statements “defend” the argument by showing that a possible avenue of attack has been eliminated.
💡 If there are no discernable weakness in the argument, expect to see a defender answer choice. 🚨 NOTE: You can pre-phase on the supporter answer choices. But for the defender answers, there are too many possibilities to consider. The Assumption Negation Technique Through this, we can decide between Contenders or confirm our answer choice. It changes an assumption question to a Weaken question type. To apply the technique:
Logically negate the answer choices under consideration Example Statement: The congressman always votes for gun control
Negation: The congressman does not always vote for gun control
The negated answer choice that attacks the argument is the correct answer 🚨 The Assumption Negation Technique is a tedious process & should only be applied on contenders & NOT every answer choice. Logical Opposites Logical opposites are different from antonyms (or as they are called “polar” opposites)
Eg. What is the logical opposite of sweet? - Not sweet.
We might be tempted to give “sour” as an answer but that doesn’t cover everything (bland, bitter) which are not under the sweet category.
Logical Opposites All Not all Some None Always Not always Sometimes Never Everywhere Not everywhere Somewhere Nowhere will might Practice: Find the logical negations of the following statements The tax increase will result in more revenue for the government
Solution The tax increase might result in more revenue for the government All of the missions succeeded Solution Polar: None of the missions succeeded
Logical: Not all of the missions succeeded
The positive effects of the US Immigration policy are everywhere Solution The positive effects of the US Immigration policy are not everywhere
NOTE: “The negative effects of the US Immigration policy are everywhere” might seem like a negation of the original statement but isn’t
Quirks of Assumption Question Answer Choices Watch for “at least one” or “at least some” answers
The chances of such answers being correct is unusually high.
🚨 But don’t just blindly assume it to be the answer. Use the proper negation to check the answer. Avoid “the primary purpose”, “top priority”, or “the main factor” answers claiming most important consideration for the author
These answers have always been wrong. And unless the author discusses prioritization of ideas in the stimulus, these answers will continue to be wrong since an author can always claim that the idea under discussion was not necessarily the most important.
Watch the use of “not” or -ves in assumption answer choices
Do not rule out a -ve answer choice just because you are used to seeing assumptions as a +ve part of the argument. Defender choices generally contain -ve terms like “no”, “not”, and “never” which make them easy to negation technique.
Correct Answer Choices (Assumptions & Causality) Because the author always assumes that the stated cause is the only cause, assumption answer choices tend to work exactly like Strengthen answer choices in arguments with causal reasoning.
So the correct answer types in Assumptions are (Same as Strengthen):
Cause_2 -/> Effect Eliminate alternate causes for the stated effect Cause → Effect Show that when the cause occurs, the effect does occur Effect -/> Cause Show that the stated relationship can not be reversed Statistical Accuracy: Show that the data used to make the causal statement is accurate Fill in the blanks Fill in the Blanks are almost always assumption questions in GMAT. And when they are not assumption type, they are often Must Be True/Main Point questions.
The stimulus is preceded by the question stem which basically tells you to fill the missing blank. The blank is placed at the last. There is a premise indicator at the start of the final sentence or just before the blank that helps us in recognizing the missing premise to fill.
In rare cases, GMAT makers will on occasion use to turn certain FIB questions into Weaken-FIB questions.
“However, this is a poor approach, because ________”
“Hence, the proposal should be discarded since _________”
Practice Solution (D) (A)- Irrelevant
(B)- The ease of learning has no context here.
(C)- The author’s argument concerns what tourists can do to assure themselves that they have done the most they can in order to assist in communicating with Zorba’s locals. Whether tourists are committed to taking those steps is not part of the argument.
(D)- Correct Answer. Since it’s negation attacks the argument.
(E)- Their being spoken in other countries is irrelevant
summary:
An assumption is simply an unstated premise that must be true in order for the argument to be true. It is an integral component of the argument that the author takes for granted and leaves unsaid.
💡 The answer to an assumption question must be something that the author must believe in order for the conclusion to be properly drawn.
The assumptions are minimal & can’t contain extraneous statements
Diff. b/w Strengthen & Assumption Questions Strengthen questions ask us to support the argument in any way possible.
title: CR-9 Assumption

contents:
These questions ask you to identify the answer choice that best supports the argument. The correct answer choice does not necessarily justify the arguments, nor is the correct answer choice necessarily an assumption of the argument.
💡 The correct answer choice simply helps the argument in some way. Rules to Solve (Same as Weaken) The stimulus will always contain an argument since you are asked to weaken the author’s reasoning. Focus on the conclusion. The more we know about its specifics, the better armed we will be to shore up the argument. Information in the stimulus is suspect. Read the arguments carefully. 🚨Pre-phase possible answers🚨 before proceeding to the answer choices. 💡Answer choices are accepted as given💡 even if they include “new information”. Identifying Question Type The stem uses words synonymous to “strenghten”. “Strengthen” synonyms: Strengthen, support, helps, most justifies The stem usually indicates that you should accept the answer choices as true Examples “Which of the following if true, most strengthen the argument above?” “Which of the following, if true, most strongly supports the statement above?” “Which of the following, if true, does most to justify the conclusion above?” Which of the following, if true, supports the claim above EXCEPT? How to Strengthen an Argument Identify the conclusion: Ask yourself “Would this answer choice assist the author in some way?” Personalize the argument: Personalizing allows you to see the argument from a very involved perspective and helps assess the strength of each answer. Look for weakness in the argument: A weak spot in an argument is tailor-made for an answer that eliminates that weakness. Bringing the premise-conclusion assumptions to light strengthens the argument because it validates part of the author’s thinking. Validating surveys/analogies: Arguments using these rely upon their validity. So answer choices validating them are usually correct. Scale of strengthen doesn’t matter Answer Traps Opposite Answers: Because of their direct relation to the conclusion, these are attractive answer choices. Shell Game Answers: An idea similar to a concept raised in stimulus but that is changed just enough to be incorrect. Out Of Scope Answers: These simply miss the point of the argument and support unrelated/tangential issues to the argument. Causality & Strengthen Questions To strengthen a causal conclusion, you take the exact opposite approach that you would in a Weaken question:
Cause_2 -/> Effect Eliminate alternate causes for the stated effect Cause → Effect Show that when the cause occurs, the effect does occur Cause → Effect Show that the effect did not occur since the cause did not Effect -/> Cause Show that the stated relationship can not be reversed Statistical Accuracy: Show that the data used to make the causal statement is accurate Practice Consumer Advocate: Many household cleaners contain ingredients which are highly toxic when ingested by children or pets. Because of this significant risk, I propose a law prohibiting the use of such toxic ingredients in household cleaners.
Which of the following, if true, provides the most support for the argument above?
(A) Most toxic household cleaners have labels which clearly warn of their toxicity.
(B) There are many different types of household cleaners, and some are more effective than others.
(C) When the use of household cleaners is discontinued, harmful bacteria are more likely to propagate in areas where children and pets are commonly found.
(D) The toxic ingredients in most household cleaners could be replaced by comparably priced, non-toxic ingredients of equal or better quality.
(E) The amount of toxic ingredients found in most household cleaners is much less than the amount contained in most types of common gasoline
Solution (D) Premise: Many household cleaners contain ingredients that are toxic when ingested.
Conclusion: There should be law prohibiting the use of such toxic ingredients in household cleaners.
(A)- Clear warning labels would reduce the need for an across-the-board prohibition of the cleaners. So this statement in fact, weakens the argument
(B)- It just says that all household cleaners are not equal. But since it doesn’t talk about the toxic ingredients, the statement is irrelevant.
(C)- The statement, if relevant at all, would weaken the assertion that household cleaners should be discontinued
(D)- This is the correct answer. It provides information to show that the advocate’s plan is practicable. If the toxic ingredients could be removed with no added cost or decrease in effectiveness, then this preempts any cost/effectiveness-based objections.
(E)- The comparison between the amount of toxic chemicals in the cleaners is irrelevant to the question of outlawing them.
summary:
These questions ask you to identify the answer choice that best supports the argument. The correct answer choice does not necessarily justify the arguments, nor is the correct answer choice necessarily an assumption of the argument.
💡 The correct answer choice simply helps the argument in some way. Rules to Solve (Same as Weaken) The stimulus will always contain an argument since you are asked to weaken the author’s reasoning. Focus on the conclusion.
title: CR-8 Strengthen

contents:
Cause & effect reasoning asserts/denies whether one thing causes another, or, is caused by another.
💡 Most tested logical concept in GMAT CR stimuli. What is Causality? Causality occurs when one event is said to make another occur. The cause is the event that makes the other occur; the effect is the event that follows from the cause. By definition, the cause must occur before the effect.
Recognizing Causality Cause & Effect relationship has a signature characteristics- a single cause makes the effect happen. Thus there is an identifiable type of expression used to indicate a causal relationship
🚨Causality Indicator Words🚨 caused by determined by because of produced by responsible for product of leads to played a role in induced by was a factor in promoted by is an effect of Causality in Conclusion Vs. Premise If the causal statement is the conclusion, then the reasoning is flawed.
💡More frequent in GMAT
Premise: In North America, people drink a lot of milk. Premise: There is a high frequency of cancer in North-America. Conclusion: Therefore, drinking milk causes cancer. If the causal statement is the premise, then the argument maybe flawed, but not because of the causal statement.
Premise: Drinking milk causes cancer. Premise: The residents of North America drink a lot of milk. Conclusion: Therefore, in North America there is a high frequency of cancer among residents. So it’s important to determine where in the argument the causal assertion is made.
Errors of Causality One event occurs before the other: An event occurring before another event doesn’t imply the 1st one caused the 2nd one. Every morning the rooster crows before the sun rises. Hence, the rooster must cause the sun to rise. ≥Two events occurring at the same time: The two events could be the result of a third event. Or they could be simply correlated without one causing the other The consumption of ice cream has been found to correlate with the murder rate. Therefore, consuming ice cream must cause one to be more likely to commit murder. 💡 These events might be explained as effects of a single cause, hot weather. When the weather is warmer, ice cream consumption & the murder rate tend to rise.
🙂This is actually true for larger cities🙂
Central Assumption of Causal Conclusions Unlike real life scenarios, a GMAT speaker concludes that the stated cause is the only possible cause of the effect & that consequently the stated cause will always produce the effect.
This is because, he or she must have weighed and considered every possible alternative and then rejected each one.
💡 Thus, in every argument with a causal conclusion that appears on the GMAT, the speaker believes that the stated cause is in fact the only cause and all other theoretically possible causes are not actual causes. Examples In the last five years there has been a significant increase in the consumption of red wine. During this same period, there have been several major news reports about the beneficial long-term effects on health that certain antioxidants in red wine can provide. Thus, the increase in red wine consumption can be directly attributed to consumers’ recognition of the beneficial effects of antioxidants.
Which of the following, if true, most seriously undermines the explanation above?
(A) Sales of other alcoholic beverages have not increased in the last five years.
(B) On average, people consume about 10 percent more red wine than they did five years ago
(C) The health benefits of red wine are usually not noticeable for several years
(D) The consumption of grape juice and other antioxidant-rich products has also increased in the last five years.
(E) Red wine prices have decreased significantly in the last five years, while the prices of other alcoholic beverages have risen steadily.
Solution (E) (A)- Doesn’t hurt the conclusion. It only states that this rise in red wine consumption is unusual. Doesn’t hurt the fact that news coverage is responsible for this increase.
(B)- Agrees with the first premise. So not hurting the conclusion
(C)- Out of scope
(D)- Additional effect to the cause in the stimulus that is not weakening the cause
(E)- The answer is presenting an alternate cause for the increase in wine consumption, namely price drop.
The gill lining of lobsters in which the disease-causing parasite An. haemophila resides is completely regenerated every 30 days. The An. haemophila parasite typically produces moderate discoloration of the gills of infected lobsters, and can occasionally lead to more chronic symptoms. However, because these parasites cannot transfer directly from infected gill lining to newly generated gill lining in their host lobster, any discoloration appearing on the gills of lobsters more than 30 days after they have been moved to parasite-free water is not due to infection by An. haemophila.
Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the argument above?
(A) Other parasites are found more frequently in lobsters than An. haemophila.
(B) Lobsters that remain in parasite-rich waters can be re-infected by new An. haemophila parasites once newly generated gill lining has been produced.
(C) An. haemophila can also cause digestive and respiratory distress in infected lobsters.
(D) In some cases An. haemophila migrates from the gill lining to the stomach, where it can then re-infect its original host.
(E) Once infected by a particular parasite, lobsters frequently develop a strong immunity to that parasite allowing them to better resist re-infection.
Solution (D) The stimulus suggests that the disease can’t go from old to new gill lining, so it seems that continued gill infections must be cause by some other factor. To weaken this argument, we are looking for an answer choice that shows how the disease could possibly re-infect a lobster and cause gill discoloration
(A)- Supports the causal relationship
(B)- “parasite-rich” water is irrelevant
(C)- Other problems of the disease are irrelevant
(D)- This is the correct answer since it provides an alternate explanation for the disease being present in the lobsters even though it doesn’t get transferred from old → new gills
(E)- Actually strengthens the argument
summary:
Cause & effect reasoning asserts/denies whether one thing causes another, or, is caused by another.
💡 Most tested logical concept in GMAT CR stimuli. What is Causality? Causality occurs when one event is said to make another occur. The cause is the event that makes the other occur; the effect is the event that follows from the cause. By definition, the cause must occur before the effect.
Recognizing Causality Cause & Effect relationship has a signature characteristics- a single cause makes the effect happen.
title: CR-7 Cause and Effect Reasoning

contents:
Weaken type question require you to select the answer choice that undermines the author’s argument as decisively as possible.
💡 MOST FREQUENT CR QUESTION TYPE Rules to Solve The stimulus will always contain an argument since you are asked to weaken the author’s reasoning. Focus on the conclusion. The more we know about its specifics, the better armed we will be to deciding the correct answer choice. Information in the stimulus is suspect. Read the arguments carefully 🚨Pre-phase possible answers🚨 before proceeding to the answer choices 💡Answer choices are accepted as given even if they include “new information”. Identifying Question Type The stem uses words synonymous to “weaken”. “Weaken” synonyms: Weaken, Attack, Undermine, Refute, Call into question, cast doubt, challenge, damage, counter The stem usually indicates that you should accept the answer choices as true Examples
“Which of the following if true, most seriously weakens the argument above?” “Which of the following, if true, casts the most doubt on the conclusion drawn above?” “Which of the following, if true, most calls into question the claim above?” Which of the following, if true, is most damaging to the conclusion above? “Which of the following, if known, is evidence that contradicts the hypothesis above?” “Which of the following, if discovered, would be evidence against the speculation above?” How to weaken an argument The key is to weaken not attack the conclusion
🚨 Attack is not the same as destroy (Attack ≠ Destroy). The answer choices most likely hurt the argument.
When evaluating an answer ask “Would this answer choice make the author reconsider his/her position or force the author to respond?”
The Premises Attacking the premises on which the conclusion rests. Rare occurrence since it makes easy spotting of answer choice.
💡 One of the few rare times a premise is attacked, it is a sub-conclusion that is being used to drive in the main conclusion 🚨The Conclusion🚨 Most likely. But the correct answer choice doesn’t simply contradict the conclusion. But rather, it often does so by showing that the conclusion fails to account for some element or possibility.
How to attack a causal conclusion There are a few ways you can prepare to weaken/strengthen a causal relationship in GMAT:
Cause_2 → Effect Find an alternate cause for the stated effect Cause → Effect Show that even when the cause occurs, the effect does not occur Cause → Effect Show that though the effect occurred, the cause did not Effect → Cause Show that the stated relationship is reversed Stat Problem: Show that a statistical problem exists with the data used to make the causal statement Common Weakening Scenarios The most common logical fallacies that are to be attacked in GMAT Weaken question stimuli are:
Incomplete Information: When author fails to consider all possibilities or relies upon incomplete evidence. This flaw is attacked by bringing up new possibilities or information. Improper Comparison: Author attempts to compare items that are essentially different Overly Broad Comparison: The author draws a conclusion that is broader than the premises support. Answer Traps Opposite Answers: These answer choices are presenting information that relates perfectly to the arguments, but instead of weakening it, they are strengthening it. Shell Game Answers: The idea/concept raised is similar to the ones present in the stimulus but changed enough to be incorrect yet attractive. Out of Scope Answers: They simply miss the point of the argument & raise unrelated/tangential issues to the argument Practice Solution (D) The stimulus presents the somewhat unusual payment system proposed by Axis Auto. The question stem asks us to attack the proposed payment system.
(A)- Nothing to do with the amount of money drivers of starlight are likely to spend on gas
(B)- Irrelevant since the question stem asks us to attack the Axis Auto proposal. Other manufacturers’ plan in coming years has nothing to do with the proposal
(C)- This actually benefits Axis since greater miles in the fuel-efficient car implies greater savings on gas & thus increasing their profits.
(D)- This is the correct answer. If the price of gasoline increases over 3 years, the overall difference in cost will be reduced.
(E)- No real affect on the proposal.
Solution (D) For the statement to be appealing to state schools, the legislators have assumed that they find greater financial benefits in getting the tax incentives
(A)- The fact doesn’t attack the presumed tax benefits in any way
(B)- A comparison of private & public school’s tuition costs does not undermine the proposed benefits
(C)- This actually supports the legislators’ beliefs by showing the difficulty applicants face in attending out-of-state universities.
(D)- This is the correct choice since it questions upon the supposed tax benefits being more financially viable than taking in other state applicants.
(E)- Supports the argument by presenting facts in favor of increasing in-state applicants.
summary:
Weaken type question require you to select the answer choice that undermines the author’s argument as decisively as possible.
💡 MOST FREQUENT CR QUESTION TYPE Rules to Solve The stimulus will always contain an argument since you are asked to weaken the author’s reasoning. Focus on the conclusion. The more we know about its specifics, the better armed we will be to deciding the correct answer choice. Information in the stimulus is suspect.
title: CR-6 Weaken

contents:
Main Point questions ask you to summarize the author’s point of view.
Fact Test + Main Point As with Must Be True, Main point answer choice must pass the fact test. But apart from that it should also capture the author’s point.
Applying the primary objectives, we’ll most likely have selected the conclusion of the stimulus already. So we have the main point with us already.
Identify Primary Conclusion → Find paraphrase of conclusion in answer choices
🚨 Many Main Point problems will place the conclusion either at the beginning or at the end. Its not necessary to have them at the end of the stimulus. Question Stem Format The format is fairly consistent with the primary feature being a request to identify the conclusion or point of the argument.
“Which of the following most accurately expresses the main conclusion of the argument?” “Which of the following most accurately expresses the conclusion of the argument?” “Which of the following most accurately restates the main point of the passage?” “The main point of the argument is that” 🚨Incorrect Answer Choices🚨 There are 2 types of incorrect answer choices that often appear in GMAT:
True but don’t encapsulate the author’s point Repeat premises of the argument Fill In the Blanks Relatively rare. In these instances, the blank is preceded by a conclusion indicator (as opposed to the usual premise indicator)
“Therefore ______” “Hence in the coming years, the rate of economic growth will likely ___________” “Thus, from the politician’s statements, we can conclude ________________” Difference from other question types The indicator modifying the blank is often a premise indicator (such as “because” or “since”) The question stem contains language indicating you are completing a different task, such as strengthening the argument. Formula Read the stimulus for clues revealing the direction of the argument and the author’s intent.
summary:
Main Point questions ask you to summarize the author’s point of view.
Fact Test + Main Point As with Must Be True, Main point answer choice must pass the fact test. But apart from that it should also capture the author’s point.
Applying the primary objectives, we’ll most likely have selected the conclusion of the stimulus already. So we have the main point with us already.
Identify Primary Conclusion → Find paraphrase of conclusion in answer choices
title: CR-5 Main Point

contents:
Must Be True Must by True questions require you to select an answer choice that is proven by the stimulus information.
The correct answer choice maybe:
Paraphrase of the stimulus Logical consequence of one or more parts of the stimulus Fact Test The correct answer choice can always be proven by referring to the facts stated in the stimulus. Any answer choice we select should not be selected on the basis that it “sounds good”.
🚨 Do not bring in information from outside the stimulus (however true it may be in your experience) aside from commonsense assumptions. Question Stem types The stem often indicates the information in the stimulus should be taken as true which indicates towards the 1st family of Qs: “If the statements above are true…” “The statements above, if true…” “If the information above is correct…” Following this, the stem asks you to identify a single answer choice that is proven or supported: “…which of the following must be true?” “…which of the following conclusions can be properly drawn on the basis of it?” “…most strongly support which of the following?” “Which of the following can be properly inferred…” 💡 “infer” means “must be true” only in the GMAT context E.g. “If the statements above are true, which of the following must be true?” “Which of the following conclusions are best supported by the statements above?” “The statements above, if true, best support which of the following assertions?” “Which of the following can be correctly inferred from the statements above?” “Which of the following is most strongly supported by the information above?” The Correct Answer Choice Stimulus Information There are essentially 4 classifications of information in the stimulus that can be used to prove an answer choice correct:
Actual statements of the Stimulus Commonsense Assumptions Consequences of the statements presented in the stimulus: Combining the statements from stimulus to produce a technically new statement, but 1 that is supported clearly & directly by the stimulus Info under the “umbrella” of the stimulus: Elements that are not explicitly mentioned in the stimulus but pertain to its broader topic of discussion. Scope of Answer Statements Broad Scope: some, could, many Narrow Scope: only, none -ve & Double -ve Let’s go the the store→ +ve Let’s not go to the store→ -ve Let’s never not go to the store→ 2 -ve = 1 +ve Mary is not un-happy→ 2 -ve = 1 +ve Incorrect Answers (Attractive but not Correct) Could be/Likely to be True: These are particularly tricky since there’s nothing wrong with the answers. But these answer choices fail the Fact Test Exaggerated Answers: These answers take the statements from stimulus and stretch the info beyond its scope “New” Information Answers: They may seem an extension of the stimulus info and are logical too, but are not explicitly mentioned in the stimulus. They can be checked by: Examining the scope of the argument to make sure the “new” info is within the range of a term or concept in stimulus Examining the answer to make sure it is not the consequence of combining stimulus elements The Shell Game: Idea similar to the one raised in stimulus but is incorrect The Opposite Answer: They are opposite to what the stimulus says. These are very attractive to the fast/careless reader The Reverse Answer: Using familiar elements from the stimulus but rearranging the elements to make it incorrect. Again a trap for fast readers. E.G.: “Many people have some type of security system in their home”
Reversed: “Some people have many types of security systems in their home”
Stimulus Opinions Vs. Assertions When a stimulus contains only the opinions of others, then in a Must Be True question, we can eliminate any answer choice that makes a flat assertion without reference to those opinions. No factual statement can be concluded in such a case
Similarly if a stimulus contains only facts, then answer choices presenting an opinion can also be eliminated
Conditional Reasoning These are logical relationships composed of sufficient and necessary conditions
Sufficient Condition (SC): An event or circumstance whose occurrence indicates that a necessary condition must also occur.
Necessary Condition (NC): An event whose occurrence is required in order for a sufficient condition to occur.
Indicator Words for conditions
Sufficient Condition Necessary Condition If Then When Only Whenever Only if Every Must All Requried Any Unless Each Except In order to Until People who Without Eg. “If someone gets an A+ on a test, then they must have studied for the test”
Necessary: Must have studied Sufficient: get an A+ Logical Features of Conditional Reasoning SC -/> NC: SC doesn’t make the NC happen Order of SC & NC not fixed The conditional relationship DOES NOT have to reflect reality. Our job is not to figure out what’s reasonable and what’s not. We just have to capture the meaning of author’s sentence. Valid/Invalid Statements Simple Formula To Remember Original Statement: A→B
Valid Statements
A → B (Valid Repeat) B → A (Contrapositive) Invalid Statements
B → A (Mistaken Reversal) A → B (Mistaken Negation) Explanation & Example Valid Repeat: A paraphrasing of the original statement John received an A+ on the test, so he must have studied for the test. Mistaken Reversal: Switches the elements in the sufficient and necessary conditions John studied for the test, so he must have received an A+ on the test. Mistaken Negation: It negates both conditions, creating a statement that doesn’t have to be true John did not receive an A+ on the test, so he must no have studied for the test. Contrapositive: It both reverses & negates the original statement. Hence by being a combination of 2 -ve, the statement becomes a +ve of sorts & hence is valid John didn’t study for the test, so he must not have received an A+ on the test Explanation & Example Practice Identify SC → NC & write it’s contrapositive in both NC → SC format & in words
The children go to the park when the sun is shining Solution SS → CP (”when” is coming before sun shining)
Contrapositive: CP → SS
If the children do not go to the park, then the sun must not be shining
Only the good die young Solution DY → G
Contrapositive: G → DY
If you’re not good, then you will not be dying young
I will be on time if I get the car by noon Solution CN → OT
Contrapositive: OT → CN
If I did not arrive on time, then I will not have gotten the car by noon
Practice --- primary_color: green secondary_color: lightgray text_color: black shuffle_questions: false shuffle_answers: false --- ## Practice Headaches can often be effectively relieved by over-the-counter medication, without necessitating a physician’s oversight. However, doctors warn against employment of this simple strategy for recurring or particularly long-lasting headaches, even if such medication can provide relief. Since such headaches are often symptomatic of more serious maladies, sufferers are strongly advised instead to consult their physicians. > Try solving it :) 1. [ ] (A) The greater the pain associated with a particular headache, the more serious the underlying cause > Correct Answer: (B) 2. [x] (B) In some cases physicians advise against seeking immediate relief from pain. 3. [ ] (C) Some headaches cannot be relieved with over-the-counter medications > Correct Answer: (B) 4. [ ] (D) Physicians tend to focus less on pain relief rather than on other physicalsymptoms > Correct Answer: (B) 5. [ ] (E) Over-the-counter medication cannot provide effective relief of a headache if the underlying cause is a serious malady > Correct Answer: (B) ## Practice Last year, the government of country A imposed large tariffs on steel imports in an effort to aid its domestic steel industry. Many domestic steel producers enjoyed record profits as a result, as foregin steel producers were in many cases unable to compete effectively under the burden of the newly imposed tarriffs. > Try solving it :)) 1. [x] (A) Not all steel producers were unaffected by country A's newly imposed tariffs. 2. [ ] (B) Some foreign steel producers were able to compete effectively in country A even after the new tariffs were imposed > Correct Answer: (A) 3. [ ] (C) After the new tariffs were imposed, most foreign steel producers were unable to compete effectively with country A's domestice steel producers > Correct Answer: (A) 4. [ ] (D) Most domestic steel producers were able to increase their profits adfter the new tariffs were imposed > Correct Answer: (A) 5. [ ] (E) If a government intends to protect a domestic industry, the imposition of tariffs on imports is generally an effective approach > Correct Answer: (A) ## Practice Blood tests used to determine pregnancy can at times be inconclusive. This means that the test has been unable to determine if the woman is pregnant or not pregnant. Regardless, some doctors refuse to perform further surgeries because of an inconclusive pregnancy test result.
If the statements above are true, which of the following conclusions is most strongly supported by them? > Try solving it :)) 1. [ ] (A) Pregnancy tests should always be given prior to surgery > Correct Answer: (C) 2. [ ] (B) Most women with inconclusive pregnancy tests are actually pregnant > Correct Answer: (C) 3. [x] (C) Some surgical procedures are affected by whether or not a woman is pregnant 4. [ ] (D) Some doctors require a pregnancy test be given when evaluating female candidates for any surgery > Correct Answer: (C) 5. [ ] (E) A pregnancy test that returns a negative result is sometimes incorrect > Correct Answer: (C) Explanations Q1- Explnanation (B) The idea in this stimulus is that while headaches can often be relieved by certain types of medication, headaches that are recurring or long-lasting can indicate a more serious condition, and doctors recommend that the sufferer seek medical attention instead of relief from over-the-counter medicine. As we consider what would be proven true from these statements, it is important to not go beyond the information and facts presented, or make unwarranted assumptions beyond what the stimulus directly supports.
Answer choice (A): There is no information in the stimulus that correlates the severity of headache pain with the seriousness of the cause of the headache.
Answer choice (B): This is the correct answer. The stimulus indicates that while a person may be able to find relief from a recurring or long-lasting headache through the use of medication, physicians recommend that in these situations (hence the “in some cases”) the sufferer should instead seek medical attention and not self-medicate (i.e. not seek immediate relief from the pain). Note that this answer can be easy to overlook because it goes against some commonly-held notions of physicians being obligated to attempt to quickly alleviate pain.
Answer choice (C): All that we know from the information presented is that “headaches can often be relieved by over the-counter medication” (italics added for emphasis). There is nothing that suggests then that some headaches cannot be relieved by these same medications. Be careful not to assume that “some cannot” just because you are not explicitly told “all can.”
Answer choice (D): This answer choice is simply much too broad to be completely supported by the information in the stimulus. In the specific instance of a recurring/long-lasting headache, physicians may be focused more on the underlying cause than on immediate pain relief, but that does not necessarily mean that physicians in general focus less on pain relief (in all instances) than on other physical symptoms.
Answer choice (E): This answer choice contradicts the information given, so it is an Opposite answer and is correct. The stimulus states that over-thecounter medications could potentially provide relief for a headache caused by something more serious (“even if such medication can provide relief”); the point is that physicians suggest sufferers forego immediate relief and seek to instead establish the underlying cause of the headache.
Q2- Explanation (A) This stimulus presents a fairly straightforward scenario: the tariffs imposed on steel imports by the government of country A have helped domestic steel producers become more competitive and make record profits, because in most cases foreign producers were unable to compete under the new tariffs. Note that there is no conclusion present, and so this is a fact set, not an argument.
Answer choice (A): This is the correct answer. Since we know that domestic steel producers benefited positively from the tariffs, and foreign steel producers were affected negatively, it must be true that not all steel producers were unaffected. Worded another way, answer choice A states “some steel producers were affected by…[the] tariffs.”
As an aside, if, upon reading (A), you determined this was a strong answer, should you have simply selected it and skipped reading the remaining answers? No, because perhaps you misread (A), or perhaps there is a better answer among the remaining choices. The only reason not to read the remaining choices would be if you were just about out of time.
Answer choice (B): The stimulus tells us that “foreign steel producers were in many cases unable to compete effectively under the burden of the newly imposed tariffs.” This does not imply that some foreign steel producers were able to compete effectively. Be careful not to assume that “some could compete” just because you are not explicitly told “all could not compete.” Although this answer choice Could Be True, it does not have to be true.
Answer choice (C): Again, we know that in many cases foreign steel producers could not compete effectively, but we cannot know anything about the percentage of foreign steel producers that could not compete effectively. Again, this answer choice Could Be True, but it does not have to be true.
Answer choice (D): The stimulus states that “many” domestic steel producers enjoyed record profits, but “many” does not necessarily equal “most” (or “majority”). Logically speaking, “many” simply means “some,” unless you have further information about the overall number under discussion (more on this in Chapter Twelve).
Answer choice (E): This answer choice is much too broad in scope to be completely supported by the information in the stimulus. We know that in country A these particular tariffs helped to aid the domestic steel industry, but that is not sufficient evidence to prove that tariffs are “generally an effective approach” to protecting any domestic industry in any country
Q3- Explanation (C) This question presents a fact set that begins by telling us that blood tests are, at times, unable to conclusively determine if a woman is pregnant or not. Then we are told that some doctors refuse to perform certain surgeries due to an inconclusive pregnancy test result. These facts indicate that definitive knowledge of whether or not a patient is pregnant must have a potential impact on some surgical procedures. Hence, doctors will not operate on that patient without conclusive pregnancy tests results. Note too that we cannot know the number or type of surgical procedures that could potentially be affected by pregnancy; we simply know that some doctors will not perform further surgeries following an inconclusive test result.
Answer choice (A): This answer choice is far too broad in scope to be proven by the stimulus. Just because some surgical procedures may be potentially impacted by a patient’s pregnancy does not mean that a pregnancy test should precede all surgeries. Further, by the logic presented in answer choice (A), all patients—men and women—should be given pregnancy tests prior to any surgery, and clearly the stimulus does not support that conclusion.
Answer choice (B): There is no information in the stimulus to indicate the frequency with which inconclusive tests turn out to ultimately be positive or negative.
Answer choice (C): This is the correct answer. As stated above, the facts of the stimulus support the notion that whether or not a patient is pregnant could potentially affect a surgical procedure to be performed on that patient.
Answer choice (D): This answer choice has very strong language, as it states that some doctors require a pregnancy test prior to “any surgery.” However we cannot know that some doctors require a pregnancy test before every surgery they perform on a female candidate; we only know that some doctors refuse to perform certain surgeries following an inconclusive test result. Always be wary of extreme or absolute language in answer choice for Must Be True questions, since it takes similarly strong language in a stimulus to prove a strongly worded answer choice correct.
Answer choice (E): The stimulus is exclusively about inconclusive (nonresult) pregnancy tests, so an answer choice that addresses tests producing a “negative result” goes beyond the facts provided in the stimulus.
summary:
Must Be True Must by True questions require you to select an answer choice that is proven by the stimulus information.
The correct answer choice maybe:
Paraphrase of the stimulus Logical consequence of one or more parts of the stimulus Fact Test The correct answer choice can always be proven by referring to the facts stated in the stimulus. Any answer choice we select should not be selected on the basis that it “sounds good”.
title: CR-4 Must Be True

contents:
The question stem poses a question directed at the stimulus. It specifies the task you must perform in order to get credit for the problem.
Question Stem Tasks Identify details of the stimulus Describe the structure of the argument Strengthen or weaken the argument Identify inferences, main points, and assumptions Recognize errors of reasoning Reconcile conflicts Find arguments that are identical in nature Analyze the Question Stem It’s important to correctly classify the question into one of the types since that’ll help us in deciding which part of the stimulus to focus on. It allows us to proceed quickly with confidence and sometimes, even help in determining the correct answer before reading any options.
Legend: V. Important Important Not that frequent
🚨 10 Critical Reasoning Question Types (& example Q-Stems) Must Be True/ Most Supported “If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?” “Which of the following can be properly inferred from the passage?” Main Point “The main point of the argument is that” Assumption “The argument in the passage relies on which of the following assumptions?” Strengthen/Support “Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?” “Which of the following, if true, would most strongly support the position above?” Resolve the Paradox “Which of the following, if true, would most help to explain the rise in revenues last year?” Weaken “Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above?” Method of Reasoning “Which of the following describes how the argument above is developed?” Flaw in Reasoning “The reasoning in the chemist’s argument is flawed primarily because of which argument?” Parallel Reasoning “Which of the following arguments is most similar in its pattern of reasoning to the argument above?” Evaluate the Argument “The answer to which of the following questions would contribute most to an evaluation of the argument” Family of Questions ABSOLUTE TRUTH → SUSPISION (Flow of Information): The part at the end of the arrow is affected/determined. Whatever is given before the arrow is simply accepted.
Prove (Stimulus → Answer Choices) Use the information in the stimulus to select one of the answer choices. Any answer choice that does not appear either directly in the stimulus or as a combination of items in the stimulus is incorrect Must be True, Main Point, Method of Reasoning, Flaw in Reasoning, Parallel Reasoning Help (Answer Choices → Stimulus) Based on helping the author’s argument in some way. Identify reasoning errors or “gaps” in the stimulus. These will be shored up by the answer choices New information in the answer choices is alright. Assumption, Strengthen, Resolve the paradox Hurt (Answer choices -/> Stimulus) Based on attacking the author’s argument. Approach is the same as Help. But here we try expanding upon/pointing out the “gaps”. Weaken Identifying the Question Type Must be True and Resolve the Paradox are frequently connected to stimuli that do not contain conclusions. Then based on whether there’s a paradox or not we can decide between Resolve the Paradox & Must be True. Practice “Which of the following, if true, most helps to explain the viewpoint described above?” Solution Resolve the Paradox Given the statement must “explain”, the question is from the Assumption, Strengthen, and Resolve the paradox. The idea of explaining is most closely aligned with Resolving the Paradox. “Which of the following is most like the argument above in its logical structure?” Solution Parallel Reasoning “The argument is flawed because it” Solution Flaw in Reasoning “The advertisement proceeds by” Solution Method of Reasoning “Mary challenges Shaun’s reasoning by” Solution Method of Reasoning Although the questions “challenges”, this is not a Weaken question since the stem asks for a description of the way Shaun’s reasoning was challenged. Thus you’re asked to identify Mary’s method of reasoning. “The answer to which of the following questions would most help in evaluating the philosopher’s argument?” Solution Evaluate the Argument ”Except” and “Least” in Q Stem (QuestionX type) These question are specially encountered in the higher-difficulty problems.
EXCEPT “except” in a Q literally turns the intent of the Q stem upside down. 🚨 QUESTIONX ≠ OPPOSITE QUESTION (eg. WEAKENX ≠ STRENGTHEN)
Having an “except” in the Q stem doesn’t mean we have to find the polar opposite. For eg. “Each of the following statements weaken the argument EXCEPT”.
A “Weaken EXCEPT” question does not ask you to find the statement that strengthen the argument. We just have to choose other than weaken.
LEAST (treat the same as “except”) “least” has a similar effect. Although it’s meaning differs from “except”, treat it the same as “except”. This is so because if the test-makers allow all the options to weaken to a certain degree, it leaves room for challenging them. 💡 For both “except” & “least”, there will be 4 options that will clearly meet the stated criteria (weaken, strengthen, resolve, etc.) and the 1 correct answer will not. Practice “Which one of the following, if all of them are true, is LEAST helpful in establishing that the conclusion above is properly drawn?” Solution StrengthenX ““Which one of the following, if true, does NOT help to resolve the apparent discrepancy between the two surveys discussed?” Solution ResolveX Answer Choices Pre-phasing Answers It’s highly effective in quickly finding correct answer choices and avoiding incorrect answer choices. Prephasing also helps you keep alert and in touch with the elements of the problem.
🚨 Always read all the answer choices Contenders Vs. Losers Sort the answer choices into contenders & losers.
Contender: Answer choices that seem somewhat attractive, interesting or even confusing Losers: Answer choices that strike as incorrect. Deciding the final Ans As we encounter a contender, we check it’s merit against the previous best contender. This process may seem tedious, but keeps us on our toes & saves time in the long run.
🚨 NEVER DWELL ON THE PREVIOUS QUESTION!!!!! Fill In the Blank Questions We will occasionally encounter questions where the stimulus is preceded by the question stem & then the stimulus ends with an underlines blank space.
The question stem in these problems directs you to fill in the blank with the answer that best completes the passage.
These often throw the test-takers a curveball since it seems new. But these are in fact Main Point, Must Be True, Strengthen, Assumption… question types.
summary:
The question stem poses a question directed at the stimulus. It specifies the task you must perform in order to get credit for the problem.
Question Stem Tasks Identify details of the stimulus Describe the structure of the argument Strengthen or weaken the argument Identify inferences, main points, and assumptions Recognize errors of reasoning Reconcile conflicts Find arguments that are identical in nature Analyze the Question Stem It’s important to correctly classify the question into one of the types since that’ll help us in deciding which part of the stimulus to focus on.
title: CR-3 The Question Stem and Answer Choices

contents:
Steps Decide upon the broad topic Read it VERY carefully to capture all the details Identify the problems between each part of the stimulus Don’t panic if you don’t understand a terminology. Focus on understanding the how the ideas connect with each other Speaker Identifiers Pay attention to the speaker. Often it will help in identifying the broad topic addressed in the stimulus Business: Manager, Executive, Business Plan Politics: Politician, Editorial, Lawmaker, Activist Science/Medicine: Doctor, Physician, Scientist, Researcher Some identifiers are confusing or too broad. For example a columnist can write about a lot of topics. But they will almost always be opinionated. Some identifiers setup a direct expectation: An “advertisement” in front of the stimulus almost always means presence of flawed reasoning. Same goes for arguments by politicians Challenging GMAT Vocabulary Advanced Words: No need to study list of words separately. It’s a rare occurrence & even then it’s decipherable from the context. Scientific Jargon: Definition/usage information will be supplied in the stimulus, often in the vicinity of the word. No need to guess the meaning. Focus on the entire message and use the most relevant parts Stimuli Categories Facts: Lack of a strong reaction on reading a statement generally points to it being a fact Arguments (IMP): Set of statements that are derived from the others. Argument Vs. Facts in Stimulus To know this we must recognize whether a conclusion exists. For this we must identify the parts of a sentence, namely 2:
Conclusion: The point the author tries to prove by using another statement Questions to ask for identifying conclusion - What is the author driving at? - What does the author want me to believe? - What point follows from the others? Premise: A fact, proposition or statement from which a conclusion is made. It gives a reason for believing something Questions to ask for identifying premise - What reasons has the author used to persuade me? - Why should I believe this argument? - What evidence exists? Identifying the Conclusion There are certain indicator words before premises & conclusions that help in differentiating between the 2.
🚨 REMEMBER + REVISE THESE INDICATORS Premise Vs. Conclusion Indicators Premise Indicators Conclusion Indicators because thus since therefore for qhence for example consequently for the reason that as a result in that so given that accordingly as indicated by clearly due to must be that owing to shows that this can be seen from conclude that we know this by follow that for this reason NOTE: Premises & Conclusion can be constructed without these indicator words also.
💡 A Confusing Form (conclusion/premise) In this, the conclusion & the premise indicators are placed back-to-back E.g. 1. “Therefore, since…” 2. “Thus, because…” 3. “Hence, due to…” Solution to conclusion/premise form Here PREMISE is present after this clause. Following this a 2nd comma will be present which will mark the start of conclusion E.g.: “Therefore, since higher debt has forced consumers to lower their savings, banks now have less money to loan.”
Solution Premise “Higher debt has forced consumers to lower their savings”
Conclusion “banks now have less money to loan”
💡 A call for action (“we should…”, “they must…”, etc.) is also a conclusion statement In matters where you are stuck, use the “why test” to the argument to identify the conclusion.
🚨 The “Why Test” (RAM BAAN ILAAJ) USE IT ON A REQUIRED BASIS ONLY
Ask “why” for statements in the argument. The statement that is answered by the argument for the “why” is the conclusion.
Additional Premise The extra premise that an author adds after an argument that supports the conclusion but is sometimes not needed.
Additional PremiseIndicators Additional Premise Indicators Furthermore Moreover Besides In Addition What more After all Counter-Premise (Adversatives) A premise that the author brings up opposing the argument. This is done to address the complain and minimize the damage if it were raised elsewhere.
Counter-Premise Indicators Counter-Premise Indicators But Yet However On the other hand Admittedly In contrast Although Even though Still Whereas In Spite of Despite After all Exercise Wine is made by crushing grapes and eventually separating the juice from the grape skins. However, the separated juice contains impurities and many wineries do not filter the juice. These wineries claim the unfiltered juice ultimately produces a more flavorful and intense wine. Since these wine makers are experts, we should trust their judgment and not shy away from unfiltered wine. Solution Features the counter-premise indicator “however” and the premise indicator “since.”
Premise: “Wine is made by crushing grapes and eventually separating the juice from the grape skins.” Counter-Premise: “However, the separated juice contains impurities and many wineries do not filter the juice.” Premise: “These wineries claim the unfiltered juice ultimately produces a more flavorful and intense wine.” Premise: “Since these wine makers are experts,” Conclusion: “we should trust their judgment and not shy away from unfiltered wine.” Phenylketonurics are people who cannot metabolize the amino acid phenylalanine. There are dangers associated with phenylketonuria, and products containing phenylalanine must carry a warning label that states, “Phenylketonurics: contains phenylalanine.” In addition, all children in developed societies receive a phenylketonuria test at birth. Hence, at the moment, we are doing as much as possible to protect against this condition. Solution Premise: “Phenylketonurics are people who cannot metabolize the amino acid phenylalanine.” Premise: “There are dangers associated with phenylketonuria, and products containing phenylalanine must carry a warning label that states, ‘Phenylketonurics: contains phenylalanine.’” Additional Premise: “In addition, all children in developed societies received a phenylketonuria test at birth.” Conclusion: “Hence, at the moment, we are doing as much as possible to protect against this condition.” 💡 Recognizing Conclusions Without Indicators Force the parts of the argument to be the premise & conclusion using indicator words & see if it’s making logical sense. If not, reverse the premise-conclusion & check again E.g.- The best way of eliminating traffic congestion will not be easily found. There are so many competing possibilities that it will take millions of dollars to study every option, and implementation of most options carries an exorbitant price tag.
Methodology Let the first sentence be the premise & the 2nd one be the conclusion.
Because the best way of eliminating traffic congestion will not be easily found, we can conclude that there are so many competing possibilities that it will take millions of dollars to study every option, and implementation of most options carries an exorbitant price tag.
This is not making sense so let’s reverse it
Because there are so many competing possibilities that it will take millions of dollars to study every option, and implementation of most options carries an exorbitant price tag, we can conclude that the best way of eliminating traffic congestion will not be easily found.
This is making sense & is the solution
Complex Arguments Contain more than one conclusion. 1 main & other sub-conclusions. The author uses 1 conclusion as base for other conclusion.
E.g.- Because Germany has the best goalkeeper in soccer, they therefore have the best defense in soccer. Because they have the best defense in football, they will win the next World Cup.
Solution Premise: “Because Germany has the best goalkeeper in soccer,” Sub-Conclusion (conclusion of the previous premise/Premise for the following conclusion): “they therefore have the best defense in soccer.” Main Conclusion: “they will win the next World Cup.” Decoding Text Killer Sentences Basically kuch nahi chamkega ki kaun kya bol raha hain.
E.g- The survival strategies of various classes of vertebrates demonstrate that, contrary to the claims of early zoologists, physical similarity does not produce strategic similarity: for example, the strategies that an electric eel uses to defend itself from attack are significantly different than those used by the hagfish, an eel-like fish also known as a slime eel, but both manifest the same basic morphological appearance to predators.
Solutions Steps:
Slow Down Break the sentence into smaller components. USE PUNCTUATION MARKS FOR THIS Comma [,]: Separate Ideas Colon [:]: Precedes explanation/list Semi-colon [;]: Independent ideas that could stand on their own too. Comma on steroids. Parentheses [()]: Additional info to the sentence Dash [—]: Extremely versatile. Typically sets apart ideas. Reference Words Refer back/forward to ideas in the stimulus without explicitly naming the idea. Makes the writing more compact.
Examples:
Opponents of solar power argue that the high cost of technology makes solar a non-feasible solution on a large scale basis, especially in rural areas where the power grid is very widely distributed. But this is not true, because in Germany—one of the world’s leaders in solar energy production—some of the largest solar arrays are built in rural areas. Solution This refers to “the high cost of technology makes solar a non-feasible solution on a large scale basis.” Those students who have exceeded the academic standards in our school will be honored at a ceremony next month. Solution Those students refers to the students who have have “exceeded the academic standards in our school” and who “will be honored at a ceremony next month,” Truth Vs Validity Validity (IMP): Logical Relationship of the pieces of argument.
Truthfulness: Absolute truth about whether the stuff happens in the real world.
🚨 Just focus on whether the conclusion is true RELATIVE to the premises. Argument Analysis If the stimulus contains an argument, we have to determine whether the argument is strong or weak.
Inferences & Assumptions Different from the general sense. Have to see in the GMAT argumentation context.
Inference An item that must be true based on the information in the argument.
Assumptions A premise that must be true in order for the argument to be true.
🚨 Know precisely what the author said. DON’T GENERALIZE! Quantity Vs. Probability Indicators Quantity Indicators Probability Indicators all must every will most always many not always some probably several likely few would sole not necessarily only could not all rarely none never Exercise For the given stimulus, answer the following questions:
What is the conclusion of the argument if any? What premises are given in support of this conclusion? Is the argument strong or weak? If you think that the argument is weak, please explain why. Admittedly, the practice of allowing students to retake a class they previously failed and receive a new grade is controversial. But the mission of any school or university is to educate their students, and allowing students to retake courses supports this mission. Therefore, for the time being, our school should continue to allow students to retake previously failed courses and receive a new grade. Solution Conclusion: Therefore, for the time being, our school should continue to allow students to retake previously failed courses and receive a new grade. Premise: Admittedly, the practice of allowing students to retake a class they previously failed and receive a new grade is controversial. Premise: But the mission of any school or university is to educate their students, and allowing students to retake courses supports this mission. The arguement is reasonably strong. A practice is stated as being controversial, but then a reasonable statement is made in support of the practice. The conclusion then advocates continuing an already-existing practice. As no viable reason has been presented against the practice, and a viable reason has been given for the practice, it is not unreasonable to conclude that the practice should continue for the time being. 🚨 While it was once believed that the health of the human body was dependent on a balance between four substances, or “humors,” the advent of medical research in the nineteenth century led to the understanding that this view was both simplistic and inaccurate. Thereafter, physicians— especially those in Europe, such as Edward Jenner—began formulating theories of treatment that are now the foundation of modern medicine. Solution Premise: While it was once believed that the health of the human body was dependent on a balance between four substances, or “humors,” the advent of medical research in the nineteenth century led to the understanding that this view was both simplistic and inaccurate. Premise: Thereafter, physicians—especially those in Europe, such as Edward Jenner—began formulating theories of treatment that are now the foundation of modern medicine. The stimulus is only a fact set and does not contain a conclusion. Therefore, there is no argument present and no evaluation of argument validity can be made. 💡Cookiecutter sharks feed on a variety of fishes and mammals by gouging round plugs of flesh out of larger animals. Although attacks on humans are documented, they are rare, and thus these sharks are rightly classified as only a minor threat to people. As many fishes that are not a threat to humans are not endangered, there should be no objection to the new ocean exploration and drilling project, which threatens a cookiecutter shark breeding ground. Solution Conclusion: There should be no objection to the new ocean exploration and drilling project. Premise: Cookiecutter sharks feed on a variety of fishes and mammals by gouging round plugs of flesh out of larger animals. Premise: Although attacks on humans are documented, they are rare. Premise: Thus these sharks are rightly classified as only a minor threat to people. Premise: As many fishes that are not a threat to humans are not endangered. Premise: [The project] threatens a cookiecutter shark breeding ground. The argument is weak. The author simply notes that many fishes that are not threats are not endangered, but no information is given that establishes whether the cookiecutter shark is endangered. Without that information, the author cannot conclude that there should be no objection to the new drilling project.
summary: Steps Decide upon the broad topic Read it VERY carefully to capture all the details Identify the problems between each part of the stimulus Don’t panic if you don’t understand a terminology. Focus on understanding the how the ideas connect with each other Speaker Identifiers Pay attention to the speaker. Often it will help in identifying the broad topic addressed in the stimulus Business: Manager, Executive, Business Plan Politics: Politician, Editorial, Lawmaker, Activist Science/Medicine: Doctor, Physician, Scientist, Researcher Some identifiers are confusing or too broad.
title: CR-2 The Stimulus

contents: Read all answer choices. Often the test-maker puts an attractive wrong choice before the right one Assumptions: Never assume anything blatantly Assumptions under common sense are alright. E.g. The sky is sometimes cloudy TV shows are transmitted as signals to homes Structure of a CR Question 🚨 Read the parts in same order as given in the question 🚨 Primary Objectives of CR 🚨 Determine whether the stimulus contains an argument or only a set of facts If it contains an argument identify the conclusion of the argument. If it contains a fact set, examine each fact Determine if the argument is strong/weak Do not generalize! Read closely and know precisely what the author said Read & Identify the Question Stem type Pre-phase Answers: Mentally formulate your answer to the question stem after reading it Always read all 5 answer choices Separate the answer choices into Contenders/Losers. Decide the answer amongst the contenders If all the answer choices appear to be Losers, return to the stimulus and re-evaluate the argument
summary: Read all answer choices. Often the test-maker puts an attractive wrong choice before the right one Assumptions: Never assume anything blatantly Assumptions under common sense are alright. E.g. The sky is sometimes cloudy TV shows are transmitted as signals to homes Structure of a CR Question 🚨 Read the parts in same order as given in the question 🚨 Primary Objectives of CR 🚨 Determine whether the stimulus contains an argument or only a set of facts If it contains an argument identify the conclusion of the argument.
title: CR-1 General Tips & Question Structure

contents:
This is a sample post intended to test the followings:
Default hero image. Different shortcodes. Alert The following alerts are available in this theme.
This is sample alert with type="success". This is sample alert with type="danger". This is sample alert with type="warning". This is sample alert with type="info". This is sample alert with type="dark". This is sample alert with type="primary". This is sample alert with type="secondary". Image A sample image without any attribute. contents:
This file exists solely to respond to /search URL with the related search layout template.
No content shown here is rendered, all content is based in the template layouts/page/search.html
Setting a very low sitemap priority will tell search engines this is not important content.
This implementation uses Fusejs, jquery and mark.js
Initial setup Search depends on additional output content type of JSON in config.toml ``` [outputs] home = [“HTML”, “JSON”] ```
Searching additional fileds To search additional fields defined in front matter, you must add it in 2 places.
Edit layouts/_default/index.JSON This exposes the values in /index.json i.e. add category ``` … “contents”:{{ .Content | plainify | jsonify }} {{ if .Params.tags }}, “tags”:{{ .Params.tags | jsonify }}{{end}}, “categories” : {{ .Params.categories | jsonify }}, … ```
Edit fuse.js options to Search static/js/search.js ``` keys: [ “title”, “contents”, “tags”, “categories” ] ```
